task: Demonstrate products to clients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single product demonstration to a client group, typically 20–40 minutes including setup, live demo, and Q&A.
robot_success_rationale: Requires natural language persuasion, dynamic social interaction, reading client reactions, adapting messaging, and handling unpredictable questions — far beyond current robot communication and social cognition capabilities.

task: Load logs or wood onto trucks, trailers, or railroad cars, by hand or using loaders or winches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a full truck/trailer/railcar with logs. A competent worker using equipment typically takes 30–60 minutes depending on load size and method.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy log loaders, winches, or vehicles in unstructured outdoor/industrial environments. Humanoid robots cannot operate this specialized heavy machinery, handle massive irregular logs, or navigate rough logging terrain safely.

task: Drive trucks, following established routes, through residential streets or alleys or through business or industrial areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one route/shift segment. A typical residential/commercial delivery or service truck route takes ~1–2 hours of driving time.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot physically operate a truck in traffic. L4 AVs exist but are narrow-domain systems, not general-purpose humanoid robots. Unstructured residential/alley/industrial routes add further complexity beyond any current deployment.

task: Rebuild or replace linings, wire sections, or missing teeth to repair dentures.
human_time_rationale: One denture repair instance (e.g., relining, wire adjustment, or tooth replacement) by a dental lab technician; typically 30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, dental-specific tool use (trimmers, curing lights, articulator), precise material application on a small irregular object, and expert judgment — far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Perform inspections of forests or forest nurseries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single forest plot or nursery section. A competent forester typically takes 1–2 hours to survey, assess health, document findings, and note hazards.
robot_success_rationale: Forests are highly unstructured outdoor terrain with dense vegetation, slopes, mud, and fallen debris. Current humanoid robots lack the robust outdoor locomotion, botanical perception, and domain-specific judgment needed. Success probability is very low.

task: Conserve and preserve manuscripts, records, and other artifacts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = conserving/preserving a single artifact (e.g., one manuscript). A competent conservator spends ~60–120 min on assessment, cleaning, stabilization, and housing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, expert material judgment, delicate chemical treatment, and nuanced tactile/visual perception far beyond current humanoid capability. Risk of irreversible damage is unacceptably high.

task: Stop automobile and pedestrian traffic on bridges, and lower automobile gates prior to moving bridges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one bridge opening event. A tender activates signals, lowers gates, and confirms traffic/pedestrian clearance before initiating the bridge move; ~5 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized bridge control panels, verifying clearance of traffic and pedestrians across a wide area, and coordinating with marine/road traffic — far beyond current humanoid robot generalization, perception range, and certified authority to act.

task: Dress in comical clown costumes and makeup, and perform comedy routines to entertain audiences.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one clown performance set, including costume/makeup and a typical comedy routine for an audience, estimated at ~60 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires applying elaborate makeup, dressing in costume, improvising social/comedic timing, audience reading, physical comedy, and emotional expression — far beyond any 2026 robot's generalization, social intelligence, or fine dexterous makeup application.

task: Prepare hazardous material for removal or storage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single batch/container of hazardous material (donning PPE, identifying, labeling, packaging, and securing it per regulatory standards).
robot_success_rationale: Requires regulatory compliance judgment, fine dexterous handling of hazmat containers/seals, PPE donning, chemical identification, and safety-critical decision-making in unstructured environments — far beyond current robot generalization and reliability.

task: Repair machines, equipment, or structures, using tools such as hammers, hoists, saws, drills, wrenches, or equipment such as precision measuring instruments or electrical or electronic testing devices.
human_time_rationale: One repair instance on a typical machine/equipment/structure; competent maintenance worker averages ~1 hour including diagnosis, tool use, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying faults, selecting correct tools, applying precise force with hammers/drills/wrenches, interpreting test instruments, and adapting to unpredictable failure modes — far beyond current general-purpose robot generalization and dexterous tool-use.

task: Apply cement to backs of tiles and press tiles into place, aligning them with layout marks or joints of previously laid tile.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying cement to one tile and pressing it into place. A skilled tiler does this in ~2–4 minutes including alignment checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise trowel cement application, controlled pressure, fine spatial alignment to joints/marks, and tactile feedback on uneven surfaces. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and perception precision for reliable tile setting.

task: Examine damaged vehicle to determine extent of structural, body, mechanical, electrical, or interior damage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one damaged vehicle end-to-end; a competent auto damage appraiser typically spends 30–60 minutes assessing all systems.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert multi-domain diagnosis (structural, mechanical, electrical, interior), flexible navigation around/under a vehicle, nuanced visual/tactile judgment of damage severity, and professional-grade reporting. Far beyond current robot generalization and diagnostic reasoning capabilities.

task: Plan and implement revegetation of disturbed sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = planning and implementing revegetation for one disturbed site (~0.5–1 acre). Includes site assessment, species selection, soil prep, seeding/planting. A full day's work for a competent restoration specialist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert ecological judgment, site-specific planning, irregular outdoor terrain, tool use (tilling, seeding equipment), and multi-step planting. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capabilities in unstructured outdoor environments.

task: Move levers to activate traffic signals, navigation lights, and alarms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving a single lever to activate one signal/light/alarm. A competent worker locates and throws the lever in under a minute.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids can grasp and pull levers in structured settings, but arbitrary lever types, orientations, required force profiles, and correct identification of the right lever in a real control panel reduce reliability significantly.

task: Wrap and secure goods using pads, packing paper, containers, or straps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wrapping and securing a single item/package. A competent worker handles padding, paper, or straps on a typical goods item in ~8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires adaptive grasping of flexible/deformable materials (pads, paper, straps), precise tensioning, and varied object geometries. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterity for consistent wrapping, folding, and strap-fastening across diverse goods.

task: Work on high ceilings, using scaffolding or other tools, such as stilts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a typical elevated work session (e.g., painting or finishing one ceiling section). Setup, work, and teardown of scaffolding/stilts averages ~1 hour.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot safely erect/use scaffolding or walk on stilts; elevated unstructured work demands extreme balance, dexterity, and fall-risk management far beyond current capabilities. No commercial deployment exists.

task: Mark livestock to identify ownership and grade, using brands, tags, paint, or tattoos.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking one animal (tag, brand, paint, or tattoo). A competent handler takes ~5–10 min including restraint, application, and release.
robot_success_rationale: Live animals are unpredictable and resist restraint. Precise marking (tattooing, branding, tagging ears) requires force-sensitive dexterity on moving targets. No current humanoid robot is capable of safe, reliable livestock handling in field conditions.

task: Attach farm implements such as plows, discs, sprayers, or harvesters to tractors, using bolts and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching one implement to a tractor. An experienced operator typically spends 15–35 min aligning, hitching, pinning, and bolting a single implement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating outdoors on uneven terrain, heavy-force bolting with large fasteners, precise three-point hitch alignment, and handling massive implements. Current humanoids lack the strength, reach, outdoor mobility, and tool-use generalization needed.

task: Check raw ingredients for maturity or stability for processing, and finished products for safety, quality, and nutritional value.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a batch of raw ingredients and finished products in a food processing context; a competent QA worker typically spends ~20 min per batch check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal sensory assessment (smell, taste, texture, color, lab testing), nuanced judgment on maturity/nutritional value, and generalization across diverse products. Current robots lack taste/smell sensors and reliable qualitative food QA reasoning.

task: Weigh crop-filled containers, and record weights and other identifying information.
human_time_rationale: One instance = weighing a single container, reading the scale, and logging weight + ID info. A competent worker completes this in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied farm environments, handling bulky/heavy irregular containers, reading a scale display, and logging data. Perception of scale readouts and unstructured outdoor terrain limit current robots significantly.

task: Perform other adjunctive therapies or treatment techniques in addition to massage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single adjunctive therapy session (e.g., hot/cold stone, cupping, stretching, hydrotherapy) performed on one client, typically 20–45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced tactile perception, safe force modulation on human body, clinical judgment, and adaptive dexterity. Current humanoids lack the fine touch sensitivity, compliance control, and therapeutic decision-making needed for safe, acceptable adjunctive therapy.

task: Research, design, evaluate, install, operate, or maintain mechanical products, equipment, systems or processes to meet requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single mechanical engineering task cycle (e.g., evaluating or inspecting a system/component). Ranges widely; 120 min is a reasonable median for a discrete unit of work.
robot_success_rationale: This task demands high-level cognitive reasoning, creative design, complex tool use, and domain expertise. Current humanoid robots lack abstract reasoning, engineering judgment, CAD/simulation capability, and autonomous decision-making for open-ended engineering problems.

task: Operate or tend equipment used in agricultural production, such as tractors, combines, and irrigation equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one operational shift (e.g., tractor plowing or combine harvesting a field). A typical shift for agricultural equipment operation runs ~4 hours of active field work.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate tractors, combines, or irrigation equipment in real field conditions. These require seated cab operation, specialized controls, outdoor rough terrain, and complex judgment. Autonomous ag vehicles exist but are separate narrow systems, not general-purpose humanoids.

task: Inspect property, examining its general condition, type of construction, age, and other characteristics, to decide if it is a good insurance risk.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single property (e.g., a house). A competent insurance inspector typically spends 30–60 minutes walking through, photographing, and assessing a property.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied indoor/outdoor environments, identifying subtle structural defects, assessing construction quality, estimating age, and making a holistic risk judgment. Current robots lack the generalization, expert domain knowledge integration, and reliable multi-environment mobility needed for autonomous underwriting-quality inspection.

task: Assess the condition of clients' hands, remove dead skin, and massage hands.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full hand treatment for one client: assessment, exfoliation/dead skin removal, and massage. Typical manicure hand prep takes ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine tactile sensitivity to assess skin condition, safe exfoliation of delicate skin, and therapeutic massage with nuanced pressure control. Well beyond current humanoid dexterity and compliant force-control on human tissue.

task: Maintain and guard stations in bridges to check waterways for boat traffic.
human_time_rationale: One instance is a full shift (8 hours) manning a bridge station, continuously monitoring waterway traffic, operating controls, and enforcing regulations.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained vigilance, communication with vessels, operating specialized bridge controls, and nuanced judgment over hours. Current humanoids lack the perception, situational awareness, communication ability, and regulatory decision-making for autonomous bridge tender duties.

task: Clean work area.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tidying and wiping down a single workstation/bench. A competent worker clears clutter, wipes surfaces, and disposes of waste in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep, wipe flat surfaces, and move common objects, but varied clutter, contextual judgment (what to keep vs. discard), and awkward tool/chemical handling reduce reliability significantly in unstructured work areas.

task: Raise drawbridges and observe passage of water traffic or lower drawbridges and raise automobile gates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full raise/lower cycle for a vessel passage: lower gates, stop traffic, raise bridge, wait for vessel, lower bridge, reopen. Typically 8–12 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a specialized control booth with custom panels, radio communication with vessel operators, monitoring multi-lane traffic, and coordinating safety gates — far beyond current humanoid generalization and tool-use capabilities.

task: Set up equipment that automatically detects area radiation deviations and test detection equipment to ensure its accuracy.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up one radiation detection unit and running a calibration/accuracy test. A competent health physicist or rad tech typically takes 30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise cable routing, powering specialized instruments, interpreting calibration software, and using calibrated radiation sources — highly specialized tool-use and domain-specific perception far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Inspect loads to ensure that cargo is secure.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single loaded truck/trailer. A competent worker visually checks straps, blocks, and weight distribution in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating around a loaded vehicle, visually assessing strap tension, weight balance, and tie-down integrity. Current robots lack reliable force-sensing, nuanced judgment on load security, and reliable locomotion around cluttered dock environments.

task: Appraise trees for certain characteristics, such as twist, rot, and heavy limb growth, and gauge amount and direction of lean, to determine how to control the direction of a tree's fall with the least damage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = appraising a single tree. A competent faller walks around the tree, checks trunk, limbs, lean, and rot, and forms a felling plan — typically 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating uneven forest terrain, multi-modal 3D perception of subtle wood defects (rot, twist, lean), and integrating findings into a safe felling plan — well beyond current robot generalization and outdoor mobility limits.

task: Perform routine engine maintenance on motorboats, such as changing oil and filters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full routine maintenance session on one motorboat engine (oil drain, filter swap, inspection). A competent marine mechanic typically takes 45–75 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working in cramped, wet, marine engine compartments; loosening corroded fittings; handling fluids; identifying boat-specific components. Beyond current humanoid dexterity, perception, and generalization in unstructured nautical environments.

task: Trim off the tops and limbs of trees, using chainsaws, delimbers, or axes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = topping/delimbing a single felled tree. A skilled logger using a chainsaw typically completes this in 10–30 minutes depending on tree size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a chainsaw or axe on irregular, heavy, outdoor terrain with unpredictable branch positions, significant forces, and serious safety hazards. No current humanoid robot can wield cutting tools at this scale reliably outdoors.

task: Clean and lubricate equipment, and make minor repairs and adjustments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning, lubricating, and making minor repairs/adjustments to one piece of equipment. A competent technician typically takes 30–60 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step dexterity (applying lubricant, tightening fasteners, adjusting components), tool use, and diagnostic judgment across varied equipment types. Current robots lack reliable generalized tool use and repair reasoning for unstructured maintenance tasks.

task: Use rotary abrasive wheels to shape and smooth nails or artificial extensions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shaping and smoothing all nails on one hand using a rotary abrasive wheel. A skilled nail technician typically takes 6–10 minutes per hand.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, precise force control near delicate skin/tissue, real-time tactile feedback, and millimeter-level tool manipulation. Current humanoid robots lack the precision and safe compliance for this task.

task: Work as a member of a team, rotating between chain saw operation and skidder operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one rotation shift segment (felling a tree with chainsaw then skidding it to landing). A competent logger cycles through both roles in ~45 min per tree.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a chainsaw safely in unstructured forest terrain AND driving a skidder — both beyond current humanoid capability. Outdoor rough terrain, heavy machinery operation, and teamwork coordination all severely limit success.

task: Install mechanical linkages and actuators, using tensiometers to verify tension of cables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a set of mechanical linkages/actuators and verifying cable tension on a single assembly (e.g., aircraft control surface, industrial machine). A competent mechanic typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise routing and fastening of cables/linkages, fine torque control, and correctly operating a tensiometer to read and verify tension — all demanding dexterity, tool-use generalization, and interpretive judgment far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Diagnose and repair furnace or air conditioning systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and repairing a single HVAC system fault. A competent technician typically spends 60–120 minutes on diagnosis, parts retrieval, and repair.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading diagnostic codes, using multimeters/manifold gauges, handling refrigerant, fine electrical work, and contextual fault reasoning. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and unstructured reasoning capabilities.

task: Manually install structural assemblies or signal crane operators to position assemblies for joining.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning and securing a single structural assembly (e.g., a steel beam or panel) by hand or by signaling a crane operator until it is ready for joining.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy-load handling, outdoor/elevated unstructured terrain, crane signaling communication, and high-precision structural alignment — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities. Safety constraints alone preclude autonomous deployment.

task: Spray fertilizer or pesticide solutions to control insects, fungus and weed growth, and diseases, using hand sprayers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = spraying a typical residential yard or garden plot (~1/4 acre) with a hand sprayer; a competent worker takes ~30–60 min including mixing and coverage.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor uneven terrain navigation, precise targeted spraying, chemical handling, nozzle control, and coverage judgment. Current humanoids lack outdoor agricultural dexterity, spray tool integration, and reliable terrain mobility for this task.

task: Drive trucks to haul crops, supplies, tools, or farm workers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one haul trip on a farm (loading, driving, unloading). Typical farm truck trip across fields or to a storage facility averages ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot drive trucks on public or farm roads. L4 AVs are narrow-domain systems, not general-purpose humanoids. Operating steering, pedals, and shifting in unstructured farm terrain is beyond 2026 frontier capability.

task: Provide groundskeeping services, such as landscaping or snow removal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = maintaining grounds of a typical property (mowing, edging, trimming, raking) or one snow-removal event; ~2 hours for a competent groundskeeper.
robot_success_rationale: Groundskeeping requires operating specialized outdoor equipment (mowers, snowblowers, plows), navigating varied terrain, and performing diverse tasks. Humanoids cannot drive/operate these vehicles; outdoor unstructured environments and heavy tools make autonomous completion extremely unlikely in 2026.

task: Tell jokes, perform comic dances, songs and skits, impersonate mannerisms and voices of others, contort face, and use other devices to amuse audiences.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a typical comedy performance set (jokes, songs, skits, impressions). A club/variety act typically runs 30–60 min; 45 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social perception, improvisation, comedic timing, facial expressiveness, voice acting, and audience interaction — all far beyond current robots. No humanoid can generate humor, read crowd reactions, or credibly impersonate voices/mannerisms.

task: Operate forklifts to deliver materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one forklift delivery trip (pick up load, travel to destination, place load). Typical warehouse trip takes ~15–25 minutes for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate forklifts in live warehouse environments. Forklift operation requires seated vehicle control, pedals, and levers — beyond current humanoid dexterity/mobility. Autonomous forklifts exist but are narrow, fixed-route systems, not general-purpose humanoids.

task: Operate vehicles or powered equipment, such as mowers, tractors, twin-axle vehicles, snow blowers, chainsaws, electric clippers, sod cutters, or pruning saws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one typical outdoor equipment operation session (e.g., mowing a standard lawn or clearing a path). Competent worker completes in ~45 min on average across equipment types.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate ride-on mowers, tractors, chainsaws, or twin-axle vehicles autonomously in unstructured outdoor environments. Rough terrain, vehicle controls, and highly variable conditions are well beyond 2026 frontier capability.

task: Turn valves to increase or decrease water levels in locks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = turning a valve to achieve the target water level in one lock chamber. A skilled lock operator adjusts the valve and monitors the level, typically taking ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Lock valves are large, industrial, often non-standard mechanisms in wet/outdoor environments. Robot must locate, grasp, and apply sustained torque correctly, then monitor water level feedback — challenging perception, tool-use, and generalization for current humanoids.

task: Load or unload trucks or help others with loading or unloading, using special loading-related equipment or other equipment as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading or unloading one truck. A competent worker with equipment (pallet jack, forklift) typically takes 30–60 min depending on cargo volume.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids cannot operate forklifts or pallet jacks in traffic/loading docks. Manual unloading of varied, heavy, or awkwardly packed items is highly unstructured. Limited dexterity, payload capacity, and tool-use generalization drag success low.

task: Perform humorous and serious interpretations of emotions, actions, and situations, using body movements, facial expressions, and gestures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single short performance (humorous or serious) of one emotion/action/situation, typically 1–5 minutes for a mime or actor.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced facial expressions, culturally legible gestures, comedic timing, and emotional authenticity — all far beyond current humanoid robots' expressive range, motor subtlety, and social intelligence.

task: Adjust, repair, rework, or replace parts or assemblies to ensure proper operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and fixing a single faulty part or sub-assembly (e.g. a misaligned bracket, worn component, or failed fitting). A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fault diagnosis, tool selection, fine dexterous manipulation, and judgment-driven rework across highly varied parts. Current humanoids lack the perception, force-feedback precision, and adaptive reasoning needed for unstructured repair tasks.

task: Stop saw engines, pull cutting bars from cuts, and run to safety as tree falls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stopping the saw, pulling the bar from the cut, and retreating to a safe zone during a single tree-felling event. A competent logger completes this in ~60–90 seconds.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a running chainsaw (extreme tool hazard), rapid reactive locomotion over rough forest terrain, and real-time fall-trajectory prediction — all well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities. High injury/failure risk.

task: Cover surfaces such as windows, doors, or sidewalks to protect from splashing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = covering surfaces (windows, doors, sidewalks) in a typical work area before a painting/sealing job; ~20 min to lay/tape plastic sheeting or drop cloths.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling large, floppy materials (plastic sheeting, drop cloths, tape), precise placement around irregular surfaces, and sustained bimanual coordination. Current robots struggle with deformable large sheets, tape application, and varied outdoor geometry.

task: Start agitators, shakers, conveyors, pumps, or centrifuge machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting a single machine (e.g., flipping switches, pressing buttons, turning dials, verifying startup). A competent worker completes this in ~3 minutes including a quick check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating correct controls (buttons, switches, dials) on varied industrial equipment, fine manipulation, and reading panel labels. Perception and dexterity are improving but industrial control panels are highly varied and unstructured, limiting reliability.

task: Secure steel cables or chains to logs for dragging by tractors or for pulling by cable yarding systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rigging one log with cables/chains. A skilled choker setter typically takes 5–12 minutes per log depending on terrain and log size.
robot_success_rationale: Extremely unstructured outdoor terrain (slash, mud, steep slopes), heavy steel cables/chains require high force and dexterity, logs vary wildly in size/position, and safe rigging demands expert judgment. Far beyond current robot capability.

task: Place and connect control cables to electronically controlled units, using hand tools, ring locks, cotter keys, threaded connectors, turnbuckles, or related devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = routing and securing a single control cable run to an electronically controlled unit, including fastening connectors, turnbuckles, cotter keys, etc. A skilled technician typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for threading connectors, installing cotter keys, and adjusting turnbuckles in tight, variable spaces. Current humanoids lack the precision, force sensing, and tool-use generalization for reliable autonomous cable routing and mechanical fastening.

task: Install accessories in swaging machines, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one set of accessories (dies, tooling, guides) in a swaging machine. A skilled operator typically takes 10–20 minutes including alignment and hand-tool work.
robot_success_rationale: Swaging machine accessory installation requires precise force-controlled torquing, tight-tolerance die alignment, and dexterous hand-tool use in constrained spaces. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tool-handling precision, and domain-specific perception needed.

task: Diagnose performance problems by reviewing reports or documentation from customers or field engineers or by inspecting malfunctioning or damaged products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reviewing one set of reports/documentation or inspecting one malfunctioning product to reach a diagnosis. A competent engineer typically takes 30–90 minutes per case.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting complex technical documents, synthesizing customer feedback, and applying deep domain reasoning to diagnose faults. Current robots lack cognitive reasoning, language comprehension of nuanced technical reports, and diagnostic judgment — far beyond physical capability limits.

task: Examine animals to detect illness, injury, or disease, and to check physical characteristics, such as rate of weight gain.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining one animal. A competent vet/farmer checks vitals, body condition, and behavioral cues in ~10–20 minutes per animal.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced multi-modal assessment (palpation, visual inspection, behavioral interpretation, auscultation) on an uncooperative, moving animal. Current robots lack veterinary sensing suites, safe animal-handling dexterity, and clinical judgment to meet acceptable quality standards.

task: Clean facilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single facility area (e.g., restroom, hallway, or office floor). A competent janitor typically takes ~45 min per area including sweeping, mopping, wiping, and restocking.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep/mop flat floors reasonably well, but struggle with restrooms (toilet scrubbing, restocking), unstructured clutter, varied surfaces, and multi-step sequences across a full facility area without human intervention.

task: Mix or blend ingredients by starting machines and mixing for specified times.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one mixing/blending batch. Human loads ingredients, starts machine, monitors, and stops it. Typical industrial or kitchen batch takes ~5–10 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Robot must measure/load varied ingredients, operate machine controls, and monitor consistency. Perception of blend completion and handling of diverse ingredient types/containers limits reliability. Generalization across machine types is weak.

task: Follow strict safety procedures when handling toxic materials to avoid contamination.
human_time_rationale: One instance = handling a single toxic material sample or container following full safety protocol: donning PPE, handling, decontaminating, and documenting. Typical duration ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires donning specialized PPE, fine dexterous manipulation of hazardous containers, leak detection, decontamination procedures, and strict protocol adherence. Current robots lack reliable dexterity, safety-critical judgment, and PPE self-donning capability.

task: Assemble or set up displays, furniture, or products in store space, using colors, lights, pictures, or other accessories to display the product.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a single retail display or furniture arrangement in a store section. A competent visual merchandiser typically spends 60–120 minutes per display setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative aesthetic judgment, complex multi-step assembly of varied furniture/props, manipulation of lights and décor accessories, and spatial design reasoning. Far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and planning capabilities in unstructured retail environments.

task: Prepare nail cuticles with water and oil, using cuticle knives to push back cuticles and scissors or nippers to trim cuticles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing cuticles on both hands for one client. A skilled nail technician spends ~8–12 minutes soaking, pushing back, and trimming all ten cuticles.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme sub-millimeter precision with sharp tools on delicate living tissue. Current humanoid robots lack the fine dexterity, tactile feedback, and safety control needed; risk of injury is prohibitively high.

task: Install metal lath where plaster applications will be exposed to weather or water, or for curved or irregular surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing metal lath on one standard wall section (~30–50 sq ft). A skilled plasterer cuts, fits, and fastens lath in ~45 minutes including layout and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires cutting and bending sharp metal mesh, precise fastening into varied substrates, navigating irregular/curved surfaces, and outdoor/wet conditions. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and generalization capabilities.

task: Collect samples of air, water, gases, or solids to determine radioactivity levels of contamination.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a full set of samples (air, water, gas, or solid) at a single site. A competent technician spends ~30–60 min setting up equipment, collecting, labeling, and packaging samples.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized sampling tools (air pumps, swabs, water samplers), precise handling of contaminated materials, strict labeling/chain-of-custody protocols, and navigation in potentially hazardous/unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack the specialized end-effectors, tool-use generalization, and safety compliance needed.

task: Place supporting limbs or poles under felled trees to avoid splitting undersides, and to prevent logs from rolling.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning supports/poles under one felled tree. A skilled logger scouts, cuts/gathers limbs or poles, and places them under the log in ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor unstructured terrain navigation, heavy/awkward object handling (large poles/limbs), precise placement under a large log, and situational judgment. Far beyond current humanoid capability in a logging environment.

task: Install electrical assemblies and wiring in aircraft camera housings and memory cards or film in cameras, following blueprints and using hand tools and soldering equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wiring one aircraft camera housing and loading media (card/film). A skilled avionics technician typically takes 30–60 min per unit following blueprints.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine-dexterity soldering, reading blueprints, routing wires in tight aerospace housings, and media insertion — all in high-precision, safety-critical context. Far beyond current humanoid capability at acceptable quality.

task: Inspect completed installations and observe operations to ensure conformance to design and equipment specifications and compliance with operational, safety, or environmental standards.
human_time_rationale: One inspection instance covers a completed installation site or operational review; a competent inspector typically spends ~1 hour per site/unit checking specs, safety, and compliance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level judgment on multi-domain standards (design specs, safety codes, environmental regs), cross-referencing documentation, interpreting anomalies, and making compliance decisions — far beyond current robot perception and reasoning generalization.

task: Portray and interpret roles, using speech, gestures, and body movements, to entertain, inform, or instruct radio, film, television, or live audiences.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single scene or performance segment. A typical film/TV scene or live stage act runs ~5–120 min; median ~2 hours including prep, blocking, and delivery.
robot_success_rationale: Acting requires nuanced emotional expression, improvisation, vocal modulation, and authentic human presence. Current humanoids lack expressive faces, naturalistic speech, emotional intelligence, and the creative generalization needed for audience-acceptable performance.

task: Make photocopies of correspondence, documents, and other printed matter.
human_time_rationale: One instance = copying a single document (1–5 pages): place on glass or feed tray, select settings, retrieve copies. Typically 2–4 minutes for a competent office worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine paper handling, precise placement on copier glass, navigating copier UI/buttons, and retrieving output without jamming or misalignment. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous paper manipulation and copier UI interaction.

task: Segregate animals according to weight, age, color, and physical condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting one animal into the correct group. A trained handler visually assesses and physically guides one animal in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/farm mobility, live-animal handling (unpredictable movement), multi-modal assessment (weight via scale, visual color/condition scoring), and herding dexterity — well beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Pick up and deliver parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = picking up a part from a source location and delivering it to a destination; typical warehouse/factory run takes ~10 minutes including walking and handoff.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids can walk flat indoor terrain and grasp common parts reasonably well, but varied part shapes, cluttered environments, navigation to correct destination, and safe handoff reduce reliability significantly.

task: Operate towed machines such as seed drills or manure spreaders to plant, fertilize, dust, and spray crops.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single field pass/session with a towed implement (e.g., seeding or spreading one field). Typical field operation runs 2–4 hours for a mid-sized field.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a tractor/towed agricultural machinery on varied outdoor terrain. Humanoid robots cannot drive tractors in the field; this is heavy vehicle operation requiring specialized controls, not reachable by 2026 general-purpose humanoids.

task: Manipulate controls to set, activate, and adjust mechanisms on machinery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting/activating/adjusting controls on one machine for one job setup. A competent operator typically takes 3–7 minutes to configure, start, and tune a machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying diverse control types (knobs, levers, touchscreens, keypads), reading displays, and precise adjustment. Current robots struggle with varied UI generalization, fine rotary control, and confirming correct parameter values.

task: Collect, sort, distribute, or prepare mail, messages, or courier deliveries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a typical batch of mail/parcels for a workday (collect, sort, and distribute to recipients in one office or route). ~20 min for a moderate volume.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading addresses/labels (perception), navigating varied environments, handling irregular packages, and knowing recipient locations. Current robots struggle with unstructured multi-step workflows, label reading reliability, and navigating full building routes autonomously.

task: Shift animals between grazing areas to ensure that they have sufficient access to food.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving a herd from one grazing area to an adjacent one. A competent farmhand typically takes 30–60 minutes to gather, herd, and secure animals in the new area.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor rough-terrain locomotion, animal behavioral understanding, herding coordination, and gate/fence operation across large unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack the mobility, animal-interaction capability, and endurance for this task.

task: Perform duties related to livestock reproduction, such as breeding animals within appropriate timeframes, performing artificial inseminations, and helping with animal births.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one reproductive event (AI procedure, supervised breeding session, or assisted birth). A competent livestock worker spends ~30–90 min per event; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (AI catheter insertion, obstetric manipulation), biological judgment, animal-handling in unstructured pens, and force-sensitive touch far beyond current humanoid capability. No deployed system approaches this.

task: Move equipment, poultry, or livestock from one location to another, manually or using trucks or carts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving a batch of equipment, poultry, or livestock between two locations on a farm/facility. Typical trip (loading, transporting, unloading) takes ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving trucks/carts (humanoids cannot operate vehicles), herding live animals (highly unstructured), and navigating outdoor/farm terrain. Extreme generalization demands and animal-handling unpredictability make autonomous success near-zero.

task: Construct puppets and ventriloquist dummies, and sew accessory clothing, using hand tools and machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing one puppet/dummy with accessory clothing. A competent craftsperson typically takes 6–10 hours for a full puppet/dummy build including sewing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for carving, sculpting, detailed sewing, and tool use across highly varied materials and complex 3D forms. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities in unstructured craft settings.

task: Maintain soil stability and vegetation for non-grazing uses, such as wildlife habitats and outdoor recreation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one site visit/maintenance session covering a defined land parcel. Includes assessing soil, vegetation, erosion, and performing corrective actions (seeding, mulching, etc.). Typically 2 hours per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor terrain navigation, ecological assessment judgment, multi-tool use (seeding, mulching, erosion control), and adaptive decision-making across unstructured environments. Frontier humanoids lack the mobility, domain knowledge integration, and specialized tool operation needed.

task: Prepare doors for hardware installation, such as drilling holes to install locks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one door (marking, drilling bore hole and latch hole, chiseling mortise). A competent carpenter typically takes 20–40 minutes per door.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, marking, power-drill and chisel use, variable door materials/positions, and force control. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tool-use generalization, and spatial accuracy needed for acceptable carpentry-grade results.

task: Operate equipment that compresses collected refuse.
human_time_rationale: One instance = activating the compaction cycle on a refuse compactor (e.g., rear-loader packer blade). A competent operator triggers and monitors one compression cycle, ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Refuse compactors require operating vehicle-mounted hydraulic controls or industrial panel controls in a dynamic outdoor environment. Humanoids cannot drive the truck, reach/operate cab controls safely, or handle the unstructured refuse-loading context reliably.

task: Dismantle machines, equipment, or devices to access and remove defective parts, using hoists, cranes, hand tools, or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling one machine/device to remove a defective part. A competent mechanic typically spends 30–90 minutes depending on complexity; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sequencing novel disassembly steps, switching between hand tools/power tools/hoists, high-force torque application, and diagnosing which part is defective — far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Measure and cut openings in panels or tiles for electrical outlets, windows, vents, plumbing, or other fixtures, using keyhole saws or other cutting tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring, marking, and cutting a single opening in a panel or tile. A skilled tradesperson typically takes 8–15 minutes per opening depending on material and fixture type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, marking, and fine dexterous tool-use (keyhole saw, jigsaw) on varied surfaces in constrained spaces. Current humanoids lack the fine motor precision, force control, and contextual judgment needed for accurate cuts without damaging panels.

task: Install horizontal and vertical metal or wooden studs to frames so that wallboard can be attached to interior walls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = framing one standard interior wall section (~10–12 ft). A skilled framer measures, cuts, and installs top/bottom plates and studs in ~1–1.5 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measuring, marking, cutting lumber/metal with power tools, lifting and positioning long members, and nailing/screwing at correct spacing — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and spatial planning in unstructured construction sites.

task: Cut metal or wood framing and trim to size, using cutting tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring, marking, and cutting a single piece of framing/trim to spec. A skilled worker takes ~5–10 min including setup, measurement, and cut.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, marking, and safe operation of power saws (miter, circular, or jigsaw) in unstructured construction environments. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use for powered cutting equipment, fine spatial reasoning for fit, and construction-site mobility.

task: Monitor functioning of equipment and make necessary modifications to ensure system operates in conformance with specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring round plus any minor adjustments on a piece of equipment; a competent technician typically takes 20–40 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading gauges/displays, diagnosing deviations, and making precise corrective adjustments across varied equipment types. Current robots lack reliable multi-modal diagnostics, contextual fault reasoning, and the dexterous tool-use needed for modifications.

task: Treat nails to repair or improve strength and resilience by wrapping.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wrapping all nails on one hand (or a single damaged nail). A nail technician takes ~10–20 min for a full wrapping treatment on one hand.
robot_success_rationale: Extreme fine dexterity required: cutting/applying thin nail wrap fabric, smoothing, trimming to precise nail contours on a moving human hand. Far beyond current humanoid manipulation precision and tactile sensitivity.

task: Decontaminate objects by cleaning with soap or solvents or by abrading with wire brushes, buffing wheels, or sandblasting machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = decontaminating a single object (e.g., a mid-sized industrial part). A competent worker typically spends 15–25 minutes selecting method, applying soap/solvent, or operating abrasive equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool-switching (brushes, buffing wheels, sandblasting nozzles), force-modulated abrasion, and coverage judgment on irregular surfaces. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use generalization and fine force control for such multi-method decontamination tasks.

task: Hang dry lines to wall moldings to guide positioning of main runners.
human_time_rationale: One instance = hanging dry lines across one room's wall moldings. A competent carpenter snaps chalk/string lines and secures them in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measuring, attaching taut string to narrow wall moldings at exact heights, and maintaining tension across a room. Fine dexterity, accurate spatial planning, and tool use on irregular surfaces exceed current humanoid capabilities.

task: Perform duties, such as taking care of plants or straightening magazines to maintain lobby or reception area.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single tidying pass of a lobby/reception area, covering plants and reading materials. A competent worker typically completes this in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Basic locomotion and surface cleaning are feasible, but plant care (watering, pruning judgment) and neatly straightening/stacking varied magazines require dexterous manipulation and contextual judgment that current humanoids handle inconsistently.

task: Clean job sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single job site (e.g., construction/trade site). A competent worker sweeps, picks up debris, and tidies tools/materials in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Job sites are highly unstructured with debris, uneven terrain, heavy/irregular objects, and changing hazards. Robots handle flat indoor cleaning well but struggle with outdoor rough terrain, varied debris types, and site-specific judgment calls.

task: Perform general cleaning of buildings or properties.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one standard room or zone (e.g., office, hallway, restroom). A competent janitor typically spends ~60 min per area including sweeping, mopping, wiping, and trash removal.
robot_success_rationale: Flat-surface sweeping/mopping is feasible, but multi-surface navigation, emptying trash, wiping varied fixtures, handling doors, and adapting to clutter in unstructured environments remain significant challenges for current humanoids.

task: Perform routine maintenance, such as inspecting drives, motors, or belts, checking fluid levels, replacing filters, or doing other preventive maintenance actions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a standard preventive maintenance round on a single machine/unit, including inspecting drives/motors/belts, checking fluids, and replacing filters. Typically 30–60 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse dexterous actions (unscrewing caps, reading dipsticks, tensioning belts, swapping filters), tool use, and contextual judgment across varied equipment. Current humanoids lack reliable multi-step tool manipulation, fluid-level reading, and generalization across machine types.

task: Perform maintenance duties, such as sweeping, painting, and yard work to keep facilities clean and in order.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a standard maintenance shift unit (e.g., sweeping a hallway, painting a wall section, or mowing a yard). Combined median across sub-tasks is ~60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Sweeping is feasible (~50%), but painting requires precise tool handling and surface prep (~10%), and yard work involves unstructured outdoor terrain and varied tools (~10%). Aggregated across all three sub-tasks, success is low.

task: Inspect the condition of underwater steel or wood structures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single underwater structure (e.g., a pier section, piling cluster, or hull segment). A diver or ROV operator typically takes 60–120 min per structure.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate underwater. Specialized underwater ROVs exist but are narrow systems, not general-purpose humanoids. Frontier humanoids lack waterproofing, buoyancy control, and underwater perception required for this task.

task: Turn valves or move controls to admit, drain, separate, filter, clarify, mix, or transfer materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = operating one valve or control to initiate a material transfer step; a competent operator locates, turns, and confirms the action in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Turning industrial valves varies widely in torque, handle shape, and location. Robots can grasp and rotate common handles ~40–60%, but confined spaces, high-torque valves, and confirming flow outcome reduce overall success significantly.

task: Mark logs for identification.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking one log with an identification tag/paint/stamp. A worker grabs a marker or tool, applies the mark, and moves on — roughly 2–4 minutes per log.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/uneven terrain mobility, grasping a marking tool, locating the correct surface on a heavy irregular log, and applying a legible mark — challenging perception, dexterity, and unstructured environment for current humanoids.

task: Prepare and analyze organ, tissue, and cell samples to identify toxicity, bacteria, or microorganisms or to study cell structure.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and analyzing a single batch of biological samples (e.g., tissue sectioning, staining, mounting, microscopic analysis). A competent lab technician typically takes 60–120 minutes end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (microtome sectioning, pipetting, slide mounting), sterile technique, complex multi-step lab protocols, and expert visual interpretation of microscopic findings — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Operate and maintain on-air and production audio equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single broadcast shift or production session. A competent audio engineer typically spends ~60 min operating/maintaining equipment per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine manipulation of mixing boards, knobs, patch bays, and cables; real-time auditory monitoring and judgment; complex tool-use in a specialized environment. Current humanoids lack the perception, dexterity, and domain-specific autonomy for this.

task: Inspect trucks prior to beginning routes to ensure safe operating condition.
human_time_rationale: One pre-trip inspection of a single truck; a competent driver walks around the vehicle checking tires, lights, fluids, brakes, mirrors, and cab controls — typically 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locomotion around a large outdoor vehicle, fine visual inspection of tires/lights/fluids/brakes, under-vehicle checks, and cab entry to test controls. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor mobility, under-vehicle access, and standardized truck-inspection protocols to meet regulatory acceptance standards.

task: Dismount garbage trucks to collect garbage and remount trucks to ride to the next collection point.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dismount, collect garbage at one stop, and remount. A sanitation worker completes this cycle in ~1–3 minutes per stop.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safely dismounting/remounting a moving truck, navigating variable outdoor terrain, handling diverse bins/bags, and coordinating with a moving vehicle — far beyond current humanoid generalization and outdoor mobility limits.

task: Inspect, maintain, and repair equipment, machinery, buildings, pens, yards, and fences.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full routine inspection-and-maintenance round of a facility (equipment, buildings, pens, fences). A competent worker typically takes 1–2 hours per shift walkthrough.
robot_success_rationale: Task bundles diverse subtasks: visual inspection, locomotion over varied outdoor terrain, and physical repair (welding, carpentry, mechanical work). Current humanoids lack specialized tools, outdoor generalization, and repair dexterity needed to complete all components acceptably.

task: Test backup batteries, keypad programming, sirens, or other security features to ensure proper functioning or to diagnose malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing all key security features (batteries, keypad, sirens, etc.) on a single panel/system. A competent technician typically takes 20–40 minutes per site visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting varied proprietary keypads and control panels, reading diagnostic readouts, triggering specific test modes, and interpreting audio/visual outputs. Current robots lack the specialized tool-use, fine UI interaction, and domain software integration needed.

task: Maintain or repair specialized equipment or machinery located in cafeterias, laundries, hospitals, stores, offices, or factories.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and completing a single repair/maintenance job on one piece of specialized equipment (e.g., commercial dishwasher, industrial washer, conveyor). Median across routine PM and moderate repairs.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert diagnosis, fine dexterous tool use, reading gauges/schematics, removing panels, replacing parts, and testing—far beyond current humanoid generalization, dexterity, and reasoning. Highly unstructured and domain-specific.

task: Maintain and clean work and inventory areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tidying and cleaning a single work/inventory area (e.g., a workstation or shelf section); a competent worker sweeps, wipes, and reorganizes in ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep/mop flat surfaces reasonably well, but mixed tasks—wiping varied surfaces, reorganizing inventory, navigating cluttered aisles, and judging cleanliness standards—challenge current dexterity, perception, and generalization.

task: Measure, test, or weigh bottles, cans, or other containers to ensure that hardness, strength, or dimensions meet specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring/testing one container (e.g., checking dimensions, hardness, or strength on a single bottle or can) using calipers, gauges, or testing equipment; ~2–4 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can grasp and reposition containers, but precise tool use (calipers, compression testers, gauges) requires fine dexterity, accurate placement, and reading analog/digital instruments — challenging for current humanoids. Structured setups improve odds slightly.

task: Inspect furrings, mechanical mountings, or masonry surfaces for plumbness and level, using spirit or water levels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single surface/mounting for plumb and level. A competent worker places a spirit level, reads it, and notes results in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot must navigate to surface, position level tool correctly against varied substrates (furring, masonry, mounting), read analog bubble or use onboard sensors, and log results. Perception of level placement and stable contact on irregular surfaces remain challenging for current humanoids.

task: Inspect facilities and equipment, such as emergency management centers and communications equipment, to determine their operational and functional capabilities in emergency situations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single facility (e.g., emergency management center) including all equipment checks, functional tests, and documentation. A competent inspector typically takes 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level cognitive judgment to assess operational readiness of specialized comms/emergency systems, interpret technical standards, and interact with complex control interfaces. Current robots lack domain knowledge, system interfacing ability, and evaluative reasoning for this task.

task: Refuel trucks or add other fluids, such as oil or brake fluid.
human_time_rationale: One instance = refueling or topping off fluids on one truck. Includes locating the fill point, adding fluid, and securing caps. Typically 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise manipulation of fuel nozzles, fluid containers, and truck-specific fill points; outdoor/unstructured environments; heavy containers; tight tolerances; spill/safety risks. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and generalization for reliable autonomous execution.

task: Protect herds from predators, using trained dogs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full shift (day or overnight) of active herd protection; a handler deploys and monitors trained dogs across grazing areas, typically 6–8 hours per rotation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained outdoor mobility over rough terrain, animal behavior interpretation, managing live dogs, and real-time predator response — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities. No suitable platform exists for this multi-hour, unstructured livestock task.

task: Control the direction of a tree's fall by scoring cutting lines with axes, sawing undercuts along scored lines with chainsaws, knocking slabs from cuts with single-bit axes, and driving wedges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = felling one tree. A skilled logger takes ~20–60 min to score, undercut, slab, and wedge a typical timber tree, depending on diameter and species.
robot_success_rationale: Requires wielding heavy tools (axe, chainsaw, wedge/maul) with high-force precision outdoors on uneven terrain, reading wood grain, and safely controlling a falling tree. Far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and safety autonomy.

task: Walk beside or ride on planting machines while inserting plants in planter mechanisms at specified intervals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full planting shift (~8 hours). Workers continuously feed seedlings into the planter mechanism throughout the day; the unit of measure is a workday/shift.
robot_success_rationale: Requires walking/riding on moving outdoor farm machinery over uneven terrain, gripping and precisely timing seedling insertion into fast-moving planter cups — extreme dexterity, dynamic balance, and outdoor mobility challenge far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Clear brush from work areas and escape routes, and cut saplings and other trees from direction of falls, using axes, chainsaws, or bulldozers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing brush and saplings from a typical work area and escape route for a felling operation. A competent logger takes ~1–2 hours depending on density.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a chainsaw, axe, or bulldozer in unstructured outdoor/forest terrain with roots, slopes, and fallen debris. Humanoids lack ability to drive bulldozers, handle chainsaw recoil safely, and navigate dense rough terrain reliably. Extreme generalization gap.

task: Mix feed, additives, and medicines in prescribed portions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single batch of mixed feed per prescription. A worker measures, weighs, and combines feed, additives, and medicines for one batch, typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise weighing of small medicine/additive quantities, handling diverse containers and powders/liquids, and following variable prescriptions. Current robots lack reliable fine-measure dispensing, generalization across container types, and safe medicine handling.

task: Herd livestock to pastures for grazing or to scales, trucks, or other enclosures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving a herd from one location (pen, pasture, or barn) to a destination. A competent farmhand typically takes 30–60 min depending on herd size and distance.
robot_success_rationale: Herding requires outdoor/rough terrain locomotion, real-time animal behavior prediction, adaptive crowd control, and sustained coordination over large areas. Current humanoids lack the mobility, animal-interaction training, and endurance for unstructured outdoor livestock work.

task: Maintain and repair chainsaws and other equipment, cleaning, oiling, and greasing equipment, and sharpening equipment properly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full maintenance cycle on one chainsaw (clean, oil, grease, sharpen chain). A competent equipment operator takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (sharpening chain teeth with a file, greasing tight fittings), tool-switching, and nuanced force control. Current humanoids lack the precision and tool-use generalization for multi-step equipment maintenance on non-standardized machinery.

task: Inspect grounds, facilities, and equipment routinely to determine necessity of repairs or maintenance.
human_time_rationale: One inspection round of a typical facility (building + grounds + key equipment); a competent facilities worker completes a standard walkthrough in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate and capture visual data, but recognizing subtle wear, damage, or maintenance needs across diverse grounds, structures, and equipment requires broad contextual judgment, outdoor terrain handling, and generalization well beyond current reliable capability.

task: Plan or conduct experimental, environmental, operational, or stress tests on models or prototypes of aircraft or aerospace systems or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = planning and conducting a single test session on one prototype/model (setup, execution, monitoring, initial data capture). Typically 2–5 hours for a competent aerospace test engineer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert aerospace engineering judgment, test protocol design, interpreting anomalous sensor readings, operating specialized test rigs and control consoles, and safety decision-making. Far beyond current robot generalization and cognitive capability.

task: Grease or oil woodworking machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lubricating all grease/oil points on a single woodworking machine (e.g., table saw or planer). A competent worker locates fittings, applies grease/oil, and wipes excess in ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating small, recessed grease fittings and oil ports, attaching/operating a grease gun with precise force, and navigating around large machinery. Poor dexterity with tools, non-standard fitting locations, and cluttered environments make this very difficult for current humanoids.

task: Prepare and perform action stunts for motion picture, television, or stage productions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and performing a single stunt for a production, including rehearsal, safety checks, coordination with crew, and the take itself — typically 1–3 hours total.
robot_success_rationale: Stunts require creative physical improvisation, precise choreography, safety judgment, interaction with other performers, and expressive body control. Current humanoids lack the agility, fall-recovery, and context-awareness for live stunt performance at acceptable quality.

task: Cut, trim, file, bend, or smooth parts to ensure proper fit and clearance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting a single part via cutting/trimming/filing/bending. A skilled tradesperson typically spends 10–20 min measuring, adjusting, and verifying clearance on one part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled dexterous tool use (files, snips, benders), real-time tactile feedback, iterative fit-checking, and adaptive judgment. Far beyond reliable autonomous capability of 2026 humanoids.

task: Use equipment such as atomic absorption spectrometers, electron microscopes, flow cytometers, or chromatography systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running a single sample analysis session on a lab instrument (e.g., loading sample, calibrating, running, recording results). ~30–60 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: These instruments require expert configuration, precise sample preparation, software interaction via GUIs, and interpretation of results. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, domain knowledge integration, and GUI/software control needed for autonomous lab instrument operation.

task: Clean and tidy up the shop.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and tidying one retail/workshop shop space. A competent worker sweeps, wipes surfaces, organizes items, and empties trash in ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Sweeping/mopping is feasible (~40–70%), but "tidying" requires recognizing where varied, domain-specific items belong and reorganizing them — poor generalization, cluttered/unstructured environments, and tool diversity reduce success significantly.

task: Assess logs after cutting to ensure that the quality and length are correct.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single cut log. A worker visually checks dimensions and quality (knots, splits, defects) in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based length measurement and basic defect detection are feasible, but assessing wood quality nuances (grain, internal splits, moisture, subtle defects) requires expert tactile/visual judgment that current robots lack in unstructured outdoor/mill environments.

task: Measure and mark surfaces to lay out work, according to blueprints or drawings, using tape measures, straightedges or squares, and marking devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and marking a single workpiece/surface per blueprint spec. A competent tradesperson typically takes 10–20 minutes including reading the drawing, measuring, and scribing marks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading 2D/3D blueprints, precise measurement with hand tools (tape, square), and accurate scribing on varied surfaces. Current robots lack reliable fine dexterity, blueprint interpretation, and sub-millimeter marking precision needed for trade-quality layout work.

task: Prepare customers' nails in soapy water, using swabs, files, and orange sticks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one customer's full set of nails: soaking, swabbing, filing, and orange-stick cuticle work; ~10 min for a skilled nail technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterity on a live human's delicate fingers, precise force control with small tools (files, orange sticks, swabs), safe interaction near skin, and adaptive responses to hand movement — far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Examine chemical or biological samples to identify cell structures or to locate bacteria or extraneous material, using a microscope.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and examining a single sample slide. A competent lab technician typically spends ~15–25 minutes on slide prep, focusing, scanning fields of view, and recording findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting, slide preparation, microscope operation, and expert visual interpretation of cell structures/bacteria. Current humanoid robots lack the fine dexterity, domain-specific perception, and analytical judgment needed for autonomous lab microscopy.

task: Store, pack, and maintain inventory records of props, products, or display items.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a batch of items (store/pack/log ~10–20 props or display items); a competent worker cycles through this in ~45 min including physical handling and record entry.
robot_success_rationale: Requires integrating physical manipulation (varied shapes, fragile props, packing), mobility across a storage area, AND digital record-keeping in an inventory system — too complex multi-modal for current humanoids to do fully autonomously end-to-end.

task: Control machinery to open and close canal locks and dams, railroad or highway drawbridges, or horizontally or vertically adjustable bridges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full lock/bridge operation cycle (open, allow passage, close). A competent operator completes this in ~10–20 minutes depending on traffic and vessel/vehicle type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized control panels, monitoring vessel/vehicle traffic, coordinating signals, and making real-time safety judgments. Humanoid robots cannot reliably operate bespoke industrial control rooms, read dynamic waterway/road conditions, or meet safety standards autonomously.

task: Clean stalls, pens, and equipment, using disinfectant solutions, brushes, shovels, water hoses, or pumps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one stall/pen. A competent worker scrapes, shovels, scrubs, disinfects, and rinses a typical livestock stall in ~30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating slippery, unstructured agricultural environments, wielding shovels/hoses/brushes, handling heavy wet waste, and operating pumps. Current humanoids lack robust outdoor/rough-floor mobility, tool-switching dexterity, and farm-environment generalization.

task: Provide medical treatment, such as administering medications and vaccinations, or arrange for veterinarians to provide more extensive treatment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating a single animal (e.g., administering a medication or vaccine): gathering supplies, restraining the animal, administering treatment, and documenting. ~15–25 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for injections, safe animal restraint, real-time behavioral judgment, and veterinary decision-making. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, animal-handling skills, and clinical judgment needed. Near-zero feasibility.

task: Extend nails using powder, solvent, and paper forms attached to tips of customers' fingers to support and shape artificial nails.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full set of 10 acrylic nail extensions on one customer. A competent nail technician typically takes 75–105 minutes for a full acrylic set.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to attach paper forms, apply liquid/powder acrylic precisely on curved fingertip surfaces, and shape each nail — far beyond current robot dexterity. Also demands safe contact with a live human hand and aesthetic judgment.

task: Determine intensities and types of radiation in work areas, equipment, or materials, using radiation detectors or other instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = surveying a single work area or piece of equipment. A competent health physicist takes ~20–40 min to set up instruments, sweep the area methodically, log readings, and document results.
robot_success_rationale: Robot could physically carry a detector, but autonomously navigating a radiation zone, positioning the instrument correctly at all required points, interpreting multi-type radiation readings, and producing an accepted survey report far exceeds current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Suspend angle iron grids or channel irons from ceilings, using wire.
human_time_rationale: One instance = suspending one grid/channel iron section. Includes measuring, threading wire, securing to ceiling anchor, and leveling — ~20 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires overhead work at height, fine wire manipulation, threading/twisting wire around anchor points, and leveling heavy metal members — combining extreme dexterity, upward-reaching locomotion stability, and tool use far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Pull up cable by hand from large reels mounted on trucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pulling cable from one large truck-mounted reel to a standard run (~100–200 ft). Competent lineworker takes ~30–60 min managing tension, guiding, and coiling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained high-force pulling on heavy cable outdoors, climbing on/around trucks, coordinating with reel rotation, and navigating rough terrain. Far beyond current humanoid strength, balance, and generalization limits.

task: Scribe and cut edges of tile to fit walls where wall molding is not specified.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scribing and cutting one tile to fit a wall edge. A skilled tile setter measures, marks, and cuts a single tile in ~10–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement/scribing against irregular wall profiles, fine tool handling (tile scriber, wet saw), and quality judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-use precision, and adaptive planning needed for reliable tile cutting.

task: Mold wax over denture setups to form the full contours of artificial gums.
human_time_rationale: One instance = waxing a full denture setup (upper or lower arch). A skilled dental lab technician takes ~30–60 min to carve and smooth wax gingival contours on one denture.
robot_success_rationale: Extremely fine dexterity required: precise wax addition, carving, and smoothing at sub-millimeter scale on irregular dental forms. Current humanoids lack the tactile sensitivity, fine motor control, and domain-specific tool-use for acceptable dental lab quality.

task: Measure felled trees and cut them into specified log lengths, using chain saws and axes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and bucking one felled tree into specified lengths. A competent logger takes ~20–30 min depending on tree size and number of cuts.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a chainsaw safely on uneven, debris-covered outdoor terrain, measuring irregular logs, and making precise cuts.
