Current humanoids lack the stability, grip strength, and tool-use capability for chainsaw operation outdoors. Extremely hazardous for autonomous systems.

task: Tag unsafe trees with high-visibility ribbons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tagging one unsafe tree. An arborist walks to the tree, assesses it, tears ribbon, and ties a visible tag on a branch or trunk — roughly 5–10 minutes per tree.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor rough terrain locomotion, visual hazard assessment of a biological structure, reaching overhead branches, and tying/securing ribbon — each step is at or beyond frontier robot limits. Generalization to unstructured forest/yard settings is very low.

task: Cut and screw together metal channels to make floor or ceiling frames, according to plans for the location of rooms or hallways.
human_time_rationale: One instance = framing a single room or hallway section (~150–300 sq ft). A skilled metal framer measures, cuts track, and screws together a complete floor or ceiling frame in roughly 60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading construction plans, operating a metal shear/saw, precisely measuring and aligning long metal channels, and driving screws in varied orientations. Combines heavy material handling, tool use, spatial planning, and precision — far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Insert jacks or drive wedges behind saws to prevent binding of saws and to start trees falling.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inserting jacks/wedges behind the saw for one tree. A skilled faller takes ~2–4 minutes to position and drive wedges during a felling cut.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor rough terrain locomotion, precise forceful striking of wedges into a narrow kerf behind a moving chainsaw, real-time judgment on tree lean and binding — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and safety capability.

task: Fit and fasten wallboard or drywall into position on wood or metal frameworks, using glue, nails, or screws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = hanging one standard 4×8 ft drywall sheet: measuring, cutting if needed, lifting into position, and fastening. A competent drywall installer takes ~30–60 min per sheet.
robot_success_rationale: Requires lifting and precisely positioning a large, heavy, fragile panel overhead or against a wall while simultaneously fastening — demanding bimanual force control, reach, and balance. No current humanoid demonstrates this reliably; panels are unwieldy and workspace is unstructured.

task: Feed and water livestock and monitor food and water supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single feeding/watering round for a typical livestock group (e.g., a pen of cattle or hogs). Includes hauling feed, filling troughs, and checking supply levels.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/unstructured terrain navigation, handling bulk feed and hoses, monitoring animal behavior, and adapting to varied pen layouts. Current humanoids lack robust outdoor mobility, tool generalization, and reliable livestock-context perception.

task: Instruct personnel in radiation safety procedures and demonstrate use of protective clothing and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single training session covering radiation safety procedures and PPE demonstration for a group; a competent instructor typically takes 45–90 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert verbal instruction, adaptive Q&A, nuanced PPE demonstration, and credibility as a safety trainer. Current robots lack reliable speech generation, pedagogical judgment, and the fine dexterity to don/demonstrate complex radiation PPE convincingly.

task: Hang drywall panels on metal frameworks of walls and ceilings in offices, schools, or other large buildings, using lifts or hoists to adjust panel heights, when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one standard 4×8 or 4×12 drywall panel hung and fastened to a metal stud frame; a competent drywaller takes ~20–30 min including positioning, lifting, aligning, and screwing off.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated two-arm handling of large, heavy, unwieldy panels; precise overhead positioning against a metal track; operating a lift/hoist; driving screws into studs at spacing. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, payload, and tool-use capabilities in unstructured construction sites.

task: Saw back-cuts, leaving sufficient sound wood to control direction of fall.
human_time_rationale: One instance = making the back-cut on a single tree. A skilled faller assesses, positions, and completes the back-cut in roughly 5–12 minutes depending on tree diameter.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a chainsaw safely on uneven forest terrain, precise angled cutting to leave a hinge, real-time fall-direction judgment, and rapid repositioning for safety — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities outdoors.

task: Perform original and stock tricks of illusion to entertain and mystify audiences, occasionally including audience members as participants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single illusion performance set (e.g., stage show or close-up session). A typical set with audience participation runs ~30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for sleight-of-hand, improvised social interaction, reading audience cues, scripted misdirection, and real-time adaptation. Far beyond current humanoid capabilities in unstructured social/performance contexts.

task: Mix, blend, or cultivate ingredients to make reagents or to manufacture food or beverage products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single batch (mixing/blending ingredients to produce a food, beverage, or reagent product); ~25 min covers measuring, combining, and processing steps.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measuring, pouring, and tool use (blenders, scales, mixers) in unstructured kitchens or labs. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation, liquid handling, and recipe-following generalization needed for consistent batch quality.

task: Drive trucks, tractors, and other equipment to distribute feed to animals.
human_time_rationale: One feed distribution run across a typical farm, including driving between pens/pastures and dispensing feed — roughly 30–60 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate trucks or tractors on unstructured farm terrain. No current general-purpose robot can drive these vehicles, navigate outdoor farm routes, and autonomously dispense feed end-to-end.

task: Examine samples to verify qualities such as clarity, cleanliness, consistency, dryness, or texture.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining a single sample for multiple quality attributes (clarity, cleanliness, consistency, dryness, texture). A trained QC worker typically takes 3–7 minutes per sample.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based inspection of clarity/cleanliness is partially feasible, but assessing texture, consistency, and dryness reliably requires haptic/tactile sensing and multi-modal perception that current humanoids lack. Generalization across varied sample types is poor.

task: Calibrate and maintain chemical instrumentation sensing elements and sampling system equipment, using calibration instruments and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = calibrating a single sensor/sampling unit. A skilled technician spends ~30–60 min per device including setup, adjustment, verification, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use, interpreting analog/digital instrument readings, applying calibration standards precisely, and adapting to varied sensor types. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation, domain-specific tool competency, and closed-loop chemical sensing feedback needed.

task: Map forest area soils and vegetation to estimate the amount of standing timber and future value and growth.
human_time_rationale: One instance = surveying and mapping a typical forest stand (~20–50 acres). A competent forester spends a full day collecting soil samples, vegetation plots, and timber cruising data.
robot_success_rationale: Requires traversing dense, uneven forest terrain; collecting soil cores; identifying species; measuring DBH and canopy; synthesizing multi-layered data into growth/value estimates. Current robots lack rough-terrain mobility, botanical perception, and domain-specific analytical integration for this complex field task.

task: Inspect merchandise or products to determine quality, value, or yield.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single item or small batch. A competent worker visually/physically examines, grades, and records results in ~5 minutes on average.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is maturing, but generalizing across varied merchandise types, subtle quality cues (texture, smell, flex, weight feel), and unstructured environments remains hard for current humanoid robots.

task: Test new products for flavor, texture, color, nutritional content, and adherence to government and industry standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single new product batch across all attributes (flavor, texture, color, nutrition, compliance). Includes sensory evaluation, lab checks, and documentation — typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Flavor and texture require human sensory perception robots cannot replicate. Nutritional/compliance testing uses lab instruments beyond humanoid capability. Color inspection is partially feasible, but the full multi-attribute evaluation is inaccessible to current robots.

task: Assemble and modify existing pieces of equipment to meet special needs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling/modifying one piece of equipment for a special need; a competent technician typically spends 30–60 min on diagnosis, adaptation, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting ambiguous "special needs," selecting appropriate parts, improvising non-standard modifications, and using varied tools with precision. Far beyond current robot generalization, planning, and dexterous tool-use capabilities.

task: Seal joints between ceiling tiles and walls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sealing all ceiling-wall joints in a standard room (~40–60 linear feet). A competent worker with caulk gun takes ~45 min including prep, application, and smoothing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained overhead work at ceiling height, precise bead application along an irregular joint, controlled caulk gun pressure, and smoothing — combining extreme reach, fine force control, and tool manipulation that 2026 humanoids cannot reliably manage.

task: Fasten metal or rockboard lath to the structural framework of walls, ceilings, or partitions of buildings, using nails, screws, staples, or wire-ties.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fastening lath to one standard wall section (~8×10 ft). A skilled lather works at moderate pace, laying and securing strips consistently; ~45 min per section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise positioning of flexible/rigid lath strips against an uneven framework, driving fasteners at correct spacing and angle, and navigating a cluttered construction site. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and construction-environment generalization needed.

task: Trim rough edges from wallboard to maintain even joints, using knives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming the rough edges of a single wallboard panel to fit a joint. A skilled drywall worker typically takes 2–4 minutes per cut/trim.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled knife strokes along irregular edges on a large, awkward panel, fine tactile feedback, and spatial judgment in tight spaces. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous knife control and adaptive trimming precision.

task: Guide products on conveyors to regulate flow through machines, and to discard diseased or rotten products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting/guiding one product item on a conveyor line; workers handle roughly 1–2 items per minute, so ~30 seconds per item.
robot_success_rationale: Machine vision can detect defects and conveyors are structured environments; however, reliably grasping/deflecting varied produce shapes at line speed, and distinguishing subtle rot, remains challenging for general-purpose humanoids in 2026.

task: Conduct standardized tests on food, beverages, additives, or preservatives to ensure compliance with standards and regulations regarding factors such as color, texture, or nutrients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one standardized test on a single sample (prep, instrument operation, recording). A QA lab tech typically spends ~45 min per test cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for lab instrument operation, precise pipetting, reagent handling, and interpreting multi-modal results. Current humanoids lack reliable lab tool use, chemical handling, and compliance-grade accuracy for food testing workflows.

task: Attach ropes or cable lines to bitts on lock decks or wharfs to secure vessels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = securing one vessel to bitts with ropes/lines. A skilled deckhand ties off lines to bitts in roughly 5–10 minutes depending on vessel size and number of lines.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating dynamic, wet, uneven dock surfaces; handling thick, heavy, coiled ropes; precise knotting/hitching around bitts; and coordinating with moving vessels. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, mobility, and generalization in unstructured maritime environments.

task: Load hoppers, containers, or conveyors to feed machines with products, using forklifts, transfer augers, suction gates, shovels, or pitchforks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a single hopper/container/conveyor. Using forklift or hand tools (shovel, pitchfork), a competent worker completes one fill cycle in roughly 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating forklifts or specialized tools (augers, shovels, pitchforks) in industrial/agricultural settings with bulk/loose materials. Humanoid robots cannot drive forklifts; bulk-material handling with tools on uneven terrain is far beyond current autonomous capability.

task: Feed cables through access holes, roof spaces, or cavity walls to reach fixture outlets, positioning and terminating cables, wires, or strapping.
human_time_rationale: One instance = routing a cable run from source to one fixture outlet. Involves access, threading, and termination; typically 30–60 min for a competent electrician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating very confined/dark cavities, forceful cable fishing, dexterous termination, and generalized tool use in highly unstructured environments. Far exceeds current humanoid locomotion, dexterity, and perception limits.

task: Check products for quality, and identify damaged or expired goods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single product (or small batch unit). A competent worker visually checks, feels, and reads labels/dates in ~1–3 minutes per item.
robot_success_rationale: Vision systems can detect obvious damage and read printed dates, but variable lighting, packaging variety, subtle defects (soft spots, odor, discoloration), and unstructured environments limit reliability. Fine sensory judgment (smell, texture) remains beyond current robots.

task: Load and unload crops or containers of materials, manually or using conveyors, handtrucks, forklifts, or transfer augers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading/unloading one vehicle or storage bay of crops or containers; a competent worker using hand trucks, conveyors, or forklifts typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating forklifts, augers, and conveyors — heavy equipment a humanoid cannot drive. Manual handling of bulky/irregular crops on uneven outdoor terrain is also well beyond current locomotion and dexterity limits.

task: Spray livestock with disinfectants and insecticides, or dip or bathe animals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating a single animal (spray, dip, or bathe). A competent handler takes ~15–25 min including setup, restraint, application, and release.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/uneven terrain mobility, safe restraint of unpredictable live animals, chemical equipment operation, and adaptive generalization across livestock species/sizes — all well beyond 2026 frontier robot capabilities.

task: Weld sections of track together, such as switch points and frogs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = welding one joint (e.g., switch point or frog section). A skilled track welder sets up, preheats, welds, and finishes one joint in ~30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized welding equipment (thermite or arc), precise torch/electrode control on heavy rail steel, preheat management, outdoor/uneven terrain, and quality inspection. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Develop or implement actions such as lockouts, tagouts, or clearances to allow equipment to be safely repaired.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying a full LOTO procedure to one piece of equipment. A competent worker identifies energy sources, isolates them, applies locks/tags, and verifies zero energy in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading equipment-specific energy diagrams, locating diverse valves/breakers, applying physical locks/tags with precision, and verifying de-energization—complex multi-step procedural reasoning and fine manipulation beyond current robot capability.

task: Design, implement, maintain, or improve electrical instruments, equipment, facilities, components, products, or systems for commercial, industrial, or domestic purposes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single design/implementation/maintenance session on one electrical system or component. A competent electrical engineer typically spends 2–4 hours on a focused unit of work.
robot_success_rationale: Task is primarily cognitive engineering work (design, diagnosis, system improvement) requiring deep technical reasoning, schematic interpretation, and complex tool use. Far beyond current robot autonomy; even fine manipulation of electrical components is <5%.

task: Drive trucks to weigh stations before and after loading and along routes in compliance with state regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one weigh-station stop during a route, including driving to the station, pulling onto the scale, waiting for clearance, and departing. Typical stop takes ~30–60 min including travel detour.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a commercial truck on public roads in mixed traffic — humanoid robots cannot drive vehicles. No frontier general-purpose robot has this capability; L4 AVs are narrow, route-specific, and a separate system class entirely.

task: Clean trucks or compactor bodies after routes have been completed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one truck/compactor body after a route. Involves hosing down, scrubbing, and deodorizing interior and exterior compactor surfaces; ~30–60 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating high-pressure hoses on a large outdoor vehicle, navigating slippery/wet surfaces, accessing confined compactor spaces, and tolerating extreme filth/odors. Current humanoids lack the specialized tool-use, reach, and robustness for this harsh, unstructured environment.

task: Build containment areas prior to beginning abatement or decontamination work.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a full containment area (plastic sheeting, negative pressure unit, airlocks, tape/sealing) before a single abatement job. Typically 1–2 hours for a trained worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires unrolling and precisely taping large plastic sheeting to walls/ceilings, sealing irregular surfaces, constructing airlocks, and positioning negative-pressure equipment — extremely complex, multi-step dexterous work in unstructured environments far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Sing or dance during dramatic or comedic performances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one song or dance number in a performance (rehearsed routine). A typical number runs ~3–5 min of stage time, plus brief setup/transition, averaging ~15 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expressive artistry, emotional nuance, vocal performance, audience engagement, and adaptive improvisation. Current humanoids lack singing ability, expressive facial/body performance, and the generalization needed for live theatrical contexts.

task: Prepare copy and edit tape so that material is ready for broadcasting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one broadcast segment. Reviewing, editing, and cueing copy and tape for a typical news/media segment takes a competent editor roughly 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Task is primarily cognitive/editorial: script review, audio/video editing on specialized software, and broadcast cueing. Requires advanced language, media judgment, and software tool-use far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Tag garbage or recycling containers to inform customers of problems, such as excess garbage or inclusion of items that are not permitted.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one container, identifying the problem, and affixing a tag. A trained worker can do this in ~3 minutes curbside.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor mobility on varied terrain, visual inspection to classify rule violations (e.g., prohibited items), fine-motor tag attachment, and generalization across container types. Current humanoids struggle with unstructured outdoor environments, semantic waste classification, and precise tag affixing.

task: Set up scaffolds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = erecting a standard scaffold section/bay (e.g., one 5×5 ft frame, ~2–3 stories). A competent scaffolder takes ~60–120 min per bay including base plates, frames, braces, planks, and guardrails.
robot_success_rationale: Scaffold assembly requires heavy lifting of large, awkward metal frames, precise alignment of couplers and pins at height, bi-manual force application, and outdoor terrain navigation. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, payload, and safety capabilities.

task: Check that bridges are clear of vehicles and pedestrians prior to opening.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one bridge span before opening. Worker walks or drives the length, visually confirms no vehicles or pedestrians remain, then signals clearance. ~5–15 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor locomotion on varied bridge surfaces, reliable human/vehicle detection across wide areas, and issuing a clearance signal. Perception is feasible but uneven terrain, weather, and safety-critical false-negative risk push success low.

task: Patrol grazing lands on horseback or using all-terrain vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One patrol instance covers a typical grazing land circuit; competent ranch hand takes ~2 hours riding/driving to survey fences, livestock, and terrain.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a horse or ATV across unstructured outdoor terrain over extended range. Humanoid robots cannot ride horses or drive ATVs autonomously in open fields; rough outdoor terrain, navigation, and vehicle operation are well beyond 2026 frontier capabilities.

task: Measure and assess vegetation resources for biological assessment companies, environmental impact statements, and rangeland monitoring programs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one field survey session measuring vegetation resources at a site (transects, species ID, cover estimates). A competent botanist/ecologist typically spends ~3 hours per site visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert plant species identification in unstructured outdoor terrain, fine-scale canopy/cover measurement, navigating rough rangeland, and producing defensible scientific documentation. Current robots lack plant ID accuracy, rough-terrain mobility, and scientific judgment needed.

task: Inspect canal and bridge equipment, and areas, such as roadbeds, for damage or defects, reporting problems to supervisors as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one canal or bridge structure/section. A competent inspector walks the area, observes, documents defects, and reports — typically 60–120 min per site.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor unstructured terrain (water edges, bridge decks, embankments) challenges locomotion (~30–50%). Requires defect recognition, contextual judgment, and supervisor reporting. Current humanoids lack robust outdoor mobility, specialized inspection perception, and autonomous reporting workflows.

task: Cut fixture or border tiles to size, using keyhole saws, and insert them into surrounding frameworks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting and fitting one border tile. Skilled worker measures, marks, cuts with keyhole saw, and inserts into framework in ~5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, marking, and controlled cutting with a handheld keyhole saw (fine dexterous tool use), then accurate fitting into a framework. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-tool manipulation, force control for sawing, and spatial precision needed for acceptable tile fitment.

task: Operate automated or semi-automated hoisting devices that raise refuse bins and dump contents into openings in truck bodies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lifting and dumping one refuse bin into a truck. A worker positions the bin, hooks it to the hoist, activates the mechanism, and clears — typically 1–2 minutes per bin.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a truck-mounted hydraulic hoisting mechanism, navigating varied outdoor terrain, identifying and hooking diverse bin types, and working safely around traffic — well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured outdoor environments.

task: Groom, clip, trim, or castrate animals, dock ears and tails, or shear coats to collect hair.
human_time_rationale: One grooming/clipping session per animal (e.g., a dog bath+clip or sheep shearing). Median across grooming types; shearing ~5–10 min/sheep, full dog groom ~60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme dexterity near sensitive tissue on a moving, unpredictable live animal. Current robots lack compliant force control, animal-behavior adaptation, and safe tool use for clipping, shearing, or surgical procedures like castration.

task: Maintain props, products, or mannequins, inspecting them for imperfections, doing touch-ups, cleaning up after customers, or applying preservative coatings as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = maintaining one prop/mannequin: inspect, touch-up, clean, and coat as needed. Competent worker takes ~15–25 min per item.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual inspection for subtle imperfections, dexterous touch-up painting/coating on irregular 3D surfaces, and adaptive cleaning — all on varied, delicate objects. Perception and fine manipulation on non-standard surfaces remain weak in 2026 robots.

task: Mount tile, using adhesives, or by nailing, screwing, stapling, or wire-tying lath directly to structural frameworks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tiling one standard wall section (~10–15 sq ft). A competent tiler spends ~45 min measuring, applying adhesive/fastening lath, and setting tiles precisely.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise spatial reasoning, fine dexterity for fastening methods (screwing, stapling, wire-tying), surface prep, and adhesive application on varied structural frameworks. Current humanoids lack reliable precision for this multi-step, tool-switching construction task.

task: Assemble parts, fittings, or subassemblies on aircraft, using layout tools, hand tools, power tools, or fasteners, such as bolts, screws, rivets, or clamps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one discrete subassembly or fitting task (e.g., riveting a panel, bolting a bracket). A skilled aerospace assembler typically spends 1–2 hours per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Aircraft assembly demands extreme precision, varied fastener types, confined/awkward spaces, safety-critical tolerances, and complex layout tool use. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force control, and aerospace-specific tool-use required. No commercial deployment exists.

task: Pull cable through ducts by hand or with winches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pulling cable through a single duct run (~30–60 ft). Attaching fish tape/rope, feeding cable, operating winch, and verifying pull takes ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires feeding stiff cable into narrow conduit openings, coordinating a winch or sustained high-force pulling, navigating tight/elevated workspaces, and verifying cable seating — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and tool-use capabilities.

task: Assemble or install metal framing or decorative trim for windows, doorways, or vents.
human_time_rationale: One instance = framing/trimming a single window, doorway, or vent opening. A competent worker measures, cuts, fits, and fastens metal trim in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measuring, cutting metal stock to length, handling sharp/awkward pieces, and driving fasteners in varied orientations at height. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use (tin snips, saws, drills), fine positioning, and task sequencing in unstructured construction environments.

task: Install blanket insulation between studs and tack plastic moisture barriers over insulation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one stud bay (insulation + vapor barrier). Cutting batts to fit, stuffing cavity, and stapling plastic sheeting takes a skilled worker roughly 45 minutes per bay/section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling large, flexible/compressible batt insulation, precise fitting in confined cavities, cutting to size, and stapling thin plastic sheeting taut — all highly deformable-material tasks beyond current humanoid dexterity and manipulation.

task: Position boxes or attach bags at discharge ends of machinery to catch products, removing and closing full containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attach one container, wait for fill, remove and close it. A common packaging-line cycle taking roughly 2–4 minutes end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment to a discharge chute, bag/box attachment (fine dexterity), monitoring fill level, then removal and closure. Perception and dexterous grasping of flexible bags or varied boxes is challenging; structured environments improve odds but generalization remains limited.

task: Hoist steel beams, girders, or columns into place, using cranes or signaling hoisting equipment operators to lift and position structural steel members.
human_time_rationale: One instance = hoisting and positioning one structural steel member. A skilled ironworker typically takes 15–35 minutes per member including rigging, signaling, guiding, and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating or signaling cranes on an unstructured outdoor construction site, rigging heavy steel members, and coordinating with a crew — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities in mobility, load handling, and safe multi-agent coordination.

task: Train, exercise, and attend dance classes to maintain high levels of technical proficiency, physical ability, and physical fitness.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single training/exercise/dance class session. A typical session (warm-up, instruction, practice, cool-down) runs ~60–120 minutes; median ~90 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Dance requires nuanced rhythm, artistic expression, balance, and fine full-body coordination far beyond current humanoid capability. Robots cannot sustain high-tempo choreography, interpret music expressively, or match a human instructor's quality standard.

task: Taste or smell foods or beverages to ensure that flavors meet specifications or to select samples with specific characteristics.
human_time_rationale: One instance = evaluating one food/beverage sample for flavor/aroma compliance or selection. A trained evaluator typically spends 3–7 minutes per sample including assessment and notation.
robot_success_rationale: Current humanoid robots lack chemical taste/olfactory sensors entirely. Flavor and aroma evaluation is a biological sensory task with no robotic analogue in general-purpose platforms; no 2026 frontier robot can perform this autonomously.

task: Shape and solder wire and metal frames or bands for dental products, using soldering irons and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shaping and soldering one dental frame/band. A skilled dental lab technician typically spends 15–35 minutes per unit, depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity with small, irregular dental components, precise heat control with a soldering iron, and tactile feedback for shaping metal bands. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured fine-craft settings.

task: Study and practice dance moves required in roles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one practice session learning/rehearsing a set of role-specific dance moves; a typical focused session runs ~60 minutes for a professional dancer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expressive full-body coordination, rhythm, stylistic nuance, and artistic interpretation far beyond current humanoid capability. Robots lack the balance, fluid motion, and aesthetic judgment needed for acceptable dance performance.

task: Provide customer assistance and information, such as giving directions or handling wheelchairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single customer interaction (e.g., giving directions or assisting with a wheelchair). Typical interaction lasts ~3–7 minutes for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires natural language understanding, navigation in crowded public spaces, and safe wheelchair handling. Current robots lack reliable conversational generalization, social awareness, and the gentle dexterity needed for safe passenger assistance.

task: Attach roofing paper to roofs in overlapping strips to form bases for other materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = papering one standard residential roof section (~1,000 sq ft). A competent roofer lays overlapping felt/underlayment strips in ~1.5 hours with a crew of two.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working on steep, sloped, slippery outdoor surfaces at height — extreme locomotion and balance challenge. Unrolling, aligning, cutting, and stapling overlapping strips demands coordinated bimanual dexterity. No current humanoid is rated for rooftop deployment or this workflow.

task: Inspect food processing areas to ensure compliance with government regulations and standards for sanitation, safety, quality, and waste management.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspection of one food processing area/room. Competent inspector spends ~45–90 min checking surfaces, equipment, temperatures, records, and compliance items.
robot_success_rationale: Requires integrating visual/olfactory/tactile assessment, interpreting regulatory standards, reading gauges/records, judging abstract compliance, and communicating findings. Far beyond current robot generalization and reasoning capabilities in unstructured environments.

task: Nail channels or wood furring strips to surfaces to provide mounting for tile.
human_time_rationale: One instance = nailing channels/furring strips to one wall or surface section (~8–10 linear feet). A competent tiler or carpenter takes ~30–60 min including measuring, cutting, and fastening.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measuring, marking, holding strips flush against surfaces, and driving nails/screws accurately. Current humanoids lack reliable two-handed coordination for holding + fastening, handling power tools safely, and maintaining positional precision on varied wall surfaces.

task: Devise and choreograph dance for self or others.
human_time_rationale: One instance = devising and choreographing a short dance piece (~2–4 min). A competent choreographer typically spends 1–3 hours on initial conception, movement sequencing, and staging.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative ideation, aesthetic judgment, expressive movement generation, and teaching/communicating choreography to others. Current robots lack generative artistic creativity, expressive nuance, and the ability to direct human performers.

task: Select the correct sealing compound or tape.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting the correct sealing compound or tape for a specific joint/application. A competent worker reads specs, identifies the fitting, and picks the right product in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading labels, understanding material compatibility specs, and contextual judgment about pipe material/pressure/temperature. Current robots lack the semantic reasoning and fine-grained product identification needed for reliable autonomous selection.

task: Fit prostheses to patients, making any necessary adjustments and modifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting a prosthesis for one patient, including trial fitting, alignment checks, and minor adjustments. A trained prosthetist typically takes 30–60 minutes per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert clinical judgment, precise tactile feedback, patient communication, fine dexterous modification of components, and adaptive problem-solving with a variable human body. Far beyond current robot generalization and dexterity.

task: Perform cash handling tasks, such as making change, balancing and recording cash drawer, or distributing tips.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one end-of-shift cash drawer balance: counting, reconciling to POS records, and logging. Typical competent cashier completes this in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous bill/coin counting, reading mixed denominations, operating POS terminals, arithmetic judgment, and secure cash handling — well beyond reliable autonomous capability for current humanoids. Making change and tip distribution add social/contextual complexity.

task: Perform classical, modern, or acrobatic dances in productions, expressing stories, rhythm, and sound with their bodies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full performance/production (e.g., a ballet or modern dance show). A typical performance runs ~45–90 minutes; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Expressive, narrative dance requires fluid full-body coordination, balance, artistry, emotional expression, and real-time adaptation to music and partners — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in dexterity, grace, and generalization.

task: Harmonize body movements to rhythm of musical accompaniment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one rehearsal/performance segment of a choreographed routine (~15 min), typical unit a choreographer or instructor would evaluate.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time beat detection, expressive full-body coordination, balance during dynamic movement, and aesthetic quality judgment. Current humanoids lack fluid, rhythmically nuanced motion and graceful expressiveness; demos exist but quality is far below human acceptance threshold.

task: Coordinate dancing with that of partners or dance ensembles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one rehearsal or performance session of coordinated ensemble/partner dancing, typically 30–60 minutes for a competent dancer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time dynamic body coordination with partners, expressive motion, rhythmic precision, and social/artistic responsiveness far beyond current humanoid capabilities. No frontier robot can perform partner or ensemble dance autonomously to an acceptable artistic standard.

task: Audition for dance roles or for membership in dance companies.
human_time_rationale: One audition instance: arrival, warm-up, performing required combinations/routines before a panel. Typical dance auditions run 30–60 minutes per candidate slot.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expressive, rhythmically precise, stylistically nuanced full-body movement, artistic interpretation, and improvisation. Current humanoids lack the grace, fine motor expressivity, balance control, and artistic judgment required to satisfy a human dance panel.

task: Attend costume fittings, photography sessions, and makeup calls associated with dance performances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attending a single session (fitting, photo call, or makeup call). Combined prep/session time for a dancer typically runs 1–2 hours per session.
robot_success_rationale: This task requires social presence, posing, communicating fit/comfort feedback, and being dressed/made up — all deeply human. A robot cannot be the subject of a costume fitting or makeup call in any meaningful sense, and lacks the social/communicative generalization needed.

task: Spread and smooth cementing material over tape, using trowels or floating machines to blend joints with wall surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing one drywall joint (tape + compound application + smoothing). A competent taper covers ~3–5 joints in an hour; ~20 min per joint including setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly precise force-controlled spreading with trowels or floats over uneven surfaces, feathering edges smoothly, and adapting to variable joint geometry. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tactile feedback capabilities.

task: Demonstrate the applications of geospatial technology, such as Global Positioning System (GPS), geographic information systems (GIS), automatic tractor guidance systems, variable rate chemical input applicators, surveying equipment, or computer mapping software.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single demonstration session covering one geospatial technology (e.g., GPS field walkthrough or GIS software demo) to a small audience; ~45 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires teaching, contextual explanation, operating specialized ag/survey equipment, and adapting to audience questions. Humanoids lack the software interfaces, domain knowledge delivery, and complex equipment operation needed.

task: Spread sealing compound between boards or panels or over cracks, holes, nail heads, or screw heads, using trowels, broadknives, or spatulas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying sealing compound to one joint/crack/panel seam. A competent worker spreads, feathers, and smooths compound in ~5–10 minutes per section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force modulation, tool-angle control, and smooth continuous strokes with a broadknife/trowel. Current robots lack the fine wrist dexterity and compliant force feedback to produce an acceptable, even spread on varied surfaces.

task: Scan labels on letters or parcels to confirm receipt.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scanning a single letter or parcel. A competent worker picks up the item, aligns the barcode to a scanner, and confirms receipt in ~30 seconds.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can grasp parcels and read barcodes with vision, but label orientation variability, reflective surfaces, crumpled/occluded labels, and mixed parcel shapes reduce reliable autonomous scan-and-confirm rates significantly.

task: Examine patients to assess abnormalities of jaw development, tooth position, and other dental-facial structures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full orthodontic/dental-facial examination of one patient, including visual inspection, palpation, and charting; typically 20–40 minutes for a competent clinician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine intraoral dexterity, clinical tactile perception, patient communication/cooperation, expert diagnostic reasoning, and regulatory licensure. No current humanoid robot approaches this complexity safely or reliably.

task: Secure passengers for transportation by buckling seatbelts or fastening wheelchairs with tie-down straps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = securing one passenger (seatbelt or wheelchair tie-down). A trained aide typically takes 1–5 minutes depending on equipment and passenger cooperation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (buckle latching, strap tensioning), adapting to varied passenger positions and equipment types, and safe interaction with a human body — all near the frontier limit. Wheelchair tie-downs add complexity with floor anchors and varied chair geometries.

task: Apply texturizing compounds or primers to walls or ceilings before final finishing, using trowels, brushes, rollers, or spray guns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying compound/primer to one average room's walls or ceiling (~400 sq ft). A competent drywall finisher takes ~45 min including setup and tool use.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained overhead/vertical large-surface work, precise pressure control across multiple tools (trowel, roller, sprayer), consistent coating thickness, and navigating ladders/scaffolding — far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Cool, package, label, and freeze foods for later consumption and provide instructions for reheating.
human_time_rationale: One instance = packaging, labeling, and freezing a single meal/batch of food, plus writing reheating instructions. Competent worker takes ~15 min end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated multi-step manipulation (sealing bags/containers, writing labels, opening freezer, placing items), plus generating & attaching written reheating instructions. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity for sealing, labeling, and legible writing at acceptable quality.

task: Treat facial pain and jaw joint problems.
human_time_rationale: One clinical encounter: evaluating and treating a patient for facial pain/TMJ issues, including examination, diagnosis, and initial treatment (e.g., splint fitting, injection, or manual therapy). ~45 min per visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical diagnosis, fine dexterous intraoral manipulation, patient communication, and medical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity, perception, and safe autonomous medical decision-making capabilities.

task: Perform in productions, singing or acting in addition to dancing, if required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full performance (show/set). A typical stage production runs ~90–150 minutes; 120 min is a reasonable median for a competent performer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires integrated singing, acting, and dancing with emotional nuance, audience responsiveness, and artistic expression. Current humanoids lack voice acting, expressive emotion, improvisational response, and artistic quality far beyond any 2026 system.

task: Reproduce and duplicate sound recordings from original recording media, using sound editing and duplication equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = duplicating one recording from source media. Includes setup, playback/capture, editing checks, and export. Competent audio tech takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized audio equipment, software GUIs, fine cable connections, and nuanced quality monitoring. Current humanoids lack audio software proficiency, fine connector dexterity, and domain-specific tool-use for this workflow.

task: Record speech, music, and other sounds on recording media, using recording equipment.
human_time_rationale: One recording session (e.g., a song, voiceover, or podcast segment). A competent audio engineer sets up, records, monitors, and wraps a typical session in ~60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced auditory perception, operating specialized mixing/recording consoles, fine cable/mic placement, real-time quality judgment, and creative decision-making. Current humanoids lack the sensory feedback, audio expertise, and tool integration needed.

task: Assemble fittings, valves, bowls, plates, disks, impeller shafts, or other parts to prepare equipment for operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a single piece of equipment (e.g., a pump or valve assembly) from its component parts. A competent technician typically takes 30–60 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high dexterity for precise alignment of shafts, impellers, and fittings; torquing fasteners to spec; and handling varied, heavy industrial parts. 2026 humanoids lack the precision, force feedback, and tool-use reliability for unstructured industrial assembly.

task: Measure and cut insulation for covering surfaces, using tape measures, handsaws, knives, and scissors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and cutting one piece of insulation to fit a surface section. A skilled worker measures, marks, and cuts in ~10–20 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement with a tape measure, interpreting spatial dimensions, marking, and controlled cutting with handsaws/knives/scissors. Multi-tool dexterity, force control, and geometric reasoning on varied surfaces are well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Press paper tape over joints to embed tape into sealing compound and to seal joints.
human_time_rationale: One instance = taping one drywall joint (~4–6 ft). A skilled taper applies compound, presses tape, and smooths it in about 2–4 minutes per joint.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled pressing along a linear joint, uniform embedding of tape into wet compound, and smooth tool manipulation over a vertical surface. Current humanoids lack the fine compliant control and surface-tracking needed for acceptable finish quality.

task: Issue ticket stubs or place numbered tags on windshields, log tags or attach tag to customers' keys, and give customers matching tags for locating parked vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one vehicle arrival: hand over ticket stub, place/log a numbered tag on windshield or keys, and give customer the matching tag. ~1–3 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires social interaction, fine dexterity (attaching tags to keys, placing stubs), outdoor mobility, and generalization across varied vehicles and customers. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation of small tags/keys and natural customer handoff.

task: Sand or patch nicks or cracks in plasterboard or wallboard.
human_time_rationale: One instance = patching and/or sanding a single nick or crack. Includes applying compound, letting it set briefly, and sanding smooth — ~15–25 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine force-controlled surface work, precise detection of small defects, consistent pressure across irregular surfaces, and tool switching (putty knife, sandpaper). Current humanoids lack the tactile feedback and surface perception needed for acceptable quality.

task: Hang ventilation tubing and ventilation curtains to ensure that the mining face area is kept properly ventilated.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing ventilation tubing and curtains across a single mining face section. Competent miners typically spend 60–120 minutes routing, hanging, and securing tubing and curtains in a confined heading.
robot_success_rationale: Underground mining environments are dark, uneven, confined, and highly unstructured. Hanging heavy flexible tubing and curtains requires significant reach, strength, and overhead dexterous manipulation. No current humanoid platform is rated for underground mining conditions or this task complexity.

task: Prescribe or administer medication, therapy, and other specialized medical care to treat or prevent illness, disease, or injury.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient encounter for prescribing/administering treatment. Includes assessment, decision-making, and administration; ~20–40 min is typical for a clinical visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced clinical reasoning, diagnosis, legal licensure, fine dexterous procedures (injections, IV lines), and patient communication. Far beyond any 2026 general-purpose robot's autonomous capability.

task: Remove existing plaster, drywall, or paneling, using crowbars and hammers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing wall covering from one standard room (approx. 400 sq ft of wall surface). A competent worker with crowbar and hammer takes ~1 hour.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force impacts with tools (crowbar/hammer) in varied, unstructured environments. Humanoids lack the sustained force output, tool-wielding robustness, debris management, and structural awareness needed. Safety and generalization are major blockers.

task: Adjust dental appliances to produce and maintain normal function.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single dental appliance (e.g., retainer, denture, brace wire) per patient visit; a skilled dental professional typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter dexterity inside the oral cavity, precise force control, real-time clinical judgment, and patient interaction. Far beyond current humanoid fine-motor and perception capabilities; safety concerns are prohibitive.

task: Use mechanical applicators that spread compounds and embed tape in one operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = taping and mudding one typical room's drywall joints with a mechanical applicator. A skilled finisher takes ~30–60 min for a standard room.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a specialized mechanical taping tool with precise pressure, speed, and angle control along irregular joints. Current humanoids lack the tool-use generalization, force feedback, and construction-environment mobility needed.

task: Provide dressing assistance to cast members or assign cast dressers to assist specific cast members with costume changes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one cast member through one costume change, including locating the costume, dressing assistance, and fastening. Typical quick-change ~3–15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for buttons/zippers/hooks on delicate theatrical costumes, nuanced human-body interaction, real-time coordination with a moving person, and situational judgment for assignments — all well beyond current robot capability.

task: Sand rough spots of dried cement between applications of compounds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sanding rough/dried cement spots on one wall section or joint between compound coats; a competent finisher takes ~5–10 minutes per pass.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying subtle surface defects via touch/vision, applying consistent variable pressure with a sanding tool, and navigating a construction environment. Current robots lack reliable tactile feedback and fine force control for surface finishing tasks.

task: Deliver and pick up medical records, lab specimens, and medications to and from hospitals and other medical facilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single delivery/pickup run between two medical facility locations; includes transit, handoff, and return, typically 20–30 minutes median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating between buildings or campuses, operating elevators, interacting with staff, and handling sensitive items (specimens, meds). Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor/multi-building navigation, vehicle operation, and compliant handoff protocols for medical-grade items.

task: Bend steel rods with hand tools or rod-bending machines and weld them with arc-welding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = bending and welding a single steel rod/component. A competent ironworker/welder takes ~20–30 min including setup, bending, and arc-welding one rod assembly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy rod-bending machines, precise force application, and skilled arc welding with high-heat equipment. Current humanoids lack the strength, tool-use fluency, and welding precision for autonomous success.

task: Manage and treat common health problems, such as infections, influenza or pneumonia, as well as serious, chronic, and complex illnesses, in adolescents, adults, and the elderly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient visit (assessment, diagnosis, and treatment planning). A competent physician/NP typically spends 30–60 minutes per patient encounter.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical reasoning, differential diagnosis, physical examination, and prescription authority — far beyond any current robot's cognitive, perceptual, or regulatory capabilities. No general-purpose humanoid can perform autonomous medical diagnosis or treatment.

task: Guard lodging of sequestered jury.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one 8-hour shift guarding a sequestered jury's lodging. A marshal/bailiff stands post continuously; the shift is the standard measurable unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained social authority, nuanced judgment (who may enter, contraband detection, emergencies), communication with jurors/staff, and legal accountability. Current humanoids lack social presence, authority, and reliable autonomous decision-making for high-stakes security scenarios over hours.

task: Prescribe or administer therapy, medication, and other specialized medical care to treat or prevent illness, disease, or injury.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient encounter (assessment, prescription/administration, documentation). Typical clinical visit or medication round takes ~20–40 min for a competent clinician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires medical licensure, clinical reasoning, diagnosis, patient communication, and fine dexterous procedures. Far beyond current robot autonomy—no general-purpose robot can legally or practically prescribe or administer specialized medical care.

task: Frame and mat artwork for display or sale.
human_time_rationale: One instance = framing and matting a single piece of artwork. A competent framer measures, cuts mat, fits artwork, assembles frame, and secures backing in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, fine cutting of mat board, careful handling of fragile artwork, and intricate assembly with tight tolerances. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use capabilities; high damage risk.

task: Fit dental appliances in patients' mouths to alter the position and relationship of teeth and jaws or to realign teeth.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting an appliance (e.g., retainer, aligner, band, or brace) for one patient; includes trial fitting, adjustment, and patient instruction. Typically 20–45 minutes in a clinical setting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity inside a patient's mouth, sterile tool use, real-time haptic feedback, patient cooperation management, and clinical judgment. Far beyond any current general-purpose robot capability; risks patient harm.

task: Apply additional coats to fill in holes and make surfaces smooth.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying additional coats to a standard wall section (~10–20 sq ft). Includes spreading compound, feathering edges, and light sanding between coats; ~45 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine force-controlled spreading, feathering, and tactile feedback to achieve a smooth, defect-free surface. Current humanoids lack the dexterous tool control and surface-quality perception needed for acceptable finishing work.

task: Measure and take impressions of patients' jaws and teeth to determine the shape and size of dental prostheses, using face bows, dental articulators, recording devices, and other materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full impression and measurement session for one patient, including tray selection, material mixing, bite registration, facebow transfer, and articulator mounting. Typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working inside a patient's mouth with extreme precision and care, using specialized dental instruments (face bows, articulators) on a moving, sensitive human subject. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, safety, and compliance capabilities.

task: Restore function and aesthetics to traumatic injury survivors, or to individuals with diseases or congenital disabilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single reconstructive procedure/session (surgical or prosthetic fitting). Median across consultation, physical intervention, and immediate post-procedure care is ~90 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sterile surgical technique, patient-specific judgment, and adaptive decision-making far beyond current humanoid capability. No frontier robot can perform reconstructive or prosthetic procedures autonomously.

task: Prepare for recording sessions by performing such activities as selecting and setting up microphones.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single recording session: selecting appropriate mics, positioning, cabling, gain-staging, and testing. A competent audio engineer typically takes 20–45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert audio knowledge for mic selection, precise positioning (inches matter acoustically), fine cable/XLR handling, and console interaction. Current humanoids lack audio domain expertise, fine dexterity for connectors, and contextual judgment for room acoustics.

task: Design and fabricate dental prostheses, or supervise dental technicians and laboratory bench workers who construct the devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single dental prosthesis (e.g., a crown or denture). A competent dental lab technician typically spends 3–5 hours on a full unit across all stages.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (sub-millimeter precision), specialized dental tools, interpretation of lab prescriptions, material knowledge, and quality judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities. CAD/CAM dental mills exist but are not general-purpose robots.

task: Wash concrete surfaces before mounting tile to increase adhesive qualities of surfaces, using washing soda and zinc sulfate solution.
human_time_rationale: One instance = washing one typical wall/floor section (~10–15 sq ft) before tiling. Mixing solution, scrubbing, and rinsing takes ~15–25 minutes for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires mixing precise chemical solutions, controlled scrubbing pressure on concrete, managing liquid/tools in a construction environment, and quality judgment of surface prep. Current humanoids lack reliable wet-chemical handling, precise force control on rough surfaces, and task-specific generalization.

task: Maintain order in courtroom during trial and guard jury from outside contact.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full trial day as a courtroom bailiff/court officer. A typical trial day runs ~8 hours of active duty including maintaining order and jury escort.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced human judgment, social authority, de-escalation, legal protocol awareness, and situational threat assessment. Robots lack credible presence, legal standing, and cannot reliably detect/intercept unauthorized jury contact or courtroom disturbances.

task: Build wheels by cutting and threading new spokes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = building one wheel (cutting, threading, and lacing all spokes). A skilled wheelbuilder takes ~60–120 min per wheel including spoke prep and lacing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise thread-cutting on thin metal rods, fine dexterous lacing through tiny hub/rim holes in a specific pattern, and tensioning. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and force-control capabilities.

task: Install and adjust brakes and brake pads.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing brake pads and adjusting brakes on one axle (two wheels). A competent mechanic takes ~30–60 minutes including inspection, removal, installation, and adjustment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires jacking a vehicle, removing wheels, torquing caliper bolts precisely, compressing pistons, and fine adjustment — all in an unstructured under-vehicle environment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and spatial reasoning in confined spaces.

task: Apply or mount acoustical tile or blocks, strips, or sheets of shock-absorbing materials to ceilings or walls of buildings to reduce reflection of sound or to decorate rooms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing acoustical tile/panels in one room section (~50 sq ft). A skilled installer measures, cuts, and mounts panels in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires overhead reaching, precise alignment on ceilings, cutting materials to fit, and adhesive/mechanical fastening — all while navigating ladders or lifts. Current humanoids lack overhead dexterity, spatial planning for ceiling work, and safe ladder use.

task: Design and fabricate appliances, such as space maintainers, retainers, and labial and lingual arch wires.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and fabricating a single dental appliance (e.g., a retainer or space maintainer). A skilled dental lab technician typically takes 60–120 minutes per appliance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (bending thin arch wires, handling dental acrylics, soldering), specialized dental lab tools, precise spatial reasoning on custom patient models, and domain-specific knowledge. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Fit parts together in pre-assembly to ensure that dimensions are accurate.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting a set of parts together for a single pre-assembly check. A competent worker inspects, aligns, and test-fits components, typically taking 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to align and seat parts precisely, tolerance judgement, and adaptive force sensing. Current humanoids handle structured pick-and-place but struggle with precise dimensional fitting, subtle misalignment detection, and iterative adjustments.

task: Appraise vendor manufacturing capabilities through on-site observations or other measurements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single vendor site visit/audit. A competent auditor typically spends 3–5 hours on-site observing processes, measuring outputs, and documenting findings.
robot_success_rationale: Task demands expert judgment, structured interviewing, document review, and contextual reasoning far beyond current robot cognition. Physical observation is only a small fraction; autonomous appraisal and reporting is infeasible for 2026 robots.

task: Make adjustments to patterns after fittings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single garment pattern after one fitting session. A skilled tailor/patternmaker spends ~20–40 min marking, measuring, and redrawing pattern corrections.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting fitting notes, fine-dexterous manipulation of thin paper/fabric pattern pieces, precise measuring, drawing curves, and expert domain knowledge. Far beyond current robot generalization and fine motor capability.

task: Make and fit prosthetic appliances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating and fitting a single prosthetic appliance for a patient. Includes casting, shaping, adjusting, and fitting — typically 2–4 hours for a skilled prosthetist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for fabrication, patient-specific anatomical assessment, iterative tactile fitting on a human body, clinical judgment, and real-time adaptation. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Treat children who have minor illnesses, acute and chronic health problems, and growth and development concerns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pediatric patient visit. A competent clinician typically spends 20–40 minutes assessing, diagnosing, and treating a child per encounter.
robot_success_rationale: Requires licensed clinical judgment, nuanced symptom assessment, child communication, physical examination, and treatment decisions. Far beyond current robot cognitive, dexterous, and social capabilities. No autonomous clinical robots exist for this.

task: Diagnose teeth and jaw or other dental-facial abnormalities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full dental/facial diagnostic exam for one patient, including history review, visual inspection, probing, and imaging interpretation. A competent dentist typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine intraoral perception, X-ray/imaging interpretation, patient communication, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack dental instrumentation, certified diagnostic AI integration, safe intraoral manipulation, and regulatory clearance for autonomous diagnosis.

task: Repair, reline, or rebase dentures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing or relining a single denture. A dental lab technician typically spends 45–90 minutes on material prep, fitting, curing, and finishing one denture.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for grinding, shaping, and fitting dental acrylic; precise tactile feedback; specialized dental tools; and clinical judgment about fit. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Operate on patients to remove, repair, or improve functioning of diseased or injured body parts and systems.
human_time_rationale: One surgical procedure; median operative time across common surgeries (e.g., appendectomy, hernia repair, cholecystectomy) is roughly 2–3 hours for a competent surgeon.
robot_success_rationale: Surgery demands sub-millimeter dexterity, sterile tool handling, real-time clinical judgment, and legal/ethical accountability far beyond any 2026 general-purpose humanoid. Dedicated surgical robots (da Vinci) are teleoperated, not autonomous humanoids.

task: Use bonding technology on the surface of the teeth to change tooth shape or to close gaps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = bonding procedure on a single tooth. A competent dentist typically takes 30–90 minutes per tooth, including etching, resin application, shaping, and curing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity inside a small, wet oral cavity, precise material layering, aesthetic judgment, and patient safety management. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability; even surgical robots lack this generalization.

task: Refill, change, and monitor the level of fluids, such as oil and coolant, in machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking and topping off oil and coolant on a single machine; locating caps, reading dipstick/sight glass, pouring fluid, and confirming level takes ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating varied fluid ports, reading dipsticks/sight glasses, handling fluid containers and funnels, and avoiding spills. Current robots lack reliable fluid-level perception, fine pouring control, and generalization across machine types.

task: Immunize patients to protect them from preventable diseases.
human_time_rationale: One instance = administering a single vaccine to one patient, including prep (drawing vaccine, swabbing site), injection, and brief post-injection observation. ~10 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous needle handling, sterile technique, precise anatomical targeting on a moving/flinching human, patient communication, and real-time safety judgment — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Assess clients' soft tissue condition, joint quality and function, muscle strength, and range of motion.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full musculoskeletal assessment of one client, covering soft tissue, joints, muscle strength, and ROM. A skilled clinician typically takes 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced tactile palpation, clinical reasoning, patient communication, and dynamic resistance testing. Current robots lack the fine-touch sensitivity, safe force modulation, and diagnostic judgment needed for autonomous clinical assessment.

task: Examine patients or order, perform, and interpret diagnostic tests to obtain information on medical condition and determine diagnosis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient encounter including history, physical exam, ordering/reviewing tests, and forming a diagnosis. Typical outpatient visit runs 30–60 minutes for a competent physician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced clinical reasoning, nuanced physical examination, ordering/interpreting tests, and communicating diagnoses — far beyond current robot cognition, dexterity, and medical licensing/integration with clinical systems.

task: Fit, wrap, staple, or glue insulating materials to structures or surfaces, using hand tools or wires.
human_time_rationale: One instance = insulating one standard wall section or surface area (~10–20 sq ft). A competent insulation worker takes ~30–60 min to measure, cut, fit, and fasten material.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied dexterous tool use (staple guns, glue, wires), handling flexible/compressible materials, precise fitting into irregular gaps, and navigating cluttered construction sites — well beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Collect data for energy conservation analyses, using jobsite observation, field inspections, or sub-metering.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full site data-collection visit (observation, inspection, and sub-metering setup/readings) at a typical commercial or industrial jobsite; 2 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex, unstructured jobsites; connecting/reading sub-meters; interpreting diverse energy equipment; and recording structured data. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor/industrial mobility, fine connector dexterity, and domain-specific sensor integration for autonomous energy auditing.

task: Position and secure steel bars, rods, cables, or mesh in concrete forms, using fasteners, rod-bending machines, blowtorches, or hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning and securing rebar/mesh in a single concrete form section (e.g., a wall panel or footing). A skilled ironworker typically takes 30–60 min per form section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy manipulation of long, unwieldy steel bars, precise bending with specialized machines, wire-tying in tight spaces, and use of blowtorches. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, payload, and tool-use capabilities in unstructured outdoor/construction environments.

task: Stop people from entering courtroom while judge charges jury.
human_time_rationale: One instance = standing guard at courtroom door during a single jury charge session, typically 30–60 min. Median ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained doorway presence, social interaction to redirect people, reading context/badges, and handling non-compliant individuals. Current robots lack reliable social negotiation, authority signaling, and situational judgment in dynamic public settings.

task: Analyze and process evidence at crime scenes, during autopsies, or in the laboratory, wearing protective equipment and using powders and chemicals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing evidence at a single crime scene, autopsy, or lab session. A competent forensic tech typically spends 1–3 hours per scene/session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous chemical application (powders, reagents), precise evidence handling with gloves, contextual judgment, sterile protocol compliance, and adaptive perception across unstructured, highly variable environments. Far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Install sheet metal around insulated pipes with screws to protect the insulation from weather conditions or physical damage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = jacketing one typical pipe section (~3–5 ft). A skilled pipefitter measures, cuts, wraps, and screws sheet metal jacketing around an insulated pipe section in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bending/shaping of thin sheet metal, aligning curved jacketing around a pipe, and driving screws in tight spaces. Beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use; sheet metal handling is highly deformable and contact-sensitive.

task: Examine children regularly to assess their growth and development.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single child's routine developmental assessment (measurements, observations, milestone checks). A competent pediatric professional typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social interaction, clinical judgment, developmental milestone interpretation, and safe handling of children. Current robots lack pediatric assessment protocols, reliable child-safe dexterity, and the cognitive/social perception needed for holistic developmental evaluation.

task: Clean windshields.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one vehicle's windshield (interior + exterior). A competent worker sprays and wipes both sides in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle flat surface wiping, but windshields are curved, angled, and require navigating around a vehicle, applying appropriate pressure, and avoiding streaks. Outdoor/unstructured environment and tool-use coordination reduce success significantly.

task: Administer anesthetic or sedation during medical procedures, using local, intravenous, spinal, or caudal methods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = administering anesthesia/sedation for a single procedure. Includes patient assessment, drug preparation, administration, and initial monitoring. Typical moderate procedure takes ~45 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires medical licensure, precise pharmacological judgment, sterile IV/spinal needle placement, real-time adaptive patient monitoring, and emergency response capability. Far beyond current robot dexterity, perception, and clinical reasoning.

task: Examine patient, obtain medical history, and use diagnostic tests to determine risk during surgical, obstetrical, and other medical procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full pre-procedural anesthesiology or surgical risk assessment for one patient, including physical exam, history intake, chart review, and test interpretation. Typically 45–90 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced clinical reasoning, medical knowledge, nuanced patient communication, interpretation of labs/imaging, and professional judgment. Far beyond current robot cognition, dexterity, and autonomous medical decision-making capabilities.

task: Cover, seal, or finish insulated surfaces or access holes with plastic covers, canvas strips, sealants, tape, cement, or asphalt mastic.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sealing/finishing one insulated surface or access hole. A competent insulator applies materials (tape, mastic, sealant, etc.) to a single section in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires applying varied viscous materials (mastic, sealant, tape) with consistent pressure and coverage on irregular surfaces. Multi-material tool-switching, fine force control, and surface conformance are beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Sort and deliver library mail and packages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting and delivering a typical daily batch of library mail/packages across a library building. A competent worker sorts items (~10–20 pieces) and walks routes to deliver them in ~45 min.
