robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep/wipe flat surfaces reasonably well, but cleaning equipment (varied shapes, knobs, crevices, hoses) requires dexterity and generalization that frontier humanoids still struggle with. Unstructured environments reduce reliability.

task: Fill cracks or breaks in surfaces of plaster articles or areas with putty or epoxy compounds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling cracks in a single plaster article or wall area. A competent worker spends ~5 min prepping and ~10 min applying/smoothing putty or epoxy compound.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (putty knife/spatula), precise force-controlled spreading into irregular cracks, and visual inspection of surface quality — well beyond current humanoid manipulation capabilities.

task: Smooth surfaces of articles to be painted, using sanding and buffing tools and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sanding/buffing one article (e.g., a panel or part) to paint-ready smoothness. A competent worker typically takes 15–25 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires controlled, consistent force application across varied surface geometries, tactile feedback to detect imperfections, and tool-switching. Current robots lack the fine force control, adaptive compliance, and surface-quality perception needed for acceptable paint-prep results.

task: Remove excess grout or residue from tile or brick joints, using sponges or trowels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning grout/residue from a ~10–15 sq ft tiled surface. A competent worker scrubs joints methodically with sponge/trowel, rinsing and repeating until clean.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise contact force along narrow grout lines, wet sponge/trowel tool-use, and adaptive tactile feedback. Current humanoids lack the fine force control, compliant manipulation, and surface-tracking needed for consistent residue removal without tile damage.

task: Perform support duties to assist painters, paperhangers, plasterers, or masons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one shift's worth of support duties (fetching materials, mixing, staging equipment) for a tradesperson. ~60 min is a reasonable unit of sustained support work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating cluttered construction sites, carrying awkward loads, mixing compounds, and interpreting ad hoc verbal instructions. Rough terrain, unstructured environments, and varied tool-use push current humanoids well below general-purpose benchmarks.

task: Mix plaster, and carry plaster to plasterers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mix one batch of plaster (~1–2 buckets) and carry it to the work area. Includes measuring, mixing, and transporting; ~15 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires pouring/measuring materials, operating a mixing tool, and carrying a heavy sloshing bucket over a cluttered construction site. Unstructured environment, heavy loads, and wet material handling are beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Pour specified amounts of chemical solutions into stripping tanks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pouring a specified amount of one chemical solution into a stripping tank. Includes locating container, measuring, and safely pouring; ~5–10 min per pour.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping heavy/sloshing chemical containers, precise volumetric measurement, safe pouring without spills, and navigating industrial floor clutter. Liquid handling, precise measurement, and chemical safety constraints are major limitations for current humanoids.

task: Clean work site after completion of job.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a typical trade work site (e.g., construction/electrical/plumbing job) after job completion; includes debris removal, tool collection, and surface cleanup.
robot_success_rationale: Work sites are highly unstructured with varied debris types, heavy materials, tools, and outdoor/rough terrain. Current robots lack the generalization, heavy lifting capacity, and situational judgment needed. Basic sweeping possible but full task completion unlikely.

task: Assemble gates, and fasten gates into position, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and fastening one gate into position. A competent worker typically spends ~30–60 min fitting components, aligning, and securing hardware with hand tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of heavy/awkward gate panels, sustained torque with hand tools, outdoor/unstructured terrain navigation, and multi-step sequencing. Current humanoids lack the strength, dexterity, and tool-use reliability for this compound task.

task: Gauge oil and gas production.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reading and recording gauges at a single wellhead or production unit, including walking the site, checking meters, and logging values. Typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating unstructured outdoor/industrial terrain, reading analog/digital gauges of varied types, operating valves, and logging data. Outdoor terrain, weather, and non-standardized equipment severely challenge current humanoids.

task: Place articles to be stripped into stripping tanks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing a single article (e.g., a metal part) into a stripping tank. A worker picks, positions, and submerges the item in ~3 minutes including handling and positioning.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of industrial parts is feasible, but chemical tank environments are hazardous and unstructured. Variable part shapes, wet/slippery surfaces, precise submersion placement, and safety constraints around corrosive chemicals reduce reliability significantly.

task: Operate heavy equipment, such as bulldozers and front-end loaders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full operator shift (e.g., grading a site or moving material all day). A typical managed work unit is a full shift of ~8 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot physically operate bulldozers or loaders in real traffic/job-site conditions. They lack the size, strength, integrated controls, and situational judgment. L4 autonomy exists only in narrow, mapped contexts. No general-purpose humanoid can do this today.

task: Remove captured animals from animal-control service vehicles and place animals in shelter cages or other enclosures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one captured animal from a vehicle trap/carrier and placing it in a shelter cage. Includes safe handling, transport across short distance, and securing the enclosure door.
robot_success_rationale: Live, unpredictable animals require adaptive gripping and behavioral awareness; robots struggle with varied animal sizes, stress responses, biting/scratching, and operating vehicle latches plus cage doors in sequence. High failure risk.

task: Apply protective coverings, such as masking tape, to articles or areas that could be damaged or stained by work processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = masking/covering one article or work area (e.g., taping off a window frame or fixture before painting). A competent worker typically takes 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires peeling, precisely aligning, and pressing adhesive tape along irregular edges and contours — demanding fine dexterity and tactile feedback. Current humanoids struggle with thin, flexible materials and precise edge-following on varied surfaces.

task: Conduct wildland firefighting training.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single wildland firefighting training session (classroom + field exercises) for a crew; a competent instructor typically runs a full-day 8-hour session.
robot_success_rationale: Training requires dynamic instruction, adaptive communication, real-time demonstration of field techniques, outdoor uneven terrain navigation, and scenario facilitation — far beyond current humanoid robot generalization, language instruction, and outdoor mobility capabilities.

task: Remove damaged tile, brick, or mortar, and clean or prepare surfaces, using pliers, hammers, chisels, drills, wire brushes, or metal wire anchors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing damaged material and preparing one surface section (e.g., a few tiles or a localized area of brick/mortar). A competent tradesperson typically spends 30–60 minutes per repair site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires forceful, precise tool use (hammers, chisels, drills) on variable surfaces, adaptive grip changes, vibration tolerance, and damage-avoidance judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and generalization capabilities.

task: Exchange money, credit, tickets, or casino chips and make change for customers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one customer transaction (exchange/change). Competent cashier/teller handles counting, verification, and handoff in ~3 minutes on average.
robot_success_rationale: Requires counting/authenticating currency, operating registers or chip trays, fine dexterous handling of bills/coins/chips, customer interaction, and fraud detection — well beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Issue clothing, tools, and other authorized items to inmates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = issuing a full set of authorized items to one inmate. Involves pulling items, checking sizes/quantities, recording issuance, and handing over. ~5–10 minutes per inmate.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a secured correctional facility, accessing storage, identifying correct items by size/type, managing paperwork/records, and interacting safely with potentially non-cooperative inmates. Perception, social interaction, and security-environment access are major blockers.

task: Wind parking meter clocks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = winding a single mechanical parking meter clock; a worker walks up, inserts a key or turns a knob, and winds it in about 1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating outdoor uneven sidewalks, locating the winding mechanism on varied meter models, inserting a specialized key, and applying precise rotational torque — fine dexterity and tool-use on diverse real-world hardware is a major frontier limitation.

task: Assess clients' cognitive abilities and physical and emotional needs to determine appropriate interventions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full client assessment session, including cognitive screening, physical evaluation, and emotional/social needs review. A competent clinician typically takes 45–90 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced clinical reasoning, nuanced emotional perception, therapeutic communication, and professional judgment to determine interventions. Current robots lack social-emotional intelligence, diagnostic reasoning, and regulatory standing for such assessments.

task: Prepare pointe shoes, by sewing or other means, for use in rehearsals and performance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one pair of pointe shoes. A professional dancer spends ~30–60 min sewing ribbons/elastics, breaking in, and padding a single pair.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine needle-and-thread sewing through thick satin/canvas, precise ribbon placement, and shoe-breaking techniques — all demanding extreme dexterity and tactile feedback well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Maintain or repair lab equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance or repair job on one piece of lab equipment (e.g., centrifuge, microscope, autoclave). Competent technician averages ~45 min including diagnosis, repair, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Lab equipment repair demands expert-level diagnosis, fine dexterity for small/varied components, tool-use with calibrated force, and domain knowledge. Current humanoids lack the perception precision, specialized tool handling, and generalization needed for autonomous repair across diverse lab instruments.

task: Inspect products to ensure that the quality standards and specifications are met.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single product unit on a line; a competent QC worker typically spends 3–7 minutes checking dimensions, surface finish, labels, and function.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is mature, but generalizing across varied products, specs, and subtle defects (texture, odor, flex) without task-specific fixtures is still limited. Handling/repositioning for multi-side inspection adds manipulation risk.

task: Supply or hold tools and materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retrieving and handing off a single tool or material to a worker. A competent human locates, picks up, and delivers in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place common objects at ~70–90%, but identifying the correct tool among clutter, navigating to a worker, and safely handing it off reduces reliability. Unstructured environments and grasp variability are key limitations.

task: Arrest persons in court when arrest warrants have been issued.
human_time_rationale: One instance = arresting one person in a courtroom on a warrant: identifying the individual, restraining them, and processing paperwork. Typically 15–25 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires legal authority, social/verbal de-escalation, reliable person ID, safe physical restraint of a potentially resisting human, and judgment under high-stakes conditions — all far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Develop, arrange for, perform, or assess executive protection activities to reduce security risks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = planning/executing a single executive protection detail (advance work, escort, threat assessment). A typical half-day engagement for a competent protective agent.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level threat assessment, social judgment, dynamic decision-making, vehicle operation, communication, and coordination with humans under stress. Far beyond current robot cognition, mobility, and autonomous social reasoning capabilities.

task: Measure and lay out fence lines and mark posthole positions, following instructions, drawings, or specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and marking a single fence line run (typical residential section ~50–100 ft). A competent worker uses tape, string lines, and stakes to lay out and mark posthole positions in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor navigation on uneven terrain, reading drawings, precise spatial measurement, driving stakes, and marking ground — combining locomotion, tool-use, and document interpretation at a level well beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Instruct patients in proper body mechanics and in ways to improve functional mobility, such as aquatic exercise.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient instruction session covering body mechanics and functional mobility (e.g., aquatic exercise). A typical session runs 20–45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced verbal instruction, real-time patient assessment, adaptive demonstration, empathy, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable speech-driven clinical coaching, patient-specific adaptation, and safe physical demonstration in therapeutic contexts.

task: Mix and pour concrete around bases of posts, or tamp soil into postholes to embed posts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting one post (mixing/pouring concrete or tamping soil). A competent worker takes ~15–25 min per post including mixing, pouring, leveling, and bracing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy tool use (mixing concrete, operating tamper), precise leveling, pouring viscous material, and sustained forceful manipulation outdoors on uneven terrain — well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Establish the location for a fence, and gather information needed to ensure that there are no electric cables or water lines in the area.
human_time_rationale: Surveying a typical residential fence line: walking the area, staking the layout, and coordinating a utility locate call (811). ~60 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires GPS/survey interpretation, phone/web interaction to call 811, understanding property boundaries, and coordinating with utilities — well beyond current autonomous robot capabilities.

task: Change water filters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing one water filter unit (e.g., under-sink or pitcher filter). Includes shutting off water, removing old filter, installing new one, and flushing the system.
robot_success_rationale: Requires turning valves, unscrewing/twisting filter housings under confined spaces (under-sink), precise grip alignment, and leak verification. Tight spaces, varied filter types, and water handling make this extremely difficult for current humanoids.

task: Drive truck equipped with power spraying equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one typical spraying route/job. A competent operator drives to site, operates the route, and returns — roughly 60–120 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a full-size truck on public/site roads — humanoid robots cannot operate truck controls in traffic. Even L4 AVs are route-limited. Combined driving + power-spray operation makes autonomous completion near-impossible in 2026.

task: Clean the projection booth.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single projection booth (dusting equipment, vacuuming floor, wiping surfaces, emptying trash). A competent cinema technician/janitor takes ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Projection booths are cluttered, confined, and filled with sensitive, irregular equipment. Robots struggle with tight spaces, delicate electronics (projectors, servers), varied surface types, and avoiding cable tangles. Navigation and safe dexterity are major blockers.

task: Monitor pumps and flow lines for gas and fluid leaks.
human_time_rationale: One inspection round covering a set of pumps and flow lines; technician walks the area, visually checks fittings, listens for hissing, reads gauges — typically 15–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can walk and use cameras for visual inspection, but detecting subtle gas/fluid leaks requires sniffing sensors, thermal imaging, or audio analysis rarely integrated in current humanoids. Unstructured industrial environments add locomotion and perception challenges.

task: Circulate among visitors, patrons, or employees to preserve order and protect property.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patrol shift circuit (e.g., a full walk-through of a venue). A competent security guard completes a full circuit in ~60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can navigate flat indoor spaces, but preserving order requires social perception, threat assessment, crowd interaction, verbal intervention, and judgment — far beyond current autonomous capability. High failure risk in unstructured social environments.

task: Clean casino areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one casino area (e.g., gaming floor section, restroom, or lounge). A competent cleaner sweeping, mopping, wiping surfaces, and emptying bins takes ~60 min per zone.
robot_success_rationale: Casino floors are dense, cluttered, and dynamic — crowded with chairs, slot machines, cables, and patrons. Navigation and obstacle avoidance are challenging; fine cleaning around equipment and furniture reduces success. Current robots lack reliable generalization here.

task: Determine amount of air leakage in buildings, using a blower door machine.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one blower door test on a typical residential building: setup, seal doors/openings, run depressurization test, record readings, and teardown — typically 45–75 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires transporting and mounting a large blower door frame into a doorway, sealing perimeter fabric, connecting a calibrated fan and manometer, operating software, and interpreting results — complex multi-tool setup beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Direct, or assist other workers in, treatment or extermination processes to eliminate or control rodents, insects, or weeds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = directing/assisting a treatment or extermination session at a single site (e.g., one building or property). A typical pest control visit runs ~45–90 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires directing workers (social/verbal authority), applying or supervising chemical treatments in complex environments, navigating tight/outdoor spaces, and operating specialized sprayers/equipment. Generalization, tool-use, and supervisory judgment are far beyond current robots.

task: Remove articles such as cabinets, metal furniture, and paint containers from stripping tanks after prescribed periods of time.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a batch of articles from one stripping tank. Lifting, draining, and setting aside items takes ~5–10 minutes for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Wet, chemically hazardous, heavy, irregularly shaped items in an industrial tank are extremely challenging. Robots face poor perception in caustic liquid, heavy payloads, slippery grasping surfaces, and no standardized item placement — well beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Supply animals with food, water, and personal care.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one feeding/watering/grooming round for a typical group of animals (e.g., farm pen or kennel). Includes filling feeders, refreshing water, and basic personal care.
robot_success_rationale: Animals are unpredictable and move; tasks vary widely (different species, enclosures, food types). Requires dexterous pouring, scooping, grooming, and safe animal interaction — well beyond reliable 2026 robot capability in unstructured settings.

task: Clean installation surfaces, equipment, tools, work sites, or storage areas, using water, chemical solutions, oxygen lances, or polishing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one surface/tool/equipment unit. A competent worker spends ~30 min per cycle depending on contamination and method used.
robot_success_rationale: Routine wiping/mopping is feasible (~40–70%), but this task adds chemical selection, oxygen lances, and polishing machines — specialized tool use requiring precise force control and chemical handling that 2026 humanoids handle poorly.

task: Dig postholes, using spades, posthole diggers, or power-driven augers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = digging one posthole. A competent worker using a manual posthole digger or spade takes ~15–25 minutes depending on soil conditions and hole depth.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained high-force tool use (spade, clamshell digger, or power auger) in unstructured outdoor terrain with variable soil. Current humanoids lack the torque, stability, and tool-operation capability for this task. Power auger operation is especially beyond current systems.

task: Perform regular maintenance tasks, such as rotating or replacing xenon bulbs, cleaning projectors and lenses, lubricating machinery, and keeping electrical contacts clean and tight.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance session covering bulb rotation/replacement, projector/lens cleaning, lubrication, and electrical contact upkeep in a cinema/AV setting — ~30–60 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (bulb handling, lens cleaning, electrical contacts), specialized tool use (lubricants, electrical tools), and safety-critical steps with high-voltage xenon lamps. Far beyond reliable autonomous robot capability in 2026.

task: Set metal or wooden posts in upright positions in postholes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting one post in a pre-dug posthole. Includes positioning, leveling, and temporary bracing. A competent worker averages ~15 min per post.
robot_success_rationale: Requires lifting and precisely aligning a long, heavy, unwieldy post outdoors in variable terrain, then holding it plumb while securing. Outdoor mobility, bimanual heavy load handling, and leveling judgment are well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Photograph crime or accident scenes for evidence records.
human_time_rationale: One instance = photographing a single crime/accident scene. A competent forensic photographer spends ~30–60 min systematically covering overview, mid-range, and close-up shots with markers and scales.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert scene interpretation, dynamic/outdoor navigation over debris/hazards, strategic framing with scales/markers, legal evidentiary standards, and judgment about what to document. Far beyond current autonomous robot capability.

task: Load machines onto trucks to prepare for transportation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading one machine onto a truck, including rigging, positioning, and securing. Competent workers with a forklift or crane typically take 30–60 minutes depending on machine size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting far beyond humanoid payload limits, operating forklifts/cranes, rigging judgment, and precise coordination on uneven outdoor terrain. Current general-purpose robots cannot safely or reliably perform this task.

task: Inspect premises to identify infestation source and extent of damage to property, wall, or roof porosity and access to infested locations.
human_time_rationale: One inspection instance covers a typical residential property; a competent pest/property inspector takes ~30–60 min to survey interior, exterior, walls, roof, and access points.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating cluttered/outdoor terrain, inspecting roof/wall voids, identifying pest evidence (droppings, damage, entry points) with expert-level perception, and forming a holistic assessment — far beyond current robot generalization and judgment.

task: Nail top and bottom rails to fence posts, or insert them in slots on posts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = securing top and bottom rails to one fence bay (between two posts). A competent worker nails or slots two rails in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor terrain navigation, precise alignment of long rails to posts, forceful nailing or slot insertion, and tool use. Current humanoids lack the stability, grip strength on long members, and precision for reliable outdoor fence assembly.

task: Package, store and retrieve evidence.
human_time_rationale: One instance = packaging a single piece of evidence, logging it, and placing it in the correct storage location. ~15 min covers labeling, sealing, chain-of-custody entry, and shelving.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation (sealing bags, affixing labels), reading/writing chain-of-custody documentation, navigating evidence storage rooms, and precise retrieval by case ID — far beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Inspect physical conditions of warehouses, vehicle fleets, or equipment and order testing, maintenance, repairs, or replacements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single facility/fleet/equipment unit and issuing follow-up orders. A competent inspector typically spends ~1 hour per unit including documentation and ordering.
robot_success_rationale: While robots can navigate and capture sensor data, autonomously diagnosing faults, judging severity, and then placing maintenance/repair/replacement orders requires high-level reasoning, domain judgment, and system integration far beyond current frontier robots.

task: Dress and reshape worn or damaged railroad switch points or frogs, using portable power grinders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dressing/reshaping one switch point or frog. A skilled trackworker inspects, sets up a portable grinder, and grinds to profile — typically 30–60 minutes per component.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled grinding on hardened steel rail profiles outdoors on uneven ballast, heavy tool handling, expert tactile/visual feedback, and profile gauging. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, perception, and outdoor stability limits.

task: Insert metal tubing through rail supports.
human_time_rationale: One instance = threading one length of metal tubing through a set of rail supports. A competent worker does this in ~2–4 minutes depending on tube length and number of supports.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual alignment of a long rigid tube through multiple constrained holes/brackets. Challenges include coordinating two arms over a long span, applying axial force while maintaining alignment, and handling varying support geometries. Current humanoids lack the bimanual coordination and fine positional accuracy for this reliably.

task: Look for trace evidence, such as fingerprints, hairs, fibers, or shoe impressions, using alternative light sources when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = searching a typical crime scene area (e.g., one room or vehicle). A competent forensic technician spends ~30–60 min systematically scanning surfaces with ALS and collecting trace evidence.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level visual interpretation of faint, irregular trace evidence (fingerprints, fibers, hairs) under varied lighting conditions, precise ALS handling, contextual judgment, and delicate collection—far beyond current robot perception and dexterity capabilities.

task: Inspect and monitor audio or video surveillance equipment to ensure it is working appropriately.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a typical set of A/V surveillance equipment (cameras, monitors, recorders) at a single site; a competent technician checks feeds, indicators, and connections in ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading screens/feeds, interpreting signal quality, checking indicator lights, and diagnosing faults across varied hardware. Current robots lack reliable screen-reading, domain-specific diagnostics, and generalized troubleshooting for diverse surveillance systems.

task: Apply caulk, sealants, or other agents to installed surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one joint/seam application (e.g., bathtub edge or window frame). Worker loads gun, runs bead, and smooths in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, continuous bead control along irregular surfaces, consistent pressure on a caulk gun, and fine smoothing — demanding dexterity and force regulation beyond current humanoid capability. Generalization to varied joints is also poor.

task: Align posts, by lines or sighting, and verify vertical alignment of posts, using plumb bobs or spirit levels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning and verifying plumb on a single post. A skilled worker sights, adjusts, and checks with a level or plumb bob in ~10–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/uneven terrain mobility, precise manipulation of levels/plumb bobs, visual line-sighting, and coordinated bracing adjustments. Perception of plumb deviation and tool handling in field conditions exceed current robot generalization limits.

task: Make rails for fences, by sawing lumber or by cutting metal tubing to required lengths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting one rail to required length. A competent worker measures, marks, and saws/cuts a single piece of lumber or metal tubing in ~8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, marking, securing workpiece in a vice/jig, and operating a saw or metal cutter. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use for power saws, fine measurement reading, and safe workpiece clamping in unstructured shop settings.

task: Conduct field surveys or study maps, graphs, diagrams, or other data to identify and correct power system problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single field survey or data-analysis session to diagnose a specific power system problem, typically 1–3 hours for a skilled electrical engineer or technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level electrical engineering judgment, reading complex maps/graphs/diagrams, outdoor/industrial mobility, and executing corrective actions on live power infrastructure — far beyond current robot autonomy or generalization.

task: Blast rock formations and rocky areas with dynamite to facilitate posthole digging.
human_time_rationale: One instance = blasting a single rocky area/formation for posthole access. Includes site assessment, drilling blast holes, placing dynamite charges, wiring, safety clearance, and detonation — roughly 60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires licensed explosives handling, precision drilling into rock, complex regulatory compliance, outdoor rough terrain, and extreme safety judgment. No current humanoid robot is remotely capable of autonomous explosive demolition work.

task: Perform therapeutic wound care.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning, treating, and dressing a single wound on one patient. A competent nurse or wound-care specialist typically takes 15–25 minutes including assessment, debridement, and redressing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for delicate tissue manipulation, sterile technique, real-time clinical judgment, pain-response adaptation, and precise tool use on irregular biological surfaces — all far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Acquire, distribute and store supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = acquiring, distributing, and storing a typical batch of supplies (e.g., office or warehouse replenishment). Includes retrieval, transport across a facility, and stowing items.
robot_success_rationale: Requires chained subtasks: navigating to source, grasping varied items, transporting across dynamic environments, and placing in correct locations. Generalization across item types, layouts, and storage logic is beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Dust selected areas of crime scene and lift latent fingerprints, adhering to proper preservation procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single scene area/surface set. A competent forensic tech spends ~30–60 min selecting surfaces, applying powder, brushing, and lifting/packaging prints.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert visual judgment to select surfaces, ultra-fine dexterous brushing with controlled pressure, precise tape-lift placement, and chain-of-custody documentation. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities; risk of evidence contamination is disqualifying.

task: Attach fence rail supports to posts, using hammers and pliers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching rail supports to one post (both sides). A competent worker hammers brackets and secures with pliers in ~8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated two-handed tool use (hammer + pliers) on an outdoor post, precise striking force, gripping small hardware, and adapting to varied post/bracket types. Outdoor terrain, tool switching, and force control are beyond current humanoid capability reliably.

task: Buff tires according to specifications for width and undertread depth.
human_time_rationale: One instance = buffing one tire to spec (width + undertread depth). A competent tire retreading technician takes ~10–15 min per tire using a buffing machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a specialized tire buffing machine, precise dimensional measurement (width/undertread depth gauges), and controlled force application. Current humanoids lack the tool-use, precision sensing, and domain-specific machine operation needed for acceptable quality.

task: Raise rails, using hydraulic jacks, to allow for tie removal and replacement.
human_time_rationale: One instance = raising rails at one tie location using hydraulic jacks. A competent track worker positions jacks, lifts the rail, allows tie work, and resets — roughly 15–25 minutes per tie location.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy hydraulic jacks on outdoor rail terrain, precise load-bearing placement, and coordination with track geometry. Humanoid robots lack the strength, tool-use capability, and outdoor mobility for this specialized heavy-infrastructure task.

task: Nail pointed slats to rails to construct picket fences.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one standard 8-ft fence panel (~11 pickets). A competent worker spaces, aligns, and nails each picket to two rails; roughly 3–4 min/picket.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise outdoor positioning on uneven ground, consistent spacing/alignment of multiple pickets, and accurate nail-gun use — a combination of fine dexterity, force control, and generalization that current humanoids handle poorly end-to-end.

task: Devise and participate in activities to improve fish hatching and growth rates, and to prevent disease in hatcheries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and implementing a hatchery activity session (e.g., adjusting water quality, feeding protocols, or disease prevention measures) across a typical hatchery unit. A skilled hatchery technician spends ~1 hour per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level biological reasoning, multi-system decision-making (water chemistry, disease diagnosis, feeding schedules), operating specialized aquaculture equipment, and adapting to dynamic aquatic environments. Far beyond current robot generalization and domain expertise.

task: Pack and ship pottery to stores or galleries for retail sale.
human_time_rationale: One instance = packing and labeling one pottery shipment (1–few pieces): wrapping fragile items, boxing, cushioning, sealing, and labeling for carrier pickup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate handling of irregular fragile ceramics, adaptive wrapping with bubble wrap/paper, choosing appropriate box sizes, and interfacing with shipping systems — well beyond current robot generalization and dexterity.

task: Attach rails or tension wire along bottoms of posts to form fencing frames.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one fence panel/bay (post-to-post span). A competent fencer attaches rails or tension wire between two posts in ~30–60 min depending on material and fastening method.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor unstructured terrain navigation, heavy rail/wire handling, precise alignment and fastening (bolts, clips, wire tensioning). Current humanoids lack the strength, outdoor dexterity, and tool-use generalization for this multi-step construction task.

task: Weld metal parts together, using portable gas welding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one weld joint on a typical metal workpiece. A competent welder sets up, positions, welds, and inspects a single joint in ~20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Gas welding demands precise torch control, flame adjustment, filler rod feeding, and heat management — extreme fine dexterity beyond current humanoids. Operating portable gas equipment (valves, hoses, ignition) adds further unstructured tool-use challenges. Safety risks amplify failure cost.

task: Inspect facilities and equipment for signs of disrepair, and perform necessary maintenance work.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single facility/equipment inspection round plus immediate minor maintenance. A competent worker typically completes a standard walkthrough and basic fixes in ~1 hour.
robot_success_rationale: Inspection requires recognizing subtle disrepair (cracks, corrosion, leaks, wear) across diverse environments; maintenance requires tool use and dexterous repair. Current humanoids lack reliable anomaly detection and complex tool-use for real repairs.

task: Post warning signs and lock building doors to secure area to be fumigated.
human_time_rationale: One instance = securing one building for fumigation: posting warning signs at entry points and locking all doors. Typical building takes ~15–25 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a full building, affixing signs to varied surfaces, and locking diverse door types. Door locking (~40–70%) is partially feasible, but sign posting (tape, adhesive, varied surfaces) and navigating all entry points autonomously at acceptable quality is unreliable for current robots.

task: Position and regulate plant irrigation systems, and program environmental and irrigation control computers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and programming a single irrigation zone/system. A competent worker spends ~1 hour positioning emitters, adjusting regulators, and entering a schedule into the control computer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise plumbing connections, fine valve adjustment, outdoor/greenhouse terrain navigation, AND complex GUI/computer programming. Current humanoids lack the dexterity for fittings and cannot operate arbitrary control software interfaces autonomously.

task: Observe leveling indicator arms to verify levelness and alignment of tracks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking leveling indicator arms along one track section. A competent worker visually inspects and interprets indicators in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating alongside tracks, visually identifying small indicator arms, interpreting alignment/levelness readings, and making a pass/fail judgment. Current robots struggle with fine visual interpretation of analog mechanical indicators in unstructured industrial environments.

task: Collate pages of reports and other documents.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collating one multi-page document set (~20–30 pages). A competent worker sorts, orders, and assembles pages in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Handling thin, flat, easily-fanned paper sheets is extremely challenging for current robots — pages stick, slip, and misalign. Perception of page order/content and fine dexterous paper manipulation remain weak spots for 2026 humanoids.

task: Monitor wildlife populations and assess the impacts of forest operations on population and habitats.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full field survey session covering a designated forest unit, including transect walks, camera trap checks, data recording, and habitat assessment. Typical session: ~4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor rough-terrain navigation, wildlife identification, habitat interpretation, and multi-day autonomous operation. Current humanoids lack outdoor endurance, ecological perception, and the expert judgment needed to assess population impacts.

task: Erect scaffolding.
human_time_rationale: One instance = erecting a standard single-bay/lift scaffolding section (~5m long, 2 lifts). A 2-person crew takes ~2 hours including unpacking, assembling, and securing components.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting of long poles and boards, precise coupling of clamps and pins, working at height, coordinating multi-part assembly in unstructured outdoor environments — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, strength, and safety limits.

task: Conduct site audits to ensure adherence to safety and environmental regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single site audit visit. A competent inspector typically spends 2–4 hours walking the site, checking equipment, documenting findings, and reviewing compliance against regulatory checklists.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex judgment, regulatory knowledge, unstructured outdoor/indoor mobility, reading signage/labels, interpreting hazards contextually, and generating compliant reports. Current humanoids lack regulatory reasoning and reliable outdoor navigation at audit quality.

task: Lubricate machines, change oil, or fill hydraulic reservoirs to specified levels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing one machine (lubricating, oil change, or filling reservoir). A competent mechanic typically takes 15–25 minutes per machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating grease fittings/fill ports, connecting hoses or grease guns precisely, reading fluid levels, and stopping at exact fill thresholds — demanding fine dexterity, tool manipulation, and fluid-handling in unstructured industrial environments well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Respond to medical emergencies, bomb threats, fire alarms, or intrusion alarms, following emergency response procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = responding to a single emergency event from alarm trigger to resolution/handoff. Median across event types (medical, fire, intrusion, bomb) is ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-stakes situational judgment, emergency protocol execution, coordination with responders, triage, and adaptive decision-making under chaos. Far beyond current robot autonomy; safety-critical unpredictability makes autonomous action infeasible.

task: Perform tests of water chemistry in boilers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a water sample from a boiler and running a standard chemistry panel (pH, hardness, dissolved oxygen, conductivity, etc.), including logging results. Typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating boiler sample ports, operating valves, handling chemical test kits or portable analyzers with precision, interpreting results, and logging data — far beyond reliable autonomous capability for current humanoids in industrial environments.

task: Provide for stewardship of plant or animal resources or habitats, studying land use, monitoring animal populations, or providing shelter, resources, or medical treatment for animals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single land-use or habitat survey session (e.g., one field plot assessment). A competent wildlife/land steward typically spends ~2 hours per survey unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor mobility over rough terrain, ecological expertise, multi-sensor data interpretation, and adaptive judgment. Current humanoids have weak outdoor terrain performance and lack ecological domain knowledge for autonomous stewardship decisions.

task: Set mechanical traps, or place poisonous paste or bait in sewers, burrows, or ditches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting/placing traps or bait at a single site (sewer, burrow, or ditch). A skilled pest controller takes ~15–25 min to inspect, prepare, and deploy materials at one location.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating confined, unstructured, wet/dark underground spaces; handling precise bait placement and mechanical trap-setting with fine dexterity; current humanoids lack the mobility, perception, and dexterity for such hazardous, unstructured subterranean environments.

task: Operate single- or multiple-head spike pullers to pull old spikes from ties.
human_time_rationale: One instance = operating a spike puller along a standard rail segment (~1 work shift unit); a competent operator pulls spikes continuously; ~60 min is a reasonable measurable unit of productive operation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized heavy rail maintenance machinery (spike pullers) that a humanoid cannot mount or control. No 2026 general-purpose robot can operate this equipment autonomously in an outdoor track environment.

task: Examine and inspect containers, materials, or products to ensure that product quality and packing specifications are met.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single container/product unit. A competent worker visually checks labeling, seals, fill level, and packaging integrity in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Vision systems can detect obvious defects and label mismatches, but nuanced quality judgment (subtle dents, texture anomalies, spec compliance) across varied container types strains generalization, dexterity for handling, and contextual reasoning in 2026 robots.

task: Engage mechanisms that lay tracks or rails to specified gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying one section of track/rail to gauge spec (~10–20m). A crew using track-laying machinery takes ~1–2 hours per section including alignment and gauge verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy specialized track-laying machines, precise gauge measurement, and coordinating on active rail beds. Humanoid robots cannot operate this machinery; outdoor unstructured terrain worsens prospects further.

task: Drive trucks, tractors, or truck-mounted drills to and from work sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one one-way trip to or from a work site. Typical transit for construction/drilling crews averages ~30–60 min, median ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate trucks, tractors, or drill rigs in real traffic/terrain. L4 AVs exist but only on mapped routes and are separate specialized systems, not general-purpose humanoids.

task: Stretch wire, wire mesh, or chain link fencing between posts, and attach fencing to frames.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a standard section of fencing (e.g., one bay between two posts, ~8–10 ft). A competent fencer stretches, tensions, and secures wire/mesh/chain-link in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force tensioning of flexible materials, wire handling, tool use (come-alongs, fence pliers, ties), and outdoor uneven terrain. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and force-control capabilities for unstructured field work.

task: Direct and monitor trapping and spawning of fish, egg incubation, and fry rearing, applying knowledge of management and fish culturing techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full daily monitoring/management cycle at a hatchery facility, covering trapping checks, spawning operations, egg incubation inspection, and fry rearing oversight. Typically 3–5 hours for a competent hatchery technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert biological judgment, adaptive decision-making, delicate wet-environment dexterity (handling live fish/eggs), operating specialized hatchery equipment, and supervising/directing human staff — all well beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Construct and repair barriers, retaining walls, trellises, and other types of fences, walls, and gates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing or repairing one fence/wall/gate section (e.g., 10–20 ft run). A competent fencing worker takes ~3–5 hours including layout, post-setting, panel/rail attachment, and finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires digging post holes, mixing/pouring concrete, lifting heavy panels, precise alignment, and using multiple power tools outdoors on uneven terrain. Far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capabilities in unstructured outdoor environments.

task: Grind ends of new or worn rails to attain smooth joints, using portable grinders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grinding both ends at a single rail joint. A skilled track worker takes ~20–30 min to set up, grind, and inspect one joint to profile.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/uneven terrain locomotion, forceful two-handed operation of a heavy portable grinder, precise rail-profile tracking, and quality inspection — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and field generalization.

task: Operate single- or multiple-head spike driving machines to drive spikes into ties and secure rails.
human_time_rationale: One instance = driving all spikes needed to secure one rail joint/tie section. A competent operator positions the machine and drives spikes for one tie, typically 4–6 spikes, in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Spike-driving machines are large, specialized rail equipment a humanoid cannot operate or drive. Requires seated cab operation, precise positioning on active track, and heavy tool control far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Train police officers in dog handling and training techniques for tracking, crowd control, and narcotics and bomb detection.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full training day/session for a cohort of officers. Canine handler training typically runs in multi-hour blocks; 8 hours is a standard daily unit a program manager would measure.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires expert instruction, live demonstration with real dogs, nuanced behavioral feedback, and adaptive coaching. Current robots lack animal-handling capability, social pedagogy, and the credibility/authority to train law-enforcement professionals autonomously.

task: Mix and apply glazes to pottery pieces, using tools, such as spray guns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing glaze and applying it to a single pottery piece. A competent potter typically spends ~20 min mixing, testing consistency, masking, and spraying/applying glaze evenly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise glaze consistency judgment, controlled spray gun manipulation, even coating on irregular ceramic forms, and masking. Current robots lack fine dexterous tool control, fluid-consistency sensing, and the perceptual feedback needed for uniform glaze coverage on curved pottery.

task: Patrol industrial or commercial premises to prevent and detect signs of intrusion and ensure security of doors, windows, and gates.
human_time_rationale: One patrol circuit of a typical industrial/commercial facility (checking doors, windows, gates, perimeter). Median circuit takes ~30–60 min for a competent guard.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate flat indoor/outdoor routes and detect anomalies via cameras, but reliable door/gate status checking, outdoor terrain variation, handling unexpected intrusions, and operating locks autonomously remain major limitations for 2026 humanoids.

task: Patrol assigned track sections so that damaged or broken track can be located and reported.
human_time_rationale: One instance = patrolling one assigned track section. A typical railroad patrol section spans several miles; a competent inspector walking or riding takes ~2 hours per section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained outdoor locomotion over rough/uneven ballast and ties for miles, reliable crack/defect perception in variable lighting and weather, and autonomous reporting — all far beyond current humanoid robot capability at acceptable reliability.

task: Clean or make minor repairs to machines or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning or performing a minor repair on a single machine/piece of equipment; a competent worker typically takes 20–40 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Surface wiping/cleaning is feasible (~40–60%), but minor repairs demand tool use, fault diagnosis, and fine dexterity on varied machine geometry—well beyond reliable autonomous capability for current humanoids.

task: Repair or adjust track switches, using wrenches and replacement parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/adjusting one track switch. A competent rail worker diagnoses the issue, retrieves parts, uses wrenches, and verifies function — typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/trackside locomotion on uneven ballast, high-force torque with large wrenches, precise rail component manipulation, fault diagnosis, and part replacement — all far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured field environments.

task: Direct crop production operations, such as planning, tilling, planting, fertilizing, cultivating, spraying, and harvesting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = directing a full day's crop production operations on a working farm, covering planning through execution of one or more field activities. A typical operational day runs ~8 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating tractors, sprayers, harvesters, and other heavy agricultural machinery across unstructured outdoor terrain, plus high-level planning and decision-making. Humanoid robots cannot drive or operate such equipment autonomously at any acceptable quality level.

task: Escort or drive motor vehicle to transport individuals to specified locations or to provide personal protection.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one trip/escort. Typical chauffeured or protective escort trip (e.g., airport run, executive transport) averages ~45 minutes including pickup and drop-off.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate passenger vehicles in traffic. L4 AVs exist but are narrow/mapped-route systems, not general-purpose humanoids. Personal protection requires situational awareness and decision-making far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Warn persons of rule infractions or violations, and apprehend or evict violators from premises, using force when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single enforcement interaction (warning, confrontation, and/or eviction). A typical security/guard encounter from detection to resolution averages ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires social judgment, verbal de-escalation, legal authority, and physical force against a resisting human — far beyond current robot autonomy. Robots lack reliable situational awareness, communication nuance, and safe human-contact force modulation.

task: Clean, grade, or level ballast on railroad tracks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one shift of ballast cleaning/grading/leveling on a track section (~1–2 km). Specialized tamping/ballast regulator machines require a full shift to set up and complete a meaningful segment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating massive specialized railroad machinery (ballast regulators, tampers, undercutters) on live or blocked track. No humanoid robot can operate such vehicles autonomously; outdoor rough terrain and machine controls add further barriers.

task: Clean tracks or clear ice or snow from tracks or switch boxes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing ice/snow/debris from one track section or switch box. Worker with hand tools or blower takes ~30–60 min depending on accumulation and conditions.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor locomotion on uneven/icy rail terrain, forceful scraping/blowing tools, and identifying packed ice in switch boxes — all highly challenging. Current humanoids lack robust outdoor mobility and specialized tool use for this environment.

task: Answer alarms and investigate disturbances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = responding to a single alarm or disturbance, from alert to resolution/report. Typical security/facility response takes ~15–25 minutes including travel, investigation, and logging.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating unpredictable environments, assessing ambiguous situations, communicating with people, and making judgment calls. Current robots lack reliable real-world navigation, threat assessment, and situational reasoning needed for autonomous security response.

task: Drive or guide truck-mounted equipment into position, level and stabilize rigs, and extend telescoping derricks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning a single rig at a job site: driving into position, leveling/stabilizing outriggers, and extending the derrick. A skilled operator typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a truck on real terrain, operating heavy outrigger controls, reading ground conditions, and extending a derrick — all highly specialized, vehicle-integrated tasks far beyond current humanoid or general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Conduct physical examinations of property to ensure compliance with security policies and regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full compliance inspection of a single property/facility. A competent security officer typically takes 30–60 minutes to walk, check, and document findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex, varied environments; reading labels/signage; judgment on policy compliance; documenting violations; and potentially interacting with occupants. Perception and generalization limits are severe; no reliable autonomous compliance reasoning exists in 2026 frontier robots.

task: Drill holes through rails, tie plates, or fishplates for insertion of bolts or spikes, using power drills.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling one set of holes through a rail/tie plate/fishplate. A competent track worker positions, aligns, and drills through hard steel in ~5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/unstructured rail environment, precise alignment of heavy industrial power drill on hard steel, sustained force application, and tool-use generalization. Current humanoids lack the force control, tool-holding stability, and field mobility for this.

task: Push controls to close grasping devices on track or rail sections so that they can be raised or moved.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pushing controls to close grasping devices on one track/rail section. A competent operator locates and actuates the control in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying and operating specialized industrial crane/hoist controls in unstructured settings. Humanoids lack familiarity with heavy-equipment control interfaces, and safe force application on rail hardware is a major generalization challenge.

task: Operate detecting devices to screen individuals and prevent passage of prohibited articles into restricted areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = screening one individual through a checkpoint (walk-through detector, bag X-ray, possible wand/pat-down). Typical airport/venue screening takes ~1–3 minutes per person.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized security equipment (walk-through scanners, X-ray consoles, handheld wands), interpreting ambiguous threat images, making judgment calls, and physically redirecting people — well beyond current humanoid generalization and safety/legal constraints.

task: Monitor environments to ensure maintenance of optimum animal or plant life.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring round of a facility (greenhouse, enclosure, aquarium, etc.). A competent worker walks the space, checks sensors/logs, and notes conditions in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read digital sensors and patrol flat indoor spaces, but interpreting subtle biological cues (plant stress, animal behavior, odors, sounds), adjusting equipment, and navigating varied terrain with cluttered enclosures remains well beyond reliable autonomous operation.

task: Test combustible appliances, such as gas appliances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one gas appliance (e.g., furnace, stove, water heater). Includes visual inspection, ignition/function test, leak detection, and noting results. ~15–25 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized tools (combustion analyzers, manometers, leak detectors), nuanced sensory interpretation, and interaction with varied appliance controls. Current humanoids lack the specialized tool use, safety judgment, and domain expertise for autonomous appliance testing.

task: Drive vehicles that automatically move and lay tracks or rails over sections of track to be constructed, repaired, or maintained.
human_time_rationale: One instance = operating a track-laying vehicle over one standard work section (~100–200 m). A skilled operator typically takes 1–3 hours per section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a large, specialized rail track-laying machine on active rail infrastructure. No humanoid robot can drive or control such heavy, specialized railway equipment autonomously. Extreme safety and precision demands further preclude current robots.

task: Adjust controls of machines that spread, shape, raise, level, or align track, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting machine controls for a single track section/pass. A skilled rail/road machine operator typically spends ~30–60 min per section adjustment cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized heavy rail/track machinery from an operator cab with complex levers, displays, and pedals. Humanoid robots cannot drive or operate such machinery autonomously; no 2026 general-purpose robot has this capability.

task: Inspect physical security design features, installations, or programs to ensure compliance with applicable standards or regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single facility or security zone. A competent inspector typically spends 60–120 minutes walking the site, checking features, and documenting findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level regulatory knowledge, nuanced judgment on compliance, report writing, and stakeholder communication. Robots lack the cognitive/domain reasoning and documentation capability needed; physical traversal alone is insufficient.

task: Drive graders, tamping machines, brooms, or ballast spreading machines to redistribute gravel or ballast between rails.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one grading/ballast-spreading pass along a typical railroad work segment (~few hundred meters); a competent operator takes ~2 hours including setup and maneuvering.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large, specialized rail-bound heavy machinery (graders, tampers, ballast spreaders) in outdoor/unstructured track environments. Humanoid robots cannot drive or operate such equipment autonomously; no viable 2026 general-purpose robot capability exists for this.

task: Examine animals for injuries or malnutrition, and arrange for any necessary medical treatment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining one animal and coordinating any needed treatment. A competent handler/vet tech spends ~15–25 min on assessment, documentation, and arranging care.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced biological perception (detecting subtle injuries, body condition scoring), safe handling of unpredictable live animals, veterinary judgment, and coordinating follow-up care — far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Operate tie-adzing machines to cut ties and permit insertion of fishplates that hold rails.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adzing one railroad tie. A skilled operator positions the machine, makes the cut, and moves on in roughly 2–4 minutes per tie.
robot_success_rationale: Tie-adzing machines are large, specialized rail-mounted equipment a humanoid cannot operate or control. Task requires driving/guiding heavy machinery on tracks, precise positioning, and tool engagement — far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Inspect cash on hand, notes receivable and payable, negotiable securities, and canceled checks to confirm records are accurate.
human_time_rationale: One audit inspection session covering cash, notes, securities, and canceled checks for a typical small-to-mid business; competent auditor takes ~1–2 hours to reconcile and verify records.
robot_success_rationale: This is a cognitive/financial audit task requiring document reading, numerical reconciliation, judgment, and system access — not a physical manipulation task. No general-purpose humanoid robot has these financial reasoning or accounting software capabilities.

task: Perform routine maintenance and upgrade work on machines and equipment, such as replacing parts, building up drill bits, and lubricating machinery.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance session (e.g., replacing a part, lubricating, or building up a drill bit) on a single machine; a mechanic/machinist typically completes a unit job in 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high dexterity for varied fasteners, torque application, and fine tool-use (e.g., drill bit build-up, greasing fittings). Unstructured industrial environments, heavy parts, and safety criticality push success very low for current humanoids.

task: Regulate air pressure, rotary speed, and downward pressure, according to the type of rock or concrete being drilled.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting drill parameters (air pressure, RPM, downward pressure) for a given rock/concrete type. An experienced driller reads conditions and makes adjustments in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized drill rig controls, interpreting rock/concrete type, and making multi-parameter adjustments in real time. Humanoids cannot operate heavy drilling machinery; no sensing or domain generalization for subsurface geology.

task: Monitor and adjust controls that regulate building systems, such as air conditioning, furnace, or boiler.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring round and adjustment cycle for a building system (HVAC, boiler, etc.); a competent operator checks readings and makes needed control changes in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interfacing with diverse physical control panels, interpreting analog/digital gauges, and making nuanced adjustments. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use on non-standardized control interfaces and the domain knowledge to judge correct setpoints safely.

task: Verify depths and alignments of boring positions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = verifying depth and alignment of a single boring position using measuring tools (tape, rod, laser level). A competent worker takes ~15–25 min per position.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise depth measurement inside a bore hole and spatial alignment checks. Current humanoids lack reliable tool manipulation for bore gauges, struggle with outdoor uneven terrain near excavations, and lack specialized sensor integration for sub-centimeter alignment verification.

task: Operate, drive, or explain the use of mechanical equipment in amusement parks, cruise ships, or other recreational facilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one operating shift cycle of a single ride/attraction (loading guests, running cycle, unloading). Typical ride cycle plus guest turnover ~20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized ride controls, interacting safely with the public, making safety judgment calls, and adapting to varied equipment across venues. Current humanoids lack the safety certification, situational judgment, and guest-interaction capability required.

task: Investigate and assess damage to property and create or review property damage estimates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single damaged property, documenting findings, and drafting or reviewing an estimate. A competent adjuster/estimator typically takes 60–120 minutes on-site plus write-up.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied, potentially hazardous damaged environments, nuanced visual damage assessment, contextual judgment, cost estimation knowledge, and report generation — far beyond current robot generalization, perception, and reasoning capabilities.

task: Complete top fence rails of metal fences by connecting tube sections, using metal sleeves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = joining one tube-section pair with a metal sleeve; worker aligns tubes, slides sleeve over joint, and fastens it in ~8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual alignment of round metal tubes at height on outdoor/uneven terrain, tight sleeve fitting with controlled force, and tool-use (mallet/crimper). Poor generalization to varied fence heights and tube tolerances.

task: Operate machines to flush earth cuttings or to blow dust from holes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = flushing/blowing out one drilled hole. A competent operator sets up and runs the flush/blow cycle per hole, typically 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized drilling/flushing machinery (pumps, compressors, hoses) in unstructured outdoor terrain. Humanoid robots cannot operate such equipment autonomously; rough terrain mobility and tool coupling are major barriers.

task: Inspect equipment, such as rides, games, and vehicles, to detect wear and damage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one ride, game, or vehicle. A trained inspector spends ~15–25 min per unit, checking mechanical components, surfaces, and safety features for wear or damage.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating around complex, varied equipment and visually detecting subtle wear, cracks, or damage. Current robots lack reliable fine-grained visual inspection, generalization across equipment types, and the tactile/experiential judgment inspectors rely on.

task: Inspect fire, intruder detection, or other security systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full inspection of a building's security/fire system (sensors, panels, detectors, alarms). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min for a standard commercial system.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading panel displays, physically testing detectors, navigating varied building layouts, operating control panels, and documenting findings. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use, fine dexterity for test equipment, and system-specific knowledge integration needed for autonomous pass/fail assessment.

task: Operate controls to stabilize machines and to position and align drills.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and stabilizing a drilling machine and aligning the drill for a single drilling operation; a skilled operator typically takes 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy specialized machinery controls (levers, pedals, joysticks) in industrial/field settings. Humanoid robots cannot reliably interface with or safely operate drilling rigs autonomously; perception, force feedback, and generalization are all severely lacking.

task: Paint railroad signs, such as speed limits or gate-crossing warnings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = painting one railroad sign (prep, apply paint/stencil, finish). A competent worker completes a typical sign in ~20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/trackside locomotion on uneven terrain, precise brush/spray painting with stencils, quality judgment, and working near live rail infrastructure. Current humanoids lack the fine painting control, terrain mobility, and safety compliance needed.

task: Operate water-well drilling rigs and other equipment to drill, bore, and dig for water wells or for environmental assessment purposes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling one well, typically a full shift (6–8 hours) including setup, drilling, and initial assessment. 480 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large specialized drilling rigs and heavy machinery in unstructured outdoor terrain. No current humanoid robot can drive, mount, or operate a water-well drill rig autonomously. Far beyond 2026 frontier capabilities.

task: Clean equipment and areas of amusement park, cruise ship, or other recreational facility.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one area or equipment unit (e.g., ride car, seating section, restroom). A competent worker spends ~30–60 min depending on size and soil level.
robot_success_rationale: Recreational facilities feature highly varied surfaces, tight spaces, irregular equipment shapes, crowds, and outdoor/wet conditions. Robots can handle flat floor cleaning but struggle with complex geometries, climbing, and navigating cluttered public environments autonomously.

task: Turn wheels of machines, using lever controls, to adjust guidelines for track alignments or grades, following specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting guideline/grade for one track section; operator reads specs, turns wheels/levers, and verifies alignment — typically 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized heavy track-laying/grading machinery via physical lever/wheel controls in outdoor, unstructured environments. Humanoid robots cannot operate such equipment autonomously; lacks vehicle operation capability, relevant sensor integration, and task-specific tool-use.

task: Store and retrieve equipment, such as vehicles, radios, and ride components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = storing or retrieving a single piece of equipment (e.g., a radio or ride component). Involves locating, moving, and placing/logging one item; vehicles take longer but are averaged in.
robot_success_rationale: Highly varied equipment (vehicles to small radios) requires broad generalization, heavy lifting, driving, and navigation across large/outdoor spaces. Current humanoids lack vehicle operation and struggle with heavy, irregular items and unstructured storage environments.

task: Replace chemical insecticides with environmentally friendly practices, such as adding pest-repelling plants to fields.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing one field and implementing companion planting across it; a competent agronomist/farmer takes ~2 hours to evaluate, plan, and plant pest-repelling plants in one field section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires agronomic judgment, outdoor rough-terrain mobility across uneven farm fields, strategic planting decisions, and fine dexterous soil/seedling handling. Current humanoids lack outdoor agricultural generalization, field-scale navigation, and integrated pest-management reasoning.

task: Administer first aid in emergency situations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single first-aid response (e.g., treating a wound, managing a choking or fainting episode) until the patient is stable or EMS arrives. Typical duration 5–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time clinical assessment, adaptive dexterous contact with a human body, dynamic decision-making under uncertainty, and safe force control. Current humanoids lack medical sensing, soft-tissue manipulation skill, and emergency judgment needed for autonomous, acceptable-quality first aid.

task: Inspect and adjust security systems, equipment, or machinery to ensure operational use and to detect evidence of tampering.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full inspection round of a facility's security systems (cameras, alarms, locks, sensors). A competent security technician typically takes 30–60 minutes per site visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual inspection of diverse equipment, detecting subtle tampering signs, making fine adjustments, and exercising judgment. Current robots lack reliable tamper-detection perception, generalization across varied security hardware, and the dexterity for adjustments.

task: Perform pumping tests to assess well performance.
human_time_rationale: One pumping test instance covers setup, steady-state pumping, and data collection for a single well — typically 2–4 hours for a standard step-drawdown or constant-rate test.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized pump test equipment, pressure transducers, flow meters, and data loggers at an outdoor well site with unstructured terrain. Humanoids lack the specialized tool interfaces, domain software control, and field mobility needed.

task: Assemble and position machines, augers, casing pipes, and other equipment, using hand and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and positioning one machine setup (auger, casing pipes, equipment) at a job site; a skilled worker typically takes 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy, irregular components (augers, casing pipes), outdoor/uneven terrain, high-force torque operations, and specialized tools. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, payload, and outdoor locomotion capabilities.

task: Cut rails to specified lengths, using rail saws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting one rail to a specified length: measuring, positioning, clamping, and sawing. A skilled worker takes roughly 10–20 minutes per cut.
