robot_success_rationale: Rail saws are large, specialized, heavy industrial tools requiring precise measurement, forceful clamping, and guided cutting on bulky steel rails. Current humanoids lack the strength, tool-operation capability, and outdoor/track-environment mobility needed.

task: Engineer, install, maintain, or repair security systems, programmable logic controls, or other security-related electronic systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full service visit (install, maintain, or repair a security/PLC system). A typical job ranges from ~1–4 hours; 120 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading wiring diagrams, routing and terminating cables, programming PLCs, and diagnosing faults — tasks demanding fine dexterity, expert tool use, domain reasoning, and adaptation to varied electrical panels. Far beyond 2026 frontier humanoid capability.

task: String and attach wire-guidelines machine to rails so that tracks or rails can be aligned or leveled.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stringing and attaching wire-guidelines machine to one rail section. Skilled track worker takes ~30–60 min to secure, tension, and verify alignment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy specialized rail equipment outdoors on uneven/active track, managing wire tension, and precise mechanical attachment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, mobility, and tool-use capabilities in unstructured outdoor rail environments.

task: Operate gas or electric kilns to fire pottery pieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading kiln, setting firing program/controls, and initiating a single firing cycle. Hands-on active time is ~20–40 min; monitoring is passive.
robot_success_rationale: Requires carefully loading fragile, irregularly shaped pottery, operating specialized kiln controls (dials, digital panels), and monitoring a multi-hour process. Generalization to varied kilns/pottery shapes is beyond current robot capability; high breakage risk.

task: Take photographs of property.
human_time_rationale: One instance = photographing one property (interior + exterior). A competent photographer walks the site, frames shots, and captures all required angles in ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots lack aesthetic framing judgment, struggle with varied outdoor/indoor terrain, cannot reliably operate a camera with professional quality, and cannot navigate unfamiliar multi-room properties autonomously to supervisor-acceptable standards.

task: Rotate and store food supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rotating and restocking a single storage area (shelf, pantry, or storeroom). A competent worker checks dates, moves older stock forward, places new stock behind, and organizes — roughly 20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading expiration dates (vision + OCR), grasping varied food packaging, navigating storage spaces, and applying FIFO logic. Perception of small printed text and reliable grasp of diverse containers remain challenging for current humanoids.

task: Conduct inspections to determine crop maturity or condition or to detect disease or insect infestation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one field plot or crop section. A competent agronomist walks rows, checks plant samples, assesses maturity cues, and looks for disease/pest signs — roughly 30–60 min per plot.
robot_success_rationale: Requires traversing uneven outdoor terrain, nuanced visual/tactile crop assessment, disease pattern recognition across varied conditions, and expert agronomic judgment. Current humanoids lack robust outdoor mobility and fine agricultural perception needed for reliable autonomous inspection.

task: Start, stop, and control drilling speed of machines and insertion of casings into holes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one drilling cycle (start, drill to depth, stop, insert casing). A competent driller typically completes this in ~20 minutes per hole.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized heavy drilling machinery, monitoring drill speed/torque feedback, and inserting large casings — none of which a general-purpose humanoid can physically operate or control autonomously in unstructured field conditions.

task: Attach handles to pottery pieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching one handle to one pottery piece. Includes scoring, applying slip, pressing/shaping the handle, and smoothing the joint — typically 5–10 minutes for a skilled potter.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity with wet, fragile, irregularly shaped ceramic clay. Precise pressure control is critical to avoid cracking or deforming; current robots lack the tactile sensitivity and compliant manipulation for this delicate craft work.

task: Conduct regular reviews of property within jurisdictions to determine changes in property due to construction or demolition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reviewing one property parcel on-site. A competent assessor spends ~30–60 min inspecting, noting construction/demolition changes, and documenting findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/varied terrain navigation, visual change detection across a full property, legal/jurisdictional judgment, and structured documentation. Current robots lack reliable outdoor generalization, contextual assessment reasoning, and reporting integration.

task: Photograph objects, places, or scenes for reference material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = photographing a single subject/scene for reference. Includes setup, framing, shooting, and basic review. Typical duration for a competent photographer.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to a scene and trigger a camera, but framing aesthetically, adjusting angles for meaningful reference, handling varied lighting, and judging shot quality requires visual-artistic reasoning far beyond current autonomy.

task: Inspect supplies, equipment, and work areas to ensure efficient service and conformance to standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single inspection round covering supplies, equipment, and one work area. A competent worker completes this in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires visual inspection, inventory checking, contextual standard-conformance judgment, and navigation across varied areas. Current robots can navigate and perceive objects but lack reliable semantic standard-compliance assessment and multi-domain generalization.

task: Pour water into wells, or pump water or slush into wells to cool drill bits and to remove drillings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running a water/slush pumping cycle for a single drill bit cooling session; a competent driller typically takes ~15–25 minutes per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized drilling-rig pumps, managing fluid levels, and monitoring downhole conditions in a hazardous outdoor environment. Current humanoid robots cannot operate this heavy, specialized equipment autonomously.

task: Manage nurseries that grow horticultural plants for sale to trade or retail customers, for display or exhibition, or for research.
human_time_rationale: One instance = managing a nursery for one operational day, covering scheduling, monitoring crops, coordinating staff, and handling customer/supplier interactions. A competent manager spends ~4 hours on active management tasks per day.
robot_success_rationale: Nursery management requires high-level planning, business decision-making, staff supervision, customer relations, and adaptive judgment across unstructured outdoor/greenhouse environments. Far beyond current robot cognitive and social capabilities.

task: Withdraw drill rods from holes, and extract core samples.
human_time_rationale: One instance = withdrawing all rods from one borehole and extracting core samples. A competent driller typically takes 30–60 minutes depending on depth and core length.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy drilling machinery, handling large/heavy rods under high force, working outdoors on rough terrain, and carefully extracting fragile core samples — far beyond current humanoid robot capability in dexterity, strength, and tool operation.

task: Conduct or supervise stock examinations to identify diseases or parasites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining a batch/group of livestock or aquaculture stock for disease/parasites. A competent inspector typically spends 30–60 min per examination session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert veterinary judgment, handling live animals safely, sample collection, and nuanced visual/tactile diagnosis. Current robots lack veterinary perception, animal-handling dexterity, and generalization for unstructured livestock environments.

task: Spray ties, fishplates, or joints with oil to protect them from weathering.
human_time_rationale: One instance = spraying a single tie, fishplate, or joint with oil. A worker with a spray can/wand covers one component in ~1–3 minutes including positioning.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor uneven rail terrain is challenging for locomotion (~30–50%). Targeting a specific joint with a spray tool requires tool-use and precision aiming. Unstructured outdoor environment, low footing stability, and coordinating spray direction reduce success significantly.

task: Operate track wrenches to tighten or loosen bolts at joints that hold ends of rails together.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tightening or loosening all bolts at one rail joint (typically 4–6 bolts). A skilled track worker with a track wrench completes this in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy-duty torque application with specialized track wrenches on large, corroded fasteners in unstructured outdoor/rail environments. Current humanoids lack the strength, stability, tool-coupling, and rough-terrain mobility needed for reliable autonomous performance.

task: Disinfect, reconstruct, and redevelop contaminated wells and water pumping systems, and clean and disinfect new wells in preparation for use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fully disinfecting/reconstructing one contaminated well or pumping system. A competent well technician typically spends 4–8 hours per well including assessment, chemical treatment, mechanical work, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized heavy equipment (pumps, drilling tools, chemical injection systems), outdoor unstructured environments, deep confined-space work, precise fluid handling, and expert judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Drill or bore holes in rock for blasting, grouting, anchoring, or building foundations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one drilled hole to specified depth in rock. A competent driller takes 30–90 min depending on rock hardness and depth; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a large drill rig or jackleg drill — heavy, vibration-intensive machinery a humanoid cannot drive or safely control. Outdoor/rough terrain navigation, high-force tool engagement, and site setup far exceed current robot capabilities.

task: Observe and test foods to determine if they have been cooked sufficiently, using methods such as tasting, smelling, or piercing them with utensils.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking doneness of a single food item during cooking; tasting, smelling, and/or piercing takes ~1–3 minutes for a competent cook.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal sensory integration: taste/smell perception is absent in current humanoids; visual/texture inference is weak for doneness. Piercing with a utensil is feasible but judging results autonomously is not reliable.

task: Photograph interiors and exteriors of properties to assist in estimating property value, substantiate findings, and complete appraisal reports.
human_time_rationale: One instance = photographing a single residential property (interior + exterior). A competent appraiser typically spends 30–60 minutes capturing all required angles, rooms, and features.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating cluttered, unpredictable indoor/outdoor environments, opening doors, climbing stairs, framing meaningful appraisal shots, and operating a camera with professional judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable multi-floor navigation, contextual photo framing, and outdoor terrain handling needed at appraisal quality.

task: Weigh, measure, and mix ingredients according to recipes or personal judgment, using various kitchen utensils and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one recipe's worth of ingredients (weighing, measuring, mixing). A competent cook typically completes this mise en place step in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires chaining fine dexterity (scooping, pouring, reading scales), tool-use generalization (varied utensils), and recipe interpretation. Current humanoids struggle with precise volumetric control, liquid handling, and adapting to varied container types.

task: Place and install screens, casings, pumps, and other well fixtures to develop wells.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing installation of screens, casings, pumps, and fixtures for a single well. A competent crew typically takes 3–5 hours for this phase of well development.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy drilling/lifting equipment, working in deep borehole environments, applying high torque to large fittings, and exercising expert judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured outdoor/industrial settings.

task: Drive moving equipment to transport materials and parts to excavation sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one transport trip moving materials/parts to an excavation site, including loading, driving, and unloading. Median trip estimated at ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy moving equipment (forklifts, dump trucks, loaders) in dynamic outdoor excavation environments. Humanoid robots cannot drive such machinery safely; rough terrain, vehicle controls, and load management far exceed current capabilities.

task: Fabricate well casings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating one well casing section/string. A competent worker measures, cuts, threads, and joins casing pipe segments, typically taking 2–4 hours per casing run.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy pipe handling, precision threading, torque-controlled makeup, outdoor/unstructured oilfield environments, and specialized equipment (tongs, elevators, slips) that humanoid robots cannot operate. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Retract augers to force discharge dirt from holes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retracting the auger from one hole. A competent operator engages the retract control, monitors discharge, and confirms clearance — typically 2–4 minutes per hole.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a drilling rig or power auger with heavy-equipment controls in outdoor/unstructured terrain. Humanoid robots cannot operate such machinery autonomously; task involves vehicle-mounted hydraulics, safety-critical judgment, and specialized controls.

task: Turn or stir foods to ensure even cooking.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single turn/stir of food in a pan or on a grill. A competent cook performs this in ~1–3 minutes including checking doneness and repositioning.
robot_success_rationale: Requires detecting food doneness via vision/heat sensing, then dexterous tool use (spatula, spoon, tongs) in a hot, cluttered, variable kitchen environment. Manipulation precision and heat-safe tool grasping remain major limits for 2026 robots.

task: Repair and maintain automotive and drilling equipment, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single repair/maintenance job (e.g., oil change, belt replacement, or drill bit swap). A competent mechanic typically spends 60–120 min on a median job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force torquing, fine dexterity with varied hand tools, navigation under/around large equipment, fault diagnosis, and adaptation to heavily unstructured, grease-covered environments — well beyond 2026 frontier robot capability.

task: Portion, arrange, and garnish food, and serve food to waiters or patrons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = plating a single dish: portioning, arranging components, adding garnish, and handing off to a waiter or patron. ~3–7 min for a competent line cook.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for garnishing, aesthetic judgment for arrangement, fluid/sauce handling, and safe dish handoff. Current humanoids lack the precision, soft-object manipulation, and culinary generalization needed.

task: Inspect personal or business property.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single property inspection visit (e.g., home, office, or commercial unit). A competent inspector walks through, checks key systems, and documents findings in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex, varied layouts; identifying subtle damage, moisture, structural issues, and code violations; and generating a coherent report. Current robots lack reliable generalization, fine perception of defects, and safe stair/ladder navigation in uncontrolled environments.

task: Inspect core samples to determine nature of strata, or take samples to laboratories for analysis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one core sample set from a single borehole. A geologist logs, photographs, and describes a typical core tray in ~20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert geological knowledge to interpret strata visually/tactilely, fine dexterous handling of fragile cores, and navigating field/lab environments. Current robots lack domain-specific perception and reasoning for geological analysis; transport to lab is also unstructured.

task: Load materials into well holes or into equipment, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading materials into a single well hole or equipment charge. A competent worker handles positioning, measuring, and inserting materials manually in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating unstructured outdoor/industrial terrain, precise insertion into narrow well holes, and force-sensitive tool use. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterity and terrain adaptability for this specialized, safety-critical task.

task: Stock shelves, sort and reshelve returned items, and mark prices on items and shelves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stocking one cart/batch of ~20–30 items onto shelves; a competent retail worker takes ~15–25 minutes including organizing and labeling.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with varied item shapes, planogram compliance, label placement, and navigating cluttered retail aisles. Pick-and-place works for simple objects but retail diversity and fine labeling tasks exceed current autonomy.

task: Assess companies as investments for clients by examining company facilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full facility inspection visit for investment assessment. A competent analyst typically spends 1–3 hours touring, observing, and note-taking on-site.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires high-level financial judgment, contextual business interpretation, stakeholder interviews, and nuanced qualitative assessment far beyond robot perception or reasoning. Locomotion is feasible but meaningful autonomous evaluation is not.

task: Substitute for or assist other cooks during emergencies or rush periods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = covering a cook's station during a rush/emergency period, typically lasting 1–2 hours in a commercial kitchen service window.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rapid adaptation to an unfamiliar station, multi-step cooking under time pressure, fine manipulation of hot/sharp tools, real-time coordination with other staff, and generalized culinary judgment — far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Provide assistance to extraction craft workers, such as earth drillers and derrick operators.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one assistance episode (e.g., handing tools, connecting pipe sections, supporting a derrick operator during a drilling shift segment). ~45 min is a typical measured unit of sustained helper activity.
robot_success_rationale: Extraction sites are unstructured, hazardous, outdoor/rough-terrain environments. Tasks involve heavy pipe, high-pressure equipment, and coordinated action with human operators. Current humanoids lack the mobility, load capacity, and situational safety awareness required.

task: Select and attach drill bits and drill rods, adding more rods as hole depths increase, and changing drill bits as needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting/attaching a bit, adding rods as depth increases during a single borehole. Competent driller takes ~10–15 min per rod-change cycle on average.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy torque application, precise thread engagement on large rods, outdoor/uneven terrain, situational judgment on bit wear, and operation of specialized drilling machinery — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Retrieve lost equipment from bore holes, using retrieval tools and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retrieving one piece of lost equipment from a single borehole. A competent driller typically spends 1–3 hours per fishing job, depending on depth and tool type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized downhole fishing tools, drill rigs, and control consoles; interpreting tactile/acoustic feedback from deep underground; and making adaptive decisions. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Inspect facilities, machinery, or safety equipment to identify and correct potential hazards, and to ensure safety regulation compliance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single facility/area safety inspection tour. A competent inspector typically takes ~45–90 minutes to walk through, assess equipment, document findings, and flag hazards.
robot_success_rationale: Requires broad generalization across diverse hazard types, regulatory knowledge, nuanced judgment, and remediation action. Current robots lack the semantic reasoning, compliance knowledge, and corrective dexterity for unstructured multi-step safety inspections.

task: Grill, cook, and fry foods such as french fries, eggs, and pancakes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single food item (e.g., a batch of fries, a fried egg, or a short stack of pancakes). Median prep+cook time across these items is ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise heat management, dexterous flipping, monitoring doneness, and handling varied tools (spatulas, fryers, grills). Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation, thermal sensing, and recipe generalization for live cooking tasks.

task: Investigate and observe tests on machinery and equipment for compliance with standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting/observing one machine or equipment test session. Includes setup review, observation, checking readings, and noting compliance. Typically 30–60 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert interpretation of test results, compliance judgment against standards, reading gauges/displays, and documenting findings. Current robots lack the cognitive reasoning, standards knowledge, and reliable multi-modal perception needed for autonomous compliance determination.

task: Evaluate operation of marine equipment during acceptance testing and shakedown cruises.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full acceptance/shakedown cruise for a vessel, typically a full workday (8 hrs) of systematic equipment trials, checks, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a vessel at sea, interpreting complex marine systems, navigating dynamic maritime environments, and making judgment calls. Humanoid robots cannot pilot vessels, operate marine controls, or handle open-water conditions autonomously.

task: Prepare relishes and hors d'oeuvres.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a standard tray/batch of relishes and hors d'oeuvres for a typical service (e.g., 20–30 portions). Includes slicing, arranging, garnishing, and plating.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for slicing, precise garnishing, and aesthetic plating; unstructured ingredients vary widely in shape/texture; multi-step sequencing in a complex kitchen environment far exceeds current robot generalization.

task: Set up and adjust equipment used to excavate geological materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and adjusting excavation equipment for a single job site or shift. A competent operator typically takes 20–40 minutes to position, configure, and calibrate equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy excavation machinery (excavators, drill rigs) with complex controls, outdoor unstructured terrain, and precise mechanical adjustments. No current humanoid robot can operate or configure such equipment autonomously.

task: Evaluate animals to determine their temperaments, abilities, or aptitude for training.
human_time_rationale: One instance = evaluating one animal. A competent trainer/evaluator observes behavior, runs aptitude tests, and documents findings — typically 30–60 min per animal.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced behavioral interpretation, reading subtle animal body language, and adapting stimuli in real time. Current robots lack animal-behavior expertise, safe animal interaction protocols, and fine-grained affective perception needed for reliable evaluation.

task: Position or mount speakers, and wire speakers to consoles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning/mounting one speaker and running/connecting its wire to a console. Includes bracket mounting, cable routing, and terminal connection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise drilling/fastening into walls or rigging, routing cables through complex paths, and making secure electrical connections — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and unstructured environment generalization.

task: Clean and prepare sites for excavation or boring.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing and preparing a single excavation/boring site. A competent crew member spends ~1.5 hrs removing debris, marking boundaries, and readying equipment access.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy machinery operation (skid steers, graders), rough outdoor terrain navigation, large-scale debris removal, and judgment calls on ground conditions. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and equipment-operation capability.

task: Check floors of plants to ensure that they are strong enough to support heavy machinery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one plant floor area for load-bearing adequacy. A competent inspector walks the zone, checks structural docs, and assesses floor condition in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert structural/civil engineering judgment, cross-referencing load specs with documentation, and subtle visual/tactile cues. Current robots lack the domain reasoning, inspection tools, and autonomous decision-making quality a supervisor would accept.

task: Splice wires with knives or cutting pliers, and solder connections to fixtures and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = splicing and soldering a single wire connection. A competent electrician strips, twists/splices, and solders one joint in ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterity: precise blade control for stripping insulation, steady two-handed manipulation for twisting wires, and controlled soldering iron use. Current humanoids lack the fine motor precision and tactile feedback needed for reliable, safe results.

task: Examine and listen to equipment, read inspection reports, and confer with customers to locate and diagnose malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One diagnostic instance: examining equipment, reviewing reports, and conferring with customer to reach a diagnosis. Typical service/repair intake for a single piece of equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires natural language conversation with customers, reading varied documents, listening to equipment sounds, and synthesizing multi-modal diagnosis. Current robots lack conversational reasoning, auditory fault diagnosis, and generalization across equipment types.

task: Unload materials, devices, and machine parts, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unloading a typical shipment/batch of materials, devices, or parts using hand tools (e.g., wrenches, pry bars, straps). ~20–30 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied hand-tool use (wrenches, pry bars, straps), grasping irregular/heavy parts, and navigating loading areas. Tool selection, force application, and unstructured item variety challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Cue or signal animals during performances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cueing/signaling an animal through a single performance cue or routine segment; a trained handler delivers a cue in seconds to a few minutes per segment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced, real-time animal behavior reading, precise timing, species-specific body language, and trust-based handler-animal relationships. Current robots lack animal behavioral understanding, expressive signaling, and safe proximity operation with live animals.

task: Butcher and dress animals, fowl, or shellfish, or cut and bone meat prior to cooking.
human_time_rationale: One instance = butchering/dressing one animal/fowl/shellfish or boning one cut of meat. A competent butcher averages ~20–30 min per unit depending on size and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme precision with sharp blades on irregular, deformable biological tissue; varied anatomy; high force control sensitivity; safety constraints around cutting tools. Far beyond current general-purpose humanoid dexterity and perception capabilities.

task: Supervise or conduct field surveys, inspections, or technical investigations to obtain data required to revise construction drawings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one field survey/inspection visit for a typical construction site. A competent engineer/technician takes ~2–4 hours to survey, document, and gather revision-grade data.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level judgment, contextual reasoning, and professional decision-making to identify what data is needed for drawing revisions. Current robots lack the cognition, domain expertise, and adaptability for unstructured outdoor construction sites, rough terrain, and supervisory coordination.

task: Diagnose or repair problems with electronic equipment, such as sound, navigation, communication, and security equipment, in motor vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and repairing one electronic system fault in a vehicle (e.g., faulty nav unit). A competent auto-electronics technician typically takes 60–120 minutes per job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting OBD/diagnostic tools, interpreting fault codes, navigating cramped vehicle interiors, fine wire/connector manipulation, and contextual reasoning about complex electronics — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Dismantle extracting and boring equipment used for excavation, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dismantling a single piece of extracting/boring equipment (e.g., a drill rig or boring unit). A competent worker with hand tools typically takes 1–3 hours depending on size and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-torque hand-tool use (wrenches, breaker bars) on large, heavy, dirty industrial components in unstructured outdoor/excavation environments. Current humanoids lack the strength, torque, tool-switching dexterity, and terrain adaptability needed.

task: Order supplies and stock them on shelves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one supply order cycle: reviewing needs, placing an order online/by phone (~15 min), then receiving and stocking items on shelves (~30 min).
robot_success_rationale: Ordering requires cognitive/digital tasks (browsing, decision-making) far beyond current robot autonomy. Stocking shelves with varied items is feasible in parts, but the end-to-end pipeline including ordering is not robotically viable in 2026.

task: Repair or replace defective parts, using hand tools, milling and woodworking machines, lathes, welding equipment, grinders, or saws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing or replacing a single defective part. A competent worker typically spends ~45 min diagnosing, selecting tools, machining/welding/cutting, and fitting the part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy, specialized machinery (lathes, welders, grinders, saws) that humanoid robots cannot safely or reliably control. Demands high dexterity, force feedback, and multi-tool generalization far beyond 2026 frontier capability.

task: Design and build safety equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and building a single piece of safety equipment (e.g., a harness, hard hat, or safety guard). Covers drafting, fabrication, and QC — roughly a full workday for a skilled technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative design reasoning, multi-step fabrication with specialized tools (sewing, molding, welding), and strict safety-standard validation. Far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and autonomous design capabilities.

task: Carve and trim meats such as beef, veal, ham, pork, and lamb for hot or cold service, or for sandwiches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = carving/trimming one cut of meat (e.g., a roast or ham). A skilled worker takes roughly 10–20 minutes depending on cut size and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, force-sensitive blade control around irregular bone/fat structures, switching grip and knife angles constantly. Current robots lack the fine dexterity, tactile feedback, and food-safe tool-use needed for acceptable quality and safety.

task: Inspect and test electrical or electronic systems to locate and diagnose malfunctions, using visual inspections and testing instruments, such as oscilloscopes and voltmeters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing one electrical/electronic fault. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min on visual inspection, instrument probing, and fault identification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise probe placement on terminals, interpreting oscilloscope/voltmeter readings, and abstract diagnostic reasoning across varied systems. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity for probing, instrument reading, and fault-tree reasoning autonomously.

task: Inspect marine equipment and machinery to draw up work requests and job specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a vessel's marine equipment/machinery and documenting findings into work requests and job specs; a competent marine engineer typically takes 1–3 hours per inspection round.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert domain knowledge to assess marine machinery condition, navigate complex vessel environments (ladders, catwalks, confined spaces), interpret wear/corrosion, and produce structured technical documentation — far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Clean facilities and equipment such as dog pens and animal control trucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one dog pen or one animal control truck interior/exterior. A competent worker scrubs, disinfects, and rinses a pen or vehicle in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor/unstructured environments, irregular surfaces (kennels, chain-link, truck interiors), hoses, disinfectant sprayers, and biohazard debris require dexterous tool-use and generalization far beyond current robot capability. Locomotion on wet/uneven surfaces also problematic.

task: Make service calls to repair units in customers' homes, or return units to shops for major repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one in-home service call or unit retrieval visit; typical appliance/electronics repair visit takes 60–120 minutes including diagnosis, repair attempt, and paperwork.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving to customer homes, navigating unfamiliar environments, diagnosing faults, using specialized tools, customer communication, and transporting bulky units — far beyond current humanoid generalization and mobility capabilities.

task: Conduct or direct testing of air quality, noise, temperature, or radiation levels to verify compliance with health and safety regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full compliance inspection round at a single site (e.g., industrial facility). Includes setup, instrument calibration, sampling at multiple points, and logging results — typically 1–2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires carrying and operating specialized calibrated instruments (gas analyzers, sound-level meters, dosimeters), interpreting multi-modal readings against regulatory thresholds, navigating varied industrial terrain, and making compliance judgments — well beyond current general-purpose robot autonomy.

task: Calibrate and test equipment, and locate circuit and component faults, using hand and power tools and measuring and testing instruments such as resistance meters and oscilloscopes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = calibrating/testing one piece of equipment or diagnosing one fault. A competent electronics technician typically spends 30–60 min per unit including setup, probing, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous probe placement, interpreting oscilloscope/meter readings, adaptive fault-tracing logic, and tool switching. Current humanoids lack precision instrument manipulation, sensor interpretation pipelines, and diagnostic reasoning needed for autonomous success.

task: Train dogs in human assistance or property protection duties.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one structured training session with a dog. Competent trainers typically conduct 45–75 minute focused sessions covering commands, reinforcement, and repetition drills.
robot_success_rationale: Dog training demands nuanced behavioral reading, dynamic social interaction with an animal, precise timing of rewards/corrections, and adaptive communication — far beyond current robot social cognition, animal-interaction capability, or generalization.

task: Administer prescribed medications to animals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = administering a single prescribed medication dose to one animal (e.g., oral tablet, injection, or topical). Includes restraint, preparation, administration, and brief observation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires restraining an unpredictable live animal, fine dexterous manipulation (syringes, pill pockets, droppers), reading animal behavior cues, and safe dosing — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured biological settings.

task: Use oral, spur, rein, or hand commands to condition horses to carry riders or to pull horse-drawn equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single training session with one horse. A competent trainer typically works a horse for 30–60 minutes per session, median ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires riding or harnessing a live animal, applying nuanced rein/spur/oral cues, reading horse body language, and maintaining balance. Current humanoids lack equestrian capability, animal-interaction safety, and the fine dynamic control required.

task: Cut openings and drill holes for fixtures and equipment, using electric drills and routers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting/drilling openings for a single fixture or equipment item (e.g., an outlet box or light fixture). Includes measuring, positioning, and cutting with drill/router.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, firm two-handed tool control, high-force operations with powered tools (drills/routers), and adaptive positioning on varied surfaces. Current humanoids lack the precision, force control, and tool-use dexterity needed for safe, accurate cutting.

task: Install, service, and repair electronic equipment or instruments such as televisions, radios, and videocassette recorders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing, servicing, or repairing a single consumer electronics device (e.g., a TV or VCR). A competent technician typically spends 45–90 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of tiny components, soldering, use of diagnostic instruments, and interpreting circuit-level faults. Far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity, tool-use, and generalization capabilities.

task: Test and replace electrical components and wiring, using test meters, soldering equipment, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and replacing a single faulty electrical component/wiring segment. Competent electrician/technician typically spends ~30–60 min including testing, desoldering/resoldering, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for soldering, precise probe placement with test meters, interpreting multimeter readings, and contextual fault diagnosis. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use generalization; soldering alone is near-surgical precision.

task: Complete orders from steam tables, placing food on plates and serving customers at tables or counters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = plating and serving a single order. A cafeteria worker scoops food from steam tables onto a plate and delivers it to a customer at a counter or table in roughly 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires scooping semi-solid foods (variable consistency), precise portioning, balancing a plate, navigating a busy dining area, and social interaction with customers. Current humanoids lack reliable food-handling dexterity, tray stability, and crowd navigation for this multi-step task.

task: Collect and examine geological matter, using hand tools and testing devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting and examining samples at a single field site. A competent geologist spends ~30–60 min gathering, logging, and running basic field tests (hardness, acid, streak) on samples.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/uneven terrain navigation, wielding hand tools (rock hammer, chisel) with appropriate force, operating test devices, and interpreting qualitative results — all in unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack field dexterity, outdoor generalization, and scientific judgment needed.

task: Clean and lubricate parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and lubricating a typical mechanical part (e.g., a gear, bearing, or tool component). Includes wiping, applying lubricant, and reassembling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step manipulation: gripping parts, wiping surfaces, applying precise amounts of lubricant to specific areas. Perception of cleanliness and lubrication coverage is difficult; tool-use (cloths, brushes, lubricant applicators) is challenging for current humanoids.

task: Drive trucks to haul tools and equipment for on-site repair of large machinery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one round trip driving a service truck to a job site. Typical drive, load/unload included; median ~60 min total transit.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot drive trucks on public/job-site roads. L4 AVs are narrow-mapped systems, not general-purpose robots. Loading/hauling tools adds further manipulation complexity. Near-zero feasibility.

task: Finish and duplicate drawings and documentation packages according to required mediums and specifications for reproduction, using blueprinting, photography, or other duplicating methods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing and duplicating one documentation package (set of drawings). Competent drafter/technician takes ~45 min to review, finish, and reproduce a typical package via blueprinting/photography/digital methods.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires operating specialized reprographic equipment (large-format printers, blueprint machines, cameras), interpreting technical specifications, and quality-checking outputs. Humanoid robots lack the perception, tool-use generalization, and software integration needed for this workflow. Near zero.

task: Observe animals' physical conditions to detect illness or unhealthy conditions requiring medical care.
human_time_rationale: One instance = observing a single animal for signs of illness. A trained handler spends ~5 minutes per animal checking posture, gait, coat, eyes, breathing, and behavior.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced multi-modal perception (gait, coat, eyes, behavior, smell), domain expertise, and reliable judgment on unpredictable animals. Current robots lack veterinary-grade sensory interpretation and safe animal interaction in unstructured settings.

task: Feed or exercise animals or provide other general care, such as cleaning or maintaining holding or performance areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single feeding/exercise/cleaning session for a typical animal group (e.g., a kennel run or stall). A competent zookeeper or animal handler takes ~30–60 min per area.
robot_success_rationale: Requires unpredictable live animal interaction, adaptive handling of varied feeds/equipment, perception of animal behavior/stress, and unstructured enclosure layouts. Current robots lack safe, reliable animal-aware manipulation and caregiving generalization.

task: Build fiberglass or wooden enclosures for sound components, and fit them to automobile dimensions.
human_time_rationale: One enclosure build: cutting/shaping fiberglass or wood, fitting to car cavity, finishing. A skilled car audio fabricator typically spends 2–5 hours per enclosure.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex multi-step fabrication (cutting, sanding, laminating fiberglass/wood), precise custom fitting inside an irregular vehicle interior, tool-switching, and quality judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and dexterity.

task: Take orders from customers and cook foods requiring short preparation times, according to customer requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = taking a single customer's order and completing the short-prep food items (e.g. fast food, grill, or short-order cook). Includes order-taking (~1–2 min) and cooking (~5–7 min).
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal customer interaction (speech/NLP), adaptive cooking with unstructured kitchen tools, and real-time customization. Current robots lack reliable NLP-to-action pipelines and unstructured food-prep dexterity in real kitchens.

task: Maintain, repair, and overhaul farm machinery and vehicles, such as tractors, harvesters, and irrigation systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one repair/maintenance job on a single piece of farm equipment (e.g., oil change, belt replacement, irrigation fix). A competent mechanic averages ~2 hours per job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults, using specialized tools, working under/around large machinery in unstructured outdoor/barn environments, and fine dexterous repair work. Far beyond 2026 frontier humanoid capability in generalization, tool-use, and heavy-equipment interaction.

task: Grill and garnish hamburgers or other meats, such as steaks and chops.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grilling and garnishing a single order (e.g., one burger or steak). Includes seasoning, placing on grill, monitoring, flipping, timing doneness, and adding garnishes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a hot grill (safety hazard), precise thermal judgment for doneness, dexterous flipping of irregular meat pieces, and nuanced garnishing. Current humanoids lack reliable heat-safe manipulation, doneness sensing, and kitchen tool generalization.

task: Bake, roast, broil, and steam meats, fish, vegetables, and other foods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and cooking one dish (e.g., roast chicken or steamed vegetables). Includes prep, setting oven/steamer, monitoring, and plating. Median ~45 min across methods.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step cooking: oven/broiler/steamer operation, temperature monitoring, food handling of varied textures, timing judgment, and safety awareness. Current humanoids lack reliable kitchen generalization and fine manipulation for raw/cooked foods.

task: Tune or adjust equipment and instruments to obtain optimum visual or auditory reception, according to specifications, manuals, and drawings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tuning/adjusting a single piece of AV equipment to spec. A competent technician typically spends 20–45 min reading specs, making adjustments, and verifying reception quality.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading technical manuals, interpreting analog/digital meters, fine dexterous knob/dial manipulation, and feedback-driven calibration decisions. Current humanoids lack the perceptual feedback loops and fine motor precision for this multi-step, judgment-heavy task.

task: Clean food preparation equipment, work areas, and counters or tables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a full prep station (equipment surfaces, counters, tables) after a shift or cooking session; a competent kitchen worker typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can wipe flat surfaces moderately well, but food prep equipment has complex shapes, tight crevices, removable parts, and requires variable force/tool use (scrapers, sanitizer spray). Generalization to cluttered, grease-laden kitchen environments remains poor in 2026.

task: Replace and clean electrical or electronic components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = identifying, removing, and replacing a faulty component (e.g. a circuit board, relay, or connector) and cleaning the area. A competent technician typically takes 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterity for connectors, screws, and delicate PCB components; fault diagnosis; tool use; and contextual judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and electrical troubleshooting autonomy for varied real-world components.

task: Modify electrical prototypes, parts, assemblies, or systems to correct functional deviations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and correcting a single functional deviation on one prototype/assembly. A competent technician typically spends 30–90 min per fix cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert diagnosis, schematic interpretation, fine soldering, component swapping, and iterative testing. Far beyond current robot dexterity, reasoning, and tool-use generalization. No frontier humanoid can do this reliably.

task: Obtain and assemble data to complete architectural designs, visiting job sites to compile measurements as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = gathering field measurements and data for a single architectural design project, including travel to and walkthrough of a job site. Typically takes 1–3 hours for a competent drafter/architect.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating unstructured construction sites (rough terrain, hazards), operating measuring tools, interpreting architectural context, recording/assembling structured data, and integrating it into design software — far beyond current robot generalization and cognitive capability.

task: Remove seats, carpeting, and interiors of doors and add sound-absorbing material in empty spaces, reinstalling interior parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full vehicle interior sound-deadening job. Removing seats, door panels, carpeting, installing damping material, and reinstalling all parts typically takes a skilled auto technician 3–5 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme dexterity for varied clips, bolts, and trim; navigating confined vehicle interior spaces; precise material cutting and placement; tool-use variety. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured, cramped vehicle environments.

task: Portion and wrap food, or place it directly on plates for service to patrons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = portioning and wrapping/plating a single food item or meal. A competent worker does this in ~1–3 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for wrapping, handling varied food textures, precise portioning, and aesthetic plating. Current humanoids lack reliable food manipulation, deformable-object handling, and presentation consistency a supervisor would accept.

task: Conduct gambling tables or games, such as dice, roulette, cards, or keno, and ensure that game rules are followed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full dealer shift (~8 hours) conducting a table game, managing chips, cards, dice, player interactions, and rule enforcement continuously.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous card/chip handling, complex social interaction, fraud/cheating detection, rule adjudication, and real-time communication with players — all beyond current humanoid capability in an unstructured, high-stakes human environment.

task: Repair bent or torn sheet metal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single bent/torn section of sheet metal (e.g., a body panel or duct). Includes assessment, hammering/straightening/welding, and finishing. Skilled worker takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level dexterity (hammering, shaping, welding, grinding), tactile force feedback, and damage assessment. Current humanoids lack the precision tools, force control, and domain-specific manipulation for quality sheet metal repair.

task: Install equipment and accessories, such as stereos, navigation equipment, communication equipment, and security systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single accessory (e.g., car stereo or GPS unit) in a vehicle: removing trim, wiring harness work, mounting, and testing. ~60–120 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working inside confined vehicle interiors, routing wiring, connecting multi-pin harnesses, removing/reinstalling trim panels, and functional testing. Extreme dexterity, tight-space manipulation, and tool use far exceed 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Operate games in which players bet that a ball will come to rest in a particular slot on a rotating wheel, performing actions such as spinning the wheel and releasing the ball.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one roulette round: taking bets, spinning the wheel, releasing the ball, announcing the result, and paying out. Typically 2–4 minutes per round.
robot_success_rationale: Spinning a roulette wheel and releasing a small ball with precise force is fine dexterous manipulation. Beyond that, managing chips, reading bets across a layout, announcing results, and handling social interaction are far beyond current humanoid capability. Very low success probability.

task: Maintain and coordinate repair of marine machinery and equipment for installation on vessels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one repair/maintenance job on a marine machinery unit (e.g., pump, valve, engine component), including diagnosis, repair execution, and coordination steps.
robot_success_rationale: Marine machinery is large, heavy, highly varied, and vessel-installed in confined spaces. Requires specialized tools, expert diagnosis, coordination with personnel, and domain-specific knowledge far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Disassemble and inspect motors to locate defective parts, using mechanic's hand tools and gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling and inspecting one motor. A competent mechanic typically takes 60–120 minutes depending on motor size and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sequential multi-step disassembly with varied fasteners, fine-tolerance gauge use, and fault diagnosis judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-switching fluency, and diagnostic reasoning for autonomous motor inspection.

task: Open or close cash floats or game tables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = opening or closing a single cash float or game table. Involves counting cash, documenting amounts, and securing/unlocking drawers. ~10 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires counting and verifying currency, operating specialized casino POS/terminal systems, handling chips and documentation, and following regulated financial procedures — far beyond current robot dexterity, perception, and software integration.

task: Deliver tickets, cards, and money to bingo callers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = gathering and handing off a set of tickets, cards, and money to a bingo caller at their station; a straightforward short errand.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a crowded social venue, identifying the correct person, handling paper money/cards (thin, deformable items), and performing a socially appropriate handoff. Perception of correct recipient and delicate item grasping reduce success significantly.

task: Perform minor repairs, such as replacing worn sprockets, or notify maintenance personnel of the need for major repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one minor repair (e.g., replacing a worn sprocket). A competent worker diagnoses the issue, sources the part, and replaces it in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing wear, selecting correct replacement parts, using tools (wrenches, pullers) on varied machinery, and judgment to escalate — all far beyond reliable autonomous robot capability in 2026. Dexterity, tool-use, and generalization gaps are severe.

task: Collect bets in the form of cash or chips, verifying and recording amounts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting bets from all players at a single table for one round; a dealer gathers, verifies, and logs amounts in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor chip/cash handling, rapid denomination recognition, transaction verification, and social interaction with players. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous currency manipulation and casino-context generalization.

task: Disassemble entertainment equipment and repair or replace loose, worn, or defective components and wiring, using hand tools and soldering irons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling one piece of entertainment equipment, diagnosing faults, and repairing/replacing defective components. A competent technician typically takes 45–90 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for soldering, fault diagnosis, handling varied screws/connectors, and interpreting defects. Current humanoids lack the precision soldering capability, multi-step diagnostic reasoning, and tool-switching needed for autonomous repair of diverse electronics.

task: Dig trenches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = digging one trench of typical utility/construction length (~20–30 m). A competent operator with a trencher or excavator takes ~1–2 hours including setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy excavation machinery (trencher, excavator, or hand tools at scale) in outdoor unstructured terrain. Humanoid robots cannot operate such machinery autonomously, and manual hand-digging at this scale is beyond current strength/endurance limits.

task: Assemble, test, or maintain circuitry or electronic components, according to engineering instructions, technical manuals, or knowledge of electronics, using hand or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling or testing a single electronic subassembly/PCB. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min per unit including component placement, soldering, and basic functional test.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (soldering, placing tiny components), tool use, reading schematics, and adaptive troubleshooting. Current humanoids lack the precision, specialized end-effectors, and cognitive flexibility for unstructured electronics work.

task: Create freehand drawings and lettering to accompany drawings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a set of freehand annotations/lettering for a single drawing. A skilled drafter typically takes 15–25 minutes per drawing sheet.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous pen control, artistic judgment, spatial layout, and legible lettering. Current humanoids lack the precision grip, haptic feedback, and creative/cognitive generalization needed for acceptable freehand drawing quality.

task: Run new speaker and electrical cables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = running a cable run (speaker or electrical) through walls/ceilings in a typical room. A competent electrician/AV tech takes ~1–2 hours per run including routing, fishing, and terminating.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fishing cables through walls, drilling, using fish tapes, navigating confined/unstructured spaces like attics and crawlspaces, and fine terminal connections. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured construction environments.

task: Remove full take-up reels and run film through rewinding machines to rewind projected films so they may be shown again.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a full take-up reel and rewinding one feature film reel (~10–20 min of footage). A competent projectionist takes ~8–12 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous handling of delicate film reels, threading film through a rewinding machine, and managing tension — fine manipulation with thin flexible media is far beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability. High failure/damage risk.

task: Place food trays over food warmers for immediate service, or store them in refrigerated storage cabinets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing a single food tray onto a warmer or into a refrigerated cabinet. A competent worker lifts, positions, and slides the tray into place in about 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping large, potentially heavy/slippery trays, navigating between warmers and fridges, opening cabinet doors, and placing trays precisely on racks. Dexterity and generalization to varied tray sizes and equipment layouts limit reliability.

task: Set up and operate specialized or standard test equipment to diagnose, test, or analyze the performance of electronic components, assemblies, or systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up test equipment and running a full diagnostic/test cycle on a single electronic component or assembly. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise cable/probe connections, interpreting multi-modal test instrument displays, configuring software parameters, and adapting to varied equipment layouts. Beyond current robot dexterity, perception, and generalization limits for complex tool-use workflows.

task: Install and repair agricultural irrigation, plumbing, and sprinkler systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing or repairing one irrigation/sprinkler zone. A competent technician takes ~2–4 hours including trenching, fitting pipes, and system testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor terrain navigation, trench digging, heavy tool use (shovels, pipe cutters), precise plumbing assembly, leak testing, and fault diagnosis — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured outdoor environments.

task: Inspect movie films to ensure that they are complete and in good condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one film reel/print. A competent projectionist or film archivist manually checks splice integrity, frame damage, and completeness of a typical reel (~20–45 min of film).
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous handling of delicate, thin film stock, precise visual inspection of individual frames, and expert judgment on defects. Current robots lack the fine manipulation, specialized perception, and domain knowledge needed.

task: Store food in designated containers and storage areas to prevent spoilage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = storing a batch of food items after a meal/prep session: transferring to containers, labeling, and placing in fridge/pantry. Typically 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct containers, matching lids, handling varied food textures, opening fridge/cabinets, and placing items correctly. High dexterity and semantic reasoning demands exceed current general-purpose robot capabilities reliably.

task: Dismantle defective machines for repair, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dismantling one defective machine. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min depending on machine complexity, using hand tools to remove fasteners and components.
robot_success_rationale: Dismantling requires identifying varied fastener types, applying torque with hand tools, navigating tight spaces, and adapting to unexpected damage/corrosion. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use dexterity and task generalization for unstructured disassembly.

task: Splice separate film reels, advertisements, and movie trailers together to form a feature-length presentation on one continuous reel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one full feature-length presentation (~2 hrs of film). A skilled projectionist/film technician splicing reels, ads, and trailers together typically takes 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterity to handle thin, delicate film strips; precise alignment of sprocket holes; use of a splicer tool; and sequencing judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot fine-manipulation capabilities. No known deployments.

task: Retrain horses to break bad habits, such as kicking, bolting, or resisting bridling or grooming.
human_time_rationale: One retraining session targeting a specific bad habit (e.g., kicking, bolting) typically lasts 1–2 hours; repeated sessions needed, but one structured session is the measurable unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced reading of equine body language, adaptive pressure/release timing, and safe physical proximity to a large, unpredictable 500+ kg animal. No current humanoid robot has the perception, behavioral intelligence, or structural safety to perform this task.

task: Inspect projection equipment prior to operation to ensure proper working order.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single projector setup before use. A competent AV technician checks connections, lens, lamp, focus, and power in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying cables, reading indicator lights, assessing image quality, adjusting focus/lens, and interpreting error states — complex perception and fine manipulation. Current robots lack reliable AV equipment-specific generalization and diagnostic reasoning.

task: Fabricate new metal parts, using drill presses, engine lathes, and other machine tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single metal part (e.g., a shaft or bracket) from stock. A competent machinist typically spends 60–120 min per part including setup, machining, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized heavy machine tools (lathes, drill presses) with precise tool-path control, workholding setup, and fine measurement. No current humanoid robot can safely operate or program these machines autonomously in unstructured settings.

task: Identify energy-conserving production or fabrication methods, such as by bending metal rather than cutting and welding or casting metal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = analyzing a production process and identifying viable energy-conserving fabrication alternatives. A skilled engineer typically spends ~45 min per process review.
robot_success_rationale: This is a cognitive/analytical engineering task requiring domain expertise, abstract reasoning, and creative problem-solving. Current robots have no meaningful autonomous capability for open-ended technical analysis and recommendation generation.

task: Repair or rework parts, using machine tools such as lathes, mills, drills, or grinders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/reworking one part on a machine tool. A competent machinist typically spends 30–60 min setting up, operating, and inspecting a single rework job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise setup of workholding, tool selection, G-code programming or manual feed control, in-process gauging, and adaptive decision-making. Current humanoid robots lack the fine dexterity, force control, and domain expertise to operate manual/CNC machine tools safely and accurately.

task: Clean up work areas and remove debris after extraction activities are complete.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning up a single extraction work area post-shift; sweeping, collecting debris, clearing equipment typically 30–60 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Extraction sites (mining, drilling, quarrying) are unstructured, outdoor/rough terrain with heavy debris, large irregular objects, and hazardous conditions. Humanoid robots lack the mobility, payload capacity, and tool-use capability for this environment.

task: Assemble electrical systems or prototypes, using hand tools or measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a single electrical prototype or subsystem. A competent technician typically spends 60–120 minutes on wiring, component placement, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for wire routing, connector insertion, and tool use (strippers, multimeters, soldering). Current humanoids lack precision for small terminals, accurate probing, and multi-step electrical reasoning without human oversight.

task: Train horses or other equines for riding, harness, show, racing, or other work, using knowledge of breed characteristics, training methods, performance standards, and the peculiarities of each animal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single daily training session with one horse. A competent trainer typically spends 45–75 minutes per horse per session, covering groundwork, riding/driving, and cool-down.
robot_success_rationale: Requires riding or handling large, unpredictable 500 kg+ animals, reading subtle behavioral cues, applying nuanced physical aids, and adapting dynamically. No current humanoid robot has the size, strength, balance, or animal-behavior perception to do this safely or effectively.

task: Mount motors to boats, and operate boats at various speeds on waterways to conduct operational tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting a motor to a boat and completing a test run on waterway. Includes hardware mounting, safety checks, and multi-speed operational testing; ~90 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy dexterous motor mounting with bolts/clamps on an unstable marine surface, then piloting a boat in open water — an unstructured, dynamic environment no current humanoid can navigate or operate safely.

task: Collect cards or tickets from players.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a card/ticket from one player. A competent human reaches out, takes the item, and sets it aside in ~30 seconds.
robot_success_rationale: Grasping thin, flat cards/tickets is dexterity-challenging; item may be held at varying angles by the player. Perception and approach are feasible indoors, but reliable grip on a flimsy card without dropping or tearing it remains unreliable for 2026 humanoids.

task: Set up and adjust picture projectors and screens to achieve proper size, illumination, and focus of images, and proper volume and tone of sound.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and adjusting a projector/screen for a single session. A competent AV tech typically takes 8–12 minutes to position, connect, focus, and calibrate image and audio.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine motor adjustments (focus rings, zoom, keystone), cable connections, interpreting projected image quality, tuning audio, and operating menus/remote controls — well beyond reliable autonomous capability for current humanoid robots.

task: Reproduce drawings on copy machines or trace copies of plans and drawings, using transparent paper or cloth, ink, pencil, and standard drafting instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reproducing/tracing one drawing or plan page. A competent drafter takes ~20–30 min depending on complexity of tracing with instruments on transparent media.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterous control of pencil/ink on transparent media, precise instrument use (compass, straightedge), and visual alignment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity; copy machine operation also demands complex sequencing with delicate originals.

task: Package take-out foods or serve food to customers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = packaging one take-out order or serving one customer; a competent worker retrieves, bags/boxes, labels, and hands off an order in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied container handling, bag/box manipulation, labeling, and customer interaction in unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity for packaging and safe, graceful customer-facing handoffs.

task: Repair engine mechanical equipment, such as power tilts, bilge pumps, or power take-offs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single piece of engine mechanical equipment (e.g., bilge pump or power tilt). A competent marine/small-engine mechanic typically takes 60–120 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level diagnosis, fine dexterous tool use (wrenches, seals, connectors) in cramped engine compartments, fault reasoning, and varied unstructured environments. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Start projectors and open shutters to project images onto screens.
human_time_rationale: One instance = powering on a projector and opening its shutter for a single screen. A competent AV technician does this in ~5 minutes including warmup checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating and pressing small buttons/controls, navigating AV equipment menus, and manipulating physical shutter levers or remote controls. High variability in equipment types; fine dexterity and interface generalization are weak points for current humanoids.

task: Tune or overhaul engines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full engine tune-up or overhaul on a typical vehicle. A tune-up takes ~1–2 hrs; a full overhaul 4–8+ hrs; median ~4 hrs for a competent mechanic.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (torque specs, small fasteners, gaskets), tool changes, diagnostic interpretation, working in confined engine bays, and multi-step judgment. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Drape and pin protective cloths around customers' shoulders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = draping and pinning a single cloth on one customer. A skilled barber/stylist completes this in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires gentle contact with a human, precise draping of a limp fabric, and secure but not-too-tight pinning near a person's neck — demanding compliant manipulation, human-body perception, and safety constraints that current humanoids handle poorly.

task: Conduct environmental, operational, or performance tests on marine machinery and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full test session on a single piece of marine machinery (e.g., engine, pump, or propulsion unit), including setup, running the test protocol, recording data, and documenting results. Typically 1–3 hours; 120 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized marine test rigs, reading analog/digital gauges, connecting instrumentation, interpreting multi-system data, and navigating a marine engine room. No current humanoid has demonstrated this domain-specific operational capability autonomously.

task: Install safety devices on machinery or direct device installation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a safety device (e.g., guard, interlock, E-stop) on one machine. A competent worker needs time to fit, align, fasten, and verify the device.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool use (torque wrenches, drills), navigating around large machinery, reading machine-specific layouts, and verifying functional safety — well beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterous capability.

task: Reproduce working drawings on copy machines or trace drawings in ink.
human_time_rationale: One instance = copying or tracing one working drawing. Copying on a machine takes ~2–5 min; ink tracing a single sheet takes ~15–20 min; median across both ~10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Copy machine operation requires precise button/touchscreen interaction and paper handling; ink tracing demands extreme fine-motor dexterity and visual precision. Both far exceed current humanoid dexterity/perception reliability for quality output.

task: Wash, peel, cut, and seed fruits and vegetables to prepare them for consumption.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one fruit or vegetable item (wash, peel, cut, seed). A competent worker spends ~2 min on simple items (apple) to ~15 min on complex ones (pineapple); ~8 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for peeling, seeding, and precise knife cuts on irregularly shaped, deformable produce. Wet/slippery surfaces, varied shapes, and safety constraints around sharp tools make this extremely challenging for 2026 humanoids.

task: Open and close facilities according to rules and schedules.
human_time_rationale: One instance = opening or closing a facility (e.g. a gym, office, or community center): checking schedule, unlocking/locking doors, enabling/disabling systems, securing the space. ~15 min per event.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a full facility, operating locks/keypads/keys, toggling HVAC/alarm/lighting panels, and following rule-based schedules. Door/keypad interaction is partially feasible but multi-step, unstructured facility-wide operation is well beyond current robot capability.

task: Exchange paper currency for playing chips or coins.
human_time_rationale: One instance = exchanging one customer's cash for chips/coins. A cashier or cage worker counts bills, calculates denominations, and hands over chips/coins in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires counting/handling flimsy paper currency, recognizing denominations, dispensing exact chip/coin values, and social interaction. Current robots lack reliable fine manipulation of paper bills and real-time currency recognition at acceptable accuracy.

task: Fabricate or assemble mechanical, electrical, or electronic components or assemblies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a single mechanical, electrical, or electronic component/subassembly. Competent technician typically takes 30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Structured pick-and-place of standardized parts is feasible (~40–80%), but varied component types, fine connectors, wire routing, and tool-use demands (soldering, fastening) across mechanical, electrical, and electronic work severely limit generalization and dexterity.
