task: Replace parts, such as gears, magneto points, piston rings, or spark plugs, and reassemble engines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing one or more worn/failed components and reassembling the engine. A competent mechanic typically takes 1–3 hours depending on part complexity; 2 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (torquing small fasteners, seating piston rings, gapping points), deep tool use, force sensing, and diagnostic judgment in a cluttered, greasy environment. Far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capabilities.

task: Visit proposed installation sites and draw rough sketches of location.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one site visit plus rough sketch. Includes walking the location, noting key features, and producing a freehand drawing; ~45 min per site for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/unstructured navigation, contextual site interpretation, and producing a meaningful sketch drawing. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor mobility, spatial semantic understanding, and drawing output capability.

task: Inspect and repair or adjust propellers or propeller shafts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and repairing/adjusting one propeller/shaft assembly. A competent marine mechanic typically spends 60–120 minutes on inspection, diagnosis, and basic repair or adjustment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized maritime environment access, fine mechanical dexterity for shaft/blade adjustment, use of torque tools, and expert diagnosis. Current humanoids lack the strength, specialized tooling, underwater/marine access, and task-specific generalization needed.

task: Adjust generators and replace faulty wiring, using hand tools and soldering irons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a generator and replacing one faulty wiring segment. A competent electrician/technician typically takes 30–60 min for diagnosis, disassembly, soldering, reassembly, and testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous soldering (sub-mm precision), fault diagnosis, tool switching, and navigating complex generator internals. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured, safety-critical electrical work.

task: Construct and evaluate electrical components for consumer electronics applications such as fuel cells for consumer electronic devices, power saving devices for computers or televisions, or energy efficient power chargers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing and evaluating a single electrical component (e.g., a fuel cell unit or power-saving PCB). Includes assembly, wiring, and bench-testing; ~90 min for a competent electronics technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation (soldering, connector insertion, PCB handling), multi-step tool use, and evaluation using test equipment. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capabilities in unstructured electronics assembly contexts.

task: Bake breads, rolls, cakes, and pastries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one batch of a baked good (e.g., a loaf of bread or dozen rolls), including mixing, shaping, proofing/prep, and baking. ~90 min is a reasonable median across item types.
robot_success_rationale: Baking requires precise measurement, dough handling (highly deformable materials), multi-step sequencing, oven operation, and sensory judgment (smell, texture, color). Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and generalization capabilities.

task: Cut and trim hair according to clients' instructions or current hairstyles, using clippers, combs, hand-held blow driers, and scissors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full haircut/trim session for a single client, including consultation, cutting, and finishing. Typical barbershop/salon service runs 20–50 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity with scissors/clippers near a moving human head, real-time 3D hair geometry perception, adaptive technique, and safe human contact — all far beyond current humanoid capability. Also poses serious safety risks.

task: Show customers how to maintain equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single equipment maintenance demonstration session with a customer group, covering key maintenance steps for one piece of equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires natural language explanation, adaptive teaching, reading customer comprehension, and demonstrating nuanced manual steps. Current robots lack conversational pedagogy, social awareness, and reliable dexterous demonstration across varied equipment types.

task: Check, test, and maintain automatic controls and alarm systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one control/alarm system unit. Technician cycles through checks, triggers tests, and performs minor upkeep; ~45 min is a typical service call duration.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading panel displays, interpreting alarm codes, actuating varied switches/terminals, using multimeters, and making diagnostic judgments. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use, electrical diagnostic capability, and fault-interpretation generalization for this unstructured, safety-critical task.

task: Grind, ream, rebore, and re-tap parts to obtain specified clearances, using grinders, lathes, taps, reamers, boring machines, and micrometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = machining a single part through required operations (grind/ream/rebore/re-tap) to spec. A skilled machinist typically takes 30–60 min per part including setup, measurement, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool changes across multiple specialized machines (grinder, lathe, boring machine), micrometer measurement to tight tolerances, adaptive decision-making, and fine dexterous manipulation. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Reassemble engines after repair or maintenance work is complete.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reassembling one engine after repair. A competent mechanic typically takes 3–5 hours for a standard automotive engine reassembly, median ~4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Engine reassembly requires extreme dexterity, precise torque sequencing, fine-tolerance part fitting, and expert judgment across hundreds of steps in a cluttered workspace. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Repair or replace defective parts such as magnetos, water pumps, gears, pistons, and carburetors, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing/repairing one defective component (e.g., a water pump or carburetor). A competent mechanic typically spends 60–120 min per component including diagnosis, removal, and reinstallation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of varied fasteners, awkward engine-bay access, force-feedback torquing, and diagnostic judgment. Current humanoids lack the precision, tool-use generalization, and mechanical reasoning for complex engine component R&R.

task: Install or maintain electrical control systems, industrial automation systems, or electrical equipment, including control circuits, variable speed drives, or programmable logic controllers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing or servicing a single electrical control unit/component (e.g., wiring a VFD or PLC panel). A competent industrial electrician typically takes 1–3 hours per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous wire termination, reading schematics, operating in confined electrical panels, using specialized tools (multimeters, crimpers), and safety-critical judgement. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Shampoo hair.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shampooing one person's hair (wet, lather, rinse). A competent person completes this in ~5 minutes in a shower or sink.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate force control on a human head, water/slippery handling, precise lathering without injury or water in eyes/ears, and generalization across varied postures. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and safety limits for body contact.

task: Participate in the development or testing of electrical aspects of new green technologies, such as lighting, optical data storage devices, and energy efficient televisions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single development/testing session on a green-tech electrical component (e.g., running a test bench evaluation). Skilled engineers typically spend ~2 hours per focused session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level cognitive engineering judgment, interpreting complex test data, configuring precision instruments, and iterative design decisions. Current robots lack the cognition, specialized tool-use, and domain expertise for autonomous R&D work.

task: Repair nanotechnology processing or testing equipment or submit work orders for equipment repair.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing a single piece of nanotechnology equipment and either performing a minor repair or submitting a work order. Competent technician typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert diagnosis of highly specialized, sensitive nanotech equipment, fine manipulation of delicate components, interpreting technical readouts, and navigating institutional work-order systems. Far beyond current robot generalization and dexterity.

task: Select cleaning materials, tools, or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting appropriate cleaning supplies/tools for a single cleaning job. A competent worker quickly identifies and gathers needed items in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires recognizing varied cleaning products, reading labels, and matching tools to task context. Object recognition and retrieval from storage are feasible, but semantic understanding of product selection and generalization to novel items remain challenging.

task: Install and connect auxiliary equipment, such as microphones, amplifiers, disc playback machines, and lights.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a typical auxiliary AV rig (mic, amp, lights, playback). A competent technician takes ~30–60 min for a standard event setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires cable routing, connector matching (XLR, TRS, power), device recognition, and functional testing across diverse equipment. Far beyond current robot generalization, fine-connector dexterity, and system-level judgment.

task: Clean and sterilize scissors, combs, clippers, and other instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/sterilizing a full set of salon/barber instruments after one client. Wiping, disinfecting, and autoclaving or UV-treating a small kit takes ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of small sharp tools, precise application of disinfectant, operating autoclave or UV sterilizer controls, and quality verification — well beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity.

task: Repair and maintain gasoline engines used to power equipment such as portable saws, lawn mowers, generators, and compressors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single engine repair/maintenance job (e.g., tune-up or fault diagnosis + fix on one small gasoline engine). A competent small-engine mechanic typically takes 45–90 minutes per job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high dexterity for small fasteners, spark plugs, carburetors, and fuel lines; multi-step diagnosis with tools; unstructured engine layouts vary widely. Far beyond current humanoid capability for autonomous, quality-accepted repair.

task: Modify, maintain, or repair electrical, electronic, or mechanical components, equipment, or systems to ensure proper functioning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one repair/maintenance job on a component or system. A competent technician typically spends ~1 hour diagnosing and fixing a typical fault (e.g., replacing a motor, circuit board, or mechanical part).
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex diagnosis, fine dexterous tool use (screwdrivers, multimeters, soldering), contextual problem-solving, and adaptation to highly varied unstructured faults — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Select and use laboratory, operational, or diagnostic techniques or test equipment to assess electromechanical circuits, equipment, processes, systems, or subsystems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing a single electromechanical system/circuit using appropriate test equipment (e.g., multimeter, oscilloscope, analyzer). A competent technician typically spends ~30–60 min selecting, setting up, testing, and interpreting results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert tool selection, fine manipulation of test probes on varied equipment, reading/interpreting analog and digital instruments, and contextual diagnosis. Current humanoids lack the precision, adaptive reasoning, and instrument-operation fluency needed for autonomous success.

task: Erect alternate panel, basket weave, and louvered fences.
human_time_rationale: One instance = erecting one standard 6-ft × 8-ft fence panel section (post setting, panel attachment, hardware). Competent crew member completes one panel in ~4 hours including post work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires post-hole digging, heavy lifting, precise leveling, driving fasteners into wood/concrete, and outdoor uneven terrain. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and force-control capabilities in unstructured outdoor environments.

task: Operate and resupply printers and computers, changing print wheels or fluid cartridges, adding paper, and loading blank tapes, cards, or disks into equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = resupplying one device (swap cartridge, add paper, or load media). A competent worker completes a typical cycle in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous manipulation of small, varied components (cartridges, paper reams, tapes, cards). Latching mechanisms, thin paper handling, and tight device access challenge current humanoid dexterity and generalization significantly.

task: Adjust points, valves, carburetors, distributors, and spark plug gaps, using feeler gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full tune-up adjustment on one vehicle engine (points, valves, carburetor, distributor, spark plug gaps). A competent mechanic typically takes ~45–75 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation in a confined engine bay, precise feeler gauge readings, and knowledge of vehicle-specific specs. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control, force feedback, and tool-use generalization needed for this highly intricate mechanical work.

task: Set up and inspect curtain and screen controls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and inspecting controls for one curtain/screen system (e.g., a stage or AV room). Includes rigging, testing motors/manual pulls, and verifying operation — ~15 min for a competent stagehand or AV tech.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying and manipulating varied rigging hardware, pull cords, motorized controls, and switches; inspecting mechanical integrity; and adapting to non-standardized setups. Beyond current robot dexterity, perception, and generalization capabilities.

task: Calibrate or adjust equipment to ensure quality production, using tools such as calipers, micrometers, height gauges, protractors, or ring gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = calibrating/adjusting one piece of equipment using precision gauges. A skilled technician typically spends 15–35 min per setup, including measurement, adjustment, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of precision instruments (calipers, micrometers, ring gauges), reading analog/digital scales, interpreting tolerances, and making precise adjustments — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and sensor-feedback integration. High failure risk.

task: Assist engineers in developing, building, or testing prototypes or new products, processes, or procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting through one prototype development/test session. A competent technician typically spends ~2 hours per session on setup, hands-on assistance, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level cognitive collaboration, adaptive problem-solving, interpreting engineer intent, using diverse specialized tools, and contextual judgment — far beyond current robot autonomy. Dexterity and generalization severely lacking for novel prototype environments.

task: Shape and trim beards and moustaches, using scissors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shaping and trimming a single client's beard/moustache. A skilled barber typically takes 10–20 minutes for a full beard shape and trim with scissors.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity with scissors near a human face, precise 3D perception of hair contours, real-time safety avoidance of skin/eyes, and aesthetic judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Apply lather and shave beards or neck and temple hair contours, using razors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full shave/beard trim on one client, including lathering and razor work on beard, neck, and temple contours. A skilled barber typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Shaving on a human face requires extreme fine dexterity, precise force control near sensitive skin, dynamic compliance to facial contours, and high-stakes safety (razor near eyes/arteries). Far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Produce images or measurements, using tools or techniques such as atomic force microscopy, scanning electron microscopy, optical microscopy, particle size analysis, or zeta potential analysis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one complete imaging/measurement session (sample prep, instrument setup, data acquisition, basic output). Typically 60–120 min for a trained lab technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized benchtop scientific instruments (AFM, SEM, zeta potential analyzers), fine sample preparation, software control, and expert judgment. No general-purpose humanoid robot can meaningfully perform this autonomously in 2026.

task: Provide skin care and nail treatments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single client session covering skin care (cleanse, exfoliate, moisturize) and basic nail treatment (file, buff, polish). A competent esthetician typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for nail polish application, gentle/calibrated pressure on skin, reading client reactions, tool switching, and safe contact with a human face and hands — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Disassemble appliances so that problems can be diagnosed and repairs can be made.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling one appliance (e.g., washing machine panel, microwave casing) to expose internal components; a competent technician typically takes 15–35 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying varied fasteners, removing panels without damage, and navigating tight spaces on highly diverse appliance designs. Current robots lack the dexterous tool-use, generalization, and diagnostic judgment needed for unstructured disassembly.

task: Reassemble machines and equipment following repair, testing operation and making adjustments, as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reassembling a single machine/equipment unit after repair, including functional testing and adjustment. A typical industrial unit takes ~1 hour for a competent mechanic.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step dexterous reassembly of varied mechanical parts, torque-sensitive fastening, functional testing with interpretation of results, and adaptive adjustments — far beyond current humanoid generalization and fine manipulation capabilities.

task: Dismantle engines, using hand tools, and examine parts for defects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dismantling one engine and inspecting parts. A competent mechanic typically takes 1.5–3 hours for full teardown and visual/tactile defect inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained high-torque tool use, fine dexterity to remove varied fasteners, spatial reasoning in a cluttered engine bay, and nuanced defect detection (visual + tactile). Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Replace motors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing one motor unit (e.g., HVAC blower, conveyor, or pump motor). Includes disconnecting power, removing fasteners, swapping motor, and reconnecting. ~45 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force torque on varied fasteners, precise electrical connector mating, lifting heavy components, and safe lock-out/tag-out compliance. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force sensing, and task-specific tool-use for reliable autonomous execution.

task: Remove engines from equipment, and position and bolt engines to repair stands.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one engine and mounting it on a repair stand; a competent mechanic takes 60–120 min depending on engine size and accessibility.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating hoists/engine lifts, disconnecting complex plumbing/wiring harnesses, maneuvering heavy loads, and torquing bolts to spec — far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity, force handling, and tool-use capabilities.

task: Set starter locks and align and repair steering or throttle controls, using gauges, screwdrivers, or wrenches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full procedure on one vehicle/vessel — setting locks, aligning, and adjusting steering or throttle. A skilled mechanic typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torque with wrenches/screwdrivers, reading gauges, navigating tight engine bays, and fine mechanical judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and domain-specific tool-use needed for reliable autonomous execution.

task: Adjust carburetor mixtures, electrical point settings, or timing while motors are running in water-filled test tanks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tuning a single outboard/marine motor in a test tank: mount motor, start it, iteratively adjust carburetor mix screws, points, and timing until optimal. Typically 20–30 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous adjustment of tiny screws/points while interpreting live running engine sound/feel, near water and hot/vibrating machinery. Current robots lack the fine motor precision, real-time auditory/tactile feedback loops, and specialized tool-use needed for iterative live tuning.

task: Cleanse clients' skin with water, creams, or lotions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleansing one client's face/skin during a beauty or care session. A competent esthetician or caregiver typically takes 5–15 minutes for a full cleanse.
robot_success_rationale: Requires gentle, adaptive touch on sensitive human skin, precise force control, product dispensing, and reading client comfort cues. Current humanoids lack the fine tactile sensitivity, safe compliance, and social awareness needed for direct skin contact caregiving.

task: Maintain work area according to cleanroom or other processing standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance pass of a cleanroom workstation/area: wiping surfaces, checking cleanliness, removing contaminants, restoring order. Typical cycle per shift.
robot_success_rationale: Cleanrooms demand strict contamination control, precise wiping protocols, gowning compliance, and contextual judgment about what is contaminated. Current robots lack the fine perception, protocol adherence, and specialized tool-use required for acceptable cleanroom standards.

task: Calibrate nanotechnology equipment, such as weighing, testing, or production equipment.
human_time_rationale: One calibration session of a single nanotechnology instrument (e.g., microbalance or AFM). Includes setup, running calibration standards, adjusting, and documenting results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (sub-micron precision), domain-specific instrument interfaces, interpreting complex calibration software, and handling delicate nano-scale standards — far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Talk to or interact with animals to familiarize them to human voices or contact.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single handling/socialization session with one animal (e.g., a young livestock animal or pet). A typical session lasts 15–30 minutes, median ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced, adaptive interaction with an unpredictable living animal — reading body language, adjusting tone, gentle touch, and building trust. Current robots lack animal-behavior perception, safe compliant contact, and the social/emotional generalization needed.

task: Curl, color, or straighten hair, using special chemical solutions and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full chemical hair treatment (perm, color, or relaxer) on one client, including application, processing time, rinsing, and styling. Typically 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme dexterity for sectioning hair, precise chemical application, client-specific judgment, heat/tool management, and safe handling of caustic solutions on a moving human — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Weigh or measure ingredients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring/weighing a single ingredient (e.g., 1 cup of flour or 200g of sugar). A competent human takes ~1–3 minutes including scooping, leveling, or taring a scale.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and pour common ingredients, but precise measurement requires fine dexterity, leveling, reading analog/digital scales, and handling varied containers and powders/liquids — still error-prone for frontier humanoids in 2026.

task: Test and inspect engines to determine malfunctions, to locate missing and broken parts, and to verify repairs, using diagnostic instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting/testing one engine. A competent mechanic spends ~45–90 min connecting diagnostics, running the engine, interpreting codes, and verifying repairs.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting diagnostic tools, interpreting multi-modal sensor data, reasoning about malfunctions, and verifying repairs — well beyond current robot generalization and dexterous tool-use in unstructured engine bays. No frontier humanoid has demonstrated this capability autonomously.

task: Provide facial and body massages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full facial + body massage session for a single client; a typical combined session runs 45–60 minutes for a competent therapist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced, calibrated touch pressure across varied anatomy, real-time client feedback interpretation, safe force control on sensitive tissue, and social/emotional attunement — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Use data entry devices, such as optical scanners, to input data into computers for revision or editing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scanning and inputting a single batch/document (e.g., a few pages) into a computer system. A competent human takes ~10 minutes including setup, scanning, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can physically handle scanners and press buttons, but operating a GUI, navigating software menus, verifying OCR output, and correcting errors requires complex perception-UI interaction that current humanoids handle poorly.

task: Copy drawings of printed circuit board fabrication using print machine or blueprinting procedure.
human_time_rationale: One instance = copying one PCB drawing set via print machine or blueprint; includes setup, running machine, and collecting output. Competent technician takes ~25–35 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized reprographic/blueprint equipment with precise paper handling, fine document alignment, and machine-specific controls. Current humanoids lack task-specific tool interfacing, fine document manipulation, and reliable machine operation generalization.

task: Remove body and facial hair by applying wax.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single waxing session (e.g., legs or face) for one client. A competent esthetician typically takes 20–40 minutes depending on area.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise temperature-controlled wax application on human skin, fine dexterity for strip placement/removal, pain/safety feedback, and working on a compliant, sensitive human body — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Replace worn and defective parts such as switches, bearings, transmissions, belts, gears, circuit boards, or defective wiring.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing a single worn/defective part (e.g., a belt or switch). Includes diagnosis, removal, and reinstallation; median across part types ~45 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing worn parts, disassembling varied housings, handling tiny fasteners/wiring, and reinstalling with precision. Current humanoids lack fine dexterity, tool-use generalization, and diagnostic reasoning for unstructured repair tasks.

task: Treat the facial skin to maintain and improve its appearance, using specialized techniques and products, such as peels and masks.
human_time_rationale: One facial treatment session (cleanse, peel/mask application, timing, removal, moisturize) for a single client; typical esthetician session runs 45–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme dexterity and gentle touch on sensitive facial skin, precise product application, real-time skin reaction monitoring, and adaptive interpersonal care — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Operate nanotechnology compounding, testing, processing, or production equipment in accordance with appropriate standard operating procedures, good manufacturing practices, hazardous material restrictions, or health and safety requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single production run (setup, operation, monitoring, shutdown) on nanotechnology equipment. A typical batch run in a nanotech lab takes 1–3 hours for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly specialized equipment interfaces, precise parameter monitoring, SOP compliance, hazmat handling, and real-time judgment under safety constraints. Current humanoid robots lack the specialized tool-use, fine-sensor reading, and regulatory compliance cognition needed.

task: Operate equipment to show films in a number of theaters simultaneously.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one multiplex screening session; loading playlists, configuring servers, verifying audio/video, monitoring multiple auditoriums. Estimated active hands-on time ~45 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized digital cinema servers, proprietary software interfaces, and physical projection equipment across multiple rooms. Humanoid robots lack the software integration, GUI interaction reliability, and domain-specific tool-use needed for autonomous operation.

task: Modify, maintain, or repair electronics equipment or systems to ensure proper functioning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and repairing a single electronics fault (e.g., a failed PCB, faulty wiring, or component replacement). A competent technician typically spends ~60 min per job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for SMD components, soldering, probing with multimeters, reading schematics, and adaptive fault diagnosis. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured, tool-rich repair contexts.

task: Clean work stations and sweep floors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one workstation (wipe surfaces) and sweeping its surrounding floor area; a competent worker completes this in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Sweeping flat floors is well within 2026 robot capability (~60%), but wiping cluttered workstation surfaces requires object-aware manipulation and varied tool use, dragging the combined success rate down to ~45%.

task: Idle motors and observe thermometers to determine the effectiveness of cooling systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = idling one motor and monitoring thermometer readings until thermal stabilization; ~15 min per test cycle for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can read analog/digital gauges with moderate reliability and navigate to the equipment, but starting/idling motors via physical controls, interpreting thermometer readings over time, and making effectiveness judgments in unstructured industrial settings remain difficult for 2026 humanoids.

task: Sterilize equipment and clean work areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sterilizing a set of equipment and wiping down a work area (e.g., lab bench or medical prep surface). A competent worker typically takes 15–25 minutes per session.
robot_success_rationale: Wiping flat surfaces is feasible (~40–70%), but sterilizing equipment requires precise handling of varied instruments, applying chemicals or autoclaving, and verifying coverage — demanding dexterity, tool-use, and procedural judgment beyond current frontier robots.

task: Measure or mix chemicals or compounds in accordance with detailed instructions or formulas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring/mixing a single batch per formula. A competent lab/industrial worker typically spends 10–20 min reading instructions, weighing/measuring, and combining components.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for pipettes/balances, reading precise measurements, interpreting formulas, handling diverse vessels/chemicals safely, and verifying outcomes. Current humanoids lack the precision, chemical perception, and reliable tool-use for lab-grade accuracy.

task: Inspect cards or equipment to be used in games to ensure they are in proper condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a full set of cards or equipment before a game session; a competent dealer/supervisor typically takes ~10 minutes to check condition, markings, and integrity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual inspection for subtle defects (marks, bends, wear) on small objects, and precise dexterous handling of cards/chips. Current robots lack reliable fine-detail visual QC and delicate manipulation at this fidelity.

task: Maintain stocks of parts used in on-site installation, maintenance, and repair of appliances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single stock-check-and-replenishment cycle for a parts inventory (counting, identifying low stock, logging, and restocking). A competent technician takes ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading small part labels/barcodes, counting heterogeneous items, navigating a storage room, and updating inventory records. Perception of small diverse parts, barcode scanning integration, and system interaction remain weak in current humanoids.

task: Assist cooks and kitchen staff with various tasks as needed, and provide cooks with needed items.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single fetch/assist action (e.g., retrieving an ingredient, restocking a station, carrying a tray). A kitchen helper does this dozens of times per shift; ~8 min per discrete errand is a reasonable unit.
robot_success_rationale: Kitchen environments are dynamic, cluttered, wet, and hot. Task variety is extreme (fetching, carrying, handing off items on demand). Current robots lack reliable generalization, safe navigation in busy kitchens, and the contextual understanding to know what cooks need without explicit instruction.

task: Clean and reinstall parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a set of parts and reinstalling them (e.g., equipment maintenance cycle). A competent technician typically takes 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step dexterity: scrubbing/wiping parts, drying, precise reinstallation with correct alignment and fastening. Current robots struggle with unstructured cleaning motions, variable part geometries, and reassembly tolerances.

task: Start motors and monitor performance for signs of malfunctioning, such as smoke, excessive vibration, or misfiring.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting a motor and observing it during a standard run-up/monitoring period (~10–20 min); includes checking gauges, vibration, sounds, and smoke.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can navigate to and visually detect smoke or gross vibration, but reliably operating motor start controls (switches, breakers, keys), reading analog gauges, detecting subtle misfiring sounds/smells, and making nuanced fault judgments remain beyond current frontier capability.

task: Reassemble units after repairs are made, making adjustments and cleaning and lubricating parts as needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reassembling one repaired unit (e.g., a mechanical/electrical component). A competent technician typically spends 30–60 minutes fitting parts, adjusting, cleaning, and lubricating.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for fasteners and adjustments, tool-switching (wrenches, lubricant applicators), perception of fit/tolerance, and contextual judgment across varied unit types — well beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterous capability.

task: Trace electrical circuits, following diagrams, and conduct tests with circuit testers and other equipment to locate shorts and grounds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing one circuit fault. Competent technician spends ~30–60 min tracing wiring, reading diagrams, and running tester equipment to isolate a short or ground.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading and interpreting schematics, navigating wiring harnesses, precisely probing terminals with test equipment, and reasoning about electrical faults — far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and fine dexterity capabilities.

task: Provide face, neck, and scalp massages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single combined face, neck, and scalp massage session for one client; typical spa/therapeutic session runs ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine, force-sensitive touch on delicate facial/scalp tissue, adaptive pressure modulation, and client comfort/safety awareness. Current humanoids lack the compliant dexterity, tactile feedback, and safety margins needed for acceptable quality near the head and neck.

task: Set up and operate production equipment in accordance with current good manufacturing practices and standard operating procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running one production equipment session/batch: includes checks, parameter configuration, startup, and initial monitoring per cGMP/SOP requirements.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading SOPs, entering precise parameters on varied HMIs, fine dexterous adjustments, regulatory documentation, and contextual judgment under cGMP compliance — far beyond current humanoid robot generalization.

task: Service and repair domestic electrical or gas appliances, such as clothes washers, refrigerators, stoves, and dryers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one appliance service/repair visit (e.g., diagnosing and fixing a washer or dryer). Includes diagnosis, part replacement, and functional test; typical technician job averages 60–90 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert fault diagnosis, reading schematics, using multimeters/specialized tools, fine dexterous disassembly/reassembly of varied appliance models, and safety handling of live electrical/gas systems — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Season and cook food according to recipes or personal judgment and experience.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and cooking a single dish from start to finish. A competent cook typically takes 20–45 minutes depending on complexity; 30 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Cooking from a recipe requires fine dexterity (pouring, stirring, tasting), multi-step planning, heat/tool management, and sensory judgment. Current humanoids lack taste/smell feedback and reliable kitchen generalization, rating this very low.

task: Advise clients about colors and types of makeup and instruct them in makeup application techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full client consultation + demonstration session. A competent cosmetologist typically spends 30–60 minutes advising on colors, products, and teaching application techniques.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social communication, personalized aesthetic judgment, skin-tone analysis, fine dexterous makeup application on a moving human face, and real-time instruction — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Develop or modify wet chemical or industrial laboratory experimental techniques for nanoscale use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adapting a single existing wet-chemical/lab technique for nanoscale application; includes literature review, protocol design, trial runs, and documentation. Typically a multi-hour bench session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert scientific reasoning, hypothesis generation, sub-microliter precision pipetting, operating complex analytical instruments (e.g., TEM, AFM), and iterative judgment. Far beyond 2026 robot autonomy in unstructured lab settings.

task: Plan, assemble, and stock product displays in retail stores, or make recommendations to retailers regarding product displays, promotional programs, and advertising.
human_time_rationale: One instance = planning, assembling, and stocking a single product display in a retail store. A competent merchandiser typically spends 30–60 minutes per display unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step cognitive planning, navigating cluttered retail environments, fine dexterous assembly of varied display fixtures, precise product placement, and advisory communication — far beyond current humanoid generalization and manipulation capabilities.

task: Tint eyelashes and eyebrows.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tinting both eyelashes and eyebrows for one client. Includes applying tint, processing time (~10–15 min), and removal/cleanup.
robot_success_rationale: Extremely fine dexterity required near the eyes of a live person; high safety risk from chemical contact. Current robots lack the sub-millimeter precision, compliant contact control, and safety guarantees needed for this sensitive cosmetic task.

task: Examine clients' skin, using magnifying lamps or visors when necessary, to evaluate skin condition and appearance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining one client's skin during a consultation. A trained esthetician or dermatologist typically spends 3–7 minutes on a focused skin assessment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced dermatological perception (color, texture, tone, lesions), professional judgment, and interpersonal interaction. Current robots lack clinical skin-analysis vision models, fine-grained tactile sensing, and the contextual reasoning needed for a supervisor-acceptable assessment.

task: Receive and store food supplies, equipment, and utensils in refrigerators, cupboards, and other storage areas.
human_time_rationale: One delivery/restocking instance: unboxing and sorting a typical food supply shipment and placing items in correct refrigerators, cupboards, and storage areas.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied grasping of diverse packaged goods, navigating multiple storage locations (fridges, shelves, cupboards), opening doors, and categorical sorting. Current robots lack reliable generalization across item variety, storage organization rules, and door/handle manipulation in unstructured kitchens.

task: Observe and test operation of appliances following installation, and make any initial installation adjustments that are necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = observing and testing a single installed appliance and making initial adjustments. A competent technician typically takes 15–25 minutes per appliance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating diverse appliance controls, interpreting varied readings/sounds/smells, diagnosing faults, and making fine adjustments (e.g., leveling, calibrating). Current robots lack reliable multi-modal diagnosis, generalization across appliance types, and precise adjustment dexterity.

task: Apply chemical peels to reduce fine lines and age spots.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying a chemical peel to one client, including prep, application, neutralization, and post-peel care. Typically 30–60 minutes for a competent esthetician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, precise chemical dosing, real-time skin response assessment, and nuanced tactile/visual perception on a human face. Safety-critical; no current humanoid has demonstrated this capability. Generalization to varied skin types adds further complexity.

task: Process nanoparticles or nanostructures, using technologies such as ultraviolet radiation, microwave energy, or catalysis.
human_time_rationale: One processing run (setup, exposure/reaction, monitoring, shutdown) for a batch of nanoparticles using UV, microwave, or catalytic equipment; typically 30–90 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-precise handling of nanoscale materials, operating specialized lab instruments (UV reactors, microwave synthesizers), reading digital controls, and interpreting subtle process signals. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and generalization.

task: Provide customers with product samples and catalogs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = handing one customer a product sample and catalog. Competent human locates items, greets customer, and delivers materials in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can pick and place objects but struggles with social interaction, identifying the right customer, navigating varied retail/trade-show environments, and handling thin/flexible catalogs reliably. Generalization and social context remain weak.

task: Observe and examine appliances during operation to detect specific malfunctions such as loose parts or leaking fluid.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one appliance during operation. A competent technician spends ~5–15 min observing, listening, and checking for loose parts, leaks, and anomalies.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can visually scan surfaces and detect obvious fluid leaks, but reliably identifying subtle malfunctions (vibration, loose parts, unusual sounds, smell) requires multi-modal sensing, contextual judgment, and generalization across appliance types that current humanoids lack.

task: Conserve, recover, and recycle refrigerants used in cooling systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = recovering/recycling refrigerant from a single cooling unit. Includes connecting recovery machine, evacuating refrigerant, and labeling/storing cylinders. Typical HVAC tech takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise coupling of refrigerant hoses to Schrader valves, operating a recovery machine, reading gauges, and handling pressurized refrigerant safely. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tool-use generalization, and safety compliance needed for refrigerant handling.

task: Assemble new or reconditioned appliances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one appliance (e.g., washer, dishwasher, refrigerator). A competent technician takes ~30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Appliance assembly requires high dexterity (routing wires, aligning heavy components, torquing fasteners), tool use, and sequenced multi-step reasoning across varied models. Current humanoids lack the precision, force control, and generalization needed.

task: Replace defective components or parts, using hand tools and precision instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing a single defective component (e.g., a PCB module, mechanical part, or fastened subassembly). Includes diagnosis confirmation, removal, fitting, and verification; ~30–60 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity with hand tools and precision instruments, identifying the correct defective part, precise torquing/fitting, and quality verification. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use precision and adaptive fault-finding for unstructured repair tasks.

task: Devise, fabricate, or assemble new or modified mechanical components for products such as industrial machinery or equipment, and measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating/assembling one mechanical component (e.g., a custom bracket or shaft). A competent machinist/technician typically spends 1–3 hours per component, including setup, machining, and fitting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative design/devising, operating lathes/mills/grinders, precise toolpath planning, and iterative measurement. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-use, machine operation, and generalization needed for novel fabrication tasks.

task: Calibrate devices by comparing measurements of pressure, temperature, humidity, or other environmental conditions to known standards.
human_time_rationale: One calibration session for a single device: setup, comparison to reference standard, adjustment, and documentation. Typical for a pressure gauge, thermometer, or humidity sensor.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool connection, reading analog/digital displays, interpreting deviation, making precise adjustments, and documenting results. Current robots lack reliable fine manipulation, multi-step reasoning across instruments, and calibration software interaction.

task: Enter shipping information into a computer by hand or by a hand-held scanner that reads bar codes on goods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = entering shipping info for a single shipment/package, including scanning or typing data fields. Competent worker takes ~2–4 minutes per item.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle barcode scanners and basic data entry in structured setups, but navigating varied computer interfaces (keyboard, mouse, GUI forms) and handling diverse package types reliably is still challenging for general-purpose humanoids in 2026.

task: Perform functional tests of nano-enhanced assemblies, components, or systems, using equipment such as torque gauges or conductivity meters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one nano-enhanced assembly using appropriate instruments, including setup, measurement cycles, and recording results. Typical bench test takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise instrument handling (torque gauges, conductivity meters), fine probe placement on nano-scale components, interpreting ambiguous readings, and adapting test protocols — all beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Perform simple extractions to remove blackheads.
human_time_rationale: One instance = extracting blackheads from a client's face in a single session; a trained esthetician typically spends 15–25 minutes on basic extractions.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, precise force control on delicate skin, real-time tactile feedback, and safe navigation of a human face. Current robots lack the sub-millimeter precision and compliant touch needed; skin damage risk is near-certain.

task: Assemble components, using techniques such as interference fitting, solvent bonding, adhesive bonding, heat sealing, or ultrasonic welding.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one component set via a single joining technique (e.g., press-fitting two parts). Competent worker takes ~8–15 min depending on method and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled manipulation, tool/machine operation (welders, heat sealers), and technique switching. Current humanoids lack reliable fine force feedback, specialized tool use, and generalization across bonding methods.

task: Level refrigerators, adjust doors, and connect water lines to water pipes for ice makers and water dispensers, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing/setting up one refrigerator. Leveling feet, aligning doors, and connecting a water line typically takes a competent appliance technician 25–45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise leveling judgment, door hinge adjustment with hand tools, and plumbing connection (threading/tightening fittings to water pipes). Fine dexterous tool-use under cabinets/tight spaces and leak-free plumbing far exceed current humanoid capability.

task: Attach address labels, identification codes, and shipping instructions to containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = labeling one container with address, ID code, and shipping instructions. A competent worker reads, prints/selects, and affixes all labels in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Peeling and precisely placing adhesive labels demands fine dexterity and accurate perception of curved/varied container surfaces. Current humanoids struggle with thin, flexible label handling, alignment, and verifying correct placement reliably.

task: Assist mechanical engineers in product testing through activities such as setting up instrumentation for automobile crash tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up all instrumentation for a single crash test (sensors, accelerometers, cameras, data loggers). A competent technician typically takes 1.5–3 hours per test setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise routing and connecting of fragile sensor cables, operating specialized diagnostic tools, interpreting engineering specs, and coordinating in a complex vehicle environment. Far beyond current general-purpose robot dexterity and task generalization.

task: Perform routine maintenance such as cleaning and oiling parts, honing cylinders, and tuning ignition systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a routine maintenance session on a single engine/machine, covering cleaning, oiling, cylinder honing, and ignition tuning. A competent mechanic typically takes 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly dexterous fine manipulation (honing cylinders, tuning ignition timing), specialized tool use, precise force feedback, and domain-specific diagnosis. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Conduct calibration tests to determine performance or reliability of mechanical, structural, or electromechanical equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = calibrating one piece of equipment. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min setting up instruments, running test sequences, recording readings, and documenting results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine instrument connection, reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting pass/fail criteria, adjusting calibration controls precisely, and documenting results. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-use finesse, and domain-specific judgment needed for reliable autonomous calibration.

task: Remove trash and clean kitchen garbage containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a full trash bag, replacing it, and wiping/rinsing the container. A competent worker completes this in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tying/removing a full liner, navigating to a bin, lifting it outdoors, scrubbing the container interior, and replacing a new bag — multi-step dexterous/deformable-object handling in varied environments robots handle poorly.

task: Assemble containers and crates used to transport items, such as machines or vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one shipping crate/container for a large item (e.g., machine or vehicle). A competent worker typically takes 45–90 minutes depending on size and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting, coordinating large panels/planks, fastening hardware with high force, and working in varied unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack the strength, tool-use precision, and task generalization for large structural assembly.

task: Assemble or disassemble complex mechanical systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling or disassembling one complex mechanical system (e.g., a gearbox, engine sub-assembly, or industrial pump). A competent technician typically takes 1–3 hours; median ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high dexterity for torquing fasteners to spec, aligning precision components, and using varied tools (wrenches, pullers, gauges) in unstructured configurations. Current humanoids lack fine force control, tool-change fluency, and generalization for complex, multi-step mechanical teardown/assembly.

task: Maintain or repair measurement devices or equipment used for calibration testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing a single measurement/calibration device (e.g., replacing a component, cleaning contacts, re-zeroing). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min per device.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle delicate precision instruments, specialized tool use, diagnostic reasoning, and adherence to tight tolerances. Current humanoids lack the precision manipulation, domain-specific tool handling, and judgment needed for calibration equipment repair.

task: Direct delivery trucks to shipping doors or designated marshaling areas and help load and unload goods safely.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one truck arrival: directing it to the correct door/area, then assisting with loading/unloading. Typically 15–25 minutes per truck.
robot_success_rationale: Directing a live truck requires outdoor mobility, traffic signaling, and clear communication with a driver. Loading/unloading is partially feasible, but coordinating vehicle positioning in dynamic outdoor dock areas far exceeds current robot generalization and safety limits.

task: Disassemble and reassemble equipment for inspection.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassemble + inspect + reassemble one piece of equipment (e.g. a pump, valve, or mechanical unit). Competent technician typically takes 30–60 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct fasteners, applying varied torques, tracking part orientation, sequencing steps, and reassembling to spec — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and task planning generalization on arbitrary equipment.

task: Operate metalworking machines to fabricate housings, jigs, fittings, or fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single part (housing, jig, fitting, or fixture) on a metalworking machine. A competent machinist typically spends 1–3 hours per part including setup, machining, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating complex, specialized metalworking machines (lathes, mills, grinders) with precise tool changes, workholding setup, and real-time feedback. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and domain-specific tool-use for autonomous machining.

task: Assist nanoscientists or engineers in processing or characterizing materials according to physical or chemical properties.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one material processing/characterization session (e.g., sample prep through instrument run and initial data review). Typically 60–120 min for a competent lab technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized nanoscience instruments (SEM, AFM, XRD, cleanroom tools), extreme precision at micron/nanoscale, interpreting complex scientific data, and adapting to varied protocols — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Level washing machines and connect hoses to water pipes, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one washing machine: positioning, adjusting leveling feet, and connecting hot/cold hoses to wall valves. A skilled installer typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires moving a heavy appliance precisely, adjusting small leveling feet under load, and threading/tightening hose fittings onto water supply valves — demanding fine dexterity, torque control, and spatial reasoning beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Install straps, braces, and padding to loads to prevent shifting or damage during shipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = securing one shipment load. A competent worker takes ~15 min to assess the load, position padding, thread/tighten straps, and install braces.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force strap tensioning, routing straps around irregular loads, positioning padding precisely, and operating ratchet buckles — all demanding dexterity, strength, and spatial reasoning in cluttered, variable cargo environments beyond 2026 frontier robots.

task: Install appliances such as refrigerators, washing machines, and stoves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single appliance (e.g., refrigerator, washer, or stove): positioning, leveling, connecting utilities (water, gas, or electrical), and testing. ~30–60 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting gas/water/electrical lines (fine dexterity, tool use), maneuvering heavy appliances in tight spaces, leveling, and safety-critical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured home environments.

task: Reline or repair ladles and pouring spouts with refractory clay, using trowels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = relining/repairing one ladle or pouring spout. A competent refractory worker typically takes 30–60 min depending on ladle size and damage extent.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly skilled trowel work inside curved, confined ladle geometry with viscous refractory clay. Demands precise force control, tactile feedback, and tool manipulation in tight spaces — well beyond current humanoid dexterity and generalization.

task: Examine animals to detect symptoms of illness or injury.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining one animal. A competent vet tech or farmer spends ~10–20 min doing a systematic visual and physical check of a single animal.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced multi-modal perception (visual, tactile, auditory), safe animal handling, and clinical judgment. Current robots lack animal-safe dexterity, behavioral interpretation, and reliable anomaly detection on living, moving subjects.

task: Transfer clay structures to curing ovens, melting tanks, and drawing kilns, using forklifts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = transferring a clay structure load to one destination (oven, tank, or kiln). Includes positioning forklift, picking up load, traveling to destination, and placing it. ~15 min per transfer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a forklift — a wheeled vehicle humanoids cannot drive. Precise load handling of fragile clay structures adds further complexity. No current general-purpose humanoid robot can autonomously operate a forklift in a live industrial setting.

task: Move containers of mail, using equipment, such as forklifts and automated "trains".
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving a container of mail from one point to another within a facility. A competent worker takes ~15 min including positioning, attaching/loading, driving, and unloading.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a forklift or automated train — vehicles a humanoid robot cannot drive. AGV systems exist but are narrow/fixed-route, not general-purpose humanoid robots. Generalization across equipment types is extremely limited.

task: Direct or participate in cargo loading to ensure completeness of load and even distribution of weight.
human_time_rationale: One instance = overseeing/participating in loading one vehicle/container. A competent worker typically spends ~30 min directing and verifying completeness and weight distribution.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinating a team, reading manifests, making dynamic weight-distribution judgments, and directing human workers — high-level cognitive and supervisory demands far beyond current robot autonomy. Physical loading assistance is partial at best.

task: Visually inspect equipment to detect surface defects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single piece of equipment for surface defects. A competent worker typically takes ~10 min to walk around, examine surfaces, and note findings.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to equipment and use cameras for basic defect detection, but reliable surface inspection requires nuanced perception of subtle cracks, discoloration, or texture anomalies across varied lighting and geometries—still challenging for generalist robots in unstructured environments.

task: Clean, lubricate, and touch up minor defects on newly installed or repaired appliances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing one appliance (e.g., a washer or fridge). Wiping surfaces, applying lubricant to hinges/seals, and touching up paint chips takes a skilled technician ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal dexterity: wiping curved surfaces, applying lubricant to small joints, and precise cosmetic touch-up painting. Current humanoids lack fine tool-use precision and task generalization for all three sub-tasks reliably.

task: Clean and oil machine parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and oiling a single machine's relevant parts (e.g., bearings, gears, shafts). A competent technician typically takes 15–25 minutes per machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires wiping, scrubbing, and precisely applying oil to specific components. Dexterity for confined spaces, identifying correct parts, and controlled oil application are beyond reliable frontier robot capability. Unstructured industrial environments add difficulty.

task: Check items to ensure that addresses are legible and correct, that sufficient postage has been paid or the appropriate documentation is attached, and that items are in a suitable condition for processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one mail item. A skilled postal worker checks address, postage, and condition in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Reading handwritten addresses is challenging for vision systems; verifying postage validity and condition judgment require nuanced perception. Robots lack reliable OCR for degraded handwriting and contextual postage rules knowledge.

task: Respond to emergency calls for problems such as gas leaks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one emergency call response: travel to site, assess hazard (e.g. gas leak), take corrective action, and clear scene. Typical field service call runs 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving to scene in traffic, navigating unpredictable outdoor/indoor environments, using specialized detection tools, making safety decisions, and interfacing with gas infrastructure. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Test machines, components, materials, or products to determine characteristics such as performance, strength, or response to stress.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single item/sample through a defined test protocol (setup, execution, data recording). Typical lab/QA test cycle runs 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Testing requires interpreting specs, configuring varied instruments, applying precise loads/stimuli, reading analog/digital gauges, and judging results. Current humanoids lack the cognitive generalization, fine tool-use, and calibrated force control for autonomous multi-step lab testing.

task: Inspect and count items received and check them against invoices or other documents, recording shortages and rejecting damaged goods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one incoming shipment/delivery. A competent receiver inspects, counts, cross-checks an invoice, notes shortages, and flags damaged goods — typically 15–35 min per delivery.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading invoices/documents, counting varied items, detecting subtle damage, and recording discrepancies — combining advanced perception, OCR, semantic understanding, and dexterous manipulation of mixed goods. Current humanoids lack reliable document comprehension and damage-judgement generalization in unstructured receiving docks.

task: Stock cupboards and refrigerators, and tend salad bars and buffet meals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restocking a single cupboard, fridge, or buffet/salad bar station; a competent worker takes ~20–30 min including fetching, arranging, and topping off items.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied kitchen/dining environments, grasping diverse food containers, opening fridge/cupboard doors, arranging items neatly, and handling open food at a buffet — too unstructured and dexterity-demanding for reliable autonomous success in 2026.

task: Pack goods for shipping, using tools such as staplers, strapping machines, and hammers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = packing one shipment unit (box/pallet). A competent worker selects materials, wraps/boxes the goods, seals with strapping or staples, and labels — typically 8–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-tool use (staplers, strapping machines, hammers), adapting to varied item sizes/shapes, and reliable force control. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-switching, machine operation, and consistent packing quality across diverse goods.

task: Operate various types of equipment, such as computer scanning equipment, addressographs, mimeographs, optical character readers, and bar-code sorters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = operating a single piece of office/mail equipment for a typical work session task (e.g., scanning a batch, sorting a tray). ~15 min median per equipment use session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interacting with diverse, legacy office machines (buttons, feeders, paper trays, touchscreens). Robots lack generalization across varied equipment interfaces, precise paper-handling dexterity, and error-recovery judgment needed for reliable autonomous operation.

task: Set appliance thermostats, and check to ensure that they are functioning properly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting and verifying one appliance thermostat. Worker turns control to target setting and confirms readout or response within a short wait period.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying and manipulating varied thermostat dials/buttons (low dexterity), reading small displays or analog scales (perception challenge), and interpreting functional verification — all poorly generalized by current humanoids.

task: Trim, align, and join prints to form photographic mosaics, maintaining scaled distances between reference points.
human_time_rationale: One mosaic assembly session: trimming ~6–12 prints, aligning reference points, and joining them. A competent technician takes ~30–60 min depending on mosaic size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-dexterous cutting, sub-millimeter alignment of reference points, and spatial reasoning about scaled distances. Current humanoids lack the perception precision and tool-use finesse for acceptable quality.

task: Dry and bake new linings by placing inverted linings over burners, building fires in ladles, or by using blowtorches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drying/baking one ladle or vessel lining. Process involves setup, sustained heating, and monitoring; typically 1–2 hours per lining in foundry/steelmaking contexts.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating industrial burners, building open fires in ladles, and wielding blowtorches in high-heat, unstructured foundry environments. Current humanoids lack heat tolerance, sustained tool-use dexterity, and safe operation around extreme temperatures.

task: Measure furnace walls to determine dimensions and cut required number of sheets from plastic block, using saws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring one furnace's walls and cutting the required sheets. Measuring, marking, and sawing multiple sheets typically takes a skilled worker 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dimensional measurement of an industrial furnace, interpreting measurements, operating a power saw safely and accurately, and handling large plastic blocks — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and safety limits.

task: Search for section corners, property irons, or survey points.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating a single survey marker in the field. A surveyor uses maps, GPS, and probing tools; median time ~45 min accounting for varied terrain and vegetation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor navigation over rough/vegetated terrain, interpreting survey records, using metal detectors or probing tools, and identifying partially buried markers — well beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Climb scaffolding, carrying hoses, and spray surfaces of cupolas with refractory mixtures, using spray equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = spraying refractory mixture on one cupola's surfaces. A competent worker spends ~1–2 hours climbing scaffolding, positioning hoses, and coating all surfaces adequately.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing scaffold structures (extreme mobility challenge), carrying/managing heavy hoses, and precisely spraying irregular cupola surfaces. Current humanoids lack the balance, load-bearing capability, and rough-structure locomotion needed; nearly impossible autonomously.

task: Adjust machine pressure gauges and thermostats.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting pressure gauge and thermostat on a single machine. A competent worker reads current values, turns dials/knobs, and confirms new settings in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges (perception challenge), precisely turning small knobs or dials (fine dexterity), and verifying setpoints. Current humanoids struggle with fine dial manipulation and accurate gauge reading in varied industrial environments.

task: Select and apply cosmetic products, such as creams, lotions, and tonics.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying a standard cosmetic routine (e.g., cleanser, toner, moisturizer) to one person's face/body. A competent esthetician or caregiver completes this in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, gentle touch on human skin, product dispensing, spreading with appropriate pressure, and adapting to facial contours. Current humanoids lack the fine tactile sensitivity and safe force control needed near a human face. High failure/injury risk.

task: Test machines to determine proper functioning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one machine. A competent technician spends ~30 min running diagnostics, checking outputs, and verifying function across key operational parameters.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting varied machine interfaces, reading gauges/displays, operating controls, and making judgment calls about "proper functioning." Current robots lack reliable generalization across diverse machine types and nuanced fault diagnosis.

task: Make service calls to maintain and repair machines.
human_time_rationale: One service call to diagnose, maintain, or repair a machine; includes travel to machine, diagnosis, and repair work — typical field service visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosis of unknown faults, use of specialized tools, navigating varied environments, and adaptive repair strategies. Current robots lack the generalized fault reasoning, tool dexterity, and real-world adaptability needed for autonomous field service calls.

task: Fill machines with products, ingredients, money, and other supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restocking a single machine (e.g., vending machine, ATM, or ingredient hopper). A competent worker typically spends ~15 min loading, organizing, and verifying one machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires opening access panels, inserting varied items into specific slots/compartments, handling cash cassettes, and verifying fill levels. High variability in machine types, tight compartments, and precise placement challenge current humanoid dexterity and generalization.

task: Open cargo containers and unwrap contents, using steel cutters, crowbars, or other hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = opening one cargo container and unwrapping its contents. A competent worker uses hand tools to cut banding, pry open crates/lids, and remove wrapping in ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force dexterous tool use (crowbars, steel cutters), varied container types, unpredictable wrapping materials, and significant generalization. Current humanoids lack the grip strength, tool-wielding precision, and task adaptability needed reliably.

task: Route received goods to first available flight or to appropriate storage areas or departments, using forklifts, hand trucks, or other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = routing one batch of received goods; includes scanning/sorting items, deciding destination (flight or storage), and transporting via forklift or hand truck — typically 15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires forklift/hand-truck operation, dynamic routing decisions, navigation across large warehouse/ramp environments, and reading labels. Humanoids cannot drive forklifts; unstructured large-scale logistics with mixed equipment is beyond current autonomous capability.

task: Chip slag from linings of ladles or remove linings when beyond repair, using hammers and chisels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = chipping/removing the lining of one ladle. A competent worker using hammers and chisels typically takes 1–2 hours depending on lining thickness and ladle size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained high-force percussive work with heavy tools in a confined, irregular, hazardous industrial environment. Current humanoids lack the torque, tool-wielding strength, heat/dust tolerance, and task-specific generalization needed for this demanding demolition task.

task: Conduct surveys to ascertain the locations of natural features and man-made structures on the Earth's surface, underground, and underwater, using electronic distance-measuring equipment, such as GPS, and other surveying instruments.
human_time_rationale: One survey instance = a full field survey session for a defined area/site. A competent surveyor typically spends 2–5 hours on-site collecting data with GPS/total station equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized instruments (GPS rovers, total stations, sonar), navigating varied outdoor/underwater terrain, making expert judgments on control points, and recording structured data. No current humanoid robot can autonomously execute a professional-grade survey end-to-end.

task: Scrape leftovers from dishes into garbage containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scraping one dish. A competent worker uses a utensil to scrape food remnants into a bin in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can grasp utensils and make scraping motions, but handling varied dish shapes, sticky/wet food residue, and accurately targeting a garbage container requires dexterity and perception that current humanoids only partially achieve.

task: Adjust and repair coin, vending, or amusement machines and meters and replace defective mechanical and electrical parts, using hand tools, soldering irons, and diagrams.
