human_time_rationale: One instance = one machine repair/adjustment visit. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min diagnosing, replacing parts, soldering, and testing a single machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading diagrams, precise soldering (fine dexterity <5%), diagnosing faults via multimeter, navigating varied machine internals, and tool-switching — far beyond current humanoid generalization and fine manipulation capability.

task: Remove worn or damaged plastic block refractory linings of furnaces, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing the full refractory lining of one furnace chamber. A competent worker using hand tools (chisels, hammers, pry bars) typically takes 3–5 hours depending on furnace size and lining condition.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained high-force percussive/prying work in a confined, hot, debris-filled industrial furnace interior. Current humanoids lack the torque, tool-use robustness, heat tolerance, and situational judgment for this unstructured demolition task.

task: Clear jams in sorting equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing a single jam event. A competent worker locates the jam, stops the machine safely, removes the obstruction, and restarts — typically 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing jam location, navigating tight machinery gaps, applying variable force to dislodge irregular objects, and safely operating machine controls. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous manipulation in confined, cluttered industrial equipment spaces.

task: Load and unload mail trucks, sometimes lifting containers of mail onto equipment that transports items to sorting stations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading/unloading one mail truck. A competent postal worker typically takes 30–60 minutes depending on volume, including lifting containers onto transport equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids can pick and place boxes but struggle with varied container weights/shapes, outdoor/ramp terrain near trucks, lifting onto moving equipment, and the unstructured dock environment. No commercial deployment at this task yet.

task: Rewrap soiled or broken parcels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rewrapping one damaged/soiled parcel. A competent worker assesses damage, removes old wrapping, cleans if needed, and repackages in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires damage assessment, removing irregular old wrapping, manipulating flexible materials (tape, paper, plastic), and neatly resealing — all highly variable tasks demanding fine dexterity and adaptive perception that current humanoids struggle with significantly.

task: Adjust and operate surveying instruments such as prisms, theodolites, electronic distance measuring equipment, or electronic data collectors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up, adjusting, and operating a surveying instrument at a single station (e.g., leveling a theodolite, taking readings). Competent surveyor takes ~30–60 min per station.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor adjustment of delicate optical/electronic instruments, tripod leveling, target acquisition, and data collection in outdoor/unstructured terrain. Current humanoids lack the precision manipulation, specialized tool-use, and surveying software integration needed.

task: Distribute incoming mail into the correct boxes or pigeonholes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting a typical batch of ~20–30 pieces of incoming mail into the correct pigeonholes; a competent mail clerk takes ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading addresses/names on varied envelopes (OCR + perception), navigating a pigeonhole array, and inserting thin flat items precisely. Current humanoids struggle with reliable document reading, fine flat-object handling, and mapping names to correct slots.

task: Implement soil or water management techniques, such as nutrient management, erosion control, buffers, or filter strips, in accordance with conservation plans.
human_time_rationale: One instance = implementing a single conservation technique (e.g., laying a filter strip or installing erosion control matting) on a defined parcel. A competent worker typically takes 2–4 hours per site visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating tractors/heavy farm equipment, interpreting conservation plans, navigating rough outdoor terrain, and making adaptive agronomic decisions. Current humanoid robots cannot drive equipment or handle unstructured large-scale outdoor fieldwork autonomously.

task: Bundle, label, and route sorted mail to designated areas, depending on destinations and according to established procedures and deadlines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a typical batch of sorted mail (bundling, labeling, routing). A competent mail handler completes a standard batch in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading labels/addresses (OCR feasible), fine dexterous bundling with rubber bands or straps, navigating to multiple designated drop zones, and meeting time deadlines — combined complexity is high for current humanoids. Unstructured mail shapes and label-reading-to-action loops remain limiting.

task: Disassemble and assemble machines, according to specifications and using hand and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassemble + reassemble one machine (e.g., a pump, gearbox, or motor). Competent mechanic typically takes 60–120 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading specs, selecting and using varied hand/power tools, managing fasteners of many types, force control, and re-assembly to spec. Current humanoids lack reliable multi-tool use, torque control, and unstructured mechanical reasoning needed here.

task: Verify part dimensions or clearances using precision measuring instruments to ensure conformance to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single part with calipers/micrometers/gauges and recording results. A skilled technician typically takes 5–12 minutes per part depending on feature count.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle and orient precision instruments (calipers, micrometers) correctly, apply consistent contact force, and read analog/digital gauges. Current humanoids lack the tactile feedback and sub-millimeter manipulation accuracy needed reliably.

task: Replace malfunctioning parts, such as worn magnetic heads on automatic teller machine (ATM) card readers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing a single malfunctioning component (e.g. magnetic read head) in an ATM card reader; includes diagnosis, disassembly, swap, reassembly, and basic functional test.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle tiny, precisely-seated components inside a compact ATM chassis, tool use (screwdrivers, connectors), and diagnostic judgment. Far beyond current humanoid general-purpose capability.

task: Open and label mail containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = opening one mail container (envelope/package) and applying a label. A competent worker takes ~2–4 minutes including slitting, unfolding, and affixing a label.
robot_success_rationale: Opening varied envelopes/packages requires fine dexterity (slitting, tearing, unfolding) and precise label placement. Current humanoids struggle with thin flexible materials, varied container shapes, and consistent label alignment at acceptable quality.

task: Cancel letter or parcel post stamps by hand.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cancelling stamps on a single letter/parcel by hand with an ink stamp or pen mark; a skilled postal worker completes this in ~30 seconds.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise stamp/pen application on a small, flat target; grasping varied mail items and aligning a cancellation tool demands fine dexterity and perception. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-motor control for consistent mark placement.

task: Check and maintain diving equipment, such as helmets, masks, air tanks, harnesses, or gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full inspection and maintenance check of a complete diving gear set (helmet/mask, tanks, harnesses, gauges). A competent diver/technician takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle valves, o-rings, gauges, and straps; nuanced tactile/pressure-sensing to detect leaks or wear; specialized tool-use; and domain-specific judgment. Far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Take measurements to determine if appliances will fit in installation locations, performing minor carpentry work when necessary to ensure proper installation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single appliance location, checking fit, and doing minor carpentry (e.g., trimming a cabinet edge) if needed. A skilled installer typically takes 15–35 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement with tools (tape measure, level), spatial reasoning about fit tolerances, adaptive decision-making, and fine carpentry (sawing, chiseling). Current humanoids lack reliable tool use for carpentry and complex multi-step reasoning in unstructured home environments.

task: Feed and water animals, and clean and disinfect pens, cages, yards, and hutches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a full round of feeding, watering, and cleaning/disinfecting enclosures for a typical set of animals (e.g., one barn or kennel section). Experienced worker takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step, highly varied tasks: navigating unstructured animal environments, safely handling live animals, dispensing feed/water, scrubbing/disinfecting enclosures, and managing animal behavior — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Inject prepared animal semen into female animals for breeding purposes, by inserting nozzle of syringe into vagina and depressing syringe plunger.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one artificial insemination procedure on a single animal. A skilled technician typically takes 5–15 minutes including animal restraint, catheter/syringe insertion, and injection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tactile insertion into a live, moving animal's anatomy — extremely fine dexterity, biological variability, and animal handling/restraint. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability; risk of injury to animal is unacceptable.

task: Make special dressings and sauces as condiments for sandwiches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single batch of a dressing or sauce (e.g., aioli, vinaigrette, special sauce). A competent cook typically takes 10–20 minutes including measuring, mixing, and tasting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measuring of liquids/spices, whisking/emulsifying, taste-based adjustment, and tool use (whisk, blender). Current humanoids lack fine fluid control, sensory feedback for taste/texture, and reliable kitchen tool manipulation for multi-step recipes.

task: Build hutches, pens, and fenced yards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = building a single hutch, pen, or fenced yard from materials. A competent worker takes ~2–4 hours depending on complexity and size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/uneven terrain navigation, heavy lumber/wire handling, precise fastening (screws, staples, hinges), long-range spatial planning, and sustained force application — far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Integrate software or hardware components, using computer, microprocessor, or control architecture.
human_time_rationale: One instance = integrating a defined set of hardware/software components into a working system. A competent engineer typically spends 1–3 hours on a single integration task (wiring, flashing, configuring, and validating one subsystem).
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level cognitive planning, reading schematics, configuring software, fine cable/connector manipulation, and validating abstract system behavior. Current humanoids lack the cognition, fine dexterity, and tool-use fluency for autonomous systems integration.

task: Clip or shear hair on animals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grooming one animal (e.g., a dog or sheep). A competent groomer typically takes 30–60 minutes depending on breed/size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate force control on a moving, unpredictable live animal, precise perception of fur/skin boundary, and safe tool use near sensitive tissue. Current robots lack the compliance, fine dexterity, and animal-handling capability needed.

task: Trim excess covering materials, tack edges, and join sections of covering material to form tight joint.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming, tacking, and joining one seam/section of covering material (e.g., upholstery, carpet, or fabric). A skilled worker completes one joint in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high dexterity for precise trimming, consistent tension control, and tight joint formation. Fabric manipulation, tool switching (knife, tack gun), and tactile feedback demands far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Descend into water with the aid of diver helpers, using scuba gear or diving suits.
human_time_rationale: One dive instance: suiting up, descent, bottom time, and ascent for a typical working dive. Median professional working dive runs ~30–60 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Current humanoid robots are not waterproofed, cannot manage buoyancy, breathe, or operate SCUBA/dry suits. Underwater locomotion and pressure tolerance are entirely outside frontier robot capabilities. No viable path without radical hardware redesign.

task: Test and examine gas pipelines and equipment to locate leaks and faulty connections, and to determine the pressure and flow of gas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a pipeline segment or equipment set (e.g., a meter station or service line). A competent technician typically spends 30–60 minutes per site using detectors, gauges, and visual checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized gas-detection instruments, connecting pressure/flow gauges to fittings, interpreting readings, and navigating varied outdoor/industrial terrain. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use for precise instrument hookups, hazardous-gas-safe certification, and the diagnostic judgment needed.

task: Cut covering and foundation materials, according to blueprints and sketches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting materials for a single blueprint/sketch specification. A skilled worker reads the plan, measures, marks, and cuts in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading blueprints/sketches, precise measurement, marking, and cutting varied materials (fabric, foam, board) with appropriate tools. Blueprint interpretation, tool selection, and precision cutting far exceed current humanoid robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Recover objects by placing rigging around sunken objects, hooking rigging to crane lines, and operating winches, derricks, or cranes to raise objects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = recovering a single sunken object. Rigging placement, hooking to crane lines, and winch/crane operation typically takes 1–3 hours depending on depth and object size; 120 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires underwater navigation, dexterous rigging in zero-visibility aquatic environments, and operating cranes/winches/derricks — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities. No 2026 general-purpose robot can perform this autonomously.

task: Install machines, making the necessary water and electrical connections in compliance with codes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one machine (e.g. washer, dishwasher, or industrial unit) including water line hookup, electrical wiring/connection, and code-compliance checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-trade skills: threading/connecting plumbing fittings, wiring electrical panels, reading codes, torquing fittings to spec, leak/continuity testing. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and compliance reasoning.

task: Sort odd-sized mail by hand, sort mail that other workers have been unable to sort, and segregate items requiring special handling.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one mail piece (odd-sized, unsortable, or special-handling); a competent postal worker takes ~1–2 min per piece including reading, deciding, and placing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading varied labels/barcodes on irregular items, understanding postal routing rules, recognizing special-handling flags, and fine dexterous handling of non-standard shapes — well beyond current robot generalization and reading/reasoning capability.

task: Install, inspect, clean, or repair piping or valves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pipe/valve job (install, inspect, clean, or repair). A competent pipefitter averages ~45 min for a typical single valve/pipe section task.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-torque tool use (wrenches, pipe cutters), access in confined/cramped spaces, leak testing, and judgment under varied plumbing conditions. Current humanoids lack the force control, specialized tooling, and spatial reasoning needed.

task: Disconnect and remove appliances, light fixtures, and worn floor and wall covering from floors, walls, and cabinets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing a single room of appliances, fixtures, and floor/wall coverings. A competent worker typically takes 60–120 minutes depending on room size and fixture count.
robot_success_rationale: Requires disconnecting electrical/plumbing fittings, prying up flooring, unscrewing fixtures, and handling varied fasteners in tight spaces. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use generalization, and force-control capabilities.

task: Mix specified amounts of sand, clay, mortar powder, and water to form refractory clay or mortar, using shovels or mixing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one batch of refractory mix. A competent worker measures, combines, and mixes materials (shovel or machine) to a consistent workable consistency in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires measuring bulk granular/powder materials, scooping with a shovel, adding water, and judging mix consistency. Loose granular manipulation, liquid pouring, and quality assessment of viscous mortar are difficult for current humanoids; consistency judgement lacks reliable tactile/visual feedback.

task: Perform activities related to underwater search and rescue, salvage, recovery, or cleanup operations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single underwater operation (search, salvage, or recovery dive). A competent diver typically spends ~2 hours including descent, task execution, and ascent.
robot_success_rationale: Current humanoid robots are not waterproof, cannot dive, and have no underwater locomotion capability. Specialized underwater ROVs exist but frontier general-purpose humanoids cannot operate in submerged environments at all.

task: Observe animals in heat to detect approach of estrus and exercise animals to induce or hasten estrus, if necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one observation session per animal group per day. Skilled farm worker visually checks animals for estrus signs and may exercise them; typically 30–60 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced behavioral interpretation of live animals (mounting, restlessness, discharge), outdoor/unstructured terrain, animal-safe physical interaction, and judgment to induce estrus via exercise. Far beyond current robot generalization and animal-handling capability.

task: Position and hold the vertical rods, or targets, that theodolite operators use for sighting to measure angles, distances, and elevations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = holding a surveying rod at a single designated point while the theodolite operator takes a reading; typically 5–20 min per station.
robot_success_rationale: Robot must navigate outdoor/uneven terrain, hold a rod precisely vertical (plumb), and remain stable on command. Outdoor mobility is ~30–60%, but precise static rod-holding with fine verticality on varied ground and coordination with a human operator lowers success significantly.

task: Inspect surface to be covered to ensure that it is firm and dry.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one surface (e.g., a wall or floor section) before covering. A competent worker visually checks and touches the surface in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can visually scan surfaces and detect obvious wetness or instability, but reliably assessing firmness and subtle moisture (e.g., damp drywall, soft substrate) requires nuanced tactile and multimodal sensing beyond current frontier robot capability.

task: Remove obstructions from strainers or marine railway or launching ways, using pneumatic or power hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing obstructions from a single strainer or section of marine railway/launching ways; a competent worker using power tools typically takes 30–60 minutes depending on debris type and severity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating pneumatic/power hand tools in wet, unstructured marine environments with heavy debris, unstable footing, and complex tool-use. Current humanoids lack waterproofing, robust outdoor mobility on slippery marine surfaces, and reliable power-tool operation.

task: Lay out, position, and apply shock-absorbing, sound-deadening, or decorative coverings to floors, walls, and cabinets, following guidelines to keep courses straight and create designs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = covering one average room (~150 sq ft). A skilled floor/wall installer typically takes 2–4 hours including layout, cutting, aligning, and applying materials.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise spatial planning, consistent straight-line alignment, cutting irregular shapes, adhesive application, and quality judgment across large surfaces. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, reach, and generalization at acceptable quality.

task: Brand, tattoo, or tag animals to allow animal identification.
human_time_rationale: One instance = identifying and applying a brand, tattoo, or tag to a single animal. A competent handler takes ~5–15 min including restraint, application, and release.
robot_success_rationale: Requires restraining a live, unpredictable animal, precisely applying a brand/tag/tattoo to a specific body location, and safely releasing it. Current robots lack animal-handling dexterity, force compliance, and safety margin for live animal interaction.

task: Measure specified amounts of semen into calibrated syringes, and insert syringes into inseminating guns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a dose of semen into one calibrated syringe and loading it into an inseminating gun; a skilled AI tech completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely precise fluid handling with calibrated syringes, secure coupling of syringe to inseminating gun, and careful temperature management of biological material. Far beyond current general-purpose robot dexterity and biohazard-safe manipulation capabilities.

task: Measure, cut, and thread pipe, and connect it to feeder lines and equipment or appliances, using rules and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring, cutting, threading, and connecting a single pipe run to equipment. A competent plumber/pipefitter typically completes this in ~30–60 minutes depending on pipe size and access.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, operating a pipe threader/cutter with significant torque, fine thread alignment, leak-free sealing, and tool generalization in cramped spaces — far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity and tool-use capabilities.

task: Measure and mark guidelines on surfaces or foundations, using chalk lines and dividers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking a set of layout guidelines on a single surface/foundation. A competent worker measures, snaps chalk lines, and marks divider points in ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, chalk line snapping (bimanual tension + release), and reading tape/dividers on varied surfaces. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tool-use coordination, and spatial accuracy needed for acceptable construction-grade layout marking.

task: Collect coins and bills from machines, prepare invoices, and settle accounts with concessionaires.
human_time_rationale: One instance covers collecting cash from a typical batch of machines, counting/sorting it, preparing invoices, and settling with concessionaires — a standard shift unit.
robot_success_rationale: Task chains physical cash handling (fine dexterity, varied machine interfaces), financial accounting, and interpersonal negotiation/settlement. Current robots lack the cognitive/software integration and fine manipulation for all sub-tasks autonomously.

task: Clean work areas, machines, or equipment, to maintain a clean and safe job site.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single work area, machine, or piece of equipment. A competent worker typically spends 15–25 minutes wiping surfaces, clearing debris, and tidying tools.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle flat surface wiping and sweeping reasonably well (~40–70%), but industrial/machine cleaning requires navigating tight spaces, applying correct force, using varied tools, and identifying hazards — limiting generalization significantly.

task: Install gas pipes and water lines to connect appliances to existing gas lines or plumbing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting one appliance to existing gas or water line; includes measuring, cutting pipe, fitting connectors, sealing, and leak-testing. Competent tradesperson takes ~60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipe cutting, threading, torquing fittings to spec, leak testing, and navigating tight under-cabinet/wall spaces. Far beyond 2026 robot dexterity, tool-use, and force-control capabilities. Safety-critical task.

task: Select tools, equipment, or materials from storage and transport items to work site.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting required items from storage and carrying/transporting them to a nearby work site. A competent worker typically spends ~10 min identifying, retrieving, and moving items.
robot_success_rationale: Requires storage navigation, item identification among varied tools/materials, grasping irregular objects, and traversing potentially cluttered paths. Perception and generalization across arbitrary tool shapes and storage layouts remain weak in 2026 humanoids.

task: Cover surfaces with laminated plastic covering material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = covering one surface (e.g., a countertop or panel) with laminated plastic. Includes measuring, cutting, applying adhesive, pressing, and trimming edges.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise large-sheet handling, adhesive application, bubble-free pressing, and edge trimming with specialized tools. Current humanoids lack the fine force control, tool dexterity, and large-surface coordination needed for acceptable quality.

task: Sweep, scrape, sand, or chip dirt and irregularities to clean base surfaces, correcting imperfections that may show through the covering.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one average room's base surface (~150 sq ft). A competent worker sweeps, scrapes, sands, and chips imperfections in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-tool use (broom, scraper, sander), tactile force control to avoid over-sanding, and visual inspection for subtle imperfections. Current humanoids lack the fine force-feedback and quality-judgment needed for acceptable results.

task: Transport machines to installation sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading, driving, and unloading a machine to a single installation site. Median trip including prep, transit, and offloading is ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a truck/flatbed or operating a forklift on public/varied terrain — humanoid robots cannot operate vehicles in traffic. Heavy machinery loading/unloading is also beyond current autonomous capability.

task: Exhibit animals at shows.
human_time_rationale: One instance = presenting one animal at one show class/ring. Includes transport to ring, grooming touch-ups, handling/gaiting, and returning. Typical show class runs ~1–2 hours end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires skilled animal handling, reading live animal behavior, crowd navigation, judge interaction, and precise grooming/gaiting cues. No current humanoid robot has the animal-behavior perception, social interaction, or safe live-animal manipulation needed. Essentially infeasible today.

task: Operate underwater video, sonar, recording, or related equipment to investigate underwater structures or marine life.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single underwater survey session (dive or ROV deployment). A competent operator typically spends ~2 hours deploying, operating, and recovering equipment for one survey.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate underwater or pilot ROVs/submersibles autonomously. Task requires specialized aquatic platforms, not general-purpose land-based manipulators. Fully outside current humanoid robot domain.

task: Perform offshore oil or gas exploration or extraction duties, such as conducting underwater surveys or repairing and maintaining drilling rigs or platforms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full offshore work shift (e.g., one dive/survey or one rig maintenance task). Offshore duties are shift-based; a competent worker completes one discrete task set per 8-hour shift.
robot_success_rationale: Requires underwater/subsea operation, heavy machinery interaction, hazardous offshore environments, and specialized tooling far beyond current humanoid capability. No frontier general-purpose humanoid can operate in subsea or offshore rig conditions autonomously.

task: Install pilings or footings for piers or bridges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single piling or footing. A crew using a pile driver or drilling rig typically takes 2–4 hours per piling, including setup, driving/pouring, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy specialized machinery (pile drivers, cranes, drilling rigs), working in unstructured outdoor/aquatic environments, precise positioning under load, and multi-crew coordination — all far beyond 2026 humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Take test samples or photographs to assess the condition of vessels or structures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single vessel or structure section, including taking samples/photos. A competent inspector typically spends ~30 min per unit assessment.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate and capture images, but collecting physical samples (scraping, swabbing, coring) from irregular vessel/structure surfaces requires precise dexterity and contextual judgment. Outdoor/marine terrain and varied surface geometry further reduce reliability.

task: Inspect machines and meters to determine causes of malfunctions and fix minor problems such as jammed bills or stuck products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and fixing one machine malfunction (e.g., jammed bill/stuck product). A competent technician typically resolves this in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing varied, unstructured faults, navigating to machines, and performing dexterous fine manipulation (e.g., clearing jammed bills). Current humanoids lack reliable fault diagnosis, fine-finger dexterity, and generalization across machine types.

task: Dump and tamp clay in molds, using tamping tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling and tamping one mold. A worker dumps clay and tamps it firm in ~3–7 min depending on mold size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires scooping viscous/heavy clay, transferring without mess, and applying controlled tamping force with a tool. Force modulation, tool grasping in variable clay conditions, and mold alignment challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Form a smooth foundation by stapling plywood or Masonite over the floor or by brushing waterproof compound onto surface and filling cracks with plaster, putty, or grout to seal pores.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one typical room (~150 sq ft floor). Includes stapling sheet material or applying compound/filler, sealing cracks. A competent flooring worker takes ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling large unwieldy sheet goods, precise stapling, troweling compound into cracks, and quality judgment of surface smoothness. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and tool-use generalization for multi-step floor prep.

task: Set out and recover stakes, marks, or other monumentation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting or recovering a single survey monument/stake at a marked location. A competent surveyor spends ~30–60 min locating, placing, and documenting one point in typical field conditions.
robot_success_rationale: Requires GPS/total-station integration, precise outdoor terrain navigation, forceful stake driving or rebar setting, and legal documentation. Current humanoids lack the specialized tool-use, precise geospatial positioning, and unstructured outdoor capability needed.

task: Carry food supplies, equipment, and utensils to and from storage and work areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single carry trip between storage and work area; includes gathering items, transporting, and placing them. Typical kitchen/food service trip takes ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate indoor spaces and carry items, but grasping varied food containers, utensils, and supplies in cluttered kitchens/storage areas is challenging. Unstructured environments, fragile items, and multi-item loads reduce reliability.

task: Remove excess cement to clean finished surface.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing excess cement from a single finished surface (e.g., a tiled or masonry panel). A competent worker scrapes, wipes, and inspects in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine force-controlled scraping on a delicate finished surface, precise perception of thin cement residue vs. surface, and adaptive tool pressure. Current humanoids lack the dexterity and tactile feedback needed without risking surface damage.

task: Adjust controls to maintain specific building temperatures required for animals' health and safety.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking and adjusting HVAC/thermostat controls for one building zone; a competent worker reads gauges and adjusts settings in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital displays, interpreting context-specific temperature targets, and manipulating specialized HVAC controls or thermostats. Current humanoids lack reliable fine control interaction with arbitrary panel interfaces and domain-specific decision-making for animal health protocols.

task: Spread mortar on stopper heads and rods, using trowels, and slide brick sleeves over rods to form refractory jackets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one refractory jacket assembly (mortar application + brick sleeve fitting on one stopper rod). A skilled refractory worker completes this in ~10–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise trowel-based mortar spreading on irregular cylindrical geometry, controlled force when sliding heavy abrasive brick sleeves, and coordination in a hot industrial environment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use generalization limits.

task: Drill holes in rock and rig explosives for underwater demolitions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling and rigging a single underwater demolition site; a dive team typically spends 3–5 hours drilling blast holes, placing charges, and rigging detonators.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained underwater operation, high-force rock drilling, precise explosive placement, and rigging — all in a hazardous, unstructured aquatic environment. No general-purpose humanoid robot has any of these combined capabilities.

task: Salvage wrecked ships or their cargo, using pneumatic power velocity and hydraulic tools and explosive charges, when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One salvage operation instance (a single wreck or major cargo recovery) typically spans a full working day of active underwater/surface work for a skilled salvage diver or crew.
robot_success_rationale: Requires deep underwater operation, explosive handling, pneumatic/hydraulic tool use in unstructured marine environments, and complex decision-making. No current humanoid robot is rated for underwater deployment, explosive use, or maritime salvage conditions.

task: Prepare and serve a variety of beverages, such as coffee, tea, and soft drinks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and serving a single beverage order (e.g., a coffee, tea, or soft drink). A competent worker typically takes 2–8 minutes depending on complexity; ~5 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step manipulation (filling, pouring, operating machines), fine dexterity (lids, cups), and navigating varied kitchen layouts. Current humanoids lack reliable beverage-machine operation, liquid handling precision, and serving generalization.

task: Carry out non-destructive testing, such as tests for cracks on the legs of oil rigs at sea.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting all accessible legs of a single offshore oil rig section. A diver/technician team typically spends 2–4 hours per inspection session covering one structural zone.
robot_success_rationale: Requires underwater operation, navigating submerged rig legs, deploying NDT sensors (UT, MPI) precisely on curved steel surfaces in currents. No 2026 general-purpose humanoid can operate underwater or handle this specialized marine environment.

task: Heat and soften floor covering materials to patch cracks or fit floor coverings around irregular surfaces, using blowtorch.
human_time_rationale: One instance = heating and fitting a single patch or irregular-surface section. A skilled flooring installer typically takes 10–20 min per patch including prep, heating, and pressing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safely operating an open-flame blowtorch, judging material softness by touch/visual cues, and conforming material to irregular surfaces — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, thermal tool control, and fine force feedback capabilities.

task: Cut flooring material to fit around obstructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring, marking, and cutting one piece of flooring to fit around a single obstruction (e.g., a door frame, pipe, or cabinet). A skilled flooring installer typically takes 10–20 minutes per cut piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement of irregular obstruction geometry, accurate marking, and controlled cutting with power tools (jigsaw, miter saw). Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tool-use fluency, and spatial reasoning needed for reliable, accurate cuts in unstructured environments.

task: Set or guide placement of pilings or sandbags to provide support for structures, such as docks, bridges, cofferdams, or platforms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing/guiding one piling or sandbag set into position. A competent worker takes ~30–60 min accounting for rigging, alignment, and signaling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating or coordinating with heavy cranes/pile drivers, working on unstable aquatic/muddy terrain, and precise load guidance under dynamic conditions — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, mobility, and tool-use capabilities.

task: Light and adjust pilot lights on gas stoves, and examine valves and burners for gas leakage and specified flame.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one gas stove. Technician lights pilots, checks/adjusts each burner valve, inspects flame color/size, and tests for leaks — typically 10–20 min per stove.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine manipulation of tiny pilot-light igniters and valves, olfactory/sensor-based leak detection, precise flame assessment, and safety-critical judgment. Current humanoids lack gas-sensing, adequate fine dexterity, and reliable flame perception for autonomous pass.

task: Hold plumb bobs, sighting rods, or other equipment to aid in establishing reference points and lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = holding equipment steady at a designated point while a surveyor takes a sighting or reading; typically lasts a few minutes per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires stable bipedal stance, precise grip on slender tools (plumb bob, rod), and sustained stillness at a commanded position. Current humanoids have adequate mobility but lack the fine positional stability and prolonged hold precision needed; outdoor/uneven terrain lowers success further.

task: Inspect, examine, and test the operation of parts or systems to evaluate operating condition and to determine if repairs are needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single system or subsystem (e.g., engine, HVAC unit, brake assembly). A competent technician typically takes 20–40 min per system.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal perception (visual, auditory, tactile), diagnostic tool use, and contextual judgment about wear/failure. Current robots lack reliable test-instrument operation, fault interpretation, and generalization across varied systems.

task: Assess, sort, characterize, or pack known or unknown materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting/packing one batch of mixed materials (e.g., a bin or table lot). A competent worker typically takes ~15 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Current humanoids can pick-and-place known objects reasonably well, but identifying unknown or irregular materials, applying judgment on category/quality, and packing efficiently remain challenging due to perception and generalization limits.

task: Inspect and test docks, ships, buoyage systems, plant intakes or outflows, or underwater pipelines, cables, or sewers, using closed circuit television, still photography, and testing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One inspection instance covers a single structure/asset (e.g., one dock section or pipeline segment). A competent commercial diver/inspector typically takes 2–4 hours including setup, dive, documentation, and equipment stow.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires underwater mobility, specialized aquatic/ROV systems, CCTV & photography tool use, and autonomous navigation in unstructured submerged environments — far beyond 2026 humanoid robot capabilities. Dedicated underwater ROVs exist but are not general-purpose humanoids.

task: Remove damaged exterior panels, repair and replace structural frame members, and seal leaks, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing one vehicle/structure section (panel removal, frame repair, resealing). A competent worker typically takes 2–4 hours; median ~3 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying damage, applying high torque with varied hand tools, bending/welding structural members, and precisely sealing leaks — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and unstructured repair generalization.

task: Repair ships, bridge foundations, or other structures below the water line, using caulk, bolts, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single underwater repair session (e.g., caulking a hull section or tightening a bolt cluster). A competent diver/repair worker typically spends 1–3 hours per repair session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires waterproof operation at depth, strong currents, near-zero visibility, precise dexterous tool use (caulking, bolting) on irregular submerged surfaces. No current general-purpose humanoid robot is rated for underwater deployment or autonomous subsea manipulation at this complexity.

task: Maintain a supply of packing materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking stock levels and restocking a packing station with materials (bubble wrap, tape, boxes, etc.); a competent worker does this in ~15 minutes per restocking cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires inventory assessment, navigating a warehouse, retrieving varied packing materials (rolls, flat boxes, tape), and organizing at a station. Perception of stock levels and handling floppy/bulky materials remain challenging for 2026 robots.

task: Position and hold timbers, lumber, or paneling in place for fastening or cutting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning and holding one timber/board for a single cut or fastening. A skilled carpenter typically takes 2–8 minutes depending on size and alignment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping large/heavy irregular lumber, precise spatial alignment to markings or framing layouts, sustained holding force, and coordinating with a human cutting or fastening. Current robots lack reliable heavy-object handling and contextual precision for construction sites.

task: Erect scaffolding, shoring, or braces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = erecting a standard scaffolding section or shoring bay. A competent worker takes ~1–2 hours including laying out components, assembling frames, inserting braces, and securing pins/locks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires lifting and aligning heavy, unwieldy steel frames outdoors on uneven terrain, inserting locking pins with precise force, and coordinating multi-piece assembly. Humanoids lack the strength, outdoor mobility, and task-specific manipulation needed.

task: Treat minor injuries and ailments and contact veterinarians to obtain treatment for animals with serious illnesses or injuries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing and treating a single animal's minor injury/ailment (e.g., cleaning a wound, applying bandage) plus contacting a vet if needed. Typical duration is 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate animal handling (unpredictable, stress-sensitive subjects), fine dexterous wound care, medical judgment, and verbal/phone communication with a vet — all well beyond current robot capabilities.

task: Package and label semen to be used for artificial insemination, recording information such as the date, source, quality, and concentration.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing and labeling one ejaculate/batch for AI use, including filling straws/vials, sealing, labeling, and recording metadata. ~15–25 min for a competent lab technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity with tiny straws/vials, precise pipetting, cryogenic handling, legible label writing, and accurate data recording — all in a lab setting. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter precision and specialized lab tool-use needed.

task: Examine semen microscopically to assess and record density and motility of gametes, and dilute semen with prescribed diluents, according to formulas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one semen sample: microscopic assessment of density/motility, recording results, and diluting per formula. Typical trained technician takes ~15–25 min per sample.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a microscope with fine focus, interpreting live-cell motility visually, recording structured data, and precisely pipetting diluents per formula — well beyond current humanoid robot fine-motor, vision interpretation, and lab-instrument generalization capabilities.

task: Install, repair, and replace units, fixtures, appliances, and other items and systems in mobile and modular homes, prefabricated buildings, or travel trailers, using hand tools or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing/repairing/replacing a single fixture or appliance unit. A competent handyman typically takes 60–120 min per item (e.g., replacing a faucet, fixture, or small appliance).
robot_success_rationale: Requires high dexterity (wrenches, screwdrivers, pliers in tight spaces), varied tool use, problem-solving in cramped unstructured environments, and multi-step sequencing. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and fine manipulation limits.

task: Cut and weld steel, using underwater welding equipment, jigs, and supports.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single underwater cut-and-weld operation (setup, cut, weld, inspect). A competent commercial diver/welder typically takes 60–120 minutes per structural weld underwater.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diving/underwater operation, specialized wet-welding equipment, precise dexterous torch control, and structural judgment in zero-visibility conditions. No 2026 humanoid robot can operate underwater autonomously at this task.

task: Smooth or sand surfaces to remove ridges, tool marks, glue, or caulking.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sanding/smoothing one surface (e.g., a door panel or wall section). A competent worker spends ~15–25 min inspecting, selecting grit, sanding, and checking quality.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced force control, tactile feedback to detect ridges/tool marks, adaptive pressure, and tool switching across uneven surfaces. Current humanoids lack reliable tactile sensing and fine force regulation for consistent finish quality.

task: Roll and press sheet wall and floor covering into cement base to smooth and finish surface, using hand roller.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rolling and pressing one room's worth of sheet covering (~100–150 sq ft). A skilled floor installer takes ~15–25 minutes to roll and smooth a standard room section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained, uniform pressure over large floor areas, precise seam alignment, and force modulation with a hand roller. Current humanoids lack the stable low-posture mobility, consistent downward force control, and spatial coverage needed for acceptable quality.

task: Align, straighten, plumb, or square forms for installation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning/squaring a single form (e.g., a concrete form panel) for installation. A competent carpenter uses levels, squares, and braces to achieve plumb/level in ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise spatial reasoning, using measuring tools (levels, plumb bobs, squares), coordinated force application in unstructured construction sites, and judgment on when tolerances are met. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use and precision adjustment in outdoor/construction environments.

task: Locate and repair frayed wiring, broken connections, or incorrect wiring, using ohmmeters, soldering irons, tape, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating and repairing a single wiring fault. Diagnosis with ohmmeter ~10 min, physical repair (strip, solder, tape, reassemble) ~35 min for a competent electrician/technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (stripping wire, soldering, wrapping tape), multi-tool switching, diagnostic interpretation, and navigation of cramped, unstructured wiring environments — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Connect water hoses to inlet pipes of plumbing systems, and test operation of plumbing fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting hoses to one fixture's inlet pipes and running a functional test. A competent plumber takes ~20–30 min including fitting, tightening, and testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation (threading/tightening hose fittings to correct torque), leak-free sealing judgment, and functional testing interpretation. Current humanoids lack the precision grip, torque control, and adaptive problem-solving for plumbing tasks.

task: Cement the edges of the paved area.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cementing the edges of a typical paved area (~10–20 m perimeter). Includes mixing mortar, applying along edges, and finishing. A competent worker takes ~60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires mixing and applying wet mortar with precise tool pressure along irregular outdoor edges, fine finishing, and sustained outdoor locomotion on uneven terrain. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and generalization limits.

task: Attach loads to rigging to provide support or prepare them for moving, using hand and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rigging and securing a single load (e.g., a beam or equipment piece) using slings, hooks, chains, and tools. A competent rigger typically takes 15–35 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex dexterous manipulation of chains, hooks, slings, and shackles under high-stakes load conditions; tools vary widely; spatial reasoning around irregular heavy loads; safety-critical judgment. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Open and close doors, windows, and drawers to test their operation, trimming edges to fit, using jackplanes or drawknives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing and trimming one door, window, or drawer: open/close to diagnose fit, then plane/shave edges until operation is smooth. ~15–25 min for a competent carpenter.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-feedback diagnosis, skilled use of jackplanes/drawknives (highly dexterous, tool-specific fine work), and adaptive material removal judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use capabilities.

task: Glue and clamp edges or joints of assembled parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying glue to one joint/edge and securing with clamps. A competent worker applies adhesive, aligns parts, and positions clamps in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise glue application (dexterity-limited), controlled clamping force without crushing parts, and part alignment. Current humanoids lack reliable fine force control and tool-use generalization for clamps and adhesives.

task: Compact bedding sand and pavers to finish the paved area, using a plate compactor.
human_time_rationale: One instance = compacting one standard paved area (~50–100 sq ft patio). A competent worker makes several passes with a plate compactor, taking ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a wheeled gas/electric plate compactor — a separate piece of equipment a humanoid cannot drive or guide effectively. Lacks the grip, force control, and tool-operation capability for this specific machinery. No known pilots exist.

task: Refinish wood surfaces on cabinets, doors, moldings, and floors, using power sanders, putty, spray equipment, brushes, paints, or varnishes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = refinishing a single surface unit (e.g., one cabinet, door, or floor section). Includes sanding, filling, priming, and applying finish coats; ~2–4 hours median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-tool use (sander, sprayer, brush), fine force control on varied surfaces, navigating tight spaces, quality judgment for finish consistency, and safe handling of chemicals — all well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Remove rubbish or pollution from the sea.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single marine cleanup session (e.g., boat-based or shoreline-adjacent debris collection). A competent crew member spends ~2 hours per outing collecting and sorting debris.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate watercraft, navigate open water, or work in marine environments. Terrain (ocean/sea surface) is entirely outside current locomotion/manipulation capabilities. Highly specialized aquatic systems exist but are not general-purpose humanoids.

task: Reset hardware, using chisels, mallets, and screwdrivers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = resetting hardware on a single door/cabinet (removing, repositioning, and re-securing hinges, locks, or strike plates using hand tools).
robot_success_rationale: Requires forceful, precise tool use (chisels, mallets, screwdrivers) with varied force control, mortising, and spatial reasoning. Current humanoids lack the fine force modulation and tool-switching needed for reliable autonomous execution.

task: Fabricate, set up, and repair rigging, supporting structures, hoists, and pulling gear, using hand and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating/setting up or repairing a single rigging assembly (e.g., a hoist or pulling gear setup). A competent rigger typically takes 1–2 hours per discrete job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting, complex rope/cable manipulation, precise load-bearing assembly, use of varied hand and power tools in unstructured environments, and safety-critical judgment — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Seal open sides of modular units to prepare them for shipment, using polyethylene sheets, nails, and hammers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sealing all open sides of one modular unit. Cutting and fitting poly sheeting, nailing it securely around the perimeter takes a competent worker ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires cutting/handling large flexible polyethylene sheets (very hard for robots), precise nailing on varied surfaces, and coordinated two-hand manipulation. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterity for flexible-material handling and accurate hammer-nail tasks at scale.

task: Cut paving stones to size and for edges, using a splitter and a masonry saw.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting a single paving stone to size. A competent worker measures, positions on splitter or masonry saw, and cuts in ~5–10 minutes including setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, marking, heavy stone handling, operating a wet masonry saw or hydraulic splitter with exact alignment, and quality judgment. Current humanoids lack the precision, tool-operation capability, and force control for this specialized task.

task: Apply adhesive cement to floor or wall material to join and adhere foundation material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying adhesive cement to one standard tile/panel section (~1–2 sq ft). A skilled worker spreads, notches, and positions adhesive in roughly 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires controlled trowel spreading of viscous adhesive with correct pressure and coverage, working near floor/walls, precise force feedback, and tool generalization — well beyond current humanoid dexterity and compliant manipulation limits.

task: Move and set up mobile homes or prefabricated buildings on owners' lots or at mobile home parks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving and fully setting up one mobile home or prefab building on a lot, including transport, positioning, leveling, and utility hookups. Typically a full crew day (~8 hours).
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving heavy transport vehicles, operating cranes/hydraulic jacks, outdoor rough terrain work, structural assembly, and utility connections. Humanoid robots cannot operate this heavy equipment or perform the full multi-step setup autonomously.

task: Fasten timbers or lumber with glue, screws, pegs, or nails and install hardware.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fastening a set of timbers/lumber and installing associated hardware for a single joint or sub-assembly (e.g., a cabinet frame). Skilled carpenter averages ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-tool use (glue, screws, nails, pegs), precise alignment of heavy/irregular lumber, and hardware installation. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-switching, torque control for driving fasteners into wood, and strength for timber manipulation.

task: Secure stakes to grids for constructions of footings, nail scabs to footing forms, and vibrate and float concrete.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing footing formwork and concrete finishing for a single footing section. Staking grids, nailing scabs, and vibrating/floating concrete together take ~45 min for a competent laborer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy outdoor construction work: driving stakes into ground, nailing scabs to forms with precision, and operating a concrete vibrator and float — all in unstructured, muddy terrain. Far beyond 2026 humanoid generalization and tool-use capability.

task: Tilt, dip, and turn suspended loads to maneuver over, under, or around obstacles, using multi-point suspension techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = maneuvering a single suspended load past an obstacle using multi-point rigging. Includes rigging adjustments, signaling, and controlled repositioning; ~20–30 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating cranes/hoists, coordinating multi-point rigging, and real-time spatial judgment under dynamic load physics. No current humanoid can operate lifting machinery in traffic or unstructured sites autonomously.

task: Align, level, and anchor machinery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning, leveling, and anchoring one piece of industrial machinery. A skilled millwright typically spends 60–120 min using precision levels, shims, and anchor bolts.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precision metrology (laser alignment, dial indicators), fine shimming to sub-mm tolerances, torquing anchor bolts into concrete, and interpreting specifications. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, perception accuracy, and tool-use generalization.

task: Test rigging to ensure safety and reliability.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single rigging setup (e.g., slings, hooks, shackles, hoists). A competent rigger typically takes 20–40 min to methodically test and verify all components.
robot_success_rationale: Rigging inspection requires expert tactile feel, load testing, visual defect detection on complex hardware (fraying, cracks, deformation), and safety-critical judgment. Current humanoids lack the specialized perception, force sensing, and domain expertise needed for reliable autonomous rigging inspection.

task: Repair leaks in plumbing or gas lines, using caulking compounds and plastic or copper pipe.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single leak. A competent plumber spends ~30–90 min locating, accessing, cutting, fitting, and sealing one repair with pipe/caulk.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (pipe cutters, soldering, caulking guns), navigating confined spaces (under sinks, crawlspaces), leak diagnosis, and force-sensitive sealing. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Prepare base for installation by removing unstable or unsuitable materials, compacting and grading the soil, draining or stabilizing weak or saturated soils and taking measures to prevent water penetration and migration of bedding sand.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a standard residential/small commercial base area (~50–100 m²). Includes excavation, removal, compaction, grading, drainage measures, and barrier placement; typically 2–4 hours for a crew.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating excavators, compactors, and graders; assessing soil saturation; making engineering judgments about drainage and water migration barriers. No current humanoid robot can operate heavy earthmoving equipment or perform geotechnical evaluation autonomously.

task: Construct forms and assist in raising them to the required elevation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing and raising one form panel to required elevation; includes assembly, bracing, and positioning. A skilled carpenter typically takes 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting of large lumber/panels, precise spatial positioning at elevation, coordinated multi-step assembly with fasteners, and working on uneven construction sites. Far beyond current humanoid payload, coordination, and outdoor terrain capabilities.

task: Resurface an outside area with cobblestones, terracotta tiles, concrete or other materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = resurfacing one standard outdoor area (~20–30 m²). A competent worker needs ~6–8 hours for prep, laying, leveling, and grouting/finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy outdoor labor: excavating, leveling substrate, cutting materials, precise placement over large unstructured terrain. Beyond current humanoid dexterity, payload, and outdoor locomotion/tool-use capabilities.

task: Set pavers, aligning and spacing them correctly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting pavers for ~1 square meter of surface. A skilled worker places, aligns, and spaces pavers in roughly 30–60 minutes per square meter.
robot_success_rationale: Requires lifting heavy/awkward slabs, precise placement on uneven ground, spatial alignment judgment, and fine leveling adjustments. Current humanoids lack the payload capacity, outdoor terrain stability, and sub-centimeter placement precision needed.

task: Sweep sand from the surface prior to opening to traffic.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sweeping sand from a typical road/pavement section (e.g., a lane or intersection) before reopening; a crew member with a push broom or mechanical sweeper takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires operating a street sweeper or large push equipment on outdoor pavement. Humanoid robots cannot drive sweeper vehicles; outdoor terrain, large-area coverage, and specialized equipment make autonomous completion extremely unlikely with 2026 general-purpose robots.

task: Supply and place base materials, edge restraints, bedding sand and jointing sand.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and laying base materials, edge restraints, bedding sand, and jointing sand for a standard paving area (~20–30 sq m). A crew takes ~3–5 hours total; median ~4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy equipment (compactors, plate vibrators), precise grading of granular layers, cutting/staking edge restraints, and spreading/screeding sand outdoors on uneven terrain — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Cut and install insulating or sound-absorbing material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring, cutting, and installing one section/panel (e.g., one batt or rigid board). A skilled worker completes this in roughly 30–60 minutes depending on material and space.
robot_success_rationale: Requires measuring irregular spaces, cutting flexible/rigid materials precisely with tools, navigating confined areas (walls, attics, crawlspaces), and pressing material firmly into place. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-use, and spatial reasoning for reliable autonomous execution.

task: Drive motor vehicles to job sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one trip to a job site. Typical commute/job-site drive is ~20–30 min for a tradesperson or field worker.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate passenger/work vehicles in real traffic. L4 AVs exist but are narrow-route systems, not general-purpose humanoids. No current robot meets this task autonomously.

task: Manipulate rigging lines, hoists, and pulling gear to move or support materials, such as heavy equipment, ships, or theatrical sets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rigging and executing a single lift/move of a heavy load. A competent rigger typically spends 30–60 min planning, attaching, and executing one rigging operation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force manipulation of chains, shackles, and slings; precise load balancing; spatial judgment under load; operating hoists/winches. Current humanoids lack the strength, dexterity, and safety-critical judgment for unstructured industrial rigging.

task: Inspect and test operation, mechanical parts, and circuitry of gate crossings, signals, and signal equipment such as interlocks and hotbox detectors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and testing one grade crossing or signal installation. A trained signal maintainer typically spends 60–120 minutes checking mechanical parts, circuitry, interlocks, and detectors at a single site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized rail-signal test equipment, reading wiring schematics, probing live circuits, and making pass/fail judgments on complex interlocked systems. Far beyond 2026 humanoid robot generalization, dexterity, and domain-specific tool-use capabilities.

task: Cut tile or linoleum to fit and spread adhesives on flooring for installation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting and fitting one tile/section of linoleum and spreading adhesive for it. A skilled flooring installer takes ~30–60 min per section including measuring, cutting, and adhesive application.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, scoring/cutting hard or flexible materials to custom shapes, and spreading adhesive evenly with a trowel — demanding fine dexterity, tool use, and spatial planning on varied floor layouts. Beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Use manual or electric appliances to clean, peel, slice, and trim foods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = prepping a single batch of one ingredient (e.g., peeling/slicing vegetables for one dish); a competent kitchen worker takes ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Peeling, trimming, and slicing require fine dexterity, adaptive force control, and safe tool use (knives, peelers). Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation for varied food shapes and textures; cooking anchors suggest ~5–20% success.

task: Clean and dress machine surfaces and component parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and dressing (applying protective coating/lubricant) the surfaces and parts of one machine. A competent worker typically spends ~15–25 minutes per machine.
robot_success_rationale: Wiping flat surfaces is feasible (~40–70%), but dressing (applying lubricant/coating) to varied machine geometries requires precise tool use, force control, and generalization across parts. Complex geometry and chemical application lower success substantially.

task: Sweep sand into the joints and compact pavement until the joints are full.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling joints on a standard paver section (~20–30 sq m). Sweeping sand, making multiple passes, and plate-compacting until joints are full takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a plate compactor (heavy vibrating machinery), sweeping sand evenly across outdoor uneven pavement, and judging joint fullness. Current humanoids lack the tool-operation capability, outdoor terrain stability under vibration, and task-specific perception for this workflow.

task: Install ground rigging for yarding lines, attaching chokers to logs and to the lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching one choker to a log and connecting it to the yarding line. Experienced choker setters complete this in roughly 5–12 minutes per log on varied terrain.
robot_success_rationale: Extremely rugged, uneven forest terrain with mud, brush, and debris defeats current humanoid locomotion. Handling heavy steel choker cables around large, irregular logs requires extreme strength and dexterity far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Observe pressure gauge and move throttles and levers to control the speed of rotary tables, and to regulate pressure of tools at bottoms of boreholes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring-and-adjustment cycle during active drilling; a competent driller checks gauges and makes throttle/lever corrections roughly every 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting drilling conditions, and precisely operating drill-floor throttles and levers in a hazardous environment. Current humanoids lack drilling domain perception, safe operation of specialized controls, and real-time downhole feedback interpretation.

task: Inspect electrical units of railroad grade crossing gates and repair loose bolts and defective electrical connections and parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and repairing one grade crossing gate unit. A skilled technician typically spends 30–60 min inspecting, tightening bolts, and replacing/repairing electrical connections.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/roadside mobility, identifying loose bolts and defective wiring in complex enclosures, using hand tools precisely, and performing electrical diagnostics — far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity in unstructured field settings.

task: Inspect switch-controlling mechanisms on trolley wires and in track beds, using hand tools and test equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one switch location (overhead wire + track bed components). Includes setup, hands-on inspection with tools, test equipment use, and recording findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating live rail/overhead wire environments (safety-critical, unstructured), operating specialized test equipment, interpreting readings, and accessing confined track-bed spaces. Far beyond current humanoid deployment capability.

task: Start and examine operation of slush pumps to ensure circulation and consistency of drilling fluid or mud in well.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting slush pumps and monitoring mud circulation until stable operation is confirmed; a competent driller typically takes 10–20 minutes per check cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized oilfield pump controls, reading fluid gauges, interpreting mud consistency, and responding dynamically to drilling conditions. Humanoids cannot interface with heavy rig equipment or exercise expert fluid judgment autonomously.

task: Maintain and adjust machinery to ensure proper performance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance/adjustment session on one machine (e.g., lubrication, belt tension, calibration check). Competent mechanic typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse tool use, fine force-feedback adjustments, interpreting machine-specific gauges/manuals, and diagnosing abnormal sounds/vibrations. Far beyond current general-purpose robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Push levers and brake pedals to control gasoline, diesel, electric, or steam draw works that lower and raise drill pipes and casings in and out of wells.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one complete pipe/casing run (lowering or raising) in a drilling operation, typically 20–40 minutes depending on depth and pipe count.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized heavy drilling machinery (drawworks) via pedals and levers under dynamic load conditions, precise force/timing control, and real-time situational awareness. No current humanoid robot can safely operate such equipment autonomously.

task: Dismantle and store rigging equipment after use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dismantling and storing a typical rigging setup (slings, shackles, hooks, blocks). A competent rigger takes ~30–60 min depending on scale.
robot_success_rationale: Rigging hardware involves heavy loads, high-tension components, complex knots/shackles, and varied configurations. Current humanoids lack the strength, fine torque control, and situational judgment needed for safe autonomous dismantling.

task: Clean and oil pulleys, blocks, and cables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and oiling a single set of pulleys, blocks, and cables (e.g., on a crane, rigging, or mechanical system). Includes wiping, degreasing, applying lubricant.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to apply oil precisely to cables and sheaves, navigating tight/awkward rigging geometry, tool-switching (rags, brushes, oil cans). Poor generalization to varied mechanical configurations and hard-to-reach components.

task: Count sections of drill rod to determine depths of boreholes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = counting rod sections for one borehole. A worker visually counts and tallies rods (each ~3–6 m long) at the drill site; typically takes a few minutes per hole.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/unstructured drill-site navigation, counting partially occluded or mud-covered rods in a string or rack, and recording depth. Perception of cluttered, dirty industrial objects and reliable counting under field conditions remains poor for current humanoids.

task: Perform tie spacing layout and measure, mark, drill or cut.
human_time_rationale: One instance = layout, measure, mark, and drill/cut tie spacing on one structural section (e.g., rebar ties on one beam). A skilled worker takes ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise spatial measurement, marking, and operating power tools (drill/saw) on a workpiece in variable construction environments. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use precision, force control, and construction-site generalization for this multi-step workflow.

task: Install handrails under the direction of a carpenter.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one handrail run on a staircase. A competent worker under carpenter direction spends ~45–75 min measuring, drilling, and securing brackets and rail.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise drilling into walls/studs at angled positions, torquing fasteners, handling long irregular objects on stairs, and following verbal direction — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, spatial reasoning, and tool-use capabilities.

task: Assemble prefabricated parts to form subassemblies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one subassembly from prefabricated parts. Typical light-to-medium industrial subassembly takes a skilled worker roughly 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle structured pick-and-place and standardized part mating reasonably well, but varied part geometries, alignment tolerances, fastening steps, and unstructured staging areas reduce reliability significantly for general-purpose humanoids.

task: Screed sand level to an even thickness, and recheck sand exposed to elements, raking and rescreeding if necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = screeding a standard paver bed (~10–20 sq m), checking levelness, raking and rescreeding disturbed sections. Competent landscaper takes ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated whole-body tool use (screed board, rake) over uneven outdoor terrain, precise force/level feedback across a large area, and adaptive re-evaluation. Current humanoids lack outdoor terrain stability, long-tool manipulation skill, and quality-check perception for this task.

task: Attach pulleys and blocks to fixed overhead structures, such as beams, ceilings, and gin pole booms, using bolts and clamps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching a single pulley/block assembly to an overhead structure. Includes positioning, aligning, bolting, and clamping securely; ~20–30 min for a competent rigger.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working overhead at height, precise torquing of bolts/clamps on varied structural geometries (beams, booms), strong force application, and safety-critical verification. Current humanoids lack the reach, overhead dexterity, tool-use precision, and structural awareness needed.

task: Connect sections of drill pipe, using hand tools and powered wrenches and tongs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting one joint (two sections) of drill pipe. An experienced driller/roughneck completes a single make-up connection in ~3–7 minutes including positioning, stabbing, spinning, and torquing.
