robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force torquing with large specialized tongs/wrenches, precise pipe alignment on a moving rig floor, outdoor/wet/oily environment, heavy pipes (100s of lbs). Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, strength, and tool-use capability.

task: Clean lenses of lamps with cloths and solvents.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one lamp lens; a competent worker applies solvent to cloth and wipes the lens clean in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating a small curved lens, applying controlled solvent to cloth without over-saturating, and wiping with appropriate pressure/angle. Fine dexterity, tool-use (cloth+solvent), and delicate surface judgement challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Bolt together pump and engine parts, and connect tanks and flow lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a single pump-engine subassembly with tank and flow line connections; a competent mechanic typically takes 30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torquing of bolts to spec, seating gaskets, aligning flanges, and connecting hydraulic/fluid fittings — high dexterity and force-feedback demands. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and leak-free connection skill for industrial fluid systems.

task: Locate and recover lost or broken bits, casings, and drill pipes from wells, using special tools.
human_time_rationale: One fishing/recovery operation in a well (locating and retrieving a lost bit, casing, or pipe) typically takes a crew 2–6 hours depending on depth and complexity; 240 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires operating specialized downhole fishing tools, rig equipment, and wireline systems in an extreme, confined wellbore environment. No general-purpose humanoid robot has any meaningful capability here; requires domain-specific machinery and expert judgment.

task: Lubricate moving parts on gate-crossing mechanisms and swinging signals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lubricating all moving parts on one gate-crossing mechanism or swinging signal unit. A competent technician applies grease/oil to pivot points, hinges, and joints in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/trackside mobility over uneven terrain, precise tool use (grease gun application to small fittings), and identifying exact lubrication points on specialized railway infrastructure — well beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Install, inspect, maintain, and repair various railroad service equipment on the road or in the shop, including railroad signal systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one service/maintenance job on a piece of railroad equipment or signal system. A typical job (e.g., signal inspection, relay replacement, equipment repair) takes ~60–120 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized tool use, outdoor/trackside mobility over rough terrain, complex electrical diagnostics, and nuanced mechanical repair across highly varied equipment. Far beyond 2026 humanoid robot generalization, dexterity, and safety compliance capabilities.

task: Tighten loose bolts, using wrenches, and test circuits and connections by opening and closing gates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and tightening bolts on a gate mechanism plus opening/closing the gate to test circuits/connections. A competent technician takes ~15–25 minutes per gate unit.
robot_success_rationale: Tightening bolts with a wrench requires precise tool grasping, torque application, and bolt localization—challenging for current humanoids. Functional gate testing adds mobility and sequencing demands. Generalization to varied bolt sizes/orientations further limits success.

task: Repair or replace defective parts of machinery, such as rotary drill rigs, water trucks, air compressors, and pumps, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One repair/replacement instance on heavy equipment (e.g., swapping a pump seal or compressor valve). Median job ranges 1–3 hrs; 120 min is a reasonable midpoint.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults on large, non-standardized industrial machinery, applying high-torque hand tools, navigating tight/oily spaces, and handling heavy irregular components. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and generalization limits.

task: Replace defective wiring, broken lenses, or burned-out light bulbs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing one defective component (bulb, lens, or wiring segment). A competent electrician/technician takes ~20 min including diagnosis, access, swap, and testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (unscrewing tiny fasteners, routing wires, snapping lenses), fault diagnosis, and working in confined/varied fixture geometries. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and contextual electrical troubleshooting for unstructured environments.

task: Control movement of heavy equipment through narrow openings or confined spaces, using chainfalls, gin poles, gallows frames, and other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving one piece of heavy equipment through a narrow opening. Rigging, aligning, and controlled movement with chainfalls/gin poles typically takes 60–120 min for a skilled rigger.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized rigging equipment (chainfalls, gin poles, gallows frames), heavy load management, spatial reasoning in confined spaces, and multi-step force control — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities. No commercial deployment exists.

task: Attach brackets, hinges, or clips to secure or support components or subassemblies, using bolts, screws, rivets, chemical bonding, or welding.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching one bracket/hinge/clip to a subassembly. A skilled worker selects hardware, positions the component, and fastens it (bolts/screws/rivets/adhesive/weld) in ~10–15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment, varied fastener types (bolts, rivets, welding), and tool changes. Robots handle simple pick-and-place but consistent positioning, torque control, and welding across varied geometries remains unreliable for frontier humanoids in 2026.

task: Weigh clay, and mix with water and chemicals to make drilling mud.
human_time_rationale: One batch: weigh clay, measure/add water and chemicals, mix in pit/tank. A competent mud engineer takes ~20–40 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating industrial weighing equipment, handling bulk bags of clay, dispensing precise chemical volumes, and running mixing machinery in an unstructured oilfield environment. Well beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities in generalization, tool-use, and heavy material handling.

task: Position and align subassemblies in jigs or fixtures, using measuring instruments and following blueprint lines and index points.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning and aligning a single subassembly in a jig/fixture. A competent worker reads blueprints, uses measuring instruments, and secures the part in ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting 2D blueprints, precise measurement tool use, and fine alignment to index points — beyond typical pick-and-place. Current humanoids lack reliable blueprint-reading, instrument manipulation, and sub-millimeter precision alignment in unstructured jig setups.

task: Connect electrical systems to outside power sources and activate switches to test the operation of appliances and light fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting a circuit/panel to an external power source and testing appliances/fixtures in a single unit or room. A competent electrician typically takes 20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying and connecting varied electrical connectors/panels, managing live-wire safety, flipping switches, and interpreting appliance/fixture responses. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, electrical tool-use, and safety compliance for unsupervised live-electrical work.

task: Apply a bead of urethane around the perimeter of each pinchweld and dress the remaining urethane on the pinchwelds so that it is of uniform level and thickness.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one vehicle. A skilled auto-body technician applies and dresses urethane around all pinchwelds of a single vehicle in ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bead application along irregular 3D contours, controlled nozzle pressure, and fine dressing/smoothing for uniform thickness—far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and force-feedback capabilities in unstructured automotive environments.

task: Inspect or test installed units, parts, systems, or assemblies for fit, alignment, performance, defects, or compliance with standards, using measuring instruments or test equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single installed unit/assembly. A competent technician typically spends ~25 min setting up instruments, checking fit/alignment/performance, and documenting findings.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can perform basic visual inspection and simple sensor readings, but attaching/repositioning precision measuring instruments, interpreting multi-modal test results, and judging compliance across varied assemblies exceed reliable autonomous capability in 2026.

task: Plug observation wells, and restore sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = plugging one observation well and restoring its immediate site. Includes setting plug materials, sealing the wellbore, and surface restoration; typically 1.5–2.5 hours per well.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy drilling/pumping equipment, mixing and injecting grout or cement into a wellbore, and earthwork for site restoration. Humanoids cannot operate this specialized outdoor equipment autonomously. Unstructured terrain and complex tool-use further limit success.

task: Join structural assemblies, such as wings, tails, or fuselage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = joining one major aircraft structural assembly (e.g., wing-to-fuselage mate). Involves alignment, fastening, and inspection; typically takes a skilled crew several hours per assembly join.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme precision alignment of massive aerospace structures, specialized tooling, torque-critical fastening, and strict quality standards. Current humanoid robots lack the payload capacity, positional accuracy, and aerospace-certified tool-use needed. No commercial deployment exists.

task: Fabricate parts needed for assembly or installation, using shop machinery or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single part using shop machinery (e.g., cutting, drilling, shaping). A competent machinist/fabricator typically takes 30–60 min per part depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating diverse shop machinery (lathes, mills, saws, presses), reading blueprints, precise tool setup, and quality judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, machine-operation skills, and generalization for safe, accurate fabrication across varied equipment.

task: Layout and mark reference points and locations for installation of parts or components, using jigs, templates, or measuring and marking instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out and marking reference points for a single component or panel. A skilled tradesperson typically takes 10–30 min depending on complexity and number of points.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, tool use (scribes, chalk lines, tape measures, templates), and fine marking dexterity. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-motor precision and tool manipulation for accurate layout work on varied surfaces/orientations.

task: Align, fit, assemble, connect, or install system components, using jigs, fixtures, measuring instruments, hand tools, or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning and assembling one system component set (e.g., a mechanical subassembly). Competent technician using jigs, fixtures, and tools typically takes 30–60 minutes per assembly unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment, tool selection/switching, and force feedback with jigs and hand/power tools. Current humanoids handle simple pick-and-place well but struggle with multi-step tool use, tight tolerances, and generalization across varied component geometries.

task: Remove broken or damaged glass windshields or window glass from motor vehicles, using hand tools to remove screws from frames holding glass.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one windshield or window from a vehicle. A skilled auto glass technician typically takes 20–40 minutes to unscrew, unseal, and extract a windshield.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torque with varied screwdrivers in tight, awkward vehicle spaces, handling large fragile glass panels, and navigating complex unstructured geometry. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and tool-use generalization needed.

task: Inspect, maintain, and replace batteries as needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting, maintaining, and replacing one battery (e.g., a standard industrial or vehicle battery). A competent technician typically takes ~30 min including testing, cleaning terminals, and swapping if needed.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step fine dexterity (terminal clamps, bolts, connectors), electrical safety judgment, condition assessment via tools, and context-specific decision-making. Current humanoids lack reliable tool use and nuanced inspection judgment for this workflow.

task: Distribute food to waiters and waitresses to serve to customers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = plating and handing off a single order to a waiter/waitress at the pass. A skilled expeditor does this in ~1–3 minutes per order.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a busy kitchen, identifying correct plates, reading tickets, coordinating with multiple staff, and safely transferring hot/unstable dishes — too dynamic and socially complex for current robots.

task: Clean, restore, and preserve artifacts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning, restoring, and preserving a single artifact. A competent conservator typically spends 1–3 hours per artifact depending on complexity and condition.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, material-specific chemical knowledge, delicate touch control, and expert visual assessment. Far beyond current robot capabilities; even minor errors cause irreversible damage.

task: Line drilled holes with pipes, and install all necessary hardware, to prepare new wells.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing one new well installation (lining, piping, hardware). A competent well driller typically spends a full workday (~8 hrs) per well on this process.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy drilling rigs, handling large/heavy pipes, torquing casing hardware, and working in unstructured outdoor excavation environments. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in dexterity, strength, tool-use, and machine operation.

task: Check for and remove moisture or contamination in damaged areas and keep areas dry until repairs are complete.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking and drying a single damaged area. A worker inspects, removes moisture/contamination, and sets up drying measures — typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires moisture/contamination sensing, judgment about damage extent, tool use (towels, blowers, desiccants), and sustained monitoring. Current robots lack reliable wet-surface manipulation, damage assessment, and long-horizon area management.

task: Verify dimensions of cable assemblies or positions of fittings, using measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = verifying dimensions/positions on a single cable assembly. A competent worker uses calipers or a tape measure and checks key measurements in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool use (calipers, gauges), fine dexterity to position instruments on small fittings, and accurate perception of measurements. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-motor precision and instrument-reading for quality-acceptable inspection.

task: Set, align, adjust, or synchronize aircraft armament or rigging or control system components to established tolerances or requirements, using sighting devices and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning/synchronizing one aircraft system (armament, rigging, or control component). A competent avionics/armament technician typically spends 60–120 min per system check and adjustment cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme precision (tight aerospace tolerances), specialized tools, sighting devices, reading technical manuals, fine dexterous manipulation in confined airframe spaces, and safety-critical verification. Well beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Set up or operate machines or systems to crimp, cut, bend, form, swage, flare, bead, burr, or straighten tubing, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and operating a tube-forming machine for one production run/batch. A competent operator takes ~10–15 min to load specs, adjust tooling, feed tubing, and verify output.
robot_success_rationale: Task demands precise tooling changeover, reading specs, fine adjustment of machine parameters, and quality verification. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, domain perception, and machine-interface generalization needed for reliable autonomous setup and operation.

task: Remove all dirt, foreign matter, and loose glass from damaged areas, apply primer along windshield or window edges, and allow primer to dry.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one windshield/window for replacement: cleaning debris, applying primer along edges, and waiting for it to dry. Competent auto glass tech typically takes ~20 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise edge-tracing with primer applicator on irregular, damaged glass surfaces, debris removal in tight spaces, and quality judgment. Fine dexterity, tool handling, and visual inspection of curved surfaces in a vehicle context far exceeds current robot capability.

task: Position and prepare truck-mounted derricks at drilling areas specified on field maps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning a truck-mounted derrick at a drill site and completing setup (leveling, outriggers, mast raise). Experienced crew typically takes 1–2 hrs.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a large truck on roads/rough terrain, operating hydraulic outriggers, reading field maps, and raising a mast — all tasks far beyond current humanoid robots' vehicle operation and heavy-equipment control capabilities.

task: Monitor progress of drilling operations, and select and change drill bits according to the nature of strata, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = monitoring a drilling cycle and performing one drill bit change. An experienced driller inspects strata logs, decides on bit type, and swaps the bit using hand tools — typically 15–25 minutes per change.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large drilling rigs, reading strata/geological data, and using heavy hand tools in rugged outdoor/underground environments. Current humanoids lack drilling rig interfaces, domain perception, and the torque/force capacity for this industrial task.

task: Fit and fasten sheet metal coverings to surface areas or other sections of aircraft prior to welding or riveting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting and fastening sheet metal covering to one aircraft section. A skilled sheet metal mechanic typically spends 60–120 minutes on layout, trimming, aligning, clamping, and temporarily fastening one panel section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise aerospace tolerances, complex bi-manual manipulation of large rigid panels, custom tooling, and spatial reasoning in confined fuselage areas. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and domain-specific perception for aviation-grade fitment.

task: Load dishes, glasses, and tableware into dishwashing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a full rack/cycle of mixed dishes, glasses, and tableware into a commercial or household dishwasher; a competent worker takes ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping varied, fragile, wet objects; recognizing correct rack placement; handling clutter and odd geometries. Dexterity and generalization across dishware shapes remain major limitations for 2026 humanoids.

task: Install rubber channeling strips around edges of glass or frames to weatherproof windows or to prevent rattling.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sealing one window frame with rubber channeling strips. A competent glazier/installer takes ~10–20 min to measure, cut, and press strips around a standard window.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained precision force application along irregular edges, cutting flexible rubber to exact length, and tucking pliable material into tight channels — all demanding fine dexterity and compliance control that current humanoids lack reliably.

task: Remove moldings, clips, windshield wipers, screws, bolts, and inside A-pillar moldings and lower headliners in preparation for installation or repair work.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling all listed components (moldings, clips, wipers, screws, bolts, A-pillar moldings, lower headliners) on one vehicle to prepare for repair/install.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse fine dexterous actions: unclipping hidden retainers, removing varied fasteners with correct tools, peeling headliners, navigating tight vehicle interior geometry. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Add cutlery, napkins, food, and other items to trays on assembly lines in hospitals, cafeterias, airline kitchens, and similar establishments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one tray on a line; a skilled worker places cutlery, napkins, and food items in ~1–2 minutes per tray.
robot_success_rationale: Tray assembly requires grasping varied, deformable items (napkins, cutlery) and placing them precisely at speed. Robots struggle with flexible/irregular objects, tight spacing, and throughput demands of an assembly line. Perception and dexterity gaps remain significant.

task: Replace defective parts, using hand tools, arbor presses, flexible power presses, or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing a single defective part. A competent technician typically spends ~25 min diagnosing, removing, and installing one part using hand/power tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool selection, significant dexterous force application (arbor/power presses), part identification, and generalization across varied defective components. Current humanoids lack reliable press-tool operation and multi-step repair sequencing in unstructured settings.

task: Allow all glass parts installed with urethane ample time to cure, taking temperature and humidity into account.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing cure conditions and determining adequate wait time for one urethane-installed glass unit; involves reading environment and product specs, ~10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading environmental sensors, interpreting product cure specs, and making a judgment call on readiness. Current robots lack reliable contextual reasoning and decision-making for dynamic, condition-dependent quality judgments without human oversight.

task: Lead field training sites and train field staff, students, and volunteers in excavation methods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one field training session (half-day); a competent field director leads crew through excavation methods, demonstrations, and supervised practice at a site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires instructional communication, adaptive teaching, outdoor uneven terrain navigation, demonstrating nuanced excavation techniques, and supervising humans — far beyond current robot generalization, social, and pedagogical capabilities.

task: Gather and analyze artifacts and skeletal remains to increase knowledge of ancient cultures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single field excavation session or laboratory analysis of a collected assemblage. Skilled archaeologists/anthropologists typically spend 2–4 hours per unit of analysis.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level cognitive interpretation, delicate fine-motor excavation, contextual cultural knowledge, and scientific judgment far beyond current robot perception, dexterity, and reasoning capabilities. No frontier humanoid approaches this.

task: Cut flat safety glass according to specified patterns or perform precision pattern making and glass cutting to custom fit replacement windows.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting one pane of flat safety glass to a specified pattern. A competent glazier scores, snaps, and trims a single piece in ~15–25 minutes including measuring and fitting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise scoring force on fragile glass, controlled snapping, fine dexterity with a glass cutter, and geometric pattern interpretation. Current humanoids lack the fine-force control and tool-use precision; glass breakage risk is very high.

task: Clean, oil, or coat system components, as necessary, before assembly or attachment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/oiling/coating a single component or small set of components before assembly. A competent technician takes ~5–10 min depending on part complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sequenced multi-step prep (wipe, apply fluid/coating evenly, inspect coverage) with force-sensitive tool use and surface perception. Current robots lack reliable fine fluid application and quality-check generalization across varied part geometries.

task: Raise vehicles, using hydraulic jacks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = raising one vehicle with a hydraulic floor jack. A competent mechanic positions the jack, locates the jack point, pumps, and locks stands in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating under/around the vehicle, precisely locating jack points, operating a hydraulic floor jack handle with sustained force, and placing jack stands safely — multi-step dexterous tool-use in a cluttered, low-clearance environment well beyond current robot capability.

task: Inspect tire casings for defects, such as holes or tears.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one tire casing. A competent worker visually and manually checks the full casing surface for defects in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Vision systems can detect obvious surface defects, but thorough inspection requires flexing/manipulating the casing, feeling for subtle tears, and inspecting inner surfaces — demanding dexterity and tactile feedback beyond current robot capability.

task: Stir and strain soups and sauces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stirring a pot and straining it through a sieve into a container; a skilled cook completes this in ~5 minutes including setup and cleanup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fluid-aware manipulation (stirring viscous liquid, lifting heavy pot, controlled pouring through a strainer), fine force control to avoid spills, and heat tolerance. Current humanoids lack reliable liquid-handling and dynamic pouring precision.

task: Test and analyze samples to determine their content and characteristics, using laboratory apparatus or testing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single sample through a standard lab protocol (setup, instrument operation, recording results). Typical lab analysis runs 30–60 min for routine samples.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting, operating specialized instruments (centrifuges, spectrometers, titrators), interpreting digital readouts, and adapting to varied sample types. Fine dexterity and domain-specific tool-use far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Create photographic recordings of information, using equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = photographing a single subject/document/scene for record-keeping. Setup, framing, shooting, and reviewing a batch typically takes ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can hold and aim cameras, but autonomous framing, focus adjustment, lighting assessment, equipment setup, and ensuring acceptable image quality across varied subjects is unreliable with current general-purpose platforms.

task: Clean bars, work areas, and tables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wiping down a bar/work area/tables in a single shift reset; a competent worker sprays and wipes multiple surfaces in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to surfaces and perform wiping motions at ~40–70% success. Cluttered bar environments, irregular objects, wet spills, and glassware to navigate around reduce reliability vs. a clean flat surface.

task: Reassemble and test subassembly units.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reassembling and functionally testing one subassembly unit; a competent worker typically takes ~20–30 min including component fitting, fastening, and a basic functional check.
robot_success_rationale: Reassembly requires precise part alignment, fastening, and tool use — at the edge of current dexterity limits. Functional testing adds perception/judgment demands (interpreting pass/fail). Generalization across varied subassemblies further reduces reliability.

task: Disassemble subassembly units and examine condition, movement, or alignment of parts, visually or using gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling one subassembly unit and inspecting its parts. A competent technician typically takes 15–35 minutes depending on complexity; 25 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step disassembly with varied fasteners, fine dexterous tool use, and gauge-based measurement interpretation. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation, tool-switching, and quantitative gauge reading needed for full autonomous success.

task: Collect artifacts made of stone, bone, metal, and other materials, placing them in bags and marking them to show where they were found.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting, bagging, and labeling a set of artifacts from a single excavation context/unit. A competent field archaeologist typically spends ~45 min per context.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/uneven terrain navigation, delicate multi-material artifact recognition, precise gentle grasping, provenance labeling, and contextual judgment. Far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and semantic understanding of fragile irregular objects.

task: Clean glasses, utensils, and bar equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a full set of glasses, utensils, and bar equipment after a typical service shift; includes washing, rinsing, and drying/stacking.
robot_success_rationale: Handling fragile glassware requires precise, gentle dexterity; varied shapes/sizes of bar tools challenge grasp planning; wet, slippery surfaces add risk; navigating bar layout is unstructured. Current robots lack reliable fine grip for fragile items.

task: Remove cylinder heads and grind valves to scrape off carbon and replace defective valves, pistons, cylinders, or rings, using hand and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing cylinder heads, grinding valves, and replacing defective components on one engine. A competent mechanic typically takes 3–5 hours for this full procedure.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force torque sequencing, fine dexterous grinding, component-specific diagnosis, and use of specialized tools (valve spring compressors, torque wrenches) in cramped engine bays. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Hammer required counterweights onto rims of wheels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = balancing one wheel. A tire tech selects, positions, and hammers clip-on counterweights onto one rim in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise weight selection, positioning a small clip on a curved rim edge, and forceful hammering with exact angle/force. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, calibrated impact force control, and rim-edge perception needed for reliable autonomous execution.

task: Mark identifying information on tubing or cable assemblies, using etching devices, labels, rubber stamps, or other methods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking a single tubing or cable assembly. A competent worker applies a label, stamp, or etching in ~2–4 minutes including reading specs and verifying placement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle flexible, slippery cables, align small labels or stamps precisely, and operate etching tools. Perception of correct marking location on irregular geometry is challenging; current humanoids struggle with delicate, compliant-object manipulation.

task: Collect geological data from potential geothermal energy plant sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full field survey of a single geothermal candidate site, including drilling supervision, sensor deployment, sample collection, and logging. Typically 4–8 hours for a skilled geologist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating drills and heavy field equipment, traversing highly uneven/outdoor volcanic terrain, interpreting complex geological cues, and handling specialized instruments — all well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Hold cut or uneven edges of glass against automated abrasive belts to shape or smooth edges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grinding/smoothing the edges of a single glass piece. A skilled worker typically spends ~2–4 minutes per piece feeding and guiding it against the abrasive belt.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled manipulation of a fragile, irregular glass piece against a moving belt. Glass is brittle and hard to grip safely; edge irregularities demand adaptive tactile feedback current robots lack. High breakage risk.

task: Replace all moldings, clips, windshield wipers, or other parts that were removed prior to glass replacement or repair.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reinstalling all trim parts removed for a single vehicle's glass job. A competent auto glass tech typically takes 15–25 minutes to refit moldings, clips, and wipers.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to seat clips/moldings flush, operate in a cramped vehicle exterior environment, identify varied part types, and apply precise force. Current humanoids lack the manipulation precision and vehicle-specific tool-use needed.

task: Cool or warm glass in the event of temperature extremes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tempering a single pane or piece of glass by applying cooling/warming measures (e.g., damp cloth, warm air). Takes ~2–4 minutes for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires detecting temperature extremes via sensors, identifying glass, and carefully applying thermal treatment without cracking it. Perception and gentle manipulation are feasible but fragile-object handling and situational judgment limit reliability.

task: Butcher and clean fowl, fish, poultry, and shellfish to prepare for cooking or serving.
human_time_rationale: One instance = butchering and cleaning a single bird, fish, or shellfish. A skilled butcher/prep cook takes ~5–30 min depending on species; ~15 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (deboning, filleting, eviscerating), wet/slippery irregular objects, sharp tools, and force-sensitive cuts. Far beyond current humanoid manipulator capability in an unstructured kitchen setting.

task: Rotate tires to different positions on vehicles, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rotating all four tires on a single vehicle. A competent mechanic typically takes 20–40 minutes including jacking, loosening/tightening lug nuts, and repositioning tires.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a floor jack, applying high torque with impact/hand tools on lug nuts, lifting heavy tires, and working under a vehicle in tight spaces. Current humanoids lack the sustained torque, tool-use reliability, and safety margins needed.

task: Cut, slice or grind meat, poultry, and seafood to prepare for cooking.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single portion/batch of meat, poultry, or seafood for one cooking session; a skilled butcher/cook completes trimming, cutting, and portioning in ~10–15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Cutting raw meat requires high-force precision on deformable, slippery surfaces with irregular shapes. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, compliant grip, and food-safe tool-change capability needed. Perception of soft tissue boundaries is also very limited.

task: Replace or adjust motorized or manual window-raising mechanisms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing/adjusting one window regulator (manual or motorized) in a vehicle door; a competent auto technician takes ~30–60 min including panel removal and reinstallation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires removing door panels with varied fasteners, navigating tight confined spaces inside a door cavity, handling delicate wiring connectors, and precisely aligning/bolting regulators — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use generalization.

task: Develop or conduct environmental studies, such as plant material field trials or wildlife habitat impact studies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single field study session (e.g., one day of habitat surveys or plant trial data collection). Competent environmental scientists typically spend a full day (6–8 hrs) per field study event.
robot_success_rationale: Requires scientific study design, expert ecological judgment, specimen identification, complex unstructured outdoor terrain, multi-day adaptive protocols, and documentation. Far beyond current robot generalization, perception, and cognitive autonomy in unstructured natural environments.

task: Adjust or repair testing, electrical, or mechanical equipment or devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and repairing/adjusting one piece of equipment. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min per device including diagnosis, adjustment, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert diagnosis, fine dexterous manipulation of small connectors/fasteners, tool-use in unstructured settings, and contextual troubleshooting. Far beyond reliable autonomous capability of 2026 humanoids.

task: Distribute menus to hospital patients, collect diet sheets, and deliver food trays and snacks to nursing units or directly to patients.
human_time_rationale: One round on a nursing unit (~10–15 patients): distributing menus, collecting diet sheets, delivering trays/snacks. Involves walking, knocking, handing items, brief patient interaction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating crowded hospital corridors, interacting with patients in varied positions/beds, handling paper menus and trays, identifying correct patients, and managing unpredictable human interaction — well beyond current humanoid generalization and reliability.

task: Remove core samples during drilling to determine the nature of the strata being drilled.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retrieving a single core sample: stopping drill, pulling core barrel, extracting and logging sample. Typically 15–25 minutes per core run.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized heavy drilling equipment, handling heavy/slippery core barrels at variable heights, and interpreting geological strata — beyond current humanoid robot dexterity, tool-use, and domain-specific perception capabilities.

task: Participate in forensic activities, such as tooth and bone structure identification, in conjunction with police departments and pathologists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single forensic examination of skeletal/dental remains for identification. A competent forensic anthropologist/odontologist typically spends 1–3 hours on analysis, documentation, and reporting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level domain knowledge, fine tactile and visual discrimination of subtle biological structures, coordinated multi-party professional collaboration, and legally defensible judgment. Far beyond current robot perception, dexterity, and reasoning capabilities.

task: Dismantle engines and repair or replace defective parts, such as magnetos, carburetors, or generators.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dismantle one engine, diagnose, and repair/replace one or more defective components. A competent aircraft/small-engine mechanic typically takes 2–4 hours for a full teardown and repair cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level dexterity for torque-sensitive fasteners, precision alignment of components (magnetos, carburetors), diagnostic reasoning, and tool switching across a complex, non-standardized workspace. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Collect or prepare solid or fluid samples for analysis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting/preparing a single sample (e.g., soil core, fluid draw, swab). Includes labeling, containment, and basic prep. Median ~15 min for a trained technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous manipulation of vials, syringes, or swabs; leak-free sealing; correct labeling; and context-aware sample handling. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity and procedural generalization for varied sample types.

task: Remount wheels onto vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = remounting all wheels on one vehicle (e.g. seasonal tire swap). A competent mechanic takes ~15–25 min using an impact wrench and jack stands.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise lug-nut torque sequencing, lifting heavy wheels, aligning hub holes, and operating hydraulic jacks — high force, precision, and tool-use demands far exceed current humanoid dexterity and payload limits.

task: Listen to engines, examine vehicle frames, or confer with customers to determine nature and extent of malfunction or damage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing one vehicle. A skilled mechanic spends ~15–25 min listening to the engine, inspecting the frame, and conferring with the customer to scope the malfunction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multimodal diagnosis (auditory engine analysis, structural frame inspection, natural language customer interaction), deep automotive domain knowledge, and mobility around/under a vehicle — far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and conversational capability.

task: Drive automobile or service trucks to industrial sites to provide services or respond to emergency calls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one trip to an industrial site or emergency call. Median drive time of ~30–60 min accounts for urban/rural variability in industrial service contexts.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate automobiles or service trucks in traffic. L4 AVs exist but are narrow-domain, not general-purpose humanoids. No current frontier robot can legally or physically drive a service truck to an arbitrary industrial site.

task: Mix ingredients for green salads, molded fruit salads, vegetable salads, and pasta salads.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing ingredients for one batch of a single salad type (green, molded fruit, vegetable, or pasta). A competent kitchen worker takes ~10 min to combine pre-prepped ingredients.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied grasping of delicate/irregularly shaped ingredients, controlled mixing forces to avoid damage, pouring dressings, and navigating a kitchen environment. Current humanoids lack reliable gentle manipulation and recipe-following in unstructured kitchens.

task: Apply safety tags to equipment needing maintenance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying a safety tag to one piece of equipment. A worker locates the tag, fills it out, and affixes it to the correct lockout point in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to write/attach tags, identifying correct lockout points on varied industrial equipment, and reliable navigation in cluttered facilities. Current robots lack reliable fine manipulation, contextual judgment, and compliance-level accuracy for safety-critical tasks.

task: Repair or replace other parts, such as headlights, horns, handlebar controls, gasoline or oil tanks, starters, or mufflers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing/repairing one part on a motorcycle (e.g., headlight, muffler, tank). A competent mechanic averages ~45 min across the range of parts listed.
robot_success_rationale: Requires vehicle-specific tool use, torque application, fluid handling, wire routing, and diagnosis in a cramped, unstructured workspace. Current humanoids lack the precision, force control, and domain knowledge for reliable autonomous motorcycle repair.

task: Perform testing, maintenance, repair, or upgrading of accelerator systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance/repair/upgrade session on an accelerator system component; competent technicians typically spend 2–4 hours per session on diagnostics, hands-on work, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Particle accelerator systems require extreme precision, specialized high-voltage/RF/vacuum tooling, deep domain expertise, and operation in radiation-controlled environments. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, knowledge, and safety-certified tool-use for this highly specialized work.

task: Repair or adjust motorcycle subassemblies, such as forks, transmissions, brakes, or drive chains, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/adjusting a single subassembly (e.g. one fork rebuild or brake adjustment). A competent mechanic typically takes 30–150 min depending on subassembly; ~90 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high dexterity for torque-spec fastening, disassembly of complex mechanical subassemblies, fluid handling, and diagnostic judgment. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation, tool-use precision, and domain reasoning needed for unstructured motorcycle repair.

task: Glue tire patches over ruptures in tire casings, using rubber cement.
human_time_rationale: One instance = patching a single tire rupture. Includes cleaning area, applying rubber cement, pressing patch, and waiting for adhesion — ~10–15 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise surface prep, controlled cement application, accurate patch placement, and sustained firm pressure — all demanding fine dexterity and tactile feedback. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and force control for adhesive work on curved, deformable surfaces.

task: Connect test panels to engines and measure generator output, ignition timing, or other engine performance indicators.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting a test panel to one engine and recording key performance readings. A skilled technician typically takes ~20 min including setup, connections, measurement, and logging.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise connector mating to engine-specific ports, tool use, interpreting analog/digital gauges, and safety judgment in a high-voltage/mechanical environment. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity, domain-specific tool handling, and autonomous diagnostic reasoning for this unstructured task.

task: Locate punctures in tubeless tires by visual inspection or by immersing inflated tires in water baths and observing air bubbles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one tire. Visual scan takes ~2 min; water bath submersion, rotation, and bubble observation takes ~5–10 min total for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Water bath method requires submerging/rotating a large, heavy tire in a tank — challenging mobility and manipulation. Visual inspection requires detecting subtle bubbles or micro-punctures; current robots lack reliable fine-detail perception and fluid-environment dexterity for this unstructured task.

task: Participate in geological, geophysical, geochemical, hydrographic, or oceanographic surveys, prospecting field trips, exploratory drilling, well logging, or underground mine survey programs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full field survey day/program session (e.g., a day of geological prospecting, well logging, or oceanographic survey). A competent geoscientist typically spends a full 8-hour shift per field session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires traversing rugged/outdoor/marine terrain, operating highly specialized instruments (sonar, drill rigs, well loggers), expert scientific judgment, and adaptability to unstructured environments. Current humanoids cannot drive vehicles, operate heavy drilling rigs, or perform expert geoscientific interpretation autonomously.

task: Prepare rims and wheel drums for reassembly by scraping, grinding, or sandblasting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one wheel assembly (rim + drum). A competent mechanic scrapes, grinds, and/or sandblasts surfaces clean in ~20–30 minutes per wheel.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating grinders, sandblasters, and scrapers with precision on curved metal surfaces, managing debris/media, and judging surface quality. Current humanoids lack the force control, tool-use dexterity, and specialized equipment integration needed.

task: Adjust doors to open or close with the correct amount of effort, or make simple adjustments to electric openers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting one door (hinge tension, latch, closer, or opener setting). A competent worker diagnoses, adjusts, and tests in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires using screwdrivers/hex keys with precise torque on small fasteners, diagnosing swing resistance, and adjusting door closer valves or opener force limits — well beyond current humanoid dexterity and diagnostic capability.

task: Visit areas affected by erosion problems to identify causes or determine solutions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one erosion site visit. A specialist walks the area, observes erosion features, documents causes, and assesses solutions — typically 60–120 minutes per site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires traversing uneven, muddy, or unstable outdoor terrain (low robot mobility), expert visual diagnosis of soil/water patterns, contextual judgment, and generating actionable recommendations — far beyond current robot generalization.

task: Measure the intensity and identify the types of radiation in work areas, equipment, or materials, using radiation detectors or other instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = surveying a single work area or piece of equipment. A competent radiation technician typically takes 20–40 minutes to sweep, log readings, and document results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex/restricted environments, precisely positioning a detector at multiple points, interpreting instrument readings, and documenting results. Current humanoids lack reliable sensor-tool integration, instrument reading recognition, and structured survey protocols in unstructured industrial spaces.

task: Measure the properties of bodies of water, such as water levels, volume, and flow.
human_time_rationale: One instance = visiting a monitoring site, deploying/reading instruments (gauge, flow meter, sonar), and recording measurements. Covers travel to site, setup, and data logging.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/riparian terrain navigation, waterproof operation near/in water, specialized instrument deployment (flow meters, sonar probes), and precise sensor placement. Current humanoids lack aquatic mobility, robust outdoor terrain handling, and task-specific hydrological tool use.

task: Mix ingredients, such as liquor, soda, water, sugar, and bitters, to prepare cocktails and other drinks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one cocktail or drink order. A competent bartender measures, combines, shakes/stirs, and pours a drink in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid measurement, pouring control, shaker handling, and generalization across many recipes. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, grip compliance, and fluid-handling precision needed for consistent, quality results.

task: Cap wells with packers, or turn valves, to regulate outflow of oil from wells.
human_time_rationale: One instance = capping or regulating one well. Involves positioning equipment, setting a packer or turning a valve, and verifying seal/flow. Typical field operation takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized wellhead/downhole equipment outdoors on rough terrain, using large torque tools and industry-specific controls. Humanoids lack the specialized tool interfaces, force capacity, and domain-specific perception needed.

task: Reassemble tires onto wheels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting one tire onto one wheel (bead seating, inflation). A competent tire tech takes ~8–12 minutes per wheel using a tire mounting machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a specialized tire mounting machine or extreme force/leverage to seat beads, precise alignment of stiff rubber on a metal rim, and inflation — all highly tool-dependent tasks far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use capabilities.

task: Set up or direct set-up of instruments used to collect geological data.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up one geological instrument station in the field (e.g., seismometer, GPS, borehole sensor): site selection, leveling, calibration, and connection checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/rough terrain mobility, precise leveling and cable connections, domain-specific instrument knowledge, and judgment about site suitability. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity in unstructured field environments.

task: Operate or adjust equipment or apparatus used to obtain geological data.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up, operating, and adjusting one geological data-collection session (e.g., seismic, resistivity, or borehole equipment) at a field site. Typically 30–60 min per setup/adjustment cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized geological instruments (seismographs, geophones, resistivity meters) in unstructured outdoor/field terrain, reading analog/digital displays, and making calibrated adjustments — well beyond current humanoid robot generalization and tool-use capabilities.

task: Patch tubes with adhesive rubber patches or seal rubber patches to tubes, using hot vulcanizing plates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = patching one tube. Involves locating puncture, cleaning surface, applying adhesive/patch, and curing with vulcanizing plate — ~15 min total for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate surface prep (buffing, solvent cleaning), precise patch alignment on a curved flexible surface, and controlled pressure/heat application with a vulcanizing plate. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tactile feedback, and tool-use precision needed for reliable success.

task: Replace valve stems and remove puncturing objects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing one valve stem and removing one puncturing object from a tire. A competent tire technician completes this in ~15–25 minutes including dismounting, patching, and remounting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a tire changer machine, applying precise torque with specialized tools, fine manipulation of rubber valve stems, and detecting/extracting embedded objects — far beyond current general-purpose humanoid dexterity and tool-use capability.

task: Prepare, install, maintain, or repair equipment used for hydrologic study, such as water level recorders, stream flow gauges, and water analyzers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing or servicing one hydrologic instrument at a field site. Includes travel-on-site, setup/teardown, calibration checks, and data verification; ~90 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/rugged terrain navigation, precise multi-step tool use for anchoring and wiring sensors, calibration with specialized equipment, and expert judgment on placement. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and field autonomy.

task: Cut door stops or angle irons to fit openings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and cutting a single door stop or angle iron to fit one opening, including measuring, marking, and cutting with a miter saw or tin snips.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement of an opening, marking material, and operating a power saw or tin snips safely and accurately. Current robots lack reliable fine measurement, tool-use generalization, and the dexterity to safely operate cutting tools on rigid stock.

task: Fasten angle iron back-hangers to ceilings and tracks, using fasteners or welding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fastening one angle iron back-hanger to a ceiling/track. A skilled tradesperson typically drills, positions, and fastens/welds one hanger in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires overhead work at height with heavy angle iron, precise positioning, then welding or power-fastening — demanding combination of reach, force, tool-use, and fine control. Current humanoids lack reliable overhead manipulation, welding capability, and load-bearing precision for this trade task.

task: Place wheels on balancing machines to determine counterweights required to balance wheels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting a single wheel onto a balancing machine, spinning it, and reading the counterweight result. A skilled tire technician completes this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires lifting a heavy wheel (~10–15 kg), precisely aligning and mounting it on a machine spindle, tightening a cone/nut to spec, then operating machine controls — multi-step dexterous manipulation with heavy payload and tight tolerances exceeds current humanoid capability reliably.

task: Prepare classrooms with a variety of materials or resources for children to explore, manipulate, or use in learning activities or imaginative play.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single classroom before a session. A teacher/aide retrieves, arranges, and sets out varied materials across stations in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a complex, cluttered environment; identifying and retrieving diverse, often irregularly shaped materials; interpreting pedagogical intent; and arranging items meaningfully across multiple stations — far beyond current robot generalization and semantic reasoning.

task: Arrange bottles and glasses to make attractive displays.
human_time_rationale: One instance = arranging a single display shelf or grouping of bottles/glasses. A skilled bartender or retail worker takes ~10–20 min to arrange attractively.
robot_success_rationale: Requires aesthetic judgment, precise handling of fragile/varied glassware, and creative spatial arrangement. Current robots lack aesthetic reasoning and struggle with delicate, irregular objects at display quality. High breakage risk.

task: Prepare classrooms with a variety of materials or resources for children to explore, manipulate, or use in learning activities or imaginative play.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up one classroom before a session. A teacher arranges materials, organizes stations, and distributes supplies across the room — typically 20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a large, cluttered environment; selecting contextually appropriate diverse materials; arranging them meaningfully by learning intent; and adapting to classroom layout. Far beyond current generalization and semantic understanding capabilities.

task: Research, survey, or assess sites of past societies and cultures in search of answers to specific research questions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single field survey/assessment day at an archaeological site. Competent archaeologists typically spend a full workday (6–8 hrs) systematically surveying, recording, and sampling a defined site area.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert domain knowledge, nuanced artifact identification, contextual judgment, unstructured outdoor terrain (excavation pits, rubble, brush), GPS/GIS tool use, and research-driven decision-making — far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Wind large springs with upward motion of arm.
human_time_rationale: One instance = winding a single large spring. A skilled worker uses rhythmic upward arm strokes to coil the spring wire, taking roughly 2–4 minutes per spring.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained coordinated arm force, precise grip on stiff spring wire, and rhythmic upward motion against high resistance. Current humanoids lack the torque consistency, grip strength, and force-feedback fidelity for reliable spring winding.

task: Repair or replace worn or broken door parts, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/replacing one door part (e.g., hinge, latch, handle). A competent handyman typically diagnoses, removes, and replaces/repairs one component in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosis, fine dexterous tool-use (screwdrivers, chisels, pliers), varied fasteners, part alignment, and adaptive problem-solving on non-standardized doors. Far beyond reliable autonomous frontier robot capability in 2026.

task: Buff defective areas of inner tubes, using scrapers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = buffing defective area on one inner tube. A skilled worker locates defect, selects scraper, and buffs the area in roughly 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tactile feedback to detect subtle surface defects, controlled scraping force on a flexible/deformable tube, and fine dexterous tool use. Current humanoids lack the compliant dexterity and defect-sensing needed for acceptable quality.

task: Ask customers who become loud and obnoxious to leave, or physically remove them.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one disruptive customer incident: verbal warning, escalation, and removal if needed. Typically resolves in 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social judgment, de-escalation communication, reading emotional cues, and potentially physical contact with an uncooperative human — all far beyond 2026 robot capabilities. Legal/safety constraints also prohibit autonomous physical removal.

task: Inflate inner tubes and immerse them in water to locate leaks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one inner tube. Inflate, submerge in water trough, rotate, and identify bubble locations — typically ~5 minutes for a skilled worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires attaching an inflation nozzle (fine dexterity), managing a water trough (mobility + tool-use), and visually detecting small bubbles (subtle perception). Bubble detection and nozzle coupling are beyond reliable frontier-robot capability.

task: Collect water and soil samples to test for physical, chemical, or biological properties, such as pH, oxygen level, temperature, and pollution.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a matched water + soil sample set at a single field site, including field measurements (pH, temp, DO) and labeling. Typical field technician time ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/unstructured terrain navigation, precise probe insertion into soil/water, operating sampling instruments, labeling containers, and adapting to varying site conditions — far beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Serve snacks or food items to customers seated at the bar.
human_time_rationale: One instance = picking up a prepared snack/food item and delivering it to a specific seated bar customer, including any brief interaction. Typically 2–4 minutes per serve.
robot_success_rationale: Navigating a busy bar environment, identifying the correct customer, carrying food/drinks without spilling, and handling social interaction are well beyond current humanoid capabilities. Crowded spaces, dynamic obstacles, and tray dexterity remain major barriers.

task: Seal punctures in tubeless tires by inserting adhesive material and expanding rubber plugs into punctures, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sealing one puncture in a tubeless tire. A skilled technician locates the puncture, inserts the plug strip with a reamer and insertion tool, and trims the excess in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled insertion of a plug into a small, often hard-to-locate hole on a curved rubber surface, plus tool switching and trim cutting. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, compliance control, and tactile feedback needed for reliable execution.

task: Provide assistive devices, supportive technology, or assistance accessing facilities, such as restrooms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one person with one need (e.g., retrieving a mobility aid, guiding to/helping access a restroom). Typical interaction takes 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires social perception, dynamic communication, adaptive physical assistance, and safe contact with a vulnerable person. Current humanoids lack reliable unstructured caregiving, gentle human-interaction skills, and context-sensitive judgment needed here.

task: Weld tubing and fittings or solder cable ends, using tack welders, induction brazing chambers, or other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = welding/soldering a single joint (tube fitting or cable end). A competent worker sets up, joins, and inspects one connection in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torch/electrode positioning, heat control, filler material handling, and quality inspection on varied geometries. Current humanoids lack fine dexterity, thermal feedback, and reliable tool-use for live welding/brazing operations.

task: Set doors into place or stack hardware sections into openings after rail or track installation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting/hanging one door or hardware section onto installed rail/track. A skilled worker aligns, lifts, and seats the panel in ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires lifting and precisely aligning a large, heavy panel into a narrow rail/track groove — demanding bimanual coordination, force sensing, and spatial precision. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-alignment dexterity for bulky, irregular objects in constrained openings.

task: Assist students with boarding or exiting school buses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one bus arrival/departure event; a monitor assists ~20–40 students boarding or exiting over ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic outdoor terrain, guiding unpredictable children, navigating bus steps, social communication, and emergency response. Current humanoids lack reliable child-interaction, outdoor mobility on curbs/stairs, and safety assurance for this duty-of-care role.

task: Install door frames, rails, steel rolling curtains, electronic-eye mechanisms, or electric door openers and closers, using power tools, hand tools, and electronic test equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single door system (frame, rails, curtain/opener). A competent installer typically takes 1.5–2.5 hrs per unit depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting, precise structural anchoring, drilling into walls, running wiring, and calibrating electronic sensors — all in unstructured job-site environments. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capability in combination.

task: Clean sides of whitewall tires.
human_time_rationale: One instance = all four whitewall tires on one vehicle. ~2 min per tire with brush and cleaner, ~8 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires crouching near ground, locating curved tire sidewalls, applying correct scrubbing force with a brush, and rinsing — challenging posture, contact force control, and tool use on a curved outdoor surface for current humanoids.

task: Run low voltage wiring on ceiling surfaces, using insulated staples.
human_time_rationale: One instance = running one wire run (~20–30 ft) along a ceiling surface with insulated staples, including measuring, routing, and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained overhead work against gravity, precise staple placement into ceiling surfaces, tool manipulation (staple gun), and cable routing — all while maintaining balance. Overhead dexterity and sustained upward force are well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Operate lifts, winches, or chain falls to move heavy curtain doors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving one heavy curtain door from one position to another using lift/winch/chain fall. Includes rigging, operating equipment, and securing the load.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized lifting equipment (winches, chain falls) with significant force and precision. Humanoids lack the strength, tool interfacing, and safety judgment for rigging heavy industrial loads autonomously.

task: Test plant equipment to ensure it is operating properly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one piece of plant equipment (pump, motor, valve). Technician spends ~30 min including setup, running checks, reading gauges, and logging results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex industrial environments, operating specialized test instruments, reading analog/digital gauges, interfacing with varied equipment controls, and making diagnostic judgments. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Install, repair, or replace safety glass and related materials, such as back glass heating elements, on vehicles or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing/repairing one vehicle glass panel (e.g., windshield or rear window). A skilled auto glass technician typically takes 60–120 minutes, ~90 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise urethane bead application, handling large fragile glass panels, electrical connector work, outdoor/uncontrolled environments, and quality sealing verification — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and tool-use capabilities.

task: Provide assistive devices, supportive technology, or assistance accessing facilities, such as restrooms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = helping one person access a facility or obtain an assistive device; typical interaction including setup, guidance, and ensuring safe use takes ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social interaction, understanding individual disability needs, safe physical contact, and navigating unpredictable environments. Current humanoids lack reliable adaptive caregiving, safe body support, and context-sensitive assistive judgment.

task: Decontaminate objects by cleaning them using soap or solvents or by abrading using brushes, buffing machines, or sandblasting machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = decontaminating a single object or batch. A competent worker typically spends ~25 min selecting method, applying soap/solvent or abrading, and verifying cleanliness.
robot_success_rationale: Requires selecting among multiple tools/methods (soap, solvents, brushes, sandblasting), adapting force and technique per object surface, and verifying decontamination quality — beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterous tool-switching capabilities.

task: Bore or cut holes in flooring as required for installation, using hand or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = boring/cutting a single hole in flooring. A competent worker marks, sets up, and drills/cuts one hole in ~10–20 minutes depending on material.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, marking, and controlled power-tool operation on varied flooring materials. Tool handling, downward force application, and spatial planning on uneven surfaces are beyond reliable frontier robot capability in unstructured sites.

task: Conduct geophysical surveys of potential sites for wind farms or solar installations to determine their suitability.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full geophysical survey of a single candidate site (e.g. ~1–5 km² plot). A competent geophysicist with a crew typically spends a full field day (6–8 hrs) deploying sensors, collecting data, and doing initial QC.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rugged outdoor terrain navigation, deploying specialized geophysical instruments (seismometers, magnetometers, GPR), expert calibration, and adaptive decision-making. Current humanoids lack the mobility, tool-use, and domain-specific autonomy for unstructured field surveys.

task: Collect air, water, gas or solid samples for testing to determine radioactivity levels or to ensure appropriate radioactive containment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a full set of samples (air, water, gas, or solid) from a designated sampling point. Includes setup, collection, labeling, and documentation; ~30–60 min is typical per sampling event.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized sampling equipment (pumps, vials, filters), navigating potentially hazardous/confined radiological environments, precise handling of sealed containers, and strict contamination protocols — well beyond current humanoid robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Adhere to safety practices and procedures, such as checking equipment regularly and erecting barriers around work areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single safety check cycle: inspecting equipment and erecting barriers around one work area. A competent worker takes ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced hazard recognition, contextual judgment, barrier placement on varied terrain, and tool/equipment inspection with domain-specific knowledge. Current humanoids lack reliable generalization for unstructured safety compliance tasks.

task: Modify, devise, or maintain nuclear equipment used in operations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance or modification session on one piece of nuclear equipment; skilled technicians typically spend 1–3 hrs including safety protocols and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Nuclear equipment maintenance demands fine dexterity, specialized tooling, strict procedural compliance, radiation-environment tolerance, and regulatory oversight. Current humanoids lack radiation hardening, nuclear-certified tool use, and the precision/judgment required.

task: Set in and secure floor treadles for door-activating mechanisms, and connect power packs and electrical panelboards to treadles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing and wiring one treadle system (setting, securing, and connecting power pack + panelboard). A skilled installer typically takes 30–60 min per door unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise floor-level positioning, fastening into concrete/subfloor, routing and connecting wiring to electrical panels — multi-step dexterous trade work with conduit, terminals, and tools a humanoid cannot reliably execute autonomously in 2026.

task: Remove or disassemble defective automatic mechanical door closers, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing/disassembling one defective door closer. A competent technician takes ~20–30 min to unmount, disconnect linkage, and partially disassemble a standard overhead closer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high torque with hand tools on varied fasteners, working overhead or at awkward angles, precise disassembly sequencing, and strong grip on small hardware — beyond reliable frontier humanoid dexterity and force control in unstructured settings.

task: Climb poles or use truck-mounted buckets to access equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = ascending/positioning at a single pole or bucket lift to reach equipment. Includes donning gear, climbing/raising, and stabilizing at height — typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Climbing utility poles requires specialized grip, body positioning, and safety harness use far beyond current humanoid capability. Operating truck-mounted bucket controls at height is also outside demonstrated frontier robot skills. Near-zero success.

task: Instruct and monitor students in the use and care of equipment or materials to prevent injuries and damage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single instructional session with a student or group on equipment/material safety. A competent teacher typically spends ~15 min covering safety rules, demonstrating use, and monitoring initial practice.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced verbal instruction, real-time social monitoring of student behavior, adaptive responses to confusion or risky actions, and authority/trust dynamics. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and social cognition capabilities.

task: Count, weigh, measure, or organize materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single batch of materials (e.g., parts, ingredients, or goods) counted, weighed, measured, or organized; ~15 min is typical for a standard workload unit.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle structured pick-and-place and some weighing, but counting irregular items, reading scales/tools, and organizing varied materials in unstructured settings remains unreliable. Perception and dexterity gaps limit success.

task: Interpret or transcribe classroom materials into Braille or sign language.
human_time_rationale: One instance = transcribing or interpreting one typical classroom lesson/document (~30–60 min of material). A skilled Braille transcriber or sign language interpreter works in real time or near-real time.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fluent sign language production (highly complex, expressive, full-body motion) or embossing Braille with precision. Current humanoids lack certified sign language generation, nuanced facial grammar, and fine-motor Braille output. Extremely low generalization to live classroom context.

task: Identify and implement appropriate decontamination procedures, based on equipment and the size, nature, and type of contamination.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing a contamination scene and executing a tailored decontamination procedure. A competent hazmat/EH&S worker typically spends 30–60 min identifying hazards, selecting agents/PPE, and completing the procedure.
robot_success_rationale: Requires hazard identification (chemical/biological/radiological sensing), adaptive decision-making on procedure selection, PPE donning, and applying varied decontaminants — far beyond 2026 robot generalization, autonomy, and safety-critical judgment capabilities.

task: Assemble and arrange display materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a single display (e.g., retail shelf, exhibit panel, or trade-show booth section). A competent worker takes ~30–60 min median.
