robot_success_rationale: Requires combining locomotion, varied object manipulation, aesthetic spatial judgment, and interpretation of unstructured layouts. Current humanoids lack reliable fine assembly of diverse display components and contextual arrangement decisions.

task: Troubleshoot problems with audio-visual equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and resolving a single AV issue (e.g., no signal, distorted audio). A competent technician typically takes 15–25 minutes per problem.
robot_success_rationale: Requires abstract diagnostic reasoning, interpreting error states on screens, manipulating varied cables/connectors, and adjusting software settings — far beyond current general-purpose robot generalization and cognitive troubleshooting ability.

task: Cover treadles with carpeting or other floor covering materials, and test systems by operating treadles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = covering one treadle with flooring material and testing it. Cutting material to fit, securing it, and operating the treadle for a functional test takes ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise cutting of flooring material to irregular shapes, adhesive application, alignment under floor surfaces, and functional testing of a mechanical system — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use generalization.

task: Clean door closer parts, using caustic soda, rotary brushes, or grinding wheels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one set of door closer parts. Soaking, scrubbing with rotary brushes or grinding wheels, and rinsing takes ~15 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling caustic chemicals safely, precise use of rotary tools/grinding wheels on small irregular metal parts, and fine dexterous manipulation. Current humanoids lack the tool-use precision and chemical-handling protocols needed.

task: Maintain hardware and software, including computers, media equipment, scanners, color copiers, and color laser printers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance session on one device (e.g., cleaning, replacing consumables, running diagnostics). ~45 min median across varied equipment types.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse tool use, software interaction, fine dexterity (cables, toner cartridges, scanner glass), and diagnostic judgment across heterogeneous equipment. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and reliability.

task: Fabricate replacements for worn or broken parts, using welders, lathes, drill presses, or shaping or milling machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single replacement part. Measuring, setting up machine (lathe, mill, welder), machining/welding, and finishing typically takes 60–120 min for a moderately complex part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating multiple specialized heavy machines (lathes, mills, welders), fine dexterous tool-use, precise measurement, and adaptive decision-making. No current humanoid can safely operate or program such industrial equipment autonomously.

task: Assist in bus loading and unloading.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting passengers during a single bus stop event (boarding/alighting). A typical stop with a few passengers takes ~10–20 minutes including any mobility aid assistance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social interaction, guiding diverse passengers (elderly, disabled, luggage), navigating crowded semi-outdoor environments, and handling wheelchair ramps/securements. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and caregiving ability.

task: Restock teaching materials or supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restocking supplies for one classroom or storage area (e.g., paper, markers, scissors). Includes gathering items, transporting, and organizing onto shelves.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating school hallways, recognizing varied supply types, opening cabinets/drawers, and placing items correctly. Generalization across unstructured classroom layouts and mixed object types is still poor for 2026 humanoids.

task: Slice and pit fruit for garnishing drinks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = slicing and pitting one piece of fruit (e.g., a lime wheel or cherry) for a single drink garnish. A bartender does this in ~2–4 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise knife handling, stable grip on slippery/irregular fruit, and pit removal — fine dexterous manipulation with sharp tools in an unstructured environment. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control and force sensitivity needed reliably.

task: Separate tubed tires from wheels, using rubber mallets and metal bars or mechanical tire changers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one tubed tire from its wheel. A competent mechanic takes ~5–15 min depending on tire size and bead tightness.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force leveraging with metal bars/mallets on a stiff, awkward object; precise bead-breaking technique; tool switching; and significant strength. Well beyond current humanoid dexterity and force-control capabilities.

task: Provide students with disabilities with assistive devices, supportive technology, or assistance accessing facilities, such as restrooms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one student with one need (e.g., setting up a device, escorting to restroom). Typical interaction takes ~10–20 minutes including setup and return.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social communication, individualized assessment of student needs, fine dexterous device configuration, navigating complex social/physical environments, and safe human-contact caregiving — all well beyond 2026 frontier robots.

task: Drive vehicles equipped with tools and materials to job sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one trip to a job site. Typical drive time for a tradesperson/crew vehicle is ~20–50 min; 35 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate trucks/vans in real traffic. L4 AVs are narrow-domain systems, not general-purpose humanoids. Loading tools/materials adds further manipulation demands on top of an already impossible driving task.

task: Inspect engines for wear or defective parts, using equipment or measuring devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one engine. A competent mechanic takes ~30–60 min to systematically check components, use gauges/micrometers, and assess wear on a typical automotive or small industrial engine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (micrometers, bore gauges, borescopes), interpreting subtle wear patterns, navigating tight engine bays, and making nuanced diagnostic judgments — well beyond current humanoid generalization and precision.

task: Install dock seals, bumpers, or shelters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single dock seal, bumper, or shelter unit. A competent worker drills, anchors, and mounts the unit to a loading dock wall/frame in ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting, precise anchor drilling into concrete/steel, use of power tools in cramped outdoor/semi-outdoor dock environments, and complex fastening. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and force control for construction-grade installation.

task: Apply rubber cement to buffed tire casings prior to vulcanization process.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying rubber cement to one tire casing. A skilled tire repair/retreading worker typically buffs and cements a single casing in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, even coating of a large curved, irregular surface with a liquid adhesive using a brush/applicator. Current humanoids lack the fine brush control, consistent pressure regulation, and chemical-handling dexterity needed for uniform, quality-accepted cement application on complex tire geometry.

task: Clean classrooms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one standard classroom (sweeping/mopping floors, wiping desks/surfaces, emptying trash). A competent janitor typically takes 20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep/mop flat floors and wipe surfaces at moderate success rates, but navigating chairs/desks, emptying trash cans, and handling varied classroom clutter (books, cables, backpacks) significantly reduces reliability. Generalization to unstructured layouts is still weak.

task: Prepare appetizers such as pickles, cheese, and cold meats.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a full appetizer spread (pickles, cheese, cold meats) for a typical serving. Includes slicing, arranging on a board/plate, and garnishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating varied refrigerated items, opening packages, slicing soft/irregular foods, and artfully arranging them. Fine dexterity for thin slicing, handling limp meats, and aesthetic plating far exceeds current robot capability.

task: Carry out therapeutic regimens, such as behavior modification and personal development programs, under the supervision of special education instructors, psychologists, or speech-language pathologists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single therapeutic session (e.g., behavior modification or personal development) with one client; typical sessions run 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social-emotional perception, adaptive communication, real-time clinical judgment, and rapport-building with vulnerable individuals. Current robots lack the cognitive, empathetic, and generalization capabilities needed for supervised therapeutic delivery.

task: Identify defective sectionalizing devices, circuit breakers, fuses, voltage regulators, transformers, switches, relays, or wiring, using wiring diagrams and electrical-testing instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing a single defective electrical component. A competent electrician/lineworker spends ~30–60 min locating the fault, reading wiring diagrams, and using test instruments.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting complex wiring diagrams, attaching precision test probes to varied live electrical components, reasoning about fault signatures, and navigating utility environments. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities autonomously and safely.

task: Install, maintain, and repair electrical distribution and transmission systems, including conduits, cables, wires, and related equipment, such as transformers, circuit breakers, and switches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single typical job (e.g., installing a circuit run, replacing a transformer, or repairing a fault). A competent electrician averages ~2–4 hours per discrete job including setup and testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing poles/ladders, working in confined conduits, precise wire termination, operating heavy tools, reading live circuits, and working outdoors on unstructured terrain. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities for autonomous end-to-end execution.

task: Distribute teaching materials, such as textbooks, workbooks, papers, and pencils, to students.
human_time_rationale: One instance = distributing materials to one classroom (~25–30 students). A teacher or aide walks the room handing out items in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Navigating a dynamic classroom with children, differentiating and handing varied items (booklets, loose papers, pencils) to specific seated individuals requires robust social navigation, fine grasp of thin/small objects, and crowd awareness — beyond reliable 2026 capability.

task: Distribute or collect tests or homework assignments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = distributing or collecting a set of papers to/from ~25–30 students in a single classroom. A teacher typically completes this in 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate a classroom and handle paper stacks, but reliably separating and handing individual sheets to specific students, managing varied desk layouts, and confirming receipt requires dexterity, social perception, and generalization that current humanoids handle inconsistently.

task: Conduct demonstrations to teach skills, such as sports, dancing, and handicrafts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single demonstration session (e.g., one skill demo in a class or workshop). A competent instructor typically spends ~30 min demonstrating and explaining a skill segment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expressive, coordinated full-body motion, real-time adaptive instruction, social cue reading, and domain expertise across diverse skills. Current humanoids lack pedagogical communication, nuanced dexterity for handicrafts, and graceful dance/sports motion at instructional quality.

task: Open switches or attach grounding devices to remove electrical hazards from disturbed or fallen lines or to facilitate repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = isolating a single hazardous line section: locating the switch/grounding point, donning PPE, operating the switch or attaching grounds. Typically 15–35 min for a trained lineworker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating high-voltage utility switches and attaching grounding clamps on live/fallen lines outdoors in unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack the specialized insulated tooling, electrical safety protocols, outdoor terrain capability, and precision dexterity needed. Extremely hazardous; no deployment exists.

task: Assemble and fasten tracks to structures or bucks, using impact wrenches or welding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and fastening one track section to a structure or buck using impact wrench or welding; a skilled worker aligns, positions, and secures in ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of heavy track components, sustained torque with impact wrenches, and/or welding — high-force tool use, spatial reasoning in unstructured industrial settings. Welding especially far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Replace or straighten damaged poles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing or straightening one damaged utility/fence pole. Involves assessment, equipment setup, extraction, and reinstallation; typically 1–3 hours for a crew.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy machinery (diggers, augers, cranes), working outdoors on uneven terrain, handling large structural loads, and precise alignment — all well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Clean classrooms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one classroom: sweeping/mopping floors, wiping desks/boards, emptying trash. A competent custodian typically completes this in ~20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep/mop flat floors and wipe surfaces reasonably well, but classrooms have chairs, desks, and obstacles requiring navigation, repositioning furniture, and erasing whiteboards — challenging for current humanoids in unstructured layouts.

task: Install watt-hour meters and connect service drops between power lines and consumers' facilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one meter and connecting one service drop at a residential or commercial property. A competent electrician/lineman typically takes 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working at height on utility poles/overhead lines, fine electrical wiring, weatherhead connections, and live-line safety protocols. No current humanoid robot can safely operate in this unstructured outdoor, elevated, high-voltage environment.

task: Carry springs to tops of doors, using ladders or scaffolding, and attach springs to tracks to install spring systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a spring system on one door. Carrying springs up a ladder and attaching to tracks takes a skilled technician roughly 20–30 minutes per door.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing a ladder or scaffolding with heavy springs, precise overhead fastening under tension, and tool use in a constrained elevated workspace — all well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured job-site environments.

task: Set up equipment that automatically detects area radiation deviations and test detection equipment to ensure its accuracy.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full setup + functional test of area radiation monitoring equipment in one location; a qualified radiation worker typically takes 45–90 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized radiation instruments, precise calibration with reference sources, interpreting dosimetry readings, and regulatory compliance judgment. Current humanoids lack the specialized tool interfacing, calibration protocols, and domain-specific sensor integration needed.

task: Organize and label materials and display students' work in a manner appropriate for their eye levels and perceptual skills.
human_time_rationale: One instance = organizing/labeling materials and mounting a typical set of student work displays in a classroom. A competent teacher takes ~20–30 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced pedagogical judgment (age-appropriate eye level, perceptual skills), reading/labeling, fine manipulation (pins, tape, paper), and adaptive placement decisions in cluttered classroom environments — far beyond current robot generalization.

task: Inspect and test power lines and auxiliary equipment to locate and identify problems, using reading and testing instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single segment/span of power line and its auxiliary equipment. A competent lineworker typically takes ~45–90 minutes per segment including setup, testing, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing poles/towers or aerial access, operating specialized high-voltage test instruments, navigating unstructured outdoor terrain, and interpreting complex electrical readings. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured utility environments.

task: Inspect job sites, assessing headroom, side room, or other conditions to determine appropriateness of door for a given location.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing one door location. Worker measures headroom, side room, checks surrounding conditions, and documents findings — typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating an active construction site (uneven terrain, debris, scaffolding), taking precise spatial measurements, interpreting contextual building conditions, and making a professional judgment call — well beyond current robot generalization and site-mobility limits.

task: String wire conductors and cables between poles, towers, trenches, pylons, and buildings, setting lines in place and using winches to adjust tension.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stringing a single conductor/cable run between two adjacent poles or structures. A crew of lineworkers typically takes 1–3 hours per span including setup, tensioning, and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing poles/towers, operating winches under high tension, working at height in outdoor terrain, and handling heavy cables. Far beyond current humanoid locomotion, dexterity, and payload capabilities. No deployed system approaches this.

task: Operate and maintain audio-visual equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up, operating, and doing basic maintenance on AV equipment for a single event/session (e.g., connecting cables, configuring a projector/mixer, adjusting levels, packing down). Typical event setup+teardown.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous cable management, interpreting varied AV interfaces and menus, diagnosing signal/audio issues, and adapting to non-standard configurations. Far beyond current robot generalization and fine manipulation capability.

task: Attach cross-arms, insulators, and auxiliary equipment to poles prior to installing them.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fully prepping one utility pole with cross-arms, insulators, and auxiliary equipment before erection. A skilled lineworker typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torquing of bolts, handling heavy irregular hardware (cross-arms, insulators), routing hardware on a large pole, and using specialized linework tools. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and strength-control at this complexity level.

task: Prepare lesson materials, bulletin board displays, exhibits, equipment, and demonstrations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a full set of materials for a single lesson (handouts, board display, equipment setup, demo). A competent teacher typically spends ~60 min on this.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires creative/pedagogical judgment, reading/printing documents, arranging bulletin boards with pins, setting up varied equipment, and staging demonstrations — far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and cognitive planning.

task: Cut and peel lead sheathing and insulation from defective or newly installed cables and conduits prior to splicing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one cable end (cutting/peeling sheathing and insulation). A competent electrician/cable splicer typically takes 10–20 min per cable end depending on cable diameter and layers.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled cutting through lead sheathing without nicking conductors, fine dexterity with specialized tools, and adaptive perception of varied cable geometries. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Lubricate door closer oil chambers, and pack spindles with leather washers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one door closer unit. A skilled maintenance worker removes the closer, lubricates the oil chamber, and packs the spindle with leather washers in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise disassembly of a compact hydraulic closer, controlled oil injection into a sealed chamber, and packing leather washers around a spindle — all highly dexterous, tool-dependent, and unstructured. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Instruct and monitor students in the use and care of equipment and materials to prevent injuries and damage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single instructional session covering equipment/material safety for a group of students; typically 15–25 minutes including demonstration and monitoring.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coherent verbal instruction, adaptive social interaction, real-time behavior monitoring of multiple students, and nuanced judgment about safety risks. Far beyond current humanoid general-purpose robot capabilities in unstructured social/educational settings.

task: Perform administrative duties, such as school library assistance, hall and cafeteria monitoring, and bus loading and unloading.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one duty shift (e.g., a cafeteria monitoring period, a bus loading/unloading session, or a library assistance block), typically 30–60 min for a school paraprofessional.
robot_success_rationale: Requires social interaction, child safety judgment, dynamic crowd navigation, verbal communication, and context-sensitive decision-making. Current humanoids lack reliable social perception, speech understanding, and authority to manage children safely in unstructured environments.

task: Set up, adjust, and operate audio-visual equipment, such as cameras, film and slide projectors, and recording equipment, for meetings, events, classes, seminars, and video conferences.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and operating AV equipment for a single event/meeting. Includes unpacking, cabling, positioning, testing, adjusting, and running equipment during the event; ~30–60 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires routing cables, connecting varied ports, adjusting projectors/cameras, operating software interfaces, and adapting to live event feedback. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tool-generalization, and situational judgment for end-to-end autonomous AV operation.

task: Assist in bus loading and unloading.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting passengers on/off one bus stop cycle (boarding + alighting). A competent transit worker guides passengers, handles luggage, and manages flow in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic crowd navigation, social interaction, varied luggage handling, ramp/step deployment, and real-time passenger assistance. Current humanoids lack robust crowd-aware social navigation, reliable communication, and safe human-contact caregiving in unstructured public environments.

task: Measure geological characteristics used in prospecting for oil or gas, using measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full measurement session at one site/borehole; includes setup, instrument use, and logging. A competent field geologist takes ~1–2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires traversing rugged outdoor terrain, handling specialized scientific instruments (magnetometers, seismographs, gravimeters), interpreting readings, and adapting to unstructured field conditions. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities in dexterity, terrain mobility, and domain-specific tool use.

task: Dig holes, using augers, and set poles, using cranes and power equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = digging one hole with an auger and setting one pole with a crane/power equipment. A competent crew member takes ~30–60 min per pole including setup, drilling, and setting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy vehicle-mounted augers and cranes in outdoor terrain. Humanoid robots cannot drive or operate such machinery autonomously; unstructured outdoor conditions, heavy loads, and specialized equipment controls make this infeasible in 2026.

task: Disassemble equipment to gain access to defect, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling one piece of equipment to expose a defect. A competent technician typically spends 15–35 min removing panels, fasteners, and components to reach the fault.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct fasteners, applying calibrated torque with hand tools, navigating varied equipment geometry, and locating a defect — beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and tool-use reliability in unstructured settings.

task: Prepare lesson materials, bulletin board displays, exhibits, equipment, and demonstrations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a full set of materials/displays/demos for a single lesson. A competent teacher typically spends ~60 min on combined prep work per lesson.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/understanding curriculum content, creative layout design, fine dexterous crafting (pinning, cutting, arranging), tool use, and contextual judgment. Far beyond current robot generalization and cognitive capabilities.

task: Provide students with disabilities with assistive devices, supportive technology, and assistance accessing facilities, such as restrooms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single interaction providing a device, tech setup, or facility access assist to one student. Typical interaction spans a few minutes to ~15 min depending on need.
robot_success_rationale: Requires social/contextual judgment, adaptive communication, personalized physical assistance (e.g., wheelchair maneuvering, restroom help), and nuanced caregiving. Current humanoids lack safe unstructured caregiving capability and reliable human interaction needed here.

task: Pull up cable by hand from large reels mounted on trucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pulling cable from one reel to span a typical run (e.g. ~100–200 ft). A crew member hand-pulling heavy cable from a truck-mounted reel takes ~30–60 min depending on cable gauge and terrain.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained high-force pulling of heavy, stiff cable across outdoor/uneven terrain, coordinating with a truck-mounted reel. Current humanoids lack the grip strength, force endurance, outdoor mobility, and multi-step coordination needed for this industrial task.

task: Splice or solder cables together or to overhead transmission lines, customer service lines, or street light lines, using hand tools, epoxies, or specialized equipment.
human_time_rationale: One splicing/soldering instance (one cable joint) for a competent lineworker, including prep, joining, insulating, and securing — typically 30–60 min depending on cable type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (soldering, epoxy application), working at height on poles/aerial lines, operating specialized tools, and handling live electrical infrastructure — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured outdoor environments.

task: Prepare classrooms with a variety of materials or resources for children to explore, manipulate, or use in learning activities or imaginative play.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up one classroom before a session. A teacher arranges materials, stations, and supplies across the room, typically taking 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a large, cluttered space; handling highly varied, irregular objects (art supplies, toys, books, manipulatives); creative judgment about layout and age-appropriateness; and multi-step sequencing across many stations. Far beyond current robot generalization and cognitive planning ability.

task: Travel in trucks, helicopters, and airplanes to inspect lines for freedom from obstruction and adequacy of insulation.
human_time_rationale: One inspection shift/route covering a segment of utility lines via truck, helicopter, or airplane. A typical patrol covers tens of miles and takes ~4 hours including travel and observation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating trucks, helicopters, or aircraft — all beyond current humanoid robot capability. Even if paired with a drone, autonomous navigation over unstructured terrain and actionable insulation/obstruction assessment at frontier quality is not reliably achieved by a general-purpose humanoid in 2026.

task: Distribute teaching materials, such as textbooks, workbooks, papers, and pencils, to students.
human_time_rationale: One instance = distributing materials to one classroom (~25–30 students). A teacher or aide hands out items row by row in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Navigating a cluttered classroom, identifying each student, handing varied items (books, papers, pencils) reliably without dropping or missing anyone is beyond current humanoid generalization. Locomotion is feasible but precise multi-item delivery to dynamic humans is not yet robust.

task: Organize and label materials and display students' work in a manner appropriate for their eye levels and perceptual skills.
human_time_rationale: One instance = organizing/labeling materials and mounting a set of student work displays in a single classroom. A competent teacher aide takes ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting student work, making pedagogical judgments about appropriate eye-level placement, fine manipulation for labeling and hanging items, and social/contextual understanding of perceptual skills — well beyond current robot generalization.

task: Trim trees that could be hazardous to the functioning of cables or wires.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming hazardous branches from one tree near cables/wires. A competent arborist with tools takes ~60–120 min including setup, climbing/positioning, cutting, and debris clearing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing trees or operating aerial lifts, identifying hazardous branches near live wires, wielding chainsaws/pole saws at height in unstructured outdoor terrain — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in dexterity, mobility, and tool-use.

task: Cut cables and tubing, using master templates, measuring instruments, and cable cutters or saws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting a single cable or tube to length using a template/measurement. A competent worker measures, aligns, and cuts in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, template alignment, tool-switching (measuring instruments + cutters/saws), and controlled force application. Current humanoids struggle with multi-step dexterous tool use, accurate measurement reading, and handling flexible cables/tubing reliably.

task: Lay underground cable directly in trenches, or string it through conduit running through the trenches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying cable in a pre-dug trench for a typical residential/commercial run (~30–50 m). Includes unspooling cable, direct burial or threading conduit, and dressing the run.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained outdoor locomotion on uneven trench terrain, high-force cable handling, threading flexible cable through conduit, and precise routing — all far beyond current humanoid general-purpose capability. No commercial deployment exists.

task: Clean and lubricate cameras and polish camera lenses, using cleaning materials and work aids.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning, lubricating, and polishing a single camera. A competent technician typically spends ~15–25 minutes on a full clean-and-lube cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to handle delicate optical surfaces, apply precise micro-amounts of lubricant, and use specialized tools (lens pens, sensor swabs) without scratching. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Learn acting, dancing, and other skills required for dramatic singing roles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single rehearsal/training session combining acting, dance, and vocal coaching for a dramatic role; typical session runs ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires artistic interpretation, emotional expression, physical grace, memorization of choreography, and nuanced vocal performance — far beyond any current general-purpose robot's cognitive, expressive, or fine-motor capabilities.

task: Maintain computers in classrooms and laboratories, and assist students with hardware and software use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = resolving a single student/equipment issue (diagnosis + fix). Median covers simple resets to moderate hardware swaps; ~20 min is realistic.
robot_success_rationale: Requires natural language interaction with students, nuanced software diagnosis, plugging/unplugging peripherals in tight spaces, and contextual IT judgment — far beyond current humanoid generalization and HRI capability.

task: Assemble parts or units, and position, align, and fasten units to assemblies, subassemblies, or frames, using hand tools and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and fastening one unit/subassembly. A competent worker on a mixed-product line typically takes 30–60 min per unit depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle standardized pick-and-place well but struggle with precise alignment, varied fasteners, tool switching, and force-feedback fastening. Generalization across part types and positional tolerances remains a key limitation in 2026.

task: Maintain computers in classrooms and laboratories, and assist students with hardware and software use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one support or maintenance interaction (e.g., diagnosing one machine or helping one student). Median across routine checks, software help, and minor hardware fixes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced verbal communication, software troubleshooting via GUI, fine dexterous hardware manipulation (cables, cards), and social judgment. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and human-interaction capabilities.

task: Adjust cameras, photographic mechanisms, or equipment such as range and view finders, shutters, light meters, or lens systems, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting/calibrating one camera or component (e.g. rangefinder, shutter, lens). A skilled technician typically spends 10–20 min per adjustment session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterous manipulation of small, delicate optical/mechanical components with hand tools. Well beyond current humanoid dexterity; high precision, tactile feedback, and tool control needed.

task: Clean, tin, and splice corresponding conductors by twisting ends together or by joining ends with metal clamps and soldering connections.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning, tinning, and splicing a single conductor pair. A skilled electrician strips insulation, applies flux/solder or clamps, and completes the splice in ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity: stripping wire insulation precisely, applying solder with a iron, twisting fine conductors, or seating metal clamps — all sub-millimeter tasks. Current humanoids lack the finger precision and tool-use for reliable soldering work.

task: Clean and lubricate parts and subassemblies, using grease paddles or oilcans.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and lubricating a single part or small subassembly. A competent worker wipes down, applies grease/oil, and inspects in roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires controlled force and precision tool-use (grease paddle, oilcan) on varied part geometries. Perception of coverage quality and grease amount is hard; dexterous handling of application tools and avoiding contamination reduces success significantly.

task: Laminate teaching materials to increase their durability under repeated use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laminating one sheet. Feeding into laminator, waiting for pass-through, and trimming edges takes ~3 minutes for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise paper alignment, feeding a thin flexible sheet into a narrow laminator slot, and trimming with scissors or a cutter. Thin/flexible material handling and precise slot insertion are at the edge of current humanoid dexterity limits. High failure risk from misalignment or jamming.

task: Cut trenches for laying underground cables, using trenchers and cable plows.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting a typical residential/utility trench run (~50–100 m). A competent operator sets up, operates a trencher or cable plow, and completes the cut in ~60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large wheeled/tracked trenching machinery on uneven outdoor terrain. Humanoid robots cannot drive or control such equipment autonomously; task demands vehicle operation, site judgment, and safety oversight beyond current robot capabilities.

task: Distribute teaching materials, such as textbooks, workbooks, papers, and pencils, to students.
human_time_rationale: One instance = distributing materials to one classroom (~25–30 students). A teacher or aide can hand out a set of items (e.g., workbooks + pencils) in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating classroom aisles, grasping varied objects (pencils, papers, books), and delivering to specific seated individuals. Cluttered, human-filled environments and dexterous handling of thin papers/pencils push success low for current humanoids.

task: Obtain windshields or windows for specific automobile makes and models from stock and examine them for defects prior to installation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retrieving one windshield/window from stock and inspecting it before installation. A skilled auto glass technician takes ~5 min to locate/retrieve and ~5–7 min to visually inspect for chips, cracks, and distortions.
robot_success_rationale: Retrieving large, heavy, fragile glass panels from varied rack storage requires precise bimanual dexterity and safe handling. Visual defect inspection demands fine optical perception. Current humanoids lack the delicate grasp force control and reliable defect-detection vision for glass panels at acceptable quality.

task: Use gestures to shape the music being played, communicating desired tempo, phrasing, tone, color, pitch, volume, and other performance aspects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = conducting one full piece/movement (~45 min avg). A competent conductor uses continuous gestural communication throughout the performance.
robot_success_rationale: Conducting requires nuanced, expressive, real-time gestural communication interpreted by human musicians. Current robots lack musical expressivity, interpretive intent, adaptive feedback perception, and fine motor artistry needed for any acceptable quality.

task: Inspect materials, products, or equipment to detect defects or malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single item/unit (product, part, or equipment). A competent worker visually and physically checks for defects in ~10–20 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can perform visual inspection via cameras and basic tactile checks, but detecting subtle defects (hairline cracks, unusual sounds, irregular textures) across varied, unstructured items strains current perception, generalization, and dexterous manipulation capabilities.

task: Place insulating or fireproofing materials over conductors and joints.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying insulating/fireproofing material to a single conductor run or joint. A skilled electrician typically takes 5–10 minutes per joint/section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise wrapping, taping, or spraying on irregular conductor shapes in confined spaces (panels, conduit). Fine dexterous manipulation of tape/coatings around small joints exceeds current humanoid robot capability reliably.

task: Measure and examine extruded products to locate defects and to check for conformance to specifications, adjusting controls as necessary to alter products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a sample from one production run/batch. A skilled operator measures dimensions, checks surface quality, and adjusts controls in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is feasible, but autonomously adjusting extrusion machine controls requires machine-specific tool-use, process knowledge, and closed-loop decision-making beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities. Integration with industrial controls is also a barrier.

task: Position, align, and adjust parts for proper fit and assembly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning and aligning one set of parts for a single assembly step. A competent worker typically spends 5–10 minutes fitting, checking tolerances, and adjusting.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle structured pick-and-place well, but precise alignment to tight tolerances across varied part geometries demands fine dexterity, force-feedback, and adaptive perception that current humanoids manage only partially.

task: Laminate teaching materials to increase their durability under repeated use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one document/sheet. Inserting into pouch, feeding through laminator, trimming edges takes ~3 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise flat-surface alignment of thin paper into a laminating pouch, operating a specific appliance, and trimming. Fine paper handling and thin-sheet dexterity remain challenging; machine operation and sequencing add further difficulty for current humanoids.

task: Measure parts to determine tolerances, using precision measuring instruments such as micrometers, calipers, and verniers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single part at multiple critical dimensions with precision instruments. A skilled machinist/inspector typically takes 5–12 minutes per part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter dexterous grip of delicate gauges, precise seating of micrometers/calipers on part features, and reading analog verniers. Current humanoids lack the fine tactile feedback and stable grip precision for reliable metrology-grade measurement.

task: Provide assistance in the production of wiring assemblies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a single wiring harness/assembly (routing, connecting, securing wires). Competent technician typically takes 15–25 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Wiring assembly demands fine dexterity (routing thin wires, seating connectors, applying clips/ties), precise bimanual coordination, and flexible object handling — well beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured settings.

task: Lay out reference points and dimensions on parts or metal stock to be machined, using precision measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out reference points and dimensions on a single workpiece. A skilled machinist typically takes 10–30 min depending on part complexity, averaging ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to use scribers, calipers, height gauges, and squares with sub-millimeter precision on irregular metal stock. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control, force sensitivity, and tool-handling accuracy needed for acceptable layout work.

task: Examine cameras, equipment, processed film, or laboratory reports to diagnose malfunction, using work aids and specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing a single piece of equipment or reviewing one lab report. A competent technician typically spends ~20–30 min examining, cross-referencing specs, and forming a diagnosis.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert visual interpretation of film defects, nuanced mechanical inspection, reading reports, and cross-referencing specs — far beyond current robot perception, reasoning, and tool-use generalization. No frontier humanoid handles this workflow autonomously.

task: Inspect, test, and adjust completed units to ensure that units meet specifications, tolerances, and customer order requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting, testing, and adjusting one completed unit. A competent QC worker typically takes 15–30 minutes depending on unit complexity and spec sheet review.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading spec sheets, using precision measurement tools, interpreting pass/fail criteria, and making fine adjustments. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity, instrument-reading accuracy, and adaptive decision-making for multi-step QC workflows.

task: File, lap, and buff parts to fit, using hand and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filing, lapping, and buffing a single part to fit. A skilled machinist/metalworker typically spends ~15–25 minutes per part depending on material and tolerance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-feedback control, fine dexterity with multiple hand/power tools, and iterative tactile judgment to meet tight tolerances. Current humanoids lack the fine haptic sensing and tool-switching fluency needed.

task: Test conductors, according to electrical diagrams and specifications, to identify corresponding conductors and to prevent incorrect connections.
human_time_rationale: One instance = verifying and labeling conductors in one wiring harness/panel section; a skilled electrician cross-references diagrams, probes, and marks wires in ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading schematics, fine probe manipulation on densely packed terminals, cross-referencing results cognitively, and making judgment calls. Current robots lack reliable diagram interpretation, fine-tip probing dexterity, and multi-step electrical reasoning autonomously.

task: Measure parts to verify specified dimensions or settings, such as camera shutter speed or light meter reading accuracy, using measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring one part/device setting (e.g., shutter speed). Technician sets up instrument, takes readings, and records results in ~10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of delicate instruments and small parts, precise tool positioning, and interpreting analog/digital readouts. Current humanoids lack the finger precision and specialized tool-use for calibration-grade inspection tasks.

task: Disassemble units to replace parts or to crate them for shipping.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling one unit (e.g. a machine or equipment piece). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min depending on complexity; 45 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Disassembly requires identifying fasteners, applying torque, handling varied parts, and sequencing steps correctly. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity for unfamiliar fastener types, tool switching, and adaptive reasoning needed for diverse units.

task: Drill, tap, ream, countersink, and spot-face bolt holes in parts, using drill presses and portable power drills.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling/tapping/reaming/countersinking/spot-facing a set of bolt holes on one part. A competent machinist takes ~15–25 min depending on hole count and material.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool changes (drill, tap, reamer, countersink, spot-face), secure part fixturing, fine force/torque control, and operating a drill press or handheld power drill safely. Current humanoids lack the precision, tool-change dexterity, and machining process control needed.

task: Encourage guests to dance using group dances, competitions, or other party games.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one organized activity session (e.g., a group dance round or party game), typically lasting ~20–40 minutes at an event.
robot_success_rationale: Requires charisma, social reading, vocal engagement, adaptive crowd interaction, and improvisation. Current robots lack social intelligence, expressive communication, and the ability to energize and lead human groups spontaneously.

task: Produce recordings of music.
human_time_rationale: One instance = recording one song/track from setup through final take. Includes instrument setup, mic placement, takes, and basic review. Typical session: ~4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires playing instruments with musical expression, operating complex audio equipment, creative judgment on tone and timing, and iterative artistic decision-making — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, nuanced perception, and generalization capabilities.

task: Install new foam dams on pinchwelds, if required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing foam dams on all pinchwelds of one vehicle. A competent auto body/assembly technician takes ~10–20 min to inspect, cut, and press foam dams onto pinchweld flanges.
robot_success_rationale: Requires crawling under/around a vehicle, identifying pinchweld locations, assessing need, cutting foam to size, and pressing precisely onto irregular flanges. Poor generalization, confined-space mobility, and fine force-control make this extremely difficult for current humanoids.

task: Assemble aircraft cameras, still or motion picture cameras, photographic equipment, or frames, using diagrams, blueprints, bench machines, hand tools, or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a single camera unit or frame. Skilled optical/mechanical assemblers typically take 60–120 min per unit depending on complexity; 90 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to handle tiny optical components, lenses, and precision fasteners per blueprints. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter precision, tool-switching fluency, and blueprint-reading integration needed for optical instrument assembly.

task: Assemble audio and video equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a typical AV setup (e.g., connecting a TV, speakers, receiver, cables). A competent technician takes ~45 min for a standard home/office system.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle small connectors, cable routing, port identification, and sequenced configuration. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation for varied cable types, ports, and device layouts in unstructured environments.

task: Specialize in playing a specific family of instruments or a particular type of music.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one dedicated practice/performance session (~8 hrs/day) of specialization work; ongoing mastery is measured in years, but a daily unit is appropriate.
robot_success_rationale: Musical specialization demands extreme fine motor dexterity, nuanced auditory feedback, expressive interpretation, and years of adaptive learning. Current humanoids lack the precision, tonal sensitivity, and artistic generalization required.

task: Mix, cut, or sample recorded music using DJ controllers, CDJs, or DJ mixers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a typical 60-minute DJ set. A competent DJ mixes continuously, managing transitions, EQ, and effects throughout a standard set.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly precise fine-motor control of jog wheels, faders, knobs, and buttons simultaneously; real-time auditory feedback interpretation; creative musical judgment; and tempo-matching — all far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Operate small cranes to transport or position large parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rigging, lifting, transporting, and positioning one large part using a small crane. Includes setup, signaling, and placement confirmation.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate crane controls in real traffic/industrial settings. Requires specialized vehicle operation, load rigging, spatial judgment under load swing, and safety-critical coordination — all beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capability.

task: Play musical instruments as soloists, or as members or guest artists of musical groups such as orchestras, ensembles, or bands.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one typical performance set or concert (e.g., 45–90 min). ~60 min is a reasonable median for a solo recital or ensemble concert set.
robot_success_rationale: Playing an instrument at professional performance quality requires extreme fine motor dexterity, nuanced force/timing control, real-time auditory feedback, and expressive musicality — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities. No 2026 general-purpose robot approaches this.

task: Obtain information about diving tasks and environmental conditions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single dive session including descent, work at depth, and ascent. A competent diver typically completes this in ~60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Current humanoid robots are not waterproof, cannot operate underwater, lack buoyancy control, and have no propulsion for aquatic environments. Specialized underwater ROVs exist but are not general-purpose humanoids.

task: Operate or tend automated assembling equipment, such as robotics and fixed automation equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one monitoring/intervention shift cycle (~30 min), covering loading materials, watching equipment, clearing jams, and logging status on an automated line.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting HMI panels, diagnosing faults, clearing jams, and making judgment calls on varied equipment. Current humanoids lack reliable UI interaction, fault diagnosis, and safe intervention around industrial machinery.

task: Set up and adjust machines and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and adjusting a single machine/piece of equipment; a competent technician typically spends 30–60 min per machine including inspection, calibration, and test runs.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying machine-specific controls, reading gauges, making precise adjustments, and verifying output quality — tasks demanding high generalization, fine dexterity, and domain knowledge far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Fabricate or modify defective electronic, electrical, or mechanical components, using bench lathe, milling machine, shaper, grinder, or precision hand tools, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating or modifying a single component. A skilled machinist/technician typically spends 30–150 min depending on complexity; 90 min is a reasonable median for precision bench/machine work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating precision machine tools (lathe, mill, shaper, grinder) or fine hand tools to tight tolerances. Current humanoid robots cannot safely operate or set up such machinery, lack the fine dexterity and force control for precision machining, and cannot interpret blueprints/specs to adjust cuts autonomously.

task: Install dies, machine screws, and sizing rings on machines that extrude thermoplastic or metal materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a die set (die, machine screws, sizing ring) on one extrusion machine. A skilled operator typically takes 20–40 min including alignment, torquing, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torquing of machine screws to spec, fine alignment of dies and sizing rings under tight tolerances, and force-feedback judgment on heavy industrial machinery. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-use precision, and torque calibration needed for this specialized industrial task.

task: Calibrate and verify accuracy of light meters, shutter diaphragm operation, or lens carriers, using timing instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = calibrating/verifying one device (light meter, shutter, or lens carrier). A competent technician spends ~20–30 min setting up, testing, adjusting, and confirming accuracy with timing instruments.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of precision optical/mechanical components, interpretation of specialized timing instrument readings, and expert diagnostic judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity and tool-use generalization.

task: Assess safety of wiring or equipment set-up to determine the risk of fire or electrical shock.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single piece of equipment or a discrete wiring setup. A competent electrician typically takes 20–40 min to visually inspect, test, and assess fire/shock risk.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert knowledge of electrical codes, multi-modal sensing (thermal, voltage, visual anomaly detection), tool use (multimeter, thermal camera), and nuanced judgment about risk. Current humanoids lack reliable electrical inspection reasoning and safe tool integration.

task: Attach name plates and mark identifying information on parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching a name plate and marking ID info on a single part. A competent worker locates, aligns, fastens/adheres the plate, and marks text in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment and fastening of small plates, fine dexterous marking/engraving, and reading/writing text. Current humanoids struggle with small-part dexterity, tool use (markers, stamps, screwdrivers), and accurate text placement on varied surfaces.

task: Change dies on extruding machines, according to production line changes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = changing a die set on one extruding machine. Includes cooling, loosening bolts, removing old die, installing/aligning new die, and verifying setup — typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high torque tool use (breaker bars, torque wrenches), heavy die handling (often 10–100+ kg), precise alignment under heat/pressure, and safety-critical verification. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Inspect, operate, and test completed products to verify functioning, machine capabilities, or conformance to customer specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting, operating, and testing one completed product unit. A competent worker typically spends ~20 min per unit cycling through functional checks, measurements, and spec verification.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle basic pick-and-inspect tasks but struggle with operating diverse product controls, interpreting multi-modal test results, adapting to varied specs, and making judgment calls on conformance without structured fixtures or pre-programmed routines.

task: Operate visual effects equipment, such as lights, fog machines, or lasers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = operating VFX equipment during a single show/event session, including setup, cueing, and running effects. A competent technician typically takes ~45 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex, event-driven decision-making, reading cues from performers/directors, operating specialized control boards/software, and real-time improvisation. Current humanoids lack the cognitive and interface-integration capability for this unstructured, judgment-heavy operation.

task: Adjust controls to draw or press metal into specified shapes and diameters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and adjusting controls for a single drawing/pressing run on a machine. A skilled operator takes ~5–10 min to dial in settings per job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial metalworking machinery (draw benches, presses) with precise control adjustments, reading gauges, and verifying tolerances. Humanoids lack the tool interfacing, domain-specific controls manipulation, and precision feedback needed.

task: Determine setup procedures and select machine dies and parts, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a machine for a single production run: reviewing specs, selecting correct dies/parts, and mounting them. Typically 20–45 minutes for a competent machinist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting technical specs, identifying correct dies from varied inventory, and precise mechanical installation. Current robots lack reliable document comprehension, part identification from specs, and the fine-force-controlled die-seating dexterity needed for autonomous success.

task: Assemble systems of gears by aligning and meshing gears in gearboxes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one gearbox system; a competent technician typically takes 30–60 min to align shafts, mesh gears correctly, and verify engagement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise shaft alignment, tight-tolerance gear meshing, and tactile feedback to confirm correct tooth engagement. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force-torque sensing, and spatial precision needed for reliable gearbox assembly.

task: Set up and operate conventional or computer numerically controlled machine tools such as lathes, milling machines, or grinders to cut, bore, grind, or otherwise shape parts to prescribed dimensions and finishes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running one part on a machine tool. A skilled machinist typically spends 30–60 min on setup, tooling, and machining a single part to spec.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating complex industrial machinery (lathes, mills, grinders), precise tool changes, reading blueprints, adjusting feeds/speeds, and measuring tolerances — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and domain generalization capabilities.

task: Operate disc jockey controller and other equipment, such as microphones.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a typical DJ set/event (e.g., 3-hour club or event performance), the standard unit a booker or manager would measure.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time auditory perception, nuanced crowd-reading, fine dexterous control of faders/knobs/platters, microphone use, and creative improvisation — far beyond 2026 humanoid robot capabilities in an unstructured, socially dynamic environment.

task: Position or align components for assembly, manually or using hoists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning/aligning a single component for assembly. A competent worker typically spends 5–10 minutes handling, orienting, and securing one part, including hoist setup if needed.
robot_success_rationale: Precise alignment requires fine perception and dexterity; hoist operation adds tool-use complexity. Robots handle simple pick-and-place but struggle with tight-tolerance alignment, irregular parts, and hoist rigging in unstructured settings.

task: Take pictures or video, and process them for inclusion in a story.
human_time_rationale: One instance = capturing and processing photos/video for a single story. Includes shooting, reviewing, selecting, and basic editing/formatting. Typical journalist/reporter workflow.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative judgment for framing/composition, aesthetic decision-making, story context understanding, and software-based post-processing. Humanoids lack camera operation dexterity, creative vision, and editing software integration at acceptable quality.

task: Remove rough spots and smooth surfaces to fit, trim, or clean parts, using hand tools or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = deburring/smoothing a single part. A skilled worker spends ~10–20 min filing, sanding, or grinding a typical machined or fabricated component to acceptable finish.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled contact, tactile feedback, and tool-switching (files, grinders, sandpaper) on irregular geometry. Current humanoids lack reliable compliant tool-use and surface-quality perception for consistent, supervisor-acceptable results.

task: Set up and operate metalworking machines, such as milling or grinding machines, to shape or fabricate parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running a milling/grinding machine to produce one batch of parts; typical setup + machining cycle for a competent machinist is ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool-change dexterity, reading blueprints, adjusting feeds/speeds, quality inspection, and operating specialized CNC/manual controls — far beyond current humanoid generalization and fine manipulation capability.

task: Troubleshoot, maintain, and make minor repairs to equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and performing a minor repair on one piece of equipment; a competent technician typically takes ~45 min including inspection, diagnosis, and fix.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step diagnosis, tool use, fine dexterity, and contextual judgment across diverse equipment types. Current humanoids lack reliable fault diagnosis, tool-switching, and precision repair capability in unstructured settings.

task: Fasten or install piping, fixtures, or wiring and electrical components to form assemblies or subassemblies, using hand tools, rivet guns, or welding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one subassembly unit (e.g., a piping run or wiring harness). A skilled worker takes ~30–60 min depending on component count and tool switching.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-tool switching (hand tools, rivet guns, welding), fine dexterity for threading/fastening, and spatial reasoning in tight spaces. Current humanoids lack reliable welding, riveting, and complex wiring dexterity at acceptable quality.

task: Connect cables, tubes, and wiring, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one connection job per spec (e.g., wiring harness or tube bundle). A skilled technician takes ~15–25 min depending on connector count and routing complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-dexterous manipulation (seating connectors, routing flexible cables/tubes, confirming clicks/seals), reading specs, and adapting to varied connector types. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-fingered insertion force control for this.

task: Maintain and lubricate parts or components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lubricating/maintaining a single component or small set (e.g., greasing a bearing, oiling a hinge). Competent worker completes in ~20 min including inspection and cleanup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct lubrication points, applying precise amounts with a tool (grease gun, oiler), and navigating varied equipment geometries. Current robots lack reliable fine tool-use, situational identification, and force-controlled application on unstructured machinery.

task: Match light fixture settings, such as brightness and color, to lighting design plans.
human_time_rationale: One instance = configuring one light fixture to match design specs. A competent electrician/lighting tech reads the plan and adjusts settings in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting design documents, identifying fixture controls (often small dials, apps, or proprietary panels), and making precise adjustments. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use for fine controls and cross-referencing plans.

task: Reel extruded products into rolls of specified lengths and weights.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reeling one roll to specified length/weight. A competent operator monitors feed, cuts, and labels/removes the finished roll, typically taking ~5–10 minutes per roll.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinating with moving machinery, guiding flexible/continuous extrudate, monitoring tension, cutting at precise spec, and handling the finished roll — challenging perception, dexterity, and machine interfacing for current humanoids.

task: Load machine hoppers with mixed materials, using augers, or stuff rolls of plastic dough into machine cylinders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a single hopper or stuffing one roll of dough into a cylinder; a competent worker completes this in ~5 minutes including positioning and verifying fill.
robot_success_rationale: Requires lifting/pouring mixed bulk materials or handling soft, deformable dough rolls with precise force control; unstructured industrial environments, variable container shapes, and deformable material manipulation remain significant challenges for 2026 humanoids.

task: Set and verify parts clearances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking and adjusting clearances on one assembly or component set (e.g., valve lash, gear mesh). A competent technician typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise feeler gauge or dial indicator manipulation, fine force feedback, and interpretation of spec tolerances (often sub-millimeter). Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, calibrated tactile sensing, and tool-use precision needed.

task: Rework, repair, or replace damaged parts or assemblies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing or replacing one damaged part/assembly. Median covers simple swaps (~10 min) to moderate rework (~90 min); ~45 min is a reasonable midpoint for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Rework/repair requires diagnosing damage, selecting correct replacement, applying variable force and fine dexterity (fasteners, adhesives, welding). Unstructured, non-repetitive nature far exceeds current humanoid generalization and precision limits.

task: Select nozzles, spacers, and wire guides, according to diameters and lengths of rods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting the correct nozzle, spacer, and wire guide set for one rod specification; a skilled worker cross-references specs and picks parts in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot must read/interpret rod specs, identify and distinguish small similar-looking parts by size, and pick correct items. Perception of small labeled components and generalization to varied part bins is still unreliable for current humanoids.

task: Clean work areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single typical work area (desk, workbench, or small office zone). Includes wiping surfaces, tidying, and disposing of waste; ~15–25 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep, wipe flat surfaces, and handle simple pick-and-place of trash, but cluttered/varied work areas require object recognition, safe manipulation around tools/equipment, and judgment about what to move vs. leave — still unreliable for general work areas.

task: Photograph or videotape news events.
human_time_rationale: One instance = covering a single news event (e.g., press conference, protest, accident scene). Includes travel to location, setup, active shooting, and wrap-up; median ~90 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating unpredictable outdoor/crowd environments, creative framing judgment, anticipating action, and operating camera equipment with editorial intent. No current humanoid robot can perform autonomous photojournalism at acceptable quality.

task: Replace worn dies when products vary from specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a worn die and installing a replacement die in a machine. Includes inspection, changeover, and verification check. A skilled machinist/operator typically takes 15–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Die replacement requires precise force-controlled fastener removal/installation, heavy component handling, and quality judgment to detect spec variation. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, torque control, and contextual inspection capability needed reliably.

task: Operate manual or automated systems to control lighting throughout productions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = operating lighting for a single production (show/event). A competent lighting operator typically spends ~2 hours actively controlling cues, adjusting levels, and responding to live performance needs.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized lighting consoles, reading cue sheets, interpreting live performance context, and making real-time creative/reactive decisions. Current humanoid robots lack the software integration, console dexterity, and situational judgment for live production control.

task: Install color effects or image patterns, such as color filters, onto lighting fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single gel/filter or gobo onto one lighting fixture. A competent stagehand or electrician takes ~3–7 minutes per fixture.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise manipulation of thin, flexible gel sheets or small gobo frames into tight fixture slots, often overhead or in cramped positions. Perception of correct orientation, fragile material handling, and awkward poses challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Install electrical cables or wire fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing/wiring a single fixture or cable run (e.g., outlet, light fixture, or short cable segment). A competent electrician typically takes 30–60 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation (stripping wires, connecting terminals, torquing screws), routing cables through conduit/walls, safety-critical precision, and tool use in cramped, unstructured environments — well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Inject contrast media into patients' blood vessels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient injection. Includes site prep, IV/catheter placement or access, injecting contrast, and post-injection check. ~10 min for a competent technologist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter venipuncture precision, sterile technique, real-time tactile/visual feedback, and patient safety monitoring. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and medical-grade reliability standards.

task: Mix catalysts into resins, and saturate cloth and mats with mixtures, using brushes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing a batch of catalyzed resin and saturating one cloth/mat layer; a competent laminator completes this in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid mixing ratios, controlled brush pressure to uniformly saturate fibrous mats, and chemical handling. Current robots lack reliable fine fluid manipulation, tactile feedback for saturation quality, and safe resin/catalyst handling in unstructured settings.

task: Verify conformance of parts to stock lists or blueprints, using measuring instruments such as calipers, gauges, or micrometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single part against a blueprint/stock list. A competent inspector measures key dimensions with calipers/micrometers and records/verifies results in ~10–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (calipers, micrometers), precise placement on irregular parts, reading analog/digital instruments, cross-referencing blueprints, and making pass/fail judgments. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity and reliable metrology tool handling needed.

task: Conduct sound checks to ensure equipment is working and appropriate for the venue.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full sound check for one venue/event setup. A competent audio tech spends 30–60 min testing mics, speakers, monitors, mixing board levels, and signal chain.
robot_success_rationale: Requires audio perception (hearing/analyzing sound quality), operating mixing consoles, repositioning equipment, and expert judgment on acoustic suitability. No current humanoid has audio engineering perception or mixing board proficiency at an acceptable standard.

task: Disassemble and store equipment after performances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = breaking down and storing all equipment after a single performance (e.g., stage/AV gear). A competent crew member typically takes ~60 minutes for a standard show strike.
robot_success_rationale: Highly unstructured environment with varied, heavy, and irregularly shaped items (cables, stands, speakers, lighting rigs). Requires complex sequencing, tool use, coiling cables, and navigating a cluttered stage — well beyond current robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Administer emergency cardiac care for life-threatening heart problems, such as cardiac arrest and heart attack.
human_time_rationale: One instance = responding to a single cardiac emergency (arrest/attack) from recognition through initial stabilization/handoff to advanced care; typically 10–20 minutes of active intervention.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise chest compressions, airway management, AED use, drug administration, and real-time clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack medical tool dexterity, force control for safe CPR, and cannot make autonomous diagnostic/treatment decisions safely.

task: Test physical properties of products with testing devices such as acid-bath testers, burst testers, and impact testers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = running one product sample through a standardized physical test (e.g., burst or impact test): setup, operation, and recording results typically takes ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise manipulation of varied specialized lab instruments, reading analog/digital gauges, loading samples correctly, and interpreting results. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, instrument-specific tool-use, and domain perception needed for reliable autonomous lab testing.

task: Check completed products for conformance to specifications and for defects by measuring with rulers or micrometers, by checking them visually, or by tapping them to detect bubbles or dead spots.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single completed product unit; competent QC worker measures dimensions, visually checks, and tap-tests in ~5 minutes on average.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is maturing, but tap-testing requires nuanced haptic/acoustic interpretation, precise micrometer handling demands fine dexterity, and generalizing across varied product types is still unreliable for frontier humanoids.

task: Release air bubbles and smooth seams, using rollers.
