human_time_rationale: One instance = rolling out one seam or panel (e.g., wallpaper strip or flooring section) to release bubbles and smooth it; a competent worker takes ~2–4 minutes per panel.
robot_success_rationale: Requires controlled, variable pressure with a roller over a surface while detecting subtle air bubbles via tactile/visual feedback. Current robots lack reliable compliant force control and real-time bubble-detection needed for consistent, supervisor-acceptable results.

task: Smooth and polish flat and contoured surfaces of parts or tools, using scrapers, abrasive stones, files, emery cloths, or power grinders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = smoothing/polishing one part or tool surface. A skilled machinist or tool-and-die worker typically spends 30–60 min per part depending on complexity and finish requirements.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous force-feedback control, precise tool-switching (scrapers, files, grinders), and adaptive pressure on complex contours. Current humanoids lack the tactile sensitivity and sub-millimeter precision needed for acceptable surface finish quality.

task: Pat or press layers of saturated mat or cloth into place on molds, using brushes or hands, and smooth out wrinkles and air bubbles with hands or squeegees.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying up one mold section (e.g., a panel or hull segment). A competent laminator typically takes 15–35 min per layer depending on part complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly compliant, force-sensitive manipulation of wet, deformable fabric; precise wrinkle/bubble detection and correction on complex curved surfaces; tool switching (brush/squeegee). Far beyond reliable 2026 robot dexterity on unstructured soft materials.

task: Conduct test runs with completed tools or dies to ensure that parts meet specifications, making adjustments as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single test run cycle: loading tooling/die, running sample parts, measuring against specs, and making adjustments. A skilled toolmaker typically takes 30–60 min per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy press/die machinery, precision metrology (calipers, CMM), interpreting spec sheets, and making fine mechanical adjustments. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use generalization, and machinery operation capabilities.

task: Stabilize patients in critical condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stabilizing one critically ill patient from initial assessment to stable condition. Trained clinicians typically spend 30–60 minutes on acute stabilization interventions.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rapid clinical judgment, fine dexterity (IV lines, airway management, drug dosing), interpreting vitals, and adaptive decision-making under uncertainty. Far beyond any current general-purpose robot's capabilities.

task: Load, unload, or position lighting equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading/unloading/positioning a lighting rig or set of lights for a typical shoot or event setup; a competent grip/gaffer takes ~15–25 min per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Lighting equipment varies widely in shape, weight, and mounting; requires precise positioning, cable management, and height adjustment on stands. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use for clamps/stands, struggle with bulky/awkward loads, and can't safely operate C-stands or fly-rigs autonomously.

task: Weigh and mix pelletized, granular, or powdered thermoplastic materials and coloring pigments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = weighing and mixing a single batch of thermoplastic materials and pigments. A competent worker typically takes ~15 min to weigh components, add pigments, and blend one batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise weighing (fine motor + tool use), handling granular/powdered materials (poor robot tactile feedback), scooping, pouring without spillage, and reading scale readouts. Generalization across container types and materials is weak in 2026 robots.

task: Test equipment performance, focus of lens system, diaphragm alignment, lens mounts, or film transport, using precision gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full precision inspection of one camera/lens unit covering focus, diaphragm alignment, lens mounts, and film transport with precision gauges; ~20–30 min for a skilled technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to handle precision optical gauges, interpret analog/digital readouts, reposition instruments at micron-level tolerances, and make pass/fail judgments. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Adjust controls to position rod and to regulate flux level, reactor period, coolant temperature, or rate of power flow, following standard procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single control adjustment sequence (rod positioning + parameter regulation) per standard procedure; a licensed reactor operator typically spends ~15–25 minutes per deliberate adjustment cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized nuclear control panels, interpreting reactor instrumentation, and following licensed safety procedures. No humanoid robot has the certified interfaces, regulatory standing, or verified reliability for autonomous nuclear plant operation.

task: Patch or wire lights to dimmers or other electronic consoles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = patching/wiring a single lighting circuit to a dimmer or console. A competent electrician/lighting tech takes ~15–25 min per circuit including cable routing, connector seating, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous wire handling, identifying correct terminals, routing cables in cluttered backstage/panel environments, and using specialized connectors (XLR, DMX, screw terminals). Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and task-specific tool use.

task: Cut, shape, and trim blanks or blocks to specified lengths or shapes, using power saws, power shears, rules, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting/shaping one blank or block to spec. A skilled worker measures, sets up, and cuts in ~10–20 min depending on material and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating power saws/shears safely and precisely, measuring to spec, and repositioning workpieces. Current humanoids lack reliable tool operation, fine measurement interpretation, and safe high-force cutting with power tools.

task: Apply layers of plastic resin to mold surfaces prior to placement of fiberglass mats, repeating layers until products have the desired thicknesses and plastics have jelled.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laminating one mold surface to desired thickness and gel point. A competent hand laminator typically takes 30–60 min depending on mold size and layer count.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, uniform resin application with a brush/roller or spray gun onto complex curved mold surfaces, tactile judgment of gel state, and iterative layering. Current robots lack the fine dexterity, wet-material handling, and chemo-tactile feedback needed.

task: Conduct exercise electrocardiogram tests to monitor cardiovascular activity under stress.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full stress ECG test per patient: setup/electrode placement (~10 min), active treadmill/ergometer protocol (~20 min), recovery monitoring (~15 min).
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise skin-prep and electrode placement on a moving patient, real-time clinical interpretation, emergency response capability, and operating specialized medical equipment — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Set up scaffolding or cranes to assist with setting up of lighting equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up scaffolding or a crane for a single lighting rig. Competent crew typically takes 1–3 hours depending on scale; median ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy machinery (cranes) or assembling large structural scaffolding with heavy components, high-force connections, and safety-critical precision. Current humanoids lack the strength, reach, specialized tool-use, and machinery operation needed.

task: Perform minor repairs or routine maintenance on lighting equipment, such as replacing lamps or damaged color filters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing a single lamp or color filter on a lighting fixture. A competent technician locates the fixture, removes/replaces the component, and verifies function in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to varied fixture locations (often overhead or on a rig), fine dexterous manipulation of small fragile components (bulbs, gel filters), and tool use in unstructured environments — all near the limits of 2026 humanoids.

task: Measure, mark, and scribe metal or plastic stock to lay out machining, using instruments such as protractors, micrometers, scribes, or rulers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out a single workpiece. A skilled machinist typically spends 10–20 min measuring, marking reference lines, and scribing features on one part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter precision dexterous tool use (scribe, micrometer, protractor) with force feedback on rigid stock. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation accuracy, stable fixturing grip, and perception resolution needed for acceptable machining layout quality.

task: Test lighting equipment function and desired lighting effects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a lighting rig/setup in a single room or stage. A competent technician checks each fixture, adjusts settings, and verifies effects in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating lighting control boards/DMX systems, visually judging subjective aesthetic effects, adjusting dimmers/gels, and interpreting creative intent. Current robots lack the tool interfacing, nuanced visual perception, and judgment needed.

task: Perform vascular procedures, such as balloon angioplasty and stents.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one percutaneous vascular procedure (e.g., balloon angioplasty with stent placement). A competent interventional cardiologist/radiologist typically takes 60–120 minutes including prep, catheterization, imaging, and closure.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme sub-millimeter dexterity inside a live human body under fluoroscopic guidance, real-time clinical judgment, and catheter/wire manipulation. Far beyond any general-purpose humanoid robot's capabilities; even specialized surgical robots require full human teleoperation.

task: Lay out and drill, ream, tap, or cut parts for assembly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out and machining (drilling/reaming/tapping/cutting) a single part or small batch for assembly; a competent machinist averages ~45 min including setup, marking, and operations.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise layout marking, tool changes (drill/reamer/tap), machine operation (drill press, lathe, mill), and fine positional accuracy. Frontier humanoids lack the tool-use, machine-operating capability, and sub-mm precision needed for this trade-level task.

task: Set pyrometer controls of heat-treating furnaces and feed or place parts, tools, or assemblies into furnaces to harden.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting pyrometer controls and loading one batch of parts into a heat-treating furnace. A competent operator typically takes ~5–15 minutes per batch cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/adjusting analog or digital pyrometer controls (fine manipulation, UI interaction), navigating industrial floor environments, and placing varied parts precisely into furnace fixtures. Control panel interaction and high-heat proximity are major barriers for current humanoids.

task: Diagnose cardiovascular conditions, using cardiac catheterization.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one cardiac catheterization procedure. Prep, catheter insertion, imaging, data interpretation, and post-procedure monitoring typically take 60–120 minutes for a competent cardiologist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for catheter navigation through vasculature, sterile surgical technique, real-time clinical judgment, and patient interaction. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid robot capabilities; even specialized surgical robots require expert teleoperation.

task: Monitor or operate boilers, turbines, wells, or auxiliary power plant equipment.
human_time_rationale: One monitoring/operations shift cycle (readings, checks, adjustments) across boilers, turbines, or auxiliary equipment; typically logged hourly by a plant operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, operating specialized valves and controls, interpreting plant-specific instrumentation, and making real-time adjustments. Current humanoids lack plant system integration, safe manipulation of high-pressure equipment, and autonomous fault response.

task: Operate shearing mechanisms to cut rods to specified lengths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting one rod to spec. Worker measures, positions rod against stop, and actuates shear mechanism in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise rod measurement and alignment, operating a heavy industrial shearing mechanism, and verifying cut length. Current humanoids lack reliable fine measurement, force-control for industrial levers, and safe heavy-machinery operation.

task: Cure materials by letting them set at room temperature, placing them under heat lamps, or baking them in ovens.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and placing one batch of material to cure (loading into oven/under lamp or setting aside). Active setup takes ~10–20 min; passive cure time excluded.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can move trays and operate simple oven controls, but reliably positioning materials under heat lamps, adjusting oven settings, and monitoring varied cure conditions across unstructured environments reduces confidence significantly.

task: Repair or modify damaged or defective glass-fiber parts, checking thicknesses, densities, and contours to ensure a close fit after repair.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing one damaged glass-fiber part. A competent technician spends ~30–150 min assessing damage, grinding, layering resin/fiber, curing, sanding, and verifying thickness/contour.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous material work (layering fiberglass, applying resin), precision tactile inspection of thickness/density/contour, and iterative judgment. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Pack or fold insulation between panels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = packing/folding insulation into a single panel bay. A competent worker cuts, fits, and tucks insulation batting in ~8 minutes per bay.
robot_success_rationale: Insulation (batts/rolls) is highly deformable, compressible, and irregular. Robots struggle severely with soft, floppy materials in tight constrained spaces, requiring precise tucking and folding that exceeds current dexterous manipulation limits.

task: File, grind, shim, and adjust different parts to properly fit them together.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting one set of parts. Skilled machinist/mechanic spends ~30–60 min filing, grinding, shimming, and test-fitting until tolerance is achieved.
robot_success_rationale: Requires iterative force-feedback grinding/filing with sub-millimeter precision, shim selection, and fit judgment. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, compliant tool-use, and closed-loop tactile sensing needed for this multi-step precision task.

task: Trim cured materials by sawing them with diamond-impregnated cutoff wheels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming one cured workpiece. A competent worker sets up, aligns, and saws through cured material in ~10 minutes depending on size/thickness.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise workpiece fixturing, cut-line alignment, sustained controlled force application with a high-speed abrasive tool, and safety management. Current humanoids lack the fine tool-use precision, rigid fixturing ability, and force control needed for consistent, accurate cuts.

task: Administer radioisotopes to clinical patients or research subjects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and administering a single radioisotope dose (IV, oral, or inhaled) to one patient, including ID verification, dose drawing, and administration.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuclear medicine license, radiation safety compliance, sterile syringe handling, IV access on a patient, patient communication, and real-time clinical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot autonomy in regulated clinical settings.

task: Start machines and set controls to regulate vacuum, air pressure, sizing rings, and temperature, and to synchronize speed of extrusion.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full machine startup sequence including adjusting vacuum, air pressure, sizing rings, temperature zones, and synchronizing extrusion speed; a skilled operator takes ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial control panels, reading analog/digital gauges, turning knobs/valves, and making interdependent process adjustments. Current humanoids lack the perception, dexterous tool-use, and domain-specific process knowledge for this complex multi-parameter startup.

task: Conduct inspections or operations outside of control rooms as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single field inspection/operations check outside the control room (e.g., walking the plant floor, checking equipment status). Typical round takes ~20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate indoor industrial floors and read gauges, but unstructured field environments, varied equipment layouts, unexpected hazards, and required interventions (valves, switches) push success probability low. Generalization remains limited.

task: Verify dimensions, alignments, and clearances of finished parts for conformance to specifications, using measuring instruments such as calipers, gauge blocks, micrometers, or dial indicators.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single finished part. A competent machinist/QC technician typically takes 10–20 min to measure critical dimensions, alignments, and clearances with multiple instruments and record results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (calipers, micrometers, dial indicators), precise seating of instruments on part features, and interpreting analog/digital readouts. Current humanoids lack the fine-finger force control and repeatable sub-millimeter positioning needed for reliable metrology-grade measurements.

task: Conduct tests of the pulmonary system, using a spirometer or other respiratory testing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient pulmonary function test session including setup, patient coaching through maneuvers, and result recording. Typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced verbal coaching of a patient, interpreting real-time effort quality, handling medical equipment hygienically, and clinical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured caregiving/clinical settings.

task: Perform emergency resuscitations on patients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one resuscitation event (e.g., CPR + AED use) from cardiac/respiratory arrest until ROSC, handoff, or termination. Typical active resuscitation lasts ~10–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rapid situational assessment, precise chest compressions calibrated to patient anatomy, airway management, defibrillator use, team coordination, and real-time adaptive clinical judgment — all far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capabilities.

task: Spray chopped fiberglass, resins, and catalysts onto prepared molds or dies using pneumatic spray guns with chopper attachments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = spraying one mold/die. A competent worker typically spends 30–60 min per mold depending on size, achieving even coverage of fiberglass, resin, and catalyst layers.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a specialized pneumatic spray gun with chopper attachment, precise multi-pass coverage of complex 3D mold geometry, and real-time thickness judgment. Current humanoids lack the tool-use proficiency, force control, and spray-pattern expertise needed; highly unstructured and chemically hazardous.

task: Adjust machine or freezer speed and air intake to obtain desired consistency and amount of product.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting machine/freezer controls until desired product consistency is reached; a competent operator checks output and tweaks settings over ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sensory evaluation of product consistency (texture, viscosity), interpreting analog/specialty controls, and closed-loop quality judgment — well beyond current robot perception and generalization capabilities in unstructured food-production settings.

task: Sever jugular veins to drain blood and facilitate slaughtering.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single sticking/bleeding cut on one restrained animal. A trained slaughterhouse worker completes this precise cut in under a minute.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme precision on a live/moving animal, force control near critical anatomy, compliance with welfare regulations, and blade handling under dynamic conditions. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and safety guarantees.

task: Inspect, clean, and assemble molds before beginning work.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one mold set (inspect, clean, assemble). A skilled worker takes ~15–25 min depending on mold complexity and contamination level.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual inspection for defects, precise cleaning of complex cavities, and exact multi-part assembly with tight tolerances. Current humanoids lack reliable defect detection, force-controlled fine manipulation, and domain-specific tool use for mold work.

task: Diagnose or treat disorders of the musculoskeletal system.
human_time_rationale: One clinical encounter (patient intake, physical examination, diagnosis/treatment plan, and documentation) for a musculoskeletal disorder; typical outpatient visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires medical licensure, clinical reasoning, nuanced physical examination, patient communication, and diagnostic judgment. Far beyond current robot cognitive, dexterous, and ethical capabilities. No autonomous clinical deployment exists.

task: Visit and assess structural and electrical layout of locations before setting up lighting equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing one location (e.g., a venue or room). A competent gaffer or lighting tech walks the space, checks structural anchor points, and surveys electrical panels/circuits in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level interpretation of structural load capacity, electrical panel layouts, and safety codes. Current robots lack domain-specific inspection reasoning, cannot read circuit labels reliably, and struggle with unstructured, multi-floor environments.

task: Trim, clean, or cure animal hides.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one hide (trimming edges, cleaning flesh/fat, applying cure). A competent worker takes ~20–30 min per hide depending on size and condition.
robot_success_rationale: Hides are large, floppy, irregular, slippery, and highly deformable — extremely challenging for robot perception and manipulation. Requires sharp tool use, variable force control, and tactile feedback far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Mask off mold areas not to be laminated, using cellophane, wax paper, masking tape, or special sprays containing mold-release substances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = masking off a single mold. A competent worker applies tape, paper, or spray to defined areas in ~15–25 min depending on mold complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise edge-following with tape/film on complex 3D mold surfaces, fine dexterity with thin flexible materials, and contextual judgment about boundaries. Current humanoids lack reliable precision for conforming masking tape/film to curved mold geometry without gaps or wrinkles.

task: Select precut fiberglass mats, cloth, and wood-bracing materials as required by projects being assembled.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting/gathering fiberglass mats, cloth, and wood bracing for one project. Competent worker reads spec and pulls correct materials from stock in ~5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading a project spec, identifying correct material types/sizes from a varied inventory, and handling floppy fiberglass cloth and irregular wood pieces. Perception of flexible, visually similar materials and cross-referencing specs are significant challenges for current humanoids.

task: Monitor pressure gauges, ammeters, flowmeters, thermometers, or products, and adjust controls to maintain specified conditions, such as feed rate, product consistency, temperature, air pressure, and machine speed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring-and-adjustment round across a set of gauges/instruments; a competent operator typically completes a full check and corrective adjustment in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Reading analog/digital gauges requires reliable perception; physically adjusting valves, knobs, and controls demands dexterous manipulation in unstructured industrial settings. Closed-loop reasoning across multiple instrument types and corrective action chains remain well beyond current humanoid capability at production quality.

task: Conduct electrocardiogram (EKG), phonocardiogram, echocardiogram, or other cardiovascular tests to record patients' cardiac activity, using specialized electronic test equipment, recording devices, or laboratory instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient cardiovascular test session (e.g., standard 12-lead EKG ~10 min; echocardiogram ~30–45 min). Median across test types ~30 min including setup, acquisition, and labeling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise electrode/probe placement on varied human anatomy, patient communication, real-time clinical adjustment, and safe handling of sensitive medical equipment on a live patient. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, medical tool integration, patient-interaction, and safety certification for autonomous clinical use.

task: Examine patient to obtain information on medical condition and surgical risk.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full pre-surgical patient examination including history-taking, physical exam, risk assessment, and documentation. A competent physician typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced clinical reasoning, nuanced communication, physical auscultation/palpation with diagnostic interpretation, and legal/ethical accountability. No 2026 general-purpose robot can perform this autonomously at acceptable clinical quality.

task: Bond wood reinforcing strips to decks and cabin structures of watercraft, using resin-saturated fiberglass.
human_time_rationale: One instance = bonding reinforcing strips to one deck/cabin section: fitting strips, mixing/applying resin, laying fiberglass, smoothing. A skilled marine tradesperson typically takes 60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise wet layup of resin-saturated fiberglass on curved, irregular marine surfaces, fine dexterous pressure/smoothing, chemical handling, and working in confined boat spaces. Far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity and task generalization.

task: Observe ultrasound display screen, and listen to signals to record vascular information, such as blood pressure, limb volume changes, oxygen saturation, and cerebral circulation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = single patient vascular monitoring session; observing display, interpreting audio signals, and recording metrics like BP, limb volume, SpO2, cerebral circulation takes ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert clinical interpretation of ultrasound imagery and audio signals, precise probe placement on a patient, and nuanced judgment. Current robots lack medical perception software, safe patient-contact dexterity, and clinical reasoning for autonomous vascular assessment.

task: Perform minimally invasive surgical procedures, such as implanting pacemakers and defibrillators.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pacemaker/defibrillator implant procedure. A competent electrophysiologist or cardiac surgeon typically takes 1–2.5 hours; ~120 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sterile surgical technique, real-time clinical judgment, fluoroscopic interpretation, and patient-specific adaptation. Far beyond any 2026 general-purpose robot; even surgical robots (da Vinci) require full human teleoperation.

task: Inspect finished dies for smoothness, contour conformity, and defects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one finished die. A skilled inspector visually and tactilely examines smoothness, contour, and defects; typically 10–15 minutes per die.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine tactile sensing, precise visual inspection of complex 3D contours, and defect recognition on reflective metal surfaces — well beyond current humanoid robots' perception and dexterity standards for quality-level acceptance.

task: Operate on patient's musculoskeletal system to correct deformities, repair injuries, prevent and treat diseases, or improve or restore patient's functions.
human_time_rationale: One orthopedic surgical procedure (e.g., knee replacement, fracture repair). Typical case runs 2–4 hours; 3 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sterile technique, real-time adaptive decision-making, and medical licensure. General-purpose humanoids have no viable path to autonomous open surgery; even specialized surgical robots (Da Vinci) require human surgeons teleoperating.

task: Shackle hind legs of animals to raise them for slaughtering or skinning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shackling one animal's hind legs and raising it. An experienced slaughterhouse worker completes this in ~1–2 minutes per animal.
robot_success_rationale: Requires restraining a live, struggling animal, precisely fitting shackles on moving hind legs, and operating overhead hoisting equipment — combining dynamic biological interaction, force control, and specialized tool use far beyond current robot capabilities.

task: Select, request, perform, or interpret diagnostic procedures, such as laboratory tests, electrocardiograms, emergency ultrasounds, and radiographs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting, ordering, and interpreting a single diagnostic procedure (e.g., ECG or X-ray) for one patient, from decision to documented interpretation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical reasoning, patient interaction, physical probe/equipment operation, and medical interpretation. Current humanoids lack medical decision-making, fine procedural dexterity (e.g., ultrasound probe), and regulatory clearance. AI imaging tools exist but are narrow, not autonomous general-purpose robots.

task: Tend assembly lines, performing a few of the many cuts needed to process a carcass.
human_time_rationale: One instance = performing one set of assigned cuts on a single carcass passing on the line; skilled meat-processing workers complete their specific cuts in under a minute per carcass.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, force-controlled cuts on irregular, deformable biological material at speed; current humanoids lack the fine dexterous tool control, food-safe compliance, and real-time adaptation to variable carcass geometry needed for acceptable yield and safety.

task: Skin sections of animals or whole animals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = skinning one medium-sized animal (e.g., deer or rabbit). A skilled worker takes ~20–45 min depending on species and carcass size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely precise, forceful, and adaptive cutting along irregular tissue boundaries on a deformable, slippery carcass. Far beyond current robot dexterity, force control, and blade-handling capability for unstructured biological tissue.

task: Diagnose bodily disorders and orthopedic conditions, and provide treatments, such as medicines and surgeries, in clinics, hospital wards, or operating rooms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full patient encounter (history, exam, diagnosis, treatment plan/procedure). Typical orthopaedic visit or surgical case averages ~45–75 min across clinic, ward, and OR settings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires medical licensure, complex clinical reasoning, tactile diagnosis, sterile surgical dexterity, patient communication, and legal accountability. Far beyond any 2026 general-purpose robot's autonomous capability.

task: Start machinery, such as pumps, feeders, or conveyors, and turn valves to heat, admit, or transfer products, refrigerants, or mixes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting a set of machinery and adjusting associated valves for a single process run or shift startup; a competent operator typically takes ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating and operating industrial control panels, switches, and valves in varied plant layouts. Valve turning and panel interaction demand high force, precise grip, and process awareness. Generalization across facilities is very low for current humanoids.

task: Monitor all systems for normal running conditions, performing activities such as checking gauges to assess output or the effects of generator loading on other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring round, checking gauges and assessing system status across a control room or plant floor; competent operators typically complete one patrol/check cycle in 15–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Reading analog/digital gauges is partially feasible, but interpreting interdependent system states (e.g., generator loading effects), navigating a complex plant environment, and making judgment calls on anomalies exceed current general-purpose robot reliability. Poor generalization and decision-making in novel conditions limit success.

task: Shave or singe and defeather carcasses, and wash them in preparation for further processing or packaging.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one poultry/meat carcass. A competent worker singeing, defeathering, and washing a single carcass takes roughly 3–5 minutes on a processing line.
robot_success_rationale: Defeathering requires precise force control on irregular, slippery, deformable carcasses; singes demand tool-use with open flame; thorough washing needs dexterous repositioning. Current humanoids lack the specialized dexterity and compliance for consistent success.

task: Examine fetuses, infants, children, and adolescents, and diagnose health issues to determine need for intervention, such as surgery.
human_time_rationale: One clinical examination and diagnostic assessment of a pediatric patient (fetus to adolescent), including history, physical exam, and forming a diagnostic impression — typical consult/visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced medical knowledge, nuanced physical exam on delicate/non-cooperative pediatric patients, clinical judgment, imaging interpretation, and ethical decision-making. Far beyond any current general-purpose robot's capability.

task: Participate in nuclear fuel element handling activities, such as preparation, transfer, loading, or unloading.
human_time_rationale: One instance = handling a single fuel element through one phase (e.g., loading/unloading a reactor). Involves setup, safety checks, and precise handling; typically 1–3 hours per element cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operation of specialized nuclear handling machinery, strict regulatory protocols, radiation-hardened equipment, and extreme precision in a highly controlled/hazardous environment. Current humanoids lack radiation hardening, specialized tooling, and certified autonomy for nuclear operations.

task: Wrap dressed carcasses or meat cuts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wrapping a single meat cut or carcass portion. A skilled butcher/packer takes ~1–3 minutes per piece to position, wrap, seal, and label.
robot_success_rationale: Meat is heavy, deformable, slippery, and irregularly shaped. Wrapping requires precise film handling, folding, and sealing. Current robots struggle with compliant/wet objects and consistent tight wrapping; dexterity and force control are insufficient for reliable commercial-quality results.

task: Assess patients' pain levels or sedation requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing a single patient's pain/sedation status, including verbal questioning, observation of behavioral cues, and documenting a score (e.g., NRS, CPOT, RASS). Typically takes 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced clinical judgment, empathetic communication, interpretation of subtle behavioral/physiological cues, and contextual medical knowledge. Current humanoids lack validated clinical assessment capability, natural language clinical dialogue, and caregiver trust required for autonomous patient evaluation.

task: Saw, split, or scribe carcasses into smaller portions to facilitate handling.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one carcass or primal cut. A skilled butcher spends roughly 5–12 minutes depending on animal size and cut complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy-force tools (bone saws, cleavers), precise anatomical knowledge, and high dexterity on deformable, slippery biological material. Current humanoids lack the strength, tool integration, and perception for safe autonomous carcass processing.

task: Place or position containers into equipment, and remove containers after completion of cooling or freezing processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading containers into cooling/freezing equipment and removing them after the process. A competent worker completes both steps in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place containers on flat surfaces but struggle with varied container shapes, tight equipment interiors, precise placement in racks, door/seal handling, and timing coordination over the cooling cycle.

task: Lift, position, and secure machined parts on surface plates or worktables, using hoists, vises, v-blocks, or angle plates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setup of one machined part on a surface plate using fixturing tools. A skilled machinist typically spends 10–20 min depending on part size and required precision.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated use of specialized tools (hoists, vises, v-blocks, angle plates) with high positional precision. Current humanoids lack fine force-control, tool-use generalization, and the metrology judgment needed to secure parts accurately.

task: Trim excess materials from molds, using hand shears or trimming knives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming one mold. A skilled worker with shears or knife completes flash/excess removal in roughly 3–7 minutes depending on part size and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled cutting along irregular parting lines with sharp hand tools. Fine dexterity, tool handling, and variable part geometry make this extremely challenging for current humanoid robots; high injury/damage risk.

task: Measure or weigh specified amounts of ingredients or materials, and load them into tanks, vats, hoppers, or other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and loading one batch of ingredients. A competent worker takes ~10–15 min to weigh/measure multiple components and transfer them into processing equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement (scale reading, scooping), handling varied container types, and accurately loading into tanks/hoppers. Current robots lack reliable fine measurement verification, liquid handling, and generalization across diverse ingredient/vessel types.

task: Trim head meat, and sever or remove parts of animals' heads or skulls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one animal head. A skilled butcher trims head meat and removes skull parts in roughly 5–12 minutes depending on animal size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme force, precise blade control around irregular bone/tissue, and expert anatomical knowledge. Current humanoid robots lack the dexterity, cutting tool integration, and safe force modulation for wet, slippery, complex carcass work.

task: Remove bones, and cut meat into standard cuts in preparation for marketing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = deboning and cutting one primal/sub-primal cut (e.g., a pork loin or beef chuck) into retail cuts. A skilled butcher typically takes 15–25 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme dexterity with sharp blades on irregular, deformable raw meat; precise anatomical knowledge; strong grip control to avoid slipping. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured settings.

task: Set up and operate drill presses to drill and tap holes in parts for assembly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up drill press for a part type then drilling and tapping one part (~5–10 holes). Includes bit selection, depth-stop setting, clamping, and tapping. ~8 min for a competent machinist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool changes (drill bits, taps), fine torque control for tapping without breaking, workpiece clamping, and machine operation via controls. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity and machine-interface capability for this multi-step, precision process.

task: Weigh packages and adjust freezer air valves or switches on filler heads to obtain specified amounts of product in each container.
human_time_rationale: One instance = weigh one package and adjust one filler head valve/switch as needed. A trained worker reads the scale, compares to spec, and makes a small mechanical adjustment in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading a scale display (perception), fine manipulation of small valves/switches on filler heads (dexterity), and closed-loop adjustment logic. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-tuning of industrial valves and integrating scale feedback autonomously in an unstructured food-processing environment.

task: Follow established surgical techniques during the operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one surgical procedure. Competent surgeons spend ~1–3 hours on a typical operation; 120 min is a reasonable median across common procedures.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sterile technique, real-time clinical judgment, and haptic feedback far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoids. Surgical robots (da Vinci) are narrow, teleoperated, not autonomous humanoids.

task: Examine instruments, equipment, and operating room to ensure sterility.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full pre-procedure sterility check of instruments, equipment, and one OR. A scrub tech/circulator typically spends ~15–25 min before a case.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading expiry dates/sterility indicators, interpreting packaging integrity, checking subtle visual/tactile cues across dozens of items, and navigating a complex clinical environment — well beyond current robot perception and judgment capabilities.

task: Monitor patient's recovery, making follow-up visits and using postoperative assessment techniques, such as blood and imaging tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single postoperative follow-up visit including assessment, reviewing blood/imaging results, and documenting findings. Typically 30–60 minutes for a competent clinician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, interpreting imaging/labs, nuanced patient interaction, and medical decision-making. Current humanoid robots lack medical cognition, diagnostic reasoning, and regulatory clearance for autonomous clinical assessment.

task: Position and secure workpieces on machines, using holding devices, measuring instruments, hand tools, and hoists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and securing a single workpiece on a machine. A skilled machinist typically takes 10–20 min depending on part complexity and fixturing method.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, coordinated tool use (clamps, wrenches, hoists), alignment to tight tolerances, and safe machine interaction. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force feedback, and spatial precision this demands reliably.

task: Correct machinery malfunctions by performing actions such as removing jams, and inform supervisors of malfunctions as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and clearing a single malfunction (e.g., a jam). A competent worker typically resolves a common jam or fault in ~5–15 minutes, including notifying a supervisor.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing varied, unstructured faults; dexterous removal of jammed material in tight spaces; context-aware judgment on when to escalate. Current robots lack reliable fault diagnosis, generalization to novel jams, and verbal/written supervisor notification.

task: Read dials and gauges on panel control boards to ascertain temperatures, alkalinities, and densities of mixtures, and turn valves to obtain specified mixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one round of readings from a panel plus valve adjustments to hit target specs. A competent operator completes this in ~5 min per check cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading varied analog/digital gauges (perception challenge), mapping readings to valve adjustments (process knowledge), and precisely turning industrial valves. Current humanoids lack reliable gauge-reading, domain reasoning, and fine valve-control integration needed autonomously.

task: Cut, trim, skin, sort, and wash viscera of slaughtered animals to separate edible portions from offal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing the viscera of one slaughtered animal carcass; a skilled meatpacker typically completes cutting, trimming, skinning, sorting, and washing in ~20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Highly deformable, slippery, irregular organs require extreme dexterity and force modulation. Perception of soft tissue boundaries is unreliable. No current humanoid has demonstrated wet, unstructured viscera processing autonomously.

task: Apply lacquers and waxes to mold surfaces to facilitate assembly and removal of laminated parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating one mold surface. Worker cleans, applies lacquer/wax coats, and buffs; typically 15–25 minutes for an average-sized mold.
robot_success_rationale: Requires controlled, even coating application with precise pressure and stroke patterns on varied mold geometries, buffing, and quality judgment. Current robots lack the fine force control, tool compliance, and surface-quality perception needed for consistent results.

task: Operate diagnostic imaging equipment to produce contrast-enhanced radiographs of heart and cardiovascular system.
human_time_rationale: One contrast-enhanced cardiovascular imaging study including patient prep, contrast injection coordination, equipment setup, and image acquisition series; typically 30–60 min for a skilled radiologic technologist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized medical imaging equipment (fluoroscopy, cath lab systems), coordinating contrast injection into patients, sterile technique, and real-time clinical judgment. Far beyond any 2026 general-purpose humanoid's capabilities — no dexterity, safety certification, or system integration exists.

task: Test dosage evaluation instruments and survey meters to ensure they are operating properly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single pair of instruments (dosimeter + survey meter) against a check source; a trained radiation safety technician typically completes this in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise handling of calibrated radiation sources, reading analog/digital meter displays, connecting instruments to calibration setups, and interpreting nuanced results. Current humanoids lack the specialized perception, tool-use, and safety compliance for radioactive source handling.

task: Stun animals prior to slaughtering.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stunning one animal. A trained slaughterhouse worker applies a captive bolt, electric, or gas stun in roughly 1–3 minutes including animal positioning.
robot_success_rationale: Requires forceful, precise contact with a moving, distressed large animal; needs controlled restraint equipment, safety-critical accuracy on anatomy, and tool operation. Current humanoids lack the strength, force control, and situational judgment for live livestock handling.

task: Scrape, dislodge, or break excess frost, ice, or frozen product from equipment to prevent accumulation, using hands and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing frost/ice from one piece of equipment. A competent worker takes ~10–20 min depending on buildup severity and equipment size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires force-controlled scraping with hand tools on irregular, slippery ice surfaces in cold/wet environments. Perception of frost buildup and adaptive tool pressure are challenging; cold conditions stress actuators and sensors. Poor generalization across equipment shapes.

task: Start agitators to blend contents, or start beater, scraper, and expeller blades to mix contents with air and prevent sticking.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting the agitator/blades on a single machine; a worker walks to the machine, checks settings, and flips the switch/starts controls — roughly 1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to industrial equipment, identifying correct controls (buttons, levers, or touchscreens), and operating them correctly. Perception and manipulation of varied panel interfaces is unreliable for current humanoids; risk of mis-operation is significant.

task: Check patrons' identification to ensure that they meet minimum age requirements for consumption of alcoholic beverages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking a single patron's ID. A competent human takes ~20–30 seconds to examine, read, and verify an ID card.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/authenticating varied ID documents (glare, holograms, worn cards), judging authenticity, and social interaction. Vision-based OCR on IDs is feasible, but hologram/forgery detection, varied lighting, and confident autonomous social enforcement remain very challenging for current humanoids.

task: Check all dies, templates, and cutout patterns to be used in the manufacturing process to ensure that they conform to dimensional data, photographs, blueprints, samples, or customer specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single die/template/pattern against specs. A competent worker spends ~15–25 min measuring, comparing to blueprints/samples, and documenting conformance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise metrology (calipers, gauges), cross-referencing complex 2D/3D specs, and fine manipulation of irregular tooling. Current robots lack reliable precision measurement tool use and blueprint interpretation in unstructured settings.

task: Inspect and flush lines with solutions or steam, and spray equipment with sterilizing solutions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting, flushing, and spraying a single equipment unit/line setup; a competent worker typically takes ~25–35 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting hoses/nozzles to varied fittings, managing fluid pressures, inspecting line conditions, and precisely spraying sterilant — complex tool-use, fine dexterity, and domain-specific generalization well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Sample and test product characteristics such as specific gravity, acidity, and sugar content, using hydrometers, pH meters, or refractometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sampling and testing a single product batch for specific gravity, acidity, and sugar content using 2–3 instruments; includes collection, measurement, and recording.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid sampling, careful instrument handling (delicate hydrometer, pH meter calibration, refractometer), and interpreting analog/digital readings. Current robots lack reliable fine dexterity and fluid manipulation for multi-instrument lab workflows.

task: Examine instruments, equipment, and operating room to ensure sterility.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting an OR and its instruments/equipment before a single surgical case. A trained scrub tech or OR nurse typically takes 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Sterility verification requires reading chemical indicators, checking packaging integrity, expiry dates, and subtle visual/tactile cues under strict aseptic protocol. Current robots lack the fine perception, domain knowledge, sterile-field awareness, and regulatory acceptability needed.

task: Inspect or test materials, stock, vehicles, equipment, or facilities to ensure that they are safe, free of defects, and consistent with specifications.
human_time_rationale: One inspection instance (e.g., a vehicle, equipment unit, or facility area); a competent inspector typically spends 20–45 min checking specs, defects, and safety criteria.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can visually scan surfaces and detect obvious defects, but diverse inspection targets (vehicles, facilities, equipment) require generalized judgment, tactile checks, instrument use, and contextual spec interpretation well beyond current frontier capability.

task: Turn valves or pull levers to start and regulate the flow of coolant or lubricant to work areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single valve/lever to start or regulate flow. A skilled worker locates, turns/pulls, and confirms flow in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Valve/lever operation requires precise grip, torque control, and confirmation of flow. Humanoids struggle with varied handle types, high-force turning, and verifying coolant/lubricant flow feedback in industrial settings.

task: Perform transplantation operations, such as organ transplants, on fetuses, infants, children, and adolescents.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single pediatric/fetal organ transplant surgery. These complex procedures typically take 4–10+ hours; ~8 hours is a reasonable median for a competent surgical team.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme sub-millimeter dexterity on tiny, fragile pediatric anatomy, real-time adaptive surgical judgment, sterile tool mastery, and intraoperative decision-making far beyond any current general-purpose humanoid robot's capability.

task: Assemble equipment, and attach pipes, fittings, or valves, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a typical pipe/fitting/valve sub-assembly (e.g., a section of plumbing or process piping). A competent pipefitter takes ~30–60 min including layout, fitting, and tightening.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-torque wrench use, precise thread alignment, leak-free sealing, and working in varied/confined spaces. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and tool-handling reliability for threaded pipe assembly to acceptable quality.

task: Observe milling or planing machine operation, and adjust controls to ensure conformance with specified tolerances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = observing a machine run and making one round of control adjustments for a single workpiece or batch setup; a skilled machinist typically spends ~10–20 min per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading gauges/displays, interpreting dimensional measurements, and making fine control adjustments on specialized CNC/manual machine panels. Current humanoids lack the precision tool-use, metrology interpretation, and machine-specific interface dexterity for autonomous success.

task: Remove workpieces from machines, and check to ensure that they conform to specifications, using measuring instruments such as microscopes, gauges, calipers, and micrometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one workpiece from a machine and inspecting it. Competent machinists typically spend 5–10 minutes per part including unloading and multi-point measurement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires: safe unloading from varied machines (moderate manipulation), then precise metrology with calipers/micrometers/microscopes (high dexterity, fine force control, tool-use). Current humanoids lack reliable fine-instrument handling and consistent measurement accuracy at micron scales.

task: Load and position wrapping paper, sticks, bags, or cartons into dispensing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading/restocking a single dispensing machine with supplies (paper, sticks, bags, or cartons). A competent worker typically takes 5–10 minutes per machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires lifting/aligning bulky, varied, flexible materials (rolls, stacks, cartons) into tight machine slots with precise orientation. Current humanoids struggle with flexible/deformable materials, precise insertion into constrained dispensers, and generalizing across machine types.

task: Follow established surgical techniques during the operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one surgical operation. Median operative time across common procedures (e.g., appendectomy, cholecystectomy) is roughly 60–180 minutes; ~120 min is a reasonable midpoint.
robot_success_rationale: Surgery demands extreme sub-millimeter dexterity, sterile tool handling, real-time adaptive judgment, and tissue differentiation. No general-purpose humanoid robot can safely perform autonomous surgery; even dedicated surgical robots (e.g., da Vinci) require surgeon teleoperation.

task: Move cutters or material manually or by turning handwheels, or engage automatic feeding mechanisms to mill workpieces to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = milling one workpiece to spec. A competent machinist typically spends ~10–20 min setting feeds, engaging mechanisms, and monitoring a single milling operation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise handwheel manipulation, reading dials/DROs, engaging levers, and monitoring cut quality. Humanoids lack the fine dexterity and force-feedback calibration needed; no commercial deployment in machine-shop contexts as of 2026.

task: Operate steam, hydraulic, or other pressing machines to remove wrinkles from garments and flatwork items, or to shape, form, or patch articles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pressing one garment or flatwork item. A skilled press operator typically takes 2–5 minutes per piece depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial press machinery (pedals, controls, steam triggers), precise garment placement/alignment on a buck, and adjusting for fabric type. Humanoids lack the machine-operation interface, fine textile handling, and garment-specific positioning skills needed.

task: Drive ambulances or assist ambulance drivers in transporting sick, injured, or convalescent persons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one ambulance transport trip. Median urban/suburban emergency transport (dispatch to hospital arrival) is roughly 25–45 minutes including patient loading.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot drive vehicles in traffic. Even L4 AVs are narrow-route only. Loading/securing a patient, navigating dynamic emergency scenes, and operating emergency vehicle controls autonomously are all beyond 2026 frontier robots.

task: Activate mechanical rakes to regulate flow of ice from storage bins to vats.
human_time_rationale: One instance = activating rakes for a single flow-regulation cycle; a worker operates controls/switches to start/adjust mechanical rakes, taking ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Task involves operating industrial control panels/switches in a cold, wet environment. Humanoids can press buttons but struggle with non-standardized industrial controls, slippery conditions, and confirming correct ice flow feedback reliably.

task: Move controls to set cutting specifications, to position cutting tools and workpieces in relation to each other, and to start machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = configuring and starting one machine job: adjusting controls, positioning tool/workpiece, verifying specs, and initiating the cut. A skilled machinist takes ~5–15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading machine-specific dials/displays, fine manipulation of levers/handwheels, precise spatial positioning of workpiece to spec, and interpreting blueprints. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, machine-specific tool-use, and situational judgment needed.

task: Operate computerized laboratory equipment to dehydrate, decalcify, or microincinerate tissue samples.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a batch of tissue samples into the equipment, configuring the program, initiating the run, and confirming completion. Active hands-on time is ~15–25 minutes; the machine does the rest.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous handling of delicate tissue cassettes/slides, precise equipment interaction (touchscreen/dial programming), and lab-specific protocol knowledge. Current humanoids lack the precision, sterile technique, and reliable GUI interaction needed for autonomous lab operation.

task: Inspect job sites to determine the extent of maintenance or repairs needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single job site. A competent worker walks the site, assesses conditions, and documents findings — typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating unstructured, often outdoor/rough terrain, multi-modal defect recognition, contextual judgment, and report generation. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor mobility, semantic fault diagnosis, and generalized inspection reasoning at acceptable quality.

task: Drive vehicles with capacities under three tons to transport materials to and from specified destinations, such as railroad stations, plants, residences, offices, or within industrial yards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one delivery trip (pickup to drop-off). Typical light-vehicle delivery trip, including loading/unloading, runs ~30–60 min for a competent driver.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot drive vehicles in traffic. L4 AVs are narrow-domain systems, not general-purpose robots. Navigating varied real-world routes, loading/unloading, and destination access remains far beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Grind meat into hamburger, and into trimmings used to prepare sausages, luncheon meats, and other meat products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grinding one batch (~20–50 lbs) of meat through an industrial grinder, including setup, feeding, collecting output, and basic cleanup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling slippery, deformable raw meat in bulk, operating industrial grinder controls, managing variable chunk sizes, and maintaining hygiene standards. Current humanoids lack reliable wet/slippery object manipulation and force control at this scale.

task: Inspect equipment for wear and for conformance to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single piece of equipment (e.g., a machine or tool). A competent technician checks wear indicators, dimensions, and spec conformance in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can use cameras for surface wear detection, but reliably interpreting spec conformance—using calipers, gauges, or reading tolerances across varied equipment types—requires fine dexterity, contextual judgment, and tool-use generalization well beyond current capability.

task: Drive vehicles or operate machines or equipment to complete work assignments or to assist workers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one work assignment or assist shift (e.g., driving a forklift load across a warehouse, operating a machine run). Median estimated at ~45 min per discrete task cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot drive vehicles or operate heavy/industrial machinery in real unstructured environments. No steering, pedal, or throttle integration; safety and licensing constraints; requires specialized controls beyond general-purpose robot capability.

task: Operate on fetuses, infants, children, and adolescents to correct deformities, repair injuries, prevent and treat diseases, or improve or restore patients' functions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pediatric surgical procedure. Median pediatric surgery (e.g., appendectomy, hernia repair) runs ~2–4 hours including prep and closure; complex cases longer, minor cases shorter.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, real-time clinical judgment, anatomy-specific adaptation, and sterile technique on fragile pediatric tissue. Far beyond any 2026 general-purpose humanoid's capabilities; even surgical robots (da Vinci) are teleoperated, not autonomous.

task: Perform the same work duties as those supervised, or perform more difficult or skilled tasks or assist in their performance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one shift segment where a supervisor steps in to perform or assist with a subordinate's task. Median hands-on duration across typical supervised roles is ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Extremely broad, context-dependent task spanning unknown physical skills. A robot could handle simple pick-and-place or cleaning subtasks but fails on most skilled trades, machinery operation, fine dexterity, or unstructured environments without task-specific training.

task: Remove and replace soiled linens or equipment to maintain sanitary conditions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stripping soiled linens from one bed/unit and replacing with fresh ones; a competent worker completes this in ~8–12 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Linen handling requires grasping large, deformable fabric, navigating tight bed/furniture geometry, and fitting fitted sheets — challenging for current humanoids. Perception of soiled vs. clean and precise tucking further reduce reliability.

task: Examine patient to obtain information on medical condition and surgical risk.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full pre-operative patient examination including history, physical exam, and risk assessment. A competent physician typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced clinical reasoning, nuanced communication, auscultation, palpation, and medical judgment far beyond current robot capabilities. No general-purpose humanoid can conduct or interpret a medical exam autonomously.

task: Replace worn tools, using hand tools, and sharpen dull tools, using bench grinders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing or sharpening one tool. A competent worker spends ~5 min replacing and ~10 min sharpening with a bench grinder, averaging ~15 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation (hand tools, fasteners), precise grinder angle control, tactile feedback for sharpness, and safe grinder operation. Current humanoids lack the precision and force-feedback needed reliably.

task: Perform electron microscopy or mass spectrometry to analyze specimens.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and analyzing a single specimen through one full run (sample prep, instrument setup, data acquisition, initial review). Typically 1–3 hours for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly specialized lab instruments, ultra-precise sample handling (nanoscale tolerances), vacuum chamber loading, calibration, and expert data interpretation. Far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity and domain-specific tool-use capabilities.

task: Perform tests by following physician instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = performing a single diagnostic test (e.g., blood draw, urine sample collection, EKG) per physician order. A trained technician typically takes 10–20 minutes including prep and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (needle insertion, sample handling), patient interaction, interpretation of physician instructions, sterile technique, and adaptive response to patient movement — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Stain tissue specimens with dyes or other chemicals to make cell details visible under microscopes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = staining a batch of tissue slides (e.g., H&E protocol). Includes specimen prep, reagent application, timed incubations, rinsing, and mounting — typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting of reagents onto delicate glass slides, timed multi-step chemical protocols, fine dexterity to avoid damaging fragile tissue, and lab-environment generalization — well beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Lower irons, rams, or pressing heads of machines into position over material to be pressed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lowering a pressing head onto a single piece of material; a skilled operator does this in ~20–30 seconds as part of a press cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise positioning over varied materials, operating machine controls/levers, and applying correct force. General-purpose humanoids lack reliable tool-operation generalization and fine force control for industrial press machinery.

task: Accompany and assist emergency medical technicians on calls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one emergency call, from dispatch to patient handoff. Typical EMS call (response, assessment, treatment, transport) averages ~30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires vehicle transport to scene (robots can't drive), dynamic unstructured environments, complex clinical judgment, fine dexterous medical procedures, patient communication, and physical caregiving — all far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Make templates or cutting tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single template or cutting tool (e.g., a jig, die, or stencil). A skilled tradesperson typically spends 30–60 min measuring, cutting, and finishing one piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, material selection, fine cutting/shaping, and iterative adjustment with specialized tools (saws, grinders, files). Current humanoids lack the precision, tool-use generalization, and dexterous feedback needed for fabrication tasks of this complexity.

task: Perform procedures associated with histochemistry to prepare specimens for immunofluorescence or microscopy.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a batch of specimens through fixation, sectioning, staining/labeling, and mounting. A competent histotechnician typically takes 1–3 hours per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (microtome sectioning, coverslipping, pipetting microliters of reagents), precise chemical sequencing, sterile technique, and specialized lab equipment. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter dexterity and domain-specific tool integration needed.

task: Embed tissue specimens into paraffin wax blocks, or infiltrate tissue specimens with wax.
human_time_rationale: One instance = embedding one tissue specimen into a paraffin block. A skilled histotechnician positions tissue in a mold, fills with wax, and places on cold plate — roughly 5–10 minutes per block.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine manipulation of delicate, small, slippery tissue specimens in molten wax; critical orientation judgment; temperature control; and lab-specific equipment operation. Well beyond current humanoid dexterity and perception for biological specimens.

task: Examine, measure, or weigh cargo or materials to determine specific handling requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining/measuring/weighing a single cargo item or lot. A competent worker reads labels, checks dimensions, uses a scale, and logs requirements in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read labels and operate scales for simple items, but identifying fragility, hazmat markings, irregular shapes, and logging nuanced handling requirements requires robust perception, generalization, and contextual judgment still unreliable in unstructured cargo environments.

task: Prepare substances, such as reagents and dilution, and stains for histological specimens according to protocols.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one batch of reagent/stain per protocol. A competent lab tech typically spends 20–40 min measuring, mixing, and labeling chemicals.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid handling, reading fine graduations on pipettes/cylinders, operating lab instruments (balances, pH meters), interpreting written protocols, and handling hazardous chemicals safely — far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Hang, fold, package, and tag finished articles for delivery to customers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one finished garment/article: hang or fold, package, and attach a tag. A skilled worker averages ~5–10 min per item.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for folding fabric, manipulating hangers, attaching small tags, and neat packaging. Current humanoids struggle with limp/deformable textiles, small fasteners, and multi-step sequencing at acceptable quality.

task: Slide material back and forth over heated, metal, ball-shaped forms to smooth and press portions of garments that cannot be satisfactorily pressed with flat pressers or hand irons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pressing one garment section (e.g., sleeve cap or collar) over a ball form; a skilled presser completes this in roughly 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, adaptive force modulation while sliding fabric over a curved heated form — demanding dexterous bimanual control, heat awareness, and nuanced tactile feedback far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Prepare or use prepared tissue specimens for teaching, research or diagnostic purposes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing or using one tissue specimen (e.g., sectioning, staining, mounting, or examining a slide). A competent histotechnician typically takes 30–60 minutes per specimen end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (microtome sectioning, slide mounting, staining pipettes), sterile handling, precise chemical steps, and visual interpretation of microscopic tissue. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured lab settings.

task: Visit development or work sites to determine projects' effect on traffic and the adequacy of traffic control and safety plans or to suggest traffic control measures.
human_time_rationale: One site visit: travel to site, observe traffic conditions, assess control measures, and document findings. A typical site inspection of this nature takes 1–2 hours for a competent traffic engineer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor navigation on rough/active construction terrain, high-level professional judgment, traffic pattern analysis, report generation, and stakeholder communication — all far beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Finish velvet garments by steaming them on bucks of hot-head presses or steam tables, and brushing pile (nap) with handbrushes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing one velvet garment. A skilled finisher spends ~5–10 min steaming on a buck/steam table and brushing the nap to acceptable quality.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous handbrush strokes on delicate pile fabric, precise pressure/angle control, and steam equipment operation. Current humanoids lack the tactile sensitivity, tool-use finesse, and garment-handling skill needed.

task: Verify alignment of workpieces on machines, using measuring instruments such as rules, gauges, or calipers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking alignment of a single workpiece on a machine. A skilled machinist typically takes 3–8 minutes to measure, interpret readings, and confirm alignment.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can hold some gauges but precise manipulation of calipers/rules against a workpiece, reading analog/digital instruments accurately, and interpreting tolerance conformance in an unstructured machine shop environment remain very challenging for current humanoids.

task: Mount attachments and tools, such as pantographs, engravers, or routers, to perform other operations, such as drilling or boring.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting one attachment (e.g., router, engraver) to a machine. A skilled worker selects, aligns, and secures it in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment, torquing fasteners to spec, and handling diverse tool geometries on varied machines. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force feedback, and generalization for reliable autonomous tool-mounting across attachment types.

task: Place patients on stretchers, and load stretchers into ambulances, usually with assistance from other attendants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning one patient on a stretcher and loading into an ambulance. With a partner, this typically takes ~5–10 minutes including patient prep, safe lifting, and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated multi-robot lifting of a fragile human patient, navigating to an ambulance, and loading via a ramp/lift — all in unstructured, high-stakes environments. Current humanoids lack the strength, coordination, and safety assurances for patient handling.

task: Archive diagnostic material, such as histologic slides and blocks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = archiving a single case's slides and blocks: labeling, logging, and placing into designated storage. A competent histology tech completes this in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise handling of fragile glass slides, reading small alphanumeric labels, navigating dense filing systems, and accurate retrieval/placement in specific slots. Current robots lack the fine dexterity, reliable OCR integration, and spatial indexing for this specialized lab task.

task: Administer first aid, such as bandaging, splinting, or administering oxygen.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single first-aid intervention (e.g., bandaging a wound or applying a splint). A competent responder typically completes one such procedure in ~5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high dexterity for bandaging/splinting on irregular, moving human bodies, real-time clinical assessment, safe oxygen equipment handling, and adaptive pain response—far beyond current humanoid generalization and fine-motor capability.

task: Restrain or shackle violent patients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying physical restraints to one violent patient. Includes assessment, calling for backup, applying restraints, and securing. Typically 5–15 minutes with a team.
