robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic force control on a resisting, unpredictable human, real-time threat assessment, legal/ethical judgment, coordinated multi-person teamwork, and nuanced safe-force application. Far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Freeze tissue specimens.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and placing a single tissue specimen into a freezer/cryogenic storage. Includes labeling, wrapping, and logging. Competent lab tech takes ~10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle delicate, small, slippery tissue samples; precise labeling; cryogenic equipment interaction; sterile technique; and protocol compliance — well beyond current humanoid general-purpose capability.

task: Position materials such as cloth garments, felt, or straw on tables, dies, or feeding mechanisms of pressing machines, or on ironing boards or work tables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning a single garment or material piece on a pressing machine, die, or ironing board. A skilled worker does this in ~1–2 minutes per item.
robot_success_rationale: Soft, deformable textiles (cloth, felt, straw) are notoriously hard for robots to handle and precisely align. Perception of floppy materials, fine repositioning, and alignment to dies or feeding mechanisms exceeds current general-purpose robot dexterity reliably.

task: Inspect and maintain vehicle supplies and equipment, such as gas, oil, water, tires, lights, or brakes, to ensure that vehicles are in proper working condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full pre/post-trip vehicle inspection covering fluids, tires, lights, and brakes for a single vehicle; a competent driver/mechanic takes ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires mobility around a vehicle, fine fluid checks (dipstick, coolant, brake fluid), tire pressure gauges, and brake inspection — highly varied poses, outdoor/garage terrain, tool use, and safety-critical judgment far exceed current humanoid autonomy.

task: Load and unload trucks, vans, or automobiles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading or unloading one typical vehicle (truck/van/car). A competent worker handles a mixed load of boxes/goods in ~25–35 min.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids can pick and place varied items but struggle with confined cargo spaces, heavy/awkward loads, unstable footing in trailers, and the wide variety of item shapes. Locomotion and spatial constraints limit reliability.

task: Finish fancy garments such as evening gowns and costumes, using hand irons to produce high quality finishes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pressing/finishing one garment (evening gown or costume). A skilled finisher typically spends 20–30 minutes per complex garment using a hand iron.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to navigate delicate fabrics, lace, beading, and 3D garment geometry. Pressure, temperature, and angle must adapt continuously. Far beyond current robot capability without damaging fabric.

task: Set up and focus light fixtures to meet requirements of television, theater, concerts, or other productions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and focusing a full lighting rig for a single production; competent lighting technician typically takes 1–3 hours depending on venue size and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working at height on rigging/trusses, precise aiming of fixtures, interpreting creative lighting plots, operating specialized controls (dimmers, DMX boards), and coordinating with a director — far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Embed tissue specimens into paraffin wax blocks, or infiltrate tissue specimens with wax.
human_time_rationale: One instance = embedding/infiltrating one tissue specimen into a paraffin block. A skilled histotechnician takes ~10–20 min per specimen including mold setup, orientation, and cooling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tissue orientation in molten wax, fine forceps dexterity, thermal management, and lab-instrument operation. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation, temperature awareness, and tissue-handling delicacy needed. No known deployments.

task: Spray water over fabric to soften fibers when not using steam irons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = spraying a single garment or fabric piece before ironing; a competent worker takes ~1–2 minutes to evenly mist the fabric.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can hold a spray bottle and aim at fabric, but achieving even, controlled misting across the whole garment requires nuanced grip force, nozzle angle adjustment, and coverage judgment — challenging for current humanoids.

task: Finish pleated garments, determining sizes of pleats from evidence of old pleats or from work orders, using machine presses or hand irons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing one pleated garment. A skilled presser inspects old pleat evidence or reads a work order, sets pleat sizes, and presses/irons the garment — typically 8–15 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting subtle fabric evidence of old pleats, reading work orders, precisely measuring and forming pleats, and applying controlled heat/pressure with a hand iron or press. Current humanoids lack fine textile perception, garment-specific spatial reasoning, and safe heat-tool handling at acceptable quality.

task: Insert heated metal forms into ties and touch up rough places with hand irons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pressing/shaping one necktie with heated forms and hand irons. A skilled presser completes this in ~2–4 minutes per tie.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to insert heated metal forms into a delicate fabric tie, precise pressure/angle control with hand irons, and heat-sensitive touch-up — far beyond current general-purpose robot dexterity and tactile feedback capabilities.

task: Moisten materials to soften and smooth them.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying moisture (spray, sponge, or dip) to a batch of material (e.g., fabric, leather, clay). A skilled worker completes this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can grasp a spray bottle or sponge and apply liquid, but judging correct moisture level, even coverage, and material sensitivity requires nuanced tactile/visual feedback current robots lack reliably.

task: Resolve problems with laboratory equipment and instruments, such as microscopes, mass spectrometers, microtomes, immunostainers, tissue processors, embedding centers, and water baths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and resolving one equipment problem. Competent lab tech typically spends 20–90 min depending on complexity; 45 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized domain knowledge, nuanced fault diagnosis, reading instrument software/error codes, fine dexterous adjustment of optics/electronics, and judgment calls. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capability.

task: Straighten, smooth, or shape materials to prepare them for pressing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single piece/batch of material (e.g., a fabric panel or sheet). A skilled worker takes ~5 min to straighten, smooth, and shape it adequately for pressing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced bimanual dexterous manipulation of deformable, flexible materials. Robots struggle with perception and fine force control on limp/soft items; consistent shaping to press-ready quality is beyond reliable current capability.

task: Cut sections of body tissues for microscopic examination, using microtomes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sectioning a single tissue block: embedding, trimming, and cutting 4–6 micron sections onto slides. A skilled histotechnician typically takes ~10–20 minutes per block.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-precise micron-level dexterity to operate a microtome, float and mount delicate tissue ribbons on slides, and make fine tactile adjustments — far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity and haptic sensitivity.

task: Cut sections of body tissues for microscopic examination, using microtomes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sectioning one tissue block: orient, clamp, trim, cut ribbon sections, float on water bath, mount on slides. A skilled histotech takes ~10–20 min per block.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-micron precision cutting, delicate tissue handling, water-bath float pickup, and expert visual feedback to detect folds/tears. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and fine-motor perception capabilities.

task: Clean and sanitize tools and work environment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and sanitizing a typical set of hand tools and a workbench/work area. A competent worker wipes down surfaces, cleans tools, and applies sanitizer in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can wipe flat surfaces moderately well, but handling varied tool shapes, applying appropriate cleaning agents, reaching all surfaces, and verifying cleanliness requires dexterity, generalization, and judgment that current humanoids lack reliably.

task: Replace supplies and disposable items on ambulances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fully restocking one ambulance after a call. A competent EMT checks compartments, retrieves supplies from a storage room, and loads/organizes them, typically taking 20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a cramped ambulance interior, identifying dozens of varied medical supply types, opening latched compartments, reading labels, and organizing items precisely. Cluttered/confined space, fine manipulation of small medical items, and high task variety push success very low for current humanoids.

task: Sew ends of new material to leaders or to ends of material in pressing machines, using sewing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sewing one set of ends together. A skilled textile worker aligns and sews one join in ~3–7 minutes depending on material width.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise handling of limp, flexible textile material, threading/operating an industrial sewing machine, and accurate seam alignment — extremely fine dexterity and tool-use far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Brush materials made of suede, leather, or felt to remove spots or to raise and smooth naps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = brushing one garment or item. A competent worker inspects, selects the right brush, and works the material in brief strokes, typically taking 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine tactile feedback to judge nap direction and spot removal pressure on delicate materials (suede, leather, felt). Current robots lack the nuanced force control and material perception needed; high risk of damage.

task: Select, install, and adjust machine components, including pressing forms, rollers, and guides, using hoists and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting, installing, and adjusting one set of components (e.g., a roller/guide swap on a machine). A skilled machine operator typically takes 30–60 min per changeover.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating hoists, selecting correct heavy components, precise mechanical fitting/torquing with hand tools, and fine adjustment under load — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use generalization, and heavy-load manipulation limits.

task: Maintain laboratory equipment, such as microscopes, mass spectrometers, microtomes, immunostainers, tissue processors, embedding centers, and water baths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = routine maintenance of one piece of lab equipment (e.g., cleaning a microscope, draining/refilling a water bath, calibrating a mass spectrometer component). ~45 min median across the equipment variety listed.
robot_success_rationale: Lab maintenance requires fine dexterity (lens cleaning, reagent handling), expert tool use, precise calibration procedures, and interpretation of instrument readouts. Current humanoids lack the precision, specialized tool familiarity, and contextual judgment required across this diverse equipment set.

task: Shrink, stretch, or block articles by hand to conform to original measurements, using forms, blocks, and steam.
human_time_rationale: One instance = blocking/shaping one garment or textile article. A skilled worker spends ~15–25 min wetting, stretching/shrinking, pinning to a form, and steam-setting it.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced bimanual force control to stretch/shrink fabric to exact measurements, precise steam tool handling, tactile feedback for tension, and accurate form-pinning — all far beyond current humanoid dexterity and sensory capability.

task: Examine slides under microscopes to ensure tissue preparation meets laboratory requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one slide. A trained histotechnician spends ~3–7 min checking staining, tissue integrity, and section quality per slide.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a microscope (fine focus, stage controls), interpreting histological image quality, and making expert QC judgments. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, microscope-operation skill, and domain-specific visual AI integration needed for autonomous lab use.

task: Perform procedures, such as linear tomography, mammography, sonograms, joint and cyst aspirations, routine contrast studies, routine fluoroscopy, or examinations of the head, trunk, or extremities under supervision of physician.
human_time_rationale: One imaging procedure instance (e.g., a mammogram or fluoroscopy exam). Competent radiologic tech typically takes 20–45 min including patient prep, positioning, imaging, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine patient handling, precise equipment positioning, clinical judgment, sterile technique for aspirations, real-time adaptation to patient anatomy, and physician-supervised decision-making — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Activate and adjust machine controls to regulate temperature and pressure of rollers, ironing shoes, or plates, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = activating machine and adjusting controls to reach target temp/pressure settings. A skilled operator reads specs and dials in settings in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting spec sheets, and manipulating varied knobs/levers precisely. Perception of displays and fine control-adjustment generalization remain weak for frontier humanoids in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Make exposures necessary for the requested procedures, rejecting and repeating work that does not meet established standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one imaging exposure (e.g., X-ray), including positioning, exposure, quality review, and repeat if substandard. ~10–20 min per view is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise patient/object positioning, operating specialized radiographic equipment, assessing image quality diagnostically, and repeating under clinical standards — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, medical tool integration, and quality-judgment capability.

task: Block or shape knitted garments after cleaning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = blocking one knitted garment. Involves wetting, shaping, pinning to measurements, and setting to dry — typically 15–25 minutes of active work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced tactile judgment to wet, stretch, and pin delicate knit fabric to precise measurements without damage. Fine dexterity with pins, deformable materials, and spatial pattern-matching far exceed current robot capabilities.

task: Explain procedures and observe patients to ensure safety and comfort during scan.
human_time_rationale: One instance = briefing and monitoring one patient through a single imaging scan (e.g., MRI/CT). Includes explanation, positioning checks, and observation throughout the scan.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced verbal communication, reading subtle patient distress cues, adaptive reassurance, and clinical judgment. Current robots lack reliable natural language interaction, empathetic response, and medical-context generalization needed for autonomous patient care.

task: Select and install cutting tools and other accessories according to specifications, using hand tools or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting and installing one cutting tool/accessory (e.g., drill bit, end mill, lathe insert) onto a machine using hand or power tools; a skilled machinist takes ~5–15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise identification of correct tool from a set, fine dexterous manipulation (tool holders, set screws, torque specs), and tool-use with wrenches/power drivers. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity and tool-use generalization for this precision work.

task: Position imaging equipment and adjust controls to set exposure time and distance, according to specification of examination.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning equipment and adjusting controls for a single examination setup. A trained radiologic tech typically takes 3–7 minutes per patient/exam prep.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise repositioning of heavy medical imaging equipment, fine control panel adjustments, reading exam specifications, and patient-context awareness. Current humanoids lack medical device interfacing, fine dial/button control reliability, and safe operation in clinical environments.

task: Operate or oversee operation of radiologic or magnetic imaging equipment to produce images of the body for diagnostic purposes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient imaging session (setup, scan, quality check). Typical radiologic exam (X-ray to MRI) takes 15–45 min for a competent technologist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized medical imaging consoles, positioning patients safely, communicating complex instructions, and making real-time clinical judgments. No current general-purpose humanoid can perform any of these steps reliably or safely.

task: Lay out pavement markings for striping crews.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out markings for a single road segment or parking lot section. A competent worker measures, snaps chalk lines, and marks reference points; ~45–90 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor locomotion on uneven pavement, precise measurement and chalk/marking tool use, reading engineering plans, and coordinating over large distances. No current humanoid combines all these reliably outdoors at job-site quality.

task: Stain tissue specimens with dyes or other chemicals to make cell details visible under microscopes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = staining a batch of tissue slides through a standard protocol (e.g., H&E). Includes fixation, reagent baths, rinsing, and coverslipping; ~30–60 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting, handling fragile glass slides, timed reagent baths, and fine dexterous manipulation in a lab setting. Current humanoids lack the fine motor precision and lab-tool generalization needed; highly specialized automated stainers exist but are not general-purpose robots.

task: Position patient on examining table and set up and adjust equipment to obtain optimum view of specific body area as requested by physician.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning one patient and adjusting imaging equipment for a single exam. Typical radiologic technologist workflow takes ~5–15 minutes per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safe physical handling of a human patient, nuanced communication, precise medical equipment adjustment, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, safety certification, and situational awareness for unassisted patient care.

task: Use radiation safety measures and protection devices to comply with government regulations and to ensure safety of patients and staff.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying/verifying radiation safety measures for a single patient procedure or shift setup, including donning PPE, checking shielding, and logging compliance. ~15–25 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading dosimeters, interpreting regulatory compliance protocols, adjusting shielding, donning lead aprons, and making clinical safety judgments — far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and domain-specific cognition.

task: Perform emergency repairs, such as changing tires or installing light bulbs, fuses, tire chains, or spark plugs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one emergency repair event (e.g., changing a tire ~20–30 min, swapping a fuse/bulb ~5–10 min). Median across repair types ~25 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force torque on lug nuts, fine insertion of fuses/spark plugs, adapting to varied vehicles and unstructured roadside environments. Current humanoids lack the force control, tool-use generalization, and situational adaptability needed.

task: Provide assistance to physicians or other technologists in the performance of more complex procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting during one complex medical procedure (e.g., imaging scan, surgical prep, interventional study); typical duration 30–60 min for a technologist assistant.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity with sterile instruments, real-time adaptive response to clinical cues, safe patient handling, and communication with physicians — far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured clinical environments.

task: Remove finished pieces from pressing machines and hang or stack them for cooling, or forward them for additional processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one pressed piece from the machine and hanging/stacking or forwarding it. A skilled operator completes this in ~1–2 minutes per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping hot, varied, flexible or rigid pieces from a machine, navigating tight press clearances, and precisely hanging/stacking. Heat, varied shapes, and machine interaction reduce reliability significantly for current humanoids.

task: Inspect bridges, dams, highways, buildings, wiring, plumbing, electrical circuits, sewers, heating systems, or foundations during and after construction for structural quality, general safety, or conformance to specifications and codes.
human_time_rationale: One inspection instance = one site visit (e.g., a building floor, bridge segment, or utility system). A competent inspector typically spends 60–120 minutes on-site per inspection event.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level judgment on codes/specs, navigation across highly varied and hazardous environments (scaffolding, trenches, crawlspaces), fine visual/tactile discrimination, and legally defensible documentation — far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Align and stitch or glue materials such as fabric, fleece, leather, or wood, to join parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning and joining one pair/set of material pieces (e.g., two fabric panels). A competent worker takes ~10–20 min depending on material and method.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to align flexible/deformable materials (fabric, leather) and operate sewing or gluing tools precisely. Deformable material handling and seam alignment are major unsolved challenges for 2026 humanoids.

task: Perform or schedule repairs or preventive maintenance of vehicles or other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one repair or PM service on one vehicle/piece of equipment. A typical oil change or scheduled PM takes ~30–90 min; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly variable, fine dexterous work (draining fluids, torquing bolts, routing cables) plus diagnostic judgment and specialized tools. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, domain tool-use, and generalization for autonomous vehicle repair.

task: Dress and otherwise finish boots or shoes, as by trimming the edges of new soles and heels to the shoe shape.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing one pair of shoes/boots; trimming, edge dressing, and heel shaping takes a skilled cobbler ~10–15 minutes per pair.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity with sharp/rotary tools along irregular curved edges, tactile feedback for consistent depth, and tool-switching. Far beyond current humanoid manipulation capability at acceptable quality.

task: Use covering cloths to prevent equipment from damaging delicate fabrics.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing/arranging covering cloths over equipment before working on a delicate fabric item. A skilled worker takes ~2–4 minutes to position and secure cloths properly.
robot_success_rationale: Draping and tucking soft, flexible cloths over irregular equipment edges requires dexterous manipulation and spatial judgment. Current humanoids struggle with limp fabric control, precise placement, and confirming full coverage of delicate areas.

task: Operate computerized laboratory equipment to dehydrate, decalcify, or microincinerate tissue samples.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a batch of tissue samples into the equipment, setting parameters, and starting the run. Active hands-on time ~15–25 min; the automated process runs unattended.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of small, fragile tissue samples and cassettes, navigating a specialized lab environment, operating complex touchscreen/control interfaces, and meeting strict precision standards. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control and domain-specific tool fluency needed.

task: Push and pull irons over surfaces of articles to smooth or shape them.
human_time_rationale: One instance = ironing a single garment (e.g., a shirt). A competent worker typically irons one shirt in ~4–6 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Ironing requires precise force control, heat management, cloth manipulation on a compliant surface, and adapting to varied fabric shapes. Current humanoids lack the fine force-compliance and perception needed to avoid scorching or missing creases reliably.

task: Operate counters and record data to assess the volume, type, and movement of vehicular or pedestrian traffic at specified times.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single counting session at a location (typically 1–2 hours). A competent human operates a tally counter, classifies traffic, and logs data for ~2 hours per standard traffic study shift.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based vehicle/pedestrian detection is mature, but a general-purpose humanoid must physically operate handheld counters, navigate outdoor/roadside terrain, record structured data, and sustain attention for hours — integration of all steps autonomously remains unreliable.

task: Slit open, eviscerate, and trim carcasses of slaughtered animals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one animal carcass. A skilled slaughterhouse worker can eviscerate and trim a single hog or beef carcass in roughly 5–12 minutes depending on species and line speed.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly precise, force-sensitive cutting through variable soft tissue and organs in a wet, slippery environment. Anatomy varies per animal. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, specialized tool handling, and perception needed for safe, consistent evisceration at acceptable quality.

task: Measure fabric to specifications, cut uneven edges with shears, fold material, and press it with an iron to form a heading.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one fabric heading (measure, cut, fold, press). A skilled seamstress/tailor completes this in ~10–15 minutes depending on fabric width.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, shear cutting of flexible fabric, multi-step folding to exact tolerances, and controlled iron pressing — all highly dexterous tasks on deformable material that exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Attach insoles to shoe lasts, affix shoe uppers, and apply heels and outsoles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one pair of shoes (attaching insoles to lasts, lasting the uppers, and applying heels and outsoles). A skilled cobbler/assembler takes ~20–30 minutes per pair.
robot_success_rationale: Shoe assembly demands extreme fine dexterity, precise force control for lasting, adhesive application, and manipulation of flexible/irregular materials. Current humanoids lack the tactile sensitivity and specialized tool-use needed for acceptable quality.

task: Transport patients to or from exam rooms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving one patient from origin to exam room or back, including positioning, guiding/wheeling, and handoff. Typical trip takes 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safely assisting/guiding a patient (frail, unpredictable, may need wheelchair or walking support), navigating crowded clinic halls, communicating, and performing safe handoff. Far beyond current humanoid caregiving capability.

task: Measure dimensions and verify level, alignment, or elevation of structures or fixtures to ensure compliance to building plans and codes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and verifying a single structure or fixture (e.g., a wall, door frame, or beam). A competent inspector uses tape, level, and plans; ~20–30 min per element.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting building plans, wielding tape measures/levels precisely, navigating cluttered construction sites, and making compliance judgments. Perception and tool-use in unstructured environments remain weak; construction-site mobility is poor (~30–60%).

task: Maintain or make minor adjustments or field repairs to equipment used in surveys, including the replacement of parts on traffic data gathering devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing/repairing one survey device in the field (e.g., replacing a tube counter or sensor on a traffic data unit). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 minutes per device.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor terrain navigation, identifying fault, fine dexterous manipulation of small fasteners/wiring, and judgment for diagnosis. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor mobility, tool-use for electrical/mechanical repair, and contextual fault diagnosis at acceptable quality.

task: Examine and measure finished articles to verify conformance to standards, using measuring devices such as tape measures and micrometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single finished article. A competent worker selects appropriate tools, takes multiple measurements, and compares to spec — typically 5–12 minutes per part.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with selecting correct measuring tools, positioning micrometers/tape measures precisely on arbitrary part geometries, reading analog gauges, and contextually judging conformance. Perception and fine dexterity are key bottlenecks.

task: Clean tables or counters after patrons have finished dining.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing and wiping down one table/counter after diners leave. A competent busser typically takes 2–4 minutes per table.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a crowded restaurant floor, grasping varied dishes/glasses, stacking items safely, then wiping surfaces. Clutter variability, fragile items, and tight spaces challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Set up examination rooms, ensuring that all necessary equipment is ready.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one examination room. A competent medical aide checks, arranges, and restocks all equipment and supplies in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse dexterous manipulation (instruments, drawers, packaging), contextual inventory judgment, sterile-field awareness, and navigating a cluttered clinical space — far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Monitor patients' conditions and reactions, reporting abnormal signs to physician.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient monitoring round, including vitals check, observation, and reporting any abnormal signs. A competent nurse typically spends ~15–25 minutes per patient per round.
robot_success_rationale: Requires integrating multi-modal clinical perception (visual, tactile, auditory, vital-sign interpretation), nuanced patient communication, contextual medical judgment, and reliable escalation — well beyond 2026 humanoid generalization and safety thresholds.

task: Prepare materials and classrooms for class activities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one classroom for one class session; gathering materials, arranging desks/chairs, setting up supplies. Typical prep takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a varied classroom, moving chairs/desks, distributing mixed materials (papers, books, supplies), and interpreting a lesson plan. Layout diversity, object variety, and contextual judgment are well beyond current robot generalization.

task: Shape and smooth ends of nails, using scissors, files, or emery boards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shaping and smoothing all nails on one client's hands (~10 nails). A competent nail technician typically spends 5–10 minutes filing and shaping a full set.
robot_success_rationale: Extreme fine dexterity required near sensitive tissue on an unpredictably shaped, compliant surface. Current robots lack the tactile sensitivity, sub-millimeter precision, and safe force control needed to file human nails without injury risk.

task: Make, modify, and repair orthopedic or therapeutic footwear according to doctors' prescriptions, or modify existing footwear for people with foot problems and special needs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating or significantly modifying a single pair of orthopedic/therapeutic footwear. Skilled orthotists/pedorthists typically spend 1–3 hours per pair including fitting, cutting, stitching, and adjusting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (stitching leather, shaping lasts, precision cutting), reading/interpreting medical prescriptions, patient-specific fitting decisions, and use of specialized cobbling tools. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Lay out wall patterns or foundations, using straight edge, rule, or staked lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out one wall segment or foundation line. Involves measuring, marking, and staking a single continuous layout run; typically 30–60 min for a competent mason/carpenter.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise outdoor navigation on uneven terrain, reading blueprints, using tape measures/chalk lines/stakes with fine tool-use, and integrating spatial reasoning. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor dexterity and precision construction tool-use.

task: Fabricate patient immobilization devices, such as molds or casts, for radiation delivery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single immobilization device (e.g., thermoplastic mask or body mold) for one patient. Includes fitting, molding, and curing, typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of thermoplastic materials on a live patient, precise anatomical contouring, patient communication/safety, and clinical judgment. Far beyond 2026 robot capabilities in unstructured caregiving and fine dexterity.

task: Finish pants, jackets, shirts, skirts and other dry-cleaned and laundered articles, using hand irons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing one garment (e.g. a shirt or pair of pants) with a hand iron; a competent presser takes ~5–12 minutes depending on garment complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Garment finishing requires repositioning limp, deformable fabric with precision, applying correct heat/pressure, avoiding scorching, and navigating complex 3D shapes (collars, seams, pleats). Far beyond current robot dexterity and cloth manipulation capabilities.

task: Decorate clients' nails by piercing or attaching ornaments or designs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = decorating one client's full set of nails (10 nails) with ornaments/piercings. A skilled nail tech takes ~10–20 min for ornament application.
robot_success_rationale: Extremely fine dexterity required — piercing tiny nails, placing sub-millimeter gems/foils on curved, compliant surfaces of a live person. Current robots lack the precision, force control, and safe human-contact capability needed.

task: Cut out parts, following patterns or outlines, using knives, shears, scissors, or machine presses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting out one pattern piece (e.g., a fabric panel or leather part). A skilled worker cuts one piece in ~5–10 minutes depending on complexity and tool used.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool control (scissors/knife/shears) along irregular outlines on deformable materials. Fine dexterity, force regulation, and pattern-following perception are at the edge of 2026 robot capability; machine press operation adds mechanical complexity.

task: Cement, nail, or sew soles and heels to shoes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching sole and heel to one shoe. A skilled cobbler/assembler takes ~10–15 min per shoe using cement, nails, or stitching.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise multi-modal dexterity (cementing, nailing, or sewing flexible, irregularly shaped soft materials under tension). Far beyond current humanoid fine-motor and tool-use capabilities; no commercial demos exist.

task: Use and maintain the tools or equipment found on commercial vehicles, such as weighing or measuring devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = using/maintaining a single weighing or measuring device on a commercial vehicle (e.g., onboard scale check, calibration, or minor maintenance). A competent operator takes ~10 minutes per occurrence.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating on or around a commercial vehicle, reading specialized gauges/scales, performing calibration or maintenance procedures. Current humanoids lack the vehicle-specific tool familiarity, fine dexterity for calibration, and safe mobility around large vehicles needed for autonomous success.

task: Roughen surfaces of fingernails, using abrasive wheel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = roughening all 10 fingernails of one client using an abrasive wheel. A skilled nail tech completes this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity on a very small, curved, living surface with safety-critical force control. Risk of injury to client, tiny target, and high precision needed far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Smooth, polish, and bevel surfaces, using hand tools and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = smoothing/polishing/beveling one workpiece surface. A competent worker spends ~20–30 min selecting tools, setting up, and finishing a typical surface to acceptable quality.
robot_success_rationale: Requires force-controlled contact, fine dexterity, and real-time tactile/visual feedback to judge surface finish quality. Tool switching and adaptive pressure control on varied geometries exceed current humanoid capabilities reliably.

task: Dye, soak, polish, paint, stamp, stitch, stain, buff, or engrave leather or other materials to obtain desired effects, decorations, or shapes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing one leather finishing/decoration operation (e.g., dyeing and buffing a wallet panel). Skilled artisan takes ~30–60 min depending on technique complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterity for stitching, stamping, and engraving; tool-switching across many specialized instruments; tactile feedback for pressure/saturation control; and artistic judgment. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robots.

task: Mix mortar or grout and pour or spread mortar or grout on marble slabs, stone, or foundation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing a batch of mortar/grout and spreading it on one section of slabs/stone. A competent mason takes ~45 min including mixing, consistency checks, and spreading.
robot_success_rationale: Requires mixing viscous material to correct consistency, controlled pouring/spreading with a trowel under variable load, fine tactile feedback, and working on uneven surfaces. Well beyond current humanoid dexterity and force-control capabilities.

task: Set vertical and horizontal alignment of structures, using plumb bob, gauge line, and level.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning a single structural element (e.g., a column or wall section). A competent tradesperson typically takes 15–25 min to set, check, and confirm alignment with plumb bob, gauge, and level.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling specialized tools (plumb bob, gauge line, spirit level) on an active construction site, interpreting readings, and making fine physical adjustments to structures. Unstructured environment, heavy loads, precision coordination, and tool dexterity far exceed current humanoid capabilities.

task: Operate fluoroscope to aid physician to view and guide wire or catheter through blood vessels to area of interest.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one fluoroscopic procedure session. A competent radiologic technologist typically operates the fluoroscope for 20–45 minutes per interventional case.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized medical imaging equipment, interpreting live fluoroscopic imagery, coordinating in real-time with a physician in a sterile field, and making safety-critical adjustments. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in perception, tool-use, and clinical judgment.

task: Measure the amount of radioactivity in patients or equipment, using radiation monitoring devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scanning a single patient or piece of equipment with a radiation monitoring device; a competent technician typically completes this in ~10 minutes including setup and recording.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise positioning of a handheld detector close to a patient or equipment, interpreting readings, and adapting to patient anatomy. Current humanoids lack reliable fine sensor-tool handling, compliant patient interaction, and domain-specific nuclear medicine protocols.

task: Clean and maintain pressing machines, using cleaning solutions and lubricants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and lubricating one pressing machine. Wiping surfaces, applying cleaning solution, and lubricating key components typically takes ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise application of liquids/lubricants to specific machine ports, navigating around bulky industrial equipment, fine tool-use, and task sequencing. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous fluid application and industrial machine familiarity.

task: Operate mobile x-ray equipment in operating room, emergency room, or at patient's bedside.
human_time_rationale: One instance = transporting portable X-ray unit to patient location, positioning equipment, taking exposure(s), and clearing. Typical bedside/ER/OR exam takes ~15–25 minutes end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating cluttered clinical environments, precisely positioning a bulky wheeled machine relative to a patient, adjusting detector/tube angles, operating console settings, ensuring radiation safety, and responding to patient condition — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Set stone or marble in place, according to layout or pattern.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting one stone/marble tile or slab in place per a layout. A competent mason takes ~30–60 min per piece including positioning, leveling, and fitting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting of irregular slabs, precise pattern-matching perception, mortar/adhesive tool use, leveling, and fine spatial alignment on varied surfaces. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and load capacity in unstructured settings.

task: Repair and recondition leather products such as trunks, luggage, shoes, saddles, belts, purses, and baseball gloves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/reconditioning one leather item (e.g., a shoe or purse). Includes cleaning, assessing damage, stitching, conditioning, and finishing. Median across item types.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly fine dexterous manipulation (stitching, cutting, cementing leather), nuanced tactile feedback, and adaptive assessment of varied damage. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured, multi-step craft tasks.

task: Develop requirements for the use of patient immobilization devices and positioning aides, such as molds or casts, as part of treatment plans to ensure accurate delivery of radiation and comfort of patient.
human_time_rationale: One instance = developing immobilization device requirements for one patient's treatment plan. A radiation therapist/dosimetrist reviews anatomy, selects devices, and documents specs; ~45 min per patient.
robot_success_rationale: Task is almost entirely clinical cognition, medical judgment, and documentation — reviewing imaging, interpreting anatomy, writing requirements. No meaningful physical manipulation component; well beyond current robot autonomy in clinical reasoning.

task: Administer first aid treatment or life support care to sick or injured persons in prehospital settings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single prehospital emergency response, from patient contact to handoff. Typical EMS/first-responder scene time is roughly 15–35 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic clinical judgment, fine dexterity (IV lines, airway management), adaptation to unstructured trauma scenes, and patient communication. Far beyond 2026 frontier robot capability; high risk of patient harm.

task: Provide assistance in dressing or changing seriously ill or injured patients or patients with disabilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dressing/changing one patient. A competent caregiver typically takes 10–20 minutes depending on patient condition, clothing type, and mobility level.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme gentleness, adaptive force control around fragile/injured bodies, complex garment manipulation on a non-static human, real-time patient feedback interpretation, and safe mobility around a bed — all well beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Place and secure automatic counters, using power tools, and retrieve counters after counting periods end.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing, securing, and retrieving one automatic counter at a single location, including power-tool fastening and post-period removal; ~20 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise positioning, operating a power drill/driver (tool-use generalization), navigating varied outdoor/roadside terrain, and multi-step sequencing over days. Current humanoids lack robust outdoor mobility, reliable power-tool use, and long-horizon task autonomy.

task: Perform quality assurance system checks, such as calibrations, on treatment planning computers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full QA session on a single treatment planning computer, including calibration checks, test plan runs, and documentation. A competent medical physicist typically takes 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires deep domain expertise in medical physics, interpreting specialized software outputs, running test protocols, and making calibration judgments. Current humanoids lack the cognitive reasoning, software interaction, and regulatory-grade decision-making needed.

task: Remove previously applied nail polish, using liquid remover and swabs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing nail polish from all 10 fingernails. A competent person soaks and wipes each nail, typically finishing in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity: gripping a small swab, applying precise pressure to curved nail surfaces without touching skin, and controlled liquid application. Current humanoids lack the fingertip sensitivity and precision for this delicate task.

task: Perform such medical procedures as emergent cricothyrotomy, endotracheal intubation, and emergency thoracotomy.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one emergent cricothyrotomy procedure; a skilled clinician completes it in ~2–5 min, but setup and confirmation bring median to ~8 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, anatomical landmark identification under stress, sterile technique, force-sensitive blade control, and real-time clinical judgment. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Whiten underside of nails with white paste or pencils.
human_time_rationale: One instance = whitening undersides of all 10 nails on one person. A skilled nail technician applies paste/pencil under each nail tip in ~2–4 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine-motor precision to apply paste/pencil under a 1–2mm nail edge on a compliant, moving human hand. Current robots lack the dexterity, delicate force control, and sub-millimeter tool manipulation needed without risk of injury.

task: Press ties on small pressing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pressing one necktie on a small pressing machine. A skilled laundry/garment worker completes this in ~1–3 minutes including positioning and finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Ties are thin, flexible, easily deformed fabric requiring precise alignment on a specialty press. Robots struggle with delicate textile handling, exact placement, and operating a foot/lever-controlled pressing machine safely without damaging the garment.

task: Assess nature and extent of illness or injury to establish and prioritize medical procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing a single patient; a competent EMT/nurse performs triage, history-taking, and physical assessment in ~5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced clinical reasoning, verbal history-taking, pain/distress interpretation, dynamic physical examination, and prioritization judgment. Current robots lack medical cognition, reliable vital-sign sensing on arbitrary patients, and safe physical exam dexterity.

task: Re-sew seams, and replace handles and linings of suitcases or handbags.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing one bag (re-sewing seams + replacing handle + relining). A skilled leatherworker/tailor typically takes 30–60 min per bag.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity: threading needles, sewing through thick leather/fabric, attaching hardware, and fitting linings precisely. Far beyond current humanoid manipulation capability; akin to fine craft work.

task: Place shoes on lasts to remove soles and heels, using knives or pliers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting one shoe on a last then removing its sole and heel with knives/pliers. A skilled cobbler typically completes this in 5–10 minutes per shoe.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual dexterity to seat a shoe on a last, grip irregularly shaped footwear, apply controlled knife/plier force at seams, and detect when material separates — far beyond 2026 frontier robot capability reliably and safely.

task: Drill or punch holes and insert or attach metal rings, handles, and fastening hardware, such as buckles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling/punching one hole and attaching one piece of hardware (ring, handle, or buckle) to a workpiece; a skilled worker completes this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool use (drill/punch), fine dexterous alignment of small metal hardware, and secure fastening. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-motor tool handling and hardware insertion accuracy needed for acceptable quality.

task: Immobilize patient for placement on stretcher and ambulance transport, using backboard or other spinal immobilization device.
human_time_rationale: One instance = immobilizing one patient with a backboard/spinal device and loading onto a stretcher. A trained EMT team typically completes this in 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, adaptive manipulation on a fragile human body in unstructured environments, coordinated multi-person technique, spinal precautions, real-time patient feedback, and device fastening. Far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Lay brick to build shells of chimneys and smokestacks or to line or reline industrial furnaces, kilns, boilers and similar installations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a standard chimney shell or furnace lining job. A competent mason typically spends a full 8-hour shift on one such structural bricklaying project.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise mortar application, heavy/irregular refractory bricks, tight tolerances in curved/vertical geometries, high-heat environments, and scaffolding navigation. Far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity, load capacity, and generalization.

task: Replace broken or missing masonry units in walls or floors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing a single broken/missing masonry unit (brick, block, or tile). Includes chiseling out old unit, cleaning cavity, mixing/applying mortar, setting new unit, and pointing joints.
robot_success_rationale: Requires forceful chiseling, precise mortar mixing/application, fine joint pointing, and surface-matching perception. Current humanoids lack the force control, dexterity, and tool-use generalization needed for masonry repair at acceptable quality.

task: Drill holes in marble or ornamental stone and anchor brackets in holes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling and anchoring brackets into one hole location in marble/stone. Marking, drilling, clearing dust, inserting anchor, and securing bracket takes ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled drilling into hard stone with specialized bits, managing vibration and dust, then seating anchors and fastening brackets — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and fine force-feedback capabilities.

task: Clean and polish shoes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and polishing one pair of shoes. A competent human removes dirt, applies polish, and buffs to a shine in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex multi-step dexterous manipulation (brushing, applying polish with cloth, buffing) on irregular curved surfaces, force-sensitive compliance, and tool-switching. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity and adaptive force control for this.

task: Apply undercoat and clear or colored polish onto nails with brush.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying undercoat + polish to all 10 fingernails on one person. A competent nail technician takes ~15–25 minutes including drying pauses.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine motor precision on curved, tiny, irregular nail surfaces while avoiding skin. Current robots lack the sub-millimeter dexterity, compliant brush control, and adaptive perception needed for acceptable quality.

task: Administer first aid treatment or life support care to sick or injured persons in prehospital settings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single prehospital emergency response, from patient contact to handoff. Typical EMS scene time is ~15–35 minutes; 25 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic clinical judgment, fine dexterity (IVs, airway management), adapting to uncontrolled environments, and real-time patient assessment. Far beyond current humanoid capability in safety-critical, unstructured prehospital settings.

task: Repair cracked or chipped areas of stone or marble, using blowtorch and mastic, and remove rough or defective spots from concrete, using power grinder or chisel and hammer.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing one cracked/chipped area on stone/marble or grinding one defective concrete spot. A skilled mason typically takes 30–60 min per repair site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (blowtorch, mastic application, power grinder, chisel/hammer), precise tactile feedback for surface quality judgment, and safe flame/heat management — all far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capabilities.

task: Fabricate beam modifying devices, such as compensators, shields, and wedge filters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating one beam-modifying device (compensator, shield, or wedge filter). A competent radiation therapist/dosimetrist typically spends 30–60 min cutting, shaping, casting, or assembling one custom device.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly precise dexterous fabrication (casting, machining, or shaping dense materials like Cerrobend) with tight tolerances, domain-specific tooling, quality verification, and safety protocols for hazardous materials — well beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capabilities.

task: Assess nature and extent of illness or injury to establish and prioritize medical procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing a single patient's condition (history, vitals, observation, triage decision). A competent EMT/nurse typically completes an initial assessment in ~5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced clinical judgment, patient communication, pain/symptom interpretation, and prioritization under uncertainty. Current robots lack reliable medical perception, diagnostic reasoning, and safe patient interaction for autonomous triage.

task: Prepare contrast material, radiopharmaceuticals, or anesthetic or antispasmodic drugs under the direction of a radiologist.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single dose/batch of contrast, radiopharmaceutical, or drug for one patient/procedure; a trained radiologic technologist typically takes ~5–15 minutes including measurement, mixing, labeling, and safety checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-dexterous manipulation (syringes, vials, sterile technique), strict aseptic protocol, reading small labels/dosing calculations, and following real-time radiologist direction — far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured clinical settings.

task: Shape shoe heels with a knife, and sand them on a buffing wheel for smoothness.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shaping and sanding one shoe heel. A skilled cobbler spends ~5–10 minutes per heel trimming with a knife and buffing for smoothness.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, force-controlled knife work on curved surfaces and controlled contact with a spinning buffing wheel — extreme fine dexterity, tactile feedback, and tool-use demands far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Construct and install prefabricated masonry units.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing and installing one prefabricated masonry unit (e.g., a precast panel or block section). A competent mason takes ~1–2 hours including placement, leveling, and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting (often crane or equipment), precise alignment of large masonry units, applying mortar, and leveling on unstructured outdoor/construction sites. Current humanoids lack the strength, specialized tooling, and outdoor terrain capability needed.

task: Shape, trim, face and cut marble or stone preparatory to setting, using power saws, cutting equipment, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shaping/trimming/cutting one stone piece to spec. A skilled marble setter/mason typically takes 30–60 min per piece depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy power saws, precise cuts on heavy stone, strong grip force, and fine tolerances. Current humanoids lack the strength, tool-operation capability, vibration tolerance, and precision for autonomous stone cutting.

task: Nail heel and toe cleats onto shoes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting and nailing heel and toe cleats onto one pair of shoes. A skilled cobbler takes ~8–12 minutes to position, align, and nail both cleats securely.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise positioning of small metal cleats on curved shoe surfaces, controlled hammer/nail driving with exact force, and fine dexterity on irregular substrates. Well beyond current humanoid manipulation capability.

task: Drive mobile intensive care unit to specified location, following instructions from emergency medical dispatcher.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one emergency dispatch trip. Typical urban/suburban ambulance response drive is ~10–30 min; 20 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate emergency vehicles in live traffic. No 2026 general-purpose robot can drive a large ambulance, interpret dispatcher radio instructions, navigate lights/sirens, and safely reach a scene autonomously.

task: Remove sections of monument from truck bed, and guide stone onto foundation, using skids, hoist, or truck crane.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unloading and placing one monument section onto its foundation using crane/hoist/skids; includes rigging, lifting, and alignment, ~45–75 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a truck crane or hoist, rigging heavy stone, and precise outdoor placement. Humanoids cannot operate vehicle-mounted cranes, handle multi-hundred-pound loads, or perform the complex rigging and alignment safely.

task: Construct, decorate, or repair leather products according to specifications, using sewing machines, needles and thread, leather lacing, glue, clamps, hand tools, or rivets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single leather product (e.g., a wallet or belt repair). A competent leatherworker takes ~90 min for a moderately complex piece involving cutting, stitching, and finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for hand-stitching, lacing, riveting, and tool-use on pliable leather; switching tools, following specs, and handling deformable material far exceeds current humanoid robot capability.

task: Clean excess mortar or grout from surface of marble, stone, or monument, using sponge, brush, water, or acid.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning excess mortar/grout from a single marble/stone surface or monument face. A competent mason spends ~20–30 min scrubbing, rinsing, and inspecting one surface section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled scrubbing on delicate stone, applying acid safely, detecting stubborn residue visually, and working on varied uneven surfaces. Current robots lack the fine tactile feedback, chemical handling protocols, and surface-awareness needed for acceptable quality.

task: Check the texture, color, and strength of leather to ensure that it is adequate for a particular purpose.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single leather piece/sample. A skilled worker examines texture by touch, color visually, and strength via flex/pull tests in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots lack fine tactile sensing for texture assessment, nuanced color judgment under variable lighting, and the ability to perform meaningful strength/flex tests. Multi-modal material QC remains far below human competence in 2026.

task: Remove wedges, fill joints between stones, finish joints between stones, using a trowel, and smooth the mortar to an attractive finish, using a tuck pointer.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a ~1 m² section of stone wall: remove wedges, fill joints, finish with tuck pointer. Skilled mason takes ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high dexterity with trowel and tuck pointer, force-sensitive mortar spreading, and aesthetic judgment on finish quality. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control and tactile feedback needed for acceptable masonry finishing.

task: Prepare tables for meals, including setting up items such as linens, silverware, and glassware.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting one table (4 seats). A competent server places linens, silverware, and glassware for a full table setting in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise placement of varied, delicate objects (glassware, silverware, linens) in exact spatial arrangements. Fragile items demand fine dexterity; linen spreading adds manipulation complexity. Current humanoids lack reliable fine placement at table-setting quality.

task: Perform emergency pharmacological interventions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single emergency drug intervention (e.g., IV push, IM injection, or epinephrine administration). A trained clinician assesses, draws up, and administers in ~5–15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, real-time patient assessment, sterile fine-dexterous manipulation (drawing up drugs, IV access), and rapid adaptation to a chaotic, high-stakes environment — far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Perform routine equipment maintenance such as cleaning and lubricating machines or replacing broken needles.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance instance (e.g., cleaning, lubricating, or replacing a needle on a single machine) takes a skilled technician roughly 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (oiling ports, threading needles, small fasteners), tool use, and task-specific knowledge across varied machine types. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and generalization for multi-step maintenance on diverse industrial equipment.

task: Select materials and patterns, and trace patterns onto materials to be cut out.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting material, laying out a pattern piece set, and tracing all pieces onto fabric. A competent tailor takes ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle limp, deformable fabric, precise pattern alignment, and controlled tracing with a tool. Deformable-material manipulation and nuanced material selection far exceed current robot capabilities.

task: Perform emergency invasive intervention before delivering patient to an acute care facility.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single emergency invasive intervention (e.g., needle decompression, surgical airway, IO access) performed on-scene or in transit before hospital handoff; typically 5–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sterile technique, real-time clinical judgment, anatomical localization on a moving/unstable patient, and life-critical decision-making. Far beyond any 2026 general-purpose robot's autonomous capability.

task: Turn setscrews on needle bars, and position required numbers of needles in stitching machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting needle bar setscrews and positioning needles on one stitching machine head. A skilled operator handles this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity: turning tiny setscrews with a screwdriver and precisely seating delicate needles in a confined machine space. Current humanoid robots lack the sub-millimeter precision and tool control needed for this consistently.

task: Polish nails, using powdered polish and buffer.
human_time_rationale: One instance = polishing all nails on both hands. A competent nail tech takes ~10–20 min to apply powdered polish and buff all nails to an acceptable finish.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, precise force control on curved nail surfaces, and safe manipulation near human skin. Current humanoids lack the fingertip sensitivity and sub-millimeter precision needed; high injury/quality-failure risk.

task: Draw patterns, using measurements, designs, plaster casts, or customer specifications, and position or outline patterns on work pieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drawing/positioning a pattern on a single workpiece. A skilled tradesperson typically spends 15–25 minutes measuring, drafting, and outlining one pattern accurately.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (scribers, chalk lines, measuring tapes), interpreting complex specs/casts, precise 2D layout on varied surfaces, and high spatial accuracy — well beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Administer drugs, orally or by injection, or perform intravenous procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = administering a single dose to one patient (oral, injection, or IV setup). A nurse typically takes 3–7 minutes including verification, preparation, and administration.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (venipuncture, syringe handling, IV line management), sterile technique, patient interaction, and real-time safety judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities; high error risk is unacceptable.

task: Read prescriptions or specifications, and take measurements to establish the type of product to be made, using calipers, tape measures, or rules.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reading a single prescription/spec sheet and taking the associated measurements for one product. A skilled worker typically spends 5–10 minutes reading, interpreting, and measuring.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading and interpreting unstructured text/specs, then precisely using calipers or tape measures on varied objects. Current robots lack reliable document comprehension integration, fine-grip tool use, and measurement precision in unstructured settings.

task: Mount tissue specimens on glass slides.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting one tissue section onto a glass slide: orienting tissue, applying medium, lowering coverslip, and labeling. Skilled histotech takes ~5–10 min per slide.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity: handling fragile micron-thin tissue sections, precise alignment on a glass slide, applying coverslip without bubbles or tears. Far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity and tactile feedback capabilities.

task: Stretch shoes, dampening parts and inserting and twisting parts, using an adjustable stretcher.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stretching one pair of shoes: inserting the stretcher, adjusting/twisting to desired width/length, dampening, and leaving to set — ~8 min active work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to insert, align, and twist a threaded stretcher mechanism inside a confined shoe cavity, apply dampener precisely, and judge correct tension — well beyond current humanoid manipulation capability.

task: Cut, insert, position, and secure paddings, cushioning, or linings, using stitches or glue.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting, fitting, and securing padding/lining for a single item (e.g., one seat cushion or garment panel); a skilled upholsterer/tailor completes this in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for precise cutting, shaping, and stitching of flexible/deformable materials; glue application and positioning demand tactile feedback and generalization well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Dig trench for foundation of monument, using pick and shovel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = digging a typical monument foundation trench (~1–2 m deep, ~2–4 m perimeter) by hand with pick and shovel; a competent laborer takes ~3–5 hours depending on soil.
robot_success_rationale: Sustained forceful digging with pick and shovel requires high torque, prolonged physical endurance, and coordinated two-tool use. Current humanoids lack the power output, tool-change dexterity, and stamina for extended excavation tasks in variable soil.

task: Provide emergency medical services as required, and perform light to heavy rescue functions at emergencies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single emergency response event (dispatch to scene clearance). Typical EMS/rescue call runs 30–60 minutes for assessment, treatment, and extraction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex medical assessment, dynamic unstructured rescue environments, heavy tool use (Jaws of Life), patient handling, clinical judgment, and real-time team coordination — far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Attach accessories or ornamentation to decorate or protect products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching one accessory/ornament to one product (e.g., a bow, badge, tag, or charm). A skilled worker completes this in roughly 3–7 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Highly varied product shapes, attachment mechanisms (clips, adhesives, stitching, pins), and delicate ornaments challenge robot dexterity and generalization. Pick-and-place is feasible but precise alignment and fastening on diverse items remains unreliable for current humanoids.

task: Stir material with spoons or paddles to mix ingredients or allow even cooling and prevent coagulation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one stirring session (mixing or cooling a batch). A competent worker typically stirs for ~3–7 minutes depending on material and goal.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can grasp spoons/paddles and perform repetitive motion, but adaptive force control, detecting coagulation onset, adjusting speed/pattern to viscosity changes, and avoiding spills in unstructured containers remain significant challenges.

task: Prepare and place meat cuts and products in display counter to appear attractive and catch the shopper's eye.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up/restocking a display counter section with meat cuts. A skilled butcher/meat clerk takes ~20–30 min to arrange, trim, label, and present a counter section attractively.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate handling of soft, deformable, slippery meat; aesthetic judgment for visual appeal; precise placement in a refrigerated case; and context-aware arrangement. Current robots lack the dexterity, compliance control, and aesthetic reasoning needed.

task: Shape, lace, and tie roasts, using boning knife, skewer, and twine.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shaping, lacing, and tying a single roast. A skilled butcher/cook takes ~5–12 minutes depending on roast size and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual dexterity with a sharp boning knife, threading twine through a skewer, and tying knots under tension on deformable meat. Far beyond current humanoid dexterous capability; knife safety and soft-body manipulation make this extremely hard.

task: Prepare special cuts of meat ordered by customers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one customer special-cut order (e.g., butterfly, debone, trim one piece). A skilled butcher takes roughly 10–20 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level dexterity with sharp blades on irregular, deformable meat, precise force control around bones/tendons, and interpreting varied customer specifications. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Process primal parts into cuts that are ready for retail use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = breaking down a single primal (e.g., beef loin or pork shoulder) into retail-ready cuts. A skilled butcher typically takes 30–60 minutes per primal.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert knife skills, precise force control, anatomical knowledge, and handling large slippery irregular cuts. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity; no deployed system handles butchery-level cutting safely or accurately.

task: Perform emergency cardiac care, such as cardioversion and manual defibrillation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one emergency cardiac intervention (e.g., cardioversion or defibrillation episode), from patient assessment through shock delivery and post-shock monitoring. Typically 5–15 minutes for a trained clinician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise medical judgment, rapid patient assessment, correct paddle/pad placement on variable anatomy, sterile technique, real-time ECG interpretation, and split-second clinical decision-making. Far beyond current humanoid capability or safety certification.

task: Test machinery to ensure proper functioning before beginning production.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pre-production machinery check on a single machine. A competent operator runs diagnostics, checks settings, listens/feels for anomalies, and confirms readiness — typically 15–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires domain-specific knowledge to interpret machine behavior, adjust controls, read gauges/displays, and make a go/no-go judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use, specialized perception, and contextual reasoning for varied industrial machines.

task: Inspect stations, uniforms, equipment, or recreation areas to ensure compliance with safety standards, taking corrective action as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One inspection round covering multiple stations/areas; a competent inspector typically spends 30–60 min checking equipment, uniforms, and spaces for compliance and documenting findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied environments, visually identifying subtle non-compliance (worn gear, missing labels, improper storage), applying judgment on standards, and taking corrective action—far beyond current robot perception and autonomous decision-making reliability.

task: Serve food or beverages to patrons, and prepare or serve specialty dishes at tables as required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = serving a single patron or table their ordered food/beverages, including carrying plates/drinks from kitchen/bar and placing them. Typically 2–4 minutes per table visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic navigation in crowded, unpredictable restaurant environments, stable tray/plate carrying, social interaction, and specialty dish preparation tableside — far beyond current humanoid reliability in unstructured public settings.

task: Perform food preparation duties, such as preparing salads, appetizers, and cold dishes, portioning desserts, and brewing coffee.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single food prep task (e.g., assembling a salad, portioning a dessert, or brewing a pot of coffee). ~20 min median across these varied sub-tasks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (portioning, plating, pouring), tool use (coffee machine, knives), and generalization across varied ingredients/equipment. Current humanoids lack reliable kitchen manipulation and recipe-following at acceptable quality.

task: Remove and examine shoes, shoe parts, and designs to verify conformance to specifications such as proper embedding of stitches in channels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one shoe. Skilled inspector removes parts, checks stitch embedding, channels, and specs in ~5 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to remove small shoe parts, close-tolerance tactile and visual inspection of stitch channels, and nuanced conformance judgment. Current robots lack sufficient fine manipulation and reliable tactile perception for this detail level.

task: Operate or tend machines to join, decorate, reinforce, or finish shoes and shoe parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one shoe/shoe part through a joining, decorating, reinforcing, or finishing machine; a competent operator cycles through this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise feeding of flexible, irregular shoe parts into specialized industrial machines, fine dexterity for alignment, and operating foot-pedal/hand controls. Current humanoids lack the precision and task-specific tool-use for consistent quality output.
