task: Turn screws to regulate size of staples.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting the screw(s) on a stapling machine to set a target staple size; a skilled worker locates the screw, turns it to the desired setting, and verifies — ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous screw-turning on a small, precise adjustment screw, tool-use or fingertip torque control, and perception of screw slot orientation. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-finger dexterity and calibrated torque feedback for such precision tasks.

task: Separate meats and byproducts into specified containers and seal containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting and sealing one batch of meats/byproducts into designated containers; a competent worker in a meat processing facility takes ~5–10 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires visually distinguishing varied meat/byproduct types, handling soft/wet/slippery items, placing into correct containers, and reliably sealing lids — challenging dexterity, perception, and categorization for current humanoids.

task: Insert forming fixtures, and start machines that cut frozen products into measured portions or specified shapes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inserting fixtures and starting one machine for a production run. A competent worker spends ~5–10 min aligning, seating, and securing fixtures, then initiating the machine cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise insertion of heavy/cold industrial fixtures into tight tolerances, operating specialized machine controls, and verifying seating — beyond reliable dexterity and tool-use of current humanoids in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Cut and trim meat to prepare for packing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting and trimming a single cut/portion of meat (e.g., one steak or roast) to pack-ready standard; a skilled butcher takes ~5–10 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Meat is deformable, slippery, and highly variable in shape. Precise knife control, adaptive grip on soft tissue, and quality-standard trimming far exceed current humanoid dexterity and perception capabilities.

task: Fill shuttle spools with thread from a machine's bobbin winder by pressing a foot treadle.
human_time_rationale: One instance = winding one shuttle spool. A competent operator threads, engages the bobbin winder, presses the treadle, and winds one spool in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated foot-treadle actuation, fine thread-handling dexterity, and monitoring tension — simultaneous foot/hand coordination with delicate thread manipulation is far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Garnish and decorate dishes in preparation for serving.
human_time_rationale: One instance = garnishing and decorating one plated dish before service. A skilled line cook typically takes 1–5 minutes per plate depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (placing micro-herbs, sauce drizzles, precise arrangement), aesthetic judgment, and delicate force control. Current humanoids lack the precision, visual creativity, and gentle touch needed for acceptable plating quality.

task: Maintain vehicles and medical and communication equipment, and replenish first aid equipment and supplies.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance round covering vehicles, medical/comms equipment checks, and first-aid restocking for a typical small unit; a competent human takes ~60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving/inspecting vehicles, operating specialized diagnostic tools, identifying and restocking diverse medical/comms equipment, and fine manipulation in unstructured settings — far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capabilities.

task: Assess nature and extent of fire, condition of building, danger to adjacent buildings, and water supply status to determine crew or company requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single size-up assessment upon arriving at a fire scene. A trained incident commander typically completes an initial 360° assessment and radio report in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert tactical judgment, multi-modal hazard perception (smoke, structural integrity, hydrant mapping), real-time communication, and command decision-making under life-safety conditions — far beyond current robot autonomy.

task: Receive, inspect, and store meat upon delivery to ensure meat quality.
human_time_rationale: One delivery instance: unloading, visually/physically inspecting cuts for freshness/temperature/packaging integrity, logging, and storing in refrigerated areas — ~30 min for a typical restaurant/butcher delivery.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step locomotion, handling slippery/irregular meat packages, sensory quality inspection (smell, texture, color judgment), temperature verification, cold-storage navigation, and documentation — well beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Produce hamburger meat and meat trimmings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a standard batch of hamburger meat/trimmings through grinding, mixing, and packaging in a commercial meat facility; ~45 min for a competent butcher/meat processor.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling raw, slippery, deformable meat; operating industrial grinders and trimmers; precise cutting force control; high hygiene standards. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, food-safe tool integration, and reliable perception for unstructured raw meat processing.

task: Remove parts, such as skin, feathers, scales or bones, from carcass.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one carcass (e.g. a chicken). A skilled butcher/food worker removes skin, feathers, scales, or bones in roughly 5–15 minutes depending on animal type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly precise dexterous cutting, gripping, and pulling on irregular, deformable biological material. Current humanoids lack the fine force control, sharp tool handling, and adaptive perception needed for consistent, safe, quality butchery/filleting.

task: Align parts to be stitched, following seams, edges, or markings, before positioning them under needles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning one set of fabric parts before stitching. A skilled sewing operator typically takes 1–3 minutes to match seams, edges, and markings and position the pieces under the needle.
robot_success_rationale: Fabric is limp, deformable, and hard to perceive precisely. Matching seams/markings requires fine dexterity and compliant manipulation that current humanoids struggle with greatly. No reliable 2026 deployment exists for this task.

task: Operate equipment, such as electrocardiograms (EKGs), external defibrillators, or bag valve mask resuscitators, in advanced life support environments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and operating one piece of life-support equipment (e.g., applying EKG leads, delivering one AED shock cycle, or performing BVM ventilation for a single resuscitation event). ~15 min is typical for initial setup through stabilization.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor placement of electrodes/leads on a human body, real-time clinical interpretation, rapid dynamic decision-making, and safe patient interaction under high-stakes conditions — all far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities in unstructured medical emergencies.

task: Clean, trim, slice, and section carcasses for future processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one animal carcass (e.g., a beef or pork carcass). A skilled butcher takes ~30–60 min for cleaning, trimming, slicing, and sectioning into primal cuts.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme precision with sharp tools on irregular, deformable biological tissue; heavy force control; bone-joint navigation; safety-critical blade handling. Far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity and generalization limits.

task: Inspect meat products for defects, bruises or blemishes and remove them along with any excess fat.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and trimming one cut/piece of meat on a processing line. A skilled worker handles this in ~1–3 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual discrimination of subtle defects, bruises, and blemishes on irregular wet/slippery surfaces, plus precise knife/trimming dexterity. Current robots lack reliable food-surface perception and delicate cutting control for variable meat shapes.

task: Perform medicolegal examinations and autopsies, conducting preliminary examinations of the body to identify victims, locate signs of trauma, and identify factors that would indicate time of death.
human_time_rationale: One full medicolegal autopsy including external examination, internal dissection, documentation, and preliminary findings typically takes 2–4 hours for a competent forensic pathologist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (surgical dissection), advanced multi-modal sensory interpretation (lividity, rigor, decomposition staging), legal documentation, and expert medical reasoning far beyond any current general-purpose humanoid robot.

task: Stock service areas with supplies such as coffee, food, tableware, and linens.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fully restocking a single service area (e.g., a break room or buffet station); a competent worker takes ~15–25 min to gather and arrange varied supplies.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to storage areas, identifying and grasping highly varied items (soft linens, fragile tableware, packaged food, liquids), and arranging them neatly. Current robots struggle with soft goods, cluttered environments, and multi-step restocking sequences without task-specific training.

task: Clean and salt hides.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and salting one animal hide. Scraping flesh/fat, rinsing, and applying salt evenly takes a competent worker ~30–60 min depending on hide size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained forceful scraping of irregular, flexible, slippery surfaces, precise salt coverage assessment, and strong grip on an unpredictable substrate. Far beyond current robot dexterity and force-control on deformable materials.

task: Weigh meats and tag containers for weight and contents.
human_time_rationale: One instance = weighing a single meat container, reading the scale, and attaching a label with weight/contents. A competent worker does this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires picking up varied meat containers, placing on scale, reading the display, printing/writing a label, and affixing it accurately. Multi-step perception, fine dexterity, and tool-use chain is beyond reliable 2026 robot capability in unstructured settings.

task: Inspect articles for defects, and remove damaged or worn parts, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single article and removing any defective/worn parts with hand tools. A competent worker typically takes ~5–10 minutes per article.
robot_success_rationale: Requires visual defect detection (variable defect types), fine dexterous manipulation with hand tools to remove parts, and judgment on damage severity. Current robots struggle with unstructured defect recognition and precise tool-use on varied articles.

task: Select and place spools of thread or pre-wound bobbins into shuttles, or onto spindles or loupers of stitching machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a single spool/bobbin into a shuttle or onto a spindle/looper. A skilled operator selects the correct thread and seats it in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle small spools/bobbins, precise alignment into narrow shuttles/spindles, and thread-routing. Small parts, tight tolerances, and varied machine configurations make this extremely difficult for current humanoids.

task: Switch on machines, lower pressure feet or rollers to secure parts, and start machine stitching, using hand, foot, or knee controls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and starting a stitching machine for one part/seam: powering on, positioning presser foot, and initiating stitch. A skilled operator does this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fabric handling, fine dexterous foot/knee/hand control of industrial machine pedals and levers, and real-time tension feedback. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control, compliant manipulation, and pedal actuation needed for textile machinery.

task: Prepare inserts, heel pads, and lifts from casts of customers' feet.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a full set of custom orthotics from one customer's foot cast; a skilled orthotist typically takes 30–60 minutes of hands-on work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of soft/irregular materials (casting plaster, foam, leather, thermoplastics), precise trimming, grinding, and fitting — well beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use capabilities.

task: Sew, join, reinforce, or finish parts of articles, such as garments, books, mattresses, toys, and wigs, using needles and thread or other materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing one sewing/joining/finishing operation on a single article (e.g., seaming a garment panel). Competent worker averages ~20–30 min depending on article complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Hand-sewing demands extreme fine dexterity, precise needle threading, consistent tension control, and tactile feedback across highly varied flexible materials — well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities. Generalization across article types (garments, wigs, toys) makes this even harder.

task: Drive crew carriers to transport firefighters to fire sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one trip transporting firefighters to a fire site. Typical response drive (urban + rural mix) estimated at ~45 minutes round-trip including staging.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot drive crew carrier vehicles on public/off-road terrain. L4 AVs exist but are route-limited, not fire-site capable. No current general-purpose robot can safely operate this vehicle autonomously in dynamic emergency conditions.

task: Wrap, weigh, label, and price cuts of meat.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wrapping, weighing, labeling, and pricing a single cut of meat. A skilled butcher/packager completes this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step dexterous manipulation (wrapping film/foam tray, reading scale, printing/applying label), tool-use chaining, and handling irregular, slippery meat. Current humanoids lack reliable end-to-end sequencing for this wet, deformable-object workflow.

task: Draw thread through machine guide slots, needles, and presser feet in preparation for stitching, or load rolls of wire through machine axles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = threading one sewing machine or loading one wire roll. A skilled operator takes ~2–4 minutes to route thread through guides, tension discs, needle, and presser foot.
robot_success_rationale: Extreme fine dexterity required: threading sub-millimeter needle eyes, routing through tight guide slots, and managing flexible filament/wire. Current humanoids lack the precision finger control and tactile feedback for reliable success.

task: Select thread, twine, cord, or yarn to be used, and thread needles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting appropriate thread/yarn for a job and threading one needle. A competent sewer does this in ~2–4 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Threading a needle requires sub-millimeter precision dexterity and fine visual alignment far beyond current humanoid robot capability. Thread selection also demands contextual/tactile judgment current robots lack.

task: Repair or replace soles, heels, and other parts of footwear, using sewing, buffing and other shoe repair machines, materials, and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/replacing soles or heels on one pair of shoes. A skilled cobbler typically spends 30–60 minutes per pair depending on repair complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity with specialized tools (lasting machines, heel nailers, buffing wheels, adhesives), precise force control on irregular curved surfaces, and complex multi-step sequencing. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Escort customers to their tables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = greeting one party and walking them from the entrance to their assigned table, typically 1–3 minutes in a standard restaurant.
robot_success_rationale: Navigating a dynamic restaurant floor with moving people, chairs, and tight spaces is challenging. Robots struggle with social cues, guest interaction, adaptive pathfinding in clutter, and managing irregular layouts safely.

task: Supervise and participate in the inspection of properties to ensure that they are in compliance with applicable fire codes, ordinances, laws, regulations, and standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one property. A competent fire inspector typically spends 45–90 minutes on a standard commercial/residential property inspection, including documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied, unstructured buildings; reading complex code compliance details; operating doors/stairs; exercising regulatory judgment; and producing authoritative legal documentation — all well beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Obtain and distribute specified meat or carcass.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retrieving a specified meat/carcass from storage and delivering it to the correct destination (e.g., cutting station or customer). Includes identification, handling, and transport.
robot_success_rationale: Carcasses and large meat cuts are heavy, irregularly shaped, slippery, and deformable — challenging for robot grippers. Identifying the correct item, navigating cold storage, and safely transferring it pushes current capability limits significantly.

task: Collect shoe parts from conveyer belts or racks and place them in machinery such as ovens or on molds for dressing, returning them to conveyers or racks to send them to the next work station.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a set of shoe parts from a conveyor/rack, placing them in a machine or on a mold, then returning them to the conveyor/rack. A trained worker repeats this continuously; ~1–2 min per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Structured conveyor pick-and-place is within robot reach, but shoe parts vary in shape, flexibility, and orientation; precise mold dressing and oven loading require fine dexterity and reliable perception of soft/irregular parts, still challenging for 2026 humanoids.

task: Investigate disturbances on the premises, such as security alarms, altercations, and suspicious activity.
human_time_rationale: One instance = responding to and resolving a single disturbance event (alarm, altercation, or suspicious activity). A competent security officer typically takes 5–30 min depending on complexity; median ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social perception, judgment, communication, de-escalation, and adaptive response to unpredictable human behavior. Current robots lack reliable speech interaction, threat assessment, and safe physical intervention capability in unstructured real-world scenarios.

task: Hammer loose staples for proper attachment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = hammering loose staples on a single work piece until properly seated. A competent worker locates and taps down loose staples in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine perception to detect proud staples, precise targeting with a hammer, and calibrated force control to seat without damage. Current robots lack reliable fine-grained contact force control and small-target accuracy for this task.

task: Staple sides of shoes, pressing a foot treadle to position and hold each shoe under the feeder of the machine.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stapling the sides of one shoe. A trained operator positions the shoe, presses the treadle, and feeds through the machine in roughly 1–2 minutes per shoe.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise foot-treadle actuation, simultaneous hand manipulation of a curved flexible shoe, and coordinated feeding through a specialized industrial machine. Humanoids lack foot pedal control and the fine bimanual dexterity needed for consistent shoe positioning at speed.

task: Maintain fire suppression equipment in good condition, checking equipment periodically to ensure that it is ready for use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and maintaining one fire suppression unit (check pressure, pin, seals, tag, condition). A competent technician averages ~15–25 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog gauges, inspecting tamper seals, manipulating safety pins, updating tags, and making maintenance judgments. Fine dexterity, reliable gauge reading, and procedural compliance in varied environments remain well beyond 2026 frontier robots.

task: Apprehend or evict trespassers, rule violators, or other security threats from the premises.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one trespasser/violator confrontation and removal. Includes identifying the threat, verbal engagement, escalation if needed, and escorting off premises. Median ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social judgment, verbal de-escalation, physical compliance enforcement, and legal authority. Current robots lack reliable speech negotiation, force-application safety, situational threat assessment, and authority to physically compel humans.

task: Turn knobs to adjust stitch length and thread tension.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting both knobs (stitch length + thread tension) on a sewing machine to desired settings; a skilled operator does this in under a minute.
robot_success_rationale: Knob-turning requires precise rotational dexterity and reading/interpreting small numbered dials. Current humanoids struggle with fine finger force modulation, exact dial positioning, and confirming settings visually on small markings.

task: Measure and align parts, fasteners, or trimmings, following seams, edges, or markings on parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and aligning a single part/fastener/trimming to a seam, edge, or marking. A skilled worker completes this in ~5 minutes on average.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise visual perception of seams/markings, fine dexterous alignment of flexible or small parts, and tactile feedback. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter precision and fabric/material handling dexterity needed for reliable success.

task: Prepare ready-to-heat foods by filleting meat or fish or cutting it into bite-sized pieces, preparing and adding vegetables or applying sauces or breading.
human_time_rationale: One batch of ready-to-heat food prep (filleting, cutting, adding veg/sauce/breading); a skilled prep cook takes ~20–30 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Filleting fish/meat demands extreme fine dexterity, force sensitivity, and knife control beyond current humanoids. Sauce application and breading add further manipulation complexity. Generalization to varied proteins/shapes is very poor.

task: Trim excess threads or edges of parts, using scissors or knives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming excess threads/edges on a single part. A competent worker snips or cuts loose threads/edges in ~1–5 minutes depending on part complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to grip scissors/knife precisely, identify tiny threads, and apply controlled cutting force without damaging the part. Current humanoids lack the precision perception and fine manipulation needed for reliable trimming.

task: Direct firefighters in station maintenance duties, and participate in these duties.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single station maintenance session (directing + participating), typically ~1 hour for a shift crew covering cleaning, equipment checks, and minor repairs.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires social authority/coordination (directing humans), unstructured multi-domain physical work (cleaning, repairs, equipment handling), and nuanced judgment. Current robots lack social leadership capability and reliable generalization across all subtasks.

task: Repair or adjust equipment, machines, or defective components, replacing worn parts, such as gaskets or seals in watertight electrical equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/adjusting one piece of equipment or component (e.g., replacing a gasket/seal). Includes diagnosis, disassembly, part replacement, reassembly, and basic testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to seat gaskets/seals precisely, tool use with calibrated torque, fault diagnosis, and working in tight/varied configurations. Current humanoids lack the precision, tactile feedback, and contextual repair reasoning needed.

task: Operate equipment to demonstrate proper use or to analyze malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one equipment demonstration or malfunction analysis session. A competent technician typically spends ~30 min setting up, operating, and explaining or diagnosing one piece of equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating varied, specialized equipment (controls, levers, vehicles, machinery), diagnosing malfunctions through expert reasoning, and communicating findings. Far beyond current humanoid generalization, tool-use, and cognitive diagnosis capabilities.

task: Perform scheduled preventive maintenance tasks, such as checking, cleaning, or repairing equipment, to detect and prevent problems.
human_time_rationale: One PM session on a typical piece of industrial equipment (e.g., HVAC unit, conveyor, pump): check fluid levels, clean filters, tighten fasteners, log findings. ~45 min median.
robot_success_rationale: PM requires diverse dexterous actions (opening panels, using torque wrenches, reading gauges, cleaning jets), contextual judgment, and fault diagnosis. Current humanoids lack the tool-use generalization, fine dexterity, and diagnostic reasoning needed across varied equipment types.

task: Calibrate testing instruments and installed or repaired equipment to prescribed specifications.
human_time_rationale: One calibration session on a single instrument/device; includes setup, applying reference standards, adjusting, and documenting results — typical across lab/field technician roles.
robot_success_rationale: Calibration demands fine dexterous manipulation of adjustment screws/potentiometers, reading analog/digital gauges, connecting reference standards, and interpreting specifications — far beyond reliable autonomous capability of 2026 humanoids. Tool diversity and precision requirements make this extremely challenging.

task: Fold, twist, stretch, or drape material, and secure articles in preparation for sewing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one garment piece or article (fold/twist/stretch/drape and secure). A skilled textile worker typically takes ~3–7 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly dexterous bimanual manipulation of limp, deformable fabric with precise positioning, stretching tension control, and pinning/clipping. Current robots struggle severely with compliant materials, fine fastening, and draping accuracy.

task: Respond to fire alarms and other calls for assistance, such as automobile and industrial accidents.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one emergency call response, from dispatch to scene clearance. Typical fire/accident calls last 30–90 minutes; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving emergency vehicles in traffic, operating heavy rescue equipment, real-time situational judgment in chaotic environments, victim extraction, and coordination with teams — all far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Inspect and test new and existing fire protection systems, fire detection systems, and fire safety equipment to ensure that they are operating properly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting/testing a single building's fire protection systems (sprinklers, alarms, extinguishers, detectors). A competent inspector typically takes 60–120 minutes for a mid-size commercial building.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading gauges, operating test valves, interpreting panel codes, navigating varied building layouts, and making pass/fail judgments. Current humanoids lack the specialized tool-use, system knowledge integration, and reliable fine dexterity across diverse equipment needed for autonomous, certifiable inspection.

task: Perform emergency diagnostic and treatment procedures, such as stomach suction, airway management, or heart monitoring, during ambulance ride.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one emergency procedure (e.g., airway management or cardiac monitoring) performed during a typical ambulance transport averaging 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity on a moving, unstable platform, real-time clinical judgment, sterile technique, patient-specific adaptation, and legally/ethically must be a licensed clinician. Far beyond current robot capability.

task: Bring wine selections to tables with appropriate glasses, and pour the wines for customers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = serving wine to one table: carrying bottles, placing appropriate glasses, and pouring for each guest. Typically 3–6 minutes for a 2–4 person table.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct glassware, carrying filled/heavy bottles, navigating a crowded dining room, precise pouring without spilling, and social interaction. Current robots lack reliable dexterous pouring, tray balance, and guest-facing service generalization.

task: Set up and test industrial equipment to ensure that it functions properly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running a functional test on a single piece of industrial equipment. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 minutes on setup, calibration, and verification checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading manuals, interpreting equipment-specific interfaces, making electrical/mechanical connections, adjusting parameters, and judging test outcomes — far beyond current humanoid generalization, dexterity, and tool-use capabilities in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Draw and cut patterns according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drawing and cutting one pattern piece to spec (e.g., a garment or craft pattern). A competent worker takes ~20–30 min including measuring, marking, and cutting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise marking on flexible/soft material, interpreting 2D spec sheets, and controlled fine cutting along curves. Current robots lack the fine dexterity, compliant-material handling, and tool-use precision needed for acceptable quality.

task: Determine appropriate methods for fabricating and joining materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reviewing a set of materials/components and selecting appropriate fabrication and joining methods. A competent engineer/technician takes ~45 min to assess, research, and decide.
robot_success_rationale: This is primarily a cognitive/engineering judgment task requiring materials knowledge, design intent interpretation, and trade-off reasoning. Current humanoid robots have no meaningful autonomous engineering decision-making capability.

task: Rescue survivors from burning buildings, accident sites, and water hazards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating and extracting one survivor from a hazardous scene (burning building, crash site, or water hazard). Trained responders typically spend 20–90 min per rescue.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating destroyed/burning/flooded unstructured terrain, perceiving trapped humans, forceful extraction, and heat/water resistance. Current humanoids lack robust hazard tolerance, strength, situational judgment, and autonomous life-safety decision-making.

task: Remove or supervise removal of bodies from death scenes, using the proper equipment and supplies, and arrange for transportation to morgues.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one body from a death scene, including bagging, documentation, equipment use, and coordinating transport. Typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy, irregular object handling of a human body, fine dexterous bagging, navigation in cluttered/unpredictable death scenes, legal documentation, and coordination with transport — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Sew buttonholes, or add lace or other trimming.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sewing one buttonhole or applying one section of trim (~12 inches). A competent seamstress takes ~5–10 min per buttonhole or trim segment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity: threading needles, guiding delicate fabric, controlling tension, and precise stitching. Far beyond current humanoid manipulation capabilities; no 2026 general-purpose robot can reliably handle fabric at this level.

task: Observe, record, and preserve any objects or personal property related to deaths, including objects such as medication containers and suicide notes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single death scene. A competent investigator (e.g., medicolegal death investigator) spends ~30–60 min observing, cataloguing, and bagging/tagging associated objects.
robot_success_rationale: Requires contextual scene understanding, legal evidence-handling protocols, fine dexterous bagging/labeling, reading handwritten notes, and nuanced judgment about relevance — far beyond current robot generalization and dexterity capabilities.

task: Adjust equipment controls to maintain specified heat, tension, and speed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single adjustment cycle (reading gauges/displays, identifying deviation, and correcting controls) for heat, tension, or speed on industrial equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital displays, interpreting process deviations, and manipulating varied industrial controls (knobs, levers, touchscreens). Perception of subtle process states and generalization across equipment types remain weak in 2026 robots.

task: Smooth seams with heated irons, flat bones, or rubbing sticks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = smoothing the seams of a single garment piece. A skilled worker presses/rubs each seam open or flat in roughly 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled contact along irregular fabric seams, heat management, and tactile feedback to avoid scorching. Current humanoids lack fine compliant dexterity and thermal tool control for textile finishing work.

task: Move toward the source of a fire, using knowledge of types of fires, construction design, building materials, and physical layout of properties.
human_time_rationale: One instance = advancing toward a fire source in a single structure/scene. A trained firefighter takes ~10–20 min to locate, assess, and advance to the fire's origin.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme-environment mobility (smoke, heat, debris, unstable floors), high-level situational reasoning about fire behavior and building construction, and real-time adaptive navigation — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Screen individuals and belongings to prevent passage of prohibited materials using walkthrough detectors, wands, or bag searches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = screening one individual and their belongings (walkthrough detector + wand sweep + bag check if needed). Typical airport/venue screening takes ~2–4 minutes per person.
robot_success_rationale: Requires social interaction, directing people, interpreting alarms, conducting pat-downs or bag searches with fine dexterity, and making judgment calls on prohibited items. Current humanoids lack reliable social compliance enforcement, nuanced decision-making, and safe physical contact with strangers.

task: Observe and record the positions and conditions of bodies and related evidence.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining and documenting a single scene/body. A competent forensic investigator typically spends ~30–60 minutes on systematic observation, notation, and photography.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced perceptual judgment, contextual interpretation of ambiguous evidence, precise documentation, and legally defensible chain-of-custody protocols. Current robots lack forensic reasoning, fine-detail perception in unstructured scenes, and the reliability required.

task: Create openings in buildings for ventilation or entrance, using axes, chisels, crowbars, electric saws, or core cutters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting/breaking one opening (door, vent, or window) in a wall or structure. A competent worker using power tools typically takes 30–60 min depending on material.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force tool use (axes, electric saws, core cutters) on unstructured construction surfaces with precise positioning. Current humanoids lack the strength, tool-wielding stability, force control, and safety compliance for demolition-grade tasks.

task: Patrol the premises to prevent or detect intrusion, protect property, or preserve order.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patrol circuit of a typical premises (building, lot, or facility). A competent security guard completes a full patrol round in ~45–90 minutes; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Wheeled security robots (Knightscope) exist but humanoids struggle with mixed indoor/outdoor terrain, stairs, dark areas, anomaly judgment, and responding to intrusions. Perception and generalization gaps make autonomous intervention unlikely at acceptable quality.

task: Dress with equipment such as fire-resistant clothing and breathing apparatus.
human_time_rationale: One instance = donning a full set of fire-resistant gear (turnout suit, SCBA, helmet, gloves, boots). A trained firefighter takes ~5–15 min depending on gear complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Donning PPE requires extreme bimanual dexterity, self-referential body awareness, buckling/zipping fasteners, and fitting SCBA straps precisely. Far beyond current humanoid manipulation and self-dressing capability.

task: Cut, trim, bone, tie, and grind meats, such as beef, pork, poultry, and fish, to prepare in cooking form.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one standard meat portion (e.g., deboning a chicken, grinding ~1 lb beef). A skilled butcher averages ~20–30 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Butchery demands extreme fine dexterity with sharp tools, precise force control on irregular, deformable meat, boning around complex anatomy, and safe blade handling — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Witness and certify deaths that are the result of a judicial order.
human_time_rationale: One instance = witnessing and certifying a single judicially ordered execution. Includes pre-procedure verification, attendance during the procedure, and completing official death certification paperwork; typically 1–3 hours total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires legal authority, professional judgment, complex documentation, interpersonal/ethical decision-making, and official certification capacity. No current robot has legal standing, credentialing, or the nuanced perceptual/cognitive ability to fulfill this role autonomously.

task: Inspect and adjust security equipment to ensure it is operational or to detect evidence of tampering.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and adjusting a set of security equipment (e.g., cameras, sensors, locks) in a single zone/room. A competent guard or technician typically takes 15–25 minutes per zone.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual inspection for tampering signs, fine adjustments to cameras/sensors, and interpreting ambiguous states. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity, domain-specific perception for tamper evidence, and tool-use for adjustments in unstructured security contexts.

task: Add dyes, water, detergents, or chemicals to tanks to dilute or strengthen solutions, according to established formulas and solution test results.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing/adjusting one tank solution. Includes reading formula/test results, measuring, and adding chemicals. Typical cycle is ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid measurement, pouring from varied containers, reading test results, and navigating industrial tanks. Current robots lack reliable fluid handling, formula interpretation, and safe chemical manipulation in unstructured environments.

task: Send defective units to the manufacturer or to a specialized repair shop for repair.
human_time_rationale: One instance = identifying, packaging, labeling, and dispatching one defective unit. A competent worker takes ~20 min including documentation and handoff prep.
robot_success_rationale: Physically moving/packaging a unit is feasible (~40–60%), but autonomously deciding routing (manufacturer vs. repair shop), completing paperwork/labels, and coordinating logistics far exceeds current robot autonomy.

task: Secure entrances and exits by locking doors and gates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = securing one entrance/exit (door or gate). A competent worker walks to it, checks it, and locks it — typically 2–4 minutes per entry point.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to doors and grasp handles at ~40–70%, but operating diverse lock mechanisms (deadbolts, padlocks, latches, gate chains) reliably requires fine dexterity and generalization that current humanoids struggle with. Outdoor/unstructured environments reduce success further.

task: Monitor material performance, and evaluate its deterioration.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a set of materials/components at a single site visit. A competent inspector takes ~45 min to observe, document, and evaluate visible deterioration signs.
robot_success_rationale: Current robots can capture visual data and navigate to inspection points, but lack reliable judgment for subtle deterioration (corrosion, fatigue cracks, delamination). Multi-modal sensing, contextual evaluation, and report generation remain weak.

task: Drive and operate fire fighting vehicles and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one emergency response call, including driving to scene, deploying equipment, and operating it. Median incident response is ~1–2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a large specialized vehicle in traffic, then operating complex pumps, hoses, and ladders in chaotic outdoor environments. No 2026 humanoid can drive a fire truck or manage unstructured emergency operations autonomously.

task: Tie, knit, weave or knot ribbon, yarn, or decorative materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single tying, knotting, or weaving of ribbon/yarn (e.g., tying a bow, knotting a bracelet). A competent crafter takes ~5–15 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly precise bimanual dexterity with flexible, deformable materials (ribbon, yarn). Manipulation of compliant/soft objects with complex looping and tensioning is a known hard frontier for current humanoid robots.

task: Inventory personal effects recovered from bodies, such as jewelry or wallets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inventorying personal effects from one body. A competent worker (e.g., morgue or forensic tech) takes ~10–20 min to collect, examine, log, and bag items from one decedent.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle delicate items (jewelry, wallets), contextual recognition of diverse personal effects, careful documentation, and operation in a highly sensitive/unstructured environment. Current robots lack the dexterity, semantic recognition, and procedural compliance needed.

task: Survey undisturbed or disturbed lands for classification, inventory, mapping, environmental impact assessments, environmental protection planning, conservation planning, or reclamation planning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one field survey of a defined land parcel. A competent surveyor/ecologist typically spends a full day (~8 hrs) traversing, sampling, and documenting a moderately sized site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert ecological judgment, navigation across rough/varied outdoor terrain, multi-modal sampling (soil, vegetation, hydrology), regulatory interpretation, and detailed report generation — far beyond current robot autonomy and generalization.

task: Participate in fire drills and demonstrations of fire fighting techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one fire drill/demonstration session, including evacuation, assembly, and firefighting technique demos (e.g., extinguisher use). Typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated locomotion, following dynamic crowd instructions, operating fire extinguishers, and social situational awareness. Current humanoids lack the generalization, social integration, and tool-use reliability for unscripted emergency drills.

task: Investigate soil problems or poor water quality to determine sources and effects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = investigating a single site (soil/water sampling, field observation, preliminary analysis). A competent environmental scientist typically spends 2–4 hours per site visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert judgment, multi-step sampling protocols, operating specialized field instruments, navigating uneven outdoor terrain, interpreting complex environmental data, and causal reasoning — far beyond 2026 humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Inspect buildings or building designs to determine fire protection system requirements and potential problems in areas such as water supplies, exit locations, and construction materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one building or design review. A competent fire protection inspector typically spends 1–3 hours assessing a mid-size building's systems, exits, materials, and water supply.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert code knowledge, multi-system reasoning, document review, 3D spatial judgment, and stakeholder communication. Current robots lack the cognitive integration, specialized domain expertise, and tool-use (e.g., reading blueprints, pressure testing) needed for autonomous acceptance-quality inspection.

task: Inspect fire sites after flames have been extinguished to ensure that there is no further danger.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single extinguished fire site (e.g., a room or structure). A competent firefighter/investigator systematically checks hotspots, smoldering materials, and structural hazards, typically taking 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires thermal perception, navigating unstable/debris-laden terrain, detecting hidden embers, and exercising safety judgment. Current robots lack reliable locomotion on post-fire rubble, sufficient thermal/multi-modal sensing integration, and the contextual judgment needed for autonomous safety certification.

task: Perform machine maintenance, such as cleaning and oiling equipment, and repair or replace worn or defective parts.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance session on a single machine: cleaning, oiling, inspecting, and swapping a worn part. Skilled technician averages ~45 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse fine dexterity (oiling ports, fastener removal, part replacement), tool use, fault diagnosis, and generalization across machine types — well beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Conduct experiments investigating how soil forms, changes, or interacts with land-based ecosystems or living organisms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing, running, and recording a single soil experiment (e.g., soil core collection + lab analysis). Competent soil scientist typically spends ~2 hours per experiment session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires scientific reasoning, hypothesis design, unstructured outdoor terrain, fine manipulation of sampling tools, and contextual judgment. No current humanoid can autonomously conduct multi-step scientific experiments end-to-end.

task: Inspect machinery to determine necessary adjustments and repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single machine unit. A competent technician typically spends 20–40 minutes checking components, listening, measuring, and documenting findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal diagnosis (visual, auditory, tactile, vibration sensing), domain knowledge to interpret findings, and judgment on repair needs. Current humanoids lack reliable diagnostic reasoning, specialized sensor integration, and contextual fault interpretation.

task: Soak specified textile products for designated times.
human_time_rationale: One instance = soaking one batch of textiles. Human spends ~5 min preparing solution, submerging items, and setting a timer; waiting time is passive.
robot_success_rationale: Robot must fill a container with water/solution, handle varied wet textiles, fully submerge them, and track time. Fluid handling, deformable-object manipulation, and multi-step sequencing in an unstructured laundry environment remain difficult for current humanoids.

task: Remove dyed articles from tanks and machines for drying and further processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a batch of dyed articles from a single tank or machine. A worker lifts, drains, and transfers wet textiles to a drying area, taking ~5–10 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Wet, heavy, deformable textiles are hard to grasp and untangle reliably. Robots struggle with sodden fabric manipulation, navigating industrial dye tanks, and transferring bulky loads safely. Perception of wet cloth is also poor.

task: Conduct experiments to develop new or improved varieties of field crops, focusing on characteristics such as yield, quality, disease resistance, nutritional value, or adaptation to specific soils or climates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full field crop breeding experiment cycle (planting through harvest, data collection, analysis). Typically spans one full growing season (~weeks of active work), estimated at ~240 hours of labor.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-month scientific reasoning, hypothesis design, cross-pollination, statistical analysis, and adaptive decision-making. Far beyond current robot autonomy — no generalization for open-ended scientific experimentation outdoors.

task: Participate in physical training activities to maintain a high level of physical fitness.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single physical training session (e.g., gym workout, run, drills). A typical session for a professional/occupational worker lasts ~60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Current humanoids can walk and do basic movements, but sustained high-intensity training (weights, varied exercise routines, self-monitoring fitness goals) across a full session autonomously is far beyond current capability. No gym equipment operation, self-pacing, or adaptive training exists.

task: Decontaminate ambulance interior following treatment of patient with infectious disease, and report case to proper authorities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full decontamination of one ambulance interior after infectious patient transport; includes PPE donning, applying EPA-approved disinfectant to all surfaces, equipment, and airing out. ~30–60 min per EMS protocols.
robot_success_rationale: Ambulance interior is a cramped, cluttered, equipment-dense space. Robot must don PPE, maneuver in tight quarters, identify and wipe all contaminated surfaces, handle biohazard waste, then complete a formal disease-reporting process — far beyond 2026 autonomy.

task: Extinguish flames and embers to suppress fires, using shovels or engine- or hand-driven water or chemical pumps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = suppressing a single fire event (e.g., a brush fire or structure hotspot). A competent firefighter typically spends 30–60 minutes actively suppressing flames and mopping up embers.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating hazardous, unstructured outdoor/indoor terrain, operating heavy hoses and pumps, wielding shovels under extreme heat, smoke-impaired perception, and split-second tactical judgment. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Patrol burned areas after fires to locate and eliminate hot spots that may restart fires.
human_time_rationale: One patrol instance covers a burned area sweep; competent firefighters typically spend 2–4 hours systematically checking and mopping up hot spots across a burn perimeter.
robot_success_rationale: Requires traversing rough, unstable, ash-covered outdoor terrain; detecting subsurface heat; wielding tools (hose, shovel) to extinguish hot spots; and making safety judgments—all far beyond current humanoid robot capability outdoors.

task: Modify properties of metal alloys, using thermal and mechanical treatments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one heat/mechanical treatment cycle (e.g., anneal, quench, temper a batch). Includes setup, furnace operation, and mechanical processing; typically 1–3 hours per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating industrial furnaces, presses, and heavy machinery; reading pyrometers; making real-time process judgments. Humanoid robots cannot operate such specialized heavy equipment autonomously or safely in 2026.

task: Select and attach hose nozzles, depending on fire type, and direct streams of water or chemicals onto fires.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting and attaching the correct nozzle, then directing the stream onto a single fire event. A trained firefighter typically completes this in ~5–10 minutes from hose deployment to active suppression.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fire-type recognition, forceful hose coupling under pressure, sustained high-force stream control, mobility in hazardous/dynamic environments, and real-time suppression judgment — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Salvage property by removing broken glass, pumping out water, and ventilating buildings to remove smoke.
human_time_rationale: One instance = salvaging one affected building/room; removing broken glass, pumping water, and ventilating typically takes 1–2 hours for a competent worker with equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step coordination across hazardous, unstructured environments: navigating debris/water, handling sharp glass safely, operating pumps and ventilation equipment. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and tool-use limits in disaster scenarios.

task: Inspect components of industrial equipment for accurate assembly and installation or for defects, such as loose connections or frayed wires.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one industrial equipment unit/component set. A competent technician systematically checks connections, wiring, and assembly quality, typically taking 15–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can visually scan surfaces but detecting subtle defects like frayed wires, loose connections, or misalignments in complex industrial equipment requires nuanced perception, fine dexterity for probing, and contextual judgment that 2026 robots lack reliably.

task: Test individual parts and products to ensure that manufacturer and governmental quality and safety standards are met.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single part/product through a standard QC inspection cycle, including setup, testing, measurement, and recording results. Typical industrial QC check takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle structured pick-and-place and simple visual inspection, but quality/safety testing often requires operating specialized test equipment, interpreting varied outputs, adapting to diverse product types, and meeting documentation standards — well beyond current generalization.

task: Create prototypes or models of objects to be crafted.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one prototype/model object. A competent craftsperson typically spends 1–3 hours designing, shaping, and finishing a single prototype depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative design interpretation, fine dexterous fabrication (cutting, shaping, sculpting, joining varied materials), and iterative judgment. Far beyond current humanoid manipulation and generalization capabilities.

task: Cut excess thread or material from shoe parts, using scissors or knives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming excess thread/material from a single shoe part. A skilled worker snips or cuts cleanly in seconds to ~2 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity with scissors/knife on small, flexible, irregular shoe parts. Locating and cutting excess thread without damaging the workpiece is a high-precision task well beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Test solutions used to process textile goods to detect variations from standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single solution batch (mixing, applying test, reading result, comparing to standard). A competent lab/textile worker typically takes ~15 minutes per test cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires pipetting, chemical handling, precise measurement, reading colorimetric/pH results, and comparing to standards — all demanding fine dexterity, fluid manipulation, and lab instrument use far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Inspect buildings for fire hazards and compliance with fire prevention ordinances, testing and checking smoke alarms and fire suppression equipment as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full inspection of a typical commercial/residential building. A competent fire inspector typically spends 45–90 minutes per building, including testing alarms and equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex multi-room buildings, interpreting codes, reading labels, operating varied test equipment, documenting compliance decisions, and exercising regulatory judgment. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Study soil characteristics to classify soils on the basis of factors such as geographic location, landscape position, or soil properties.
human_time_rationale: One instance = classifying soils at a single field site. Includes field sampling, observation of profile, lab/reference consultation, and classification write-up; ~2 hours for a competent soil scientist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert scientific judgment, multi-modal sensory integration (texture, color, smell, moisture), navigation across varied terrain, literature synthesis, and formal taxonomic reasoning — far beyond current robot autonomy and cognition.

task: Protect property from water and smoke, using waterproof salvage covers, smoke ejectors, and deodorants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = protecting one structure/room during or after a fire incident. Deploying covers, positioning ejectors, and applying deodorants typically takes a trained firefighter 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires unfolding and draping large heavy salvage covers over furniture/openings, positioning and operating smoke ejectors in doorways, and applying deodorants — all in cluttered, smoke-filled, debris-strewn environments. Beyond current humanoid dexterity, perception, and mobility limits in unstructured hazardous scenes.

task: Mount rolls of cloth on machines, using hoists, or place textile goods in machines or pieces of equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting/placing one roll of cloth or textile good on a machine. A competent worker lifts, aligns, and secures the roll in roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Heavy, bulky rolls require coordinated lifting, precise axle alignment, and hoist operation — challenging for humanoids. Perception of roll ends and machine mounting points is complex; force control and payload limits are significant barriers.

task: Ravel seams that connect cloth ends when processing is completed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = raveling the seam connecting two cloth ends on one piece. A skilled textile worker can locate, grip, and pull threads to unravel a seam in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Raveling seams requires extreme fine dexterity — isolating and pulling individual threads from fabric. Current humanoids lack the fingertip sensitivity, precision grip, and deformable-material perception needed for this delicate textile task.

task: Thread ends of cloth or twine through specified sections of equipment prior to processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = threading ends through one piece of equipment (e.g., a loom, sewing machine, or textile machine). A skilled operator typically completes this in 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to handle limp, deformable thread/twine and guide it through narrow, precise openings. Flexible material perception and manipulation remain far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Orient self in relation to fire, using compass and map, and collect supplies and equipment dropped by parachute.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating fire on a map with compass bearing, then physically retrieving a parachute drop. A competent wildland firefighter needs ~10 min for orientation plus ~35 min to locate and collect a drop cache.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rugged outdoor terrain navigation, map/compass interpretation, visual search over wide unstructured areas, and hauling heavy supply caches — all far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capabilities in unpredictable wilderness environments.

task: Identify or classify species of insects or allied forms, such as mites or spiders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = identifying a single specimen. A trained entomologist/arachnologist typically spends ~10–20 min examining morphology, consulting keys, and recording a classification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine-scale visual perception of tiny morphological features (wing venation, leg spination, mouthparts), expert taxonomic knowledge, and precise specimen handling. Current robots lack the micro-optics, dexterity, and taxonomic reasoning needed.

task: Remove defects in cloth by cutting and pulling out filling.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one defect from cloth. A skilled textile worker locates, cuts, and pulls out a single filling defect in a few minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine motor precision to locate a subtle fabric defect, make controlled micro-cuts, and extract specific threads without damaging surrounding cloth. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tactile perception.

task: Place patterns on top of layers of fabric and cut fabric following patterns, using electric or manual knives, cutters, or computer numerically controlled cutting devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out patterns on a fabric layer stack and cutting one garment's worth of pieces. Competent cutter takes ~20–30 min including pattern placement and cutting.
robot_success_rationale: Fabric is highly deformable and hard to perceive/manipulate precisely. Pattern alignment requires fine dexterity and judgment. Operating CNC cutters is plausible but handling limp fabric layers autonomously is beyond current robot capability.

task: Start machines, monitor operations, and make adjustments as needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting a machine, monitoring a standard operational cycle, and making any needed adjustments; typical across manufacturing/industrial settings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting diverse machine interfaces/controls, reading gauges, diagnosing anomalies, and making nuanced adjustments. Current robots lack generalized machine-operation understanding, fine control, and adaptive decision-making for unstructured industrial equipment.

task: Examine and feel products to identify defects and variations from coloring and other processing standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single product unit by hand and eye. A trained inspector typically spends 30–90 seconds examining, touching, and judging one item against color/processing standards.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is mature, but combining tactile "feel" (texture, firmness, surface anomalies) with nuanced color judgment across varied product types strains current robot tactile sensing and generalization. High false-positive/negative risk on subtle defects.

task: Sew ends of cloth together, by hand or using machines, to form endless lengths of cloth to facilitate processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = joining two cloth ends into one endless loop; a skilled textile worker aligns, pins, and sews one seam in ~5 minutes by hand or machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of limp fabric edges, threading/operating a sewing machine or hand-sewing needle, and maintaining seam tension. Deformable textile manipulation and fine dexterous tool-use remain near the frontier's limits; generalization across fabric types further reduces success.

task: Prepare dyeing machines for production runs, and conduct test runs of machines to ensure their proper operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one dyeing machine for a production run plus a test run cycle; skilled operators typically spend 30–60 min on setup, chemical loading, parameter entry, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial dyeing machinery with proprietary controls, handling liquid chemicals, interpreting color/quality test results, and adjusting parameters — well beyond 2026 humanoid robot generalization and tool-use capabilities.

task: Administer first aid and cardiopulmonary resuscitation to injured persons or provide emergency medical care such as basic or advanced life support.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single emergency intervention (e.g., CPR until handoff to EMS or AED restore). Median active intervention time is ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: CPR requires precise chest-compression force, airway management, real-time patient assessment, and split-second clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack medical sensors, certified protocols, and the reliability needed in life-or-death, unstructured emergency scenarios.

task: Position and climb ladders to gain access to upper levels of buildings, or to rescue individuals from burning structures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning a ladder and climbing to an upper level or rescue point. A trained firefighter/worker typically takes 5–10 minutes including setup, securing, and ascent.
robot_success_rationale: Ladder climbing requires dynamic balance, grip adaptation on rungs, and maneuvering in unstructured environments under stress. Current humanoids struggle severely with ladder climbing, carrying loads, and operating in chaotic fire/rescue scenes.

task: Install, level, and align components such as gears, chains, dies, cutters, and needles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing and aligning a set of mechanical components (e.g., gears/dies/cutters) on one machine. A skilled mechanic typically takes 30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity, precise torque control, and sub-millimeter alignment verification with gauges/tools. Current humanoids lack the tactile feedback, specialized tool use, and precision needed for leveling and aligning industrial components reliably.

task: Collect and analyze water samples as part of field investigations or to validate data from automatic monitors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a water sample at a single field site: travel to site, locate sampling point, collect/label/seal sample, record field data. Typically 30–60 min per site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating unstructured outdoor/aquatic terrain, precisely submerging/filling sample bottles, sealing and labeling containers, and operating field instruments — far beyond current humanoid capability in real outdoor environments.

task: Package collected pieces of evidence in securely closed containers, such as bags, crates, or boxes, to protect them.
human_time_rationale: One instance = packaging a single piece of evidence into a container, labeling, and sealing it. A trained technician typically takes ~5 minutes per item.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (sealing bags, closing latches), chain-of-custody labeling, handling varied/fragile evidence shapes, and strict procedural compliance. Current robots lack reliable fine manipulation and contextual judgment for forensic standards.

task: Repair or replace worn or defective parts or components, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing or replacing a single worn/defective part (e.g., a bolt, seal, or small component). A competent technician typically spends ~30–60 min diagnosing, removing, and replacing one part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing worn parts, selecting correct tools, applying variable torque, and handling diverse fasteners/components in unstructured settings. Far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and tool-use reliability.

task: Examine fire sites and collect evidence such as glass, metal fragments, charred wood, and accelerant residue for use in determining the cause of a fire.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single fire site investigation. A competent fire investigator typically spends 1–3 hours collecting physical evidence from a scene, averaging ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating severely hazardous, structurally unstable, debris-filled terrain; identifying subtle forensic evidence (accelerant residue, burn patterns, micro-fragments); and using specialized collection tools — all far beyond 2026 robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Train new employees to control and suppress fires.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one training session for a group of new employees, covering fire control/suppression theory, equipment use, and practical drills. A typical session runs ~3–4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Training requires verbal instruction, adaptive explanation, live demonstration with fire equipment, and reading trainee comprehension. Current humanoids lack pedagogical reasoning, dynamic risk management, and safe live-fire operation capability.

task: Adjust machine controls, such as heating mechanisms, tensions, or speeds, to produce specified products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a set of machine controls (dials, levers, or digital inputs) to hit a specification. A competent operator typically takes 3–7 minutes per adjustment cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading gauges/displays, interpreting specs, and manipulating varied control interfaces (knobs, levers, touchscreens). Perception and fine dexterity on industrial controls is weak; contextual judgment to hit product specs adds further difficulty.

task: Start and control machines and equipment to wash, bleach, dye, or otherwise process and finish fabric, yarn, thread, or other textile goods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a batch of textile goods, setting machine parameters, starting the cycle, and monitoring until completion. Typical setup and handoff takes ~20 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial textile machinery with varied controls, loading bulky/soft goods, mixing chemicals precisely, and monitoring process quality. Humanoids lack the dexterity, domain-specific tool-use, and chemical handling capability for this industrial task.

task: Take photographs of displays or signage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = photographing a single display or sign. A competent human takes ~1–3 min to frame, focus, and capture an acceptable photo of one display/sign.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to a sign and trigger a camera, but consistent framing, handling glare/reflections, adjusting for varied lighting, and confirming image quality require perception and autonomy still unreliable in unstructured environments.

task: Create functional or decorative objects by hand, using a variety of methods and materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing one handcrafted object (e.g., a small pottery piece, woven basket, or carved item). Skilled artisans typically spend 1–3 hours on a single moderate object.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine, adaptive dexterity across highly varied materials and methods (clay, wood, fiber, glass, etc.), creative planning, and tactile feedback. Far beyond current robot generalization and fine motor capabilities.

task: Perform chemical analyses of the microorganism content of soils to determine microbial reactions or chemical mineralogical relationships to plant growth.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a soil sample, preparing it, and running a full chemical/microbial analysis in a lab. A competent soil scientist typically takes 3–5 hours end-to-end per sample batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level lab technique: pipetting, culturing, spectroscopy, interpreting biochemical results. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and cognitive generalization; no frontier robot can autonomously execute full soil microbiology workflows.

task: Observe display screens, control panels, equipment, and cloth entering or exiting processes to determine if equipment is operating correctly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring scan of displays, panels, and cloth flow at one workstation; a competent operator completes a full visual check in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read digital displays via vision but struggle with analog gauges, nuanced cloth defect detection, and interpreting control panel states reliably. Autonomous fault judgment in unstructured textile environments remains immature.

task: Clean, oil, and lubricate machines, using air hoses, cleaning solutions, rags, oilcans, and grease guns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning, oiling, and lubricating a single industrial machine. A competent maintenance worker typically takes 20–40 minutes depending on machine size and access points.
robot_success_rationale: Requires switching between multiple tools (air hose, oilcan, grease gun, rags), locating precise lubrication points on varied machinery, applying correct pressure/amount, and navigating tight spaces. Current robots lack the dexterity, tool-switching, and task generalization needed reliably.

task: Install repaired equipment in various settings, such as industrial or military establishments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one repaired equipment unit in an industrial/military site; includes positioning, securing, connecting, and basic verification. Median ~90 min covers moderate-complexity jobs.
robot_success_rationale: Highly variable, unstructured environments (industrial/military); requires heavy lifting, precise alignment, tool use, electrical/mechanical connections, and domain judgment. Far beyond reliable autonomous capability in 2026.

task: Test metals to determine conformance to specifications of mechanical strength, strength-weight ratio, ductility, magnetic and electrical properties, and resistance to abrasion, corrosion, heat, and cold.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full test battery on a single metal sample. Setup, calibration, running multiple standardized tests, and logging results typically takes ~1.5 hours for a trained metallurgical technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized lab instruments (tensile testers, spectrometers, furnaces, electrochemical cells), precise sample handling, calibration, and multi-step test sequencing. Far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Inspect machinery to determine whether repairs are needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single machine. A competent technician spends ~15–25 min checking components, listening, looking for wear, leaks, or damage, and making a repair determination.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can visually scan surfaces and detect obvious anomalies, but diagnosing repair needs requires multi-modal sensing (sound, vibration, smell, tactile), contextual judgment, and accessing tight spaces — well beyond reliable current capability.

task: Install, maintain, and calibrate instruments such as those that monitor water levels, rainfall, and sediments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = visiting a field instrument station to install, maintain, or calibrate one device (e.g., a water-level gauge or rain gauge). Typical field technician visit runs 60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/rough-terrain mobility to reach remote field sites, fine dexterous tool-use for calibration and wiring, domain-specific instrument knowledge, and safe operation near water. Current humanoids fail on nearly all these axes in unstructured outdoor environments.

task: Inspect buildings to locate hazardous conditions and fire code violations, such as accumulations of combustible material, electrical wiring problems, and inadequate or non-functional fire exits.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one building inspection. A competent fire inspector typically spends 60–120 minutes surveying a mid-sized commercial building for hazards, code violations, and exit conditions.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex, unfamiliar multi-floor buildings, reading signage, interpreting ambiguous code violations, assessing wiring conditions, and writing a compliant report — well beyond current generalization, perception, and judgment capabilities of 2026 humanoids.

task: Acquire, distribute and store supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single supply run: retrieving, transporting, and shelving a typical batch of supplies (e.g., office or warehouse restock). Includes checking inventory and organizing storage.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step locomotion, object recognition across varied items, navigation to multiple locations, and organized placement. Current humanoids struggle with unstructured environments, varied object types, and multi-room coordination without human guidance.

task: Search to locate fire survivors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = searching a single structure or defined search zone. A trained firefighter/SAR responder typically takes 30–90 min to systematically clear a burning or post-fire building.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating smoke-filled, structurally unstable, debris-laden environments; detecting unconscious survivors; withstanding extreme heat; communicating finds. Current humanoids lack thermal tolerance, robust SLAM in chaotic scenes, and certified SAR protocols.

task: Thread yarn, thread, or fabric through guides, needles, and rollers of machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = threading a single machine (e.g., industrial sewing or knitting machine). A skilled operator typically takes 3–7 minutes depending on machine complexity.
