task: Prepare daily food items, and cook simple foods and beverages, such as sandwiches, salads, soups, pizza, or coffee, using proper safety precautions and sanitary measures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and cooking a single simple food item or beverage (e.g., a sandwich, salad, soup bowl, or coffee). A competent worker completes this in ~15 minutes including prep and plating.
robot_success_rationale: Cooking requires chaining many fine-dexterous steps (cutting, pouring, spreading, heat management) in unstructured kitchen environments. Current humanoids lack reliable manipulation, recipe reasoning, and safe tool-use for end-to-end cooking tasks. ~5–20% per anchors.

task: Construct and fabricate appliances, or supervise others constructing the appliances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating or overseeing fabrication of one appliance unit (e.g., HVAC unit, water heater). A competent technician/supervisor typically takes 1–3 hours per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Appliance fabrication requires complex multi-step assembly, precision welding/forming, tool-switching, quality judgment, and supervisory coordination — far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and dexterity capabilities.

task: Press buttons to stop machines when processes are complete or when malfunctions are detected.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pressing the stop button on one machine. Worker identifies the correct button and presses it; typically takes ~30 seconds including recognition.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can press clearly labeled buttons on flat panels (~60–70%), but detecting a malfunction or process completion requires contextual perception and situational awareness, significantly lowering reliability. Button identification on varied industrial machinery adds further challenge.

task: Clean and organize eating, service, and kitchen areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full cleaning/organizing cycle of a typical eating, service, and kitchen area (e.g., restaurant or cafeteria); includes wiping surfaces, resetting tables, tidying storage.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep/mop and wipe flat surfaces moderately well, but organizing varied items across multiple distinct zones (dining, service counter, kitchen) requires generalization, context-aware placement, and navigation through cluttered, dynamic spaces—well beyond reliable current capability.

task: Wrap menu items such as sandwiches, hot entrees, and desserts for serving or for takeout.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wrapping a single menu item (sandwich, entree, or dessert). A skilled worker takes ~1–2 minutes per item including grabbing wrap material, folding, and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dexterous bimanual manipulation of flexible, deformable wrapping materials (foil, paper, plastic wrap) around irregularly shaped food items. Folding and securing edges neatly is a major challenge for current robots. Perception of soft/deformable materials and precise in-hand re-grasping remain weak spots for 2026 frontier humanoids.

task: Observe bobbins as they are winding and cut threads to remove loaded bobbins, using knives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = monitoring one bobbin until full, cutting the thread with a knife, and removing it. Experienced textile workers complete this in roughly 1–3 minutes per bobbin.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise visual detection of bobbin fullness, fine dexterous knife use on thin threads, and coordinated removal — all in a dynamic, fast-moving textile machine environment. Current robots lack the fine perception and manipulation reliability needed.

task: Use traditional or digital cameras, along with a variety of equipment, such as tripods, filters, and flash attachments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a typical photo shoot session (setup, shooting, breakdown). A competent photographer spends ~30–60 min setting up tripods, attaching filters/flash, adjusting settings, and shooting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of small camera accessories (filters, flash shoes, tripod knobs), nuanced aesthetic judgment, creative composition decisions, and dynamic setting adjustments — all far beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Design orthopedic and prosthetic devices, based on physicians' prescriptions and examination and measurement of patients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing a single orthopedic/prosthetic device for one patient, including reviewing prescription, taking/interpreting measurements, and producing a design plan. Typically 1–3 hours for a skilled orthotist/prosthetist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, patient interaction, precise anatomical measurement, and complex CAD/design expertise. No current humanoid robot can integrate physician prescriptions, conduct patient assessments, and produce an accepted orthotic/prosthetic design autonomously.

task: Add relishes and garnishes to food orders, according to instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = garnishing/adding relishes to a single food order. A competent worker does this in roughly 1–2 minutes per plate.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for precise placement of small, varied garnishes; interpreting per-order instructions adds complexity. Current robots struggle with delicate food handling, sauce dispensing, and aesthetic placement standards.

task: Remove polymer deposits from spinnerettes and equipment, using silicone spray, brass chisels, and bronze-wool pads.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning deposits from one spinnerette and its associated equipment. A skilled technician typically spends 30–60 min scrubbing, chiseling, and spraying per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterity with multiple specialized tools (chisel, bronze-wool, spray) on tiny, delicate spinnerette orifices, tactile force control to avoid damage, and fine perception of polymer deposits — far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Clean and maintain extruding and forming machines, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and maintaining a single extruding/forming machine; a competent operator typically spends ~30–60 min per machine per maintenance cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating around large industrial machinery, using varied hand tools (wrenches, scrapers, brushes) with precision, accessing tight/awkward spaces, and judging contamination/wear — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and generalization in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Inspect installed exhibits for conformance to specifications and satisfactory operation of special-effects components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one exhibit inspection. Technician checks physical specs, triggers/tests special-effects components, and documents results; typically 30–60 min per exhibit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading spec sheets, interpreting complex multi-modal exhibit configurations, triggering and evaluating special-effects (lighting, sound, motion, pyro), and making conformance judgments. Far beyond current general-purpose robot perception and reasoning capabilities.

task: Place people in protective custody.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing one individual into protective custody, including assessment, physical escort/transport, paperwork, and transfer to a secure facility. Estimated ~45 min end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires legal authority, complex social/verbal negotiation, safe physical restraint of a potentially non-compliant person, situational judgment, and inter-agency coordination — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities. Safety and legal constraints make autonomous operation virtually impossible.

task: Set up dining areas for meals, and clear them following meals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up + clearing one dining area (e.g. 4–6 place settings). A competent worker takes ~7 min to set and ~8 min to clear.
robot_success_rationale: Requires placing varied tableware precisely, carrying trays, navigating chairs/tables, and clearing dirty dishes — moderate dexterity and clutter handling. Current humanoids struggle with full sequential multi-step table service reliably.

task: Observe machine operations, control boards, and gauges to detect malfunctions such as clogged bushings and defective binder applicators.
human_time_rationale: One inspection round of a machine station — checking gauges, control boards, and operational behavior for faults. A skilled operator completes this in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read gauges and navigate to control panels, but detecting subtle anomalies (clogged bushings, defective binder applicators) requires domain-specific visual/tactile interpretation, contextual judgment, and generalization across machine states that current systems lack reliably.

task: Record operational data on tags, and attach tags to machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = recording data on a tag and attaching it to one machine. A competent worker reads/writes data and secures the tag in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Writing legibly on tags is a fine-motor challenge; attaching tags (wire loops, adhesives, zip ties) to varied machine surfaces requires dexterous manipulation and generalization. Perception of correct attachment points also adds difficulty.

task: Accompany patients on outings, providing transportation when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one outing (e.g., a medical appointment or recreational trip). Includes travel to/from destination plus time at the location; typical outing ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving or operating a vehicle (humanoids cannot drive in traffic), navigating unpredictable outdoor environments, providing social/emotional accompaniment, and responding to patient needs — all far beyond 2026 frontier robot capabilities.

task: Adjust apertures, shutter speeds, and camera focus according to a combination of factors, such as lighting, field depth, subject motion, film type, and film speed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting camera settings for a single shot setup. A skilled photographer assesses conditions and dials in aperture, shutter speed, and focus in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine finger dexterity to operate small camera dials/rings, nuanced perceptual judgment of lighting/depth/motion, and creative/contextual decision-making. Current humanoids lack the precision manipulation and photographic reasoning needed.

task: Distribute food to servers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = plating and handing off one table's order to waiting servers at a kitchen pass. A competent expediter takes ~2–4 minutes per table's worth of dishes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct dishes, reading tickets, dynamic coordination with multiple humans, carrying loaded plates without spilling, and communicating verbally — well beyond current robot generalization and dexterity in a busy kitchen environment.

task: Repair, rebuild, and modify prosthetic and orthopedic appliances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing or modifying a single prosthetic/orthopedic appliance. A competent orthotist/prosthetist typically spends 60–120 minutes on assessment, disassembly, fabrication/adjustment, and fitting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level fine dexterity, material fabrication (thermoforming, grinding, laminating), individualized clinical judgment, patient interaction, and custom tool use — all far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Make and modify plaster casts of areas to be fitted with prostheses or orthoses to guide the device construction process.
human_time_rationale: One instance = casting one limb segment. Includes prep, wrapping, setting (~10 min), removal, inspection, and cast modification by a skilled clinician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly precise, adaptive dexterous manipulation of wet plaster on irregular human anatomy, patient interaction/positioning, tactile feedback for correct fit, and clinical judgment for modifications — far beyond current robot capability.

task: Treat snoring problems, using laser surgery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one laser-assisted uvulopalatoplasty session per patient, including prep, anesthesia, laser application, and post-op check. Typically 30–60 minutes in clinic.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity inside a moving patient's oral cavity, real-time clinical judgment, sterile technique, precise laser targeting on soft tissue, and medical licensure. Far beyond any 2026 general-purpose humanoid robot's capabilities.

task: Replenish foods at serving stations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = refilling one food item at one serving station. A worker retrieves the food from storage and transfers it to the station, typically taking ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a dynamic food-service environment, identifying depleted items, retrieving correct containers from storage, and pouring/placing food without spillage. Perception of fill levels, grasping varied containers, and precise pouring remain significant challenges for current humanoids.

task: Perform sales floor work, such as greeting or assisting customers, stocking shelves, or taking inventory.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a standard shift block of sales floor work (greeting/assisting customers, stocking shelves, taking inventory). ~30 min is a reasonable unit a supervisor would measure for a discrete work cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Stocking shelves and inventory scanning are partially within reach (~40-60%), but natural language customer interaction and social navigation in a dynamic retail environment remain very limited. Combined task lowers overall probability significantly.

task: Assist occupational therapists in planning, implementing, and administering therapy programs to restore, reinforce, and enhance performance, using selected activities and special equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one therapy session with a single patient. A competent OT assistant typically spends ~45 min planning, setting up equipment, and guiding a patient through a structured session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical reasoning, adaptive communication, empathetic patient interaction, dynamic activity modification, and safe handling of diverse patients and therapeutic equipment — far beyond current robot cognition, social, and dexterous capabilities.

task: Press metering-pump buttons and turn valves to stop flow of polymers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stopping flow on a single metering pump: locate pump/valves, press stop button, turn shutoff valve. A trained operator completes this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Pressing industrial buttons is feasible (~60–70%), but turning industrial valves (especially handwheels or tight quarter-turn valves) requires significant torque, precise grip, and generalization to varied valve types — challenging for current humanoids. Navigation to correct pump adds further uncertainty.

task: Encourage patients and attend to their physical needs to facilitate the attainment of therapeutic goals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient interaction/session involving emotional encouragement plus physical assistance (e.g., repositioning, hydration, comfort). Typical duration ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced emotional communication, adaptive social judgment, and unstructured physical caregiving. Current humanoids lack reliable empathetic interaction, safe patient-body handling, and therapeutic context awareness needed for acceptable quality.

task: Examine furniture frames, upholstery, springs, and webbing to locate defects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one furniture piece. A skilled upholstery inspector takes ~5–10 min to examine frame, springs, webbing, and fabric for defects.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal tactile + visual inspection of hidden substructure (springs, webbing beneath fabric), flex/stress testing of joints, and defect judgment. Current robots lack the nuanced tactile sensing, tool manipulation (lifting/probing fabric), and defect-recognition generalization needed.

task: Prepare and serve cold drinks, frozen milk drinks, or desserts, using drink-dispensing, milkshake, or frozen-custard machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one drink/dessert order. A competent worker takes ~1–3 min to dispense, assemble toppings, and serve.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized dispensing machines (buttons/levers), holding cups, adding toppings, and handing off to a customer. Machine interfaces vary widely; cup handling and precise dispensing under a nozzle demand reliable dexterity and perception that current humanoids lack in unstructured food-service settings.

task: Prepare and maintain work area, materials, and equipment and maintain inventory of treatment and educational supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single session of preparing/tidying the work area, restocking supplies, and logging inventory. A competent worker typically takes ~30 min per shift.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a mixed clinical/educational environment, identifying and restocking varied small supplies, and tracking inventory via cognitive record-keeping. Current humanoids lack reliable generalization across supply types, labeling, and inventory logging software integration.

task: Cure, smoke, tenderize, and preserve meat.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying a cure (salt/sugar/nitrate rub or brine) to one cut of meat. Active prep takes ~30–90 min; median ~60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires measuring precise ingredient ratios, coating/rubbing uneven meat surfaces uniformly, and packing into containers. Dexterous manipulation of soft, irregular meat and fine measurement challenge current robots.

task: Observe flow of finish across finish rollers, and turn valves to adjust flow to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one observation-and-adjustment cycle: visually check finish flow across rollers and turn valve(s) to spec. Competent operator completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting subtle visual flow patterns on wet finish rollers, then precisely turning industrial valves to spec. Current robots lack reliable fluid-flow perception and fine valve-manipulation in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Restrain, secure, or control offenders, using chemical agents, firearms, or other weapons of force as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one restraint/control event. A typical use-of-force incident, from initial contact to secured offender, lasts ~10–20 minutes for a trained officer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time threat assessment, legal/ethical judgment, dynamic physical struggle, and safe use of lethal/less-lethal weapons. Current humanoids lack the perception, force control, social reasoning, and legal authorization for autonomous use of force.

task: Lower pans inside cabinets to catch molten filaments until flow of polymer through packs has stopped.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning catch pans under spinning packs inside a cabinet until polymer flow stops; a skilled worker handles this in ~5 minutes per cabinet.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating tight industrial cabinet interiors, handling hot/slippery molten polymer catch pans, monitoring variable flow cessation, and adapting to cramped, hazardous, unstructured geometry — well beyond current robot dexterity and perception limits.

task: Draw cutting lines on material following patterns, templates, sketches, or blueprints, using chalk, pencils, paint, or other methods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking cutting lines on a single piece of material using a pattern/template. A skilled worker typically spends ~10–20 min laying out, aligning, and marking one piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise perception of flexible/varied materials, accurate fine-motor marking with chalk/pencil along irregular templates, and spatial reasoning to align patterns. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity and pattern-alignment accuracy for acceptable marking quality.

task: Interweave and fasten strips of webbing to the backs and undersides of furniture, using small hand tools and fasteners.
human_time_rationale: One instance = webbing a single furniture piece (e.g. chair seat/back). A skilled upholsterer takes ~20–30 min to interweave and staple/tack all strips.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual dexterity to tension, interweave, and fasten narrow flexible strips in tight spaces; current humanoids lack the fine grip force control, weaving dexterity, and tool-use accuracy needed for consistent quality.

task: Pick up and deliver furniture.
human_time_rationale: One instance = picking up a single furniture item and delivering it to its destination (e.g., loading, transporting, and placing one piece). Includes handling, carrying, and positioning.
robot_success_rationale: Furniture is heavy, bulky, and irregularly shaped, requiring strong grasp, coordinated two-arm/whole-body lifting, stair navigation, and precise placement. Current humanoids lack the payload capacity, robust outdoor/stair locomotion, and generalization needed for real-world furniture delivery.

task: Transfer luggage, trunks, and packages to and from rooms, loading areas, vehicles, or transportation terminals, by hand or using baggage carts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = transferring a typical guest's set of luggage (2–3 bags) between a vehicle/terminal and a room or loading area, including cart use and navigation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating mixed indoor/outdoor terrain, operating luggage carts, grasping varied bag shapes/handles, using elevators, and finding correct rooms — too many unstructured steps for reliable autonomous completion in 2026.

task: Plan, prepare, and deliver meals to individuals with special dietary needs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = planning, preparing, and delivering a single meal for one individual. Includes ~15 min planning/customizing, ~40 min cooking, ~20 min delivery/setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dietary knowledge/reasoning, complex multi-step cooking, safe navigation for delivery, and interpersonal caregiving. Current robots lack the integrated cognition, fine culinary dexterity, and reliable generalization across all three sub-tasks.

task: Repair furniture frames and refinish exposed wood.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing and refinishing one furniture piece. Includes sanding, gluing/clamping, applying stain/finish, and drying prep — typically 1–2 hours of active work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly variable dexterity: diagnosing structural damage, precise sanding, clamping, and even brush/stain application on irregular surfaces. Far beyond current general-purpose robot generalization and fine motor capability.

task: Measure and cut new covering materials, using patterns and measuring and cutting instruments, following sketches and design specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and cutting a single piece of covering material per pattern/spec. A skilled upholsterer/tailor takes ~15–25 min including layout, marking, and cutting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting 2D sketches/specs, precise pattern layout on flexible fabric/material, accurate marking, and controlled cutting with shears or rotary tools — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, perception, and tool-use generalization.

task: Position, align, or adjust workpieces or electrical parts to facilitate wiring or assembly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning/aligning a single workpiece or electrical part for wiring or assembly. A competent worker typically takes 3–7 minutes depending on part complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle structured pick-and-place well, but precise alignment of electrical parts demands fine dexterity, accurate perception of small features, and tolerance for varied geometries — pushing success rates down significantly.

task: Perform minor repairs or make adjustments to slot machines, resolving problems such as machine tilts and coin jams.
human_time_rationale: One instance = resolving a single slot machine issue (e.g., coin jam or tilt). A skilled technician typically clears, adjusts, and verifies the fix in ~10–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing varied faults inside complex proprietary machinery, fine dexterous manipulation in confined spaces, and interpreting machine-specific error states. Current robots lack the specialized perception, tool-use, and fault-diagnosis needed for reliable autonomous repair.

task: Make, restore, or create custom upholstered furniture, using hand tools and knowledge of fabrics and upholstery methods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = upholstering a single piece of furniture (e.g., a chair). A competent upholsterer typically takes 3–5 hours per piece; 4 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly complex dexterous manipulation: cutting, stretching, tacking, and sewing fabric over irregular 3D forms with precise tension. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and generalization. No demonstrated capability exists.

task: Deliver orders to kitchens, and pick up and serve food when it is ready.
human_time_rationale: One instance = delivering one order to the kitchen and returning with the ready food to serve a table; typical restaurant cycle takes ~5 minutes end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a crowded, dynamic restaurant, communicating orders, carrying trays with full plates (spill risk), serving to correct customers — high perception, social, and dexterous demands far exceed current humanoid capabilities in unstructured environments.

task: Transfer or transport offenders on foot or by driving vehicles, such as trailers, vans, or buses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one offender transport trip. Median trip includes loading, transit, and handoff; typical correctional transport runs 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving vans/buses on public roads (humanoids cannot operate vehicles in traffic), plus managing non-compliant individuals, restraints, and legal custody handoffs — all far beyond current robot capability.

task: Transport patients to and from the occupational therapy work area.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient transport trip (room to OT area and/or return). Includes fetching wheelchair/bed, navigating hallways, and handing off patient, ~10 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safely maneuvering a patient in a wheelchair/gurney through busy hospital corridors, interacting with a vulnerable person, operating elevators, and ensuring patient safety — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, patient-handling, and liability limits.

task: Arrange tables and decorations according to instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = arranging tables and decorations for a single event setup (e.g., banquet room). A competent worker typically spends 30–60 minutes following layout instructions.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting layout diagrams, moving heavy/awkward furniture across varied floor space, placing diverse decorative items precisely, and generalizing to novel environments. Current humanoids lack reliable heavy-object mobility, spatial plan interpretation, and fine decorative placement at acceptable quality.

task: Build furniture up with loose fiber stuffing, cotton, felt, or foam padding to form smooth, rounded surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = padding a single furniture piece (e.g., a chair seat/back). A skilled upholsterer takes ~30–60 min to layer, shape, and smooth stuffing/foam into rounded, even surfaces.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly nuanced force control to distribute loose fiber/foam evenly, judge surface contour by touch and vision, and achieve a smooth rounded finish. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tactile sensing for unstructured soft-material shaping.

task: Take customers' orders and write ordered items on tickets, giving ticket stubs to customers when needed to identify filled orders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = taking one customer's order, writing items on ticket, handing stub. A competent worker completes this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires natural language conversation, handwriting on paper tickets, handing small stub to customer, and social interaction. Current humanoids lack reliable speech-driven order-taking, legible handwriting, and nuanced customer interaction generalization.

task: Respond to emergencies, such as escapes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = responding to a single escape/emergency event, from alert to resolution (containment, pursuit, communication, lockdown). Typical correctional/security incident lasts ~15–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rapid outdoor/unstructured pursuit, real-time decision-making, authority to act, communication with humans, and physical intervention. Current humanoids lack speed, legal authority, situational judgment, and robust outdoor mobility for dynamic emergencies.

task: Exchange currency for customers, converting currency into requested combinations of bills and coins.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one currency exchange transaction: counting received currency, calculating conversion, and dispensing correct bills/coins to a single customer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous handling of paper bills and coins (high slip/deformation risk), real-time arithmetic with customer interaction, and reliable denomination recognition. Current humanoids lack sufficient finger sensitivity and cash-handling reliability for unsupervised teller work.

task: Inspect work areas or operating equipment to ensure conformance to established standards in areas such as cleanliness or maintenance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single work area or piece of equipment. A competent worker walks the area, checks surfaces/equipment against standards, and notes findings — roughly 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate and use cameras for basic visual inspection, but reliably detecting subtle cleanliness issues, maintenance defects, or standards conformance across varied, unstructured environments requires nuanced perception and contextual judgment beyond current frontier capability.

task: Verify conformance of workpieces to specifications, using squares, rulers, and measuring tapes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single workpiece with squares, rulers, and measuring tapes; a competent worker typically completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with precise manual tool manipulation (rulers, squares, tape measures), consistent contact alignment, and reading analog scales. Perception of exact measurements and handling flexible tapes is still unreliable for frontier humanoids.

task: Stretch webbing and fabric, using webbing stretchers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stretching webbing/fabric on a single piece of furniture (e.g., a chair seat). A skilled upholsterer takes ~5–10 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force, precisely controlled tensioning with a specialized hand tool (webbing stretcher), fine grip on flexible fabric, and tactile feedback to avoid over/under-stretching. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity.

task: Attach "out of order" signs to malfunctioning machines, and notify technicians when machines need to be repaired or removed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tagging one malfunctioning machine with a sign and notifying a technician via phone/system. A competent worker completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot must detect malfunction (perception challenge), attach/hang a physical sign (dexterous manipulation on varied surfaces), and send a notification (digital integration). Each step is partially feasible but combined reliability is low; sign attachment on arbitrary machines is especially hard.

task: Detain persons found to be in violation of customs or immigration laws and arrange for legal action, such as deportation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = detaining one violator and initiating legal/deportation paperwork. Involves assessment, physical detention, documentation, and coordination with legal authorities — typically 1–2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires legal authority, complex social/cognitive judgment, physical restraint of uncooperative humans, and coordination with legal systems. Far beyond any current general-purpose robot's autonomous capability or legal mandate.

task: Greet incoming guests and escort them to their rooms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = greeting one guest/party at reception and walking them to their assigned room, including brief conversation and door access. Typically 5–15 minutes end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires natural social interaction, speech, navigation through unpredictable hotel environments, operating elevators/keycards, and handling luggage. Generalization across varied spaces and guest expectations is well beyond current frontier robots.

task: Fit, install, and secure material on frames, using hand tools, power tools, glue, cement, or staples.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting and securing material onto one frame (e.g., fabric, screen, panel). Competent worker takes ~15–35 min depending on material and fastening method used.
robot_success_rationale: Requires bimanual dexterity to tension and align flexible/varied materials, precise tool use (staple gun, glue, cement), and adaptive gripping. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and material-handling generalization for this multi-step task.

task: Clean parts, using cleaning solutions, air hoses, and cloths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single part (e.g., a mechanical or manufactured component). A competent worker applies solution, scrubs, blows with air hose, and wipes dry in ~8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated multi-tool use (spray bottle, air hose, cloth), grasping irregularly shaped parts, and judging cleanliness. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-switching dexterity and cleaning-quality perception for varied industrial parts.

task: Reset slot machines after payoffs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = resetting one slot machine after a single payoff event: acknowledging the win, clearing the display/credits, restocking hopper if needed, and returning machine to ready state. ~2 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a busy casino floor, interfacing with machine-specific touchscreens/buttons, interpreting varied UI states, and potentially handling coin hoppers. High variability in machine models, crowded environment, and fine UI interaction make autonomous success very unlikely for current humanoids.

task: Sew rips or tears in material, or create tufting, using needles and thread.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single rip/tear or completing one tufting section; a competent sewist takes ~15–25 min depending on size and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Threading a needle, maintaining precise tension, and guiding stitches through flexible fabric requires extreme fine dexterity and tactile feedback far beyond current humanoid robot capability. Generalization across tear shapes adds further difficulty.

task: Operate sewing machines or sew upholstery by hand to seam cushions and join various sections of covering material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = seaming/joining one cushion cover or upholstery section. A skilled upholsterer takes ~20–30 min to cut, align, and sew a typical seat cushion cover.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fabric handling, guiding limp/deformable material through a sewing machine or hand-sewing with consistent tension and seam alignment. Far beyond current robot dexterity and deformable-object manipulation capabilities.

task: Adjust and repair assistive devices and make adaptive changes to other equipment and to environments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting/repairing one assistive device or making one adaptive modification. A competent technician/therapist typically spends 30–60 min per device/environment adjustment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert clinical judgment, fine dexterous tool use (screwdrivers, straps, custom fittings), diagnosis of mechanical faults, and adaptive problem-solving across highly varied devices and environments — far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Fix minor problems, such as equipment jams, and notify repair personnel of major equipment problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = identifying and clearing a single equipment jam or flagging a major fault. A competent worker typically diagnoses and resolves or escalates in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can detect some jams via sensors, but clearing jams requires unstructured dexterity in tight spaces, and notifying personnel requires communication systems. Generalization across equipment types is very limited in 2026.

task: Supervise or perform searches of inmates or their quarters to locate contraband items.
human_time_rationale: One instance = searching one inmate and/or their cell. A corrections officer typically takes 15–25 min for a pat-down, cell search, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced human interaction, physical pat-downs, opening concealed spaces, recognizing novel contraband, and operating in legally/socially sensitive custodial environments. Current robots lack the legal authority, social compliance mechanisms, and fine tactile search capabilities needed.

task: Adjust or replace webbing, padding, or springs, and secure them in place.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting/replacing webbing, padding, or springs on a single piece of furniture (e.g., a chair or sofa cushion seat). A competent upholsterer takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high dexterity for threading/stretching webbing, compressing/hooking springs, and securing fasteners across varied materials. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation, force control, and tool-use needed for upholstery work.

task: Clean and maintain slot machines and surrounding areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one slot machine and its immediate surrounding area: wiping surfaces, emptying nearby trash, and cleaning the floor around it. ~15 min for a competent casino attendant.
robot_success_rationale: Navigating a busy casino floor, wiping curved/irregular machine surfaces, emptying small compartments, and avoiding patrons is complex. General-purpose robots lack the dexterity and situational awareness for reliable, accepted-quality autonomous cleaning here.

task: Deliver messages and room service orders, and run errands for guests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one delivery/errand trip (picking up item, navigating to guest room, handing off). Typical hotel errand takes ~10 min round-trip.
robot_success_rationale: Navigation in hotel corridors is feasible, but elevator use, door knocking, tray/item handling, guest interaction, and adapting to varied errand types remain unreliable for autonomous end-to-end completion in 2026.

task: Pulverize remaining bone fragments into smaller pieces, using specialized equipment, such as a cremulator or grinder.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one set of cremated remains through a cremulator/grinder. A trained operator loads, runs, and empties the machine in ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires loading irregular bone fragments into specialized equipment, operating controls, and safely handling fine remains. Moderate dexterity and perception demands, but unfamiliar specialized equipment, dusty/fine-particle environment, and safety/hygiene requirements reduce reliability significantly for current humanoids.

task: Wipe finish rollers with cloths and wash finish trays with water when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wiping rollers and washing one set of finish trays. A skilled worker takes ~10 min for this routine cleaning cycle at a finishing station.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping and manipulating cloths on curved rollers (precision wiping), then handling trays with water — wet, slippery surfaces, fine force control, and unstructured tool-use challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Paint metal surfaces electrostatically, or by using a spray gun or other painting equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = painting one typical metal workpiece/panel. A competent painter spends ~45 min including setup, masking, spraying, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Dedicated spray robots exist in auto factories, but a general-purpose humanoid lacks the specialized spray gun control, consistent motion paths, surface-distance regulation, and masking dexterity needed for acceptable finish quality.

task: Make, repair, or replace automobile upholstery and convertible and vinyl tops, using knowledge of fabric and upholstery methods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a typical upholstery job (e.g., re-covering a seat or repairing a convertible top). A competent auto upholsterer takes ~2–4 hours per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly precise dexterous manipulation of flexible fabrics, cutting, sewing, stapling, and fitting to complex curved surfaces. Far beyond current robot dexterity; no frontier humanoid can handle unstructured soft-goods work at this complexity.

task: Receive and mark baggage by completing and attaching claim checks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one piece of baggage: printing/writing a claim check, attaching it to the bag, and handing the stub to the passenger. Typically takes ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle paper/adhesive tags and attach them securely to varied bag shapes; also involves reading/printing labels and social interaction with passengers. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-manipulation and workflow integration for this multi-step task.

task: Attach bindings or apply solutions to edges of cut material to prevent raveling.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing the edges of one cut piece (e.g., a fabric panel). A competent worker pins, folds, and sews or applies binding tape/solution to all edges in roughly 10–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to align and attach flexible binding to irregular fabric edges, handle limp materials, and apply consistent tension. Current humanoids lack the precision and tactile feedback for reliable sewing or tape application on deformable cloth.

task: Embalm, dress, or otherwise prepare the deceased for viewing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one deceased person. Full embalming, cosmetic restoration, dressing, and positioning for viewing typically takes a licensed mortician 2–4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires invasive surgical-like fluid replacement, precise cosmetic restoration, delicate dressing of a limp human body, and nuanced aesthetic judgment. Far beyond current robot dexterity, tool-use, and generalization capabilities. No credible 2026 deployment exists.

task: Follow blueprints to produce specific designs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating/assembling one design from a blueprint. Complexity varies widely; 2 hours is a reasonable median for a skilled tradesperson completing one specification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading and interpreting 2D/3D blueprints, selecting correct materials/tools, and executing precise multi-step fabrication. Blueprint comprehension, adaptive planning, and fine dexterous tooling far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Carry injured offenders or employees to safety and provide emergency first aid when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = carrying one injured person to safety and administering basic first aid (e.g., wound care, CPR); typically 10–20 minutes depending on distance and injury severity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safe physical handling of an uncooperative/limp human body (extreme dexterity/strength), dynamic navigation in chaotic emergency environments, triage judgment, and skilled first aid (CPR, bandaging). All far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Mark and tag components so that stock inventory can be tracked and identified.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking and tagging a single component (e.g., applying a label, barcode, or RFID tag and logging it). A competent worker handles this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place labels on flat surfaces, but precise tag application on varied component shapes, reading/verifying codes, and operating labeling tools or printers requires fine dexterity and perception generalization that current humanoids handle inconsistently.

task: Locate and seize contraband, undeclared merchandise, and vehicles, aircraft, or boats that contain such merchandise.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one enforcement operation: searching a vehicle/vessel/aircraft, identifying contraband, and completing the seizure. Typically 1–2 hours for a trained officer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires legal authority, dynamic unstructured environments (vehicles, aircraft, vessels), complex perception of hidden contraband, interaction with people, and operation of specialized scanning/seizure equipment—far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Fill and smooth cracks or depressions, remove marks and imperfections, and repair broken parts, using plastic or wood putty, glue, nails, or screws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single surface area or broken part (e.g., filling/sanding a crack, regluing a joint). Includes prep, application, and smoothing; ~25 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for putty application, sanding, and targeted glue/nail/screw placement; precise force control and tactile feedback for smoothing; highly unstructured surfaces; well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Smooth, shape, and touch up surfaces to prepare them for finishing, using sandpaper, pumice stones, steel wool, chisels, sanders, or grinders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one surface (e.g., a furniture panel or door). A competent worker spends ~20–30 min assessing, selecting tools, and progressively smoothing/shaping one surface.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced force feedback, tool switching (sander, chisel, steel wool), surface quality judgment by touch and sight, and adaptive motion across irregular geometries. Current humanoids lack the fine haptic control and surface-assessment perception needed for acceptable finish quality.

task: Distress surfaces with woodworking tools or abrasives before staining to create an antique appearance, or rub surfaces to bring out highlights and shadings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = distressing one furniture piece or surface panel. A competent finisher spends ~20–30 min applying dents, scratches, and rub-through with tools/abrasives to achieve a convincing antique look.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly nuanced, variable force control and artistic judgment to create naturalistic wear patterns. Current robots lack the fine dexterity, tactile feedback, and aesthetic reasoning needed; risk of over-distressing or damaging the workpiece is very high.

task: Drill or tap holes in specified equipment locations to mount control units or to provide openings for elements, wiring, or instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling/tapping holes at a specified location on one piece of equipment. A competent technician selects the right bit, positions, drills, and taps 2–4 holes in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool selection, chuck-loading a drill/tap, applying controlled axial force and torque at exact marked locations, and verifying thread quality. Current humanoids lack reliable fine tool-use, torque control, and positional accuracy for this precision task.

task: Disassemble items to prepare them for finishing, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling a single item (e.g., furniture piece or manufactured part) using hand tools before a finishing process; ~20 min median for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying fastener types, applying correct torque, switching hand tools, and handling varied item geometries. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-switching dexterity and generalization across arbitrary disassembly tasks.

task: Pick up and handle human or pet remains in a respectful manner.
human_time_rationale: One instance = transferring/handling a single set of remains (e.g., placing in a bag or container). A trained mortuary or animal-control worker typically takes 20–40 minutes including wrapping, documentation, and respectful placement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme dexterity and sensitivity for irregular, fragile, variable-weight remains; demands situational awareness of "respectful" behavior (positioning, covering, cultural norms); current humanoids lack the fine-force control, social cognition, and ethical judgment needed.

task: Remove excess solvent, using cloths soaked in paint thinner.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wiping down a single surface/workpiece to remove excess solvent. A competent worker soaks a cloth and wipes the area clean in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires soaking a cloth in liquid (fluid handling is error-prone), then wiping a surface with appropriate pressure and coverage. Perception of "excess solvent" is ambiguous; cloth manipulation and chemical handling add further difficulty for current humanoids.

task: Measure and adjust voltages to specified values to determine operational accuracy of instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and adjusting voltages on one instrument to verify calibration. A competent technician needs ~20 min for setup, probing, reading, and fine adjustment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous probe placement on precise terminals, reading analog/digital meters, interpreting readings, and making delicate potentiometer/trim adjustments. Current humanoids lack the precision, tool-use fluency, and closed-loop electrical calibration reasoning needed.

task: Weigh envelopes containing statements to determine correct postage and affix postage, using stamps or metering equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one envelope: place on scale, read weight, determine postage rate, affix stamp or run through meter. A competent mail clerk takes ~1–3 minutes per envelope.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise grasping of thin envelopes, reading a scale display, interpreting postage rate tables, operating a postage meter or peeling/placing stamps accurately — multi-step dexterity and tool-use beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Remove covering, webbing, padding, or defective springs from workpieces, using hand tools such as hammers and tack pullers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stripping one furniture piece of covering, webbing, and padding. A skilled upholsterer typically needs 20–30 min to remove fabric, tacks/staples, and old padding.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force, precise prying with small hand tools (tack pullers, hammers) on irregular, deformable surfaces. Current robots lack the fine dexterity, adaptive grip, and force control needed for consistent tack/staple removal without damage.

task: Select food items from serving or storage areas and place them in dishes, on serving trays, or in take-out bags.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a single order (plate, tray, or bag). A competent worker identifies, retrieves, and plates/bags items in roughly 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying varied food items by appearance, grasping soft/irregular objects without damage, navigating storage areas, and meeting food-safe presentation standards — beyond reliable 2026 robot capability in unstructured food environments.

task: Load machines with statements, cancelled checks, or envelopes to prepare statements for distribution to customers or stuff envelopes by hand.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a batch/run of envelopes or statements into a machine, or hand-stuffing a batch (~50 envelopes). A competent clerk takes ~10–20 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Loading paper/envelopes into feeders requires precise alignment and thin-sheet handling; robots struggle with floppy paper, jam recovery, and machine interface diversity. Hand-stuffing demands delicate, high-speed dexterity robots lack reliably.

task: Brush, spray, or hand-rub finishing ingredients, such as paint, oil, stain, or wax, onto and into wood grain and apply lacquer or other sealers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing one medium wood surface (e.g., a tabletop). Includes applying stain/paint/oil, hand-rubbing into grain, and sealing with lacquer; ~30–60 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced force control for hand-rubbing grain, consistent spray/brush stroke pressure, and product sequencing. Current humanoids lack the fine-force feedback, spray tool handling, and judgment for finish quality a supervisor would accept.

task: Design, create, and decorate entire pieces or specific parts of furniture, such as draws for cabinets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing, constructing, and decorating a single furniture piece or component (e.g., a cabinet drawer). A skilled craftsperson typically spends 2–4 hours on a unit piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative design cognition, precise woodworking with specialized tools (saws, chisels, routers), fine dexterous joining, and aesthetic decorating. Current humanoids lack tool mastery, design agency, and the fine motor precision for quality craftsmanship.

task: Pack finished assemblies for shipment, and transport them to storage areas, using hoists or handtrucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = packing one finished assembly and transporting it to storage. Includes wrapping, boxing, labeling, loading onto handtruck, and moving to storage area. ~15 min for a mid-size item.
robot_success_rationale: Packing requires variable wrapping and boxing dexterity (low-moderate success). Operating a hoist or handtruck adds significant tool-use/mobility challenge. End-to-end autonomous completion across all sub-steps is unlikely with current humanoids.

task: Distribute materials, supplies, or subassemblies to work areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one distribution run delivering materials/supplies/subassemblies to multiple work areas in a typical facility; a competent worker completes this in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigation across varied indoor terrain, identifying correct items, carrying varied loads, and delivering to specific stations. Mobile manipulation is improving but item diversity, dynamic factory floors, and handoff complexity limit reliability.

task: Inspect guests' rooms to ensure that they are adequately stocked, orderly, and comfortable.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one guest room. A competent housekeeper/inspector walks the room, checks linens, amenities, cleanliness, and comfort items in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a furnished room, visually auditing dozens of varied stocking items, assessing subjective comfort/orderliness, and reporting issues. Current robots lack reliable semantic scene understanding, inventory counting, and soft-quality judgment in unstructured hotel environments.

task: Inspect cargo, baggage, and personal articles entering or leaving U.S. for compliance with revenue laws and U.S. customs regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single traveler's baggage or a cargo unit at a border checkpoint. A competent CBP officer typically spends 5–12 minutes per passenger/item inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating X-ray/scanning equipment, legal judgment on customs violations, interrogating passengers, and navigating complex port-of-entry environments. Current humanoids lack regulatory reasoning, specialized tool integration, and authority to make enforcement decisions. Perception of concealed contraband is highly specialized.

task: Set up and operate fabricating machines, such as brakes, rolls, shears, flame cutters, grinders, and drill presses, to bend, cut, form, punch, drill, or otherwise form and assemble metal components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setup plus one production run on a fabricating machine (e.g., brake press or drill press). Includes machine configuration, material positioning, operation, and quality check; ~30–60 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large, specialized industrial machines (brakes, shears, flame cutters, grinders, drill presses) with precise control, tooling changes, and safety interlocks. Humanoids cannot currently interface with or safely operate these machines autonomously.

task: Sweep or vacuum the cremation chamber to retrieve remains for storage in an urn or other container.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sweeping/vacuuming a single cremation chamber and collecting all remains. A trained mortuary worker typically takes 15–25 minutes to thoroughly retrieve, process, and transfer remains.
robot_success_rationale: Extremely confined, high-heat environment with irregular bone fragments, fine ash, and strict completeness requirements. Requires specialized tools, delicate handling, total retrieval assurance, and regulatory compliance — far beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Locate and mark workpiece bending and cutting lines, allowing for stock thickness, machine and welding shrinkage, and other component specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking layout lines on a single workpiece. A skilled layout mechanic/fitter reads specs, calculates offsets, and marks bend/cut lines, typically 15–35 min per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precision measurement, mental math for tolerances/shrinkage, scribing fine lines on metal, and interpreting engineering drawings. Current robots lack reliable fine-mark dexterity, drawing interpretation, and adaptive calculation for these compounded offsets.

task: Transport the deceased to a funeral home or crematory using a van, hearse, or other vehicle.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one transport trip: loading the deceased, driving to the funeral home/crematory (avg ~20–30 min transit), and transferring the body upon arrival. Total ~60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a hearse/van on public roads (humanoids cannot do this), plus careful handling of a body bag/stretcher in varied environments. Both driving and sensitive unstructured caregiving-like handling are far beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Lay out and examine metal stock or workpieces to be processed to ensure that specifications are met.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single workpiece or small batch. A competent worker measures, visually checks, and verifies spec compliance in roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can retrieve and position stock, and cameras can read markings, but precise dimensional measurement with calipers/gauges, cross-referencing spec sheets, and judgment on marginal defects remain unreliable for general-purpose humanoids in 2026.

task: Clean the crematorium, including tables, floors, and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one crematorium room (tables, floors, equipment) with disinfectants. A trained worker typically takes 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Crematorium cleaning requires biohazard-level protocols, precise disinfection of irregular equipment surfaces, handling specialized chemicals, and navigating tight spaces around heavy retorts. Current humanoids lack the biohazard compliance, tool-use precision, and task generalization needed.

task: Wash surfaces to prepare them for finish application.
human_time_rationale: One instance = washing one surface (e.g., a wall section or furniture piece) before finishing; a competent worker scrubs, rinses, and dries in ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can wipe flat surfaces but struggle with consistent pressure, detecting surface contamination, handling varied geometries, reaching edges/corners, and knowing when the surface is adequately clean for finish application.

task: Fabricate or form parts, coils, or structures according to specifications, using drills, calipers, cutters, or saws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating one part or structure. A competent machinist spends ~30–60 min measuring, cutting, drilling, and verifying tolerances on a typical workpiece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool-use (drills, saws, calipers), interpreting specs/blueprints, repositioning workpieces, and maintaining tight tolerances. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force control, and tool-switching needed for unsupervised fabrication work.

task: Distribute promotional materials at meetings, conferences, or trade shows.
human_time_rationale: One instance = distributing materials at one event/session (e.g., setting up a table, engaging attendees, handing out brochures/flyers for ~1 hour typical session).
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating crowded, dynamic environments, social interaction, handing objects to moving people, reading context, and adapting to varied materials/layouts. Far beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capabilities in unstructured social settings.

task: Design and construct templates and fixtures, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and constructing a single template or fixture. A skilled tradesperson typically spends 60–120 min measuring, marking, cutting, and assembling with hand tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting design intent, precise marking/measuring, controlled cutting, and skilled hand-tool use (saws, files, chisels). Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force feedback, and planning needed for multi-step fabrication from scratch.

task: Assign tasks such as feeding and treatment of animals, and cleaning and maintenance of animal quarters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assigning daily tasks (feeding, treatment, cleaning) to a team of animal care workers; a supervisor reviews needs and delegates in ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: This is a supervisory/cognitive coordination task — assigning work to humans. Robots lack authority structures, workforce management software integration, and judgment to assess animal health needs and delegate accordingly.

task: Replace or refurbish upholstery of items, using tacks, adhesives, softeners, solvents, stains, or polish.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reupholstering or refurbishing one furniture item (e.g., a chair seat). A competent upholsterer typically takes 1–3 hours per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly varied fine dexterity: cutting/stretching fabric, driving tacks, applying adhesives/solvents precisely, judging tension and aesthetics. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capability in unstructured settings.

task: Set up, operate, and adjust a variety of woodworking machines such as bandsaws and planers to cut and shape sections, parts, and patterns, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up, operating, and adjusting a machine for a single batch/job. A skilled woodworker typically spends 30–60 min on setup, cuts, and adjustments per job lot.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large stationary power machinery (bandsaws, planers), reading specs, making precise mechanical adjustments, and safely feeding stock. Current humanoid robots lack the tool-operation interfaces, force control, and safety protocols for industrial woodworking machinery.

task: Sanitize toys and play equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sanitizing a typical batch of toys/play equipment (e.g., a daycare bin or classroom set). Wiping, spraying, and air-drying ~10–20 items takes ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can grasp and wipe flat surfaces, but toys vary wildly in shape, size, and material. Applying even sanitizer coverage to irregular objects, reaching crevices, and judging completion quality are challenging for current humanoids.

task: Remove jewelry, watches, or other personal items from the deceased prior to cremation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing all jewelry/watches/personal items from one decedent. A trained worker inspects and carefully removes items; typically 5–15 min depending on quantity and difficulty of removal.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (rings, clasps, watch buckles on stiff/swollen limbs), careful force control on fragile tissue, and contextual judgment about what to remove. Current robots lack reliable fine manipulation on irregular, compliant surfaces.

task: Mix finish ingredients to obtain desired colors or shades.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing a single batch of finish (paint/stain/glaze) to a target color/shade; includes measuring, combining, stirring, and visual check. ~10 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise colorimetric perception, nuanced liquid handling/pouring, iterative visual judgment of subtle hues, and fine motor control for measuring/stirring. Current robots lack reliable color-matching feedback loops and liquid-mixing dexterity at this precision level.

task: Trim, smooth, and shape surfaces, and plane, shave, file, scrape, and sand models to attain specified shapes, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing a single model/surface to spec. Skilled craftworkers typically spend 30–60 min trimming, filing, sanding, and shaping one workpiece with hand tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained fine dexterity, variable force feedback, tool-switching, and nuanced tactile judgment across irregular surfaces. Current humanoids lack the precision force control and tool-use generalization needed for acceptable quality.

task: Remove old finishes and damaged or deteriorated parts, using hand tools, stripping tools, sandpaper, steel wool, abrasives, solvents, or dip baths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stripping/cleaning one furniture piece or surface panel. A skilled worker typically spends 30–60 min per item depending on size and finish type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires adaptive force control, varied tool use (scrapers, sandpaper, solvents), and nuanced tactile feedback to avoid damaging substrate. Unstructured surfaces and chemical handling far exceed current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Demonstrate therapy techniques, such as manual and creative arts and games.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one therapy demonstration session (e.g., showing a technique to a client/trainee). A typical demonstration of manual or creative arts therapy takes ~20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social-emotional communication, adaptive instruction, fine motor art/craft handling, therapeutic judgment, and rapport-building — all far beyond current humanoid capabilities. Limited dexterity and zero therapeutic cognition.

task: Treat warped or stained surfaces to restore original contours and colors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating one warped/stained surface (e.g., a wood panel or furniture piece). Competent restorer spends ~45 min assessing, applying treatments, and finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert tactile/visual diagnosis of damage type, precise chemical application, sanding, heat/moisture treatment, and color-matching — far beyond current robot dexterity, perception, and material understanding. No viable 2026 system exists.

task: Monitor functioning of slot machine coin dispensers and fill coin hoppers when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one machine's coin dispenser and refilling hopper if needed; a worker walks over, checks level, and pours coins in ~5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a casino floor, visually reading dispenser status (often via small display or window), opening access panels, and precisely pouring bulk coins into a hopper—challenging dexterity, manipulation of heavy coin bags, and panel access for current humanoids.

task: Treat animal illnesses or injuries, following experience or instructions of veterinarians.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating a single animal patient (e.g., cleaning a wound, administering medication, bandaging). A competent vet tech typically spends ~20–40 min per treatment episode.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity on uncooperative, unpredictably moving animals, nuanced tactile sensing, clinical judgment, and safe restraint. Current robots lack the compliance, bimanual precision, and animal-behavior perception needed.

task: Construct wooden models, patterns, templates, full scale mock-ups, and molds for parts of products and production tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing a single wooden model, pattern, template, mock-up, or mold. A competent patternmaker/model-maker typically spends 2–4 hours per unit depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting technical drawings, precise multi-step woodworking (sawing, chiseling, sanding, fitting), tool changes, and quality judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, planning depth, and tool-use generalization.

task: Examine furniture to determine the extent of damage or deterioration, and to decide on the best method for repair or restoration.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one piece of furniture. A skilled restorer spends ~15–25 min examining surfaces, joints, finish, and structural integrity before deciding on a repair approach.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert tactile assessment, nuanced visual diagnosis of finish type, wood grain damage, structural flex, and restoration decision-making. Current robots lack the fine haptic perception, domain expertise, and judgment needed for acceptable outcomes.

task: Prepare credit cards or charge account plates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing (personalizing, encoding, embossing, packaging) a single credit card or charge plate; a trained worker processes one card in ~2–4 minutes on specialized equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized embossing/encoding machines, handling thin cards with precision, and quality-checking magnetic strips/chips. Current humanoid robots lack the fine dexterity, tool interfacing, and domain-specific machine operation needed.

task: Move parts into position, manually or with hoists or cranes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving a single part into position. Includes rigging, hoist/crane operation, and final placement; ~15 min median covers light manual to moderate crane-assisted moves.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids cannot operate industrial hoists/cranes; manual heavy-part repositioning requires high payload capacity, precise force control, and rigging skills far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities. Occasional light parts feasible but overall very low success.

task: Care for children in institutional setting, such as group homes, nursery schools, private businesses, or schools for people with disabilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full shift block of active child care (e.g., a half-day session or 4-hour supervised care period) in an institutional setting; typical measurable unit for staffing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires continuous social/emotional responsiveness, safety judgment, dynamic unstructured interaction, and handling of children with disabilities. Current robots lack reliable child-safe dexterity, social perception, and autonomous decision-making across unpredictable caregiving scenarios.

task: Position, align, fit, and weld parts to form complete units or subunits, following blueprints and layout specifications, and using jigs, welding torches, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one complete unit/subunit. Typical structural weldment (frame, bracket assembly) takes a skilled welder ~60–120 min including fit-up, jigging, and welding passes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting blueprints, precise multi-step part alignment, jig use, and high-heat welding tool operation. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity, blueprint comprehension, and safe autonomous torch control for unstructured weld geometries.

task: Select wooden stock, determine layouts, and mark layouts of parts on stock, using precision equipment such as scribers, squares, and protractors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting stock and marking layout for a single part or small batch. A skilled woodworker spends ~20–30 min examining grain, measuring, and scribing precise lines.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual inspection of wood grain/defects, precise dexterous tool use (scriber, square, protractor), and complex spatial reasoning on irregular stock surfaces. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Position or tighten braces, jacks, clamps, ropes, or bolt straps, or bolt parts in position for welding or riveting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning/tightening all fixturing hardware for a single weld/rivet setup. A competent fitter typically takes 8–15 minutes per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires strong dexterous force application, tool switching (clamps, jacks, bolts, ropes), and precise spatial alignment under load. Current humanoids lack reliable torque-controlled fine manipulation and situational judgment for varied fixturing hardware.

task: Align and fit parts according to specifications, using jacks, turnbuckles, wedges, drift pins, pry bars, and hammers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning and fitting a single assembly/joint to spec. Skilled tradesperson (e.g. ironworker, millwright) typically takes 30–60 min depending on part complexity and tolerance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated use of multiple heavy, specialized tools (jacks, pry bars, hammers, drift pins) with high force, precise alignment feedback, and adaptive judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and tool-use generalization.

task: Erect ladders and scaffolding to fit together large assemblies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = erecting a standard scaffold/ladder setup at a single worksite section; a competent worker typically takes 30–60 minutes depending on height and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting, outdoor/uneven terrain, precise multi-component assembly at height, and safety-critical judgment. Current humanoids lack the payload, stability, and generalization for scaffold erection in unstructured environments.

task: Pick up and return items for laundry and valet service.
human_time_rationale: One instance = picking up and returning laundry/valet items for a single guest/customer, including collecting bags, transporting to/from service area, and delivery. ~20 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied indoor environments (hallways, elevators, guest rooms), handling soft/bulky laundry bags, interacting with guests, and managing door access — beyond reliable autonomous capability in unstructured hospitality settings.

task: Remove accessories prior to finishing, and mask areas that should not be exposed to finishing processes or substances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single workpiece (e.g., a furniture part or metal component) by removing accessories and masking areas before finishing. Typically 10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying and removing varied fasteners/accessories, then precisely applying masking tape to irregular boundaries. Fine dexterity, complex perception of "what to mask," and tool-use generalization are well beyond current robot capability.

task: Observe, feel, taste, or otherwise examine products during and after processing to ensure conformance to standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining a single product or batch sample during/after processing. A competent inspector typically spends 3–7 minutes per sample across sensory checks (visual, tactile, taste/smell).
robot_success_rationale: Visual/machine-vision inspection is feasible, but tasting, smelling, and nuanced tactile "feel" are far beyond current humanoid robot sensors. Multi-modal sensory evaluation and generalization across diverse products remain very limited.

task: Fit, fasten, and assemble wood parts together to form patterns, models, or sections, using glue, nails, dowels, bolts, screws, and other fasteners.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a single wood subassembly (e.g., a cabinet frame or furniture section). A competent woodworker typically takes 30–60 min per unit including fitting, fastening, and gluing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-tool dexterity (glue, nails, screws, dowels, clamps), precise alignment of irregular wood parts, force feedback, and sequential reasoning. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and fine manipulation capability.

task: Place corpses into crematory machines to reduce remains to bone fragments using flame, heat, or alkaline hydrolysis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading one body (in container) into a crematory retort. Includes positioning, verifying ID tag, sliding/pushing remains in, and sealing the chamber door. ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires maneuvering a heavy, irregularly shaped, bagged/casketed body (~50–150 kg) onto a loading tray and into a tight chamber opening. Extreme weight, deformable payload, precise alignment with retort, and safety/dignity requirements far exceed current humanoid dexterity and force-handling limits.

task: Build jigs that can be used as guides for assembling oversized or special types of box shooks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = building one jig for a specific box shook configuration. A skilled woodworker measures, cuts, and fastens jig components; ~30–60 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting custom blueprints, precise measuring/marking, sawing lumber to spec, and fastening components accurately. Current humanoids lack the fine tool-use, planning, and generalization for custom jig fabrication.

task: Discipline children and recommend or initiate other measures to control behavior, such as caring for own clothing and picking up toys and books.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single behavioral intervention episode (redirect, correct, guide a child). A competent caregiver spends ~5–15 minutes addressing the behavior and following through.
