robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social-emotional judgment, verbal authority, understanding child psychology, and dynamic interpersonal rapport. Current robots lack the cognitive, social, and emotional intelligence to discipline children safely or effectively.

task: Hammer, chip, and grind workpieces to cut, bend, and straighten metal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one workpiece. A skilled metalworker typically spends 15–25 min hammering, chipping, and grinding a single piece to spec.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force, precision tool-use (hammer, chisel, grinder) with dynamic force feedback on irregular metal. Current humanoids lack the grip strength, tool-switching dexterity, and real-time force-torque control needed for acceptable quality metalwork.

task: Tack-weld fitted parts together.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tack-welding a single fitted joint/assembly. A competent welder positions and applies several tack welds to hold parts in alignment, typically taking 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Tack welding demands precise torch placement, real-time arc control, heat management, and seam tracking on varied geometries. Current humanoid robots lack reliable welding tool integration, fine dexterous control, and the perception needed for weld quality assurance.

task: Remove high spots and cut bevels, using hand files, portable grinders, and cutting torches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing high spots and cutting a bevel on a single workpiece. A competent metalworker typically spends ~15–25 minutes per part using files, grinders, and torches.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated use of multiple specialized tools (torch, grinder, file), precise force feedback, and real-time surface inspection on irregular metal. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, thermal safety management, and multi-tool switching needed for autonomous metalwork.

task: Observe animals for signs of illness, injury, or unusual behavior, notifying veterinarians or managers as warranted.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single observation round of a typical animal group/enclosure. A competent caretaker spends ~20–40 min checking animals, noting behavior, and logging/reporting findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced behavioral interpretation, subtle symptom recognition (posture, gait, coat, eyes), and contextual judgment about when to escalate. Current robots lack animal-behavior models, reliable anomaly detection in living creatures, and safe navigation around unpredictable animals.

task: Straighten warped or bent parts, using sledges, hand torches, straightening presses, or bulldozers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = straightening one warped/bent part. A competent metalworker spends ~15–35 min assessing, heating, pressing/hammering, and verifying flatness on a typical industrial part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy specialized equipment (presses, bulldozers, hand torches), force-feedback judgment, iterative heat application, and precision assessment of warp — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and sensory capabilities.

task: Set up conference rooms, display tables, racks, or shelves, and arrange merchandise displays for sales personnel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up one room or display area; arranging furniture, racks, shelves, and merchandise. A competent worker typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires moving heavy/bulky furniture, interpreting layout plans, fine placement of varied merchandise, and aesthetic judgment. Current humanoids lack the strength, generalization, and spatial planning for this multi-step, unstructured task.

task: Spread graining ink over metal portions of furniture to simulate wood-grain finish.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying graining ink to the metal portions of one piece of furniture. A skilled finisher takes ~15–25 min to spread, texture, and blend ink for a convincing wood-grain effect.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly controlled, variable-pressure brushwork and drag techniques to simulate realistic wood grain patterns. Demands nuanced force feedback, artistic judgment, and fine dexterity far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities. No known system approaches this.

task: Set up face blocks, jigs, and fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a face block, jig, or fixture on a machine. A skilled machinist typically takes 15–35 min to select, position, align, and secure the workholding device.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise mechanical alignment (sub-millimeter tolerances), torque-controlled fastening, interpretation of engineering drawings, and tool/fixture selection in a cluttered shop environment — all far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Lift or move materials and finished products, using large cranes.
human_time_rationale: One lift/move cycle: rigging, hoisting, repositioning, and unrigging a load. Competent operators average ~15–25 minutes per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Large cranes require seated cab operation, complex rigging judgment, load-sway control, and spatial awareness over large distances. No humanoid robot can operate crane controls or perform rigging safely at this scale.

task: Install boilers, containers, and other structures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single boiler or large container unit, including positioning, securing, and connecting. Skilled tradespeople typically take 3–5 hours per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting beyond humanoid payload limits, precise torquing of large bolts, plumbing/gas/electrical connections, and use of specialized tools (cranes, pipe wrenches). Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Transport or arrange for transport of animals, equipment, food, animal feed, and other supplies to and from work sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single transport run (loading, driving, unloading). Typical agricultural/fieldwork trip including loading and delivery averages ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating vehicles (trucks, trailers) on public/rural roads, handling live animals, and navigating unstructured outdoor terrain — all well beyond 2026 humanoid robot capabilities. Driving alone disqualifies most platforms.

task: Control operation of compressors, scrubbers, evaporators, and refrigeration equipment to liquefy, compress, or regasify natural gas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one operational shift monitoring cycle (~1 hour). Operator continuously monitors and adjusts multiple interconnected systems; managers measure performance per shift.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial control panels, interpreting complex process instrumentation, and making safety-critical adjustments to high-pressure gas equipment. Humanoids lack the dexterity, domain-specific tool-use, and process expertise needed; serious safety risks eliminate autonomous operation.

task: Operate or tend equipment that roasts, bakes, dries, or cures food items such as cocoa and coffee beans, grains, nuts, and bakery products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one roasting/baking/drying batch cycle. A competent operator monitors, adjusts settings, loads/unloads, and logs results; typical batch runs 20–45 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating industrial equipment controls, loading bulk materials, monitoring sensors, and making adaptive adjustments. Perception and button/dial manipulation are improving but unstructured industrial settings, varied equipment interfaces, and nuanced quality judgments remain challenging for 2026 humanoids.

task: Coordinate dismantling, moving, and setting up equipment at new work sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = coordinating dismantle, transport, and setup of a typical equipment set at a new site. A competent worker managing a crew takes ~3–4 hours end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires high-level coordination, planning, crew management, vehicle operation, and varied heavy equipment handling across multiple unstructured environments. Far beyond current humanoid robot autonomy and generalization capabilities.

task: Mark identifying information on patterns, parts, and templates to indicate assembly methods and details.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking a single pattern, part, or template with identification info (labels, notches, alignment marks). A competent worker takes ~5–10 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (marking pens, punches, stamps) on varied flat/3D surfaces with precise placement of small annotations. Perception of correct mark locations and generalizing across diverse pattern types is beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Start and shut down plant equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full startup or shutdown sequence for a piece of plant equipment (e.g., pump, compressor, or conveyor). A competent operator typically spends 10–30 min on checks, sequencing, and confirmation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating industrial facilities, reading gauges/HMIs, operating specialized panels and valves, following strict safety interlocks, and interpreting plant-specific procedures. Current humanoids lack the perception, tool-use, and procedural generalization for this safely.

task: Fabricate work aids such as scrapers or templates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single work aid (e.g., one scraper or template). A competent worker measures, cuts, shapes, and finishes the item; ~30–60 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step tool use (measuring, cutting, grinding, filing), material selection, and precision shaping. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous tool manipulation, force control, and fabrication planning for unstructured shop tasks.

task: Inspect buildings, fences, fields or ranges, supplies, and equipment to determine work to be performed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single inspection round of a farm/ranch property (buildings, fences, fields, supplies, equipment). A competent worker typically takes 30–60 min per walkthrough.
robot_success_rationale: Requires traversing varied outdoor/rural terrain, identifying damage across diverse structures, assessing supply levels, and making maintenance judgments. Rough terrain locomotion, open-world generalization, and contextual decision-making far exceed 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Harvest fruits and vegetables by hand.
human_time_rationale: One instance = harvesting a single crop row or picking session (~45 min). A competent farmworker moves systematically, identifying ripe produce and picking efficiently.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor locomotion on uneven terrain, fine dexterous grasping of delicate produce without bruising, ripeness perception across varying lighting, and generalization across crop types — all remain major challenges for 2026 humanoids.

task: Observe temperature, humidity, pressure gauges, and product samples and adjust controls, such as thermostats and valves, to maintain prescribed operating conditions for specific stages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one monitoring round: reading gauges, sampling product, and adjusting controls. A skilled operator typically completes a single check-and-adjust cycle in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog gauges (moderate perception), interpreting product sample quality (high cognition/sensing), and precisely adjusting valves/thermostats (moderate dexterity). Integration of all three sub-tasks reliably is beyond current frontier robot capability.

task: Observe flow of materials and listen for machine malfunctions, such as jamming or spillage, and notify supervisors if corrective actions fail.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring shift/patrol cycle (~30 min) where a worker observes material flow and listens for anomalies, then escalates if needed.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can use cameras and microphones for basic anomaly detection, but reliably distinguishing jamming/spillage sounds in noisy industrial environments, contextually evaluating severity, and deciding when to escalate remains well beyond current autonomous capability.

task: Weigh or measure products, using scale hoppers or scale conveyors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = weighing/measuring a single product batch on a scale hopper or conveyor; a competent worker takes ~1–3 minutes including loading, reading, and recording.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place objects onto scales and read digital displays, but varied product shapes, hopper/conveyor integration, precise placement, and recording results add failure points. Structured industrial setups score higher; unstructured environments lower.

task: Stencil, gild, emboss, mark, or paint designs or borders to reproduce the original appearance of restored pieces, or to decorate new pieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = decorating/restoring one piece (e.g. a furniture panel or frame). A skilled artisan typically spends 30–60 min per piece depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine-motor dexterity, artistic judgment, precise pressure control, and aesthetic matching. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter precision, compliant tool-use, and visual art evaluation needed for gilding, embossing, or stenciling to an acceptable quality.

task: Finish patterns or models with protective or decorative coatings such as shellac, lacquer, or wax.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying a protective/decorative coating to one pattern or model. A competent worker typically spends ~20 min preparing, applying, and finishing one piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced brush/spray control, consistent thin-coat coverage over irregular 3D geometry, and quality judgment (runs, bubbles, missed spots). Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force regulation, and visual inspection feedback needed for acceptable finish quality.

task: Inspect crops, fields, or plant stock to determine conditions and need for cultivating, spraying, weeding, or harvesting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single field/plot. A competent agronomist walks rows, visually assesses crop health, soil, weeds, and pests, and makes cultivation recommendations — typically 30–60 min per field.
robot_success_rationale: Requires traversing uneven outdoor terrain, nuanced visual/agronomic judgment across diverse crop types, pest/disease identification, and integrated decision-making. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor mobility, domain-specific perception, and expert agronomic reasoning for autonomous field assessment.

task: Maintain clean lobbies or entrance areas for travelers or guests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a typical hotel/transit lobby once (sweeping, mopping, wiping surfaces, emptying bins). A competent cleaner takes ~25–35 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep/mop flat floors moderately well, but lobbies have varied furniture, spills, glass surfaces, high foot traffic, and require judgment (e.g., wet-floor signs, obstacle avoidance around guests). Generalization and multi-surface cleaning remain limited.

task: Direct or assist with the adjustment or repair of equipment or machinery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one equipment/machinery adjustment or repair job. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min diagnosing and executing a single repair or adjustment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosis, tool selection, fine dexterity with wrenches/fasteners, and domain-specific problem-solving. Current humanoids lack reliable fault diagnosis, specialized tool use, and the generalization needed for unstructured machinery repair.

task: Brush bleaching agents on wood surfaces to restore natural color.
human_time_rationale: One instance = brushing bleaching agent onto one wood surface (e.g., a tabletop). Includes dipping brush, spreading evenly, and covering the full area; ~25 min for a typical piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires controlled, even brush strokes with consistent pressure and chemical handling on varied wood grain/shapes. Current robots lack the fine pressure modulation, tool-use finesse, and chemical-awareness needed for acceptable quality.

task: Observe fish and beds or ponds to detect diseases, monitor fish growth, determine quality of fish, or determine completeness of harvesting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one pond or bed; skilled aquaculture worker walks perimeter, observes fish behavior/color/density, checks water quality in ~20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires aquatic/waterside locomotion, expert visual interpretation of fish health and growth cues through water, and nuanced judgment. Current humanoids lack waterproof mobility, underwater perception, and domain-specific fish-health assessment capability.

task: Operate tractors, tractor-drawn machinery, and self-propelled machinery to plow, harrow and fertilize soil, or to plant, cultivate, spray and harvest crops.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one field operation (e.g., plowing or planting a mid-sized field ~50 acres). A competent operator typically completes such a run in 3–5 hours; 4 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate tractors or self-propelled farm machinery in the field. Autonomous ag vehicles exist but are narrow, purpose-built systems, not general-purpose robots. Unstructured outdoor terrain, complex equipment coupling, and dynamic field conditions make success near-zero.

task: Harvest plants, and transplant or pot and label them.
human_time_rationale: One instance = harvesting a batch of plants, then repotting/transplanting and labeling them; a competent horticulturist takes ~45 min for a typical tray or small batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate multi-step manipulation (uprooting, potting soil, inserting plants without damage, writing/placing labels), fine dexterity with fragile biological material, and task sequencing — far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Drive or operate farm machinery, such as trucks, tractors, or self-propelled harvesters, to transport workers or supplies or to cultivate or harvest fields.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one typical field operation session (e.g., harvesting or cultivating one field, or a supply/worker transport run). A competent operator typically spends ~2 hours per session.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate trucks, tractors, or harvesters in real agricultural conditions. Ag-specific autonomous vehicles exist but are narrow systems. A general-purpose humanoid lacks the physical interface, cabin controls integration, and outdoor navigation needed.

task: Clean, maintain, and repair equipment, using hand tools, or request that repair and maintenance work be performed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one piece of equipment cleaned, maintained, or repaired. A competent worker typically spends 30–60 min per unit, covering inspection, cleaning, minor repair, and tool use.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults, selecting correct hand tools, applying variable force, and performing dexterous repairs in unstructured settings. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use generalization, fault diagnosis, and nuanced force control for maintenance tasks.

task: Clean equipment with steam, hot water, and hoses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one piece of equipment (e.g., industrial machine or food-processing unit) with steam/hot water/hoses; a competent worker typically takes 20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires manipulating hoses and steam wands, directing high-pressure water precisely, and navigating around complex equipment geometry. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use with flexible hoses, sufficient force control, and the perception to judge cleanliness coverage.

task: Preheat workpieces to make them malleable, using hand torches or furnaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preheating a single workpiece to target temperature. A competent worker takes ~5–15 min depending on material size and method (torch vs. furnace).
robot_success_rationale: Requires safe handling of open-flame torches or hot furnaces, fine thermal judgment (color/temperature reading), and heat-resistant tooling. Current humanoids lack reliable pyrometric sensing, safe high-heat tool use, and generalization across workpiece geometries.

task: Test gas, chemicals, and air during processing to assess factors such as purity and moisture content, and to detect quality problems or gas or chemical leaks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single test cycle (sample collection, instrument setup, measurement, logging). A competent technician typically takes 15–25 minutes per test round.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise manipulation of valves, sampling ports, and delicate analytical instruments; handling hazardous chemicals; interpreting multi-modal sensor data; and responding to anomalies — well beyond current general-purpose robot dexterity and chemical-environment generalization.

task: Verify dimensions and contours of models during hand-forming processes, using templates and measuring devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = verifying dimensions/contours of one workpiece mid-hand-forming, using templates and measuring devices; typically 5–12 min per cycle for a skilled worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool handling (calipers, templates), fine dexterous repositioning against curved/irregular surfaces, and contextual judgment about pass/fail. Current robots lack reliable fine manipulation and in-process adaptive measurement on freeform parts.

task: Adjust temperature, pressure, vacuum, level, flow rate, or transfer of gas to maintain processes at required levels or to correct problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single process adjustment event (reading gauges, diagnosing deviation, turning valves/controls). A competent operator typically takes ~10 min per adjustment cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting process state, manipulating industrial valves and control panels in complex environments. Current humanoids lack the perception, tool-use precision, and process-domain reasoning needed; industrial control is typically purpose-built, not humanoid-accessible.

task: Repair farm buildings, fences, and other structures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single repair job (e.g., patching a fence section or fixing a barn wall). A competent farmhand typically spends ~2 hours per discrete repair task.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse tools (hammers, saws, drills, post-drivers), heavy material handling, unstructured outdoor terrain, and highly variable tasks. Current humanoids lack the generalization, strength, and tool dexterity for autonomous farm repair.

task: Set temperature and time controls, light ovens, burners, driers, or roasters, and start equipment, such as conveyors, cylinders, blowers, driers, or pumps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting controls and starting one piece of equipment (e.g., oven or conveyor). A competent operator reads settings, adjusts dials/buttons, and confirms ignition/startup in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital control panels, turning knobs, pressing buttons, and confirming ignition — varied industrial interfaces are poorly generalized by current humanoids. Perception of flame/pilot light confirmation adds further difficulty.

task: Patrol units to monitor the amount of oil in storage tanks, and to verify that activities and operations are safe, efficient, and in compliance with regulations.
human_time_rationale: One patrol circuit of a typical oil storage tank farm, checking gauges, valves, and safety compliance. A competent operator walks the full route and logs findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor navigation over varied industrial terrain, reading analog/digital gauges, identifying safety hazards, and regulatory judgment. Current robots lack reliable outdoor mobility, instrument-reading accuracy, and compliance reasoning for unsupervised industrial patrol.

task: Control equipment to regulate flow and pressure of gas to feedlines of boilers, furnaces, and related steam-generating or heating equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one regulation cycle (checking gauges, adjusting valves/controls to maintain target flow/pressure). A competent stationary engineer takes ~10–20 min per adjustment round.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, manipulating industrial valves and control panels, and making real-time pressure/flow judgments. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, industrial tool familiarity, and safety-critical feedback loops needed. High-stakes environment further limits autonomous deployment.

task: Install equipment, such as spray units, cutting blades, or screens, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single piece of equipment (e.g., a spray unit, blade, or screen) onto a machine using hand tools; a competent worker typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment, fastener handling, and tool use (wrenches, screwdrivers) in varied, unstructured settings. Current humanoids struggle with tool switching, torque control, and tight-tolerance fastening on non-standardized equipment.

task: Mark reference points onto floors or face blocks and transpose them to workpieces, using measuring devices, squares, chalk, and soapstone.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking and transposing reference points for one workpiece; a skilled layout worker takes ~10–20 min to measure, square, scribe chalk/soapstone lines, and verify alignment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool use (squares, chalk, soapstone scribing), fine dexterity for accurate marking, and spatial reasoning to transpose measurements. Current humanoids lack the precision manipulation and measurement verification needed for acceptable quality.

task: Inspect facilities to determine maintenance needs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single facility (e.g., a building floor or mechanical room). A competent inspector walks the space, checks equipment, notes issues, and logs findings — typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate and capture visual data, but identifying subtle maintenance needs (corrosion, unusual sounds, leaks, structural wear) across unstructured, varied environments requires nuanced multi-modal perception and domain judgment far beyond current autonomous capability.

task: Maintain and repair equipment, or report malfunctioning equipment to supervisors so that repairs can be scheduled.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting, diagnosing, and either performing a minor repair or filing a report on one piece of malfunctioning equipment. A competent worker typically takes ~30 min end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults via perception, using varied tools for repair, and verbal/written reporting to supervisors. Current humanoids lack reliable fault diagnosis, tool-use generalization, and communication workflows needed for autonomous end-to-end completion.

task: Control fractioning columns, compressors, purifying towers, heat exchangers, and related equipment to extract nitrogen and oxygen from air.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one monitoring/adjustment cycle of an air separation unit; a competent operator checks instrumentation, adjusts controls, and logs readings roughly every 30 minutes per shift.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting complex industrial HMI panels, adjusting specialized valves/controls, and responding to dynamic process deviations. Current humanoids lack the process-control cognition, specialized tool use, and industrial interface integration needed for autonomous operation.

task: Regulate greenhouse conditions, and indoor and outdoor irrigation systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one regulation cycle: checking sensors, adjusting vents/heaters/humidity, and setting irrigation schedules for both indoor and outdoor systems.
robot_success_rationale: Regulating greenhouse conditions requires integrating sensor data, adjusting multiple mechanical systems (vents, valves, irrigation heads), navigating varied terrain, and making contextual decisions. Current humanoids lack the system integration, tool-use generalization, and environmental reasoning needed. Narrow automation (PLCs, smart controllers) handles this, but a general-purpose humanoid cannot autonomously manage the full task end-to-end.

task: Open valves, gates, or chutes or use shovels to load or remove products from ovens or other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = opening a valve/gate/chute or shoveling a load into/out of one oven or equipment unit; a competent worker completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating industrial valves, gates, and shovels in hot, cluttered, unstructured environments. Humanoids lack reliable tool-use with shovels, force-controlled valve turning, and heat/dust robustness for industrial settings.

task: Take product samples during or after processing for laboratory analyses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a single product sample during/after processing. A competent worker locates the sample point, collects the sample, labels/seals it, and logs it — typically 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating variable industrial environments, identifying correct sample points, manipulating containers/valves, and labeling — all in unstructured settings. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use, fluid handling, and process-awareness needed for consistent success.

task: Heat-treat parts, using acetylene torches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = heat-treating one part or small batch; worker spends ~10–20 min heating, monitoring color/temperature, and completing the treatment cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safely operating an open-flame acetylene torch, precise thermal judgment via color/visual cues, controlled torch movement, and process expertise. Current humanoids lack tool-use safety clearance, fine thermal perception, and flame-tool manipulation capability.

task: Sell and deliver plants and flowers to customers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one delivery trip to a customer (drive to location, carry arrangement, hand off). Median ~30 min including travel, parking, and handoff.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving on public roads (humanoids cannot), navigating to arbitrary addresses, climbing stairs, social handoff, and handling fragile floral arrangements — well beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Push racks or carts to transfer products to storage, cooling stations, or the next stage of processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pushing a loaded rack/cart from one point to the next stage/location; typical short-distance transfer in a facility takes ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids can navigate flat indoor floors and grip handles, but pushing heavy loaded carts requires sustained force control, collision avoidance in dynamic environments, and coupling with varied cart designs — reliability remains moderate.

task: Conduct general housekeeping of units, including wiping up oil spills and performing general cleaning duties.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing general housekeeping of a single industrial/residential unit, including spill cleanup and general cleaning. A competent worker typically takes 30–60 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Basic sweeping/mopping is feasible (~40–70%), but oil spill identification and safe cleanup, navigating varied unit layouts, and multi-step unstructured cleaning tasks reduce reliability significantly. Generalization across clutter and spill types remains a challenge.

task: Use different sewing techniques such as felling, tacking, basting, embroidery, and fagoting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying one sewing technique (e.g., felling a seam on a garment section). Competent sewist takes ~15–25 min per technique application.
robot_success_rationale: Hand-sewing requires extreme fine dexterity: threading needles, controlling thread tension, producing consistent stitch patterns. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter finger control and tactile feedback required for any of these techniques.

task: Load agricultural products into trucks, and drive trucks to market or storage facilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a truck with agricultural products and driving it to a market or storage facility. Typical loading + a short regional haul averages ~90 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires two very hard sub-tasks: loading varied/bulky agricultural goods (low dexterity/strength for heavy sacks, crates, loose produce) AND driving a truck on public roads — which no current humanoid can do autonomously.

task: Operate auxiliary equipment and control multiple processing units during distilling or treating operations, moving controls that regulate valves, pumps, compressors, and auxiliary equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one operational shift cycle of monitoring and adjusting multiple processing units; ~45 min represents a typical active control/adjustment interval for a distillation operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated operation of valves, pumps, compressors, and control panels across a complex industrial plant. Humanoids lack the process knowledge integration, specialized tool-use, safety certification, and fine control needed for autonomous plant operation in 2026.

task: Feel plants' leaves and note their coloring to detect the presence of insects or disease.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one plant. A competent worker spends ~3 min feeling leaves and visually assessing coloring across a typical plant for signs of pest/disease.
robot_success_rationale: Requires simultaneous tactile sensing (leaf texture/firmness) and fine visual color-change detection. Current robots lack reliable plant-safe tactile discrimination and nuanced discoloration perception needed for accurate agronomic diagnosis.

task: Read to children and teach them simple painting, drawing, handicrafts, and songs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single structured activity session (e.g., reading + one craft/song) with a small group of children; typically 30–60 min in a daycare/classroom setting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rich social-emotional intelligence, adaptive child engagement, fine dexterous art demonstration, spontaneous singing, and safe unstructured interaction with children — all far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Set up and operate irrigation equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and operating irrigation for one field/zone: connecting lines, adjusting heads, starting and monitoring the system. ~60 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dragging heavy hoses, coupling fittings under variable terrain/mud, adjusting mechanical heads, and operating pumps/valves outdoors. Outdoor unstructured terrain, heavy manipulation, and specialized tool-use far exceed 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Control or operate manifold and pumping systems to circulate liquids through a petroleum refinery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pump/manifold operation cycle (adjusting valves, monitoring flow, logging readings). A competent operator typically spends ~30 min per circuit check/adjustment round.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial valve systems, reading analog/digital gauges, and making safety-critical process decisions in a hazardous environment. Humanoids lack process control integration, fine valve manipulation, and refinery-specific tool familiarity.

task: Travel on foot, by vehicle, or by equipment such as boats, snowmobiles, helicopters, snowshoes, or skis to reach hunting areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = travel from staging point to hunting area. Median trip across foot, vehicle, and specialty transport modes estimated at ~1 hour.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating diverse vehicles (trucks, boats, snowmobiles, helicopters) or traversing rugged outdoor terrain on skis/snowshoes — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities. Outdoor unstructured navigation and vehicle operation are near-zero reliability.

task: Change charts in recording meters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = swapping one chart roll/disc in a single recording meter. A competent worker removes the old chart, installs a new one, and resets — typically ~5 minutes per meter.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle thin paper chart rolls/discs, precise alignment inside the meter housing, and manipulation of small latches or spindles. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation reliability for such delicate, precise paper handling tasks.

task: Dig, rake, and screen soil, filling cold frames and hot beds in preparation for planting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one cold frame or hot bed (~4×6 ft). Digging, raking, and screening soil, then filling the frame takes a competent worker ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor uneven terrain locomotion, forceful tool use (spade, rake, soil screen), coordinated bimanual manipulation with heavy loads, and precise filling — well beyond current humanoid dexterity and outdoor generalization.

task: Plant, spray, weed, fertilize, water, and prune plants, shrubs, and trees, using gardening tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one gardening session covering a typical residential plot. A competent gardener completes planting, spraying, weeding, fertilizing, watering, and pruning in ~1 hour.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor terrain navigation, varied tool-switching (trowel, pruners, hose, sprayer), fine force-sensitive pruning, and weed identification — far beyond current general-purpose robot generalization and outdoor dexterity.

task: Monitor equipment functioning, observe temperature, level, and flow gauges, and perform regular unit checks to ensure that all equipment is operating as it should.
human_time_rationale: One round of gauge readings and unit checks across a typical equipment area; a competent operator completes this in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate industrial floors and read digital displays, but analog gauges, varied layouts, inconsistent lighting, logging requirements, and anomaly judgment reduce reliability significantly. Generalization across real plant environments remains limited in 2026.

task: Smooth out products in bins, pans, trays, or conveyors, using rakes or shovels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = smoothing product in one bin/pan/tray/conveyor section. A skilled worker completes this in roughly 2–4 minutes using a rake or shovel.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping and wielding a long-handled tool (rake/shovel) with controlled force across varied surfaces and product types. Tool-use generalization, force modulation, and surface-level perception remain challenging for 2026 humanoids in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Train workers in techniques such as planting, harvesting, weeding, or insect identification and in the use of safety measures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one training session for a group of farm workers covering a topic (e.g., harvesting technique or insect ID). A typical hands-on field training session runs ~1 hour.
robot_success_rationale: Training requires verbal instruction, adaptive explanation, demonstrating nuanced techniques, reading learner comprehension, and social interaction. Current robots lack the language pedagogy, social intelligence, and reliable dexterous demonstration needed for autonomous worker training.

task: Start pumps and open valves or use automated equipment to regulate the flow of oil in pipelines and into and out of tanks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pump/valve operation cycle (starting pumps, adjusting valves, verifying flow/tank levels). A competent pipeline operator typically takes 15–25 minutes per regulated flow event.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial valves, pump control panels, and monitoring gauges in complex, hazardous pipeline environments. Current humanoids lack the tool-use, domain-specific interfacing, and safety-critical reliability needed for autonomous oil flow regulation.

task: Perform tests to check the qualities and grades of products, such as assessing levels of bottom sediment, water, and foreign materials in oil samples, using centrifugal testers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = running a centrifugal test on one oil sample: prepare sample, load centrifuge, run cycle (~10 min), read/record results. ~20 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting, operating specialized centrifuge controls, reading graduated glassware, and interpreting subtle sediment levels. Fine dexterity with lab glassware, instrument calibration, and result interpretation are well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Operate construction equipment to install and maintain gas distribution systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one section of gas distribution line installed/maintained (e.g., a single trench excavation, pipe laying, and backfill). A competent operator typically completes this in ~4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating excavators, backhoes, and trenchers on unpredictable outdoor terrain. No humanoid robot can drive or control heavy construction machinery autonomously in real-world conditions as of 2026. Extreme complexity, safety-critical.

task: Measure and inspect machined parts to ensure conformance to product specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single machined part using calipers, micrometers, gauges, and visual checks against spec sheet; ~10–20 min for a typical part.
robot_success_rationale: Robots lack reliable fine-measurement tool use (calipers, micrometers, CMM operation) and precise part manipulation for gauging. Perception of tight tolerances (±0.01 mm) and reading analog instruments remain major limitations for general-purpose humanoids.

task: Clear or dislodge blockages in bins, screens, or other equipment, using poles, brushes, or mallets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing a single blockage event. A worker locates the clog, selects a tool (pole, brush, or mallet), and physically dislodges it; typically 5–10 minutes per occurrence.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing blockage location, selecting/wielding varied tools (poles, mallets) with appropriate force in confined, cluttered industrial spaces. Current robots lack reliable tool-switching, force-feedback judgment, and unstructured-environment generalization needed here.

task: Verify that incoming and outgoing products are moving through the correct meters, and that meters are working properly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = verifying one set of meters (e.g., at a loading dock or pipeline station). A competent worker reads meters, cross-checks flow/product routing, and logs results — typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital meters of varied types, interpreting product routing logic, cross-referencing documentation, and diagnosing faults. Current robots lack reliable gauge-reading, domain reasoning, and generalization across industrial meter configurations.

task: Maintain and repair irrigation and climate control systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting, diagnosing, and repairing a single irrigation or climate control system (e.g., one greenhouse zone or one HVAC/drip system). A competent technician typically spends 60–120 minutes per service call.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults across plumbing, electrical, and mechanical components; replacing valves, nozzles, filters, sensors, and thermostats; and working in varied, unstructured environments. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities in dexterity, tool use, and fault diagnosis.

task: Start conveyors to move roasted grain to cooling pans and agitate grain with rakes as blowers force air through perforated bottoms of pans.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting conveyors and raking one batch of grain through cooling cycle (~10–20 min); includes activating equipment and continuous agitation until cooled.
robot_success_rationale: Conveyor start-up via controls may be feasible, but rhythmic rake agitation over a large pan, monitoring grain flow, and coordinating blower timing require sustained dexterous tool-use, force feedback, and situational judgment beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Dig, cut, and transplant seedlings, cuttings, trees, and shrubs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = transplanting one plant (seedling to small shrub). Digging, cutting roots/stems, and replanting takes a competent worker ~15–25 min depending on size and soil.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor locomotion on uneven soil, force-controlled digging with tools, precise cutting of roots/stems, and delicate handling of living plants. Current humanoids lack robust outdoor terrain navigation and the specialized tool-use needed for consistent, safe transplanting.

task: Repair, maintain, and replace parts on dies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting, repairing/maintaining, and replacing parts on a single die setup. A skilled tool-and-die worker typically takes 60–120 minutes per die depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle precision die components, use of specialized hand tools, interpretation of wear patterns, and expert judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured, high-precision tooling environments.

task: Fill or remove product from trays, carts, hoppers, or equipment, using scoops, peels, or shovels, or by hand.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling or emptying one tray/cart/hopper load. A competent worker scoops, peels, or hand-loads a standard batch in roughly 5–12 minutes depending on volume.
robot_success_rationale: Bulk scooping of uniform material is feasible, but varied container shapes, tools (peels, shovels), granular/loose product flow, and unstructured cart/hopper geometries challenge perception, grasp planning, and force control for 2026 humanoids.

task: Turn handles or knobs to set pressures and depths of ram strokes and to synchronize machine operations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting handles/knobs for a single machine setup. A skilled operator typically takes 3–7 minutes to dial in pressure, depth, and synchronization settings.
robot_success_rationale: Turning knobs/handles requires moderate dexterity, but reading analog gauges, interpreting machine feedback, and fine-tuning synchronized parameters across multiple controls pushes beyond current reliable robot capability in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Clean interiors of processing units by circulating chemicals and solvents within units.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one cleaning cycle of a processing unit, including setup, chemical circulation, monitoring, and shutdown. Typically 45–90 minutes depending on unit size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting hoses/fittings to industrial equipment, operating valves and pumps, monitoring chemical flow, and ensuring safety compliance. Current humanoids lack the fine tool-use, chemical handling capability, and process knowledge for autonomous industrial CIP operations.

task: Test products for moisture content, using moisture meters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single product/sample with a moisture meter: position probe, take reading, record result. Typically 2–5 minutes for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Robot must grasp and position a handheld meter, press probes against the product at correct locations, and read the display. Dexterity and tool-use are feasible but probe placement precision and display reading reliability remain challenging for general-purpose humanoids in 2026.

task: Participate in the inspection, grading, sorting, storage, and post-harvest treatment of crops.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one batch/bin of harvested crop through inspection, grading, sorting, storage placement, and post-harvest treatment. A competent worker typically takes ~45 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Task spans multiple sub-tasks: visual inspection, grading judgment, sorting by quality, moving product to storage, and applying treatments. Current robots lack the integrated perception, agronomic judgment, dexterous handling of irregular produce, and tool-use for treatments across all steps autonomously.

task: Inspect pipelines, tightening connections and lubricating valves as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a typical pipeline segment (e.g., a single room or bay of pipes), including visual checks, tightening loose fittings, and lubricating valves. A competent pipefitter completes this in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating cluttered industrial environments, visually detecting subtle faults, applying calibrated torque to varied fittings, and precisely lubricating valves — combining fine dexterity, tool-use, and unstructured perception well beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Inspect plants and bud ties to assess quality.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single plant and its bud ties for quality issues; a competent horticulture worker checks growth, tie tension, damage, and disease signs in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual discrimination of plant health, subtle tie tension, and delicate foliage — beyond current robot perception and dexterity. Unstructured, variable plant geometry further limits generalization; current robots lack reliable agricultural plant inspection capability.

task: Skin quarry, using knives, and stretch pelts on frames to be cured.
human_time_rationale: One instance = skinning one medium-sized animal (e.g., deer or fox), scraping the hide, and stretching/securing it on a drying frame. Skilled trapper/hunter typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity with knives on irregular, slippery biological tissue, precise force control to avoid hide damage, and complex manipulation to lace/stretch pelts on frames. Far beyond current robot capability.

task: Steer vessels and operate navigational instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one watch shift (typically 4 hours) of active vessel steering and instrument monitoring; standard maritime rotation unit.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate a ship's helm and navigational instruments in dynamic, real-world maritime conditions. Requires vehicle-specific controls, situational awareness, regulatory compliance, and complex real-time decision-making far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Install, adjust, and remove dies, synchronizing cams, forging hammers, and stop guides, using overhead cranes or other hoisting devices, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing, adjusting, and removing a full set of dies/cams/hammers/guides on one forging machine. An experienced die setter typically takes 60–120 minutes per changeover.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating overhead cranes/hoisting devices, heavy precision torquing, fine alignment of large forging tooling, and navigating a hazardous industrial floor — all far beyond current humanoid robot capability or safe autonomous operation.

task: Start machines to produce sample workpieces, and observe operations to detect machine malfunctions and to verify that machine setups conform to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting a machine, running a sample, and observing/verifying output. A competent machinist typically spends ~15–25 minutes per setup verification cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating industrial machinery controls, interpreting multi-modal sensor feedback, recognizing subtle malfunctions, and making judgment calls about spec conformance — well beyond current humanoid robot generalization and tool-use capabilities in unstructured factory environments.

task: Haul and spread topsoil, fertilizer, peat moss, and other materials to condition soil, using wheelbarrows or carts and shovels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = hauling and spreading materials to condition a typical garden bed (~10–20 sq ft). A competent worker loads, transports, and spreads in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating uneven outdoor terrain with a loaded wheelbarrow, shoveling loose bulk materials, and evenly spreading across a bed—beyond current humanoid locomotion, tool-use, and outdoor generalization capabilities.

task: Locate fish, using fish-finding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scanning a fishing area with sonar/fish-finder until fish are located. A competent angler typically takes 15–25 minutes to identify a productive spot.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a boat on open water, interpreting sonar/fish-finder displays, and navigating to fish locations. Humanoid robots cannot pilot watercraft in open water; aquatic navigation and marine equipment operation are well outside current capabilities.

task: Tie and bunch flowers, plants, shrubs, and trees, wrap their roots, and pack them into boxes to fill orders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fulfilling one order: tying/bunching, wrapping roots, and boxing a typical floral/plant order; competent worker takes ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly variable object shapes (live plants, irregular root balls), fine dexterous tying/bunching, delicate handling to avoid damage, and adaptive packing. Current robots lack reliable fine manipulation and generalization for this complex, multi-step task.

task: Lower thermometers into tanks to obtain temperature readings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lowering a thermometer into one tank, waiting for stabilization, and reading the value. Typically takes ~2–4 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping a slender thermometer, precisely inserting it into a tank opening (variable geometry), waiting, and reading an analog display. Dexterous manipulation of thin instruments and reliable gauge-reading remain weak points for current humanoids.

task: Apply solutions and chemicals to equipment, objects, or parts, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying a solution/chemical to a single piece of equipment or part. A competent worker spends ~5–10 min selecting the right chemical, preparing, and applying it with hand tools.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can grasp tools and perform broad surface application, but precise chemical dosing, tool selection, surface coverage judgment, and avoiding contamination in unstructured settings challenge current dexterity and perception.

task: Repair and maintain farm vehicles, implements, and mechanical equipment.
human_time_rationale: One repair/maintenance instance on a farm vehicle or implement (e.g., fixing a tractor, servicing a plow). A typical job ranges from a quick fluid change (~30 min) to complex engine repairs (~4+ hrs); ~2 hrs is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force torque application, fine dexterity with varied fasteners/tools, diagnosis under dirty/unstructured farm conditions, and deep mechanical reasoning. Far beyond current humanoid capability in generalization, strength, and tool-use breadth.

task: Remove dies from machines when production runs are finished.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a die set from one machine after a run; includes powering down, unbolting/unclamping, lifting die out, and staging it safely. Typically 15–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Dies are heavy, require high-torque tool use (wrenches, clamps), precise alignment in confined machine cavities, and situational judgment. Current humanoids lack the force capacity, specialized tool-use, and safety-critical dexterity needed.

task: Move containerized shrubs, plants, and trees, using wheelbarrows or tractors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving a single containerized plant from one location to another using a wheelbarrow or tractor; typical nursery/landscaping move takes ~15 minutes including loading, transporting, and unloading.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a wheelbarrow or tractor across variable outdoor terrain with heavy, awkward loads. Humanoids lack ability to drive tractors; wheelbarrow operation on uneven ground is mechanically demanding. Outdoor generalization and heavy payload handling remain very limited.

task: Smooth workpiece edges and fix taps, tubes, and valves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = deburring/smoothing edges of a single workpiece. A skilled machinist or fitter takes ~5–15 min depending on size and material.
robot_success_rationale: Deburring requires consistent force control, precise tool orientation, and tactile feedback on irregular edges. Current humanoids lack reliable fine force-compliance and adaptive path planning for arbitrary workpiece geometries.

task: Examine, inspect, and measure raw materials and finished products to verify conformance to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single batch/item of raw material or finished product; competent QC worker checks dimensions, appearance, and records results in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based inspection is improving but autonomous measurement with calipers/gauges in unstructured settings, interpreting specs, and handling varied materials/geometries remains unreliable for frontier humanoids. Narrow fixed-camera AOI systems excluded.

task: Verify quality of finished workpieces by inspecting them, comparing them to templates, measuring their dimensions, or testing them in working machinery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single finished workpiece; includes visual check, template comparison, dimensional measurement, and/or functional test. Typical cycle time for a skilled QC worker.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is maturing, but combining visual inspection, template comparison, precise measurement with calipers/gauges, AND functional machine testing in unstructured environments demands dexterity, tool-use, and adaptive judgment that current humanoids handle poorly together.

task: Repair and maintain equipment, objects, or parts, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single repair/maintenance job (e.g., tightening, replacing a part, adjusting). Typical trade task runs 30–60 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires selecting correct tools, applying precise torque, diagnosing faults, and navigating unstructured workspaces. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use, fine dexterity, and generalizable mechanical diagnosis for arbitrary repair tasks.

task: Trim, scrape, or deburr objects or parts, using chisels, scrapers, and other hand tools and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = deburring/trimming one part. A competent worker typically takes 5–10 minutes per part depending on complexity and material.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled scraping/chiseling on irregular surfaces, fine tactile feedback, and adaptive tool handling. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, compliant force control, and perception needed to meet quality standards reliably.

task: Clamp seals around valves to secure tanks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sealing one valve with a clamp. A skilled worker positions the seal, aligns the clamp, and tightens fasteners in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of a deformable seal around a valve, followed by controlled clamping/torquing of fasteners. Bimanual dexterity, compliance sensing, and part-in-hand manipulation in confined spaces challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Grind, sand, clean, or polish objects or parts to correct defects or to prepare surfaces for further finishing, using hand tools and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one part/object surface prep cycle. A skilled worker spends ~15–25 min grinding, sanding, and cleaning a single workpiece to acceptable finish quality.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained force control, tool switching, surface-quality perception, and adapting to varied part geometries. Current humanoids lack reliable contact-force regulation and finish-quality inspection for consistent, supervisor-acceptable results.

task: Collect product samples by turning bleeder valves, or by lowering containers into tanks to obtain oil samples.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a single oil sample via bleeder valve or container dip. A competent worker locates the point, prepares container, collects sample, and labels it in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise manipulation of small bleeder valves or careful lowering of containers into tanks, often in industrial/outdoor environments with varied equipment. Fine valve turning, fluid handling, and leak-free sample capture exceed current humanoid dexterity and generalization reliably.

task: Position, align, and secure arbors, spindles, coils, mandrels, dies, and slitting knives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a full set of tooling components (arbors, spindles, coils, mandrels, dies, slitting knives) on a machine. A skilled operator takes ~20–30 min per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity to align and torque-secure tight-tolerance industrial tooling (slitting knives, dies, mandrels). Heavy components, exact angular/axial positioning, and tool-use (wrenches, torque tools) exceed current humanoid robot capability reliably.

task: Activate shears and grinders to trim workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = activating and operating shears/grinders to trim a single workpiece; a competent worker sets up, activates, and trims in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool activation, safe workpiece positioning, and force-controlled grinding/shearing in an industrial setting. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use generalization, safety compliance, and dexterity for powered industrial cutting equipment.

task: Sharpen abrasive grinding tools, using machines and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sharpening one abrasive grinding tool. A competent worker inspects, mounts, and dresses/sharpens one tool using a machine or hand tool in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool manipulation, precise force control, and skilled tactile/visual feedback to achieve correct edge geometry. Current humanoids lack the precision and specialized tool-use needed for acceptable quality output.

task: Maintain engines, fishing gear, and other on-board equipment and perform minor repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a routine maintenance session on a fishing vessel covering engine checks, gear inspection, and minor repairs; ~1.5 hrs for a competent marine mechanic/deckhand.
robot_success_rationale: Requires marine environment mobility (wet, slippery, confined bilge spaces), highly varied tools, fine mechanical dexterity, domain-specific diagnostics, and unstructured repair judgment — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Sharpen cutting tools and drill bits, using bench grinders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sharpening one tool or drill bit on a bench grinder. A skilled worker typically takes 5–10 minutes to inspect, position, grind, and check the edge.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise angle control, tactile/visual feedback on edge geometry, and coordinated two-handed manipulation against a spinning wheel. Current robots lack the fine dexterity, force sensing, and grinding expertise for acceptable results.

task: Move controls to adjust, start, or stop equipment during grinding and polishing processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting, starting, or stopping equipment controls once during a grinding/polishing cycle; a competent operator takes ~1–3 minutes per control intervention.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can reach and press buttons/levers on structured panels, but grinding/polishing machinery varies widely in control layout, requires fine knob/dial manipulation, real-time sensory feedback, and safety awareness in loud, debris-filled environments — limiting reliable autonomy.

task: Position and move metal wires or workpieces through a series of dies that compress and shape stock to form die impressions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = feeding and positioning one workpiece through a die sequence. A skilled operator typically takes 5–12 minutes per piece including alignment, threading, and quality checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise threading of stiff metal wire/workpiece through sequential dies under high force. Poor robot generalization to varied stock shapes, tight tolerances, force feedback demands, and industrial fixture variability limits success significantly.

task: Calculate gas ratios to detect deviations from specifications, using testing apparatus.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one gas sample test cycle: setup, running the apparatus, reading ratio results, and checking against specifications. Typically 15–25 minutes for a skilled technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized lab/industrial testing apparatus (valves, digital readouts, calibration controls), interpreting numeric ratio outputs, and making specification comparisons. Current humanoids lack reliable fine tool-use and domain-specific instrument operation for autonomous lab work.

task: Measure and mark equipment, objects, or parts to ensure grinding and polishing standards are met.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and marking a single part/workpiece. A skilled worker uses calipers, gauges, or scribes to check and mark one part in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tactile use of hand tools (calipers, scribes, gauges), fine dexterity for marking, and accurate spatial perception on metallic surfaces. Current humanoids lack the fine-motor precision and tool-use reliability needed for quality-acceptable results.

task: Set up, operate, or tend presses and forging machines to perform hot or cold forging by flattening, straightening, bending, cutting, piercing, or other operations to taper, shape, or form metal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running one forging press job: tooling install, parameter setting, stock feeding, and quality check; ~45 min for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large industrial forging presses/machinery with complex controls, heavy stock handling, die changes, and safety-critical judgment. No current humanoid can operate such specialized heavy equipment autonomously.

task: Participate in animal damage control, wildlife management, disease control, and research activities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single field activity session (e.g., setting traps, surveying a zone, or collecting samples). Typical wildlife/damage-control field sessions run ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor rough terrain navigation, animal handling, species identification, trap operation, biological sampling, and adaptive judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured field environments.

task: Attach nets, slings, hooks, blades, or lifting devices to cables, booms, hoists, or dredges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching one rigging device (net, sling, hook, blade, or lifting device) to a cable, boom, hoist, or dredge. A competent rigger typically takes 10–20 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy-duty, high-stakes rigging with large irregular hardware (hooks, shackles, slings) under tension. Demands strong force application, precise load alignment, outdoor/industrial terrain, and safety-critical judgment — well beyond current robot dexterity and generalization.

task: Test and adjust machine speeds or actions, according to product specifications, using gauges and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing and adjusting a single machine to spec. A competent worker reads gauges, diagnoses deviation, and makes adjustments, typically taking 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting spec tolerances, selecting correct hand tools, and making precise mechanical adjustments. Current humanoids lack reliable gauge-reading perception, fine tool-use dexterity, and adaptive closed-loop calibration judgment.

task: Disassemble sizing mills removed from rolling lines, and sort and store parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling one sizing mill unit removed from a rolling line, then sorting and storing its parts. A competent millwright team typically takes 3–5 hours per mill stand.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy-duty tool use (wrenches, hoists, impact tools), high-force torque application, and complex multi-step sequencing on large industrial components. Current humanoids lack the strength, specialized tooling, and task planning for this environment.

task: Adjust and correct machine set-ups to reduce thicknesses, reshape products, and eliminate product defects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing a defect/issue and making the necessary machine adjustments. A competent operator typically takes 15–25 minutes per setup correction cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading machine gauges, interpreting product defects, and making precise mechanical/control adjustments on industrial equipment. Current robots lack the sensory interpretation, diagnostic reasoning, and fine-tuned machine interfacing needed for autonomous setup correction.

task: Install equipment such as guides, guards, gears, cooling equipment, and rolls, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single piece of equipment (e.g., a guard or roll) onto machinery using hand tools; a competent worker typically takes 30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of heavy/irregular industrial parts, sustained torque with hand tools, and spatial reasoning in cluttered machinery environments. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and generalization for reliable autonomous installation.

task: Trim and compress finished forgings to specified tolerances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming and compressing one finished forging to tolerance. A skilled operator cycles through setup, press operation, and inspection in ~5 minutes per part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy industrial trim/compression presses, precise part placement in dies, and tolerance verification with gauges. Humanoids lack the strength, tool-interfacing, and precision process control for industrial forging equipment.

task: Thread or feed sheets or rods through rolling mechanisms, or start and control mechanisms that automatically feed steel into rollers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = feeding/threading one sheet or rod through a rolling mechanism and confirming feed is running. A competent operator typically takes 3–7 minutes per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of stiff/heavy metal stock into tight roller gaps, force feedback judgment, and operating industrial machine controls. Current humanoids lack the specialized dexterity, high-force handling, and machine interfacing needed for reliable autonomous execution.

task: Remove scratches and polish roll surfaces, using polishing stones and electric buffers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = polishing one roll surface. A skilled worker spends ~30–60 min identifying scratches, applying polishing stones, and buffing to acceptable finish quality.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous pressure control, tactile feedback, and tool-use (polishing stones + electric buffer) on curved surfaces. Current humanoids lack the precision force modulation and surface-quality perception needed for acceptable finish.

task: Clean brass particles from files by drawing file cards through file grooves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one file with a file card. A competent worker draws the card through the grooves several times, taking ~2–4 minutes per file.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of a narrow tool into fine file grooves, firm controlled dragging force, and tactile feedback to confirm cleaning. Current robots lack the fine dexterity and force-sensitive groove-tracking needed for reliable success.

task: Maintain and repair trapping equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and repairing one piece of trapping equipment (e.g., a trap). A competent trapper checks, cleans, and fixes minor issues in ~15–25 minutes per trap.
robot_success_rationale: Traps are mechanically complex, varied, and require nuanced dexterity (bending springs, replacing triggers, sharpening jaws). Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation, force control, and domain-specific tool use needed. Damage diagnosis is also highly unstructured.

task: Remove completed workpieces from equipment or work tables, using hand tools, and place workpieces in containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one completed workpiece from equipment or table and placing it in a container, including any hand-tool use; typically ~1–2 min per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of known objects on flat surfaces is moderately reliable (~70–80%), but adding hand-tool use, varied workpiece shapes, and precise container placement in semi-structured industrial settings lowers reliability significantly. Grasp generalization and tool-use coordination remain weak points.

task: Compute positions and plot courses on charts to navigate vessels, using instruments such as compasses, sextants, and charts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = plotting a course and computing positions for a single voyage leg; a competent navigator typically takes 30–60 min for chart work, fixes, and course planning.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine manipulation of sextants/compasses, reading analog charts, and maritime cognitive reasoning. Humanoids cannot operate vessel controls or navigate at sea; no deployed system handles this task autonomously in 2026.

task: Transport fish to processing plants or to buyers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one delivery trip (loading, driving to processing plant or buyer, unloading). Typical regional trip ~1–2 hours total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a refrigerated truck or boat on public roads/waterways — humanoid robots cannot operate vehicles in traffic. Loading/unloading fish adds further complexity. No current general-purpose robot can do this autonomously end-to-end.

task: Manipulate controls and observe dial indicators to monitor, adjust, and regulate speeds of machine mechanisms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single speed-check/adjust cycle: glance at dials, interpret values, and manipulate controls. A competent operator completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog dial indicators (moderate vision challenge), interpreting values in context, and making precise control manipulations (knobs, levers). Current humanoids lack reliable fine-motor control of arbitrary industrial controls and closed-loop process judgment.

task: Sort, pack, and store catch in holds with salt and ice.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single haul/batch of catch. A competent fisherman sorts, salts, ices, and stows one batch in a ship's hold in roughly 30–60 minutes depending on volume.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating on a moving, wet, cramped fishing vessel; handling slippery, varied-shape fish; navigating tight hold spaces; shoveling ice; and applying salt — all in unstructured, dynamic maritime conditions far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Remove catches from fishing equipment and measure them to ensure compliance with legal size.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one catch from fishing equipment and measuring it. A competent worker takes ~2–4 minutes per catch including unhooking, measuring, and compliance check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires wet, slippery, irregular surfaces outdoors; handling live, wriggling fish with variable shapes; unhooking from nets/lines; and precise measurement. Generalization, dexterous wet-grip, and outdoor mobility all severely limit current robots.

task: Mark or discard items with defects such as spots, stains, scars, snags, chips, scratches, or unacceptable shapes or finishes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and marking/discarding one item. A competent worker visually scans, identifies defects, and marks or discards in ~1–2 minutes per item.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection (spots, scratches, chips) is maturing but generalizing across varied defect types, materials, and finishes remains hard. Physical marking/discarding adds manipulation complexity. False positives/negatives limit autonomous acceptance.

task: File grooved, contoured, and irregular surfaces of metal objects, such as metalworking dies and machine parts, to conform to templates, other parts, layouts, or blueprint specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filing a single grooved/contoured metal part to spec. A skilled die-maker/fitter typically spends 30–90 min per part depending on complexity; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, precise force/tactile feedback, and iterative template-fitting judgment. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter control, adaptive filing pressure, and inspection capability needed for metalworking die surfaces.

task: Scrape fat, blubber, or flesh from skin sides of pelts with knives or hand scrapers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scraping one pelt clean. A skilled worker typically takes 10–15 min per pelt with a knife or hand scraper.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained fine-force-controlled scraping on a soft, irregular, deformable surface (pelt). Extreme sensitivity to blade pressure and angle needed to avoid cutting hide. Far beyond current robot dexterity and compliant force control capabilities.

task: Set distance points between rolls, guides, meters, and stops, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting distances for one machine configuration. A skilled operator measures, adjusts, and locks components per spec in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-adjustment of multiple mechanical components to tight tolerances, reading specs, and verifying measurements. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force control, and metrology integration needed for reliable autonomous setup.
