task: Shape or cut materials to specified measurements, using hand tools, machines, or power saws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting/shaping one piece of material to spec. A skilled worker measures, positions, and cuts a single piece in ~15 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, safe handling of power saws/hand tools, and fine dexterity with varied materials. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use, precision cutting, and the safety control needed for powered cutting equipment.

task: Fill oil cups, adjust valves, and observe gauges to control flow of metal coolants and lubricants onto workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling, adjusting, and observing for one machine/workpiece setup. A skilled machinist does this in roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires pouring liquids precisely into small oil cups, manipulating fine valves, and interpreting analog/dial gauges — demanding dexterity, fine motor control, and contextual judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable fluid-handling and valve-tuning precision in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Select, align, and bolt positioning fixtures, stops, and specified dies to rams and anvils, forging rolls, or presses and hammers.
human_time_rationale: One setup/changeover on one forging machine: selecting correct dies, aligning precisely to ram/anvil, and torquing bolts. Skilled setup takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy industrial tool use, precise torque application, navigation in cluttered forge shops, selecting correct dies from inventory, and fine alignment under load — far beyond current humanoid generalization and force-control capabilities.

task: Release quarry from traps or nets and transfer to cages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = releasing one animal from a trap/net and transferring it to a cage. A skilled handler takes ~5–10 min accounting for animal handling, safety, and securing the cage.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate dexterous manipulation of varied trap/net mechanisms, safe handling of live/struggling animals, outdoor unstructured terrain, and dynamic force control. Far beyond current robot generalization and bio-interaction capability.

task: Put fishing equipment into the water and anchor or tow equipment, according to the fishing method used.
human_time_rationale: One instance = deploying gear for a single set (nets, lines, traps, or trawl gear), including placement in water and anchoring/towing setup. Typical commercial fishing operation takes ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating on a moving vessel at sea, handling heavy/wet/tangled gear, dynamic marine environment, anchoring or towing coordination — far beyond current humanoid robot mobility, stability, and tool-use capabilities outdoors on water.

task: Transfer equipment, objects, or parts to specified work areas, using moving devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving one load of equipment/parts to a specified work area via cart, dolly, or hand truck; typical industrial transfer takes 5–15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids can carry and place objects but struggle with varied item shapes, operating moving devices (carts, dollies, forklifts), navigating cluttered industrial floors, and reliably reaching exact specified destinations autonomously.

task: Organize and store toys and materials to ensure order in activity areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tidying one activity area (e.g., a classroom corner or playroom). A competent worker sorts, groups, and stores items in bins/shelves in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires recognizing diverse, irregular toy shapes, understanding categorical sorting rules, navigating cluttered spaces, and placing items correctly in varied containers. Current robots lack robust semantic sorting and generalizable manipulation of toy-sized clutter.

task: Collect samples of merchandise for examination, appraisal, or testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a set of merchandise samples from a store/warehouse floor. A competent worker identifies, retrieves, labels, and packages representative items in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied retail/warehouse environments, identifying correct items among many, grasping diverse merchandise shapes/sizes, and labeling/packaging — challenging perception, generalization, and dexterous tool-use for current humanoids.

task: Count or weigh and bundle items.
human_time_rationale: One instance = counting/weighing and bundling a single batch (e.g., a stack of papers, produce, or parts). A competent worker completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and count uniform objects reasonably well, but bundling (wrapping, banding, tying) requires fine dexterity and tool-use with varied items. Perception of count/weight is feasible; secure bundling is error-prone.

task: Select, bait, and set traps, and lay poison along trails, according to species, size, habits, and environs of birds or animals and reasons for trapping them.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting, baiting, and setting one trap/poison placement along a trail. A skilled trapper takes ~30–60 min accounting for site assessment, species-specific bait prep, and proper placement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert naturalist judgment (species ID, habitat reading, scent masking), fine dexterous trap-setting on uneven outdoor terrain, poison handling, and complex contextual decision-making far beyond current robot generalization. Outdoor unstructured environment compounds all limitations.

task: Patrol trap lines or nets to inspect settings, remove catch, and reset or relocate traps.
human_time_rationale: One patrol of a typical trap line (10–30 traps/nets across varied terrain) takes a competent trapper roughly 1.5–3 hours; median ~2 hours per patrol circuit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires traversing unstructured outdoor/wilderness terrain, locating hidden traps, manipulating spring-loaded mechanisms, handling live/dead animals, and resetting/relocating traps — all far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities in unstructured field environments.

task: Measure completed workpieces to verify conformance to specifications, using micrometers, gauges, calipers, templates, or rulers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single workpiece with appropriate tools and recording/checking results against specs. A skilled machinist or quality inspector typically completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (calipers, micrometers), precise alignment and grip on small instruments, and interpreting analog/digital readings. Current humanoids lack the fine-motor precision and reliable tool-handling needed for consistent metrology work.

task: Load and unload vessel equipment and supplies, by hand or using hoisting equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading or unloading a typical batch of vessel equipment/supplies (mixed items). A competent dock worker completes one such cycle in ~30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/maritime terrain navigation, operating hoisting equipment, handling highly varied heavy items, and working near vessel edges. Current humanoids lack maritime tool operation, robust outdoor mobility, and safe heavy-load handling at this scale.

task: Install, align, and lock specified punches, dies, cutting blades, or other fixtures in rams or beds of machines, using gauges, templates, feelers, shims, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a single punch/die or tooling fixture on one machine. Competent toolsetter spends ~30–60 min on alignment, shimming, gauging, and locking.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-dexterous manipulation with multiple hand tools, shims, and gauges; sub-millimeter alignment verification; force-controlled locking; and contextual judgment about fixture fit — all far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Set blade tensions, heights, and angles to perform prescribed cuts, using wrenches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting blade tension, height, and angle for a single prescribed cut setup. A competent operator spends ~5–10 min checking specs, using wrenches, and verifying settings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying multiple adjustment points, applying precise torque with wrenches, reading/interpreting analog scales or gauges, and verifying blade geometry — demanding fine dexterity, multi-step tool use, and tactile feedback well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Clean and lubricate machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and lubricating a single industrial machine. A competent worker wipes down surfaces, removes debris, applies lubricant to key points — typically 20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can wipe flat surfaces (~50%) but precise lubrication requires locating specific grease fittings, applying controlled amounts, and using tools (grease guns). Unstructured machine geometry and fine tool-use lower success significantly.

task: Start operation of rolling and milling machines to flatten, temper, form, and reduce sheet metal sections and to produce steel strips.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting up a rolling/milling machine for a production run, including pre-checks, parameter setting, and initiating the run. ~15 min for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Rolling/milling machines are large, industrial, fixed heavy equipment with complex controls, gauges, and safety interlocks. Current humanoid robots cannot operate such specialized industrial machinery autonomously or safely.

task: Cut, shape, and trim materials, such as textiles, food, glass, stone, and metal, using knives, scissors, and other hand tools, portable power tools, or bench-mounted tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one cutting/shaping/trimming operation on a single workpiece (e.g., cutting fabric to a pattern, trimming a stone tile). ~15 min covers setup, measurement, and execution for a typical piece.
robot_success_rationale: Highly variable materials and tools demand precise force control, fine dexterity, and perception. Robots struggle with tool changes, material variability (glass, stone, textiles), accurate path following under resistance, and safe power-tool use.

task: Turn valves to start flow of coolant against cutting areas or to start airflow that blows cuttings away from kerfs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = turning a valve to start coolant or airflow at a cutting area. A competent worker does this in ~30 seconds as a routine setup step.
robot_success_rationale: Turning industrial valves requires locating, grasping, and rotating a handle with moderate force. Perception and mobility are feasible, but valve variety, torque requirements, and precise positioning in cluttered machine environments reduce reliability significantly.

task: Adjust ram strokes of presses to specified lengths, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting ram stroke on one press. A skilled operator uses hand tools to locate, loosen, reposition, and re-tighten adjustment mechanisms, typically taking 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torquing with specific hand tools, reading mechanical indicators, fine dexterous manipulation in tight press spaces, and task-specific knowledge of press mechanisms. Current humanoids lack the precision, force control, and generalization for this industrial adjustment task.

task: Trim excess material or cut threads off finished products, such as cutting loose ends of plastic off a manufactured toy for a smoother finish.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming excess material from one finished product. A skilled worker with clippers or a blade completes this in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to locate, grip, and precisely cut small flash/threads without damaging the product. Perception of exact trim points on varied geometries and controlled blade force are challenging for current humanoids.

task: Position templates or measure materials to locate specified points of cuts or to obtain maximum yields, using rules, scales, or patterns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning templates/measuring one workpiece to mark cut lines. A competent worker typically takes 5–12 minutes depending on material complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, fine dexterous tool use (rules, scales, patterns), spatial optimization reasoning for yield, and accurate marking on varied material surfaces — all challenging for current humanoid robots.

task: Track animals by checking for signs such as droppings or destruction of vegetation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = surveying a defined trail or plot for animal signs. A skilled tracker inspects, identifies, and records findings across a typical area in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires traversing unstructured outdoor terrain (low locomotion success), detecting subtle biological cues (droppings, chewed vegetation, tracks) with expert-level visual interpretation, and contextual reasoning far beyond current robot perception and generalization.

task: Clean work areas, and maintain grounds and landscaping.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a typical work area (e.g., a workshop or office zone). A competent worker sweeps, wipes surfaces, and tidies in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Sweeping and wiping flat surfaces is within robot range, but varied clutter, tool storage, and surface types reduce reliability. Generalization to unstructured work areas remains limited.

task: Load and adjust workpieces onto equipment or work tables, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading/adjusting one workpiece onto a machine or work table. A skilled worker takes ~5 min to lift, position, align, and secure it with hand tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping varied workpieces, precise alignment, and tool use (wrenches, clamps) — challenging for 2026 humanoids. Perception of exact positioning tolerances, force feedback for securing, and generalization across part geometries limit success.

task: Install structures or fixtures, such as windows, frames, floorings, trim, or hardware, using carpenters' hand or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single fixture/structure (e.g., one window or door frame). A competent carpenter typically takes 45–90 minutes depending on type; ~60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, heavy lifting, fine fastening with varied power/hand tools, and adaptive problem-solving in unstructured construction environments. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capability at acceptable quality.

task: Position, align, and secure workpieces against fixtures or stops on machine beds or on dies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning and securing a single workpiece on a machine bed or die. A skilled operator typically takes 2–5 minutes per setup depending on part complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment against fixtures and applying securing force (clamps, bolts). Current humanoids struggle with fine positional accuracy, force-controlled fastening, and interpreting varied fixture geometries without task-specific tooling.

task: Scribe reference lines on workpieces as guides for cutting operations, according to blueprints, templates, sample parts, or specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scribing reference lines on a single workpiece. A skilled layout worker typically takes 5–10 minutes to interpret specs, position/clamp part, and scribe accurate lines.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-tool manipulation of a scriber on hard material surfaces, reading blueprints/templates, accurate spatial measurement, and consistent sub-millimeter positioning — well beyond current humanoid dexterity and perception reliability.

task: Measure and mark cutting lines on materials, using a ruler, pencil, chalk, and marking gauge.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking a single workpiece. A competent worker measures, references a ruler/gauge, and marks lines in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor control of pencil/chalk/marking gauge, accurate force feedback, stable visual measurement reading, and coordinated bimanual work — well beyond reliable 2026 humanoid dexterity and precision.

task: Separate materials or products according to size, weight, type, condition, color, or shade.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting a typical batch/bin of mixed items (e.g., ~20–50 objects) by a single attribute. A competent worker takes ~5–15 min depending on quantity and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sort by size/weight with structured setups and reasonable perception, but color/shade discrimination, irregular shapes, fragile items, and condition assessment reduce reliability. Generalization across varied materials and sorting criteria remains inconsistent in 2026.

task: Load workpieces, plastic material, or chemical solutions into machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a single batch/workpiece or material into a machine. A competent worker picks, aligns, and secures the item in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Structured pick-and-place into known fixtures is feasible (~70–80%), but variability in workpiece geometry, precise alignment tolerances, liquid/chemical handling, and machine-specific interfaces (latches, doors) pulls success down significantly.

task: Signal and assist other workers to remove and position equipment, fill hoppers, and feed materials into machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full signal/assist cycle: directing equipment, filling a hopper, or feeding materials into a machine. A competent worker completes this in roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated multi-worker signaling, interpreting dynamic industrial environments, operating heavy/specialized equipment, and feeding bulk materials precisely. Humanoids lack reliable communication, heavy-load handling, and situational awareness for unstructured industrial coordination.

task: Measure, weigh, or mix cleaning solutions, using measuring tanks, calibrated rods or suction tubes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single batch of cleaning solution; a competent worker measures, mixes, and verifies concentration in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid handling with calibrated rods/suction tubes, reading graduated markings, pouring without spilling, and verifying concentration — demanding fine dexterity, fluid perception, and tool generalization well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Examine completed workpieces for defects, such as chipped edges or marred surfaces and sort defective pieces according to types of flaws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single workpiece for defects and sorting it. A competent worker visually scans and categorizes a part in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can do basic visual inspection with machine vision, but reliably detecting subtle defects (chipped edges, marred surfaces) across varied parts, then physically sorting by flaw type in unstructured settings, challenges current perception and manipulation generalization.

task: Fold or shape materials before or after cutting them.
human_time_rationale: One instance = folding or shaping one piece of material around a single cut operation; a skilled worker completes this in a few minutes depending on material complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires bimanual dexterity, force sensing, and material-specific knowledge (fabric, metal, paper behave differently). Deformable material manipulation remains a major frontier-robot weakness; inconsistent generalization across material types.

task: Stack cut items and load them on racks or conveyors or onto trucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stacking and loading a batch of cut items (e.g., sheet metal, lumber, or fabric cuts) onto a rack, conveyor, or truck. A competent worker takes ~15 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Cut items vary widely in shape, size, weight, and material. Loading onto trucks requires navigating ramps/dock environments and handling irregular loads. Current humanoids lack reliable grasp planning and strength for varied heavy industrial cuts; truck loading is especially unstructured.

task: Mark identification numbers, trademarks, grades, marketing data, sizes, or model numbers on products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking a single product (e.g. stamping, labeling, or engraving one item). A competent worker completes this in ~1–3 minutes depending on method.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and orient objects, but precise marking (stamping, engraving, label placement) requires fine dexterity, consistent alignment, and tool-use generalization. Structured setups raise odds; unstructured product variety lowers them significantly.

task: Harvest marine life for human or animal consumption, using diving or dredging equipment, traps, barges, rods, reels, or tackle.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one fishing/harvesting trip. A typical commercial fishing or diving trip (travel to site, deploy gear, harvest, return) runs ~3–5 hours median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating boats/vessels on open water, deploying/retrieving traps or nets, underwater diving, and working in highly unstructured maritime environments — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities. Perception, waterproofing, and vessel operation are major blockers.

task: Lower table-mounted cutters such as knife blades, cutting wheels, or saws to cut items to specified sizes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lowering a cutter and making a single cut on one item. A competent worker positions the material and actuates the cutter in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of material to cutting line, controlled force application on a table-mounted tool, and quality verification. Current humanoids lack reliable fine positioning and safe tool-press dexterity for varied cutter types; perception of cut quality is limited.

task: Apprehend or remove trespassers or thieves from railroad property or coordinate with law enforcement agencies in apprehensions and removals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one trespasser/thief incident from detection to removal/handoff. Includes locating, confronting, and coordinating with law enforcement; median ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires legal authority, social negotiation, threat assessment, restraint, and coordination with law enforcement — far beyond current robot autonomy. Outdoor/rail terrain, unpredictable human behavior, and liability constraints make autonomous apprehension essentially infeasible.

task: Unroll, lay out, attach, or mount materials or items on cutting tables or machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unrolling/laying out one batch of material (e.g. fabric roll) on a cutting table and securing/aligning it. A skilled worker typically takes 5–12 minutes per lay.
robot_success_rationale: Handling large, flexible/limp materials (fabric, film) is extremely challenging for robots — deformable object manipulation, precise alignment, tensioning, and mounting require dexterity and perception capabilities still well below human level in 2026.

task: Set up, operate, or tend machines to saw, cut, shear, slit, punch, crimp, notch, bend, or straighten metal or plastic material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running a machine for a single production batch/job order. A competent operator typically spends ~20 min on setup, tooling, and a short run cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large industrial machinery (press brakes, shears, saws), loading sheet/bar stock, adjusting tooling, and reading job parameters. Humanoids lack the tool-use generalization, force sensing, and safety interlocks needed for autonomous industrial machine operation in 2026.

task: Erect scaffolding or ladders for assembling structures above ground level.
human_time_rationale: One instance = erecting a typical scaffolding section or positioning/securing a ladder at a single work site location; a competent worker takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy, unwieldy components, outdoor/uneven terrain, high-force connections, and overhead work at elevation. Current humanoids lack the strength, stability, and manipulation precision for safe scaffold assembly. Severe safety constraints further limit autonomy.

task: Bake breads, rolls, or other pastries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one batch (e.g., a dozen rolls or one loaf). Includes mixing, shaping, proofing, and baking; ~90 min total active+passive time for a competent baker.
robot_success_rationale: Baking requires precise dough handling, multi-step sequencing, sensory judgment (texture, smell, color), fine manipulation of soft/deformable materials, and oven operation — all well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Mark cutting lines around patterns or templates, or follow layout points, using squares, rules, and straightedges, and chalk, pencils, or scribes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking cutting lines for a single pattern/template piece. A skilled cutter positions the template, aligns guides, and scribes lines in roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor control to hold a straightedge flush while scribing accurately along a template edge; perception of faint chalk/pencil lines on varied fabric/material surfaces is unreliable; tool-use generalization for rules and scribes remains weak in 2026 robots.

task: Apportion and serve food to facility residents, employees, or patrons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = portioning and serving a single meal to one person (e.g., cafeteria line). A competent worker takes ~2–4 minutes per person including portioning, plating, and handing off.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise portioning of varied food textures, dynamic social interaction, tray/utensil handling, and adapting to patron requests. Current humanoids lack reliable soft-food manipulation, ladle control, and real-time human interaction for this unstructured environment.

task: Assemble and fasten materials to make frameworks or props, using hand tools and wood screws, nails, dowel pins, or glue.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one framework or prop (e.g., a stage flat or wooden frame). A competent carpenter typically takes 30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step sequencing: positioning irregular wood pieces, driving screws/nails precisely, applying glue, and maintaining alignment. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity for tool use (screwdrivers, hammers) on unstructured workpieces and struggle with force-controlled fastening.

task: Sterilize bottles and prepare formulas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sterilizing a batch of bottles (boiling/steam ~10 min) plus mixing and filling formula for one feeding session (~15 min total).
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise water measurement, powder scooping, bottle assembly, sterilization equipment operation, and safe temperature judgment. Multi-step, dexterous, hygiene-critical task far beyond current humanoid generalization and reliability.

task: Kill or stun trapped quarry, using clubs, poisons, guns, or drowning methods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dispatching one trapped animal. A competent trapper locates, assesses, and dispatches in ~3–8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/unstructured terrain navigation, recognizing a live struggling animal, selecting a dispatch method, and executing it safely and humanely. Unpredictable animal movement, rough terrain, tool handling (firearm, club), and ethical precision are far beyond current robot generalization.

task: Cook foodstuffs according to menus, special dietary or nutritional restrictions, or numbers of portions to be served.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and cooking a meal service batch (e.g., a menu item for a sitting). A competent cook typically spends ~60 min from prep to plating for a standard batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting menus, adapting to dietary restrictions, multi-step cooking (chopping, sautéing, timing, seasoning), and quality judgment. Current humanoids lack robust kitchen dexterity, recipe generalization, and sensory feedback for cooking.

task: Build or repair cabinets, doors, frameworks, floors, or other wooden fixtures used in buildings, using woodworking machines, carpenter's hand tools, or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = building or repairing one typical fixture (e.g., a cabinet or door frame). A competent carpenter takes ~1–3 hours depending on complexity; 2 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step planning, precise measuring/cutting, operating power saws and hand tools, fine joinery, and generalization across unstructured environments. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in real worksites.

task: Position guides, stops, holding blocks, or other fixtures to secure and direct workpieces, using hand tools and measuring devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up fixtures/stops for a single workpiece run. A skilled machinist or woodworker typically measures, positions, and secures guides/stops in ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, coordinated bimanual manipulation of clamps/stops, and spatial reasoning about workpiece geometry. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-force feedback and tool-use precision needed for accurate fixture placement; perception of reference marks is also challenging.

task: Place workpieces on cutting tables, manually or using hoists, cranes, or sledges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing one workpiece on a cutting table, including rigging hoist/crane if needed and aligning correctly; typically 5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating cranes, hoists, or sledges — heavy industrial equipment a humanoid cannot drive or rig. Even manual placement of heavy workpieces demands strength, precise alignment, and tool-use beyond current robot capability.

task: Remove damaged or defective parts or sections of structures and repair or replace, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing and repairing/replacing one damaged section (e.g., a board, panel, or fitting). A competent tradesperson typically takes ~45–90 minutes depending on scope.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing damage, selecting correct tools and replacement parts, applying varied forces with precision, and adapting to unpredictable structural contexts. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use generalization, and perceptual reasoning capabilities.

task: Clean, treat, buff, or polish finished items, using grinders, brushes, chisels, and cleaning solutions and polishing materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing one item (e.g., a metal part or casting). A competent worker spends ~15–25 min selecting tools, cleaning, treating, and buffing a single piece to acceptable quality.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-tool switching (grinders, brushes, chisels), force-sensitive surface contact, and quality judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable tool changeovers, nuanced force control, and surface-finish perception needed for consistent acceptable results.

task: Fill salt, pepper, sugar, cream, condiment, and napkin containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = refilling a single salt shaker. A competent worker pours from a bulk supply, checks level, and caps it in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine motor control to open/close small caps, pour without spilling fine granules, and judge fill level. Current robots lack reliable dexterity for small containers and precise pouring of granular material.

task: Replace defective blades or wheels, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing one defective blade or wheel. Involves loosening fasteners, removing old part, fitting new part, and re-securing with hand tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires selecting correct hand tools, applying precise torque to fasteners, and handling varied blade/wheel geometries. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use dexterity and force control for consistent, safe fastener work across unstructured settings.

task: Route items to provide cutouts for parts, using portable routers, grinders, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = routing one cutout in a part. A skilled worker takes ~15–25 min to set up, guide, and finish a single cutout with portable router/grinder.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool-path control with handheld powered tools (router, grinder), firm bimanual grip under vibration, and fine spatial judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and tool-use generalization for safe, accurate routing.

task: Patrol railroad yards, cars, stations, or other facilities to protect company property or shipments and to maintain order.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full patrol shift (~8 hours) covering a railroad yard, station, or facility. Standard shift length for a security/patrol officer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating vast, unstructured outdoor/industrial terrain, handling unpredictable human interactions, exercising judgment for order maintenance, and responding to emergencies. Current humanoids lack endurance, outdoor mobility, and social authority for autonomous security patrol.

task: Observe machine operations, gauges, or thermometers, and adjust controls to maintain specified conditions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single observation-and-adjustment cycle at a machine or control panel; a competent operator scans gauges and makes corrections in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read digital displays and turn knobs but struggle with analogue gauges, varied control layouts, interpreting "specified conditions," and making precise multi-step adjustments across diverse industrial environments without task-specific programming.

task: Specialize in preparing fancy dishes or food for special diets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single fancy/special-diet dish from mise en place to plated presentation; a skilled chef typically takes ~60 min end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Fancy cooking demands fine dexterity (piping, garnishing, delicate cuts), multi-sensory feedback (taste, smell, texture), complex sequencing, and recipe adaptation for special diets — far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Verify trueness of structure, using plumb bob and level.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking one structural element (wall, post, or frame). A competent worker sets up plumb bob or level, takes readings, and records results in ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robot must handle, position, and read a plumb bob (swinging pendulum, fine cord) and spirit/laser level on varied surfaces. Tool manipulation is feasible but precise placement, reading analog instruments, and interpreting structural geometry in unstructured sites is very challenging for 2026 humanoids.

task: Clean, cut, and cook meat, fish, or poultry.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning, cutting, and cooking a single portion of meat/fish/poultry. ~5 min cleaning, ~5 min cutting, ~15 min cooking (e.g. pan-searing a chicken breast).
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (deboning, filleting), safe handling of slippery/irregular raw protein, knife control, and monitoring cooking doneness. Generalization across protein types, temperatures, and textures far exceeds 2026 robot capability.

task: Replace or sharpen dulled cutting tools such as saws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sharpening or replacing one dulled tool (e.g., a hand saw). A competent worker inspects, files/replaces blade or takes to a grinder, and verifies sharpness in ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool-use (files, wrenches, blade tensioners), precise force control on small irregular teeth, and quality judgment of sharpness. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and perception capabilities.

task: Install rough door and window frames, subflooring, fixtures, or temporary supports in structures undergoing construction or repair.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one rough frame (door or window) or a section of subflooring. A competent carpenter typically takes 30–60 minutes per unit depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lumber handling, precise measurement, nailing/screwing into structural members, plumb/level verification, and operation in cluttered, uneven construction sites. Current humanoids lack the strength, tool-use precision, and unstructured environment navigation needed.

task: Mark identifying data on workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking one workpiece with identifying data (e.g., stamping, engraving, labeling, or writing). A competent worker typically takes 1–5 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool use (stamp, marker, engraver) on varied workpiece surfaces and orientations. Perception for placement is manageable, but fine dexterous control, consistent force, and legibility on irregular surfaces remain challenging for 2026 humanoids.

task: Wash decks, conveyors, knives, and other equipment, using brushes, detergents, and water.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full washdown of decks, conveyors, knives, and ancillary equipment in one processing area; a competent worker typically completes this in 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating wet, cluttered industrial environments, handling brushes and hoses with targeted pressure, reaching varied surfaces/angles, and safely managing sharp knives. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, wet-environment resilience, and task generalization needed.

task: Wash pots, pans, dishes, utensils, or other cooking equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = washing a typical batch of mixed cooking items (pots, pans, dishes, utensils) in a commercial or home sink; ~5–12 min depending on volume and soil level.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex wet dexterity (scrubbing curved/heavy pans, handling slippery surfaces), precise force control, bimanual coordination, and water/soap management — well beyond current frontier humanoid capability in unstructured sink environments.

task: Inspect ceiling or floor tile, wall coverings, siding, glass, or woodwork to detect broken or damaged structures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one room or one wall/surface section. A competent worker systematically scans ceiling tiles, walls, flooring, and woodwork, taking ~15–25 minutes per area.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate indoors and use cameras for visual detection, but reliably identifying subtle cracks, discoloration, soft/damaged tiles, or concealed damage across varied materials and lighting requires fine perception and judgment beyond current frontier capability.

task: Peel, wash, trim, and cook vegetables and meats, and bake breads and pastries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a typical meal-prep session covering several vegetables, a meat, and one baked item. A competent cook handles this in ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Peeling, trimming, and baking demand fine dexterity, force modulation, and recipe generalization well beyond 2026 frontier robots. Unstructured kitchen environments, sharp tools, and varied food textures compound failure risk severely.

task: Prepare meals in private homes according to employers' recipes or tastes, handling all meals for the family and possibly for other household staff.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a full day's meals (breakfast, lunch, dinner) for a household. A competent private chef typically spends ~2 hours of active cooking per day.
robot_success_rationale: Requires adapting to personal recipes/tastes, complex multi-dish sequencing, fine dexterity (chopping, plating), navigating an unfamiliar home kitchen, and implicit social awareness — far beyond current humanoid cooking capability.

task: Stock, organize, and clean kitchens and cooking utensils.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a full stock, organize, and clean cycle in a single kitchen; a competent worker typically takes 25–35 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse sub-tasks: navigating kitchen layout, identifying/placing varied utensils, scrubbing/washing items, and restocking shelves. Each step degrades reliability; grasping wet/irregular utensils, cabinet opening, and learned placement rules are still weak in 2026 frontier robots.

task: Select, clean, and install spacers, rubber sleeves, or cutters on arbors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting, cleaning, and installing spacers/sleeves/cutters on a single arbor setup. A skilled machinist typically completes this in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to select correct components, clean mating surfaces, and precisely seat tight-tolerance parts onto an arbor. High positional accuracy, force control, and tool-use demands exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Set stops on machine beds, change dies, and adjust components, such as rams or power presses, when making multiple or successive passes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a machine for one production run changeover (stops, die swap, ram/press adjustment). A skilled press operator typically takes 20–30 minutes per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torque on heavy dies, fine adjustment of rams/stops, tool use in tight industrial spaces, and job-specific knowledge. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force sensing, and domain-specific tool-use needed for reliable autonomous die changes.

task: Lubricate workpieces with oil.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying oil to a single workpiece. A competent worker brushes, sprays, or wipes oil onto one part in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Pick up an applicator and coat a surface is within reach of 2026 manipulators, but consistent, even oil coverage, tool handling, and adapting to varied workpiece geometry limit reliability. Perception of coverage completeness is also weak.

task: Apply shock-absorbing, sound-deadening, or decorative paneling to ceilings or walls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing paneling on one standard wall or ceiling section (~10–20 sq ft). A competent installer measures, cuts, positions, and fastens panels in ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires overhead work, precise measurement/cutting, sustained force against ceilings/walls, and tool use (drills, adhesives). Current humanoids lack the reach stability, fine tool-use, and overhead manipulation needed for acceptable quality.

task: Examine and inspect machines to detect malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single machine. A competent technician typically spends 15–30 minutes checking components, listening, looking, and probing for faults.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can do basic visual/thermal inspection but struggle with multi-modal sensing (sound, vibration, smell), accessing cramped areas, interpreting ambiguous fault signs, and generalizing across diverse machine types without task-specific programming.

task: Plan and prepare food for parties, holiday meals, luncheons, special functions, and other social events.
human_time_rationale: One instance = planning and preparing food for a single social event. A competent caterer/cook typically spends 3–5 hours on prep, shopping coordination, and cooking for a moderate-sized gathering.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step meal planning, menu creativity, grocery coordination, complex cooking techniques, timing across multiple dishes, and adapting to event-specific needs — far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and autonomous decision-making in unstructured kitchen environments.

task: Draw samples for laboratory analysis, or test solutions for conformance to specifications, such as acidity or specific gravity.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drawing a sample and running a single conformance test (e.g., acidity or specific gravity). A competent lab tech typically spends ~10–20 min per sample including collection, testing, and recording.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fluid handling (pipettes, syringes), operating lab instruments (pH meters, hydrometers), fine dexterity, and interpreting readings. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation and lab tool proficiency needed for reliable autonomous execution.

task: Engrave and print patterns, designs, etchings, trademarks, or lettering onto flat or curved surfaces of a wide variety of metal, glass, plastic, or paper items, using hand tools or hand-held power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = engraving a single item (e.g., a trophy, pendant, or nameplate). A competent engraver spends ~30–60 min setting up and executing a typical design.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine motor precision, tool-path planning on varied curved/flat surfaces, and material-adaptive force control. Current humanoid robots lack the sub-millimeter dexterity and tactile feedback needed for acceptable engraving quality.

task: Sharpen dulled blades, using bench grinders, abrasive wheels, or lathes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sharpening one blade (e.g., a chisel, knife, or lawn mower blade) on a bench grinder or abrasive wheel; a skilled worker typically takes 10–20 minutes per blade.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force/angle control against a spinning abrasive, real-time tactile/visual feedback to judge sharpness, and safe operation of powered bench grinders. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tool-use fluency, and sharpness-sensing needed for acceptable quality.

task: Examine structural timbers and supports to detect decay, and replace timbers as required, using hand tools, nuts, and bolts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a section of structural timbers and replacing any decayed members. A competent carpenter typically spends ~1 hour per section, including assessment, removal, and fastening.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert tactile/visual detection of decay, heavy timber manipulation, precise bolting in confined/unstructured spaces, and judgment-based decision-making. Current humanoids lack the perception, strength integration, and generalization for this multi-step structural task.

task: Bore boltholes in timber, masonry or concrete walls, using power drill.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling one bolthole. A competent worker marks the spot, positions the drill, and bores through the material in ~2–4 minutes depending on depth and material.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise hole placement, high lateral force application against a wall (often vertical), sustained pressure, tool alignment, and material-specific technique (e.g. hammer-drill mode). Current humanoids lack the force control, bracing stability, and reliable tool-use generalization for this task.

task: Transport items to work or storage areas, using carts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading items onto a cart, transporting to a work/storage area, and unloading. A typical facility trip takes ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids can walk and carry objects but reliably gripping/loading varied items onto a cart, navigating shared spaces, and unloading at correct destinations remains unreliable. Cart-pushing and door navigation add further challenges.

task: Take inventory of supplies and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One inventory check of a typical supply/equipment area (e.g., stockroom or tool crib). A competent worker identifies, counts, and logs items — roughly 20–45 minutes per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied storage areas, visually identifying and counting diverse items, reading labels, and logging data. Current robots lack reliable object recognition across cluttered, unstructured inventories and struggle with data entry integration.

task: Anchor and brace forms and other structures in place, using nails, bolts, anchor rods, steel cables, planks, wedges, and timbers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = anchoring/bracing a single form or structure section. A skilled carpenter typically spends 30–60 min selecting hardware, positioning, and securing with mixed fasteners and bracing elements.
robot_success_rationale: Requires combining highly varied tools (nails, bolts, rods, cables, wedges, timbers) in unstructured construction environments. Heavy material handling, precise force application, and situational judgment far exceed current humanoid dexterity and generalization limits.

task: Finish surfaces of woodwork or wallboard in houses or buildings, using paint, hand tools, or paneling.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing one average room (walls + woodwork). A competent painter/finisher takes ~2 hours including prep, cutting in, rolling, and touch-ups.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated locomotion, tool-use (brushes, rollers, sprayers), fine edge-cutting, surface prep judgment, and working in unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack the precision, reach adaptability, and quality control for acceptable finish work.

task: Create and explore new cuisines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = creating and testing one new cuisine concept, including ideation, ingredient selection, cooking, and tasting. A skilled chef typically spends ~2 hours per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative ideation, novel flavor pairing, cultural knowledge, improvisation, and sensory taste evaluation — all far beyond current robot cognition, dexterity, and gustatory perception. Even basic novel recipe execution is ~5–20%; true cuisine creation is near zero.

task: Hone cutters with oilstones to remove nicks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = honing one cutter with an oilstone until nicks are removed. A skilled worker inspects and hones a single cutter in roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained precise contact force, controlled angle, and tactile feedback across a sharp edge to detect and remove nicks without over-honing. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and force-control precision; no deployed system handles this reliably.

task: Adjust guides and stops to control depths and widths of cuts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting guides/stops on a single machine for a specific cut spec; a skilled worker reads the spec and makes adjustments in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading measurement markings, fine-tuning set screws/knobs to precise tolerances, and verifying with calipers. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, calibrated force control, and tool-reading perception needed for reliable precision adjustment.

task: Cover subfloors with building paper to keep out moisture and lay hardwood, parquet, or wood-strip-block floors by nailing floors to subfloor or cementing them to mastic or asphalt base.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying flooring in one average room (~200 sq ft). Includes rolling/stapling building paper, cutting and nailing/gluing hardwood or parquet strips, and finishing edges. A competent flooring installer typically takes 6–8 hours per room.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rolling and stapling large paper sheets, precise board alignment, pneumatic nailer or mastic adhesive application, cutting boards to fit, and maintaining tight tolerances across a full room. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and spatial planning capabilities.

task: Set controls to regulate temperature and length of cycles, and start conveyors, pumps, agitators, and machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting all controls and starting equipment for a single production run. A competent operator takes ~10 min to adjust settings and activate all systems.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interfacing with varied industrial control panels, switches, dials, and HMIs across multiple machines. Humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation of arbitrary industrial controls, system knowledge integration, and safe multi-machine sequencing in unstructured plant environments.

task: Turn controls to set cutting speeds, feed rates, or table angles for specified operations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting controls (knobs/dials/levers) for one machining operation. A skilled machinist reads specs and dials in settings in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying and precisely turning specific knobs/levers on varied machine tools, reading scales/displays, and verifying values. Current robots lack reliable fine-motor dial manipulation, machine-specific tool recognition, and closed-loop verification on arbitrary industrial equipment.

task: Inspect and evaluate the physical condition of facilities to determine the type of work required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single facility (e.g., a room, floor, or building unit). A competent inspector walks through, assesses structural/mechanical/cosmetic condition, and documents findings in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced multi-domain judgment (structural, electrical, plumbing, cosmetic), navigating unstructured spaces, and generating actionable work-order decisions. Current robots lack expert-level defect reasoning and reliable generalization across varied facility types.

task: Determine machine settings, and move bars or levers to reproduce designs on rollers or plates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a roller/plate design on a machine; a skilled operator typically reads specs, adjusts levers/bars, and verifies the reproduction in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting design specs, identifying and precisely adjusting multiple levers/bars on industrial machinery, and verifying output quality. Demands high dexterity, domain-specific perception, and tool generalization — well beyond 2026 frontier robot capabilities.

task: Grind out burrs or sharp edges, using portable grinders, speed lathes, or polishing jacks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = deburring one workpiece. A competent machinist typically spends 5–15 minutes inspecting and grinding burrs/sharp edges off a single part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled tool contact, detecting micro-scale burrs via touch/vision, and manipulating a handheld grinder safely. Current humanoids lack sufficient fine dexterity, compliant force control, and reliable burr-detection perception for autonomous quality-acceptable deburring.

task: Remove housings, feed tubes, tool holders, or other accessories to replace worn or broken parts, such as springs or bushings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling one tool/component assembly, replacing one worn part (e.g., spring or bushing), and reassembling. Typical maintenance job takes 15–35 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying worn parts, using hand tools with precision, navigating varied hardware (screws, clips, springs), and fine force-controlled disassembly/reassembly—well beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use generalization.

task: Move machine controls to index workpieces, and to adjust machines for pre-selected operational settings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = indexing one workpiece and adjusting machine settings for a single pre-selected operation; a skilled machinist completes this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise manipulation of industrial machine controls (levers, dials, buttons), fine workpiece positioning, and reading machine state. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterity and machine-interface generalization for this industrial context.

task: Build sleds from logs and timbers for use in hauling camp buildings and machinery through wooded areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = building one functional log/timber sled. A competent worker selects, cuts, and assembles logs/timbers into a load-bearing sled; typically 3–5 hours of skilled manual labor.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy timber handling, outdoor unstructured terrain, chainsaw/axe use, notching/joining large logs, and load-bearing structural judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity, tool-use, and strength capabilities.

task: Cut, thread, or hammer pipes to specifications, using tools such as saws, cutting torches, pipe threaders, or pipe benders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting/threading/bending one pipe segment to spec. A skilled plumber/pipefitter typically spends 10–30 min per pipe, including measuring, setup, and operation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, selecting/switching heavy specialized tools (torches, threaders, benders), applying high force, and verifying tolerances. Current humanoids lack the tool-use dexterity, force control, and safety handling for torch/threading equipment.

task: Drain, clean, and refill machines or tanks at designated intervals, using cleaning solutions or water.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one machine/tank cycle: drain, scrub, rinse, refill. Typical industrial or food-service tank takes ~30–60 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating valves/fittings, handling hoses, applying cleaning solutions inside confined tanks, and managing fluid levels. Complex tool-use, varied configurations, fluid handling, and tight interior access exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Inspect or measure finished workpieces to determine conformance to specifications, using measuring instruments, such as gauges or micrometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single finished workpiece. A competent inspector selects appropriate gauges/micrometers, takes multiple measurements, and records results in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with precise tool selection, correct seating of gauges/micrometers on varied part geometries, and fine dexterous manipulation of handheld instruments. Perception of exact measurement readings and adaptive part handling add further difficulty.

task: Fill cracks or other defects in plaster or plasterboard and sand patch, using patching plaster, trowel, and sanding tool.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling and sanding one defect/crack site. Competent worker applies patching plaster, lets it set briefly, then sands smooth — roughly 20–30 min including prep.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced force control to trowel compound smoothly, detect surface imperfections visually/tactilely, feather edges, and sand flush — all on a vertical surface. Current robots lack the fine dexterity, compliant tool-use, and surface-quality perception needed for acceptable results.

task: Position and clamp workpieces, plates, or rollers in holding fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning and clamping a single workpiece/plate/roller into a holding fixture. A competent worker typically takes 3–7 minutes depending on size and fixture complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment, bimanual coordination, and applying correct clamping force. Current humanoids struggle with fine positional tolerance, varied fixture types, and confirming secure seating without tactile feedback.

task: Measure workpieces and lay out work, using precision measuring devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and laying out a single workpiece. A skilled machinist/fabricator typically spends 10–20 min selecting tools, taking measurements, and scribing layout lines.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool-use (calipers, micrometers, scribes, squares), precise force control, and interpretive judgment on tolerances. Current humanoids lack the fine-motor precision and tool-handling reliability needed for acceptable measurement accuracy.

task: Serve meals and snacks to employing families and their guests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = serving a single meal or snack to a household. Includes plating, carrying dishes to the table, and attending to guests' needs; ~20–30 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a dynamic home environment, carrying filled dishes safely, social awareness of guest needs, and fine dexterity for plating/pouring. Current humanoids lack reliable fluid-carrying stability, social cue reading, and generalization across varied home layouts and tableware.

task: Load machines with objects to be processed and unload them after cleaning, placing them on conveyors or racks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a machine (e.g. industrial washer/dishwasher), waiting for cycle, then unloading and placing items on a conveyor or rack. Typical cycle turnaround handled in ~8 min of active labor.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of varied wet/clean objects onto conveyors or racks requires reliable grasping of diverse items, spatial navigation, and rack alignment. Frontier humanoids can handle structured pick-and-place but struggle with wet surfaces, item variety, and precise rack placement without fixtures.

task: Gather and empty trash.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting trash from multiple bins in a typical office/home area and emptying them into a main bag or dumpster. ~10 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to multiple bins, grasping flexible/heavy bags, tying or removing liners, and transporting to a dumpster. Bag manipulation and varied bin types challenge current dexterity and generalization; locomotion to outdoor dumpsters adds difficulty.

task: Clean and inspect galley equipment, kitchen appliances, and work areas to ensure cleanliness and functional operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full galley cleaning & inspection pass covering surfaces, appliances, and work areas in a typical commercial/vessel kitchen; ~45 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires combining surface wiping, appliance cleaning (ovens, fryers, grills), and functional inspection across cluttered, confined galley spaces. Robots lack the dexterity for multi-surface scrubbing, safe appliance disassembly/reassembly, and judgment-based functional checks needed to meet supervisor standards.

task: Set up, operate, or tend grinding and related tools that remove excess material or burrs from surfaces, sharpen edges or corners, or buff, hone, or polish metal or plastic workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and completing one grinding/buffing/honing job on a single workpiece. A competent machinist takes ~15–25 min including setup, operation, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force control, fine tactile feedback, and adaptive tool manipulation against varying surfaces. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force sensing, and process knowledge to reliably set up and operate grinding tools autonomously at acceptable quality.

task: Dig or direct digging of post holes and set poles to support structures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = digging one post hole and setting one pole. A competent worker using a manual or power auger takes ~30–60 min including alignment, tamping, and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy auger/digging equipment, handling large poles, precise vertical alignment, and outdoor uneven terrain. Current humanoids lack the strength, tool-operation ability, and outdoor generalization for this task.

task: Perform or assist with cleaning duties as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a standard cleaning duty (e.g., sweeping/mopping a room, wiping surfaces). A competent worker typically takes ~30 min per area.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle sweeping, mopping, and wiping flat surfaces reasonably well, but "as necessary" implies variable, unstructured tasks (spills, clutter, tight spaces) that challenge generalization, dexterity, and task recognition.

task: Add specified amounts of chemicals to equipment at required times to maintain solution levels and concentrations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single chemical addition event: reading levels, measuring dose, and adding to equipment. Typical for a process or lab technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading gauges/sensors, measuring precise liquid volumes, handling chemical containers, and pouring without spill — fine dexterity + fluid handling + process-awareness are all weak spots for 2026 humanoids.

task: Operate or tend machines to wash and remove impurities from items such as barrels or kegs, glass products, tin plate surfaces, dried fruit, pulp, animal stock, coal, manufactured articles, plastic, or rubber.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a batch into an industrial washing machine, setting parameters, running the cycle, and unloading. Typical hands-on time ~10–20 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires loading/unloading heavy, varied industrial items (barrels, kegs, glass), operating machine controls, and handling diverse materials. Current humanoids lack robust heavy-load manipulation, generalization across item types, and reliable industrial machine interfacing.

task: Dress children and change diapers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dressing one child AND changing one diaper. A competent caregiver typically takes ~5 min per diaper change and ~5 min to dress a young child.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme dexterity on a moving, unpredictable human subject; soft/deformable clothing and diapers are very hard to manipulate; safety constraints with children are critical. Far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Adjust, clean, and lubricate mechanical parts of machines, using hand tools and grease guns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing a single machine or maintenance point; a competent mechanic spends ~20–40 min adjusting, cleaning, and lubricating parts with hand tools and a grease gun.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying fittings, selecting correct tools, applying precise torque, and operating a grease gun — demanding dexterity, tool-use generalization, and contextual judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable unstructured tool-switching and force-feedback finesse for mechanical maintenance.

task: Observe machine operations to detect any problems, making necessary adjustments to correct problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single machine observation-and-adjustment cycle. A competent operator typically spends ~15–25 minutes monitoring, diagnosing, and correcting a machine issue.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can monitor sensors and detect anomalies, but diagnosing ambiguous machine faults, deciding the correct adjustment, and physically manipulating varied controls/valves in unstructured environments remains very difficult for current humanoids.

task: Sandblast exposed areas of glass to cut designs in surfaces, using spray guns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sandblasting a single design onto one glass surface. A competent worker takes ~30–60 min including masking, spraying, and checking the cut depth/pattern.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, consistent spray-gun manipulation, artistic/design judgment, fine pressure control, and interpreting stencil coverage on glass. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, closed-loop quality feedback, and tool-use generalization needed.

task: Slide spacers between buffs on spindles to set spacing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = spacing one spindle. A competent worker slides spacers between buffs on a single spindle in roughly 1–3 minutes depending on buff count.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise axial alignment and sliding thin spacers onto a narrow spindle between tightly-spaced soft buffs. Fine dexterity, force feedback, and compliant manipulation of deformable parts challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Preheat workpieces, using heating furnaces or hand torches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preheating one workpiece. Worker applies furnace or hand torch until target preheat temperature is reached; ~5–15 min depending on material thickness.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating an open-flame hand torch or loading furnaces safely, reading surface temperature, and controlling heat application precisely. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use with live-flame torches, thermal sensing integration, and safe autonomous fire handling.

task: Clean and polish engraved areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and polishing engraved areas on a single item (e.g., trophy, jewelry, plaque). A skilled worker takes ~15 min to carefully clean recesses and buff to a finish.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to clean and polish intricate engraved recesses without damaging the piece. Tactile feedback, precise force control, and tool manipulation in tight geometry far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Mark defects, such as knotholes, cracks, and splits, for repair.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking defects on a single board/panel. A skilled worker visually scans and marks knotholes, cracks, and splits in ~2–4 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual inspection to detect subtle cracks/splits, precise marking on irregular surfaces, and judgment about defect severity. Current robots lack reliable fine-grained surface defect perception and dexterous marking on uneven wood grain.

task: Mix water and detergents or acids in containers to prepare cleaning solutions, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single batch of cleaning solution (measuring, mixing, labeling). A competent worker completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid measurement, pouring from varied containers, following specifications, and safe chemical handling. Current humanoids lack reliable fine fluid-control, accurate volumetric dispensing, and label-reading/specification compliance at acceptable quality.

task: Plan pipe system layout, installation, or repair, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = planning layout/installation/repair for a single pipe system job (e.g., one room or section). A competent plumber typically spends 60–120 min reviewing specs, measuring, and drafting a plan.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading blueprints, interpreting specs, spatial reasoning, code knowledge, and engineering judgment. Current humanoid robots lack high-level cognitive planning, schematic interpretation, and domain expertise for autonomous pipe system design.

task: Work with or remove hazardous material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = identifying, handling, and/or removing a hazardous material from a single site/container. Includes donning PPE, assessment, remediation, and documentation; ~90 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized PPE donning, fine dexterous handling of unknown hazardous substances, regulatory compliance, real-time judgment in unstructured environments, and safe containment/disposal — all far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capability.

task: Mount and position tools in machine chucks, spindles, or other tool holding devices, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting/positioning a single tool in a chuck or spindle. A skilled machinist typically takes 3–7 minutes including selection, alignment, and tightening.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-dexterous manipulation (torquing chuck keys, aligning tool concentrically), force-feedback sensitivity, and tool recognition in varied contexts. Current humanoids lack the precision and specialized tool-use needed for consistent success.

task: Examine engraving for quality of cut, burrs, rough spots, and irregular or incomplete engraving.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one engraved workpiece. A skilled worker visually and tactilely checks cut quality, burrs, and completeness in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual perception of micro-scale surface defects and tactile sensing for burrs/rough spots. Current robots lack reliable high-resolution surface inspection, fine fingertip tactile feedback, and the nuanced judgment to assess engraving quality acceptably.

task: Set and adjust machine controls according to product specifications, using knowledge of machine operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting/adjusting controls for a single production run or product changeover. A skilled operator typically takes 5–15 minutes to read specs, locate controls, and dial in settings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading spec sheets, interpreting machine-specific control layouts, turning dials/knobs precisely, and verifying output quality. Current humanoids lack the cognitive-physical integration for unstructured machine interfaces and spec interpretation.

task: Modify, clean, or maintain pipe systems, units, fittings, or related machines or equipment, using hand or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single repair/maintenance job on a pipe section or fitting; a competent plumber/pipefitter typically spends ~1 hour per discrete task (e.g., replacing a fitting, clearing a joint).
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-torque tool use (wrenches, pipe cutters), navigating tight confined spaces, interpreting pipe layouts, and fine force-controlled manipulation. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use dexterity and situational adaptability for plumbing tasks.

task: Adjust depths and sizes of cuts by adjusting heights of worktables, or by adjusting machine-arm gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single worktable or machine-arm gauge to a specified cut setting; a skilled operator reads and dials in the setting in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor control of cranks, knobs, or levers on industrial machinery, reading analog/digital gauges, and verifying calibration. Current humanoids lack reliable precision manipulation and machine-specific tool-use in unstructured shop environments.

task: Travel with employers to vacation homes to provide meal preparation at those locations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a full meal at a vacation home. Includes travel setup, kitchen orientation, cooking, and serving. ~2 hours per meal session is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires autonomous travel to remote/unfamiliar locations, adapting to unknown kitchens, complex cooking, and social interaction. Current humanoids lack mobility autonomy across travel legs, generalization in novel kitchens, and reliable dexterous cooking skills.

task: Start machines and lower cutting tools to beginning points on patterns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting a cutting machine and lowering the tool to the pattern start point for one workpiece setup. A skilled operator does this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool positioning to a specific pattern point, operating industrial machine controls, and interpreting pattern layouts. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, domain-specific perception, and machine interface familiarity needed reliably.

task: Clean building floors by sweeping, mopping, scrubbing, or vacuuming.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning the floors of a typical room or zone (~500–1000 sq ft). A competent janitor sweeping, mopping, or vacuuming takes ~30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep/mop flat indoor floors reasonably well (Roomba-style and humanoids), but navigating furniture, avoiding obstacles, switching tools, and handling varied floor types/clutter reduces reliability. Scrubbing and corners remain challenging.

task: Steam-clean or shampoo carpets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one average room (~150 sq ft). Includes setup, machine passes, and light post-cleaning steps for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a bulky, specialized steam/shampoo machine, managing water tanks, navigating cord/hose routing, and applying consistent pressure/overlap patterns. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use with large wheeled appliances and fluid management.

task: Clean windows, glass partitions, or mirrors, using soapy water or other cleaners, sponges, or squeegees.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one standard window or glass panel (interior). A competent cleaner sprays, wipes, and squeegees a typical window in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to surfaces and perform wiping motions, but consistent squeegee pressure, streak-free results, reaching edges/corners, handling varied glass sizes, and managing wet tools/buckets remain significant challenges for current humanoids.

task: Drain, transfer, or remove molten metal from furnaces, and place it into molds, using hoists, pumps, or ladles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pour cycle (drain/transfer molten metal from furnace into molds). A skilled foundry worker typically takes ~15–25 minutes including setup, positioning, and pouring.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy industrial hoists, pumps, or ladles with extreme precision in high-heat environments with molten metal. Current humanoid robots lack heat tolerance, the strength/control for ladle operation, and ability to operate furnace-specific heavy equipment autonomously.

task: Repair or replace machine parts, using hand tools, or notify engineering personnel when corrective action is required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and completing a single part repair/replacement on a machine. A competent mechanic/technician typically takes 30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults, selecting correct tools, applying precise torque, and judgment to escalate — far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and situational reasoning in unstructured machine environments.

task: Attach pipes to walls, structures, or fixtures, such as radiators or tanks, using brackets, clamps, tools, or welding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = securing one pipe run to a wall/fixture with brackets, clamps, or welding. A competent pipefitter typically takes 15–35 min depending on method and access.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force application, drilling into walls, handling varied brackets/clamps, and potentially welding — all in cluttered, unstructured jobsite environments. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-use generalization, and positional accuracy for reliable autonomous pipe attachment.

task: Perform minor plumbing, welding, or concrete mixing work.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one minor job (e.g., fixing a pipe joint, a small weld bead, or mixing a batch of concrete). A competent tradesperson typically takes 30–60 min per minor task.
