robot_success_rationale: Each sub-task demands specialized tools (torches, pipe wrenches, mixers), fine dexterity, heat/fluid management, and strong situational judgment. Current humanoids lack the tool-use repertoire, force control, and safety handling required for any of these trades reliably.

task: Install automatic controls to regulate pipe systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing automatic controls (sensors, actuators, valves, wiring) on a single pipe system segment. A competent pipefitter/plumber typically takes 1–3 hours depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires threading pipes, torquing fittings, routing/connecting electrical wiring, and configuring control logic in cramped, variable environments. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and multi-domain (plumbing + electrical + controls) generalization capabilities.

task: Install pipe assemblies, fittings, valves, appliances such as dishwashers or water heaters, or fixtures such as sinks or toilets, using hand or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single fixture or appliance (e.g., a toilet, sink, or water heater). A competent plumber typically spends 60–120 minutes per unit including fitting, sealing, and testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high dexterity (threading pipes, tightening fittings to torque spec, applying sealant), under-sink/confined-space mobility, leak-free assembly, and tool switching. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and fine manipulation capability.

task: Activate machine start-up switches to grind, lap, hone, debar, shear, or cut workpieces, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting up a grinding/cutting machine for a single workpiece run: locate switches, verify settings, activate in sequence. Typically takes a few minutes per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can press buttons but struggle with identifying correct switches on varied industrial panels, reading specifications, and verifying machine state. Industrial panel diversity, fine motor precision on toggles, and safety-critical confirmation steps limit success significantly.

task: Lay out full scale drawings of pipe systems, supports, or related equipment, according to blueprints.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out a single pipe system or support assembly from blueprints. A competent pipefitter/drafter typically takes 1–3 hours depending on complexity; 2 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting engineering blueprints, translating abstract dimensions to full-scale physical marks on floors/surfaces, using chalk lines, tape measures, and scribing tools with high precision. Current humanoids lack blueprint comprehension, fine spatial layout accuracy, and the specialized tool fluency this demands.

task: Remove completed workpieces and place them in trays.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one completed workpiece from a machine/station and placing it in a tray. A competent worker does this in ~1 minute including inspection and placement.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of known objects in semi-structured industrial settings is a core robot strength (~70–90%). Slight reduction for variability in workpiece orientation, tray layout, and need to confirm "completed" status via perception.

task: Service, clean, or supply restrooms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing one restroom (restock supplies, wipe surfaces, clean fixtures, mop floor, empty trash). A competent janitor averages ~15 minutes per restroom.
robot_success_rationale: Requires combining locomotion, wiping curved/irregular surfaces (toilets, sinks), restocking dispensers (fine manipulation of paper/soap rolls), and bin emptying in a cluttered space. Each sub-task is partially feasible but chaining all reliably in an unstructured restroom remains beyond current humanoids.

task: Transfer image to workpiece, using contact printer, pantograph stylus, silkscreen printing device, or stamp pad.
human_time_rationale: One instance = transferring one image to one workpiece. Setup, alignment, and printing a single piece takes a skilled worker ~5 minutes depending on method.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of image to workpiece, controlled pressure application, and tool-specific handling (stylus, silkscreen, stamp). Current humanoids lack the fine positional accuracy, compliant force control, and tool-switching generalization needed for acceptable print quality.

task: Thread and hand-feed materials through machine cutters or abraders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = threading and hand-feeding one material batch through a cutter/abrader; a skilled operator completes this in roughly 2–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual dexterity to thread flexible or rigid materials through tight, moving machine pathways, maintain correct tension, and guide feed rate safely. Current robots lack the fine touch, compliance, and situational judgment needed near active cutting blades.

task: Inspect, examine, or test installed systems or pipe lines, using pressure gauge, hydrostatic testing, observation, or other methods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single pipeline segment or installed system. A competent technician spends ~30–60 min connecting gauges, pressurizing, observing, and documenting results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting pressure gauges/hoses to varied fittings (high dexterity), operating valves, interpreting analog/digital gauges, and diagnosing leaks. Tool-use generalization and fine manipulation remain well below task demands for current humanoids.

task: Set up, arrange, or remove decorations, tables, chairs, ladders, or scaffolding to prepare facilities for events, such as banquets or meetings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one event space (banquet/meeting room). A competent worker spends ~60–120 min arranging tables, chairs, decor, and equipment for a typical event.
robot_success_rationale: Requires moving heavy/varied furniture, precise spatial arrangement per a layout plan, handling fragile decor, climbing ladders/scaffolding, and generalizing across unstructured environments — well beyond current humanoid capabilities at acceptable quality.

task: Prepare etching chemicals according to formulas, diluting acid with water to obtain solutions of specified concentration.
human_time_rationale: One batch preparation: measuring volumes, safely diluting acid into water, checking concentration. Typical lab technician time ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid handling, reading graduated vessels, safely managing corrosive acids, and verifying concentration (e.g., titration or hydrometer). Current humanoids lack reliable fine liquid-pouring control, chemical safety protocols, and sensor integration for concentration verification.

task: Prepare workpieces for etching or engraving by cutting, sanding, cleaning, polishing, or treating them with wax, acid resist, lime, etching powder, or light-sensitive enamel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one workpiece (cutting, sanding, cleaning, coating). A skilled artisan typically spends 20–30 min per piece depending on material and treatment steps.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for sanding and coating, precise force control, chemical handling (acid resist, wax, enamel), and multi-step sequencing. Current humanoids lack the precision and tool-use generalization for craft-quality surface prep.

task: Construct forms or chutes for pouring concrete.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing a single standard form/chute setup (e.g., a wall or footing form). A competent carpenter typically takes 1.5–2.5 hours including measuring, cutting, fastening panels, bracing, and aligning.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lumber/panel handling, precise measurement, nailing/screwing braces, outdoor uneven terrain, and structural judgment. Current humanoids lack the strength, tool-use fluency, and construction-site generalization needed for this complex multi-step task.

task: Dust and polish furniture and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dusting and polishing a single room's furniture/equipment. A competent cleaner typically takes 10–20 minutes per room.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate and wipe flat surfaces, but dusting irregular shapes, reaching tight spots, applying polish evenly, and handling varied objects/materials requires dexterity and generalization still challenging for 2026 humanoids.

task: Lift and position workpieces, manually or with hoists, and secure them in hoppers or on machine tables, faceplates, or chucks, using clamps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = securing one workpiece to a machine table/chuck. Skilled machinists typically spend 5–12 min per piece depending on size, weight, and fixturing complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled placement, hoist operation, and tightening clamps/chuck jaws to spec. Current humanoids lack reliable heavy-load manipulation, hoist interfacing, and fine torque-regulated fastening in unstructured industrial setups.

task: Move heavy furniture, equipment, or supplies, either manually or with hand trucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving a single heavy item (e.g., desk, appliance, pallet) from one location to another within a facility, including positioning and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Heavy payloads exceed humanoid lift limits (~20kg); gripping bulky irregular furniture is hard; navigating with hand trucks requires tool-use generalization; cluttered/uneven paths add failure risk. Occasional light items feasible but heavy loads largely not.

task: Clean and polish furniture and fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and polishing one piece of furniture or a set of fixtures in a typical room; a competent worker spends ~20 min dusting, wiping, and buffing surfaces.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can wipe flat surfaces but struggle with varied furniture geometry, applying consistent polish pressure, reaching corners/joints, judging finish quality, and switching tools (cloth, spray, buffer) across diverse fixture types.

task: Brush or spray lubricating compounds on workpieces, or turn valve handles and direct flow of coolant against tools and workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying lubricant/coolant to one workpiece or tool. A competent machinist takes ~1–3 minutes: selecting compound, brushing/spraying, and adjusting valve/nozzle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping brushes/spray tools, precise compound application, and turning valve handles in an industrial environment. Valve manipulation (~40–60%), but coordination of flow direction, coverage judgment, and tool switching in unstructured shop conditions lowers overall success significantly.

task: Inspect etched work for depth of etching, uniformity, and defects, using calibrated microscopes, gauges, fingers, or magnifying lenses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one etched workpiece. A skilled inspector examines depth, uniformity, and defects using multiple tools (microscope, gauges, touch); typically 5–10 minutes per part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine tactile sensing (fingers for feel), precise tool-switching (microscope, gauges, lenses), and nuanced defect judgment. Current robots lack calibrated fingertip tactile discrimination and reliable multi-instrument sequencing for quality inspection at acceptable accuracy.

task: Observe air and temperature gauges or metal color and fluidity, and turn fuel valves or adjust controls to maintain required temperatures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single check-and-adjust cycle: reading gauges/metal color, then turning valves or adjusting controls to correct temperature. Typical cycle takes a few minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal perception (gauges, glowing metal color/fluidity), nuanced dexterous valve/control manipulation in hot industrial environments, and closed-loop judgment. Current robots lack reliable pyrometric vision and fine control in unstructured furnace settings.

task: Adjust air cylinders and setting stops to set traverse lengths and feed arm strokes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting air cylinders and setting stops on a single machine setup. A skilled technician typically spends 15–25 min calibrating, testing, and confirming correct stroke lengths.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine manipulation of adjustment screws, lock nuts, and pneumatic fittings; reading gauges; iterative test-and-verify cycles. Current humanoids lack the precision, tactile feedback, and domain-specific tool-use needed for reliable autonomous calibration.

task: Dust furniture, walls, machines, or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dusting all surfaces in a typical room or workstation. A competent cleaner covers furniture, walls, and equipment in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can reach flat surfaces and wipe them, but variable object layouts, wall heights, tight spaces around machines, and avoiding fragile items limit reliable autonomous success. Perception and arm reach generalization remain challenging.

task: Remove wax or tape from etched glassware by using a stylus or knife, or by immersing ware in hot water.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing wax/tape from a single piece of etched glassware. A skilled worker scrapes or soaks one item in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Delicate scraping on fragile curved glass demands fine dexterity and force control beyond current humanoid capability. Hot-water immersion is more feasible but handling fragile glassware safely and precisely remains unreliable.

task: Shut off steam, water, or other gases or liquids from pipe sections, using valve keys or wrenches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating and shutting off one valve. A competent worker locates the valve, applies a wrench or key, and confirms closure in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Valve types vary widely (gate, ball, wheel, buried curb stops). Robots struggle with high-torque wrench use, irregular valve keys, awkward access locations, and confirming successful shutoff. Perception and force control limit success.

task: Insert cutting tools or bits into machines and secure them with wrenches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inserting and securing one cutting tool/bit into a machine. A skilled machinist or operator typically takes 2–4 minutes including selecting the tool, seating it, and torquing with a wrench.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of bit into chuck/collet, controlled torque application with a wrench, and fine grip force. Current humanoids lack the dexterity and torque feedback needed for reliable, supervisor-acceptable tool changes on varied machines.

task: Set reduction scales to attain specified sizes of reproduction on workpieces, and set pantograph controls for required heights, depths, and widths of cuts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = configuring reduction scales and pantograph controls for a single workpiece. A skilled engraver/pantograph operator reads job specs and adjusts mechanical dials and linkages in ~5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise identification and manipulation of specialized pantograph mechanical linkages, ratio scales, and fine adjustment knobs. No current humanoid robot has demonstrated tool-specific setup of engraving/pantograph machinery autonomously.

task: Carry linens, towels, toilet items, and cleaning supplies, using wheeled carts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading and pushing a wheeled cart of linens/supplies from storage to a destination (e.g., one hotel floor). Typical for housekeeping staff.
robot_success_rationale: Navigating corridors with a wheeled cart is plausible, but loading varied soft/bulky items, maneuvering an external cart through tight spaces, and handling elevators/doors reliably is still difficult for current humanoids.

task: Weigh materials to be charged into furnaces, using scales.
human_time_rationale: One instance = weighing a single batch/charge of materials. A worker scoops/moves materials onto a scale, reads the weight, and logs it — typically 3–7 minutes per charge.
robot_success_rationale: Requires scooping/handling bulk or irregular materials, precise weight reading, and logging — challenging for generalist humanoids. Perception of scale readouts and dexterous scooping of varied industrial materials are difficult. No task-specific fixture assumed.

task: Strip, seal, finish, and polish floors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full strip/seal/finish/polish cycle on a typical room (~500 sq ft). A skilled floor technician takes ~2 hours including machine operation and coat application.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a heavy floor buffer/auto-scrubber, applying chemicals in sequence, managing wet coats and drying times, and navigating around furniture. Humanoids lack the specialized tool integration, force control, and task sequencing needed for this multi-step process.

task: Remove impurities from the surface of molten metal, using strainers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = skimming impurities from a single batch/ladle of molten metal. A competent worker typically spends ~5 minutes per skim cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating near extreme heat (~1000°C+), precise tool-use (strainer/skimmer) over a fluid surface, and real-time visual perception of molten metal impurities — far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity and thermal/sensor tolerance.

task: Make adjustments or minor repairs to heating, cooling, ventilating, plumbing, or electrical systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single minor repair/adjustment (e.g., tightening a fitting, replacing a filter, resetting a breaker). Competent technician typically takes 30–60 min including diagnosis.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults across diverse systems, fine dexterous tool use (wrenches, screwdrivers, multimeters), navigating tight mechanical spaces, and safe handling of live electrical/plumbing — far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Clean chimneys, flues, and connecting pipes, using power or hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single chimney/flue system. A competent chimney sweep typically takes 45–90 minutes including setup, brushing flues, and cleanup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating ladders/rooftops, inserting/rotating brushes through narrow vertical flues, adapting to varied chimney geometries, and using specialized tools. Far beyond current humanoid mobility, dexterity, and confined-space perception capabilities.

task: Draw smelted metal samples from furnaces or kettles for analysis, and calculate types and amounts of materials needed to ensure that materials meet specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drawing a single sample from a furnace/kettle, then performing associated calculations to verify spec compliance; a competent metallurgist completes this in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating near extreme-heat industrial furnaces, handling specialized ladles/sampling tools, tolerating molten-metal hazards, and performing analytical calculations — far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity, thermal tolerance, and cognitive tool-use in unstructured heavy-industry environments.

task: Remove snow from sidewalks, driveways, or parking areas, using snowplows, snow blowers, or snow shovels, or spread snow-melting chemicals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing a typical residential driveway/sidewalk or a small parking area section. A competent worker with a snow blower or shovel takes ~20–40 min on average.
robot_success_rationale: Snowplows/blowers are large vehicles/equipment a humanoid cannot operate. Shoveling unstructured snow outdoors on varied terrain is very challenging. Spreading chemicals is simpler but whole task requires outdoor mobility, tool use, and vehicle ops—mostly out of reach in 2026.

task: Spray insecticides or fumigants to prevent insect or rodent infestation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating a typical residential or commercial space. Includes mixing/loading chemicals, inspecting the area, and spraying all target zones — ~45 min for an average job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling specialized spray equipment, navigating varied/cluttered environments, identifying target zones with pest-expert judgment, and managing hazardous chemicals safely. Current humanoids lack the perception, tool integration, and domain reasoning for autonomous pest control.

task: Maintain or repair plumbing by replacing defective washers, replacing or mending broken pipes, or opening clogged drains.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one plumbing repair job (e.g., replacing a washer, fixing a pipe, or clearing a drain). A competent plumber typically completes a standard repair in 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Plumbing requires high-force torque on fittings, fine dexterity with small washers/seals, working in confined/awkward spaces under sinks, diagnosing leaks, and tool-switching — all well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Follow procedures for the use of chemical cleaners and power equipment to prevent damage to floors and fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one floor-cleaning cycle in a typical room: reading labels, mixing/diluting chemical cleaner, applying solution, operating power equipment (e.g., floor scrubber or buffer), and inspecting for damage.
robot_success_rationale: Robot must identify correct chemicals, safely dilute/apply them, and operate specialized power floor equipment. Chemical handling, label reading, procedure compliance, and operating ride-on/upright power machines are well beyond current humanoid generalization.

task: Locate and mark the position of pipe installations, connections, passage holes, or fixtures in structures, using measuring instruments such as rulers or levels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating and marking a single pipe installation point or fixture. A skilled tradesperson measures, marks, and verifies one location in ~10–20 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement with rulers/levels, interpreting blueprints, marking exact spots on walls/floors, and navigating cluttered construction sites. Current humanoids lack the fine-tool-use precision, blueprint reasoning, and construction-environment generalization needed.

task: Measure and visually inspect products for surface and dimension defects to ensure conformance to specifications, using precision measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single part/product. A competent inspector typically spends ~5–10 minutes measuring dimensions and visually checking surfaces with calipers, micrometers, or gauges.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can move and hold instruments, but reliably wielding precision tools (calipers, micrometers) to spec-level accuracy, repositioning parts, and detecting subtle surface defects visually in unstructured settings remains very challenging for current humanoids.

task: Sweep, scrub, wax, or polish floors, using brooms, mops, or powered scrubbing and waxing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a typical mid-sized room/hallway (~500 sq ft). A competent janitor sweeps, mops, and/or waxes that area in ~25–35 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Flat floor sweeping/mopping is within reach, but waxing/scrubbing with powered machines, navigating furniture, changing tools, and achieving supervisor-acceptable quality across varied surfaces stretches current humanoid dexterity and generalization.

task: Repair or remove and replace system components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing or replacing a single system component (e.g., a circuit board, valve, or mechanical part). Median across component types and repair complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults, using hand tools with precision, handling diverse fasteners/connectors, and adapting to varied system configurations. Current humanoids lack reliable fault diagnosis, fine tool-use, and generalization across component types.

task: Expose workpieces to acid to develop etch patterns such as designs, lettering, or figures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and acid-exposing a single workpiece. Includes applying resist, immersing in acid bath, monitoring etch depth, and rinsing. Typically 30–60 minutes depending on design complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous resist application, precise timing/monitoring of chemical reactions, safe handling of corrosive acids, and nuanced visual inspection of etch depth — well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured settings.

task: Sketch, trace, or scribe layout lines and designs on workpieces, plates, dies, or rollers, using compasses, scribers, gravers, or pencils.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out lines/designs on a single workpiece. A skilled tradesperson typically spends 10–30 min measuring, marking, and scribing layout geometry on one part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter precision tool control (scribers, compasses, gravers), interpreting design intent, and applying calibrated pressure on varied surfaces. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force control, and planning needed for accurate freehand layout work.

task: Connect accessories such as floats, weights, flags, lights, or markers to nets, lines, or traps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rigging a set of accessories onto a single net, line, or trap. A competent fisher typically takes 10–20 minutes depending on gear complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to tie knots, clip, or lash accessories to irregular rope/netting in unstructured marine/outdoor environments. Wet, tangled, flexible materials and varied connector types are well beyond current robot generalization.

task: Inspect work sites for obstructions or holes that could cause structural weakness.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single work site (e.g., a floor/structure area). A competent inspector walks the area, visually checks for holes, obstructions, and structural issues; ~45 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating uneven, cluttered construction terrain (poor robot mobility), detecting subtle structural anomalies (limited perception generalization), and making expert safety judgments. Current humanoids lack domain-specific structural assessment capability.

task: Empty wastebaskets, empty and clean ashtrays, and transport other trash and waste to disposal areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one room or area visit: collect wastebasket, empty/clean ashtray, bag loose trash, carry to disposal. Typical janitorial unit of work.
robot_success_rationale: Simple pick-and-carry is feasible, but varied bin sizes/shapes, lifting liner bags, scrubbing ashtrays, navigating to disposal areas, and handling messy/wet waste challenge current dexterity and generalization.

task: Neutralize workpieces to remove acid, wax, or enamel, using water, solvents, brushes, or specialized machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one workpiece or small batch. Worker applies solvent/water, scrubs with brush, or loads a machine; typically 5–15 min depending on size and contamination.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping irregularly shaped workpieces, applying brushes/solvents with appropriate force, and judging cleanliness. Dexterity and force-control on varied part geometries challenge current humanoids; machine-loading step is more feasible (~50%), but full autonomous brushing/rinsing is not.

task: Install underground storm, sanitary, or water piping systems, extending piping as needed to connect fixtures and plumbing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single underground piping run (e.g., one residential lateral or section). A competent plumber/pipefitter typically takes 3–5 hours including trenching coordination, pipe laying, joining, and connection to fixtures.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating excavation machinery, working in unstable trenches, heavy pipe handling, precise grading, solvent/mechanical joining, and fixture connection — far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity, heavy-equipment operation, and unstructured outdoor capability.

task: Replenish supplies, such as drinking glasses, linens, writing supplies, and bathroom items.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restocking a single guest room or service area with all listed supplies; a competent hotel/hospitality worker typically takes 10–20 minutes per room.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied room layouts, identifying and placing many heterogeneous items (glasses, linens, toiletries, pens), opening cabinets/drawers, and folding/arranging soft goods — beyond reliable 2026 frontier robot generalization and dexterity.

task: Cut plies at splice points, and press ends together to form continuous bands.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting and splicing one band. A skilled worker trims plies and presses ends together in roughly 5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise cutting of flexible/layered ply material and firm, aligned pressing of ends — demanding fine dexterity, force control, and deformable-material handling well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Inspect structures to assess material or equipment needs, to establish the sequence of pipe installations, or to plan installation around obstructions, such as electrical wiring.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single structure or work zone (e.g., a room or floor) to plan pipe routing. A competent plumber typically spends 30–60 minutes assessing layout, obstructions, and materials.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level spatial reasoning, reading blueprints, identifying wiring/obstructions, and planning multi-step pipe sequences. Current robots lack trade knowledge, semantic understanding of building systems, and reliable 3D planning in cluttered, unstructured construction environments.

task: Keep storage areas and carts well-stocked, clean, and tidy.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restocking and tidying one storage area or cart. A competent worker checks inventory, reorganizes, wipes down, and restocks in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigation, object recognition across varied items, precise placement on shelves/carts, and inventory judgment. Current robots struggle with diverse unstructured restocking, varied item types, and cleanliness assessment. Basic tidying is partially feasible but full task reliability is low.

task: Print proofs or examine designs to verify accuracy of engraving, and rework engraving as required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reviewing a single engraved proof and reworking it; a skilled engraver typically spends ~15–25 minutes per piece on inspection and correction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual comparison of intricate engraved detail, aesthetic judgment, and extremely precise dexterous rework (sub-millimeter tool control on hard surfaces). Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and perception standards.

task: Position and secure workpieces on machines, and start feeding mechanisms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning and securing a single workpiece on a machine and starting the feed. A competent machinist typically takes 3–7 minutes per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment, clamping force control, and interacting with varied machine controls. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity for fixture tightening and generalization across machine types; perception of exact workpiece registration is also challenging.

task: Remove finished or cured products from dies or molds, using hand tools, air hoses, and other equipment, stamping identifying information on products when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a single product from its die/mold, including optional stamping. A competent worker cycles through this in ~5 min depending on part complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied tool use (air hoses, hand tools), force-controlled extraction of stuck parts, and fine stamping—challenging for 2026 humanoids due to variable part geometry, grip precision, and multi-tool coordination.

task: Clean rugs, carpets, upholstered furniture, and draperies, using vacuum cleaners and shampooers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one average room's carpet/rugs plus one upholstered piece. Vacuuming ~15 min, shampooing ~30 min for a competent cleaner.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can vacuum flat floors but shampooing carpets, maneuvering around furniture, handling draperies, and operating upholstery attachments require complex tool-use, dexterous manipulation, and unstructured environment navigation beyond current frontier capability.

task: Kindle fires, and shovel fuel and other materials into furnaces or onto conveyors by hand, with hoists, or by directing crane operators.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single fire-kindling or shoveling cycle (loading a furnace/conveyor). A competent worker typically completes one load/cycle in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy shoveling of bulk/loose materials, directing crane operators (communication), and kindling fires in industrial environments. Current humanoids lack the force output, tool interoperability, and unstructured industrial environment generalization needed. Operating hoists adds further complexity.

task: Depress pedals to rotate drums, and wind specified numbers of plies around drums to form tire bodies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = building one tire body on a drum. A skilled tire builder takes roughly 5–12 minutes per tire, including pedal operation and winding multiple plies.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise coordinated foot pedal actuation, bimanual tension control while winding multiple sticky rubber plies around a rotating drum, and strong generalization across ply counts/widths. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and coordination limits.

task: Turn valves and dials of machines to regulate pressure, temperature, and speed and feed rates, and to set cycle times.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a set of valves/dials on a machine to reach target settings. A skilled operator locates, turns, and verifies each control in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Turning arbitrary valves and dials demands precise grip, torque control, and reading analog gauges to verify outcomes. Generalization across dial types, orientations, and feedback loops remains unreliable for 2026 humanoids.

task: Fill etched characters with opaque paste to improve readability.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling etched characters on a single part/panel. A competent worker applies paste, works it into grooves, and wipes excess in ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor control to fill narrow etched grooves with paste and cleanly wipe excess without smearing. Current robots lack the dexterity, tactile feedback, and visual precision for consistent acceptable quality.

task: Inspect worn tires for faults, cracks, cuts, and nail holes, and to determine if tires are suitable for retreading.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one worn tire. A skilled technician visually and manually checks tread, sidewalls, inner liner, and bead in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual and tactile inspection of irregular curved surfaces, detecting subtle cracks, nail holes, and internal damage. Current robots lack reliable fine-surface tactile sensing, expert judgment for retreadability, and consistent manipulation of heavy tire geometry.

task: Sprinkle chemicals over molten metal to bring impurities to the surface.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sprinkling flux/chemicals over one batch of molten metal in a furnace or ladle; a skilled metallurgical worker completes this in ~5 minutes including positioning and dispersal.
robot_success_rationale: Extreme heat (~1000°C+) damages standard robot hardware; precise chemical dispersal over turbulent molten metal requires high thermal tolerance, accurate perception in harsh conditions, and fine powder-handling dexterity — well beyond 2026 general-purpose robots.

task: Guide stylus over template, causing cutting tool to duplicate design or letters on workpiece.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tracing one design or letter set on a workpiece; competent operator takes ~15 min including template setup, careful stylus guidance, and quality check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, sustained contact force along a 2D template path with a stylus while a coupled cutting tool replicates it. Current humanoids lack the fine force-controlled compliance and path-tracking accuracy needed; high failure risk from slip or over-cut.

task: Set up, operate, or tend metal or plastic molding, casting, or coremaking machines to mold or cast metal or thermoplastic parts or products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running one production batch/job on a molding or casting machine, including setup, parameter adjustment, and initial run verification; ~30 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy specialized industrial machinery, adjusting complex controls, interpreting process feedback, and handling molten/hot materials. Humanoid robots lack the tool interfaces, thermal tolerance, and machine-specific generalization needed.

task: Clean and paint completed tires.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one tire. Competent worker wipes/cleans a finished tire then applies tire paint/dressing, taking ~15 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Cleaning a curved tire surface and applying uniform paint/dressing demands controlled force, precise tool handling, and surface-conforming motion. Current humanoids struggle with compliant tool use on irregular curved objects and consistent coating quality.

task: Position ply stitcher rollers and drums according to width of stock, using hand tools and gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up rollers/drums for one stock width. A skilled worker measures with gauges, loosens/repositions, and re-tightens components in ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise gauge reading, controlled torquing with hand tools, and fine spatial adjustment of mechanical components. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity and calibrated force control for tool-based mechanical setup tasks.

task: Sort clothing and other articles, load washing machines, and iron and fold dried items.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full laundry cycle's worth of clothing: sorting (~10 min), loading washer (~5 min), ironing and folding dried items (~30 min).
robot_success_rationale: End-to-end laundry is extremely challenging: sorting by fabric/color requires semantic understanding, operating washer controls needs precise manipulation, and ironing requires delicate force control with a hot tool. Current robots succeed at sub-tasks in isolation but chaining all three autonomously fails reliably.

task: Operate controls to move or discharge metal workpieces from furnaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving/discharging one workpiece or batch from a furnace. A skilled operator takes ~5 min per cycle including control input, positioning, and safe extraction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial furnace controls, high-heat environment (thermal stress on sensors/actuators), precise manipulation of heavy metal workpieces, and safety-critical decision-making. General-purpose humanoids lack furnace-specific tooling, heat tolerance, and force capacity.

task: Start rollers that bond tread and plies as drums revolve.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting rollers for one drum cycle on a tire-building machine. A skilled operator completes this step in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial tire-building machinery with specific controls and precise sequencing. Current humanoid robots lack training on such equipment, struggle with industrial panel interfaces, and must reliably coordinate roller engagement timing with drum rotation.

task: Clean rooms, hallways, lobbies, lounges, restrooms, corridors, elevators, stairways, locker rooms, and other work areas so that health standards are met.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one distinct area (e.g., a hotel room, restroom, or lobby). A competent housekeeper/janitor typically takes 20–60 min per area depending on size/type; 45 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Current humanoids can sweep/mop flat surfaces and wipe counters at ~40–70%, but multi-zone cleaning across varied layouts (restrooms, elevators, stairways) with health-standard verification, restocking, and obstacle avoidance drops success significantly. Stairways and tight restroom spaces are particularly limiting.

task: Align treads with guides, start drums to wind treads onto plies, and slice ends.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one tire tread application: align tread to guides, wind onto drum, slice end. Skilled tire builder takes ~5–10 min per tire.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise lateral alignment of a flexible, heavy tread strip, coordinating drum start timing, and clean slicing. Deformable material handling, force feedback, and tool-use in an industrial setting exceed current humanoid generalization reliably.

task: Disinfect equipment and supplies, using germicides or steam-operated sterilizers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disinfecting a batch of equipment/supplies. Includes applying germicide or loading/running a sterilizer cycle and unloading, ~15–25 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise chemical handling, operating sterilizer controls, and careful placement of varied items. Sterilizer interfaces, chemical dosing, and quality verification exceed current humanoid dexterity/generalization limits reliably.

task: Cut openings in structures to accommodate pipes or pipe fittings, using hand or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting one opening in a wall/floor/ceiling for a pipe or fitting. A competent plumber/pipefitter typically marks, selects the tool, and cuts in ~20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, marking, and high-force power-tool operation (hole saw, reciprocating saw) in cramped, unstructured construction environments. Current humanoids lack the force control, tool-change dexterity, and spatial reasoning for reliable autonomous cutting in structures.

task: Perform housekeeping duties, such as laundry, cleaning, dish washing, and changing of linens.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full housekeeping round for a typical dwelling/room covering laundry, cleaning, dishwashing, and linen change; a competent housekeeper typically takes ~60 minutes per room/unit.
robot_success_rationale: Task bundles highly varied subtasks: linen changes require dexterous fabric handling, dishwashing involves wet/slippery objects, laundry loading/sorting is semi-reliable but chaining all steps autonomously in an unstructured home environment remains well beyond current robots' reliable autonomous capability.

task: Fit inner tubes and final layers of rubber onto tires.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting an inner tube and final rubber layer onto one tire. A skilled tire builder takes roughly 5–10 minutes per tire on a manual workstation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained high-force manipulation of large, heavy, deformable rubber components with precise alignment. Humanoids lack the grip strength, bimanual force coordination, and tactile feedback needed for consistent, defect-free tire building.

task: Anchor steel supports from ceiling joists to hold pipes in place.
human_time_rationale: One instance = anchoring one steel support to a ceiling joist: positioning, drilling pilot hole, and fastening. A competent tradesperson takes ~10–20 min per hanger.
robot_success_rationale: Requires overhead work against gravity, precise joist location (stud-finder reasoning), power-drill tool use, and securing hardware in a cramped ceiling space. Current humanoids lack reliable overhead dexterous tool use and safe construction site autonomy.

task: Install pipe systems to support alternative energy-fueled systems, such as geothermal heating or cooling systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a complete pipe loop/run for one geothermal system section. A skilled pipefitter typically spends a full day (6–8 hrs) on trenching connections, fitting, and pressure-testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/underground terrain navigation, heavy pipe handling, threading/soldering, trenching tools, and code-compliant pressure testing. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities in unstructured job-site environments.

task: Wash windows, walls, ceilings, and woodwork, waxing and polishing as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one average room's windows, walls, ceiling, and woodwork, including waxing/polishing where needed. A competent cleaner typically takes 30–60 minutes per room.
robot_success_rationale: Task demands overhead reach for ceilings, varied surface materials, tool-switching (squeegee, wax applicator, polisher), and quality judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable overhead manipulation, adaptive force control for polishing, and multi-tool coordination at acceptable quality.

task: Polish silver accessories and metalwork, such as fixtures and fittings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = polishing one silver accessory or fixture. A competent worker applies polish, buffs, and inspects one item in ~15–25 minutes depending on size/complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled buffing, chemical polish application, and quality inspection on irregular metalwork surfaces. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, compliant tool control, and visual inspection capability needed for acceptable polish quality.

task: Scrape accumulations of metal oxides from floors, molds, and crucibles, and sift and store them for reclamation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scraping, sifting, and storing oxide accumulations from a typical set of floors/molds/crucibles; ~45 min for a competent foundry worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires forceful scraping on irregular/hot surfaces, fine sifting of powdery material, tool switching, and precise containment—well beyond current humanoid dexterity and generalization in unstructured industrial environments.

task: Operate motorized pumps to remove water from flooded manholes, basements, or facility floors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pumping out one flooded space. Includes setup, running pump until water is cleared, and breakdown. Typical job takes 30–60 minutes depending on water volume.
robot_success_rationale: Requires deploying heavy motorized pump equipment, connecting hoses, navigating confined/wet/uneven spaces (manholes, basements), monitoring water level, and adjusting operations. Current humanoids lack the strength, tool-use generalization, and wet-environment safety to do this reliably.

task: Observe continuous operation of automatic machines to ensure that products meet specifications and to detect jams or malfunctions, making adjustments as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one hour of continuous monitoring shift-watch over automatic machines; measured in hourly intervals by supervisors on a production floor.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can monitor via sensors/cameras, but detecting subtle jams, diagnosing root causes, and making nuanced mechanical adjustments across varied machine types requires generalization, dexterity, and contextual judgment well beyond current frontier capability.

task: Monitor drilling operations, by checking gauges and listening to equipment to assess drilling conditions and to determine the need to adjust drilling or alter equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring round: checking gauges and listening to equipment across an active drill site, typically done every 30 minutes per industry practice.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading multiple analog/digital gauges, auditory diagnosis of equipment sounds, integrating multimodal sensory data, and making expert operational decisions. Current robots lack domain-specific drilling expertise, reliable auditory fault detection, and ability to operate heavy drill controls autonomously.

task: Wind chafers and breakers onto plies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = winding chafers and breakers onto one tire ply on a building drum. A skilled tire builder completes this step in roughly 2–4 minutes per tire.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tensioned winding of flexible rubber strips onto a rotating drum with exact alignment, overlap, and pressure control. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterous coordination and specialized tool integration needed for this industrial tire-building process.

task: Clean, fuel, service, and perform safety checks on all equipment, and repair and replace parts as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full service cycle on one piece of equipment (clean, fuel, safety check, minor repair). ~60 min is typical for grounds/farm/construction equipment servicing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating fuel nozzles, torquing fasteners, diagnosing faults, replacing varied parts, and navigating around heavy machinery. Current humanoids lack the tool-use generalization, fluid handling, and diagnostic reasoning needed across equipment types.

task: Measure tires to determine mold size requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single tire (OD, ID, width, bead dimensions) with calipers/tape for mold spec. A competent worker takes ~5–10 min per tire.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise metrology (calipers, tape measure) on a curved, heavy rubber object. Robots struggle with tool manipulation for measurement instruments, interpreting curved surfaces accurately, and producing structured spec output reliably.

task: Set up and operate equipment such as hoists, jackhammers, and drills, in order to bore charge holes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and drilling one charge hole. Setup, positioning, and drilling a single blast hole typically takes ~30–60 minutes for a competent blaster/driller.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy specialized equipment (hoists, jackhammers, drills) in rugged, unstructured outdoor/mining environments. Current humanoids lack the strength, stability, tool-interfacing ability, and safety-critical judgment for this work.

task: Fill cuts and holes in tires, using hot rubber.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing one cut/hole in one tire. A skilled tire technician preps the surface, applies hot rubber/vulcanizing compound, and finishes in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to locate damage, apply hot vulcanizing rubber precisely, and buff/finish surfaces. High heat tool handling, force control, and quality inspection far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Spray, smoke, or coat molds with compounds to lubricate or insulate molds, using acetylene torches or sprayers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating one mold surface with a sprayer or torch; a competent foundry/molding worker typically completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torch or sprayer handling near complex mold geometries, coordinated tool-use, heat management, and even coverage assessment. Current humanoids lack reliable fine tool-use, thermal feedback, and safe acetylene torch operation.

task: Repair hydraulic or air pumps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing one pump. A competent technician typically spends 60–120 min diagnosing, disassembling, replacing seals/components, and reassembling/testing a hydraulic or air pump.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert diagnosis, fine dexterous manipulation of seals/valves in confined spaces, torque-critical fastening, and functional testing. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, generalization, and diagnostic reasoning capabilities.

task: Prepare rooms for meetings and arrange decorations, media equipment, and furniture for social or business functions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up one meeting/event room; includes arranging chairs, tables, AV gear, and decorations. A competent worker typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy furniture moving, precise layout planning, connecting AV cables, and aesthetic decoration judgment — far beyond 2026 robots' reliable dexterity, object diversity handling, and contextual reasoning in unstructured environments.

task: Rub cement sticks on drum edges to provide adhesive surfaces for plies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying cement stick to the edges of one drum. A competent worker can coat the rim edges of a single drum in roughly 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires firm, controlled pressure along curved drum edges with a small stick — demanding dexterity and force regulation. Current humanoids struggle with compliant tool-use on curved surfaces and consistent coverage quality.

task: Accompany children to and from school, on outings, and to medical appointments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one round-trip escort (e.g., walk/drive to school and back). Typical trip is ~20–30 min each way including wait time, averaging ~60 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating mixed outdoor/indoor terrain, crossing streets, managing a child's safety and behavior, using vehicles, and interacting with staff. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor navigation, vehicle operation, and child supervision judgment.

task: Weld small pipes or special piping, using specialized techniques, equipment, or materials, such as computer-assisted welding or microchip fabrication.
human_time_rationale: One instance = welding one small pipe joint using specialized technique. A skilled welder takes ~30–60 min for setup, precise positioning, and completing a quality weld on small/specialty pipe.
robot_success_rationale: Micro-scale and specialty welding demands extreme dexterity, fine tool control, precise heat management, and real-time adaptive perception. Current humanoid robots lack the fine motor precision and specialized tool-use required for microchip-scale or computer-assisted pipe welding at acceptable quality.

task: Prepare material to load into furnaces, including cleaning, crushing, or applying chemicals, by using crushing machines, shovels, rakes, or sprayers.
human_time_rationale: One batch of furnace feed material prepared end-to-end, including cleaning, crushing, and/or chemical application. Typical industrial cycle ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy industrial crushing machines, wielding shovels/rakes in unstructured dusty environments, and using chemical sprayers—tools and force levels far beyond current humanoid dexterity and safety limits. Generalization to heavy industrial settings is very poor.

task: Mix and measure compounds, or weigh premixed compounds, and dump them into machine tubs, cavities, or molds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing/measuring one batch of compound and loading it into a tub/cavity/mold. A competent worker typically takes 5–10 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step sequencing: measuring, pouring, mixing, and dumping into varied containers. Liquid/viscous compound handling, precise weighing, and avoiding spills challenge current robot dexterity and perception significantly.

task: Inspect furnaces and equipment to locate defects and wear.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one furnace unit. A skilled technician spends ~30–60 min checking burners, refractory, seals, controls, and wear points.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating around hot, cluttered industrial equipment, visually identifying subtle wear/defects, interpreting anomalies, and accessing confined spaces. Current robots lack the thermal tolerance, fine defect-recognition perception, and unstructured environment generalization needed.

task: Roll hand rollers over rebuilt casings, exerting pressure to ensure adhesion between camelbacks and casings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rolling one tire casing. A skilled tire retreader takes ~5–10 min to thoroughly hand-roll a camelback onto a casing ensuring full adhesion.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained, calibrated pressure across a curved, irregular surface with force feedback. Current humanoids lack the precise compliance control, grip geometry, and domain-specific tool-use needed for consistent adhesion quality acceptance.

task: Fill pipes or plumbing fixtures with water or air and observe pressure gauges to detect and locate leaks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pressurizing a single pipe/fixture section, observing pressure gauges, and recording results. Typical time 15–35 min including setup and teardown.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting pressurization equipment to varied fittings (fine dexterity), reading analog/digital gauges (perception), interpreting pressure drop trends, and locating leaks — multi-step, unstructured, tool-use-heavy; well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Pull plies from supply racks, and align plies with edges of drums.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pulling one ply from a supply rack and aligning it to a drum edge. A skilled worker completes this in roughly 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Handling large, flexible, deformable plies is extremely challenging — thin fabric/rubber sheets shift, stick, and wrinkle. Precise edge alignment on a curved drum requires fine perception and bimanual dexterity well beyond current robot capability.

task: Place tires into molds for new tread.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing one tire into a retreading mold. A skilled worker positions, aligns, and seats the tire in roughly 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Tires are large, heavy, deformable objects requiring precise alignment in a mold. Current humanoids lack the force control, payload capacity, and fine positional accuracy needed for consistent mold seating. Limited generalization to industrial tooling.

task: Trim excess material from parts, using knives, and grind scrap plastic into powder for reuse.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming one part and feeding scrap into grinder. Skilled worker takes ~5–10 min per part depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Knife trimming requires precise, force-controlled dexterity on irregular surfaces; current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation with bladed tools. Operating an industrial grinder adds tool-use and safety complexity well beyond current capabilities.

task: Skim or pour dross, slag, or impurities from molten metal, using ladles, rakes, hoes, spatulas, or spoons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = skimming/pouring dross from a single batch of molten metal. A skilled foundry worker takes ~5–10 minutes to skim, check, and clear impurities from one heat.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme heat tolerance, precise tool manipulation over hazardous molten metal, and nuanced force/visual feedback to distinguish dross from metal. Current humanoids lack heat-rated hardware, fine fluid-surface perception, and safe operation near high-temperature open crucibles.

task: Install green plumbing equipment, such as faucet flow restrictors, dual-flush or pressure-assisted flush toilets, or tankless hot water heaters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one green plumbing fixture (e.g., a tankless water heater or dual-flush toilet). A competent plumber takes ~60–120 min depending on complexity; 90 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires shutting off water supplies, cutting/threading pipes, torquing fittings, leak-testing, and working in tight under-sink or behind-toilet spaces. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and generalization in unstructured plumbing environments.

task: Operate machines to flush earth cuttings or to blow dust from holes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = flushing/blowing out a single drilled hole. A competent operator sets up and runs the machine to clear one hole, typically taking ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized drilling/flushing machinery (air compressors, water pumps, drill rigs) in outdoor/rough terrain environments. Humanoid robots cannot drive or operate such equipment autonomously; unstructured outdoor conditions and heavy machinery controls make this nearly impossible.

task: Deliver television sets, ironing boards, baby cribs, and rollaway beds to guests' rooms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = delivering one bulky item (e.g., TV, crib) to a guest room: retrieving from storage, transporting via cart/elevator, setting up in room.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating elevators, long hotel corridors, and tight guest rooms while maneuvering large/heavy bulky items. Current humanoids lack the payload capacity, reliable multi-floor navigation, and setup dexterity needed end-to-end.

task: Depress pedals to collapse drums after processing is complete.
human_time_rationale: One instance = depressing pedals to collapse one drum. A competent worker steps on the pedal mechanism and confirms collapse — typically under a minute.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise foot/leg pedal actuation on an industrial drum, balancing while applying downward force. Humanoids have limited foot-pedal dexterity, poor force control at foot level, and risk instability during pedal depression.

task: Pour or load metal or sand into melting pots, furnaces, molds, or hoppers, using shovels, ladles, or machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading/pouring a single batch of material into a furnace, mold, or hopper. A skilled foundry worker shovels, ladles, or positions equipment in roughly 5–10 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Extreme heat, molten metal, and heavy irregular loads challenge perception, thermal tolerance, and force control. Operating industrial ladles or pouring machinery requires specialized tool-use and safety compliance far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Accompany aircraft as a member of the flight crew to monitor and handle cargo in flight.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one flight segment. A typical domestic cargo flight averages ~3–4 hours of crew duty time including monitoring and in-flight cargo management.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating within a moving aircraft at altitude, responding to emergencies, and making complex judgments. No humanoid robot can serve as certified flight crew; environment is dynamic, safety-critical, and highly regulated. Near-zero feasibility.

task: Brush or spray solvents onto plies to ensure adhesion, and repeat process as specified, alternating direction of each ply to strengthen tires.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying solvent to all plies for one tire, alternating directions per spec. A skilled tire builder takes ~5–10 minutes per tire for this step.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise solvent application (brush/spray) on flexible, deformable ply material, tracking alternating orientation, and consistent coverage quality. Current humanoids lack the fine wet-coating control, material-handling dexterity for limp plies, and process-monitoring needed for acceptable adhesion quality.

task: Steer ships under the direction of commanders or navigating officers or direct helmsmen to steer, following designated courses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one watch shift (typically 4 hours) steering a ship along a designated course under officer supervision.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot physically operate ship helm controls in real marine environments; requires specialized vehicle operation, situational awareness, real-time course correction, and coordination with officers — far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Install dies onto machines or presses and coat dies with parting agents, according to work order specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a die set onto one machine/press and applying parting agent coating. A skilled worker typically takes 15–35 min depending on die size and machine type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting/precise alignment of large dies, torquing bolts to spec, reading work orders, and applying coating agents — combining heavy manipulation, force control, and tool-use in an unstructured industrial environment well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Preheat tools, dies, plastic materials, or patterns, using blowtorches or other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preheating a single tool, die, or material to required temperature. A competent worker takes ~5–15 min depending on mass and target temp.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling a live blowtorch safely, controlling flame direction/distance, and monitoring temperature—demanding fine dexterity, thermal perception, and safety judgement. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use and heat-sensing for this task.

task: Move and arrange furniture and turn mattresses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rearranging furniture in a typical room plus flipping one mattress; a competent worker takes ~30–60 min including clearing, moving, and repositioning pieces.
robot_success_rationale: Furniture is heavy, bulky, and irregularly shaped; mattresses require sustained two-handed force and spatial coordination. Current humanoids lack the payload capacity, grip strength, and whole-body coordination for such large, unwieldy objects without human help.

task: Perform maintenance work such as cleaning and oiling machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one machine maintenance session (cleaning + oiling). A competent technician typically spends ~45 min covering all access points, wiping parts, and applying lubricant.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to machine, identifying lubrication points, handling tools/oil cans, and cleaning crevices. Poor generalization across machine types, limited fine dexterity for fittings, and unstructured environments lower success significantly.

task: Mow or edge lawns, using power mowers or edgers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mowing/edging a typical residential lawn (~5,000–8,000 sq ft). A competent worker completes this in ~30–60 min including setup.
robot_success_rationale: Robotic lawn mowers exist but are narrow-purpose appliances, not general-purpose humanoids. A humanoid robot cannot operate a walk-behind mower or edger safely on varied outdoor terrain, manage obstacles, and achieve acceptable edge quality autonomously in 2026.

task: Use hand tools, such as shovels, rakes, pruning saws, saws, hedge or brush trimmers, or axes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one focused hand-tool work session (e.g., raking a lawn section, pruning a shrub, or chopping debris). ~30 min is a representative unit of outdoor hand-tool labor.
robot_success_rationale: Wielding long-handled tools (shovels, rakes, axes) outdoors requires high-force, dynamic two-handed manipulation, uneven terrain navigation, and adaptive grip under load — well beyond current humanoid dexterity and force-control limits.

task: Measure, weigh, or verify levels of rock, gravel, or other excavated material to prevent equipment overloads.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking/verifying a single load of excavated material (rock/gravel) using gauges, scales, or visual inspection before equipment dispatch. Typically takes a few minutes per load.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating truck scales, reading onboard load sensors, or physically gauging haul trucks/excavators — all heavy industrial equipment a humanoid cannot operate or interface with autonomously. Perception of levels is possible but acting on readings is not.

task: Smooth and clean inner surfaces of molds, using brushes, scrapers, air hoses, or grinding wheels, and fill imperfections with refractory material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one mold. A competent worker inspects, cleans, scrapes, and fills imperfections in a typical industrial mold in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires simultaneous fine dexterous tool-switching (brushes, scrapers, grinders), confined-space interior work, tactile defect detection, and precise refractory filling. Far beyond reliable autonomous capability of 2026 humanoids.

task: Drive loaded shuttle cars to ramps and move controls to discharge loads into mine cars or onto conveyors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one loaded shuttle car trip from loading point to ramp/discharge point. A typical underground mine cycle takes ~5–10 minutes including driving and operating discharge controls.
robot_success_rationale: Shuttle cars are specialized heavy mine vehicles requiring seated operation in narrow, unstructured underground tunnels. Humanoid robots cannot drive such equipment; no 2026 general-purpose robot has this capability. Extreme environment, confined space, and bespoke controls further reduce probability.

task: Operate, maintain, or repair ship equipment, such as winches, cranes, derricks, or weapons system.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one operation/maintenance/repair session on a single piece of ship equipment (e.g., inspecting and lubricating a winch). Competent sailor takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Ship equipment operation requires specialized controls, heavy-force tool use, unstable maritime environment, and weapons-system safety protocols far beyond current humanoid dexterity, locomotion stability, and generalization capabilities.

task: Repair or replace damaged molds, pipes, belts, chains, or other equipment, using hand tools, hand-powered presses, or jib cranes.
human_time_rationale: One repair/replacement instance (e.g., swapping a belt, section of pipe, or mold component); competent maintenance worker typically takes 30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force torquing, precise alignment, use of varied hand tools, jib crane operation, and fault diagnosis in cluttered industrial environments — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and generalization limits.

task: Install fixtures, appliances, or equipment designed to reduce water or energy consumption.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one fixture/appliance (e.g., low-flow faucet, LED fixture, smart thermostat). A competent tradesperson takes ~60–120 min including prep, installation, and testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise plumbing/electrical connections, torquing fittings, wire stripping, sealing, and leak/function testing across highly varied fixture types and mounting conditions — well beyond current general-purpose robot dexterity and generalization.

task: Connect water hoses to cooling systems of dies, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting hoses to one die's cooling system. A skilled worker selects correct fittings, threads/clamps hoses with hand tools, and checks for leaks in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation — threading hose fittings onto tight ports, applying torque with hand tools, and confirming leak-free seals. Current humanoids lack reliable torque-controlled fine tool-use and connector alignment in cluttered industrial settings.

task: Adjust equipment and workpiece holding fixtures, such as mold frames, tubs, and cutting tables, to ensure proper functioning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single fixture or holding device (e.g., a mold frame or cutting table) to the correct position/setting for a job; a competent worker typically takes 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting setup specs, locating and manipulating varied fasteners/clamps, applying precise force, and verifying alignment. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use, fine torque control, and generalization across fixture types in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Move mine cars into position for loading and unloading, using pinchbars inserted under car wheels to position cars under loading spouts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning one mine car under a loading spout using a pinchbar. A skilled worker levers and aligns the car in roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme force leverage with a pinchbar on heavy rail equipment in a confined, unstructured underground environment. Current humanoids lack the strength, force feedback, stability, and specialized tool-use for this task.

task: Select coolants and lubricants, and start their flow.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting appropriate coolant/lubricant for a machining or industrial operation and initiating flow via valves/controls. A trained operator takes ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct fluid type, locating and operating valves or control panels, and confirming flow. Generalization across varied machine interfaces and fluid selection logic is beyond current robot capability reliably.

task: Build semi-raw rubber treads onto buffed tire casings to prepare tires for vulcanization in recapping or retreading processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one tire casing: buffing inspection, aligning and pressing semi-raw tread strip around the casing, and securing it for vulcanization. Skilled tire retreader typically takes 20–30 minutes per tire.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled wrapping of a heavy, flexible rubber tread around a curved toroidal casing, alignment under tension, and stitching — demanding bimanual dexterity, high grip force, and tactile feedback well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Cool products after processing to prevent distortion.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cooling one batch of processed products (e.g., placing on a rack or in a cooling area). A competent worker takes ~5 minutes to transfer and arrange items.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of warm/finished products onto cooling racks is feasible, but varied shapes, heat sensitivity, and distortion-prevention precision (gentle handling, flat placement) reduce reliability. Perception of deformable or hot items adds challenge.

task: Rake, mulch, and compost leaves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = raking, mulching, and composting leaves in a typical residential yard (~1/4 acre). A competent worker takes ~1 hour total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor locomotion on uneven terrain, sustained forceful tool use (raking), navigating a yard, operating a mulcher/composter, and transporting loose material — far beyond current humanoid generalization and outdoor dexterity.

task: Remove parts, such as dies, from machines after production runs are finished.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a die or tooling set from one machine after a production run; includes loosening fasteners, extracting the part, and staging it safely. Typically 15–30 min for a skilled operator.
robot_success_rationale: Dies are heavy, vary widely in shape/size, require high-torque fastener removal, precise lifting, and situational awareness around machinery. Current humanoids lack the strength, torque tooling, and generalization for industrial die-change tasks.

task: Unload finished products from conveyor belts, pack them in containers, and place containers in warehouses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unloading a batch of finished products from a conveyor, packing into one container, and placing it in a warehouse location; a competent worker does this in ~8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Multi-step task requiring conveyor unloading, varied product handling, packing arrangement, and warehouse navigation/placement. Each step is feasible in isolation but chaining all reliably—especially packing neatly and navigating warehouse aisles—remains a significant challenge for current humanoids.

task: Trim excess rubber and imperfections during retreading processes.
human_time_rationale: Trimming one retread tire of excess rubber/flash; skilled worker uses hand tools, typically 5–10 min per tire.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise cutting force control, sharp-tool handling, and visual detection of subtle rubber imperfections on a curved tire surface. Fine dexterous blade work with safety-critical tolerances is well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Decorate gardens with stones or plants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = decorating one average garden bed with stones or plants; a competent landscaper takes ~1 hour to arrange, place, and adjust materials.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor mobility on uneven terrain, heavy/irregular stone/plant handling, aesthetic judgment, digging, and spatial planning. Current humanoids lack the generalization, outdoor dexterity, and design reasoning needed.

task: Calibrate and align components, using scales, gauges, and other measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = calibrating/aligning a single component or assembly. A competent technician spends ~30–60 min reading instruments, adjusting, and verifying tolerances.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to manipulate precision instruments, interpret analog/digital gauges, and make iterative micro-adjustments. Current humanoids lack sub-millimeter tool control, reliable gauge reading, and closed-loop precision feedback needed for acceptable calibration quality.

task: Provide proper upkeep of sidewalks, driveways, parking lots, fountains, planters, burial sites, or other grounds features.
human_time_rationale: One instance = upkeep of a standard grounds area (e.g., a sidewalk segment, driveway, or planter bed). A competent groundskeeper typically spends ~60 min per area per session.
robot_success_rationale: Task spans highly varied outdoor subtasks (sweeping, weeding, blowing debris, fountain maintenance, burial site care). Outdoor unstructured terrain, tool-switching, and fine horticultural work exceed current humanoid robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Sort, count, and mark clean linens and store them in linen closets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting, counting, marking, and storing a typical batch of clean linens (e.g., one laundry load, ~10–20 items) into a linen closet.
robot_success_rationale: Requires folding soft/deformable linens, reading/applying labels, counting, navigating to closet, and stacking neatly on shelves. Multi-step dexterous manipulation of deformable textiles and fine labeling remain major 2026 robot limitations.

task: Lower and man lifeboats when emergencies occur.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lowering and manning a single lifeboat during an emergency drill or real event; includes preparation, boarding, and controlled descent to water, typically 10–20 minutes.
