robot_success_rationale: Requires operating ship-specific davit/winch systems, coordinating crew boarding, navigating unstable marine deck surfaces, and high-stakes real-time decision-making. No general-purpose humanoid robot has these capabilities.

task: Care for established lawns by mulching, aerating, weeding, grubbing, removing thatch, or trimming or edging around flower beds, walks, or walls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = caring for a typical residential lawn (~5,000 sq ft). A competent groundskeeper completes mulching, aerating, weeding, edging, etc. in ~60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor unstructured terrain, diverse tool-use (aerator, edger, weed grubber), fine discrimination (weed vs. plant), and precise edging along irregular beds/walls — far beyond current humanoid generalization and outdoor dexterity.

task: Examine roadway and clear obstructions from the path of travel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scanning a roadway segment and clearing one obstruction (debris, branch, cone). Includes travel to site, inspection, and manual removal. ~15 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Roadway environments are unstructured and outdoor (rough terrain challenge). Identifying varied obstructions requires robust perception; physically clearing heavy or irregular objects demands strength and dexterity. Humanoids also cannot operate road vehicles to reach remote sites.

task: Select and install blades, tools, or other attachments for each operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting the correct blade/tool/attachment and installing it on a machine for a single operation. A competent worker typically takes 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying the correct attachment from varied inventory, aligning and securing it precisely (often with locking mechanisms, set screws, or snap-fits), and verifying seating — challenging for generalist humanoids due to fine dexterity, perception of small fasteners, and task variety.

task: Operate hoists to position dies or patterns on foundry floors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning one die/pattern via hoist: rigging, lifting, maneuvering, and precise placement on foundry floor. Typical cycle for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating overhead hoist controls, rigging heavy dies, and precise placement in a hazardous industrial environment. Humanoids cannot operate hoist machinery, handle heavy rigging hardware, or safely maneuver large loads autonomously.

task: Handle lines to moor vessels to wharfs, to tie up vessels to other vessels, or to rig towing lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mooring one vessel or rigging one tow line. A competent deckhand typically takes 10–20 minutes to handle, position, and secure all lines for a standard vessel.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working on unstable, wet, dynamic marine environments; throwing/catching heavy lines with precision; navigating docks with cleats and bollards; strong force application. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and robustness outdoors near water.

task: Observe machine operation to detect workpiece defects or machine malfunctions, adjusting machines as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one monitoring/inspection cycle at a machine station, including observation, defect detection, and any adjustment. Competent operator typically takes 15–25 minutes per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can visually inspect for defects but struggle with nuanced anomaly detection on varied workpieces, interpreting machine malfunctions, and making precise mechanical adjustments autonomously. Generalization across machine types is very limited.

task: Make minor electrical and mechanical repairs and adjustments to machines and notify supervisors when major service is required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single minor repair/adjustment on one machine. A competent technician typically diagnoses, fixes, and tests a minor issue in ~30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults, using tools (screwdrivers, multimeters, wrenches) on varied machine configurations, fine dexterous manipulation, and judgment to escalate. Well beyond current general-purpose robot capability in unstructured environments.

task: Water lawns, trees, or plants, using portable sprinkler systems, hoses, or watering cans.
human_time_rationale: One instance = watering a typical residential yard/garden area. A competent worker sets up hose/sprinkler, waters all plants/lawn zones, and stows equipment — ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied outdoor terrain, coupling/uncoupling hoses, adjusting water flow, and targeting diverse plants. Outdoor unstructured environments, hose manipulation, and valve operation are well beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Trim or pick flowers and clean flower beds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming/picking flowers and cleaning one standard flower bed (~10–15 sq ft). A competent gardener takes ~45 min including deadheading, cutting, and debris removal.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous cutting of delicate stems, distinguishing dead vs. live flowers, crouching on uneven outdoor terrain, and handling tools like shears — all challenging for 2026 humanoids outdoors with unstructured plants.

task: Follow planned landscaping designs to determine where to lay sod, sow grass, or plant flowers or foliage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and executing planting/sodding for a typical residential landscaping zone (~500–1000 sq ft), including reading the plan and laying materials.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor terrain navigation, interpreting 2D/3D design plans, fine placement of sod/plants, and varied tool use across uneven ground. Current humanoids lack robust outdoor mobility, plan interpretation, and precise horticultural manipulation.

task: Push or ride cars down slopes, or hook cars to cables and control cable drum brakes, to ease cars down inclines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving one rail car down a single incline (push/ride or cable-control). A competent worker typically takes ~5–10 minutes per car including setup and brake management.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy rail equipment, riding or physically pushing multi-ton cars, and managing cable drum brakes — specialized machinery a humanoid robot cannot physically operate or control safely at scale.

task: Observe hand signals, grade stakes, or other markings when operating machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single observation/response cycle during machine operation; a worker continuously interprets signals/stakes in real time, but each discrete recognition-and-react event takes ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires simultaneously operating heavy construction machinery AND interpreting dynamic hand signals or field markings in unstructured outdoor environments. Current humanoids cannot drive/operate such machines, and multi-modal real-time signal interpretation in the field remains unsolved.

task: Maintain or repair tools, equipment, or structures, such as buildings, greenhouses, fences, or benches, using hand or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single discrete repair/maintenance job (e.g., fixing a fence section, repairing a bench). A competent worker typically spends ~1 hour per job including assessment, tool selection, and execution.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse tool use (hammers, drills, saws), task planning, force control, and generalization across wildly varied structures. Current humanoids lack reliable autonomous tool selection, multi-step repair sequencing, and outdoor/unstructured-environment robustness.

task: Position, adjust, and secure stock material or workpieces against stops, on arbors, or in chucks, fixtures, or automatic feeding mechanisms, manually or using hoists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading/securing a single workpiece into a fixture, chuck, or feeding mechanism. A skilled machinist typically takes 3–7 minutes depending on part size and fixturing complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment, force-controlled clamping, and use of varied fixtures (chucks, arbors, stops). Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity and torque feedback for secure, repeatable workpiece fixturing. Hoist use adds further complexity.

task: Insert plugs into receptacles and bolt or screw leads to terminals to connect equipment to power sources, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting one piece of equipment (plugging in + bolting/screwing leads to terminals). A competent electrician completes this in ~5–10 minutes depending on terminal count.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to align plugs into receptacles and drive screws/bolts onto small terminals. Varied connector types, tight tolerances, and tool-use precision challenge current humanoids significantly. Success rate is low but non-zero for simple plug-in subtasks.

task: Splice and repair ropes, wire cables, or cordage, using marlinespikes, wire cutters, twine, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = splicing or repairing a single rope/cable joint. A competent rigger takes ~30–60 min depending on splice type; 45 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity: threading strands, using marlinespikes, maintaining precise tension on flexible/deformable cordage or wire. Current humanoids lack the tactile feedback and finger precision for reliable rope splicing.

task: Stand watch in ships' bows or bridge wings to look for obstructions in a ship's path or to locate navigational aids, such as buoys or lighthouses.
human_time_rationale: One watch shift is typically 4 hours; the sailor continuously scans for obstructions and navigational aids throughout.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor maritime mobility on a moving vessel (waves, wet decks, rolling), sustained long-range visual perception, and communicating sightings to crew. Current humanoids lack maritime locomotion stability, waterproofing, and the range/reliability of perception needed for safety-critical watchkeeping.

task: Brush or wipe acid over engraving to darken or highlight inscriptions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying acid to a single engraved piece. A skilled engraver/finisher brushes or wipes acid over the inscription in a few minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, controlled application of a chemical with a brush/swab on fine engraved surfaces; demands fine dexterity, delicate force control, and chemical handling safety — well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Control conveyors that run the entire length of shuttle cars to distribute loads as loading progresses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = controlling conveyors during one shuttle car loading cycle, typically 5–12 minutes of active monitoring and adjustment.
robot_success_rationale: Shuttle cars operate in underground/industrial mining environments with specialized vehicle-mounted controls. A humanoid robot cannot operate the shuttle car itself, cannot access the control cab, and lacks domain-specific tool interfaces. Extreme environment, specialized machinery, and vehicle operation push success near zero.

task: Test equipment functions such as signal strength and quality, transmission capacity, interference, and signal delay, using equipment such as oscilloscopes, circuit analyzers, frequency meters, and wattmeters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full test suite on a single piece of equipment/circuit (signal strength, quality, capacity, interference, delay). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise probe placement, instrument knob/dial manipulation, reading analog/digital displays, and interpreting multi-parameter results. Fine dexterity for probing, tool-switching, and contextual judgment are well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Mount equipment on transmission towers and in vehicles such as ships or ambulances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting a piece of equipment (e.g. antenna, relay, bracket) on a tower or in a vehicle. Includes positioning, fastening, and basic verification; median ~90 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing transmission towers or working inside constrained vehicle bays, using specialized tools, high-strength fastening, and precise positioning at height or in tight spaces — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, mobility, and safety capabilities.

task: Set up and operate machines, such as lathes, cutters, shears, borers, millers, grinders, presses, drills, or auxiliary machines, to make metallic and plastic workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and completing a machining run on one workpiece/batch. A competent machinist typically spends 30–60 min on setup plus operation for a typical part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating complex specialized industrial machines (lathes, mills, presses), reading blueprints, precise tool selection, measurement, and adjustment. Frontier humanoids lack the tool-use generalization, force feedback, and machine-interface capability needed.

task: Provide engineers with assistance in repairing or adjusting machinery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting an engineer during a single repair/adjustment session on a machine; ~30 min covers typical diagnostic, tool-fetching, and hands-on support steps.
robot_success_rationale: Requires contextual understanding of engineer's intent, fetching diverse tools, fine-force-controlled adjustments on varied machinery, and real-time reactive collaboration — well beyond current autonomous robot generalization.

task: Start machines and turn handwheels or valves to engage feeding, cooling, and lubricating mechanisms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting a machine and adjusting its valves/handwheels for a single setup. A competent operator typically completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Turning handwheels and valves requires significant torque, precise grip, and locating varied industrial controls. Robots struggle with force-controlled rotation of non-standard hardware, unstructured industrial environments, and sequencing multi-step machine startup reliably.

task: Perform first aid or cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) when required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = responding to a single emergency event; CPR typically lasts until EMS arrives (~5–15 min), with initial first aid assessment adding ~1–2 min. Median ~10 min.
robot_success_rationale: CPR requires precise chest compression force/depth on an unpredictably positioned human body, airway management, real-time patient assessment, and situational awareness in chaotic environments. Current humanoids lack the medical sensing, adaptive force control, and reliability needed for safe autonomous emergency care.

task: Repair circuits, wiring, and soldering, using soldering irons and hand tools to install parts and adjust connections.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single circuit/wiring fault, including diagnosis, desoldering, resoldering, and verifying the fix. Competent technician typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (sub-millimeter soldering), precise tool control, thermal management, and fault diagnosis. Far beyond current humanoid robots' dexterous manipulation and perception capabilities.

task: Prune or trim trees, shrubs, or hedges, using shears, pruners, or chain saws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pruning/trimming a single tree, shrub, or hedge section. A competent landscaper typically spends 20–60 min depending on plant size and tool used.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor unstructured terrain, identifying correct branches, wielding shears/chain saws safely, and making horticultural judgment calls. Current humanoids lack the outdoor mobility, tool mastery, and plant-recognition precision needed.

task: Attach wires from planted trees to support stakes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching wire(s) from one newly planted tree to its support stake(s). A competent worker threads/ties/fastens wire at both ends in a few minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor navigation on uneven soil, precise manipulation of flexible wire/cables, securing fasteners around irregular organic shapes (trunk, stake), and applying appropriate tension — all poorly handled by current humanoids.

task: Examine malfunctioning radio equipment to locate defects such as loose connections, broken wires, or burned-out components, using schematic diagrams and test equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing one malfunctioning radio unit. A skilled technician spends ~30–60 min tracing faults via schematics and test instruments.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading schematics, interpreting multimeter/oscilloscope readings, fine probe placement on PCB traces, and multi-step reasoning to localize defects. Far beyond current autonomous robot capability in unstructured electronics diagnosis.

task: Maintain irrigation systems, including winterizing the systems and starting them up in spring.
human_time_rationale: One instance = winterizing or spring startup of a typical residential/commercial irrigation system. A competent technician takes ~1.5–2.5 hrs per visit for inspection, valve operation, blow-out/pressurization, and controller adjustment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized compressors/valves, navigating outdoor terrain, connecting hoses, adjusting controllers, and diagnosing faults across varied sites. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured outdoor utility work.

task: Remove and replace defective components and parts such as conductors, resistors, semiconductors, and integrated circuits, using soldering irons, wire cutters, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing and replacing a single defective component on a PCB. A skilled technician spends ~20–40 min including diagnosis, desoldering, and resoldering.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter dexterity for soldering iron control, fine wire cutting, and precise PCB component placement. Current humanoids lack the fine-motor precision and thermal control needed for quality electronics rework.

task: Mix and spray or spread fertilizers, herbicides, or insecticides onto grass, shrubs, or trees, using hand or automatic sprayers or spreaders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing and applying chemicals to a typical residential lawn/garden area (~5,000–10,000 sq ft). A competent groundskeeper takes ~45 min including mixing, loading, and spreading/spraying.
robot_success_rationale: Requires mixing chemicals precisely, loading/operating wheeled spreaders or backpack sprayers, navigating uneven outdoor terrain, and covering full area evenly. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor navigation, chemical handling, and tool operation for this multi-step task.

task: Inspect workpieces for defects, and measure workpieces to determine accuracy of machine operation, using rules, templates, or other measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single workpiece. A skilled machinist visually checks for defects and measures with calipers/rules/templates in ~3–7 minutes per part.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is maturing, but precise physical measurement (calipers, micrometers, templates) requires fine dexterity and tool manipulation that current humanoids struggle with. Unstructured part handling adds further difficulty.

task: Overhaul lifeboats or lifeboat gear and lower or raise lifeboats with winches or falls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = overhauling/inspecting a single lifeboat and its gear, including lowering and raising via winch. A competent marine crew typically takes 1.5–2.5 hours per boat.
robot_success_rationale: Requires maritime vessel navigation, operating specialized marine winches/falls, heavy rigging work in wet/unstable environments, and nuanced mechanical inspection. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization, mobility, and tool-use capabilities.

task: Install rock gardens, ponds, decks, drainage systems, irrigation systems, retaining walls, fences, planters, or playground equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one landscaping feature (e.g., a small retaining wall, deck section, or pond). Median across feature types is roughly a half to full day for a competent crew member.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy digging, grading, concrete/masonry work, plumbing, carpentry, and outdoor unstructured terrain. Far beyond current humanoid robots' dexterity, strength, tool-use, and generalization capabilities.

task: Select the proper coolants and lubricants and start their flow.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting appropriate coolant/lubricant type and activating flow for a single machine/process setup. A skilled operator typically takes ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading labels/gauges, identifying correct fluid types, operating valves or control panels, and verifying flow. Complex perception, decision-making, and fine manipulation of industrial controls challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Plant seeds, bulbs, foliage, flowering plants, grass, ground covers, trees, or shrubs, and apply mulch for protection, using gardening tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = planting a typical batch (e.g., a flat of plants or a small tree) plus mulching that area. A competent gardener takes ~30–60 min including digging, placing, and mulching.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor uneven terrain, varied soil conditions, grasping delicate roots/bulbs, operating shovels and rakes, judging planting depth, and spreading mulch uniformly far exceed current humanoid robot generalization and outdoor dexterity capabilities.

task: Guide and stop cars by switching, applying brakes, or placing scotches, or wooden wedges, between wheels and rails.
human_time_rationale: One instance = guiding/stopping one rail car via brake application or scotch placement. A trained worker completes this in ~3–7 minutes including positioning and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/rail-yard terrain navigation, precise placement of heavy wedges between wheels and rails, coordination with moving rail cars, and situational awareness under dynamic hazardous conditions — well beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Gather and remove litter.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing litter from a typical outdoor area (e.g., a park path or parking lot section). A competent worker with a bag and grabber takes ~30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Litter is highly varied in shape, size, and material; scattered on uneven outdoor terrain. Robots struggle with unstructured outdoor locomotion, detecting small/flat items, and grasping irregular objects like crumpled wrappers or wet paper.

task: Care for natural turf fields, making sure the underlying soil has the required composition to allow proper drainage and to support the grasses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing and amending soil composition for one natural turf field (testing, aerating, topdressing, etc.); a competent groundskeeper typically spends ~2 hours per field per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized turf equipment (aerators, topdressers, core samplers), interpreting soil lab data, and making agronomic judgments. No current humanoid robot can operate such machinery or perform this unstructured, outdoor multi-step workflow autonomously.

task: Hang draperies and dust window blinds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = hanging drapes on one window and dusting its blinds. A competent worker takes ~15 min to hang drapes and ~10 min to dust blinds per window.
robot_success_rationale: Hanging drapes requires reaching overhead, threading hooks/rings onto a rod under load, and precise alignment — well beyond current dexterity/force-control. Blind dusting is simpler but awkward multi-step manipulation. Both tasks are rarely demonstrated by current humanoids.

task: Arrange costumes in order of use to facilitate quick-change procedures for performances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = organizing all costumes for one performer's show. A wardrobe technician reviews cue sheets, sequences garments, and arranges them on racks/hooks in order — typically ~25 min per performer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading production cue sheets, understanding abstract performance sequencing, identifying varied costume pieces, and arranging them meaningfully. Far beyond current robot generalization, planning, and context comprehension capabilities.

task: Shovel snow from walks, driveways, or parking lots, and spread salt in those areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing a typical residential driveway/walkway (~50–100 sq ft of walk + driveway). A competent adult with a shovel and salt spreader takes ~25–35 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor uneven/slippery terrain severely challenges bipedal robots. Forceful shoveling requires high torque and balance under dynamic loads. Salt spreading adds tool-use complexity. No commercial humanoid reliably handles this unstructured winter environment.

task: Test emergency transmitters to ensure their readiness for immediate use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one emergency transmitter: power on, run self-test, verify signal/indicator outputs, log readiness status. Competent technician takes ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of specialized controls/connectors, reading indicator lights/displays, interpreting test results, and using specialized RF test equipment — well beyond current humanoid generalization and tool-use capabilities.

task: Use irrigation methods to adjust the amount of water consumption and to prevent waste.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting irrigation settings and checking a field/zone for a single irrigation cycle. A competent worker assesses soil/crop conditions, adjusts controls, and verifies output in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized irrigation controllers, evaluating soil moisture and crop conditions across varied terrain, and making agronomic judgments. Current humanoids lack the sensors, domain software integration, and outdoor mobility for this complex task.

task: Examine machinery to verify specified pressures or lubricant flows.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one machine/system for pressure and lubricant flow readings; a competent technician reads gauges, checks ports, and logs results in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating gauges/ports on varied machinery, reading analog or digital displays (perception challenge), interpreting values, and possibly manipulating valves or test fittings. Current humanoids lack reliable gauge-reading and unstructured industrial navigation.

task: Obtain and move specified patterns to work stations, manually or using hoists, and secure patterns to machines, using wrenches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retrieving one pattern, transporting it to a workstation, and securing it to a machine with wrenches. Typically 15–25 min depending on pattern weight and distance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires hoist operation, heavy load handling, long-distance navigation in a busy shop floor, and precise wrench torquing to secure a pattern — all combined. Current humanoids lack reliable hoist tool-use and sufficient dexterous wrench manipulation under load.

task: Test and adjust repaired systems to meet manufacturers' performance specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing and adjusting one repaired system (e.g., HVAC, engine, appliance). A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min running diagnostics, comparing readings to spec sheets, and making adjustments.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading spec sheets, interpreting multi-modal diagnostic outputs, fine dexterous tool adjustments, and judgment-based iteration. Current humanoids lack the cognitive generalization, tool-use precision, and system-specific domain knowledge needed across varied repaired systems.

task: Relay specified signals to other ships, using visual signaling devices, such as blinker lights or semaphores.
human_time_rationale: One instance = transmitting a single short message via blinker light or semaphore to another ship; a typical signal takes ~2–8 minutes depending on message length and acknowledgment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires maritime-specific signaling knowledge (Morse/semaphore encoding), precise coordinated arm/flag movements or operating blinker controls, outdoor maritime environment, and real-time feedback perception from the receiving ship — far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Return borrowed or rented items when productions are complete and return other items to storage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = returning a batch of items from one completed production; involves gathering, checking, packing, transporting to drop-off/storage, and logging returns.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating diverse outdoor/indoor environments, driving to return locations, coordinating logistics, handling varied item types, and managing paperwork/communication — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Measure depth of water in shallow or unfamiliar waters, using leadlines, and telephone or shout depth information to vessel bridges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single depth sounding: cast leadline, read depth, and relay reading to bridge. A competent mariner completes this in ~5 minutes including communication.
robot_success_rationale: Requires maritime mobility (standing on a vessel deck in wave motion), casting/retrieving a weighted leadline with precision, reading wet markings, and shouting intelligible reports — combined maritime environment and dexterous tool-use far exceeds current humanoid capability.

task: Participate in shore patrols.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single shore patrol shift. Typical military/coast guard shore patrols last 4–8 hours; 4 hours (240 min) is a reasonable median for one patrol assignment.
robot_success_rationale: Shore patrol requires navigating outdoor/coastal terrain, situational awareness, social judgment, law enforcement decisions, communication, and potential physical intervention — far beyond current humanoid robot autonomy, perception, and legal authority.

task: Operate engine throttles and steering mechanisms to guide boats on desired courses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one typical boat trip/transit leg; a competent operator continuously manages throttle and steering, averaging ~60 minutes per trip.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate boat helm controls in dynamic open-water environments. Requires specialized vessel control, real-time navigation judgment, and situational awareness far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Break out, rig, and stow cargo-handling gear, stationary rigging, or running gear.
human_time_rationale: One instance = break out, rig, and stow one complete set of cargo gear for a single lift/operation. Experienced rigger typically takes 30–60 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting of chains/blocks/shackles, complex knot-tying and pin-securing, spatial reasoning across large deck/hold areas, and safety-critical force judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, payload, and marine/industrial environment generalization.

task: Measure and mark reference points and cutting lines on workpieces, using traced templates, compasses, and rules.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking a single workpiece with reference points and cutting lines. A competent worker using templates, compass, and rule typically takes 5–10 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous tool-use (compass, rule, scribe), accurate force control for marking, and geometric reasoning. Current humanoids lack fine-motor precision and reliable tool-handling for consistent, accurate layout work on varied workpieces.

task: Attach hoses and operate pumps to transfer substances to and from liquid cargo tanks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting hoses, operating pump controls, monitoring transfer, and disconnecting hoses for a single liquid cargo tank transfer. Typically 30–60 minutes for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy hose coupling (high force, varied fittings), valve manipulation, pump control panels, and leak monitoring in an unstructured industrial environment. Current humanoids lack the strength, tool-use generalization, and process-safety awareness needed.

task: Test drive vehicles and test components and systems, using equipment such as infrared engine analyzers, compression gauges, and computerized diagnostic devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one vehicle diagnostic session: a short test drive (~10–15 min) plus hooking up analyzers and gauges and reviewing results (~15 min).
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a passenger vehicle in traffic (humanoids cannot do this), plus connecting specialized diagnostic tools (compression gauges, OBD scanners) and interpreting multi-modal sensor outputs — far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Select, install, and adjust alignment of drills, cutters, dies, guides, and holding devices, using templates, measuring instruments, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting, installing, and aligning a full set of tooling for a single machining job setup. A skilled machinist typically takes 20–30 min per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (tight tolerances, torque-sensitive tightening), precise measurement with calipers/indicators, tool identification, and multi-step decision-making. Far beyond reliable autonomous robot capability in 2026.

task: Handle high voltage sources and hang electrical cables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = handling a high-voltage source and routing/hanging one run of electrical cable (e.g., in a panel or overhead tray). A competent electrician typically takes ~30–60 min per run.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of live high-voltage wiring, precise cable routing, safety-critical judgment, and compliance with electrical codes. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, safety protocols, and reliable perception for live electrical work.

task: Haul or spread topsoil, or spread straw over seeded soil to hold soil in place.
human_time_rationale: One instance = spreading topsoil or straw over a typical small-to-medium seeded area (~1,000 sq ft). A competent worker with a wheelbarrow/spreader takes ~45 min including loading and spreading.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating loaders, dump trucks, or pull-behind spreaders over uneven outdoor terrain. Humanoid robots cannot drive or operate such machinery; straw spreading is also bulky and unstructured. Generalization and outdoor mobility severely limit success.

task: Set up, operate, or tend welding machines that join or bond components to fabricate metal products or assemblies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and completing one welding job/batch on a machine. Includes machine setup, parameter adjustment, tending during operation, and basic quality check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized welding machinery, adjusting parameters, monitoring weld quality, and repositioning workpieces. Humanoids lack reliable tool-use for heavy industrial equipment, fine process control, and safety-compliant operation in hazardous welding environments.

task: Extract or lift jammed pieces from machines, using fingers, wire hooks, or lift bars.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing one jam. Worker diagnoses, accesses, and extracts the piece using fingers, hooks, or bars; typically 5–15 minutes depending on severity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing jam location in confined/occluded spaces, selecting the right tool, and applying precise force in tight quarters. Current robots lack the dexterous perception and adaptive force control for unstructured machine interiors.

task: Care for artificial turf fields, periodically removing the turf and replacing cushioning pads or vacuuming and disinfecting the turf after use to prevent the growth of harmful bacteria.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full maintenance session on a standard artificial turf field (vacuum/disinfect or pad replacement). Routine vacuuming/disinfecting takes ~1–2 hrs; pad replacement is longer but less frequent.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor locomotion across large uneven turf, operating specialized vacuums/sprayers, and heavy pad removal/replacement. Current humanoids lack the mobility range, tool operation, and strength for full autonomous field maintenance.

task: Plan or cultivate lawns or gardens.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one session of planning/cultivating a typical residential lawn or garden (e.g., mowing, weeding, planting, raking). A competent worker completes this in ~1 hour.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor unstructured terrain, varied tools (shovels, rakes, hoses), fine manipulation of plants, soil sensing, and adaptive planning are all well beyond current humanoid capabilities. Narrow autonomous mowers exist but are not general-purpose robots.

task: Build forms and mix and pour cement to form garden borders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = building forms, mixing, and pouring cement for a single garden border section (~10–15 ft). A competent worker takes ~2 hours including form setup, mixing, pouring, and finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a cement mixer, precise form building with stakes and boards outdoors on uneven terrain, heavy lifting, and controlled pouring. Humanoids lack the tools, strength, and outdoor unstructured dexterity for this multi-step construction task.

task: Lubricate machinery, equipment, or engine parts, such as gears, shafts, or bearings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lubricating a single machine/component set (e.g., applying grease to bearings or oil to gears on one machine). A competent worker locates fittings, selects lubricant, and applies it in ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating small grease fittings or ports on varied machinery, precise tool attachment (grease gun coupling), controlled force application, and navigation around industrial equipment. Perception of small fittings and tool-use dexterity remain major bottlenecks for current humanoids.

task: Examine costume fit on cast members and sketch or write notes for alterations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining one costume on one cast member and recording alteration notes. A skilled costumer spends ~15–25 min per fitting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual/tactile assessment of fabric drape, fit on a human body, and translating observations into meaningful sketches or written notes — well beyond current robot perception, social interaction, and fine creative documentation capabilities.

task: Set machine stops or guides to specified lengths as indicated by scales, rules, or templates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single stop/guide on one machine. A competent worker reads the scale/rule/template and physically sets the stop, typically taking 2–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise reading of measurement scales/templates, fine dexterous manipulation of adjustment mechanisms (knobs, bolts, sliding guides), and accurate positioning — all challenging for current humanoids with limited fine motor control and tool-use generalization.

task: Remove burrs, sharp edges, rust, or scale from workpieces, using files, hand grinders, wire brushes, or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one workpiece. A skilled worker spends ~10–20 min inspecting edges and using files, grinders, or brushes to remove burrs, rust, or scale to an acceptable finish.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine force-controlled dexterous tool use, tactile sensing to detect burrs and sharp edges, adaptive repositioning, and quality judgment. Current humanoids lack the compliance, precision grip, and surface-feel needed for consistent, safe deburring.

task: Assemble pipe sections, tubing, or fittings, using couplings, clamps, screws, bolts, cement, plastic solvent, caulking, or soldering, brazing, or welding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a single pipe/tubing run or joint. A competent plumber/pipefitter takes ~30–60 min depending on method (threading, soldering, welding, cementing).
robot_success_rationale: Requires high dexterity for threading, soldering, welding, or cementing in varied orientations; tool-switching (wrenches, torches, clamps); spatial reasoning in cramped spaces. Well beyond current humanoid capability at acceptable quality.

task: Align layout marks with dies or blades.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning a single die or blade to layout marks. A skilled worker visually positions and registers the tool to marks in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise visual perception of fine layout marks and sub-millimeter alignment of sharp/heavy tooling. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force feedback, and specialized perception needed for reliable, safe registration to tight tolerances.

task: Assign lockers to employees and maintain locker rooms, dressing rooms, wig rooms, or costume storage or laundry areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assigning one locker to one employee plus routine maintenance check of the locker room area; a competent attendant handles this in ~15 min combined.
robot_success_rationale: Task blends administrative record-keeping (locker assignment), social interaction, and varied physical upkeep (sorting costumes, laundry, wig storage). Current robots lack the cognitive/social coordination and generalization for this multi-domain, unstructured workflow.

task: Perform minor machine maintenance, such as oiling or cleaning machines, dies, or workpieces, or adding coolant to machine reservoirs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance event (e.g., oiling a machine, wiping dies, or topping off coolant reservoir). A skilled worker typically completes this in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating cluttered industrial floor, identifying correct fill points or surfaces, handling fluid containers/dispensers, and applying precise amounts. Perception of maintenance targets, fluid handling, and generalization across varied machine types are significant barriers for 2026 humanoids.

task: Maintain equipment such as range markers, fire extinguishers, boat fenders, lines, pumps, and fittings.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance session covering a set of marina/dock equipment (markers, extinguishers, fenders, lines, pumps, fittings) — checking, cleaning, adjusting, or replacing items.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse tool use, outdoor/wet unstructured environments, fine dexterity for fittings and pump components, judgment on wear/damage, and safe handling of pressurized extinguishers. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Check dimensions of materials to be used and assign numbers to the materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking dimensions of one material item and assigning it a number. A competent worker uses calipers/tape measure and labels the piece in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can grasp and scan objects, but reliably using measurement tools (calipers, tape measure), reading results, and applying labeled numbers/tags with acceptable precision and generalization across varied materials remains difficult for current humanoids.

task: Turn setscrews to adjust receivers for maximum sensitivity and transmitters for maximum output.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting setscrews on one receiver–transmitter pair; a competent technician spends ~10 min turning screws while monitoring signal output to hit optimal settings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (small screwdriver on tiny setscrews), real-time closed-loop feedback from electronic measurements, and domain-specific calibration judgment — well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Repair, reline, replace, and adjust brakes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full brake service on one axle (inspect, remove, reline/replace pads/shoes, adjust, reinstall). A competent mechanic takes ~60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a vehicle lift, removing wheels with high-torque fasteners, fine dexterous reassembly of brake components, and quality validation — far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and unstructured automotive environment generalization.

task: Inspect vehicles for damage and record findings so that necessary repairs can be made.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one vehicle. A competent inspector walks around the vehicle, checks all panels, lights, tires, undercarriage, and interior, then documents findings — typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Current humanoids lack reliable damage-detection perception (dents, scratches, cracks under varied lighting), struggle with undercarriage access, and cannot consistently use inspection recording tools or software to log structured findings acceptably.

task: Remove completed workpieces or parts from machinery, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a single completed workpiece from a machine using hand tools. A skilled operator typically does this in ~1–3 minutes including tool use and safe handling.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can grasp and extract parts in structured settings, but varied workpiece geometries, tight tolerances, confined machine spaces, and safe tool use in semi-structured industrial environments remain challenging for current humanoids.

task: Distribute costumes or related equipment and keep records of item status.
human_time_rationale: One instance = distributing one costume/equipment item to a person and logging its status. Includes retrieval, handoff, and record entry — ~8 min per transaction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating varied garments, handling delicate fabrics, interacting socially with recipients, and updating records in a system. Current robots lack reliable garment handling, social coordination, and seamless record-keeping integration.

task: Move controls or mount gears, cams, or templates in machines to set feed rates and cutting speeds, depths, and angles.
human_time_rationale: One setup instance: adjusting controls and mounting gears/cams/templates on a machine to configure feed rate, speed, depth, and angle before a machining run.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise identification and installation of gears/cams into tight mechanical fits, fine manipulation of machine controls, and domain-specific knowledge of machining setups. Far beyond reliable dexterous capability of 2026 humanoids in unstructured industrial environments.

task: Change worn machine accessories, such as cutting tools or brushes, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing one worn accessory on a machine; includes removal, part swap, and re-securing with hand tools. Typical shop task takes ~10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying worn parts, selecting correct tools, applying precise torque, and navigating cluttered machine environments. Fine dexterous tool-use and generalization across machine types remain weak in 2026 humanoids.

task: Service motors by performing tasks such as changing oil and lubricating parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing a single motor (oil change + lubrication). A competent mechanic typically takes 30–60 minutes depending on motor accessibility and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle drain plugs, oil fittings, and grease fittings; precise torque application; fluid handling without spills; and working in tight, unstructured spaces around machinery — all well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Test batteries, using hydrometers and ammeters, and charge batteries as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing and charging one battery. Connecting hydrometer/ammeter, reading values, and initiating a charge cycle takes ~10–20 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool connection (hydrometer, ammeter probes) to battery terminals, reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting results, and operating a charger. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, instrument-reading reliability, and contextual judgment needed.

task: Tie barges together into tow units for tugboats to handle, inspecting barges periodically during voyages and disconnecting them when destinations are reached.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a tow unit (tying barges together), covering lashing/rigging lines, plus periodic inspection during voyage and disconnection at destination. Roughly 45 min total per tow unit cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor maritime environment with moving/floating platforms, heavy rope/cable rigging, extreme dexterity under wet/unstable conditions, and navigation across decks. Current humanoids lack waterborne mobility, marine tool-use, and safe operation on shifting barge surfaces.

task: Design or construct costumes or send them to tailors for construction, major repairs, or alterations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing/constructing one costume or coordinating its dispatch to a tailor. Includes design decisions, fitting, sewing, or packaging/sending. Typical garment takes 1–4 hours; median ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative design judgment, fine dexterous sewing, fabric manipulation, fitting assessment, and coordination with tailors. Far beyond current robot dexterity and generalization; no commercial system handles open-ended garment construction.

task: Lay out, fit, or connect parts to be bonded, calculating production measurements, as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out, fitting, and connecting parts for a single bonding operation. A competent worker measures, positions, and joins parts in ~15–25 minutes on average.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, alignment of varied parts, and calculating production tolerances. Current humanoids lack reliable in-hand measurement, fine spatial reasoning for fit/bonding prep, and adaptive repositioning for non-standard parts.

task: Chip and clean rust spots on decks, superstructures, or sides of ships, using wire brushes and hand or air chipping machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating rust spots on one defined section of a ship (deck, superstructure, or side). A competent marine maintenance worker typically spends ~60–120 min per section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating uneven, cluttered ship surfaces (outdoor/maritime environment), detecting rust spots, wielding heavy vibrating tools with sustained force, and working at height or on curved hull sides — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Perform general labor duties such as repairing booms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single boom (e.g., crane or sprayer boom). A competent laborer typically spends ~1 hour diagnosing, disassembling, fixing, and reassembling.
robot_success_rationale: Boom repair requires diagnosing faults, using varied hand tools, applying force precisely, and working in unstructured/outdoor environments. Current humanoids lack the tool-use generalization, strength control, and fault-diagnosis capability needed.

task: Sweep, mop, and wash down decks to remove oil, dirt, and debris, using brooms, mops, brushes, and hoses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a typical ship/vessel deck section. A competent deckhand sweeping, mopping, and hosing down a moderate-sized deck area takes ~30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Basic sweeping/mopping on flat indoor surfaces is feasible (~40–70%), but marine deck environments add serious challenges: wet, slippery, uneven surfaces with coamings/drains/obstacles, hose management, oil removal requiring scrubbing force, and outdoor/maritime terrain. Tool switching (broom→mop→hose) and generalization to cluttered deck layouts further reduce success. Locomotion stability on wet, sloped decks is poor for current humanoids.

task: Take depth soundings in turning basins.
human_time_rationale: One instance = surveying a single turning basin. A hydrographic crew deploys equipment, conducts systematic sounding passes, and records data — typically 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires operating a vessel or sonar deployment equipment on open water. Humanoid robots cannot pilot boats, deploy hydrographic sonar systems autonomously, or safely operate in marine environments without extreme specialization.

task: Clean and press costumes before and after performances and perform any minor repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one costume fully cleaned, pressed, and minor repairs completed. A skilled wardrobe technician typically spends 30–60 min per costume.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation (sewing, ironing delicate fabrics, operating steamers), precise force control to avoid damage, and generalization across wildly varied costume types. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Monitor and test operations of engines or other equipment so that malfunctions and their causes can be identified.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring/testing session on one engine or equipment unit, including running diagnostics, observing readings, and noting anomalies. ~30–60 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read gauges and listen for anomalies, but interpreting complex multi-sensor engine behavior, connecting diagnostic tools, and identifying root causes in unstructured environments requires high-level cognition and dexterity beyond current general-purpose robots.

task: Care for non-clothing items, such as flags, table skirts, or draperies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = caring for a single non-clothing textile item (e.g., inspect, spot-clean, press, fold, and store a flag or drapery panel). ~15–25 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Large, floppy, irregularly shaped textiles are extremely hard to manipulate; requires delicate handling, inspection, pressing, and folding of non-standard items. Poor generalization and dexterity for such varied, unwieldy objects.

task: Correct problems by adjusting controls or by stopping machines and opening holding devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and correcting a single machine problem (adjusting controls or stopping/opening a holding device); a competent operator typically resolves this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fault diagnosis, context-dependent decision-making, and manipulating unfamiliar industrial controls or holding devices. Current humanoids lack reliable generalization to arbitrary machine interfaces and in-context fault reasoning.

task: Prepare metal surfaces or workpieces, using hand-operated equipment, such as grinders, cutters, or drills.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single metal workpiece (grinding, cutting, or drilling). A competent metalworker typically spends ~20 min per piece including setup, operation, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force control, tool-swapping, and adapting to varied metal geometries. Current humanoids lack reliable high-torque tool handling, fine surface-quality feedback, and safe grinder/drill operation autonomously.

task: Turn and press knobs and buttons or enter operating instructions into computers to adjust and start welding machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and starting a welding machine for a job: selecting parameters, turning knobs/buttons, and entering settings. A skilled operator does this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Turning knobs and pressing buttons requires moderate dexterity and precise force control; current humanoids struggle with small industrial controls. Entering instructions into a welding computer panel adds perception/UI challenges. Generalization across machine types is poor.

task: Perform or participate in emergency drills, as required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full emergency drill (e.g., fire/evacuation). Competent worker participates from start to finish, typically 30–60 minutes including briefing and debrief.
robot_success_rationale: Drills require understanding spoken/written instructions, navigating dynamic crowds, following role-specific protocols, responding to unpredictable scenarios, and social coordination — well beyond 2026 humanoid generalization and situational awareness capabilities.

task: Inventory stock to determine types or conditions of available costuming.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inventorying a typical costume stock room/collection. A competent wardrobe worker inspects, identifies, and logs each item's type and condition across a medium-sized inventory.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine garment handling, nuanced condition assessment (stains, wear, fabric damage), reading labels, and navigating cluttered racks. Current robots lack reliable cloth manipulation, detailed visual inspection judgment, and structured reporting for unstructured costume environments.

task: Assemble, align, and clamp workpieces into holding fixtures to bond, heat-treat, or solder fabricated metal components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and clamping one set of workpieces into a fixture. A skilled fabricator typically takes 10–20 min depending on part complexity and fixture type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise multi-part alignment, force-controlled clamping, and fixture-specific tool use. Current humanoids struggle with tight-tolerance alignment, varied clamp mechanisms, and verifying correct seating — especially across non-standardized workpieces.

task: Install, adjust, and repair stationary and mobile radio transmitting and receiving equipment and two-way radio communication systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing/adjusting/repairing a single radio unit. A competent technician typically spends 60–120 minutes per unit including diagnosis, physical work, and testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous work (coaxial connectors, small fasteners, antenna tuning), use of specialized test equipment (SWR meters, spectrum analyzers), complex troubleshooting, and often working in confined or elevated spaces. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Inspect vehicles or equipment to ensure compliance with rules, standards, or regulations.
human_time_rationale: One inspection instance of a vehicle or piece of equipment; a competent inspector typically spends 30–60 minutes checking systems, documentation, and compliance points.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading gauges, interpreting regulatory standards, accessing confined areas, and making compliance judgments. Current humanoids lack the regulatory knowledge integration, tool-use versatility, and reliable fine-detail perception needed for a full autonomous inspection.

task: Check to ensure that food, beverages, blankets, reading material, emergency equipment, and other supplies are aboard and are in adequate supply.
human_time_rationale: One pre-departure supply check of a vehicle/vessel (e.g., aircraft, boat, or bus); a competent crew member walks through all categories and verifies quantities.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a complex vehicle interior, visually identifying and counting diverse items, checking expiry dates, and verifying emergency equipment status. Generalization across item types, cluttered spaces, and pass/fail judgment is well beyond current robot reliability.

task: Draw freehand sketches of designs, trace finished drawings onto designated paper for the reproduction of blueprints, and reproduce working drawings on copy machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one design sketch or tracing session. A competent drafter takes ~15–25 min to sketch, trace, or copy one working drawing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous pen/pencil control, creative freehand sketching, and precise tracing — all far beyond current humanoid capability. Copy machine operation is simpler but the full compound task scores very low.

task: Tow, push, or guide other boats, barges, logs, or rafts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one towing/pushing/guiding trip (e.g., moving a barge between two points). Typical harbor/river towing operation takes ~1–3 hours; 120 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a tugboat or vessel on open water — humanoid robots cannot pilot watercraft in dynamic maritime environments. No current general-purpose robot has marine vehicle operation capability. Extreme perception, navigation, and control challenges.

task: Clean boats and repair hulls and superstructures, using hand tools, paint, and brushes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and hull/superstructure repair on a single average recreational boat. A competent marine worker typically needs 3–5 hours per boat cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/wet uneven surfaces, complex multi-step hull repair (sanding, fairing, painting), fine tool-use, and large-scale surface judgment. Current humanoids lack marine environment robustness, specialized tool generalization, and repair quality assessment.

task: Devise or build fixtures or jigs used to hold parts in place during welding, brazing, or soldering.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and fabricating a single fixture/jig for a specific welding/brazing/soldering application. Includes layout, material selection, cutting, and assembly — typically 1–3 hours for a competent toolmaker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level engineering design cognition, material selection, precise metalworking (cutting, drilling, grinding), and creative problem-solving. Far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and autonomous planning capabilities.

task: Fabricate engine replacement parts, such as valves, stay rods, or bolts, using metalworking machinery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single engine replacement part (e.g., a bolt or valve) on metalworking machinery, from raw stock to finished part; ~30–60 min for a skilled machinist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating lathes, mills, and grinders with precision tolerances; setting tooling, reading blueprints, measuring with calipers, and making adaptive decisions. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use generalization, and precision manipulation capabilities.

task: Conduct vehicle or transportation equipment tests, using diagnostic equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = running a full diagnostic test on one vehicle using OBD/diagnostic tools, reviewing codes, and documenting results. ~30–60 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating and plugging into OBD port, navigating complex diagnostic software, interpreting fault codes, and making judgment calls — beyond current humanoid dexterity, software interaction, and domain reasoning capabilities.

task: Inspect and clean cabins, checking for any problems and making sure that cabins are in order.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one cabin turnover. A competent housekeeper typically spends 20–40 minutes inspecting, cleaning, and resetting a standard cabin.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-room navigation, varied surface cleaning, linen changing, fault inspection, and nuanced judgment across unstructured environments. Current robots lack reliable generalization for this full multi-step workflow at acceptable quality.

task: Announce and demonstrate safety and emergency procedures, such as the use of oxygen masks, seat belts, and life jackets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full pre-flight safety demonstration on a commercial flight, typically lasting 4–6 minutes covering seatbelts, oxygen masks, life jackets, and exits.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced verbal communication, passenger-facing social presence, fine prop manipulation (life jacket demo, mask demo), mobile navigation in a narrow cabin aisle, and real-time audience responsiveness — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Load or feed workpieces into welding machines to join or bond components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading/positioning one set of workpieces into a welding machine. Skilled operator takes 1–5 min per cycle depending on part size and fixturing.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of industrial parts is feasible but welding fixtures demand precise alignment, varied part geometries, and safe operation near high-heat equipment. Generalization across part types and fixture designs limits reliability significantly.

task: Clean and polish wood trim, brass, or other metal parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and polishing one trim section or metal part (e.g., a door frame or brass fixture). Competent worker applies product, buffs, and inspects in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, consistent pressure and motion on irregular surfaces, identifying material type, applying correct product, and judging polish quality visually. Dexterity, force control, and surface-quality perception are beyond reliable frontier robot capability.

task: Monitor, maintain, or secure inventories of costumes, wigs, or makeup, providing keys or access to assigned directors, costume designers, or wardrobe mistresses/masters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single inventory check/access session; a competent wardrobe technician takes ~30 min to audit, log, and manage key/access handoffs for a typical costume inventory.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a cluttered wardrobe room, reading/logging varied items, handling keys and access credentials, and social handoff to specific authorized personnel — well beyond current robot generalization and social/contextual reasoning capabilities.

task: Direct and assist passengers in emergency procedures, such as evacuating a plane following an emergency landing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one emergency evacuation event, from incident onset to last passenger cleared. Competent flight crew typically evacuates a full aircraft in under 90 seconds per FAA standards, but total directed procedure takes ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time crowd communication, emotional authority, adaptive decision-making under chaos, physical assistance of panicked/injured passengers, operating in a cramped/dynamic aircraft cabin, and coordinating multi-exit strategy. Far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Dress electrodes, using tip dressers, files, emery cloths, or dressing wheels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dressing one pair of electrodes. A skilled worker quickly tips a dresser or uses a file/emery cloth; typically 2–4 minutes per electrode set.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool selection, controlled force application, and fine dexterity to dress electrode tips without over-removing material. Perception of electrode wear and quality check post-dressing are challenging for current humanoids. Tool handling with files/emery cloths is unstructured and error-prone.

task: Start engines to propel ships, and regulate engines and power transmissions to control speeds of ships, according to directions from captains or bridge computers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting engines and regulating speed per a single order/maneuver from the bridge. A competent marine engineer completes this in ~10–20 minutes per maneuver sequence.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating ship engine room controls, interpreting bridge orders, and regulating complex marine propulsion systems. Humanoid robots cannot operate ship machinery, lack maritime-specific tool integration, and face extreme safety/liability constraints.

task: Walk aisles of planes to verify that passengers have complied with federal regulations prior to takeoffs and landings.
human_time_rationale: One cabin walk-through on a typical single-aisle aircraft (~150 seats); checking seatbelts, tray tables, seat backs, carry-ons. Competent flight attendant completes this in ~5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Extremely cramped, dynamic aircraft cabin; narrow aisles, uneven passenger behavior, social compliance interactions, and regulatory judgment all required. No humanoid is certified or capable in this environment.

task: Conduct trial runs before welding, soldering, or brazing, and make necessary adjustments to equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running a trial weld/solder/braze, inspecting results, and adjusting equipment settings. A competent worker typically takes 10–20 minutes per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool manipulation, precise multi-parameter equipment adjustment, and nuanced quality judgment of trial results. Current humanoids lack the perception, dexterity, and domain reasoning to close this feedback loop autonomously.

task: Maintain or repair engines, electric motors, pumps, winches, or other mechanical or electrical equipment, or assist other crew members with maintenance or repair duties.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance or repair job on one piece of equipment (e.g., servicing a pump). Competent marine mechanic typically takes 60–120 min per job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level dexterous tool use (wrenches, multimeters, torque specs) in cramped, vibrating marine spaces, diagnosing faults, and handling heavy/oily irregular components. Far beyond current humanoid capabilities in unstructured mechanical repair contexts.

task: Set dials and timing controls to regulate electrical current, gas flow pressure, heating or cooling cycles, or shut-off.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a set of dials/controls for a single regulation task (e.g., setting a thermostat, gas valve, or timer). A competent worker completes this in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Turning dials and reading analog/digital displays requires fine dexterity and precise perception. Current humanoids struggle with small rotary controls, varied dial formats, and confirming correct set-point feedback. Generalization across dial types is poor.

task: Assist passengers entering or disembarking the aircraft.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one passenger board or deplane (e.g., guiding, lifting carry-on, supporting mobility-impaired traveler). Typically 1–3 minutes per passenger.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a narrow, crowded jet bridge/aisle, dynamic social interaction, adapting to diverse passengers (elderly, disabled, children), and safe physical assistance. Current humanoids lack reliable social perception, safe human-contact handling, and confined-space agility.

task: Maintain electrical power, heating, ventilation, refrigeration, water, or sewerage systems.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance visit/instance on a single building system (e.g., HVAC unit, electrical panel, or pump). Includes inspection, diagnosis, and routine servicing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-system expertise, use of specialized tools (multimeters, pipe wrenches, refrigerant gauges), working in cramped utility spaces, and safety-critical decision-making. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Perform general marine vessel maintenance or repair work, such as repairing leaks, finishing interiors, refueling, or maintaining decks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance/repair job on a vessel (e.g., patching a leak, refinishing a deck section, or refueling). A competent marine technician averages ~2 hours per discrete task.
robot_success_rationale: Marine environments are unstructured, wet, cramped, and dynamic (vessel movement, tides). Tasks demand specialized tools, fine dexterity (sealing leaks, interior finishing), heavy manipulation, and domain-specific judgment far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Add chemicals or materials to workpieces or machines to facilitate bonding or to cool workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying adhesive/flux/coolant to a single workpiece or machine. A skilled worker typically takes 1–5 minutes depending on method (brush, spray, pour).
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid/chemical dispensing, tool-use (brush, nozzle, bottle), and surface targeting. Current humanoids struggle with fluid handling, variable containers, and ensuring correct coverage/quantity without spilling.

task: Clean engine parts and keep engine rooms clean.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a set of engine parts and tidying one engine room; a competent mechanic/cleaner typically takes ~30–60 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Engine rooms are cluttered, confined, and hazardous; parts have complex geometry requiring solvent use and scrubbing with precise force. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, chemical tool-use, and unstructured-environment navigation needed for acceptable quality.

task: Anneal finished workpieces to relieve internal stress.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading workpieces into a furnace, setting annealing parameters, and initiating a cycle. Hands-on setup/loading takes ~20–40 min; monitoring is passive.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating industrial furnace controls, safely handling hot/heavy workpieces, and precise parameter setting. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use with industrial equipment, thermal safety handling, and process calibration in unstructured factory settings.

task: Clean and maintain machinery, equipment, and work areas to ensure proper functioning and safe working conditions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one cleaning/maintenance cycle of a machine or work area. A competent worker typically spends 30–60 min inspecting, wiping, lubricating, and tidying a workstation or machine.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep/wipe flat surfaces (~50%) but struggle with reaching into machinery, applying lubricants precisely, removing debris from complex geometries, and verifying "safe working conditions" — requiring multi-step judgment and tool-use generalization.

task: Remove materials and products from machines and equipment, and place them in boxes, trucks or conveyors, using hand tools and moving devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one batch/item from a machine and placing it into a box, truck, or conveyor. A competent worker repeats this cycle in roughly 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place from machines to containers is improving; robots handle tidy, structured environments reasonably well. However, varied part geometries, machine access constraints, hand-tool use, and loading trucks/conveyors reduce reliability significantly.

task: Operate or maintain off-loading liquid pumps or valves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one off-loading operation (opening/closing valves, starting/monitoring pump, verifying flow, shutting down). Typical liquid transfer takes ~20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large industrial valves, monitoring gauges, and managing hose connections in potentially wet/hazardous environments. Current humanoids lack fluid-handling tool-use, leak detection judgment, and safety-critical process control generalization.

task: Administer first aid to passengers in distress.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing and treating one passenger in distress (e.g., applying pressure, CPR, stabilizing). Median first-aid episode is ~10–20 minutes until handoff to EMS.
robot_success_rationale: First aid demands adaptive touch, fine dexterity (bandaging, chest compressions at correct force/rate), real-time patient assessment, and dynamic decision-making. Current humanoids lack medical sensing, reliable gentle-yet-firm contact control, and safety certification for human body contact in emergencies.

task: Assist passengers in placing carry-on luggage in overhead, garment, or under-seat storage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one passenger with their carry-on. A flight attendant typically lifts or repositions one bag in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a very narrow, crowded aircraft aisle, handling bags of highly varied size/weight/shape, lifting overhead in confined space, avoiding passengers, and socially coordinating — all far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured real environments.

task: Secure boats to docks with mooring lines, and cast off lines to enable departure.
human_time_rationale: One instance = securing or casting off one boat at a dock. A competent deckhand takes ~5–10 min to loop, tie, and tension all mooring lines (or release them for departure).
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/waterside terrain with moving, wet surfaces; coiling and throwing heavy ropes with precise tension; tying marine knots on cleats/bollards under boat motion. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and stability outdoors.

task: Immerse completed workpieces into water or acid baths to cool and clean components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = immersing a single workpiece into a bath and retrieving it. A competent worker grabs the part, submerges it, waits briefly, and removes it — typically 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of workpieces is feasible, but hazardous liquids (acid), wet/slippery parts, precise immersion depth/timing, and varied part geometries challenge current robots' dexterity, safety compliance, and generalization.

task: Add chemicals, solutions, or ingredients to machines or equipment as required by the manufacturing process.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adding a single chemical/solution/ingredient to a machine. Involves locating material, measuring/dispensing, and pouring/loading. Typical cycle: ~5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct container, precise pouring/dispensing, and handling varied vessel types. Robots struggle with fluid handling, label reading, accurate measurement, and generalization across equipment interfaces and container formats.

task: Mark weld points and positions of components on workpieces, using rules, squares, templates, or scribes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking weld points/component positions on a single workpiece. A skilled worker using rules, squares, templates, or scribes typically takes 5–15 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor scribing, accurate measurement with hand tools (rules, squares), and interpreting blueprints. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-handling precision, and blueprint-reading integration needed for reliable marking quality.

task: Heat and serve prepared foods.
