human_time_rationale: One instance = heating a single prepared food item (e.g., a meal tray or dish) and serving it. Includes retrieving, heating (microwave/oven), plating, and delivering — ~8 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can open microwaves and place containers, but reliably operating varied appliance controls, handling diverse packaging, judging doneness, and serving without spills remain significant challenges for current humanoids.

task: Provide boarding or disembarking assistance to passengers needing special assistance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one passenger board or disembark (e.g., a flight, train, or bus). Includes greeting, guiding, using a wheelchair/jet bridge, and handing off safely — typically 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social interaction, reading passenger needs, operating wheelchairs/ramps in dynamic crowded environments, physical steadying of humans, and safe handoffs — far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and caregiving capability.

task: Select, position, align, and bolt jigs, holding fixtures, guides, or stops onto machines, using measuring instruments and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full setup of one jig/fixture on one machine; skilled machinist typically takes 15–35 min including measuring, aligning, and bolting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise sub-millimeter alignment using measuring instruments, torquing bolts to spec, selecting correct tooling from inventory, and interpreting setup sheets — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, perception accuracy, and generalization on real shop floors.

task: Inspect repairs to transportation vehicles or equipment to ensure that repair work was performed properly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single repaired vehicle/equipment. A competent inspector walks through key repair areas, checks components, tests functions — typically 15–35 minutes per vehicle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating around/under large vehicles, interpreting varied repair contexts, using expert mechanical judgment, running functional tests, and accessing tight spaces. Current humanoids lack the specialized perception, tool-use, and domain knowledge needed at acceptable quality.

task: Replace light bulbs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing one bulb. A competent worker locates the bulb, removes old one, installs new one in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reaching overhead or awkward positions, unscrewing/unclipping a bulb, and inserting a new one with precise alignment. Overhead reach, varied fixture types, fragile glass, and fine rotational dexterity challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Compute layout dimensions, and determine and mark reference points on metal stock or workpieces for further processing, such as welding and assembly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out and marking a single workpiece. A skilled metalworker reads blueprints, measures, and scribes reference points in ~10–20 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires blueprint interpretation, precise measurement with calipers/squares, and fine scribing/marking on metal. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tool-use fluency, and drawing/measurement precision needed for acceptable quality.

task: Maintain a ship's engines under the direction of the ship's engineering officers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance session (inspection, adjustment, or minor repair) on a ship's engine system; ~60 min is typical for a directed routine maintenance task.
robot_success_rationale: Ship engine rooms are cramped, hazardous, and highly unstructured. Tasks require heavy-duty tool use, complex diagnostics, and coordination with officers. No current humanoid robot is rated or deployed for maritime engineering maintenance.

task: Mark curves, lines, holes, dimensions, and welding symbols onto workpieces, using scribes, soapstones, punches, and hand drills.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking up a single workpiece layout. A skilled layout worker/fitter typically spends ~15–25 min scribing lines, punching centers, and adding weld symbols on one part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor tool use (scribe, punch, hand drill) on varied surfaces, reading blueprints, interpreting weld symbols, and applying exact force. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and perception for autonomous precision marking.

task: Operate audio and video systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and operating an A/V system for a single event or session (e.g., a meeting or presentation). Includes powering on, routing signals, adjusting levels, and monitoring. ~20 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced auditory/visual perception to monitor output quality, fine manipulation of knobs/buttons/cables, software-based mixing decisions, and real-time adaptive judgment. Current robots lack audio perception feedback loops and specialized A/V tool generalization.

task: Inspect vehicles or other equipment for evidence of abuse, damage, or mechanical malfunction.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one vehicle or equipment unit. A competent inspector walks around, checks panels, fluids, tires, undercarriage, and mechanical components — typically 15–35 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual/tactile judgment across diverse vehicles, crawling under vehicles, interpreting subtle damage cues, and accessing confined areas. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-grained damage perception, fluid inspection, and flexible mobility for full vehicle underbody access.

task: Inspect, measure, or test completed metal workpieces to ensure conformance to specifications, using measuring and testing devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single metal workpiece. A competent quality technician typically spends 10–20 min measuring dimensions, checking tolerances, and recording results with calipers, micrometers, gauges, etc.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with precise tool handling (calipers, micrometers, CMMs), interpreting varied part geometries, and repositioning measuring devices accurately. Vision-based inspection is improving but tactile/instrument-based measurement remains very unreliable for general workpieces.

task: Fill hoppers and position spouts to direct flow of flux or manually brush flux onto seams of workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fluxing a single workpiece seam. Filling a hopper/positioning a spout or brushing flux onto a seam takes a skilled worker roughly 3–5 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise seam identification via perception, dexterous brush control or spout alignment on irregular weld seams, and handling viscous flux material. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity and seam-tracing accuracy for industrial quality standards.

task: Identify and mark materials, products, and samples, following instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = labeling/marking a single item (material, product, or sample) per instructions. A competent worker reads the label spec and applies a mark or sticker in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting instructions, selecting correct labels, precise placement on varied surfaces/shapes, and fine dexterous handling. Current robots struggle with instruction parsing, label peeling, and accurate application on irregular objects.

task: Examine merchandise to ensure correct pricing and display, and that it functions as advertised.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single item/display unit for correct price tag, proper placement, and basic functional check. A retail worker handles this in ~3 minutes per item.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate retail floors and read price tags via vision, but verifying correct pricing against a database, checking display arrangement standards, and functionally testing diverse merchandise (electronics, toys, appliances) requires generalized perception, tool-use, and judgment beyond current reliable capability.

task: Observe meters, gauges, or machine operations to ensure that soldering or brazing processes meet specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = monitoring gauges/meters through one soldering or brazing cycle; a competent worker checks readings and confirms spec compliance in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read digital displays via vision, but interpreting analog gauges, detecting subtle process anomalies, integrating multi-sensor context, and acting on out-of-spec conditions in an unstructured industrial environment remains unreliable for general-purpose humanoids.

task: Tend auxiliary equipment used in welding processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one tending cycle (checking, adjusting, replenishing, or restarting auxiliary welding equipment such as wire feeders, gas regulators, coolant systems, or fume extractors) per work session or shift check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting gauges/displays, adjusting valves/knobs, swapping consumables (wire spools, gas cylinders), and diagnosing faults — demanding fine dexterity, tool use, and domain-specific perception well beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Check baggage and cargo and direct passengers to designated locations for loading.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one passenger's baggage check-in and directing them to their gate/loading area. A competent agent handles this in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires social interaction, verbal communication, reading documents/tags, operating check-in systems, and navigating a busy public terminal. Current humanoids lack reliable speech dialogue, document handling, and safe crowd navigation for this multi-step service task.

task: Verify that first aid kits and other emergency equipment, including fire extinguishers and oxygen bottles, are in working order.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a full set of emergency equipment (first aid kit, fire extinguisher, oxygen bottle) in a single location/vehicle. A competent worker checks seals, gauges, expiry dates, and completeness in ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading gauges/expiry dates, checking seal integrity, opening kit contents, and interpreting pass/fail status. Multi-modal perception (small text, color-coded gauges, tactile seals) and semantic judgment are beyond reliable 2026 robot capability. Locomotion to equipment is feasible but inspection quality is very low.

task: Drive tractors for building or repairing logging and skid roads.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one shift of road building/repair on a logging site. A competent operator typically completes a meaningful road segment in ~4 hours of active machine operation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a large specialized tractor/dozer on rough, unstructured outdoor terrain. Humanoid robots cannot drive or control heavy construction machinery autonomously; task demands extreme terrain adaptability, tool-use, and judgment far beyond 2026 frontier capability.

task: Locate center lines and verify template positions, using measuring instruments such as gauge blocks, height gauges, and dial indicators.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one workpiece setup. A skilled machinist uses gauge blocks, height gauges, and dial indicators; typical time is 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-dexterous manipulation of metrology instruments (dial indicators, gauge blocks), reading analog/digital gauges, and interpreting subtle measurements to micron-level tolerances — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Push dual control buttons and move controls to start, stop, or adjust machinery and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pressing dual buttons and moving controls to start/stop/adjust a machine; a trained operator completes this in about a minute per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Simultaneously pressing two spatially separated buttons (dual-control) requires precise bimanual coordination and accurate panel perception. Frontier humanoids have improving dexterity but bimanual simultaneous button-press on varied industrial panels remains unreliable; lever/knob manipulation adds further variability.

task: Transfer components, metal products, or assemblies, using moving equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = transferring a batch of components/assemblies from one station to another using a forklift, pallet jack, or conveyor. Typical cycle is 5–15 minutes including loading, transit, and unloading.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating forklifts, pallet jacks, or overhead cranes — specialized moving equipment a humanoid robot cannot drive or control. Narrow AMRs handle structured warehouses well, but a general-purpose humanoid lacks the tool interfaces and load-bearing capacity for this task.

task: Load materials and products into machines and equipment, or onto conveyors, using hand tools and moving devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a single batch/lot of materials into a machine or onto a conveyor; a competent worker typically takes ~5–10 minutes including positioning, securing, and verifying placement.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place common objects, but varied material types, hand-tool use, moving conveyors, and precise machine-feed alignment demand strong generalization and dexterity that current humanoids handle inconsistently.

task: Install engine controls, propeller shafts, or propellers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one complete set of engine controls, shaft, or propeller. A skilled mechanic takes ~1–3 hrs depending on component; 120 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy torque application, working in confined/awkward bilge or engine spaces, precise alignment of rotating shafts, and specialized tooling. Beyond current humanoid dexterity, strength, and spatial reasoning limits.

task: Drive crawler or wheeled tractors to drag or transport logs from felling sites to log landing areas for processing and loading.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one skidding cycle (travel to felling site, attach log(s), drag to landing). Typical forest skidding cycle is 15–35 min depending on terrain and distance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a specialized crawler/wheeled skidder in unstructured, rough forest terrain. Humanoid robots cannot drive heavy forestry equipment; no current general-purpose robot has this capability. Terrain, cable/choker rigging, and dynamic log handling add further barriers.

task: Conduct periodic trips through the cabin to ensure passenger comfort and to distribute reading material, headphones, pillows, playing cards, and blankets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full cabin walk-through on a typical commercial flight, checking on passengers and distributing requested items. Competent flight attendant completes this in ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Navigating a narrow, dynamic aircraft aisle, reading passenger social cues, handling varied soft items (pillows, blankets), and safely interacting with seated passengers in a confined space far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Design and prepare templates of wood, paper, or metal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and cutting one template. Includes measuring, marking, and shaping the material; moderate complexity for a skilled tradesperson.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative design interpretation, precise marking/cutting of varied materials (wood, metal, paper), tool changes, and fine dexterity. Current humanoids lack the precision tooling, multi-step planning, and material-adaptive cutting skill needed.

task: Fasten, package, or stack materials and products, using hand tools and fastening equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fastening/packaging/stacking a single batch or unit (e.g., one box sealed and stacked). A competent worker completes this in ~5–10 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place and stacking on flat surfaces are within frontier robot capability (~60–80%), but tool-use for fastening (staplers, strapping guns, wrenches) and variable packaging shapes reduce reliability significantly. Generalization across material types remains a limiting factor.

task: Conduct annual, interim, and special housing reviews and home visits to ensure conformance to regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one housing review/home visit, including travel, walkthrough, documentation, and compliance check; typically 60–120 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires regulatory knowledge, judgment-based compliance assessment, interviewing occupants, documenting nuanced findings, and navigating varied home environments. Current robots lack the legal reasoning, social interaction, and contextual inspection judgment needed.

task: Inspect materials and products for defects, and to ensure conformance to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single item or batch unit. A competent inspector takes ~5–10 min to visually check, measure, and document conformance for one unit/batch sample.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is maturing but generalization across varied materials, surface finishes, and spec types is weak. Handling/repositioning parts for full inspection, using gauges/calipers, and interpreting non-visual defects remain significant limitations for current humanoids.

task: Clean, lubricate, maintain, and adjust equipment to maintain efficient operation, using air hoses, cleaning fluids, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full PM cycle on a single piece of equipment (cleaning, lubing, adjusting). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-tool use (air hose, fluids, hand tools), precise lubrication points, varied equipment geometries, and judgment-based adjustments — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-switching, and generalization capabilities.

task: Inspect equipment for safety prior to use, and perform necessary basic maintenance tasks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a pre-use safety inspection of a single piece of equipment (e.g., a machine, vehicle, or tool) plus any minor maintenance (lubrication, tightening). Typical walkaround/checklist takes ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires visual/tactile anomaly detection across diverse equipment types, interpreting wear/damage, and performing varied maintenance actions (tightening, lubricating). Generalization across equipment types and judgment calls remain well beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Check survey responses for errors, such as the use of pens instead of pencils, and set aside response forms that cannot be used.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking one survey response form for errors (pen vs. pencil, incomplete fields, stray marks). A competent human scans and decides in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Vision systems can detect ink vs. pencil marks and flag incomplete forms, but subtle error types (ambiguous marks, faint pencil, mixed media) challenge reliable classification. Physically sorting/setting aside forms adds manipulation complexity.

task: Coordinate or perform activities associated with shipping, receiving, distribution, or transportation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = coordinating or performing a single shipping/receiving/distribution cycle (e.g., processing an inbound or outbound shipment), including sorting, labeling, staging, and logging. Typical warehouse shift unit is ~45 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Task spans coordination (software/communication), documentation, and physical handling across unstructured environments. Robots handle pick-and-place in structured warehouses but struggle with multi-step coordination, paperwork, varied package types, and unloading trucks autonomously.

task: Brace parts in position within hulls or ships for riveting or welding.
human_time_rationale: One instance = bracing a single structural part (plate, frame, or beam) into position within a hull section, including alignment, clamping/tacking. Typical skilled worker takes ~30–60 min per part.
robot_success_rationale: Shipyard hulls are vast, confined, irregular, and structurally complex. Requires heavy manipulation of large steel parts, precise alignment in tight curved spaces, and specialized tooling. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, payload, and navigation capabilities.

task: Lay out and fabricate metal structural parts such as plates, bulkheads, and frames.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out and fabricating a single structural metal part (e.g., a frame or bulkhead). A competent boilermaker/shipfitter takes ~1–3 hours per part including measuring, marking, cutting, and fitting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy industrial tools (grinders, torches, press brakes), precise layout on large stock, and force-intensive manipulation in unstructured shipyard/fab shop environments — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Inspect machined parts to verify conformance to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single machined part using calipers, gauges, or CMM checks; a competent inspector typically takes 5–15 minutes per part depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity to reliably handle precision metrology tools (calipers, micrometers, CMM probes). Vision-based inspection is feasible but sub-micron tolerance verification, tool manipulation, and part repositioning remain major hurdles.

task: Take inventory of headsets, alcoholic beverages, and money collected.
human_time_rationale: One inventory session: counting and recording headsets, alcohol bottles, and cash across a typical storage/event area.
robot_success_rationale: Counting varied objects requires perception and manipulation; cash counting demands fine dexterity and OCR; reading bottle labels and logging data needs generalization. No reliable end-to-end autonomous pipeline exists for combined multi-category inventory with financial reconciliation.

task: Lift and position workpieces in relation to surface plates, manually or with hoists, and using parallel blocks and angle plates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lifting and positioning a single workpiece onto a surface plate with blocks/angle plates. A skilled machinist/inspector takes ~10–20 min including alignment checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise metrology-grade positioning, hoist operation, coordinating parallel blocks/angle plates, and fine force control on heavy workpieces. Current humanoids lack the precision, tool interfacing, and heavy-load dexterity for this industrial task.

task: Weigh or measure materials or products to ensure conformance to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = weighing/measuring a single material or product batch, including reading the instrument and recording/comparing to spec. Typically 2–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place items onto scales and read digital displays via vision, but handling diverse containers, operating calipers/tape measures, and reliably recording/comparing results to specs across varied environments remains inconsistent with 2026 hardware.

task: Inspect coated or plated areas for defects, such as air bubbles or uneven coverage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single coated/plated part or panel. A competent worker visually scans for defects in ~2–4 minutes depending on part size.
robot_success_rationale: Robotic vision can detect obvious defects but struggles with subtle coating irregularities (minor bubbles, thin uneven coverage) under varied lighting. Lacks human perceptual nuance and contextual judgment; generalization across part geometries is limited.

task: Drive straight or articulated tractors equipped with accessories such as bulldozer blades, grapples, logging arches, cable winches, and crane booms to skid, load, unload, or stack logs, pull stumps, or clear brush.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a typical logging/skidding cycle (travel to site, attach/manipulate logs, skid/stack/load, return). A competent operator takes ~60–120 min per productive cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large, specialized heavy machinery across rough, unstructured outdoor terrain. No humanoid robot can drive or control articulated tractors, manage cable winches, or safely attach grapples to logs in real-world forestry conditions.

task: Install doors, hatches, brackets, and clips.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single door or hatch with associated brackets/clips. Competent worker handles alignment, fastening, and adjustment in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of heavy/large panels, multi-step fastening, hinge fitting, and force-controlled adjustments. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, strength calibration, and generalization for varied door/hatch types in unstructured settings.

task: Fit and align fabricated parts to be welded or assembled.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting and aligning a set of fabricated parts for a single weld joint or sub-assembly. A competent fitter typically spends 15–25 minutes measuring, positioning, clamping, and verifying alignment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise 6-DOF part placement, force-controlled clamping, and tight tolerance verification. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, rigid gripping force, and sub-millimeter perception needed for reliable industrial fit-up. Highly unstructured variability in part shapes adds further challenge.

task: Open and close gates of belt and pneumatic conveyors on machines that are fed directly from preceding machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = opening or closing a single gate on a belt/pneumatic conveyor. A competent worker locates the gate, actuates it (lever, knob, or switch), and confirms flow — typically under 2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Gates vary widely in handle type, force, and location. A humanoid can navigate the floor and attempt manipulation, but variable gate mechanisms, confined industrial spaces, and confirming flow state reduce reliability significantly.

task: Perform equipment maintenance, such as cleaning tanks and lubricating moving parts of conveyors.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance session covering cleaning a tank and lubricating conveyor parts; a competent worker typically completes this in ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated tool use (grease gun, cleaning equipment), navigating industrial environments, accessing confined spaces, and adapting to varied machinery — well beyond reliable autonomous capability for 2026 humanoids.

task: Drive and maneuver tractors and tree harvesters to shear the tops off of trees, cut and limb the trees, and cut the logs into desired lengths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single tree (shearing, felling, limbing, bucking to length). A skilled operator typically completes one tree in 30–60 minutes depending on size and terrain.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large specialized forestry vehicles on rough, unstructured outdoor terrain. Humanoid robots cannot drive or operate heavy machinery cabins; no current general-purpose robot has this capability at any useful reliability.

task: Transfer materials and products to and from machinery and equipment, using industrial trucks or hand trucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = transferring a single load between a machine/equipment station and staging area using a hand truck or industrial truck, including loading, transit, and unloading.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids cannot operate forklifts/powered industrial trucks; hand truck use requires gripping, balancing heavy loads, and navigating factory floors. Cluttered industrial environments, varied load shapes, and tool operation limit reliability significantly.

task: Examine products purchased for resale or received for storage to determine product condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one received product. A competent worker visually checks, handles, and logs condition in roughly 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can visually scan surfaces and detect obvious damage, but struggle with nuanced condition assessment (subtle dents, odors, texture, packaging integrity, cross-referencing specs), generalization across diverse product types, and reliable gripping for inspection.

task: Apply pigment to layout surfaces, using paint brushes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = painting one layout surface (e.g. a sign panel or wall section). A competent worker takes ~30 min including loading brush, applying even coats, and edging.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise brush pressure, stroke consistency, paint loading control, and edge following on varied surfaces. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, compliant force control, and visual feedback loops needed for acceptable paint quality.

task: Move controls and turn valves to start compressor engines, pumps, and auxiliary equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting a single compressor/pump unit: walk to panel, check gauges, move controls, turn valves, confirm startup. Typical startup sequence takes ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Turning industrial valves and control panels requires precise force, varied handle types, and safety-critical judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable torque sensing and generalization for industrial valve operation; misoperation risks are high.

task: Control hydraulic tractors equipped with tree clamps and booms to lift, swing, and bunch sheared trees.
human_time_rationale: One instance = bunching a single group of sheared trees (grab, swing, deposit). A skilled feller-buncher operator cycles through this in ~2–4 minutes per bunch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires seated operation of a large hydraulic tractor with specialized controls, outdoor rough terrain, complex real-time judgment. No humanoid robot can operate heavy forestry machinery autonomously at this level in 2026.

task: Monitor meters and pressure gauges to determine consumption rate variations, temperatures, and pressures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring round, reading and logging gauges/meters across a typical panel or facility section; a competent worker completes this in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate and use cameras to read digital displays, but analog gauge reading, varied gauge placements, poor lighting, parallax error, and logging results reliably remain significant challenges for current humanoids.

task: Measure log lengths and mark boles for bucking into logs, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and marking one felled tree bole for bucking. A competent timber worker uses a measuring tape and marker/chalk to complete this in ~5–10 minutes depending on tree length.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor rough terrain mobility over slash and debris, precise measurement along a large irregular log, applying physical marks, and interpreting bucking specs. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor forestry mobility, tool use, and judgment for this unstructured environment.

task: Adjust valves and equipment to obtain specified performance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a set of valves/equipment to hit a target operating spec; a skilled technician typically takes 10–20 min including reading gauges and fine-tuning.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting process feedback, and applying precise torque to varied valve types. Current humanoids lack reliable closed-loop process control, fine valve manipulation, and domain instrument perception.

task: Blend marble chip mixtures, place into panels, and push a roller over the surface to embed the chips.
human_time_rationale: One instance = blending a batch, filling one panel, and rolling the surface. A skilled terrazzo/composite worker completes this in ~20–30 minutes per panel.
robot_success_rationale: Requires blending viscous aggregate mixtures, controlled pouring/spreading into panel forms, and applying consistent rolling pressure — demanding force control, fluid handling, and surface-quality judgment that current humanoids lack reliably.

task: Read gas meters, and maintain records of the amounts of gas received and dispensed from holders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reading one gas meter and recording received/dispensed amounts. Worker walks to meter, reads dials/digital display, and logs data; ~15 min including travel within a facility.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to outdoor/industrial meters, reading analog or digital dials (perception challenge), and recording data. Current robots struggle with varied meter formats, outdoor terrain, and reliable data logging integration.

task: Immerse workpieces in coating solutions or liquid metal or plastic for specified times.
human_time_rationale: One instance = immersing a single workpiece (or batch) into a coating solution, holding for the specified dwell time, then withdrawing. Typical dip/immersion cycles run 1–10 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Gripping and lowering a workpiece into a vat is within reach, but variable workpiece geometries, precise timing, chemical/heat hazards, drip control, and handling fragile or awkwardly shaped parts reduce reliability significantly for a general-purpose humanoid.

task: Set up, operate, or tend plating or coating machines to coat metal or plastic products with chromium, zinc, copper, cadmium, nickel, or other metal to protect or decorate surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running a plating/coating machine for a production batch. A competent operator typically spends 30–60 min on setup, chemical checks, loading, and monitoring per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial machinery, managing caustic chemical baths, adjusting process parameters, and interpreting quality feedback. Current humanoids lack domain-specific tool interfaces, chemical sensing, and safe handling of hazardous liquids at production scale.

task: Position and feed materials into processing machines, by hand or by using automated equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning and feeding a single batch/item into a processing machine. A competent worker typically completes this in ~3 minutes including aligning, loading, and confirming feed.
robot_success_rationale: Structured pick-and-place feeding is feasible for current humanoids, but precise alignment, varied material types, and machine interface diversity reduce reliability. Automated equipment interaction adds complexity beyond simple bin picking.

task: Remove objects from solutions at periodic intervals and observe objects to verify conformance to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one object from solution and inspecting it for spec conformance; a skilled worker takes ~5 min including observation and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can grasp and lift objects from liquid containers but struggles with wet/slippery surfaces, precise visual inspection for spec conformance, and judgment calls on borderline cases. Perception of surface quality in solution is limited.

task: Turn knobs or switches to regulate pressures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a knob/switch to set a target pressure reading; a skilled worker locates the control, turns it, and confirms the reading in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Turning knobs requires precise finger dexterity and torque control; reading analog gauges adds perception challenge. Current humanoids can grasp knobs but struggle with fine rotational precision and closed-loop feedback from gauges.

task: Paint or varnish decks, superstructures, lifeboats, or sides of ships.
human_time_rationale: One instance = painting one major ship section (e.g., a deck or superstructure panel). A competent marine painter takes ~3–5 hours per section including prep, masking, and application.
robot_success_rationale: Ship surfaces are large, unstructured, and outdoors with complex geometry. Requires navigating ladders, scaffolding, and curved hull surfaces. Humanoid robots lack the mobility, reach, and tool-use capability for marine painting at acceptable quality.

task: Immerse objects to be coated or plated into cleaning solutions, or spray objects with conductive solutions to prepare them for plating.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single batch/object: dipping or spraying one item or rack of items with cleaning/conductive solution, including handling, immersion, and retrieval.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping varied part geometries, precise immersion into chemical baths, and controlled spraying — challenging for dexterity and perception. Chemical handling safety, wet/slippery conditions, and process consistency reduce reliability significantly for current humanoids.

task: Shovel or scoop materials into containers, machines, or equipment for processing, storage, or transport.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shoveling/scooping a typical batch of material (e.g., one bin, hopper, or container load). A competent worker completes this in ~5–10 minutes depending on volume and material.
robot_success_rationale: Requires forceful, coordinated shoveling or scooping into a target container. Humanoids lack the sustained force output, stable bipedal bracing, and reliable tool-use generalization needed; loose/variable materials (grain, gravel, powder) add perception and manipulation difficulty.

task: Measure felled logs or loads of pulpwood to calculate volume, weight, dimensions, and marketable value, using measuring devices and conversion tables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single load/set of felled logs. A skilled scaler measures diameters, lengths, and tallies conversions for one truckload in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor mobility on rough terrain, precise dimensional measurement of irregular logs, reading/applying conversion tables, and judgment on defects. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor locomotion, fine measurement tool use, and domain-specific scaling knowledge.

task: Paint identification marks of specified colors on logs to identify grades or species, using spray cans, or call out grades to log markers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking one log. A competent grader sprays or calls out a color mark on a single log in roughly 1–3 minutes including inspection and marking.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/uneven terrain navigation around large irregular logs, gripping and aiming a spray can accurately on rough bark surfaces, and making grading judgments. Perception of log species/grade adds cognitive complexity well beyond standard pick-and-place.

task: Position containers to receive parts, and load or unload materials in containers, using dollies or handtrucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning a container and loading/unloading its contents using a dolly or handtruck. A competent worker typically completes this in ~8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick-and-place common objects but struggle to reliably operate handtrucks/dollies, navigate varied warehouse floors, position containers precisely, and handle diverse part shapes autonomously at acceptable quality.

task: Adjust controls to set temperatures of coating substances and speeds of machines and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting temperature and speed controls on one machine or equipment unit; a skilled operator typically reads gauges and turns dials/enters values in 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, identifying varied control interfaces (knobs, keypads, touchscreens), and making precise fine adjustments. Current humanoids lack reliable perception+dexterous manipulation of industrial control panels and generalization across equipment types.

task: Test machinery to ensure that it is operating properly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single machine unit. A competent technician runs diagnostics, checks outputs, and verifies operation — typically 15–30 minutes per machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting varied gauges, controls, and feedback; operating machine-specific interfaces; and making judgment calls on abnormal readings. Current humanoids lack generalized tool/control operation and contextual fault diagnosis for arbitrary machinery.

task: Operate power-driven pumps that transfer liquids, semi-liquids, gases, or powdered materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one transfer cycle (startup, monitor, shutdown). Competent operator spends ~20 min on checks, adjustments, and safe shutdown.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial controls, valves, gauges, and hoses in varied environments. Current humanoids lack the tool-use generalization, fluid-system awareness, and safety judgment needed for autonomous pump operation.

task: Cut metal division strips and press them into the terrazzo base for joints or changes of color to form designs or patterns or to help prevent cracks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting and pressing strips for a typical design section (~10–15 linear feet). A skilled terrazzo worker measures, cuts, and seats strips in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measuring/cutting of metal strips, then pressing them flush into a semi-wet terrazzo base at exact positions to form patterns. Demands fine dexterity, force control, tool use (snips, mallet), and spatial pattern planning — well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Clean and maintain equipment, using water hoses and scrapers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one piece of equipment (e.g., a machine or vehicle). A competent worker using a hose and scraper typically takes 20–30 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated tool use (hose + scraper), forceful scraping on irregular surfaces, and judgment on when surfaces are clean. Current humanoids lack robust tool-switching, adequate force control, and reliable perception of dirt/residue on varied equipment geometries.

task: Take samples of gases and conduct chemical tests to determine gas quality and sulfur or moisture content, or send samples to laboratories for analysis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a gas sample and conducting on-site chemical tests (or preparing sample for lab). A competent technician typically takes 20–40 minutes per sample point.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of gas sampling equipment, valves, and test kits in potentially hazardous industrial environments; chemical test interpretation, safe handling of pressurized/toxic gases, and lab coordination are far beyond current robot generalization.

task: Grade logs according to characteristics such as knot size and straightness, and according to established industry or company standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grading one log. A skilled log grader visually inspects knot size, straightness, and defects per industry standards — typically 2–5 minutes per log.
robot_success_rationale: Requires 360° visual inspection of large, heavy, irregular logs outdoors/in mills, measuring knot sizes, assessing straightness, and applying nuanced grading standards. Current robots lack reliable outdoor mobility, precise measurement tools, and domain-specific grading judgment for unstructured log geometry.

task: Diagnose problems with installed equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing a problem on one piece of installed equipment. A competent technician typically spends ~30 min inspecting, testing, and identifying the fault.
robot_success_rationale: Diagnosis requires multi-modal sensing, domain knowledge, fault reasoning, and often tool use on varied installed equipment. Current robots lack reliable autonomous fault diagnosis, interpretive reasoning, and generalization across equipment types.

task: Identify logs of substandard or special grade so that they can be returned to shippers, regraded, recut, or transferred for other processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and identifying one log for grade. A skilled grader visually assesses defects, dimensions, and species characteristics in ~2–4 minutes per log.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert visual/tactile inspection of complex natural surfaces (knots, splits, rot, sweep, taper) in an outdoor/mill yard environment. Current robots lack reliable grading perception, uneven terrain mobility, and domain-specific judgment needed for autonomous quality decisions.

task: Turn valves and start pumps to start or regulate flows of substances such as gases, liquids, slurries, or powdered materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating and adjusting one valve/pump to initiate or regulate flow; a competent operator typically completes this in ~5 minutes including verification.
robot_success_rationale: Turning industrial valves requires moderate dexterity and significant grip force on varied valve types (gate, ball, lever). Robots struggle with torque demands, non-standard valve locations, and confirming correct flow outcome. Perception and generalization remain limited in industrial environments.

task: Grind surfaces with a power grinder, or polish surfaces with polishing or surfacing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grinding/polishing a single workpiece or surface area. A competent worker sets up, grinds/polishes, and inspects in ~20 minutes on average.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained force control, precise tool-path planning, and real-time surface quality feedback. Current humanoids lack reliable compliant force regulation and tactile/visual surface inspection needed for acceptable finish quality.

task: Operate hoists to place workpieces onto machine feed carriages or spindles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching rigging to a workpiece, hoisting it, positioning it onto a feed carriage or spindle, and securing it. Typical cycle in a machine shop: ~5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating overhead hoist controls, precise load positioning onto spindles/carriages, rigging attachment, and spatial reasoning under load dynamics. Current humanoids lack hoist-operation interfaces, load-control precision, and force feedback for tight-tolerance spindle seating.

task: Measure, mark, and mask areas to be excluded from plating.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring, marking, and masking a single workpiece. A skilled plating technician typically takes 15–25 min depending on part complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement of irregular surfaces, fine-motor marking, and careful application of masking tape/plugs to tight tolerances. Current robots lack the dexterity, tactile feedback, and adaptive masking skills needed for acceptable quality.

task: Connect pipelines between pumps and containers that are being filled or emptied.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting a single pipeline segment between a pump and one container; includes fetching hose/pipe, aligning couplings, and securing fittings. Typical industrial task takes ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of threaded or cam-lock fittings, high insertion force, leak-free sealing, and tool use in cluttered industrial environments. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force control, and generalization for reliable coupling connections.

task: Saw felled trees into lengths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sawing one felled tree into standard lengths. A competent logger with a chainsaw takes ~10–20 minutes depending on tree size and number of cuts.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a chainsaw or heavy bucking equipment on uneven outdoor terrain with irregular log shapes. Humanoid robots lack the stability, grip strength, tool-operation capability, and safety control needed for chainsaw use in unstructured forest environments.

task: Weigh log trucks before and after unloading, and record load weights and supplier identities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one truck weighing cycle (before + after unloading). Includes directing truck onto scale, recording tare/gross weights, and logging supplier ID — ~10 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating an industrial truck scale terminal, reading truck/supplier IDs, and coordinating with moving vehicles outdoors on rough surfaces. Humanoids lack vehicle-interface tools, outdoor mobility reliability, and systems integration for scale terminals.

task: Monitor and measure thicknesses of electroplating on component parts to verify conformance to specifications, using micrometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring plating thickness on a single component part. A skilled worker picks up the part, takes several micrometer readings, and records/compares to spec in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise grasping of small parts, fine dexterous manipulation of a micrometer, consistent contact force for accurate readings, and interpretation of analog/digital gauge output. Current humanoids lack the fine-motor precision and calibrated tool-use for reliable metrology work.

task: Clean, lubricate, and adjust equipment, and replace filters and gaskets, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full clean/lube/adjust cycle on a single piece of equipment, including filter and gasket replacement. A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse dexterous hand-tool use, precise torque/adjustment judgment, gasket seating, lubrication targeting, and fault diagnosis across highly variable equipment types — well beyond current humanoid general-purpose capability.

task: Rinse coated objects in cleansing liquids and dry them with cloths, centrifugal driers, or by tumbling in sawdust-filled barrels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rinsing and drying a batch of coated objects. A competent worker handles immersion, draining, and drying method selection in ~5 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Grasping wet/coated irregular objects, transferring them through liquid baths, and managing centrifugal driers or sawdust barrels requires robust dexterity, tool-use, and process judgment. Current humanoids struggle with wet/slippery items and multi-step industrial equipment operation.

task: Test materials and solutions, using testing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single material/solution sample. Includes setup, running the test, reading equipment output, and recording results. ~20 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise handling of vials/pipettes, operating varied lab instruments, interpreting readings, and adapting to unstructured lab environments. Fine dexterity and tool-use generalization remain major limitations for current humanoids.

task: Tend vessels that store substances such as gases, liquids, slurries, or powdered materials, checking levels of substances by using calibrated rods or by reading mercury gauges and tank charts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking and recording levels on a single vessel/tank. A competent worker walks to the tank, uses a calibrated rod or reads a gauge/chart, and logs the reading — roughly 10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating industrial terrain, locating gauges/rods, reading analog mercury gauges and tank charts (fine visual perception), inserting calibrated rods into vessels, and interpreting results. Locomotion is feasible, but fine tool-use, analog gauge reading, and varied vessel configurations are major barriers for current humanoids.

task: Replace worn parts and adjust equipment components, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing a worn part and adjusting one component (e.g., a belt, bearing, or fastener). A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min diagnosing, swapping, and adjusting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing worn parts, selecting correct tools, applying precise torque, and making fine adjustments. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use dexterity, fault diagnosis, and generalization across varied equipment types.

task: Drive to sawmills, wharfs, or skids to inspect logs or pulpwood.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single drive to a sawmill, wharf, or skid site for inspection. Includes travel, parking, and arriving on-site; typical round-trip drive estimated at ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a vehicle on public/rural roads to remote industrial sites — humanoid robots cannot operate trucks or ATVs in traffic. Outdoor terrain, unstructured roads, and vehicle operation are beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Measure designated amounts of ingredients for terrazzo or grout, according to standard formulas and specifications, using graduated containers and scales, and load ingredients into portable mixer.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and loading one batch of terrazzo/grout mix. A skilled worker reads specs, weighs/measures components, and loads the mixer in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading spec sheets, using graduated containers and scales precisely, handling bulk granular/powdery materials, and loading a portable mixer. Perception of graduated markings, fine pouring control, and generalization across varied containers are weak spots for current humanoids.

task: Clean, lubricate, and repair pumps and vessels, using hand tools and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing a single pump or vessel unit (clean, lube, minor repair). A competent mechanic typically takes 60–120 minutes per unit depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires strong dexterous tool use (wrenches, grease guns, seals), fault diagnosis, working in confined/unstructured industrial spaces, and judgment-based repair decisions — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Conduct materials test and analysis, using tools and equipment and applying engineering knowledge.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one materials test cycle (setup, specimen prep, running test, recording results). A competent technician typically takes 60–120 min depending on test type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous specimen handling, operating specialized lab equipment (tensile testers, furnaces, spectrometers), interpreting complex results, and applying engineering judgment — far beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Monitor gauges and flowmeters and inspect equipment to ensure that tank levels, temperatures, chemical amounts, and pressures are at specified levels, reporting abnormalities as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One inspection round of a typical process area — reading gauges, flowmeters, and tank levels — takes a competent operator ~10–20 min per round.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate and read digital displays reasonably well, but analog gauge reading in varied industrial lighting, identifying abnormalities, and reliably reporting across diverse equipment layouts remain significant challenges for current frontier humanoids.

task: Examine completed objects to determine thicknesses of metal deposits, or measure thicknesses by using instruments such as micrometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring metal deposit thickness on a single object. A competent worker picks up the part, uses a micrometer or visual inspection, and records the result in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can grasp objects and hold instruments, but precise micrometer placement requires sub-millimeter dexterity and tactile feedback. Generalizing across varied part geometries and tool handling remains unreliable for current humanoids.

task: Repair concrete by cutting out damaged areas, drilling holes for reinforcing rods, and positioning reinforcing rods, using power saw and drill.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single damaged concrete area. Cutting, drilling multiple holes, and positioning rebar typically takes a skilled worker 60–120 minutes depending on damage size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a heavy power saw and hammer drill with high force and precision on variable concrete surfaces, then positioning rebar accurately. Current humanoid robots lack the strength, tool-handling stability, and task generalization for this multi-step construction workflow.

task: Jab logs with metal ends of scale sticks, and inspect logs to ascertain characteristics or defects such as water damage, splits, knots, broken ends, rotten areas, twists, and curves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one log. Skilled log scaler jabs and visually assesses one log for defects in ~2–4 minutes including recording findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/unstructured terrain locomotion near heavy logs, forceful jabbing with a tool, and nuanced visual+tactile defect detection (rot, splits, knots, twists). Current humanoids lack the force control, domain-specific perception, and rough-terrain stability needed.

task: Grind curved surfaces or areas inaccessible to surfacing machine, such as stairways or cabinet tops, with portable hand grinder.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grinding one curved/inaccessible surface area (e.g., a stairway tread edge or cabinet top). A competent worker typically takes ~20–30 min per section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires compliant force control on curved/irregular surfaces, navigating tight/inaccessible spaces, and operating a vibrating handheld power tool with precision. Current humanoids lack reliable contact-force regulation and surface-adaptive grinding on non-flat geometry.

task: Suspend sticks or pieces of plating metal from anodes, or positive terminals, and immerse metal in plating solutions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = suspending a batch of metal sticks/plates from anodes and immersing them in a plating solution tank; a skilled plater takes ~5–10 minutes per batch setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise hanging/hooking of irregular metal pieces onto anode terminals, careful immersion into chemical baths, and spatial awareness around hazardous liquids. Current robots lack reliable fine dexterity for hooking and safe chemical-environment operation.

task: Adjust dials to regulate flow of current and voltage supplied to terminals to control plating processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting dials to set current/voltage for a single plating run; a skilled operator reads gauges and turns dials to target values in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting process feedback, and precisely turning small dials to exact setpoints. Perception of panel readings and fine rotational dial control remain unreliable for current humanoids; process judgment adds further difficulty.

task: Preheat workpieces in ovens.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a workpiece (or batch) into an industrial oven, setting temperature/timer, and confirming it's preheating. Excludes the wait time itself.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to oven, opening heavy industrial door, placing/orienting workpieces, and operating controls. Door handles, heavy loads, and non-standard oven interfaces challenge current humanoids' dexterity and generalization.

task: Clean workpieces, using wire brushes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one workpiece with a wire brush. A competent worker scrubs off debris/rust/scale from a typical part in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can grasp a brush and move it over a surface, but applying consistent pressure, adapting to irregular workpiece geometry, and judging cleanliness requires fine force control and perception that current humanoids handle poorly.

task: Observe gauges to ensure that machines are operating properly, making adjustments or stopping machines when problems occur.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single gauge-observation round on one machine, including reading values, judging normalcy, and making any adjustment. A competent operator completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read digital gauges via vision, but analog gauges, varied layouts, and the judgment + physical intervention needed (adjusting valves/controls, deciding to stop a machine) require complex reasoning, dexterity, and generalization that frontier robots handle poorly in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Spread, level, or smooth concrete or terrazzo mixtures to form bases or finished surfaces, using rakes, shovels, hand or power trowels, hand or power screeds, or floats.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing a standard concrete pour (e.g., ~100 sq ft slab). A skilled finisher spreads, screeds, and trowels to acceptable flatness in ~45–75 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated whole-body force application with heavy tools (screeds, power trowels) on variable wet surfaces outdoors. Current humanoids lack the strength, dynamic balance, and tactile feedback for quality concrete finishing. No commercial deployment exists.

task: Position and secure moisture membrane and wire mesh in preparation for pouring base materials for terrazzo installation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a typical room (~200–400 sq ft). Laying, cutting, overlapping, and stapling/taping moisture membrane then placing and securing wire mesh takes a skilled worker ~60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires unrolling, cutting, overlapping, and securing large flexible sheet membrane, then positioning and fastening heavy wire mesh rolls across a floor — demanding bimanual coordination, force control, and spatial planning far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Perform ongoing hardware and software maintenance operations, including installing or upgrading hardware or software.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance session (install/upgrade of one hardware component or software package). A competent IT tech typically takes ~60 min including prep, execution, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation (swapping RAM, HDDs, PCIe cards), navigating complex software UIs/CLIs, contextual troubleshooting, and system-specific knowledge. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot autonomy.

task: Fill slight grinding depressions with matching grout material and hand-trowel for a smooth, uniform surface.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling and troweling grout into depressions across a typical small surface section (~1–2 sq m). A skilled worker mixes, applies, and smooths in ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous trowel control, tactile feedback to judge fill depth and surface uniformity, color/texture matching, and working on varied floor surfaces. Current humanoids lack the precision and haptic feedback needed for acceptable surface finishing quality.

task: Assess performers' skin type to ensure that makeup will not cause break-outs or skin irritations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing one performer's skin. A competent makeup artist visually inspects, asks questions, and possibly patch-tests skin in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced skin-tone/texture perception, medical-adjacent allergy history intake, and tactile skin assessment. Current robots lack reliable dermatological vision, social communication skills, and contextual medical judgment needed here.

task: Suspend objects, such as parts or molds from cathode rods, or negative terminals, and immerse objects in plating solutions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = hanging a batch of parts/molds onto cathode rods and immersing them in a plating tank; a skilled plater takes ~5–10 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise hook/wire attachment to irregularly shaped parts, correct orientation on cathode rods, and controlled immersion in hazardous liquid. Dexterous manipulation of varied parts and fine positional accuracy near chemical baths challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Wash polished terrazzo surface, using cleaner and water, and apply sealer and curing agent according to manufacturer's specifications, using brush or sprayer.
human_time_rationale: One instance = washing and sealing one standard terrazzo floor section (~100 sq ft). Competent worker spends ~30 min washing/rinsing and ~30 min applying sealer/curing agent.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated multi-step chemical workflow: washing, rinsing, then precision application of sealer/curing agent via brush or sprayer per spec. Current humanoids lack reliable fluid-tool handling, consistent coverage judgment, and chemical sequencing autonomy.

task: Set up and operate grinding or polishing machines to grind metal workpieces, such as dies, parts, and tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setup plus grinding/polishing one typical metal workpiece (die, part, or tool). A competent machinist takes ~45 min including setup, alignment, and finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise machine setup, workpiece fixturing, parameter adjustment, and quality judgment. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force-feedback control, and domain-specific tool-use needed to operate grinding machines safely and to spec.

task: Mold expansion joints and edges, using edging tools, jointers, or straightedges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing the joints/edges of a single concrete slab pour (e.g., sidewalk panel or floor section). A skilled worker typically spends 5–12 minutes per panel running edgers and jointers.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled tool manipulation on wet concrete, reading surface consistency, navigating around formwork outdoors, and producing aesthetically/structurally acceptable results. Current humanoids lack the fine haptic feedback, tool-use dexterity, and outdoor terrain adaptability needed.

task: Inspect project sites to monitor progress and ensure conformance to design specifications and safety or sanitation standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single site inspection visit. A competent inspector typically spends 60–120 minutes walking the site, checking work against drawings, documenting findings, and noting safety/sanitation issues.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating unstructured, hazardous construction terrain, reading plans, comparing work to spec, and making expert judgments. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor mobility, document comprehension, and domain-specific inspection reasoning.

task: Connect hoses and pipelines to pumps and vessels prior to material transfer, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting a hose/pipeline to one pump or vessel. A competent worker locates fittings, aligns, and tightens connections with hand tools in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of threaded or flanged fittings, high-torque hand-tool use, leak-free sealing, and navigation in cluttered industrial environments. Dexterity and force-control for tool use remain well below human standards in 2026.

task: Tend auxiliary equipment such as water treatment and refrigeration units, and heat exchangers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single inspection/tending round covering auxiliary equipment (water treatment, refrigeration, heat exchangers), reading gauges, checking levels, and making minor adjustments.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating industrial environments, reading analog/digital gauges, operating valves and controls, and interpreting equipment status. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation of industrial controls, gauge-reading accuracy, and domain-specific fault diagnosis in unstructured plant environments.

task: Maintain each station by performing general housekeeping duties such as painting, washing, and cleaning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one maintenance session at a single station, covering light painting touch-ups, washing surfaces, and general cleaning; a competent worker typically spends ~45 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Sweeping/wiping surfaces is feasible (~40–70%), but painting (requires brush control, even coating, masking) and washing varied station equipment demand fine dexterity, tool-switching, and generalization that current humanoids handle poorly together.

task: Inspect, feel, and measure workpieces to ensure that surfaces and dimensions meet specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single workpiece. A competent worker uses calipers, gauges, and tactile feel to check surfaces and dimensions, typically taking ~5 minutes per part.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can use vision for surface checks but tactile sensing and precision measurement tool use (calipers, micrometers) remain unreliable. Grasping and repositioning varied workpieces adds further difficulty. Generalization across part geometries is limited.

task: Wet surface to prepare for bonding, fill holes and cracks with grout or slurry, and smooth with a trowel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a typical surface section (e.g., ~1–2 m²): wetting, filling holes/cracks with grout/slurry, and trowel-smoothing. A competent mason completes this in ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated multi-step tool use (water application, grout mixing/application, trowel smoothing) with force-sensitive finishing, crack detection, and unstructured surfaces. Far beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Alter or maintain makeup during productions as necessary to compensate for lighting changes or to achieve continuity of effect.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single touch-up session on one performer between scenes/takes; a skilled makeup artist typically spends 5–15 minutes adjusting, blending, and checking under lights.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity on a human face, nuanced color/lighting perception, real-time aesthetic judgment, and social compliance from the subject. Current humanoids lack the precision, soft-touch control, and creative perception needed.

task: Write makeup sheets and take photos to document specific looks and the products used to achieve the looks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = documenting one makeup look: applying makeup, writing the product sheet, and photographing the finished look. A skilled makeup artist/coordinator takes ~45 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine facial application (sub-5% dexterity), creative aesthetic judgment, written documentation, and controlled photography. No current humanoid robot can reliably apply makeup to a human face or produce acceptable documentation autonomously.

task: Clean chipped area, using wire brush, and feel and observe surface to determine if it is rough or uneven.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and inspecting a single chipped area. A competent worker scrubs with a wire brush and tactilely/visually assesses the surface in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Wire brush scrubbing requires forceful, controlled dexterity on irregular surfaces. Tactile sensing to detect roughness/unevenness is rudimentary in current humanoids. Visual inspection of subtle surface defects also remains unreliable. Combined, success probability is very low.

task: Duplicate work precisely to replicate characters' appearances on a daily basis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying full costume makeup/hair/wardrobe for one character on one actor. A skilled makeup artist typically takes 30–60 minutes for a standard daily look.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterous manipulation on a human face (makeup application, hair styling, costume fitting), precise color matching, and high aesthetic judgment. Far beyond 2026 robot capability; risk of injury is a hard blocker.

task: Signal truck driver to position truck to facilitate pouring concrete and move chute to direct concrete on forms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = signaling one truck into position and adjusting the chute for a single pour setup. A competent worker does this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor unstructured terrain navigation, interpreting/communicating hand signals to a human truck driver, and physically repositioning a heavy concrete chute — all highly challenging for current humanoid robots. No frontier robot handles this reliably.

task: Position objects to be plated in frames, or suspend them from positive or negative terminals of power supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning/hanging a single workpiece in a plating frame or on a terminal. A competent worker does this in ~5 minutes including alignment and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous manipulation to hang or clip small parts onto frames/terminals, fine grip force control to avoid damage, and reliable electrical contact verification. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity and tactile feedback needed for consistent success.

task: File or finish surfaces of workpieces, using prescribed hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filing/finishing one workpiece surface. A competent machinist/metalworker typically spends 10–20 min per surface depending on material and tolerance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained, precise force-controlled strokes with a hand file, constant tactile feedback to gauge surface flatness/finish, and fine positional accuracy. Current humanoids lack the force sensitivity and dexterous grip consistency needed for acceptable surface quality.

task: Mix cement, sand, and water to produce concrete, grout, or slurry, using hoe, trowel, tamper, scraper, or concrete-mixing machine.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing one batch (~1–2 cubic feet) of concrete/grout/slurry by hand or small machine; a competent mason typically takes 15–25 minutes including measuring, mixing, and checking consistency.
robot_success_rationale: Requires measuring and combining bulk materials, wielding a hoe or trowel in a trough/mixer, and judging slump consistency. Current humanoids lack reliable manipulation of heavy bulk materials, fluid consistency perception, and tool-force control for mixing motions.

task: Measure and set stops, rolls, brushes, and guides on automatic feeders and conveying equipment or coating machines, using micrometers, rules, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One setup instance on a single machine — measuring, adjusting, and setting multiple components (stops, rolls, brushes, guides) typically takes 20–30 min for a skilled operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise micrometer readings, fine tool manipulation on varied machine components, and nuanced judgment about tolerances. Beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and generalization across machine types.

task: Precast terrazzo blocks in wooden forms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = casting one batch of terrazzo blocks in a set of wooden forms: mixing material, pouring, leveling, and setting to cure. Roughly 30–60 min of active labor.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise mixing of aggregate/cement slurry, controlled pouring into wooden forms, leveling, and vibrating to remove air — all high-dexterity, force-sensitive steps with wet/heavy materials. Current humanoids lack the tool-use fluency and material-handling precision needed.

task: Build wooden molds, clamping molds around areas to be repaired, or setting up frames to the proper depth and alignment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = building/setting one mold or frame for a single repair area. A skilled worker typically spends 30–60 min measuring, cutting, fitting, clamping, and aligning.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, sawing lumber to spec, multi-step spatial alignment, and applying clamping force with judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use generalization; no 2026 robot handles this end-to-end.

task: Create character drawings or models, based upon independent research, to augment period production files.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one character drawing/model created from research. A competent costume/production artist typically spends 2–4 hours researching and drafting a detailed period character rendering.
robot_success_rationale: This is a creative, cognitively driven task requiring art skills, historical research, and aesthetic judgment. Current humanoid robots have no capacity for independent artistic research, illustration, or 3D model creation.

task: Clean installation site, mixing and storage areas, tools, machines, and equipment, and store materials and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing and cleaning a typical installation/mixing/storage site, wiping/rinsing tools and machines, and stowing materials. A competent worker takes ~45 min end-of-shift.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating cluttered, unstructured outdoor/industrial sites; identifying and sorting varied tools, chemicals, and equipment; operating hoses/brushes; and making contextual storage decisions — far beyond current general-purpose robot generalization and manipulation capabilities.

task: Modify mixing, grouting, grinding, or cleaning procedures, according to type of installation or material used.
human_time_rationale: One instance = evaluating one installation/material context and adapting the procedure. A skilled tradesperson takes ~10–20 min to assess and adjust their mixing, grouting, grinding, or cleaning approach.
robot_success_rationale: Requires contextual judgment, material identification, and adaptive decision-making across variable trade scenarios. Current robots lack reliable material perception, procedural reasoning, and the dexterous tool-use needed to autonomously modify complex trade workflows.

task: Pump two or more materials into one tank to blend mixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up, running, and confirming one blending cycle in a tank. Includes connecting lines, setting flow rates, monitoring, and shutting off pumps. Typical industrial batch: ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating industrial pump controls, valves, and flow meters — specialized equipment a humanoid cannot reliably interface with. Fluid monitoring, hose coupling, and process verification add further perception and dexterity challenges beyond current capability.

task: Spread roofing paper on surface of foundation and spread concrete onto roofing paper with trowel to form terrazzo base.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying roofing paper and troweling concrete base over a standard ~10 sq ft section; a skilled terrazzo worker takes ~45 min per section.
