robot_success_rationale: Requires precise large-area material laying, then skilled troweling of wet concrete to uniform thickness and smoothness — demanding bimanual dexterity, force modulation, and tool-use generalisation far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Move terrazzo installation materials, tools, machines, or work devices to work areas, manually or using wheelbarrow.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving a load of materials/tools to a work area. A competent worker loads and transports one wheelbarrow or armful of terrazzo materials across a job site in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating cluttered, uneven construction sites, loading/unloading heavy/varied materials, and operating a wheelbarrow. Outdoor rough terrain, tool variety, and unpredictable job-site layouts severely limit current humanoid capability.

task: Provide performers with makeup removal assistance after performances have been completed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one performer in removing stage makeup. Competent makeup assistant typically takes 10–20 minutes depending on makeup complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate touch near eyes/face, precise force control, skin-safe tool handling, and responsive interaction with a human subject. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tactile sensitivity, and safe face-contact capability needed.

task: Adjust handwheels and depress pedals to control attachments, such as blades, buckets, scrapers, or swing booms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one continuous work session operating attachments (e.g., grading a section, loading a bucket). ~30 min is a typical measurable unit of productive equipment operation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires seated operation of heavy construction equipment via handwheels and foot pedals. Humanoid robots cannot safely operate such machinery in dynamic, unstructured outdoor environments; no commercially viable autonomous humanoid solution exists for this.

task: Select and mount grinding wheels on machines, according to specifications, using hand tools and applying knowledge of abrasives and grinding procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting and mounting one grinding wheel on one machine. A skilled machinist spends ~10–20 min inspecting, selecting, fitting flanges, torquing, and ring-testing a wheel.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading specs/labels, selecting correct abrasive wheel from inventory, precise torquing with hand tools, ring-testing for cracks, and safety-critical alignment — demanding perception, dexterity, and domain knowledge beyond 2026 frontier robots.

task: Mix and test solutions, and turn valves to fill tanks with solutions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing a batch solution, running a quick test (e.g. pH strip or meter), and turning valves to fill one tank. Typical industrial cycle ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid handling, use of test instruments (pH meter, pipettes), interpreting results, and operating industrial valves — multi-step dexterous + perception task with high failure risk at each step for current humanoids.

task: Align machines, cutterheads, or depth gauge makers with reference stakes and guidelines or ground or position equipment, following hand signals of other workers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning a single machine/cutterhead to reference stakes for one setup pass; a competent operator typically takes 10–20 minutes per alignment setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy ground-engaging machinery, interpreting hand signals from co-workers, reading physical reference stakes, and making fine positional adjustments via machine controls — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured outdoor/construction environments.

task: Shade or color drawings to clarify and emphasize details and dimensions or eliminate background, using ink, crayon, airbrush, and overlays.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shading/coloring one technical drawing. A competent drafter/illustrator typically spends 30–60 min applying ink, crayon, airbrush, or overlays to a moderately complex drawing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine artistic dexterity with multiple specialized tools (airbrush, ink, crayon), aesthetic judgment, and creative interpretation. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control, tool-switching fluency, and visual-artistic reasoning needed.

task: Dress grinding wheels, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dressing a single grinding wheel to spec; a skilled operator typically takes 5–15 minutes to mount the dresser, traverse the wheel face, and verify profile/condition.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force/feed control of a dressing tool across a spinning wheel, reading spec tolerances, and operating grinding machine controls — fine dexterous tool-use on industrial machinery far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Cleanse and tone the skin to prepare it for makeup application.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleansing and toning one person's face before makeup. A competent human (e.g., makeup artist or esthetician) typically takes ~5 minutes for both steps.
robot_success_rationale: Requires gentle, precise contact with a human face, applying liquids with controlled pressure, and reading subtle skin reactions. Current humanoids lack the delicate touch, safe force control near the eyes, and generalization needed for this intimate caregiving task.

task: Chip, scrape, or grind high spots, ridges, or rough projections to finish concrete, using pneumatic chisel, hand chisel, or other hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing one surface section (~1–2 sq meters). A skilled concrete finisher takes ~15–25 min to locate, chip, scrape, and smooth high spots to acceptable quality.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled contact with irregular concrete surfaces, accurate perception of subtle high spots/ridges, and skilled tool-use (pneumatic/hand chisel). Current robots lack the fine haptic feedback, surface-reading, and generalized tool-use needed for acceptable finish quality.

task: Sprinkle colored marble or stone chips, powdered steel, or coloring powder over surface to produce prescribed finish.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sprinkling decorative material over one surface (e.g., a countertop slab). A skilled worker spreads and adjusts coverage uniformly in roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires controlled, even dispersal of loose granular/powder materials over a surface with aesthetic judgment. Robots lack fine powder-handling dexterity, even distribution control, and visual quality assessment for prescribed finishes.

task: Remove excess materials or impurities from objects, using air hoses or grinding machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one workpiece (e.g., a casting or machined part) with an air hose or grinder; a competent worker typically takes 5–12 minutes per part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool-switching (air hose vs. grinder), force-regulated contact on irregular surfaces, and adaptive perception of impurities/burrs. Current humanoids lack consistent dexterous tool-use and fine force control for varied workpieces.

task: Perform basic maintenance, such as cleaning and lubricating machine parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one maintenance session on a single machine (e.g., cleaning debris and applying lubricant to moving parts). A competent worker typically takes 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires generalized tool use (brushes, rags, lubrication guns), precise application to small/varied parts, and adaptive perception in cluttered environments. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and task generalization for unstructured maintenance workflows.

task: Apply makeup to enhance or alter the appearance of people appearing in productions such as movies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full makeup application for one actor/performer in a production. A competent makeup artist typically takes 30–60 minutes depending on complexity; 45 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity near the human face, skin-tone perception, artistic judgment, and adaptive technique. Current humanoids lack the precision, compliant touch, and creative generalization needed. Safety risk near eyes/face further limits viability.

task: Select desired makeup shades from stock, or mix oil, grease, and coloring to achieve specific color effects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting or mixing makeup for a single color effect. A skilled makeup artist takes ~5–10 min to identify, blend, and verify a target shade.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine color perception with artistic judgment, precise small-volume mixing of viscous substances, and subjective quality validation. Current robots lack the dexterity, tactile sensitivity, and aesthetic reasoning needed for reliable success.

task: Duplicate workpiece contours, using tracer attachments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tracing and duplicating a single workpiece contour. A skilled machinist sets up the tracer attachment, follows the template, and completes the cut in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized machine-tool controls, mounting/aligning tracer attachments, and making precision adjustments. Humanoid robots lack the fine dexterity, domain-specific tool-use, and machine-interfacing capability needed for this task.

task: Add chemicals and solutions to tanks to ensure that specifications are met.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dosing one tank. Includes reading specs, selecting, measuring, and adding chemical — typical in water treatment or industrial settings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading gauges/sensors, identifying correct chemicals, precise liquid handling, operating valves or pumps, and meeting safety/spec tolerances. Unstructured environment, hazardous materials, and fine dexterous control push success very low for current humanoids.

task: Monitor machine operations to determine whether adjustments are necessary, stopping machines when problems occur.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one monitoring cycle per machine (visual/auditory check, assess output, decide to adjust or stop). A competent operator typically spends ~15–25 minutes per active inspection round.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read gauges and detect anomalies via vision/audio, but interpreting subtle multi-modal machine faults, deciding on adjustments, and physically intervening (stopping/adjusting controls) across varied machinery requires high generalization, judgment, and reliable manipulation — still largely beyond current platforms.

task: Massage, shampoo, and condition patron's hair and scalp to clean them and remove excess oil.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shampooing, conditioning, and scalp massage for a single patron at a salon sink; typically takes 8–12 minutes for a skilled stylist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate, adaptive pressure on a human scalp, precise water temperature control, avoiding eyes/ears, and handling wet hair — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and safe human-contact capabilities. High injury risk.

task: Inspect project site and evaluate contractor work to detect design malfunctions and ensure conformance to design specifications and applicable codes.
human_time_rationale: One site inspection visit for a typical construction project — walking the site, reviewing work against drawings, documenting findings. A competent inspector takes ~1.5–2.5 hours per visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires cross-referencing 2D/3D design docs with physical work, expert code knowledge, judgment on conformance, and navigation of active, unstructured construction sites — far beyond current robot generalization and reasoning capability.

task: Remove frames when the foundation is dry.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing all formwork frames from a single poured foundation section. A competent worker inspects dryness, loosens fasteners, and pulls frames clear in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/uneven terrain navigation, high-force torque on varied fasteners, heavy panel handling, and judgment on cure state. Current humanoids lack the strength, tool-use reliability, and outdoor robustness needed.

task: Attach prostheses to performers and apply makeup to create special features or effects, such as scars, aging, or illness.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full prosthetics and special-effects makeup application for one performer. Skilled artists typically spend 60–120 minutes depending on complexity; 90 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, precise force control on a human face, artistic judgment, chemical knowledge (adhesives, pigments), and tactile sensitivity. Far beyond any 2026 general-purpose robot's capabilities.

task: Inspect the cleanliness and practices of establishment employees.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting all employees on a single shift at one establishment; a trained inspector observes hygiene, attire, and practices for ~30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social/behavioral observation, judgment on hygiene standards, interaction with people, and contextual interpretation of practices — far beyond current robot perception and reasoning capabilities.

task: Perform routine and scheduled maintenance services, such as oil changes, lubrications, and tune-ups.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one routine maintenance service (e.g., oil change + lubrication + tune-up check) on a single vehicle. A competent technician typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating under vehicles, handling varied fasteners, fluid systems, and diagnostic tools in unstructured garage environments. Extreme dexterity, tool-use generalization, and under-vehicle mobility far exceed 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Operate tractors or bulldozers to perform such tasks as clearing land, mixing sludge, trimming backfills, or building roadways or parking lots.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one shift-scale earthmoving task (e.g., clearing a land parcel or grading a lot). A competent operator typically completes such a discrete task in ~4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate tractor/bulldozer controls in traffic or field conditions. No current general-purpose humanoid can enter, actuate, and safely control heavy earthmoving machinery autonomously at acceptable quality.

task: Load and move dirt, rocks, equipment, or other materials, using trucks, crawler tractors, power cranes, shovels, graders, or related equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one load/move cycle (load, transport, dump). A competent operator completes a typical earthmoving cycle in ~45–90 min depending on distance and material.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate trucks, crawler tractors, cranes, graders, or shovels in real unstructured outdoor conditions. Heavy equipment requires seated cab operation, complex controls, and site navigation far beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Place plated or coated materials on racks and transfer them to ovens to dry for specified periods of time.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a rack with coated items and transferring it to the oven; a competent worker takes ~5–10 min depending on rack size and oven layout.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping varied coated items without damaging wet/delicate surfaces, precise rack placement, navigating to oven, and operating oven door — compounding failure points. Wet/fragile surfaces and varied item geometries challenge current dexterity and force control.

task: Operate sandblasting equipment to roughen and clean surfaces of workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sandblasting one workpiece to acceptable surface finish. A competent operator sets up, blasts, and inspects a typical industrial part in ~20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping and maneuvering a pressurized blast nozzle with precision, managing hoses/equipment, and visually judging surface quality. Current humanoids lack the fine tool control, blast-pressure management, and surface-inspection perception needed. Highly unstructured environment with safety hazards further limits autonomy.

task: Take actions to avoid potential hazards or obstructions, such as utility lines, other equipment, other workers, or falling objects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single hazard-avoidance action/decision on a job site. A competent worker scans, identifies, and repositions or signals in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time situational awareness, dynamic hazard perception (moving workers, falling objects, live utilities), and adaptive decision-making in cluttered, unstructured outdoor/construction environments — well beyond current robot generalization and safety reliability.

task: Align vehicles' front ends.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning one vehicle's front end (wheel alignment). A competent technician takes ~15–25 minutes to set toe, camber, and caster on a lift.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a wheel alignment rack, attaching calibrated sensors to hubs, adjusting tie rods with precise torque, and reading alignment software — demanding specialized tool-use, fine dexterity, and domain-specific perception far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Compute numbers, widths, and angles of cutting tools, micrometers, scales, and gauges, and adjust tools to produce specified cuts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = computing measurements and adjusting one cutting tool setup. A skilled machinist typically spends ~10 min reading instruments, calculating, and making adjustments per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of precision instruments (micrometers, gauges), mathematical computation, and precise tool adjustment. Current robots lack the fine dexterity, tactile feedback, and integrated reasoning needed for reliable autonomous precision machining setup.

task: Drive and maneuver equipment equipped with blades in successive passes over working areas to remove topsoil, vegetation, or rocks or to distribute and level earth or terrain.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a typical grading/clearing pass over a working area (e.g., a construction site section). A competent operator takes ~3–4 hours per area segment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a bulldozer/grader in unstructured outdoor terrain. Humanoid robots cannot drive or operate heavy cab-based machinery. L4 autonomy for earthmoving exists only in narrow, proprietary pilot systems, not general-purpose robots.

task: Examine sketches, photographs, and plaster models to obtain desired character image depiction.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reviewing a set of reference materials (sketches, photos, plaster models) for a single character. A skilled artist/designer typically spends ~15–25 minutes studying and synthesizing the desired depiction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level aesthetic interpretation, cross-modal synthesis (2D sketches, photos, 3D plaster models), and subjective creative judgment. Current robots lack the vision-language reasoning and artistic understanding needed to reliably form a coherent character image concept.

task: Set up and conduct tests of complete units and components under operational conditions to investigate proposals for improving equipment performance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running one complete test cycle on a unit/component, including prep, execution, and observation. A competent technician typically takes 1–2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires adaptive reasoning to design/configure test conditions, interpret novel performance data, and make improvement proposals. Far beyond current robot generalization; involves engineering judgment, tool use in varied setups, and hypothesis-driven decision-making.

task: Wet concrete surface and rub with stone to smooth surface and obtain specified finish.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing one typical concrete slab section (~10–20 sq ft). Wetting, rubbing with a carborundum stone, and achieving a smooth specified finish takes a skilled finisher ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained, calibrated pressure with a rubbing stone over a wet uneven surface, tactile feedback to detect finish quality, and physical endurance. Current humanoids lack the precise force control, surface-quality perception, and wet-environment robustness needed.

task: Inspect dies to detect defects, assess wear, and verify specifications, using micrometers, steel gauge pins, and loupes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single die. A skilled inspector uses micrometers, gauge pins, and loupes to check dimensions, surface defects, and wear; typically 15–25 minutes per die.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of precision hand tools (micrometers, gauge pins, loupes), nuanced tactile feedback, and expert visual interpretation of micro-scale defects. Current humanoids lack the finger dexterity, force control, and specialized perception needed.

task: Straighten workpieces and remove dents, using straightening presses and hammers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = straightening a single workpiece. A skilled metalworker typically spends 10–30 min inspecting, positioning, pressing, and hammer-finishing one piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires skilled force-feedback judgment, precise hammer strikes, operating a foot/hand press, and iterative inspection. Current humanoids lack fine force control, tool-switching fluency, and the tactile/visual feedback loops needed for metal straightening.

task: Remove and replace worn or broken machine parts, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing and replacing a single worn/broken part on a machine. A competent mechanic/technician typically spends 30–60 min including diagnosis, disassembly, replacement, and reassembly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying worn parts, selecting correct hand tools, applying precise torque, accessing confined spaces, and generalizing across varied machine types — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and perception capabilities in unstructured settings.

task: Design rubber or plastic prostheses that can be used to change performers' appearances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing one prosthetic piece (sculpting, molding, and fitting). A skilled makeup/prosthetics artist typically spends 3–5 hours per piece from concept to finished prototype.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires high-level creative design, fine artistic sculpting, material knowledge, and iterative fitting to a human face/body. Current robots lack creative vision, fine dexterous art sculpting, and human-contour modeling capability.

task: Treat scalp conditions and hair loss, using specialized lotions, shampoos, or equipment such as infrared lamps or vibrating equipment.
human_time_rationale: One treatment session for a single client, including product application, equipment use (e.g., infrared lamp, vibrating tools), and scalp massage — typical salon/clinical session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate dexterity on a human head, precise product application, safe use of specialized equipment (infrared lamps, vibrators), and adaptive sensory feedback to avoid injury — far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured caregiving contexts.

task: Place workpieces in electroplating solutions or apply pigments to surfaces of workpieces to highlight ridges and grooves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing a single workpiece into solution or applying pigment to one workpiece. A skilled worker completes this in ~10 minutes including setup, dipping/brushing, and removal.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling varied workpiece shapes, precise immersion into liquid baths or controlled pigment brushing along ridges/grooves. Liquid handling, dexterous surface application, and quality judgment are challenging for 2026 humanoids.

task: Demonstrate products to clients, and provide instruction in makeup application.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single client consultation/demo session, including product demonstration and hands-on makeup application instruction. Typical beauty counter or studio session runs 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity near eyes/face, real-time social reading of client reactions, personalized instruction, nuanced color/texture judgment, and trust-building. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured, person-facing settings.

task: Maintain cleanliness of work area.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tidying/cleaning a single workstation or immediate work area; a competent worker spends ~10 min wiping surfaces, removing debris, and organizing.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep/wipe flat surfaces moderately well, but recognizing what counts as "clean," handling varied clutter, disposing of waste, and navigating a dynamic work area still challenge generalization and dexterity.

task: Inspect agricultural commodities or related operations, as well as fish or logging operations, for compliance with laws and regulations governing health, quality, and safety.
human_time_rationale: One inspection visit of an agricultural, fish, or logging operation — reviewing records, walking the site, sampling commodities, and documenting findings. Typically 1.5–2.5 hours per site visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires regulatory knowledge, document review, multi-environment mobility (fields, boats, log yards), nuanced judgment on compliance, and stakeholder communication. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Provide food, drinking water, and field sanitation facilities to contracted workers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single daily service cycle for a crew: setting up food, water, and sanitation supplies for contracted workers. A competent worker takes ~1 hour.
robot_success_rationale: Task spans outdoor/unstructured environments, heavy container handling, sanitation facility setup, and coordination with workers. Current humanoids lack the mobility, tool-use generalization, and situational judgment needed across all sub-tasks reliably.

task: Follow checklists to ensure all important parts are examined, including belts, hoses, steering systems, spark plugs, brake and fuel systems, wheel bearings, and other potentially troublesome areas.
human_time_rationale: One full vehicle inspection checklist covering all listed systems (belts, hoses, brakes, spark plugs, etc.); a competent mechanic typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires crawling under vehicles, reaching into tight engine bays, tactile feel of belts/hoses, operating lifts, and interpreting multi-system findings. Current humanoids lack the specialized perception, tool use, and confined-space dexterity needed.

task: Wash and reset wigs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = washing and resetting one wig: wetting, shampooing, conditioning, rinsing, detangling, styling/resetting on a stand. Typically 30–60 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate dexterous manipulation of fine fibers, gentle water handling, styling judgment, and tool use (combs, pins, wig stands). Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and perception for fragile textile/hair care tasks.

task: Attach workpieces to grinding machines and form specified sections and repair cracks, using welding or brazing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching one workpiece to a grinding machine and completing one weld/braze repair cycle. A skilled machinist/welder typically takes 20–30 min per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precision fixturing, operating industrial grinding machinery, and skilled welding/brazing — all demanding fine dexterity, tool-use, and process control far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capabilities. Multi-step, high-stakes task.

task: Tear down, repair, and rebuild faulty assemblies, such as power systems, steering systems, and linkages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = teardown, repair, and rebuild of one faulty assembly (e.g., a steering linkage or power system). A competent mechanic typically takes 1–3 hours depending on complexity; 2 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults, disassembling complex mechanical systems, applying torque specs, replacing varied parts, and reassembling to safety-critical tolerances — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool use, and generalization capabilities.

task: Turn valves to direct flow of coolant against cutting wheels and workpieces during grinding.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting coolant valves for a single grinding setup. A skilled operator locates and turns valve(s) to aim coolant in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Turning knob/lever valves is feasible for frontier humanoids, but the task demands precise nozzle aiming in a cluttered machine environment, visual confirmation of coolant flow direction, and dexterity around tight fixtures — challenging for current systems.

task: Troubleshoot fuel, ignition, and emissions control systems, using electronic testing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing a single vehicle complaint. A competent auto tech typically spends 45–90 min connecting scanners, reading codes, running live data, and isolating the fault.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving/operating a vehicle, connecting specialized OBD tools, interpreting multi-system live data, and performing physical under-hood probing. Current humanoids lack vehicle operation ability and the generalized diagnostic reasoning needed for complex fault trees.

task: Furnish tools for employee use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = gathering and distributing a set of tools to employees for a shift or task. A competent worker typically takes ~15 min to locate, check, and hand out tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying varied tools, navigating to storage and employee locations, and handing off correctly. Unstructured environment, diverse tool shapes, and social handoff interaction challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Operate compactors, scrapers, or rollers to level, compact, or cover refuse at disposal grounds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full shift (~8 hours) of continuous compactor/scraper/roller operation at a disposal ground, which is how the task is typically managed and measured.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate heavy landfill machinery (compactors, scrapers, rollers). These require seated cab controls, large-scale force inputs, and complex unstructured outdoor terrain judgment far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Tune automobile engines to ensure proper and efficient functioning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tuning one engine on one vehicle. A competent mechanic typically spends 60–120 minutes on diagnostics, adjustments, and verification testing.
robot_success_rationale: Engine tuning requires connecting OBD tools, interpreting live sensor data, making precise mechanical adjustments (timing, fuel trim, idle), and test-driving — far beyond current humanoid robot generalization, dexterity, and vehicle-operation capability.

task: Coordinate machine actions with other activities, positioning or moving loads in response to hand or audio signals from crew members.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one load movement/lift cycle from signal receipt to placement. A competent crane/hoist operator completes a typical lift in ~10–20 minutes including signaling coordination.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy lifting machinery (cranes, hoists) AND interpreting live hand/audio signals from crew. Humanoids cannot operate such equipment in real traffic conditions; signal interpretation under noisy, dynamic job sites adds further failure risk.

task: Examine unexposed photographic plates to detect flaws or foreign particles prior to printing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single photographic plate. A competent worker visually scans each plate under appropriate lighting for flaws/particles in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual perception of subtle defects on a flat, delicate surface under controlled lighting. Current robots lack the precision optical inspection and nuanced defect-classification ability; handling fragile plates without damage is also risky.

task: Help prepare and serve nutritionally balanced meals and snacks for children.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and serving one balanced meal/snack for a group of children (e.g., daycare/school setting); includes prep, cooking, portioning, and serving.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step cooking, nutritional judgment, child-safe portioning, allergen awareness, and safe interaction near children. Current robots lack reliable cooking generalization, safety compliance, and caregiving context.

task: Inspect or test horticultural products or livestock to detect harmful diseases, chemical residues, or infestations and to determine the quality of products or animals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one batch/lot of produce or a small group of livestock, including visual checks, sampling, and preliminary assessment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal expert judgment (visual, olfactory, tactile, chemical testing), handling live animals, interpreting ambiguous biological signs, and using specialized lab tools. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Operate or tend stationary engines, boilers, and auxiliary equipment, such as pumps, compressors, or air-conditioning equipment, to supply and maintain steam or heat for buildings, marine vessels, or pneumatic tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one operational shift check-and-tend cycle (~1 hour), including monitoring gauges, adjusting controls, logging readings, and responding to minor issues.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, adjusting valves and controls, diagnosing abnormal sounds/smells, and responding to faults. Current humanoids lack reliable multi-modal sensing, equipment-specific dexterity, and certified autonomous operation in safety-critical utility environments.

task: Start engines, move throttles, switches, or levers, or depress pedals to operate machines, such as bulldozers, trench excavators, road graders, or backhoes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a typical operational work cycle on one machine (startup through shutdown), e.g., grading a section or excavating a trench segment. ~45 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate cab-based heavy machinery in real environments. Controls require seated operation, foot pedals, and coordinated multi-limb inputs. No commercial humanoid has demonstrated autonomous bulldozer/excavator operation as of 2026.

task: Maintain, adjust, and clean equipment, and perform minor repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance/adjustment/cleaning cycle on one piece of equipment. A competent technician typically spends ~30 min per unit including inspection, cleaning, minor fixes.
robot_success_rationale: This task bundles diagnostics, fine adjustments, tool use, and minor repairs — requiring generalized dexterity, tool selection, and fault reasoning. Current humanoids lack reliable multi-step maintenance autonomy across varied equipment types.

task: Operate loaders to pull out stumps, rip asphalt or concrete, rough-grade properties, bury refuse, or perform general cleanup.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one discrete job segment (e.g., stump removal, asphalt ripping, or grading a property). A competent operator typically completes such a segment in ~1 hour.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate heavy loaders in real traffic/field conditions. No 2026 general-purpose robot can drive, control, or safely manage large construction equipment autonomously in unstructured outdoor environments.

task: Inspect food products and processing procedures to determine whether products are safe to eat.
human_time_rationale: One inspection instance = a single food processing line or product batch inspection, including visual checks, sampling, and procedure review. A competent food safety inspector typically takes 30–60 minutes per line/batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal sensory judgment (smell, taste, texture, temperature), procedural compliance review, regulatory knowledge, and context-dependent decision-making. Current robots lack chemical/olfactory sensors, nuanced food-safety reasoning, and cannot autonomously navigate complex food processing environments to inspector standards.

task: Repair, replace, or adjust defective fuel injectors, carburetor parts, and gasoline filters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing one component (injector, carb part, or filter) on a typical vehicle. Diagnosis, removal, repair/replacement, and reinstallation average ~60 min for a competent mechanic.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating under a hood, applying precise torque, disconnecting fuel lines with clips, diagnosing faults, and handling varied vehicle layouts. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and generalization capabilities in unstructured automotive environments.

task: Repair and service air conditioning, heating, engine cooling, and electrical systems.
human_time_rationale: One service/repair instance on a vehicle system (AC, heating, engine cooling, or electrical). A typical job ranges from diagnosis to fix, averaging ~90 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires vehicle-bay mobility, diagnostic tool use, fine dexterous manipulation in tight engine compartments, interpreting multi-system faults, and safe handling of refrigerants/electrical — well beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Operate and maintain laser plate-making equipment that converts electronic data to plates without the use of film.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing and inspecting one printing plate job: loading media, configuring settings, running the laser imager, and performing quality checks. Typically 30–60 min per plate set.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial prepress equipment with complex software interfaces, precise plate loading/alignment, fine calibration, and quality inspection. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, domain-specific tool-use, and software integration needed.

task: Connect hydraulic hoses, belts, mechanical linkages, or power takeoff shafts to tractors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting all hoses/belts/linkages/PTO for one implement attachment. A competent operator typically takes 10–30 minutes depending on implement complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating near or on heavy agricultural machinery outdoors, aligning and coupling high-force hydraulic fittings, PTO shafts, and linkages with tight tolerances. Current humanoids lack the strength, outdoor dexterity, and specialized tool-use needed; no viable 2026 deployment exists.

task: Repair or replace parts such as pistons, rods, gears, valves, and bearings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing/repairing a single component (e.g., a bearing or valve) in a typical engine/machine. Includes diagnosis, disassembly, swap, and reassembly — ~90 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force torquing, fine dexterous manipulation of heavy/greasy precision parts, tool changes, spatial reasoning in confined engine bays, and fault diagnosis. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capability.

task: Examine finished plates to detect flaws, verify conformity with master plates, and measure dot sizes and centers, using light boxes and microscopes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one finished plate. A skilled inspector uses a light box and microscope to check flaws, conformity, dot sizes/centers — roughly 10–15 minutes per plate.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine optical perception (microscopy, dot measurement), precise tool manipulation, and nuanced quality judgment against a master reference. Current robots lack the microscope operation skills, sub-millimeter visual acuity, and pass/fail expertise needed.

task: Analyze originals to evaluate color density, gradation highlights, middle tones, and shadows, using densitometers and knowledge of light and color.
human_time_rationale: One instance = analyzing one original (photo/artwork). A skilled operator spends ~10–20 min measuring density ranges, gradations, highlights, midtones, and shadows with a densitometer and interpreting results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level color science knowledge, precise densitometer handling, interpreting subtle tonal gradations, and professional judgment. Current robots lack the fine manipulation, calibrated tool use, and domain cognition needed for acceptable quality.

task: Rewire ignition systems, lights, and instrument panels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rewiring a single vehicle's ignition system, lights, and instrument panel. A competent auto electrician typically takes 2–4 hours for a full rewire of these systems.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to handle small connectors, wire harnesses, and terminals in tight, cluttered spaces. Current robots lack the precision, contextual understanding, and tool-use needed for complex automotive electrical work.

task: Operate presses to print proofs of plates, monitoring printing quality to ensure that it is adequate.
human_time_rationale: One instance = printing and evaluating one proof. A skilled press operator sets up the press, runs the proof, and inspects print quality, typically taking 20–30 minutes per proof cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial press machinery (controls, levers, ink adjustment), fine perceptual quality inspection of print output, and nuanced judgment on color/registration — far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and tool-use capabilities.

task: Activate valves to maintain required amounts of water in boilers, to adjust supplies of combustion air, and to control the flow of fuel into burners.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single round of valve adjustments across a boiler system; a skilled stationary engineer takes ~10 min to check levels, turn valves, and verify fuel/air/water flow.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating an industrial boiler room, identifying and operating diverse manual valves, reading gauges, and sequencing fuel/air/water controls correctly. Perception of gauges, unstructured valve types, and safety-critical precision limit current robots severely.

task: Check fuel supplies at sites to ensure adequate availability.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting fuel levels at one site; reading gauges/tanks, logging results. A competent worker takes ~20 min per site.
robot_success_rationale: Robot must navigate outdoor/industrial terrain, read analog or digital gauges, open access panels, and log data. Locomotion on uneven terrain (~40%), gauge reading perception, and contextual judgment about "adequacy" are all challenging for current humanoids.

task: Rebuild parts, such as crankshafts and cylinder blocks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rebuilding a single major engine component (e.g., crankshaft or cylinder block). A competent machinist/mechanic typically spends 3–5 hours on disassembly, inspection, machining, and reassembly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precision machining, fine torque control, specialized tools (lathes, hones, micrometers), and expert judgment on tolerances. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use capabilities; no demonstrated frontier-robot performance in this domain.

task: Read dials and meters to determine amperage, voltage, electrical output and input at specific operating temperature to analyze parts performance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reading a set of dials/meters on a single piece of equipment; locating instruments, reading values, and recording results typically takes ~10 minutes for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to panels and use cameras to read digital displays, but analog dial/needle reading under varied lighting and angles is still unreliable. Recording values in context and correlating to operating temperature adds further perception and reasoning challenges.

task: Turn valves to control air or water output of compressors or pumps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = turning one valve to set desired output. A competent worker locates, grips, and adjusts a valve in about 1–3 minutes including verification.
robot_success_rationale: Valve turning requires precise grip, torque application, and rotational control on varied handle types (wheel, lever, ball). Current humanoids struggle with tool-like force control, irregular handle geometries, and confirming correct output level.

task: Align wheels, axles, frames, torsion bars, and steering mechanisms of automobiles, using special alignment equipment and wheel-balancing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full wheel alignment job on one vehicle. A skilled technician typically takes 45–75 minutes to mount sensors, measure, adjust, and verify all four corners.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a vehicle lift, attaching specialized alignment sensors, interpreting proprietary alignment software, and making precise mechanical adjustments (tie rods, camber bolts, torsion bars) under a raised car — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and system-integration capability.

task: Scrape and sand floor edges and areas inaccessible to floor sanders, using scrapers, disk-type sanders, and sandpaper.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scraping and edge-sanding one average room (~150–200 sq ft). A competent floor finisher typically spends 30–60 min on edges and corners per room.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained forceful tool contact at tight floor-wall junctions, precise angle control, and tool swaps (scraper, disk sander, sandpaper) in highly constrained spaces. Current humanoids lack the compliance, force feedback, and dexterity for consistent acceptable finish quality.

task: Signal operators to guide movement of tractor-drawn machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = signaling an operator through one maneuver (e.g., backing, turning, aligning). A competent human takes ~2–8 minutes per discrete guidance event.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor uneven terrain navigation, real-time situational awareness of large moving machinery, interpretable gestural signaling, and dynamic coordination with a human operator — all well beyond current humanoid robot generalization and outdoor mobility limits.

task: Select and fasten bulldozer blades or other attachments to tractors, using hitches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting and attaching one blade/attachment to a tractor. A skilled operator typically takes 15–25 minutes to align, connect, and secure the hitch and hydraulic lines.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy-force manipulation of large, dirty, high-tolerance hitch pins and hydraulic couplers on outdoor/uneven terrain. Current humanoids lack the strength, tool-use capability, and precision to handle such large industrial components autonomously.

task: Apply filler compound and coats of finish to floors to seal wood.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying filler and finish coats to a typical room's wood floor (~200 sq ft). Includes prep, filler application, drying/light sanding between coats, and final finish coat.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated use of specialized floor tools (applicators, rollers, buffers), precise compound spreading, multi-step chemical process judgment, and consistent coverage. Current humanoid robots lack the specialized tool handling, force control, and process sequencing needed for acceptable quality.

task: Attach sandpaper to rollers of sanding machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching sandpaper to one roller. A competent operator aligns, wraps, and secures sandpaper to a roller in roughly 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual dexterity to align, tension, and fasten flexible abrasive sheet around a cylindrical roller. Handling thin, flexible material with exact tension and secure fastening is well beyond current humanoid robots' reliable capability.

task: Guide sanding machines over surfaces of floors until surfaces are smooth.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sanding one average room (~200 sq ft). A competent floor finisher takes ~1–1.5 hours including setup, passes, and smoothness checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a large, heavy walk-behind sanding machine, applying consistent pressure/speed, navigating edges, and judging surface smoothness — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and tool-operation generalization.

task: Operate equipment to demolish or remove debris or to remove snow from streets, roads, or parking lots.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one shift-level job site or route (e.g., clearing a parking lot or road segment). A competent operator typically completes a discrete area in ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large specialized vehicles (excavators, snowplows, bulldozers) in dynamic outdoor environments. Humanoid robots cannot drive or control such heavy equipment autonomously at acceptable quality in 2026.

task: Apply coats of plaster or stucco to walls, ceilings, or partitions of buildings, using trowels, brushes, or spray guns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = plastering one standard wall/ceiling section (~10–15 m²). A competent plasterer applies, levels, and finishes a coat in ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained force-controlled troweling across large vertical/overhead surfaces, tool switching, material consistency judgment, and high-quality finish. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and generalization in unstructured construction environments.

task: Remove excess glue from joints, using knives, scrapers, or wood chisels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing excess glue from all joints on a single workpiece. A skilled woodworker spends ~5 minutes scraping/chiseling glue squeeze-out after it has cured.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled scraping along irregular joints without gouging wood. Current robots lack the fine haptic feedback, edge-detection accuracy, and dexterous tool control needed; high risk of surface damage.

task: Monitor and inspect equipment, computer terminals, switches, valves, gauges, alarms, safety devices, and meters to detect leaks or malfunctions and to ensure that equipment is operating efficiently and safely.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single inspection round of a facility/panel area. A competent operator walks the floor, checks all listed devices, and logs findings — typically 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read digital gauges and walk flat floors reasonably well, but reading analog gauges, detecting subtle leaks (smell/feel), interpreting alarms in context, and operating valves/switches safely in unstructured industrial environments remain significant challenges.

task: Disassemble units and inspect parts for wear, using micrometers, calipers, and gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling one mechanical unit and inspecting all parts; a competent technician typically takes 30–60 minutes depending on unit complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sequential disassembly with varied fasteners, precise tool switching (wrenches, micrometers, calipers), fine dexterous measurement reads, and wear interpretation — far beyond current humanoid generalization and fine manipulation capability.

task: Operate road watering, oiling, or rolling equipment, or street sealing equipment, such as chip spreaders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one road treatment run (e.g., a chip-spreading or oiling pass on a typical road segment). A competent operator typically completes a section in ~3–4 hours including setup, operation, and teardown.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large specialized road equipment on public roads. Humanoid robots cannot drive or control heavy machinery in traffic. No 2026 general-purpose robot has this capability; task demands vehicle operation, situational awareness, and specialized controls.

task: Push other equipment when extra traction or assistance is required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pushing assist event (positioning, engaging, pushing until equipment is freed/moved). Typical duration 10–30 min depending on terrain and equipment size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy pushing machinery (dozer, tow vehicle) on rough/unstructured terrain. Humanoid robots cannot operate such vehicles, lack the force output, and struggle with unstructured outdoor conditions.

task: Clean and lubricate boilers and auxiliary equipment and make minor adjustments as needed, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning, lubricating, and minor adjustment of a single boiler unit with auxiliary equipment. A competent stationary engineer typically takes 60–120 minutes per boiler.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex industrial environments, applying lubricants precisely, using varied hand tools, and making calibrated adjustments. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-use reliability, and situational judgment for this multi-step industrial task.

task: Examine, weigh, and measure commodities, such as poultry, eggs, meat, or seafood to certify qualities, grades, and weights.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting, weighing, and grading a single lot/batch of commodity (e.g., a tray of eggs or a cut of meat); a competent inspector completes this in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine tactile/visual perception for grading (color, texture, defects), precise manipulation of varied items, and certified judgment. Current robots lack the nuanced sensory discrimination and regulatory-grade reliability needed for autonomous certification.

task: Monitor boiler water, chemical, and fuel levels, and make adjustments to maintain required levels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring-and-adjustment round: checking water, chemical, and fuel gauges/sensors and making any needed corrections. Competent operator typically takes 10–20 minutes per round.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, operating valves and chemical-dosing controls, and interpreting boiler system state. Perception of varied gauges, fine valve manipulation, and safe chemical handling in an unstructured plant environment are well beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Set up prototype and test apparatus and operate test controlling equipment to observe and record prototype test results.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a prototype test apparatus, running the test, and recording results. A competent technician typically takes 1–3 hours depending on complexity; 2 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting engineering specs, fine-dexterous equipment setup, operating specialized test-controlling instruments, reading analog/digital gauges, and making judgment calls on anomalies — well beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Test atmosphere for adequate oxygen or explosive conditions when working in confined spaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing atmosphere in a single confined space entry point. Includes attaching sensors, taking readings at multiple levels, and logging results — typically 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires carrying and operating a multi-gas detector, positioning probe at various depths/heights in a confined space, interpreting readings, and making a go/no-go safety decision. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use integration for calibrated gas detectors, confined-space navigation, and safety-critical judgment. Some narrow-purpose robots (e.g. inspection drones) do this, but a general-purpose humanoid cannot reliably complete this autonomously.

task: Adjust controls and/or valves on equipment to provide power, and to regulate and set operations of system or industrial processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a set of controls/valves to bring a system to target operating state. A competent operator typically takes 5–10 minutes per adjustment cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Industrial valves, dials, and levers vary widely in form and force requirements. Robots lack reliable torque-controlled fine dexterity for unmarked controls, can't read analog gauges well, and cannot interpret process feedback to know when the correct setpoint is reached.

task: Overhaul or replace carburetors, blowers, generators, distributors, starters, and pumps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = overhauling or replacing one component (e.g., a carburetor or pump). A competent mechanic typically takes 60–120 minutes depending on component complexity and accessibility.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle small fasteners, seals, jets, and wiring; working in cramped engine bays with varied layouts; diagnosing wear; and using specialized tools. Far beyond current humanoid manipulation capabilities in unstructured environments.

task: Switch from automatic to manual controls and isolate equipment mechanically and electrically to allow for safe inspection and repair work.
human_time_rationale: One instance = switching one piece of equipment to manual control and completing full lockout/tagout (LOTO) isolation. A trained worker typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial control panels, physical lockout/tagout hardware, and verifying electrical/mechanical isolation — highly safety-critical with low tolerance for error. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, contextual understanding, and safety-certified autonomy needed.

task: Repair or rebuild transmissions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fully repairing or rebuilding a single transmission. A competent auto technician typically takes 6–10 hours; median ~8 hours (480 min).
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (torque specs, snap rings, bearing presses, seals), specialized heavy tooling, deep sequential reasoning, and force-feedback precision far beyond current humanoid capability. No deployed system can do this autonomously.

task: Install, adjust, or repair hydraulic or electromagnetic automatic lift mechanisms used to raise and lower automobile windows, seats, and tops.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing/adjusting/repairing one lift mechanism (e.g., a power window regulator). A competent auto technician typically takes 45–75 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working inside confined vehicle cavities, using specialized tools, diagnosing faults, routing wiring, and fine mechanical adjustment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and fault-diagnosis capabilities in unstructured automotive environments.

task: Cure freshly plastered surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = curing one freshly plastered wall/surface. Human applies water misting or curing compound, monitors, and re-mists as needed over ~15–20 min of active labor per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying plastered surface boundaries, controlling a sprayer or applying curing compound evenly, judging surface moisture, and navigating in a construction environment. Current robots lack reliable surface-condition perception and precise fluid application control in unstructured sites.

task: Move levers and turn valves to activate power hammers, or to raise and lower drophammers that drive piles to required depths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = operating levers/valves to drive a single pile to required depth. A competent operator typically takes 10–20 minutes per pile depending on soil and depth.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large, specialized industrial machinery (power hammers, pile drivers) with force-feedback judgment, depth monitoring, and dynamic control. No current humanoid can operate such heavy equipment autonomously in real field conditions.

task: Apply insulation to building exteriors by installing prefabricated insulation systems over existing walls or by covering the outer wall with insulation board, reinforcing mesh, and a base coat.
human_time_rationale: One instance = insulating one standard exterior wall section (~10–20 sq m). A skilled insulator takes ~3–4 hours including measuring, cutting boards, fixing mesh, and applying base coat.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working at height on scaffolding, handling large awkward boards, precise adhesive/mesh application over uneven outdoor surfaces, and multi-step coordination. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured outdoor construction environments.

task: Diagnose and replace or repair engine management systems or related sensors for flexible fuel vehicles (FFVs) with ignition timing, fuel rate, alcohol concentration, or air-to-fuel ratio malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and repairing/replacing a single FFV engine management fault. A competent technician spends ~1–2 hrs scanning codes, testing sensors, and swapping or repairing the faulty component.
robot_success_rationale: Requires OBD-II scanner connection, interpreting multi-system diagnostics, fine dexterous disassembly/replacement of engine sensors, and automotive domain reasoning — well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured real-world garage settings.

task: Repair or replace defective equipment, components, or wiring.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and repairing/replacing a single defective component or wiring run. Covers inspection, part swap, and basic testing. Median across varied repair types.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fault diagnosis, fine dexterity for wiring/connectors, tool use (screwdrivers, pliers, multimeters), and contextual judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable autonomous diagnosis, fine electrical manipulation, and safe tool-switching in unstructured environments.

task: Mix mortar and plaster to desired consistency or direct workers who perform mixing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing one batch (~1–2 buckets) of mortar or plaster to desired consistency; skilled worker takes ~10–20 min including measuring and adjusting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a mixer or paddle drill, measuring water/powder ratios, and judging consistency by touch/sight — all in an unstructured construction site. Sensory feedback, tool use, and generalization far exceed current robot capabilities.

task: Test electrical circuits or components for continuity, using electrical test equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single circuit or component for continuity. A competent technician probes, reads the meter, and records results in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise probe placement on small terminals, handling a multimeter, interpreting readings, and navigating varied panel layouts. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity and contextual electrical knowledge for reliable autonomous execution.

task: Perform or arrange for repairs, such as complete overhauls, replacement of defective valves, gaskets, or bearings, or fabrication of new parts.
human_time_rationale: One repair instance (e.g., replacing a valve, gasket, or bearing, or a partial overhaul) for a competent mechanic/pipefitter; a complete overhaul could take hours, but a typical unit repair averages ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults, applying high torque with specialized tools, fine dexterous work (seating gaskets, threading bearings), and fabricating custom parts — far beyond 2026 humanoid generalization, tool-use, and force-control capabilities.

task: Inspect floors for smoothness.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single room's floor for smoothness; a worker walks the area, visually scans, and may use touch or a straightedge tool, taking ~10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate flat floors and use cameras/lidar for surface mapping, but detecting subtle smoothness defects (minor ridges, micro-bumps) reliably in unstructured environments remains limited. Tactile sensing and fine surface discrimination are weak in current humanoids.

task: Label and seal graded products and issue official grading certificates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one product unit/lot: affix label, apply tamper seal, and print/sign/issue grading certificate. Competent grader averages ~10–15 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous label/seal application, navigating official certificate systems, signing/stamping documents, and legal authority to issue certificates — far beyond current autonomous robot capability in unstructured, compliance-critical contexts.

task: Rough the undercoat surface with a scratcher so the finish coat will adhere.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scratching the undercoat surface of a single standard wall section (~10 sq ft). A competent plasterer works quickly with a scarifier/scratcher tool.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained, controlled force application across a surface at correct angle and depth. Wall coverage, tool handling, and consistent pressure are challenging for humanoids; perception of surface coverage quality adds difficulty.

task: Trim rough edges from strips, using straightedges and trimming knives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming one strip. A competent worker aligns a straightedge and runs a knife along it in roughly 2–4 minutes depending on strip length.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual coordination: firmly holding a straightedge while drawing a knife with consistent pressure and alignment. Current humanoids lack the fine force control, grip stability, and blade-path accuracy needed for acceptable trim quality.

task: Organize and conduct age-appropriate recreational activities, such as games, arts and crafts, sports, walks, and play dates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = planning and running a single recreational activity session (e.g., a craft session or game). A competent caregiver spends ~60 min total including setup, facilitation, and cleanup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires social judgment, adaptive interaction, safety monitoring, and age-appropriate improvisation. Current humanoids lack the social intelligence, emotional reading, generalization across unstructured play scenarios, and trust required for unsupervised child/elder care activities.

task: Clean and prepare surfaces for applications of plaster, cement, stucco, or similar materials, such as by drywall taping.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one standard wall/surface section (~10–12 sq ft) including cleaning, sanding, and taping drywall seams before plastering/stucco application.
robot_success_rationale: Requires large-area coordinated arm sweeps, fine tactile control for tape embedding, working at varying heights, and quality judgment of surface uniformity. Current humanoids lack reliable force-controlled, extended-reach surface prep dexterity and have poor generalization across wall geometries.

task: Clean, lubricate, and refill equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning, lubricating, and refilling a single piece of equipment (e.g., a machine or tool). A competent technician typically takes 20–40 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step sequencing: wiping surfaces, applying lubricant precisely, and refilling fluids — all demanding fine dexterity, tool-use, and fluid handling. Current humanoids lack reliable precision for fluid dispensing and varied equipment layouts.

task: Drive pilings to provide support for buildings or other structures, using heavy equipment with a pile driver head.
human_time_rationale: One instance = driving a single piling to required depth. A competent operator typically takes 60–120 minutes per pile including positioning, alignment, and driving.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a large, specialized pile-driving rig in dynamic outdoor terrain. No humanoid robot can operate heavy construction equipment autonomously in 2026; task demands vehicle control, precision alignment, and site judgment far beyond current capabilities.

task: Install guide wires on exterior surfaces of buildings to indicate thickness of plaster or stucco and nail wire mesh, lath, or similar materials to the outside surface to hold stucco in place.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and installing guide wires plus attaching mesh/lath on a typical exterior wall section (~10–20 sq ft). A competent plasterer takes ~1–1.5 hrs including measuring, fastening, and tensioning.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor unstructured environments, precise tensioning of fine guide wires, nailing into varied exterior surfaces, handling flexible mesh/lath, and working at height — all well beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and generalization limits.

task: Conduct pre-operational checks on equipment to ensure proper functioning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full pre-op check on a single piece of equipment (e.g., forklift, vehicle, or machine). A competent operator typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse sensory checks (fluid levels, lights, leaks, gauges, controls), judgment calls on pass/fail, and operating equipment controls. Current humanoids lack reliable multi-modal inspection perception and contextual pass/fail reasoning across varied equipment types.

task: Inspect and test systems to verify system compliance with plans and specifications or to detect and locate malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and testing one system (e.g., HVAC, electrical panel, plumbing loop). A competent technician typically spends 30–60 minutes per system check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting plans/specs, using varied diagnostic tools, reasoning about faults, and making accept/reject judgments. Current robots lack reliable tool-use, technical reasoning, and generalization across diverse system types.

task: Retrofit vehicle fuel systems with aftermarket products, such as vapor transfer devices, evaporation control devices, swirlers, lean burn devices, and friction reduction devices, to enhance combustion and fuel efficiency.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retrofitting one vehicle's fuel system with aftermarket devices. A competent mechanic typically spends 60–120 min accessing fuel lines, fitting devices, and verifying installation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use in confined, grease-covered engine bays with highly variable layouts, plumbing connections, and torque specifications. Far beyond 2026 humanoid generalization and dexterity limits. No commercial deployment exists.

task: Install, connect, or adjust thermostats, humidistats, or timers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing/connecting/adjusting a single thermostat, humidistat, or timer. A competent HVAC tech typically takes 20–40 minutes including wiring and calibration.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine wire manipulation, reading small terminals, screwing into varied wall boxes, and interpreting wiring diagrams. Current humanoids lack the precision dexterity, tool-use reliability, and contextual electrical reasoning needed for safe, accepted installation.

task: Spray acoustic materials or texture finish over walls or ceilings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = spraying one average room's walls/ceiling (~150–200 sq ft). A competent worker sets up, sprays, and does basic cleanup in ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a spray rig/hopper gun, maintaining consistent distance and angle over large overhead surfaces, managing hoses and pressure settings, and achieving uniform texture quality — all far beyond current humanoid dexterity, reach, and tool-use generalization.

task: Test boiler water quality or arrange for testing and take necessary corrective action, such as adding chemicals to prevent corrosion and harmful deposits.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full test-and-treat cycle per boiler: sample collection, multi-parameter lab/field testing, result interpretation, and chemical dosing. Competent operator typically takes ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating valves/sampling ports, using test kits or instruments, interpreting chemical readings, and precisely dosing chemicals — multi-step dexterous and cognitive chain in a high-heat industrial environment; well beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Ignite fuel in burners, using torches or flames.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lighting a single burner. A competent worker locates the burner, opens the fuel valve, and applies a torch or flame — typically takes 1–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise handling of an ignition tool (torch/lighter) near a fuel source, detecting flame confirmation, and managing fire safely. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity, pyro-safe manipulation, and flame-perception feedback for autonomous ignition.

task: Remove finished workpieces from machines and place them in boxes or on racks, setting aside pieces that are defective.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one finished workpiece from a machine, inspecting it, and placing it correctly. A skilled worker completes this in roughly 1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of known objects is feasible (~70–90%), but defect detection via inspection, handling varied workpiece geometries, and navigating industrial machine interfaces significantly reduce reliability. Generalization across part types is still limited.

task: Fire coal furnaces by hand or with stokers and gas- or oil-fed boilers, using automatic gas feeds or oil pumps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one firing/startup cycle per furnace or boiler. A competent operator inspects, feeds fuel or adjusts feed systems, ignites, and sets draft — roughly 15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy, hot, industrial equipment with specialized controls (stoker mechanisms, oil pumps, gas valves), high-heat environments, and safety-critical judgments. Current humanoid robots lack the tool-use, thermal tolerance, and generalization needed.

task: Cover interior walls and ceilings of rooms with decorative wallpaper or fabric, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = papering one average room (walls + ceiling). A competent paperhanger typically takes 3–5 hours per room, median ~4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained bimanual dexterity handling large, floppy, wet-pasted sheets; precise pattern alignment over tall vertical/overhead surfaces; ladder/scaffold mobility; and fine smoothing without tearing. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Install auxiliary components to heating or cooling equipment, such as expansion or discharge valves, air ducts, pipes, blowers, dampers, flues, or stokers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one auxiliary component (e.g., a valve, duct section, or damper) on HVAC equipment. A skilled technician typically takes 30–60 min depending on component complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating tight mechanical spaces, precise pipe/duct alignment, torquing fittings, threading fasteners, and interpreting HVAC schematics. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and generalization in unstructured real-world installations.

task: Weigh, measure, and record fuel used.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and recording fuel used for a single vehicle/equipment fill or shift. A competent worker reads gauges or weighs fuel, logs the value — typically 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges or operating scales, recording data (writing or entering into a system), and navigating to fuel storage. Perception of varied gauge types, fine manipulation of logs, and generalization across equipment types are significant barriers for 2026 robots.

task: Listen to mud pumps and check regularly for vibration and other problems to ensure that rig pumps and drilling mud systems are working properly.
human_time_rationale: One inspection round: a competent driller walks to the pump area, listens, checks gauges, feels for vibration, and logs findings — typically 10–20 minutes per check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a loud, wet, hazardous rig floor; acoustically diagnosing pump anomalies; interpreting vibration signatures and gauges. Current robots lack the specialized auditory perception, domain knowledge, and rugged mobility for this unstructured industrial environment.

task: Create decorative textures in finish coat, using brushes or trowels, sand, pebbles, or stones.
human_time_rationale: One instance = texturing one standard wall section (~10–15 sq ft) of finish coat. A skilled plasterer/mason takes ~45 min including material prep and technique application.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly skilled, force-sensitive, artistic tool manipulation (brushes, trowels) on wet plaster to create aesthetically acceptable decorative patterns. Current robots lack the fine haptic feedback, artistic generalization, and consistent pressure control needed.

task: Smooth strips or sections of paper with brushes or rollers to remove wrinkles and bubbles and to smooth joints.
human_time_rationale: One instance = smoothing one strip/section of wallpaper or similar material; a competent worker takes ~2–4 minutes to brush/roll out wrinkles, bubbles, and joints per strip.
robot_success_rationale: Requires compliant, spatially coordinated two-arm force control over a large, deformable surface while detecting subtle wrinkles/bubbles via touch and vision. Current humanoids lack reliable deformable-material manipulation and nuanced pressure regulation at this scale.

task: Lay out and connect electrical wiring between controls and equipment, according to wiring diagrams, using electrician's hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wiring one control panel or equipment connection per a typical diagram. A competent electrician takes ~60–120 min for a moderately complex wiring job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (stripping, routing, terminating wires in tight spaces), diagram interpretation, spatial reasoning in cluttered enclosures, and precise tool use — well beyond 2026 humanoid capability at acceptable quality.
