task: Move hand and foot levers of hoisting equipment to position piling leads, hoist piling into leads, and position hammers over pilings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning one pile: rigging, hoisting into leads, and seating the hammer. A skilled pile driver operator typically takes 15–25 minutes per pile setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a crane/crane-like hoisting rig via physical hand and foot levers in a cab, coordinating heavy load dynamics outdoors. Humanoid robots cannot drive or operate such specialized heavy construction machinery autonomously.

task: Smooth rough spots on walls and ceilings, using sandpaper.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sanding one rough patch/area on a wall or ceiling. A competent worker locates, assesses, and sands a typical patch in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained, force-controlled contact against vertical/overhead surfaces, fine tactile feedback to detect smoothness, and overhead reach. Current humanoids lack the compliant force control and surface-quality perception needed for acceptable finish quality.

task: Regulate or shut down equipment during emergency situations, as directed by supervisory personnel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = responding to a single emergency directive: locating the equipment, actuating controls (switches, valves, E-stops), and confirming shutdown. Typically 2–10 minutes depending on facility layout.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can press buttons and flip switches in structured environments, but emergency scenarios involve novel layouts, varied controls, urgent timing, real-time communication with supervisors, and high-stakes decision-making. Generalization and situational awareness remain weak. Mobility under stress/hazards further limits reliability.

task: Check the air quality of ventilation systems and make adjustments to ensure compliance with mandated safety codes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and adjusting one ventilation system/zone. Includes sensor readings, log review, damper/filter checks, and minor control adjustments. ~45 min for a competent HVAC technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, navigating complex mechanical rooms, operating HVAC controls, interpreting regulatory codes, and making calibrated adjustments — far beyond current humanoid generalization and tool-use capability.

task: Draw samples of products and conduct quality control tests to monitor processing and to ensure that standards are met.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drawing a sample batch and running QC tests on it. A competent technician typically spends ~20–30 min sampling, labeling, testing, and logging results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating production floor, operating varied sampling tools, running instruments (calipers, spectrometers, titrators), interpreting multi-modal results, and logging outcomes — well beyond current humanoid generalization and fine dexterous tool-use.

task: Measure and cut strips from rolls of wallpaper or fabric, using shears or razors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and cutting one strip from a roll. A skilled worker measures, marks, and cuts one strip in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, controlled unrolling of flexible/floppy material, accurate straight-line cutting with shears or razor — all demand fine dexterity and bimanual coordination with deformable materials, well beyond current robot capability.

task: Provide assistance to plumbers in repairing or replacing water, sewer, or waste lines, and in daily maintenance activities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting a plumber through one repair/replacement job (e.g., a pipe section or fitting). Typical helper tasks (handing tools, holding pipe, trenching support) take ~45 min per job.
robot_success_rationale: Plumbing assistance requires confined-space mobility, fine dexterous tool-use (wrenches, pipe cutters), situational awareness in cluttered/unstructured environments, and dynamic collaboration with a human worker — all well beyond current robot capabilities.

task: Repair and maintain equipment, making emergency adjustments or assisting with major repairs as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one equipment repair or maintenance event. A competent technician typically spends 30–90 minutes diagnosing and fixing a single issue, averaging ~60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex diagnosis, tool selection, fine dexterity with varied fasteners, and adaptive problem-solving across unpredictable failure modes. Far beyond reliable autonomous capability of 2026 frontier humanoids.

task: Mold or install ornamental plaster pieces, panels, or trim.
human_time_rationale: One instance = molding or installing one ornamental plaster piece/panel/trim section. A competent plasterer takes ~60–120 min including measuring, mixing, fitting, and finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for mixing/applying plaster, precise overhead or wall positioning, strong gripping of irregular fragile pieces, and skilled finishing. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and generalization; no known pilots exist.

task: Fit appliances onto patients, and make any necessary adjustments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting a single appliance (e.g., brace, prosthetic, orthotic) on one patient, including assessment and minor adjustments. Typically 15–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for straps/buckles, sensitive patient interaction, clinical judgment for fit assessment, and adaptive response to patient anatomy/pain feedback — all far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Apply thinned glue to waterproof porous surfaces, using brushes, rollers, or pasting machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying thinned glue to one surface area (e.g., a wall section or panel). A competent worker preps and applies glue to a typical surface in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool-use (brush/roller/machine), controlled force, uniform coverage on varied surfaces, and handling viscous liquids. Current humanoids struggle with consistent fluid application, tool switching, and surface generalization in unstructured environments.

task: Apply adhesives to the backs of paper strips, using brushes, or dunk strips of prepasted wallcovering in water, wiping off any excess adhesive.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one wallpaper strip (brushing adhesive or dunking prepasted strip). A competent worker does this in ~2–4 minutes per strip.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling large, limp, deformable paper without tearing, precise even adhesive brush application, and wiping excess — all highly challenging for current humanoids lacking fine dexterity and deformable-object manipulation.

task: Connect heating or air conditioning equipment to fuel, water, or refrigerant source to form complete circuit.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting one HVAC unit to its supply source (fuel line, water pipe, or refrigerant line). A skilled technician typically takes 30–60 min including fitting, sealing, and checking connections.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torque on threaded fittings, leak-free sealing, handling flexible refrigerant/fuel lines in tight mechanical spaces, and using specialized tools (flaring, brazing, manifold gauges). Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use capability.

task: Inspect derricks, or order their inspection, prior to being raised or lowered.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one derrick before a raise/lower operation. A competent rigger/supervisor walks the structure, checks components, and documents findings — typically 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Derricks are large, outdoor, structurally complex equipment. Current humanoids lack the mobility for climbing/traversing derrick structures, the specialized perception for structural defect detection, and cannot legally/practically order inspections or operate in such hazardous industrial sites autonomously.

task: Guide lengths of pipe into and out of elevators.
human_time_rationale: One instance = guiding one length of pipe into or out of an elevator car. A worker manually aligns, steadies, and slides the pipe through the doors, taking ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinating long, heavy, unwieldy object through narrow elevator doors with precise spatial awareness. Current humanoids lack the force control, bimanual coordination, and situational judgment needed for safely maneuvering oversized loads in confined spaces.

task: Start pumps that circulate mud through drill pipes and boreholes to cool drill bits and flush out drill cuttings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting the mud circulation pumps for a single drilling operation. A competent driller checks gauges, opens valves, and engages pumps in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized rig control panels, valves, and monitoring pressure/flow gauges in a hazardous, unstructured industrial environment. Current humanoids lack the tool-use, domain-specific interfacing, and safety certification for oilfield equipment.

task: Patrol work areas to ensure that solutions in tanks or troughs are not in danger of overflowing.
human_time_rationale: One patrol circuit of a work area checking tank/trough fill levels; a competent worker walks the area and visually inspects each vessel, taking ~10 minutes per round.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can walk flat indoor terrain and use cameras to observe liquid levels, but reliably reading fluid levels in varied tanks, interpreting overflow risk, and navigating cluttered industrial floors without intervention remains challenging for current humanoids.

task: Inspect operating units, such as towers, soap-spray storage tanks, scrubbers, collectors, or driers to ensure that all are functioning and to maintain maximum efficiency.
human_time_rationale: One inspection round of multiple industrial units (towers, tanks, scrubbers, etc.) in a processing facility; a competent operator typically takes 30–60 min per shift walkthrough.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex industrial environments, reading gauges/indicators, identifying anomalies, and climbing ladders or grating. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor/industrial terrain mobility, gauge-reading accuracy, and anomaly-recognition generalization needed for acceptable autonomous inspection.

task: Operate mechanical hoppers and provide assistance in their adjustment and repair.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one operational session of running/monitoring a hopper plus minor adjustment or repair. A competent operator typically spends ~45 min per shift segment on this cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids cannot operate heavy industrial hopper controls, perform mechanical adjustments requiring significant force/specialized tools, or safely navigate the dynamic industrial floor environment autonomously at acceptable quality. Repair assistance adds further dexterous and diagnostic complexity beyond 2026 frontier capability.

task: Inspect derricks for flaws, and clean and oil derricks to maintain proper working conditions.
human_time_rationale: One inspection + cleaning + oiling cycle of a single derrick; a competent rig hand typically spends ~45–90 min per derrick per maintenance session.
robot_success_rationale: Derricks are large, complex, outdoor/elevated structures requiring climbing, hazard identification, and applying lubricants to hard-to-reach joints. Current humanoids lack the climbing ability, reach, and specialized tool use needed. Rough-terrain mobility and height are disqualifying constraints.

task: Mark vertical guidelines on walls to align strips, using plumb bobs and chalk lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking one vertical guideline on a wall. A skilled worker sets a plumb bob or chalk line and snaps/marks a line in ~5–10 minutes per strip.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling delicate string tools (plumb bob, chalk line), precise vertical alignment perception, snapping a taut line against a wall, and navigating unstructured construction environments — all near the frontier's limits. Fine tool manipulation and accurate line snapping are major blockers.

task: Adjust system controls to settings recommended by manufacturer to balance system.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting all system controls on a single unit to manufacturer-recommended balance settings; involves reading specs, locating controls, and iterating adjustments — typically 20–45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading manufacturer specs, identifying non-standard controls (valves, dampers, knobs), interpreting real-time sensor feedback, and fine-tuning to spec — far beyond current robot generalization, perception, and adaptive tool-use capabilities.

task: Repair pumps, mud tanks, and related equipment.
human_time_rationale: One repair instance on a pump, mud tank, or related equipment; median job (e.g., replacing a pump liner/seal or fixing a tank fitting) takes ~2 hours for a competent rig mechanic.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy-force tool use, navigating cramped/hazardous oilfield environments, diagnosing faults, and performing high-torque mechanical work. Far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity, strength, and generalization limits.

task: Repair or replace damaged equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and repairing/replacing one damaged piece of equipment. A competent technician typically spends ~1 hour including diagnosis, sourcing parts, and execution.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosis, tool selection, fine dexterity, and generalization across highly varied equipment types and failure modes. Current humanoid robots lack the perception, reasoning, and dexterous tool-use needed for autonomous repair tasks.

task: Braze or solder parts to repair defective joints and leaks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single defective joint or leak. A competent plumber/technician locates, prepares, heats, and solders/brazes one joint in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise flame/torch control, reading heat color cues, applying filler material at exact moment, and working in tight spaces. Fine dexterous tool-use with thermal feedback far exceeds current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Cut or drill holes in floors, walls, or roof to install equipment, using power saws or drills.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting/drilling one set of holes for a single equipment installation. A competent tradesperson measures, marks, and cuts/drills in ~20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement/marking, high-force tool use (power saws, drills) on varied surfaces (drywall, concrete, wood, roofing), complex positioning, and safety judgment. Current humanoids lack the force control, tool-wielding stability, and situational awareness for this reliably.

task: Set up equipment, such as pasteboards and scaffolds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a standard pasteboard or scaffold unit at a worksite. A competent worker typically takes 20–40 minutes to assemble and position such equipment safely.
robot_success_rationale: Scaffolding setup requires heavy lifting, precise structural assembly, safety-critical locking mechanisms, outdoor/uneven terrain navigation, and multi-step sequencing with large unwieldy components — well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Weigh clay, and mix with water and chemicals to make drilling mud, using portable mixers.
human_time_rationale: One batch of drilling mud: weighing clay/chemicals, adding water, and mixing with a portable mixer until spec. Typical oilfield mud batch takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a portable mixer, weighing bulk materials (clay bags/chemicals), precise pouring, and fluid consistency judgment in an outdoor/industrial setting. Poor generalization to heavy bags, wet/uneven terrain, and chemical handling safety requirements.

task: Place strips or sections of paper on surfaces, aligning section edges and patterns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing one strip/section of wallpaper or similar material on a surface with edge and pattern alignment. A skilled worker takes ~10–15 min per strip including measuring, positioning, and aligning.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual handling of large, floppy, wet/adhesive paper; fine visual pattern-matching alignment; working on vertical surfaces at varying heights. Current robots lack the dexterity, compliant manipulation, and spatial reasoning for this task.

task: Install burners and auxiliary equipment, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one burner with auxiliary equipment (valves, fittings, ignition components). A competent technician typically takes 45–90 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torquing of fittings, threading pipes, routing gas/fuel lines, and working in tight or varied industrial spaces. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and tool-use reliability for safe, code-compliant burner installation.

task: Repair, modify, or maintain medical supportive devices, such as artificial limbs, braces, or surgical supports, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing or modifying a single device (e.g., a prosthetic limb or brace). A skilled orthotist/prosthetist typically spends 30–60 min per repair/modification session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity with specialized tools, patient-specific fit assessment, material shaping (heat, grinding, padding), and clinical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and domain-specific tooling capability.

task: Start pumps to wash and rinse reactor vessels, to exhaust gases or vapors, to regulate the flow of oil, steam, air, or perfume to towers, or to add products to converter or blending vessels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting a pump for a specific process step (e.g., initiating a wash cycle or flow regulation). A competent operator locates the pump, checks gauges, and engages controls in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating industrial environments, reading analog/digital gauges, operating specialized valves and control panels, and interpreting process context. Current humanoids lack reliable industrial panel interaction, sensor reading, and process-aware decision-making at this fidelity.

task: Make orthotic or prosthetic devices, using materials such as thermoplastic and thermosetting materials, metal alloys and leather, and hand or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single custom orthotic/prosthetic device from raw materials to finished product; skilled orthotist/prosthetist typically spends 2–4 hours per device.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level fine dexterity across wildly varied materials (thermoplastics, leather, metals), custom shaping, heat-forming, fitting judgments, and precise hand/power tool use — far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Measure, cut, thread, or bend pipe or tubing, using pipe fitter's tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring, cutting, threading, or bending a single pipe/tube section to spec. A competent pipefitter typically takes 15–25 minutes per piece depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, force-controlled tool use (pipe cutters, threaders, benders), and interpreting specs. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, grip strength calibration, and tool-use generalization for reliable autonomous pipefitting.

task: Turn valves to regulate flow of products or byproducts through agitator tanks, storage drums, or neutralizer tanks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating and turning the relevant valve(s) to set/adjust flow for a single tank or drum, including checking the result. Typically a brief, routine operation.
robot_success_rationale: Valves vary widely in type, size, torque, and orientation. Robots struggle with forceful wrist-torque tasks, precise valve identification in cluttered industrial settings, and confirming flow outcomes. Generalization across valve styles is poor in 2026.

task: Test electrical systems to determine voltages, using voltage meters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing voltage at a single circuit or terminal. A competent electrician positions probes, reads the meter, and records results in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous probe placement on specific terminals, often in tight/cluttered panels. Robots struggle with precise two-probe contact, reading analog/digital meters, and safely navigating live electrical hazards without task-specific training.

task: Recommend, develop, or perform preventive or general maintenance procedures, such as cleaning, power-washing, or vacuuming equipment, oiling parts, or changing filters.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance session on a piece of equipment (e.g., cleaning, oiling, filter change). Typical technician job takes 30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Task bundle spans vacuuming (moderate), power-washing (tool setup hard), oiling joints (fine dexterity), and filter swaps (varied connectors/latches). Each sub-task is partially feasible but combined autonomy across unstructured equipment types is very low.

task: Remove old paper, using water, steam machines, or solvents and scrapers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stripping wallpaper from one average room (~150 sq ft of walls). A competent worker using steam/solvents and scrapers typically takes 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained forceful scraping against irregular, wet wall surfaces; coordinated tool use (steam machine + scraper); high variability in paper type, adhesion, and wall condition. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capability at acceptable quality.

task: Lay out reference points for installation of structural and functional components, using measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out reference points for a single structural component setup. A skilled tradesperson measures, marks, and verifies layout points, typically 30–60 min per component/assembly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement instrument use (tape, laser level, total station), fine marking dexterity, spatial reasoning in unstructured construction environments, and interpretation of blueprints — well beyond current humanoid capability at acceptable quality.

task: Remove paint, varnish, dirt, and grease from surfaces, using paint remover and water soda solutions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating one surface/panel (e.g., a door or wall section). Applying chemical stripper, dwell time, and scrubbing typically takes ~30–60 minutes for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires applying liquid chemicals, scrubbing with variable force/technique, judging dwell time, and adapting to irregular surface conditions. Current robots lack reliable wet-chemical handling, tactile feedback for scrubbing efficacy, and generalization across surface types.

task: Clamp holding fixtures on ends of hoisting cables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clamping a single holding fixture onto one cable end. A competent rigger positions, aligns, and tightens the clamp in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torquing of fasteners onto a flexible steel cable end, managing tool alignment under tension, and safety-critical force control. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, grip strength calibration, and tool-use reliability for this task.

task: Drive tractor-trailer trucks to move equipment from site to site.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one site-to-site trip. Typical haul including loading, driving, and unloading averages ~3 hours for a regional equipment move.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot drive tractor-trailers on public/site roads. L4 AVs are narrow-domain systems, not general-purpose robots. Maneuvering a large articulated vehicle in unstructured site environments is far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: String cables through pulleys and blocks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rigging a single pulley/block system (2–4 pulleys). A competent rigger threads, tensions, and secures cable in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires threading flexible cable through small, precise channels/sheaves in 3D space with significant force management. Bimanual dexterity, cable-handling, and topological reasoning far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Observe children's behavior for irregularities, take temperature, transport children to doctor, or administer medications, as directed, to maintain children's health.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one health-check cycle per child (observation, temperature reading, decision, and any medication/transport action). A competent caregiver typically completes this in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced behavioral observation, social/emotional perception, safe dexterous medication handling, autonomous transport judgment, and child-safe interaction — all far beyond current humanoid capabilities. Safety liability is extreme.

task: Fill holes, cracks, and other surface imperfections preparatory to covering surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling all holes/cracks on a typical wall section before painting. Includes inspection, mixing filler, applying, and rough smoothing — ~20–30 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires subtle surface inspection to find defects, precise tool use (putty knife, caulk gun), force-sensitive application, and aesthetic judgment on finish quality — well beyond current robot dexterity and perception reliability.

task: Trim excess material at ceilings or baseboards, using knives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming one wall's ceiling or baseboard edge; a skilled flooring/paint finisher takes ~5–10 min to score, cut, and clean a typical run.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise blade control along irregular, paint/adhesive-coated joints at extreme angles (floor/ceiling). Fine dexterity, accurate force regulation, and consistent edge-following against variable surfaces far exceed current humanoid capability.

task: Place safety cones around blast areas to alert other workers of danger zones, and signal workers as necessary to ensure that they clear blast sites prior to explosions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up cones around one blast area and signaling workers to clear. Includes walking the perimeter, placing cones, and coordinating clearance — typically 20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor rough-terrain locomotion, carrying/placing cones, and critically, communicating with human workers to coordinate clearance. Worker signaling demands social/verbal interaction, situational awareness, and safety-critical judgment far beyond current robot capabilities.

task: Estimate, order, pick up, deliver, and install materials and supplies needed to maintain equipment in good working condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full cycle for one maintenance job: estimating needs, ordering, picking up, delivering to site, and installing materials. Typical skilled maintenance worker takes ~1 hour end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Task spans cognitive planning (estimating, ordering), vehicle operation (pickup/delivery), and installation dexterity. No 2026 humanoid can autonomously handle multi-step procurement workflows, drive to suppliers, and install arbitrary maintenance materials end-to-end.

task: Install expansion and control valves, using acetylene torches and wrenches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one set of expansion/control valves in an HVAC/refrigeration system, including brazing with acetylene torch and tightening fittings. Typical HVAC tech takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise brazing with an acetylene torch (open flame, heat control), fine torque-specific wrenching on varied fittings, leak-free joints, and working in cramped mechanical spaces — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use capability.

task: Control the viscosity and weight of the drilling fluid.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one mud check cycle: sampling, running viscometer/density tests, interpreting results, and adding additives to bring fluid into spec. Typically 20–40 min per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized oilfield instruments (Marsh funnel, mud balance, viscometer), interpreting fluid readings, and dosing chemical additives at a rig mud pit — highly unstructured, hazardous, tool-specialized environment far beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Apply acetic acid to damp plaster to prevent lime from bleeding through paper.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating one typical wall section (~10–20 sq ft). A competent worker mixes/prepares acid solution and applies it evenly with a brush or sponge in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires controlled liquid application to a damp, uneven plaster surface, tool handling (brush/sponge), and coverage judgment. Unstructured surface texture, liquid management, and quality assessment challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Steady pipes during connection to or disconnection from drill or casing strings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = steadying a single pipe joint during one make-up or break-out connection. A roughneck stabilizes the pipe for ~2–4 minutes per connection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires forceful grip on heavy, oily, swinging pipe in a loud, dynamic rig floor environment with high safety stakes. Current humanoids lack sufficient strength, robust perception in dirty/wet conditions, and real-time reactive balance for this hazardous task.

task: Produce rough concrete surface, using broom.
human_time_rationale: One instance = brooming a freshly poured concrete slab (e.g. ~10–20 sq ft). A skilled worker drags a stiff broom across wet concrete in a few passes, taking ~10–20 minutes including setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force/speed control on semi-liquid concrete, correct timing window, coordinated two-handed broom dragging over uneven wet surfaces, and outdoor mobility on fresh concrete — all beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Position and align derrick elements, using harnesses and platform climbing devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning and aligning one derrick element. Involves rigging, climbing to height, guiding, and securing — typically 1–2 hours for a skilled ironworker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing a derrick structure with harnesses and platform devices, working at extreme heights, aligning heavy structural elements — far beyond any 2026 humanoid's mobility, load-bearing, and high-altitude dexterity capabilities.

task: Measure depths of drilled blast holes, using weighted tape measures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring one blast hole. A competent worker lowers a weighted tape, reads the depth, and records it — typically 2–4 minutes per hole.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/rough terrain mobility near blast holes (low stability, fall risk), precise manipulation of a weighted tape in a narrow opening, accurate depth reading, and recording — well beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity in unstructured mining environments.

task: Examine blast areas to determine amounts and kinds of explosive charges needed and to ensure that safety laws are observed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining a single blast site, assessing geology/structure, calculating charge requirements, and verifying safety compliance. A competent blaster typically spends ~1 hour per site assessment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level geological/structural judgment, regulatory knowledge, hazard risk assessment, and complex multi-factor decision-making. Current robots lack the cognitive reasoning, domain expertise, and environmental adaptability for unstructured blast sites.

task: Tie specified lengths of delaying fuses into patterns in order to time sequences of explosions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tying a single fuse pattern for a blasting sequence. A competent blaster measures, cuts, and knots multiple fuse lengths into a timed pattern, typically taking 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-dexterous knot-tying of flexible cord, exact length measurement, and safety-critical accuracy. Current humanoids lack the finger dexterity and compliance control for reliable fuse knotting; any failure is catastrophically unsafe.

task: Drill and tap holes for rivets, and glue, weld, bolt, or rivet parts together to form prosthetic or orthotic devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one prosthetic/orthotic device. A skilled orthotist/prosthetist spends ~30–60 min drilling, tapping, and joining all parts for a single device.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme precision dexterity (drilling/tapping to tight tolerances), multi-process tool switching (drill, tap, welder, rivet gun, adhesives), and highly varied custom geometry. Far beyond current humanoid manipulation capability.

task: Repair or service heating, ventilating, and air conditioning (HVAC) systems to improve efficiency, such as by changing filters, cleaning ducts, and refilling non-toxic refrigerants.
human_time_rationale: One standard HVAC service visit: filter change, duct cleaning check, and refrigerant top-off for a typical residential unit takes ~45–75 minutes for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating tight mechanical spaces, identifying varied HVAC components, handling refrigerant gauges/hoses, and diagnosing system issues. Current humanoids lack the specialized tool-use, fine dexterity, and domain perception needed across diverse, unstructured HVAC environments.

task: Conduct visual inspections of compressed natural gas fuel systems to identify cracks, gouges, abrasions, discoloration, broken fibers, loose brackets, damaged gaskets, or other problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full visual inspection of a single vehicle's CNG fuel system. A trained technician systematically checks cylinders, lines, brackets, and fittings, typically taking 20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating under/around a vehicle, contorting to access confined spaces, and detecting subtle defects (micro-cracks, fiber damage, discoloration). Current robots lack the perception acuity, flexible mobility, and domain-specific visual defect recognition needed for safety-critical pass/fail judgment.

task: Sweep and clean roofs to prepare them for the application of new roofing materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sweeping and cleaning one typical residential roof section (~1,500 sq ft). A competent roofer takes ~45–75 min using brooms, blowers, and scrapers.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor uneven sloped roof surface is extremely hazardous for locomotion; current humanoids lack the balance, fall-arrest capability, and tool-use adaptability for pitched roof traversal. Near-zero practical success.

task: Bend, form, and shape fabric or material to conform to prescribed contours of structural components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = conforming one fabric/material piece to a structural component. A skilled worker typically spends 15–25 min depending on material stiffness and contour complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained fine bimanual dexterity, force-controlled shaping, and precise contour-following on varied 3D surfaces. Current humanoids lack the tactile feedback, compliance control, and tool-use precision needed for consistent quality results.

task: Construct or receive casts or impressions of patients' torsos or limbs for use as cutting and fabrication patterns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = casting one body segment (torso or limb) for one patient. Includes preparation, application of casting material, curing wait time, and removal — typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, compliant contact with a human body, fine dexterous material handling (plaster/fiberglass bandaging), patient communication, and anatomical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and safety standards for body contact.

task: Lift and align components into position, using hoist or block and tackle.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lifting and aligning a single heavy component using a hoist/block-and-tackle until it is correctly positioned; typical in industrial/construction settings, ~20–30 min per component.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a hoist or block-and-tackle (specialized rigging equipment), precise 3-D alignment under load, and heavy-load force management — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, payload, and tool-use generalization limits.

task: Staple or tack advertising posters onto fences, walls, billboards, or poles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = posting one poster on a surface. Includes unrolling/positioning poster, aligning it, and stapling/tacking it securely. A competent worker does this in ~3–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor mobility on varied terrain, precise two-handed coordination to hold and align a flexible poster, and operating a staple gun or tack against unstructured surfaces (poles, fences, walls). Flexible material handling and outdoor generalization remain major limitations for 2026 humanoids.

task: Defrost frozen valves, using steam hoses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = defrosting one frozen valve. Worker positions steam hose, applies steam until thawed, and confirms valve moves freely — typically 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating industrial/outdoor environments, identifying frozen valves, connecting and maneuvering a flexible steam hose safely, and judging thaw completion. Current robots lack robust tool-coupling dexterity, steam-hose handling, and hazardous-environment judgment.

task: Connect electrical wire to primers, and cover charges or fill blast holes with clay, drill chips, sand, or other material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wiring a primer and stemming one blast hole. A skilled blaster typically takes 15–25 minutes per hole accounting for wire connection, tamping, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating in hazardous outdoor/underground terrain, fine dexterous wire-to-primer connection, force-controlled stemming in irregular boreholes, and safety-critical judgment. Far beyond 2026 robot capabilities in unstructured blasting environments.

task: Weld components in flat, vertical, or overhead positions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single weld joint (prep, tack, and full weld pass) in a given position. A competent welder typically takes 20–40 minutes per joint depending on size and position.
robot_success_rationale: Welding requires extreme precision, heat/arc management, torch control, and real-time quality judgment across multiple body positions (especially overhead). General-purpose humanoids lack welding tool integration, thermal sensing, and the fine dexterity needed for consistent weld quality. Dedicated robotic welders exist but are fixed/task-specific, not general-purpose humanoids.

task: Mount compressor, condenser, and other components in specified locations on frames, using hand tools and acetylene welding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting compressor, condenser, and related components onto one unit frame; a skilled HVAC/refrigeration technician takes ~45–90 min total including layout, bolting, and welding.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy-component manipulation, precise positioning on a frame, torqued fastening with hand tools, AND acetylene welding — a complex multi-skill task. Current humanoids lack the strength, dexterous tool-use, and welding capability for this reliably.

task: Assemble eyeglass frames and attach shields, nose pads, and temple pieces, using pliers, screwdrivers, and drills.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one complete eyeglass frame with all attachments. A skilled optical technician typically takes 10–20 minutes per frame.
robot_success_rationale: Eyeglass components are tiny, lightweight, and require extreme precision dexterity (micro-screws, small hinges, delicate shields/nose pads). Current humanoid robots lack the fine motor control and tool manipulation needed for sub-millimeter assembly with pliers, screwdrivers, and drills.

task: Lay out and mark dimensions of parts, using templates and precision measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out and marking one part. A competent worker uses templates and measuring tools to scribe/mark a single workpiece, typically taking 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool-use (scribers, calipers, height gauges), precise force control, and accurate spatial reasoning on varied part geometries. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-manipulation and precision marking capability at acceptable quality.

task: Check to ensure that completed roofs are watertight.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one completed roof. A competent roofer walks the surface, checks seams, flashing, penetrations, and may use water testing or moisture meters; ~45 min median for a typical residential roof.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating steep, sloped, uneven outdoor surfaces (poor robot mobility), applying domain-specific inspection techniques (water testing, probing flashing), and making nuanced waterproofing judgments. Current humanoids lack the mobility, tools, and perception for this unstructured outdoor task.

task: Mix paste, using paste powder and water, and brush paste onto surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing a batch of paste and brushing onto one surface section. Includes measuring/mixing powder+water and brushing application; ~10 min for a typical panel or sheet.
robot_success_rationale: Requires pouring/measuring powder and water (moderate dexterity), stirring to correct consistency (hard to judge), then brushing evenly across a surface (force/coverage control). Perception of paste consistency and even coating quality are major hurdles for current humanoids.

task: Install dehumidifiers or related equipment for spaces that require cool, dry air to operate efficiently, such as computer rooms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one dehumidifier unit: positioning, connecting drainage line and electrical, configuring settings, and verifying operation. Typically 60–120 min for a skilled technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires routing and connecting electrical conduit/plugs, threading drain hoses, navigating tight server room spaces, and judgment-based placement. Multi-step tool use, fine electrical connections, and adaptive problem-solving far exceed current humanoid capabilities.

task: Check finished wallcoverings for proper alignment, pattern matching, and neatness of seams.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one room's wallcovering. A competent worker walks the perimeter checking seams, pattern alignment, and neatness; ~5–10 min for a typical room.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locomotion along walls, fine visual pattern-matching, and seam-quality judgment. Current robots struggle with close-range detailed visual inspection, navigating tight room perimeters, and applying nuanced aesthetic quality standards.

task: Place explosive charges in holes or other spots; then detonate explosives to demolish structures or to loosen, remove, or displace earth, rock, or other materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing and detonating charges for a single blast event (e.g., one rock face or demolition shot). Includes charge placement, wiring, safety checks, and detonation; typically 1–3 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating unstructured outdoor/demolition terrain, precise placement of explosives in irregular holes, complex wiring, regulatory compliance, and safety judgment. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities; extremely high-stakes error cost.

task: Apply weatherproof, decorative coverings to exterior surfaces of buildings, such as by troweling or spraying on coats of stucco.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying stucco to one typical exterior wall section (~100 sq ft). Includes mixing, applying base/finish coats by trowel or spray, and texturing. ~3–4 hours for a skilled plasterer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained outdoor mobility on scaffolding/uneven surfaces, precise force-controlled troweling over large irregular surfaces, mixing heavy material, and aesthetic texture judgment. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capability in real exterior job sites.

task: Mix pigments to match patients' skin coloring, according to formulas, and apply mixtures to orthotic or prosthetic devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing pigments per formula and applying to a single device. Includes color matching, blending, and careful application — a skilled technician typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced color perception for patient-matched skin tones, precise liquid pigment mixing, fine brush/applicator control, and aesthetic judgment. Current robots lack reliable color-matching feedback, fine dexterity for blending, and perceptual standards needed for cosmetic quality outcomes.

task: Insert, pack, and pour explosives, such as dynamite, ammonium nitrate, black powder, or slurries into blast holes; then shovel drill cuttings, admit water into boreholes, and tamp material to compact charges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading and tamping a single blast hole. Includes inserting explosive, pouring/packing material, shoveling cuttings, admitting water, and tamping — typically 30–60 min per hole for a competent blaster.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor unstructured terrain, heavy/irregular materials, precise explosive handling, and borehole alignment demand dexterity and situational judgment far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities. Safety-critical nature makes autonomous deployment essentially prohibited.

task: Locate worn or torn areas in roofs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one roof. A competent roofer walks the full surface, checks edges, flashing, and penetrations, typically taking 30–60 minutes for a standard residential roof.
robot_success_rationale: Roof surfaces are uneven, sloped, slippery, and outdoors — challenging for humanoid locomotion (~30–60% rough terrain). Detecting subtle wear/tears requires fine visual discrimination. Fall risk and generalization across roof types further limits success.

task: Inventory or estimate plant and wildlife populations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single field survey session (e.g., one transect or plot). A competent biologist typically spends 2–4 hours per survey unit counting, identifying, and recording species.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor unstructured terrain navigation, species identification across diverse flora/fauna, camouflaged or moving animals, and expert taxonomic judgment. Current robots lack robust outdoor mobility, bio-identification, and autonomous field survey capability.

task: Gauge tank levels, using calibrated rods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = gauging one tank. A worker walks to the tank, inserts a calibrated rod, reads the wetted mark, and logs the level. Typically 3–5 minutes per tank.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise rod insertion into a small tank opening, reading a wetted analog mark, and recording — combining fine dexterity, close visual inspection, and outdoor/industrial mobility. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-tool insertion and analog-scale reading in unstructured field settings.

task: Set up and operate ground support and test equipment to perform functional flight tests of electrical and electronic systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single functional flight test session on one aircraft system; setup, test execution, and documentation typically takes 1–3 hours for a competent avionics technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized avionics ground support equipment, precise connector mating, interpreting complex test procedures, and expert-level judgment on pass/fail criteria. Far beyond 2026 humanoid robot capability in unstructured aerospace environments.

task: Install or repair air purification systems, such as specialized filters or ultraviolet (UV) light purification systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing or repairing one air purification system. A skilled HVAC tech spends ~60–90 min on inspection, fitting, sealing, wiring, and functional testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating confined HVAC spaces, fine dexterous wiring, tool use with torque precision, leak/UV testing, and situational troubleshooting. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity in unstructured environments.

task: Attach roofing paper and composition shingles, using nails.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing roofing paper + shingles on one standard roof square (100 sq ft). A competent roofer takes ~3–4 hours per square including layout, nailing, and overlap alignment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working on steep, unstable outdoor roof surfaces (severe mobility/balance challenge), handling large flexible materials, precise overlapping and nailing patterns, and safe navigation at height — far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Lower perforating guns into wells, using hoists; then use measuring devices and instrument panels to position guns in correct positions for taking samples.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lowering guns into a single well and positioning them correctly. Rigging, hoisting, measuring, and instrument-panel adjustment typically takes 1–2 hours for a skilled well perforator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy hoisting equipment, reading specialized downhole instrument panels, and precise depth positioning in a hazardous oilfield environment. Current humanoid robots cannot operate such specialized well-service machinery autonomously or safely.

task: Lay primacord between rows of charged blast holes, and tie cord into main lines to form blast patterns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying and tying primacord across one blast pattern (row of holes). A trained blaster needs ~30–60 min to route cord, make tie-ins, and verify connections.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor rough terrain mobility, precise knot-tying of detonating cord, hazardous materials handling, and regulatory compliance. No current humanoid robot has demonstrated explosive ordnance handling or reliable outdoor unstructured fine manipulation at this level.

task: Install or repair self-contained ground source heat pumps or hybrid ground or air source heat pumps to minimize carbon-based energy consumption and reduce carbon emissions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing or repairing one heat pump system. A competent HVAC technician typically spends a full day (6–8 hours) on installation or a few hours on repair; 480 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires licensed refrigerant handling, complex electrical/plumbing connections, outdoor/indoor terrain navigation, heavy equipment manipulation, fine torque-critical assembly, and regulatory compliance — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Shape lenses appropriately so that they can be inserted into frames.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shaping a single pair of lenses to fit a specific frame. An experienced optician takes ~10–15 minutes including edging, beveling, and checking fit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterous manipulation of fragile optical-grade lenses, specialized lens edging machinery, and micron-level tolerance fitting — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use capability. High breakage/quality risk.

task: Move controls to start and regulate movement of conveyors and to start and position drill cutters or torches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting conveyors and positioning drill cutters/torches for a single production run setup; a skilled operator completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial control panels, precisely positioning drill cutters or torches, and regulating conveyor movement. Humanoids lack the trained tool-use, force feedback, and system integration needed for safe, autonomous operation of such machinery.

task: Guide and direct flames or electrodes on or across workpieces to straighten, bend, melt, or build up metal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single straightening, bending, melting, or build-up operation on one workpiece. A competent welder/metalworker typically takes 15–25 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torch/electrode path control, real-time heat sensing, adaptive feedback on metal behavior, and fire-safe tool handling. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, thermal perception, and generalization for open-flame metalworking autonomously.

task: Assemble and position equipment, explosives, and blasting caps in holes at specified depths, or load perforating guns or torpedoes with explosives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and loading one borehole or perforating gun setup. Skilled blasters typically spend 45–90 min per hole including handling, positioning, and depth verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling live explosives and blasting caps with extreme precision in unstructured outdoor/underground environments. Current robots lack the fine dexterity, safety-critical compliance, terrain adaptability, and regulatory certification needed. Essentially zero viable deployment.

task: Set up machines to polish, bevel, edge, or grind lenses, flats, blanks, or other precision optical elements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a machine for one batch/job of optical element processing. A skilled optical technician typically spends 20–30 min selecting tooling, mounting fixtures, dialing in parameters, and verifying alignment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter precision fixture alignment, fine dexterous tool changes, reading/interpreting job specifications, and tuning machine parameters. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and perception for unstructured precision optical setups.

task: Remove lenses from molds and separate lenses in containers for further processing or storage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing and separating a single lens from its mold into a container. A skilled worker handles this quickly on a production line.
robot_success_rationale: Contact lenses are tiny, transparent, fragile, and deformable — demanding extreme fine dexterity and precise tactile/visual feedback far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities. High risk of damage or misplacement.

task: Clean work areas and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one work area/station including associated equipment; wiping surfaces, clearing debris, and cleaning tools takes a competent worker ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep/mop flat surfaces and wipe broad areas reasonably well, but cleaning equipment (varied shapes, nooks, attachments) demands dexterity, tool-switching, and generalization that current humanoids handle poorly.

task: Perform emergency leak repairs and general maintenance for a variety of roof types.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing an emergency leak repair plus general maintenance on one roof. A competent roofer typically spends ~2 hours locating the leak, patching/sealing it, and performing basic maintenance checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor locomotion on steep, slippery, uneven roof surfaces; fine dexterous tool use (caulk guns, pry bars, torches); leak diagnosis; and adapting to highly varied roof types. Far beyond 2026 frontier humanoid capability.

task: Preheat workpieces prior to welding or bending, using torches or heating furnaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preheating one workpiece. Worker sets up torch or furnace, applies heat evenly, and confirms target temperature with a pyrometer or temp stick in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating an oxy-fuel/propane torch or furnace controls, precise heat application to a metal workpiece, and temperature verification — demanding tool-use, thermal sensing, and safety management well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Install electrical and electronic components, assemblies, and systems in aircraft, using hand tools, power tools, or soldering irons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single electrical/electronic component or assembly in an aircraft. A competent avionics technician typically takes 1–3 hours per component, depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme precision soldering, routing wires in confined airframe spaces, FAA-standard workmanship, tool changes, and connector seating verification. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, aviation-spec compliance awareness, and confined-space manipulation needed.

task: Chip or grind off excess weld, slag, or spatter, using hand scrapers or power chippers, portable grinders, or arc-cutting equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning excess weld/slag/spatter from a single weld joint or seam. A competent welder/grinder takes ~5–15 min depending on weld length and method.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled tool use (grinder, chipper, arc-cutter) against irregular weld geometry, fine tactile feedback, and adaptive repositioning. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool compliance, and perception accuracy for consistent autonomous grinding/chipping to acceptable finish quality.

task: Position and adjust cutting tools to specified curvature, dimensions, and depth of cut.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and adjusting a cutting tool on a machine (lathe, mill, etc.) to specified parameters. A skilled machinist typically takes 5–15 minutes per tool setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of precision tooling, reading/interpreting machine dials or digital readouts, and micron-level adjustments. Current humanoids lack the fine motor precision, force feedback, and domain-specific tool knowledge for reliable success.

task: Clean or degrease parts, using wire brushes, portable grinders, or chemical baths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/degreasing a single part or small batch. A competent worker typically spends ~15 min handling, scrubbing, rinsing, and inspecting one part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool switching (brush, grinder, chemical bath), force-controlled scrubbing, chemical handling safety, and quality inspection. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use generalization and tactile feedback for unstructured degreasing tasks.

task: Perform mechanical overhauls and refrigerant reclaiming.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full mechanical overhaul of a typical HVAC/refrigeration unit, including disassembly, inspection, part replacement, and reassembly. Competent technician takes ~2–4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for torquing fittings, handling refrigerant hoses, operating recovery machines, diagnosing faults, and navigating tight mechanical spaces. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Hoist tar and roofing materials to roofs, using ropes and pulleys, or carry materials up ladders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = hoisting or carrying one load of materials to a roof. A competent roofer takes ~15–25 min to rig, hoist, and secure a typical bundle of shingles or bucket of tar.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing ladders, operating rope-and-pulley rigs, and navigating uneven rooftops under heavy load — well beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities in outdoor, unstructured, height-risk environments. Severe locomotion, strength, and safety limitations.

task: Inspect lens blanks to detect flaws, verify smoothness of surface, and ensure thickness of coating on lenses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one lens blank for flaws, surface smoothness, and coating thickness; a trained optical tech completes this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-precision optical/tactile sensing and specialized instruments (interferometers, coating gauges) beyond standard humanoid perception. Fine dexterous handling of delicate glass and interpreting subtle surface defects is well outside current frontier robot capability.

task: Clean finished lenses and eyeglasses, using cloths and solvents.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single pair of eyeglasses/lenses; a skilled optical worker wipes, inspects, and buffs each pair in roughly 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely precise dexterous grip on small, smooth, fragile lenses; controlled solvent application; visual inspection for smears/scratches. Current humanoids lack the fine fingertip sensitivity and delicate force control needed without risking damage.

task: Maintain tools and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting, cleaning, lubricating, and/or minor servicing of a typical tool or piece of equipment (e.g., a power drill or hand saw). A competent worker takes ~30 min per item.
robot_success_rationale: Maintenance requires nuanced visual inspection for wear/damage, fine dexterous manipulation (oiling, tightening, replacing parts), and contextual judgment about tool condition — well beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Align and clamp workpieces together, using rules, squares, or hand tools, or position items in fixtures, jigs, or vises.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning and clamping a single workpiece setup. A skilled worker spends ~5–10 min measuring, positioning, and securing parts in fixtures or vises.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual coordination, force-sensitive clamping, tool use (squares, rules, clamps), and fine spatial alignment judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous tool manipulation and sub-millimeter positioning accuracy needed here.

task: Apply shingles, gravel, or asphalt over the top layer of tar to protect the roofing material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying surface material (shingles, gravel, or asphalt) to one standard residential roof section (~100 sq ft / 1 square). A competent roofer takes ~1.5–2 hours per square.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working on steep, sloped, unstable outdoor surfaces at height; heavy material handling; precise fastening patterns; and navigating ladders/scaffolding. Far beyond 2026 humanoid locomotion, dexterity, and safety capabilities.

task: Take patients' body or limb measurements for use in device construction.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single patient's relevant body/limb dimensions (e.g. for a prosthetic or orthotic). Includes positioning, measuring, and recording results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires gentle physical contact, precise tactile feedback, patient cooperation/repositioning, and clinical judgment about landmark placement. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, compliant force control, and medical-grade perception needed for reliable autonomous measurement.

task: Select lens blanks, molds, tools, and polishing or grinding wheels, according to production specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting all required materials/tools for a single lens production job. A skilled optical worker reads specs and gathers items in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading production specs, identifying small specialized optical items (blanks, molds, grinding wheels) by type/grade, and precise retrieval. High perception and classification demands for visually similar fine optical components exceed current robot generalization and dexterity reliably.

task: Test medical supportive devices for proper alignment, movement, or biomechanical stability, using meters and alignment fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single device (e.g., orthotic, prosthetic, brace). A technician sets up fixtures, attaches meters, checks alignment/movement/stability, and records results — typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of specialized measurement fixtures and meters, precise alignment judgment, and domain-specific interpretation of biomechanical data. Current humanoids lack the precision, tool-use fluency, and clinical reasoning for autonomous pass/fail assessment.

task: Set dials and start machines to polish lenses or hold lenses against rotating wheels to polish them manually.
human_time_rationale: One instance = polishing one lens. Skilled optical tech sets dials and either runs the machine or hand-holds the lens against a rotating wheel; roughly 5–10 min per lens.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dial-setting on specialized machinery, fine force-feedback control while holding a small lens against a rotating wheel, and quality judgment. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, calibrated tactile sensing, and domain-specific tool-use needed.

task: Melt and apply solder along adjoining edges of workpieces to solder joints, using soldering irons, gas torches, or electric-ultrasonic equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = soldering one joint/seam on a workpiece. A competent solderer typically takes 3–15 minutes depending on joint complexity; ~8 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise thermal control, fine tip manipulation along irregular edges, tool-switching (iron/torch), and real-time visual/tactile feedback. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, heat regulation, and multi-tool generalization needed for consistent quality solder joints.

task: Provide assistance to skilled roofers installing and repairing roofs, flashings, and surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting a roofer for a half-day shift (e.g., one residential roof section), including carrying materials, handing tools, and supporting flashing/surface work.
robot_success_rationale: Steep, sloped, slippery outdoor surfaces are far beyond current humanoid locomotion. Material handling, nail guns, flashing bending, and working at height with dynamic footing are all extremely challenging. No frontier robot is close to this.

task: Assemble prototypes or models of circuits, instruments, and systems for use in testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a single prototype circuit/instrument/system. A competent technician typically takes 60–120 min depending on complexity; 90 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (soldering, routing wires, seating connectors, reading schematics), precise tool use, and judgment about component placement. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Clean, gauge, and lubricate gun ports.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one gun port. A competent armorer cleans, gauges (measures with bore gauge), and lubricates one port in ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation inside a narrow bore/port, precise gauging tool use, and controlled lubricant application in a confined, irregular space — well beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use capability.

task: Grind, cut, buff, or bend edges of workpieces to be joined to ensure snug fit, using power grinders and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing edges of a single workpiece/joint. A skilled tradesperson spends ~10–20 min grinding, cutting, and fitting one weld joint to spec.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled tool use with angle grinders and hand tools, adaptive perception of irregular metal edges, and quality judgment for fit-up — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and generalization capabilities.

task: Test and troubleshoot instruments, components, and assemblies, using circuit testers, oscilloscopes, or voltmeters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing and troubleshooting a single instrument/component/assembly. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 minutes probing, interpreting readings, and diagnosing a fault.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise probe placement on small terminals, interpreting oscilloscope/voltmeter readings, adaptive troubleshooting logic, and tool manipulation. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, contextual electrical reasoning, and reliable probe-tip precision needed for autonomous diagnosis.

task: Chop tar into small pieces, and heat chopped tar in kettles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = chopping a batch of tar and heating it in a kettle; ~45 min covering chopping, loading, and bringing tar to working temperature.
robot_success_rationale: Tar is a hard, irregular, sticky material requiring forceful chopping with tools, then loading into a kettle and operating heating controls. Force application, hazardous heat management, and unstructured material handling are well beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Assemble components such as switches, electrical controls, and junction boxes, using hand tools or soldering irons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a single electrical sub-assembly (e.g., one junction box or control panel). A competent electrician/assembler typically takes 30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for soldering, wire routing, and fastening small terminals; varied part geometries challenge perception and grasp planning; tool switching (screwdriver, soldering iron) and precise force control are beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Check aircraft prior to flights to ensure that the engines, controls, instruments, and other systems are functioning properly.
human_time_rationale: One pre-flight inspection of a typical general aviation or commercial aircraft; includes walkaround, cockpit checks, and systems verification — typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing around large aircraft, operating cockpit controls, reading specialized instruments, and making airworthiness judgments. Humanoids lack aviation tool integration, domain knowledge actuation, and regulatory certification for this safety-critical task.

task: Select and install torches, torch tips, filler rods, and flux, according to welding chart specifications or types and thicknesses of metals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting and installing all consumables (torch, tip, filler rod, flux) for a single welding job setup. A skilled welder typically takes 5–10 minutes to consult specs and swap/install components.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting welding charts, identifying small labeled components (torch tips, filler rods by spec), fine-threaded torch tip installation, and cross-referencing metal type/thickness — far beyond current general-purpose robot dexterity, perception, and semantic reasoning.

task: Conduct in-flight tests and evaluations at specified altitudes and in all types of weather to determine the receptivity and other characteristics of equipment and systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single test flight sortie. Includes pre-flight setup, airborne testing at specified altitudes/conditions, and post-flight data review. Typical test flight runs 2–4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires piloting an aircraft, operating specialized avionics/test equipment, making real-time judgments in dynamic weather, and evaluating complex system outputs. No humanoid robot can operate aircraft in flight autonomously at this level.

task: Inspect completed work to certify that maintenance meets standards and that aircraft are ready for operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one aircraft post-maintenance. A certified A&P/IA mechanic typically spends 30–60 minutes reviewing work orders, physically inspecting surfaces, systems, and signing off airworthiness.
robot_success_rationale: Requires FAA/regulatory certification (legally impossible for a robot), expert aviation knowledge, fine visual/tactile inspection of complex systems, access to confined airframe areas, and judgment-based sign-off. Far beyond current robot capability.

task: Operate mining machines to gather coal and convey it to floors or shuttle cars.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full shift of continuous mining machine operation, coal extraction, and conveying to shuttle cars or floors; ~8-hour shift is the standard unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating massive specialized underground mining machines in hazardous, unstructured environments. Humanoid robots cannot interface with or control continuous miners, conveyors, or shuttle cars; extreme dust, low visibility, confined spaces, and safety-critical decisions compound the difficulty.

task: Repair, oil, and adjust machines, and change cutting teeth, using wrenches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full maintenance cycle on a single machine: inspecting, oiling, adjusting settings, and swapping cutting teeth. A skilled mechanic typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires torque-controlled wrench use, precise oiling of specific ports, diagnosing wear/misalignment, and swapping sharp cutting teeth — all demanding high dexterity, force feedback, and mechanical judgment far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Instruct patients in use of prosthetic or orthotic devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient instruction session covering fitting, donning/doffing, use, and care of one device. A competent clinician typically spends 30–60 minutes per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires adaptive verbal/nonverbal communication, clinical judgment, hands-on physical guidance, and empathetic patient assessment. Current robots lack natural language instruction ability, nuanced tactile guidance, and the clinical reasoning needed to safely train patients on medical devices.

task: Locate grain line patterns to determine how rocks will split when cut.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining one rock/stone specimen to identify grain line patterns and determine optimal splitting direction. A skilled stonemason or geologist takes ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert geological perception—identifying subtle surface grain, cleavage planes, and bedding layers. Current robots lack the specialized vision models, tactile feedback, and domain knowledge needed to reliably interpret rock microstructure for cutting guidance.

task: Repair broken parts, using precision hand tools and soldering irons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single broken part (e.g., a PCB solder joint or small mechanical component). A competent technician typically spends 30–60 minutes diagnosing, preparing, and completing the repair.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for precision hand tools and soldering iron control, tactile feedback, fault diagnosis, and adaptive manipulation of irregular broken parts — well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Lay out lenses and trace lens outlines on glass, using templates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out and tracing one lens outline on glass using a template. A skilled optical worker does this in ~5 minutes per lens.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous handling of smooth, transparent glass (hard to perceive/grip), accurate template alignment, and fine tracing with a marking tool — well beyond current humanoid robot dexterity and perception for transparent fragile objects.

task: Weld separately or in combination, using aluminum, stainless steel, cast iron, and other alloys.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single weld joint/seam on a workpiece. A competent welder spends ~30–60 min setting up, selecting parameters, and welding one typical joint.
robot_success_rationale: Welding demands precise torch control, material-specific parameter tuning, fume/heat tolerance, and quality inspection. General-purpose humanoids lack welding tool integration, fine dexterity for torch positioning, and material-adaptive process control.

task: Check baggage or cargo to ensure that it has been loaded correctly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single aircraft/vehicle's cargo hold or baggage compartment for correct loading; a competent ramp agent typically takes ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating tight, cluttered cargo holds, visually verifying load plans, reading labels, and confirming weight distribution — far beyond current humanoid perception/mobility/generalization in unstructured, constrained aircraft environments.

task: Set up and use ladders and scaffolding as necessary to complete work.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting, positioning, and climbing a ladder or erecting a basic scaffold section for a single work task. Typical setup takes 10–30 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot safely climb extension ladders or erect scaffolding autonomously. Requires dynamic balance on rungs, heavy load handling, and complex multi-step assembly on uneven ground — far beyond 2026 frontier capability.

task: Melt and apply solder to fill holes, indentations, or seams of fabricated metal products, using soldering equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling holes/seams on a single fabricated metal part. A skilled solderer typically prepares, applies, and finishes one part in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise thermal control, fine dexterity with a soldering iron, and visual/tactile feedback to detect fill quality. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation and heat regulation needed for consistent, inspection-passing solder joints on varied seam geometries.

task: Ignite torches or start power supplies and strike arcs by touching electrodes to metals being welded, completing electrical circuits.
human_time_rationale: One instance = igniting a torch/power supply and striking an arc on a single workpiece. A skilled welder does this in ~1–3 minutes including setup checks.
robot_success_rationale: Striking a welding arc requires precise electrode-to-metal contact, fine dexterity, high-temperature tool handling, and real-time feedback. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and welding-specific perception for autonomous arc initiation.

task: Hammer out bulges or bends in metal workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = correcting bulges/bends on a single metal workpiece. A skilled metalworker typically spends 10–20 minutes assessing, positioning, and hammering out defects on one piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled strikes, real-time tactile/visual feedback to judge deformation progress, and adaptive repositioning of blows. Current humanoid robots lack the fine force modulation, metal-working tool expertise, and closed-loop correction needed for acceptable results.
