task: Diagnose or troubleshoot problems with gas collection systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing/troubleshooting a single problem on a gas collection system; a competent technician typically spends ~1 hour inspecting, testing, and identifying the fault.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized instrument use (gas analyzers, pressure gauges), reading system schematics, interpreting complex sensor data, and expert-level diagnostic reasoning across unpredictable fault types — far beyond current robot generalization and cognition.

task: Test pipes, lines, components, and connections for leaks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a typical pipe/line system segment (e.g., a single circuit or section). A competent technician uses pressure gauges, soap solution, or electronic detectors over ~30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool use (pressure gauges, leak detectors), connecting fittings, interpreting readings, and navigating varied plumbing layouts. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity for coupling tools and contextual judgment for pass/fail assessment.

task: Move levers to raise and lower hydraulic safety bars supporting roofs above machines until other workers complete framing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = raising/lowering hydraulic safety bars for one machine; worker moves levers and confirms bar position in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating and operating heavy-duty industrial levers in a dynamic underground/mining environment, monitoring structural status, and coordinating with other workers—far beyond current humanoid dexterity, contextual awareness, and safety reliability.

task: Mark dimensions or outlines on stone prior to cutting, using rules and chalk lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking a single stone piece with dimensions/outlines before cutting. A skilled mason takes ~5–15 min depending on complexity of cuts required.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, chalk line snapping, and marking on irregular stone surfaces. Robots lack the fine dexterity, tactile feedback, and spatial planning needed for accurate layout work on unstructured stone; tool-use (chalk line, rule) on rough surfaces is very difficult.

task: Use instrumentation to pilot aircraft when visibility is poor.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one IFR flight segment under instrument meteorological conditions (IMC); median en-route/approach leg ~90 minutes for a typical commercial or general aviation flight.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate aircraft cockpit controls in real flight. No current general-purpose robot has the certified avionics interface, seat-harness integration, rudder-pedal reach, or real-time instrument-scan loop needed. Autopilot/AI copilot systems are separate, task-specific tools.

task: Prepare all material surfaces to be welded, ensuring that there is no loose or thick scale, slag, rust, moisture, grease, or other foreign matter.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing surfaces of a single weld joint. A competent welder spends ~10–20 min grinding, wire-brushing, degreasing, and visually inspecting both sides of a typical joint.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying contaminants via vision, selecting and using multiple tools (grinder, wire brush, solvent rag), applying appropriate force on varied surfaces, and judging cleanliness quality — beyond current humanoid generalization and fine tool-use capability.

task: Smooth soldered joints and rough spots, using hand files and emery paper, and polish smoothed areas with polishing wheels or buffing wire.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing one soldered joint. A skilled worker spends ~5–10 min filing, sanding with emery paper, then polishing to acceptable quality.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous force-modulated tool use (files, emery paper, buffing wheels), precise tactile feedback to avoid over-removing material, and complex multi-step finishing. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tactile sensing.

task: Check grooves, angles, or gap allowances, using micrometers, calipers, and precision measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single feature (groove, angle, or gap) on one part; a competent machinist or QC inspector takes ~5–10 min including setup, measurement, and recording.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bi-manual dexterity to seat and read analog/digital calipers or micrometers on small features; fine-finger force control and sub-millimeter positioning exceed current humanoid capability. Perception of measurement readings adds further uncertainty.

task: Rescue and evacuate injured persons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating, stabilizing, and evacuating one injured person to a safe zone. Includes assessment, movement, and handoff — typical for emergency responders.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating unpredictable, hazardous terrain, assessing injury severity, safely handling a limp/uncooperative human body, and making real-time decisions. Current humanoids lack the strength, situational judgment, and robustness for unstructured emergency environments.

task: Operate metal shaping, straightening, and bending machines, such as brakes and shears.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one metal workpiece: measuring, positioning, operating the brake/shear, and verifying the result. Typical cycle is 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate industrial press brakes or shears: foot pedals, specialized controls, precise material feeding, and force-feedback interpretation are far beyond current general-purpose robot capability. No commercial deployment exists.

task: Use fire suppression methods in industrial emergencies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = responding to and suppressing a single industrial fire emergency, from alarm to extinguishment. A trained firefighter/worker typically takes 10–30 min depending on fire class and scale.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating hoses, extinguishers, or suppression systems in chaotic, high-heat, smoke-filled, unstructured environments; real-time hazard judgment; specialized tool use; and life-safety decisions far beyond current robot generalization and robustness.

task: Maneuver trucks into loading or unloading positions, following signals from loading crew and checking that vehicle and loading equipment are properly positioned.
human_time_rationale: One instance = backing/positioning a truck into a single dock or loading zone. Skilled drivers typically complete this in 5–15 minutes depending on lot layout and congestion.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate full-size trucks in real traffic/yard environments. This requires vehicle operation, dynamic spatial judgment, and crew signal interpretation — all beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Construct preliminary models of wax, metal, clay, or plaster, and form sample castings in molds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing one preliminary model and forming one sample casting. A skilled craftsperson typically spends 60–120 min shaping, molding, and casting a single piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly skilled fine dexterity to sculpt, shape, and pour soft/fluid materials (wax, clay, plaster) with artistic precision. Current robots lack the tactile feedback, force control, and generalization needed for freeform artistic fabrication.

task: Fabricate parts and test aids as required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating one part or test aid (e.g., a bracket, jig, or fixture). A competent machinist/technician typically spends 60–120 min per piece including setup, cutting, and finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating machine tools (lathes, mills, drills), interpreting blueprints, selecting materials/tooling, and making adaptive quality judgments. Humanoids cannot operate machine shop equipment autonomously; far beyond current dexterity and tool-use capabilities.

task: Soften metal to be used in designs by heating it with a gas torch and shape it, using hammers and dies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = heating and shaping a single metal workpiece. A competent metalsmith typically spends ~20–40 min per piece across heating cycles and hammering.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torch control, heat-sensing judgment, forceful yet accurate hammer strikes with dies, and adaptive shaping feedback. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and thermal perception capabilities.

task: Locate and mark dimensions and reference lines on defective or replacement parts, using templates, scribes, compasses, and steel rules.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking up a single part. A skilled metalworker/machinist typically spends 10–20 min locating, scribing, and marking dimensions on one part using hand tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity with scribes/compasses, precise force control, reading templates, and accurate spatial referencing on irregular surfaces. Current humanoids lack the sub-mm precision and tool-use finesse needed for reliable scribing work.

task: Mount, secure, and align finished lenses in frames or optical assemblies, using precision hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting and aligning a single lens into one frame/optical assembly. A skilled optician or optical technician typically takes 10–20 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter precision alignment, fine dexterous tool-use, and tactile feedback to avoid scratching or cracking delicate lenses. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation, force control, and optical verification needed for acceptable quality.

task: Remove old roofing materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stripping old roofing materials from a typical single-story residential roof (~1,500 sq ft). A crew of 2–3 takes ~4 hours; median solo competent worker estimate ~4 hrs.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating steep, sloped, unstable roof surfaces outdoors; wielding pry bars/shovels forcefully; managing debris safely. Current humanoids lack outdoor roof locomotion stability, tool leverage, and safety tolerance for this hazardous environment.

task: Remove pieces of stone from larger masses, using jackhammers, wedges, and other tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one piece of stone from a larger mass. A skilled quarry worker spends ~60 min per extraction using jackhammers, wedges, and hand tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy vibrating tools (jackhammer) with sustained force, wedge placement requiring geological judgment, rough outdoor terrain, and high-impact physical labor. Current humanoids lack the force output, tool-handling robustness, and domain perception needed.

task: Conduct routine and special inspections as required by regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one inspection visit. A competent inspector typically spends 45–90 minutes checking equipment, documenting findings, and verifying regulatory compliance.
robot_success_rationale: Inspections require regulatory knowledge, contextual judgment, reading labels/gauges, interpreting anomalies, and producing compliant documentation. Robots lack domain expertise, legal reasoning, and reliable fine-detail perception across unstructured environments.

task: Drive trucks with capacities greater than 13 tons, including tractor-trailer combinations, to transport and deliver products, livestock, or other materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one delivery trip. Median long-haul/regional trucking trip including loading, driving, and delivery is ~3–4 hours; 240 min is a reasonable central estimate.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot physically operate a heavy tractor-trailer in live traffic. No current general-purpose robot can sit in a cab, work foot pedals, steer, and navigate public roads with loaded cargo. L4 AV trucks exist but are narrow, route-specific systems, not general-purpose humanoids.

task: Attach sheets of metal to roof boards or building frameworks when installing metal roofs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching one metal sheet/panel to roof boards or framework. A skilled roofer takes ~30–60 min per panel accounting for alignment, fastening, and safety on a roof.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working at height on a sloped, unstable surface with large, heavy metal panels. Current humanoids lack the stability, reach, load-bearing capacity, and tool-use (screw guns at awkward angles) needed for rooftop panel installation.

task: Cut slabs of stone into sheets that will be used for floors or counters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting one stone slab into sheets. A skilled operator sets up, cuts, and clears a slab using a bridge saw or CNC stone cutter, typically 30–60 minutes per slab.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy industrial bridge saws or CNC stone cutters, precise measurement, material handling of large heavy slabs, and coolant management. Humanoid robots cannot operate such specialized heavy machinery safely or autonomously.

task: Install casings to prevent cave-ins.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing casing segments into a single borehole or excavation section. A crew typically takes 60–120 minutes per section, depending on depth and soil conditions.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy drilling/casing equipment, managing large steel segments under high load in unstable terrain, and making judgment calls about soil conditions. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in dexterity, force, and tool operation.

task: Inventory and inspect goods to be moved to determine quantities and conditions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inventorying and inspecting a typical load of goods (e.g., a moving truck's worth). A competent worker counts, examines, and documents condition in ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires visual perception, object recognition, counting, damage assessment across highly varied items, and documentation — all in unstructured environments. Current robots lack reliable generalized object ID, condition grading, and record-keeping integration at acceptable quality.

task: Fly with other pilots or pilot-license applicants to evaluate their proficiency.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one evaluation flight (checkride or proficiency check). Typical flight evaluation lasts 60–120 minutes in the air, plus brief pre/post briefing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires piloting or co-piloting an aircraft, making real-time aeronautical judgments, and evaluating human performance — far beyond any current humanoid robot's locomotion, perception, or control authority in a cockpit.

task: Clean, strip, prime, and sand structural surfaces and materials to prepare them for bonding.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single structural surface/panel (e.g., a beam face or joint area). A competent worker cleans, strips, primes, and sands one surface in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step tool switching (scraper, sander, primer brush/roller), force-controlled surface work, chemical handling, and quality judgment on varied structural geometries. Far beyond reliable autonomous robot capability in 2026.

task: Secure cargo for transport, using ropes, blocks, chain, binders, or covers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = securing a typical load on one truck/flatbed. A competent worker spends ~15–25 min routing chains/straps, tensioning binders, placing blocks, and inspecting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires routing ropes/chains around irregular cargo, tensioning binders with high force, judging load stability, and working in unstructured outdoor/dock environments. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and generalization.

task: Check vehicles to ensure that mechanical, safety, and emergency equipment is in good working order.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full pre-trip inspection of one vehicle, covering lights, brakes, fluid levels, tires, mirrors, safety/emergency gear. Competent driver/mechanic takes ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating around/under a vehicle, reading gauges, manipulating caps and latches, testing brake pressure, and interpreting diverse vehicle layouts. Current humanoids lack the reliable dexterity, under-vehicle mobility, and multi-sensor integration needed for full autonomous inspection across varied vehicle types.

task: Trim and shape replacement body sections to specified sizes and fits and secure sections in place, using adhesives, hand tools, and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming, shaping, and securing one replacement body section (e.g., auto body panel). A skilled technician typically spends 60–120 minutes per panel including fitting and adhesive/fastener work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise multi-step dexterity: measuring, cutting/grinding curved panels, test-fitting to tight tolerances, applying adhesives, and using varied hand/power tools in sequence. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capability for unstructured body shop work.

task: Clear drains and downspouts and clean gutters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = gutters/downspouts/drains on a typical single-family home. Includes ladder setup, scooping debris, flushing, and cleanup; ~45–75 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing or safely positioning on a ladder at height, scooping wet debris from narrow channels, and flushing downspouts—well beyond current humanoid mobility, ladder-climbing, and dexterous outdoor manipulation capabilities.

task: Set up and operate short-wave radio or field telephone equipment to transmit and receive blast information.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up radio/field phone and completing one blast communication exchange; a trained operator takes ~10–20 min including setup, tuning, transmission, and confirmation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine manipulation of specialized radio controls, tuning knobs, and handsets; voice communication; situational judgment in hazardous blast zones; and reliable outdoor/rough-terrain mobility. Current humanoids lack the specialized tool-use, communication, and safety protocols needed.

task: Fabricate defective sections or parts, using metal fabricating machines, saws, brakes, shears, and grinders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating one defective section/part. A competent metalworker spends ~30–60 min per part, including setup, machine operation, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating multiple specialized heavy machines (saws, brakes, shears, grinders), precise force control, and skilled sequencing across tools. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, strength, and tool-operation capability for multi-machine metal fabrication.

task: Perform housekeeping and cleaning duties related to children's care.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full housekeeping/cleaning session in a children's care area (e.g., daycare room or nursery), including tidying toys, wiping surfaces, sanitizing, and mopping. ~45 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a cluttered, dynamic children's environment; handling diverse toy/item shapes; sanitizing safely around kids; context-aware judgment (e.g., hazardous vs. non-hazardous items). Far beyond current robot generalization and safety capabilities.

task: Start engines, operate controls, and pilot airplanes to transport passengers, mail, or freight, adhering to flight plans, regulations, and procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one flight leg. Median U.S. domestic flight is ~2.5–3 hrs including pre-flight checks, taxi, cruise, and shutdown procedures.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate aircraft cockpit controls safely in live conditions. Autopilot/UAV systems are separate; a general-purpose humanoid lacks the certified integration, fine control authority, and real-time aeronautical decision-making required.

task: Unload materials and tools from work trucks, and unroll roofing as directed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unloading one truck's worth of materials/tools and unrolling roofing for a typical job setup; a crew member takes ~20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/uneven terrain navigation, climbing in/out of trucks, handling heavy/bulky roofing rolls, and precise directed unrolling. Current humanoids lack the strength, dexterity, and situational generalization for this unstructured outdoor task.

task: Lay out designs on metal stock, and cut along markings to fabricate pieces used to cast metal molds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out a design on metal stock and cutting one mold piece. A skilled metalworker typically spends ~30–60 min marking, measuring, and cutting a single piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise scribing/marking on metal, reading technical drawings, and operating cutting tools (angle grinder, bandsaw, shear) with fine positional accuracy. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and tool-use generalization for acceptable quality output.

task: Assemble and install electrical, plumbing, mechanical, hydraulic, and structural components and accessories, using hand or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and installing one set of mixed-system components (e.g., a subsystem on a machine or vehicle). A competent technician typically takes 60–120 min per major subsystem installation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires cross-domain dexterity (wiring, plumbing, torquing fasteners, hydraulic fittings), tool-switching, force control, and spatial reasoning in unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack the reliability, tool generalization, and fine manipulation for multi-system installs.

task: Cut specified lengths of primacord and attach primers to cord ends.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting a specified length of primacord and attaching one primer to each end. A skilled blaster/explosives tech takes ~5–10 minutes including measuring, cutting, and crimping/attaching primers.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement and cutting of flexible cord, then fine dexterous crimping of detonator primers onto cord ends — extremely delicate, safety-critical work. Current robots lack the fine manipulation, force sensitivity, and certified safety protocols required.

task: Cover roofs with layers of roofing felt or asphalt strips before installing tile, slate, or composition materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = underlayment installation on a typical single-story residential roof section (~1,500–2,000 sq ft). A competent roofer takes ~2 hours with a partner.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working on steep, sloped, unstable outdoor surfaces at height with large flexible materials (felt rolls). Current humanoids lack the mobility, balance, and coordinated bimanual dexterity for safe outdoor rooftop work. Near-zero feasibility.

task: Rotate molds to distribute alloys and to prevent formation of air pockets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rotating a single mold through its required cycle. A competent foundry worker positions, rotates, and monitors one mold in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force manipulation of heavy, hot molds with precise rotational control and real-time feedback. Current humanoids lack the payload capacity, heat tolerance, and task-specific perception needed for reliable autonomous operation.

task: Perform emergency roadside repairs, such as changing tires or installing light bulbs, tire chains, or spark plugs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one roadside repair job (e.g., tire change). A competent human takes ~15–20 min for a tire swap, slightly more for chains/spark plugs; ~25 min median across repair types.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating vehicle-specific jacks, loosening lug nuts with high torque, working under a vehicle on uneven/outdoor terrain, handling chains and spark plugs with fine dexterity — well beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities outdoors.

task: Instruct and assist children in the development of health and personal habits, such as eating, resting, and toilet behavior.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single instructional/guidance session with a child on one habit (e.g., handwashing, mealtime, toilet routine). A competent caregiver spends ~15–25 minutes per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic social-emotional rapport, adaptive communication, and behavioral reading of children — far beyond current robot capabilities. Robots lack nuanced child psychology, verbal persuasion, and safe unsupervised caregiving judgment.

task: Drive trucks to transport explosives and blasting equipment to blasting sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one round trip to a blasting site. Typical drive plus loading/unloading at origin and destination averages ~60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot drive trucks on public or off-road work sites. No current general-purpose robot can operate a truck's controls in traffic/rough terrain. Hazmat regulations add further barriers. Near-zero success.

task: Inspect, weigh, and measure mounted or unmounted lenses after completion to verify alignment and conformance to specifications, using precision instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one lens/assembly. Skilled optical technician uses lensometer, micrometer, and precision balance; typically 5–15 min per unit including documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter dexterous handling of delicate optics, precise instrument manipulation (lensometers, micrometers), and nuanced result interpretation. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tactile sensitivity, and optical metrology tool-use needed.

task: Anneal precious metal objects such as coffeepots, tea sets, and trays in gas ovens for prescribed times to soften metal for reworking.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading one batch of objects into a gas oven, running the prescribed anneal cycle, and unloading. Includes setup, monitoring, and safe removal; typically 15–25 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling large, irregular, high-value objects (coffeepots, trays), operating oven controls precisely, and judging heat state. Dexterity for awkward shapes, safe thermal handling, and oven interaction remain major hurdles for current humanoids.

task: Grade stones based on their color, perfection, and quality of cut.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grading one stone. A trained gemologist inspects color, clarity, and cut quality under magnification, taking ~5 minutes per stone.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level optical inspection with spectroscopic/microscopic tools, nuanced color discrimination, and fine dexterity to handle tiny stones. Current humanoid robots lack the specialized sensors, sub-millimeter dexterity, and grading expertise needed.

task: Make repairs, such as enlarging or reducing ring sizes, soldering pieces of jewelry together, and replacing broken clasps and mountings.
human_time_rationale: One repair instance (e.g., resizing a ring, soldering a joint, or replacing a clasp) takes a skilled jeweler ~20–45 min depending on complexity; 30 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Jewelry repair demands sub-millimeter dexterity, precise torch/solder control, and tactile sensitivity far beyond current humanoid robots. Tiny, delicate, high-value parts and heat management make autonomous success essentially impossible in 2026.

task: Ride on moving cars by holding onto grab irons and standing on ladder steps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = riding a moving railcar from one point to the next (e.g., coupling/switching move). Typical yard switching move lasts ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires gripping grab irons and balancing on ladder steps on a moving, vibrating vehicle outdoors. Current humanoids lack the grip strength, dynamic balance on moving external surfaces, and safety tolerance for this high-risk task.

task: Apply and release hand brakes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying then releasing a hand brake on a vehicle or rail car. A competent worker completes this in about 1 minute including approach and confirmation.
robot_success_rationale: Hand brakes vary widely (levers, wheels, ratchets) on vehicles/rail cars, often requiring high torque, precise grip, and awkward postures. Humanoids lack the specialized tool-use and force control for reliable autonomous operation in unstructured environments.

task: Set and bolt crown blocks to posts at tops of derricks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting and bolting one crown block atop a derrick post. Involves rigging, lifting, aligning, and torquing bolts at significant height; a skilled crew takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing or working at extreme heights on a derrick structure, heavy-load rigging, precise alignment, and high-torque bolting in unstructured outdoor environments — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities in mobility, strength, and safety.

task: Couple or uncouple trailers by changing trailer jack positions, connecting or disconnecting air or electrical lines, or manipulating fifth-wheel locks.
human_time_rationale: One coupling/uncoupling instance for a truck-trailer pair; experienced driver typically takes 10–20 minutes including all steps (jacks, airlines, electrical, fifth wheel).
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating trailer jacks, connecting air/electrical lines with precise alignment, and manipulating fifth-wheel locks under a heavy vehicle. High-force, unstructured connectors, tight clearances, and outdoor/lot terrain make this nearly impossible for current humanoids.

task: Rout out locations where parts are to be joined to items, using routing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = routing a single join location on one workpiece. A skilled operator sets up the router, positions the part, and completes the cut in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool-use (routing machine operation), part fixturing, and path accuracy. Current humanoids lack fine tool-control, safe high-speed cutter handling, and reliable workpiece positioning for acceptable quality.

task: Follow appropriate safety procedures for transporting dangerous goods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and transporting a single dangerous goods shipment (checking labels, securing load, verifying documentation, completing transit). Typical end-to-end compliance process for a competent handler.
robot_success_rationale: Requires vehicle operation on public roads, hazmat regulatory knowledge, multi-step documentation, and judgment under legal liability. Humanoids cannot drive; autonomous hazmat compliance is far beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Install and align repaired or replacement parts for subsequent riveting or welding, using clamps and wrenches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing and aligning one set of parts for a single weld/rivet joint. A skilled worker spends ~20–30 min fitting, clamping, and aligning parts to spec.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force tool use (wrenches, clamps), precise 3D alignment to tight tolerances, and adaptive force sensing on irregular/repaired parts. Current humanoids lack the strength, dexterity, and feedback loops for reliable unsupervised performance.

task: Crank trailer landing gear up or down to safely secure vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cranking landing gear fully up or down on one trailer. A competent driver typically takes 2–4 minutes to crank both legs to full extension or retraction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating a trailer-mounted crank handle outdoors, applying sustained high-torque rotational force, shifting gear ratios, and working in unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack the torque output, tool-finding generalization, and outdoor terrain stability needed reliably.

task: Position and secure workpieces, using hoists, cranes, wire, and banding machines or hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning and securing a single workpiece. A competent worker typically spends ~10–20 min rigging, lifting, aligning, and securing one piece using available equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating hoists, cranes, banding machines — heavy industrial equipment a humanoid cannot control. Rigging, load assessment, and precise securing under variable conditions far exceed current robot generalization and tool-use limits.

task: Remove, inspect, repair, and install in-flight refueling stores and external fuel tanks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full remove/inspect/repair/reinstall cycle on a single external fuel tank or refueling store. Skilled avionics/fuels technician typically takes 2–4 hours total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized aerospace ground equipment, high-torque fastener removal, precise aeronautical inspection, plumbing/electrical reconnection, and safety-critical sign-off — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities. No deployment precedent.

task: Repair products by dismantling, straightening, reshaping, and reassembling parts, using cutting torches, straightening presses, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single product (e.g., a metal frame/panel). Dismantling, reshaping, and reassembling a typical item takes a skilled technician ~1–2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized heavy tools (cutting torches, straightening presses), high-force dexterous manipulation, and adaptive judgment on material deformation — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Remove debris from loaded trailers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing debris from one loaded trailer. A worker with a broom/blower spends ~15–25 min navigating around cargo and sweeping out loose material.
robot_success_rationale: Confined, cluttered trailer interior with uneven footing, varied debris types, and cargo obstacles challenge locomotion and manipulation. Poor generalization in unstructured semi-dark spaces; no reliable tool-use (broom/blower) demonstrated at scale.

task: Remove or cut out defective parts or drill holes to gain access to internal defects or damage, using drills and punches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing/cutting one defective part or drilling access holes on a single workpiece. A skilled technician typically takes 15–25 minutes depending on material and defect depth.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool selection, high-force drilling/punching, damage localization via inspection, and adaptive positioning. Current humanoids lack the force control, tool-change dexterity, and defect-diagnosis perception needed for autonomous success.

task: Clean equipment and work areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single equipment station/work area. A competent worker wipes down surfaces, cleans tools/equipment, and tidies the space in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can wipe flat surfaces and sweep, but cleaning equipment requires identifying soiled areas, maneuvering around varied obstacles, applying appropriate pressure/tools, and handling irregular equipment shapes — challenging for current systems.

task: Adjust, repair, or replace malfunctioning components or assemblies, using hand tools or soldering irons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and fixing a single malfunctioning component/assembly. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min identifying, adjusting, or soldering/replacing one faulty part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fault diagnosis, fine dexterous tool use (hand tools, soldering iron), and adaptive decision-making on varied components. Current humanoids lack precision soldering ability, robust fault inference, and reliable fine-manipulation at this complexity level.

task: Adjust repaired or replaced units as needed to ensure proper operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single repaired/replaced unit (e.g., a mechanical or electrical component) until it operates within spec; typically 10–30 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing operational correctness, making precise iterative adjustments, and using tools on varied equipment. Current robots lack the adaptive perception, fine dexterity, and domain-specific judgment needed for autonomous calibration and verification.

task: Examine and inspect aircraft components, including landing gear, hydraulic systems, and deicers to locate cracks, breaks, leaks, or other problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full inspection of landing gear, hydraulics, and deicers on one aircraft; a certified A&P mechanic typically spends 60–120 minutes on these systems combined.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex, confined aircraft structures; detecting subtle cracks/leaks via specialized NDT tools; interpreting ambiguous findings under strict aviation safety standards. Current robots lack the dexterity, certified sensor integration, and domain-specific judgment needed.

task: Provide assistance in aligning drawbars, using available equipment to lift, pull, or push on the drawbars.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning one drawbar coupling. A competent worker uses jacks, chains, or forklifts to reposition and align the drawbar, typically taking 15–35 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy lifting/pulling equipment (jacks, forklifts, come-alongs), applying high forces with precision in unstructured outdoor/industrial settings, and judging alignment — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Measure parts for wear, using precision instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single part with a precision instrument (e.g., micrometer, caliper). A competent technician selects the tool, takes readings, and records results in ~5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of precision instruments (micrometers, calipers), exact placement under load, and reading analog/digital gauges. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter fingertip control and stable grip needed for reliable, calibrated measurement.

task: Prepare and paint aircraft surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and painting one aircraft surface section (e.g., a fuselage panel or wing). Includes sanding, masking, priming, and applying paint coats; a full aircraft takes days, but a single surface section averages ~8 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise surface prep (sanding, masking), spray equipment operation across large curved aerospace surfaces, strict quality standards, and working at height/in confined areas. Far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity, tool-use, and quality-control capabilities.

task: Perform routine repair and maintenance duties.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single routine maintenance/repair job (e.g., replacing a part, lubricating equipment, tightening fixtures). A competent worker typically completes such a task in ~60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Routine repairs require diverse tool use (wrenches, screwdrivers, testers), fault diagnosis, and adapting to varied unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous tool manipulation, fault reasoning, and generalization across repair contexts.

task: Disassemble, clean, and reassemble sprayers or power equipment, using solvents, wire brushes, and cloths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fully disassemble, clean, and reassemble one sprayer or power equipment unit. A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool-use (wire brushes, cloths, solvents), precise disassembly/reassembly of varied fasteners and small components, and tactile judgment for cleanliness—well beyond current humanoid dexterity and generalization limits.

task: Follow special cargo-related procedures, such as checking refrigeration systems for frozen foods or providing food or water for livestock.
human_time_rationale: One instance = special cargo check per shipment: reading refrigeration gauges, logging temps, or feeding/watering livestock. Typically 15–25 min per load for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied cargo environments, reading analog/digital gauges, operating reefer controls, and safely interacting with live animals — combining unstructured locomotion, fine tool-use, and animal-handling that current humanoids cannot reliably do autonomously.

task: Design and fabricate molds, models, and machine accessories, and modify hand tools used to cast metal and jewelry pieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and fabricating one mold, model, or accessory. A skilled moldmaker/jeweler typically spends 1–3 hours per piece depending on complexity; 2 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level fine dexterity (carving, filing, modifying hand tools), creative design judgment, material intuition, and precision sub-millimeter work. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured, craft-level settings.

task: Couple and uncouple air hoses and electrical connections between cars.
human_time_rationale: One instance = coupling/uncoupling air hoses and electrical connectors between one pair of rail cars. A competent worker completes this in roughly 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires strong grip, precise alignment of gladhand air couplers and multi-pin electrical connectors in tight, dirty, outdoor spaces at low heights. High force, awkward posture, connector variability, and outdoor terrain make this very challenging for 2026 humanoids.

task: Control equipment that coats lenses to alter their reflective qualities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one coating cycle (load lenses, set parameters, run coating machine, verify output). A competent optical technician typically takes ~15–25 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized vacuum/coating machinery with precise parameter programming, fine lens handling, and quality verification. Current humanoids lack the tool-use interface familiarity, fine dexterity for delicate optics, and domain-specific machine control needed.

task: Operate track switches, derails, automatic switches, and retarders to change routing of train or cars.
human_time_rationale: One instance = routing a single train or cut of cars through a switch/retarder sequence. A skilled switchman typically completes one routing operation in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized rail infrastructure controls (levers, panels, retarder consoles), navigating live rail yards, interpreting train orders, and coordinating with moving equipment. Current humanoids lack rail-yard tool familiarity, safety-critical judgment, and yard mobility needed for autonomous operation.

task: Pull knuckles to open them for coupling.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pulling open one knuckle coupler on a rail car or trailer. A trained worker lifts/pulls the knuckle pin and opens the knuckle in under a minute.
robot_success_rationale: Knuckle couplers are heavy, oily, and require precise grip with significant force in a constrained outdoor/trackside environment. Perception of the knuckle pin and forceful pulling exceed current humanoid dexterity/strength limits reliably.

task: Observe and listen to equipment operation to detect binding or stoppage of tools or other equipment malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single inspection pass of equipment in operation; a competent worker listens/observes for anomalies over a typical monitoring cycle of ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can use microphones and cameras for anomaly detection, but interpreting subtle auditory/vibrational cues (binding, irregular rhythms) across diverse equipment types in unstructured environments remains unreliable. Generalization is a major bottleneck.

task: Fill small dents or scratches with body fillers and smooth surfaces to prepare for painting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling and sanding one dented/scratched panel area. A skilled auto body technician takes ~30–60 min including mixing filler, application, curing, and sanding smooth.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced tactile feedback to blend filler seamlessly, precise force-controlled sanding over complex curved surfaces, visual/tactile surface quality inspection, and multi-step chemical handling — far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Clean parking areas, offices, restrooms, or equipment, and remove trash.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single zone (e.g., one restroom, one office, or one parking area segment) including trash removal; ~45 min is a reasonable median per zone.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep/mop flat floors and pick up trash in structured settings, but multi-environment scope (parking lots, restrooms, offices, equipment) requires locomotion over varied terrain, fine manipulation (toilet cleaning, emptying bins), and generalization across unstructured clutter — well beyond current reliable capability.

task: Recognize, set up, and operate hand and power tools common to the welding trade, such as shielded metal arc and gas metal arc welding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = recognizing, setting up, and performing a weld with SMAW or GMAW equipment on a single workpiece. A competent welder takes ~15–25 min including equipment check and setup.
robot_success_rationale: Welding requires precise torch manipulation, arc gap control, real-time visual/tactile feedback, and multi-step equipment setup with varied tooling. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force control, and process sensing needed for acceptable weld quality.

task: Adjust controls on infrared ovens, heat lamps, portable ventilators, or exhaust units to speed the drying of surfaces between coats.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting controls on one drying unit between coats. Turning dials/switches and repositioning equipment takes ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to equipment and grasp knobs/switches, but reading analog dials, interpreting context (which coat, drying stage), and precisely adjusting varied control types on industrial dryers remains unreliable for current humanoids.

task: Replace defective wiring and insulation, and tighten electrical connections, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing wiring/insulation and tightening connections in a single electrical panel or device. A competent electrician typically takes 30–60 min depending on scope.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity (stripping wire, routing insulation, torquing terminals), fault identification, and tool manipulation in confined spaces. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation, electrical diagnostics, and safety compliance for live or near-live wiring work.

task: Drill holes along outlines, using jackhammers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling a single outlined pattern along a marked cut line. A competent jackhammer operator takes ~30–60 min including repositioning and setup.
robot_success_rationale: Jackhammer operation requires enormous reactive forces, vibration tolerance, precise positioning on hard surfaces, and heavy tool handling. No current humanoid robot has the structural strength, force control, or tool-use capability for this task.

task: Remove locomotives, car mechanical units, or other components, using pneumatic hoists and jacks, pinch bars, hand tools, and cutting torches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a major locomotive/railcar component (e.g., truck assembly or engine block). Skilled mechanics typically take 1–3 hours depending on component complexity and access.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating pneumatic hoists, jacks, pinch bars, and cutting torches on heavy rail equipment in variable industrial environments. Current humanoids lack the force output, tool specialization, safety-critical judgment, and coordinated multi-tool dexterity needed for this task.

task: Maintain, repair, and rebuild aircraft structures, functional components, and parts, such as wings and fuselage, rigging, hydraulic units, oxygen systems, fuel systems, electrical systems, gaskets, or seals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance/repair/rebuild task on one aircraft system or component (e.g., repairing a hydraulic unit). Skilled A&P mechanics typically spend 2–4 hours per discrete repair job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme dexterity for fine fasteners, seals, and wiring; specialized tool use; safety-critical judgment; navigation around complex aircraft geometry; regulatory compliance documentation. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Service or repair machinery used in the fabrication of appliances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and repairing a single machine fault. A competent technician typically spends 60–120 min on diagnosis, part replacement, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert diagnosis, use of specialized tools, fine dexterous manipulation of machinery internals, interpreting error states, and judgment-based decision-making — far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Inspect the interior and exterior of rail cars coming into rail yards to identify defects and to determine the extent of wear and damage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full interior + exterior inspection of one rail car. Experienced inspector walks around and through the car checking all surfaces, undercarriage, couplings, and wear points; ~15–25 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Rail yard terrain is uneven, cluttered, and hazardous. Climbing into varied car interiors, navigating tight undercarriages, and reliably identifying subtle defects/wear across diverse car types far exceed current humanoid robot locomotion, perception, and generalization capabilities.

task: Immerse eyeglass frames in solutions to harden, soften, or dye frames.
human_time_rationale: One instance = immersing one frame in solution for a full treatment cycle (dip, soak, remove). Typically 5–10 minutes including prep and timing.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can grasp and dip objects, but handling delicate eyeglass frames without scratching, managing chemical solutions safely, timing soaks precisely, and working in an unstructured lab environment challenge current dexterity and generalization.

task: Perform minor repairs, such as adjusting brakes, replacing spark plugs, or changing engine oil or filters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one minor repair job (e.g., oil change ~30–45 min, brake adjustment ~20–40 min, spark plug replacement ~30–60 min). Median across these common tasks ≈ 45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating under/around a vehicle, using specialized tools (torque wrenches, sockets), high-force fastener removal, fluid handling, and tight-space dexterity. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capability in unstructured garage environments.

task: Plate articles such as jewelry pieces and watch dials, using silver, gold, nickel, or other metals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = plating a single batch of small articles (e.g., a tray of jewelry pieces). Includes setup, masking, immersion, rinsing, and inspection — ~20–30 min for a skilled plater.
robot_success_rationale: Electroplating requires fine dexterous handling of tiny delicate articles, precise chemical bath management, masking, and quality inspection. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation, chemical process control, and specialized tooling needed for acceptable results.

task: Operate flatcars equipped with derricks or railcars to transport personnel or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one transport trip moving personnel or equipment via flatcar/railcar with derrick, including setup and unloading; ~45 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized rail vehicles and derrick controls in dynamic, safety-critical environments. Humanoid robots cannot drive/operate railcars or crane derricks autonomously; no current general-purpose robot has these capabilities.

task: Create jewelry from materials such as gold, silver, platinum, and precious or semiprecious stones.
human_time_rationale: One instance = crafting a single piece of jewelry (e.g., a ring or pendant). Skilled jewelers typically spend 2–4 hours per piece including shaping, soldering, and stone setting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for stone setting, precise soldering, filing, and polishing with sub-millimeter accuracy. Current humanoid robots lack the tactile sensitivity, tool control, and precision needed for jewelry-grade craftsmanship.

task: Replace or repair worn, defective, or damaged components, using hand tools, gauges, and testing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and replacing/repairing a single worn or defective component (e.g., a belt, valve, or fitting). A competent mechanic/technician averages ~45 min including diagnosis, disassembly, repair/swap, and testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step diagnosis with gauges/testing equipment, fine dexterous tool-use, context-dependent decision-making, and adapting to varied worn/damaged components — well beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Repair, fabricate, and install steel or wood fittings, using blueprints, shop sketches, and instruction manuals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single fitting job (repair, fab, or install). A competent tradesperson typically takes 60–120 min per fitting task reading specs, fabricating/modifying, and installing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading and interpreting blueprints/sketches, fine metalworking or carpentry (cutting, welding, drilling, fastening), precise spatial installation, and adaptive problem-solving. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and dexterous tool-use capabilities.

task: Connect components to assemblies such as radio systems, instruments, magnetos, inverters, and in-flight refueling systems, using hand tools and soldering irons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting components to one avionics assembly (e.g., a radio system or magneto). A skilled avionics technician typically takes 1–3 hours per assembly, depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for soldering, routing wires in tight aircraft spaces, reading schematics, and using specialized hand tools. Current humanoids lack the precision, contextual judgment, and tool-use capability for aerospace-grade avionics work.

task: Inspect engines before and after use to ensure proper operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single pre- or post-use engine inspection covering fluid levels, belts, leaks, and visual checks; ~10–20 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating around an engine bay, visually identifying anomalies (leaks, wear, fluid levels via dipstick), interpreting gauges, and tactile checks. Current robots lack reliable multi-modal inspection generalization in cluttered, oily environments.

task: Transport children to schools, social outings, and medical appointments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one trip (pickup to drop-off). Typical school/appointment run is ~20–50 min including loading/unloading children.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a vehicle in traffic — humanoid robots cannot operate passenger vehicles. L4 AVs exist but are narrow/mapped-route systems, not general-purpose humanoids. Child safety and unstructured caregiving add further barriers.

task: Accompany aircraft on flights to make in-flight adjustments and corrections.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one flight, typically 2–4 hours for a domestic flight engineer/technician accompanying an aircraft to monitor and adjust systems in-flight.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating aircraft cockpit controls, reading complex avionics, and making real-time adjustments mid-flight. Humanoid robots cannot safely operate in aircraft cockpits autonomously; extreme safety requirements, dynamic environment, and specialized tool use make this nearly impossible for current robots.

task: Clean and polish metal items and jewelry pieces, using jewelers' tools, polishing wheels, and chemical baths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and polishing a single jewelry piece or small metal item. Competent jeweler spends ~15–25 min across inspection, chemical bath, polishing wheel, and finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to handle tiny, delicate pieces with jewelers' tools and polishing wheels. Precise force control, small-object manipulation, and chemical bath handling far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities. High damage risk.

task: Polish artificial limbs, braces, or supports, using grinding and buffing wheels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = polishing one prosthetic limb or orthotic brace/support. A skilled orthotist/prosthetist technician typically spends ~15–25 minutes grinding and buffing a single device to acceptable finish.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled contact with spinning grinding/buffing wheels on complex, irregular surfaces. High risk of over-grinding, poor perception of finish quality, inability to safely handle high-speed bench tools, and extreme fine dexterity demands exceed current robot capabilities.

task: Teach and perform age-appropriate activities, such as lap play, reading, and arts and crafts, to encourage intellectual development of children.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one structured activity session with a child (e.g., a reading or arts-and-crafts session). A typical session runs ~20–40 minutes, median ~30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires social-emotional attunement, adaptive child interaction, creative engagement, and safe physical participation with children. Current robots lack child-safe unstructured caregiving, nuanced communication, and pedagogical responsiveness needed for acceptable quality.

task: Use brush to hand-paint areas in need of retouching or unreachable with a spray gun.
human_time_rationale: One instance = touching up one surface/panel. A skilled painter inspects, selects brush, and carefully applies paint to tight or missed spots, typically 15–25 minutes per panel.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual inspection to identify defects, precise dexterous brush control for small/irregular areas, adaptive force, and navigation in constrained spaces. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control and paint-quality perception needed.

task: Examine assembled or finished products to ensure conformance to specifications, using magnifying glasses or precision measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single assembled/finished product unit. A competent QC inspector typically spends 5–15 minutes per unit using calipers, gauges, or magnifying tools.
robot_success_rationale: Robots lack reliable fine dexterous tool-use (calipers, micrometers) and the perceptual judgment to interpret magnified defects. Vision-based inspection exists but operating precision instruments autonomously in varied contexts remains very limited.

task: Assemble, insert, and adjust wires, tubes, cores, fittings, rods, or patterns into molds, using hand tools and depth gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling/inserting/adjusting all required components into a single mold setup. A skilled worker typically takes 10–25 min per mold depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity with multiple small, varied components (wires, tubes, rods) into tight mold geometries, use of depth gauges for verification, and adaptive adjustment — all well beyond current humanoid manipulation capability.

task: Cure bonded structures, using portable or stationary curing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running a curing cycle for one bonded structure. Includes positioning equipment, setting parameters, monitoring, and confirming cure completion; typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise equipment setup, parameter configuration on controls, and quality monitoring. Robots can manipulate objects but struggle with varied curing equipment interfaces, fine dial/button operation, and judgment-based quality verification.

task: Monitor painting operations to identify flaws, such as blisters or streaks, and correct their causes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one painted workpiece or batch section, identifying defects, and applying a corrective action. A skilled inspector typically takes 15–25 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Detecting obvious defects via vision is feasible, but diagnosing root causes (humidity, viscosity, equipment faults) and executing corrective actions requires deep process knowledge, tool use, and adaptive decision-making well beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Grease and lubricate vehicles or specified units, such as springs, universal joints, or steering knuckles, using grease guns or spray lubricants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lubricating all designated points on a single vehicle (e.g., a car or light truck). A competent lube tech typically completes this in 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating under/around a vehicle on a lift, locating specific grease fittings (zerks) in tight, occluded spaces, coupling a grease gun precisely under pressure, and judging grease saturation — far beyond current robot dexterity and tool-use in unstructured under-vehicle environments.

task: Align and assemble parts to produce completed products, using gauges and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one completed product unit. A competent worker aligning parts, checking gauges, and fastening with hand tools typically takes ~20–30 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle structured pick-and-place well (~70–80%), but precise alignment using gauges, tool switching, and tolerance verification requires fine dexterity, tactile feedback, and adaptive tool-use that current humanoids handle unreliably.

task: Engrave or stamp identifying symbols, letters, or numbers on products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = engraving/stamping one product. A competent worker positions the item, aligns the tool/stamp, and applies the mark in roughly 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of stamp/engraving tool on varied product surfaces, fine force control, and perception of correct positioning. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity and calibrated force feedback needed for consistent, legible marks without damaging parts.

task: Mark, engrave, or emboss designs on metal pieces such as castings, wire, or jewelry, following specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = engraving/embossing a single metal piece (e.g. jewelry or casting). A skilled engraver typically spends 30–60 min per piece depending on design complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine motor dexterity, precise force control on hard metal surfaces, and interpretation of complex design specs. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter precision and specialized tool control needed for quality engraving.

task: Lay out installation of aircraft assemblies and systems, following documentation such as blueprints, manuals, and wiring diagrams.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out one aircraft assembly/system installation (e.g., a wiring harness run or structural sub-assembly). A skilled aircraft mechanic typically spends 1–3 hours interpreting docs, marking, and positioning components.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading and interpreting complex aerospace blueprints/wiring diagrams, precise spatial layout in tight fuselage spaces, and high-stakes decision-making. Current robots lack aviation-doc comprehension, fine layout dexterity, and certified autonomous judgment needed here.

task: Measure the tension of control cables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring tension on a single control cable run. A competent technician attaches a tensiometer, reads the value, and logs it in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise attachment of a tensiometer to a taut cable, fine dexterity to seat the tool correctly, and accurate reading of an analog/digital gauge. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-grip precision and tool-seating accuracy for this specialized task.

task: Inspect each car periodically during runs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single periodic inspection of one car during a run; a competent worker walks around and checks key systems (tires, fluids, lights, body) in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Visual inspection of exterior surfaces is feasible, but checking fluid levels, listening for abnormal sounds, assessing tire pressure by feel, and navigating around/under a vehicle in an active environment strains current dexterity, mobility, and multi-modal perception.

task: Drive machines into position at working faces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repositioning a machine (e.g. continuous miner, shuttle car, or drill) to a new working face in a mine or quarry; typically takes 15–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large, specialized heavy mining machines in unstructured, hazardous underground/surface environments. Humanoid robots cannot operate such machinery; remote-autonomous mining systems exist but are narrow, task-specific, and not general-purpose humanoids.

task: Remove excess materials and level and smooth wet mold mixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = leveling and smoothing one mold. A competent foundry/casting worker completes scraping, striking off excess, and smoothing a single wet mold in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled scraping and smoothing on a wet, deformable surface. Current robots lack reliable tactile feedback and compliant tool-use finesse needed to avoid gouging or under-leveling a fragile wet mold.

task: Smooth surfaces of molds, using scraping tools or sandpaper.
human_time_rationale: One instance = smoothing one mold surface. A skilled worker spends ~20–30 min scraping and sanding a typical mold to acceptable finish quality.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled scraping and sanding with tactile feedback, complex surface contouring, and quality judgment by feel/sight. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, compliant force control, and surface-finish perception needed.

task: Service and maintain aircraft and related apparatus by performing activities such as flushing crankcases, cleaning screens, and or moving parts.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance service session on a single aircraft covering crankcase flushing, screen cleaning, and moving-part checks; a competent A&P mechanic typically takes 60–120 minutes per session.
robot_success_rationale: Aircraft maintenance demands extreme precision, specialized tooling, confined-space access, safety-critical torque/inspection standards, and FAA-regulated sign-off. 2026 humanoids lack the dexterity, domain knowledge, and certification authority for autonomous aerospace servicing.

task: Remove or install aircraft engines, using hoists or forklift trucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing or installing one aircraft engine. A skilled aviation mechanic team typically takes 4–8 hours per engine, covering disconnection, hoisting, and securing/reinstalling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating hoists/forklifts, precise high-force coupling of heavy engine mounts, complex electrical/hydraulic disconnects, and working at height around aircraft structures. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Monitor well fields periodically to ensure proper functioning and performance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one periodic inspection round of a well field (multiple wells). A competent technician typically spends ~45–90 min checking gauges, flow rates, equipment status, and logging data.
robot_success_rationale: Well fields are outdoor, unstructured, variable-terrain environments. Reading gauges, connecting monitoring equipment, and logging data require specialized tool use and generalization. Current humanoids lack the mobility, weatherproofing, and domain-specific tool interfaces needed.

task: Verify detonation of charges by observing control panels, or by listening for the sounds of blasts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = verifying one detonation event. Worker monitors control panel and listens for blast, then confirms success — typically 3–5 minutes including pre/post checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading specialized control panels, interpreting auditory blast cues, and operating in hazardous outdoor/industrial environments. Current robots lack reliable panel-reading generalization, acoustic blast interpretation, and safe mobility in blast zones.

task: Position stones and metal pieces, and set, mount, and secure items in place, using setting and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning and setting a single stone or metal piece into a jewelry setting. A skilled setter typically takes 20–60 min per stone depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Extreme fine dexterity required — sub-millimeter precision, tactile feedback, and specialized hand tools for gem-setting far exceed current humanoid robot capability. High failure/damage risk.

task: Mount and secure lens blanks or optical lenses in holding tools or chucks of cutting, polishing, grinding, or coating machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading one lens blank into a chuck/holding tool. A skilled optical technician takes ~2–4 min to align, seat, and secure it correctly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity to align and secure a small, often transparent/curved optical blank into a tight tolerance chuck. Perception of glass/optical surfaces is challenging; misalignment risks costly damage. Current humanoids lack required sub-millimeter precision and delicate force control for this specialized task.

task: Inspect the condition of stationary trains, rolling stock, and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one stationary train/vehicle unit. A competent inspector walks the length, checks wheels, brakes, couplings, and equipment — typically 30–60 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate around a train and capture visual data, but identifying subtle defects (cracks, wear, brake shoe condition, coupling integrity) across highly varied, partially occluded equipment in an outdoor rail yard requires expert perception and generalization far beyond current capability.

task: Sand and apply sealer to properly dried finish.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sanding and sealing one average surface/piece (e.g., a furniture panel or floor section). Sanding, cleaning dust, and applying sealer coat takes ~45 min for a competent finisher.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, consistent pressure control during sanding, fine tactile feedback to detect surface readiness, managing flexible tools (sandpaper), and evenly applying liquid sealer — all highly challenging for current humanoids. Poor generalization across surfaces/shapes.

task: Rotate, test, and repair or replace tires.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rotating all four tires on a single vehicle, including inspection and any repair/replacement. A competent technician typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a hydraulic floor jack or lift, pneumatic impact wrench, torque wrench, and tire mounting/balancing machines — heavy specialized tools a humanoid cannot reliably operate. High-force fastener work, precision torque specs, and hazardous lifting make autonomous success near-zero.

task: Place tiles, nail them to roof boards, and cover nailheads with roofing cement.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing, nailing, and sealing one tile section (a few tiles). A competent roofer handles this in ~45 min per small tile run including cement application.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working on sloped, outdoor rooftop terrain (severe mobility/balance challenge), precise nail gun use, and fine cement application. Current humanoids lack the stability, tool dexterity, and outdoor generalization for this safely.

task: Repair or replace equipment components or parts such as blades, rolls, and pumps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing/repairing a single component (e.g., one blade, roll, or pump). A competent technician typically takes 30–90 min depending on complexity; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force torque application, precise alignment of heavy industrial parts, use of specialized tools (wrenches, presses), and fault diagnosis. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use generalization, and force-control capabilities.

task: Withdraw cores or other loose mold members after castings solidify.
human_time_rationale: One instance = withdrawing cores/loose members from a single casting. A skilled foundry worker locates, grips, and removes each core in ~2–4 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force pulling on irregularly shaped, hot, sand-bonded cores in an unstructured foundry environment. Poor generalization across varied mold geometries, heat/debris perception challenges, and grip-force precision limit current humanoids severely.

task: Post visual display boards and status boards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = posting/mounting one display or status board; includes carrying, aligning, and securing it to a wall or stand (~15 min median).
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to a wall, precise vertical alignment, applying fasteners or hooks, and handling large/awkward boards. Current humanoids lack reliable fine placement, fastener use, and wall-mounting dexterity in unstructured environments.

task: Buff and wax the finished paintwork.
human_time_rationale: One instance = buffing and waxing one average vehicle panel set or room. A competent detailer takes ~45 min for a full car exterior buff and wax.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, consistent pressure and circular motion across complex curved surfaces, tactile feedback to avoid burn-through, tool-switching (buffer, applicator), and quality inspection. Current humanoids lack the fine force control and surface-tracking needed.

task: Obtain fuel and oil samples and check them for contamination.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sampling fuel and oil from one machine and checking for contamination; includes locating ports, drawing samples, and inspecting results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating and accessing fluid sampling ports on varied machinery, using specialized sampling tools, and interpreting contamination results. High dexterity, tool-use, and domain-specific perception demands far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Inspect aircraft for defects and malfunctions, according to pre-flight checklists.
human_time_rationale: One pre-flight inspection of a typical general aviation or commercial aircraft following a standardized checklist; experienced technician/pilot takes ~20–45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires traversing complex 3-D aircraft exterior, fine visual/tactile defect detection, climbing surfaces, accessing panels, and aviation-grade judgment. Current humanoids lack the mobility, specialized perception, and regulatory-acceptable autonomy for this safety-critical task.

task: Set up portable equipment, such as ventilators, exhaust units, ladders, or scaffolding.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up one piece of portable equipment (e.g., a ventilator unit, ladder, or section of scaffolding) at a worksite; a competent worker typically takes 15–35 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting, multi-step assembly, outdoor/unstructured terrain, tool use, and safety-critical judgment (e.g., leveling scaffolding). Current humanoids lack the strength, dexterity, and generalization for reliable autonomous setup of varied heavy equipment.

task: Inspect freight cars for compliance with sealing procedures, and record car numbers and seal numbers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one freight car inspection; visually check seal integrity, verify compliance, and log car/seal numbers. A competent inspector takes ~3–7 min per car.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/rail-yard locomotion (uneven terrain, ~30–50%), reading small alphanumeric codes on dirty surfaces (OCR challenging), assessing seal integrity, and structured data logging — combined reliability is low.

task: Apply primer over any repairs made to surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying primer to repaired areas on a single surface (e.g., a wall patch). Includes prep (stirring, masking edges), brushing/rolling primer on, and light cleanup. ~20 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying repaired spots via perception, handling a paint brush/roller, controlling pressure and stroke consistency, and avoiding overspray on adjacent surfaces. Current robots lack reliable fine-coat application and adaptive surface navigation for painting tasks.

task: Affix identifying information to materials or products, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = affixing a label/tag/stamp to a single item using a hand tool (e.g., label gun, stamp, punch). A competent worker completes this in ~2–4 minutes including locating materials.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping small hand tools (label guns, stamps), aligning labels precisely on varied surfaces, and applying consistent pressure. Dexterity and tool-use generalization remain challenging; success varies widely by tool type and product geometry.

task: Disassemble units such as water pumps, control valves, and compressors so that repairs can be made.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling a single unit (e.g., a water pump or control valve). A competent mechanic typically takes 30–60 min depending on unit complexity and fastener count.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying diverse fastener types, applying high torque with correct tools, handling corroded/seized parts, and following unit-specific sequences. Current humanoids lack the torque, tool-changing dexterity, and generalization for unstructured industrial disassembly.

task: Provide assistance in the installation or repair of rails and ties.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing or repairing one section of track (rail + ties). A crew typically completes a standard section in ~2 hours including alignment, spiking, and inspection.
