robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy machinery operation (rail cranes, tampers, spike drivers), outdoor rough terrain locomotion, high-force manipulation of 600+ lb rails, and coordinated multi-step work. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Drill holes into sides of stones broken from masses, insert dogs or attach slings, and direct removal of stones.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and directing removal of a single large stone: drilling holes, inserting dogs/attaching slings, and signaling/directing lift. Typical quarry operation takes ~30–60 min per stone.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a heavy drill on irregular stone surfaces, inserting rigging hardware into drilled holes, attaching slings, and directing crane/machinery removal — all in a harsh, unstructured quarry environment. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities in mobility, force, tool-use, and coordination.

task: Inspect airframes for wear or other defects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full airframe inspection of a single aircraft. A competent aviation technician typically spends 60–120 minutes on a structured visual/tactile walkthrough inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level defect recognition (cracks, corrosion, fatigue, delamination) on complex curved surfaces, confined spaces, and varied materials. Current robots lack the specialized perception, domain knowledge, and fine tactile sensing needed for certifiable airframe inspection.

task: Align car sides for installation of car ends and crossties, using width gauges, turnbuckles, and wrenches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning both sides of one railcar for end/crosstie installation; involves iterative measuring, turnbuckle adjustments, and wrench work until width gauges confirm spec.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force application with large wrenches and turnbuckles on a heavy railcar structure, reading analog width gauges, and iterative adjustment under load. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, strength, and tool-use generalization.

task: Observe water levels and oil, air, and steam pressure gauges to ensure proper operation of equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single inspection round reading all relevant gauges (water, oil, air, steam pressure). A competent operator walks the equipment and logs readings in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate and use cameras to read digital gauges, but analog gauge reading in varied industrial environments, identifying correct gauges, interpreting readings in context, and flagging anomalies reliably remains challenging. Locomotion over industrial floors adds further risk.

task: Check conditions of trailers after contents have been unloaded to ensure that there has been no damage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one trailer post-unload. A competent worker walks through, checks walls, floor, ceiling, and doors for damage in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a semi-trailer interior, visually identifying subtle structural damage (dents, cracks, wet spots). Current robots lack reliable damage-detection perception in unstructured, dim trailer environments and may struggle with ingress/egress over dock gaps.

task: Weigh or measure materials and products, using scales or other measuring instruments, or read scales on conveyors that continually weigh products, to verify specified tonnages and prevent overloads.
human_time_rationale: One instance = weighing/measuring a single batch or reading a conveyor scale once. A competent worker reads instruments, records values, and checks against targets in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read digital displays and perform pick-and-place onto scales, but reading analog scales, operating varied weighing equipment, and interpreting conveyor readouts in unstructured industrial settings remain unreliable for current humanoids.

task: Pour molten metal alloys or other materials into molds to cast models of jewelry.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pouring one mold batch of jewelry castings; includes preparing the mold, heating/pouring molten metal, and allowing initial set. Competent jeweler takes ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling extremely high-temperature molten metal with precise, controlled pouring into small intricate molds. Current humanoids lack heat-safe end-effectors, fine force/flow control, and the safety-critical precision needed for jewelry casting.

task: Drive gasoline, diesel, or electrically powered multi-passenger vehicles to transport students between neighborhoods, schools, and school activities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one school bus route/trip (pickup or dropoff). A typical route serving a neighborhood to school runs ~30–60 minutes, median ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate full-size buses in live traffic. L4 AVs exist but are not general-purpose humanoid robots. Task also requires student supervision, emergency response, and dynamic road navigation — all beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Examine engines through specially designed openings while working from ladders or scaffolds, or use hoists or lifts to remove the entire engine from an aircraft.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one aircraft engine, either via access panels or engine removal. Skilled aviation mechanic typically takes 60–120 min depending on method.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating ladders/scaffolds, using hoists, interpreting specialized access panels on aircraft, and performing detailed visual inspection in constrained spaces. Far beyond current humanoid robot autonomy, mobility, and aviation-domain tool-use capabilities.

task: Light fuses, drop detonating devices into wells or boreholes, or activate firing devices with plungers, dials, or buttons, in order to set off single or multiple blasts.
human_time_rationale: One blast setup: connecting/verifying fuses or detonators, dropping devices into well/borehole, confirming clearance, then activating the firing device. ~15–25 min per blast event.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating hazardous outdoor/industrial terrain, precise insertion of detonators into boreholes, manipulating specialized firing controls, and making safety-critical judgments. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, domain tool-use, and safety-critical autonomy required.

task: Repair and service blasting, shooting, and automotive equipment, and electrical wiring and instruments, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One repair/service instance on a single piece of equipment (e.g., one blasting unit or automotive component); a competent technician typically takes ~1 hour per job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level diagnostics, fine dexterity with hand tools, and domain-specific knowledge across highly varied equipment types. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation, force feedback, and generalized repair reasoning needed.

task: Set ladders, scaffolds, and hoists in place for taking supplies to roofs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a ladder, scaffold section, or hoist at a single work site. A competent worker takes ~20–30 min to position, secure, and verify the equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/uneven terrain navigation, heavy equipment handling, precise anchoring of ladders/scaffolding, and operating mechanical hoists — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, strength, and safety-critical judgment.

task: Inspect cables or grappling devices for wear and install or replace cables, as needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a cable/grappling system and replacing if needed. A competent worker spends ~15 min inspecting and ~30 min replacing a cable, averaged across inspect-only and replace cases.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual/tactile inspection of cable wear, navigating industrial environments, and precise mechanical replacement (threading, tensioning, fastening). Current humanoids lack reliable cable-wear perception and dexterous tool use for such specialized hardware.

task: Inspect and adjust crane mechanisms or lifting accessories to prevent malfunctions or damage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and adjusting a single crane's mechanisms/accessories. A competent operator/rigger typically takes 30–60 min for a pre-shift or scheduled inspection with minor adjustments.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing/navigating large crane structures, reading mechanical wear indicators, operating heavy machinery controls, and making precise mechanical adjustments. Current humanoids lack the mobility, specialized tool-use, and force feedback for this unstructured heavy-industrial environment.

task: Change spark plugs, fuel filters, air filters, and batteries in hybrid electric vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing all four items on one hybrid vehicle. A competent mechanic needs ~15 min for spark plugs, ~10 for fuel filter, ~10 for air filter, ~10 for 12V battery.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating inside a vehicle engine bay: unbolting varied fasteners, routing fuel lines, managing ignition coil harnesses. Confined, oily, unstructured space; high-voltage HV battery proximity adds safety constraints; far beyond current humanoid dexterity/generalization.

task: Make minor repairs to vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one minor repair (e.g., replacing a wiper blade, changing a bulb, tightening a belt). A competent mechanic typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Vehicle repairs require fine dexterity in confined, cluttered engine bays, tool selection, force control, and diagnosis. Current humanoids lack the precision, tool-use generalization, and situational awareness needed for reliable autonomous repair.

task: Disassemble engines and inspect parts, such as turbine blades or cylinders, for corrosion, wear, warping, cracks, and leaks, using precision measuring instruments, x-rays, and magnetic inspection equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling one engine and completing full inspection of all major parts. A competent aviation/power-plant mechanic typically takes 3–5 hours for a moderate engine teardown and inspection cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex multi-step disassembly with varied fasteners/tools, fine dexterity for precision instruments, operating X-ray and magnetic NDT equipment, interpreting subtle defect signals, and adapting to unstructured heavy mechanical environments — far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Pick up and drop off students at regularly scheduled neighborhood locations, following strict time schedules.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one complete route (morning or afternoon run). A typical school bus route covers multiple stops over ~45–75 minutes; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate a full-size bus in traffic. L4 AVs are narrow-route systems, not general-purpose robots. Safe student boarding/alighting supervision, door operation, and schedule adherence in dynamic neighborhoods are far beyond current capability.

task: Spray prepared surfaces with specified amounts of primers and decorative or finish coatings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = spraying primer or finish coat on a single prepared surface (e.g., one panel or wall section). A competent painter takes ~30–60 min including setup, application, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Spray painting requires precise distance/angle control, consistent motion speed, even coverage judgment, and tool handling. Current humanoids lack the fine motion consistency, spray gun manipulation, and coating-quality perception needed for acceptable finish results.

task: Load or unload bundles from trucks, or move containers to storage bins, using moving equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unloading or loading one truckload of bundles/containers; a competent dock worker typically takes 30–60 min with moving equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids lack ability to operate forklifts/pallet jacks in traffic; varied bundle sizes, confined trailer spaces, and unstructured layouts challenge perception and manipulation; some pick-and-place feasible but full autonomous truck unloading remains very limited in 2026.

task: Measure diameters of axle wheel seats, using micrometers, and mark dimensions on axles so that wheels can be bored to specified dimensions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring one axle's wheel seats with a micrometer and marking dimensions. A skilled machinist takes ~5–10 min per axle for precise measurement and marking.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter precision micrometer handling, reading analog/digital gauges, and legibly marking dimensions on cylindrical metal surfaces — far beyond current general-purpose robot dexterity and fine manipulation reliability.

task: Install or remove special equipment, such as tire chains, grader blades, plow blades, or sanders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing or removing one piece of special equipment (e.g., tire chains or plow blade). A competent worker takes ~20–40 min depending on equipment type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating under heavy vehicles or attaching large, heavy, irregular implements outdoors. Demands high-force manipulation, specialized tool use, precise alignment, and working in awkward positions — far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Inspect track for defects such as broken rails and switch malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a ~1-mile track segment. A competent inspector walking and checking rails, joints, and switches takes ~60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained outdoor locomotion over uneven ballast/ties, long-distance navigation, specialized defect perception (cracks, gauge faults, switch alignment), and reliable anomaly reporting — far beyond current humanoid generalization and terrain mobility.

task: Drive shuttle busses, limousines, company cars, or privately owned vehicles to transport passengers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one passenger trip (pickup to drop-off). Typical shuttle/limo trip averages ~30–40 minutes including boarding and alighting.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate steering wheels, pedals, and controls in a standard vehicle cockpit autonomously in real traffic. L4 AVs are narrow-route systems, not general-purpose humanoids. Extremely low feasibility.

task: Perform housekeeping duties, such as cooking, cleaning, washing clothes or dishes, or running errands.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a typical daily/shift housekeeping session covering light cooking, cleaning, laundry, dishes, and one errand; ~90 min is a reasonable median for a competent housekeeper.
robot_success_rationale: Task bundles cooking, laundry, dishes, errands, and outdoor travel — each individually hard for humanoids. Errand-running requires navigating public spaces/vehicles; cooking requires generalization. Compounding failure probabilities across subtasks yields very low overall success.

task: Tap or tilt molds to ensure uniform distribution of materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tapping/tilting one mold until material is uniformly distributed; a skilled worker takes ~1–2 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires controlled force/vibration application and visual/tactile feedback to judge uniformity. Current humanoids lack fine force modulation and reliable material-flow perception needed to confirm success consistently.

task: Stop equipment or machinery and clear jams, using poles, bars, and hand tools, or remove damaged materials from conveyors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stopping a machine, diagnosing and clearing one jam or removing damaged material. A competent worker typically takes 5–20 min depending on jam severity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying a jam in an unstructured industrial environment, wielding poles/bars with significant force, navigating tight conveyor spaces, and operating emergency stops — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and generalization limits.

task: Patch broken edges or fractures, using clay or plaster.
human_time_rationale: One instance = patching a single fracture or broken edge on an object (e.g., a ceramic, wall crack, or sculpture). Includes prep, applying material, and smoothing. ~15–25 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to apply, press, and smooth clay/plaster into irregular fractures. Tactile feedback, force control, and perception of crack geometry are well beyond current humanoid robots' reliable capabilities.

task: Perform healthcare-related tasks, such as monitoring vital signs and medication, under the direction of registered nurses or physiotherapists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient check covering vital signs monitoring and medication administration; a competent healthcare aide typically completes this in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (pills, IV lines, equipment), safe patient interaction, contextual clinical judgment, and reliable navigation in busy hospital environments. Current humanoids lack medical-grade reliability, compliance, and dexterity for autonomous patient care.

task: Clean engines, sediment bulk and screens, and carburetors, adjusting carburetor float levels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning an engine, sediment bowl/screens, and carburetor plus adjusting float level on a single engine. A competent mechanic typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires disassembly of small fuel-system components, fine dexterous manipulation of carburetor float settings, and precise torque/adjustment — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use generalization. Near-zero success probability.

task: Repair and maintain electrical and electronic controls for propulsion and braking systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and repairing a single fault in a propulsion/braking control system. A competent technician typically spends 60–120 min per repair event including diagnostics, component replacement, and testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level electrical diagnostics (multimeter, oscilloscope, software tools), precise connector manipulation, fault interpretation, and contextual judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and domain-specific reasoning capabilities.

task: Inspect components such as bearings, seals, gaskets, wheels, and coupler assemblies to determine if repairs are needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a full set of components (bearings, seals, gaskets, wheels, couplers) on one machine/assembly. A competent technician takes ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual and tactile assessment of wear, cracks, and deformation on varied, often occluded parts. Current robots lack reliable fine-grained anomaly detection and diagnostic judgment across diverse component types in unstructured environments.

task: Repair mold defects, such as cracks or broken edges, using patterns, mold boxes, or hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single mold defect (crack or broken edge). A skilled molder typically inspects, selects materials/tools, and performs the repair in ~20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to apply patching material precisely into cracks or broken edges, tactile feedback for surface blending, and skilled tool use with patterns/hand tools. Far beyond reliable autonomous robot capability in 2026.

task: Carry needed tools or supplies from storage or trucks and return them after use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single round trip fetching tools/supplies from storage or a truck and returning them after use; ~10 min covers walking, loading, carrying, and returning.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids can walk and carry objects, but unstructured storage areas, truck beds, varied tool shapes, and outdoor/uneven terrain (truck access) challenge perception, dexterity, and generalization significantly.

task: Conduct flight test programs to test equipment, instruments, and systems under a variety of conditions, using both manual and automatic controls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one flight test sortie; includes pre-flight checks, test execution, and post-flight debriefing. Typical test sortie runs 2–3 hours total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires piloting an aircraft, interpreting complex real-time instrument data, and exercising adaptive judgment under dynamic flight conditions. No 2026 general-purpose humanoid can operate aircraft controls in flight or perform test-pilot cognitive evaluation.

task: Apply rust-resistant undercoats and caulk and seal seams.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating one panel or section; includes surface prep, brush/roller undercoat application, caulking seams, and smoothing. ~45 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool switching (brush/roller, caulk gun), consistent pressure along irregular seams, quality judgment of coverage and bead uniformity, and working in varied orientations. Far beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Thread strapping through strapping tools and secure battens with strapping to form protective pallets around extrusions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pallet secured with battens and strapping. Threading, tensioning, and sealing multiple straps around a loaded pallet takes a competent worker ~10–15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires threading flexible strapping through a tensioning tool, applying precise tension, crimping/sealing, and repositioning around a bulky irregular pallet — demanding bimanual dexterity, force control, and tool-use far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Bore holes or cut grates, risers, or pouring spouts in molds, using power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one mold. A skilled worker with power tools drills/cuts required features in ~15 min per mold, depending on size and number of holes/cuts.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool positioning, high-force power tool operation, and workpiece fixturing on irregular mold surfaces. Current humanoids lack the rigidity, force control, and fine spatial accuracy needed for accurate boring/cutting under load.

task: Assist workers in tasks, such as loading vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting a worker to load one vehicle (e.g., a delivery van or truck) with varied packages/items; a competent worker typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Loading vehicles involves varied item shapes, weights, and stacking logic in a confined, dynamic space. Current humanoids struggle with generalized grasping, load sequencing, and tight-space mobility, limiting reliable autonomous success.

task: Install and repair interior flooring, fixtures, walls, plumbing, steps, and platforms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single interior repair/install job (e.g., one room of flooring or one fixture). A skilled tradesperson typically spends 2–4 hours per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly varied dexterous manipulation (cutting, fastening, plumbing connections), tool switching, spatial reasoning in confined/unstructured spaces, and multi-step planning across very different subtasks. Far beyond 2026 frontier robot capability.

task: Maintain equipment storage areas to ensure that inventory is protected.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance check/organization session of one storage area. Worker inspects, reorganizes, and secures items in ~30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating cluttered storage spaces, recognizing disorganized or at-risk inventory, repositioning varied items, and making judgment calls about protection. Current robots lack reliable generalization for unstructured, varied storage environments.

task: Assemble product containers or crates, using hand tools and precut lumber.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one container/crate from precut lumber using hand tools. A competent worker can nail/screw together a standard wooden crate in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated two-handed dexterity with hand tools (hammers, screwdrivers, nail guns), precise alignment of lumber pieces, and force control. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use and bimanual coordination for this unstructured carpentry task.

task: Perform minor vehicle repairs, such as cleaning spark plugs, or take vehicles to mechanics for servicing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one minor repair (e.g., cleaning spark plugs ~20–30 min) or one trip to a mechanic and back (~30–45 min). Median ~30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Driving to a mechanic is impossible for a humanoid (cannot operate vehicles in traffic). Minor repairs like spark plug cleaning require fine dexterity in confined engine bays, tool use, and domain-specific knowledge — well beyond current robot capability.

task: Spot cars for loading and unloading at customer locations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = guiding/positioning one vehicle into a loading/unloading spot at a customer location, including signaling, directing, and confirming placement. Typically 5–15 minutes per vehicle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor mobility, dynamic traffic perception, communication with drivers (gestures/signals), and situational judgment in unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor navigation, driver interaction, and authoritative traffic-direction capability.

task: Manipulate controls, levers, and valves to start pumps, auxiliary equipment, or conveyors, and to adjust equipment positions, speeds, timing, and material flows.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting/adjusting one equipment unit: locating controls, operating levers/valves, and confirming response. Typical operator cycle ~5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct industrial controls across highly varied panel layouts, turning stiff valves/levers with precision force, reading gauges, and verifying outcomes. Poor generalization to unstructured industrial environments; dexterity and contextual judgment are weak spots for 2026 humanoids.

task: Reposition machines to make additional holes or cuts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repositioning a machine (e.g. drill press, punch, CNC) to a new hole/cut position; a skilled operator adjusts fixtures, clamps, and aligns in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise manipulation of heavy industrial machines, clamps, and fixtures; fine positional alignment demands dexterity and force control beyond current humanoid capability; safety-critical environment further limits autonomous operation.

task: Spread plastic film over areas to be repaired to prevent damage to surrounding areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = spreading and securing plastic film over a single repair area. A competent worker typically takes ~5 minutes to position, unfold, and fix the sheeting in place.
robot_success_rationale: Handling large, thin, flexible plastic film is extremely difficult for robots — deformable material perception and manipulation are poor. Securing edges neatly without tearing, while navigating around a repair site, pushes beyond current frontier capabilities.

task: Weigh bundles, using floor scales, and record weights for company records.
human_time_rationale: One instance = weighing a single bundle: place on scale, read weight, record it. A competent worker completes this in ~3 minutes including logging.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can pick and place bundles and read digital displays, but grasping irregular bundles, navigating to a floor scale, reading the display reliably, and recording data in a system requires robust perception, dexterity, and tool integration—still error-prone in 2026.

task: Measure ingredients and mix molding, casting material, or sealing compounds to prescribed consistencies, according to formulas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and mixing a single batch. A competent worker reads the formula, weighs/volumes ingredients, and blends to consistency in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement (scales, graduated containers), reading formulas, pouring viscous materials, and judging consistency by feel/sight. Current humanoids lack fine pour control, reliable scale reading, and sensory feedback to verify consistency.

task: Repair or replace gas piping.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing or replacing a single gas pipe section/joint. A competent plumber/gasfitter typically takes 60–120 min including shutoff, removal, fitting, and leak-testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires threading or soldering pipe with high precision under strict safety tolerances, operating shutoff valves in confined/awkward spaces, and leak-testing with instruments. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and safety-critical judgment capabilities.

task: Adjust controls to guide, position, or move equipment, such as cranes, booms, or cameras.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one positioning/movement operation (e.g., swinging a crane to a target location). A skilled operator typically takes 5–20 minutes per deliberate move.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate crane/boom/camera crane controls in real environments; requires seated cab operation, specialized levers/pedals, spatial judgment under load, and safety-critical precision. No commercial deployment exists.

task: Press console buttons to deflect packages to predetermined accumulators or reject lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pressing a console button to deflect one package. A competent operator does this in ~15 seconds per package decision and button press.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can press buttons but must identify correct console button amid many options, read displays, and time presses accurately. Panel variability, small buttons, and real-time decision-making reduce reliability significantly.

task: Connect and turn regulator valves to activate and adjust gas flow and pressure so that desired flames are obtained.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting a regulator valve and adjusting gas flow until a desired flame is achieved; a competent worker does this in ~5 minutes including inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise threaded valve connections (fine dexterity), gas-leak-safe handling, flame perception feedback, and pressure fine-tuning — well beyond current humanoid dexterity and multimodal sensory integration. Safety stakes further restrict autonomous operation.

task: Drive engines within railroad yards or other establishments to couple, uncouple, or switch railroad cars.
human_time_rationale: One switching move (couple, uncouple, or reposition a cut of cars) in a railroad yard; median ~20–30 min for a competent yard engineer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a full-scale locomotive with specialized cab controls, throttle, and brakes on live rail infrastructure. No humanoid robot can operate heavy rail vehicles autonomously at any acceptable safety standard as of 2026.

task: Attach identifying tags to containers or mark them with identifying information.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching or marking one container. A worker picks a label/tag, reads identifying info, and affixes or writes it — typically 1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Peeling and precisely placing adhesive labels or writing legibly requires fine dexterity and alignment that current humanoids handle inconsistently. Perception of container surface and label placement adds further complexity.

task: Monitor food preparation methods, portion sizes, and garnishing and presentation of food to ensure that food is prepared and presented in an acceptable manner.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single dish/plate before service. A competent chef or supervisor visually checks prep method, portion, garnish, and plating in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual judgment of aesthetics, portion accuracy, garnish placement, and cooking quality. Current robots lack the culinary domain knowledge, fine visual discrimination, and contextual standards to reliably pass a human supervisor's quality bar.

task: Operate vehicles with specialized equipment, such as wheelchair lifts, to transport and secure passengers with special needs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one passenger pickup-to-dropoff trip, including deploying wheelchair lift, securing passenger, driving, and releasing — typical paratransit run ~25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a specialized vehicle in traffic (humanoids cannot drive), plus fine dexterous caregiving (securing wheelchair tie-downs, lift operation). No current general-purpose robot can combine both reliably.

task: Distribute materials, supplies, and equipment to work stations, using lifts and trucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single distribution run delivering materials/supplies/equipment to one or more workstations; typical run takes ~15–25 minutes including loading, transit, and unloading.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating forklifts/pallet trucks — vehicles a humanoid cannot drive. Loading varied items adds manipulation complexity. AMRs handle narrow versions of this, but a general-purpose humanoid cannot reliably operate the required lift/truck equipment autonomously.

task: Shape and straighten damaged or twisted articles by hand or using pliers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = straightening/reshaping one damaged article. A competent worker assesses damage, applies hand/plier force iteratively, and checks result — typically 5–15 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine force-feedback control, precise grip with pliers on irregular deformed shapes, and iterative tactile judgment to avoid over-correction. Current humanoids lack the nuanced force modulation and tool-use dexterity needed reliably.

task: Test units for operability before and after repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one unit before and after a repair cycle; technician runs checks, observes outputs, and logs results in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting varied test procedures, reading displays/indicators, connecting test equipment, and making pass/fail judgments. High cognitive and perceptual demands with unstructured tool-use; current humanoids lack reliable generalization here.

task: Sell and install accessories, such as batteries, windshield wiper blades, fan belts, bulbs, or headlamps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selling and installing one accessory (e.g., wiper blade or battery). Includes customer interaction, item retrieval, and hands-on installation at the vehicle. ~25 min median across accessory types.
robot_success_rationale: Requires customer-facing sales interaction, navigating a retail environment, locating correct parts, and performing varied under-hood/exterior installations (battery terminals, wiper arms, headlamp housings) — diverse dexterity, tool-use, and social tasks far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Pour, pack, spread, or press plaster, concrete, or other materials into or around models or molds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling/packing one mold or form with plaster/concrete. A competent worker mixes, pours/packs, and smooths material in roughly 15–25 minutes depending on mold size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires controlled pouring of viscous/semi-liquid material, tactile feedback for packing density, and precise spreading — all highly unstructured. Current humanoids lack reliable fluid/paste handling, force-sensitive packing, and finishing dexterity at acceptable quality.

task: Cut designs in molds or other materials to be used as models in the fabrication of metal and jewelry products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting a single mold/model design. A competent jeweler or moldmaker typically spends 60–120 minutes on precision hand-cutting or engraving one mold pattern.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sub-millimeter precision tool control, and artistic/technical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability; even specialized CNC robots lack the generalization and tactile feedback needed autonomously.

task: Maintain and repair materials, work sites, and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance/repair session on a piece of equipment or work site area; a competent worker typically spends ~1 hour per session.
robot_success_rationale: Maintenance and repair spans highly varied, unstructured tasks requiring diagnosis, fine dexterity, tool use, and judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable fault diagnosis, adaptive tool manipulation, and generalization across repair contexts.

task: Administer bedside or personal care, such as ambulation or personal hygiene assistance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single caregiving session (e.g., helping one patient with hygiene or ambulation). A competent aide typically spends 20–30 minutes per session.
robot_success_rationale: Unstructured caregiving demands high dexterity, safe force control near fragile humans, adaptive mobility, and social responsiveness. Current humanoids lack reliable gentle contact, real-time patient state reading, and safe fall-prevention support.

task: Test vehicle equipment, such as lights, brakes, horns, or windshield wipers, to ensure proper operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting all listed equipment (lights, brakes, horn, wipers) on a single vehicle. A competent driver/technician completes a full pre-trip or safety check in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires entering/operating a vehicle, pressing pedals, activating controls, and visually verifying outputs. Humanoids cannot safely drive or operate vehicle controls in context; perception of brake feel and light confirmation across vehicle exterior adds further complexity.

task: Perform minor vehicle repairs, such as cleaning spark plugs, or take vehicles to mechanics for servicing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one minor repair (e.g., cleaning spark plugs ~20–30 min) or one trip to a mechanic (~45 min round trip including drop-off/pick-up). Median across both sub-tasks ≈ 45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Driving to a mechanic requires operating a vehicle on public roads — impossible for current humanoids. Minor engine repairs demand fine dexterity in confined, oily engine bays with non-standardized layouts; far beyond current robot capability.

task: Inspect bundle packaging for conformance to regulations or customer requirements, and remove and batch packaging tickets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one bundle, checking conformance, removing and batching its ticket. A competent worker handles this quickly per bundle on a production line.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can perceive labels and detect gross defects, but reliably reading/interpreting regulatory conformance details, peeling/removing tickets, and batching them requires fine dexterity and nuanced judgment — current humanoids struggle here.

task: Move levers, depress foot pedals, or turn dials to operate cranes, cherry pickers, electromagnets, or other moving equipment for lifting, moving, or placing loads.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one lift-move-place cycle (rigging, operating controls, positioning load). A competent operator typically completes a single lift cycle in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate crane/cherry-picker cockpit controls in live environments. Requires seated cab operation, complex multi-axis lever/pedal coordination, load sway management, and spatial judgment far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Attach slings, hooks, or other devices to lift cargo and guide loads.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rigging a single load (attaching slings/hooks, verifying attachment, signaling lift). Typical rigging of a standard cargo item takes ~5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires selecting correct sling type, threading around irregular loads, applying precise tension, and guiding a dynamic suspended load — demands high dexterity, load-aware force sensing, and situational judgment well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Inspect waste pretreatment, treatment, and disposal facilities and systems for conformance to federal, state, or local regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one facility inspection visit. A competent environmental inspector typically spends 1–3 hours reviewing pretreatment, treatment, and disposal systems, taking samples, and documenting findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex industrial outdoor/indoor environments, interpreting regulatory standards, reading gauges/labels, collecting samples, and producing a legally defensible compliance report — far beyond current robot generalization, judgment, and tool-use capabilities.

task: Observe and record field conditions, gathering, interpreting, and reporting data such as flow meter readings and chemical levels.
human_time_rationale: One field inspection round: visiting multiple monitoring points (flow meters, chemical sensors), reading/recording values, and logging a basic report. Typical utility/environmental technician route.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with outdoor/unstructured terrain, reading analog gauges, accessing varied field equipment, and interpreting chemical test results. Perception of non-digital displays and report generation remain weak. Navigation across varied field sites is unreliable.

task: Connect electrical equipment to power sources so that it can be tested before use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting a single piece of electrical equipment to a power source for testing. A competent technician identifies the correct power source, routes cables, and makes secure connections in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct ports/connectors, routing cables precisely, and making secure electrical connections — demanding fine dexterity and semantic understanding of equipment. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-connector insertion and safety-critical judgment needed here.

task: Disassemble timepieces and inspect them for defective, worn, misaligned, or rusty parts, using loupes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling and inspecting one timepiece. A skilled watchmaker typically takes 30–60 min to fully disassemble and loupe-inspect a mechanical watch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (sub-mm components, jewel screws, hairsprings), loupe-aided micro-inspection, and expert defect recognition. Far beyond current humanoid manipulation and perception capabilities.

task: Follow safety rules as students board and exit buses or cross streets near bus stops.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one bus stop event covering boarding, exiting, and street crossing. A competent crossing guard/bus monitor manages this in roughly 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time social judgment, crowd management of unpredictable children, traffic interaction, verbal/gestural communication, and rapid safety decisions in chaotic outdoor environments — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Brush or spray mold surfaces with parting agents or insert paper into molds to ensure smoothness and prevent sticking or seepage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying parting agent to one mold surface (brushing/spraying or inserting paper). A competent worker completes this in ~5 minutes per mold.
robot_success_rationale: Requires controlled coating application (even spray/brush strokes), precise paper insertion into mold cavities, and quality judgment of coverage. Current robots lack reliable fine surface-coating dexterity and coverage verification in unstructured mold geometries.

task: Test operation and fit of timepiece parts and subassemblies, using electronic testing equipment, tweezers, watchmakers' tools, and loupes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single timepiece subassembly (e.g., a movement or module). A skilled watchmaker spends ~10–20 min checking fit, function, and electronic readings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to handle sub-millimeter watch parts with tweezers and loupes, precise force control, and interpretation of electronic test results. Far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity limits.

task: Check the condition of a vehicle's tires, brakes, windshield wipers, lights, oil, fuel, water, and safety equipment to ensure that everything is in working order.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full pre-trip vehicle inspection of 8 systems. A competent driver/mechanic walks around, visually and physically checks each; ~20 min for a thorough routine check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating around a vehicle, visual/tactile inspection of varied components, checking fluid levels under a hood, testing lights/wipers, and assessing brake pad thickness — multi-modal, unstructured tasks far beyond current humanoid generalization.

task: Escort small children across roads and highways.
human_time_rationale: One instance = guiding a group of small children across one road/crossing, including waiting for safe gap/signal, typically 3–5 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time traffic judgment, child herding/communication, emergency intervention, and operating in unpredictable outdoor road environments. No current humanoid has the safety-critical perception, social control, or liability clearance for this task.

task: Reassemble engines following repair or inspection and reinstall engines in aircraft.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reassembling and reinstalling one aircraft engine. Skilled aviation mechanics typically spend 6–10 hours on a full engine reassembly and reinstallation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme precision torquing, tight-tolerance fasteners, routing complex harnesses/plumbing in confined engine bays, and FAA-regulated quality verification. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and safety compliance capabilities.

task: Pick up passengers at prearranged locations, at taxi stands, or by cruising streets in high-traffic areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = navigating to a pickup location and collecting a passenger. Median time from dispatch/spot to passenger aboard, including curb maneuvering, is ~10–15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate passenger vehicles in traffic. L4 AVs exist but are narrow/mapped-route systems, not general-purpose humanoids. Street cruising, dynamic pickups, and passenger interaction are far beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Drive locomotives to and from various stations in roundhouses to have locomotives cleaned, serviced, repaired, or supplied.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving a locomotive from one point to another within a roundhouse yard. Typical short repositioning runs take ~15–25 minutes including coupling, signaling, and positioning.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate locomotive controls in live rail environments. Requires specialized vehicle operation, track navigation, safety protocols, and coupling tasks. No frontier humanoid has this capability in 2026.

task: Direct or participate in surveying to lay out installations or establish reference points, grades, or elevations to guide construction.
human_time_rationale: One survey session for a typical construction site reference layout — setting points, grades, and elevations — takes a skilled surveyor roughly 1–2 hours including setup, measurements, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized instruments (total stations, GPS rovers), navigating rough outdoor terrain, making precise spatial judgments, communicating with a crew, and interpreting engineering drawings — far beyond 2026 humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Test electrical systems of cars by operating systems and using testing equipment such as ammeters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing the electrical systems of one vehicle. A competent auto technician typically spends 20–40 minutes running diagnostics, operating systems, and using test equipment like ammeters.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting probes precisely to terminals, interpreting multimeter/ammeter readings, operating vehicle controls, and applying diagnostic reasoning. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, vehicle-specific tool handling, and contextual electrical diagnosis capability needed.

task: Test timepiece accuracy and performance, using meters and other electronic instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one timepiece. A competent watchmaker connects instruments, runs accuracy/rate tests, and logs results — typically 15–25 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise manipulation of tiny timepieces, connecting delicate electronic probes/meters, interpreting specialized test equipment readouts, and making fine adjustments — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and domain-specific tool-use capability.

task: Perform minor aircraft maintenance and repair work, or arrange for major maintenance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single minor maintenance/repair job (e.g., replacing a filter, tightening fasteners, inspecting a component, or coordinating a major repair). Typical minor task takes ~1–2 hrs for a competent A&P mechanic.
robot_success_rationale: Requires aviation-specific dexterity (tight airframe spaces, torque specs), certified judgment, specialized tools, hazardous materials handling, and regulatory compliance. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities in unstructured, safety-critical aerospace environments.

task: Join sections of conveyor frames at temporary working areas, and connect power units.
human_time_rationale: One instance = joining frame sections and connecting a power unit at one temporary worksite. Competent worker takes ~45 min for alignment, bolting, and electrical/mechanical connection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy frame manipulation, precise structural alignment, fastening with torque specs, and electrical/power connections. Unstructured temporary worksites, large/heavy components, and mixed mechanical+electrical steps far exceed current humanoid capability.

task: Perform routine vehicle maintenance, such as regulating tire pressure and adding gasoline, oil, and water.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a routine maintenance session on one vehicle: checking/adjusting tire pressure, topping off fuel, oil, and coolant. ~20 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating fluid fill points under a hood, handling a fuel nozzle, operating a tire pressure gauge/inflator, and pouring fluids accurately — all highly unstructured, dexterous, multi-tool tasks far beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Oil moving parts of timepieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = oiling all moving parts of a single timepiece (e.g., a watch or clock). A skilled watchmaker takes ~15–30 min to disassemble, apply micro-drops of oil to pivots/jewels/escapement, and reassemble.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity: applying micro-drops of oil (picoliters) to submillimeter watch jewels and pivots with a specialized oiler. Far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity and precision perception limits.

task: Construct or form molds for use in casting clay or plaster objects, using plaster, fiberglass, rubber, casting machines, patterns, or flasks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing one mold. A competent moldmaker typically spends 60–120 min preparing, pouring, and finishing a single plaster or rubber mold for a clay/plaster object.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise multi-step sequencing: mixing materials, applying release agents, positioning patterns/flasks, pouring, and trimming. High dexterity, tool-switching, wet-material handling, and domain knowledge far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Reassemble timepieces, replacing glass faces and batteries, before returning them to customers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reassembling one timepiece, replacing glass face and battery. A skilled watchmaker typically takes 10–20 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Watchmaking demands extreme fine dexterity: handling tiny screws, press-fitting crystal glass, inserting coin-cell batteries into tight casings. Far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity and precision tolerances; high damage risk.

task: Start aircraft and observe gauges, meters, and other instruments to detect evidence of malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = startup sequence for a single aircraft. A competent pilot/technician runs through checklists, starts engines, and monitors all instruments for anomalies — typically 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating aircraft-specific cockpit controls, interpreting aviation instruments, and executing precise startup procedures. Humanoid robots cannot operate aircraft controls in situ; no commercial deployment exists; extreme safety stakes demand near-perfect reliability.

task: Set charges of explosives to split rock.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling blast holes, placing explosive charges, connecting detonators, and preparing a single rock-splitting blast setup. A competent blaster typically takes ~45–90 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires drilling into rock, precise explosive placement, wiring detonators, and safety compliance in highly unstructured outdoor terrain. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and regulatory/safety autonomy capabilities.

task: Keep bus interiors clean for students.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning the interior of one school bus (sweeping floor, wiping seats/handrails, removing trash). A competent custodian typically takes 20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Confined, cluttered bus interior with narrow aisles, raised/uneven floor steps, varied debris, and overhead handrails challenge mobility and manipulation. Current humanoids lack reliable navigation and cleaning in such tight, non-standard vehicle interiors.

task: Pick up and drop off passengers at regularly scheduled neighborhood locations, following strict time schedules.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one scheduled route/run through neighborhood stops. A typical fixed-route shuttle or paratransit run averages ~60 minutes end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a passenger vehicle in mixed traffic on public roads — humanoid robots cannot do this. L4 AVs exist but are narrow, mapped-route systems, not general-purpose robots. Boarding/alighting assistance adds further barriers.

task: Perform scheduled maintenance, and clean units and components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing scheduled maintenance and cleaning on a single unit/component (e.g., HVAC unit, machinery). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Scheduled maintenance involves varied, multi-step procedures: inspection, fastener torquing, filter replacement, lubrication, and cleaning. Requires tool use, fine dexterity, judgment, and generalization across diverse equipment types — well beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Finish vehicle instrumentation and deinstrumentation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fully instrumenting or deinstrumenting one vehicle (attaching/removing sensors, data loggers, wiring). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min per vehicle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for routing and connecting wiring harnesses, routing cables through tight spaces, securing connectors and fasteners precisely, and interpreting vehicle-specific layouts. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Load or stack filled molds in ovens, dryers, or curing boxes, or on storage racks or carts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading one batch of filled molds onto a rack, cart, or into a curing unit. A competent worker handles this in ~5 minutes including positioning and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Filled molds vary in size, weight, shape, and fragility, demanding precise grasp and placement. Navigating oven interiors, tight rack spacing, and avoiding spillage challenges current robot dexterity, perception, and generalization.

task: Inspect parts for surface defects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single part for surface defects. A competent worker visually examines all surfaces, taking ~2–4 minutes per part.
robot_success_rationale: Frontier humanoids can use cameras for basic visual inspection, but detecting subtle surface defects (scratches, pits, discoloration) across varied part geometries requires fine perception, controlled lighting, and reliable defect classification—still unreliable in unstructured settings.

task: Perform regular adjustment and maintenance on timepieces, watch cases, and watch bands.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full service on a single timepiece (adjustment, case & band maintenance). A competent watchmaker typically spends 30–60 min on routine adjustments and cleaning.
robot_success_rationale: Watchmaking demands extreme fine dexterity (sub-millimeter tolerances), specialized micro-tools, tactile feedback for tension/timing, and expert-level visual inspection of tiny components — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Observe the structure and properties of matter, and the transformation and propagation of energy, using equipment such as masers, lasers, and telescopes, to explore and identify the basic principles governing these phenomena.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single experimental observation session (setup, data collection, preliminary review). A competent physicist typically spends ~2 hours per focused session with specialized equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires abstract scientific reasoning, hypothesis-driven experimentation, expert instrument operation (lasers, masers, telescopes), and interpretation of physical phenomena. Far beyond current robot cognitive and dexterous generalization capabilities.

task: Insert wedges and feathers into holes, and drive wedges with sledgehammers to split stone sections from masses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = splitting one stone section. A skilled quarry worker drills holes, inserts wedges/feathers, and drives wedges repeatedly until the stone cracks — typically 30–60 minutes per split.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise insertion of wedges/feathers into tight drill holes, powerful accurate sledgehammer swings with force calibration, and reading stone crack propagation — all outdoors on rough terrain. Far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity, strength, and perception capabilities.

task: Comply with traffic regulations to operate vehicles in a safe and courteous manner.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one typical local driving trip (~5–10 miles). A competent licensed driver completes this in ~15–25 minutes including parking.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate passenger vehicles in traffic. L4 AVs are narrow, map-dependent systems — not general-purpose robots. Real-time traffic compliance, courtesy, and dynamic judgment remain far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Vacuum and clean interiors and wash and polish exteriors of automobiles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full automobile. Competent detailer takes ~30–60 min to vacuum/clean interior and wash/polish exterior of a typical car.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating around/inside a vehicle, using a vacuum, wash tools, and polisher across complex curved surfaces with tight interior spaces. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, mobility, and generalization for full autonomous car detailing.

task: Transport clients to locations outside the home, such as to physicians' offices or on outings, using a motor vehicle.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one round trip outing (e.g., home to physician's office and back). Typical urban/suburban trip including loading/unloading client is ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate passenger vehicles in traffic. Task also requires assisting/loading a client, navigating public roads, and adapting to unpredictable environments — all well beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Check for corrosion, distortion, and invisible cracks in the fuselage, wings, and tail, using x-ray and magnetic inspection equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full NDI inspection of fuselage, wings, and tail on a single aircraft. A trained NDT technician typically takes 3–5 hours using X-ray and magnetic particle/eddy-current equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized NDT equipment (X-ray emitters, magnetic particle guns), precise positioning on curved aircraft surfaces, interpreting radiographic/magnetic images, navigating around large structures, and meeting strict aerospace quality standards — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Modify aircraft structures, space vehicles, systems, or components, following drawings, schematics, charts, engineering orders, and technical publications.
human_time_rationale: One modification instance (e.g., a single engineering order) on an aircraft/spacecraft structure typically involves planning, accessing the work area, modifying components, and inspecting — averaging ~3 hours for a competent aerospace tech.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting complex technical documents, navigating confined airframe spaces, extreme precision (aerospace tolerances), specialized tooling, and high-stakes decision-making. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Operate metalworking machines to fabricate housings, jigs, fittings, or fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single part (housing, jig, fixture). A competent machinist typically spends ~2 hours setting up and running a metalworking machine for one moderately complex part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized metalworking machines (lathes, mills, grinders), precise tool changes, workpiece fixturing, measurement/inspection, and adaptive decision-making mid-process. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and machine-interfacing capability for this.

task: Examine components of timepieces such as watches, clocks, or chronometers for defects, using loupes or microscopes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting the components of a single timepiece. A skilled watchmaker examines gears, jewels, springs, and escapement parts under magnification, typically taking 10–20 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to handle micro-scale components, precise tool-use (loupe/microscope), and expert visual interpretation of sub-millimeter defects. Current robots lack the dexterity, tactile sensitivity, and domain-specific visual reasoning for watchmaking inspection.

task: Start engines, operate controls, and pilot airplanes to transport passengers, mail, or freight according to flight plans, regulations, and procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one commercial flight segment. Average US domestic flight is ~2.5–3 hours including taxi, takeoff, cruise, and landing procedures.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate aircraft cockpits autonomously in real flight conditions. Requires extreme precision, FAA certification, complex multi-axis control, real-time decision-making, and emergency handling far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Visit people in homes, hospitals, or prisons to provide them with comfort and support.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single visit to one person. A typical pastoral/social visit lasts 45–90 minutes including travel, conversation, and emotional support.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced human empathy, trust-building, and contextual emotional intelligence. Robots lack genuine social/emotional presence; navigating secure facilities (prisons, hospitals) and earning human comfort is far beyond current capability.

task: Bend parts, such as hairsprings, pallets, barrel covers, and bridges, to correct deficiencies in truing or endshake, using tweezers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = bending and adjusting a single watchpart (e.g., hairspring or pallet) until truing/endshake is corrected; a skilled watchmaker typically spends ~5–15 minutes per part.
robot_success_rationale: Watchmaking is ultra-fine dexterous work on sub-millimeter parts requiring precise tactile feedback and force control far beyond current humanoid robot capability. Tweezers manipulation at this scale is essentially impossible for 2026 robots.

task: Collect fares or vouchers from passengers, and make change or issue receipts as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fare collection for one passenger, including change and receipt. A transit worker completes this in roughly 1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a fare machine or cash register, handling varied currency/vouchers, social interaction with passengers, and working in a moving vehicle environment. Current humanoids lack reliable cash handling, receipt printing tool-use, and transit vehicle integration.

task: Change timing weights on balance wheels to correct deficient timing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting timing weights on one balance wheel. A skilled watchmaker/clockmaker needs ~30–60 min to diagnose, access, adjust, and verify timing correction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity on sub-millimeter watch components, precise torque control, and diagnostic interpretation of timing data. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities; comparable to surgical/jewelry-level manipulation.

task: Test vehicle equipment, such as lights, brakes, horns, or windshield wipers, to ensure proper operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing all listed equipment on a single vehicle. A competent inspector systematically checks lights, brakes, horn, and wipers in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires entering/operating a vehicle, pressing pedals, toggling switches, and interpreting results. Humanoids cannot safely drive/operate vehicle controls in traffic; in-bay static checks are partially feasible but generalization across vehicle types is very poor.

task: Move, assemble, and connect hoses or nozzles to material hoppers, storage tanks, conveyor sections or chutes, and pumps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving, assembling, and connecting hoses/nozzles to a single piece of equipment (hopper, tank, conveyor, or pump). A competent worker typically takes 15–25 minutes per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires carrying heavy/bulky hoses, precise alignment of couplings, threading or clamping connections, and navigating cluttered industrial environments. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force feedback, and generalization for reliable fluid-coupling assembly.

task: Vacuum and clean interiors, and wash and polish exteriors of automobiles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fully cleaning one automobile interior (vacuum + wipe) and exterior (wash + polish). A competent detailer takes ~30–60 min for a standard vehicle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating multiple tools (vacuum, wash mitts, polisher) on curved, reflective, large surfaces; navigating around/inside a vehicle; applying consistent pressure for polishing. Far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability at acceptable quality.

task: Fabricate parts for watches and clocks, using small lathes and other machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single watch/clock part (e.g. a gear or arbor) on a lathe, from setup through finishing. A skilled horologist typically takes 30–60 minutes per part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-fine dexterity to operate a watchmaker's lathe with micron-level precision, tool changes, and real-time tactile feedback on sub-millimeter parts. Far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Load, unload, or adjust materials or products on conveyors by hand, by using lifts, hoists, and scoops, or by opening gates, chutes, or hoppers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one load/unload/adjustment cycle at a conveyor station; a worker handles this in ~5 min including positioning items, operating lifts/hoists, or opening gates/chutes.
robot_success_rationale: Task involves varied object sizes/weights, operating lifts/hoists/gates/chutes, and precise placement on moving conveyors. Generalization across tools and unstructured item types is still weak; heavy payloads and gate/chute operation add further challenges for current humanoids.

task: Estimate spaces between collets and first inner coils to determine if spaces are within acceptable limits.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one spring/collet assembly. A skilled worker visually estimates or uses a feeler gauge/caliper to check the gap in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual measurement of sub-millimeter gaps between small mechanical components (collets and inner coils). Current robots lack the precise close-range vision, fine positional understanding, and measurement tool manipulation needed for reliable pass/fail inspection at required tolerances.

task: Weigh, mix, and melt metal alloys or materials needed for jewelry models.
human_time_rationale: One instance = weighing, mixing, and melting a single batch of alloy for a jewelry model; a skilled jeweler typically takes 20–40 min including setup, precise measurement, and melt.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise gram-scale weighing, fine dexterous handling of small metal pieces, operating a jeweler's torch or furnace at high heat, and monitoring melt visually — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, heat-safety limits, and fine tool-use generalization.

task: Dispose of hazardous waste in an appropriate manner.
human_time_rationale: One instance = identifying, packaging, labeling, and delivering one batch of hazardous waste to a designated disposal point; includes PPE donning, container sealing, and transport.
robot_success_rationale: Requires chemical identification, reading labels, selecting correct containers, donning PPE, safe handling of liquids/solids with spill risk, regulatory compliance judgment, and multi-step logistics — far beyond current robot generalization and safety guarantees.

task: Perform laboratory tests on samples collected, such as analyzing the content of contaminated wastewater.
human_time_rationale: One instance = running a standard analysis (e.g., BOD, pH, turbidity, contaminant panel) on a single wastewater sample batch; ~45 min including prep, instrument operation, and logging.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous pipetting, precise reagent handling, operating specialized lab instruments, interpreting results, and adapting to varied protocols. Current humanoids lack the precision, lab-tool integration, and chemical reasoning needed.

task: Clean, rinse, and dry timepiece parts, using solutions and ultrasonic or mechanical watch-cleaning machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a full set of parts for one timepiece. Includes loading parts into baskets, running cleaning/rinse cycles (~10–15 min machine time), and drying — roughly 20 min total hands-on + wait.
robot_success_rationale: Tiny, delicate watch parts demand extreme fine dexterity and precise handling; current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter manipulation, specialized micro-basket loading, and fluid-handling finesse needed. High risk of part loss or damage.

task: Repair or replace defective or worn parts such as bearings, pistons, and gears, using hand tools, torque wrenches, power tools, and welding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/replacing one defective component (e.g., a bearing, piston, or gear). A competent mechanic typically spends 60–120 min per component including diagnosis, disassembly, replacement, and reassembly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force torque wrench use, welding, fine dexterous manipulation of heavy/greasy parts in constrained engine bays, and adaptive problem-solving for worn/seized components — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Observe operation of timepiece parts and subassemblies to determine accuracy of movement, and to diagnose causes of defects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single timepiece movement/subassembly. A skilled watchmaker spends ~10–20 min observing, timing, and diagnosing a movement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme precision visual inspection of micro-scale parts, fine dexterity to handle delicate subassemblies, and expert diagnostic reasoning. 2026 robots lack the micro-vision, fine manipulation, and fault-diagnosis cognition needed.

task: Test performance of electromechanical assemblies, using test instruments such as oscilloscopes, electronic voltmeters, or bridges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single electromechanical assembly end-to-end. A competent technician sets up instruments, connects probes, runs test sequences, and records results in ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous probe placement on varied connectors, interpreting oscilloscope/voltmeter readings, adjusting settings, and making pass/fail judgments. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and instrument-reading perception for unstructured bench work.

task: Perform routine vehicle maintenance, such as regulating tire pressure and adding gasoline, oil, and water.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full routine maintenance check (tires, fuel, oil, coolant) on one vehicle. A competent worker takes ~15–25 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating fuel pumps, unscrewing caps, handling hoses/nozzles, reading dipsticks, and using tire pressure gauges — all fine dexterous tool-use in unstructured outdoor/garage environments. Current humanoids lack reliable generalization for this multi-step fluid/tool workflow.

task: Check to see that trains are equipped with supplies such as fuel, water, and sand.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking all supply levels (fuel, water, sand) on a single train before departure. A competent worker physically inspects gauges, reservoirs, and sandboxes in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating around/under a large train, reading analog/digital gauges in varied lighting, accessing hatches, and interpreting supply levels. Outdoor/unstructured environment, heavy equipment interfaces, and multi-step inspection logic are well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Connect air hoses to cars, using wrenches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting one air hose fitting to a car (e.g., tire valve or pneumatic port) using a wrench. A competent mechanic completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment and torquing of threaded fittings with a wrench — demanding dexterity and force control. Current humanoids struggle with tool-use (wrench grip, rotation), small fittings, and applying calibrated torque reliably.

task: Plan, shop for, or prepare nutritious meals or assist families in planning, shopping for, or preparing nutritious meals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = planning, shopping for, and preparing one meal for a family. Includes ~15 min planning, ~20 min shopping, ~25 min prep/cooking.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step reasoning (nutrition planning), navigation in a grocery store, and cooking in an unstructured kitchen. Each sub-task is individually hard; chaining all three autonomously is beyond current frontier robots.

task: Drive taxicabs or privately owned vehicles to transport passengers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one passenger trip. Median urban taxi/rideshare trip is roughly 20–30 minutes including pickup and dropoff.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot physically operate a standard vehicle in live traffic. L4 AVs exist but are narrow, map-dependent systems — not general-purpose humanoid robots. No current frontier humanoid meets this task.

task: Repair or replace broken, damaged, or worn parts on timepieces, using lathes, drill presses, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing or replacing a single broken/worn part on a timepiece. A skilled watchmaker typically spends 30–90 minutes per part, including diagnosis, machining, and fitting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme sub-millimeter dexterity to handle tiny watch components, operate precision lathes and drill presses, and fit micro-parts. Far beyond current humanoid robot fine-motor and tool-use capabilities. Essentially zero success probability.

task: Copy and distribute classroom materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = copying and distributing one set of materials for a typical classroom (~25–30 students). Includes printing/copying and handing out sheets.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a copier (complex UI, paper loading), then navigating a classroom and distributing to each student. Copier interaction and reliable per-person delivery in a dynamic, crowded room exceed current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Create sketches, profiles, or likenesses of posed subjects or photographs, using any combination of freehand drawing, mechanical assembly kits, and computer imaging.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing one sketch/likeness of a subject. A competent forensic or portrait artist typically spends 30–60 minutes producing an acceptable likeness.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level creative perception, fine motor freehand drawing, and artistic judgment far beyond current robot capability. No frontier humanoid can produce an accepted portrait likeness autonomously; fine dexterous artistry and aesthetic evaluation are unsolved.

task: Examine gems during processing to ensure accuracy of angles and positions of cuts or bores, using magnifying glasses, loupes, or shadowgraphs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single gem during processing. A skilled gemologist spends ~2–4 minutes examining one stone's cut angles and bore positions under magnification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-fine dexterous handling of tiny, reflective gems, precise optical tool use (loupe/shadowgraph), and expert visual interpretation of sub-millimeter cut angles. Current robots lack the micro-dexterity, specialized optics integration, and gemological perceptual judgment needed.

task: Construct and maintain test facilities for aircraft parts and systems, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing or performing a maintenance cycle on a test facility setup. A full build/maintain session for an aircraft parts test rig typically takes a full workday (8 hours).
