robot_success_rationale: Requires reading complex engineering specs, operating heavy/specialized tools, precise structural assembly, electrical/hydraulic integration, and quality verification. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured, high-stakes aerospace environments.

task: Hold stones, gems, dies, or styluses against rotating plates, wheels, saws, or slitters to cut, shape, slit, grind, or polish them.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single stone/gem/die against a rotating tool. A skilled lapidary or gem cutter typically spends ~5–20 minutes per piece depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, precise force feedback, and tactile sensing to hold tiny gems/stones at exact angles against rotating tools without cracking them. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Test aircraft systems under simulated operational conditions, performing systems readiness tests and pre- and post-operational checkouts, to establish design or fabrication parameters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full systems readiness test/checkout cycle for one aircraft. Competent aerospace technician/engineer typically spends 2–4 hours on pre-op, simulated operational tests, and post-op documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized cockpit controls, interpreting multi-system avionics readouts, applying engineering judgment, and interfacing with ground support equipment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, domain-specific tool use, and autonomous reasoning capabilities.

task: Collect samples of materials or products, checking them to ensure conformance to specifications or sending them to laboratories for analysis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a single sample batch from a production line or site, performing a basic conformance check, and/or preparing it for lab submission. Typically 15–30 minutes for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied environments, identifying correct sampling points, using collection tools precisely, labeling, and making conformance judgments. Current robots lack reliable generalization for unstructured sampling contexts, fine tool use, and quality assessment reasoning.

task: Cut, bend, laminate, arrange, and fasten individual or mixed raw and manufactured materials and products to form works of art.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single mixed-media artwork. A competent artist typically spends 1–3 hours cutting, bending, laminating, arranging, and fastening materials for a moderate-complexity piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative judgment, fine dexterity across wildly varied materials (cutting, bending, laminating, fastening), and aesthetic evaluation. Current robots lack artistic intent, generalization across unstructured materials, and the nuanced touch needed for art-quality output.

task: Examine aircraft access plates and doors for security.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full walkaround inspection of all access plates/doors on one aircraft; a competent technician checks latches, fasteners, and seals in ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating uneven tarmac, reaching overhead/undersurface panels, visually identifying subtle security flaws (missing fasteners, improper latching), and handling complex aviation-specific hardware. Current humanoids lack aviation-trained perception, needed reach envelopes, and reliability on outdoor terrain around aircraft.

task: Turn wheels of calipers and examine springs, using loupes, to determine if center coils appear as perfect circles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one spring examined. Skilled inspector adjusts calipers, uses loupe, and judges coil circularity in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to manipulate tiny caliper wheels and position a loupe, plus high-acuity close-up visual inspection for subtle geometric defects. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in precision and perception.

task: Check the condition of a vehicle's tires, brakes, windshield wipers, lights, oil, fuel, water, and safety equipment to ensure that everything is in working order.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full pre-trip vehicle inspection covering 8 systems. A competent driver/mechanic walks around and checks each item; ~20 min is typical for a thorough but routine check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse sensing (visual, tactile, fluid-level reading), mobility under/around a vehicle, operating hood latches and dipsticks, and interpreting varied vehicle formats. Current humanoids lack reliable fluid-level reading, under-vehicle navigation, and multi-system generalization.

task: Sort rough diamonds into categories based on shape, size, color, and quality.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting a single rough diamond. A trained sorter assesses shape, size, color, and quality in ~1–3 minutes per stone using loupes and reference charts.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level visual perception for subtle color/quality grading, fine dexterous manipulation of small irregular gems, and highly specialized domain knowledge. Current robots lack the precision optics, tactile sensitivity, and grading expertise needed.

task: Instruct workers to set warning signals in front and at rear of trains during emergency stops.
human_time_rationale: One instance = instructing crew and deploying warning signals at both ends of a stopped train. A competent worker walks both ends (~300m), places/activates signals. ~10 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating live rail environment, climbing on/off train, traversing long outdoor track (rough terrain, weather), deploying specialized railway signal devices, and issuing verbal crew instructions — far beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Order stock, and price and shelve incoming goods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reviewing inventory levels, placing a stock order via system/phone. A competent worker typically takes 20–40 min per ordering session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating procurement software, supplier portals, or phone calls; making judgment calls on quantities/suppliers. Current humanoids lack the cognitive interface and business-process integration for autonomous ordering.

task: Immerse stones in prescribed chemical solutions to determine specific gravities and key properties of gemstones or substitutes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one gemstone. Human immerses stone in solution, observes behavior/suspension point, records specific gravity reading — typically 5–10 minutes per stone.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-fine dexterity to handle tiny gemstones, precise immersion in chemical solutions, and nuanced visual interpretation of suspension behavior. Current robots lack the fine manipulation and specialized perception for reliable gemological testing.

task: Monitor trains as they go around curves to detect dragging equipment and smoking journal boxes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = observing one train passing a curve checkpoint; a worker watches the full consist (~5–15 min) plus readiness time, ~30 min total per train event.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained outdoor trackside positioning, thermal/visual detection of smoke and dragging equipment on fast-moving trains, and real-time anomaly judgment. Current humanoids lack rail-environment durability, reliable thermal sensing integration, and sustained autonomous vigilance outdoors near moving trains.

task: Replace specified parts to repair malfunctioning timepieces, using watchmakers' tools, loupes, and holding fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and replacing a faulty part in one timepiece. A competent watchmaker typically spends 30–90 minutes depending on complexity; ~60 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Watchmaking demands extreme fine dexterity (sub-millimeter parts), precise loupe-aided vision, force sensitivity, and specialized tool use. Far beyond current humanoid robots' dexterous manipulation capabilities.

task: Move freight, stock, or other materials to and from storage or production areas, loading docks, delivery vehicles, ships, or containers, by hand or using trucks, tractors, or other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving a typical load (pallet or batch of items) from point A to point B within a facility or between dock/vehicle/storage. Median ~20 min including retrieval, transit, and placement.
robot_success_rationale: Highly varied environments, mixed object types, and equipment operation (forklifts, pallet jacks, vehicles) challenge humanoids. Flat indoor pick-and-carry is feasible; outdoor terrain, heavy loads, and vehicle/dock interfaces drop success sharply.

task: Remove grease, dirt, paint, or rust from surfaces in preparation for paint application, using abrasives, solvents, brushes, blowtorches, washing tanks, or sandblasters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one medium-sized surface (e.g., a car panel or metal component). A competent worker using abrasives, solvents, or sandblasting typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool-switching (brushes, sandblasters, blowtorches, solvents), force-controlled scrubbing on varied surfaces, and quality inspection. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use generalization, precise force control, and safety handling of blowtorches/solvents.

task: Demonstrate use of laboratory equipment and enforce laboratory rules.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single lab demonstration/orientation session for a group of students, covering equipment use and rules. ~30 min is typical for a lab intro.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex verbal instruction, social authority, adaptive communication, and nuanced rule enforcement. Robots lack natural language teaching ability, social presence, and situational judgment needed to monitor and correct student behavior reliably.

task: Measure sizes of stones' bore holes and cuts to ensure adherence to specifications, using precision measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring bore holes/cuts on a single stone. A skilled lapidary uses calipers or gauges and checks key dimensions in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to position precision instruments (calipers, gauges) accurately on irregular stone surfaces, interpret readings, and compare to specs. Current robots lack the fine motor control and tactile feedback needed for reliable, accepted-quality results.

task: Raise levers to couple and uncouple cars for makeup and breakup of trains.
human_time_rationale: One instance = coupling or uncoupling one pair of rail cars. A skilled car-knocker typically takes 2–4 minutes including walking to position and verifying the connection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating uneven, outdoor rail yard terrain, locating and grasping a heavy coupler lever under varying orientations/conditions, and timing with moving cars — far beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Provide passengers with assistance entering and exiting trains.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one passenger board or alight a train; typically takes 1–3 minutes including guiding, steadying, and gap/step management.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic balance on moving/uneven platform gaps, physical steadying of unpredictable humans, social cue interpretation, and safe force application — all in crowded, unstructured public environments. Far beyond current humanoid reliability.

task: Pull or push track switches to reroute cars.
human_time_rationale: One instance = throwing one track switch (pull or push lever/handle). A competent switchman completes this in about 1–2 minutes including walking to the switch stand and confirming alignment.
robot_success_rationale: Track switch stands are large, heavy, and require forceful pushing/pulling of a rigid iron lever in outdoor/uneven rail-yard terrain. Humanoids lack the grip strength, foot stability on ballast, and specialized tool familiarity; outdoor rough terrain locomotion and high-force lever operation compound failure risk.

task: Examine gem surfaces and internal structures, using polariscopes, refractometers, microscopes, and other optical instruments, to differentiate between stones, to identify rare specimens, or to detect flaws, defects, or peculiarities affecting gem values.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining one gem stone. A skilled gemologist spends ~10–20 min per stone across multiple optical instruments to characterize it fully.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise manipulation of tiny gems onto specialized optical instruments (polariscopes, refractometers, microscopes), expert interpretation of optical data, and fine dexterity with millimeter-scale objects — all far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Adjust sizes or positioning of timepiece parts to achieve specified fit or function, using calipers, fixtures, and loupes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single timepiece part (e.g., fitting a gear, jewel, or spring) including measuring, repositioning, and verifying fit. A skilled watchmaker takes ~15–35 min per adjustment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter dexterity with loupes, calipers, and tiny fixtures on microscopic parts. Far beyond current humanoid fine-motor capability; even specialized robotic systems struggle with watch-scale manipulation and multi-tool coordination.

task: Align, fit, or assemble component parts, using hand or power tools, fixtures, templates, or microscopes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning/fitting/assembling one component set. Typical bench assembly job takes ~15–30 min depending on complexity; 20 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Structured pick-and-place with jigs is feasible (~40–80%), but fixtures, templates, and especially microscope-guided fine alignment require precision dexterity and visual servoing beyond current humanoid capability. Tool switching adds further complexity.

task: Direct truck drivers backing vehicles into loading bays and cover, uncover, or secure loads for delivery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = directing one truck into a loading bay plus securing/covering the load. Signaling takes 2–5 min; covering/strapping a load takes 5–15 min depending on cargo type.
robot_success_rationale: Task blends outdoor traffic directing (requires clear human-like signaling and spatial judgment) with heavy tarp/strap securing (highly variable, forceful manipulation). Humanoids cannot drive trucks; directing requires dynamic hand-signal communication rarely mastered by current robots. Tarp/bungee/strap work on irregular loads is beyond reliable autonomous capability.

task: Ride atop cars that have been shunted, and turn handwheels to control speeds or stop cars at specified positions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = riding a shunted railcar and adjusting handwheels to stop/position it. A typical car rider operation takes roughly 5–10 minutes per car from push to final positioning.
robot_success_rationale: Requires balancing on a moving railcar in outdoor/uneven conditions, operating specialized handwheels under dynamic loads, and making real-time speed/positioning judgments — far beyond current humanoid robot locomotion, balance, and tool-use capabilities.

task: Shade and fill in sketch outlines and backgrounds, using a variety of media such as water colors, markers, and transparent washes, labeling designated colors when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing shading/fill on a single sketch (e.g., one fashion illustration or design drawing). A competent illustrator typically takes 30–60 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine artistic dexterity with multiple media (watercolor, markers, washes), aesthetic judgment for shading gradients, color selection, and labeling. Far beyond current robot artistic manipulation and creative generalization capabilities.

task: Secure gems or diamonds in holders, chucks, dops, lapidary sticks, or blocks for cutting, polishing, grinding, drilling, or shaping.
human_time_rationale: One instance = securing a single gem/diamond in its holder/dop/chuck. A skilled lapidary takes ~3–7 min to align, seat, and fix one stone securely.
robot_success_rationale: Extremely fine dexterity required: sub-millimeter alignment of tiny, irregular, often transparent stones into precise holders. Current robots lack the tactile sensitivity, vision precision, and tool-use finesse for this jeweler-grade task.

task: Observe tracks from left sides of locomotives to detect obstructions on tracks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one shift or segment of active track observation from the left side of a locomotive cab during a typical train run (~1 hour of active scanning).
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating aboard a moving locomotive (vehicle access), sustained outdoor perception across varied lighting/weather, and real-time hazard judgment. Humanoid robots cannot operate locomotive controls or safely ride/mount in cab positions autonomously. Vision-only track inspection is a narrow specialized system, not a general-purpose humanoid task.

task: Teach students, dancers, and other performers about rhythm and interpretive movement.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one class/session teaching rhythm and interpretive movement; typical dance/movement class runs ~60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expressive, nuanced full-body movement, real-time adaptive instruction, emotional communication, and musical interpretation. Current humanoids lack expressive pedagogy, fluid artistic motion, and social teaching capability.

task: Pack containers and re-pack damaged containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = packing or re-packing one container (box/crate). A competent worker selects items, arranges, cushions, seals, and labels in ~8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place items but struggle with irregular/damaged containers, adaptive packing judgment, cushioning materials, and re-packing varied goods without structured environments. Generalization and dexterity remain limiting.

task: Cover or pad metal or plastic structures or devices, using coverings such as rubber, leather, felt, plastic, or fiberglass.
human_time_rationale: One instance = covering/padding a single metal or plastic structure (e.g., a handle, frame, or panel). A skilled worker fits, trims, and secures the covering material in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise multi-material handling, cutting, stretching, and conforming soft/irregular materials (rubber, leather, felt) onto varied 3D structures. Current robots lack the dexterous fine-motor control and adaptive force feedback needed for consistent quality results.

task: Assemble and install components of timepieces to complete mechanisms, using watchmakers' tools and loupes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and installing one complete timepiece movement. A competent watchmaker typically spends 1–3 hours depending on complexity; 2 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme sub-millimeter dexterity to handle hairsprings, jewels, and escape wheels with loupes. Current humanoid robots lack the fine-motor precision, tactile feedback, and tool-use capability needed for micro-scale watchmaking assembly.

task: Conduct brake tests to determine the condition of brakes on trains.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full brake test on a single train consist (initial terminal test + continuity check). A competent inspector typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating locomotive cab controls, walking the entire train length on ballast/uneven terrain, coupling air hoses, reading gauges, and applying expert judgment. No humanoid robot can operate train controls or navigate rail yards autonomously at this standard.

task: Bend inner coils of springs away from or toward collets, using tweezers, to locate centers of collets in centers of springs, and to correct errors resulting from faulty colleting of coils.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting one spring-collet assembly. A skilled watchmaking/spring technician can inspect and correct a single spring's centering in ~2–4 minutes using tweezers.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity with tweezers on sub-millimeter watch spring coils, precise tactile feedback, and micro-visual inspection. Far beyond current humanoid robot fine manipulation capabilities.

task: Select shaping wheels for tasks, and mix and apply abrasives, bort, or polishing compounds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting and mounting a shaping wheel then mixing and applying abrasive/polishing compound for one workpiece setup; a skilled worker takes ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level tactile judgment to select the correct wheel grade, precise fine motor control for mixing compounds to correct consistency, and tool/fixture manipulation—well beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Examine diamonds or gems to ascertain the shape, cut, and width of cut stones, or to select the cuts that will result in the biggest, best quality stones.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining a single stone to assess shape, cut, and width or plan optimal cut. A skilled gemologist/cutter typically spends 10–20 minutes per stone.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level optical inspection (loupe/microscope), fine haptic sensing, and deep gemological knowledge to plan cuts. Current robots lack gem-grade visual precision, specialized tool integration, and domain expertise for autonomous quality judgments.

task: Induce changes in composition of substances by introducing heat, light, energy, or chemical catalysts for quantitative or qualitative analysis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one analytical procedure (e.g., a single titration, combustion, or photolysis run). Setup, reaction, monitoring, and recording results typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting, chemical handling, reading analog/digital instruments, adjusting lab equipment, and interpreting results. Fine dexterity, chemical safety awareness, and adaptive judgment far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Provide entertainment at special events by performing activities such as drawing cartoons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one cartoon drawn for a guest at a special event. A competent caricature/cartoon artist typically spends 5–15 minutes per subject.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous drawing, artistic creativity, social interaction, real-time facial interpretation, and crowd engagement — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured event settings.

task: Position balls of clay in centers of potters' wheels, and start motors or pump treadles with feet to revolve wheels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = centering one clay ball on a wheel and starting it spinning. A skilled potter does this in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual force-sensitive centering of deformable clay (very hard for robots), foot-operated treadle control, and coordinated whole-body posture. Current robots lack the fine haptic feedback and compliance needed.

task: Tighten or replace loose jewels, using watchmakers' tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tightening or replacing one loose jewel in a watch movement. A skilled watchmaker takes ~10–20 min including inspection, tool selection, and re-seating/replacing the jewel.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter dexterity with tiny specialized tools on micro-scale watch components. Far beyond current humanoid robots' manipulation precision, force sensing, and tool-use capability. Effectively impossible autonomously in 2026.

task: Design clay forms and molds, and decorations for forms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and producing one clay form/mold with decorations. A skilled ceramicist typically spends 60–120 minutes on a single piece from concept to finished raw form.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative design judgment, sustained ultra-fine dexterous manipulation of soft deformable clay, and artistic decoration. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control, force sensitivity, and generative creativity needed for ceramics work.

task: Operate lifting or moving devices to move equipment or materials to access areas to be painted.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning a lift or moving device to grant access to a paint area. A competent operator typically takes 15–25 minutes to set up, operate, and secure the equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating forklifts, boom lifts, or scissor lifts — heavy vehicle-class equipment a humanoid cannot drive or safely control. No current general-purpose robot has demonstrated this autonomously in real environments.

task: Collaborate with engineers, mechanics, and other technical experts as necessary to build and install creations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single collaborative build/install session. Competent workers typically spend 1–3 hours coordinating, assembling, and installing a moderately complex creation with a team.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level communication, negotiation, and coordination with human experts; adaptive problem-solving in unstructured environments; and operating specialized tools. Far beyond current robot generalization and social collaboration capabilities.

task: Inspect couplings, air hoses, journal boxes, and handbrakes to ensure that they are securely fastened and functioning properly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one railcar's couplings, air hoses, journal boxes, and handbrakes. A competent car inspector (carman) walks around one car checking all components, typically 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating uneven rail yard terrain, crawling/bending under cars, identifying and functionally testing specialized railroad components. Highly unstructured, dirty environment; current humanoids lack rail-specific perception, dexterity, and mobility for this domain.

task: Activate fuel pumps and fill fuel tanks of vehicles with gasoline or diesel fuel to specified levels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fueling one vehicle to the specified level; includes selecting fuel type, inserting nozzle, filling to target, and replacing cap. Typical fill takes 5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise nozzle insertion into a small fuel port, gripping a heavy hose under tension, reading pump controls, and handling flammable fluids safely. Dexterity and tool-use demands are high; no commercial deployment exists for this task.

task: Test cooked food by tasting and smelling it to ensure palatability and flavor conformity.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tasting and smelling a single dish or batch. A cook takes a small sample, evaluates flavor, aroma, and palatability, and forms a judgment — typically 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires biological taste and olfaction — senses current humanoid robots entirely lack. No frontier robot has chemical taste/smell sensors integrated for food QA. Physical sampling gesture is also extremely delicate and unsafe.

task: Signal crew members or deckhands to rig tow lines, open or close gates or ramps, or pull guard chains across entries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = signaling crew for a single docking/departure operation (rigging tow lines, opening/closing gates/ramps, pulling chains). Typically takes ~5 minutes per maneuver.
robot_success_rationale: Requires maritime-specific communication (hand signals, radio, verbal), coordinating with human crew, situational awareness on a dynamic vessel deck, and real-time judgment. Current humanoids lack maritime mobility, crew-coordination protocols, and context-appropriate signaling ability.

task: Operate locomotives in emergency situations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = handling a single emergency event (e.g., brake failure, track obstruction) from detection to resolution; a trained engineer typically resolves this in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate locomotive cabs designed for humans under dynamic, high-stakes emergency conditions. Requires specialized controls, real-time judgment, rail-system knowledge, and coordination with dispatch. No current robot platform is remotely capable.

task: Make minor repairs to couplings, air hoses, and journal boxes, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing one component (coupling, air hose, or journal box). A competent rail/mechanical worker typically spends ~20–30 min diagnosing and fixing a minor defect with hand tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool-use (wrenches, pliers) on heavy, irregular railroad components in outdoor/industrial settings. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and domain-specific generalization for reliable autonomous repair at acceptable quality.

task: Create and prepare sketches and model drawings of cartoon characters, providing details from memory, live models, manufactured products, or reference materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one finished character sketch/model drawing. A competent animator/illustrator typically spends 30–60 min on a clean, detailed character sketch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative ideation, fine artistic dexterity with drawing tools, visual memory, and aesthetic judgment. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control, creativity, and drawing-tool manipulation needed for acceptable artistic output.

task: Fabricate and install parts and systems to be tested in test equipment, using hand tools, power tools, and test instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating and installing a set of parts/systems into test equipment. A competent technician typically takes 60–120 minutes per setup, depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step fabrication, precise tool selection, fine dexterity with hand/power tools, and interpreting test instruments — far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured, variable workbench environments.

task: Smooth surfaces of finished pieces, using rubber scrapers and wet sponges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = smoothing the surface of one finished piece (e.g., a ceramic or clay item) using a scraper and wet sponge; a skilled worker completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced tactile feedback to apply consistent, gentle pressure across irregular 3D surfaces without damaging the piece. Current robots lack fine wet-tool compliance, adaptive force control, and surface-quality perception needed for acceptable results.

task: Experiment with different types of dancers, steps, dances, and placements, testing ideas informally to get feedback from dancers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single informal rehearsal/experiment session trying variations with a group of dancers; typically 60–120 min for a choreographer to test ideas and gather feedback.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative choreographic ideation, nuanced social communication, reading subtle dancer feedback, aesthetic judgment, and directing humans — far beyond current robot cognition, social intelligence, or creative generalization.

task: Conduct safety drills such as man overboard or fire drills.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full safety drill (e.g., man overboard or fire drill) aboard a vessel, including briefing, execution, and debrief. Typical maritime drills run 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinating a crew, issuing verbal commands, operating marine-specific equipment (life rings, fire hoses, alarms), navigating a ship's complex and hazardous environment, and making real-time safety decisions — far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Clean and lubricate timepiece parts and assemblies, using solvents, buff sticks, and oil.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and lubricating parts of a single timepiece movement. A skilled watchmaker spends ~30–60 min on ultrasonic cleaning, drying, and precision oiling of all components.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity: handling sub-millimeter watch parts, applying microscopic oil droplets to jewels, and using buff sticks precisely. Far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity and tactile feedback capabilities.

task: Raise and shape clay into wares, such as vases and pitchers, on revolving wheels, using hands, fingers, and thumbs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = throwing a single piece (e.g., a vase or pitcher) on the wheel, from centering clay to lifting off a finished form; a competent potter typically takes 15–35 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Wheel-throwing requires extraordinarily nuanced bimanual force/torque feedback through wet clay — a highly deformable, dynamic material. No current humanoid has the fine dexterity, tactile sensitivity, or learned clay-specific motor control to replicate this reliably.

task: Change aircraft oil, coolant, or other fluids.
human_time_rationale: One instance = changing one fluid type on one aircraft (e.g., engine oil). Draining, replacing filter, refilling, and inspecting takes a competent A&P mechanic ~45–90 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating around aircraft, locating aircraft-specific drain ports, operating safety-critical fasteners, handling fluid systems, and meeting strict aviation maintenance standards. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Operate and adjust controls of heating equipment to melt material or to cure, dry, or bake filled molds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one heating cycle (setup, monitoring, and adjustment) for a batch of molds/material; typically 20–40 minutes depending on material and equipment type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial heating controls, reading gauges/displays, and making nuanced adjustments. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use with industrial control panels, fine-tuned process judgment, and safe operation near high-heat equipment.

task: Administer first aid according to prescribed procedures and notify emergency medical personnel when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one first-aid event (e.g., treating a wound, responding to a faint) from assessment to stabilization or EMS handoff; ~10–20 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-stakes clinical judgment, fine dexterity (wound dressing, CPR depth/rate), situational assessment, and reliable EMS communication — well beyond current humanoid autonomy and safety thresholds.

task: Press thumbs into centers of revolving clay to form hollows, and press on the inside and outside of emerging clay cylinders with hands and fingers, gradually raising and shaping clay to desired forms and sizes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = throwing one clay vessel on the wheel, from centering to rough final form. A competent potter typically takes 15–30 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Wheel-throwing demands extreme bilateral force sensitivity, continuous haptic feedback, and nuanced micro-adjustments against spinning wet clay — far beyond current robot dexterity, compliance control, and tactile sensing capabilities.

task: Demagnetize mechanisms, using demagnetizing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = demagnetizing a single mechanism. A worker picks up the part, passes it through/over the demagnetizer, and verifies. Typically takes 1–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can grasp and move parts, but operating a specific demagnetizer (switches, positioning protocol, verification) requires tool-use generalization and precise part handling. No reliable feedback loop for magnetization verification.

task: Inspect new, repaired, or modified aircraft to identify damage or defects and to assess airworthiness and conformance to standards, using checklists, hand tools, and test instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full aircraft inspection (e.g., 100-hour or post-repair check). A competent A&P mechanic typically spends 1–3 hours per inspection cycle depending on aircraft size and scope.
robot_success_rationale: Requires FAA-certificated judgment, fine dexterity with specialized tools, access to tight airframe spaces, and regulatory sign-off authority. Current humanoids lack aviation domain knowledge, certification, and reliable fine-tool manipulation in complex confined environments.

task: Collect and dissect animal specimens and examine specimens under microscope.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting, dissecting one specimen, and examining slides under microscope. A competent biologist/lab tech takes ~45 min end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for dissection (scalpel, forceps on soft tissue), precise microscope operation/focusing, and specimen handling. Far beyond current humanoid robots' dexterous manipulation and tool-use capabilities.

task: Prepare and distribute leaflets, pamphlets, and visual aids for educational and informational purposes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a batch of materials (collating, folding, stacking) and distributing to a defined area/group. A competent worker takes ~45 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied public spaces, socially handing leaflets to people, handling thin/floppy paper reliably, and adapting to human interaction — all well beyond current humanoid generalization and social perception.

task: Examine and adjust hairspring assemblies to ensure horizontal and circular alignment of hairsprings, using calipers, loupes, and watchmakers' tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single hairspring assembly. A skilled watchmaker spends ~20–30 min inspecting and correcting horizontal/circular alignment under magnification with fine tools.
robot_success_rationale: Hairsprings are sub-millimeter, ultra-delicate coils requiring extreme fine dexterity and tactile sensitivity far beyond any 2026 general-purpose robot. Perception, force control, and tool use at this scale are entirely out of reach.

task: Teach pottery classes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pottery class session. A typical beginner/intermediate pottery class runs ~90 minutes including instruction, demos, and hands-on guidance.
robot_success_rationale: Teaching requires natural language instruction, adaptive social interaction, real-time student feedback, and complex dexterous demos on a pottery wheel. Current humanoids lack conversational pedagogy, nuanced tactile demonstration, and classroom management capabilities entirely.

task: Greet guests, escort them to their seats, and present them with menus and wine lists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = greeting one party, walking them to their table, and handing out menus/wine lists. Typically 2–5 minutes in a restaurant setting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires natural language social interaction, navigating a dynamic crowded restaurant, pulling out chairs, and gracefully handing documents — all while managing unpredictable guest behavior. Current humanoids lack reliable social fluency, safe crowd navigation, and polished guest-facing dexterity.

task: Create finished art work as decoration, or to elucidate or substitute for spoken or written messages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one finished artwork (e.g., a painting, illustration, or decorative piece). A competent artist typically spends 1–3 hours on a single moderate-complexity piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level creative intent, fine dexterous tool use (brush, pen, chisel), aesthetic judgment, and open-ended composition. Current humanoid robots lack creative cognition and the fine motor precision for expressive art-making.

task: Prepare test solutions, compounds, or reagents for laboratory personnel to conduct tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single solution/reagent batch. A competent lab tech spends ~15–25 min measuring, mixing, labeling, and verifying one preparation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting, handling fragile glassware, reading measurements, operating balances/centrifuges, and following variable protocols. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot dexterity and lab-environment generalization.

task: Render drawings, illustrations, and sketches of buildings, manufactured products, or models, working from sketches, blueprints, memory, models, or reference materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one completed technical or artistic rendering of a building/product. A competent drafter/illustrator typically takes 1–2 hours depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine artistic/technical drawing skill, creative interpretation of references, and precise pen/stylus control. Current humanoid robots lack the dexterity, cognitive creativity, and spatial reasoning needed for acceptable-quality freehand or technical illustration.

task: Pick up or meet passengers according to requests, appointments, or schedules.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one passenger pickup trip (navigating to location, waiting, greeting passenger). Typical rideshare/taxi pickup averages ~15–25 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot drive vehicles in traffic. L4 AVs handle driving but aren't general-purpose humanoids. Task also requires social interaction, schedule interpretation, and real-world navigation — all beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Measure depths of water, using depth-measuring equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single depth measurement at one location: deploy equipment, take reading, record result. Typical field survey stop.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating near/on water, deploying specialized equipment (sonar, lead line, echo sounder), and recording data. Humanoids lack waterproofing, stable aquatic platforms, and reliable tool-use in dynamic outdoor marine environments.

task: Clean, sterilize, and maintain equipment, machinery, and work stations, using hand tools, shovels, brooms, chemicals, hoses, and lubricants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning, sterilizing, and maintaining a single work station or piece of equipment; a competent worker typically takes 30–60 min covering all steps.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-tool use (broom, hose, chemical application, lubrication), varied surfaces, and sterilization verification. Current humanoids handle simple wiping/sweeping but lack reliable tool-switching, chemical handling, and maintenance judgment needed here.

task: Insert waterproof sealers, bullets, and/or powder charges into guns, and screw gun ports back into place.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading one gun with sealer, bullet/powder charge, and screwing the port back in. A competent armorer completes this in roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise insertion of small components (bullets, powder, sealers) into a gun port and screwing parts back in — fine dexterity, tight tolerances, and controlled torque. Beyond current frontier humanoid capabilities reliably.

task: Test and replace batteries and other electronic components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing and replacing one battery or electronic component (e.g., in a consumer device). Includes locating, testing with a meter/tester, and swapping the part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle small components, use of test probes/meters, and identifying correct parts. Current humanoids lack reliable precision for small electronics manipulation and tool use in unstructured settings.

task: Install electrical or electronic parts and hardware in housings or assemblies, using soldering equipment and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing parts into a single housing/assembly. A competent technician spends ~45 min routing wires, placing components, soldering joints, and fastening hardware.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for soldering (sub-mm precision), wire routing, and component placement in confined spaces. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-motor control and tool-use precision for quality solder joints. High failure risk.

task: Tow and maneuver barges or signal tugboats to tow barges to destinations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one barge towing trip/maneuver from origin to destination, typically a few hours of navigation, docking, and signaling on inland waterways.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating marine vessels or signaling on open water — humanoid robots cannot pilot tugboats or barges. Extreme environmental variability, maritime controls, and safety demands make autonomous success essentially impossible with current general-purpose robots.

task: Steer and operate vessels, using radios, depth finders, radars, lights, buoys, or lighthouses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one typical harbor-to-harbor voyage or watch shift (~3 hours), using navigation instruments, radio comms, and visual aids throughout.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate vessel helm controls, interpret dynamic maritime navigation instruments, manage radio comms, and react to sea conditions autonomously. No 2026 general-purpose robot has demonstrated maritime vessel operation; requires highly specialized control interfaces and situational judgment.

task: Conduct in-flight tests and evaluations at specified altitudes and in all types of weather to determine the receptivity and other characteristics of equipment and systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single test flight mission, including pre-flight setup, flight at specified altitudes/conditions, and post-flight logging. Typical test flights run 2–4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires piloting an aircraft, operating specialized avionics test equipment, and exercising expert judgment across dynamic weather/altitude conditions. Humanoid robots cannot operate aircraft controls or function in open airborne environments.

task: Mix paints to match color specifications or original colors, stirring or thinning paints, using spatulas or power mixing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing a single batch of paint to match a color spec. Includes reading spec, selecting pigments, mixing/thinning, and verifying color match; ~10–20 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise colorimetric perception, fine-motor pouring/stirring, tool-switching (spatulas to power mixers), and iterative color judgment. Current robots lack reliable color-matching feedback loops and nuanced fluid-handling dexterity needed for acceptable quality.

task: Move pieces from wheels so that they can dry.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing freshly thrown/trimmed pottery pieces from a potter's wheel and placing them on a drying rack or board. Typically takes 2–4 minutes per wheel/batch.
robot_success_rationale: Wet clay pieces are extremely fragile, irregular in shape, and easily deformed. Grasping without crushing or distorting requires highly sensitive, compliant manipulation that current robots lack. Perception of wet clay geometry is also poor.

task: Use materials such as pens and ink, watercolors, charcoal, oil, or computer software to create artwork.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one finished artwork. A competent artist typically spends 1–3 hours on a single piece depending on medium and complexity; 120 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Creating aesthetically acceptable, intentional artwork requires creative vision, fine motor control for varied media, and artistic judgment. Current humanoids lack generative artistic intent, nuanced pressure/stroke control, and multi-medium dexterity needed for quality output a supervisor would accept.

task: Inspect aircraft components to locate cracks, breaks, leaks, or other problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a set of aircraft components (e.g., one zone/system during a line inspection). A competent A&P mechanic typically spends 30–60 min on a focused component inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level visual/tactile perception of subtle defects (micro-cracks, leaks) in complex, varied geometries; navigation around confined aircraft structures; regulatory-grade reliability. Current robots lack the certified perception accuracy and confined-space dexterity needed.

task: Prepare collections of preserved specimens or microscopic slides for species identification and study of development or disease.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single preserved specimen or microscopic slide. A competent lab technician/biologist spends ~30–60 min on fixation, sectioning, staining, mounting, and labeling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (microtome sectioning, coverslip mounting, staining pipetting), precise chemical handling, and expert judgment on specimen quality. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capability; even specialized lab robots struggle with full workflow.

task: Perform some food preparation or service tasks, such as cooking, clearing tables, and serving food and drinks when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a simple meal or dish (e.g., a short-order cook item). Competent worker completes in ~30 min including prep, cooking, and plating.
robot_success_rationale: Cooking requires fine dexterity, heat/tool management, and adaptive decision-making in cluttered kitchens. Current humanoids lack reliable manipulation for cutting, stirring, and stove operation at acceptable quality.

task: Inspect specified areas to ensure the presence of fire prevention equipment, safety equipment, or first-aid supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one designated area/zone. A safety officer walks the space, visually verifies equipment presence, condition, and tags in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can navigate and visually detect labeled objects (extinguishers, AEDs, first-aid kits) in structured environments, but struggles with reading expiry tags, checking seal integrity, verifying contents, and handling cluttered/varied layouts reliably.

task: Refuel and lubricate engines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = refueling and lubricating a single engine (e.g., industrial/vehicle engine). Includes accessing fuel/oil points, filling to spec, and checking levels — ~15–25 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (handling fuel nozzles, oil caps, dipsticks), navigation to equipment, fluid management without spills, and safety-critical precision. Current humanoids lack reliable fluid-handling and unstructured engine-bay manipulation capability.

task: Empty aircraft lavatory systems or refill them with sanitizer fluid.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing all lavatories on one aircraft; emptying waste tank and refilling sanitizer fluid, typically 15–25 minutes for a ground crew member.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized lavatory service trucks/carts, connecting large hoses under an aircraft in an outdoor tarmac environment, managing pressurized waste/fluid systems — well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in mobility, tool-use, and generalization.

task: Move trailing electrical cables clear of obstructions, using rubber safety gloves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing a single cable or cable run from one obstruction. A competent worker locates, grips, and repositions the cable safely in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with detecting and grasping flexible/deformable cables, donning safety gloves, and navigating cluttered environments with trailing cables. Cable manipulation and PPE requirements push success very low with current systems.

task: Inspect work areas or operating equipment to ensure conformance to established standards in areas such as cleanliness or maintenance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single work area or piece of equipment. A competent worker walks through, checks key standards, and documents findings in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate and capture visual data, but reliably judging cleanliness, subtle maintenance faults, or contextual standard conformance across unstructured environments requires nuanced perception and domain knowledge current systems lack. Pass/fail judgment quality is inconsistent.

task: Load baggage or cargo for crew or passengers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading baggage/cargo for a single flight or vehicle. A ramp agent typically spends 30–60 min loading varied bags and cargo into a hold.
robot_success_rationale: Highly unstructured environment: outdoor tarmac, varied bag sizes/weights, tight cargo-hold geometry, conveyor belt use, and stacking coordination. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor mobility, heavy-load handling, and spatial reasoning for dense packing.

task: Start diesel engines to warm engines before runs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting one diesel engine and allowing it to warm up to operating temperature; a competent operator takes ~2 min to start plus ~3 min idle warm-up.
robot_success_rationale: Starting diesel engines requires entering a cab or reaching vehicle-specific controls, inserting/turning a key or pressing ignition, and monitoring gauges — all highly vehicle-specific. Humanoid robots cannot reliably operate vehicle controls in-cab autonomously in 2026.

task: Position deflector bars, gates, chutes, or spouts to divert flow of materials from one conveyor onto another conveyor.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repositioning one deflector/gate/chute/spout to redirect flow. A trained worker locates the device, adjusts it to the correct angle, and confirms diversion in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating industrial floor, identifying the correct mechanism, and applying precise force/angle adjustments to levers, handles, or bolted chutes. Generalization across varied hardware designs and confirmation of correct diversion is beyond reliable 2026 capability.

task: Brush or spray protective or decorative finishes on completed background panels, informational legends, exhibit accessories, or finished paintings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying a finish coat to a single panel or exhibit piece. A competent worker typically spends 15–35 min per piece including prep, application, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires even, controlled brush/spray strokes, pressure regulation, edge avoidance, and surface-quality judgment on irregular exhibit pieces. Current robots lack the fine dexterity, consistent force control, and visual quality assessment needed for acceptable finish work.

task: Supply, operate, or maintain personal protective equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = supplying/fitting/inspecting PPE for one worker before a shift; includes retrieval, fitting check, and logging. ~10 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Retrieving and handing over PPE items is feasible, but fitting a helmet/harness on a person, inspecting condition, and adjusting straps requires fine dexterity, human interaction, and safety judgment — well beyond reliable 2026 capability.

task: Model substances such as clay or wax, using fingers and small hand tools to form objects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = forming a single small sculpted object from clay/wax. A competent sculptor typically spends 30–60 minutes on a small piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly nuanced bimanual fine motor control, precise force modulation on deformable materials, and artistic intent/judgment. Current robots lack the dexterity, tactile feedback, and generative creativity needed for free-form sculpting.

task: Prepare or calibrate equipment used to collect or analyze samples.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing/calibrating a single piece of equipment (e.g., a pH meter, spectrophotometer, or pipette). A competent technician typically takes 15–30 minutes per device.
robot_success_rationale: Calibration requires precise manipulation of small controls/knobs, reading displays, interpreting instrument-specific software, and making judgment-based adjustments. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, domain-specific tool-use, and sensor interpretation needed for lab instrument calibration.

task: Adjust wheel speeds according to the feel of the clay as pieces enlarge and walls become thinner.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one adjustment event during wheel-throwing. A skilled potter reads clay feel and tweaks wheel speed in ~1–5 minutes per piece enlargement phase.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced haptic feedback through wet clay, real-time force/texture interpretation, and fine bilateral hand control on a spinning wheel. No current humanoid has the tactile sensitivity or closed-loop haptic dexterity for this.

task: Pump water to clear machinery pipelines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = flushing/clearing a single pipeline section: connecting hoses, activating pump, monitoring flow, and confirming clearance. Typically 15–25 minutes for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating industrial pump controls, connecting hoses with varied fittings, monitoring gauges, and making judgment calls on flow/pressure. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use for industrial fluid systems and contextual fault diagnosis.

task: Observe train signals along routes and verify their meanings for engineers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = observing and verifying signals along a single route segment (~20–40 min trip). A railroad employee checks signal aspects and confirms meanings per operating rules.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating aboard or alongside a moving train on active rail infrastructure, interpreting domain-specific signal aspects in varied lighting/weather, and communicating findings to engineers — well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in dynamic, safety-critical rail environments.

task: Apply de-icing fluid to aircraft from baskets lifted by truck-mounted cranes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = de-icing a single aircraft. A competent crew typically takes 10–30 minutes depending on aircraft size, ice accumulation, and fluid application passes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a specialized truck-mounted crane on an active ramp, operating the crane/basket, and directing a high-pressure hose across large irregular surfaces. Humanoids cannot drive or operate heavy specialized vehicles; no current system handles this multi-machine workflow autonomously.

task: Regulate heating, lighting, and ventilation systems for student comfort.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking and adjusting HVAC, lighting, and ventilation controls for a single room or zone; a competent facilities worker takes ~15 min per zone.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read sensors and move to panels but struggle with interacting with arbitrary thermostats, switches, and damper controls in varied layouts. Generalization across building systems is poor; software integration adds complexity.

task: Check temperatures and humidities of shipping and storage areas to ensure that they are at appropriate levels to protect cargo.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking temperature and humidity across a single shipping/storage area. A competent worker walks the area, reads sensors/gauges, and logs readings — ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can navigate flat indoor warehouse terrain and read digital displays moderately well, but reading varied analog gauges, locating sensors in cluttered aisles, and logging/interpreting results reliably remains challenging. Perception and generalization limit consistency.

task: Refill aircraft potable water tanks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = refilling the potable water tanks on a single aircraft. Includes connecting water cart hose, filling to required level, disconnecting, and logging. Typically 20–30 minutes for a competent ground crew member.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating outdoor ramp environment, locating aircraft-specific water service panel, coupling a pressurized hose fitting precisely, monitoring fill levels, and operating a water cart — all highly unstructured, outdoors, and equipment-dependent. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Adjust pressures, temperatures, and trimming tool settings as required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single adjustment cycle across pressures, temperatures, and trimming tool settings on one machine/workstation; a competent technician typically takes ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting process feedback, and operating varied knobs/valves/tool controls. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dial manipulation, process-context understanding, and multi-parameter decision-making needed here.

task: Oil, lubricate, and adjust conveyors, crushers, and other equipment, using hand tools and lubricating equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing a single piece of equipment (conveyor or crusher). A competent maintenance worker typically takes 30–60 min to inspect, lubricate, and adjust one machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating industrial environments, identifying lubrication points on varied heavy machinery, coupling grease guns/oil cans precisely, and making mechanical adjustments with hand tools — all with high dexterity and generalization demands far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Test and charge batteries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing and placing one battery on a charger. Connecting leads, reading a tester, and placing on charger takes ~15 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise connector mating, reading displays, and interpreting multi-step test results. Current humanoids struggle with small connector insertion, reading analog/digital meters reliably, and adapting to varied battery form factors.

task: Estimate sizes and characteristics of fires, and report findings to base camps by radio or telephone.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating a fire, assessing its size/behavior/characteristics, and radioing a full report to base camp. A trained fire lookout or scout typically takes ~30–60 min per report cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/rough-terrain mobility, smoke/fire perception, expert size estimation judgment, and voice radio communication. No current humanoid robot can reliably navigate wildland terrain, assess fire behavior, and autonomously transmit structured radio reports.

task: Dock or undock vessels, sometimes maneuvering through narrow spaces, such as locks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one docking or undocking event; competent mariner takes ~20–30 min including approach, line work, and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires piloting a marine vessel through dynamic water conditions — entirely outside humanoid robot capability. No current general-purpose robot can operate vessel controls, handle mooring lines at scale, or navigate locks autonomously.

task: Extinguish smaller fires with portable extinguishers, shovels, and axes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = extinguishing a single small fire; a competent worker locates, approaches, selects tool, and suppresses the fire, typically within 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-tool use (extinguisher, shovel, axe), dynamic fire perception, rough/outdoor terrain navigation, and fast adaptive decision-making under heat/smoke. 2026 humanoids lack reliable tool-switching, fire localization, and safety-critical autonomy in unstructured hazardous environments.

task: Pull wires through bases of articles and wheels to separate finished pieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pulling a wire through the base/wheel of a single article to separate it. A skilled worker does this quickly, ~1–3 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise thread-like wire manipulation through small, constrained openings — extreme fine dexterity, tactile feedback, and compliance control well beyond current humanoid capability. Perception of wire-hole alignment is also challenging.

task: Inspect materials, stock, vehicles, equipment, or facilities to ensure that they are safe, free of defects, and consistent with specifications.
human_time_rationale: One inspection instance covers a typical batch of materials, a vehicle, or a facility area; a competent inspector averages ~30 min per unit/area checked.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can visually scan surfaces and flag obvious defects, but generalizing across varied assets (vehicles, facilities, equipment), interpreting specs, navigating complex environments, and making nuanced pass/fail judgments remain well beyond reliable autonomous capability in 2026.

task: Climb ladders to tops of cars to set brakes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = climbing a ladder to the top of one railcar and setting its hand brake. A competent railroad worker completes this in ~2–4 minutes per car.
robot_success_rationale: Climbing a vertical railcar ladder onto a curved, elevated, outdoor surface is extremely challenging for current humanoids. High fall risk, poor generalization to ladder rungs, outdoor/unstructured terrain, and fine brake-wheel manipulation combine to make autonomous success near-zero.

task: Examine and inventory firefighting equipment, such as axes, fire hoses, shovels, pumps, buckets, and fire extinguishers, to determine amount and condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full inventory check of a typical equipment cache. A competent firefighter inspects, counts, and notes condition of all items in ~30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating cluttered storage, identifying diverse equipment types, assessing condition (damage, pressure gauges, seals), and logging results. Vision-based inventory is improving, but nuanced condition assessment, varied object recognition, and autonomous logging remain unreliable in 2026.

task: Advance machines to gather material and convey it into cars.
human_time_rationale: One instance = operating mining/excavation machinery to gather bulk material and load it into rail cars or haul trucks; a typical loading cycle takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large specialized machinery (conveyor systems, loaders, shuttle cars) in dynamic outdoor/underground environments. Humanoids cannot operate such equipment; even autonomous mining systems are task-specific, not general-purpose robots.

task: Move levers to position dredges for excavation, to engage hydraulic pumps, to raise and lower suction booms, and to control rotation of cutterheads.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single dredging setup-and-operation cycle (positioning, engaging pumps, deploying boom, activating cutterhead). A competent operator typically spends ~30–60 min per excavation site setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized dredge control consoles with complex lever arrays, hydraulic feedback interpretation, real-time situational judgment on water, and heavy machinery coordination — far beyond any 2026 general-purpose humanoid's capabilities.

task: Operate and drive locomotives, diesel switch engines, dinkey engines, flatcars, and railcars in train yards and at industrial sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one yard switching/industrial rail operation shift segment; competent engineer completes a typical switching assignment or industrial haul in ~3–4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate locomotive controls, manage throttle/brake systems, couple railcars, or navigate rail yards safely. Requires specialized cab operation, situation awareness, and heavy equipment interaction far beyond current robot capabilities.

task: Provide direct assistance to farmers by performing activities such as purchasing or selling products and supplies, supervising properties, and collecting soil and herbage samples for testing.
human_time_rationale: One farm-visit instance covering purchasing/selling liaison, property check, and collecting soil/herbage samples; a competent farm agent typically takes ~60 min per visit.
robot_success_rationale: Task bundles commerce/negotiation (requires communication, judgment), outdoor terrain navigation, and precise soil/herbage sampling. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor mobility, tool-use for sampling equipment, and any capacity for purchasing/selling transactions.

task: Pack and unpack items to be stocked on shelves in stockrooms, warehouses, or storage yards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unpacking or packing one standard box/carton of shelf stock. A competent worker opens, removes/places items, and stages them in ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can grasp common objects and navigate warehouses, but varied box types, packing materials, irregular items, tight shelf placement, and unstructured environments make reliable autonomous completion difficult with 2026 hardware.

task: Stop gathering arms when cars are full.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking if a car is full and stopping the loading of arms (firearm/automotive parts) accordingly. A human takes ~2 min to assess fullness and cease activity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires perceiving vehicle cargo capacity, recognizing "full" state, and halting a complex gathering task. Situational awareness and semantic decision-making about stopping criteria are challenging for current humanoids; vehicle interaction adds further difficulty.

task: Monitor traffic flow to locate safe gaps through which pedestrians can cross streets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = identifying a safe gap and initiating a single street crossing. A competent pedestrian continuously monitors traffic and finds a gap within ~1–2 minutes on a typical urban street.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time vehicle speed/distance perception, gap timing judgment, and safe locomotion across live traffic. Current humanoids have limited outdoor mobility, unreliable dynamic scene understanding, and no validated pedestrian-crossing autonomy in real traffic.

task: Guide aircraft to designated areas using hand signals, batons, or other methods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marshalling a single aircraft from arrival to its designated parking/gate position; typically 5–15 minutes per aircraft.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor tarmac navigation, precise standardized signaling visible to pilots at distance, real-time situational awareness around moving aircraft, and split-second safety decisions. No current humanoid is deployed or capable in this safety-critical airside environment.

task: Perform errands for customers or employers, such as delivering or picking up mail and packages.
human_time_rationale: One errand instance = a single pickup or delivery trip (e.g., post office run or package drop-off), typically 15–35 minutes including travel and handoff.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor navigation, road/sidewalk travel, interacting with strangers, and entering varied environments. Humanoids lack autonomous vehicle capability and cannot safely traverse public routes end-to-end in 2026.

task: Advise farmers and demonstrate techniques in areas such as feeding and health maintenance of livestock, growing and harvesting practices, and financial planning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single advisory farm visit/session covering livestock, crop, and financial topics. A competent agricultural extension agent typically spends ~1.5 hours on-site per visit.
robot_success_rationale: Task demands expert agricultural knowledge, nuanced verbal communication, contextual judgment, financial planning, and live demonstration across diverse farm environments. No current humanoid robot can advise, explain, or teach at an acceptable professional standard autonomously.

task: Start and stop engines to operate equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting and stopping an engine on a single piece of equipment. A competent operator takes ~1–3 minutes including pre-check, ignition, and shutdown.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating and operating ignition controls, switches, or keys on varied heavy equipment. Humanoids lack the seated position, reach geometry, and specialized tool-use needed; controls vary wildly across machines. Generalization is very poor.

task: Receive and count stock items, and record data manually or on computer.
human_time_rationale: One instance = receiving and counting one shipment/delivery lot. A competent worker counts items, verifies against a manifest, and logs data — typically 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can scan barcodes and pick-and-place items but struggle with varied packaging, counting irregularly arranged stock, navigating a receiving dock, and entering data into arbitrary computer systems reliably end-to-end.

task: Count money and make bank deposits.
human_time_rationale: One instance = counting a cash drawer/deposit bag and completing one bank deposit trip. Counting ~5–10 min, preparing deposit slip ~5 min, travel/deposit ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Counting mixed bills/coins requires fine dexterity and currency recognition (marginal). Driving to a bank or operating a drive-through ATM is entirely beyond current humanoid robots. Multi-step, multi-environment task severely limits success.

task: Direct rehearsals to instruct dancers in dance steps and in techniques to achieve desired effects.
human_time_rationale: One rehearsal session directing dancers typically lasts 60–120 minutes; 90 min is a reasonable median for a structured rehearsal block.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced verbal/physical instruction, artistic judgment, real-time feedback on subtle movement quality, and social authority. No current robot has the communication, pedagogical, or aesthetic reasoning to direct human dancers autonomously.

task: Care for individuals or families during periods of incapacitation, family disruption, or convalescence, providing companionship, personal care, or help in adjusting to new lifestyles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single care shift (e.g., half-day visit). Caregiving is sustained; a typical unit a manager measures is a 4-hour block covering companionship, personal care, and assistance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social/emotional intelligence, adaptive personal care (bathing, dressing), crisis response, and lifestyle coaching — far beyond current robot capabilities in perception, dexterity, and human interaction generalization.

task: Monitor and perform tests on water, food, and the environment to detect harmful microorganisms or to obtain information about sources of pollution, contamination, or infection.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting samples from a site and running standard microbial/chemical tests. Includes sampling, labeling, preparation, and initial analysis; ~90 min for a typical field-to-lab cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile sampling technique, operating lab instruments (pipettes, centrifuges, PCR machines), interpreting complex biological results, and contextual judgment about contamination sources — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Operate levers to move conveyor booms or shovels so that mine contents such as coal, rock, and ore can be placed into cars or onto conveyors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning and loading one car or conveyor batch; an experienced operator cycles through this repeatedly per shift, ~15–25 min per discrete loading event.
robot_success_rationale: Requires seated cab operation of multi-axis levers on large mining machinery in a harsh, unstructured environment. Humanoids cannot operate heavy mining equipment controls; no commercial deployment exists. Perception, force feedback, and safety demands are extreme.

task: Inspect shipments to ensure that freight is securely braced and blocked.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one shipment/load in a truck or container. A competent inspector walks through, checks bracing, blocking, and tie-downs — typically 10–20 minutes per load.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating cluttered, confined trailer/container spaces, visually assessing varied cargo configurations, and judging adequacy of bracing — demanding unstructured perception and contextual reasoning well beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Perform test-fires of pottery to determine how to achieve specific colors and textures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and firing a small test batch of pottery samples, including glaze application, kiln loading, firing cycle, and evaluating results. Typically 1.5–3 hours of active work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous glaze application, kiln operation, nuanced aesthetic judgment of color/texture outcomes, and iterative creative decision-making — all far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured studio environments.

task: Clean aircraft interiors by picking up waste, wiping down windows, or vacuuming.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one aircraft cabin (e.g. narrow-body). Competent cabin cleaner picks up trash, wipes windows, and vacuums seats/aisles in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Aircraft cabins are extremely constrained (narrow aisles, overhead bins, curved walls, tight seat rows). Navigating, bending into rows, wiping curved windows, and vacuuming under seats require high dexterity and mobility in unstructured tight spaces — well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Distribute traffic control signs and markers at designated points.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up signs/markers across a typical work zone (~5–10 placements). A competent flagger/crew member walks the zone and positions each item in ~30 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor unstructured terrain (roads, curbs, traffic), carrying and precisely placing heavy signs, navigating live traffic safely, and reading situational cues. Current humanoids lack outdoor mobility, load-bearing capacity, and traffic-aware judgment.

task: Clean, lubricate, and maintain mechanisms such as cables, pulleys, or grappling devices, making repairs, as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing a single mechanism (cable, pulley, or grappling device). A competent technician inspects, cleans, lubricates, and makes minor repairs in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (applying grease, threading cables, tightening fasteners), multi-step diagnosis, use of varied hand tools, and conditional repair decisions — far beyond current general-purpose robot capability at acceptable quality.

task: Lower anchor poles to verify depths of excavations, using winches, or scan depth gauges to determine depths of excavations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring depth at a single excavation point. A worker lowers a pole or reads a gauge, records the depth — typically takes a few minutes per measurement.
