robot_success_rationale: Outdoor unstructured terrain, unstable edges, and operating a winch or reading analog depth gauges are challenging. Perception of gauge faces and cable control near excavation edges push success low; some depth-sensing via lidar is feasible but full autonomy is not.

task: Explain principles, techniques, and safety procedures to participants in recreational activities and demonstrate use of materials and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one instructional/safety briefing session. A competent recreation instructor typically spends ~45 min covering principles, safety rules, and equipment demos for a group.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fluid natural-language instruction, adaptive social interaction, dynamic equipment demonstration, and situational judgment. Current humanoids lack robust speech-driven teaching, real-time audience reading, and reliable multi-tool demo dexterity in unstructured recreational environments.

task: Analyze organic or inorganic compounds to determine chemical or physical properties, composition, structure, relationships, or reactions, using chromatography, spectroscopy, or spectrophotometry techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a sample and running a full analytical procedure (e.g., GC-MS or UV-Vis). Competent chemist typically spends ~60–120 min including prep, instrument setup, data interpretation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized lab instruments (spectrometers, chromatographs), fine dexterous sample prep (pipetting, cuvettes, columns), and expert data interpretation. Current humanoids lack lab-specific dexterity, software integration, and analytical cognition.

task: Adjust, repair, or replace faulty components of test setups and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and fixing a single faulty component in a test setup (e.g., replacing a sensor, tightening a connection, swapping a module). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fault diagnosis, fine dexterous manipulation (connectors, screws, delicate electronics), tool use, and domain-specific reasoning about test equipment. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities across arbitrary setups.

task: Perform laboratory analyses or physical inspections of samples to detect disease or to assess purity or cleanliness.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one sample batch analyzed/inspected. Includes prep, running assays or physical checks, and recording results; ~30–60 min is typical for a trained lab tech.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous pipetting, operating specialized lab instruments (centrifuges, analyzers, microscopes), interpreting ambiguous results, and maintaining sterile technique — well beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capabilities.

task: Mark stock items, using identification tags, stamps, electric marking tools, or other labeling equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking one stock item with a tag, stamp, or label. A competent worker typically selects the label/tool, applies it, and verifies placement in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping varied items, handling small labels/stamps/tools, precise placement, and verification. Pick-and-place is feasible but fine dexterous tool use (stamps, electric markers, tag guns) on diverse stock shapes is inconsistent for current humanoids.

task: Perform various marine duties, such as checking for oil spills or other pollutants around ports or harbors or patrolling beaches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patrol/inspection shift covering a port, harbor, or beach zone. A competent marine officer typically completes one patrol circuit in ~3–4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating watercraft on open water, navigating dynamic marine environments, and conducting environmental inspections. Humanoid robots cannot pilot vessels in traffic; aquatic mobility and tool-use in unstructured marine settings far exceed current capabilities.

task: Pack customer purchases in bags or cartons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = packing one customer's order. A competent cashier/packer typically bags or boxes a typical retail purchase in 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place common objects, but varied item shapes, fragility sorting, bag/carton opening, and efficient spatial packing require high dexterity and contextual judgment that current humanoids still struggle with reliably.

task: Conduct field demonstrations of new products, techniques, or services.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single field demo event. A competent human typically spends ~60 min setting up, presenting, and answering questions for one product/technique demonstration.
robot_success_rationale: Requires persuasive verbal communication, adaptive Q&A, reading audience reactions, and contextual improvisation. Current humanoids lack natural language fluency, social intelligence, and the credibility needed to conduct a convincing sales/educational demo autonomously.

task: Polish aircraft exteriors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = polishing the full exterior of a single commercial/regional aircraft. A crew of workers typically takes 6–10 hours; median ~8 hrs for a competent team.
robot_success_rationale: Aircraft exteriors are enormous curved surfaces at varying heights requiring scaffolding, specialized polishing tools, and precise pressure control. Current humanoids lack reach, mobility on elevated platforms, and endurance for such large-scale, unstructured industrial tasks.

task: Investigate the adequacy of ventilation, exhaust equipment, lighting, or other conditions that could affect employee health, comfort, or performance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full workplace inspection of a single area/zone. A competent industrial hygienist or safety officer typically spends 30–60 minutes assessing ventilation, lighting, exhaust, and environmental conditions in one area.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can carry sensors (light meter, CO2, airflow) and navigate a space, but lacks judgment to interpret findings contextually, interview workers, identify subtle hazards, or produce an actionable compliance report. Generalization and reasoning gaps are severe.

task: Operate equipment such as forklifts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one typical forklift operation cycle (loading/moving/stacking a pallet), averaging ~30 min including setup and travel within a warehouse.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate seated, pedal-and-lever-controlled vehicles like forklifts in real environments. No current general-purpose humanoid can mount, control, and safely drive a forklift autonomously. Autonomous forklifts exist but are narrow, fixed systems — not general-purpose humanoids.

task: Post warning signs on vehicles containing explosives or flammable or radioactive materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = posting required placards on one vehicle (typically 4 sides). A trained worker selects correct DOT placards, mounts, and secures them in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires hazmat classification knowledge, correct placard selection, outdoor/vehicle-surface mobility, and precise mounting on varied vehicle surfaces. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor mobility, tool-use for bracket/clip fastening, and regulatory decision-making integration.

task: Inspect conditions of locks, window bars, grills, doors, and gates at correctional facilities to ensure security and help prevent escapes.
human_time_rationale: One inspection round of a typical correctional facility wing, checking locks, bars, grills, doors, and gates — a competent officer completes a full circuit in ~30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating secure, access-controlled environments, visually and physically testing varied hardware (locks, bars, grills). Current robots lack reliable fine-grained force/tactile inspection of lock mechanisms, struggle with security door access sequences, and cannot operate in unstructured correctional layouts autonomously.

task: Pierce and cut open designs in ornamentation, using hand drills and scroll saws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one ornamental piece with a pierced/cut design. A skilled craftsperson typically spends 30–60 min drilling entry holes and scroll-sawing an intricate decorative pattern.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual tool use (hand drill + scroll saw), fine dexterity, tight positional accuracy on fragile decorative material, and adaptive path-following. Far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capabilities without task-specific fixtures.

task: Clean and maintain supplies, tools, equipment, and storage areas to ensure compliance with safety regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single cleaning/maintenance session covering tools, supplies, equipment, and storage area. A competent worker typically spends ~45 min inspecting, cleaning, organizing, and logging compliance.
robot_success_rationale: Task demands multi-step reasoning (safety compliance standards), varied manipulation (wiping, sorting, stowing tools), perception of hazards, and judgment on regulatory compliance—well beyond current general-purpose robot generalization and inspection capabilities.

task: Perform crisis interventions to help ensure the safety of the patients and others.
human_time_rationale: One crisis intervention instance covers initial contact through stabilization. Typical episodes last 30–90 minutes; 60 min is a reasonable median for a trained clinician.
robot_success_rationale: Crisis intervention demands nuanced verbal de-escalation, emotional attunement, real-time threat judgment, and trust-building — all far beyond current robot social cognition, NLP reliability, and autonomous decision-making in high-stakes, unstructured human emotional situations.

task: Test workplaces for environmental hazards, such as exposure to radiation, chemical or biological hazards, or excessive noise.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full hazard survey of a single workplace area. Includes setup, sensor deployment, sampling, readings, and documentation. Typical industrial hygienist visit per zone ~60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can carry sensors and navigate, but deploying specialized sampling equipment (swabs, wipes, dosimeters, calibrated noise meters), accessing varied areas, and documenting results end-to-end autonomously is well beyond current reliable capability. Perception and tool-use for multi-hazard testing remain immature.

task: Inspect, diagnose, maintain, and operate test setups and equipment to detect malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full inspect-diagnose-maintain cycle on one test setup; a competent technician typically takes 30–60 min including checks, diagnostics, and minor fixes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level multi-step reasoning, reading instruments/displays, interpreting faults, using varied tools, and performing precise maintenance. Current humanoids lack reliable fault diagnosis, instrument reading, and adaptive tool-use for complex lab/test environments.

task: Clean and restock operating room, gathering and placing equipment and supplies and arranging instruments according to instructions, such as a preference card.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fully cleaning and restocking a single OR between cases. Competent surgical tech/OR aide typically takes 30–60 min covering wipe-down, waste removal, linen, instrument setup per preference card.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting preference cards, identifying hundreds of sterile surgical instruments by type, precise sterile-field arrangement, navigating a cluttered clinical environment, and meeting strict infection-control standards — far beyond 2026 frontier robot capability.

task: Inspect vessels to ensure efficient and safe operation of vessels and equipment and conformance to regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full vessel inspection covering equipment, safety systems, and regulatory compliance. A competent marine inspector typically takes 60–120 minutes per vessel.
robot_success_rationale: Requires maritime mobility (ladders, gangways, wet decks), expert regulatory knowledge, complex multi-system assessment, documentation, and judgment calls. No current humanoid is certified or capable for this unstructured, safety-critical marine environment.

task: Observe and monitor patient food intake and body weight, and report changes, progress, and dietary problems to dietician.
human_time_rationale: One instance = observing a single patient's meal, logging intake and weight, and preparing a brief report for the dietician. Covers one meal/weigh-in cycle per patient.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual estimation of food consumed, accurate patient weighing with cooperation, social interaction, anomaly detection, and structured reporting — all far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities in unstructured care settings.

task: Inspect game sites for compliance with regulations or safety requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one game site (e.g., casino floor, gaming room, or sports betting venue). A competent inspector typically takes ~1 hour to walk the site, check equipment, records, and signage.
robot_success_rationale: Inspection requires nuanced regulatory judgment, document review, interviewing staff, and contextual understanding of compliance standards. Robots lack the cognitive/legal reasoning, communication skills, and authority to conduct regulatory inspections autonomously.

task: Provide assistance in maritime rescue operations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating, reaching, and extracting one person from the water to safety. Median time for a trained rescue crew from alert to recovery.
robot_success_rationale: Maritime rescue demands swimming/water locomotion, boat operation, dynamic wave environments, victim handling, and real-time judgment — all far beyond 2026 humanoid robot capabilities. No general-purpose robot can operate at sea autonomously.

task: Schedule and receive food and beverage deliveries, checking delivery contents to verify product quality and quantity.
human_time_rationale: One delivery instance: receiving a typical food/beverage shipment, checking invoice, verifying item counts, inspecting quality/condition, and logging results — ~45 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires scheduling (cognitive/communication), reading invoices, identifying varied packaged goods, judging freshness/quality, and coordinating with delivery personnel. Current robots lack reliable generalization across all these multi-step, socially embedded subtasks.

task: Perform annual gynecologic exams, including pap smears and breast exams.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one complete annual gynecologic exam (pap smear + breast exam + consultation) for one patient; a competent clinician typically completes this in 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sterile technique, nuanced tactile perception, patient communication, and clinical judgment. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capability; also legally/ethically prohibited without licensed oversight.

task: Restage traditional dances and works in dance companies' repertoires, developing new interpretations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restaging a single dance work for a company; includes rehearsal direction, choreographic adaptation, and staging decisions over multiple sessions, typically spanning weeks. ~40 hours is a conservative median.
robot_success_rationale: Restaging requires artistic interpretation, choreographic memory, abstract creative decision-making, directing human performers, and cultural understanding — far beyond any current robot's cognitive, creative, or social capabilities.

task: Operate landfill gas, methane, or natural gas fueled electrical generation systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single operational check/monitoring round of a gas-fueled generation system, including readings, adjustments, and logging. Competent operator takes ~30–60 min per round.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading gauges, operating control panels, adjusting valves, and responding to alarms on complex industrial equipment. Humanoids lack certified interfaces, tool compatibility, and fail-safe judgment for high-risk energy infrastructure.

task: Observe patients during treatments to compile and evaluate data on their responses and progress and provide results to physical therapist in person or through progress notes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = observing a single patient during one treatment session (~20–30 min), compiling notes, and reporting findings to the physical therapist verbally or in writing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced clinical observation (pain cues, gait quality, affect), medical knowledge to evaluate responses, judgment to compile meaningful progress notes, and natural communication with the PT — far beyond current robot capability.

task: De-grease aircraft exteriors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = degreasing one full aircraft exterior. A competent crew member using sprayers, brushes, and rinse equipment takes ~2–4 hours depending on aircraft size; 3 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Aircraft exteriors are vast, curved, outdoor structures with ladders/lifts needed for reach. Current humanoids lack the mobility, reach range, chemical spray tool integration, and safe navigation around fuselage/wings at height to complete this autonomously.

task: Install protective devices, such as bracing, padding, or strapping, to prevent shifting or damage to items being transported.
human_time_rationale: One instance = securing one vehicle/container load with bracing, padding, or straps; a skilled worker typically spends 10–20 min depending on load size and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires routing and tensioning straps, positioning foam/bracing around irregular cargo, and assessing load stability — all demanding bimanual dexterity, force control, spatial reasoning, and generalization to highly varied loads. Well beyond current frontier robot capability.

task: Set up 24-hour Holter and event monitors, scan and interpret tapes, and report results to physicians.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full cycle: applying Holter monitor to patient (~10 min), scanning/analyzing the 24-hr tape (~20 min with software), and reporting results to physician (~15 min).
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise electrode placement on human skin, operating clinical scanning hardware, interpreting ECG waveforms medically, and communicating findings to a physician. Current robots lack medical dexterity, clinical software integration, and safe patient interaction needed for all sub-tasks.

task: Inspect checked baggage for signs of tampering.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one piece of checked baggage. A trained agent visually and physically checks a bag for tampering signs in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal perception (visual anomaly detection, tactile checks, smell), handling irregular bag shapes/materials, and nuanced judgment on subtle tampering signs. Current robots lack reliable generalization for this unstructured security inspection task.

task: Examine finished ware for defects and measure dimensions, using rule and thickness gauge.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single finished piece; a competent worker visually checks for defects and takes a few measurements with rule/gauge in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is feasible but fine tactile gauge manipulation, precise alignment of rule on irregular ware, and interpreting subtle surface defects remain unreliable for general-purpose humanoids in 2026.

task: Mix cleaning compounds or solutions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single batch of cleaning solution (e.g., diluting concentrate into a spray bottle or bucket). A competent worker measures, mixes, and labels in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading labels, measuring liquids precisely, pouring without spilling, and potentially operating faucets/dispensers. Liquid handling and precise measurement remain challenging for current humanoids; perception of fluid levels and fine pouring control are weak points.

task: Provide passengers with assistance entering and exiting vehicles, and help them with any luggage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one passenger boarding or alighting, including opening the door, steadying the passenger, and loading/unloading 1–2 bags. Typically takes 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires simultaneous door-opening, physical steadying of an unpredictable human body, and luggage handling in dynamic outdoor environments. Current humanoids lack reliable safe physical human-contact, adaptive grip for varied luggage, and smooth coordination of all three sub-tasks.

task: Administer active or passive manual therapeutic exercises, therapeutic massage, aquatic physical therapy, or heat, light, sound, or electrical modality treatments, such as ultrasound.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient treatment session (e.g., one modality or exercise block). Typical PT treatment sessions run 20–45 minutes; 30 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly nuanced dexterity for hands-on massage/exercise guiding, real-time pain/feedback interpretation, safe aquatic maneuvering, and precise placement of therapeutic devices. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capability at acceptable clinical quality.

task: Tow aircraft to gates or hangars using tugs, tractors, or other vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = towing one aircraft from its current position to a gate or hangar. Includes attaching towbar, driving the tug, and disconnecting at destination. Typical ramp operation takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized ground support vehicles (tugs/tractors) on active aprons, attaching towbars to aircraft under-gear, navigating crowded ramps. Humanoids cannot drive towing vehicles or safely operate in high-stakes airside environments autonomously.

task: Dismantle lapping, boring, cutting, polishing, and shaping equipment and machinery to clean and lubricate it.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dismantling, cleaning, lubricating, and reassembling one piece of industrial equipment. A competent machinist typically takes 30–60 min depending on machine complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-torque unfastening of varied bolts/fittings, fine dexterous manipulation of precision components, applying lubricants to specific points, and safe reassembly — far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Inspect locomotives to detect damaged or worn parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full inspection of one locomotive. A competent rail technician typically takes 45–90 minutes to walk around, check undercarriage, mechanical, and electrical components.
robot_success_rationale: Locomotive inspection requires navigating tight undercarriage spaces, operating specialized inspection tools, recognizing subtle wear patterns across hundreds of component types, and working in an industrial rail yard — well beyond current humanoid generalization and mobility limits.

task: Prepare patients for surgery, including positioning patients on the operating table and covering them with sterile surgical drapes to prevent exposure.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one patient: transferring, positioning on the OR table, and applying sterile drapes. A skilled surgical tech typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires gentle human handling, sterile technique discipline, complex draping around a moving/fragile patient, and context-aware positioning. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, safety, and sterile-field awareness capabilities.

task: Inspect and monitor work areas, examine tools and equipment, and provide employee safety training to prevent, detect, and correct unsafe conditions or violations of procedures and safety rules.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full safety inspection round of a work area, including tool/equipment checks and a brief safety training session; ~1 hour is typical for a safety officer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires integrated visual inspection, hazard recognition, compliance judgment, verbal training delivery, and adaptive communication — well beyond current robot generalization. Perception and locomotion are partial, but reasoning, authority, and social interaction are severely lacking.

task: Climb ladders to reach aircraft surfaces to be cleaned.
human_time_rationale: One instance = climbing a ladder to reach one aircraft surface section. A competent worker positions, climbs, and arrives at the target surface in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Climbing non-fixed, industrial ladders on uneven tarmac outdoors is highly unreliable for current humanoids. Outdoor terrain, ladder variability, load-bearing safety requirements, and carrying cleaning equipment push success very low.

task: Install motorcycle accessories.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single accessory (e.g., handlebar grips, mirrors, or a windshield) on a motorcycle. Competent mechanic takes ~30–60 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating in cramped, curved, unstructured vehicle geometry; fine torque-controlled fastening; part alignment; and tool changes. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force feedback, and mechanical knowledge for reliable autonomous motorcycle work.

task: Conduct inspections of the airport property and perimeter to maintain controlled access to airfields.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full perimeter/property inspection circuit at a typical regional airport; a competent security officer completes this patrol in ~45–75 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating large, mixed outdoor terrain (tarmac, fencing, vehicle traffic), identifying security anomalies, and taking access-control action. Current humanoids lack outdoor range, adverse-weather reliability, regulatory authorization, and situational judgment needed for airfield operations.

task: Obtain merchandise from bins or shelves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retrieving a single item from a bin or shelf. A competent worker locates and picks one item in roughly 1–2 minutes including walking to location.
robot_success_rationale: Frontier humanoids handle pick-and-place of common objects well (~70–90% on tidy bins), but shelf retrieval adds variability in object shape, placement, occlusion, and navigation to location, reducing reliability to ~55%.

task: Pry off loose material from roofs and move it into the paths of machines, using crowbars.
human_time_rationale: One instance = prying loose material from one section of roof and repositioning it into a machine's path; a competent worker takes ~30 min including tool use and maneuvering.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating unstable, uneven mine/roof terrain, applying high-force leverage with a crowbar, and coordinating with heavy machinery paths — all severely beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and rough-terrain mobility limits.

task: Manually or mechanically load or unload materials from pallets, skids, platforms, cars, lifting devices, or other transport vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading or unloading a single pallet/skid of mixed materials. A competent worker typically completes this in ~15–25 minutes depending on item count and weight.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle structured pick-and-place but struggle with varied item shapes, weights, tight stacking, awkward transport vehicle geometries, and the physical strength/stability needed for heavy or irregular loads. Generalization is still limited.

task: Assist engineers to implement electromechanical designs in industrial or other settings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting with a single electromechanical installation task (e.g., mounting a motor, routing wiring, fitting a panel). A competent technician takes ~1–2 hours per discrete sub-task.
robot_success_rationale: Requires mixed fine dexterity (wire routing, connector insertion, fastening), reading schematics, collaborating with engineers, navigating unstructured industrial environments, and adapting to varied components. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Operate pug mills to blend and extrude clay.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one production batch: loading clay, configuring mill settings, monitoring blending/extrusion, and clearing output. Typical cycle is 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Pug mills require operating specialized industrial controls, loading heavy/sticky bulk clay, monitoring extrusion consistency, and making tactile/visual quality judgments. Humanoid robots cannot safely operate this heavy industrial machinery autonomously in 2026.

task: Measure vessels' holds and depths of fuel and water in tanks, using sounding lines and tape measures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring all holds and tanks on a typical vessel. A competent worker navigates the vessel, drops sounding lines, reads tapes across multiple tanks/holds — roughly 45 min per full survey.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex, confined marine environments (ladders, hatches, wet decks), manipulating sounding lines and tape measures with precision, and reading analog results. Outdoor/maritime terrain, tight spaces, and tool-use generalization far exceed current humanoid capabilities.

task: Maintain massage areas by restocking supplies or sanitizing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restocking and/or sanitizing one massage room between sessions; a competent therapist or aide typically completes this in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a cluttered spa room, wiping curved/padded equipment, identifying and restocking varied small supplies (oils, linens, sanitizer). Perception of soft surfaces, fine manipulation of bottles/linens, and generalization across room layouts limit current robots significantly.

task: Clean, finish, and lubricate molds and mold parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning, finishing, and lubricating one mold assembly. A competent worker takes ~15–25 min depending on mold complexity and size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to reach interior cavities, apply release agents evenly, and use tools (brushes, rags, spray). Unstructured mold geometry, precise surface finishing, and chemical handling push success probability very low for current humanoids.

task: Set up and conduct chemical experiments, tests, and analyses, using techniques such as chromatography, spectroscopy, physical or chemical separation techniques, or microscopy.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running a single chemical experiment/analysis (e.g., one chromatography or spectroscopy run). A competent lab technician typically takes 60–120 minutes including setup, execution, and initial data check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous manipulation of delicate glassware, pipettes, and calibrated instruments; interpreting results; adapting to unexpected outcomes. Current humanoids lack fine lab dexterity, chemical domain reasoning, and safe handling of hazardous materials needed for full autonomy.

task: Administer radiopharmaceuticals or radiation intravenously to detect or treat diseases, using radioisotope equipment, under direction of a physician.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and IV-administering one radiopharmaceutical dose to one patient, including verification, venipuncture, injection, and documentation. Typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise venipuncture, sterile technique, handling of radioactive materials with shielding, real-time patient communication, and clinical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, safety certification, and regulatory compliance capabilities.

task: Dispose of damaged or defective items, or return them to vendors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = identifying one damaged/defective item, deciding its fate (dispose or return), and completing the action (binning, labeling, staging for pickup). ~15 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires visual defect detection, decision-making (dispose vs. return), paperwork/labeling, and routing items — multi-step cognitive + dexterous workflow. Current robots lack reliable damage perception and vendor-return logistics autonomy.

task: Measure and sketch crime scenes to document evidence.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one crime scene. A competent forensic investigator typically spends 60–120 minutes measuring, photographing, and producing a sketch of a single scene.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating unpredictable, often outdoor/cluttered environments; identifying and measuring legally relevant evidence; producing annotated forensic sketches; and exercising expert judgment. Current robots lack the perception, semantic understanding, and legal-context reasoning needed.

task: Wash the aircraft exteriors using lifts, cranes, detergent, or other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = washing one aircraft exterior. A commercial aircraft (e.g., narrow-body) typically takes 2–4 hours for a crew; median ~3 hours for a competent team.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy specialized equipment (lifts, cranes, pressure washers) on large outdoor structures, coordinating multi-step procedures around complex curved surfaces. Current humanoids cannot operate such equipment, work at height safely, or generalize to aircraft-scale tasks autonomously.

task: Conduct safety training or education programs and demonstrate the use of safety equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single safety training session, including preparation, instruction, demonstrations, and Q&A for a group; typically 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic verbal instruction, adaptive communication, reading audience comprehension, and credible equipment demonstration. Current humanoids lack conversational teaching ability, social nuance, and curriculum adaptability needed for autonomous training delivery.

task: Drive machines into piles of material blasted from working faces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one loading cycle pushing/scooping a blasted rock pile at a mining/quarry face; a competent operator typically spends 30–60 min per working face pass.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy mining equipment (bulldozer, front-end loader) in unstructured, hazardous outdoor terrain with blasted rock. Humanoid robots cannot drive or control such machinery autonomously at any commercially viable level in 2026.

task: Provide for entertainment and set up related decorations and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up decorations and AV/entertainment equipment for a typical event. Includes arranging décor, connecting/positioning equipment, and testing. ~90 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse dexterous manipulation (hanging items, routing cables, connecting AV gear), aesthetic judgment, climbing/reaching, and contextual planning. Far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capabilities in unstructured event environments.

task: Maintain a proper sterile field during surgical procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = maintaining sterile field for a single surgical procedure. A typical general surgery case runs ~45–90 min; 45 min is a conservative median for a competent scrub tech/nurse.
robot_success_rationale: Requires continuous fine dexterous manipulation, sterile gowning/gloving, recognizing contamination events, adapting to unpredictable surgical flow, and operating in a tightly constrained sterile zone—far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Comfort patients during exams, or request sedatives or other medication from physicians for patients with anxiety or claustrophobia.
human_time_rationale: One instance = comforting one patient during a single exam encounter, including assessment and any physician communication. Typically takes 5–15 minutes depending on anxiety level.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced emotional intelligence, verbal rapport, real-time clinical judgment, and licensed authority to request medications. Current robots lack social-emotional perception, empathetic communication, and legal/clinical agency to perform this task autonomously.

task: Train, exercise, and attend dance classes to maintain high levels of technical proficiency, physical ability, and physical fitness.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one training/exercise/dance class session. A typical session (warm-up, class, cool-down) runs ~60–120 minutes; 90 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Dance requires expressive, rhythmically precise, highly coordinated full-body movement far beyond current humanoid capability. Robots lack musicality, artistic expression, balance in complex choreography, and cannot self-direct a training regimen autonomously.

task: Visually inspect cargo for damage upon arrival or discharge.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single cargo unit/shipment upon arrival. A competent inspector visually checks for damage, marks findings, and logs results in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can detect obvious surface damage via vision, but struggle with nuanced damage assessment (subtle dents, moisture, seal integrity), navigating cluttered dock environments, and generating acceptable inspection reports without human judgment.

task: Pose for artists and photographers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one modeling session. Typical artist/photographer session runs ~1–2 hours; 60 min is a reasonable median for a single booking.
robot_success_rationale: Posing requires nuanced body awareness, fluid transitions, holding static poses on request, and reading subtle artistic direction. Current humanoids lack expressive control, naturalistic stillness, and the social/aesthetic judgment needed to satisfy artists or photographers.

task: Buff and vacuum floors to ensure their cleanliness prior to the application of finish.
human_time_rationale: One instance = buffing and vacuuming a typical commercial floor area (~1,000 sq ft). A competent janitor takes ~45 min combining both passes.
robot_success_rationale: Autonomous floor-buffing requires operating a large, heavy buffer machine with precise pressure control, then switching to vacuum — tool operation, cord management, and edge navigation are beyond current humanoid capability. Specialized floor-care robots exist but are narrow, not general-purpose humanoids.

task: Follow those who breach security until police or other security personnel arrive to apprehend them.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trailing a security breach suspect from detection until police arrive. Typical response/wait time ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained mobile tracking of an unpredictable, evasive person through varied indoor/outdoor terrain, crowd navigation, real-time re-identification, and safe autonomous following without intervention — well beyond current humanoid reliability.

task: Perform quality control checks on laboratory equipment or cameras.
human_time_rationale: One instance = QC check on a single piece of lab equipment or camera. A competent technician spends ~30 min running calibration checks, visual inspection, and logging results.
robot_success_rationale: QC requires reading instrument displays, operating specialized controls, interpreting calibration data, and fine manipulation of delicate equipment. Current humanoids lack the perception precision, domain-specific tool-use, and judgment needed for autonomous pass/fail decisions.

task: Verify dimensions of products, using measuring instruments, such as calipers, vernier gauges, or protractors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring key dimensions of a single product/part. A skilled worker selects the right instrument, measures, and records results in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise grasping of delicate instruments (calipers, vernier gauges), fine dexterous manipulation to seat them correctly on a part, accurate reading, and tool switching. Current humanoids lack sufficient fine-motor precision and reliable instrument-reading for production-quality inspection.

task: Inspect loaded cargo, cargo lashed to decks or in storage facilities, and cargo handling devices to determine compliance with health and safety regulations and need for maintenance.
human_time_rationale: One inspection round of a loaded vessel/storage facility — checking lashing, cargo condition, and handling devices. A competent marine/cargo inspector typically takes 30–60 min per inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating cluttered, uneven maritime decks, interpreting complex lashing configurations, reading labels/markings, and applying regulatory knowledge. Current humanoids lack the robust outdoor mobility, domain-specific perception, and compliance reasoning needed.

task: Inspect tracks, cars, and engines for defects and to determine service needs, sending engines and cars for repairs as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one railcar or locomotive. Inspector checks wheels, trucks, brakes, couplers, and engine components; typically 30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires traversing uneven rail yards, inspecting under and around large vehicles, identifying subtle mechanical defects, and making routing decisions. Perception at scale, rough-terrain mobility, and domain-specific defect recognition far exceed current humanoid capabilities.

task: Clean hoppers, and clean spillage from tracks, walks, driveways, and conveyor decking.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full cleaning cycle of a hopper and surrounding spillage areas (tracks, walks, driveways, conveyor decking); a competent worker with standard tools takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex industrial terrain (tracks, conveyor decking, driveways), entering/cleaning confined hoppers, and handling bulky/heavy spillage material. Current humanoids lack the mobility, tool-use, and environment generalization for this rugged, unstructured setting.

task: Fit and assemble parts to make, repair, or modify dies, jigs, gauges, and tools, using machine tools, hand tools, or welders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting and assembling one die, jig, gauge, or tool. A skilled toolmaker typically spends 2–4 hours per unit depending on complexity; 3 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level fine dexterity, precise multi-step fitting, tolerance checking, and operating machine tools/welders. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured, high-precision toolmaking environments. No commercial deployment exists.

task: Clean funeral home facilities and grounds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning the interior facilities and exterior grounds of a funeral home in a single shift. Includes restrooms, viewing rooms, chapel, hallways, and outdoor areas.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle basic sweeping/mopping on flat surfaces (~40–70%), but funeral homes require nuanced multi-room navigation, delicate handling near sensitive decor/caskets, outdoor grounds work, and high cleanliness standards across varied surfaces. Generalization and unstructured layout reduce success significantly.

task: Monitor product quality to ensure compliance with standards and specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a batch or production run of products for quality compliance; a competent QC worker typically spends ~30 min per batch check including measurement, documentation, and pass/fail decisions.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle visual inspection and simple measurements, but full compliance monitoring requires interpreting varied specs, using multiple gauges/instruments, contextual judgment, and documentation — beyond reliable autonomous capability in 2026.

task: Start machine units and conveyors and observe lights and gauges on panel board to verify operational efficiency.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting a machine line and completing a panel-board inspection pass. A competent operator typically takes 5–15 minutes to power up units, confirm conveyor motion, and scan all indicator lights/gauges.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating physical start controls, reading analog/digital gauges and panel lights, and interpreting system status — all in an unstructured industrial environment. Current humanoids lack reliable panel-reading perception, precise button/switch actuation on varied hardware, and operational judgment to declare "efficient."

task: Troubleshoot and make minor repairs to geothermal plant instrumentation or electrical systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing a single fault and completing the associated minor repair (e.g., replacing a sensor, tightening terminals, swapping a fuse/relay). A competent instrumentation technician typically takes 60–120 min per job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized electrical/instrumentation tools, reading diagnostic displays, fine wire-terminal manipulation, safety lockout/tagout procedures, and expert fault reasoning in a harsh industrial environment — well beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Verify accuracy of shapes and sizes of objects, using calipers and templates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single object with calipers and/or templates to verify dimensions. A competent inspector typically completes this in ~5 minutes including recording results.
robot_success_rationale: Precise caliper gripping and reading requires fine dexterity and accurate force control; template alignment demands deft manipulation. Perception of caliper dial/digital readout and subtle fit feedback is unreliable for current humanoids. High failure risk on fine measurement tasks.

task: Provide passengers with assistance entering and exiting vehicles, and help them with any luggage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one passenger boarding/alighting a vehicle plus handling their luggage. Typically takes 2–4 minutes for a competent porter or driver.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic human contact (steadying a person), outdoor/vehicle-side terrain, opening car doors, lifting varied luggage, and safe physical interaction with passengers — all highly challenging for 2026 humanoids. Safety constraints alone severely limit deployment.

task: Read draft markings to determine depths of vessels in water.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reading draft markings on one vessel. A competent mariner visually locates the markings at bow/stern, reads the numerals at the waterline, and records the depth in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor mobility on uneven dock/quayside, precise visual perception of partially submerged, wet, barnacled markings at waterline, and interpreting specialized numeral conventions. Current robots lack reliable marine-environment locomotion and fine waterline-detection perception.

task: Clean work areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single work area (desk/bench/station): wiping surfaces, tidying clutter, emptying waste bin. Competent worker takes ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can wipe flat surfaces and sweep/mop (~40–70%), but work areas have clutter, tools, cables, and varied objects requiring judgment to reposition or avoid. Generalization and safe object handling in unstructured workspaces remain limited.

task: Disassemble timepieces such as watches, clocks, and chronometers so that repairs can be made.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fully disassembling one timepiece (e.g., a mechanical watch). A skilled watchmaker takes ~30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Extremely fine dexterity required: components are sub-millimeter, fragile, and numerous. Current humanoids lack the precision, tactile sensitivity, and specialized micro-tooling needed. Far beyond frontier capability.

task: Fit garments on clients, altering as needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting a single garment on one client, including pinning, assessing fit, and making minor alterations. Typical tailor/seamstress session per garment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (pinning, sewing, cutting fabric), nuanced social/physical interaction with a human client, dynamic body assessment, and skilled needle/thread work — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Collect and study blood samples to determine the number of cells, their morphology, or their blood group, blood type, or compatibility for transfusion purposes, using microscopic techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting and analyzing a single patient blood sample (CBC, morphology, or blood typing). A skilled lab tech typically takes 5–10 min to collect/prepare and 20–30 min for microscopic review and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires venipuncture dexterity, preparing blood smears, operating a microscope, and interpreting cellular morphology — all demanding fine motor skills, specialized tool use, and expert visual cognition far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Perform or direct the performance of preventative maintenance on geothermal plant equipment.
human_time_rationale: One PM instance covers a defined equipment unit (e.g., pump, heat exchanger, or turbine section); a competent technician typically spends 1–3 hours per unit including inspection, lubrication, filter changes, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Geothermal PM requires specialized tools, high-temperature/high-pressure hazard navigation, torque-specific fastener work, fluid sampling, and multi-step judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool repertoire, domain-specific perception, and safety-critical autonomy needed.

task: Complete laboratory projects prior to assigning them to students so that any needed modifications can be made.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing one lab project end-to-end for review. A typical lab exercise takes a competent instructor ~30–90 min to run through and evaluate modifications needed.
robot_success_rationale: Task demands deep domain expertise, reading/interpreting lab instructions, conducting experiments, and making pedagogical judgments. Current robots lack cognitive reasoning, scientific expertise, and generalized lab manipulation for arbitrary experiments.

task: Examine landing gear, tires, and exteriors of fuselage, wings, and engines for evidence of damage or corrosion and the need for repairs.
human_time_rationale: One full exterior walk-around inspection of a typical aircraft by a competent A&P mechanic, covering all specified components; standard industry time is roughly 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex outdoor/tarmac terrain around large structures, close-up visual inspection for subtle corrosion/damage cues, reaching engine nacelles and high fuselage surfaces, and exercising expert aeronautical judgment — well beyond 2026 frontier robot capabilities.

task: Inspect forest tracts and logging areas for fire hazards such as accumulated wastes or mishandling of combustibles, and recommend appropriate fire prevention measures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single forest tract or logging area. A competent inspector typically walks the area, assesses hazards, and documents findings in ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires traversing rough outdoor forest terrain (very low robot mobility success), identifying context-dependent fire hazards (poor generalization), and formulating written recommendations — far beyond current humanoid capability outdoors.

task: Draw samples of biofuels products or secondary by-products for quality control testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drawing a sample from a single biofuel product or by-product stream; includes locating the sample point, attaching/using sample equipment, and labeling the container. ~15 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise manipulation of valves, sample ports, and containers in industrial settings; hazardous liquids/gases demand safety interlocks; non-standard equipment layouts and fine dexterity with sampling tools make autonomous success very unlikely for current humanoids.

task: Organize and conduct experimental studies with live animals in controlled or natural surroundings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single experimental session (setup, running trials, data recording) with live animals. Typical sessions in behavioral/biological research run 2–5 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert experimental design, animal handling/husbandry intuition, adaptive response to unpredictable animal behavior, ethical judgment, and fine manipulation. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Design and set up advertising signs and displays of merchandise on shelves, counters, or tables to attract customers and promote sales.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a single display or promotional sign arrangement at one location (shelf, counter, or table). A competent retail merchandiser typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative spatial design judgment, precise placement of varied signage/merchandise, aesthetic evaluation, and navigation in a busy retail environment. Current robots lack design cognition, reliable fine placement of irregular items, and goal-directed merchandising generalization.

task: Receive, unload, open, unpack, or issue sales floor merchandise.
human_time_rationale: One instance = receiving and unpacking one shipment delivery (e.g., one cart/pallet of mixed boxes), staging items for the sales floor. Typical retail receiving task ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse manipulation: cutting tape, opening varied cartons, recognizing mixed items, navigating warehouse/sales floor. Current robots lack reliable generalization across packaging types, item variety, and unstructured environments end-to-end.

task: Operate energized high- or low-voltage hydroelectric power transmission system substations, according to procedures and safety requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full operational shift (8 hrs) at a substation. Operators monitor, adjust, and respond to events continuously over a shift; sub-hour units aren't meaningful for this task.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert electrical knowledge, safety-critical decision-making, operating specialized high-voltage controls, switches, and breakers, reading instrumentation panels, and responding to emergencies. No current humanoid robot can do this autonomously at acceptable safety standards.

task: Install medical equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single piece of medical equipment (e.g., patient monitor, IV pole, hospital bed rail, imaging unit). Includes unpacking, positioning, mounting, and basic connection checks.
robot_success_rationale: Medical equipment installation demands precise fine-motor work (cable routing, locking mechanisms, calibration), compliance with strict safety standards, navigation in cluttered clinical spaces, and generalization across highly varied device types — all far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Inspect hydroelectric facilities, including switchyards, control houses, or relay houses, for normal operation or adherence to safety standards.
human_time_rationale: One inspection tour of a hydroelectric facility (switchyard, control house, relay house) by a competent technician; includes visual checks, readings, and documentation — roughly 1–2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex outdoor/indoor industrial environments, reading analog gauges, interpreting abnormal equipment states, and applying domain-specific safety standards. Current robots lack reliable generalization, specialized perception, and the judgment needed for safety-critical assessments.

task: Send checked baggage through automated screening machines, and set bags aside for searching or rescreening as indicated by equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one checked bag through screening; a competent screener takes ~1–3 min per bag including monitoring the readout and diverting flagged bags.
robot_success_rationale: Requires lifting/placing varied heavy bags onto conveyors, reading machine status displays, and diverting flagged bags — complex perception plus heavy manipulation in a dynamic, cluttered environment. Current humanoids lack reliable bag-handling and HMI integration.

task: Take inventory or examine merchandise to identify items to be reordered or replenished.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scanning/checking a typical retail shelf section or stockroom area. A competent worker reviews stock levels, checks labels, and notes reorder needs in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied store environments, reading shelf labels, counting stock, and cross-referencing inventory systems. Current robots lack reliable OCR-in-motion, inventory DB integration, and judgment about reorder thresholds in unstructured retail settings.

task: Perform or direct preventive or corrective containment or cleanup to protect the environment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single containment/cleanup event (e.g., a spill or minor environmental hazard). Competent worker assesses situation, deploys materials, executes cleanup, and documents. Median ~90 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires hazard assessment, situational judgment, deploying specialized containment tools (booms, absorbents, pumps), outdoor/unstructured terrain navigation, regulatory compliance decisions, and multi-step coordination — far beyond current robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Monitor conduct of prisoners in housing unit, or during work or recreational activities, according to established policies, regulations, and procedures, to prevent escape or violence.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full shift (8 hours) of monitoring a housing unit or activity area. Correctional officers work shift-based continuous surveillance; the unit of measure is one shift.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic social perception, intent recognition, de-escalation judgment, legal authority, and mobility across complex multi-zone environments. Current robots lack behavioral/threat interpretation, social authority, and reliable real-time response to unpredictable human conduct.

task: Prepare chemical solutions for products or processes, following standardized formulas, or create experimental formulas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one batch of a chemical solution. A competent lab/process technician typically spends 30–60 min measuring, mixing, verifying pH/concentration, and labeling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid handling, fine dexterity with glassware and pipettes, reading instruments, following multi-step chemical protocols, and safety judgments. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation, lab tool integration, and chemical reasoning needed.

task: Maintain boats or equipment on board, such as engines, winches, navigational systems, fire extinguishers, or life preservers.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance session covering multiple onboard systems (engine, winches, nav, safety gear). A competent marine technician typically spends ~1.5 hours per routine maintenance round.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating in a confined, wet, moving marine environment; using specialized tools on diverse equipment; diagnosing faults; and handling safety-critical gear. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Count money in cash drawers at the beginning of shifts to ensure that amounts are correct and that there is adequate change.
human_time_rationale: One instance = counting a single cash drawer at shift start. A competent cashier counts and verifies bills/coins in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous handling of loose bills and coins, denomination recognition, counting accuracy, and drawer interaction. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation of currency and cognitive cash-counting workflows.

task: Collect samples of gases, soils, water, industrial wastewater, or asbestos products to conduct tests on pollutant levels or identify sources of pollution.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a single set of field samples (e.g., one site visit). A competent environmental technician typically spends ~30–60 min locating the site, preparing containers, collecting samples, and labeling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied/outdoor terrain, handling specialized sampling equipment (soil corers, gas probes, water samplers), precise liquid transfer, asbestos handling protocols, and contextual judgment about sample locations — well beyond current robot generalization and dexterity in unstructured field environments.

task: Direct maintenance and repair of firefighting equipment, or requisition new equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting, maintaining, or repairing one piece of firefighting equipment (e.g., SCBA, hose, extinguisher). A competent firefighter/technician takes ~30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly specialized tool use, fine dexterity (torque testing, valve inspection, hose coupling checks), domain-specific knowledge, and judgment on when to requisition vs. repair. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured settings.

task: Inspect geothermal plant or injection well fields to verify proper equipment operations.
human_time_rationale: One inspection round of a geothermal plant or injection well field; a competent technician walks the site, checks gauges, valves, and wellheads, and logs findings — typically 1–2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/rough terrain navigation, reading analog gauges, operating valves, interpreting equipment state, and making judgment calls. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor mobility, specialized tool use, and domain-specific perception for industrial geothermal sites.

task: Measure and cut products to specified dimensions, using measuring and cutting instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and cutting a single product/piece to spec. A competent worker typically takes ~5 min including measuring, marking, and cutting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement reading, marking, and controlled cutting tool use. Robots struggle with generalizing across varied instruments, materials, and precision tolerances. Perception and fine dexterity are key bottlenecks.

task: Direct traffic movement or warn of hazards, using signs, flags, lanterns, and hand signals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full work shift (e.g., 8 hours) of directing traffic at a single post; the measurable unit a manager tracks per assignment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time situational awareness of chaotic, dynamic traffic; reliable gestural communication legible to drivers; sustained outdoor locomotion; and split-second safety judgments. Current humanoids lack the perception robustness, reliable signaling, and weather/terrain tolerance for this high-stakes role.

task: Select and implement corrosion control or mitigation systems for geothermal plants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting and implementing a corrosion control system for a single plant or major subsystem. A competent engineer spends ~4 hours on assessment, system selection, specification writing, and initial implementation oversight.
robot_success_rationale: Task is primarily cognitive/engineering: chemical analysis, materials science judgment, system design, procurement coordination, and regulatory compliance. No current robot can perform autonomous engineering design or systems-level decision-making at this level.

task: Operate experimental pilot plants, assisting with experimental design.
human_time_rationale: One instance = running a single experimental pilot plant trial/run, including setup, monitoring, adjustment, and shutdown. Typical runs last several hours; 4 hrs is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex cognitive tasks: experimental design, interpreting sensor data, making adaptive decisions, operating specialized valves/controls in unstructured industrial environments. Far beyond current general-purpose robot autonomy.

task: Confiscate dangerous items and hazardous materials found in opened bags and turn them over to airlines for disposal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = confiscating one item from one opened bag and handing it off to airline staff. A trained TSA officer completes this in ~3–7 minutes including documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying dangerous/hazardous items (complex perception/classification), safely handling unknown materials, navigating a crowded checkpoint, and coordinating handoff to airline staff. Current robots lack reliable hazmat recognition, safe grasp strategies for irregular dangerous items, and situational judgment. Highly unstructured, safety-critical context.

task: Start power winches that draw in or let out cables to change positions of dredges, or pull in and let out cables manually.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repositioning a dredge via winch (powered or manual), from setup to confirmed new position. Typical cable operation takes 10–20 minutes depending on distance and conditions.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating marine/industrial winch controls on a dredge in a wet, unstable, outdoor environment. Current humanoids lack the mobility, tool interfacing, and safety-critical judgment for marine vessel operations.

task: Separate models or patterns from molds and examine products for accuracy.
human_time_rationale: One instance = separating one model/pattern from mold and inspecting it for accuracy; a skilled worker takes ~5 min per part.
robot_success_rationale: Demolding requires precise force modulation and dexterous grip to avoid damaging delicate parts; inspection requires nuanced tactile and visual quality judgment. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity and calibrated force control needed reliably.

task: Develop or conduct programs of sampling and analysis to maintain quality standards of raw materials, chemical intermediates, or products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one sampling-and-analysis cycle for a single batch or lot. A competent chemist/quality technician typically spends ~30–90 min collecting samples, running analyses, and recording results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires designing/adapting sampling programs (cognitive), operating lab instruments (e.g., HPLC, titrators), interpreting results against specs, and making QC decisions. Current humanoids lack lab instrument integration, precise fluid handling, and analytical judgment needed end-to-end.

task: Teach fitness classes to improve strength, flexibility, cardiovascular conditioning, or general fitness of participants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one fitness class session. Typical group fitness classes run 45–60 minutes; 55 min accounts for setup, instruction, and cool-down.
robot_success_rationale: Teaching a class requires adaptive verbal instruction, real-time participant monitoring, motivational coaching, safety corrections, and social rapport — far beyond current robot generalization. No frontier humanoid can lead, cue, and safely supervise a live group fitness class.

task: Test baggage for any explosive materials, using equipment such as explosive detection machines or chemical swab systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = screening one piece of baggage. A TSA/security officer swabs or feeds a bag through an ETD machine, interprets results, and clears or escalates — typically 2–4 minutes per bag.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise swabbing of irregular bag surfaces, operating specialized ETD consoles, interpreting alarm outputs, and making judgment calls. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, certified tool integration, and decision-making needed for regulated security screening.

task: Isolate and maintain cultures of bacteria or other microorganisms in prescribed or developed media, controlling moisture, aeration, temperature, and nutrition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = isolating a culture and setting up maintenance conditions (plating, inoculation, incubator setup). A competent microbiologist takes ~30–60 min per culture session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile technique, precise pipetting, aseptic media handling, environmental monitoring, and scientific judgment. Current humanoids lack lab-grade dexterity, sterile field awareness, and autonomous decision-making for culture health assessment.

task: Maintain or arrange for maintenance of fitness equipment or facilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and performing routine maintenance on a typical set of gym equipment (e.g., treadmills, weight machines) in one facility session; ~30–60 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing mechanical/electrical faults, lubricating parts, tightening bolts, and coordinating with vendors — complex tool use, judgment, and unstructured multi-step reasoning far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Monitor, inspect, or test mechanical equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single piece of mechanical equipment (e.g., a pump, motor, or conveyor section). A competent technician typically spends ~30 min on checks, readings, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can walk to equipment and use cameras for visual checks, but interpreting gauges, attaching sensors, performing functional tests, and logging structured findings requires dexterity, contextual judgment, and tool use that 2026 robots handle poorly in unstructured industrial environments.

task: Operate heavy equipment, such as backhoes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a typical half-day operating session (e.g., digging a trench or foundation cut). A competent operator runs equipment for a sustained productive shift segment, ~4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot physically operate a backhoe's cab controls, pedals, and joysticks in dynamic outdoor terrain. No 2026 general-purpose robot has demonstrated autonomous heavy equipment operation in unstructured jobsite conditions.

task: Bag, box, wrap, or gift-wrap merchandise, and prepare packages for shipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = packaging a single item or order (bagging, boxing, wrapping, or gift-wrapping one package). Typical retail/fulfillment worker takes ~3–7 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied dexterous manipulation: folding paper, taping, sizing boxes, handling irregular items. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-motor control for wrapping, ribbon-tying, and adaptive packing of diverse merchandise shapes.

task: Store items in an orderly and accessible manner in warehouses, tool rooms, supply rooms, or other areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = organizing/stowing one batch of incoming items (e.g., a cart or pallet load) into their designated locations in a storage area. A competent worker takes ~30 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied storage environments, identifying diverse items, placing them precisely on shelves/bins at varying heights, and maintaining organizational logic. Current humanoids handle structured pick-and-place but struggle with unstructured environments, varied item types, and high-shelf access.

task: Verify part dimensions or clearances to ensure conformance to specifications, using precision measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single part with calipers/micrometers/gauges and recording results. A skilled technician takes ~5–10 minutes per part including setup and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise grasping of measuring instruments (calipers, micrometers), correct seating on part features, and reading analog/digital displays. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity and tactile feedback needed for reliable, calibrated measurement; perception of small tolerances is a key bottleneck.

task: Inspect camp sites to ensure that campers are in compliance with forest use regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single campsite. A ranger walks the site, checks fire rings, permits, waste, and equipment for regulation compliance — roughly 15–25 minutes per site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/rough terrain locomotion, reading permits, identifying subtle violations (e.g., illegal fire rings, waste), and social interaction with campers. Unstructured outdoor environments, nuanced judgment, and human communication far exceed current robot generalization.

task: Investigate health-related complaints and inspect facilities to ensure that they comply with public health legislation and regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one facility inspection or complaint visit, covering on-site walk-through, sampling, documentation, and compliance judgment. Typically 1–3 hours total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires legal/regulatory reasoning, interviewing complainants, contextual judgment, report writing, and authority to cite violations. Beyond physical mobility, cognitive, social, and legal-interpretation demands are far outside current robot capability.

task: Dismount, mount, and repair or replace tires.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dismount, repair/replace, and remount one tire on a standard passenger vehicle, including bead breaking, patching or swapping, and re-inflation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a tire changer machine, balancer, and impact wrench; high-force torque application; fine inspection for punctures. No frontier humanoid can operate specialized shop machinery autonomously in 2026.

task: Repair parts or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one repair job on a typical part or piece of equipment (e.g. replacing a component, fixing a mechanism). Median skilled-trade repair takes ~1 hour.
robot_success_rationale: Repairs demand highly variable dexterity (fasteners, wiring, seals), tool selection, fault diagnosis, and force control in unstructured configurations. Current humanoids lack reliable generalization for multi-step, tool-using repair tasks.

task: Adjust lenses and frames to correct alignment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single pair of eyeglasses. A skilled optician inspects, bends frame/temples, and verifies alignment in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterity to bend delicate metal/plastic frames without breaking them, precise perception of sub-millimeter misalignment, and tactile feedback. Far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Issue or distribute materials, products, parts, and supplies to customers or coworkers, based on information from incoming requisitions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fulfilling a single requisition: locating items in storage, picking/staging them, and handing off to recipient. Typical storeroom transaction takes ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting requisitions, navigating a real storeroom, identifying and retrieving diverse items, and handing off to humans. Current robots struggle with unstructured inventory environments, varied item types, and social hand-off interactions.

task: Inspect, test, or troubleshoot malfunctioning medical or related equipment, following manufacturers' specifications and using test and analysis instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and troubleshooting a single malfunctioning medical device. A competent biomedical tech typically spends 45–90 minutes per device, including testing and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading manufacturer specs, interpreting complex test instruments, adaptive diagnostic reasoning, fine dexterous probe placement, and safety-critical judgment. Far beyond current frontier humanoid generalization and tool-use capabilities.

task: Conduct chemical or physical laboratory tests to assist scientists in making qualitative or quantitative analyses of solids, liquids, or gaseous materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one standard lab test (e.g., titration, spectroscopy run, or pH analysis). A competent lab technician typically spends ~30–60 min including setup, execution, and recording results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous manipulation (pipetting, operating instruments), chemical safety awareness, interpreting results, and adapting to unstructured lab environments. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and fine motor capability.

task: Conduct experiments to test new or alternate feedstock fermentation processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up, running, and monitoring a single fermentation experiment (inoculation to initial data collection). A competent lab technician typically takes 3–5 hours for one experimental run.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting, sterile technique, instrument calibration, hypothesis-driven decision-making, and adaptive protocol adjustments. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, fine motor control, and cognitive autonomy in unstructured lab settings.

task: Use equipment designed to join sheet metal, such as spot welders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = joining one sheet metal assembly (e.g., a panel or bracket) using a spot welder; includes positioning, clamping, and completing the weld pattern (~15 min).
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment, firm bimanual clamping, tool operation (spot welder triggers/controls), and quality judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable force control, tool-use dexterity, and welding process feedback needed for acceptable joins.

task: Stamp, attach, or change price tags on merchandise, referring to price list.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stamping/attaching/changing one price tag on one item. A competent worker can locate the price, apply or swap a tag in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle small tags/stickers, read a price list, and precisely place labels on varied merchandise. Current humanoids struggle with small object manipulation, tag tools, and cross-referencing printed/digital lists reliably.

task: Weigh items sold by weight to determine prices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = weighing a single item/batch on a scale and reading/recording the price. A competent worker does this in ~1–2 minutes.
