robot_success_rationale: Robot can pick and place items on a scale and read a digital display via vision, but variable item shapes, fragility, and correctly associating weight to price on a POS system reduce reliability. Flat surfaces help.

task: Conduct field inspections of biomass plants, stations, or substations to ensure normal and safe operating conditions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full inspection tour of one biomass plant/substation; inspector walks facility, checks equipment, reads gauges, and documents findings — typically 60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex, cluttered industrial outdoor/indoor environments, reading varied analog/digital gauges, identifying subtle anomalies (leaks, odors, sounds), and making safety judgments. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor mobility, domain-specific sensor interpretation, and contextual safety reasoning at required quality.

task: Perform specific protocol procedures such as interviewing subjects, taking vital signs, and performing electrocardiograms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full protocol session per subject: ~10 min interview, ~10 min vitals, ~20 min ECG setup/recording, plus documentation. Typical clinical research visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires natural-language interviewing, fine dexterous electrode placement, blood pressure cuffing, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack conversational depth, precise skin-contact finesse, and regulatory-grade autonomy for human subjects.

task: Develop lab scale models of industrial scale processes, such as fermentation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and assembling one lab-scale model (e.g., a bench-top bioreactor). Includes setup, calibration, and initial validation run — typically a full working day for a skilled technician.
robot_success_rationale: Task demands high-level scientific reasoning, process engineering design, precise assembly of glass/tubing/sensors, and experimental validation. Current robots lack the cognitive planning, domain expertise, and fine dexterity required for this end-to-end scientific workflow.

task: Operate 10-key calculators, typewriters, and copy machines to perform calculations and produce documents.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a typical office calculation/document task (e.g., running a column of figures and producing a short document). ~15 min covers data entry, calculation, and copying.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise key-by-key data entry on small-keyed devices, reading source documents, interpreting numeric data, and operating multiple distinct machines in sequence. Fine dexterity, OCR accuracy, and multi-device tool-use are all weak spots for current humanoids.

task: Search prisoners and vehicles and conduct shakedowns of cells for valuables and contraband, such as weapons or drugs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = searching one prisoner/vehicle OR conducting one cell shakedown. A pat-down takes ~5 min; a thorough cell shakedown takes ~20–30 min. Median ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced physical pat-downs on uncooperative humans (safety/legal risk), fine dexterity to find concealed contraband, authoritative social presence, and legal accountability. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capability in correctional settings.

task: Move and store inventories of explosives, loaded perforating guns, and other materials, according to established safety procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving and storing a typical inventory lot of explosives/perforating guns in a single session. Competent workers take ~1 hour accounting for safety checks, documentation, and careful handling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating in hazardous/outdoor oilfield environments, handling irregular explosive-laden equipment, following strict regulatory safety protocols, precise dexterous placement, and situational judgment — all far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Operate drying chambers to dry or finish molded ceramic ware.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a batch of molded ceramic ware into a drying chamber, setting parameters, and unloading when dry. Competent worker completes this in ~20 minutes excluding passive drying time.
robot_success_rationale: Ceramic ware is fragile and irregularly shaped, demanding careful handling. Robot must also adjust chamber controls, inspect ware condition, and navigate industrial environments — challenging for current humanoids lacking specialized perception and delicate grip reliability.

task: Verify that raw materials, purchased parts or components, in-process samples, and finished products meet established testing and inspection standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one batch/lot inspection including sampling, physical/instrument testing, and pass/fail documentation; ~30 min for a competent QC technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse instrument operation (calipers, spectrometers, gauges), interpretation of results against standards, documentation, and adaptive judgment across material types. Current robots lack reliable multi-instrument tool use, contextual standard-lookup, and documentation workflows.

task: Place new merchandise on display.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stocking a batch of new merchandise onto a display (e.g., a shelf section or display table). A competent retail worker takes ~15–25 min to unbox, arrange, and face products.
robot_success_rationale: Requires unboxing varied items, reading labels/planograms, navigating cluttered retail aisles, and placing products neatly and facing correctly. Perception of diverse SKUs, fine placement, and planogram compliance are well beyond current general-purpose robot reliability.

task: Set flares, flags, lanterns, or torpedoes in front and at rear of trains during emergency stops to warn oncoming trains.
human_time_rationale: One instance = deploying warning devices (flares/flags/lanterns/torpedoes) at both ends of a stopped train during one emergency stop; a trained railworker completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor locomotion on/near active rail tracks, carrying and deploying pyrotechnic devices (flares, torpedoes), navigating uneven ballast terrain, and operating in emergency conditions. Current humanoids lack rail-environment mobility, tool handling for pyrotechnics, and the required safety autonomy.

task: Count currency, coins, and checks received, by hand or using currency-counting machine, to prepare them for deposit or shipment to branch banks or the Federal Reserve Bank.
human_time_rationale: One instance = counting and reconciling a typical batch of currency, coins, and checks for a single deposit preparation; a bank teller typically spends ~15–25 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous handling of paper bills, coins, and checks; operating counting machines; reading denominations; and accurate tallying. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation, OCR-in-context, and the precision needed for financial accuracy standards.

task: Set up equipment or stations to monitor and collect pollutants from sites, such as smoke stacks, manufacturing plants, or mechanical equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up monitoring equipment at a single site (e.g., one smoke stack). A competent technician typically spends 60–120 minutes installing, calibrating, and verifying sensors.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized tools, precise probe insertion into stacks, electrical/pneumatic connections, calibration software interaction, outdoor/industrial terrain navigation, and complex multi-step sequencing. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Monitor the fitting, burning, and welding processes to avoid overheating of parts or warping, shrinking, distortion, or expansion of material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = monitoring a single welding/fitting/burning job from start to finish. A typical weld joint or small assembly takes ~20–40 min to complete under supervision.
robot_success_rationale: Requires continuous multi-modal sensing (thermal, visual, tactile), real-time judgment, and adaptive intervention in a dynamic, high-heat environment. Current humanoids lack reliable thermal monitoring integration, nuanced material-state interpretation, and safe operation near open flames/arc welding.

task: Examine patients to diagnose oral health conditions and diseases.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient oral examination. A competent dentist typically spends 20–40 minutes examining, probing, and documenting findings for one patient.
robot_success_rationale: Requires licensed clinical judgment, fine intraoral dexterity with dental instruments, patient interaction/communication, tactile probing, and diagnostic reasoning. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capabilities; no deployed system approaches this.

task: Monitor or inspect hydroelectric equipment, such as hydro-turbines, generators, or control systems.
human_time_rationale: One inspection round of a hydro facility (turbines, generators, control systems) by a competent operator; includes visual checks, readings, and logging — typically 45–90 min per shift walk-through.
robot_success_rationale: Hydroelectric plants involve wet, confined, and hazardous environments, specialized gauges, multi-level navigation, and expert interpretation of anomalies. Current humanoids lack waterproofing, confined-space mobility, and domain-specific sensor-reading generalization needed for autonomous acceptance-quality inspection.

task: Lay gas and oil pipelines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying a standard segment/section of pipeline (e.g., one day's crew progress, ~100–200 ft of trench, pipe lowering, and joining). A full crew completes this in a typical 8-hour shift.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy excavators, side-booms, and welding rigs in unstructured outdoor terrain. Humanoid robots cannot drive or operate such specialized heavy machinery, and the task demands coordinated multi-machine crew work, precision welding, and hazardous-environment safety compliance far beyond current robot capabilities.

task: Check the quality of raw or cooked food products to ensure that standards are met.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single food item or small batch. A competent worker visually, tactilely, and olfactorily checks quality in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Visual inspection possible with machine vision, but assessing texture, smell, taste, and subtle defects across varied raw/cooked foods requires multi-modal sensory integration far beyond current robot capability. Generalization across food types is very limited.

task: Adjust equipment controls to generate specified amounts of electrical power.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a set of controls (dials, switches, levers) on a power generation panel to hit a specified output target; a trained operator typically takes ~5 minutes per adjustment cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting live feedback, and precisely manipulating industrial control panels (knobs, switches, levers). Current humanoids lack reliable gauge-reading, closed-loop fine-tuning, and safe operation in high-voltage industrial environments.

task: Mark and store parts in stockrooms, according to prearranged systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking and storing a batch of parts in a stockroom. A competent worker takes ~15 min to label, organize, and shelve a typical batch.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place on flat shelves is feasible, but reading/applying labels, navigating cluttered stockrooms, handling varied part shapes, and following a prearranged organizational system all challenge 2026 humanoids significantly.

task: Gather materials to be filed from departments or employees.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a batch of filing materials from multiple departments/desks in a typical office. Includes walking, interacting with staff, and gathering documents.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating dynamic office environments, identifying correct materials, interacting socially with staff, and handling loose/varied paper documents. Current robots struggle with unstructured social interaction, document identification, and mixed-item retrieval across multiple locations.

task: Conduct well field site assessments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing a single well field site. A competent technician typically spends 1–3 hours inspecting equipment, measuring water levels, checking infrastructure, and documenting findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/unstructured terrain navigation, operating specialized field instruments (water level meters, flow gauges), reading gauges, and producing compliant assessment reports. Current humanoids lack the mobility, tool-use, and domain judgment for full autonomy.

task: Calibrate microscopes or test instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = calibrating a single microscope or test instrument; a competent technician typically takes 20–40 minutes including setup, reference standards, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of tiny adjustment knobs/dials, precise optical alignment, reading analog/digital readouts, and judgment-based verification against standards — well beyond current humanoid robot dexterity and perception reliability.

task: Examine returned parts for defects, and exchange defective parts or refund money.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one returned part and processing the exchange/refund. A competent worker visually/physically checks the part and completes the transaction in ~8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Visual defect inspection on varied parts is improving but unreliable; processing a refund/exchange requires customer interaction, POS system use, and judgment calls robots cannot reliably handle autonomously in 2026.

task: Shut down and restart biofuels plant or equipment in emergency situations or for equipment maintenance, repairs, or replacements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single emergency or planned shutdown-and-restart cycle of a biofuels plant unit. A competent operator typically takes 45–90 min for a coordinated, safe shutdown and restart sequence.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial control panels, valves, PLCs, and safety interlocks across a large, hazardous plant environment. Current humanoids lack the plant-specific tool-use, procedural judgment, and safety certification needed. Far beyond 2026 frontier capability.

task: Conduct, or oversee the conduct of, chemical, physical, and biological water quality monitoring or sampling to ensure compliance with water quality standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one water quality sampling/monitoring round at a site (travel to site, collect samples at multiple points, log data, pack samples). Typically 60–120 min for a competent field technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied outdoor/aquatic terrain, operating sampling equipment, handling chemical reagents, and making compliance judgments. Current humanoids lack waterproof mobility, fine fluid-handling dexterity, and autonomous field generalization needed for unsupervised sampling.

task: Take fingerprints.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fingerprinting one person (all 10 fingers). A trained human takes ~3–7 minutes using ink/paper or a digital scanner.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, compliant pressure on each fingertip, correct rolling motion, and cooperative subject positioning. Current robots lack the fine dexterity, force control, and procedural exactness needed for legally/forensically acceptable prints.

task: Dispense medical devices or drugs, and calculate dosages and provide instructions as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dispensing a single prescription/dose to one patient, including dosage calculation and verbal/written instructions. Typical pharmacy or clinical encounter.
robot_success_rationale: Requires cognitive dosage calculation, regulatory compliance, patient-specific judgment, precise fine-motor dispensing, and clear verbal/written instruction. Current humanoids lack the cognitive integration, pharmaceutical knowledge, and regulatory authority for autonomous dispensing.

task: Compose images of products, using video or still cameras, lighting equipment, props, or photo or video editing software.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one product photography/video shoot setup and capture. Includes arranging props, adjusting lighting, framing shots, and basic editing — typically 30–60 min per product.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative composition judgment, nuanced lighting adjustment, prop styling, and skilled use of cameras/editing software — far beyond current robot generalization. No humanoid robot can operate cameras or editing tools at professional quality.

task: Clean, refuel, and change oil in line service aircraft.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one general aviation line-service aircraft (interior wipe-down, windows, exterior surfaces). A competent line technician takes ~45 min per aircraft.
robot_success_rationale: Aircraft surfaces are varied, curved, and at height; access requires ladders or lifts; windows and control surfaces need careful pressure. Current humanoids lack the mobility, reach, and aviation-specific tool use required.

task: Test soils or materials to determine the adequacy and strength of foundations, concrete, asphalt, or steel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one soil/material test (e.g., a standard compaction or compression test). Includes sample collection, setup, running test, and recording results — typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized lab/field equipment (compactors, load frames, sieves), precise sample handling, and interpreting analog or digital gauges. Current humanoids lack tool-use generalization, fine dexterity for specimen prep, and domain-specific instrument operation in unstructured outdoor/lab settings.

task: Direct installation of covert surveillance equipment, such as security cameras.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one covert camera (mounting, routing cables/power, aiming, testing). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires drilling into walls/ceilings, precise concealment in tight/unusual spots, cable routing, and tool use in highly unstructured environments. Far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity and generalization limits.

task: Search carry-on or checked baggage by hand when it is suspected to contain prohibited items such as weapons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = manually searching one bag. A competent TSA/security officer typically takes 3–7 minutes to thoroughly hand-search a single suspect bag.
robot_success_rationale: Requires unzipping varied bag types, tactile probing of irregular contents, judgment on prohibited items, legal/procedural compliance, and passenger interaction. Far beyond current robot dexterity and contextual reasoning capabilities.

task: Measure parts, using precision measuring instruments, to determine whether similar parts may be machined to required sizes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single part with calipers/micrometers and recording results. A skilled machinist typically takes 5–10 minutes per part including setup and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise grasping and positioning of small measuring instruments (calipers, micrometers), fine dexterous manipulation, accurate seating of tools on part features, and reading analog/digital gauges — all well beyond current humanoid dexterity and perception reliability.

task: Operate and maintain laboratory equipment and apparatus.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single operate-and-maintain session on one piece of lab equipment (e.g., centrifuge, spectrophotometer). Includes setup, operation, basic cleaning/calibration check.
robot_success_rationale: Lab equipment spans highly varied interfaces (knobs, touchscreens, fine connectors, glass vessels). Robots lack reliable generalization for operating arbitrary instruments, reading displays, and performing precise maintenance like recalibrating or replacing consumables.

task: Demonstrate proper operation of fitness equipment, such as resistance machines, cardio machines, free weights, or fitness assessment devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = demonstrating proper use of one piece of equipment to a client. A trainer typically spends ~10 min covering setup, form, range of motion, and safety cues for a single machine or exercise.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fluid, precise athletic movement on varied equipment, real-time social communication, and dynamic balance under load. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control, expressive coaching ability, and safe load-handling needed to demonstrate convincingly and safely.

task: Weigh ingredients, such as dye, to be mixed together for use in textile processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = weighing and staging one batch of ingredients (e.g., dye + additives) for a single mix order. A competent worker reads the formula, tares the scale, scoops/pours each ingredient, and records the weight in ~5 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle powders/liquids, precision scooping, reading a scale display, and avoiding cross-contamination. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation with granular/liquid materials and precise weight targeting in unstructured lab/floor settings.

task: Test and repair track circuits.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one track circuit section tested and repaired. A signal technician typically takes 45–90 min including setup, diagnostics, and repair work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized rail signal test equipment, traversing live rail environments, diagnosing electrical faults, and performing precise wiring repairs. Current humanoids lack the domain tools, outdoor rail mobility, and diagnostic reasoning needed.

task: Adjust temperature, pressure, vacuum, level, flow rate, or transfer of biofuels to maintain processes at required levels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single adjustment event (reading gauges/sensors, identifying deviation, and making the necessary valve/control adjustments) for one process parameter in a biofuel facility.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting process states, operating industrial valves and control panels in complex facility environments. Current humanoids lack reliable perception of small gauge readings, precise valve operation, and process-level reasoning needed for safe biofuel control.

task: Collect evidence from crime scenes, storing it in conditions that preserve its integrity.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single crime scene. A competent forensic tech spends ~60–120 min photographing, cataloguing, bagging, and labeling evidence items at a typical scene.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced perception to spot trace evidence, precise dexterous handling to avoid contamination, chain-of-custody documentation, contextual judgment, and operation in completely unstructured environments — all far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Place food servings on plates or trays according to orders or instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = plating a single order. A competent worker reads the order, portions, and arranges food on a plate/tray in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting varied orders, handling soft/irregularly shaped foods, precise portioning, and aesthetic placement — all challenging for current humanoids. Perception of food items and gentle dexterous manipulation remain major limitations.

task: Patrol work areas to detect any suspicious items.
human_time_rationale: One patrol instance = a single circuit of a defined work area (e.g., warehouse floor, office building floor). A competent security officer typically takes 20–40 minutes per round.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate and visually scan structured indoor spaces, but reliably detecting contextually "suspicious" items requires nuanced semantic understanding, anomaly reasoning, and judgment that current systems lack. Cluttered or outdoor environments add locomotion risk.

task: Audit and inspect subcontractor facilities including external laboratories.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one facility audit visit. A competent auditor typically spends 2–5 hours on-site inspecting a subcontractor or external lab, averaging ~4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Auditing requires reading documents, interviewing staff, exercising professional judgment, interpreting regulatory standards, and writing formal findings. Far beyond current robot cognitive/social capabilities; locomotion alone is insufficient.

task: Monitor meters, flow gauges, or other real-time data to ensure proper operation of biofuels production equipment, implementing corrective measures as needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring round/shift check of gauges and meters; a competent operator typically completes a full instrument walkthrough in ~20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading diverse analog/digital gauges, interpreting process deviations, and executing corrective actions (adjusting valves, controls) in complex industrial environments. Current humanoids lack reliable gauge-reading perception, process knowledge, and dexterous valve operation at this fidelity.

task: Prepare solutions, reagents, or sample formulations needed for laboratory work.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single solution/reagent/formulation. Competent lab tech spends ~10–40 min measuring, mixing, adjusting pH, and labeling; 25 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (pipetting, weighing milligram quantities), reading instrument displays, precise liquid handling, pH adjustment, and safety compliance. Far beyond reliable autonomous capability of 2026 humanoids.

task: Deliver death certificates to medical facilities or offices to obtain signatures from legally authorized persons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = delivering a death certificate to one facility and obtaining a signature. Includes travel to the facility, locating the authorized person, waiting, and securing signature — typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving to external facilities (humanoids cannot operate vehicles), navigating unfamiliar buildings, identifying the correct legally authorized person, and obtaining a valid witnessed signature — far beyond current autonomous robot capabilities.

task: Visit properties to assess them before showing them to clients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = visiting and assessing one property. A competent real estate agent typically spends 30–60 minutes walking through, noting condition, features, and suitability for clients.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied, unfamiliar indoor/outdoor environments, nuanced aesthetic and structural judgment, client-context reasoning, and often operating door locks/gates. Current robots lack reliable generalization across unstructured real-world properties and high-level evaluative judgment.

task: Replace hydraulic hoses, headlight bulbs, and gathering-arm teeth.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing all hydraulic hoses on a single machine. A competent mechanic depressurizes, disconnects, routes, and reconnects hoses; ~45–90 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force torque on fittings, routing hoses through tight channels, managing fluid spillage, and using specialized crimping/flare tools. Current humanoids lack the force control, tool dexterity, and domain-specific knowledge needed.

task: Adjust timing regulators, using truing calipers, watch-rate recorders, and tweezers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting the timing regulator on a single watch movement, including measurement, micro-adjustment, and re-testing with a watch-rate recorder.
robot_success_rationale: Extremely fine dexterity required with sub-millimeter tweezers and truing calipers on delicate watch components. Haptic precision, tool compliance, and micro-manipulation far exceed current humanoid robot capability.

task: Operate and maintain mobile dispatch vehicles and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one dispatch shift segment or a single vehicle deployment/maintenance cycle; ~60 min covers typical operation plus routine checks between dispatches.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot drive vehicles in traffic; mobile dispatch vehicles require road operation, real-time navigation, and interaction with specialized equipment — all far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Examine workpieces for defects and measure workpieces with straightedges or templates to ensure conformance with specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single workpiece. A competent worker visually checks for defects and measures with a straightedge or template in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is improving but generalizing across varied defect types is hard. Precisely aligning and reading a straightedge or template against a workpiece requires fine dexterity and tactile feedback that current humanoids lack reliably.

task: Process new materials including books, audio-visual materials, and computer software.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one item (book, AV, or software): stamping, labeling, barcoding, cataloging entry, and shelving prep. ~15 min median per item.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/cataloging metadata (cognitive), affixing precise labels, operating a keyboard/system, handling varied fragile media formats. Current humanoids lack the software integration and fine dexterity for reliable autonomous end-to-end processing.

task: Assist guests with special needs by providing equipment such as wheelchairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one guest: locating/retrieving a wheelchair, bringing it to the guest, and helping them get seated — typically 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires understanding guest needs via communication, navigating crowded public spaces, retrieving and unfolding a wheelchair, and safely assisting a person into it — combining social perception, dexterous manipulation, and delicate caregiving beyond current robot capability.

task: Mark or tag material with proper job number, piece marks, and other identifying marks as required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking a single piece of material with job number, piece marks, and ID marks. A competent worker typically takes ~5 minutes per piece using paint sticks, stamps, or markers.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting job documentation, selecting correct marks, and legibly writing or stamping on varied material surfaces. Current robots lack reliable handwriting, tool-switching, and mark-verification workflows; perception of correct info is also challenging.

task: Sort books, publications, and other items according to established procedure and return them to shelves, files, or other designated storage areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting and reshelving a typical batch (~20–30 items) in a library or office. A competent worker takes ~25–35 minutes including sorting, locating correct spots, and placing items.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading spine labels/call numbers (OCR + perception), navigating narrow library aisles, precisely locating correct shelf positions, and inserting items upright in order — complex multi-step cognitive + dexterous task beyond current humanoid generalization.

task: Design or fabricate experimental apparatus to develop new products or processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and fabricating a single experimental apparatus (e.g., a custom test rig). A competent R&D technician/engineer typically needs 2–4 hours for a moderately complex build.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative design reasoning, interpreting engineering specs, selecting non-standard materials/tools, precise custom fabrication, and iterative problem-solving. Far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and autonomous design capability.

task: Clean supplies such as makeup brushes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a set of ~5–10 makeup brushes: rinsing, working in cleanser, rinsing again, reshaping, and laying flat to dry.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate dexterous manipulation of small, flexible bristles under running water, applying gentle pressure, reshaping, and avoiding damage. Fine motor control and force sensitivity far exceed current humanoid robot capability.

task: Maintain a proper sterile field during surgical procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = maintaining a sterile field throughout a typical surgical procedure (~45 min median operative time), from draping through wound closure.
robot_success_rationale: Requires continuous contextual judgment, real-time breach detection, instrument handling with sterile technique, team coordination, and dynamic adaptation. Far beyond current robot dexterity, perception, and safety reliability in unstructured OR environments.

task: Fill customer orders by packaging sold items and documentation for direct shipping or by transferring orders to manufacturers or third-party distributors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fulfilling a single customer order: picking items, packaging, adding documentation, and labeling for shipment or routing to a third party. Typical e-commerce/warehouse order takes ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle structured pick-and-place moderately well, but varied item types, inserting documentation, selecting correct packaging, printing/affixing labels, and routing decisions require generalization, tool-use, and process logic that current humanoids struggle with reliably.

task: Inspect condition of premises, and arrange for necessary maintenance or notify owners of maintenance needs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single property/unit and logging maintenance needs. A competent property manager walks the space, checks systems, and notifies owners in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied premises, visually diagnosing subtle defects (leaks, cracks, wear), contextual judgment, and then communicating findings/arranging contractors — far beyond current autonomous robot capability in unstructured real-world environments.

task: Monitor food distribution, ensuring that meals are delivered to the correct recipients and that guidelines, such as those for special diets, are followed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = overseeing one full meal service round (e.g., a lunch distribution shift), checking trays match recipients and diet guidelines. Typically 30–60 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading diet labels, identifying individuals (face/ID recognition), navigating dynamic crowded spaces, communicating with recipients, and making judgment calls on exceptions — all simultaneously. Far beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Conduct pressure tests on vessels, such as boilers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pressure test on a single vessel (e.g., boiler). Includes setup, pressurization, monitoring, leak checks, and documentation — typically 45–90 minutes for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting specialized hoses/fittings, operating valves, reading gauges, and interpreting results on varied industrial equipment. Current humanoids lack the precise dexterity, tool-use generalization, and safety-critical judgment needed for autonomous pressure testing.

task: Conduct field surveillance to gather case-related information.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single field surveillance session for a case, typically involving travel to a site, observation, and data collection, averaging ~2 hours for a competent investigator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level contextual reasoning, covert navigation in unstructured outdoor/public environments, social judgment, legal compliance, and adaptive decision-making — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Inspect product load for accuracy and safely move it around the warehouse or facility to ensure timely and complete delivery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single product load and moving it to its destination within a warehouse. A competent worker typically spends ~5 min inspecting and ~15 min transporting.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids lack ability to operate forklifts or pallet jacks in traffic; load inspection requires reading labels/counts accurately; navigating a busy warehouse with varied obstacles and handoffs adds further difficulty. Current robots struggle with the full combined task.

task: Design or execute solvent or product recovery experiments in laboratory or field settings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and executing a single solvent/product recovery experiment. A competent chemist spends ~2 hours on setup, running, and initial data collection per experiment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced chemistry cognition, protocol design, precise liquid handling, operating specialized lab equipment (rotary evaporators, distillation columns), interpreting results, and adapting to unexpected outcomes — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Transport packages to customers' vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retrieving a package and walking it to one customer's vehicle in a parking lot; typically 1–5 minutes depending on distance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/parking-lot navigation over uneven surfaces, locating the correct vehicle, handing off to a customer, and handling varied package sizes/weights. Outdoor terrain, dynamic obstacles, and social handoff are still weak spots for 2026 humanoids.

task: Analyze gunshot residue and bullet paths to determine how shootings occurred.
human_time_rationale: One instance = analyzing GSR and bullet trajectory evidence for a single shooting scene. A forensic examiner typically spends 1–3 hours collecting samples, examining wounds/surfaces, and documenting findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level forensic perception (microscopy, chemical GSR analysis, wound interpretation), complex multi-modal reasoning, specialized lab tools, and legal-quality documentation. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Arrange monies received in cash boxes and coin dispensers according to denomination.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting and arranging one cash box or coin dispenser. A competent cashier organizes bills by denomination and loads coins into slots in ~8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous handling of thin, flexible bills and small coins, reliable denomination recognition across worn/varied currency, and precise placement into tight cash drawer slots — all at the edge of 2026 robot capability.

task: Demonstrate new cooking techniques or equipment to staff.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single demonstration session for one technique/equipment item, including setup, live demo, Q&A, and cleanup. Typical culinary demo runs 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex multi-step cooking, verbal explanation, adaptive teaching, and audience interaction. Current robots lack reliable cooking generalization, natural language instruction, and the social/pedagogical capability to "demonstrate" to humans acceptably.

task: Place materials into storage receptacles, such as file cabinets, boxes, bins, or drawers, according to classification and identification information.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing a single item (document, object, or small batch) into the correct receptacle. A competent worker reads the label/classification and files it in ~1–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle basic pick-and-place reasonably well, but reading classification labels, opening file cabinet drawers, navigating to the correct location, and reliably inserting varied items (folders, boxes, loose parts) in the right slot requires perception, dexterity, and semantic reasoning that current humanoids struggle with.

task: Determine where patients or patrons would like to eat their meals and help them get situated.
human_time_rationale: One instance = asking one patient/patron their preference, guiding or assisting them to a seat, and getting them settled. Typically 5–10 minutes per person.
robot_success_rationale: Requires natural language preference elicitation, social navigation around people, gentle physical assistance (guiding, steadying), and adaptive responses to mobility needs — all poorly handled together by current humanoids in unstructured care environments.

task: Check shipments when they arrive to ensure that orders have been filled correctly and that goods meet specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = verifying one arriving shipment: counting items, matching packing list, inspecting for damage/spec compliance. Typically 15–25 min per shipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading documents/labels, counting varied items, assessing quality/damage, and cross-referencing order specs. Current robots lack robust OCR-integrated reasoning, adaptive inspection of diverse goods, and reliable handling of unstructured shipment contents.

task: Mail newsletters, promotional material, or other information.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and mailing a batch of newsletters/promo materials (stuffing, sealing, labeling ~50 envelopes). A competent worker takes ~30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires inserting folded paper into envelopes, sealing, applying labels, and sorting — all fine dexterous manipulation with limp/deformable materials. Current humanoids struggle greatly with paper handling, folding accuracy, and label placement consistency.

task: Sort and file deposit slips and checks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a typical daily batch of deposit slips and checks; a competent clerk sorts, organizes, and files into labeled folders/drawers in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading fine printed text on varied paper documents, precise paper handling (thin, floppy, easily damaged), semantic categorization, and navigating filing systems. Current robots lack reliable fine-print OCR-integrated dexterous paper manipulation and filing cabinet navigation.

task: Collect impressions of dust from surfaces to obtain and identify fingerprints.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a dust/latent fingerprint impression from a single surface area. Competent forensic tech applies powder, brush, lifts tape, and mounts on card in ~5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterous brushing with calibrated pressure, precise tape lifting without smearing, and forensic-quality perception of latent prints. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tactile sensitivity; no demonstrated capability.

task: Retrieve documents stored in microfilm or microfiche and place them in viewers for reading.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating a single microfilm/microfiche from storage, retrieving it, and loading it into a viewer ready for reading. A competent worker takes ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle tiny, fragile film cartridges/sheets, precise alignment into specialized viewer slots, navigation of filing cabinets/drawers, and recognition of small labels. Current humanoids lack the precision and task-specific tool knowledge needed.

task: Repair, rework, or calibrate hydraulic or pneumatic assemblies or systems to meet operational specifications or tolerances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing or calibrating a single hydraulic/pneumatic assembly. A competent technician typically spends 60–120 min diagnosing, disassembling, reworking, reassembling, and verifying tolerances.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level diagnosis, fine dexterous manipulation of seals/fittings/valves, pressure testing, and precision calibration. Current humanoids lack the tool-use fluency, force-controlled fine dexterity, and domain-specific sensing needed for autonomous success.

task: Transmit, deliver, or mail publication master to printer for production into film and plates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and sending one publication master (digital or physical) to a printer via upload, courier, or mail. Includes packaging/labeling or file transfer steps.
robot_success_rationale: Task is primarily digital (file transfer, email, FTP upload) or requires driving to a courier. Humanoid robots cannot operate computers autonomously for complex workflows, access mailing systems, or drive deliveries. Extremely low feasibility.

task: Stock service stations with items, such as ice, napkins, or straws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restocking a single service station (e.g., refilling ice, napkins, straws). A competent worker typically completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to stations and pick/place common items, but varied container shapes (napkin dispensers, ice bins, straw holders), refill mechanisms, and judgment on fill levels pose significant perception and dexterity challenges for current humanoids.

task: Load trays with accessories, such as eating utensils, napkins, or condiments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading one tray with a standard set of utensils, napkins, and condiments. A cafeteria worker completes this in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Picking varied, lightweight items (napkins, utensils, small bottles) requires dexterous grasping and precise placement. Napkins are deformable, utensils are thin/slippery, and tray layout must be neat — challenging for 2026 humanoids but partially feasible in structured settings.

task: Examine and analyze material for presence and concentration of contaminants, such as asbestos, using variety of microscopes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = analyzing a single sample for contaminants (prep, microscope setup, examination, documentation). A trained analyst typically spends 30–60 minutes per sample.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous sample preparation, precise microscope operation/adjustment, and expert visual interpretation of microscopic imagery. Current humanoids lack the micro-dexterity, instrument interfacing, and analytical judgment needed for autonomous contaminant identification.

task: Date-stamp, sort, and rack incoming mail and messages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a typical batch of incoming mail/messages (e.g., a morning delivery of ~10–20 items): stamping, sorting by recipient/category, and placing in labeled racks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting variable text on envelopes, operating a date-stamp tool precisely, sorting by semantic categories, and placing items in labeled racks — combined perception, fine dexterity, and semantic reasoning beyond 2026 frontier robots' reliable capability.

task: Make copies of correspondence or other printed material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = copying a single document (1–5 pages). Includes walking to copier, placing document, selecting settings, and retrieving copies. ~3 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to a copier, precisely placing thin paper sheets on a glass platen or into a feeder, and operating a touchscreen interface — fine dexterity with limp paper and small UI controls remains a major limitation for current humanoids.

task: Examine and inspect stock items for wear or defects, reporting any damage to supervisors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single stock item. A competent worker visually and physically checks one item for wear/defects and notes findings in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is improving but generalizing across varied item types, subtle wear patterns, and unstructured stock environments is unreliable. Reporting to supervisors adds communication/social complexity beyond current robot capability.

task: Examine firearms to determine mechanical condition and legal status, performing restoration work on damaged firearms to obtain information, such as serial numbers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one firearm. Examiner checks mechanical function, legal status, and restores obliterated markings (e.g., acid/chemical etching). Typically 30–60 min per firearm.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly specialized forensic techniques (acid etching, microscopy), expert legal knowledge, fine dexterous manipulation of sensitive parts, and professional judgment. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Clean display cases, shelves, and aisles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning display cases, shelves, and aisles in a typical retail section. A competent worker wiping surfaces, dusting, and sweeping/mopping takes ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Flat floor sweeping/mopping is feasible (~50-70%), but wiping glass display cases and shelves at varied heights with acceptable quality requires precise force control, reach, and generalization. Multi-surface variety and obstacle avoidance in cluttered aisles lower reliability significantly.

task: Scrape and stack dirty dishes and carry dishes and other tableware to kitchens for cleaning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing and transporting one table's worth of dirty dishes to the kitchen; a competent busser typically completes this in ~8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires scraping food waste, stacking varied dish shapes/sizes, carrying loaded trays while navigating crowded restaurant floors. Fragile items, irregular surfaces, and collision avoidance in dynamic spaces challenge current humanoid dexterity and mobility.

task: Deposit guests' valuables in hotel safes or safe-deposit boxes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = receiving one guest's valuables and securing them in a safe/safe-deposit box, including logging and handing back a receipt or key. Typically 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine manipulation of small items and varied containers, operating keypads/locks, guest interaction, and secure logging. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity, social interaction, and secure transaction workflows for this context.

task: Determine how food should be presented and create decorative food displays.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and assembling one decorative food display. A skilled food stylist/chef takes ~30–60 min per display.
robot_success_rationale: Requires aesthetic judgment, creative design decisions, fine dexterous manipulation of fragile/irregular food items, and improvisation. Far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and artistic reasoning capabilities.

task: Measure heights and widths of loads to ensure they will pass over bridges or through tunnels on scheduled routes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single load (height + width) before a trip. A competent worker uses a tape measure or laser tool, checks dimensions against route clearances, and documents results — ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can use vision/LiDAR to estimate dimensions but lack reliable precision for regulatory clearance checks, struggle with uneven load shapes, and cannot autonomously cross-reference route clearance databases or operate measuring tools with required accuracy.

task: Roll out, measure, mark, and cut carpeting to size with a carpet knife, following floor sketches and allowing extra carpet for final fitting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting a single room's carpet piece. Rolling out, measuring, marking, and cutting a typical room carpet takes a skilled installer ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting floor sketches, precise measuring/marking on a large flexible material, controlled carpet-knife cutting along a line, and managing a bulky heavy roll — far beyond current robot dexterity, planning, and force-control capabilities.

task: Provide assistance to librarians in the maintenance of collections of books, periodicals, magazines, newspapers, and audio-visual and other materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting with a single collection maintenance session (e.g., re-shelving, sorting, or processing a batch of materials). A competent library aide typically spends ~30 min per session unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading labels/call numbers (OCR partially feasible), precise shelving in varied formats (books, magazines, AV media), navigating cluttered aisles, and handling fragile or oddly shaped items. Current humanoids lack reliable label-reading-while-navigating and consistent multi-format handling.

task: Accompany buyers during visits to and inspections of property, advising them on the suitability and value of the homes they are visiting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one property showing/inspection visit. A competent real estate agent typically spends 45–75 minutes walking through, advising, and answering buyer questions per property.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced verbal advisory, market knowledge, negotiation insight, social trust, and contextual judgment far beyond current robot capabilities. Locomotion through homes is feasible but meaningful buyer advising is not.

task: Carry trays from food counters to tables for cafeteria patrons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = picking up a loaded tray at the counter and delivering it to a patron's table. A competent cafeteria worker does this in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires stable bipedal locomotion in a crowded, dynamic space while balancing a loaded tray, plus identifying the correct patron/table. Spill avoidance, crowd navigation, and social interaction remain major challenges for 2026 humanoids.

task: Serve food to customers when waiters or waitresses need assistance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = delivering a single table's food order from kitchen to table and placing dishes in front of correct guests. A skilled food runner does this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a crowded, dynamic restaurant floor, carrying multiple plates without spilling, identifying correct guests, and placing dishes gracefully. Locomotion in tight spaces, load-bearing stability, and social interaction all remain major barriers for 2026 humanoids.

task: Maintain inventory of shipping supplies, such as boxes, labels, tape, bubble wrap, loose packing materials, or tape guns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full inventory check of all shipping supply types in a stockroom. A competent worker counts, records, and flags reorder needs across ~6–10 supply categories in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires counting varied, irregularly stacked items (boxes, rolls, loose fill), reading labels, and logging data. Perception of amorphous materials (bubble wrap, loose fill) and inventory system integration are major blockers for current humanoids.

task: Perform basic vehicle maintenance tasks, such as adding oil, fuel, or radiator fluid, performing minor repairs, or washing trucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one maintenance task (e.g., checking/adding oil or fluid, or washing one truck). ~10 min for fluids, ~20–30 min for a wash/minor repair; median ~25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Tasks involve opening hoods/fuel caps, pouring fluids, manipulating tools in tight engine bays, and washing large vehicles — requiring high dexterity, force control, and mobility around unstructured outdoor equipment. Current humanoids lack reliability for these multi-step, varied interactions.

task: Provide technical support to clients through activities, such as rearranging workplace fixtures to reduce physical hazards or discomfort or modifying task sequences to reduce cycle time.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single workplace consultation visit: assessing the site, rearranging fixtures, and/or modifying a task sequence. Typical ergonomic/process support engagement runs 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level cognitive reasoning, ergonomic knowledge, client communication, and strategic decision-making about workplace design. Far beyond current robot generalization, tool-use, and social/advisory capability.

task: Order and dispense supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = ordering supplies via system and physically dispensing/distributing them to a storage area or requestor. Typical cycle for a moderate supply request.
robot_success_rationale: Ordering requires navigating procurement software/interfaces (very limited for humanoids). Physical dispensing involves varied objects, shelves, and handoffs. Both sub-tasks together are beyond current autonomous capability.

task: Provide care for park program animals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = daily care session for a park program's animal collection (feeding, watering, cleaning enclosures, health checks). A competent keeper typically spends ~1 hour per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied outdoor/indoor enclosures, handling unpredictable live animals, assessing animal health, and using diverse tools. Generalization across animal species, enclosure types, and unstructured environments far exceeds 2026 robot capability. Safety risks with animals add further constraints.

task: Check the quantity and quality of received products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one incoming shipment. A competent worker counts items, checks for damage/defects, and reconciles against a packing list — typically 15–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can scan barcodes and count uniform items but struggle with varied packaging, reading documents/packing slips, detecting subtle quality defects (dents, discoloration), and cross-referencing records without structured integration.

task: Stock cabinets or serving areas with condiments and refill condiment containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restocking condiments in one cabinet/serving area, including retrieving supplies, refilling containers, and organizing. Typical in restaurant or cafeteria settings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires pouring liquids into small containers, handling varied bottle shapes/caps, navigating storage areas, and judging fill levels. Liquid handling, fine manipulation of caps/lids, and generalization across container types remain challenging for current humanoids.

task: Manage and maintain temporary or permanent lodging facilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one shift's routine maintenance of a lodging facility (checking rooms, restocking supplies, coordinating housekeeping, minor repairs). Competent manager handles this in ~1 hour per shift cycle.
robot_success_rationale: This task is fundamentally managerial: scheduling staff, handling guest issues, enforcing policies, coordinating vendors, and making judgment calls. Current robots lack cognition, communication, and authority for facility management; physical sub-tasks are only a small fraction.

task: Arrest individuals to be charged with fraud.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating, apprehending, and securing one individual. Includes approach, verbal commands, physical restraint, and handcuffing. Median ~90 min with paperwork prep.
robot_success_rationale: Requires legal authority, complex social/verbal negotiation, dynamic force judgment, restraint application, and unpredictable subject behavior. Current robots lack all of these: policy, situational judgment, safe restraint, and legal agency.

task: Conduct experiments on biomass or pretreatment technologies.
human_time_rationale: One experiment instance includes setup, running a pretreatment protocol (e.g., steam explosion or chemical soak), sampling, and recording results — typically 1–3 hours in a lab.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex lab procedures: precise chemical handling, operating specialized reactors, titration, sampling under heat/pressure, and interpreting results. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and fine dexterity in unstructured lab settings.

task: Upgrade the design of existing devices by adding mechatronic elements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = upgrading a single device (e.g., adding sensors, actuators, or control electronics). Competent engineer/technician takes ~2 hrs including design review, fitting, and wiring.
robot_success_rationale: Requires engineering judgment, novel design decisions, fine soldering, custom part placement, and adaptive problem-solving across diverse device types. Far beyond current robot generalization and dexterous capability.

task: Mix and prepare flavors for mixed drinks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one mixed drink order: selecting ingredients, measuring, mixing/shaking/stirring, and garnishing. A skilled bartender takes ~2–6 minutes per drink.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pouring, measuring, shaking, and garnishing with varied bottle shapes and tools. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity, liquid handling precision, and generalization across bar equipment and recipes.

task: Inspect sites to determine physical configuration, such as device locations and conduit pathways.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single site (e.g., a floor/room). A competent technician typically spends 45–90 min surveying device locations, conduit runs, and documenting findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied/cluttered real-world sites, identifying unmarked conduits (often hidden in walls/ceilings), mapping spatial configurations, and producing actionable documentation. Current robots lack reliable semantic mapping, judgment about infrastructure, and report generation for unstructured sites.

task: Weigh, analyze, or measure collected sample particles, such as lead, coal dust, or rock, to determine concentration of pollutants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single collected sample (weigh, log, prep for analysis). A trained lab technician typically spends 15–25 minutes per sample.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle tiny sample containers, operate precision analytical balances, and use lab instruments (spectrometers, sieves). Contamination risk, unstructured lab environments, and sub-gram precision demand far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Clean work area.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tidying and wiping down a single workstation/work area; a competent worker typically spends ~10 minutes clearing debris, wiping surfaces, and organizing tools.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep, wipe flat surfaces, and move common objects, but cluttered, varied work areas require generalized perception and manipulation of unpredictable items, tool placement, and fine wiping motions that current humanoids handle inconsistently.

task: Nail tack strips around area to be carpeted or use old strips to attach edges of new carpet.
human_time_rationale: One instance = nailing tack strips around a typical room (~12×12 ft perimeter). A skilled flooring installer takes ~20–30 min to nail or reuse strips around the full perimeter.
robot_success_rationale: Requires kneeling/prone posture along floor edges, precise hammer/nail-gun use near walls, repositioning frequently, and reusing fragile existing strips — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, posture control, and force precision.

task: Create sculptures, statues, and other three-dimensional artwork by using abrasives and tools to shape, carve, and fabricate materials such as clay, stone, wood, or metal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing one sculpture/carving project. A competent sculptor typically spends several hours to days per piece; 480 min (8 hrs) is a reasonable median for a single artwork session/unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly skilled fine dexterity, creative judgment, adaptive force control across diverse materials, and artistic vision. Far beyond current robot capability in generalization, tactile feedback, and open-ended creative planning.

task: Clean or sterilize dishes, kitchen utensils, equipment, or facilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/sterilizing a typical batch of dishes or utensils (e.g., after one meal service). A competent worker loads, washes, and sanitizes in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle pick-and-place of dishes but scrubbing varied, soiled items, loading dishwashers, and operating sterilization equipment autonomously across unstructured kitchen environments remains unreliable. Perception of cleanliness and dexterity with wet/slippery items are key limitations.

task: Trace drawings onto clear acetate for painting or coloring, or trace them with ink to make final copies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tracing a single drawing onto acetate or paper with ink. A competent human takes ~15–25 minutes depending on complexity and detail level.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterous pen control, consistent pressure, smooth continuous strokes, and visual precision to follow lines accurately. Far beyond current humanoid robot fine-motor capabilities; near-surgical dexterity needed.

task: Inspect work sites to identify physical hazards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single worksite inspection tour. A competent safety inspector typically walks the site, documents hazards, and completes a checklist in ~30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate and capture visual data, but identifying diverse physical hazards (unlabeled chemicals, structural risks, trip hazards, unsafe behaviors) requires broad semantic understanding, contextual judgment, and unstructured outdoor/industrial terrain traversal — well beyond current generalization.

task: Operate controls to start, stop, or regulate biomass-fueled generators, generator units, boilers, engines, or auxiliary systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one start/stop or regulation cycle of a biomass plant system. A competent operator checks gauges, adjusts controls, and confirms stable operation, typically taking 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Biomass plant control rooms require interpreting analog/digital gauges, operating specialized panels, and exercising process judgment. Humanoids lack plant-specific interfaces, fail at nuanced regulation decisions, and cannot safely intervene in hazardous industrial environments autonomously.

task: Operate mechanized files that rotate to bring needed records to a particular location.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retrieving or filing one record using a mechanized rotary file system; includes activating the unit, locating the correct section, and retrieving/inserting the record.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can press buttons/controls to rotate the carousel, but precisely locating a thin file folder among densely packed records, grasping and extracting it without damage requires fine dexterity and reliable label-reading perception — still marginal in 2026.

task: Assist physicians in the diagnosis and treatment of cardiac or peripheral vascular treatments, such as implanting pacemakers or assisting with balloon angioplasties to treat blood vessel blockages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one procedure (e.g., pacemaker implant or balloon angioplasty). A competent cardiac/vascular technologist typically assists for 1–3 hours per procedure; 120 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile surgical dexterity, real-time clinical judgment, instrument handling inside a cath lab, and coordination with a physician team. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability; fine dexterous surgical work scores <5%.

task: Replenish supplies of food or equipment at steam tables or service bars.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restocking a single steam table pan or service bar section; a worker typically grabs a replacement tray/container and swaps it in, taking ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a busy kitchen, lifting heavy/hot pans, precise placement in steam table slots, and handling liquids/food. Current humanoids lack reliable hot-item handling, fluid management, and crowded-environment dexterity at acceptable quality.

task: Prepare badges, passes, and identification cards, and perform other security-related duties.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and issuing one badge/pass/ID card, including data entry, printing, laminating, and handing off. ~8 min per card for a competent clerk.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires operating specialized badge printers, computer data entry, fine handling of cards/lanyards, identity verification, and judgment-based security duties. Current humanoids lack reliable keyboard/GUI interaction and security-protocol reasoning.

task: Remove trays and stack dishes for return to kitchen after meals are finished.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing and stacking dishes from one dining table (~4–6 place settings). A competent worker removes trays and stacks dishes in roughly 8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a cluttered dining area, identifying varied dishware, grasping fragile/stacked items, and safely transporting trays. Perception of messy post-meal tables and reliable grasping of slippery or oddly shaped items remain major limitations for 2026 humanoids.

task: Compare objects, such as tools, with impression marks to determine whether a specific object is responsible for a specific mark.
human_time_rationale: One instance = comparing one tool/object to one impression mark. A forensic examiner inspects, tests, photographs, and documents the comparison; ~30–60 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert forensic reasoning, microscopic visual analysis, and nuanced judgment about class/individual characteristics. Current robots lack the fine perception, specialized tooling, and analytical cognition for this forensic task.

task: Inspect workplaces to ensure the absence of health and safety hazards, such as high noise levels, radiation, or potential lighting hazards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one workplace inspection tour. A competent safety inspector typically spends 45–90 minutes surveying a facility, measuring hazards, and documenting findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied/cluttered environments, operating specialized sensors (sound meters, lux meters, radiation detectors), interpreting readings against standards, and generating compliant reports. Frontier humanoids lack reliable tool-use integration, regulatory reasoning, and unstructured environment navigation for full autonomy.

task: Inspect sites to assess environmental damage or monitor cleanup progress.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single site inspection visit, including travel within the site, sampling, visual assessment, and notes. Typical field inspection takes 2–4 hours; median ~3 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor unstructured terrain navigation, expert environmental judgment, sample collection, and regulatory-quality documentation. Current robots lack reliable outdoor mobility, domain expertise, and validated sensor integration for environmental compliance inspection.

task: Set tables with clean linens, condiments, or other supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting one dining table (linens, condiments, cutlery, etc.). A competent server takes ~3–5 minutes per table.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise linen draping, placing varied small items (condiments, cutlery) in exact positions, navigating crowded restaurant floor, and generalizing across table shapes/sizes — well beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Inspect guest rooms, public areas, and grounds for cleanliness and appearance.
human_time_rationale: One inspection round covering a guest room, adjacent public areas, and grounds; a housekeeping supervisor typically takes 30–60 min per full circuit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating mixed indoor/outdoor terrain, detecting subtle cleanliness issues (stains, odors, disarray), assessing aesthetic appearance, and generating actionable reports — well beyond current robot generalization and perception reliability.

task: Test, maintain, or repair electrical power distribution machinery or equipment, using hand tools, power tools, and testing devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single test/maintenance/repair job on one piece of electrical distribution equipment (e.g., a transformer or switchgear panel). A competent electrician typically takes 60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (hand/power tools, multimeters, insulation testers), complex fault diagnosis, safe navigation around high-voltage equipment, and adaptive decision-making. Far beyond reliable autonomous robot capability in 2026.

task: Wipe tables or seats with dampened cloths or replace dirty tablecloths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wiping down one table/set of seats or replacing one tablecloth. A competent worker completes this in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Wiping flat surfaces is feasible but requires navigating varied furniture, applying correct pressure, managing a damp cloth, and replacing tablecloths demands bimanual dexterity with flexible fabric — challenging for current humanoids.

task: Close entry areas following security breaches or reopen areas after receiving notification that the airport is secure.
human_time_rationale: One instance = responding to a single breach or clearance notification: assess the area, communicate with security, and physically close/open barriers or access points. ~5–10 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires situational security judgment, radio/intercom communication, coordination with human teams, and operating specialized airport access-control hardware. Current robots lack autonomous decision-making authority and reliable interaction with such systems.

task: Patrol assigned areas, looking for forest fires, hazardous conditions, and weather phenomena.
human_time_rationale: One patrol instance = a single assigned area patrol shift. A typical forest fire lookout/ranger patrol covers several miles of terrain, averaging ~4 hours per circuit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires traversing rugged, unstructured outdoor terrain over long distances (miles), detecting subtle hazards (smoke, weather changes), and operating autonomously for hours. Current humanoids have very limited outdoor range, endurance, and environmental perception for wilderness patrol.

task: Carry food, silverware, or linen on trays or use carts to carry trays.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading and transporting a tray or cart to a destination; competent worker completes in ~5 min including loading, carrying, and delivery.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can push carts on flat terrain (~60%) but balancing loaded trays while walking, navigating dynamic restaurant/hospital environments, and safely handing off items to people is unreliable. Generalization and stability under load remain limiting.

task: Refuel aircraft using hoses connected to fuel trucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fueling one aircraft. A competent fuel technician takes ~20–30 min including connecting hoses, fueling to spec, and disconnecting/stowing equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a fuel truck to the aircraft, coupling large pressurized fuel hoses to aircraft-specific fuel ports, monitoring gauges, and operating truck controls — all outdoors on uneven ramps. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, mobility, and vehicle-operation capability.

task: Maintain adequate supplies of items, such as clean linens, silverware, glassware, dishes, or trays.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single supply check-and-restock cycle for a station (linens, silverware, glassware, dishes, trays). A competent worker counts, retrieves, and restocks in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires inventory perception, navigating to storage, carrying varied fragile items (glassware, stacked dishes), and restocking organized shelves. Fragile-item handling, stack management, and multi-step autonomy across an unstructured hospitality environment are well beyond current reliable robot capability.

task: Collect and analyze production samples to evaluate quality.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting samples from a production line and performing a quality evaluation (visual, dimensional, or lab check). Typically 20–40 min for a competent QC technician.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate and pick items but struggle with unstructured sampling protocols, handling varied containers/tools, and applying nuanced quality judgment (smell, texture, subtle defects). Evaluation step requires domain-specific instrumentation and interpretation.

task: Create mechanical models to simulate mechatronic design concepts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = creating one mechanical model simulating a mechatronic concept; a competent engineer typically spends ~2 hours on design, assembly, and validation of a prototype model.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level engineering reasoning, CAD/simulation software use, creative conceptual design, and precise fine assembly. Current humanoids lack cognitive design capability, software tool use, and the required fine dexterity for precision mechatronic modeling.

task: Mix, apply, and spread plaster, concrete, mortar, cement, mastic, glue or other adhesives to form a bed for the tiles, using brush, trowel and screed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing and spreading adhesive/mortar bed for a standard ~10–15 sq ft tile section. Includes mixing, brushing, troweling, and screeding to level.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-modulated troweling, screeding to flat tolerances, and mixing viscous materials — all demanding high dexterity and tactile feedback far beyond current humanoid capabilities. Generalization to varied substrates adds difficulty.

task: Remove any old tile, grout and adhesive using chisels and scrapers and clean the surface carefully.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing old tile, grout, and adhesive from a standard bathroom floor (~20–30 sq ft). A competent human takes ~1.5 hours using chisels and scrapers.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained high-force chisel/scraper work on irregular, brittle surfaces with precise force control to avoid substrate damage. Current robots lack the tool-wielding strength, compliance, and surface-reading perception needed for this unstructured demolition task.

task: Work with personnel and facilities management staff to install, remove, or relocate user connectivity equipment and devices.
