human_time_rationale: One instance = installing, removing, or relocating one set of user connectivity equipment (e.g., a workstation, router, or network jack). Coordination + physical work averages ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinating with human staff, navigating offices, handling diverse cables/connectors, routing wires, and using tools like screwdrivers in unstructured environments. Multi-step social coordination and fine dexterous tool-use far exceed 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Prepare surfaces for tiling by attaching lath or waterproof paper, or by applying a cement mortar coat to a metal screen.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single wall/floor section (~10–20 sq ft). Attaching lath/paper or applying mortar coat to a surface takes a skilled tile setter ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated use of specialized tools (staple gun, trowel, mortar), precise surface coverage, force control on uneven surfaces, and material handling in constrained spaces. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Direct operations, maintenance, or repair of hydroelectric power facilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single directed operation, maintenance, or repair task at a hydro facility (e.g., inspecting a turbine, adjusting controls, replacing a component). Median ~2 hours for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial controls, navigating complex wet/mechanical environments, heavy tool use, and safety-critical judgment. Current humanoids lack the specialized tool interfaces, domain knowledge integration, and reliability for autonomous hydroelectric operations.

task: Locate suspicious bags pictured in printouts sent from remote monitoring areas, and set these bags aside for inspection.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reviewing one set of printouts, identifying flagged bags, and physically setting them aside. A trained screener takes ~5 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Task couples paper-image interpretation (reading/understanding 2D printouts) with physical retrieval of specific bags in an unstructured area. Current robots lack reliable document-reading perception, semantic cross-referencing of image-to-object, and the judgment to identify "suspicious" items autonomously.

task: Serve ice water, coffee, rolls, or butter to patrons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = serving one table of patrons with ice water, coffee, rolls, or butter; includes fetching items and delivering to table, ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigation in crowded restaurant, pouring liquids without spilling, handling soft rolls and small butter pats, social interaction, and tray balancing — all highly challenging for current humanoids in unstructured public environments.

task: Stock shelves, racks, cases, bins, and tables with new or transferred merchandise.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stocking one cart/batch of merchandise onto shelves in a retail/warehouse setting; a competent worker takes ~15–25 minutes per cart.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place known items, but retail stocking demands high generalization across varied product shapes, sizes, orientations, tight shelf spacing, and label-forward placement. Current humanoids struggle with unstructured SKU variety and precise shelf positioning.

task: Clean or prepare various foods for cooking or serving.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single ingredient or small batch of food (e.g., washing, peeling, chopping vegetables). A competent cook typically completes this in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Food prep demands high dexterity for varied textures, shapes, and sizes (peeling, chopping, deboning). Current humanoids lack fine manipulation precision and generalization needed for safe, quality food handling in unstructured kitchen environments.

task: Fill beverage or ice dispensers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = refilling a single dispenser (e.g., a beverage urn or ice bin). A worker retrieves the supply, opens/pours, and closes in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires pouring liquids precisely, handling varied dispenser lids/spigots, carrying heavy containers, and avoiding spills. Current humanoids lack reliable liquid-handling and generalized dispenser-interface skills.

task: Fasten metal treads across door openings or where carpet meets flooring to hold carpet in place.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one metal tread strip at one doorway/transition. A skilled flooring installer positions, aligns, and fastens the strip in roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise low-posture work on the floor, handling small metal strips, operating a hammer/nail gun or screwdriver at exact positions, and applying consistent force — far beyond reliable 2026 humanoid dexterity and tool-use in unstructured flooring environments.

task: Test and adjust communication and alarm systems, and report malfunctions to maintenance units.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing all comm/alarm systems in one zone, logging results, and reporting faults. Competent worker typically takes 20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating diverse control panels, interpreting audio/visual alarm outputs, navigating mixed environments, and filing structured reports. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use on non-standard interfaces and autonomous fault diagnosis/reporting workflows.

task: Place clean dishes, utensils, or cooking equipment in storage areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = putting away a typical load of clean dishes/utensils after washing. A competent worker completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping varied objects (plates, pots, utensils), navigating cabinets/drawers, and correctly sorting items into correct storage locations. Dexterous handling of fragile dishes, opening cabinet doors, and semantic understanding of "correct" storage placement remain major challenges for current humanoids.

task: Move furniture from area to be carpeted and remove old carpet and padding.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing a typical room (e.g., bedroom/living room): moving furniture out, pulling up carpet, and removing padding. A two-person crew takes ~1–1.5 hrs.
robot_success_rationale: Requires moving heavy, bulky furniture (beyond payload/stability limits), precise gripping of carpet edges, sustained pulling/tearing force, and navigating cluttered spaces — all well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Conduct acceptance tests, vendor-qualification protocols, surveys, audits, corrective-action reviews, or performance monitoring of incoming materials or components to ensure conformance to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one incoming lot inspection or audit review. A quality engineer spends ~45–90 min on sampling, measurement, documentation, and disposition per lot.
robot_success_rationale: Task demands complex judgment, document interpretation, supplier communication, metrology tool use, and adaptive decision-making across varied materials. Current humanoids lack the cognitive workflow integration and specialized instrument operation needed; only simple physical sampling steps are feasible.

task: Oversee operation of microelectromechanical systems (MEMS) fabrication or assembly equipment, such as handling, singulation, assembly, wire-bonding, soldering, or package sealing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = overseeing one production batch/run through a MEMS fab/assembly step (e.g., wire-bonding or package sealing). A competent technician typically spends ~30–60 min monitoring, adjusting, and verifying one batch.
robot_success_rationale: MEMS oversight demands ultra-fine dexterity (micron-scale wire-bonding, soldering), specialized equipment operation, real-time quality judgment, and process control — far beyond any 2026 general-purpose humanoid's capabilities.

task: Load or unload trucks that deliver or pick up food or supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unloading or loading one delivery truck at a food/supply dock. A competent worker typically takes 30–60 min depending on load size.
robot_success_rationale: Food/supply trucks have highly varied, irregular items (loose boxes, bags, pallets, produce). Humanoids struggle with heavy loads, awkward shapes, tight truck interiors, and stacking precision. Perception and generalization remain limiting factors.

task: Prepare and mail checks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single check (filling out payee, amount, signature) and mailing it (envelope, stamp, drop in mailbox). ~15 min total for a typical payment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous writing/signing on a check, folding into an envelope, applying a stamp, and navigating to a mailbox — multiple high-precision steps; current humanoids lack reliable fine motor writing and integrated multi-step mail handling.

task: Organize space for study equipment and supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = organizing a typical study area (desk/shelves). A competent person sorts, arranges, and stores equipment and supplies in ~20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires semantic understanding of object categories, spatial planning, user preferences, and varied grasps. Current humanoids lack contextual judgment for "organized" layouts and struggle with diverse small items like pens, books, and cables.

task: Trim or remove excess material, using scrapers, knives, or band saws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming/removing excess material from a single workpiece. A competent worker typically takes ~5–10 minutes depending on material and tool used.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled cutting with varied tools (scrapers, knives, band saws) on irregular workpieces. Tool switching, edge-finding, and consistent cut quality push beyond current humanoid dexterity and generalization limits; band saw operation adds safety and precision challenges.

task: Prepare hazardous, radioactive, or mixed waste samples for transportation or storage by treating, compacting, packaging, and labeling them.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single waste container through treatment, compaction, packaging, and labeling; a trained technician typically takes 45–90 min per container.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly specialized PPE donning, operating compactors and treatment equipment, precise regulatory labeling, and fine manipulation in contaminated environments. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and safety standards.

task: Receive and count daily inventories of cash, drafts, and travelers' checks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one daily inventory count of cash, drafts, and travelers' checks at a typical bank/business. Sorting, counting, and reconciling takes ~20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of paper currency/documents, precise counting, reading serial numbers, and reconciling totals — far beyond current humanoid capability. No reliable commercial demo exists for this task.

task: Maintain kitchen work areas, equipment, or utensils in clean and orderly condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single cleaning/tidying pass of a kitchen work area (surfaces, equipment, utensils). A competent kitchen worker typically spends ~20 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse subtasks: wiping varied surfaces, scrubbing equipment, organizing utensils, navigating cluttered kitchen. Cleaning flat surfaces is feasible (~50%), but handling wet/greasy tools, varied equipment geometry, and safe chemical use drops overall success significantly.

task: Spread mastic or other adhesive base on roof deck to form base for promenade tile, using serrated spreader.
human_time_rationale: One instance = spreading adhesive over a typical roof deck section (~100 sq ft). A competent roofer takes ~45 min including setup, even spreading, and edge work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/rooftop locomotion on uneven surfaces, sustained forceful tool-use (serrated spreader), consistent pressure/angle control over a large area, and working with viscous adhesive — all well beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Install, test, or maintain radio frequency identification device (RFID) systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing/testing/maintaining a single RFID reader or tag array at one location; a competent technician typically spends ~60 min per unit including setup, configuration, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous wiring, configuring electronics via software interfaces, diagnosing RF signal issues, and using specialized tools. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, domain-specific tool-use, and diagnostic software integration needed.

task: Test wind turbine components, using mechanical or electronic testing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single wind turbine component (e.g., a blade, gearbox, or generator). A competent technician typically spends ~1 hour setting up equipment, running tests, and logging results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized mechanical/electronic test rigs, interpreting multi-modal sensor data, connecting instrumentation, and making judgment calls on results. Current humanoids lack the tool-use dexterity, domain-specific equipment interfacing, and diagnostic reasoning needed for autonomous success.

task: Conduct audits at hazardous waste sites or industrial sites or participate in hazardous waste site investigations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single site audit or investigation visit. A competent environmental professional typically spends 2–6 hours on-site for sampling, documentation, and assessment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert judgment, regulatory knowledge, adaptive sampling decisions, hazardous chemical identification, and navigation of highly unstructured outdoor/industrial terrain. Far beyond current robot generalization and cognitive autonomy.

task: Pack products for shipping.
human_time_rationale: One instance = packing a single product for shipment: selecting a box, adding padding, placing the product, sealing, and labeling. Competent warehouse worker averages ~8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick-and-place items but struggle with selecting correct box size, manipulating packing materials (bubble wrap, tape, foam), and sealing/labeling reliably. High variability in product shapes limits generalization.

task: Implement or test design solutions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = implementing or testing a single design solution (e.g., a prototype or design change). A competent engineer typically spends ~1 hour per cycle of build/test/evaluate.
robot_success_rationale: Requires abstract reasoning, engineering judgment, tool use, interpreting test results, and adaptive problem-solving. Far beyond current general-purpose robot cognitive and dexterous capabilities in unstructured engineering contexts.

task: Remove and replace cracked or damaged tile.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one cracked tile and installing a replacement. Includes chiseling out old tile, cleaning substrate, applying adhesive/mortar, setting new tile, and grouting. ~30–60 min for a competent tiler.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise chisel force to remove tile without damaging substrate, fine mortar spreading, exact tile alignment, and grouting — all highly dexterous, contact-rich, multi-step tasks with varied materials that far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Garnish foods and position them on tables to make them visible and accessible.
human_time_rationale: One instance = garnishing and positioning a single dish/plate. A skilled food worker applies garnish and places the item on a table in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation (delicate garnish placement), aesthetic judgment, and precise positioning. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control, visual-artistic reasoning, and gentle touch needed for acceptable food presentation quality.

task: Stretch carpet to align with walls and ensure a smooth surface, and press carpet in place over tack strips or use staples, tape, tacks or glue to hold carpet in place.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing/securing carpet in one average room (~150 sq ft). Includes stretching, aligning, and fastening edges; competent installer takes ~1–1.5 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force stretching tools (knee kicker, power stretcher), precise alignment perception, complex bimanual dexterity along floor edges, and varied fastening methods. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured environments.

task: Determine orientation of tracts of land, including position, boundaries, size, and shape, using theodolites, electronic distance-measuring equipment, satellite-based positioning equipment, land information systems, or other geodetic survey equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = surveying a single tract of land. A competent surveyor typically spends 2–5 hours on-site setting up equipment, measuring, and recording data for a typical parcel.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized geodetic instruments (theodolites, total stations, GPS rovers) in unstructured outdoor terrain, interpreting complex survey plans, and making professional judgments. Current humanoids lack the tool-use, outdoor mobility, and domain-specific operational capability for autonomous surveying.

task: Reconstruct crime scenes to determine relationships among pieces of evidence.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reconstructing a single crime scene. A trained investigator typically spends 2–6 hours examining, documenting, and correlating physical evidence at a scene.
robot_success_rationale: Requires abstract reasoning, inferential judgment, domain expertise, and contextual integration of heterogeneous evidence. Current robots lack the cognitive and investigative intelligence for autonomous forensic analysis; perception and manipulation are insufficient for nuanced evidence correlation.

task: Study, examine, and test acquisitions to authenticate their origin, composition, history, and to assess their current value.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining a single acquisition (e.g., artwork, artifact, or collectible). A competent curator/appraiser typically spends 1–3 hours on research, physical inspection, testing, and documentation per item.
robot_success_rationale: Requires deep domain expertise, archival research, provenance reasoning, connoisseurship, and nuanced judgment far beyond current robot perception or cognition. Physical tests (chemical, spectroscopic) need specialized lab tools robots cannot autonomously operate or interpret holistically.

task: Install or maintain landscaped areas, performing tasks such as removing snow, pouring cement curbs, or repairing sidewalks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single landscaping maintenance job (e.g., snow removal of a driveway, one sidewalk repair, or one curb pour) — median ~90 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy/specialized equipment (snow blowers, cement mixers, compactors), outdoor unstructured terrain, and skilled concrete work. Frontier humanoids lack the tool-use, equipment operation, and generalization for these tasks.

task: Perform pat-down or hand-held wand searches of passengers who have triggered machine alarms, who are unable to pass through metal detectors, or who have been randomly identified for such searches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one passenger pat-down or wand search. A trained TSA/security officer completes a standard secondary screening in roughly 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safe physical contact with a human body, nuanced social/legal protocols, precise tactile discrimination on varied clothing/body types, and authoritative verbal communication. Current humanoids lack safe compliant touch, social trust, and legal authority for such contact.

task: Investigate mechanical failures or unexpected maintenance problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = investigating a single mechanical failure or maintenance issue, including inspection, diagnosis, and documentation. Competent technician typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert diagnosis combining multi-sensory cues (sound, vibration, smell, visual anomalies), deep domain reasoning, and often tool use in unstructured environments. 2026 robots lack reliable fault-diagnosis generalization and causal reasoning for unexpected failures.

task: Finish and dress the joints and wipe excess grout from between tiles, using damp sponge.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grouting and cleaning joints in a typical small-to-medium tiled area (e.g. bathroom floor or backsplash ~10–20 sq ft); a competent tiler takes ~25–35 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine tactile control to dress grout joints uniformly, controlled sponge pressure/angle, and precise wiping without smearing or gouging. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force feedback, and wet-surface perception needed for acceptable quality.

task: Measure and mark surfaces to be tiled, following blueprints.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and marking one typical room/surface (e.g. bathroom floor or wall). A competent tiler spends ~30–60 min reading blueprints, measuring, snapping chalk lines, and marking layout points.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting 2D blueprints, precise spatial measurement, chalk-line snapping, and marking irregular surfaces. Current humanoids lack reliable blueprint comprehension, fine tool use (tape measure, chalk line), and accurate dimensional transfer to real surfaces.

task: Carry linens to or from laundry areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = carrying a load of linens one way between a room/storage area and laundry. Typically involves gathering, transporting a short distance, and depositing.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can walk flat indoor terrain and carry bundles, but gathering loose/varied linens, navigating hallways, doors, and elevators autonomously remains inconsistent. Soft, irregular items challenge grasping and bundling reliably.

task: Perform serving, cleaning, or stocking duties in establishments, such as cafeterias or dining rooms, to facilitate customer service.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one table-service cycle (clearing, wiping, resetting) or restocking a station in a cafeteria/dining room; ~15–25 min per cycle for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep/mop flat floors and do basic pick-and-place, but bussing varied dishes, wiping tables, navigating crowded dining rooms, and restocking shelves with mixed items requires robust dexterity, perception, and social navigation still unreliable in 2026.

task: Mix and apply mortar or cement to edges and ends of drain tiles to seal halves and joints.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing and applying mortar/cement to seal one drain tile joint/seam. A skilled mason takes ~5–10 minutes per tile joint including mixing and application.
robot_success_rationale: Requires mixing wet mortar to correct consistency, applying it precisely to curved/irregular tile edges in an outdoor or trench environment, and achieving a watertight seal — demanding fine dexterity, force control, and tool use well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Fabricate prototypes of fuel cell components, assemblies, stacks, or systems.
human_time_rationale: One prototype fuel cell assembly (stack or system). Involves precision layering of membranes, gaskets, bipolar plates, and end plates — typically 2–6 hours for a skilled technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme precision with fragile, thin membranes and gaskets, torque-controlled fastening, and multi-step quality checks. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and specialized tool-use for prototype R&D environments.

task: Install carpet on some floors using adhesive, following prescribed method.
human_time_rationale: One instance = carpeting a typical room (~150–200 sq ft). A competent installer preps the floor, applies adhesive, lays and trims carpet in ~3–4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy material handling of large flexible rolls, precise adhesive spreading, tight seam cutting, and stretching/tucking — all highly unstructured. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-use, and force control for this multi-step trade task.

task: Inspect installation sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single installation site (e.g., HVAC, electrical, plumbing). A competent inspector walks the site, checks components, and documents findings — typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied/unstructured environments, identifying faults visually and contextually, and making judgment calls. Current robots lack reliable generalization for multi-step inspection with quality documentation across diverse site conditions.

task: Inventory supplies of tools, equipment, or materials to ensure that sufficient supplies are available and items are in usable condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single inventory check of a typical supply area/room. A competent worker counts, inspects condition, and logs results in ~30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating storage areas, visually identifying/counting varied items, assessing condition, and recording data. Perception and generalization across diverse tools/materials remain weak; logging/reporting integration is limited on current humanoids.

task: Shut down and restart biomass power plants or equipment in emergency situations or for equipment maintenance, repairs, or replacements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full emergency or planned shutdown-and-restart cycle of a biomass plant/major equipment unit. A competent operator typically takes 60–120 minutes following established procedures.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating complex industrial control systems, valves, switchgear, and HMIs in a high-stakes, safety-critical environment. Current humanoids lack the specialized tool-use, plant-specific situational awareness, and safety certification needed; risk of catastrophic failure is extreme.

task: Demonstrate miniaturized systems that contain components, such as microsensors, microactuators, or integrated electronic circuits, fabricated on silicon or silicon carbide wafers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and presenting a MEMS demo (e.g., a single wafer/device demo session). A skilled engineer typically spends ~45 min setting up equipment, explaining, and demonstrating one system.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-fine dexterous handling of fragile silicon/SiC wafers at micron scale, precise instrument operation, and live technical narration. Far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity, perception resolution, and unstructured lab generalization.

task: Measure, cut and install tackless strips along the baseboard or wall.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one average room (~12×12 ft perimeter). A skilled flooring installer measures, cuts strips to length, and nails them ~1/4 inch from the wall around the perimeter in ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise low-to-floor kneeling posture, accurate tape-measure reading, controlled hacksaw/guillotine cutting, and nail-gun or hammer nailing at tight baseboard angles. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tool-switching, and floor-level stability for this multi-step trade task.

task: Collect data through direct observation of work activities or witnessing the conduct of tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = observing one work activity session or test event; human watches, takes notes, and records findings, typically ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained contextual understanding, judgment about what is noteworthy, natural language note-taking, and social situational awareness. Current robots lack the cognitive interpretation and adaptive attention needed for unstructured observational data collection.

task: Connect wires to circuit breakers, transformers, or other components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting a single wire to one component (breaker, transformer, terminal). A competent electrician strips, routes, and torques a wire in ~10–15 minutes including verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to strip insulation, thread wire into terminals, and apply precise torque with a screwdriver or torque tool. Electrical panels are confined, cluttered spaces with safety-critical tolerances — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Sweep or scrub floors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sweeping or scrubbing one average-sized room (~200 sq ft). A competent worker typically takes 10–20 minutes depending on method and clutter.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids can navigate flat indoor floors and wield tools like brooms or mops at ~40–70% capability. Challenges include adapting pressure/technique, navigating obstacles, and handling corners or furniture legs consistently.

task: Develop or validate specialized materials characterization procedures, such as thermal withstand, fatigue, notch sensitivity, abrasion, or hardness tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = developing or validating one specialized test procedure (e.g., a hardness or fatigue protocol). A competent materials engineer typically spends 2–4 hours designing, running, and documenting a single test procedure validation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level materials science knowledge, procedure design, interpretation of results, and regulatory/standards judgment. Far beyond current robot cognition, dexterous instrument setup, and autonomous decision-making for novel technical workflows.

task: Tour grounds, such as parks, botanical gardens, cemeteries, or golf courses, to inspect conditions of plants and soil.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single inspection tour of a grounds area (park, garden, golf course, etc.). A competent groundskeeper typically spends 45–90 minutes walking and assessing plants and soil conditions.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained outdoor locomotion over varied terrain (grass, gravel, slopes), nuanced plant/soil health perception, and expert agronomic judgment. Outdoor navigation and plant-condition assessment far exceed current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Stock supplies, such as food or utensils, in serving stations, cupboards, refrigerators, or salad bars.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restocking a single station/area (e.g., one salad bar or cupboard). A competent worker gathers items and organizes them in ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied environments, identifying and grasping diverse items (food, utensils, containers), opening refrigerators/cupboards, and placing items correctly. High variability in objects, layouts, and hygiene standards challenge current robots significantly.

task: Test electrical systems or continuity of circuits in electrical wiring, equipment, or fixtures, using testing devices, such as ohmmeters, voltmeters, or oscilloscopes, to ensure compatibility and safety of system.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single circuit or fixture. A competent electrician spends ~20–30 min setting up instruments, probing test points, interpreting readings, and documenting results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise probe placement on small terminals, reading analog/digital instrument displays, and interpreting variable electrical contexts. Current humanoids lack fine dexterity for probe handling, safe live-circuit navigation, and generalized instrument operation.

task: Cut and shape tile to fit around obstacles and into odd spaces and corners, using hand and power cutting tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring, marking, and cutting a single tile to fit around an obstacle or corner. A skilled tiler takes ~10–15 min per cut piece including measuring and test-fitting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement of irregular gaps, marking tiles, safely operating angle grinders/wet saws, test-fitting, and fine positional control. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and spatial reasoning for unstructured shapes.

task: Compute horizontal and vertical coordinates of control networks, using direct leveling or other geodetic survey techniques, such as triangulation, trilateration, and traversing, to establish features of the Earth's surface.
human_time_rationale: One instance = establishing a full control network survey session (field setup, observations, and computation for one site/traverse). A competent surveyor typically spends a full day (6–8 hours) per control network establishment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized geodetic instruments (total stations, levels, GNSS receivers), navigating varied outdoor terrain, precise instrument setup over benchmarks, and complex computational judgment. Far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capabilities.

task: Sort, count, and wrap currency and coins.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting, counting, and wrapping a typical cash drawer's worth of mixed bills and coins (~$200–$500 in mixed denominations); a competent teller takes ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterity to handle thin, flexible bills and small coins, discriminate denominations visually, and wrap coin rolls tightly. Beyond 2026 humanoid capability; even specialized coin/bill sorters are narrow fixed machines.

task: Perform preventive or corrective maintenance on robotic systems or components.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance session on a single robot/component — inspecting, lubricating, replacing worn parts, running diagnostics. Typical technician cycle is 45–90 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized diagnostic knowledge, fine dexterous manipulation of fasteners and electronics, tool-use, and fault interpretation. No current humanoid can autonomously service another robot end-to-end at acceptable quality.

task: Store copies of publications on paper, magnetic tape, film, or diskette.
human_time_rationale: One instance = storing a single publication (filing paper copy, labeling and shelving a tape/diskette, or placing film in archive). A competent worker takes ~5–10 min per item including labeling and placement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle thin paper, fragile film, or small diskettes/tapes; precise insertion into slots/shelves; reading labels for correct placement. Current humanoids struggle with thin, flexible media and precise archival filing tasks.

task: Use a variety of tools or equipment, such as power construction equipment, measuring devices, power tools, and testing equipment, such as oscilloscopes, ammeters, or test lamps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single equipment-use session (e.g., taking a measurement or cutting/drilling a component). Competent worker selects the right tool, sets it up, uses it, and reads/records results in ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires selecting from a diverse, unstructured toolkit, operating heavy power equipment, driving machinery, and interpreting analog/digital test instruments — all far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use generalization, and safety limits.

task: Inspect completed transportation projects to ensure safety or compliance with applicable standards or regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one completed transportation project (e.g., a road segment, bridge, or tunnel). A competent inspector typically spends 1–3 hours on-site reviewing compliance, safety features, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert regulatory knowledge, multi-modal judgment (visual, structural, documentation review), outdoor/unstructured terrain navigation, and contextual decision-making far beyond current robot generalization and cognitive capabilities.

task: Test wind turbine equipment to determine effects of stress or fatigue.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full stress/fatigue test cycle on a single piece of turbine equipment (e.g., a blade or gearbox), including setup, instrumentation, running the test protocol, and logging results. Typically several hours; 180 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized heavy test rigs, hydraulic actuators, and data acquisition systems; interpreting sensor/fatigue data; and adapting to non-standard large-scale equipment. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and tool-use capabilities.

task: Receive and store supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = receiving and storing a typical delivery of supplies (e.g., office or warehouse shipment). Includes checking items, unpacking, and placing in designated storage locations.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied object handling, navigating to storage locations, reading labels, verifying counts, and placing items correctly. Current humanoids struggle with unstructured item variety, label reading, and multi-step logistics sequencing without human oversight.

task: Operate testing equipment, such as heat stress meters, octave band analyzers, motion analysis equipment, inclinometers, light meters, thermoanemometers, sling psychrometers, or colorimetric detection tubes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single measurement session with one instrument at one location; setup, calibration, sampling, and recording takes ~20–40 min for a skilled industrial hygienist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying, retrieving, and correctly configuring highly varied, specialized instruments; interpreting analog/digital readouts; performing calibration steps; and recording data — all demanding fine manipulation, tool generalization, and domain-specific procedural reasoning far beyond current robot capability.

task: Examine physical evidence, such as hair, biological fluids, fiber, wood, or soil residues to obtain information about its source and composition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining a single evidence sample (e.g., one hair, fiber, or fluid specimen). A forensic scientist typically spends 30–90 min on microscopy, chemical tests, and documentation per sample.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level dexterous handling of microscopic/delicate evidence, operating specialized lab instruments (microscopes, spectrometers), interpreting complex multi-modal scientific data, and documenting findings — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Prepare food items, such as sandwiches, salads, soups, or beverages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single food item (e.g., one sandwich, one salad, one bowl of soup, or one beverage). A competent worker completes this in ~8 minutes on average.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step manipulation (slicing, pouring, assembling), tool use, and generalization across varied items. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity, recipe reasoning, and consistent kitchen navigation needed for full autonomy.

task: Cut tile backing to required size, using shears.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and cutting one piece of tile backing to size with shears; a skilled worker measures, marks, and cuts in roughly 3–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measuring/marking, firm bimanual grip on shears, controlled force along a straight line through stiff material, and accurate sizing — beyond reliable 2026 humanoid dexterity and force control on non-standard materials.

task: Inspect electrical systems, equipment, or components to identify hazards, defects, or the need for adjustment or repair, and to ensure compliance with codes.
human_time_rationale: One inspection instance = a single electrical system/panel/component inspection by a licensed electrician. Covers visual checks, testing, code compliance review; ~30–60 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert code knowledge, fine visual/tactile perception of wiring, probing energized components safely, navigating varied environments, and professional judgment. Current humanoids lack the specialized tooling, safety certifications, and reasoning depth needed.

task: Level concrete and allow to dry.
human_time_rationale: One instance = leveling a standard residential pour (e.g., ~10–20 sq ft slab). Screeding, floating, and finishing takes a skilled worker ~30–60 min; drying/curing is passive and not counted as active labor.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained, precise screed/float tool use across a large wet surface, significant force modulation, outdoor uneven terrain, and real-time quality judgment. No current humanoid has demonstrated this skill autonomously at acceptable quality.

task: Investigate or test specific construction project materials to determine compliance to specifications or standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing/inspecting one material sample (e.g., concrete core, soil, rebar) on-site or in a field lab; includes collection, test procedure, and recording results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized test equipment (slump cones, compaction gauges, coring tools), navigating unstructured construction sites, interpreting variable results, and documenting findings — far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity.

task: Cut and trim carpet to fit along wall edges, openings, and projections, finishing the edges with a wall trimmer.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting and trimming carpet to fit one average room (~150 sq ft). A skilled carpet installer takes ~30–60 min accounting for measuring, cutting, and edge finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, forceful cutting of flexible/deformable carpet, operating a wall trimmer tool along baseboards, and adapting to irregular edges/projections — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use generalization.

task: Connect and disconnect utility services at specific locations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting or disconnecting one utility service (e.g., gas, water, electric) at a single location. Includes travel to the site, locating the shutoff/connection point, and performing the switch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/unstructured terrain navigation, locating buried or varied utility access points, operating specialized valves and tools, and ensuring safety compliance — far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Inspect finished products to ensure that shoes have been completed according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one pair of finished shoes. A trained quality inspector checks stitching, sole bonding, shape, color, and labeling in ~2–4 minutes per pair.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual inspection of stitching, glue lines, color consistency, and flex points on irregular 3D surfaces. Current robots lack reliable close-tolerance defect perception, tactile feedback, and generalization across shoe styles to meet QC standards.

task: Wash dishes, glassware, flatware, pots, or pans, using dishwashers or by hand.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one sink load/batch of mixed dishes. A competent kitchen worker scrapes, washes, rinses, and stacks a typical batch in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to sink, grasping wet/slippery varied items, scrubbing, loading a dishwasher or hand-washing, and stacking without breakage. Fragile glassware and slippery surfaces push dexterity and perception to near limits; high breakage/drop risk for current humanoids.

task: Assemble, install, test, or maintain electrical or electronic wiring, equipment, appliances, apparatus, or fixtures, using hand tools or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single electrical installation/maintenance job (e.g., wiring an outlet, installing a fixture, or testing a panel). Competent electrician completes a typical unit task in ~60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of wires, connectors, and terminals; reading wiring diagrams; navigating varied real-world environments; using diverse hand/power tools; and making safety-critical electrical judgments — all far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Maintain, clean, or sterilize laboratory instruments or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/sterilizing a typical batch of lab instruments (e.g., glassware, tools). A competent lab tech takes ~20–30 min including inspection, washing, autoclaving setup, and drying.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle fragile/varied lab instruments, operating autoclaves/ultrasonic cleaners, verifying sterility, and navigating cluttered lab benches. Current humanoids lack precision, domain-specific tool operation, and contamination-awareness needed.

task: Stock or distribute resources, such as samples or promotional or educational materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stocking or distributing a batch of materials (e.g., filling a rack/shelf or delivering samples to a set of recipients in one session). A competent worker typically takes 20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied environments, identifying diverse items, placing them in correct locations or handing to people, and adapting to dynamic social contexts. Perception, generalization across item types, and safe human interaction remain significant limitations for 2026 robots.

task: Inspect, test, and measure completed work, using devices such as hand tools or gauges to verify conformance to standards or repair requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single completed work piece/assembly using hand tools or gauges; a competent technician typically takes 10–20 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with selecting and wielding varied hand tools/gauges, interpreting analog readings, and applying contextual judgment about conformance standards. Perception and dexterous tool-use across unstructured setups remain weak.

task: Cut, surface, polish, and install marble and granite or install pre-cast terrazzo, granite or marble units.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting, surfacing, polishing, and installing a single marble/granite slab or pre-cast unit (e.g., a countertop or floor section). A skilled mason typically takes 3–5 hours per unit including all steps.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting, precision wet-saw cutting, diamond grinding, polishing with specialized tools, and accurate placement/leveling of rigid stone slabs. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and force-control capabilities in unstructured job-site environments.

task: Plant or maintain vegetation through activities such as mulching, fertilizing, watering, mowing, or pruning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one vegetation maintenance session (e.g., a typical residential yard). Covers mulching, fertilizing, watering, mowing, or pruning; ~30–60 min for a competent groundskeeper.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor unstructured terrain, varied tools (mowers, pruners, hoses), and diverse plant/soil conditions challenge current humanoids. Mowing alone requires operating ride-on or push equipment; pruning requires fine dexterity and judgment. Generalization across all subtasks is very low.

task: Clean trucks used in the fields.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one field truck (exterior mud/debris + basic interior). A competent worker with a pressure washer and basic supplies takes ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating pressure washers or hoses on large, irregular outdoor vehicles with heavy mud. Humanoids lack vehicle-cleaning tools, outdoor uneven terrain stability, and the reach/force needed for truck-scale cleaning. No commercial deployment exists.

task: Make radiographic images to detect flaws in objects while leaving objects intact.
human_time_rationale: One instance = radiographing a single object (setup, exposure, imaging). A competent NDT technician typically takes 15–30 minutes per object including positioning and safety checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized radiographic equipment (X-ray/gamma-ray sources), precise object positioning, radiation safety protocols, and regulatory compliance. Current humanoid robots lack the tool integration, safety awareness, and certified autonomy for this task.

task: Inspect sanitary conditions at public facilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single public facility (e.g., restroom block or food-service area). A trained inspector walks through, checks surfaces, fixtures, and records findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied public spaces, visually assessing cleanliness/contamination, interpreting ambiguous sanitary standards, and documenting findings. Current robots lack reliable odor/contamination sensing, contextual judgment, and structured reporting for uncontrolled public environments.

task: Wash glasses or other serving equipment at bars.
human_time_rationale: One instance = washing a batch of ~4–6 glasses at a bar sink or glass washer. A competent bartender/barback rinses, washes, and sets them to dry in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling fragile, wet, variably-shaped glassware with precision grip, navigating a cluttered bar environment, and operating sinks/glass washers. High breakage risk, wet surfaces, and tight spaces challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Integrate human factors requirements into operational hardware.
human_time_rationale: One instance = integrating human factors requirements (ergonomics, accessibility, safety) into a single hardware component or system. Requires review, modification, and validation — typically 1–3 hours per component.
robot_success_rationale: Task is fundamentally cognitive/engineering: interpreting human factors standards, redesigning hardware, and validating compliance. Requires abstract reasoning, judgment, and domain expertise — far beyond current robot autonomy.

task: Test or evaluate photovoltaic (PV) cells or modules.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing/evaluating a single PV cell or module (setup, electrical measurement, IV curve characterization, visual inspection, and recording results).
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise probe placement, specialized test equipment operation (solar simulators, IV tracers), interpreting nuanced electrical data, and reconfiguring lab setups. Current humanoids lack fine-probe dexterity and domain-specific instrumentation integration.

task: Diagnose malfunctioning systems, apparatus, or components, using test equipment and hand tools to locate the cause of a breakdown and correct the problem.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing a single malfunctioning system/component. A competent technician typically spends ~30–60 min testing, isolating the fault, and making the correction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires adaptive problem-solving, interpreting test equipment readings, selecting correct hand tools, and making contextual repairs across wildly varied equipment types — far beyond current robot generalization, tool-use flexibility, and diagnostic reasoning.

task: Clean or service machinery to ensure operating efficiency, using water, gasoline, lubricants, or hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/servicing one machine. A competent worker spends ~30–60 min inspecting, wiping, lubricating, and hand-tool servicing a typical industrial machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to varied machine geometries, selecting correct fluids/tools, applying precise lubrication, and judging cleanliness quality. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-use generalization, and domain knowledge for unstructured machinery servicing.

task: Develop or implement microelectromechanical systems (MEMS) processing tools, fixtures, gages, dies, molds, or trays.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing/implementing a single MEMS tooling item (fixture, die, mold, etc.). A skilled engineer typically spends 2–6 hours per item across design, iteration, and validation steps.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced engineering cognition, CAD/EDA software use, cleanroom microfabrication judgment, and novel problem-solving. Current humanoid robots have no capacity for MEMS-scale design, tool development, or creative engineering workflows.

task: File, grind, sand, and smooth filled or repaired surfaces, using power tools and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filing/grinding/sanding one filled or repaired surface area (e.g. a body panel patch or wall repair). A competent worker typically spends 15–25 minutes per surface.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled contact, real-time tactile/visual feedback to judge smoothness, and fluid tool manipulation (sander, file, grinder). Current robots lack reliable force regulation and surface-quality perception for such nuanced finishing work.

task: Sort incoming and outgoing mail, according to type and destination, by hand or by operating electronic mail-sorting and scanning devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting one batch of mail (e.g., a standard mail tray/bin, ~50–100 pieces). A competent postal worker sorts a tray in ~20–40 minutes by hand or machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading addresses (OCR feasible), grasping varied flat/rigid envelopes (moderate dexterity), and routing to correct bins. Handling mixed mail types, operating sorter controls, and generalizing to arbitrary address formats remains unreliable for current humanoids.

task: Diagnose, troubleshoot, and resolve hardware, software, or other network and system problems, and replace defective components when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and resolving a single IT support ticket. A competent technician typically spends ~30–60 min on an average hardware/software/network issue including diagnosis, fix, and component swap if needed.
robot_success_rationale: Requires abstract reasoning, system diagnostics, software interaction, and contextual troubleshooting far beyond current robot cognition. Physical component replacement adds fine dexterity needs (PCIe cards, cables, screws). No frontier robot can reliably do this autonomously end-to-end.

task: Install ground leads and connect power cables to equipment, such as motors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grounding and connecting power cables to a single motor/equipment unit; includes routing, stripping, and torquing terminals to spec.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity to strip wires, align and seat terminal lugs, and torque fasteners to spec. Spatial variability in cable routing, connector types, and confined spaces severely challenge current humanoids.

task: Test performance of robotic assemblies, using instruments such as oscilloscopes, electronic voltmeters, or bridges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single robotic assembly unit; connecting instruments, running tests, and recording results typically takes ~15–25 minutes for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise probe placement on specific test points, interpreting oscilloscope/voltmeter readings, managing cables/leads, and making judgment calls on results. Current humanoids lack fine dexterity for probe work and reliable instrument interpretation.

task: Sand body areas to be painted and cover bumpers, windows, and trim with masking tape or paper to protect them from the paint.
human_time_rationale: One instance = prepping one full vehicle for paint: sanding body areas and masking bumpers, windows, and trim. A competent auto body worker typically takes 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for precise masking tape application along curved trim lines, consistent sanding pressure across contoured panels, and adaptive tool use. Current humanoids lack the precision, force control, and generalization for acceptable auto-body prep quality.

task: Fit and weld replacement parts into place, using wrenches and welding equipment, and grind down welds to smooth them, using power grinders and other tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting, welding, and grinding one replacement part/joint. A skilled welder typically spends 45–90 min per part including fit-up, tack welding, full weld passes, and grinding.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated multi-tool use (wrenches, welding torch, grinder), high-heat process control, precise seam tracking, and quality judgment. No 2026 general-purpose humanoid can autonomously operate welding equipment and grinders at acceptable quality.

task: Operate and maintain audio-visual equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up, operating, and doing basic maintenance on AV equipment for a single event/session (e.g., a meeting or presentation). ~30 min median for a competent AV technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous cable management, navigating complex menus/interfaces, diagnosing audio-visual faults, and contextual judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use, GUI interaction, and troubleshooting for diverse AV systems.

task: Clean up spilled food or drink or broken dishes and remove empty bottles and trash.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single spill/breakage event and removing associated trash/bottles; includes wiping, sweeping shards, and disposing of bottles.
robot_success_rationale: Spills and broken glass require varied responses: mopping liquids, safely handling sharp shards, identifying and grasping irregular bottles/trash. Unstructured debris and fragile/sharp objects challenge current robot dexterity and perception significantly.

task: Fill small dents that cannot be worked out with plastic or solder.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling one small dent on a body panel: mix filler, apply, rough shape. Includes prep and initial cure wait; excludes final sanding/finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tactile assessment of dent depth/contour, mixing viscous filler, skilled trowel application with consistent pressure, and quality inspection — far beyond current robot dexterity and surface-finishing capability.

task: Examine trays to ensure that they contain required items.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one tray. A competent worker visually scans and verifies all required items in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Current robots can use vision to detect and classify objects on flat trays, but reliably identifying all required items (varied shapes, colors, occlusions, subtle missing items) in unstructured settings remains error-prone. Perception generalization and false-negative rates limit reliability.

task: Assist scientists or engineers in the conduct of photonic experiments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting with a single photonic experiment session (aligning optics, adjusting equipment, logging data). A competent lab technician typically spends ~45 min per experiment run.
robot_success_rationale: Photonic experiments demand sub-millimeter optical alignment, fine dexterous manipulation of delicate components, interpretation of specialized instrumentation, and adaptive scientific judgment — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Plant grass with seed spreaders, and operate straw blowers to cover seeded areas with mixtures of asphalt and straw.
human_time_rationale: One instance = seeding and covering one standard area (~1 acre). A competent operator loads, calibrates, and drives seed spreader then straw blower across the area in ~1 hour.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large ride-on/tow-behind machinery (seed spreaders, straw blowers) on open terrain. Humanoid robots cannot drive or control these vehicles/machines autonomously; no viable general-purpose robotic solution exists for this workflow.

task: Return incorrectly addressed mail to senders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one piece of mail. A postal worker takes ~1–3 min to inspect the address, mark/label it "Return to Sender," and place it in the outgoing mail stream.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading handwritten/printed addresses, cross-referencing records to confirm misaddressing, applying a stamp/label, and routing correctly — combining fine manipulation, OCR, and decision-making beyond current robot generalization.

task: Apply mortar to tile back, position the tile, and press or tap with trowel handle to affix tile to base.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting one tile. Experienced tilers take ~2–4 min per tile: spreading mortar, aligning, and tapping into place.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled spreading of viscous mortar, accurate spatial alignment under tight tolerances, and calibrated tapping — demanding dexterity, compliance control, and tactile feedback well beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured job-site settings.

task: Fabricate housings, jigs, fittings, or fixtures, using metalworking machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single housing, jig, fitting, or fixture. A competent machinist typically spends 1–3 hours per part including setup, machining, and quality checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating metalworking machines (lathes, mills, grinders), reading technical drawings, precise tool changes, measuring tolerances, and multi-step decision-making. 2026 humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and specialized machine-operation capability for this.

task: Provide customers with change of address cards and other forms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retrieving and handing a set of forms (e.g., change-of-address card) to one customer at a counter or display rack. A competent clerk does this in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Picking and handing thin paper forms is moderately challenging (slippery, lightweight, easily torn). Social interaction/navigation to customer adds complexity. Grasping single sheets reliably remains difficult for current humanoids.

task: Cut and tape plastic separating film to outside repair areas to avoid damaging surrounding surfaces during repair procedure and remove tape and wash surfaces after repairs are complete.
human_time_rationale: One instance = masking one repair area (cutting, taping film around perimeter) plus removal and surface wash afterward. A typical body/surface repair prep takes ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise cutting of flexible plastic film, accurate tape placement on irregular curved surfaces, clean edge alignment, and targeted surface washing — all highly dexterous, compliance-sensitive tasks that current humanoids handle poorly on unstructured panels.

task: Direct or perform mixing or application of fertilizers, insecticides, herbicides, or fungicides.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing and applying treatment to a typical field section or crop area. Includes measuring, mixing, loading, and operating spray equipment — ~45 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized agricultural machinery (sprayers, spreaders), precise chemical mixing, and outdoor terrain navigation. Humanoid robots cannot drive/operate farm equipment; outdoor unstructured environments and chemical handling further limit success.

task: Travel to post offices to pick up the mail for routes or pick up mail from postal relay boxes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one route pickup trip: driving to post office/relay boxes, collecting mail, and returning. Typical rural/suburban mail collection run takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a vehicle on public roads between stops — humanoid robots cannot operate vehicles in traffic. Even if dropped at each location, opening relay boxes, navigating outdoor terrain, and handling varied mail volumes adds further barriers.

task: Cut carpet padding to size and install padding, following prescribed method.
human_time_rationale: One instance = padding one average room (~150 sq ft). Cutting, fitting, and stapling/taping padding to a floor takes a competent installer roughly 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires measuring, cutting large flexible rolls with a utility knife, precise fitting around irregular edges, and stapling/taping to floor — multi-step dexterous manipulation of a large, floppy, low-stiffness material in varied floor layouts. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Design, build, or modify fixtures used to assemble parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing/building or modifying one fixture for a specific assembly application. A skilled machinist/toolmaker typically spends 1–3 hours depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative design engineering, precision machining/fabrication, tool selection, and iterative physical modification — far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and tool-use capabilities. No current system can do this autonomously.

task: Install and anchor fixtures in designated positions, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing and anchoring one fixture (bracket, shelf, light fitting) in a designated position; includes measuring, fastening, and checking stability.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise drilling, torquing fasteners to spec, handling varied fixture types, and working in constrained vertical/overhead spaces. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous tool-use and positional accuracy for anchoring tasks.

task: Mix polyester resins and hardeners to be used in restoring damaged areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing a single batch of polyester resin and hardener for one repair job. A competent worker measures, combines, and stirs the mixture in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid measuring/pouring, controlled stirring, and ratio accuracy — challenging for current humanoids. Perception of viscous fluids and fine motor control for dispensing hardener in small quantities are weak points. Unstructured benchtop chemistry setting adds difficulty.

task: Install equipment, such as instrumentation, test equipment, engines, or aftermarket products, to ensure proper interfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single piece of equipment (e.g., an instrument, test unit, or engine component) including mounting, wiring/plumbing connections, and interface verification. Typical skilled technician time: 60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high dexterity for varied connectors, torque-critical fasteners, cable routing, and interface alignment across unpredictable configurations. Tool-use, force feedback, and generalization to non-standardized setups far exceed current humanoid capability.

task: Set up mechanical, hydraulic, or electric test equipment in accordance with engineering specifications, standards, or test procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a single test apparatus for one test run. A competent technician reads specs, mounts equipment, connects lines/wiring, and calibrates — typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting engineering specs, routing hydraulic/electric lines, torquing fittings to spec, and verifying calibration. Demands fine dexterity, tool use, and deep contextual reasoning far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Transfer supplies or equipment between storage and work areas, by hand or using hand trucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one transfer trip (locate, load, transport, place). A competent worker averages ~10–20 min depending on distance and load size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigation between areas, item identification, grasping varied objects, and hand-truck use. Current humanoids handle simple pick-and-place but struggle with unstructured item variety, hand-truck manipulation, and multi-room navigation reliably.

task: Position dolly blocks against surfaces of dented areas and beat opposite surfaces to remove dents, using hammers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one dent. A skilled auto body worker spends ~15–25 min positioning a dolly block and hammering out a typical dent.
robot_success_rationale: Requires bimanual coordination of two tools simultaneously (dolly + hammer), precise tactile feedback to feel surface contour and strike force, and visual/haptic judgment of dent progress — well beyond current robot dexterity and force-control capabilities.

task: Slice and pit fruit used to garnish drinks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = slicing and pitting a single piece of fruit (e.g. a cherry or citrus wedge) for one drink garnish. A skilled bartender completes this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous knife control, pit removal, and thin/consistent slicing of deformable fruit. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation and force control needed for safe, accurate cutting of varied fruit shapes and sizes.

task: Fabricate new or modify existing prototype components or fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating or modifying a single prototype component/fixture. A competent technician typically spends 1–3 hours accounting for setup, machining/modification, and quality checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating machine tools (mills, lathes, grinders), interpreting engineering drawings, applying skilled judgment for tolerances, and improvising mid-task. Far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and generalization capabilities.

task: Remove small pits and dimples in body metal, using pick hammers and punches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing pits/dimples from one panel section. A skilled auto body worker typically spends 20–30 min per area, assessing and carefully working metal back to shape.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert tactile feedback, precise force-controlled strikes with pick hammers/punches, and real-time visual/haptic assessment of subtle surface geometry. Far beyond current robot dexterity and force-control capabilities.

task: Report any unusual circumstances concerning mail delivery, including the condition of street letter boxes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reporting unusual circumstances for a single mail delivery route segment, including inspecting several street letter boxes and noting anomalies. Typically 5–10 minutes per reporting event.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor navigation on varied terrain, visually inspecting box condition, recognizing "unusual" anomalies (damage, tampering, obstruction), and generating a coherent report. Current robots lack reliable unstructured outdoor inspection and natural-language anomaly reporting autonomy.

task: Receive letters and parcels, and place mail into bags.
human_time_rationale: One instance = receiving and sorting a single batch of letters/parcels into bags; a competent mail handler does this in ~2–4 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of varied mail items (envelopes, parcels, irregular shapes) into bags is partially within reach, but handling deformable envelopes, opening/holding bags, and reliably grasping mixed items remains challenging for current humanoids.

task: Build instrumentation or laboratory test equipment for special purposes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = building a single custom instrument/test rig from components. Skilled technician/engineer typically spends a full workday (4–8 hrs) on design, wiring, assembly, and initial verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level schematic interpretation, fine electronic wiring, soldering, calibration judgment, and open-ended problem-solving across highly varied, non-standardized configurations. Far beyond current robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Install new robotic systems in stationary positions or on tracks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single robotic system at its position or on a track, including mechanical mounting, alignment, and initial securing. Typically 2–4 hours for a skilled technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting, precise mechanical alignment, bolting to floors/tracks, and use of specialized tools (torque wrenches, levels, hoists). Current humanoids lack the strength, tool repertoire, and precision for this unstructured heavy industrial task.

task: Prepare and cook foods of all types, either on a regular basis or for special guests or functions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and cooking a full meal (multiple dishes). A competent cook typically spends 60–120 minutes; 90 min is a reasonable median for a complete meal service.
robot_success_rationale: Cooking from scratch requires chaining dozens of dexterous sub-tasks (chopping, stirring, plating), adapting to varied ingredients and recipes, managing heat, and working safely around sharp/hot hazards — far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Build underbeds and install anchor bolts, wires, and brackets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = building and installing underbeds with anchor bolts, wires, and brackets for one unit/station. A competent tradesperson takes ~45–75 min including layout, drilling, and fastening.
robot_success_rationale: Requires power tools (drills, wrenches), precise torque on anchor bolts, routing wires/brackets in confined under-structure spaces, and complex multi-step sequencing. Far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity and tool-use generalization.

task: Join edges of carpet and seam edges where necessary, by sewing or by using tape with glue and heated carpet iron.
human_time_rationale: One instance = joining one carpet seam (~3–5 ft). A competent carpet installer aligns, trims, applies seam tape/glue or sews, and irons in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise edge alignment on heavy flexible material, controlled heat-iron pressure, seam tape placement, and optional sewing — all highly dexterous, force-sensitive tasks on deformable surfaces. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Remove damaged sections of vehicles using metal-cutting guns, air grinders and wrenches, and install replacement parts using wrenches or welding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a damaged panel/section and installing a replacement part. A skilled auto body technician typically spends 1.5–2.5 hours per panel section including cutting, grinding, fitting, and welding.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinating multiple heavy power tools (cutting guns, grinders, wrenches, welders) on irregular, damaged surfaces in tight spaces. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-switching, force sensing, and unstructured environment generalization capabilities.

task: Place conduit, pipes, or tubing, inside designated partitions, walls, or other concealed areas, and pull insulated wires or cables through the conduit to complete circuits between boxes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = running conduit through one wall/partition section and pulling wire through it. A competent electrician typically takes 60–120 minutes depending on length and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires drilling into walls, threading rigid/flexible conduit through tight concealed spaces, using fish tapes, and pulling cables under tension — highly unstructured, forceful, dexterous work in confined areas well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Align and straighten tile using levels, squares, and straightedges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning and straightening a single tile or small tile section using layout tools; a skilled tiler checks, adjusts, and confirms alignment in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle tiles and precision tools, visual/tactile feedback to detect minute misalignment, and coordinated adjustment while tile is still in mortar — well beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Maintain test equipment in operational condition by performing routine maintenance or making minor repairs or adjustments as needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance/minor repair session on one piece of test equipment. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min inspecting, cleaning, calibrating, and making minor adjustments.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults, fine dexterous manipulation of small electronic/mechanical components, tool use, and contextual judgment about equipment state. Current humanoids lack the precision, diagnostic reasoning, and tool-handling for unstructured repair tasks.

task: Weigh letters and parcels, compute mailing costs based on type, weight, and destination, and affix correct postage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single letter or parcel: place on scale, read weight, look up/calculate postage, select and affix correct stamps or labels. ~2–4 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Weighing and reading a digital scale is feasible, but computing variable postage costs requires software integration, and precisely peeling/affixing stamps or adhesive labels demands fine dexterity that current humanoids struggle with reliably.

task: Check mail to ensure correct postage and that packages and letters are in proper condition for mailing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking a single piece of mail (letter or package). A competent mail clerk inspects postage and condition in ~1–2 minutes per item.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read labels and scan barcodes via vision, but reliably assessing postage sufficiency, damage, seal integrity, and addressing correctness across varied mail formats requires nuanced perception and generalization still limited in 2026 systems.

task: Conduct engineering site audits to collect structural, electrical, and related site information for use in the design of residential or commercial solar power systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full site audit (residential or commercial). A competent solar engineer typically spends 1.5–2.5 hours on-site collecting structural, electrical, and environmental data.
robot_success_rationale: Requires roof access, electrical panel inspection, shade analysis, structural assessment, and professional judgment. Current humanoids lack safe roof mobility, electrical meter reading expertise, and the contextual engineering reasoning needed for a complete, supervisor-acceptable audit.

task: Put undelivered parcels away, retrieve them when customers come to claim them, and complete any related documentation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = storing or retrieving one parcel and completing its documentation. A competent worker scans, shelves/retrieves, and records in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied parcel handling (shapes, weights, fragility), navigating a storage area, and completing documentation/digital systems. Perception and dexterous pick-place at ~40–60%, but integrated documentation, customer interaction, and generalization across parcel types drops overall success sharply.
