task: Fit and secure windows, vinyl roofs, and metal trim to vehicle bodies, using caulking guns, adhesive brushes, and mallets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting and securing all windows, vinyl roof, and metal trim on one vehicle body. A skilled auto-trim worker typically takes 30–60 minutes per vehicle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled seating of large glass panels, caulking along irregular curves, mallet strikes at calibrated force, and handling flexible vinyl — all in varied vehicle geometries. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and multi-tool coordination.

task: Demonstrate equipment to customers, and explain functioning of equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single demonstration session for one customer or group, covering key features and explaining how equipment works; typical sales/showroom demo runs ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fluid natural-language explanation, reading customer cues, adapting rhetoric, and confidently operating varied unfamiliar equipment. Current robots lack conversational persuasion, social intelligence, and generalized equipment operation needed to satisfy a reasonable supervisor.

task: Perform skilled repair or maintenance operations, using equipment such as hand or power tools, hydraulic presses or shears, or welding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single skilled repair/maintenance job (e.g., replacing a component, welding a joint). A competent tradesperson typically takes ~1 hour per discrete task.
robot_success_rationale: Requires switching between diverse specialized tools (wrenches, welders, hydraulic presses), high-force precision dexterity, contextual fault diagnosis, and safe tool operation — well beyond 2026 humanoid generalization and force-control limits.

task: Perform diagnostic analyses of processing steps, using analytical or metrological tools, such as microscopy, profilometry, or ellipsometry devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = single diagnostic analysis of one sample with one tool (e.g., load, calibrate, scan, record results). A trained technician typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires loading delicate samples into precision instruments (microscopes, ellipsometers, profilometers), operating specialized lab software, interpreting complex metrology data, and making diagnostic judgments — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Clean garbage cans with water or steam.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one garbage can. A competent worker rinses, scrubs, and steam/water-cleans a single can in ~10 minutes including setup and drying.
robot_success_rationale: Requires maneuvering a hose/steam wand inside a large container, handling water/steam equipment, repositioning the can, and judging cleanliness. Current humanoids lack reliable wet tool-use, fluid management, and forceful scrubbing in unstructured outdoor settings.

task: Conduct harsh environmental testing, accelerated aging, device characterization, or field trials to validate devices, using inspection tools, testing protocols, peripheral instrumentation, or modeling and simulation software.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full test session (setup, run, data collection, teardown) for a single device under a defined protocol; typically 2–4 hours for a competent engineer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level protocol interpretation, fine connector/instrument manipulation, software interaction, environmental chamber operation, and judgment-based troubleshooting. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Cut grooves along outlines, using chisels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting grooves along one outlined shape (e.g., a wood panel or joint). A skilled craftsperson takes ~15–25 min depending on length and material.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled fine dexterity to guide a chisel along a curved/straight outline without slipping or over-cutting. Current humanoids lack the tactile feedback, wrist stability, and tool-control precision needed for acceptable quality.

task: Clean and disinfect areas in which bodies are prepared and embalmed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and disinfecting a single preparation/embalming room after a procedure; a competent mortuary technician typically takes 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can mop/wipe flat surfaces but struggle with biohazard protocols, drain handling, navigating tight mortuary tables/equipment, applying correct disinfectant concentrations, and verifying cleanliness to regulated standards.

task: Measure and mark vinyl material and cut material to size for roof installation, using rules, straightedges, and hand shears.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring, marking, and cutting a single vinyl panel/piece to size. A skilled roofer takes ~15–25 min per piece including layout, marking, and hand-shear cutting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement reading, marking on flexible vinyl, coordinated two-handed shear cutting of a large floppy sheet, and outdoor/rooftop mobility. Current robots lack precision for flexible material handling, accurate marking, and coordinated bimanual cutting tools.

task: Load machines with required input or output media, such as paper, cards, disks, tape, or Braille media.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a single machine (e.g., printer with paper, drive with tape/disk). A competent worker locates the media, aligns, and inserts it in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick flat/rigid media but precise alignment into slots (paper trays, tape drives, Braille feeders) demands fine dexterity and force feedback. Varied media types and machine interfaces hurt generalization significantly.

task: Set up banquet tables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up all tables for a typical banquet room (~10–20 tables): unfolding, positioning, and locking legs. ~20 min for an experienced worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires unfolding heavy, awkward folding tables, locking leg mechanisms, and precise spatial placement. Current humanoids lack the strength, bimanual coordination, and force control for large furniture manipulation reliably.

task: Install, program, or repair programmable controllers, robot controllers, end-of-arm tools, or conveyors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single install, programming session, or repair job on one controller/robot/conveyor unit. Competent technician typically takes 1–3 hours depending on complexity; 2 hrs is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level electrical/software reasoning, fine connector work, ladder-logic or robot programming, and fault diagnosis. Far beyond current humanoid generalization, dexterity, and autonomous technical decision-making capabilities.

task: Repair or calibrate products, such as surgical lasers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fully repairing or calibrating one surgical laser unit. A competent biomedical technician typically spends 60–120 minutes on diagnostics, adjustment, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use, specialized equipment knowledge, optical alignment precision, and diagnostic interpretation. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured, safety-critical instrument servicing.

task: Locate forest fires on area maps, using azimuth sighters and known landmarks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating one fire event: taking azimuth sightings, cross-referencing landmarks, and plotting on a map. A trained lookout typically completes this in 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a specialized azimuth sighter (Osborne Fire Finder or similar), reading analog map overlays, outdoor terrain navigation, and geometric triangulation — well beyond current humanoid robot generalization and tool-use capabilities outdoors.

task: Perform laboratory procedures following protocols including deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) sequencing, cloning and extraction, ribonucleic acid (RNA) purification, or gel electrophoresis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one complete protocol run (e.g., a gel electrophoresis or DNA extraction). Competent lab tech takes ~1–3 hrs; 2 hrs is a reasonable median across the listed procedures.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (pipetting µL volumes, loading gel wells), precise instrument operation (centrifuges, thermocyclers), sterile technique, and multi-step protocol adherence. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured lab settings.

task: Set up or operate prototype or test apparatus, such as control consoles, collimators, recording equipment, or cables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up or operating a single prototype/test apparatus session. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min configuring equipment, routing cables, and verifying readiness.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level interpretation of technical schematics, precise cable routing, fine connector mating, operating non-standard consoles, and adaptive troubleshooting — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, perception, and task generalization limits.

task: Specialize in repairing and maintaining parts of the engine, such as fuel injection systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and repairing/servicing a single fuel injection system on one engine; a competent mechanic typically takes 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle injectors, fittings, and seals; specialized tools (torque wrenches, diagnostic scanners); deep mechanical reasoning; and working in cramped engine bays. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Perform or execute manual or automated tests of automotive system or component performance, efficiency, or durability.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single test session on one automotive system/component (e.g., an engine durability run or brake efficiency test). Setup, execution, and logging typically take 1–3 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized test equipment, dynamometers, and data acquisition systems; interpreting real-time results; adjusting parameters mid-test. Humanoids lack the tool-use, domain-specific interfaces, and judgment needed. Highly specialized environment far beyond current robot generalization.

task: Clean work areas, using air hoses, to remove damaged material and discarded fiberglass strips used in repair procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one fiberglass repair work area post-job; a skilled worker uses an air hose to clear debris and strips in roughly 10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping and maneuvering an industrial air hose, directing airflow into irregular workspaces, and recognizing scattered fiberglass debris. Unstructured shop environments, hose handling, and fine directional control are well beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Fabricate devices, such as optoelectronic or semiconductor devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single batch/device through a standard process sequence (e.g., photolithography, deposition, doping steps). A competent technician typically spends 1–3 hours per fabrication run.
robot_success_rationale: Semiconductor fabrication demands sub-micron precision, cleanroom protocols, specialized equipment (CVD, lithography tools, ion implanters), and complex multi-step chemical processes. Current humanoid robots lack the fine dexterity, tool integration, and process knowledge required.

task: Deliver and retrieve items to and from departments by hand or using push carts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one delivery/retrieval trip to a department; includes loading, transporting, and unloading items, typically 10–20 min in a mid-sized facility.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids can walk indoors and do basic pick-and-place, but navigating real-world office/facility environments with elevators, doors, varied cart types, and human traffic remains unreliable. Push-cart maneuvering and multi-room navigation are still challenging autonomously.

task: Inspect and verify dimensions and clearances of parts to ensure conformance to factory specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single part or assembly. A competent worker uses calipers/gauges to measure key dimensions and clearances, typically taking 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool use (calipers, feeler gauges), fine dexterity, and interpretation of spec sheets. Current humanoids lack reliable sub-millimeter measurement tool manipulation and structured decision-making against factory tolerances.

task: Fill sprayer tanks with water and chemicals, according to formulas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling one sprayer tank. Involves measuring chemicals per formula, adding water, and mixing. Typical backpack/equipment sprayer takes ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid measurement of hazardous chemicals, operating valves/hoses, reading formula labels, and handling varied containers. Current humanoids lack the fine fluid-handling dexterity, chemical safety compliance, and formula interpretation needed.

task: Cut openings in vehicle bodies for the installation of customized windows, using templates and power shears or chisels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting one opening in a vehicle body panel. Includes template placement, marking, cutting with power shears/chisel, and deburring. A skilled auto body worker typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise template alignment on curved vehicle surfaces, forceful controlled cutting with power shears/chisels, and fine dexterity in a cramped, unstructured environment. Current humanoids lack the tool-use force control, rigid fixturing, and surface-conforming precision needed.

task: Measure and cut metal lath to size for walls and ceilings, using tin snips.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and cutting one sheet/section of metal lath to fit a specific wall or ceiling area. A skilled plasterer/lather takes ~10–15 min per piece including measuring, marking, and cutting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, marking, and forceful two-handed tin-snip cutting of stiff metal mesh. Current humanoids lack the grip strength, bimanual coordination, and tool-use generalization needed for reliable snip control and accurate cuts on irregular lath.

task: Store samples of finished products in labeled cartons and record their location.
human_time_rationale: One instance = storing one batch of product samples into a labeled carton and logging its location. ~8 min including labeling, packing, and recording.
robot_success_rationale: Picking and placing items into cartons is feasible (~70%), but reading/printing labels, interpreting product variety, and logging location in a real warehouse system requires reliable perception, tool use, and software integration — well beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Inspect active remediation sites to ensure compliance with environmental or safety policies, standards, or regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single active remediation site. A competent inspector typically spends 1–3 hours on-site reviewing operations, sampling, documentation, and compliance checks.
robot_success_rationale: Remediation sites are unstructured, outdoor/hazardous terrain. Robots lack regulatory knowledge, judgment, document review capability, and authority to make compliance determinations. Rough terrain mobility and contextual interpretation severely limit autonomy.

task: Collect or prepare measurement, weight, or identification labels and attach them to products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting and attaching a label to one product. A competent worker in a packaging/warehouse setting does this in ~2–4 minutes including retrieval and application.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise grasping of thin/flexible labels, accurate placement/alignment on varied product surfaces, and possible peeling. Perception of small text/barcodes and dexterous peel-and-stick manipulation remain challenging for 2026 humanoids.

task: Maintain, monitor, and clean work areas, such as recycling collection sites, drop boxes, counters and windows, and areas around scale houses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = single cleaning/monitoring round of a recycling site (bins, drop boxes, counters, scale house perimeter). A competent worker completes this in ~30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor/semi-structured environments with debris, varied terrain, and contaminated recyclables are challenging. Robots can sweep/mop flat surfaces but struggle with litter pickup, cluttered drop zones, inspection judgment, and rough outdoor ground around scale houses.

task: Terminate, cure, polish, or test fiber cables with mechanical connectors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one fiber cable end (terminate, cure, polish, test). A skilled technician typically takes 10–20 min per connector end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter dexterity to strip, cleave, insert, epoxy, and polish a glass fiber; precise tool sequencing; optical quality inspection. Far beyond current general-purpose robot dexterity and tactile precision.

task: Provide assistance during emergencies by operating floodlights or generators, placing flares, or driving needed vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One emergency support action (deploy flares, start generator, move vehicle). Covers arrival, setup, operation, and securing — ~20 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Driving vehicles in dynamic emergency environments is beyond humanoid capability. Placing flares and operating generators require outdoor mobility, tool-use, and high-stakes generalization — all weak spots for 2026 robots. Unstructured, safety-critical context lowers probability further.

task: Identify defects in concrete or other building materials, using thermal or infrared testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single structural element or surface area (e.g., a wall section or floor panel). Setup, scanning, and preliminary interpretation takes ~45 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can carry thermal cameras and move along surfaces, but interpreting infrared anomalies as defects requires domain expertise and contextual judgment. Sensor positioning, surface access on uneven terrain, and autonomous defect classification remain weak. No commercial humanoid does this reliably in unstructured field conditions.

task: Adjust or align headlights, wheels, and brake systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting all three systems (headlights, wheel alignment, brakes) on one vehicle. A competent mechanic typically spends ~15 min per system, totaling ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torque application, specialized alignment equipment operation, and fine dexterous adjustments under a vehicle. Current humanoids lack the tool-use finesse, equipment interfacing, and calibration judgment needed for acceptable quality.

task: Remove upholstery, accessories, electrical window-and-seat-operating equipment, and trim to gain access to vehicle bodies and fenders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stripping a single vehicle's interior/trim for body access. A competent auto body tech typically takes 30–60 min depending on vehicle complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires removing clips, fasteners, wiring harnesses, and panels in tight, awkward spaces with varied connector types. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use generalization; no commercial deployment exists for this task.

task: Prepare sketches or follow blueprints to determine the location of wiring or equipment and to ensure conformance to building and safety codes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reviewing/preparing sketches or blueprints for a single job site or room. A competent electrician typically spends 30–60 min interpreting plans and marking equipment locations.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting 2D blueprints, cross-referencing electrical codes, spatial reasoning, and producing annotated sketches. Current robots lack reliable document comprehension, code knowledge application, and drafting capability autonomously.

task: Conduct site assessments to certify a habitat or to ascertain environmental damage or restoration needs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one field site assessment visit. A competent environmental scientist typically spends 2–5 hours on-site surveying habitat conditions, collecting data, and documenting findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert ecological judgment, traversal of highly unstructured outdoor terrain (wetlands, forests, slopes), multi-modal sensing, species identification, regulatory knowledge, and adaptive sampling. Far beyond current frontier robot capabilities.

task: Fasten small metal or plastic boxes to walls to house electrical switches or outlets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting one electrical box to a wall. A competent electrician marks, drills, anchors, and secures the box in ~10–20 minutes depending on wall type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise hole marking, drilling into varied wall materials, anchoring, and aligning a small box flush to the wall—combining fine dexterity, force control, and tool use (drill, screwdriver) in unstructured environments well beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Stock refrigerating units with wines or bottled beer or replace empty beer kegs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stocking a single refrigerating unit or replacing one keg. A competent worker loads/arranges bottles or swaps a keg in ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Grasping varied bottle shapes and navigating cooler shelves is feasible at low rates, but heavy keg handling (~50–160 lbs), coupling tap lines, and tight refrigerator spaces challenge current humanoid dexterity and payload limits significantly.

task: Visually examine materials, structures, or components for signs of corrosion, metal fatigue, cracks, or other flaws, using tools and equipment such as endoscopes, closed-circuit television systems, and fiber optics.
human_time_rationale: One inspection instance of a component/structure (e.g., a weld seam, pipe section, or structural member) using NDT tools; ~30–60 min is typical for setup, scan, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires manipulating specialized tools (endoscopes, fiber optics, CCTV), navigating confined/complex spaces, and making expert visual judgments on subtle defects. Current robots lack reliable tool-use dexterity, domain-specific defect recognition, and autonomous setup in unstructured industrial environments.

task: Take fingerprints of arrestees, prisoners, or the general public.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fingerprinting one individual (all ten fingers). A trained officer typically completes ink or live-scan fingerprinting of one person in ~5–15 minutes including prep and paperwork.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous hand manipulation of an uncooperative/variable human hand, correct finger placement on scanner, and social compliance management. Current robots lack reliable fine dexterity and safe human-contact handling for this sensitive, variable task.

task: Bell, bead with power hammers, or weld pressure vessel tube ends to ensure leakproof joints.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one tube end (belling, beading, or welding). A skilled boilermaker/pipefitter takes ~10–20 min per end including setup, tooling, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy specialized tooling (power hammers, welding equipment), applying precise force to pressure-critical joints, and meeting strict leak-free quality standards. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and process-control capabilities.

task: Soak fiberglass matting in resin mixtures and apply layers of matting over repair areas to specified thicknesses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one repair area. Mixing resin, soaking matting, applying and smoothing layers to spec thickness takes ~30–60 min for a competent composite technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise resin mixing, saturating pliable fibrous matting, and conforming wet layers to curved/irregular surfaces at exact thickness — demanding tactile feedback, fine dexterity, and chemical handling far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Conduct scientific protection, mitigation, or restoration projects to prevent resource damage, maintain the integrity of critical habitats, and minimize the impact of human activities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single project session (field survey, intervention, or restoration action). Competent ecologist/ranger spends ~4 hours on a typical habitat protection or mitigation project visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires scientific judgment, ecological expertise, adaptive planning, multi-terrain navigation, tool use, and stakeholder coordination. Current humanoids lack autonomous scientific reasoning, outdoor rough-terrain capability, and specialized environmental tools needed.

task: Replace damaged glass on vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing one pane of damaged vehicle glass (e.g., windshield). Includes removal, prep, adhesive application, installation, and cure check; ~60–120 min for a trained technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool use (suction cups, urethane guns, cutting wires), handling large fragile curved glass panels, working around vehicle body contours, applying adhesive beads consistently, and fine force control — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Examine and adjust protective guards, loose bolts, and specified safety devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full inspection-and-adjustment round on a single machine/workstation, checking guards, tightening bolts, and verifying safety devices. A competent worker typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires visual inspection across varied guard types, torque-sensing bolt tightening with correct tools, and verifying diverse safety devices. Generalization across machine layouts, tool changes, and force-controlled fastening are beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Feed mail into postage canceling devices or hand stamp mail to cancel postage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one piece of mail fed into a canceling machine or hand-stamped. A competent worker processes mail in ~15 seconds per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Grasping and orienting flat mail items is feasible but challenging due to thin, flexible, varied-size objects. Precise alignment for machine feeding or hand-stamping requires reliable dexterity and perception that current humanoids partially achieve but inconsistently.

task: Deliver mail to residences and business establishments along specified routes by walking or driving, using a combination of satchels, carts, cars, and small trucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full delivery route. A typical letter carrier route takes 3–5 hours including walking, driving, sorting at stops, and navigating varied terrain.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving vehicles on public roads (humanoids cannot), navigating highly varied outdoor terrain, reading addresses, operating mailboxes, climbing steps, and covering miles autonomously — far beyond current frontier robot capabilities.

task: Conduct surveys to determine exact positions, measurement of points, elevations, lines, areas, volumes, contours, or other features of land surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full land survey of a typical parcel (~1 acre). A competent surveyor spends ~3–5 hours on-site setting up, traversing, and recording measurements.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized survey instruments (total stations, GPS rovers, levels) across varied outdoor terrain, making precise judgments, and navigating legal/technical survey protocols. Current humanoids lack the tools, outdoor terrain capability, and domain-specific instrument operation needed.

task: Inspect brake systems, steering mechanisms, wheel bearings, and other important parts to ensure that they are in proper operating condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full undercarriage/brake/steering inspection of one vehicle. A competent mechanic takes ~30–60 min to check brakes, bearings, steering, and related components thoroughly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires crawling under vehicles, applying torque to check bearing play, tactile assessment of wear, and interpreting subtle visual/auditory cues. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, specialized tool use, and diagnostic judgment for this unstructured mechanical inspection.

task: Bolt or arc weld pressure vessel structures and parts together, using wrenches or welding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one weld seam or bolted joint on a pressure vessel. A competent welder/fitter takes ~30–90 min depending on joint complexity; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Pressure vessel welding demands certified precision, high-heat tool use, positional awareness, and code compliance. Humanoids lack certified welding torch control, heavy-part manipulation, and the fine seam-tracking needed for structural welds. Fixed robotic welders exist but are not general-purpose humanoids.

task: Perform physically demanding tasks, such as digging trenches to lay conduit or moving or lifting heavy objects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = digging a single trench segment (~10–20 ft) or moving/lifting one heavy object on a job site; a competent laborer typically takes ~1 hour.
robot_success_rationale: Trench digging requires powered tools (shovels, trenchers) a humanoid cannot reliably operate outdoors; rough terrain mobility, sustained force output, and tool-use generalization are well below needed levels in 2026.

task: Collect verification data on the ground, using equipment such as global positioning receivers, digital cameras, or notebook computers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single field verification session at a site, using GPS/camera/computer to log ground-truth data. A competent surveyor typically spends ~1 hour per site visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated outdoor mobility over varied terrain, operating specialized instruments (GPS receivers, cameras, laptops), logging structured data, and exercising contextual judgment about what to verify — far beyond current humanoid generalization and tool-use outdoors.

task: Monitor raw data quality during collection, and make equipment corrections as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one monitoring session/check cycle during active data collection. A competent technician reviews data streams and adjusts equipment, typically ~30 min per meaningful check cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level cognitive judgment to interpret data quality metrics, diagnose root causes, and make context-specific equipment corrections. Current robots lack the reasoning, domain expertise, and fine tool-use needed for autonomous equipment calibration/correction in varied field setups.

task: Prepare and package individual place settings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and packaging one individual place setting (e.g., napkin, utensils, condiments). A competent worker can complete this in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires picking varied utensils/napkins, precise placement/folding, and packaging into a wrapper or bag. Dexterity challenges with thin/flexible items (napkins, packaging film) and consistent quality are significant barriers for current humanoids.

task: Deliver and retrieve items throughout the library by hand or using pushcart.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one delivery/retrieval trip with a batch of items across a library floor, including loading cart, navigating aisles, and shelving/collecting. Roughly 5–10 min per trip.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate flat indoor terrain and do basic pick-and-place, but pushing a loaded cart, reading call numbers, navigating narrow library aisles, and precisely shelving varied book sizes/shapes remain challenging. No reliable pushcart manipulation in unstructured environments yet.

task: Prime and paint repaired surfaces, using paint sprayguns and motorized sanders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = priming and painting one repaired surface panel (e.g. auto body or wall section). Includes sanding, masking, priming, and spray painting a typical repair area.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise spray gun control, consistent distance/angle, masking, motorized sander operation, and color-matching judgment. Current humanoids lack the fine tool-use, surface perception, and quality-control feedback needed for acceptable finish work.

task: Operate valve-grinding machines to grind and reset valves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grinding and resetting one valve. A skilled machinist typically takes 30–60 minutes per valve, including setup, grinding, checking seat angle, and resetting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a specialized valve-grinding machine with precise setup, force control, and quality inspection. Frontier humanoids cannot operate such specialized industrial machinery autonomously; lacks tool interfacing, fine tactile feedback, and metrology capability.

task: Attach rigging and signal crane or hoist operators to lift heavy frame and plate sections or other parts into place.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rigging one heavy section and signaling the crane/hoist to lift and place it. Includes selecting/attaching rigging hardware, guiding the lift, and confirming placement. Typical industrial lift takes ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires attaching heavy rigging hardware to large irregular loads (high dexterity + strength demands), interpreting load geometry, and coordinating live crane signaling. No 2026 humanoid can safely or reliably operate in this high-stakes, unstructured heavy-lift environment.

task: Mix, pour, or use processing chemicals or gases according to safety standards or established operating procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one mixing/pouring cycle of a chemical batch. A competent worker reads the procedure, measures, mixes, and pours safely, typically taking 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, handling of liquids/gases, reading labels, operating valves and dispensers, and strict safety compliance. Current humanoids lack reliable fine fluid handling, chemical perception, and safety judgment in unstructured environments.

task: Shape seams, joints, or irregular edges of pressure vessel sections or structural parts to attain specified fit of parts, using cutting torches, hammers, files, or metalworking machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting one seam/joint on a pressure vessel section. A skilled boilermaker/welder typically spends 30–60 min per joint measuring, cutting, filing, and test-fitting to spec.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-tool use (torch, hammer, file, machine), high-force metalworking, precise tactile feedback on heavy industrial parts, quality judgment to spec, and operating specialized machinery — all far beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Locate, measure, and mark site locations or placement of structures or equipment, using measuring and marking equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = surveying and marking a single site layout session (e.g., staking out one structure footprint). A competent surveyor/construction worker takes ~30–60 min using tape measures, levels, and marking tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise outdoor navigation on uneven terrain, interpreting site plans, using specialized tools (total stations, tape measures, spray markers), and placing accurate physical marks. Current humanoids lack surveying tool integration, GPS precision, and reliable outdoor mobility for this workflow.

task: Load floppy disks or tapes containing information into system.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inserting a single floppy disk or tape into a drive. A competent worker picks up the media, orients it correctly, and inserts it in ~1 minute.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise grasp of a thin, flat object, correct orientation detection, and smooth guided insertion into a narrow slot — demanding fine dexterity and alignment. Current humanoids struggle with such precise insertion tasks and legacy hardware variability.

task: Set up or operate assembly or processing equipment, such as lasers, cameras, die bonders, wire bonders, dispensers, reflow ovens, soldering irons, die shears, wire pull testers, temperature or humidity chambers, or optical spectrum analyzers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running one equipment cycle (e.g., loading a die bonder, configuring parameters, executing a bond run). A competent technician takes ~30–60 min per setup/run cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level fine dexterity (micron-scale die bonding, wire bonding), complex GUI/software interaction, multi-modal diagnostics, and specialized tool operation. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capability in unstructured semiconductor settings.

task: Generate high-resolution images or measure force-distance curves, using techniques such as atomic force microscopy.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one AFM session (sample prep, probe engagement, image/curve acquisition, and saving results). A competent operator typically takes ~45–90 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: AFM requires sub-nanometer precision tip alignment, vibration isolation, and expert software interpretation. No general-purpose humanoid can operate this highly specialized scientific instrument autonomously at acceptable quality.

task: Serve meals, distribute commissary items, and dispense prescribed medication to prisoners.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full distribution round across a prison housing unit (~20–50 inmates). Serving meals, handing out commissary, and dispensing meds per inmate takes ~1–2 min each; ~45 min total per round.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating dynamic, high-security environments with uncooperative or adversarial people, precise medication dispensing with legal accountability, identity verification, and handling diverse items — far beyond 2026 robot capabilities safely or reliably.

task: Inspect, repair, and maintain automotive and mechanical equipment and machinery, such as pumps and compressors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single piece of equipment (e.g., a pump or compressor) inspected, repaired, and returned to service. A competent mechanic typically takes 60–120 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults, fine dexterous tool use (wrenches, gauges, sealants), adaptive problem-solving on varied worn parts, and safety judgment. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities in unstructured real-world settings.

task: Disassemble and overhaul internal combustion engines, pumps, generators, transmissions, clutches, and differential units.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full disassembly, inspection, overhaul, and reassembly of a single major mechanical unit (e.g., an engine or transmission). A competent mechanic typically takes 6–10 hours per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme dexterity for torque-specific fasteners, seals, gaskets, and precision alignment; diagnostic judgment; use of specialized tools (pullers, micrometers, torque wrenches); and fluid/contamination management — far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Overhaul and test machines or equipment to ensure operating efficiency.
human_time_rationale: One instance = overhauling and testing one machine/piece of equipment. A competent mechanic/technician typically spends 2–5 hours disassembling, inspecting, replacing parts, reassembling, and running functional tests.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults, disassembling complex machinery, replacing varied components, using specialized tools, and interpreting test results — far beyond 2026 humanoid capability in unstructured, high-variability equipment environments.

task: Operate and inspect machines or heavy equipment to diagnose defects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and diagnosing defects on a single machine or piece of heavy equipment; a competent technician typically takes 30–60 minutes including operation, observation, and diagnosis.
robot_success_rationale: Heavy equipment requires specialized operation controls a humanoid cannot use; diagnosis demands expert multi-sensory judgment (sound, vibration, smell, visual anomalies) and deep mechanical reasoning far beyond current robot capability.

task: Select, calibrate, or operate equipment used in the non-destructive testing of products or materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up, calibrating, and operating NDT equipment (e.g., ultrasonic, X-ray, eddy current) on a single product/batch. A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: NDT requires selecting correct equipment, precise calibration with reference standards, interpreting ambiguous sensor readings, and adjusting technique in real time — demanding expert judgment and fine dexterity far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Test mechanical products and equipment after repair or assembly to ensure proper performance and compliance with manufacturers' specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single mechanical product/assembly post-repair. A competent technician spends ~20–30 min running functional checks, diagnostics, and comparing readings to spec sheets.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating varied test equipment, interpreting multi-modal diagnostic outputs, comparing to spec sheets, and making quality judgments. Current humanoids lack the tool-use generalization, fine instrument reading, and contextual reasoning needed across diverse mechanical products.

task: Measure, mark, or record openings or distances to layout areas where construction work will be performed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and marking a single layout area (e.g., a wall opening or floor section). A skilled worker uses tape, chalk line, and pencil in ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, tool use (tape measure, chalk line, marker), and legible marking on varied construction surfaces. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity for accurate layout marking and generalization to unstructured job sites.

task: Apply solvents and cleaning agents to clean surfaces of paintings, and to remove accretions, discolorations, and deteriorated varnish.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one painting surface. A conservator typically spends 1–3 hours per painting applying solvents, testing reactions, and carefully removing varnish/accretions under magnification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, tactile sensitivity, and expert visual perception to avoid irreversible paint damage. No current humanoid robot has the precision, solvent-handling capability, or nuanced judgment for art conservation work.

task: Examine products or materials, parts, subassemblies, and packaging for damage, defects, or shortages, using specification sheets, gauges, and standards charts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single batch/lot or workstation queue of items (e.g., a tray of parts or a packaged unit) against spec sheets and gauges; ~15 min is typical on a production line.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is maturing, but cross-referencing spec sheets, manipulating gauges on varied parts, interpreting standards charts, and making accept/reject judgments across diverse items requires perception, dexterity, and reasoning that current humanoids handle inconsistently.

task: Lay out plate, sheet steel, or other heavy metal and locate and mark bending and cutting lines, using protractors, compasses, and drawing instruments or templates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out and marking a single workpiece. A skilled metalworker takes ~15–25 min to measure, scribe lines, and mark bend/cut points using tools and templates.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine-dexterous tool use (scribers, compasses, protractors), precise force control on hard metal surfaces, geometric reasoning, and accurate template positioning. Current humanoids lack the precision, tool-use dexterity, and planning needed for acceptable quality.

task: Assess students' physical progress or needs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing one student's physical progress/needs (observation, movement checks, notes). A competent PE teacher or therapist typically spends ~10 minutes per student.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social perception, clinical judgment, interpreting subtle movement quality, and communicating findings. Current robots lack reliable human-body-motion assessment, empathetic interaction, and professional reasoning needed for acceptable quality.

task: Bundle mail in preparation for delivery or transportation to relay boxes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = bundling one batch of sorted mail (e.g., for one route or relay box). A competent postal worker sorts, groups, bands, and stacks a typical batch in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Mail items vary wildly in size, shape, and floppiness; precise stacking, aligning, and rubber-banding require fine dexterity and adaptive grasping. Current humanoids struggle with thin, flexible, irregularly shaped objects and consistent bundling quality.

task: Calibrate data collection equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = calibrating a single piece of data collection equipment (e.g., sensor, meter, or analyzer). A competent technician typically takes 20–45 min including setup, reference checks, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Calibration requires precise fine manipulation of adjustment controls, reading analog/digital displays, interpreting drift values, and applying domain-specific procedures. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, domain tool-use, and procedural reasoning needed for autonomous success.

task: Sort products or materials into predetermined sequences or groupings for display, packing, shipping, or storage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting a batch/lot of items (e.g., a conveyor load or bin) into designated groups. A competent worker takes ~5–10 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Modern humanoids handle pick-and-place and basic object recognition well in structured environments. Struggles with varied shapes, soft/fragile items, cluttered bins, and generalizing to new product types without retraining.

task: Position, align, and secure structural parts or related assemblies to boiler frames, tanks, or vats of pressure vessels, following blueprints.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning, aligning, and securing one structural part/assembly to a pressure vessel. A skilled boilermaker typically takes 60–120 min per component given blueprint reading, alignment checks, and fastening.
robot_success_rationale: Requires blueprint interpretation, precise heavy-part alignment (tight tolerances), use of specialized tools (clamps, torque wrenches, plumb lines) on large industrial structures. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, payload, and task-planning capabilities.

task: Dig ditches or trenches, backfill excavations, or compact and level earth to grade specifications, using picks, shovels, pneumatic tampers, or rakes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = digging, backfilling, or compacting one trench/excavation section (~10–20 ft). A competent laborer typically completes this in ~1 hour depending on soil and depth.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained high-force tool use (picks, shovels, pneumatic tampers) in unstructured outdoor terrain with variable soil. Current humanoids lack the strength, endurance, tool-coupling, and outdoor mobility for this at acceptable quality.

task: Shape or fabricate parts, such as stacks, uptakes, or chutes, to adapt pressure vessels, heat exchangers, or piping to premises, using heavy-metalworking machines such as brakes, rolls, or drill presses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single custom metal part (e.g., a chute or uptake section). A skilled boilermaker/metalworker typically spends 1–3 hours measuring, marking, cutting, and forming one piece on heavy machinery.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large, heavy, purpose-built machines (brakes, rolls, drill presses) with precise force and positioning control. Current humanoids lack the strength, reach, tooling interfaces, and process knowledge to operate such industrial machinery autonomously.

task: Inspect, test, and listen to defective equipment to diagnose malfunctions, using test instruments such as handheld computers, motor analyzers, chassis charts, or pressure gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing one piece of defective equipment. A competent technician typically spends ~20–30 min inspecting, connecting instruments, running tests, and interpreting results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting varied diagnostic tools, interpreting multi-modal sensor data (sound, pressure, electrical readings), and reasoning about faults. Current robots lack reliable tool-use generalization, nuanced auditory diagnosis, and complex technical reasoning needed for autonomous pass/fail judgment.

task: Assemble gear systems, and align frames and gears.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a single gear system (mounting gears onto a frame and aligning them). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Gear alignment requires precise fine dexterity, torque sensing, and tight positional tolerances. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter repeatability and force-feedback needed for reliable gear meshing and frame alignment without fixtures.

task: Use handtools, such as screwdrivers, pliers, wrenches, pressure gauges, or precision instruments, as well as power tools, such as pneumatic wrenches, lathes, welding equipment, or jacks and hoists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single tool-use job (e.g., loosening bolts, taking a pressure reading, or a short weld). A competent mechanic/technician typically completes such a unit task in ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Selecting, gripping, and applying varied hand/power tools demands high dexterity, precise torque control, and tool-change generalization. Current humanoids struggle with tool selection, secure gripping of handles, and safe operation of powered equipment like welders or lathes.

task: Carry food, dishes, trays, or silverware from kitchens or supply departments to serving counters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = carrying a load of food/dishes/trays/silverware from kitchen to serving counter; a competent worker completes a single trip in ~3–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate flat indoor environments and carry trays, but balancing loaded trays, avoiding dynamic obstacles (staff, carts), and reliably grasping varied dishware/silverware in cluttered kitchens remains error-prone for current humanoids.

task: Inspect assembled vessels or individual components, such as tubes, fittings, valves, controls, or auxiliary mechanisms, to locate any defects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one assembled vessel or component set. A skilled inspector visually checks, probes, and documents defects across multiple parts in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced multi-modal defect detection (cracks, leaks, misalignment, corrosion) across complex 3D geometry, navigating tight spaces, interpreting standards, and documenting findings — far beyond current robot perception and judgment.

task: Align front ends and suspension systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning one vehicle's front end and suspension. A skilled technician takes ~30–60 min including setup, adjustment, and verification on an alignment rack.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a specialized alignment rack, reading sensor readouts, making precise mechanical adjustments (tie rods, camber bolts) under a vehicle, and validating results. No current humanoid is capable of this full workflow in a real shop environment.

task: Ensure that lab cleanliness and safety standards are maintained.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single lab cleanliness/safety inspection and corrective round in a typical lab; a competent lab technician spends ~30 min checking surfaces, equipment, hazards, and tidying.
robot_success_rationale: Requires hazard recognition, chemical/biohazard awareness, nuanced safety judgment, spill handling, equipment checks, and compliance reporting — far beyond current robot perception and generalization. Mobility is feasible but reliable autonomous safety auditing is not.

task: Adjust, maintain, and repair or replace subassemblies, such as transmissions and crawler heads, using hand tools, jacks, and cranes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing a single subassembly (e.g., transmission or crawler head). Removal, inspection, repair/replace, and reinstallation typically takes 3–5 hours for a competent mechanic.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy lifting equipment (jacks, cranes), extreme force application, fine torque control on large fasteners, and working in confined, unstructured spaces under heavy machinery. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Clean pressure vessel equipment, using scrapers, wire brushes, and cleaning solvents.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one pressure vessel. Scraping, wire-brushing, and solvent application on a typical industrial vessel takes ~30–60 min depending on size/fouling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires forceful scraping and wire-brushing on curved, fouled industrial surfaces, tool switching, solvent handling, and reaching confined interior spaces. Current humanoids lack the force control, tool-change dexterity, and confined-space mobility needed.

task: Dismantle and reassemble heavy equipment using hoists and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dismantling and fully reassembling one major heavy equipment component (e.g., engine or gearbox). A skilled mechanic typically takes 3–5 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating hoists, applying high torque with hand tools, managing very heavy/irregular parts, precise reassembly with tight tolerances, and safety-critical judgment — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Install refractory bricks or other heat-resistant materials in fireboxes of pressure vessels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lining a single firebox with refractory brick. A competent mason typically takes 3–5 hours depending on firebox size and pattern complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires confined-space entry, precise mortar application, heavy/irregular brick placement, and complex geometric fitting in curved/tight fireboxes. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, payload, and spatial reasoning limits.

task: Conduct liquid penetrant tests to locate surface cracks by coating objects with fluorescent dyes, cleaning excess penetrant, and applying developer.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one part/object tested. Applying dye, dwell time (~15–20 min), cleaning excess, applying developer, and inspecting under UV light typically takes 30–60 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, even coating with liquid dye, controlled dwell time, careful wiping of excess without removing penetrant from cracks, then applying developer and UV inspection — multi-step fine dexterity and chemical process control beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Process print and non-print library materials to prepare them for inclusion in library collections.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single library item (book, DVD, etc.): stamping, labeling, applying barcodes, spine labels, security strips, and entering data. Competent library tech takes ~10–15 min per item.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise label placement, adhesive application, security strip insertion, and coordinated computer data entry across highly varied item types/sizes. Fine dexterity, multi-step tool use, and system integration far exceed current humanoid capabilities.

task: Splice fibers, using fusion splicing or other techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = splicing a single fiber pair. A skilled technician strips, cleans, cleaves, aligns, and fuses two fibers, then protects the splice — typically 15–25 minutes per splice.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme sub-millimeter precision: stripping ~250µm fiber coatings, cleaving cleanly, and aligning fiber cores (9µm for SM). Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tactile feedback. Specialized tools also needed.

task: Synthesize, process, or characterize nanomaterials, using advanced tools or techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single synthesis/processing/characterization run (e.g., one batch synthesis + instrument setup + data collection). Competent lab technician typically takes 1–3 hours per run.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-fine dexterity (pipetting at nanoliter scale, operating SEM/TEM/AFM), precise chemical handling, interpretation of complex spectra, and domain expertise. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capabilities.

task: Position or dismantle forms for pouring concrete, using saws, hammers, nails, or bolts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up or dismantling a typical concrete form section (e.g., a wall or footing panel). A competent carpenter takes ~1–2 hours per form section including cutting, nailing/bolting, and aligning.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool use (saws, hammers, nail guns, bolts) on large, heavy, irregularly shaped lumber/panel forms in unstructured outdoor construction sites. Beyond current humanoid dexterity, strength, tool-switching, and terrain navigation capabilities.

task: Provide therapies such as intralesional steroids, chemical peels, or comodo removal to treat age spots, sun damage, rough skin, discolored skin, or oily skin.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient treatment session (e.g., a chemical peel or comedo removal). A competent aesthetician or dermatologist typically spends 20–40 minutes per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterous tool use on delicate human skin, real-time clinical judgment, patient communication, sterile technique, and adaptive response to pain/tissue reaction — far beyond 2026 frontier robot capability.

task: Tend pumps, compressors, or generators to provide power for tools, machinery, or equipment or to heat or move materials, such as asphalt.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one monitoring/adjustment cycle (startup, check gauges, adjust settings, log status). A competent operator typically spends ~30 min per cycle on a job site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, adjusting valves and controls, diagnosing faults, and operating in unstructured outdoor/industrial environments. Current humanoids lack the specialized perception, tool-use, and domain knowledge for safe autonomous operation.

task: Erect or dismantle scaffolding, shoring, braces, traffic barricades, ramps, or other temporary structures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = erecting or dismantling a typical small-to-medium scaffolding/temporary structure setup (e.g., one bay of scaffolding or a barricade line). A crew member spends ~1–2 hours on a representative unit of work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting of long poles/planks, outdoor uneven terrain, complex multi-step assembly with connectors, bolts, and pins, plus safety-critical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, payload, and unstructured-environment generalization.

task: Perform visual inspections of finished products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one finished product unit. A competent QC worker typically spends 2–5 minutes visually checking a product for defects, completeness, and conformance.
robot_success_rationale: Modern robots have capable vision systems for structured defect detection, but generalizing across varied product types, orientations, lighting, and subtle defects (scratches, color variance, misalignment) remains unreliable without task-specific training. Perception and judgment gaps limit success.

task: Assemble or adjust parts or related electrical units of prototypes to prepare for testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling/adjusting one prototype unit. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min handling varied parts, making connections, and verifying fit before handoff to testing.
robot_success_rationale: Prototype work involves non-standardized, one-off parts requiring fine dexterity, connector alignment, wire routing, and adaptive judgment. Current humanoids lack the precision, contextual reasoning, and tool flexibility for novel electrical assemblies.

task: Rewire ignition systems, lights, and instrument panels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rewiring ignition system, lights, and instrument panel on a single vehicle. A competent auto electrician typically takes 2–4 hours depending on vehicle complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to handle small wire connectors, terminals, and harness routing in tight, cluttered vehicle interiors. Demands electrical knowledge, fault diagnosis, and tool use far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Receive and inspect raw materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one incoming shipment. A competent worker checks paperwork, counts/weighs items, and inspects quality in ~30 min for a typical delivery.
robot_success_rationale: Requires cross-referencing documents, interpreting purchase orders, measuring/weighing varied materials, judging quality defects, and communicating findings — all largely beyond current humanoid generalization and cognitive integration.

task: Provide assistance to staff members by inspecting rooms, setting tables, or doing laundry.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one room for cleanliness/readiness; a competent worker walks through, checks surfaces, fixtures, and amenities in ~10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate rooms and use cameras for basic visual checks, but nuanced quality inspection (stains, odors, misaligned items, missing amenities) requires fine perception and contextual judgment not yet reliable in frontier humanoids.

task: Rebuild gas or diesel engines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rebuilding one engine (teardown to reassembly). A competent mechanic typically takes 6–10 hours; median ~8 hours (480 min).
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme precision torque specs, fine dexterity (seals, gaskets, bearings), specialized tools (torque wrenches, pullers), and expert diagnostic judgment. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Smooth or finish freshly poured cement or concrete, using floats, trowels, screeds, or powered cement finishing tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing a typical concrete pour (e.g., ~100 sq ft slab). A skilled finisher spends ~45–75 min on screeding, floating, and troweling a section to acceptable smoothness.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained outdoor/semi-outdoor locomotion on wet concrete, precise force-controlled tool use (screeds, trowels), timing judgment as concrete sets, and broad-area coverage. No current humanoid has demonstrated this capability.

task: Perform skin surgery to improve appearance, make early diagnoses, or control diseases such as skin cancer.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one surgical procedure (e.g., excision of a skin lesion or cosmetic procedure). A competent dermatologic/plastic surgeon typically takes 30–90 minutes; ~60 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Skin surgery demands extreme sub-millimeter dexterity, haptic feedback, real-time clinical judgment, sterile technique, and patient interaction. Current humanoid robots lack surgical-grade precision, sterile compliance, and the autonomy for clinical decision-making required.

task: Test drive trucks and buses to diagnose malfunctions or to ensure that they are working properly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one test drive of a truck or bus. A competent mechanic typically drives a route of 15–30 minutes to warm up systems, stress-test components, and confirm or reproduce symptoms.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate a full-size truck or bus in live traffic. No current general-purpose robot can sit in a cab, work pedals/steering, and navigate public roads safely. Entirely outside 2026 frontier capability.

task: Perform incisional biopsies to diagnose melanoma.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one incisional biopsy procedure, including site prep, local anesthesia, incision, tissue removal, and wound closure. A competent dermatologic surgeon typically completes this in ~20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for precise incision on human tissue, sterile technique, real-time tactile judgment, local anesthesia administration, and clinical decision-making. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities; patient safety stakes prohibit autonomous operation.

task: Map the presence of imperfections within objects, using sonic measurements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = ultrasonic/sonic inspection of a single object (e.g., weld, casting, or structural component). A competent NDT technician takes ~30–60 min to scan, interpret, and map defects.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise transducer coupling/positioning, specialized NDT equipment, skilled interpretation of sonic data, and adaptive probe maneuvering. Current humanoid robots lack NDT tool integration, couplant handling, and defect-mapping software autonomy.

task: Perform routine maintenance such as changing oil, checking batteries, and lubricating equipment and machinery.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance session covering oil change, battery check, and lubrication on a typical piece of equipment (e.g., a vehicle or machine); ~30–60 min is standard.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (drain plugs, filler caps, grease fittings), tool use (wrenches, grease guns), awkward postures under machinery, and contextual judgment. Far beyond reliable autonomous robot capability in 2026.

task: Monitor or operate specialized equipment, such as gas chromatographs and high pressure liquid chromatographs, electrophoresis units, thermocyclers, fluorescence activated cell sorters, and phosphorimagers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up, running, and monitoring one instrument session (e.g., a single HPLC or GC run). Includes sample loading, parameter entry, and observation. Typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: These instruments require precise sample injection, software interaction on specialized UIs, interpreting real-time readouts, and responding to anomalies. Current humanoid robots lack the dexterity, lab-software integration, and domain-specific perception to operate such equipment autonomously.

task: Straighten or reshape bent pressure vessel plates or structure parts, using hammers, jacks, or torches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = straightening/reshaping one bent plate or structural part. A skilled boilermaker/metalworker typically spends 30–90 min per piece depending on severity of distortion.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy-force hammering, precise torch heating, hydraulic jack operation, and continuous tactile/visual judgment on thick metal deformation — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, force output, and unstructured tool-use capabilities.

task: Diagnose and treat pigmented lesions such as common acquired nevi, congenital nevi, dysplastic nevi, Spitz nevi, blue nevi, or melanoma.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining and diagnosing one pigmented lesion per patient visit, including history, dermoscopy, clinical assessment, and initiating treatment or referral. Typically 20–40 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires medical licensure, clinical judgment, dermoscopy interpretation, biopsy/surgical skill, and nuanced patient communication. Far beyond current robot autonomy — even AI-assisted imaging tools require physician oversight and cannot treat.

task: Align, fit, or assemble components, using hand tools, power tools, fixtures, templates, or microscopes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning/fitting/assembling one component set. Median across industries (mechanical, electronic, structural) a skilled worker takes ~20–30 min per assembly unit.
robot_success_rationale: Broad task spanning simple pick-and-place to fine microscope-guided work. Robots handle basic fixture-guided assembly (~50-70%) but struggle with arbitrary tool use, templates, and precision alignment. High variance drags probability down significantly.

task: Brush glue onto manila paper on which design has been drawn and position tiles, finished side down, onto paper.
human_time_rationale: One instance = gluing and tiling one manila paper sheet with a pre-drawn design; involves brushing glue and carefully placing multiple tiles face-down.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine brush control for glue application, precise tile placement aligned to a drawn design (perception of freehand lines), and delicate handling of small tiles face-down without slipping — all severely challenging for current humanoid robots.

task: Locate and mark reference points for columns or plates on boiler foundations, following blueprints and using straightedges, squares, transits, or measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating and marking reference points for one boiler foundation layout. A skilled boilermaker/millwright typically takes 30–60 min to set up instruments, read blueprints, and mark all column/plate points.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting complex engineering blueprints, operating precision surveying instruments (transits, squares, straightedges), and making fine measurements on an industrial construction site with uneven terrain. Far beyond current robot generalization and tool-use capabilities.

task: Diagnose and treat skin conditions such as acne, dandruff, athlete's foot, moles, psoriasis, or skin cancer.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient consultation: history, visual/tactile skin exam, diagnosis, and treatment plan. A competent dermatologist or GP typically takes 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires medical licensing, nuanced clinical reasoning, dermoscopy interpretation, patient communication, and hands-on procedures (biopsies, prescriptions). Current robots lack medical cognition, certified diagnostic software integration, and regulatory approval for autonomous clinical practice.

task: Mark up, paste, and assemble final layouts to prepare layouts for printer.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one layout page prepared for print (marked up, pasted, assembled). A competent paste-up/prepress worker typically takes ~30–60 min per layout.
robot_success_rationale: Task is primarily cognitive/visual-design (markup, alignment, color correction, software-based assembly). General-purpose humanoids lack design software fluency, fine visual judgment, and the 2D prepress workflow needed; near-zero autonomous success.

task: Attach test instruments to equipment, and read dials and gauges to diagnose malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching instruments to one piece of equipment and reading/interpreting gauges to reach a diagnosis. A competent technician typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise probe/clip attachment to varied terminals, reading analog/digital gauges, and interpreting diagnostic meaning. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, domain reasoning, and reliable probe-placement accuracy needed for autonomous success.

task: Clean or lubricate shafts, bearings, gears, or other parts of machinery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/lubricating one machine or component set. A competent mechanic typically spends 15–25 min per machine accessing, wiping, and applying lubricant to bearings, shafts, or gears.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied industrial environments, identifying specific lubrication points, handling grease guns or oilers, and applying precise amounts. Current robots lack reliable tool use, fine targeting of hidden ports, and generalization across machine types.

task: Clean and polish counters, shelves, walls, furniture, or equipment in food service areas or other areas of restaurants and mop or vacuum floors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single cleaning session covering counters, shelves, walls, furniture, equipment, and floors in one restaurant area. Competent worker averages ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle flat floor mopping/vacuuming reasonably well, but wiping vertical walls, polishing varied surfaces, navigating cluttered restaurant equipment, and switching tools mid-task remain major challenges for current humanoids.

task: Repair and replace damaged or worn parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing, removing, and replacing one damaged/worn part (e.g. a belt, bearing, or panel). Typical skilled-trade repair job runs 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing damage, selecting correct replacement part, applying variable force/torque, and using diverse tools (wrenches, screwdrivers, pliers) in unstructured settings — well beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Operate jackhammers or drills to break up concrete or pavement.
human_time_rationale: One instance = breaking up a typical pavement section (~10–20 sq ft). A competent worker takes ~30–60 min including setup, jackhammer operation, and clearing broken pieces.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained high-force operation of a heavy pneumatic/electric jackhammer, precise positioning on variable outdoor terrain, and managing vibration/recoil. No current humanoid robot has demonstrated this tool-use capability at acceptable quality or safety.

task: Provide assistance to craft workers, such as carpenters, plasterers, or masons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting a craft worker for a typical task segment (e.g., holding materials, passing tools, mixing compound). ~45 min per meaningful work session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires adapting to highly unstructured construction environments, anticipating craft worker needs, handling varied heavy/awkward materials, navigating cluttered job sites, and reacting safely near humans using tools. Current robots lack this generalization and situational awareness.

task: Extract deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) or perform diagnostic tests involving processes such as gel electrophoresis, Southern blot analysis, and polymerase chain reaction analysis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full DNA extraction + one diagnostic test (e.g., PCR + gel). A competent lab technician typically takes 2–4 hours end-to-end including prep, incubation, and analysis.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting at microliter scale, operating centrifuges, thermocyclers, and gel rigs, interpreting UV-imaged bands, and sterile technique. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and lab-instrument generalization.

task: Operate or maintain air monitoring or other sampling devices in confined or hazardous environments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = entering a confined/hazardous space, deploying/checking air monitoring equipment, recording readings, and exiting. Typical confined space entry procedure takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating tight, unstructured confined spaces (manholes, tanks, tunnels), manipulating specialized sampling devices, interpreting sensor readings, and operating safely in hazardous atmospheres — well beyond 2026 humanoid generalization and safety certification limits.

task: Fill orders for products and samples, following order tickets, and forward or mail items.
human_time_rationale: One instance = picking, packing, labeling, and staging one order for shipment. Covers reading the ticket, retrieving items, packaging, and addressing — ~10–15 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading order tickets (OCR/comprehension), navigating a warehouse, picking varied items, packing/labeling, and placing in outbox. Each step is partially feasible but chaining all reliably in an unstructured setting remains very low probability for a general-purpose humanoid in 2026.

task: Lay out cutting lines for machining, using drafting tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out cutting lines on a single workpiece. A skilled machinist typically takes 15–25 minutes using scribers, squares, calipers, and surface plates.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter precision scribing with specialized drafting tools, interpreting engineering drawings, repositioning a workpiece, and applying consistent pressure — far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot dexterity and fine-motor accuracy.

task: Raise trucks, buses, and heavy parts or equipment using hydraulic jacks or hoists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = raising a single vehicle or heavy component with a hydraulic jack/hoist. A competent mechanic takes ~5–15 min to position, attach, and safely lift.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy industrial jacks/hoists, precise jack-point identification on varied vehicles, high force application, and safety-critical judgment. Current humanoids lack the strength, specialized tool-use, and reliable force control for this task.

task: Assemble large vessels in an on-site fabrication shop prior to installation to ensure proper fit.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one large vessel in a fab shop. A competent boilermaker/pipefitter team typically spends a full shift (6–8 hrs) on fit-up, tacking, and checking alignment of a large pressure vessel or tank.
robot_success_rationale: Large vessel assembly requires heavy lifting (cranes/rigging), precise alignment of massive components, welding, and use of specialized industrial tools. Current humanoid robots lack the payload capacity, heavy equipment operation, and precision for this industrial-scale task.

task: Provide adapted physical education services to students with intellectual disabilities, autism, traumatic brain injury, orthopedic impairments, or other disabling condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single adapted PE session for a student/small group. Typical sessions run 30–60 min including setup, instruction, and cool-down.
