robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced real-time social/emotional responsiveness, adaptive instruction, behavioral de-escalation, trust-building, and safety judgment with vulnerable individuals — far beyond current humanoid capabilities in generalization and caregiving.

task: Inspect meters for unauthorized connections, defects, and damage, such as broken seals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one meter. A trained technician visually checks seals, connections, and physical damage, typically 5–15 min per meter.
robot_success_rationale: Requires close visual inspection, recognizing subtle defects (broken seals, tampered wiring), and navigating varied outdoor/indoor meter locations. Current robots lack reliable fine-detail anomaly detection and generalization across meter types and environments.

task: Paint or repair roofs, windows, doors, floors, woodwork, plaster, drywall, or other parts of building structures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = painting/repairing one standard surface area (e.g., one room's walls or one door/window). A competent painter or handyman averages ~2–3 hours per unit task.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied locomotion (ladders, roofs, scaffolding), fine tool use (brushes, rollers, caulk guns, putty knives), surface prep judgment, and working in highly unstructured environments. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Provide therapies, such as allergen immunotherapy or immunoglobin therapy, to treat immune conditions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one therapy session (e.g., allergen injection or IV immunoglobulin infusion). Prep, administration, and post-observation for a typical patient takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, patient assessment, sterile IV/injection technique, adverse-reaction monitoring, and legal medical licensure. Far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity, perception, and safety standards for autonomous medical procedures.

task: Apply a sealer to make grout stain- and water-resistant.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sealing grout in a typical bathroom (e.g., ~50 sq ft tiled surface). Competent worker applies sealer with a brush/applicator, wipes excess, and allows brief dwell time — ~45 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-line applicator control along narrow grout lines, uniform liquid dispensing, wiping excess off tile without streaking, and navigating confined spaces. Current robots lack the dexterity and precision for consistent sealer application on grout joints.

task: Turn in money and receipts collected along mail routes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single end-of-route turn-in; worker walks to the collection window/office, submits cash and receipts, and gets confirmation. Typically 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to a postal facility window, handling loose cash/paper receipts, interacting with staff or a kiosk, and completing a verified handoff — complex social, dexterous, and procedural steps far beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Fabricate or install metal sashes or moldings for glass installation, using aluminum or steel framing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating/installing one metal sash or molding unit for a standard window/door frame. A competent glazier typically takes 60–120 minutes including measuring, cutting, bending, and fitting aluminum or steel framing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise metal cutting, bending, and fitting tools (saws, brakes, crimpers) that humanoid robots cannot reliably operate; tight tolerances, heavy framing members, and complex spatial alignment far exceed current dexterity and tool-use capabilities.

task: Walk or drive vehicles along established routes to take readings of meter dials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full daily meter-reading route. A utility meter reader typically covers dozens of stops over a 3–5 hour shift, driving/walking between meters and logging readings.
robot_success_rationale: Task combines driving/navigating a route (humanoids can't drive vehicles in traffic) with reading analog/digital dials in varied outdoor environments. Navigation, vehicle operation, and reliable dial-reading across weather/lighting conditions are all beyond 2026 frontier humanoids.

task: Adjust or maintain equipment, such as lasers, laser systems, microscopes, oscilloscopes, pulse generators, power meters, beam analyzers, or energy measurement devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting/maintaining one piece of precision optical/electronic equipment (e.g., realigning a laser or calibrating an oscilloscope); a competent technician typically takes 20–45 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for sub-millimeter optical alignment, expert tool use (hex keys, micrometer adjusters), and domain-specific perception (reading beam profiles, scopes). Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Assemble fiber optical, optoelectronic, or free-space optics components, subcomponents, assemblies, or subassemblies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one optical subassembly (e.g., fiber connector, lens mount). Skilled technician spends 30–60 min including alignment and verification steps.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme sub-millimeter precision, micro-scale component handling, active optical alignment feedback, and clean-room dexterity. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capability; even specialized robots struggle here.

task: Weigh or measure materials, equipment, or products to maintain relevant records, using volume meters, scales, rules, or calipers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring/weighing a single item or batch. A competent worker selects the tool, measures, reads the value, and logs it — typically 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick up and place items on scales, but precise tool selection (calipers, rules, volume meters), correct positioning, accurate reading of analog/digital displays, and reliable data logging across varied instruments and materials remain challenging for general-purpose humanoids.

task: Operate cutting torches or welding equipment to cut or join metal parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one cut or weld joint on a typical workpiece. A competent welder/torch operator completes a standard weld or cut in ~15–25 minutes including setup and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torch positioning, sustained fine dexterity, high-heat tool handling, real-time seam tracking, and safety management. Current humanoids lack the specialized end-effectors, thermal sensing, and weld-quality perception needed for autonomous operation.

task: Work from ladders, scaffolds, or roofs to install, maintain, or repair electrical wiring, equipment, or fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single work session at elevation (e.g., installing a fixture or running conduit from a ladder/scaffold). A competent electrician typically spends 60–120 minutes per discrete elevated task.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing/stabilizing on ladders or scaffolds, fine dexterous electrical work, and safe operation at height. Current humanoids lack reliable ladder-climbing, load-bearing balance at elevation, and the precision tool-use needed for wiring tasks. Extreme safety risk compounds the limitation.

task: Clean parts by spraying them with grease solvent or immersing them in tanks of solvent.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one batch of parts via spray or tank immersion, including handling and repositioning; typically 5–10 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping varied oily parts, manipulating spray tools or submerging into tanks, and navigating an industrial workspace. Wet/oily surfaces challenge perception and grip; solvent handling adds safety complexity beyond current robot generalization.

task: Examine boilers, pressure vessels, tanks, or vats to locate defects, such as leaks, weak spots, or defective sections, so that they can be repaired.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single vessel/boiler unit. A competent inspector typically spends 30–60 minutes conducting a thorough visual and physical examination of one unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating confined/industrial spaces, interpreting subtle surface defects (corrosion, micro-cracks, leaks), applying specialized NDT tools, and making expert judgments. Current humanoids lack the specialized sensing, confined-space mobility, and defect-recognition reliability needed.

task: Eliminate outdated or unnecessary materials, destroying them or transferring them to inactive storage, according to file maintenance guidelines or legal requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a batch of files/materials for purging (reviewing, sorting, shredding/boxing). A typical purge session for a records clerk takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Task is overwhelmingly cognitive/legal: reading retention schedules, interpreting guidelines, making compliance decisions. Physical steps (shredding, boxing) are secondary. Current robots lack the semantic understanding and policy reasoning required; high failure risk.

task: Repair or replace defective pressure vessel parts, such as safety valves or regulators, using torches, jacks, caulking hammers, power saws, threading dies, welding equipment, or metalworking machinery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing or replacing one defective component (e.g., a safety valve) on a pressure vessel, including diagnosis, tool setup, replacement/repair, and verification. Skilled boilermaker/pipefitter takes ~60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy specialized tools (torches, welding equipment, power saws, threading dies), high-force manipulation, precise metallurgical judgment, and safety-critical decision-making in unstructured industrial environments — well beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Inspect used parts to determine changes in dimensional requirements, using rules, calipers, micrometers, or other measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single used part with measuring instruments. A competent machinist/technician takes ~5–10 min to measure key dimensions and assess wear or deviation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool handling (calipers, micrometers), interpreting analog/digital gauges, and making judgment calls on dimensional tolerances. Fine dexterity and tool-use for precision instruments remain weak in 2026 frontier robots; reading instrument displays and reasoning about wear adds further difficulty.

task: Remove exhaust stains from aircraft using cleaning fluids.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning exhaust stains from one aircraft's affected area. A competent human applies cleaning fluids, scrubs, and rinses one stained section, typically taking 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working at height on large curved aircraft surfaces, handling chemical fluids safely, applying variable scrubbing pressure, and navigating unstructured outdoor/hangar environments. Current humanoids lack the reach, stability, and chemical-handling precision needed.

task: Fit bearings to adjust, repair, or overhaul mobile mechanical, hydraulic, and pneumatic equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting bearings on a single assembly point (e.g., one axle or shaft) during overhaul. A skilled mechanic spends ~30–60 min selecting, pressing/seating, and verifying fit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled press-fitting, torque sensing, and tolerance verification on heavy/varied mobile equipment in unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack the specialized tooling use, fine force feedback, and dexterity for reliable bearing seating at required tolerances.

task: Set up and operate machine tools to repair or fabricate machine parts, jigs, fixtures, or tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and operating a machine tool (e.g., lathe, mill) to complete one part or repair job; a competent machinist typically takes 1–3 hours per job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level multi-step planning, precise tool selection, workpiece fixturing, G-code/manual machine operation, in-process measurement, and quality judgment — well beyond current humanoid robot generalization and fine dexterity capabilities.

task: Order or perform diagnostic tests such as skin pricks and intradermal, patch, or delayed hypersensitivity tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = administering a standard allergy test panel (e.g., skin prick or patch) on one patient, including prep, application, wait, and initial read — typically 20–45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (intradermal needle placement, patch positioning on skin), real-time clinical judgment, patient communication, and sterile technique on a compliant human body — far beyond 2026 robot capability. Patient safety stakes are critical.

task: Build prototype optomechanical devices for use in equipment such as aerial cameras, gun sights, or telescopes.
human_time_rationale: One prototype optomechanical device (e.g., a lens mount, sight assembly, or telescope module). Skilled technician spends ~2–4 hours on fabrication, alignment, and iterative fitting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter precision optical alignment, fine dexterous handling of delicate glass/metal components, iterative testing, and specialized tooling. Far beyond current humanoid robots' dexterity, perception resolution, and task generalization.

task: Clean or prepare construction sites to eliminate possible hazards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing/cleaning a typical construction site area per shift. A competent worker spends ~1 hour identifying and removing debris, tools, and hazards from a work zone.
robot_success_rationale: Construction sites are highly unstructured, with rough/uneven terrain, heavy/awkward debris, mud, and constantly changing layouts. Hazard identification requires contextual judgment. Current humanoids lack the terrain mobility, load capacity, and situational awareness needed.

task: Insert shims, adjust tension on nuts and bolts, or position parts, using hand tools and measuring instruments, to set specified clearances between moving and stationary parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shimming/adjusting one assembly point to spec. A skilled mechanic spends ~20–30 min measuring, selecting shims, adjusting fasteners, and verifying clearance with gauges.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled torquing, feeler-gauge reading, shim selection, and iterative micro-adjustment. Beyond 2026 frontier robots' fine dexterity, tactile feedback, and closed-loop precision tool-use capabilities.

task: Repair or replace wiring, equipment, or fixtures, using hand tools or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one wiring/fixture repair or replacement job (e.g., replacing an outlet or splice). A competent electrician typically spends 30–60 min per discrete repair.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (stripping wires, connecting terminals, screwing fixtures), fault diagnosis, safe live-wire handling, and tool switching. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and precision; safety constraints also prohibit autonomous electrical work.

task: Load, unload, or identify building materials, machinery, or tools, distributing them to the appropriate locations, according to project plans or specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading/unloading and distributing materials for a single delivery or work stage. A competent worker typically spends ~45 min sorting, moving, and placing varied items per delivery cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading project plans, identifying diverse/irregular materials, navigating unstructured construction sites (rough terrain, obstacles), and precise placement. Current robots lack robust outdoor locomotion, plan comprehension, and material-type generalization needed here.

task: Adjust and reline brakes, align wheels, tighten bolts and screws, and reassemble equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full brake adjustment/relining, wheel alignment, bolt tightening, and reassembly on one vehicle; a competent mechanic typically takes 60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating lift equipment, precise torque application, specialized automotive tools, wheel alignment machines, and high-force dexterous manipulation in confined spaces — well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Obtain specimens by performing procedures, such as biopsies or fine needle aspirations (FNAs) of superficial nodules.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one biopsy or FNA procedure on a single nodule, including prep, needle insertion, specimen collection, and wound care. Competent clinician typically completes this in 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile technique, sub-millimeter needle precision, real-time tactile/ultrasound feedback, clinical judgment, and patient interaction. Far beyond current general-purpose humanoid dexterity and medical safety standards.

task: Drive trucks to installation sites and unload mirrors, glass equipment, or tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one delivery trip: driving a truck to an installation site and unloading the cargo. Typical round-trip drive plus unloading averages ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot drive trucks in traffic. Unloading fragile items (mirrors, glass) requires careful dexterity robots struggle with. Both sub-tasks are beyond current frontier robot capability at commercial quality.

task: Create or distribute offline promotional material, such as brochures, pamphlets, business cards, stationary, or signage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and distributing a batch of offline promo materials (e.g., stuffing/stacking brochures, placing signage, handing out cards) at one location or event session.
robot_success_rationale: Involves handling thin, flexible, varied materials (brochures, cards), navigating social/public spaces, and making placement judgments. Poor dexterity with flimsy paper, no social navigation capability, and no creative/contextual distribution logic in current humanoids.

task: Instruct interns, residents, or others in ophthalmologic procedures and techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single instructional session (e.g., teaching slit-lamp use or surgical technique to a trainee). Typically 30–60 min including demonstration, supervised practice, and feedback.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert ophthalmologic knowledge, nuanced verbal/visual instruction, real-time adaptive coaching, and fine dexterous demonstration of microsurgical techniques. Far beyond current robot cognition, communication, and dexterity capabilities.

task: Apply caulking compounds by hand or caulking guns to protect against entry of water or air.
human_time_rationale: One instance = caulking one typical joint/seam (e.g., a window frame or bathtub edge). A competent worker preps, applies, and smooths a bead in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise continuous bead application along irregular seams, controlled tool pressure, and smooth finishing. Current robots lack the fine force control, path-following accuracy, and generalization across varied joint geometries needed for acceptable quality.

task: Read electric, gas, water, or steam consumption meters and enter data in route books or hand-held computers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reading a single utility meter and logging the value. A competent meter reader typically handles one meter in ~2–4 minutes including walking to it, reading the display, and entering data.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/indoor navigation to varied meter locations, visually reading analog or digital gauges in variable lighting, and accurately entering data. Current humanoids struggle with unstructured outdoor terrain, precise gauge reading, and data-entry on handheld devices.

task: Visit morgues, examine scenes of crimes, or contact other sources to obtain evidence or information to be used in investigations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single site visit or source contact (morgue, crime scene, or interview). A competent investigator typically spends 1–3 hours per visit gathering evidence.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigation of unstructured real-world sites, nuanced social interaction, legal authority, contextual judgment, and evidence interpretation. Current humanoids lack the cognitive reasoning, legal standing, and adaptive social skills this task demands.

task: Fasten glass panes into wood sashes or frames with clips, points, or moldings, adding weather seals or putty around pane edges to seal joints.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one glass pane into a wood sash/frame, including fitting, fastening clips/points/molding, and applying putty or weather seal around all edges.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise handling of fragile, large glass panes, fine dexterous application of putty/weather seal, and manipulation of small clips/points in tight frame grooves — well beyond current humanoid dexterity and force-control limits.

task: Maintain clean working environments, according to clean room standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/maintaining a single cleanroom session (gowning, wiping surfaces, mopping floors, checking particulate). Competent tech takes ~45 min per shift cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Cleanrooms demand strict gowning protocols, contamination-avoidance, ultra-precise surface wiping techniques, and particle monitoring — far beyond standard cleaning. Robots lack gowning ability, contamination awareness, and protocol compliance needed for ISO-certified cleanroom standards.

task: Examine patients to obtain information about functional status of areas, such as vision, physical strength, coordination, reflexes, sensations, language skills, cognitive abilities, and mental status.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full functional status examination of one patient, covering vision, strength, coordination, reflexes, sensation, language, cognition, and mental status; typically 20–45 min for a clinician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, nuanced verbal interaction, interpreting subtle patient responses, fine sensory testing (e.g., tuning fork, reflex hammer), and complex cognitive assessment — far beyond current humanoid perception, dexterity, and generalization capabilities.

task: Construct foundation for machines, using hand tools and building materials such as wood, cement, and steel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing a single machine foundation (forming, mixing/pouring cement, setting steel, curing prep). A competent tradesperson typically takes 2–4 hours per foundation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated heavy material handling, mixing/pouring concrete, precision steel placement, and skilled hand-tool use across unstructured environments. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, strength, and construction generalization.

task: Diagnose infections, such as Hepatitis B and Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome (AIDS), by conducting tests to detect the antibodies that patients' immune systems make to fight such infections.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a patient sample through antibody testing (e.g., ELISA): collecting specimen, preparing reagents, running the assay, and interpreting results. ~45 min total for a skilled lab technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly precise pipetting, sterile technique, complex lab instrument operation, clinical judgment, and interpretation of results. Current humanoid robots lack the fine dexterity, biosafety compliance, and cognitive reasoning needed for autonomous diagnostic lab work.

task: Fabricate or repair counters, benches, partitions, or other wooden structures, such as sheds or outbuildings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating or repairing one mid-sized wooden structure (e.g., a workbench or partition). A competent carpenter typically takes 2–5 hours; 4 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires measuring, sawing lumber to spec, drilling, fastening, and adapting to varied materials and environments. Current humanoids lack the precision tool-use, multi-step planning, and force control for sustained carpentry. No commercial deployment exists.

task: Return to the post office with mail collected from homes, businesses, and public mailboxes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single return trip to the post office after completing a mail collection route. Median drive time for a typical urban/suburban route return is ~20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a postal vehicle on public roads through mixed traffic. Humanoid robots cannot operate road vehicles autonomously. L4 AVs exist but are not general-purpose humanoid robots and lack postal fleet integration.

task: Assemble and install equipment, using hand tools and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and installing one piece of equipment (e.g., a machine unit or fixture). A competent technician typically takes ~1 hour including setup, fastening, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires switching between hand and power tools, applying variable torque, interpreting instructions, and adapting to diverse equipment geometries. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-switching, precision fastening, and generalization across unstructured install environments.

task: Align and balance new equipment after installation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning and balancing one newly installed piece of industrial/mechanical equipment (e.g., motor, pump, conveyor). Competent technician typically takes 60–120 min including measurement, adjustment, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precision metrology tools (dial indicators, laser alignment systems, vibration analyzers), fine mechanical adjustments under load, iterative feedback loops, and expert interpretation of readings. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and domain reasoning capabilities.

task: Lay out mounting holes, using measuring instruments, and drill holes with power drill.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring, marking, and drilling a set of mounting holes for a single fixture/component. A competent worker takes ~15 min including layout, marking, and drilling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement with hand tools, accurate marking, and controlled power-drill operation. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tool-use precision, and positional accuracy needed for acceptable hole placement and drilling control.

task: Mix, pour, or spread concrete, using portable cement mixers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing, pouring, and spreading a single batch of concrete for a typical small job (e.g., one footing or pad). A competent worker takes ~30–60 min including setup and cleanup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a portable mixer (heavy machinery), shoveling aggregate/cement, controlling water ratios, pouring and spreading viscous material—demanding strength, balance, tool manipulation, and tactile judgment far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Develop or use new non-destructive testing methods, such as acoustic emission testing, leak testing, and thermal or infrared testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = developing/applying an NDT method (e.g., setting up equipment, running a full test, and interpreting results) on a single component or structure; ~90 min median for a competent NDT technician.
robot_success_rationale: NDT requires expert sensor setup, calibration, nuanced interpretation of acoustic/thermal/leak signals, and adaptive problem-solving. Current humanoid robots lack specialized NDT tool integration, domain reasoning, and reliable autonomous diagnosis in unstructured environments.

task: Chain or clamp frames and sections to alignment machines that use hydraulic pressure to align damaged components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = securing one frame/section to alignment machine. Skilled auto body tech takes ~20–30 min to assess damage, position chains/clamps correctly, and verify secure attachment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized hydraulic alignment machinery, attaching heavy chains/clamps to specific damaged frame points with high precision and force, interpreting damage geometry, and working in a cramped shop environment — far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Operate and maintain audio-visual equipment, such as projectors, tape recorders, and videocassette recorders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up, operating, and basic maintenance of one AV session (e.g., connecting a projector, running a presentation, and wiping/storing equipment afterward).
robot_success_rationale: Requires plugging in cables, pressing specific buttons, reading displays, adjusting focus, and troubleshooting varied equipment. Poor generalization across legacy devices (VCRs, projectors), fine connector handling, and contextual fault diagnosis all challenge current robots.

task: Dismantle machines, using hammers, wrenches, crowbars, and other hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dismantling a single machine (e.g., industrial pump or motor). A competent worker typically takes 45–90 minutes depending on complexity; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force torque application, tool switching, varied fastener types, and adaptive disassembly sequencing. Current humanoids lack the grip strength, tool-change dexterity, and unstructured reasoning needed for full autonomous success.

task: Adjust functional parts of devices or control instruments, using hand tools, levels, plumb bobs, or straightedges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single functional part or control instrument; a competent technician typically spends 15–25 minutes locating, measuring, and fine-tuning one component to spec.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool-use (screwdrivers, wrenches, levels, plumb bobs), fine dexterous adjustments, and reading measurements to tolerance. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity and calibrated force control needed for reliable success.

task: Raze buildings or salvage useful materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = demolishing one average residential building. A skilled crew with excavators typically takes a full 8-hour shift for a small-to-mid structure.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy demolition machinery (excavators, bulldozers) in dynamic, hazardous outdoor environments. Humanoid robots cannot operate such equipment autonomously; far beyond current locomotion, perception, and tool-use capabilities.

task: Provide dermabrasion or laser abrasion to treat atrophic scars, elevated scars, or other skin conditions.
human_time_rationale: One treatment session for a single patient; includes prep, device application across affected area, and post-procedure assessment. Typical clinical session runs 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter precision on live human skin, real-time pain/tissue response feedback, clinical judgment for depth/pressure, and medical licensing. Far beyond current robot dexterity, perception, and safety standards.

task: Place, consolidate, or protect case-in-place concrete or masonry structures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pour/section of concrete or masonry structure. Skilled workers spend ~2 hrs covering setup, placement, consolidation (vibrating), and protective measures like curing blankets.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy equipment (concrete vibrators, pumps, mixers), handling bulky/wet materials outdoors, and adaptive judgment on consolidation quality. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured construction sites.

task: Dismantle machinery and equipment for shipment to installation site, performing installation and maintenance work as part of team.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dismantling a single piece of industrial machinery for shipment prep. A competent millwright/mechanic typically spends 2–4 hours on a mid-sized machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force torque on large fasteners, varied tool use, spatial reasoning on complex assemblies, coordination within a human team, and generalization across diverse machinery — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Prescribe or administer medications, such as anti-epileptic drugs, and monitor patients for behavioral and cognitive side effects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient medication administration plus monitoring session. A clinician typically spends ~15–30 min reviewing, dispensing, administering, and documenting behavioral/cognitive observations.
robot_success_rationale: Requires licensed clinical judgment, prescription authority, and nuanced cognitive/behavioral monitoring far beyond any robot's current capability. Physical administration alone is highly complex; autonomous clinical decision-making is entirely outside 2026 robot scope.

task: Inspect or test parts to determine nature or cause of defects or malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting/testing a single part. A competent technician spends ~15 min examining, testing, and diagnosing one part's defect or malfunction.
robot_success_rationale: Current robots lack reliable multi-modal diagnosis (visual, tactile, acoustic, functional testing) and causal reasoning about defects. Basic visual anomaly detection exists but determining root cause of malfunctions autonomously is well beyond frontier capability.

task: Mix ingredients to create compounds for covering or cleaning surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing one batch of compound (e.g., paint, cleaning solution, plaster). A competent worker measures, combines, and stirs ingredients to target consistency in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires measuring liquids/powders precisely, pouring, and stirring to a quality threshold. Current humanoids lack reliable fine liquid handling, consistent force-feedback stirring, and sensory checks (color, viscosity) needed for acceptance.

task: Perform ophthalmic surgeries such as cataract, glaucoma, refractive, corneal, vitro-retinal, eye muscle, or oculoplastic surgeries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one complete ophthalmic surgical procedure. Cataract surgery ~20–30 min; glaucoma/corneal/vitreoretinal procedures can run 60–180 min. Median across all types ~90 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme sub-millimeter dexterity inside a living eye, specialized micro-instruments, real-time surgical judgment, and sterile technique. Far beyond any current general-purpose humanoid robot's capabilities.

task: Prescribe or administer treatments, such as transcranial magnetic stimulation, vagus nerve stimulation, and deep brain stimulation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single treatment session (e.g., TMS session ~30–45 min, DBS programming ~60–90 min). Median across modalities ~60 min for a competent clinician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires licensed clinical judgment, patient assessment, precise neural targeting, sterile surgical technique (DBS), and regulatory/legal authority to prescribe. Far beyond any 2026 general-purpose robot's capabilities—medical, legal, and dexterity barriers are insurmountable.

task: Cut, assemble, fit, or attach metal-framed glass enclosures for showers, bathtubs, display cases, skylights, solariums, or other structures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and installing a single metal-framed glass enclosure (e.g., shower door unit). A competent glazier typically takes 1.5–3 hours; 2 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires cutting glass/metal, precise measuring, drilling into walls, leveling, sealing, and multi-step fitting in cramped spaces. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and precision for construction-grade installation work.

task: Transport mail from one work station to another.
human_time_rationale: One instance = carrying a batch of mail from one workstation to another within the same building; typical indoor walk of ~1–3 minutes including pickup and drop-off.
robot_success_rationale: Flat indoor navigation and pick-and-place of mail stacks is feasible for 2026 humanoids, but variable item shapes, doors, crowded corridors, and elevator use reduce reliability meaningfully.

task: Clean, lubricate, and perform other routine maintenance work on equipment and vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = routine maintenance session on a single piece of equipment/vehicle (e.g. oil check, lubrication, cleaning). Competent worker typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to/around vehicles, using grease guns, rags, and fluids, accessing tight spaces, and making judgment calls about wear. Highly varied tools, surfaces, and access points far exceed current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Perform allergen provocation tests such as nasal, conjunctival, bronchial, oral, food, or medication challenges.
human_time_rationale: One allergen provocation challenge (e.g., oral food or bronchial challenge) typically takes 60–120 minutes including preparation, staged dosing, monitoring, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, precise dose titration, real-time patient assessment, emergency intervention capability, and regulatory/ethical oversight. Far beyond current humanoid robot autonomy in medical procedures.

task: Perform preventive maintenance or service, such as cleaning, lubricating, or adjusting equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one preventive maintenance session on a single piece of equipment (e.g., a machine or HVAC unit). Cleaning, lubricating, and adjusting typically takes 30–60 minutes for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: PM tasks demand multi-step tool use (wrenches, grease guns, brushes), precise adjustments, and contextual judgment across varied equipment types. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use generalization, fine torque control, and the diagnostic reasoning needed for adjustments.

task: Grind or polish glass, smoothing edges when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grinding/polishing edges of a single glass piece (e.g., a cut pane or lens blank). A skilled glazier or optician takes ~5–15 min depending on size and finish required.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force control, fine dexterity, and tactile feedback to avoid cracking glass; tool handling of grinders/polishers on delicate curved or irregular edges is beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability. High breakage risk.

task: Recondition and replace parts, pistons, bearings, gears, and valves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reconditioning and replacing a set of parts (pistons, bearings, gears, valves) in a single engine or mechanical assembly. A competent mechanic typically takes 1.5–3 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torque application, fine dexterity for seating bearings/valves, use of specialized tools (torque wrenches, pullers), and complex multi-step sequencing in tight spaces — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Attach moving parts and subassemblies to basic assembly unit, using hand tools and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching a set of moving parts/subassemblies to one base unit (e.g., a single product station cycle). Competent assembler typically takes 15–25 min depending on part count.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated tool use (hand + power tools), precise alignment of moving parts, and generalizing across varied subassembly geometries. Current humanoids struggle with tool switching, torque control, and fitting articulated parts reliably.

task: Measure, cut, fit, and press anti-glare adhesive film to glass or spray glass with tinting solution to prevent light glare.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying film or tint spray to one standard glass pane/window. Measuring, cutting, peeling, aligning, and pressing film or spraying takes ~20–30 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, accurate cutting of thin film, delicate peeling/alignment without bubbles or creases, and controlled spray application — all demanding fine dexterity, surface sensing, and tool-use that current humanoid robots handle very poorly.

task: Conduct complete skin examinations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full-body skin exam of one patient by a dermatologist/clinician; typically 10–20 minutes including documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual/tactile clinical assessment of skin lesions, patient communication, repositioning a cooperative or non-cooperative human, and medical judgment. Current robots lack clinical perception, safe patient handling, and regulatory/safety clearance for autonomous medical diagnosis.

task: Position steel beams to support bedplates of machines and equipment, using blueprints and schematic drawings to determine work procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning steel beams for one machine's bedplate. A skilled millwright/ironworker reads blueprints, rigging, and sets beams, typically taking 1–3 hours per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading and interpreting engineering blueprints, operating cranes/rigging equipment, handling heavy steel beams, and precise leveled placement — all beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Calibrate, validate, or maintain laboratory equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = calibrating/validating one piece of lab equipment (e.g., a pipette, balance, or pH meter). A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min per instrument.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of small lab instruments, precise tool use, reading analog/digital displays, following complex protocols, and adapting to diverse equipment types — well beyond current humanoid robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Perform comprehensive examinations of the visual system to determine the nature or extent of ocular disorders.
human_time_rationale: One full ocular examination per patient, including history, visual acuity, refraction, slit-lamp, fundoscopy, tonometry — typically 30–60 min for a competent optometrist/ophthalmologist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-fine dexterity for instrument placement on a cooperative patient, clinical reasoning, differential diagnosis, patient communication, and operation of specialized ophthalmic equipment. Far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Cut and remove broken glass prior to installing replacement glass.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing broken glass from a single window/frame. A competent glazier scores, breaks out, and clears residual shards in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise scoring tools, safe handling of sharp irregular shards, varied frame types, and fine force control to avoid further breakage. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-use precision, and safety compliance needed for this hazardous task.

task: Trim material and arrange bouquets, wreaths, terrariums, and other items, using trimmers, shapers, wire, pins, floral tape, foam, and other materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one floral arrangement (bouquet, wreath, or terrarium). A skilled florist typically spends 15–35 minutes per piece depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly dexterous manipulation of delicate, irregular, deformable materials (stems, petals, wire, tape, foam). Fine trimming, shaping, and aesthetic judgment far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities. Generalization across item types is very poor.

task: Operate cranes or hoists with suction cups to lift large, heavy pieces of glass.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lifting and placing a single large glass panel. Attaching suction cups, operating crane/hoist controls, and positioning takes ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a crane or hoist — specialized heavy machinery a humanoid robot cannot drive or control. High-stakes fragile load (glass breakage risk). No current general-purpose robot has demonstrated this capability.

task: Select flora and foliage for arrangements, working with numerous combinations to synthesize and develop new creations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and assembling one floral arrangement. A skilled florist typically spends 30–60 minutes selecting, combining, and arranging flora for a single custom piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level aesthetic judgment, creative synthesis, and fine dexterous handling of delicate, irregular stems and petals. Current robots lack the creativity, taste reasoning, and gentle dexterity needed for novel floral design.

task: Attend games and competitions to provide evaluation and treatment of activity-related injuries or medical conditions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attending a single game/competition (e.g., a 2–3 hour sporting event) including sideline presence, monitoring, and any injury evaluation/treatment that arises.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time clinical assessment, diagnostic reasoning, triage decisions, and hands-on treatment (taping, splinting, CPR, concussion protocols) in a chaotic, outdoor, crowd-filled environment. Far beyond current robot capability in perception, dexterity, and medical judgment.

task: Signal crane operator to lower basic assembly units to bedplate, and align unit to centerline.
human_time_rationale: One instance = signaling one unit lowered and aligned to centerline; includes rigging checks, hand/radio signals, and precision alignment using measurement tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating crane controls or issuing standardized crane signals, coordinating with remote operators, and performing precision alignment on a bedplate — all in a heavy industrial environment. Humanoids lack crane operation ability, reliable industrial signaling protocols, and sub-millimeter alignment capability on large assemblies.

task: Provide coaches and therapists with assistance in selecting and fitting protective equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting and fitting protective equipment for one individual; includes assessment, gear selection, and fit adjustments. Typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced body measurement, fine dexterous adjustment of straps/buckles, clinical judgment on fit quality, and interpersonal communication. Current robots lack tactile sensitivity, reliable fine manipulation, and domain knowledge integration needed here.

task: Collect product samples and prepare them for laboratory analysis or testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a single product sample and preparing it (labeling, packaging, logging) for lab submission. A competent worker typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise sampling tools, correct volume/quantity extraction, contamination-free handling, labeling, and documentation. High dexterity demands, varied containers/materials, and protocol compliance make autonomous success unlikely for current humanoids.

task: Score glass with cutters' wheels, breaking off excess glass by hand or with notched tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scoring and breaking one glass piece to a marked line. A skilled glazier completes this in ~3–7 minutes including marking, scoring, and snapping.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, consistent pressure along a scored line (too light = no score; too heavy = cracking), then controlled snap-breaking. Fine force modulation, brittle material handling, and tool-use precision far exceed current humanoid dexterity reliability.

task: Perform electrodiagnosis, including electromyography, nerve conduction studies, or somatosensory evoked potentials of neuromuscular disorders or damage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a complete electrodiagnostic session (EMG + NCS ± SSEPs) for one patient. A competent neurologist/physiatrist typically takes 45–90 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise needle insertion into specific muscles, real-time clinical interpretation, adaptive patient communication, and licensed medical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, perception, and autonomous clinical reasoning.

task: Provide individual or small groups of students with adapted physical education instruction that meets desired physical needs or goals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single adapted PE session for 1–3 students. A typical adapted PE session runs ~30–60 minutes; 45 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic social interaction, real-time pedagogical judgment, adaptive communication, motivating students with disabilities, and modifying activities on the fly — far beyond current robot generalization, social cognition, or safe caregiving capability.

task: Diagnose diseases or study medical conditions, using techniques such as gross pathology, histology, cytology, cytopathology, clinical chemistry, immunology, flow cytometry, or molecular biology.
human_time_rationale: One instance = evaluating a single case/specimen through relevant techniques and rendering a diagnosis. A competent pathologist typically spends 60–120 minutes per complex case including review, analysis, and reporting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level cognitive reasoning, multi-modal laboratory interpretation (microscopy, immunoassays, molecular assays), clinical judgment, and medical licensure. Current robots lack the cognition, lab dexterity, and integrative diagnostic reasoning needed. AI software assists but no autonomous robot system can perform end-to-end diagnosis.

task: Integrate robotics with peripherals, such as welders, controllers, or other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = integrating one robot system with a peripheral (e.g., welder or controller). Includes physical connection, wiring, configuration, and functional testing. Typically 1–3 hours for a skilled technician.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires expert-level systems knowledge, precise electrical/mechanical interfacing, tool use (crimpers, screwdrivers, multimeters), reading schematics, and software configuration. Far beyond current general-purpose robot autonomy.

task: Measure and mark outlines or patterns on glass to indicate cutting lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and marking a single pane or pattern piece of glass. A skilled glazier takes ~5–10 minutes to measure, position a template, and mark cutting lines accurately.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement on a reflective/transparent surface, fine-motor marking with a glass scribe or marker, and careful handling of fragile glass. Current robots lack the dexterity, tactile feedback, and transparent-surface perception needed for reliable success.

task: Cover surfaces with dropcloths or masking tape and paper to protect surfaces during painting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a typical room (floors, trim, fixtures). A competent painter lays dropcloths and applies masking tape/paper in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tape application along irregular edges/trim, handling large floppy dropcloths, and navigating cluttered rooms. Fine-edge masking demands dexterity and spatial reasoning far beyond current humanoid capability. Generalization across varied surfaces is poor.

task: Assemble, erect, or dismantle scaffolds, rigging, or hoisting equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling or dismantling a standard scaffold section/bay. A competent ironworker/scaffold erector typically takes 1–2 hours per bay including layout, component handling, and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy component lifting, high-elevation mobility, precise mechanical coupling of large irregular parts, and working at height on unstable surfaces — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, payload, and safety capabilities.

task: Perform or interpret the outcomes of procedures or diagnostic tests, such as lumbar punctures, electroencephalography, electromyography, and nerve conduction velocity tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = performing and/or interpreting a single neurodiagnostic procedure (e.g., LP, EEG, EMG/NCV). Median total time including setup, execution, and interpretation is ~45–75 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced fine dexterous manipulation (needle insertion, electrode placement), real-time clinical judgment, patient communication, sterile technique, and nuanced interpretation of complex waveforms. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Apply primers or sealers to prepare new surfaces, such as bare wood or metal, for finish coats.
human_time_rationale: One instance = priming one typical surface (e.g., a door or wall section). Includes prep, mixing/stirring primer, applying evenly by brush/roller, and cleanup. ~45 min for a competent painter.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated brush/roller tool use, consistent pressure and stroke uniformity, surface navigation, and drip avoidance. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control, fluid handling, and adaptive coverage judgment needed for acceptable finish quality.

task: Secure mirrors in position, using mastic cement, putty, bolts, or screws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = securing one mirror. Involves alignment, applying mastic/putty or driving fasteners, and checking stability. A skilled glazier completes this in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise large-panel handling, controlled adhesive application, and fine fastening—all while keeping a fragile, reflective surface aligned. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, grip force control, and tool-use generalization needed reliably.

task: Decorate, or supervise the decoration of, buildings, halls, churches, or other facilities for parties, weddings and other occasions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = decorating a single venue for an event (e.g., wedding hall). A competent decorator typically spends 2–4 hours setting up décor, draping, floral arrangements, lighting, and signage.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative aesthetic judgment, climbing ladders, handling delicate/varied materials (fabric, flowers, balloons), interpreting client vision, and working in unstructured environments. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities autonomously.

task: Replace defective parts of machine, or adjust clearances and alignment of moving parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing one defective part or performing one alignment/clearance adjustment on a typical industrial machine. A skilled mechanic takes ~30–60 min on average.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing defects, selecting correct parts, applying precise torque, and fine-tuning clearances/alignments — demanding tool use, force feedback, and contextual judgment far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Examine structures or vehicles such as aircraft, trains, nuclear reactors, bridges, dams, and pipelines, using non-destructive testing techniques.
human_time_rationale: One inspection session of a single structure/component (e.g., a weld zone, pipe segment, or bridge section) using NDT equipment; 1–3 hours is typical for a qualified technician.
robot_success_rationale: NDT requires specialized sensor deployment (ultrasonic, radiographic, eddy-current), precise probe placement, navigation over complex/confined structures, expert interpretation of results, and regulatory certification. Current humanoids lack the tool integration, fine probe control, and domain judgment needed.

task: Install robot and modify its program, using teach pendant.
human_time_rationale: One instance = physically mounting/wiring a robot and modifying its program via teach pendant. A skilled technician typically takes 1–3 hours; 2 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires mechanical installation (bolting, wiring), then operating a teach pendant with precise button sequences and proprietary UI. Humanoids lack specialized tool-use, torque control for mounting, and cannot meaningfully operate vendor-specific teach pendants autonomously.

task: Transport materials, products, or samples to processing, shipping, or storage areas, manually or using conveyors, pumps, or hand trucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one transport trip (load, move, deliver, unload). Typical warehouse/factory trip across short-to-medium distances, ~5–10 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle flat indoor navigation and simple pick-and-place moderately well, but varied item types, hand trucks, conveyors, tight spaces, and unstructured environments reduce reliability significantly. Loading/unloading varied items ~20–50%.

task: Diagnose, treat, or provide continuous care to hospital inpatients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient encounter (diagnosis, treatment, or care round). A competent physician/nurse spends ~20–40 min per inpatient interaction on average.
robot_success_rationale: Requires medical reasoning, physical examination, sterile procedures, IV/medication management, and dynamic patient interaction. Far beyond 2026 robot capability in dexterity, clinical judgment, and safety standards.

task: Provide assistance to workers engaged in construction or extraction activities, using hand tools or other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one discrete assistance task (e.g., hauling materials, holding components, hand-digging). A typical assist job on a construction site averages ~45 min of active work per assignment.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor uneven terrain, varied heavy tools, unpredictable site hazards, and coordinated teamwork severely challenge current humanoids. Rough-terrain locomotion (~30–50%), heavy payload handling, and context-specific tool use are all near-frontier limits.

task: Move furniture to clear work sites and cover floors or furnishings with drop cloths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one room: moving several pieces of furniture to the perimeter and spreading drop cloths over floors/furnishings. A competent worker takes ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires moving heavy, bulky, varied furniture (sofas, tables, chairs) requiring high strength, planning, and coordination; then unfolding and draping large drop cloths precisely. Current humanoids lack the strength, bimanual coordination, and generalization for this multi-step unstructured task.

task: Install or replace machinery, equipment, and new or replacement parts and instruments, using hand or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing/replacing a single piece of machinery, equipment, or part. A competent technician typically spends 30–90 min depending on complexity; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct parts, precise alignment, torque-controlled fastening with varied hand/power tools, and generalizing across highly diverse machinery types. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-switching, torque feedback, and unstructured mechanical task generalization.

task: Tend and observe equipment and machinery to verify efficient and safe operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring/observation round of equipment in a facility; a competent worker typically walks the floor and checks key systems in ~20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate and read gauges/displays with moderate success, but verifying safe/efficient operation requires anomaly detection, contextual judgment, and responding to unexpected states across diverse machinery—currently unreliable for autonomous sign-off.

task: Identify defects in solid materials, using ultrasonic testing techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single component/weld zone. A trained UT technician sets up equipment, scans the part, interprets A/B/C-scan data, and logs findings — typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise couplant application, controlled probe pressure/angle, real-time signal interpretation, and adaptive scanning paths. Current humanoids lack the fine-force control, domain-specific sensor integration, and expert signal interpretation needed for autonomous UT inspection.

task: Install sewer, water, or storm drain pipes, using pipe-laying machinery or laser guidance equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single pipe section/run in a trench. A crew typically installs several sections per shift; ~2 hours covers excavation alignment, laser setup, pipe laying, and jointing per segment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy excavators, pipe-laying machinery, and laser guidance equipment in unstructured outdoor trenches. Humanoid robots cannot drive or operate such machinery, and the task demands coordinated multi-machine control, load handling, and precision grading.

task: Record athletes' medical histories, and perform physical examinations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one athlete's intake session: recording medical history via interview plus a standard physical exam (vitals, musculoskeletal check, etc.).
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced verbal interview, clinical judgment, auscultation, palpation, neurological testing, and documentation. Current humanoids lack medical sensing tools, clinical reasoning, and the dexterity/perception for a full physical exam.

task: Unload or unpack incoming shipments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unloading one typical incoming shipment (e.g., a pallet or box lot). A competent warehouse worker takes ~20–40 min to unload and unpack a standard delivery.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with highly varied box sizes, weights, and packing materials; unstructured truck/dock environments; and unpacking steps (cutting tape, removing wrapping). Pick-and-place of uniform items is better, but real shipment variability keeps success low.

task: Create patterns on glass by etching, sandblasting, or painting designs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing one decorative glass piece. A competent artisan typically spends 30–90 minutes designing, masking, and etching/sandblasting/painting a single piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for masking and painting, precise pressure control for sandblasting/etching, artistic pattern interpretation, and safe handling of fragile glass and chemical etchants — all far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Collect samples or specimens for diagnostic testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a single specimen (e.g., blood draw, swab, or urine sample) from a patient, including prep, collection, labeling, and packaging.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterous manipulation on a human body (needles, swabs, tubes), sterile technique, patient cooperation management, and clinical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Examine patients to assess mobility, strength, communication, or cognition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient assessment session covering mobility, strength, communication, and cognition; a competent clinician typically takes 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, nuanced interpretation of verbal/nonverbal cues, standardized test administration, and adaptive interaction. Current humanoids lack medical reasoning, reliable speech understanding in clinical contexts, and validated assessment protocols.

task: Prepare glass for cutting by resting it on rack edges or against cutting tables and brushing thin layer of oil along cutting lines or dipping cutting tools in oil.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single pane/sheet of glass: positioning it on rack/table and applying oil along cut lines. A skilled glazier completes this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise handling of large, fragile, reflective glass sheets (hard to perceive), controlled oil application along exact lines, and stable positioning on rack edges — all highly challenging for current humanoids; breakage risk is high.

task: Perform, order, or interpret the results of diagnostic or clinical tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = ordering, performing, and interpreting a single diagnostic/clinical test (e.g., blood draw + lab review). Median across common tests is ~30 min including documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical reasoning, nuanced patient interaction, fine dexterity for sample collection, regulatory authority, and medical judgment. Well beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities in autonomous clinical settings.

task: Diagnose or treat diseases or disorders of genitourinary organs and tracts including erectile dysfunction (ED), infertility, incontinence, bladder cancer, prostate cancer, urethral stones, or premature ejaculation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient consultation/diagnostic workup or treatment session. A competent urologist spends ~45 min on history, exam, tests, and a treatment plan per visit.
robot_success_rationale: Diagnosing/treating complex medical conditions requires clinical reasoning, nuanced patient communication, physical examination judgment, and licensed medical expertise. No current humanoid robot approaches this autonomously at acceptable quality.

task: Hold or supply tools, parts, equipment, and supplies for other workers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retrieving and handing off a single tool/part/supply to a coworker on demand; a typical handoff takes ~5 minutes including retrieval and positioning.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place common objects reasonably well, but identifying the correct item on demand, navigating to the right worker, and timing a safe handoff in a dynamic work environment reduces reliability significantly.

task: Diagnose or treat disorders of the musculoskeletal system.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient consultation including history-taking, physical examination, diagnosis, and initial treatment plan; typical orthopaedic/physio appointment ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires medical licensure, clinical reasoning, nuanced palpation, symptom interpretation, and patient communication — far beyond any current robot's cognitive, dexterous, or regulatory capability.

task: Apply protective materials to equipment, components, and parts to prevent defects and corrosion.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying protective material (coating, wrap, sealant, etc.) to a single piece of equipment or component. A competent worker typically spends 15–25 minutes prepping and applying coverage.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, uniform coating application with varied tools (brushes, sprayers, tape) on complex geometries. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, material-feel feedback, and generalization needed for consistent, defect-free coverage across diverse parts.

task: Fill cracks, holes, or joints with caulk, putty, plaster, or other fillers, using caulking guns or putty knives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling cracks/holes in a single surface area (e.g., one wall section or window frame). A competent worker preps, applies, and smooths filler in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to control caulking gun pressure, bead consistency, and putty knife smoothing; perception of irregular crack geometry; force modulation for smooth finish. Current humanoids lack the precision and adaptive tool-use for acceptable quality output.

task: Waterproof buildings, using waterproofers or caulking.
human_time_rationale: One instance = waterproofing one building section (e.g., a wall, roof seam, or foundation area). A competent worker typically spends ~2 hours applying caulking/sealant to a defined area.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied outdoor/semi-outdoor terrain, identifying gaps and seams, precise continuous bead application with a caulking gun, and surface prep. Current robots lack the fine motor consistency, tool-use generalization, and outdoor mobility needed reliably.

task: Grind, scrape, sand, or polish surfaces, such as concrete, marble, terrazzo, or wood flooring, using abrasive tools or machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating one average room (~30–50 sq ft of surface). A skilled floor finisher takes ~1 hour including setup, passes, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy specialized floor machines (grinders, sanders, buffers) with precise pressure and path control, recognizing surface quality, and adapting to varied materials. Humanoids lack the grip strength, tool-mounting ability, and surface-quality perception needed for acceptable results.

task: Remove fixtures such as pictures, door knobs, lamps, or electric switch covers prior to painting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing all fixtures in a typical room (4–8 items: switch covers, door knobs, picture hooks, lamps). A competent worker takes ~20 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse tool use (screwdrivers, unscrewing varied knobs/covers), precise torque control, and adapting to many fixture types/locations. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity and generalization across unstructured fixtures.

task: Mop, brush, or spread paints, cleaning solutions, or other compounds over surfaces to clean them or to provide protection.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mopping/brushing/spreading compound over a typical surface area (e.g., one room floor or one wall section); a competent worker finishes in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Current humanoids can handle mopping/wiping on flat surfaces reasonably well, but spreading coatings evenly with a brush or mop, managing liquid control, and adapting to varied surfaces/compounds challenge dexterity and force regulation.

task: Polish final coats to specified finishes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = polishing a final coat on one workpiece/surface (e.g. a painted panel or furniture piece). A skilled finisher typically spends 15–35 min per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, consistent force control, nuanced tactile feedback to judge finish quality, and tool manipulation (orbital polisher, pad changes). Current humanoids lack reliable surface-quality perception and variable-pressure polishing dexterity to meet professional finish standards.

task: Bolt parts, such as side and deck plates, jaw plates, and journals, to basic assembly unit.
human_time_rationale: One instance = bolting a set of parts (side/deck plates, jaw plates, journals) to one basic assembly unit. A skilled assembler takes ~20–30 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Heavy, irregular industrial parts require precise alignment, high-torque bolting, and tool changes. Humanoids lack the force control, specialized torque tools, and part-handling strength for heavy jaw/journal plates reliably.

task: Sort and remove trash, placing it in designated pickup areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting and moving trash/recyclables from a single area (e.g., office, room, or small zone) to designated pickup points. A competent worker takes ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires category recognition of varied waste items, sorting decisions, bag handling, and navigating to designated areas. Perception and grasping of irregular/soft waste items and locomotion to multiple drop points challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Install manholes, handholes, taps, tubes, valves, gauges, or feedwater connections in drums of water tube boilers, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single fitting (e.g., one manhole or valve) in a boiler drum. Includes positioning, sealing, torquing bolts, and verifying fit — typically 60–120 min for a skilled boilermaker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires confined-space work inside large pressure vessel drums, high-torque wrench use, precise sealing of safety-critical joints, and professional-grade judgment. Far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity, strength, and situational awareness limits.

task: Compute power and space requirements for installing medical, dental, or related equipment and install units to manufacturers' specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single medical/dental unit (e.g., dental chair, X-ray unit). Competent technician typically takes 2–4 hours including power/space calculations and physical installation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading specs, electrical/plumbing reasoning, heavy lifting, precise alignment, torquing connections, and compliance verification — far beyond current humanoid generalization. No demonstrated capability for this complex multi-step technical task.

task: Remove from stock products or loads not meeting quality standards, and notify supervisors or appropriate departments of discrepancies or shortages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = identifying and physically removing one non-conforming product/load unit, then logging and notifying a supervisor. Typical cycle ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step reasoning: visual quality inspection (defect detection varies widely), decision-making on rejection criteria, physically isolating the item, and communicating discrepancies to supervisors—each step carries significant failure risk for current humanoids.

task: Diagnose or treat performance-related conditions, such as sports injuries or repetitive-motion injuries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient diagnosis/treatment session. A competent sports medicine clinician typically spends 30–60 minutes per visit including assessment, diagnosis, and initial treatment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical reasoning, nuanced physical examination (palpation, range-of-motion testing), differential diagnosis, and therapeutic interventions (taping, manual therapy). Far beyond current robot cognitive and dexterous capabilities; no frontier humanoid approaches clinical medicine autonomously.

task: Cut pipes to required lengths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting a single pipe to a required length. Includes measuring, marking, securing, and cutting with a pipe cutter or saw. ~5 minutes for a competent tradesperson.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, marking, securing a round/cylindrical object, and operating a pipe cutter or saw with controlled force. Challenging dexterity, tool-use, and force-control demands exceed current humanoid capabilities reliably.

task: Assist in the application of monitoring techniques, such as pulmonary artery catheterization, electroencephalographic spectral analysis, echocardiography, or evoked potentials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting with one monitoring procedure (e.g., PAC insertion or EEG setup). Setup, calibration, and initial monitoring run ~30–60 min for a skilled clinical technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile technique, fine dexterous catheter/probe manipulation, real-time clinical judgment, patient interaction, and interpretation of complex physiological signals. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Water plants, and cut, condition, and clean flowers and foliage for storage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single work session covering a standard batch of plants and cut flowers in a florist/greenhouse; a competent worker completes this in ~30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise cutting with shears, gentle handling of delicate stems, identifying conditioning needs, navigating crowded greenhouse environments, and managing water tools — well beyond current general-purpose robot dexterity and botanical perception.

task: Cut stencils and brush or spray lettering or decorations on surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting one stencil and applying it to one surface. Cutting ~10 min, masking/spraying/brushing ~15 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise knife-cutting of intricate stencil shapes, controlled paint application with even pressure and masking, and aesthetic quality judgment — all far beyond current humanoid dexterity and perception capabilities.

task: Pretest and calibrate anesthesia delivery systems and monitors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full pre-use checkout of one anesthesia workstation; a competent anesthesia provider typically completes the standardized checkout in 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of specialized valves/connectors, reading proprietary displays, interpreting multi-modal sensor feedback, and clinical judgment to pass/fail safety checks. No current humanoid has demonstrated this capability in real clinical environments.

task: Weld or solder broken parts and structural members, using electric or gas welders and soldering tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing one broken part or joint. Competent welder inspects, preps surfaces, positions pieces, and welds/solders, averaging ~45 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Welding demands precise torch/electrode control, heat management, seam tracking, and fume-safe operation. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, specialized tool handling, and multi-modal sensing needed for autonomous, quality-acceptable welds.

task: Solder loose connections, using soldering iron.
human_time_rationale: One instance = soldering one loose connection. A skilled technician heats, applies solder, and inspects in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, precise thermal control, bimanual tool coordination, and close visual inspection of tiny joints. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter precision and heat management needed for reliable, safe soldering.

task: Inspect the surface to be covered to determine its condition, and correct any imperfections that might show through carpet or cause carpet to wear unevenly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and correcting imperfections on a single room's subfloor. A skilled installer typically spends ~20–30 min assessing, patching, sanding, or screwing down problem areas.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced tactile and visual inspection of floor conditions, diagnosing subtle unevenness or soft spots, then applying compound, sanding, or fastening — multi-step dexterous remediation in unstructured environments well beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Select and prepare medical equipment or medications to be taken to athletic competition sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a full medical kit for one competition event. A competent athletic trainer checks inventory, selects, and packs appropriate supplies in ~25–35 minutes.
