robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment to select appropriate medications/equipment per sport, event, and athlete needs; fine dexterous handling of syringes, vials, and delicate instruments; and regulatory/safety compliance. Far beyond 2026 robot generalization.

task: Apply paint, stain, varnish, enamel, or other finishes to equipment, buildings, bridges, or other structures, using brushes, spray guns, or rollers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = painting a standard surface area (e.g., one room wall or structural section). A competent painter typically covers ~200–400 sq ft/hour; 120 min reflects a meaningful unit of work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained tool use (brush/roller/sprayer) across large, varied, often outdoor/elevated surfaces. Poor generalization to scaffolding, uneven substrates, masking, and quality control. Current humanoids lack spray-finishing precision and reach versatility.

task: Mix and match colors of paint, stain, or varnish with oil or thinning and drying additives to obtain desired colors and consistencies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing a single batch to a target color/consistency. A skilled painter typically spends 10–20 min measuring, blending, and testing the mix.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise color-matching via perception, fine-controlled pouring/measuring of liquids, iterative sensory feedback (visual color, viscosity), and chemical knowledge. Current robots lack reliable liquid handling, colorimetric judgment, and iterative mixing loops.

task: Examine patients using equipment, such as radiograph (x-ray) machines or fluoroscopes, to determine the nature and extent of disorder or injury.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient imaging exam (setup, positioning, image acquisition, preliminary review). A competent radiologic technologist typically takes 15–25 minutes per standard exam.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise patient positioning, safe handling of a potentially injured/fragile person, operating specialized medical imaging equipment, clinical judgment, and regulatory licensure. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities in unstructured clinical environments.

task: Create and change in-store and window displays, designs, and looks to enhance a shop's image.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single display changeover (window or in-store). A competent visual merchandiser typically takes 1–3 hours per display setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative spatial design judgment, handling diverse props/mannequins, precise aesthetic arrangement, and working in cluttered retail environments. Far beyond current humanoid generalization, dexterity, and aesthetic reasoning capabilities.

task: Move machinery and equipment, using hoists, dollies, rollers, and trucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving a single piece of machinery/equipment to a new location. A competent mover spends ~1 hour rigging, moving, and positioning one item.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating hoists, dollies, rollers, and trucks — heavy industrial tools a humanoid cannot safely control. Tasks involve high forces, heavy loads, specialized rigging, and vehicle operation far beyond current robot capability.

task: Test, evaluate, and classify excess or in-use medical equipment and determine serviceability, condition, and disposition, in accordance with regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = evaluating a single piece of medical equipment. A competent biomedical technician needs ~45 min to inspect, test, document condition, and determine disposition per regulatory standards.
robot_success_rationale: Requires regulatory knowledge, nuanced judgment, specialized diagnostic tools, documentation, and contextual decision-making about serviceability/disposition. Far beyond current robot cognition, tool-use, and compliance reasoning.

task: Instruct students, using adapted physical education techniques, to improve physical fitness, gross motor skills, perceptual motor skills, or sports and game achievement.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one adapted PE class session (~45 min), the standard unit a school administrator measures for teacher planning and delivery.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time adaptive pedagogy, reading students' emotional/physical states, verbal instruction, motivational coaching, and safe physical assistance for diverse abilities — far beyond 2026 robot generalization and social cognition.

task: Perform laser surgeries to alter, remove, reshape, or replace ocular tissue.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one eye surgery procedure (e.g., LASIK). A competent ophthalmologist typically completes pre-op setup, the laser procedure, and immediate post-op check in ~20–40 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme sub-millimeter precision on live ocular tissue, real-time adaptive judgment, sterile technique, and patient interaction. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, perception, and safety certification. No general-purpose robot is close.

task: Examine and evaluate athletes prior to participation in sports activities to determine level of physical fitness or predisposition to injuries.
human_time_rationale: One pre-participation physical exam per athlete; a competent athletic trainer/physician typically spends 20–40 minutes assessing fitness, range of motion, history, and injury risk.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced clinical judgment, verbal history-taking, palpation for tenderness, neurological assessment, and contextual interpretation of findings. Current robots lack medical diagnostic cognition, soft-tissue perception, and the communication skills required for autonomous clinical exams.

task: Conduct physical examinations of patients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a standard physical exam on one patient (vitals, auscultation, palpation, reflexes, etc.), typically 15–25 minutes for a competent clinician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly precise dexterous touch (palpation, auscultation placement, reflex testing), nuanced patient interaction, clinical judgment, and safe force control on a human body — far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Plan and carry out work assignments, using blueprints, schematic drawings, technical manuals, wiring diagrams, or liquid or air flow sheets, following prescribed regulations, directives, or other instructions as required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = planning and executing a single work assignment (e.g., one maintenance or installation job). A competent technician typically spends ~60 min reviewing docs and completing the task.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading and interpreting complex 2D technical documents (blueprints, wiring/flow diagrams), cross-referencing regulations, planning multi-step work, and executing dexterous skilled trades tasks autonomously — far beyond current robot generalization.

task: Perform abdominal, pelvic, or retroperitoneal surgeries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one surgical procedure. Abdominal/pelvic/retroperitoneal surgeries typically range from 1–5+ hours; ~3 hours is a reasonable median for common procedures (e.g., appendectomy, colectomy).
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sterile technique, real-time clinical judgment, and specialized surgical tools. General-purpose humanoids have no capability for autonomous surgery; even robotic surgical systems (e.g., da Vinci) require full human surgeon teleoperation.

task: Position vehicles, machinery, equipment, physical structures, and other objects for assembly or installation, using hand tools, power tools, and moving equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning a single large item (vehicle, machine, or structure) for assembly/install. Includes rigging, aligning, and securing; typically 30–60 min for a competent tradesperson.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating forklifts, cranes, and heavy moving equipment a humanoid cannot drive; coordinating rigging of large/heavy objects; precise spatial alignment under variable industrial conditions. Current robots lack vehicle operation and heavy-load manipulation capabilities.

task: Assist anesthesiologists in performing anesthetic procedures, such as epidural or spinal injections.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting with a single epidural or spinal injection procedure, from setup through patient positioning, needle guidance support, and post-procedure monitoring. Typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile field compliance, sub-millimeter precision near the spine, real-time patient response monitoring, and high-stakes clinical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, safety certification, and medical-context generalization.

task: Check work pieces to ensure that they are properly lubricated or cooled.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking a single workpiece for adequate lubrication/cooling. A skilled worker visually inspects and/or feels the piece in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based detection of lubricant/coolant presence is feasible but unreliable in noisy industrial settings. Assessing adequacy (not just presence) requires thermal or tactile sensing that current humanoids lack robustly. Generalization across workpiece types is limited.

task: Support metalworking projects from planning and fabrication through assembly, inspection, and testing, using knowledge of machine functions, metal properties, and mathematics.
human_time_rationale: One instance = supporting a single metalworking project phase (e.g., planning a cut, fabricating a part, or assembling a sub-assembly). A competent machinist/fabricator typically spends ~2 hours per discrete project support task.
robot_success_rationale: Requires integrated cognitive planning, math reasoning, metal property knowledge, precision tool operation, multi-step fabrication, inspection judgment, and adaptive problem-solving across unstructured environments — far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Verify vertical and horizontal alignment of structural steel members, using plumb bobs, laser equipment, transits, or levels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking alignment of one structural steel member using survey tools. A competent ironworker/surveyor takes ~15–25 min per member including setup, measurement, and recording.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized survey instruments (transits, lasers, levels) outdoors on active construction sites with uneven terrain, interpreting fine angular readings, and navigating scaffolding. Current humanoids lack construction-grade survey tool operation, outdoor mobility, and precision measurement workflows.

task: Shrink-fit bushings, sleeves, rings, liners, gears, and wheels to specified items, using portable gas heating equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = heating and fitting one component (e.g., a bushing or gear) onto a specified item. A skilled worker needs time to set up gas equipment, evenly heat the part to spec temp, and press/seat it correctly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating portable gas heating equipment (open-flame torch), precise temperature judgment, timed manual placement under thermal expansion, and controlled force fitting — all well beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and thermal-sensing capabilities.

task: Connect pipe pieces and seal joints, using welding equipment, cement, or glue.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting and sealing one pipe joint. A competent pipefitter/plumber spends ~20–30 min including fitting, aligning, and sealing/welding one joint.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipe alignment, fine dexterity for sealing/welding, torch or cement tool handling, and working in cramped spaces. Current humanoids lack the precision, tool mastery, and situational adaptability for real-world pipe joining.

task: Assist anesthesiologists in monitoring of patients, including electrocardiogram (EKG), direct arterial pressure, central venous pressure, arterial blood gas, hematocrit, or routine measurement of temperature, respiration, blood pressure or heart rate.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring cycle/check covering all listed vitals and values for one patient; a skilled anesthesiology technician completes a full round in ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting live physiological data streams, adjusting medical sensors on a patient, and interfacing with clinical equipment. Current humanoids lack medical tool integration, fine clinical dexterity, and safe patient-contact protocols in dynamic OR environments.

task: Connect power unit to machines or steam piping to equipment, and test unit to evaluate its mechanical operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting one power unit to a machine or steam piping system and running an operational test; a skilled technician typically takes 45–90 min including alignment, fastening, and testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy industrial tool use, precise flange/pipe alignment, torque application, valve operation, and multi-step functional testing. Current humanoids lack the strength, specialized tooling, and domain reasoning for this industrial task.

task: Conduct physical tests, such as functional capacity evaluations, to determine injured workers' capabilities to perform the physical demands of their jobs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one functional capacity evaluation (FCE) for one injured worker. A full FCE typically takes 2–4 hours; 120 min is a reasonable median for a competent evaluator.
robot_success_rationale: FCEs require clinical judgment, dynamic patient interaction, adaptive test sequencing, pain/effort observation, and professional communication. Current humanoids lack medical assessment cognition, safe patient handling, and the nuanced behavioral interpretation required.

task: Organize and maintain periodicals and reference materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = organizing/maintaining a collection of periodicals and reference materials in a typical library or office section; ~25 min to sort, shelve, and tidy a batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading labels/titles (OCR-level perception), navigating shelving aisles, handling varied floppy/thin materials, and applying sorting logic. Current humanoids lack reliable fine perception of text on irregular media and consistent manipulation of magazines/binders.

task: Remove finished packaged items from machine and separate rejected items.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a batch of packaged items from a machine and sorting out rejects; a competent worker does this in ~1–3 minutes per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place uniform packaged items reasonably well, but identifying and separating rejected items requires reliable visual inspection, varied object handling, and adapting to irregular packaging — limiting autonomous success on current platforms.

task: Machine parts to specifications, using machine tools, such as lathes, milling machines, shapers, or grinders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = machining a single part to specification on a lathe/mill/grinder. A competent machinist typically spends 30–90 min per part including setup, tooling, and measurement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating complex machine tools with precise controls, reading technical drawings, setting tooling offsets, and measuring tolerances. Current humanoid robots lack the dexterity, domain-specific tool operation, and precision feedback loops needed for CNC/manual machining.

task: Install or repair sanitary or stormwater sewer structures or pipe systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing or repairing a single sewer line segment or structure (e.g., manhole, pipe run). A competent crew typically takes 2–6 hours depending on depth and complexity; median ~4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating excavators and heavy equipment, working in confined/underground spaces, handling large/heavy pipes, making sealed joints, and navigating highly unstructured outdoor terrain — all well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Locate existing pipes needing repair or replacement, using magnetic or radio indicators.
human_time_rationale: One instance = surveying a single pipe segment or section of a job site (~100–200 ft). A trained technician walks the route with a magnetic/radio locator device, marks findings, and logs results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/unstructured terrain navigation, operating specialized locator instruments, interpreting ambiguous electromagnetic signals, and marking findings — well beyond current humanoid generalization and tool-use capabilities.

task: Cover pipes with earth or other materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = covering pipes in a single trench section (~10–20 m). A competent worker using machinery (backhoe/excavator) or hand tools takes ~1 hour including backfilling and compacting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating an excavator or heavy earth-moving equipment on outdoor, uneven terrain. Humanoid robots cannot drive or operate such machinery. Manual shoveling alternative is feasible but impractical at scale; outdoor terrain and heavy loads further reduce success.

task: Teach cane skills, including cane use with a guide, diagonal techniques, and two-point touches.
human_time_rationale: One instructional session covering cane use with a guide, diagonal technique, and two-point touch for a single student; a typical orientation & mobility lesson runs ~45–90 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic verbal/tactile instruction, real-time adaptive coaching, empathy, and specialized pedagogy for visually impaired individuals. No current humanoid has the perception, social intelligence, or certified O&M expertise for this.

task: Perform brachytherapy, cryotherapy, high intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU), or photodynamic therapy to treat prostate or other cancers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient treatment session. Brachytherapy/HIFU/cryotherapy procedures typically take 60–120 minutes including setup, imaging guidance, and delivery.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter surgical precision, real-time medical imaging interpretation, sterile technique, intraoperative decision-making, and specialized medical equipment. Far beyond any 2026 general-purpose humanoid robot's capabilities.

task: Align and secure holding fixtures, cutting tools, attachments, accessories, or materials onto machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a single fixture/tool/attachment on one machine. A skilled machinist typically takes 10–20 min to align, secure, and verify one setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment tolerances (often sub-mm), applying calibrated torque, using wrenches/chucks/clamps in varied machine configurations. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force feedback, and tool-use generalization needed for reliable machine setup.

task: Adjust and maintain industrial machinery, using control and regulating devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting and verifying settings on a single machine. A skilled technician typically spends ~30 min diagnosing, adjusting controls/regulators, and confirming stable operation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting analog/digital gauges, operating diverse industrial control panels, fine-tuning regulators, and diagnosing feedback — demanding perception, tool-use generalization, and contextual judgment well beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Ride on girders or other structural steel members to position them, or use rope to guide them into position.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning a single girder or structural steel member. An ironworker typically spends ~10–20 min riding, guiding, and securing one beam into place.
robot_success_rationale: Requires riding crane-lifted steel at height, extreme balance on unstable surfaces, precise rope guidance outdoors in variable conditions. No 2026 humanoid has the mobility, safety certification, or force-control for this hazardous, unstructured high-altitude task.

task: Perform autopsies to determine causes of deaths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one complete autopsy. A competent forensic pathologist typically takes 2–4 hours including external examination, evisceration, organ weighing, specimen collection, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (surgical incisions, organ dissection), nuanced sensory judgment, legal/medical expertise, and precise tool use. Far beyond any current general-purpose humanoid robot's capabilities.

task: Disassemble malfunctioning equipment and remove, repair, or replace defective parts, such as motors, clutches, or transformers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling one piece of malfunctioning equipment, identifying/replacing one defective component (e.g., motor or clutch). A competent technician typically takes 45–90 min per job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults, handling varied fasteners/wiring, fine dexterity for clutches/transformers, and adaptive problem-solving across diverse equipment types. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterous capability.

task: Adjust machine components and machine tension and pressure according to size or processing angle of product.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting machine components, tension, and pressure settings for a single product size/angle changeover; a competent operator typically takes ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct machine components, turning knobs/levers with precise force feedback, reading gauges, and generalizing across varied machine layouts. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-tuned force control and contextual machine-reading for unstructured industrial settings.

task: Level bedplate and establish centerline, using straightedge, levels, and transit.
human_time_rationale: One instance = leveling and centering a single bedplate. A skilled millwright takes ~45–90 min for iterative shimming, straightedge checks, precision level readings, and transit sightings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires using a surveying transit, precision machinists' levels, and straightedge with sub-millimeter tolerances; iterative shimming; and spatial reasoning across multiple reference planes. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and precision tool-use capability.

task: Treat medical conditions, using techniques such as acupressure, shiatsu, or tuina.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full therapeutic session (acupressure/shiatsu/tuina) on a single patient. Typical sessions run 30–60 minutes; 45 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced tactile force calibration, precise anatomical landmark identification on varied body types, dynamic patient interaction, and clinical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, compliance control, and medical reasoning capabilities.

task: Connect columns, beams, and girders with bolts, following blueprints and instructions from supervisors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting one joint (column, beam, or girder connection) with bolts per blueprint. A skilled ironworker takes ~30–60 min per connection including alignment, bolt insertion, and torquing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working at height on active construction sites, reading blueprints, aligning heavy structural steel, and using impact wrenches/torque tools. Current humanoids lack the strength, outdoor mobility, safety compliance, and tool-use generalization for this hazardous, high-precision task.

task: Train clients with visual impairments to use mobility devices or systems, such as human guides, dog guides, electronic travel aids (ETAs), and other adaptive mobility devices (AMDs).
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single training session with one visually impaired client on a mobility device/system. A typical structured session runs ~45–90 minutes, median ~60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert verbal/tactile instruction, nuanced real-time feedback, adaptive communication, and trust-building with a vulnerable person. Current robots lack the social intelligence, adaptive pedagogy, and caregiving nuance for this specialized training role.

task: Dismantle machines or equipment, using hand tools or power tools to examine parts for defects and replace defective parts where needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling one machine/equipment unit, inspecting parts, and replacing defective ones. A typical industrial machine teardown + inspection + part replacement takes ~1–2 hours for a competent mechanic.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step dexterous tool use (wrenches, screwdrivers, power tools), defect detection via tactile/visual inspection, contextual judgment on part condition, and precise reassembly — far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Bolt aligned structural steel members in position for permanent riveting, bolting, or welding into place.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning and bolting one structural steel connection (e.g., a beam-to-column joint). A skilled ironworker typically takes 15–35 minutes per connection including alignment, spud wrench work, and temporary bolting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/elevated unstructured environments, heavy steel manipulation, precise load-bearing alignment, and high-torque bolting. Current humanoids lack the strength, stability, fall protection, and generalization for construction steel work. Near-zero feasibility.

task: Adjust, connect, or disconnect wiring, piping, tubing, and other parts, using hand or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single wiring/piping/tubing adjustment or connection task. A competent technician typically spends ~20 min per discrete connection or adjustment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous manipulation of varied connectors, fittings, and tools in constrained spaces. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control, force feedback, and generalization needed for reliable, safe wire/pipe work.

task: Apply moxibustion directly or indirectly to patients using Chinese, non-scarring, stick, or pole moxa.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one moxibustion session on a single patient, typically involving locating acupoints, igniting moxa, and applying heat for 15–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise anatomical acupoint identification on a moving human body, safe heat modulation near skin, fine tool manipulation of a lit moxa stick, and real-time patient feedback interpretation — all far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Clean or lubricate vehicles, machinery, equipment, instruments, tools, work areas, and other objects, using hand tools, power tools, and cleaning equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/lubricating one vehicle, machine, or piece of equipment. A competent worker typically takes 20–45 min depending on object size and method; 30 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Task spans highly varied objects, tools, and environments (vehicles, heavy machinery, outdoor areas). Robots struggle with selecting correct lubricants/tools, reaching awkward points, applying precise force, and operating around or on large equipment. Generalization and tool-switching remain major limits.

task: Inspect and remove defective products and packaging material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and removing one defective item/package from a production line. A competent worker takes ~1–3 minutes per unit including visual check and removal.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is maturing but generalizing across varied defect types and packaging materials is unreliable. Physical removal requires dexterous grasping of irregular/damaged items, which current humanoids handle inconsistently in unstructured line environments.

task: Examine medical equipment or facility's structural environment and check for proper use of equipment to protect patients and staff from electrical or mechanical hazards and to ensure compliance with safety regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a structured safety inspection round of a medical unit/room, checking equipment and structural environment. A trained biomedical/safety technician typically spends 30–60 min per area.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level interpretation of electrical/mechanical compliance standards, reading instrument displays, probing wiring, and formal documentation. Current robots lack the regulatory knowledge, fine-probe dexterity, and autonomous decision-making for accepted safety sign-off.

task: Unpack stock as it comes into the shop.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unboxing and sorting a single delivery of stock items; a competent retail worker typically spends ~30 min per average delivery consignment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires opening boxes, recognising varied products, navigating a cluttered stockroom, and placing items correctly. Current robots lack reliable generalised grasping of diverse packaged goods and unstructured environment navigation at this scale.

task: Administer medications, including those administered by injection.
human_time_rationale: One instance = administering a single medication dose to one patient, including preparation, ID verification, injection/delivery, and documentation. Typical nursing workflow ~10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for syringe handling, sterile technique, vein/injection-site identification, patient interaction/safety, and clinical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid capability; even narrow medical robots cannot do this autonomously in general settings.

task: Prepare samples or photomicrographs for testing and analysis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single sample/photomicrograph slide (sectioning, mounting, staining, labeling). A competent lab tech typically takes 20–45 minutes per sample.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterity (microtome sectioning, coverslip placement, precise staining pipetting), sterile technique, and domain-specific judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot fine-motor and lab-generalization capabilities.

task: Pull, push, or pry structural steel members into approximate positions for bolting into place.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning one structural steel member into alignment for bolting; ironworkers typically spend 10–20 min per member using pry bars, come-alongs, and team coordination.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme force output, outdoor/elevated unstructured environments, heavy tool use (pry bars, come-alongs), and precise coordination with crane signals. Current humanoids lack the strength, stability, and safety margins for this high-risk heavy structural work.

task: Train clients to use tactile, auditory, kinesthetic, olfactory, and proprioceptive information.
human_time_rationale: One training session with a client covering all five sensory modalities; a competent trainer typically runs a structured 45-minute session per client.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced verbal/physical instruction, adaptive feedback, reading subtle client responses, and multi-modal demonstration. Current robots lack conversational depth, pedagogical judgment, and the ability to guide clients through internal sensory experiences.

task: Test or perform failure analysis for optomechanical or optoelectrical products, according to test plans.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a full test/failure analysis cycle on a single optomechanical/optoelectrical product per a test plan, including setup, execution, data logging, and preliminary diagnosis. Typically 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting complex test plans, setting up precision optical/electrical instruments, making fine alignments, reading specialized equipment outputs, and applying expert-level diagnostic reasoning — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Perform general cleaning duties in the store to ensure the shop is clean and tidy.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full cleaning round of a typical retail store (sweeping, mopping, wiping surfaces, emptying bins). A competent worker takes ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle sweeping/mopping on flat floors reasonably well, but retail stores have cluttered aisles, varied surfaces, obstacles, bins to empty, and surfaces to wipe — requiring generalization and dexterity that current humanoids struggle with reliably.

task: Insert needles to provide acupuncture treatment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full acupuncture session for one patient: locating acupoints, inserting ~10–20 needles, and monitoring. Typical session runs 20–45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter dexterous needle insertion at precise anatomical points on a moving human, real-time pain/response feedback, and clinical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, perception, and safety standards.

task: Install experimental parts or assemblies, such as hydraulic systems, electrical wiring, lubricants, or batteries into machines or mechanisms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single experimental assembly (e.g., a hydraulic unit or wiring harness) into a machine. A competent technician typically takes 45–90 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires routing flexible hoses/wires, torquing fittings, reading non-standard schematics, and adapting to experimental (non-production) configurations. High dexterity, tool-use precision, and generalization demands exceed current robot capabilities.

task: Perform interventional procedures such as image-guided biopsy, percutaneous transluminal angioplasty, transhepatic biliary drainage, or nephrostomy catheter placement.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one interventional radiology procedure (e.g., biopsy or angioplasty). A competent interventional radiologist typically takes 30–90 minutes per procedure, median ~60 min including prep and imaging guidance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme sub-millimeter dexterity, real-time fluoroscopic/ultrasound interpretation, sterile field management, clinical judgment, and patient-specific anatomy adaptation — far beyond any 2026 general-purpose humanoid robot's capabilities.

task: Examine microscopic samples to identify diseases or other abnormalities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining a single prepared slide/sample. A competent pathologist or lab tech typically spends 10–20 minutes per sample including focusing, scanning fields, and documenting findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert visual interpretation of microscopic imagery, clinical reasoning, and pathology knowledge — far beyond current robot perception/cognition. Physically operating a microscope with fine focus control is also highly dexterous. AI image analysis exists but is not a general-purpose robot.

task: Fabricate metal parts, such as steel frames, columns, beams, or girders, according to blueprints or instructions from supervisors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single structural metal part (e.g., a beam or frame section). A skilled ironworker/fabricator typically takes 1–3 hours depending on complexity; 2 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy industrial equipment (plasma cutters, welders, grinders, press brakes), interpreting complex blueprints, precise torch/arc welding on large steel stock, and navigating a cluttered fabrication shop — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Remove damaged panels, and identify the family and properties of the plastic used on a vehicle.
human_time_rationale: Removing panels from one vehicle and identifying plastic family via markings/testing; includes unclipping, unbolting, reading ISO codes, and documenting properties.
robot_success_rationale: Panel removal requires varied fasteners, clips, and force control on fragile parts. Plastic identification demands reading small embossed codes or chemical testing. Both sub-tasks push limits of current dexterity, perception, and tool-use generalization.

task: Treat patients using tools, such as needles, cups, ear balls, seeds, pellets, or nutritional supplements.
human_time_rationale: One patient treatment session (e.g., acupuncture/auriculotherapy) including prep, tool application, and monitoring; typical clinical session runs 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for needle/seed placement at precise anatomical points, patient assessment, safety judgment, and adaptive response to patient feedback. Far beyond current robot capability; patient safety risk is critical.

task: Assist in the provision of advanced life support techniques including those procedures using high frequency ventilation or intra-arterial cardiovascular assistance devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = initiating and stabilizing a patient on an advanced life support intervention (e.g., HFV setup or IABP insertion/monitoring). Skilled clinician takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (intra-arterial cannulation, ventilator circuit assembly), real-time clinical judgment, sterile technique, and patient-specific adaptation. Far beyond any current general-purpose robot's capabilities. Safety-critical; no deployment exists.

task: Supply materials to spindles, conveyors, hoppers, or other feeding devices and unload packaged product.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one supply/unload cycle (loading a feeding device and unloading finished product). A competent worker completes this in ~5–10 min depending on batch size.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of bulk materials and varied packaged product is partially feasible, but varied container shapes, hopper interfaces, precise pouring/loading, and unloading packaged goods reliably remain challenging for current humanoids.

task: Build or assemble robotic devices or systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one robotic device/system (e.g. an industrial arm or mobile robot). Covers mechanical assembly, wiring, and basic verification; a competent technician typically takes 1–3 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly precise multi-step dexterous manipulation (fasteners, cables, circuit boards), tool use, and spatial reasoning across diverse, non-standardized components. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and fine-motor capability.

task: Monitor the production line, watching for problems such as pile-ups, jams, or glue that isn't sticking properly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one hour of continuous line monitoring; production monitoring is a sustained watchkeeping duty typically measured in hourly shifts or shift segments.
robot_success_rationale: Fixed vision systems already do some inspection, but a general-purpose humanoid must patrol, detect varied fault types (jams, pile-ups, glue failures), interpret context, and intervene or alert—requiring robust perception, judgment, and mobility in a dynamic industrial environment. Current frontier robots lack reliable multi-fault generalization and real-time corrective action.

task: Lay out pipe routes, following written instructions or blueprints and coordinating layouts with supervisors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out a single pipe route segment per blueprint. A competent pipefitter reads specs, marks/measures the route, and coordinates with a supervisor — typically 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires blueprint reading/interpretation, spatial planning across large work areas, precise measurement marking, tool use, and real-time supervisor coordination. Far beyond current humanoid generalization, mobility, and cognitive task-planning capabilities.

task: Fabricate, dress down, or substitute parts or major new items to modify equipment to meet unique operational or research needs, working from job orders, sketches, modification orders, samples, or discussions with operating officials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single equipment modification job. Skilled trades workers typically spend 1–4 hours per job order depending on complexity; 3 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting ambiguous job orders/sketches, high-level planning, fabricating custom parts, fine dexterous machining/fitting, and adapting to unique/novel equipment — well beyond 2026 robot generalization, tool-use, and reasoning capabilities.

task: Locate malfunctions in brakes, motors, switches, and signal and control systems, using test equipment.
human_time_rationale: One diagnostic session on a single system (e.g., one brake assembly or motor unit), including setup, testing with equipment, and interpreting results — typical for an industrial technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires attaching probes/meters to varied connectors, interpreting multimeter/oscilloscope readings, navigating electrical schematics, and exercising judgment on fault causation — well beyond current robot tool-use, perception, and reasoning capabilities.

task: Operate equipment to verify operational efficiency.
human_time_rationale: One instance = verifying operational efficiency of one piece of equipment; includes startup, observation of performance indicators, and logging results — typically 20–40 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating varied equipment controls, reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting efficiency metrics, and making judgments — well beyond current humanoid generalization. Perception and tool-use limitations are severe for unstructured industrial equipment.

task: Inspect, operate, or test machinery or equipment to diagnose machine malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing a single machine malfunction. A competent technician typically spends ~30 min inspecting, running tests, and identifying the fault.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating diverse machinery controls, interpreting multi-modal sensor feedback, and reasoning about fault causation. Current humanoids lack the diagnostic cognition, specialized tool-use, and generalization needed across arbitrary equipment types.

task: Prescribe or administer antibiotics, antiseptics, or compresses to treat infection or injury.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing a patient and applying/administering a single treatment (e.g., applying a compress or topical antiseptic, or dispensing a prescribed antibiotic dose). Includes brief assessment, preparation, and application.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, patient assessment, precise dexterous wound care, and legal authority to prescribe. Current robots lack medical reasoning, safe patient interaction, fine manipulation for wound dressing, and regulatory authorization.

task: Align and position pipes to prepare them for welding or sealing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning and positioning a single pipe joint/connection. A skilled pipefitter takes ~15–25 min to measure, fit, clamp, and check alignment of one joint.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual force control, use of specialized clamps/tools, tight tolerances, and spatial reasoning in cluttered environments. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, perception accuracy, and tool-use generalization needed for reliable pipe alignment.

task: Wrap and price completed arrangements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wrapping and pricing one completed floral or gift arrangement; includes selecting wrap material, securing it, and attaching a price tag.
robot_success_rationale: Wrapping delicate floral arrangements requires fine dexterity with soft, crinkly materials, precise folding, and affixing small price tags—all highly challenging for current humanoids. Fragile items increase failure risk significantly.

task: Inspect wiring connections, control panel hookups, door installations, and alignments and clearances of cars and hoistways to ensure that equipment will operate properly.
human_time_rationale: One full elevator inspection covering wiring, control panel, doors, and hoistway alignment — a licensed elevator inspector typically takes 60–120 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating confined hoistway spaces, reading wiring diagrams, probing connections, measuring clearances with tools, and making expert safety judgments. Far beyond current humanoid generalization, dexterity, and certified inspection capability.

task: Tend or operate machine that packages product.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one shift-segment of tending a packaging machine (~30 min of active monitoring, feeding product, clearing jams, adjusting settings).
robot_success_rationale: Requires monitoring machine state, clearing jams, reloading materials, and adjusting controls — all in a semi-structured but dynamic environment. Current humanoids lack reliable fault detection, fine manipulation for jam-clearing, and adaptive control-panel interaction at production quality.

task: Prescribe therapy services, such as electrotherapy, ultrasonography, heat or cold therapy, hydrotherapy, debridement, short-wave or microwave diathermy, and infrared or ultraviolet radiation, to enhance rehabilitation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient assessment and therapy prescription session, including reviewing patient status, selecting modalities, and documenting the plan. Typically 15–25 minutes for a trained physiotherapist.
robot_success_rationale: This is a licensed clinical decision-making task requiring medical diagnosis, patient communication, legal prescription authority, and nuanced judgment. No current general-purpose robot can prescribe therapy or hold clinical licensure autonomously.

task: Direct or provide home health services.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single home health visit/shift, typically 2–4 hours of direct patient care, coordination, and documentation by a home health aide or nurse.
robot_success_rationale: Requires unstructured caregiving (bathing, feeding, medication admin), clinical judgment, emotional rapport, and adaptive response to unpredictable patient needs — all far beyond current humanoid capability. Safety and liability further prohibit autonomous deployment.

task: Select or prepare specimens and media for cell cultures using aseptic techniques, knowledge of medium components, or cell nutritional requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a batch of media and inoculating a culture. A skilled lab tech typically spends ~30–60 min including aseptic setup, media preparation, and specimen handling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (pipetting, sterile technique), precise liquid handling, knowledge-driven decision-making, and contamination-free execution in a biosafety cabinet — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Prepare biological specimens such as amniotic fluids, bone marrow, tumors, chorionic villi, and blood, for chromosome examinations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single specimen batch (e.g., one amniotic fluid sample) through culture setup, harvesting, slide preparation, and staining — typically 60–120 min of hands-on work over a multi-day protocol.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (pipetting microliters, handling fragile slides), sterile technique, centrifuge operation, chemical handling, and expert judgment on specimen quality. Far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Test experimental models under simulated operating conditions, for purposes such as development, standardization, or feasibility of design.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single test session on one experimental model. Setup, running the simulation/test protocol, monitoring, and logging results typically takes 1–3 hours for a competent engineer.
robot_success_rationale: Task demands high-level reasoning, experimental design judgment, instrument operation, interpreting novel results, and adapting protocols—far beyond current robot cognition and generalization. Physical manipulation is secondary to intellectual/engineering work.

task: Assemble elevator cars, installing each car's platform, walls, and doors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fully assembling one elevator car (platform, walls, doors). A skilled crew typically takes 6–8 hours per car for structural fit-out and door installation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy panel handling, precise multi-bolt structural alignment, and complex door mechanism installation in a confined shaft. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, payload, and task-generalization limits.

task: Examine parts for damage or excessive wear, using micrometers and gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one part. A skilled technician takes ~5–10 min to measure dimensions with micrometers/gauges, check tolerances, and assess visible damage.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool handling (micrometers need <0.01 mm positioning), correct seating of gauges on irregular surfaces, and interpreting readings. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity and tactile feedback for reliable metrology-grade measurement.

task: Examine specimens, using microscopes, to evaluate specimen quality.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining a single specimen slide under a microscope. A competent lab technician typically takes ~5 minutes to mount, focus, scan, and assess quality.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation to handle delicate slides, operating a microscope's focus/stage controls, and interpreting microscopic imagery with domain-level quality judgement — all well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Hold rivets while riveters use air hammers to form heads on rivets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = holding a single rivet steady while a riveter sets it with an air hammer; typically takes ~20–30 seconds per rivet.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled grip on a small rivet, tight coordination/timing with a human using a pneumatic tool, and vibration tolerance. Current robots lack reliable fine-force feedback and human-synchronization for this reactive, dynamic task.

task: Clean and remove damaged or otherwise inferior materials to prepare raw products for processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/sorting one standard batch of raw product. A skilled worker inspects, removes damaged material, and prepares the batch in roughly 15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual/tactile perception to distinguish damaged from acceptable material across varied product types, plus fine dexterous manipulation to remove defects. Current robots lack robust generalization for unstructured, variable raw-material sorting.

task: Erect scaffolding or swing gates, or set up ladders, to work above ground level.
human_time_rationale: One instance = erecting a standard scaffold section, swing gate, or positioning a ladder at a single work site. A competent worker typically takes 30–60 min for a basic setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy component handling, precise structural assembly, load-bearing judgment, and working at height — all beyond current humanoid capability. No 2026 general-purpose robot can reliably erect scaffolding autonomously to safety standards.

task: Obtain, distribute, or maintain low vision devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = obtaining, distributing, or maintaining one low vision device (e.g., retrieving from storage, logging it, and handing it to a patient/client). A competent worker takes ~30 min including documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying specialized, varied optical/electronic devices, navigating inventory systems, careful handling of fragile optics, and patient interaction/communication — far beyond current robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Assist pathologists or other physicians to collect cell samples by fine needle aspiration (FNA) biopsy or other method.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting with a single FNA biopsy procedure, from patient prep through sample collection and specimen handling; typically 15–25 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile technique, precise needle guidance on a live patient, real-time tactile/visual feedback, and adaptive collaboration with a physician. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, safety certification, and medical procedural generalization.

task: Stock and sort product for packaging or filling machine operation, and replenish packaging supplies, such as wrapping paper, plastic sheet, boxes, cartons, glue, ink, or labels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restocking and sorting supplies for one machine cycle/shift prep. A worker checks inventory, retrieves supplies, and loads/organizes them near the machine in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a warehouse, identifying varied supply types (boxes, glue, labels, film), grasping irregularly shaped/stacked items, and precisely placing them near machinery. Perception of labels/inks and handling flexible materials (wrap, plastic sheet) challenge current robots significantly.

task: Examine cell samples to detect abnormalities in the color, shape, or size of cellular components and patterns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining one slide/sample. A skilled cytotechnologist or pathologist typically spends 10–30 minutes screening and analyzing a single prepared cell sample slide.
robot_success_rationale: Task is primarily cognitive/perceptual microscopy work requiring expert-level visual interpretation under high magnification. A humanoid robot cannot manipulate a microscope with the required precision, interpret subtle cellular morphology, or meet clinical quality standards autonomously.

task: Examine, evaluate and treat athletes who have been injured or who have medical problems such as exercise-induced asthma.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining, evaluating, and treating one athlete for an injury or medical condition; a typical sports medicine/athletic trainer encounter runs ~20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, nuanced physical examination (palpation, range-of-motion, auscultation), differential diagnosis, and individualized treatment decisions — far beyond current robot perception, dexterity, and autonomous medical reasoning capabilities.

task: Provide or direct the provision of postoperative care.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one postoperative care session for one patient (monitoring vitals, wound checks, medication, positioning, communication). A typical session spans ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, sterile wound management, IV/medication administration, patient communication, and adaptive response to unstable conditions — far beyond current robot dexterity, medical tool-use, and autonomous decision-making capabilities.

task: Disassemble defective units, and repair or replace parts such as locks, gears, cables, and electric wiring.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling one defective unit and repairing/replacing its faulty components (e.g., a lock, gear, cable, or wiring). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults, fine dexterous disassembly, routing cables, and replacing varied internal parts (locks, gears, wiring). Multi-step troubleshooting, tool switching, and high dexterity far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Lay out, measure, and mark metal stock to display placement of cuts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one piece of metal stock. A skilled metalworker spends ~10–15 min measuring dimensions, scribing reference lines, and marking cut placements accurately.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor use of measuring tapes, calipers, scribers, and marking tools on hard metal surfaces. Current humanoids lack the dexterous tool control, force sensitivity, and measurement precision needed; high failure risk.

task: Take numbered tags from customers, locate vehicles, and deliver vehicles, or provide customers with instructions for locating vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = receiving a tag, locating a parked vehicle in a lot/garage, and driving/walking it to the customer. Typical valet retrieval takes ~5–12 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading tags, navigating a dynamic parking lot, operating a passenger vehicle in traffic, and social interaction with customers. Humanoid robots cannot drive vehicles on public/lot roads; locomotion and vehicle operation together make this infeasible.

task: Inspect vehicles and check gas, oil, and water levels prior to departure.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pre-departure inspection of a single vehicle, including visual check and fluid level verification (gas gauge, dipstick, coolant reservoir). Typical competent worker takes ~10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires opening hood latches, reading dipsticks, interpreting fluid levels, and navigating around/under a vehicle. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous manipulation for engine-bay tasks, hood operation, and dipstick reading in unstructured settings.

task: Diagnose or treat injuries, disorders, or diseases of the eye and eye structures including the cornea, sclera, conjunctiva, or eyelids.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient encounter (exam + diagnosis/treatment plan). A competent ophthalmologist/optometrist typically spends 30–60 minutes per patient including history, slit-lamp exam, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced medical judgment, fine ocular examination, nuanced patient communication, and licensed clinical decision-making. Current robots lack medical AI integration, specialized ophthalmic instruments, and safe autonomous clinical authority.

task: Arrange merchandise and sales promotion displays or issue sales promotion materials to customers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = arranging a single display or distributing promo materials to one customer/area. A competent retail worker typically takes 15–25 minutes per display setup or materials round.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a dynamic retail environment, handling diverse merchandise shapes/sizes, aesthetic placement judgment, and social interaction for handing materials to customers. Current robots lack reliable generalization for unstructured retail layouts and nuanced visual merchandising decisions.

task: Attach guide shoes and rollers to minimize the lateral motion of cars as they travel through shafts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching guide shoes and rollers to one elevator car. Competent mechanic aligns, fits, and fastens multiple components per car, typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment in a confined vertical shaft, high-torque fastening, and fine dexterous manipulation of guide shoes/rollers. Current humanoids lack the precision, tool-use, and confined-space mobility for this specialized elevator installation task.

task: Set glass doors into frames and bolt metal hinges, handles, locks, or other hardware to attach doors to frames and walls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting and fully hardware-mounting one glass door into its frame, including hinge, handle, and lock installation. A skilled glazier typically takes 30–60 minutes per door.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of heavy glass panels, fine-torque bolting of multiple hardware types, and coordination of two-handed force/compliance control. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, payload, and tool-use reliability for this complex, high-consequence assembly.

task: Assemble hoisting equipment or rigging, such as cables, pulleys, or hooks, to move heavy equipment or materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a full rigging setup for one lift job (cables, pulleys, hooks). A skilled rigger typically spends 20–50 minutes selecting, inspecting, and securing components.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy-load manipulation, threading cables through pulleys, securing shackles and hooks under tension, and safety-critical inspection judgment. Current humanoids lack the strength, dexterity, and load-handling capability for this complex, high-stakes rigging work.

task: Remove old finishes by stripping, sanding, wire brushing, burning, or using water or abrasive blasting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stripping/sanding/blasting one typical surface area (e.g., a door, furniture piece, or wall section). A competent worker takes ~45–90 min depending on method and surface.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained forceful tool use (sanders, wire brushes, heat guns, blast equipment), varied surface navigation, and quality judgment on finish removal. Current humanoids lack the force control, tool-change dexterity, and surface-assessment needed for autonomous, acceptable-quality results.

task: Examine and test machinery, equipment, components, and parts for defects to ensure proper functioning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one machine/equipment unit. A competent technician spends ~20–40 min checking components, identifying defects, and verifying proper function.
robot_success_rationale: Robots lack reliable tactile/haptic defect sensing, nuanced visual anomaly detection across varied machinery types, and judgment to interpret subtle malfunctions. Structured visual inspection in constrained settings is feasible; unstructured real-world generalization remains poor.

task: Install pre-assembled metal or wood frameworks for windows or doors to be fitted with glass panels, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one pre-assembled window/door framework. A skilled glazier/carpenter typically takes 20–30 min per unit including measuring, positioning, shimming, fastening, and checking plumb/level.
robot_success_rationale: Requires carrying/maneuvering large, heavy, awkward frames in a construction environment, precise alignment (plumb/level/square), shimming, and fastening into rough openings. Unstructured site terrain, bimanual force control, and safety demands far exceed current humanoid capabilities.

task: Assist in the preparation of book displays.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a single book display (selecting, arranging, and positioning books on a shelf or table). A competent library/retail worker typically takes ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping varied book sizes/orientations, aesthetic arrangement judgment, reading titles for thematic grouping, and navigating a library/retail floor. Perception and semantic understanding of display intent are significant barriers for current robots.

task: Operate engine lathe to grind, file, and turn machine parts to dimensional specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = machining a single part to spec on an engine lathe. A competent machinist takes ~30–60 min including setup, turning, filing, and measuring.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a large engine lathe with precision controls, tool changes, and live measurement to tight tolerances. Humanoid robots cannot safely operate or interface with industrial lathes autonomously in 2026.

task: Drive drift pins through rivet holes to align rivet holes in structural steel members with corresponding holes in previously placed members.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning one set of rivet holes. A skilled ironworker drives drift pins and achieves alignment in roughly 2–4 minutes per connection point.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working at height on structural steel in unstructured, hazardous environments; precise force-guided insertion of drift pins into misaligned holes; strong grip and impact tool use; perception of tight tolerances. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Review lists of dealers, customers, or station drops and load trucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reviewing a delivery list and loading one truck. A competent worker reads the manifest, picks, organizes, and loads items — typically 30–60 min per truck.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting paper or digital manifests, navigating a warehouse, identifying varied items, and safely loading a truck in correct order. Current humanoids lack reliable document parsing, heavy-item handling, and truck-loading generalization.

task: Stop or reset machines when malfunctions occur, clear machine jams, and report malfunctions to a supervisor.
human_time_rationale: One instance = detecting a malfunction, stopping/resetting the machine or clearing a jam, and reporting to a supervisor. A competent worker completes this in ~5–10 minutes on average.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can detect anomalies and press e-stops, but clearing varied jams requires unstructured dexterous manipulation in tight spaces. Reporting to a supervisor demands communication. Generalization across machine types is very limited in 2026.

task: Clean, oil, and make minor adjustments or repairs to machinery and equipment, such as opening valves or setting guides.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one maintenance cycle on a single machine (cleaning, oiling, adjusting). A skilled technician typically takes 20–45 min per machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step dexterous manipulation (oiling ports, setting guides, opening valves) in cluttered industrial environments. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use, fine force control, and task sequencing needed across varied machinery types.

task: Apply heat or cold therapy to patients using materials, such as heat pads, hydrocollator packs, warm compresses, cold compresses, heat lamps, or vapor coolants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and applying a heat/cold therapy modality to one patient area, including setup, placement, monitoring, and removal. Typically 10–15 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safe contact with a human patient (safety constraints), precise placement on a body part, temperature monitoring, and patient communication. Dexterity and compliance sensing are insufficient; patient unpredictability and safety liability make autonomous success very low.

task: Regulate machine flow, speed, or temperature.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single parameter (flow, speed, or temperature) on a machine: reading gauges/displays, making a control input, and confirming the new set point. Typically takes 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting values, and operating varied physical controls (knobs, touchscreens, levers). Perception of non-standardized interfaces and fine dexterous adjustment are still weak in 2026 humanoids; generalization across machine types is very limited.

task: Maintain effective laboratory operations by adhering to standards of specimen collection, preparation, or laboratory safety.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single shift's routine compliance cycle: checking safety standards, preparing/labeling specimens, and maintaining lab readiness. Competent lab tech takes ~30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for specimen handling, strict sterile technique, contextual safety judgment, reading labels/instruments, and nuanced protocol adherence. Current robots lack reliable lab-grade precision, sterility awareness, and autonomous compliance reasoning.

task: Install electrical wires and controls by attaching conduit along shaft walls from floor to floor and pulling plastic-covered wires through the conduit.
human_time_rationale: One instance = running conduit and pulling wire along one shaft floor-to-floor segment. A skilled electrician typically takes 2–4 hours depending on shaft length and conduit count.
robot_success_rationale: Requires anchoring conduit to vertical shaft walls, routing through tight vertical spaces, using specialized tools (conduit benders, fish tape), and sustained overhead/confined work — far beyond 2026 humanoid capability in generalization, dexterity, and spatial navigation.

task: Patrol parking areas to prevent vehicle damage and vehicle or property thefts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patrol shift/round of a parking area. A typical patrol circuit covering a standard parking lot takes ~60 minutes for a security guard.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids can walk flat surfaces but lack reliable outdoor terrain navigation, suspicious-activity recognition, deterrence credibility, and the judgment to intervene or report theft in progress. No robust autonomous security patrol capability exists at scale in 2026.

task: Examine chromosomes found in biological specimens to detect abnormalities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining a single karyotype slide/image set for one patient specimen. A cytogeneticist spends ~30–60 min preparing, staining, imaging, and interpreting chromosomal spreads.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for slide handling, microscope operation, and expert cytogenetic interpretation. AI software can assist analysis, but a humanoid robot cannot perform the full lab workflow autonomously at acceptable clinical quality.

task: Treat urologic disorders using alternatives to traditional surgery such as extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy, laparoscopy, or laser techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one minimally invasive urologic procedure (e.g., ESWL or laser lithotripsy). Setup, treatment, and patient monitoring typically take 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires licensed surgical judgment, fine dexterous instrument control inside/near a patient, sterile technique, real-time clinical decision-making, and specialized medical equipment operation — all far beyond current humanoid robots.

task: Operate elevators to determine power demands, and test power consumption to detect overload factors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one elevator through full power/overload test cycle, including setup, operation, and reading instrumentation. ~45 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating elevator controls, attaching power meters to electrical panels, interpreting multi-meter readings, and diagnosing overload factors — combining fine dexterity, specialized tool use, and electrical diagnostics far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Set up or operate metalworking, brazing, heat-treating, welding, or cutting equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single setup-and-operate session (e.g., one weld joint or cut). Competent welder/metalworker spends ~15 min setup + ~30 min operating per job unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling heavy, hot, hazardous equipment; precise torch/electrode control; safety-critical judgment; parameter configuration; and tool-use generalization far beyond current humanoid capabilities. No commercial deployment exists.

task: Smooth surfaces, using sandpaper, scrapers, brushes, steel wool, or sanding machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sanding/smoothing a single surface (e.g., one door panel or furniture piece). A competent worker typically takes 15–35 minutes depending on size and tool.
robot_success_rationale: Requires consistent force control, tool switching, and surface-quality judgment (tactile/visual). Current humanoids lack reliable compliant force regulation and quality inspection feedback needed for acceptable finish. Structured flat surfaces raise odds slightly.

task: Insert sealing strips, wiring, insulating material, ladders, flanges, gauges, or valves, depending on types of structures being assembled.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inserting/fitting one type of component (e.g., a sealing strip or valve) into a structure. A competent worker typically takes 10–30 min depending on component complexity.
robot_success_rationale: High variability across component types (strips, valves, gauges, ladders) and structure configurations demands strong generalization. Fine insertion, torque control, and tool-use with diverse fasteners are at the edge of current humanoid capability. Low reliability expected.

task: Park and retrieve automobiles for customers in parking lots, storage garages, or new car lots.
human_time_rationale: One instance = driving a customer's car from drop-off to a parking spot (or vice versa), including walking back. Typical valet cycle is ~5–12 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot drive passenger vehicles in traffic or unstructured lots. Requires vehicle ingress, steering, pedal operation, navigation in dynamic environments — all beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capability.

task: Force structural steel members into final positions, using turnbuckles, crowbars, jacks, or hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning and forcing a single structural steel member into final position using tools. A skilled ironworker typically takes 15–35 minutes per member depending on size and fit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force tool use (crowbars, jacks, turnbuckles) on heavy steel in dynamic construction sites with poor footing, tight tolerances, and significant physical force demands far beyond current humanoid strength and stability limits.

task: Calibrate, troubleshoot, or repair equipment and correct malfunctions, as needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one equipment calibration/troubleshooting/repair cycle. Competent technician typically spends 30–60 min diagnosing and correcting a single malfunction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert diagnosis, fine dexterous tool-use, reading instruments, and adaptive problem-solving across unpredictable failure modes. Current humanoids lack the cognitive reasoning, specialized tool manipulation, and generalization needed for unstructured repair tasks.

task: Dig trenches to desired or required depths, by hand or using trenching tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = digging a single trench of typical utility/drainage length (~10–20 ft) and depth (~2 ft) by hand or with a hand trenching tool.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained forceful tool use (shovel/trencher) on uneven outdoor terrain, precise depth control, and soil adaptation. Current humanoids lack the strength endurance, ground stability, and tool-use generalization for this heavy outdoor task.

task: Remove asbestos or lead from surfaces, using hand or power tools such as scrapers, vacuums, or high-pressure sprayers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing asbestos/lead from a typical room or surface area (e.g., one wall or ceiling section). A trained abatement worker takes ~2 hours including setup, scraping/spraying, and containment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires hazmat suit donning, sustained forceful scraping/vacuuming across irregular surfaces, containment setup, and regulatory compliance. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and tool-switching needed in unstructured, contaminated environments.

task: Assess patients' general physical appearance to make diagnoses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient visual assessment during a clinical encounter; a competent clinician spends ~5–15 min observing appearance, gait, skin, posture, and integrating findings into a working diagnosis.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert clinical reasoning, nuanced multi-modal perception (skin tone, affect, odor, subtle posture cues), and diagnostic judgment. Current robots lack medical AI integration, clinical reasoning, and the perceptual sensitivity for reliable autonomous diagnosis.

task: Erect metal or precast concrete components for structures, such as buildings, bridges, dams, towers, storage tanks, fences, or highway guard rails.
human_time_rationale: One instance = erecting a single major structural component (e.g., a steel beam or precast panel). A skilled ironworker/rigger typically takes 1–2 hours per component including rigging, positioning, and bolting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating cranes and heavy rigging equipment, precise alignment of large heavy components outdoors on uneven terrain, and high-force bolting — all well beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured construction environments.

task: Check slopes for conformance to requirements, using levels or lasers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking slopes on one surface/section (e.g., a ramp or drain run). Worker sets up level or laser, takes multiple readings, and records conformance in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can carry a level/laser and navigate a surface, but reliably setting up a laser level, interpreting slope readings against spec, traversing uneven construction terrain, and documenting conformance autonomously is beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Render design ideas in form of paste-ups or drawings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one design rendering (paste-up or sketch). A competent designer typically spends ~45 min creating a single polished paste-up or detailed concept drawing.
robot_success_rationale: This is a creative cognitive task requiring artistic judgment, ideation, and fine motor rendering (pen/marker work or collage assembly). Current humanoid robots lack generative design creativity and the fine dexterity for expressive drawing or precise paste-up layout.

task: Arrange and attach chromosomes in numbered pairs on karyotype charts, using standard genetics laboratory practices and nomenclature, to identify normal or abnormal chromosomes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing one karyotype chart (one patient sample). A trained cytogenetics tech typically spends 20–45 min arranging, pairing, and annotating all 23 chromosome pairs and flagging abnormalities.
robot_success_rationale: Task is primarily cognitive/visual (digital image analysis + fine manual cutting/mounting), not gross physical manipulation. Robots lack trained cytogenetic pattern recognition, precise microscale paper/image handling, and domain-specific nomenclature application. Software tools exist but not general humanoid robots.

task: Move controls to drive gasoline- or electric-powered trucks, cars, or tractors and transport materials between loading, processing, and storage areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one transport trip (loading area to storage/processing area). A competent driver completes a typical short-haul internal facility trip in ~15 minutes including maneuvering.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate steering wheels, pedals, or truck/tractor controls in traffic or dynamic facility environments. L4 AVs are narrow, mapped-route systems. No general-purpose robot can reliably drive these vehicles autonomously in 2026.

task: Lift, position, and remove barricades to open or close parking areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = opening or closing one parking area by repositioning its set of barricades (typically 2–6 units). A competent worker walks the perimeter, lifts, moves, and places each barricade in ~10 min.
