robot_success_rationale: Barricades are heavy, awkward, and outdoors on uneven pavement. Robots lack reliable outdoor locomotion, robust grasp of bulky irregular objects, and the spatial planning to reposition multiple units safely. Improving but not deployment-ready.

task: Measure patients' body parts and mark locations where electrodes are to be placed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and marking electrode placement sites on one patient (e.g., for ECG/EEG). A competent technician typically takes ~8–12 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise anatomical landmark identification on varied human bodies, gentle contact with compliant skin, accurate measurement, and marking — all on a moving, sensitive patient. Current robots lack medical-grade tactile perception, safe gentle force control on humans, and reliable anatomical generalization.

task: Select and administer tests to evaluate hearing or related disabilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full hearing evaluation session for one patient, including test selection, setup, administration, and basic result logging. Typically 30–60 minutes for an audiologist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment to select tests, fine dexterous probe placement (e.g., tympanometry tips), patient communication/instruction, interpreting real-time responses, and adapting to patient behavior — all far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Measure mirrors and dimensions of areas to be covered to determine work procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring one mirror and its surrounding area. A worker uses a tape measure and records dimensions in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can use vision/LiDAR for rough spatial estimation, but precise measurement of mirror edges, irregular surfaces, and recording usable dimensions autonomously at job-site quality remains unreliable. Tool handling (tape measure) is poor.

task: Maintain trucks and food-dispensing equipment and clean inside of machines that dispense food or beverages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing one truck or food/beverage dispenser (inspection, cleaning internal components, minor maintenance). A competent technician typically spends ~45–90 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating/driving trucks (robots cannot), accessing tight internal machine spaces, using specialized tools, and fine dexterous cleaning of food-contact surfaces. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Examine specimens to detect abnormal hormone conditions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = analyzing one specimen batch for hormonal abnormalities, including sample prep review, instrument readout interpretation, and flagging results. Typically 15–25 minutes for a trained lab tech.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert clinical interpretation of quantitative assay data, pattern recognition across reference ranges, and medical judgment. No current humanoid robot has the domain cognition, lab instrument interfacing, or regulatory-grade decision-making for autonomous diagnostic analysis.

task: Cut prefabricated sections of framework, rails, and other components to specified dimensions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting one prefabricated section. Includes measuring, marking, tool setup, cutting, and verification. Typical cut takes ~5–10 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling long/heavy sections, precise measurement, operating power cutting tools (circular saw, miter saw, grinder), and verifying dimensions — beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and force-control capabilities in unstructured settings.

task: Connect electrical wiring to control panels and electric motors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wiring one control panel or motor. A skilled electrician takes ~30–60 min to route, strip, terminate, and secure wires correctly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise wire stripping, terminal insertion, torque-controlled screw tightening, and reading wiring diagrams — all in cramped enclosures. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use reliability; safety stakes are high.

task: Identify asbestos, lead, or other hazardous materials to be removed, using monitoring devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = surveying a single room or defined area for hazardous materials. Includes setup, device use, sampling, and initial logging — typically 30–60 min for a trained abatement inspector.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized monitoring devices (XRF guns, air samplers, bulk samplers), interpreting ambiguous readings, navigating cluttered spaces, and making regulatory-grade judgments. Current humanoids lack certified tool integration, fine sensor manipulation, and domain-specific decision logic for compliance-quality results.

task: Attach electrodes to patients, using adhesives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching a full set of electrodes to one patient (e.g., 10-lead ECG). A competent technician takes ~8–12 minutes including skin prep and placement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise anatomical landmark identification on varied human bodies, gentle touch on sensitive skin, fine dexterous peel-and-stick placement, and safe interaction with a live patient — all far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Dismantle structures or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dismantling a single mid-sized structure or piece of equipment (e.g., a scaffold section, machinery unit). A competent worker typically takes ~1 hour.
robot_success_rationale: Dismantling requires identifying fasteners, applying high torque, handling heavy/awkward components, and sequencing steps safely. Current humanoids lack the force output, tool-use reliability, and situational reasoning for unstructured disassembly tasks.

task: Connect car frames to counterweights, using steel cables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting one car frame to its counterweights via steel cables (e.g., elevator or amusement ride car). A skilled worker routes, tensions, and secures cables in ~20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires threading and tensioning heavy steel cables through anchor points with precise force control, spatial routing in a large mechanical structure, and tool use (torque wrenches, cable clamps). Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and strength-precision limits.

task: Mix or pour concrete into forms to encase waste material for disposal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling one form/mold with concrete to encase waste. Includes mixing, pouring, and leveling for a single disposal unit, typically ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires controlled pouring of viscous material into a specific form, managing heavy equipment (mixer), and operating in potentially hazardous/unstructured environments. Fluid handling, tool use, and safety compliance are major barriers for current humanoids.

task: Upload baskets of irradiated elements onto machines that insert fuel elements into canisters and secure lids.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one basket loaded, canister filled, and lid secured. A trained nuclear worker in PPE completes this in roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Highly specialized nuclear environment: radiation hardening required, precise basket alignment, lid-securing (torque-critical fastening), and strict safety protocols. Current humanoids lack radiation tolerance, fine force-controlled fastening, and nuclear facility certification.

task: Cut and attach mounting strips, metal or wood moldings, rubber gaskets, or metal clips to surfaces in preparation for mirror installation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one mirror installation surface; cutting and attaching strips/moldings/clips typically takes ~20–30 min for a competent installer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measuring/cutting of varied materials, fine dexterous fastening, and tool use (saw, drill, adhesive) in unstructured environments. Multi-step sequencing with tight tolerances far exceeds current humanoid capability.

task: Test newly installed equipment to ensure that it meets specifications, such as stopping at floors for set amounts of time.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one newly installed unit (e.g., elevator) across all floors and timing specs; a competent technician typically spends ~45 min running systematic checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating multi-floor buildings, pressing controls, riding elevators, recording precise timing data, and making spec-compliance judgments. Current humanoids lack the reliable multi-floor mobility, tool-use, and structured test-execution needed for autonomous pass/fail certification.

task: Cut, fit, install, repair, or replace glass or glass substitutes, such as plastic or aluminum, in building interiors or exteriors or in furniture or other products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting, fitting, and installing/replacing a single pane of glass or glass substitute (e.g., a window or door panel). A competent glazier typically takes 30–90 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise scoring and snapping of brittle glass, fine-force-controlled fitting into frames, use of specialized tools (glass cutters, suction cups, putty knives), and safe handling of large fragile sheets — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and perception limits.

task: Identify correct anatomical and proportional point locations based on patients' anatomy and positions, contraindications, and precautions related to treatments, such as intradermal needles, moxibustion, electricity, guasha, or bleeding.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing and locating acupoints on one patient for one treatment session. A trained practitioner spends ~10–20 min on palpation, landmark identification, and contraindication review.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert anatomical knowledge, nuanced palpation of soft tissue landmarks, patient-specific proportional measurement (cun), contraindication reasoning, and clinical judgment — all far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Stain slides to make chromosomes visible for microscopy.
human_time_rationale: One instance = staining one batch of slides (~4–8 slides). Includes fixing, applying stain (e.g. Giemsa), rinsing, and coverslipping; ~20–30 min for a competent lab tech.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting, timed reagent application, delicate slide handling, and lab bench dexterity. Current humanoids lack fine liquid-handling accuracy, controlled timing workflows, and the specialized perception needed for cytology lab protocols.

task: Drive trucks to transport crews, materials, and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one trip transporting crew/materials/equipment. Typical work trip (loading, driving, unloading) averages ~1 hour for a competent driver.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate trucks in traffic. L4 AVs exist but only on mapped routes with specialized hardware. Loading/unloading varied cargo adds further manipulation complexity. Fully autonomous end-to-end truck operation is not yet feasible for general-purpose robots.

task: Drive trucks to deliver such items as food, medical supplies, or newspapers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one delivery trip (pickup to drop-off). Typical local delivery route leg averages ~2 hours including loading, driving, and unloading.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate trucks in real traffic. L4 AVs exist but are narrow/mapped-route systems, not general-purpose humanoids. Loading, navigating, and unloading in unstructured environments compounds failure risk dramatically.

task: Direct motorists to parking areas or parking spaces, using hand signals or flashlights as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = guiding a single vehicle from entry point to a parked position; typically takes 1–5 minutes depending on lot size and congestion.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor locomotion, real-time vehicle/driver perception, dynamic hand-signal communication, and safe positioning near moving cars. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor mobility, vehicle intent prediction, and socially legible gesture coordination with human drivers.

task: Diagnose and treat hearing or related disabilities under the direction of an audiologist.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient diagnostic/treatment session. A competent audiology assistant typically spends ~30–60 min per patient for testing, fitting, or therapy under supervision.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, precise instrument handling (audiometers, hearing aids, otoscopes), nuanced patient interaction, and interpretation of audiological data. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, perception, and medical-decision generalization.

task: Verify that cytogenetic, molecular genetic, and related equipment and instrumentation is maintained in working condition to ensure accuracy and quality of experimental results.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full maintenance/verification check of a lab's genetic equipment suite (microscopes, PCR machines, sequencers, centrifuges). A competent lab tech typically takes 30–60 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly specialized perception (reading instrument displays, interpreting calibration outputs, running diagnostic software), fine dexterous manipulation of fragile lab instruments, domain-specific judgment about QC pass/fail criteria, and corrective action — far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Adjust equipment to optimize viewing of the nervous system.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a microscope/imaging device (focus, contrast, zoom, staining settings) to optimally visualize nervous tissue on a single specimen or slide.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine motor manipulation of delicate lab equipment knobs/dials, real-time visual feedback interpretation of neural tissue quality, and domain-specific judgment. Current humanoids lack the precision and neuroscience-specific perception needed.

task: Count numbers of chromosomes and identify the structural abnormalities by viewing culture slides through microscopes, light microscopes, or photomicroscopes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = analyzing a single patient karyotype preparation: scanning slides, locating metaphase spreads, counting chromosomes, and identifying structural abnormalities. A skilled cytogenetics technologist typically spends 30–60 minutes per case.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a specialized light/photomicroscope, fine manual focus adjustments, slide handling with extreme precision, and expert visual interpretation of banding patterns. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, specialized optics control, and domain-specific vision AI integration needed for autonomous cytogenetic analysis.

task: Inspect vehicles to detect any damage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one vehicle exterior and interior for damage; a competent inspector walks around, checks panels, glass, and underbody in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can walk around a vehicle and use cameras, but reliably detecting subtle dents, scratches, cracks, or fluid leaks across varied lighting and vehicle types requires fine perception and generalization that current humanoids lack. Underbody inspection adds locomotion difficulty.

task: Assemble, install, repair, and maintain elevators, escalators, moving sidewalks, and dumbwaiters, using hand and power tools, and testing devices such as test lamps, ammeters, and voltmeters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single service visit (install, repair, or maintenance job) on a vertical transport unit; median skilled elevator mechanic job runs ~3–4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert electrical diagnosis (voltmeters, ammeters), heavy mechanical assembly in confined shafts, fine tool use, and complex multi-step reasoning. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities in dexterity, safety, and generalization.

task: Place blocks under reinforcing bars used to reinforce floors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing a single block (rebar chair/spacer) under one rebar or rebar intersection. A worker can grab and position a plastic/concrete block in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating uneven concrete/rebar-covered ground, picking small plastic/concrete blocks, and precisely placing them under rebar at correct height. Unstructured outdoor terrain, cluttered environment, and fine placement demands challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Clean contaminated equipment or areas for reuse, using detergents or solvents, sandblasters, filter pumps, or steam cleaners.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one contaminated piece of equipment or area. A competent worker spends ~45 min selecting agents, applying detergents/solvents/steam, scrubbing, rinsing, and verifying cleanliness.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized tools (sandblasters, steam cleaners, filter pumps), handling hazardous chemicals, and verifying decontamination quality across unstructured surfaces. Current humanoids lack tool-use generalization and safety compliance for industrial decon tasks.

task: Assist audiologists in performing aural procedures, such as real ear measurements, speech audiometry, auditory brainstem responses, electronystagmography, and cochlear implant mapping.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting with a single aural procedure (e.g., ABR or cochlear implant mapping). A competent audiology assistant typically spends 30–60 min per procedure including prep, patient positioning, probe placement, and equipment setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous probe/electrode placement on a patient, real-time clinical judgment, patient communication, and safe handling of sensitive medical equipment. Well beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities; clinical safety standards alone preclude autonomous use.

task: Prescribe or administer topical or systemic medications to treat ophthalmic conditions and to manage pain.
human_time_rationale: One instance = prescribing or administering medication for one patient visit; includes assessment, selection, and administration/instruction, typically 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, diagnosis, legal prescribing authority, and precise ocular administration (e.g., eye drops, injections). Far beyond current robot dexterity, cognition, and regulatory scope. No frontier robot approaches this.

task: Install repaired parts into equipment or install new equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one repaired or new component/unit into equipment. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min aligning, fastening, and verifying a part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires variable dexterity (fasteners, connectors, alignments), tool use (wrenches, screwdrivers), and generalization across diverse equipment types. Current humanoids lack reliable fine tool-use and situational adaptability for unstructured repair tasks.

task: Administer blood, blood products, or supportive fluids.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and initiating one infusion (verify order, prepare IV line, hang bag, set rate, monitor initial response). Typical nurse time ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile IV line setup, fine dexterity for catheter/tubing connections, clinical judgment for patient monitoring, and strict safety protocols. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, perception, and medical-context generalization.

task: Clean and repair septic tanks, sewer lines, or related structures such as manholes, culverts, and catch basins.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and/or repairing one septic tank or sewer structure. A typical service call (pump, clean, inspect, minor repair) takes 2–4 hours for a crew.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating vacuum/jetter trucks, confined-space entry, heavy lid removal, hazardous-gas exposure, and unstructured underground environments. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in mobility, tool-use, and safety compliance.

task: Collect specimens for use in laboratory tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a single specimen (e.g., blood draw, swab, or urine sample) from one patient, including prep, collection, and labeling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity (venipuncture, swabbing), patient interaction, sterile technique, and real-time adaptation to human anatomy. Well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities; safety constraints make autonomous execution impractical.

task: Turn valves and open chutes to dump, spray, or release materials from dump cars or storage bins into hoppers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = operating valves/chutes on a single dump car or bin. A competent worker locates, turns valves, and opens chutes in roughly 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Industrial valves and chute levers vary widely in size, torque, and orientation. Robots struggle with high-torque rotary valves, unstructured industrial environments, tool-use generalization, and safe material-flow monitoring. Perception of completion state is also unreliable.

task: Lubricate, clean, or repair machinery, equipment, or tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance session on one machine/tool (e.g., lubricating a conveyor, cleaning a press). Typical session for a competent technician is ~20–45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool identification, precise lubrication points, torque application, and fault diagnosis across highly varied equipment. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use, generalization to novel machinery, and the dexterous repair skills needed.

task: Suture perineal lacerations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = suturing one perineal laceration. A skilled clinician spends ~30–60 min depending on degree/complexity; 45 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sterile field management, real-time haptic feedback, anatomical judgment, and patient safety compliance far beyond any current general-purpose humanoid robot's capabilities.

task: Service, adjust, and make minor repairs to equipment, machines, and attachments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing/adjusting one piece of equipment or machine. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min diagnosing, adjusting, lubricating, and making minor repairs.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults, selecting correct tools, fine dexterous manipulation (bolts, adjustments, fittings), and generalization across diverse machinery types. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use, fault diagnosis, and the precision for minor repairs across unstructured equipment.

task: Treat patients for routine physical health problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one routine patient visit (e.g., cold, minor injury, hypertension check). Includes assessment, diagnosis, and basic treatment; typical primary care encounter is 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, differential diagnosis, communication, and fine physical examination skills far beyond 2026 robots. No regulatory pathway exists; patient safety concerns are extreme. Essentially infeasible autonomously.

task: Issue identification cards to borrowers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = issuing one borrower's library/ID card: verify info, print/retrieve card, hand it over. Typically 2–4 minutes per person.
robot_success_rationale: Requires verifying identity (reading IDs, checking databases), operating card printers, social interaction, and handing a small card to a person. Current humanoids lack reliable integration with card-issuance systems and nuanced person-facing interaction.

task: Monitor fetal growth and well-being through heartbeat detection, body measurement, and palpation.
human_time_rationale: One prenatal monitoring session: auscultating fetal heartbeat, measuring fundal height, and performing Leopold's maneuvers/palpation on one patient — typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level tactile sensitivity for palpation, precise Doppler probe placement, clinical judgment, and safe patient interaction. Current robots lack the fine haptic perception, medical training integration, and safe bedside manner needed.

task: Break asphalt and other pavement so that pipes can be accessed, using airhammers, picks, and shovels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = breaking a typical pavement section (~2–4 sq meters) to expose a pipe. A competent worker with an airhammer takes ~30–60 min including setup and cleanup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy pneumatic airhammers and applying sustained high-force impacts on hard pavement outdoors. Current humanoids lack the strength, tool-coupling, vibration tolerance, and outdoor terrain stability for this work.

task: Cut and bind material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting a piece of material to size and binding/fastening it (e.g., fabric, paper, or packaging). A competent worker typically completes this in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Cutting requires precise tool control and material handling; binding (stapling, tying, gluing) demands fine dexterity and adaptive force. Current humanoids struggle with flexible/deformable materials and multi-step tool sequencing reliably.

task: Measure excavation sites, using plumbers' snakes, tapelines, or lengths of cutting heads within sewers, and mark areas for digging.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and marking a single excavation site in/around a sewer. Includes deploying measurement tools, recording readings, and marking the dig area; ~30–60 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires inserting flexible tools (plumber's snake, tapeline) into confined underground sewers, interpreting measurements, and marking ground—demanding unstructured environments, confined-space navigation, and precise tool-use far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Move levers or controls that operate lifting devices, such as forklifts, lift beams with swivel-hooks, hoists, or elevating platforms, to load, unload, transport, or stack material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one load/unload/transport cycle with a lifting device (e.g., forklift). A competent operator typically takes 10–20 minutes per cycle including positioning, lifting, and stacking.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate forklifts, hoists, or elevating platforms in real traffic/warehouse conditions. These require seated vehicle operation with foot pedals and specialized controls. Autonomous forklifts exist but are narrow, non-humanoid systems. General-purpose humanoids lack the ability to mount, drive, and precisely operate such machinery safely.

task: Set up or monitor the administration of oxygen or medications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up OR checking one oxygen/medication administration session; includes patient assessment, equipment prep, dosage verification, and initial monitoring. Typical clinical workflow: ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (IV lines, O2 tubing, syringes), clinical judgment, patient interaction, safety-critical dosage decisions, and regulatory constraints. Current humanoids lack medical tool proficiency and cannot safely make autonomous care decisions.

task: Greet customers and open their car doors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = greeting one arriving customer and opening their car door. A valet or doorman completes this in ~1–2 minutes per vehicle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor locomotion to approach a stopped vehicle, locating and gripping a varied car door handle, pulling it open smoothly, AND delivering a socially appropriate greeting — each step is moderately hard; combined reliability is low. Social nuance and varied door handle geometry reduce success further.

task: Package the product in the form in which it will be sent out, for example, filling bags with flour from a chute or spout.
human_time_rationale: One instance = packaging one unit/bag. A competent worker filling bags from a chute/spout takes ~1–2 minutes per bag including positioning, filling, and sealing.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can position and hold bags under spouts, but reliably opening flexible bags, aligning to a chute, managing variable fill weights, and sealing cleanly requires dexterity and perception that current humanoids handle inconsistently. Structured setups help; unstructured settings hurt.

task: Assemble and cement sections of stained glass together.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one stained glass panel. A skilled artisan spends ~1–3 hrs fitting, grozing, leading/soldering, and cementing a moderately complex piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to handle fragile, irregular glass shards, apply lead came or copper foil precisely, solder joints, and apply/brush cement—all without cracking pieces. Far beyond current humanoid manipulation capability.

task: Inspect manholes to locate sewer line stoppages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one manhole: removing cover, assessing flow/blockage, logging findings. A competent worker typically takes 15–25 minutes per manhole.
robot_success_rationale: Requires lifting a heavy (~50 kg) cast-iron cover, descending into or peering into a confined space with hazardous gases, navigating uneven outdoor terrain, and interpreting flow conditions — far beyond current humanoid capability outdoors.

task: Operate mechanized equipment, such as pickup trucks, rollers, tandem dump trucks, front-end loaders, or backhoes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one operator shift or work session on a piece of mechanized equipment (e.g., grading a road section, completing a dig/fill cycle). Typical continuous operation block is ~4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate heavy vehicles/machinery in real-world, unstructured jobsite conditions. Requires seated cab control, pedal/lever fine motor coordination, spatial judgment, and dynamic terrain navigation far beyond current platforms. Narrow autonomous excavator research demos exist but are not general-purpose humanoid robots.

task: Pack spaces between moldings and glass with glazing compounds and trim excess material with glazing knives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = glazing one standard window pane. A competent glazier takes ~10–20 min to apply compound, press into molding gap, and trim excess cleanly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained fine-force dexterous tool use (glazing knife), precise bead application along irregular molding geometry, and pressure control to avoid cracking glass. Far beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Provide necessary medical care for infants at birth, including emergency care such as resuscitation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attending to one newborn at birth, from delivery through initial stabilization. Routine care takes ~10–15 min; emergency resuscitation extends to ~30 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (airway intubation, IV lines, chest compressions on neonates), real-time clinical judgment, sterile technique, and adaptive response to a fragile, unpredictable patient. Far beyond current robot capabilities.

task: Perform post-partum health assessments of mothers and babies at regular intervals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single post-partum assessment visit for one mother-baby pair; a competent midwife/nurse typically spends ~20–40 min covering vitals, wound checks, feeding, and newborn exam.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced clinical judgment, fine-touch palpation (uterine involution, fontanelle), patient communication, and safe infant handling. 2026 robots lack the dexterity, medical sensing, and relational safety margins needed.

task: Travel to customers' stores or offices to service machines or to provide emergency repair service.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one trip to a customer site. Typical field service travel averages ~30–60 min one-way, depending on territory size and urban/rural mix.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot drive vehicles on public roads. No current general-purpose robot can autonomously navigate to an off-site customer location via road travel. Requires L4 AV infrastructure not available to humanoids.

task: Assist maternal patients to find physical positions that will facilitate childbirth.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting a single patient into and through a labor/birth position adjustment. A skilled nurse or midwife typically spends 10–30 minutes per repositioning episode.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced physical support of a vulnerable, pain-stressed patient, real-time verbal/nonverbal communication, clinical judgment, safe force modulation on a pregnant body, and trust — all far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Collect coins from vending machines, refill machines, and remove aged merchandise.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing one vending machine: unlocking, collecting coins, restocking products, and pulling expired items. Typical route worker spends ~15–25 min per machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a keyed lock, handling a cash drawer, identifying/pulling expired SKUs, and precisely loading varied product into slots — multi-step, high-dexterity, tool-dependent tasks well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Clean and disinfect domestic basements and other areas flooded by sewer stoppages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and disinfecting a single flooded basement. A competent worker with equipment typically needs 2–4 hours for pumping, scrubbing, and disinfecting a typical residential basement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating pumps, wet-vacs, and chemical sprayers in a hazardous, unstructured, slippery, and waterlogged environment. Current humanoids lack waterproofing, biohazard tool-use, and the generalization needed for sewage remediation tasks.

task: Rotate cleaning rods manually, using turning pins.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rotating cleaning rods through one cleaning pass (e.g., a barrel or pipe). A competent worker inserts, attaches turning pins, and rotates the rod in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise grip on small turning pins, coordinated two-handed rotation, and force feedback to avoid jamming. Fine dexterity with small tool interfaces and torque control remain weak points for 2026 humanoids.

task: Inspect meals served for conformance to prescribed diets and standards of palatability and appearance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one meal tray. A dietitian/supervisor visually checks portions, diet compliance, presentation, and palatability cues in ~2–4 minutes per tray.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual/olfactory judgment of palatability, color, texture, and diet-compliance cross-referencing. Current robots lack reliable food-quality perception, smell, and contextual dietary rule reasoning to meet supervisor standards.

task: Dig out sewer lines manually, using shovels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = digging out a typical residential sewer line segment (~10–20 ft). A competent crew member with a shovel takes ~3–4 hours depending on depth and soil conditions.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained high-force digging in unpredictable outdoor terrain, deep trenching, heavy spoil removal, and navigation around buried utilities. Current humanoids lack the power, endurance, and ground stability for this prolonged, forceful outdoor task.

task: Assemble machines according to specifications, using hand or power tools and measuring devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one machine unit. Typical industrial machine assembly (e.g., motor, pump, gearbox) with tools and measurement checks takes 1–3 hours for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool use (torque wrenches, calipers, power tools), reading specs, multi-step sequencing, and measurement verification. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity, tool-switching, and spec-interpretation for complex unstructured machine assembly.

task: Prepare suites or rooms according to endoscopic procedure requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one endoscopy suite/room for a single procedure: arranging equipment, stocking supplies, positioning scope, checking settings. Typically 15–25 minutes per room turnover.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise clinical knowledge, sterile technique, specialized equipment handling (scopes, towers, IV setups), fine dexterity, and safety-critical judgment. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities in unstructured medical environments.

task: Fabricate needed parts or items from sheet metal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single sheet metal part (cutting, bending, forming). A competent sheet metal worker typically takes 30–60 min per part depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating industrial machinery (shears, press brakes, rollers), interpreting blueprints, precise positioning, and multi-step tool changes. Humanoids lack the strength, specialized tool interfaces, and precision needed for sheet metal fabrication.

task: Reassemble machines after making repairs or replacing parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reassembling one machine after a repair. Competent mechanic/technician typically spends 30–60 min depending on machine complexity; 45 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Reassembly requires precise torque, varied fastener types, spatial reasoning around complex geometries, and contextual judgment about part fit — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and generalization. No commercial system handles unstructured machine reassembly reliably.

task: Administer basic hearing tests including air conduction, bone conduction, or speech audiometry tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full basic hearing test battery (air conduction, bone conduction, speech audiometry) for one patient; a trained audiologist typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise headphone/bone oscillator placement on a patient, operating audiometry equipment, interpreting patient responses, adjusting protocol dynamically, and clinical communication — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities in an unstructured clinical setting.

task: Install, calibrate, operate, or maintain robots.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single robot installation, calibration, operation session, or scheduled maintenance visit. A typical calibration or maintenance task runs ~60 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert technical reasoning, fine tool use, system diagnostics, and judgment to work on another robot. Current humanoids lack the cognitive and dexterous capability to autonomously service complex robotic systems.

task: Tap mainline sewers to install sewer saddles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one sewer saddle on a mainline pipe: locating tap point, cutting/drilling into pipe, fitting and securing the saddle, and sealing. Typically 30–60 min for a skilled plumber.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working in confined underground spaces (manholes, trenches), heavy pipe cutting/drilling with specialized tools, precise torque/sealing, and unstructured environments. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities in dexterity, mobility, and tool-use.

task: Calibrate testing instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = calibrating a single instrument (e.g., a multimeter, pressure gauge, or sensor). A competent technician typically takes 20–45 minutes including setup, reference checks, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Calibration requires precise dexterous manipulation of fine adjustment screws/knobs, reading analog/digital displays, interpreting multi-step procedures, connecting delicate leads, and making iterative corrections — all well beyond current humanoid dexterity and generalization limits.

task: Start machines to feed revolving cables or rods into openings, stopping machines and changing knives to conform to pipe sizes.
human_time_rationale: One instance covers starting the machine, feeding cable/rod, stopping, and changing knives for one pipe size — a skilled operator takes ~10–20 min per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating industrial machinery controls, precise alignment of cables/rods into narrow pipe openings, and fine dexterous knife-swapping with tight tolerances — well beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Clean, oil, or adjust mechanical parts to maintain machines' operating efficiency and to prevent breakdowns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing a single machine or component (cleaning, oiling, adjusting). A competent mechanic typically spends ~30 min per machine on a routine maintenance cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct lubrication points, applying precise amounts of oil, adjusting tension/clearances, and navigating varied machinery configurations. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tool-use generalization, and domain perception needed for reliable autonomous maintenance across arbitrary machines.

task: Maintain or repair hearing aids or other communication devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and repairing/maintaining a single hearing aid or communication device. A trained technician typically spends 30–60 min per device.
robot_success_rationale: Hearing aids are tiny, intricate devices requiring extreme fine dexterity, micro-soldering, and diagnostic tool use far beyond current humanoid capability. Perception of miniature components and fault diagnosis also unsolved.

task: Disassemble machines to examine parts, such as wires, gears, or bearings for wear or defects, using hand or power tools and measuring devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling one machine unit (e.g., motor/gearbox), inspecting components, and noting defects. A competent mechanic typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sequential multi-tool use, fine torque control for varied fasteners, flexible part identification, and nuanced defect perception (wear, cracks). Current humanoids lack reliable tool-switching, tactile inspection acuity, and unstructured disassembly generalization.

task: Load or unload materials into containers or onto trucks, using hoists or forklifts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading or unloading a typical truckload of materials using a hoist or forklift. A competent operator takes ~30–60 min depending on load count and layout.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a forklift or hoist — vehicles/equipment a humanoid robot cannot drive or control autonomously in real environments. Spatial judgment, load stability, and safety demands further preclude current robots.

task: Fill machines with toners, inks, or other duplicating fluids.
human_time_rationale: One instance = refilling one machine (printer/copier/duplicator). A competent worker locates the correct cartridge/fluid, opens the compartment, installs or fills, and closes — typically 5–12 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise manipulation of varied cartridge formats, latches, and fluid containers; locating small access doors; avoiding spills. Poor generalization across machine types, high dexterity and perception demands for current humanoids.

task: Note differences in wire and cable colors so that work can be performed correctly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = noting wire/cable color differences at a single work site or panel. A competent electrician visually identifies and records color distinctions in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Modern robots have solid color-differentiation via RGB cameras and can log observations, but cluttered wiring, poor lighting, overlapping cables, and context-dependent interpretation (e.g., faded or non-standard colors) reduce reliability significantly.

task: Assist speech-language pathologists in the conduct of client screenings or assessments of language, voice, fluency, articulation, or hearing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one client screening/assessment session. A typical speech-language screening or assessment takes 30–60 minutes, median ~45 min for a competent SLP assistant.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social interaction, clinical judgment, adaptive communication, precise perceptual discrimination of speech/voice, and rapport-building with clients. Far beyond current robot generalization and caregiving capability.

task: Demonstrate assistive listening devices (ALDs) to clients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = demonstrating ALDs to one client session, covering device handling, explanation, and Q&A. A trained audiologist/specialist typically spends ~20–40 minutes per client.
robot_success_rationale: Requires adaptive communication, reading client comprehension, fine dexterous handling of tiny hearing devices, and personalized explanation. Current robots lack natural language tutoring, social sensitivity, and reliable fine-motor manipulation of miniature ALD components.

task: Respond to emergency situations by providing cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR), basic cardiac life support (BLS), advanced cardiac life support (ACLS), or pediatric advanced life support (PALS).
human_time_rationale: One instance = one emergency response episode (from collapse recognition to handoff to EMS or ROSC). Typical CPR/BLS episode lasts ~10–20 min; 15 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise chest compressions, airway management, defibrillator use, drug administration (ACLS/PALS), and real-time clinical judgment on a moving human body. Current humanoids lack medical tool integration, force-controlled CPR, and emergency protocol reliability. Extremely high stakes make autonomous success near-zero.

task: Cover repaired pipes with dirt, and pack backfilled excavations, using air and gasoline tampers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = backfilling and compacting a single pipe repair excavation (~3–6 ft trench). A competent worker covers, positions tamper, and compacts in ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating gasoline/air-powered tamper equipment in an outdoor excavation with uneven, unstable terrain. Humanoid robots lack the ability to safely operate such powered compaction tools, manage hoses/fuel lines, and navigate trench edges reliably.

task: Instruct individuals or groups in sports rules, game strategies, and performance principles, such as specific ways of moving the body, hands, or feet, to achieve desired results.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single coaching session (individual or group). A typical structured sports practice or instructional session runs ~60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires adaptive verbal communication, real-time observation of human movement, contextual judgment, motivational nuance, and dynamic physical demonstration. Current humanoids lack natural language coaching fluency, nuanced bio-mechanical feedback, and social-emotional adaptability needed for accepted instruction.

task: Use special finishing techniques such as sponging, ragging, layering, or faux finishing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying a special finish technique to one average wall or surface (~50 sq ft). A competent painter takes ~30–60 min including setup, application, and blending.
robot_success_rationale: Faux/decorative finishing demands highly nuanced tactile control, artistic judgment, variable pressure/texture, and aesthetic evaluation. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and perception; no demonstrated capability for such creative, fine-motor surface work.

task: Maintain laboratory equipment such as photomicroscopes, inverted microscopes, and standard darkroom equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = routine maintenance of a single piece of lab equipment (e.g., cleaning lenses, checking alignment, replacing bulbs). A competent lab tech takes ~30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for delicate optics (lens cleaning, alignment), specialized tool use, and nuanced judgment about calibration quality. Current humanoids lack the precision and optical domain knowledge needed.

task: Grade or level trench bases, using tamping machines or hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grading/leveling the base of a single trench segment (~10–20m). A competent worker using a tamping machine or hand tools takes ~30–60 min depending on trench length and soil conditions.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy tamping machinery or vigorous hand-tool use in a confined, unstructured outdoor trench. Humanoids lack the force output, tool integration, stability on uneven soil, and grading precision needed. No viable 2026 deployment exists.

task: Operate sewer cleaning equipment, including power rodders, high-velocity water jets, sewer flushers, bucket machines, wayne balls, and vac-alls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one sewer segment/blockage. A competent worker sets up equipment, runs the cleaning cycle, and confirms clearance; ~45–90 min median per segment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized vehicle-mounted heavy equipment (vac-alls, flushers, jet trucks) in outdoor/underground environments. Humanoids cannot drive or control these machines; task demands exceed all current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Apprehend shoplifters in accordance with guidelines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = detecting, pursuing, and detaining one shoplifter per guidelines; includes observation, confrontation, and handoff to authorities, typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time social/behavioral judgment, legal compliance, physical restraint, and de-escalation in unpredictable human interactions. Current humanoids lack reliable suspect identification, authority-delegated force, and safe physical apprehension capability.

task: Clean or sanitize work areas, utensils, or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/sanitizing a typical work area, set of utensils, or a piece of equipment; ~15 min covers wiping surfaces, applying sanitizer, and rinsing/drying.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can wipe flat surfaces reasonably well, but sanitizing varied utensils, equipment crevices, and confirming cleanliness requires dexterity, tool-switching, and contextual judgment that current humanoids handle inconsistently.

task: Test or maintain equipment to ensure correct performance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single equipment maintenance/test cycle (e.g., one machine or unit). A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min on checks, diagnostics, and minor adjustments.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse tool use, reading gauges/displays, interpreting results, and adaptive troubleshooting across highly varied equipment. Current humanoids lack the specialized perception, domain knowledge, and dexterous tool manipulation needed for autonomous end-to-end success.

task: Tap and drill holes into pipes to introduce auxiliary lines or devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tapping and drilling one pipe location. Includes marking, clamping, drilling, and threading the hole. A skilled pipefitter takes ~15–25 min per hole.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise positioning, high torque tool use (drill, tap wrench), force-controlled threading on curved metal surfaces, and working in constrained industrial environments. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use generalization.

task: Assemble electrically powered stairs, steel frameworks, and tracks, and install associated motors and electrical wiring.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and installing one complete escalator/powered stair unit (steel framework, tracks, motors, wiring). A skilled crew typically takes a full 8-hour shift per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy structural lifting, precision alignment of large steel frameworks, complex electrical wiring, multi-step tool use, and judgment in unstructured construction sites — far beyond current humanoid robots' dexterity, strength, and generalization.

task: Operate cameras, radios, or other surveillance equipment to intercept communications or document activities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one surveillance shift or monitoring session (~1 hour typical unit); involves setting up/operating equipment and logging observations.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized surveillance/radio equipment, making contextual judgments about targets, repositioning covertly, and adapting to unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack tool-use generalization, covert mobility, and mission-level reasoning for this task.

task: Operate cranes to move or load baskets, casks, or canisters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one lift-and-place cycle (rig, lift, move, set down a basket/cask/canister). A competent crane operator completes this in ~15–25 minutes including coordination and positioning.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate crane cabins or overhead crane controls in real environments. Crane operation requires specialized vehicle/equipment control, spatial judgment, and load management far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Perform maintenance on cars in storage to protect tires, batteries, or exteriors from deterioration.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full storage maintenance on one car (tire pressure check/inflation, battery tender hookup, exterior cover/wax). A competent technician takes ~45 min per vehicle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating an air compressor, connecting battery tenders, applying wax, and handling covers — diverse, dexterous, tool-use tasks across an unstructured environment. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-switching, fine connector manipulation, and chemical application finesse.

task: Adjust or modify equipment to enhance equipment performance or to respond to customer requests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one equipment adjustment/modification session. A competent technician typically spends ~30 min diagnosing, adjusting settings, swapping parts, and verifying performance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing customer needs, identifying correct adjustment procedure, using varied tools on diverse equipment, and verifying outcomes. Current robots lack reliable tool-use generalization, contextual reasoning, and adaptive fine-dexterity for arbitrary equipment types.

task: Perform safety checks to verify proper equipment functioning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single safety check round on a piece or set of equipment (e.g., machinery, PPE, fire extinguishers). A competent worker typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Safety checks require diverse perception (visual, auditory, tactile), reading gauges/labels, operating controls, and contextual judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable multi-modal inspection, nuanced fault diagnosis, and generalization across varied equipment types.

task: Spray materials, such as water, sand, steam, vinyl, paint, or stucco, through hoses to clean, coat, or seal surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = spraying one surface/area (e.g., a wall, deck, or vehicle). A competent worker typically takes 30–60 min to set up equipment, spray, and inspect a typical surface.
robot_success_rationale: Requires managing heavy hoses under pressure, navigating varied outdoor/unstructured terrain, judging coverage quality, and adapting spray distance/angle. Current humanoids lack the force handling, tool-use generalization, and outdoor mobility needed reliably.

task: Park vehicles at loading areas so that passengers can board.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pulling a vehicle into a designated loading zone, stopping, and securing it. A skilled driver completes this in ~3–7 minutes including approach and positioning.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot drive or park passenger vehicles in traffic. L4 AVs exist but are narrow, mapped-route systems. No general-purpose robot can operate a vehicle in a dynamic loading area autonomously at acceptable quality.

task: Locate problems, using specially designed equipment, and mark where digging must occur to reach damaged tanks or pipes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating and marking one fault site. Technician deploys specialized detection equipment, interprets readings, and marks the excavation point — typically 45–90 min per site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized diagnostic equipment (GPR, acoustic sensors, tracer gas detectors), interpreting ambiguous subsurface data, and precisely marking dig points outdoors on uneven terrain — well beyond current humanoid robot autonomy.

task: Clean and maintain tools, test equipment, and motor vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/maintaining a set of tools, equipment, or a vehicle in a single session; ~45 min covers wiping, inspecting, lubricating, and storing a typical kit.
robot_success_rationale: Highly varied objects (hand tools, test equipment, vehicles) requiring inspection, lubrication, and fine manipulation. Robots lack reliable object recognition across this diversity, dexterous tool handling, and cannot operate or reposition vehicles autonomously.

task: Position lifting devices under, over, or around loaded pallets, skids, or boxes and secure material or products for transport to designated areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning a lifting device under/around one loaded pallet and securing it ready for transport; a competent worker does this in roughly 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a forklift, pallet jack, or hoist — wheeled/heavy equipment a humanoid cannot drive or steer in practice. Precise alignment under loaded pallets in cluttered warehouses, plus securing loads, far exceeds current autonomous capability.

task: Conduct tests to determine cerebral death, the absence of brain activity, or the probability of recovery from a coma.
human_time_rationale: One full cerebral death/coma evaluation protocol (clinical exam, apnea test, reflex checks, documentation) typically takes 60–120 min for a competent physician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced medical judgment, nuanced neurological examination, pharmacological interpretation, legal/ethical responsibility, and highly delicate patient interaction. Far beyond any 2026 general-purpose robot's capabilities.

task: Check temperatures of freezers, refrigerators, or heating equipment to ensure proper functioning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking the temperature of a single unit (e.g., one freezer). A competent worker reads a display or inserts a thermometer in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot must navigate to unit, read a digital/analog display or handle a thermometer, and log the value. Display reading via vision is feasible but varied UI, door-opening, and logging steps reduce reliability significantly.

task: Respond to critical incidents, such as catastrophic events, violent weather, or civil disorders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = responding to a single critical incident from dispatch through stabilization/handoff. Typical incidents (e.g., structure fire, civil disorder scene, severe weather response) take 1–3 hours; 120 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic decision-making, coordination with personnel, operating vehicles, handling unpredictable hazardous environments, and social/command judgment. Current humanoids lack vehicle operation, situational authority, and resilience in chaotic unstructured conditions.

task: Implement treatment plans or protocols as directed by speech-language pathologists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one therapy session with a patient, implementing a prescribed SLP protocol. Typical sessions run 30–60 minutes, median ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced verbal interaction, real-time clinical judgment, adaptive communication, and physical prompting for swallowing/articulation exercises. Far beyond current robot generalization, social cognition, and therapeutic dexterity capabilities.

task: Position, join, align, or seal structural components, such as concrete wall sections or pipes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning/joining one structural component (e.g., one pipe joint or wall panel). Includes alignment, sealing, and inspection; ~30–60 min per unit for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy-load handling, precise outdoor/unstructured site navigation, and specialized tools (levels, cranes, sealant guns). Humanoids lack the strength, payload capacity, and construction-equipment operation needed for large structural components.

task: Keep parking areas clean and orderly to ensure that space usage is maximized.
human_time_rationale: One instance = patrolling and tidying a typical parking lot (litter pickup, cone/barrier repositioning, organizing vehicles/carts). A competent attendant covers one lot in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor uneven terrain, varied litter types, moving vehicles, and judgment calls on space optimization challenge current humanoids. Basic sweeping possible but full autonomous lot management, cart retrieval, and orderliness decisions are well beyond reliable capability.

task: Prepare or serve hot or cold beverages, such as coffee, espresso drinks, blended coffees, or teas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and serving a single beverage order (e.g., espresso drink or brewed tea). A skilled barista typically takes 2–6 minutes per drink, median ~4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (tamping, steaming, pouring latte art), operating specialized espresso equipment, precise force control, and adapting to varied drink orders. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and appliance interfacing for this multi-step, precision task.

task: Place devices, such as blood pressure cuffs, pulse oximeter sensors, nasal cannulas, surgical cautery pads, and cardiac monitoring electrodes, on patients to monitor vital signs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing a full set of monitoring devices on one patient (BP cuff, SpO2 sensor, nasal cannula, cautery pad, ECG electrodes). A competent nurse/tech takes ~5–12 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, gentle dexterity on an uncooperative/variable human body, skin-prep, accurate anatomical placement, and soft-tissue awareness. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation, tactile feedback, and clinical judgment needed; safety risk is extreme.

task: Weigh, grind, or pack coffee beans for customers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = serving one customer's order: scooping/weighing beans, grinding if requested, and bagging/packing. Typically 2–4 minutes for a competent barista or retail worker.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle pick-and-place and operate simple appliances, but weighing to spec, operating a grinder's controls, sealing bags, and adapting to varied customer requests in an unstructured retail environment remain challenging. Poor generalization and dexterous tool-use limits success.

task: Perform routine maintenance on equipment, including adjusting and lubricating components and painting worn or exposed areas.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance session on a piece of equipment: inspecting, adjusting, lubricating components, and touch-up painting worn areas. Typical skilled-trade unit of work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step tool use (wrenches, grease guns, paint brushes), fine dexterous adjustments, visual inspection of wear, and context-specific judgment. Well beyond reliable autonomous capability for current humanoids.

task: Monitor patients during tests or surgeries, using electroencephalographs (EEG), evoked potential (EP) instruments, or video recording equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = monitoring one patient through a typical EEG/EP session or surgical procedure, including setup, active monitoring, and wrap-up (~60–120 min median).
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert clinical interpretation of neurological waveforms, real-time adaptive decision-making, and artifact recognition. No current humanoid robot has the specialized perception, domain knowledge, or certified clinical judgment for autonomous neuromonitoring.

task: Verify proper functioning of physical security systems, such as closed-circuit televisions, alarms, sensor tag systems, or locks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full inspection round of a typical facility's security systems (cameras, alarms, sensors, locks). A competent security technician takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires visually reading camera feeds, interpreting alarm panel status, testing sensor tags, and manipulating varied locks. Multi-system integration, UI interaction, and judgment-based pass/fail assessment exceed current humanoid generalization and tool-use capabilities.

task: Test patients' hemoglobin, hematocrit, and blood glucose levels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = running all three tests on one patient: fingerstick/venipuncture, loading analyzer, reading results. ~10 min for a competent phlebotomist/nurse.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise venipuncture or fingerstick on a human, sterile technique, handling fragile capillary tubes, and operating a clinical analyzer. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and medical-safety standards.

task: Position or transport patients in accordance with instructions from medical personnel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving/repositioning one patient (e.g., bed to wheelchair or repositioning in bed). A competent aide typically takes 5–15 minutes including setup and safety checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires gentle, adaptive handling of fragile, uncooperative, or unconscious humans; real-time force sensing; precise body mechanics; and fluid communication with staff. Current humanoids lack safe, reliable human-contact dexterity and compliant whole-body control for this task.

task: Operate or tend automatic stacking, loading, packaging, or cutting machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one production run cycle (setup, monitoring, intervention, clearance). A competent operator typically manages this over ~30 min per cycle/shift segment.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can do basic pick-and-place near machines, but operating/tending automated industrial equipment requires interface interaction, fault diagnosis, material replenishment, and adaptive intervention — well beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Install rotary knives on flexible cables mounted on machine reels, according to the diameters of pipes to be cleaned.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting and mounting the correct rotary knife on a cable reel for one pipe diameter; a skilled plumber/technician takes ~10–15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct knife size by pipe diameter, threading onto a flexible cable end, and securing it tightly — highly dexterous fine manipulation of cables, small fasteners, and non-rigid components; far beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Install telephone station equipment, such as intercommunication systems, transmitters, receivers, relays, and ringers, and related apparatus, such as coin collectors, telephone booths, and switching-key equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a complete telephone station setup (equipment + apparatus) at a single location. A skilled technician typically takes 60–120 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous wiring, mounting hardware in confined spaces, interpreting wiring schematics, and handling highly varied equipment types. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capability in unstructured telecom environments.

task: Provide airway management interventions including tracheal intubation, fiber optics, or ventilary support.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient airway management episode (assessment through securing airway/confirming placement). A competent clinician typically takes 5–20 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, anatomical perception, sterile technique, real-time clinical judgment, and force control in a dynamic human airway. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities; patient safety stakes make autonomous action untenable.

task: Prepare work stations for use by mechanics and repairers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single workstation before a shift. A competent mechanic/repairer sets up tools, equipment, and supplies in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fetching, sorting, and placing diverse tools across a semi-structured workspace; contextual judgment about what each mechanic needs; varied object shapes and storage locations challenge generalization and dexterity significantly.

task: Demonstrate equipment to customers and explain its use, responding to any inquiries or complaints.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single customer demonstration session, covering equipment walkthrough, Q&A, and handling complaints. Typical retail/showroom demo lasts ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires natural language conversation, reading customer intent, adaptive social responses, complaint handling, and persuasive explanation. Current robots lack robust open-ended dialogue and social generalization needed for autonomous customer interaction.

task: Perform routine maintenance on vehicles or auxiliary equipment, such as cleaning, lubricating, recharging batteries, fueling, or replacing liquefied-gas tank.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one routine maintenance session on a single vehicle/unit (e.g., fluid top-off, battery recharge, fueling, lubrication). A competent worker completes this in ~30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating fuel nozzles, grease guns, battery chargers, and gas tanks — high-force, precise tool-use with varied connectors. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterity and domain-specific tool generalization for vehicle service tasks.

task: Assist physicians or registered nurses in the conduct of endoscopic procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting through one endoscopic procedure (e.g., colonoscopy or upper GI scope), from prep to scope withdrawal, typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile fine dexterity, handing instruments, patient monitoring, and real-time clinical judgment in a safety-critical medical setting. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured, high-stakes clinical environments.

task: Escort customers to their vehicles to ensure their safety.
human_time_rationale: One instance = walking one customer from a building entrance to their parked vehicle. Typical parking lot escort takes 2–8 minutes depending on distance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor navigation over varied terrain, crowd awareness, social interaction, and adaptive response to unpredictable people/vehicles. Current humanoids lack robust outdoor mobility, social cue interpretation, and meaningful protective capability.

task: Wrap, label, or date food items for sale.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wrapping, labeling, or dating a single food item. A competent worker completes this in ~1–3 minutes including material handling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (handling wrap/labels, applying stickers neatly), perception of varied food shapes, and tool use. Current humanoids lack reliable label application and compliant wrapping on irregular food items.

task: Prepare cell samples by applying special staining techniques, such as chromosomal staining, to differentiate cells or cell components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single cell sample with a specialized staining technique (e.g., chromosomal stain). Includes fixation, staining, washing, and mounting steps; typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely precise pipetting of micro-volumes, delicate handling of fragile slides/coverslips, temperature control, and chemical timing. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and lab-tool generalization; high contamination/breakage risk.

task: Bolt or weld steel rails to the walls of shafts to guide elevators, working from scaffolding or platforms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one rail section (typically ~5 ft) on a shaft wall. A competent elevator mechanic takes ~1.5–2 hours per section including alignment, fastening, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating on scaffolding/platforms in a confined vertical shaft, handling heavy steel rails, precise alignment, high-torque bolting or welding — far beyond current humanoid mobility, strength, and dexterous tool-use capabilities in unstructured environments.

task: Fasten structural steel members to hoist cables, using chains, cables, or rope.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rigging one structural steel member with chains/cables/rope and securing it to a hoist cable, ready for lift. A skilled ironworker/rigger typically takes 5–12 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy rigging hardware manipulation, precise load balancing, strong grip on large steel members, and safety-critical knot/chain attachment outdoors on uneven terrain. Current humanoids lack the force, reach, and dexterity for safe autonomous rigging.

task: Produce images of objects on film, using radiographic techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and exposing one radiographic image of an object. Includes positioning, technique selection, exposure, and film/detector handling — ~30 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized radiographic equipment (X-ray generator, positioning), precise geometry, radiation safety protocols, and technique selection. Humanoid robots lack the specialized tooling, radiation-safe operation capability, and fine parameter judgment needed.

task: Assemble and maintain physical structures, using hand or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling or maintaining a typical physical structure (e.g., shelving unit, frame, fixture). A competent worker completes this in ~1 hour including tool use and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse tool use (hand & power tools), spatial reasoning over varied structures, and fine dexterous manipulation. Current humanoids struggle with unscripted tool switching, torque control, and structural variability in real environments.

task: Tend machines that pump concrete, grout, cement, sand, plaster, or stucco through spray guns for application to ceilings or walls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full application session (e.g., coating a room or wall section). A competent operator spends ~2–4 hours setting up, monitoring, adjusting pressure/flow, and shutting down the pump machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a specialized pump machine, monitoring pressure gauges, adjusting valves, clearing clogs, and coordinating with a sprayer — tasks involving heavy equipment controls, dynamic judgment, and high-consequence interventions far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Assemble, install, or repair wiring, electrical or electronic components, pipe systems, plumbing, machinery, or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single repair or installation job (e.g., wiring a outlet, fitting a pipe joint, replacing a component). Median across varied subtasks is ~90 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for wiring/plumbing, tool use (wrenches, screwdrivers, crimpers), fault diagnosis, and navigation of confined/unstructured spaces. Current humanoids lack the precision, tool-use generalization, and diagnostic reasoning needed.

task: Clean service or seating areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one service or seating area (e.g. a dining section or counter area), including wiping surfaces, clearing debris, and resetting. ~20 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep, mop, and wipe flat surfaces reasonably well, but seating areas involve varied furniture arrangements, pushing/lifting chairs, clearing scattered items, and navigating tight spaces — challenging for current humanoids.

task: Measure distances from landmarks to identify exact installation sites for equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating and marking one equipment installation site using measuring tools from reference landmarks; a competent worker typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise spatial measurement (tape measure or laser), landmark identification, and accurate marking in varied environments. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use for precision measurement, fine marking, and generalized site interpretation.

task: Run wires between components and to outside cable systems, connecting them to wires from telephone poles or underground cable accesses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wiring a single installation/connection point (e.g., one premises drop or panel). A telecom technician typically spends 60–120 min routing, stripping, terminating, and testing wires.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing poles/ladders, navigating unstructured outdoor/indoor environments, fine dexterous wire stripping and termination, and safe high-voltage/telecom awareness. Far beyond current humanoid capability in real-world deployment.

task: Create signs to advertise store products or events.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing, printing/writing, and placing one sign for a product or event. A competent worker takes ~25 min including layout, printing, and posting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative graphic design (cognitive), operating a computer/printer, and fine placement. Current humanoids lack design software fluency, reliable computer use, and the judgment to produce acceptable signage autonomously.

task: Assemble electrical parts such as alternators, generators, starting devices, and switches, following schematic drawings and using hand, machine, and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one electrical unit (e.g., alternator or starter). A competent assembler typically takes 60–120 min depending on complexity; 90 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading schematics, precise multi-tool use (hand, machine, power tools), fine wire/connector manipulation, and quality verification. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-switching generalization in unstructured assembly contexts.

task: Operate machines or equipment to remove, package, store, or transport loads of waste materials.
