human_time_rationale: One instance = one load cycle (remove, package, store, or transport one load of waste). A competent operator typically completes this in ~45 min including setup, operation, and securing the load.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized heavy machinery (compactors, forklifts, trucks) in unstructured, outdoor/industrial environments. Humanoid robots cannot drive or operate such vehicles autonomously; terrain, controls, and safety demands are far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Inspect and test equipment to locate damage or worn parts and diagnose malfunctions, or read work orders or schematic drawings to determine required repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing one equipment unit. Competent technician spends ~30–60 min on visual check, functional tests, and reviewing work orders or schematics.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading schematics, interpreting work orders, running diagnostic tests, and recognizing subtle wear/damage across diverse equipment types. Robots lack reliable multi-modal diagnostic reasoning, schematic comprehension, and generalization across varied machinery.

task: Prepare slides of cell cultures following standard procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one slide batch from cell culture: fixation, staining, mounting, coverslipping. A competent lab tech takes ~20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterity (pipetting microliter volumes, handling fragile glass coverslips, precise chemical application), sterile technique, and sequential multi-step lab protocol. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Install and configure new equipment, including operating software or peripheral equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing and configuring one piece of equipment (e.g., a workstation, printer, or server). A competent IT tech typically takes 45–90 minutes end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires cable management, fine connector insertion, navigating GUIs/software, reading prompts, and adapting to varied hardware. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity for connectors, have no GUI interaction ability, and cannot generalize across diverse equipment configurations.

task: Slice fruits, vegetables, desserts, or meats for use in food service.
human_time_rationale: One instance = slicing one ingredient batch (e.g., one vegetable, fruit, or meat portion) for service; a competent prep cook takes ~5–12 min.
robot_success_rationale: Slicing requires precise grip force, blade control, and handling of deformable/irregular objects. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, food-safe tool-use, and consistent cutting accuracy needed for acceptable food-service quality.

task: Identify, monitor, or treat pregnancy-related problems such as hypertension, gestational diabetes, pre-term labor, or retarded fetal growth.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single clinical encounter (assessment, diagnosis, and treatment planning) for a pregnancy-related complication; a competent OB/GYN typically spends 30–60 minutes per visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced medical judgment, interpretation of labs/imaging, patient communication, and clinical decision-making. Far beyond any current general-purpose robot's cognitive or dexterous medical capability. No autonomous clinical deployment exists.

task: Perform basic screening procedures, such as pure tone screening, otoacoustic screening, immittance screening, and screening of ear canal status using otoscope.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient screened across all four modalities (pure tone, OAE, immittance, otoscopy). A competent audiologist or technician typically completes all in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise insertion of probes into ear canal, operating clinical audiological equipment, interpreting results, and safe patient interaction. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, medical tool-use, and patient-care generalization needed. No deployed system approaches this.

task: Provide routine physical health screenings to detect or monitor problems such as heart disease and diabetes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single routine screening appointment (vitals, blood draw, basic exam). A competent clinician/nurse typically completes this in ~20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (venipuncture, auscultation), clinical judgment, patient communication, and use of specialized medical instruments. Current humanoids lack the precision, trust certification, and adaptive patient interaction needed.

task: Apply weather sealing to array, building, or support mechanisms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sealing one array/structure section. Applying caulk, tape, or gaskets to joints/edges of a panel array or support frame takes a competent worker ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise caulk/sealant bead application along irregular outdoor joints, tool-use (caulk gun), and working at height or on varied surfaces. Current humanoids lack reliable fine tool control and generalization for consistent sealing quality outdoors.

task: Serve prepared foods, such as muffins, biscotti, or bagels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = serving a single prepared food item to a customer (e.g., plating/bagging a muffin, biscotti, or bagel). A competent worker completes this in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Grasping varied baked goods (irregular shapes, fragile surfaces) is moderately challenging. Robots can pick-and-place but risk crushing or dropping items; handling bags, tongs, or wrappers adds complexity. No customer interaction capability.

task: Create or modify impressions for earmolds and hearing aid shells.
human_time_rationale: One instance = creating/modifying one earmold impression per ear. A trained audiologist/hearing instrument specialist takes ~20–30 min including prep, material injection, cure wait, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity inside the ear canal, tactile sensitivity to avoid injury, precise syringe control, and clinical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities; safety risks make autonomous operation essentially infeasible.

task: Adjust working parts, such as fan belts, contacts, and springs, using hand tools and gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single working part (e.g., tensioning a fan belt or setting a contact gap). A competent mechanic takes ~15–25 min including access, measurement, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled hand-tool use, reading gauges, and working in tight, cluttered engine bays. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tool manipulation skill, and spatial reasoning needed for reliable autonomous adjustment of varied mechanical parts.

task: Operate machines to test functioning of parts or mechanisms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single part/mechanism on a machine: setup, operation, and observation of results. Typical cycle is 10–20 minutes for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct machine controls, operating them precisely, interpreting test results (gauges, sounds, outputs), and making pass/fail judgments. Current humanoids lack reliable generalization across varied industrial test setups and nuanced sensory interpretation.

task: Collect specimens from patients, using standard medical procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one specimen collection (e.g., a blood draw or swab). A competent phlebotomist/clinician typically takes 5–15 minutes including patient prep, collection, and labeling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (venipuncture, swabbing), safe patient interaction, sterile technique, real-time tactile/visual feedback on a living human, and regulatory compliance. Far beyond 2026 frontier humanoid capabilities.

task: Repair and operate battery-charging equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing a fault and repairing/operating a single battery charger unit; includes inspection, troubleshooting, part replacement, and test cycle. ~45 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine electrical diagnostics, tool use with connectors/wiring, interpreting charger status displays, and repair judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity, multimeter use, and fault-diagnosis reasoning for electrical equipment repair.

task: Scrape and clean units or parts, using cleaning solvents and equipment such as buffing wheels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one unit/part with solvent and buffing equipment. Typical part takes 15–25 min depending on size and contamination level.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping and maneuvering irregular parts, applying solvents with appropriate pressure/coverage, and operating a spinning buffing wheel safely — demanding dexterity, force control, and tool-use that current humanoids handle poorly in unstructured settings.

task: Assess birthing environments to ensure cleanliness, safety, and the availability of appropriate supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single birthing room/suite. A competent nurse or midwife checks cleanliness, equipment function, and supply inventory in ~20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced clinical judgment, sterility assessment, recognizing subtle safety hazards, and verifying specialized medical supplies. Current robots lack reliable perception for contamination, clinical context, and safe navigation in complex medical environments.

task: Repair or replace faulty equipment, such as defective and damaged telephones, wires, switching system components, and associated equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and repairing/replacing a single faulty telecom component (e.g., a defective phone, wire, or switching module). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fault diagnosis, fine dexterous manipulation of small connectors/wires, use of specialized tools (multimeters, crimpers, soldering), and contextual judgment. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Treat patients' symptoms with alternative health care methods such as herbs or hydrotherapy.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient consultation and treatment session. A competent alternative health practitioner typically spends ~30–60 min assessing symptoms, selecting remedies/methods, and administering treatment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced clinical reasoning, patient communication, individualized remedy selection, and safe hands-on care. Current robots lack the judgment, fine dexterity for herb preparation, and social/cognitive capacity for unstructured therapeutic caregiving.

task: Align, adjust, or calibrate equipment according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning/calibrating a single piece of equipment. Competent technician typically spends 20–45 min reading specs, making adjustments, and verifying results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading specs, fine dexterous adjustment (knobs, bolts, sensors), interpreting feedback instruments, and iterative correction. Current humanoids lack precision fine-motor control, instrument-reading integration, and domain-specific calibration know-how.

task: Remove and replace plug-in circuit equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one plug-in circuit component (e.g., breaker, relay, fuse block) and installing a replacement; includes identification, safe disconnection, seating new part, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise locating of clip/latch release, controlled force to unplug/reseat connectors, and confirmation of correct seating. Fine dexterity and variability in panel layouts challenge current humanoids significantly. Safety-critical context further lowers acceptable autonomy.

task: Test circuits and components of malfunctioning telecommunications equipment to isolate sources of malfunctions, using test meters, circuit diagrams, polarity probes, and other hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing a single malfunctioning telecom unit. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min tracing faults with test meters, diagrams, and probes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting circuit diagrams, precise probe placement on small PCB test points, reading analog/digital meters, and adaptive reasoning to isolate faults. Current humanoids lack fine dexterity, instrument literacy, and diagnostic cognition for this unstructured electrical troubleshooting.

task: Connect water heaters and storage tanks to power and water sources.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting one water heater/storage tank unit to both power and water supply lines, including pipe fittings, electrical hookup, and testing. Typically 1.5–2.5 hrs for a competent plumber/electrician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex multi-trade skills: threading/soldering pipes, making safe electrical connections, torquing fittings to spec, and leak/power testing. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and safety compliance capabilities.

task: Inspect and test electrical systems and equipment to locate and diagnose malfunctions, using visual inspections, testing devices, and computer software.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing a single electrical malfunction. A competent electrician typically spends 30–90 min on visual inspection, testing, and software diagnostics for one fault.
robot_success_rationale: Requires combining visual inspection, handheld test devices (multimeters, clamp meters), and interpreting diagnostic software — a multi-modal, highly unstructured cognitive-physical task far beyond 2026 frontier robot generalization.

task: Apply operation or identification tags or labels to system components, as required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying a single label/tag to one system component. A competent worker reads, positions, and affixes one label in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise peel-and-place of small labels, accurate reading/matching of component IDs, and alignment on varied surfaces/orientations. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity and text-matching perception for consistent label placement quality.

task: Clean cells, cell assemblies, glassware, leads, electrical connections, and battery poles, using scrapers, steam, water, emery cloths, power grinders, or acid.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one battery cell assembly (leads, poles, glassware). A competent technician spends ~20–30 min selecting and using appropriate tools (scraper, steam, emery cloth, grinder, acid).
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-tool selection and switching (scrapers, grinders, acid), fine dexterity on small terminals and glassware, chemical handling safety, and contextual judgment on cleaning method per surface. Far beyond current humanoid generalization.

task: Cut damaged sections of pipe with cutters, remove broken sections from ditches, and replace pipe sections, using pipe sleeves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting, removing, and replacing one damaged pipe section in a ditch. A competent pipefitter typically takes 45–75 minutes depending on pipe size and depth.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating pipe cutters, working in unstructured outdoor ditch terrain, heavy manipulation, and precise sleeve fitting. Current humanoids lack the tool-use, force control, and outdoor mobility for this multi-step task.

task: Activate photovoltaic (PV) systems to verify system functionality and conformity to performance expectations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = activating and verifying one PV system. Technician powers on inverters, checks monitoring panels, measures electrical output, and confirms performance against specs. Typically ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating breakers/disconnects, reading meters, interfacing with inverter displays, and interpreting electrical data against specs. Fine dexterous switch operation, specialized tool use, and complex multi-step electrical reasoning exceed current humanoid capabilities.

task: Ensure that repaired sewer line joints are tightly sealed before backfilling begins.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting joints on one repaired sewer line segment. Worker visually inspects, probes, and confirms seal integrity; roughly 15 min per segment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires descending into or navigating a confined trench, identifying sealant integrity under poor lighting, applying tactile or pressure testing, and making quality judgments — all beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability in unstructured outdoor excavation sites.

task: Assemble machines, and bolt, weld, rivet, or otherwise fasten them to foundation or other structures, using hand tools and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and fastening a single machine to its foundation. A competent millwright/assembler typically takes 1–3 hours depending on machine complexity; 2 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force torquing, welding, riveting, and precise alignment on large/heavy industrial structures. Current humanoids lack the force output, tool-change capability, welding skill, and structural reasoning needed for this complex multi-step industrial task.

task: Stack finished packaged items, or wrap protective material around each item, and pack the items in cartons or containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = packing one carton with items (wrapping + stacking + sealing). A skilled warehouse packer completes this in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place regular items (~70–80%) but wrapping protective material requires high dexterity and adaptability. Varied item shapes, flexible materials, and tight packing configurations push success down significantly for current humanoids.

task: Conduct ongoing prenatal health assessments, tracking changes in physical and emotional health.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single prenatal assessment visit covering physical checks (BP, weight, fundal height) and emotional health screening; typical midwife/OB appointment runs 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced clinical judgment, empathetic communication, auscultation with a Doppler, emotional assessment, and longitudinal tracking. Current humanoids lack medical instrumentation, clinical reasoning, and safe patient interaction for this context.

task: Remove and replace defective parts such as coil leads, carbon brushes, and wires, using soldering equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing and replacing one defective component (e.g., a coil lead, carbon brush, or wire) including desoldering, fitting the new part, and resoldering. A competent electrician/motor repair tech takes ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor dexterity for soldering iron control, desoldering, and fitting tiny components (brushes, leads). Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter precision, thermal control, and tool handling needed for quality solder joints. Near-zero success.

task: Prepare and analyze samples, such as Papanicolaou (PAP) smear body fluids and fine needle aspirations (FNAs), to detect abnormal conditions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and analyzing a single cytology sample (e.g., one PAP smear). Includes slide prep, staining, and microscopic screening — typically 20–30 min for a trained cytotechnologist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-fine dexterity for slide prep, precise staining protocols, and expert-level microscopic visual interpretation of cellular morphology — well beyond current robot perception, dexterity, and diagnostic reasoning capabilities.

task: Test battery charges, and replace or recharge batteries as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one battery and performing the appropriate action (replace or recharge). Competent worker uses a multimeter/tester, reads result, and swaps or connects charger in ~5 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (multimeter probes), reading analog/digital displays, interpreting variable battery types, and connecting chargers or swapping batteries — highly variable, unstructured steps beyond current robot generalization.

task: Hammer out dents and twists in tools and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restoring one dented/twisted tool or piece of equipment. A competent metalworker takes ~15–35 min depending on severity, averaging ~25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled hammer strikes, tactile/visual feedback to judge metal deformation, and skilled adaptive technique. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and contact-force control. No deployed system approaches this.

task: Install required labels on solar system components and hardware.
human_time_rationale: One instance = labeling all required components on one solar installation (panels, inverter, disconnects, conduit, breakers). A competent electrician takes ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading and selecting correct regulatory labels, precise placement on varied surfaces/orientations, navigating tight electrical enclosures, and fine peel-and-stick dexterity. Current humanoids lack reliable label selection, fine manipulation, and compliance verification.

task: Load and arrange glass or mirrors onto delivery trucks, using suction cups or cranes to lift glass.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading and arranging one truck's worth of glass/mirrors, including rigging, lifting, and securing. Skilled workers typically take 30–60 min per load.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating cranes or suction-cup rigs, handling large fragile glass sheets, navigating loading docks, and securing loads safely. Current humanoids lack crane operation, heavy-lift tool use, and precision glass-handling at this scale.

task: Weigh materials or products and record weight or other production data on tags or labels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = weigh a single item/batch, read the scale, and record/attach the label or tag. A competent worker does this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot must grasp varied items, place on scale, read display (perception), then write/print and attach a label — multi-step chain with dexterous labeling and legible recording steps where current humanoids remain unreliable.

task: Adjust safety controls, counterweights, door mechanisms, and components such as valves, ratchets, seals, and brake linings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full adjustment cycle across safety controls, counterweights, doors, valves, ratchets, seals, and brake linings on a single machine/unit. A skilled mechanic typically needs 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of diverse, tightly-toleranced mechanical components (valves, seals, brake linings), force-calibrated adjustments, and diagnostic judgment. Far beyond reliable current humanoid capability; no commercial deployment exists for this complexity.

task: Cut and form insulation, and insert insulation into armature, rotor, or stator slots.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and inserting insulation for one armature/rotor/stator; a skilled technician takes roughly 10–20 min depending on slot count and part size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise cutting of flexible insulation material, forming it to shape, and inserting thin sheets into narrow, tight slots — extreme fine dexterity with compliant materials. Well beyond current humanoid manipulation capability.

task: Withdraw cables from pipes and examine them for evidence of mud, roots, grease, and other deposits indicating broken or clogged sewer lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = withdrawing a cable from one pipe run and inspecting it. A skilled sewer technician typically takes 15–35 min per pipe section including retrieval, visual/tactile inspection, and noting findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a cable drum or rod machine (specialized tool), navigating constrained/dirty manholes, and interpreting ambiguous deposit evidence. Humanoids lack reliable tool-interfacing, confined-space mobility, and domain-specific perception for this workflow.

task: Demonstrate system functionality and performance, including start-up, shut-down, normal operation, and emergency or bypass operations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full system demonstration covering startup, shutdown, normal ops, and emergency/bypass. A competent technician typically needs ~45 min to walk through all four phases thoroughly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires understanding system-specific procedures, operating control panels/HMIs, interpreting indicator states, and executing emergency protocols. Current humanoids lack the generalized procedural knowledge, tool-use reliability, and situational judgment this demands.

task: Arrange delivery or storage of goods at destinations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = arranging delivery/storage for a single shipment/order, including coordination, documentation, and communication with carriers or warehouse staff.
robot_success_rationale: Task is primarily logistical coordination — phone calls, emails, scheduling, negotiation — not physical manipulation. Humanoid robots lack the communication, planning, and system-integration capabilities needed for autonomous logistics coordination.

task: Assess sites to determine suitability for solar equipment, using equipment such as tape measures, compasses, and computer software.
human_time_rationale: One site assessment visit: measuring dimensions, checking orientation/shading with compass, photographing, and entering data into software — typically 1–2 hours for a residential or small commercial site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor mobility on varied terrain, precise tool use (tape measure, compass), judgment about shading/obstructions, and running/interpreting specialized software — well beyond current autonomous humanoid generalization and decision-making capability.

task: Measure, weigh, and count products and materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring, weighing, and counting a single batch/lot of products or materials at a workstation; ~3 min for a competent worker using standard tools.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place items onto scales and use vision for counting, but varied product shapes, small/irregular items, reading analog instruments, and coordinating multiple measurement tools challenge reliable autonomous execution.

task: Sort, grade, weigh, and inspect products, verifying and adjusting product weight or measurement to meet specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one product unit: sort, grade, weigh, inspect, and adjust. A line worker typically handles this in ~2–4 minutes per item/batch.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can weigh and do basic visual inspection, but grading quality, making nuanced sorting decisions, and physically adjusting irregular products requires perception, dexterity, and generalization that current humanoids handle inconsistently.

task: Sharpen tools such as saws, picks, shovels, screwdrivers, and scoops, either manually or by using bench grinders and emery wheels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sharpening a single tool (e.g., one shovel or screwdriver). A competent worker spends ~10–20 min depending on tool condition and method.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise angle control, consistent force, and tactile feedback across varied tool geometries. Operating bench grinders safely and adapting to each tool type far exceeds current humanoid dexterity and fine force-control capabilities.

task: Demonstrate use of solar and related equipment to customers or dealers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single demonstration session for a customer/dealer, covering key solar equipment features, Q&A, and hands-on walkthrough; typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires persuasive communication, dynamic Q&A, adaptive explanation, and social rapport-building — far beyond current robot language/social interaction capabilities in unscripted customer-facing contexts.

task: Provide comfort and relaxation measures for mothers in labor through interventions such as massage, breathing techniques, hydrotherapy, or music.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single continuous comfort/relaxation session for one laboring mother, covering massage, breathing coaching, hydrotherapy, or music. Typical active-labor support session lasts ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced empathetic communication, real-time pain-response reading, safe therapeutic touch on a vulnerable patient, hydrotherapy management, and adaptive coaching — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, social perception, and safety thresholds.

task: Locate and remove or repair circuit defects such as blown fuses or malfunctioning transistors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and repairing a single circuit defect (e.g., blown fuse or bad transistor). Includes testing, locating the fault, and replacing/repairing the component.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of tiny components, precise probing with multimeters, soldering, and diagnostic reasoning. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter precision, tool-use finesse, and electronic diagnostics capability needed.

task: Repair and rebuild defective mechanical parts in electric motors, generators, and related equipment, using hand tools and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/rebuilding a single defective component (e.g., replacing worn bearings, rewinding a coil, or resurfacing a commutator) in a motor or generator. A competent electrician/mechanic typically takes 1–3 hours per repair session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level dexterous tool use (hand tools, power tools), diagnostic judgment, fine manipulation of windings/bearings, and adaptation to highly variable defects. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and fine-motor capability.

task: Wash and treat surfaces with oil, turpentine, mildew remover, or other preparations, and sand rough spots to ensure that finishes will adhere properly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one average room's surfaces (walls/trim) before painting: washing, applying treatment chemicals, and sanding rough spots.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sequenced multi-step chemical application, precise sanding pressure on irregular surfaces, and tactile judgment of surface readiness. Current robots lack the nuanced force control, tool-switching dexterity, and surface-quality perception needed.

task: Remove and remake connections to change circuit layouts, following work orders or diagrams.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one work-order circuit layout change involving multiple connection changes. A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min per job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading diagrams, identifying correct terminals, fine dexterous manipulation of wires/connectors, and safety-aware sequencing. Current humanoids lack sufficient fine dexterity, diagram comprehension, and reliable fault-safe execution for electrical wiring tasks.

task: Explain and demonstrate the use of sports and training equipment, such as trampolines or weights.
human_time_rationale: One instance = demonstrating one piece of equipment to a group; includes setup, verbal explanation, and physical demonstration, typically 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated verbal explanation, dynamic physical demonstration (e.g., jumping on trampoline, controlled weightlifting with proper form), and audience interaction. Current humanoids lack the balance, dynamic locomotion, and speech-integrated instruction needed.

task: Measure, cut, and install frameworks and conduit to support and connect wiring, control panels, and junction boxes, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring, cutting, and installing a typical conduit run with framework for a single circuit segment. A competent electrician takes ~60–120 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measuring, conduit bending/cutting with specialized tools, threading, mounting hardware into walls/ceilings, and multi-step sequencing in unstructured environments—far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity and tool-use generalization.

task: Maintain or repair endoscopic equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing one endoscopic device. Skilled biomedical techs spend ~30–60 min on inspection, functional testing, cleaning channels, and minor repair per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to handle delicate fiber-optic scopes, tiny internal components, and precise leak testing. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter manipulation, specialized tooling, and domain-specific diagnostics needed.

task: Install heat exchangers and heat exchanger fluids according to installation manuals and schematics.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one heat exchanger: mounting, connecting fluid lines, and charging fluid per manual. A skilled mechanical technician typically takes 2–4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading schematics, precise torque on varied pipe fittings, handling fluids, routing hoses in cramped spaces, and cross-checking manuals — far beyond current humanoid generalization, dexterity, and tool-use capabilities.

task: Construct, test, maintain, and repair substation relay and control systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing a single relay/control panel in a substation. A competent electrician/relay technician typically spends 2–4 hours on construction, testing, or maintenance of one relay system unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly specialized electrical knowledge, precise wiring of high-voltage control circuits, use of relay test sets, and safety-critical judgment. Current humanoid robots lack the fine dexterity, domain-specific tool use, and safety protocols for live substation work.

task: Clean containers, materials, supplies, or work areas, using cleaning solutions and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one container/work area. A competent worker takes ~15 min to apply solution, scrub, rinse, and dry a typical surface or container.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can wipe flat surfaces adequately, but handling varied containers, applying/dispensing cleaning solutions, scrubbing irregular shapes, and judging cleanliness remain challenging for current humanoids.

task: Verify and adjust alignments and dimensions of parts, using gauges and tracing lathes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = verifying and adjusting one part; a skilled machinist typically spends 15–25 min setting up gauges, tracing, interpreting readings, and making fine adjustments.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool manipulation (gauges, tracing lathe controls), precise tactile/force feedback, and interpretation of subtle measurement readings. Current humanoids lack the precision, specialized tool-use, and adaptive adjustment capability needed.

task: Reassemble and test equipment after repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reassembling and functional-testing one piece of equipment after a repair. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min across mechanical reassembly, fastening, reconnecting wiring/hoses, and running a functional test.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse dexterous manipulation (fasteners, connectors, cables), tool use, sequenced assembly logic, and functional test interpretation across highly varied equipment types. Far beyond reliable autonomous capability of 2026 humanoids.

task: Install circulating pumps using pipe, fittings, soldering equipment, electrical supplies, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single circulating pump with pipe, fittings, soldering, and electrical hookup. A competent plumber/HVAC tech typically takes 1.5–2.5 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires soldering copper pipe (extreme fine dexterous heat work), threading/fitting pipes in tight mechanical spaces, and electrical wiring — all multi-step, tool-heavy, high-precision tasks far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Inspect equipment on a regular basis to ensure proper functioning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one equipment inspection round. A competent worker checks key components, observes operation, and notes issues; ~20 min is typical for a standard piece of industrial/facility equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to equipment and use cameras/sensors for visual checks, but interpreting anomalies, checking fluid levels, testing mechanical feel, and generalizing across varied equipment types remains unreliable for autonomous pass/fail judgments.

task: Dig holes or trenches as necessary for equipment installation and access.
human_time_rationale: One instance = digging one trench or hole for equipment installation. A competent worker using hand tools or compact excavator typically takes 60–120 minutes depending on size and soil.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating shovels, backhoes, or excavators in unstructured outdoor terrain. Humanoid robots lack the strength, tool-operation capability, and outdoor terrain robustness needed for sustained ground excavation work.

task: Provide presentation and product demonstration support during the introduction of new products and services to field staff and customers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single product demo/presentation session for a group; a competent presenter typically spends ~90 min including setup, delivery, and live demonstration.
robot_success_rationale: Task demands adaptive verbal communication, persuasion, audience reading, and live Q&A — all far beyond current humanoid capabilities. No physical manipulation advantage compensates for the cognitive/social requirements.

task: Obtain, move, and sort products, materials, containers, and orders, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pick/move/sort cycle for a batch of items (e.g., a pallet or order). A competent warehouse worker completes this in ~15 minutes on average.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle structured pick-and-place moderately well, but varied containers, hand-tool use (hooks, dollies, pry bars), and sorting logic across unstructured environments challenge perception, dexterity, and generalization significantly.

task: Prepare or serve menu items, such as sandwiches or salads.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and plating a single menu item (e.g., one sandwich or salad). A skilled worker assembles ingredients and plates in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step dexterous manipulation (spreading, layering, portioning, plating) with varied, deformable ingredients in an unstructured kitchen. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity and food-handling generalization for full autonomous execution.

task: Clean switches and replace contact points, using vacuum hoses, solvents, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and replacing contact points on a single switch assembly. A competent technician takes ~30 min including vacuuming, solvent application, and hand-tool work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for small contact points, solvent handling, precise hand-tool use, and inspection judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterous precision and multi-tool coordination needed for electrical maintenance tasks like this.

task: Open and close switches to isolate defective relays, performing adjustments or repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = isolating one defective relay via switching, diagnosing, and performing adjustment/repair. A competent technician typically takes 15–25 minutes per relay.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying defective relays via electrical diagnostics, operating panel switches with precision, and performing fine adjustments/repairs. Current humanoids lack the diagnostic reasoning, tool-use precision, and domain-specific electrical expertise needed.

task: Inspect and test equipment and circuits to identify malfunctions or defects, using wiring diagrams and testing devices such as ohmmeters, voltmeters, or ammeters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting/testing one equipment unit or circuit. A competent technician spends ~30–60 min consulting wiring diagrams, probing test points, and interpreting meter readings to diagnose a fault.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading wiring diagrams (complex cognition), precisely attaching meter probes to small terminals (fine dexterity), interpreting readings contextually, and navigating varied electrical panels. Well beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capability.

task: Measure velocity, horsepower, revolutions per minute (rpm), amperage, circuitry, and voltage of units or parts to diagnose problems, using ammeters, voltmeters, wattmeters, and other testing devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing one unit/part. Technician attaches probes, reads velocity/RPM/voltage/amperage/circuitry, interprets results — roughly 20–30 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to attach probes to specific terminals, tool-switching across multiple instruments, contextual reasoning to interpret readings, and safe navigation of live electrical hazards — all beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Lift units or parts such as motors or generators, using cranes or chain hoists, or signal crane operators to lift heavy parts or subassemblies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rigging and lifting one heavy part (e.g., motor or generator) via crane/hoist, including attaching rigging, signaling/operating, and positioning. Typically 15–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating overhead cranes/chain hoists or signaling crane operators — specialized equipment a humanoid cannot drive/control. Rigging heavy industrial parts demands precise force judgment, specialized hardware, and safety-critical coordination beyond current robot capability.

task: Install copper or plastic plumbing using pipes, fittings, pipe cutters, acetylene torches, solder, wire brushes, sand cloths, flux, plastic pipe cleaners, or plastic glue.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single plumbing run/joint set (e.g., connecting fixtures in one room). A competent plumber typically spends ~45–90 minutes cutting, fitting, soldering/gluing, and testing one section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool-use (torch, pipe cutter, soldering), crawling into tight spaces, multi-step sequencing with hazardous materials (open flame), and strong generalization to unstructured environments — far beyond 2026 frontier robot capabilities.

task: Perform embalming duties, as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = embalming one body. A licensed embalmer typically takes 1.5–2.5 hours per case depending on condition, arterial/cavity work, and cosmetic finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires licensed expertise, extreme fine dexterity (arterial injection, cavity aspiration, incision/suturing), nuanced tissue assessment, chemical handling, and regulatory compliance. Far beyond any current general-purpose robot's capability.

task: Receive or usher people to their seats for services.
human_time_rationale: One instance = greeting and walking one person/group to their seat. Typical usher interaction takes 1–5 minutes including greeting, assessing seating needs, and escorting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires natural social interaction, reading crowd dynamics, understanding preferences, navigating crowds, and communicating warmly. Current robots lack reliable conversational social intelligence and graceful crowd navigation needed here.

task: Visually inspect and test photovoltaic (PV) modules or systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and testing a single residential PV system (~20–30 panels). A competent technician takes ~30–60 min for visual checks, electrical tests (IV curve, continuity), and logging results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires roof locomotion on angled, slippery surfaces (~30% mobility), precise probe placement for electrical tests, nuanced visual defect recognition (microcracks, delamination), and tool-use with test equipment — combined success very low.

task: Stock customer service stations with paper products or beverage preparation items.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restocking a single customer service station (e.g., napkins, cups, lids, stirrers, sugar packets). A competent worker takes ~5–10 min per station.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a semi-public space, identifying low-stock items, grasping varied small/flexible items (napkins, lids, packets), and placing them neatly in dispensers. Perception and fine manipulation of lightweight, deformable, or stacked items remain challenging for 2026 robots.

task: Attach identification labels to finished packaged items, or cut stencils and stencil information on containers, such as lot numbers or shipping destinations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = labeling or stenciling one packaged item. A competent worker applies a label or cuts/applies a stencil in ~1–2 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Peel-and-place labeling on flat, uniform surfaces is feasible (~60–80%), but stencil-cutting and precise label alignment on varied container shapes, plus legibility checks, reduce overall reliability. Generalization across container types is a key limitation.

task: Perform routine maintenance or repairs to restore solar thermal systems to baseline operating conditions.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance/repair visit on a solar thermal system — diagnosing issues, checking fluid levels, inspecting collectors, tightening fittings, and restoring baseline operation — typically takes a skilled technician 1–3 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rooftop access, fine dexterous work (valves, fittings, fluid handling), multi-step diagnosis, and specialized tools. Current humanoids lack the mobility, situational reasoning, and tool-use precision needed for unstructured outdoor/rooftop HVAC-class repairs.

task: Install photovoltaic (PV) systems in accordance with codes and standards, using drawings, schematics, and instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a standard residential rooftop PV system (6–10 panels). A competent 2-person crew typically takes 6–8 hours; median ~8 hrs for a single installer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rooftop locomotion on sloped/uneven surfaces, heavy panel lifting, precise electrical wiring, code-compliant torque specs, and interpreting schematics. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured outdoor/rooftop environments.

task: Place or pour products or materials into containers, using hand tools and equipment, or fill containers from spouts or chutes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling a single container (e.g., a box, bag, or bin). A competent worker handles grab, position, fill, and set-down in ~2–4 minutes depending on product type.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place and pouring are improving rapidly; robots handle structured bins well (~70–90%), but varied container shapes, spout alignment, and avoiding spills reduce reliability. Generalization across product types limits success.

task: Delineate aerial photographic detail, such as control points, hydrography, topography, and cultural features, using precision stereoplotting apparatus or drafting instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = delineating a single aerial photo sheet or stereo model. A skilled cartographer typically takes 1–3 hours to extract and draft all required features from one stereo model.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly precise optical instrument operation (stereoplotters), fine drafting dexterity, expert cartographic judgment, and complex visual interpretation. No current humanoid robot has these capabilities or tool interfaces.

task: Assemble solar modules, panels, or support structures, as specified.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one solar panel/module or one support structure section. A skilled installer typically takes 30–60 min per unit including mounting, wiring connections, and fastening.
robot_success_rationale: Solar assembly involves large/heavy panels, outdoor/rooftop terrain, precise rail alignment, torquing bolts to spec, and electrical connections. Current humanoids lack load capacity, outdoor mobility on slopes, and the multi-step tool use required.

task: Take out garbage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a full bin liner, tying it off, and carrying it to an outdoor receptacle. A competent adult completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping a soft, deformable bag, tying or removing it, navigating doors/steps to an outdoor bin, and lifting a lid — each step challenging. Door/outdoor terrain and deformable-object handling reduce reliability significantly.

task: Clean, disinfect, or calibrate scopes or other endoscopic instruments according to manufacturer recommendations and facility standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/disinfecting/calibrating one endoscope. Full cycle (pre-cleaning, leak testing, manual cleaning, HLD soak, rinse, drying, storage) typically takes 20–45 min per scope.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (threading brushes through tiny channels), precise chemical handling, leak testing, and strict protocol compliance. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation, specialized tooling, and validated sterility assurance needed.

task: Install flat-plat, evacuated glass, or concentrating solar collectors on mounting devices, using brackets or struts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one solar collector onto a mount. Skilled installer takes ~60–120 min per unit including positioning, bracket/strut attachment, alignment, and fastening.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rooftop locomotion on uneven/sloped outdoor surfaces, heavy panel lifting (20–50 kg), precise alignment, multi-step fastening with varied hardware, and tool use — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities in unstructured outdoor settings.

task: Load materials and products into package processing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a batch/cycle of materials into packaging equipment (e.g., filling a hopper, stacking carton blanks, loading film rolls). A competent worker completes this in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping varied packaging materials (film rolls, carton blanks, bulk product), aligning them precisely with machine feed points, and navigating industrial floor clutter. Current humanoids lack reliable fine alignment and force-feedback for machine-loading tasks in unstructured environments.

task: Transport packages to customers' vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = carrying one package from a pickup point (e.g. store entrance or staging area) to a customer's vehicle in the parking lot. Typical trip including handoff takes ~3–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/parking-lot navigation (uneven surfaces, moving vehicles, pedestrians), locating a specific customer vehicle, and safe handoff to a person — all challenging for current humanoids. Perception and generalization in dynamic outdoor environments remain weak.

task: Provide vision therapy and low-vision rehabilitation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single therapy/rehabilitation session with a patient. A typical vision therapy or low-vision rehab session runs 45–75 minutes with a trained specialist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, nuanced patient assessment, adaptive therapeutic dialogue, fine visual-function testing, and emotional attunement. Far beyond current robot generalization, perception of subtle patient responses, and dexterous tool use in a clinical context.

task: Provide patients undergoing eye surgeries, such as cataract and laser vision correction, with pre- and post-operative care.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full pre- and post-operative care session for one patient (assessment, instructions, eye drops, comfort, discharge guidance). Typically 30–60 min total per surgical visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine periocular dexterity (eye drops, lid handling), nuanced patient communication, clinical assessment (visual acuity, comfort), and adaptive judgment. Far beyond current humanoid capability in medical settings.

task: Transfer tools, parts, equipment, and supplies to and from work stations and other areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single transfer trip (picking up items from one location, moving to destination, placing them). Typical shop floor or warehouse run takes ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids can carry and navigate indoor flat terrain reasonably well, but varied item shapes, cluttered factory floors, handoffs, and locating correct stations in dynamic environments reduce reliability significantly.

task: Adjust surveying instruments to maintain their accuracy.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single surveying instrument (e.g., total station or level). A competent surveyor typically takes 20–40 minutes to check and calibrate one instrument.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of small adjustment screws, precise optical/electronic calibration routines, and domain-specific tool knowledge. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, specialized perception, and procedural accuracy this demands.

task: Assess collector sites to ensure structural integrity of potential mounting surfaces or the best orientation and tilt for solar collectors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing one collector site (e.g., a rooftop or ground area). A competent technician spends ~60–120 min measuring surfaces, checking structural integrity, and calculating optimal tilt/orientation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level structural assessment, solar angle calculations, rooftop navigation over varied/uneven surfaces, and tool use (inclinometers, load testers). Current humanoids lack the judgment, specialized sensing, and rooftop mobility for autonomous success.

task: Disconnect voltage regulators, bolts, and screws, and connect replacement regulators to high-voltage lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing one voltage regulator unit. Includes de-energizing, unbolting old regulator, disconnecting HV lines, mounting and connecting replacement. Skilled electrician takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working on energized or recently de-energized high-voltage electrical infrastructure, precise torque on HV line connections, safety-critical sequencing, and heavy component handling. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and safety certification capabilities.

task: Identify and resolve any deficiencies in photovoltaic (PV) system installation or materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and resolving deficiencies in a single residential/small commercial PV system installation. A competent solar technician typically spends ~90 min diagnosing issues and making corrections.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rooftop locomotion on steep/sloped surfaces, fine electrical inspection (wiring, connectors, torque specs), domain-specific tool use (multimeter, torque wrench), and code-based reasoning. Current humanoids lack rooftop mobility, specialized electrical diagnostics, and the generalization to resolve diverse PV deficiencies safely.

task: Turn meters on or off to establish or close service.
human_time_rationale: One instance = turning a utility meter on or off at a single address. Includes travel to meter, locating it, operating shutoff/switch, and verifying status. Typically 15–25 min per visit.
robot_success_rationale: Meters are outdoors, often in varied/cluttered locations. Operating shutoff valves or breaker switches requires precise torque and tool use. Outdoor terrain, meter diversity, and safety-critical nature make autonomous success very unlikely with 2026 humanoids.

task: Arrange and conduct sports-related activities, such as training camps, skill-improvement courses, clinics, and pre-season try-outs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = organizing and running a single sports activity session (e.g., a clinic or training camp day). Includes setup, instruction, and coordination; typically 3–5 hours per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level planning, social coordination, verbal instruction, athlete assessment, and adaptive coaching — far beyond current robot capabilities in perception, language-driven leadership, and unstructured human interaction.

task: Plan and conduct ground surveys designed to establish baselines, elevations, and other geodetic measurements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single ground survey session establishing baselines/elevations for a typical site. A competent surveyor typically spends ~3–4 hours setting up instruments, traversing the site, recording measurements, and verifying data.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized geodetic instruments (total stations, GPS rovers, levels), traversing varied outdoor terrain, interpreting survey control points, and exercising professional judgment. No current humanoid robot can operate survey equipment autonomously to professional standards.

task: Select, acquire, store, and issue equipment and other materials as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single requisition cycle (select, acquire, store, and issue one batch of materials). A competent storeroom worker typically takes ~30 min per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires cognitive decision-making (selecting/acquiring supplies), navigating suppliers or procurement systems, and issuing to personnel — far beyond physical pick-and-place. Current humanoids lack procurement reasoning, vendor interaction, and end-to-end workflow autonomy.

task: Cut openings and drill holes for fixtures, outlet boxes, and fuse holders, using electric drills and routers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting/drilling openings for a single fixture or outlet box. A competent electrician/carpenter typically marks, drills, and routes one opening in ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, marking, tool switching (drill/router), sustained force control, and working in confined or vertical surfaces. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use dexterity, accurate hole placement, and safe power-tool operation in unstructured environments.

task: Direct preparations and shipment of bodies for out-of-state burial.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and shipping one body: embalming/dressing, casketing, completing interstate transit paperwork, and coordinating pickup. Typically 2–4 hours total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate, sensitive manipulation of human remains, complex multi-step embalming procedures, legal paperwork/coordination, and logistics involving vehicles and third parties — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Lubricate wearing surfaces of mechanical parts, using oils or other lubricants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lubricating the wearing surfaces of a single mechanical assembly (e.g., a bearing, chain, or joint). A competent worker locates the point, selects lubricant, and applies it in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying small, often recessed lubrication points, precise tool-use (grease gun, oil can), and controlled application force/quantity. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity and generalization for varied mechanical parts in unstructured environments.

task: Assemble, line, and pad cartons, crates, and containers, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one carton/crate/container fully assembled, lined, and padded. A competent warehouse worker takes ~5–10 min depending on size and padding complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step dexterous manipulation: folding cardboard, inserting liners, placing padding materials with correct fit. Tool use and varied materials challenge current humanoids; no reliable generalization across container types.

task: Install new fuses, electrical cables, or power sources as required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing/installing fuses, cables, or a power source in one device/panel. Includes diagnosis, component swap, and basic verification; ~20–40 min for a competent electrician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation (seating fuses, routing/stripping cables, securing terminals), hazard awareness (live voltage), fault diagnosis, and tool use in cramped/unstructured panels — well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Prescribe, supply, fit and adjust eyeglasses, contact lenses, and other vision aids.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full patient visit: refraction exam, prescription, selection, fitting, and adjustment of eyewear. A competent optometrist/optician takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires licensed clinical judgment, precise refractive diagnostics, patient communication, fine dexterous frame adjustment, and contact lens fitting — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in an unstructured clinical setting.

task: Demonstrate the use of retail equipment, such as espresso machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single demonstration of a retail espresso machine to a customer or trainee, covering key features and pulling a shot. Typically 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (tamping, portafilter locking, steaming milk), complex multi-step sequencing, verbal explanation, and adapting to audience questions. Current humanoids lack reliable speech-guided demo and fine barista manipulation.

task: Provide information on funeral service options, products, or merchandise, and maintain a casket display area.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single client consultation covering service/product options plus one pass of casket display maintenance; a competent funeral director spends ~30–60 min combined.
robot_success_rationale: Task demands nuanced grief counseling, personalized verbal consultation, and careful repositioning of heavy/delicate caskets in a display room. Current robots lack social/emotional communication skills and reliable heavy-object repositioning in unstructured showroom layouts.

task: Install, inspect and test electric meters, relays, and power sources to detect causes of malfunctions and inaccuracies, using hand tools and testing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing, inspecting, and testing a single meter/relay/power source unit. A skilled electrician typically takes 45–75 min per unit including diagnostics.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use, reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting faults, connecting terminals safely under live voltage, and adaptive troubleshooting — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured field settings.

task: Treat lower urinary tract dysfunctions using equipment such as diathermy machines, catheters, cystoscopes, or radium emanation tubes.
human_time_rationale: One clinical treatment session including patient prep, procedure execution, and post-procedure steps. Median across catheterization (~10 min) to cystoscopy/diathermy (~60 min).
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced medical dexterity, sterile technique, real-time clinical judgment, patient communication, and operation of specialized medical equipment inside the human body. Far beyond any current general-purpose robot's capabilities.

task: Install or use instruments such as lasers, grade rods, or transit levels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and using one instrument (laser, grade rod, transit level) for a single survey or layout operation; a competent surveyor typically takes 15–35 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise leveling, tripod setup, fine optical/laser alignment, and reading/recording measurements outdoors on uneven terrain. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation, tool-specific knowledge, and reliable outdoor mobility for this specialized workflow.

task: Trace and tag meters or house lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tracing and tagging a single meter or house line run. A competent utility/electrician worker typically spends ~30 min locating, tracing, and labeling one line.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading meters, identifying unlabeled lines in cluttered utility spaces, precise tagging, and domain-specific tool use. Current robots lack reliable perception for wire tracing, labeling dexterity, and generalization in unstructured utility environments.

task: Measure, examine, or test completed units to check for defects and ensure conformance to specifications, using precision instruments, such as micrometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single completed unit with precision instruments (e.g., micrometer checks on key dimensions). A competent QC technician typically takes 5–15 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle and position parts, but reliably wielding a handheld micrometer with the fine dexterity and consistent grip force required, reading analog/digital gauges, and making pass/fail judgments across varied part geometries exceeds current humanoid capability reliably.

task: Take samples to assess the amount and quality of oil, the depth at which resources lie, and the equipment needed to properly extract them.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full sampling session at a single site, including setup, drilling/coring, sample collection, and initial field assessment. Typically a full workday for a crew.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy drilling machinery, interpreting geological data, and making expert assessments in rugged outdoor terrain. Humanoid robots cannot operate drilling rigs, vehicles, or specialized extraction equipment autonomously.

task: Install active solar systems, including solar collectors, concentrators, pumps, or fans.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full residential active solar system install (collectors, pump/fan, piping, controls). A skilled crew typically takes 3–5 hours; median ~4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rooftop access on uneven surfaces, heavy panel lifting, precise pipe fitting, electrical hookups, and multi-step sequencing across diverse environments. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured outdoor/rooftop settings.

task: Reface, ream, and polish commutators and machine parts to specified tolerances, using machine tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = refacing, reaming, and polishing one commutator/machine part to tolerance. A skilled machinist typically takes 30–60 min per part including setup, machining, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a lathe or specialized machine tools, making precision adjustments to tight tolerances, and using hand-finishing tools — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and metrology capabilities. No viable 2026 deployment.

task: Travel over photographed areas to observe, identify, record, and verify all relevant features.
human_time_rationale: One instance = surveying a single photographed area on foot. A competent field surveyor typically spends 1–3 hours traversing, observing, and logging all relevant features across a typical mapped zone.
robot_success_rationale: Requires autonomous outdoor navigation over varied/unstructured terrain, cross-referencing aerial imagery, identifying and classifying diverse features, and producing verified records — well beyond current humanoid generalization and outdoor mobility reliability.

task: Weld, braze, or solder electrical connections.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one electrical connection joined. A skilled technician spends ~2–5 min on prep, heating, applying filler, and inspecting the joint.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise thermal control, fine dexterous tool-use (soldering iron/torch), and quality inspection of tiny joints. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter dexterity, heat management, and reliable visual inspection needed for acceptable electrical connections.

task: Repair leaks in valve seats or bellows of automotive heater thermostats, using soft solder, flux, and acetylene torches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing one thermostat. A skilled technician must disassemble, identify the leak, apply flux and solder with a torch, and verify the seal — roughly 20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of tiny valve seats/bellows, precise torch control, flux application, and soldering judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and thermal/process control capabilities. No known demo at this fidelity.

task: Mount and install meters and other electric equipment such as time clocks, transformers, and circuit breakers, using electricians' hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting and wiring a single piece of electrical equipment (e.g., a meter, time clock, or circuit breaker). A competent electrician takes ~30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous tool use (screwdrivers, wire strippers, torque wrenches) in cramped panel enclosures, terminal wiring, and safety-critical electrical connections. Far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity and generalization limits.

task: Mount raceways and conduits and fasten wires to wood framing, using staplers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one typical wall/run (~10–15 ft) of raceway/conduit mounting and wire stapling. A competent electrician takes ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool use (staple gun), navigating tight framing cavities, routing flexible conduit, and applying consistent force at varied angles. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-handling precision, and task-specific generalization needed.

task: Remove foreign bodies from the eye.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a foreign body from one eye. A trained clinician inspects, irrigates, and/or uses instruments to extract the object; typically 10–20 minutes in a clinical setting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme sub-millimeter dexterity on a live, sensitive, moving eye. Far beyond current humanoid robot fine-motor capability; any slip risks permanent injury. Safety constraints alone make autonomous execution implausible.

task: Cut, bend, or weld steel pieces, using metal shears, torches, or welding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting, bending, or welding a single steel piece/joint. A competent metalworker completes a typical cut, bend, or weld in ~20–30 minutes including setup and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Welding/torch operation requires extreme precision, heat tolerance, and specialized tool handling far beyond current humanoid dexterity. Sparks, high temps, and variable workpiece geometry pose major perception and safety barriers. Industrial welding robots exist but are fixed-arm, task-specific, not general-purpose humanoids.

task: Repair, replace, and clean equipment and components such as circuit breakers, brushes, and commutators.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing a single piece of electrical equipment (e.g., replacing a circuit breaker or cleaning commutator brushes). A competent electrician typically takes 30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of small electrical components, precise torque, diagnostic judgment, and tool use in tight panel spaces. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control, electrical safety compliance, and generalization needed for such tasks.

task: Disassemble broken or defective equipment to facilitate repair and reassemble equipment when repairs are complete.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassemble one piece of equipment, await repair, then reassemble. Typical equipment (e.g. a motor, appliance, or machine component) takes ~20–30 min each way for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Disassembly/reassembly of arbitrary broken equipment demands high dexterity, tool use, diagnostic judgment, and generalization across vastly different form factors — well beyond current humanoid robots' reliable capability.

task: Conduct tests or studies such as electroencephalography (EEG), polysomnography (PSG), nerve conduction studies (NCS), electromyography (EMG), and intraoperative monitoring (IOM).
human_time_rationale: One EEG session: electrode placement, calibration, recording, and removal for a single patient typically takes 30–60 min for a competent neurodiagnostic technologist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise placement of electrodes on a human scalp, patient communication/consent, clinical judgment, gel application, and real-time waveform interpretation. Far beyond current robot dexterity and medical safety standards.

task: Test oil in circuit breakers and transformers for dielectric strength, refilling oil periodically.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing and refilling oil on one breaker or transformer; includes sampling, running dielectric test, interpreting results, and refilling — typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized electrical safety protocols, precise fluid sampling/handling with dielectric test equipment, high-voltage hazard management, and judgment-based interpretation. No current humanoid has demonstrated this capability or relevant tool-use chain.

task: Test insulators and bushings of equipment by inducing voltage across insulation, testing current, and calculating insulation loss.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one insulator/bushing unit: connect test leads, apply voltage, record current, calculate insulation loss. Typically 20–40 min per unit for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise connection of high-voltage test leads to specific terminals, operating specialized electrical test equipment (e.g., Doble/Megger), reading instrument outputs, and performing calculations. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tool-use, and domain-specific equipment-interfacing needed for safe, autonomous HV testing.

task: Install fixtures, outlets, terminal boards, switches, and wall boxes, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single fixture/outlet/switch unit. A competent electrician takes ~15–25 min per unit including mounting, wiring, and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to strip/connect wires, insert terminals, and fasten screws in tight wall boxes. Poor generalization across fixture types, confined spaces, and precise wire seating make this extremely difficult for current humanoids.

task: Direct aerial surveys of specified geographical areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one aerial survey flight over a specified area. Includes pre-flight planning, flying the survey route, and post-flight wrap-up; typical mission is ~2–3 hours total.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot pilot aircraft. L4 autonomous drones exist but are narrow/task-specific systems, not general-purpose mobile manipulators. No frontier humanoid can operate cockpit controls or fly a survey aircraft in 2026.

task: Splice wires with knives or cutting pliers, and solder connections to fixtures, outlets, and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = splicing and soldering a single connection point. A skilled electrician strips, joins, and solders one splice/connection in ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool-use (knife, cutting pliers, soldering iron) on small, deformable wires with precise force control, heat management, and inspection. Far beyond reliable humanoid capability in 2026.

task: Order, receive, or stock supplies or retail products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single restocking or supply order cycle (checking stock, placing order, or physically stocking a shelf). Competent worker completes this in ~30 min on average.
robot_success_rationale: Stocking shelves with varied products requires perception, navigation, and dexterous placement across diverse items and layouts. Ordering requires cognitive/IT system access. Current humanoids lack reliable generalization for full autonomous cycles.

task: Remove completed or defective products or materials, placing them on moving equipment, such as conveyors, or in specified areas, such as loading docks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a single product/item and placing it on a conveyor or in a designated area. A competent worker does this in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place on conveyors or loading docks is within current humanoid capability for regular-shaped items, but varied object shapes, defective/irregular items, and unstructured placement zones reduce reliability significantly.

task: Vary air pressure flowing into regulators and turn handles to assess functioning of valves and pistons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one valve/piston assembly. A competent technician spends ~5–10 min adjusting regulator pressure, turning handles, and observing/recording results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torque-controlled handle turning, reading analog gauges, and interpreting subtle pneumatic feedback. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-force-controlled manipulation of arbitrary valve handles and multi-step diagnostic reasoning in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Adjust, repair, or replace defective wiring and relays in ignition, lighting, air-conditioning, and safety control systems, using electrician's tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and repairing a single defective wiring/relay fault in a vehicle system. A competent auto electrician typically takes 45–90 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use in confined, cluttered vehicle interiors; tracing and testing wiring harnesses; diagnosing faults; and precise connector/relay manipulation. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured, high-stakes electrical work.

task: Inspect electrical connections, wiring, relays, charging resistance boxes, and storage batteries, following wiring diagrams.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full inspection of one electrical system (connections, wiring, relays, resistance boxes, batteries) following wiring diagrams; a competent technician typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading wiring diagrams, identifying diverse components, probing terminals with test equipment, and interpreting nuanced faults. Current robots lack reliable fine dexterity, electrical tool-use, and schematic-driven reasoning for autonomous pass/fail judgments.

task: Survey bodies of water to determine navigable channels and to secure data for construction of breakwaters, piers, and other marine structures.
