human_time_rationale: One survey instance = a full hydrographic survey of a defined water body or section; a competent marine surveyor typically spends a full workday (6–8 hrs) collecting, validating, and logging data.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a survey vessel, deploying sonar/ADCP equipment, navigating open water, and interpreting complex bathymetric data. Humanoid robots cannot operate watercraft or marine instruments autonomously at required fidelity in 2026.

task: Test valves and regulators for leaks and accurate temperature and pressure settings, using precision testing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single valve or regulator unit: connecting test equipment, pressurizing, checking for leaks, verifying temp/pressure settings, and logging results. Typically 15–35 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise connection of test equipment to varied valve fittings, reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting subtle leak indicators, and adjusting settings — demanding fine dexterity, tool-use generalization, and nuanced sensor interpretation beyond 2026 frontier robot capability.

task: Test equipment for overheating, using speed gauges and thermometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one piece of equipment; technician attaches/positions gauges and thermometers, runs checks, and logs results — roughly 10 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool attachment (thermometers, speed gauges) to varied equipment, interpreting analog/digital readings, and diagnosing overheating — beyond reliable autonomous capability of 2026 humanoids; perception and fine dexterity with instruments are limiting factors.

task: Conduct chemical analyses of substances such as acids, alcohols, and enzymes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one chemical analysis (e.g., titration, spectrophotometry, or enzyme assay). A competent lab technician typically takes 30–90 minutes end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting, operating lab instruments (spectrophotometers, centrifuges), interpreting readings, and adapting to varied protocols. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, sterile technique, and domain-specific tool integration needed for autonomous lab chemistry.

task: Steam-clean polishing and buffing wheels to remove abrasives and bonding materials, and spray, brush, or recoat surfaces as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and recoating one polishing/buffing wheel. Steam-cleaning, inspecting, and recoating a single wheel takes ~10–15 min for a skilled worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling a high-pressure steam cleaner, precisely spraying/brushing abrasive compounds onto an irregular wheel surface, and judging coating quality — demanding tool use, fine dexterity, and process judgment beyond 2026 frontier robots.

task: Monitor maternal condition during labor by checking vital signs, monitoring uterine contractions, or performing physical examinations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring assessment round (vitals check, contraction timing, physical exam) for one patient; a skilled nurse/midwife completes this in ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical-grade sensor placement, intimate physical examination (cervical checks), real-time interpretation of fetal/maternal data, and safe interaction with a vulnerable patient — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, medical tool-use, and clinical reasoning capabilities.

task: Demonstrate equipment functions and features to machine operators.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single demonstration session for one piece of equipment to a group of operators, covering key functions and features. Typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires natural language explanation, adaptive teaching, reading operator comprehension, and complex tool/machine interaction. Current humanoids lack instructional communication, social cognition, and reliable operation of arbitrary industrial machinery.

task: Rewind coils on cores in slots, or make replacement coils, using coil-winding machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = winding/rewinding one coil on a core; a skilled coil winder typically takes 30–60 min depending on wire gauge, number of turns, and core geometry.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise wire tensioning, guiding fine wire through slots, operating specialized coil-winding machinery, and quality judgment. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tool-use integration, and machine-operation capability needed.

task: Check spa equipment to ensure proper functioning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full equipment check of one spa unit (jets, pumps, heater, filters, control panel). A competent technician completes a routine inspection in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading gauges, operating control panels, interpreting subtle sensory cues (vibration, sound, water flow), navigating wet/tight spaces, and making diagnostic judgments — well beyond current robot generalization and perception capabilities.

task: Splice and connect cables from meters or current transformers to pull boxes or switchboards, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one cable run connection (strip, splice, terminate, secure). A competent electrician typically takes 30–60 min per connection set.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of cables, hand tools, and connectors in confined electrical enclosures; precise force control for stripping and terminating wires; spatial reasoning in tight, unstructured spaces far exceeds current humanoid robot capability.

task: Clean, lubricate, or adjust parts, equipment, or machinery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance cycle (clean, lube, or adjust one piece of equipment). Competent worker typically spends 20–40 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool selection, applying lubricants precisely, making calibrated adjustments, and navigating varied machinery. Fine dexterity, contextual judgment, and tool-use generalization remain major bottlenecks for 2026 robots.

task: Examine systems to locate problems, such as loose connections or broken insulation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single system/panel/circuit run. A competent technician visually traces connections, wiggles wires, and checks insulation in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual perception of subtle defects (hairline cracks, slight discoloration, loose terminals), dexterous probing of tight spaces, and semantic fault diagnosis. Current robots lack reliable fine-detail inspection and diagnostic reasoning in unstructured electrical environments.

task: Perform routine photovoltaic (PV) system maintenance on modules, arrays, batteries, power conditioning equipment, safety systems, structural systems, weather sealing, or balance of systems equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = routine maintenance visit on a small PV system; technician inspects modules, cleans panels, checks wiring/BOS, tests safety equipment — typically ~90 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rooftop locomotion on sloped surfaces, fine electrical connections, multi-system diagnostics, tool use across diverse equipment, and safety judgment. Current humanoids lack roof traversal, electrical testing competence, and generalization across varied PV hardware.

task: Train clients to use adaptive equipment, such as large print, reading stands, lamps, writing implements, software, and electronic devices.
human_time_rationale: One training session with a client on a specific adaptive equipment item; includes demonstration, guided practice, and feedback. Typical occupational/vision therapy session runs 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires adaptive communication, real-time assessment of client comprehension, dynamic instruction tailored to individual disability and learning pace, emotional sensitivity, and nuanced tool handling — far beyond current robot generalization and social cognition capabilities.

task: Make adjustments to meter components, such as setscrews or timing mechanisms, so that they conform to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single meter's components (setscrews, timing mechanisms) to spec. A skilled technician typically takes 15–25 minutes per meter including inspection and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of tiny setscrews and precision timing mechanisms, reading tolerances, and verifying conformance to spec. Current robots lack the fine motor control, tactile feedback, and metrology judgment needed for this precision calibration work.

task: Mount and fasten control panels, door and window contacts, sensors, or video cameras, and attach electrical and telephone wiring to connect components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting and wiring a single security component (e.g., one camera or contact sensor). A competent installer takes ~30–60 min including drilling, fastening, routing, and connecting wires.
robot_success_rationale: Requires drilling into walls, routing wires through conduit, making precise electrical/telephone terminations, and working in tight/varied spaces. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and unstructured environment generalization.

task: Inspect buildings, equipment, or access points to determine security risks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single security inspection sweep of a building or facility zone, including checking doors, windows, equipment, and access points. A competent guard typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can patrol and detect obvious anomalies but struggle with nuanced risk assessment, identifying subtle tampering, operating in unstructured environments, and accessing all entry points. Judgment-heavy inspection remains difficult.

task: Repair or rebuild equipment such as starters, generators, distributors, or door controls, using electrician's tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/rebuilding one unit (e.g., a starter or generator). A competent auto/electrical technician typically takes 60–120 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-precision disassembly/reassembly, fine dexterity with varied hand tools, fault diagnosis, and component testing. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured repair contexts; multi-step reasoning and fine manipulation are severe bottlenecks.

task: Plan or conduct geological, geochemical, or geophysical field studies or surveys, sample collection, or drilling and testing programs used to collect data for research or application.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single field survey day (planning + travel + data collection + sampling). Competent geologist typically spends a full 8-hour field day per survey unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert geological judgment, unstructured outdoor terrain navigation, operating specialized drilling/sampling equipment, adaptive decision-making, and multi-day mission planning — far beyond 2026 humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Lay out materials such as puzzles, scissors and eating utensils for use in therapy, and clean and repair these tools after therapy sessions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setup + cleanup/repair for a single therapy session. Laying out materials, then cleaning and checking tools afterward typically takes 10–20 minutes combined.
robot_success_rationale: Grasping and placing varied objects (scissors, puzzle pieces, utensils) is partially feasible, but identifying damaged tools, performing minor repairs, and arranging items purposefully for therapy context requires nuanced perception, judgment, and fine manipulation that current humanoids struggle with.

task: Reassemble machines after the completion of repair or maintenance work.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reassembling one machine after repair. Median across machine types (e.g., motors, pumps, conveyors) a competent mechanic takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied fine dexterity (fasteners, gaskets, alignment), tool use, and task-specific knowledge of each machine's configuration. Current humanoids lack reliable generalized reassembly across diverse, real-world machinery with tight tolerances.

task: Disassemble and repair mechanical control devices or valves, such as regulators, thermostats, or hydrants, using power tools, hand tools, and cutting torches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling, repairing, and reassembling one mechanical control device (e.g., a valve or regulator). A competent technician typically takes 60–120 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity with diverse hand/power tools, use of cutting torches (open flame, gas control), fault diagnosis, and highly unstructured manipulation. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and tool-use capability.

task: Perform routine maintenance on park structures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = routine maintenance on a single park structure (e.g., a bench, picnic table, or play equipment). Includes inspection, tightening fasteners, minor repairs, and light cleaning. ~90 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Task spans highly varied outdoor terrain, unstructured structures, and mixed sub-tasks (inspection, tightening bolts, painting, replacing boards). Robots lack reliable outdoor locomotion on uneven ground, tool-switching, and judgment for damage assessment. Current humanoids far below required capability.

task: Investigate the composition, structure, or history of the Earth's crust through the collection, examination, measurement, or classification of soils, minerals, rocks, or fossil remains.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a field session collecting and classifying samples (soils, rocks, minerals, fossils). A competent geologist typically spends ~60–120 min per site visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level geological perception (mineral ID, stratigraphic context), unstructured outdoor mobility over rough terrain, delicate fossil extraction, and scientific judgment — far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Disassemble defective equipment so that repairs can be made, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling one piece of defective equipment (e.g., a motor, pump, or appliance) with hand tools to expose the fault; typical technician job takes 20–45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse hand-tool use (wrenches, screwdrivers, pliers), high dexterity for varied fasteners, and adaptive problem-solving on non-standard, damaged parts. Current humanoids lack reliable multi-tool manipulation and fault-driven disassembly planning.

task: Install module array interconnect wiring, implementing measures to disable arrays during installation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wiring one solar array section, including disabling the array, routing/connecting DC interconnect cables, verifying polarity, and restoring. Typically 60–120 min for a competent PV installer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rooftop/outdoor mobility on uneven surfaces, precise MC4/DC connector mating, torque-critical terminations, voltage-safety lockout procedures, and multi-step sequencing — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured field environments.

task: Construct historical, scientific, and nature visitor-center displays.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing one full display (historical, scientific, or nature themed). A competent exhibit installer typically spends 2–5 hours assembling panels, mounts, lighting, and artifacts.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse, unstructured steps: mounting large irregular panels, handling fragile artifacts, precise alignment, tool use (drills, levels), semantic understanding of layout instructions, and fine placement in varied environments. Far beyond current robot generalization.

task: Clean internal compartments and moving parts, using rags and cleaning compounds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning internal compartments and moving parts of one machine/unit. A competent worker spends ~15–25 min with rags and cleaning compounds.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating confined internal spaces, applying precise pressure with rags, avoiding delicate moving parts, and judging cleanliness quality. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and confined-space adaptability needed.

task: Test machinery and equipment to ensure that it is safe and conforms to performance specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one machine/equipment unit. A competent technician spends ~45 min on checks, instrument readings, safety verification, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting test instruments, interpreting varied gauges/interfaces, running diagnostic sequences, and making judgment calls on conformance. Current humanoids lack the tool-use generalization, fine dexterity, and contextual reasoning needed for autonomous pass/fail assessments across diverse machinery types.

task: Oversee the preparation and care of the remains of people who have died.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full preparation of one deceased person (washing, embalming, dressing, cosmetizing). A competent mortician typically takes 2–4 hours per case.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (embalming incisions, suturing, cosmetics), handling of highly variable and fragile human remains, specialized fluid/chemical tools, regulatory compliance, and sensitive judgment. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Test new food products and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = evaluating a single new food product or piece of equipment; includes preparation, sensory testing, and documenting results. ~45 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced sensory perception (taste, smell, texture), subjective judgment, and equipment operation in varied contexts. Current robots lack gustatory/olfactory sensors and qualitative evaluation ability.

task: Operate newly repaired machinery or equipment to verify the adequacy of repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single post-repair verification run on one machine; a competent technician typically runs a functional check for ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting machine-specific controls, reading gauges/displays, recognizing abnormal sounds/vibrations, and making judgment calls about adequacy — far beyond current robot generalization and sensory integration capabilities.

task: Test and repair circuits and sensors, following wiring and system specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one circuit/sensor fault cycle: test, diagnose, repair. A skilled technician typically takes ~45 min including setup, testing with instruments, fault identification, and physical repair.
robot_success_rationale: Requires wielding multimeters/oscilloscopes, interpreting schematics, fine soldering or connector manipulation, and adaptive reasoning about faults. Current humanoids lack tool-use dexterity, schematic comprehension, and diagnostic judgment for this complex task.

task: Turn valves to allow measured amounts of air or gas to pass through meters at specified flow rates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting one valve and verifying the flow rate on a meter. A competent worker locates, turns, and confirms reading in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping and precisely turning an industrial valve, reading an analog/digital meter, and closed-loop adjusting to hit a target flow rate. Current humanoids lack reliable fine rotational control and meter-reading integration for this precision task.

task: Seal containers or materials, using glues, fasteners, nails, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sealing one container/joint. A competent worker applies adhesive, fasteners, or nails with hand tools in ~3–7 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated bimanual dexterity, tool grasping (glue gun, hammer, fastener gun), precise force control, and material alignment. Current humanoids struggle with multi-step tool use, variable container types, and consistent sealing quality.

task: Repair or replace broken or malfunctioning components of machinery or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and repairing/replacing one faulty component on a typical machine (e.g., motor, belt, valve). Median across simple to moderately complex repairs.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fault diagnosis, tool selection, precise disassembly/reassembly, and contextual judgment across highly varied equipment. Far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and autonomous reasoning capabilities in unstructured environments.

task: Examine animals and specimens to detect the presence of disease or other problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining a single animal or specimen for signs of disease. A competent veterinarian/technician typically spends ~10–20 minutes on a thorough physical inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert multi-modal perception (visual, tactile, olfactory), nuanced behavioral interpretation, and clinical judgment. Current robots lack veterinary diagnostic reasoning, gentle animal-handling dexterity, and reliable bio-sensing integration.

task: Establish fixed points for use in making maps, using geodetic and engineering instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = establishing a single survey control point. A competent surveyor sets up, levels, and records a fixed point with geodetic instruments in ~60–120 minutes depending on terrain.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise leveling/operation of specialized geodetic instruments (total stations, GPS receivers, theodolites), outdoor unstructured terrain navigation, fine dexterous setup, and professional-grade measurement accuracy. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Transport and accompany clients to shopping areas or to appointments, using automobile.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one round trip to a shopping area or appointment. Typical outing including travel, waiting, and return is ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate automobiles in real traffic. L4 AVs exist but are narrow/mapped-route systems, not general-purpose humanoids. Accompanying/assisting clients adds unstructured caregiving complexity.

task: Reassemble equipment after completion of inspections, testing, or repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reassembling one piece of equipment after inspection/repair. Median across equipment types (e.g., motors, HVAC units, pumps) is ~45 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Reassembly requires tool use, fine dexterity, torque-specific fastening, part sequencing knowledge, and generalization across highly varied equipment types — well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured settings.

task: Measure, mix, prepare, and test chemical solutions used to clean or repair machinery and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single batch of chemical solution: measuring components, mixing, and testing concentration/pH. Typically 20–40 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid measurement, fine pouring/mixing dexterity, reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting chemical test results, and safe handling of hazardous substances — all well beyond current humanoid robot generalization and safety limits.

task: Check electrical installation for proper wiring, polarity, grounding, or integrity of terminations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single electrical panel, outlet, or circuit run. A competent electrician checks wiring, polarity, grounding, and terminations per inspection point in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (multimeter probes, torque checks), reading live circuits safely, interpreting wiring diagrams, and navigating cramped enclosures. Current humanoids lack the precision, electrical safety protocols, and reliable tool-use for autonomous electrical inspection.

task: Secure finished packaged items by hand tying, sewing, gluing, stapling, or attaching fastener.
human_time_rationale: One instance = securing one packaged item (tying, sewing, gluing, stapling, or fastening). A competent worker completes this in ~1–3 minutes depending on method.
robot_success_rationale: Highly varied fastening methods (tying, sewing, gluing, stapling) demand fine dexterity and tool-switching. Robots can staple or apply glue in structured settings, but tying knots and sewing remain very difficult for 2026 humanoids.

task: Replace or repair metal, wood, leather, glass, or other lining in machines, or in equipment compartments or containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing/repairing lining in one machine/compartment. A competent worker typically spends ~1 hour fitting, trimming, and securing lining material.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, cutting, fitting, and fastening of varied materials (metal, leather, glass) inside confined machine compartments. Highly unstructured, demands fine dexterity, material-specific tool use, and force judgment well beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Test repaired, newly installed, or updated equipment to ensure that it functions properly and conforms to specifications, using test equipment and observation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single piece of equipment post-repair/install. A competent technician spends ~20–30 min connecting test equipment, running diagnostics, observing operation, and verifying spec conformance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires selecting and connecting appropriate test instruments, interpreting readings against specs, and making pass/fail judgments on varied equipment. Current robots lack the tool-use generalization, contextual reasoning, and multi-modal diagnostic interpretation needed for autonomous competence.

task: Reassemble repaired electric motors to specified requirements and ratings, using hand tools and electrical meters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reassembling one repaired electric motor. A competent technician typically spends 60–120 minutes fitting components, torquing fasteners, and verifying electrical specs with meters.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for winding connections and meter probing, precise torque control, multi-step sequencing with varied hardware, and interpreting electrical readings — well beyond reliable 2026 general-purpose robot capability.

task: Remove hardened material from machines or machine parts, using abrasives, power and hand tools, jackhammers, sledgehammers, or other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing hardened material (e.g., scale, slag, concrete) from one machine or major machine part. A competent worker typically spends 30–60 minutes per part depending on buildup severity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force tool use (jackhammers, sledgehammers, grinders) with precise targeting on irregular, hardened surfaces. Current humanoids lack sufficient force output, tool-change dexterity, and perception to handle unstructured industrial buildup safely and effectively.

task: Drill holes for wiring in wall studs, joists, ceilings, or floors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling one hole in a stud/joist/ceiling/floor. A competent electrician locates the spot, positions a drill, and bores through in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating tight framing cavities, applying precise axial force with a power drill, and perceiving stud location — all in confined, variable spaces. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use force control and spatial reasoning for this constrained, overhead/floor-level work.

task: Set up or restock product displays.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up or fully restocking a single product display (e.g., a retail shelf section or end-cap). A competent retail worker takes ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a retail floor, grasping varied product shapes/packaging, reading planograms, precise placement, and facing/fronting items. Current robots struggle with product diversity, label orientation, and tight shelf spacing.

task: Survey park to determine forest conditions and distribution and abundance of fauna and flora.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full-day field survey of a park unit, including traversing plots, recording flora/fauna observations, and noting forest conditions. Typical field survey day ~8 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained outdoor navigation over rough/uneven terrain, species identification across diverse taxa, and systematic data collection over hours. Current robots lack robust unstructured outdoor autonomy, ecological perception, and endurance for a full survey day.

task: Connect regulators to test stands, and turn screw adjustments until gauges indicate that inlet and outlet pressures meet specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one regulator connected and adjusted to spec. Competent tech takes ~5–10 min: fitting, tightening, screw adjustment, and confirming gauge readings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires threading/torquing fittings accurately, fine screw adjustments with tactile feedback, and reading analog gauges while closing a pressure control loop — beyond reliable 2026 robot dexterity and closed-loop precision for fluid systems.

task: Evaluate, fit, or adjust prosthetic or orthotic devices or recommend modification to orthotist.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient fitting/evaluation session. A competent prosthetist/orthotist typically spends 30–60 minutes assessing fit, making adjustments, and documenting recommendations.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced tactile assessment of patient comfort, clinical judgment, fine dexterous adjustments on a human body, and interpersonal communication. Far beyond current robot dexterity, perception, and medical reasoning capabilities.

task: Use a variety of specialized equipment, such as electron microscopes, gas and high-pressure liquid chromatographs, electrophoresis units, thermocyclers, fluorescence-activated cell sorters, and phosphorimagers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and operating one specialized lab instrument for a single run/session (e.g., loading a gel for electrophoresis or running an HPLC gradient). Competent technician averages ~45 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: These instruments require highly precise fine-motor manipulation (pipetting, loading columns, inserting gels), software interfacing, and domain-specific calibration. No current humanoid robot has the dexterity, perception, or specialized tool-use capability to autonomously operate such varied, sensitive lab equipment reliably.

task: Cut and weld metal to repair broken metal parts, fabricate new parts, or assemble new equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single repair/fabrication job (e.g., welding a broken bracket or assembling a small component). A competent welder/fabricator typically spends ~45–90 min per job.
robot_success_rationale: Welding requires precise torch control, heat management, seam tracking, and repositioning in varied, unstructured workspaces. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, specialized tool integration, and task planning needed for autonomous metal cutting and welding at acceptable quality.

task: Measure signal strength at utility poles, using electronic test equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring signal strength at one utility pole. Includes climbing/accessing the pole, connecting test equipment, taking readings, and logging results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing or safely accessing a utility pole (extreme mobility challenge), connecting electronic test probes precisely to live utility hardware (high-stakes dexterity), and interpreting equipment readings — all far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capabilities.

task: Start machines and observe mechanical operation to determine efficiency and to detect problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting a machine and observing its operation through a typical inspection cycle. A competent worker spends ~5 min starting up and ~15 min observing/assessing performance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to machine controls, operating varied switches/panels, then interpreting auditory/visual/vibrational cues for anomaly detection. Current robots lack reliable multi-modal diagnostic perception and generalized panel operation in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Demonstrate use and care of equipment for tenant use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single demonstration session for one tenant covering one piece or set of equipment (e.g., HVAC, laundry, appliances). Typically 20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires verbal/visual communication, contextual explanation, answering questions, and operating varied equipment for demonstration. Current robots lack reliable speech interaction, social fluency, and generalizable tool operation needed to satisfy a supervisor.

task: Collaborate with other workers to repair or move machines, machine parts, or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single collaborative repair or relocation job on a machine/part. Typical industrial maintenance tasks range 30–90 min; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-robot/human coordination, variable tool use, diagnosing faults, handling heavy/irregular machinery, and adaptive problem-solving — well beyond current autonomous humanoid capabilities in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Specialize in particular materials or types of object, such as documents and books, paintings, decorative arts, textiles, metals, or architectural materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = specializing/developing expertise in one material category for a conservation project; ~45 min for initial focused assessment/consultation on a specific object type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires deep domain expertise, scholarly judgment, sensory connoisseurship, and contextual knowledge across art history/chemistry. Far beyond current robot cognition, perception calibration, and generalization capabilities.

task: Lubricate moving parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lubricating the moving parts of a single machine/mechanism (e.g., a conveyor joint or door hinge set). Includes identifying points, applying grease/oil, and wiping excess.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying lubrication points, controlling a grease gun or oil applicator with precision, and avoiding over/under-application. Fine dexterity and tool-use with varied fittings challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Isolate, identify and prepare specimens for examination.
human_time_rationale: One instance = isolating, identifying, and preparing a single specimen (e.g., biological, geological, or forensic). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 minutes per specimen end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level fine dexterity (micro-manipulation, dissection tools), domain-specific perceptual identification, and sterile/precise lab technique far beyond current humanoid robot capability. Generalization across specimen types is extremely limited.

task: Prepare artifacts for storage and shipping.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single artifact (wrapping, padding, boxing, labeling) for storage or shipping. Competent museum/warehouse worker takes ~45 min per artifact on average.
robot_success_rationale: Artifacts vary wildly in shape, fragility, and material. Requires delicate, adaptive grasping, custom padding placement, and contextual judgment to avoid damage. Far beyond reliable autonomous robot capability in 2026.

task: Measure or weigh compounds and solutions for use in testing or animal feed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring/weighing a single compound or solution. A competent lab/feed technician takes ~3–7 min including setup, taring, dispensing, and recording.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle vials, pipettes, and balances; precise force control to avoid spills; reading digital displays; and generalizing across varied containers and powders/liquids. Current humanoids lack reliable lab-grade precision for such tasks.

task: Replace, empty, or replenish machine and equipment containers such as gas tanks or boxes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing/refilling one container (e.g., gas tank, supply box). A competent worker locates, removes, refills/swaps, and reinstalls in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied dexterity (caps, valves, latches), liquid/gas handling, and machine-specific tool use. Current humanoids struggle with unstructured containers, connectors, and safe fluid transfer without spills or leaks.

task: Determine longitudes and latitudes of important features and boundaries in survey areas, using theodolites, transits, levels, and satellite-based global positioning systems (GPS).
human_time_rationale: One instance = determining coordinates of a single survey point/feature using theodolite, transit, level, or GPS. A competent surveyor typically spends ~1–2 hours per control point including setup, observation, and recording.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized survey instruments (theodolites, transits, GPS rovers), precise leveling, fine adjustment of optical equipment, and professional judgment in unstructured outdoor terrain. Well beyond current humanoid robot generalization and tool-use capabilities.

task: Explain diagnostic procedures such as chorionic villus sampling (CVS), ultrasound, fetal blood sampling, and amniocentesis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one CVS procedure on a patient; a skilled OB or MFM specialist takes ~30–60 min including prep, needle guidance, sample collection, and post-procedure monitoring.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultrasound-guided needle insertion into placental tissue with sub-millimeter precision, sterile technique, real-time clinical judgment, and patient interaction. Far beyond current general-purpose robot dexterity and safety limits.

task: Repair or maintain the operating condition of industrial production or processing machinery or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one maintenance/repair job on a single machine. A competent industrial mechanic spends ~60 min on a typical preventive or corrective maintenance task.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults, handling varied tools and fasteners, navigating tight machinery spaces, and making judgment calls. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, domain reasoning, and tool-use generalization needed for autonomous industrial repair.

task: Install, arrange, assemble, and prepare artifacts for exhibition, ensuring the artifacts' safety, reporting their status and condition, and identifying and correcting any problems with the set up.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single exhibition display (multiple artifacts). A competent art/museum installer typically spends 2–5 hours on layout, mounting, labeling, inspection, and condition reporting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous handling of fragile, irreplaceable artifacts, spatial aesthetic judgment, condition assessment, written reporting, and problem-solving across highly varied objects and display formats — far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Harvest cell cultures using substances such as mitotic arrestants, cell releasing agents, and cell fixatives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = harvesting a batch of cell cultures (e.g., one flask/plate set). Includes adding agents, incubation monitoring, centrifugation, and fixation steps; ~45 min for a competent lab tech.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting, sterile technique, centrifuge operation, reagent sequencing, and real-time visual judgment in a lab setting. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, sterile-field compliance, and domain-specific tool-use needed for reliable autonomous execution.

task: Fill water tanks and check tanks, pipes, and fittings for leaks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling one tank and inspecting connected pipes/fittings for leaks; a competent worker typically takes 20–40 min depending on system size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating valves/faucets (moderate dexterity), visually or tactilely detecting subtle leaks (drips, moisture, pressure drops), and navigating possibly cramped utility spaces. Current robots lack reliable leak-detection perception and valve manipulation generalization.

task: Transport machine parts, tools, equipment, and other material between work areas and storage, using cranes, hoists, or dollies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving one load (parts, tools, or equipment) from a source work area/storage to a destination. Includes rigging, operating crane/hoist/dolly, and unloading. Typical move takes ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Cranes, hoists, and dollies require specialized operation a humanoid cannot perform autonomously. Rigging loads safely, operating overhead crane controls, and navigating industrial floors with heavy loads are far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Test operation or functionality of mechanical, plumbing, electrical, and control systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing all major systems (mechanical, plumbing, electrical, controls) in a single unit/facility. A competent technician typically spends ~45 min cycling through checks, readings, and functional tests.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating valves, switches, breakers, and reading diverse gauges/indicators across heterogeneous equipment. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use, multi-system generalization, and the diagnostic reasoning needed to judge pass/fail across all four system types.

task: Install outer doors and door frames at elevator entrances on each floor of a structure.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing outer door panels and frame at one elevator entrance on one floor. Includes positioning, aligning, fastening, and checking operation — typically 1–1.5 hours for a skilled elevator installer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting, precise millimeter-level alignment of large steel frames, fastening in constrained spaces, and use of specialized tools. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, strength, and generalization limits. No known robot can do this autonomously.

task: Observe and test the operation of machinery or equipment to diagnose malfunctions, using voltmeters or other testing devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing a single machine/equipment malfunction. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min observing, probing, and interpreting results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step reasoning, precise probe placement, interpreting ambiguous sensor readings, and adapting to varied equipment layouts. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-dexterous tool use and contextual fault-diagnosis cognition at acceptable quality.

task: Replace defective parts, such as bellows, range springs, and toggle switches, and reassemble units according to blueprints, using cam presses and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and replacing one defective part (e.g., a bellow, spring, or switch) and reassembling the unit. A skilled technician typically takes 30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires blueprint interpretation, precise dexterous tool-use (cam presses, hand tools), fine-part manipulation (springs, bellows, toggle switches), and quality judgment. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity capabilities.

task: Inspect mining areas for unsafe structures, equipment, and working conditions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspection of one mining zone/area; a trained inspector walks through, assesses structures, equipment, air quality, and conditions, and documents findings — typically 60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Mining environments are highly unstructured, dark, dusty, and hazardous with uneven/rough terrain, confined spaces, and unpredictable hazards. Current humanoids lack robust outdoor/underground mobility, gas/structural-hazard sensing integration, and safety judgment needed for autonomous inspection at acceptable quality.

task: Test and measure patient's strength, motor development and function, sensory perception, functional capacity, or respiratory or circulatory efficiency and record data.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full clinical assessment session for one patient covering strength, motor, sensory, and functional domains; a competent therapist/clinician typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced patient interaction, calibrated tactile/sensory testing, clinical judgment, adaptive communication, and precise instrument use on a human body. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, medical tool integration, and clinical reasoning for autonomous, supervisor-acceptable execution.

task: Install solar collector mounting devices on tile, asphalt, shingle, or built-up gravel roofs, using appropriate materials and penetration methods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one mounting device (bracket/standoff) on a roof. A skilled roofer/solar installer takes ~30–60 min per mount, including layout, penetration, sealing, and fastening.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor locomotion on sloped, varied roof surfaces (tile, shingle, gravel), precise drilling/fastening into structural members, weatherproof sealing, and tool-use in unstructured environments — all far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Install equipment such as amplifiers or repeaters to maintain the strength of communications transmissions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single amplifier or repeater unit, including mounting, cabling, and basic configuration. A competent technician typically takes ~45–90 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied environments (rooftops, utility closets, poles), precise cable routing/termination, tool use (drills, crimpers), and configuration decisions. Far beyond current general-purpose robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Solder, wrap, and coat wires to ensure proper insulation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = soldering, wrapping, and coating a single wire connection or splice. A skilled technician completes this in ~15 min including prep, soldering, heat-shrink/tape wrap, and coating.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity: precise soldering iron control, tinning, joint inspection, heat-shrink positioning, and coating application. Current humanoids lack the fingertip precision, thermal control, and multi-step fine manipulation needed for acceptable quality.

task: Clean or maintain tools or test equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/maintaining a single tool or piece of test equipment (wiping, lubricating, inspecting). A competent worker typically takes 10–20 minutes per item.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct tool, applying appropriate cleaning agent/lubricant, and fine dexterous wiping/brushing in varied configurations. Perception of tool condition, grasp diversity, and safe handling of delicate test equipment are significant challenges for 2026 humanoids.

task: Take photographs and motion pictures for use in lectures and publications and to develop displays.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single photography/filming session (setup, shooting, basic review). A competent photographer typically spends ~30–60 min per session for lecture/publication-quality content.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative framing, subject direction, lighting judgment, equipment operation (DSLR, tripod, motion camera), and contextual awareness for publication quality. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, aesthetic judgment, and specialized tool-use needed.

task: Route and connect cables and lines to switches, switchboard equipment, and distributing frames, using wire-wrap guns or soldering irons to connect wires to terminals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = routing and terminating a cable run to a switchboard/frame. A competent technician spends ~30–60 min per run including measuring, dressing, and wrapping/soldering terminals.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (wire-wrap guns, soldering irons, sub-mm terminal placement), precise tool use, and reading complex wiring diagrams. Far beyond current humanoid manipulation capability; high failure risk on every micro-step.

task: Perform laboratory studies of steps in manufacture of new products and test proposed processes in small-scale operation, such as a pilot plant.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single lab study/pilot-plant test run of a manufacturing process step. A competent chemical/process engineer typically spends 2–4 hours setting up, running, and recording results for one such experiment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level scientific reasoning, hypothesis-driven experimental design, precise instrument operation, interpretation of novel results, and adaptive decision-making — far beyond current general-purpose robot autonomy. Fine dexterous lab manipulation and generalization also remain very limited.

task: Construct, maintain, or repair medical supportive devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing, maintaining, or repairing a single medical supportive device (e.g., brace, splint, orthotic). Skilled orthotist/prosthetist typically takes 30–60 min per device.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for precise fitting, cutting, and fastening of custom components; patient-specific geometry; use of specialized tools (heat guns, grinders, drills); and safety-critical quality judgment — all beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Test equipment to ensure proper operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single piece of equipment. A competent worker runs checks, reads indicators, and verifies outputs; ~15 min is typical across many equipment types.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading varied displays/gauges, connecting test probes, interpreting results, and making judgment calls. Perception of diverse interfaces and semantic understanding of "proper operation" are major current limitations.

task: Determine plumb of walls or ceilings, using plumb lines and levels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking plumb on a single wall or ceiling section. A competent worker uses a level or plumb line and reads the result in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can hold a level and read it visually, but positioning a plumb line precisely against an irregular surface, interpreting subtle deviations, and navigating to varied wall sections in unstructured construction sites challenge current dexterity and generalization.

task: Perform tests and examinations to establish storage and conservation requirements, policies, and procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = conducting a full round of tests/examinations on a collection item or set of conditions and drafting resulting storage/conservation requirements. A competent conservator typically spends ~2 hours per session.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires expert-level knowledge synthesis, nuanced material assessment, policy formulation, and written documentation. Current robots lack domain expertise, abstract reasoning for policy creation, and reliable fine-grained material inspection capability.

task: Lay underground cable directly in trenches, or string it through conduits running through trenches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying/pulling cable through ~50m of trench or conduit. A crew completes this in ~2 hours depending on terrain, conduit bends, and cable gauge.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor trench navigation, heavy cable management, conduit threading, and coordinated pulling — well beyond current humanoid mobility, strength, dexterity, and tool-use capabilities in unstructured outdoor environments.

task: Install, replace, or change machine parts and attachments, according to production specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = swapping or installing a single machine part/attachment. A competent worker typically spends 15–45 min accounting for identification, removal, fitting, fastening, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool use (wrenches, screwdrivers), precise force feedback, part identification across highly varied machine types, and reading specs. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous tool manipulation and generalization across unstructured industrial equipment.

task: Play instruments to evaluate their sound quality and to locate any defects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = evaluating one instrument. A skilled technician plays through scales, chords, and registers to assess tone and locate defects, typically taking ~10 minutes per instrument.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly nuanced fine dexterous manipulation (fingering, bowing, embouchure), sensitive auditory perception to judge tonal quality, and musical domain expertise. Current robots lack the dexterity, touch sensitivity, and acoustic judgment needed across diverse instrument types.

task: Receive, set up, test, or decontaminate equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = receiving, setting up, testing, and/or decontaminating a single piece of equipment. A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min depending on equipment complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step reasoning, reading labels/manuals, fine dexterous connections, operating varied controls, and applying decontaminants correctly. Current humanoids lack the generalization, tool-use, and procedural adaptability for autonomous success.

task: Cut seats to receive new orifices, tap inspection ports, and perform other repairs to salvage usable materials, using hand tools and machine tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a repair cycle (cutting seats, tapping ports, and related repairs) on a single valve or fitting component; a skilled machinist/pipefitter typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise machining (cutting seats, tapping threads) with hand and machine tools, high-force dexterity, fine tolerances, and adaptive decision-making on irregular salvage parts — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Connect hoses from provers to meter inlets and outlets, and raise prover bells until prover gauges register zero.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting hoses to one prover/meter setup and raising bells to zero. A skilled technician takes ~15–25 min for hose connections, sealing, and careful bell adjustment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting threaded/sealed hose fittings with leak-free precision, managing flexible hoses, and delicately raising a prover bell to an exact gauge reading — highly dexterous, fluid-handling tasks well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Build, repair, and install wooden steps, scaffolds, and walkways to gain access to or permit improved view of exhibited equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = building/repairing/installing one set of wooden steps or walkway section. A competent carpenter typically takes 1–3 hours depending on complexity; 2 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sawing lumber to measure, nailing/screwing structural joints, carrying heavy boards, and precise leveling in varied environments. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and structural reasoning capabilities.

task: Install regulators and related equipment such as gas meters, odorization units, and gas pressure telemetering equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single regulator station with associated equipment (meter, odorization unit, telemetry). A competent gas fitter typically takes 1.5–2.5 hours per installation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy pipe wrenches, torquing threaded fittings to spec, working outdoors in unstructured environments, connecting gas/electrical systems, and making safety-critical judgments. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Set up and operate devices, such as mechanical ventilators, therapeutic gas administration apparatus, environmental control systems, or aerosol generators, following specified parameters of treatment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and initiating a single respiratory therapy device (e.g., ventilator) for one patient, including parameter configuration, circuit assembly, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of medical circuits/tubing, interpreting prescription parameters, interacting safely with a patient, and operating complex clinical equipment. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured clinical settings.

task: Administer manual exercises, massage, or traction to help relieve pain, increase patient strength, or decrease or prevent deformity or crippling.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single therapeutic session (exercise, massage, or traction) for one patient; typical PT treatment sessions run 20–45 minutes, median ~30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced tactile force control, real-time pain-response adaptation, and safe physical contact with a human body — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, haptic sensing, and clinical safety standards. No deployed system approaches this.

task: Disassemble instruments and parts for repair and adjustment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling a single instrument (e.g. a sensor, gauge, or musical/scientific instrument) to expose components for repair. Competent technician typically takes 15–35 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle tiny screws, clips, and fragile parts; tool switching (screwdrivers, spanner wrenches); and instrument-specific knowledge. Current humanoids lack the precision, force control, and tool-use generalization needed.

task: Inspect facilities to monitor compliance with regulations governing substances, such as asbestos, lead, or wastewater.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one facility inspection visit. A competent inspector typically spends 1–3 hours walking the site, collecting samples, documenting findings, and checking compliance records.
robot_success_rationale: Requires regulatory knowledge, judgment, documentation, sample collection with specialized tools, interviewing staff, and navigating varied industrial environments. Current humanoids lack the cognitive authority, legal standing, and multi-modal inspection capability needed; generalization to unstructured industrial sites is poor.

task: Participate in the research, development, or manufacturing of medicinal and pharmaceutical preparations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one discrete R&D or manufacturing activity (e.g., preparing a batch, running an assay). A competent pharmaceutical technician typically spends ~60 min per unit procedure.
robot_success_rationale: Pharmaceutical work demands fine dexterity, sterile technique, precise measurement, complex tool use, and adaptive decision-making in specialized lab/GMP environments. Current humanoid robots lack the precision, compliance with sterile protocols, and domain-specific manipulation required.

task: Record commercials for later broadcast.
human_time_rationale: One instance = recording a single commercial shoot session. Setup, direction, takes, and wrap typically take ~1 hour for a competent broadcast technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating broadcast cameras, audio equipment, switchers, and recording systems in a production environment. Humanoid robots lack the specialized tool-use, creative judgment, and system integration needed for professional broadcast recording.

task: Clean plant growth, scale, paint, soil, or rust from meter housings, using wire brushes, scrapers, buffers, sandblasters, or cleaning compounds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one meter housing. A competent worker spends ~20–30 min scrubbing, scraping, and/or sandblasting contaminants off a single housing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool-switching (wire brush, scraper, sandblaster), forceful yet controlled scrubbing on irregular, corroded surfaces in varied field environments. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use, variable-force regulation, and outdoor unstructured-site mobility for this task.

task: Locate and mark sites selected for geophysical prospecting activities, such as efforts to locate petroleum or other mineral products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating and marking a single prospecting site. Involves field navigation, GPS/map use, physical staking/flagging. A competent surveyor/geophysicist typically takes 60–120 min per site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor rough-terrain navigation, GPS/map interpretation, professional judgment on site selection, and physical marking (stakes, flags). Current humanoids lack reliable unstructured outdoor mobility, geophysical expertise, and tool-use for staking in varied terrain.

task: Wrap air ducts and water lines with insulating materials, such as duct wrap and pipe insulation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = insulating a single typical run (e.g., one duct section or 10 ft of pipe). A competent insulator measures, cuts, wraps, and secures material in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating tight mechanical spaces, measuring irregular surfaces, cutting flexible insulation, and precisely wrapping/securing material — demanding bimanual dexterity, tool use, and spatial reasoning in cluttered, confined environments well beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Polish instruments, using rags and polishing compounds, buffing wheels, or burnishing tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = polishing one instrument. A competent worker applies compound, buffs, and inspects a single instrument in ~15–25 minutes depending on size and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced force control, varied tool use (rags, buffing wheels, burnishing tools), and surface-quality perception across complex curved geometries. Current humanoids lack the fine tactile feedback and adaptive pressure control needed for acceptable finish quality.

task: String cables between structures and lines from poles, towers, or trenches, and pull lines to proper tension.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stringing and tensioning a single cable run between two structures/poles. Includes rigging, pulling, and tensioning with come-alongs or tensioning equipment; typically 1–2 hours for a crew.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing poles/towers or navigating trenches, handling heavy cables, operating specialized tensioning tools, and making precise tension measurements outdoors on uneven terrain — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in mobility, strength, and tool-use generalization.

task: Cut, miter, and glue piping insulation to insulate plumbing pipes and fittings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = insulating a single pipe run with fittings (~2–3 meters). A competent plumber/insulator cuts, miters, and glues foam/rubber tubing in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise miter-angle cuts with a blade or scissors, shaping foam tubing to fit bends/tees, applying contact adhesive, and pressing seams flush — all demanding fine dexterity, tool use, and spatial reasoning on irregular geometry. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Demonstrate start-up, shut-down, maintenance, diagnostic, and safety procedures to thermal system owners.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full demonstration session covering all five procedures (start-up, shut-down, maintenance, diagnostics, safety) for one thermal system owner/group. A competent technician typically takes ~45–75 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires verbal explanation, adaptive communication, contextual teaching, and responding to owner questions. Current robots lack reliable speech-based instruction, dynamic knowledge presentation, and generalization across varied thermal systems and audiences.

task: Inspect oil and gas wells to determine that installations are completed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one well site for installation completion, verifying equipment, connections, gauges, and documentation. A competent field inspector typically takes ~90 minutes per well.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex, hazardous outdoor terrain, reading gauges/valves, interpreting technical schematics, and making compliance judgments. Current humanoids lack robust outdoor mobility, domain-specific inspection knowledge, and regulatory decision-making capability.

task: Perform and document an initial exam, evaluating data to identify problems and determine a diagnosis prior to intervention.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full initial patient/client exam, including history-taking, physical assessment, and preliminary diagnosis. A competent clinician typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced clinical reasoning, natural language history-taking, fine sensory examination (palpation, auscultation, reflexes), and diagnostic judgment. Current humanoid robots lack the cognitive, sensory, and social capability for autonomous clinical diagnosis.

task: Adjust string tensions to tune instruments, using hand tools and electronic tuning devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tuning one instrument (e.g., a guitar). A competent technician adjusts all strings to pitch using a tuner in roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torque on small tuning pegs, auditory/electronic pitch feedback integration, and fine dexterity across varied instrument shapes. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control and audio-feedback loop needed reliably.

task: Design layouts of audio and video equipment and perform upgrades and maintenance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing a layout for a single AV system (e.g., a conference room or home theater) and performing associated upgrades/maintenance. A competent AV technician typically spends ~90 minutes on planning, cable routing, mounting, and testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level spatial planning, reading schematics, routing/terminating cables, rack mounting, configuring software, and fine connector work. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and dexterous capability in unstructured AV environments.

task: Clean, maintain and prepare supplies and work areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single work area session: wiping surfaces, restocking supplies, and organizing tools/materials. A competent worker completes this in ~30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Combines cleaning, supply inventory/restocking, and area organization. Robots handle basic wiping/sweeping moderately well, but identifying, fetching, and properly restocking varied supplies across an unstructured work area is beyond reliable autonomous performance in 2026.

task: Compare instrument pitches with tuning tool pitches to tune instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tuning a single instrument. A competent musician/technician takes ~5 min to tune all strings/keys to a reference using a tuning tool.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine auditory pitch perception, extremely precise dexterous manipulation of tuning pegs/keys under tension, and adaptive feedback control. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-motor tuning touch and musical acoustic sensing pipelines.

task: Use a variety of construction equipment to complete installations, such as digger derricks, trenchers, or cable plows.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a typical utility installation shift segment (e.g., trenching and laying cable for one section/run). A competent operator takes ~3–4 hours per installation segment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large, specialized heavy machinery (digger derricks, trenchers, cable plows) in outdoor unstructured terrain. No humanoid robot can operate such equipment autonomously; requires trained operator controls, situational judgment, and safety compliance far beyond 2026 frontier robots.

task: Repair cracks in wood or metal instruments, using pinning wire, lathes, fillers, clamps, or soldering irons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single crack on one instrument. Skilled luthier/technician assesses damage, selects method (pinning, filling, soldering), applies repair, clamps/cures. Typically 30–90 min depending on severity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert tactile assessment of crack depth/type, fine dexterity for pinning wire and soldering, tool switching, and quality judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured, varied repair contexts.

task: Observe action of microorganisms upon living tissues of plants, higher animals, and other microorganisms, and on dead organic matter.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and observing one specimen set (slides, cultures) under microscope and recording findings; a trained microbiologist typically spends ~45 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous lab manipulation (slide prep, pipetting, microscope focusing), high-level scientific interpretation of biological imagery, and adaptive reasoning — well beyond current general-purpose humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Disconnect or remove defective or unauthorized meters, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one defective/unauthorized meter. Includes accessing meter, using hand tools to disconnect fittings/wiring, and safe removal. Typical utility tech task.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torque with hand tools on varied fittings (electrical/gas/water), working in tight/outdoor enclosures, and safely handling live utility connections. Current robots lack the dexterity, tool-use reliability, and safety judgment needed.

task: Conduct research, or assist in the conduct of research, including the collection of information and samples, such as blood, water, soil, plants and animals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single field research session collecting samples (e.g., blood, water, soil, plants, or animals). A competent researcher typically spends ~1 hour per session including travel, collection, and labeling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly varied dexterous manipulation (needles, vials, nets, trowels), unstructured outdoor terrain, and contextual judgment to identify/select correct samples. Current robots lack reliable fine-tool use, biological sampling protocols, and outdoor generalization.

task: Set up service for customers, installing, connecting, testing, or adjusting equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one customer service visit involving installing, connecting, testing, and adjusting a piece of equipment (e.g., appliance, modem, HVAC unit). Median visit ~60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly variable environments, fine dexterous tool-use (wiring, connectors), customer interaction, safety judgment, and adaptive troubleshooting. Current humanoids lack the generalization and reliability for unsupervised field service work.

task: Mix and measure glue that will be used for instrument repair.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and mixing a single batch of glue for one repair job; a skilled luthier/technician completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement of small volumes, fine dexterity to mix without spilling, and judgment on consistency/ratio. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control and tactile/visual feedback for reliable precision mixing of small quantities.

task: Perform minor repairs and routine cleaning of audio and video equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single piece of A/V equipment serviced (cleaned and minor repair). A competent technician typically spends ~30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (opening housings, replacing small components, precise cleaning of delicate parts), tool use, and diagnostic judgment. Far beyond reliable frontier humanoid capability in unstructured settings.

task: Coordinate exhibit installations, assisting with design, constructing displays, dioramas, display cases, and models, and ensuring the availability of necessary materials.
human_time_rationale: One full exhibit installation involving design coordination, constructing displays/dioramas/cases/models, and material prep typically spans a full workday (8 hrs) for a competent exhibit technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-day coordination, design judgment, fine carpentry, artistic diorama work, tool use, logistics planning, and cross-team communication — far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and task-planning capabilities.

task: Disassemble machinery or equipment to remove parts and make repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling a single machine or major subsystem to access a faulty part. A competent mechanic/technician typically spends 30–90 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct fasteners, applying high torque, handling diverse hardware in unstructured orientations, and sequencing disassembly steps. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and force-control capability in real-world machinery contexts.

task: Test and diagnose air flow systems, using furnace efficiency analysis equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing and diagnosing one furnace/air flow system. A competent HVAC technician typically spends 30–60 minutes connecting analyzers, running diagnostics, and interpreting results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting specialized combustion/airflow analyzers to furnace ports, interpreting multi-sensor readings, navigating confined utility spaces, and making diagnostic judgments. Current humanoids lack the fine tool-use, specialized equipment interfacing, and diagnostic reasoning needed.

task: Locate and secure settings, properties, effects, and other production necessities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scouting, negotiating, and securing one production location or set of props/effects for a single project. Involves travel, communication, and logistics, typically taking several hours.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires complex negotiation, creative judgment, legal/contractual reasoning, interpersonal communication, and unstructured outdoor/urban scouting. Far beyond current robot autonomy in cognition, social interaction, and decision-making.

task: Organize, develop, analyze, test, and prepare special meals, such as low-fat, low-cholesterol, or chemical-free meals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = planning and preparing a single special-diet meal (e.g., low-fat dinner). Includes menu planning, ingredient selection, cooking, and plating; ~60 min for a competent dietitian/chef.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step reasoning (nutritional analysis, recipe development, ingredient substitution), complex kitchen manipulation, and quality judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robots' generalization and dexterous cooking ability.

task: Mark and label containers, container tags, or products, using marking tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking/labeling a single container or product. A competent worker picks up a marker or applies a label in roughly 1–3 minutes including reading and placement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to hold a marking tool or peel-and-place a label accurately, plus reading/writing content correctly. Current humanoids struggle with precise pen control, label alignment, and generalizing across varied container shapes.

task: Install electrical equipment such as air-conditioning, heating, or ignition systems and components such as generator brushes and commutators, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single electrical system/component (e.g., an A/C unit or ignition system). A competent electrician/mechanic typically takes 1–2 hours per install.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous hand-tool use, routing wires through confined spaces, reading schematics, and precise connector seating. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, contextual judgment, and tool-handling finesse for such complex, varied electrical installs.

task: Read prescription, measure arterial blood gases, and review patient information to assess patient condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reviewing one patient's prescription, ABG results, and chart to form a clinical assessment. A competent respiratory therapist or clinician typically takes 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading handwritten/printed Rx, interpreting complex clinical ABG values, integrating patient history, and forming a medical judgment. Current robots lack clinical cognition, fine document handling in unstructured settings, and medical decision-making capability.

task: Study object documentation or conduct standard chemical and physical tests to ascertain the object's age, composition, original appearance, need for treatment or restoration, and appropriate preservation method.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining a single artifact. A trained conservator spends ~1–3 hours studying documentation, running standard chemical/physical tests, and writing up findings per object.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level interpretation of chemical/physical test results, historical documentation research, and nuanced judgment on preservation needs. Current robots lack the specialized sensory instruments, domain reasoning, and dexterous handling for fragile artifacts at acceptable risk.

task: Adjust, maintain, or repair laboratory equipment, such as microscopes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting/maintaining/repairing a single piece of lab equipment (e.g. a microscope). A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min on calibration, cleaning, and minor repairs.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (optics alignment, tiny screws, delicate components), specialized tool use, and expert diagnostic reasoning. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability; risk of damaging sensitive equipment is very high.

task: Access specific areas to string lines, or install terminal boxes, auxiliary equipment, or appliances, using bucket trucks, climbing poles or ladders, or entering tunnels, trenches, or crawl spaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = accessing a single work site/point (e.g., climbing a pole or entering a trench) to perform the access portion. Typical lineworker access task takes ~30–90 min including setup.
