robot_success_rationale: Requires operating bucket trucks (vehicle control), climbing utility poles/ladders in outdoor unstructured environments, or navigating confined spaces like tunnels and crawl spaces. Current humanoids lack vehicle operation, outdoor climbing, and confined-space navigation at this level.

task: Test industrial diamonds or abrasives, soil, or rocks to determine their geological characteristics, using optical, x-ray, heat, acid, or precision instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one sample tested. Competent technician spends ~30–60 min on prep, instrument setup, running tests (XRD, acid, optical), and logging results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized lab instruments (XRD, optical microscopes, acid baths), fine dexterous sample handling, and expert interpretation of results. Current humanoids lack lab-tool interfacing, precision manipulation at this scale, and analytical reasoning for geological characterization.

task: Test or calibrate components or equipment, following manufacturers' manuals and troubleshooting techniques, using hand tools, power tools, or measuring devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing/calibrating a single component or piece of equipment. A competent technician spends ~30–60 min reading the manual, connecting tools, running tests, and adjusting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting varied manuals, selecting/connecting correct tools, reading analog/digital gauges, making fine calibration adjustments, and troubleshooting — complex multi-step tool-use and reasoning far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Operate chemical processing equipment for the production of biofuels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full operating shift (8 hours) monitoring, adjusting, and controlling chemical processing equipment in a biofuel production facility.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating complex industrial control systems, reading analog/digital gauges, adjusting valves and process parameters, and responding to alarms. Current humanoids lack the specialized tool interfacing, process knowledge integration, and safety-critical decision-making needed for autonomous chemical plant operation.

task: Set and monitor product standards, examining samples of raw products or directing testing during processing, to ensure finished products are of prescribed quality.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one quality inspection cycle (sampling, testing, and standards review for a single production batch), typically 30–60 minutes for a competent QC technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex judgment: setting standards, interpreting test results, directing testing protocols, and making accept/reject decisions. Current robots lack the reasoning, domain expertise, and supervisory agency needed; basic sensor-based inspection demos exist but fall far short of autonomous standards-setting and oversight.

task: Inspect buildings to identify required weatherization measures, including repair work, modification, or replacement.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one typical residential building. A competent auditor walks through checking insulation, seals, windows, HVAC, and documenting findings in ~45–90 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating stairs/tight spaces, recognizing subtle defects (drafts, moisture intrusion, degraded caulking, insulation gaps), using diagnostic tools (blower door, thermal camera), and producing a professional report — well beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Apply ultraviolet radiation protection to prevent degradation of plumbing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying UV protection (tape, paint, or foam wrap) to exposed plumbing on a typical residential or light-commercial job. Includes prep, application, and cleanup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying exposed pipes, cutting/applying UV-protective tape, foam, or paint precisely around irregular geometries, fittings, and tight spaces. Current robots lack the dexterity, tool-use, and spatial reasoning for this unstructured, contact-rich task.

task: Fill and tamp holes, using cement, earth, and tamping devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling and tamping a single hole (e.g., post hole or utility cut). A competent worker typically spends ~15–25 min mixing/pouring material and tamping in layers.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinating shoveling loose/heavy material, layered pouring, and forceful rhythmic tamping with a heavy tool—demanding strength, balance on uneven outdoor terrain, and precise force control well beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Record sound onto tape or film for radio or television, checking its quality and making adjustments where necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one typical recording session (e.g., a radio segment or TV scene take). A competent audio engineer sets up, records, monitors, and adjusts quality in ~30–60 minutes on average.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced auditory perception, operating specialized mixing/recording consoles, real-time quality judgment, and creative decision-making. Current humanoid robots lack the audio cognition, fine control of complex studio equipment, and professional judgment needed.

task: Organize recording sessions and prepare areas, such as radio booths and television stations, for recording.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single recording area (e.g., radio booth or TV studio) for one session, including arranging equipment, checking connections, and coordinating logistics. Typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires complex cognitive coordination (scheduling, communication, logistics), operating specialized AV equipment with many non-standard interfaces, and nuanced setup judgment. Far beyond current robot generalization and tool-use capabilities.

task: Inspect or review projects to monitor compliance with building and safety codes or other regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one project/site inspection visit. A competent inspector typically spends 60–120 minutes on-site reviewing structural, electrical, and safety compliance elements.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex, dynamic construction sites (rough terrain, obstacles), interpreting blueprints and codes, making regulatory judgments, and producing legally defensible reports — far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Troubleshoot or repair mechanical, hydraulic, or electrical malfunctions related to variable pitch systems, variable speed control systems, converter systems, or related components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and repairing a single malfunction in a complex system (variable pitch, hydraulic, electrical). Skilled technician typically takes 60–120 min per fault.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level diagnostic reasoning, operating specialized test equipment, interpreting sensor/error codes, and precision repair of hydraulic/electrical components. Far beyond current robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Inspect or repair fiberglass turbine blades.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting or performing a repair on a single turbine blade. A trained technician typically spends 1–3 hours per blade for inspection plus minor repair work.
robot_success_rationale: Blades are large, often elevated (requiring rope access or scaffolding), require precise composite repair (sanding, filling, laminating), fine tactile judgement, and specialized tooling. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured field environments.

task: Install monitoring system components, such as flow meters, temperature gauges, and pressure gauges, according to system design and manufacturer specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one monitoring component (flow meter, gauge) including fitting, sealing, torquing, and verifying against specs. Roughly 60–90 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine torque-controlled threading onto pipe fittings, reading/interpreting spec sheets, working in cramped/varied industrial environments, using wrenches and sealant tape, and validating alignment — all far beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Measure tolerances of assembled and salvageable parts for conformance to standards or specifications, using gauges, micrometers, and calipers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single part with gauges/micrometers/calipers and recording conformance. A competent technician typically takes ~5–10 minutes per part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool handling (micrometer, caliper), exact positioning on irregular salvaged parts, and fine dexterous grip force control — well beyond current humanoid dexterity. Perception of small tolerances (sub-mm) and tool reading add further difficulty.

task: Dig holes for power poles, using power augers or shovels, set poles in place with cranes, and hoist poles upright, using winches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling/digging one hole, setting one pole, and hoisting it upright. A crew typically takes 60–120 minutes per pole including setup, auger operation, crane placement, and winching.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a power auger truck, crane, and winch — heavy specialized vehicles/equipment a humanoid cannot drive or control. Outdoor uneven terrain, heavy loads, and multi-machine coordination push success probability to near zero.

task: Measure characteristics of the Earth, such as gravity or magnetic fields, using equipment such as seismographs, gravimeters, torsion balances, or magnetometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setup, calibration, measurement, and data logging at a single field station with one instrument type (e.g., gravimeter reading). Typical geophysical survey station takes 60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires transporting and precisely leveling sensitive scientific instruments in outdoor/unstructured terrain, operating specialized controls, and ensuring calibration quality. Current humanoids lack terrain robustness, fine instrument-leveling dexterity, and domain-specific tool operation for geophysical equipment.

task: Align pads and keys on reed or wind instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning pads and keys on a single instrument. A skilled instrument repair technician typically takes 30–60 minutes for this precise adjustment work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sub-millimeter tactile feedback, and expert acoustic judgment to test seal quality. Far beyond current humanoid robot manipulation capability; analogous to surgical/jewelry-level precision work.

task: Lay cable and hook up electrical connections between machines, power sources, and phone lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = running and terminating a cable run between two endpoints (e.g., machine to panel). Includes routing, securing, stripping, and connecting. Typical industrial job takes 1–2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for stripping wires, inserting terminals, and tightening connectors; routing cable through conduit or tight spaces; safety-critical precision. Far beyond current humanoid general capability.

task: Adjust felt hammers on pianos to increase tonal mellowness or brilliance, using sanding paddles, lacquer, or needles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = voicing a full piano (88 hammers). A skilled piano technician typically spends 60–120 minutes needling, sanding, or lacquering all hammers to achieve consistent tone.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine-motor precision (needle insertion at exact angles into felt), nuanced auditory feedback to judge tonal change, and expert domain knowledge. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and sensory-motor integration capabilities.

task: Provide emergency care, such as artificial respiration, external cardiac massage, or assistance with cardiopulmonary resuscitation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = responding to a single emergency event; CPR/resuscitation typically administered for ~10 min until EMS arrives or ROSC achieved.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled chest compressions on a human body, airway management, real-time physiological assessment, and adaptive decision-making. Current humanoids lack medical sensing, safe force calibration on humans, and reliable generalization in chaotic emergency scenes.

task: Wrap water heaters with water heater blankets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wrapping one water heater. A competent worker measures, cuts, fits, and secures the insulating blanket around the tank in ~25–35 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires measuring a large cylindrical appliance, cutting flexible insulation material to size, fitting it tightly around valves/pipes, and securing with tape/straps — demanding bimanual dexterity, precise manipulation of floppy materials, and spatial reasoning in a cluttered utility space. Well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Examine eyes, using observation, instruments, and pharmaceutical agents, to determine visual acuity and perception, focus, and coordination and to diagnose diseases and other abnormalities, such as glaucoma or color blindness.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full eye examination for one patient, including history, visual acuity tests, refraction, slit-lamp, tonometry, and dilation; typical comprehensive exam takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, safe pharmaceutical administration, fine instrument manipulation (slit-lamp, tonometer), patient communication, and medical diagnosis — far beyond current humanoid robot capability in perception, dexterity, and autonomous clinical reasoning.

task: Construct and position properties, sets, lighting equipment, and other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a full scene/set with props, lighting rigs, and equipment. A competent crew member takes ~2 hours for a typical stage or film set setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse tool use (rigging, clamps, cables), precise spatial positioning per creative direction, heavy/awkward object handling, climbing ladders, and interpreting abstract artistic intent — far beyond current robot generalization.

task: Repair or replace musical instrument parts and components, such as strings, bridges, felts, and keys, using hand and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing or replacing a single component (e.g., restringing a guitar, replacing a piano key felt). A competent luthier/technician takes ~30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterity (threading strings, seating tiny felts, precise bridge fitting), acoustic judgment, and use of specialized hand tools. Well beyond current humanoid fine-motor and force-feedback capabilities.

task: Reassemble instruments following repair, using hand tools and power tools and glue, hair, yarn, resin, or clamps, and lubricate instruments as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reassembling one repaired instrument (e.g., a violin or woodwind). A competent luthier/technician typically takes 30–90 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Extreme fine dexterity required: applying glue, setting hair/yarn, positioning delicate parts, clamping precisely, and lubricating mechanisms. Highly varied instruments, unstructured steps, and sub-millimeter tolerances far exceed current humanoid robot capability.

task: Calibrate liquid flow devices and meters, including fuel, chemical, and water meters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = calibrating a single flow meter/device. A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min including setup, running test flows, adjusting, and documenting results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of calibration fittings and valves, reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting flow readings, making precise adjustments, and using specialized test equipment — far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Solder posts and parts to hold them in their proper places.
human_time_rationale: One instance = soldering a set of posts/parts on a single board or assembly. A competent technician typically takes ~10 min per board for placement and soldering.
robot_success_rationale: Soldering requires extreme fine dexterity, precise thermal control, and steady tool manipulation well beyond current humanoid capability. No frontier general-purpose robot can reliably solder in unstructured settings as of 2026.

task: Assemble and install new pipe organs and pianos in buildings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and installing one pipe organ or piano in a building. Pipe organs take days to weeks; pianos several hours. Median across both ~2 full working days (2,880 min).
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme precision with thousands of delicate pipes/components, heavy lifting, acoustic tuning, multi-story structural work, and expert craftsmanship. Far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity, planning, and tool-use capabilities.

task: Collect and discard worn machine parts and other refuse to maintain machinery and work areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance round collecting worn parts and refuse from a machine/work area. A competent worker typically takes 15–25 minutes per round.
robot_success_rationale: Mixed loose/heavy/irregular worn parts in cluttered industrial environments challenge perception and grasping. Navigation around machinery is feasible but identifying "worn" vs. good parts requires semantic understanding. Disposal step adds complexity. Current robots struggle with unstructured industrial debris.

task: Refinish instruments to protect and decorate them, using hand tools, buffing tools, and varnish.
human_time_rationale: One instance = refinishing one instrument (e.g., a guitar). Includes stripping, sanding, applying varnish coats, and buffing; a competent luthier typically spends ~2 hours of active hands-on work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for sanding contours, brush/spray control, multi-step sequencing, tactile pressure sensitivity, and aesthetic judgment across complex curved surfaces. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Splice cables, using hand tools, epoxy, or mechanical equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = splicing one cable joint. A competent technician strips, aligns, joins, and seals/secures a typical cable splice in ~30–60 min depending on cable type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity (stripping insulation, aligning conductors, applying epoxy, using hand tools), tactile feedback, and sequenced multi-step manipulation far beyond current humanoid capabilities in unstructured field settings.

task: Explain or demonstrate correct operation or preventive maintenance of medical equipment to personnel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one training/demonstration session for a piece of medical equipment (e.g., ventilator, infusion pump). Includes setup, demo, Q&A; ~30–60 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires verbal instruction, contextual medical knowledge, adaptive Q&A, and social communication. Current humanoids lack reliable speech-based teaching, domain expertise delivery, and the interpersonal judgment needed for medical training contexts.

task: Lubricate or apply adhesives or other materials to machines, machine parts, or other equipment according to specified procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one lubrication/adhesive application job on a machine or part set. Competent worker spends ~15 min including locating points, applying material, and wiping excess.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise targeting of lubrication/adhesive points on varied machinery, controlled dispensing pressure, tool handling (grease gun, brush, nozzle), and procedural compliance. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-motor dispensing control and generalization across machine types.

task: Perform on-site field work which may involve interviewing people, inspecting and identifying artifacts, note-taking, viewing sites and collections, and repainting exhibition spaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single field visit/session. A competent curator/archaeologist typically spends 2–5 hours on-site interviewing, inspecting artifacts, taking notes, and assessing spaces.
robot_success_rationale: Task bundles highly disparate subtasks: open-ended human interviewing (requires natural language + social intelligence), artifact identification (expert perception), note-taking, and painting walls. No 2026 robot handles this full suite autonomously and acceptably.

task: Dismantle meters, and replace or adjust defective parts such as cases, shafts, gears, disks, and recording mechanisms, using soldering irons and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling one meter, diagnosing and replacing/adjusting defective parts, and reassembling. A skilled technician typically takes 30–60 minutes per meter.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of small gears, shafts, and precision parts; soldering; and diagnostic judgment. Current humanoids lack the fine-motor precision, tool-switching, and fault-diagnosis capability needed for sub-millimeter meter repair.

task: Conduct standardized biological, microbiological or biochemical tests and laboratory analyses to evaluate the quantity or quality of physical or chemical substances in food or other products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one standard lab test/analysis (e.g., microbial plate count, pH, titration). Includes sample prep, running the assay, recording results; ~60–120 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting, sterile technique, equipment calibration, reagent handling, and data interpretation. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and cognition; no commercially deployed system handles full lab workflows autonomously.

task: Drive crew trucks to and from work areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one trip (to or from a work area). Typical crew truck transit on a job site or public road averages ~15–25 minutes per leg.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot drive trucks in traffic. L4 AVs are narrow-domain systems, not general-purpose humanoids. Operating a crew truck on varied/unpaved routes with passengers is well beyond 2026 frontier robot capability.

task: Examine and test wired electrical components such as motors, armatures, and stators, using measuring devices, and record test results.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single component (e.g., one motor/armature/stator): connect leads, run measurements (resistance, continuity, insulation), log results. ~20–30 min for a competent tech.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise probe placement on small terminals, connecting test leads to specific pins, operating a multimeter/megger, interpreting readings, and logging results. Fine dexterity and tool-use with electrical test equipment far exceeds 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Feed livestock or laboratory animals.
human_time_rationale: One feeding session for a typical pen/group of animals — gathering feed, filling troughs/dispensers, and confirming all animals are fed. Scales with herd/colony size; 20 min is a reasonable median unit.
robot_success_rationale: Highly unstructured environments (pens, cages, mud, animals moving unpredictably), varied feed types and dispensing methods, animal avoidance behavior, and outdoor/rough terrain all severely limit current humanoid robot capability.

task: Set up cameras, optical printers, and related equipment to produce photographs and special effects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a full camera/optical printer rig for a shoot or effects session; a competent technician typically spends 30–60 minutes configuring, aligning, and testing equipment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise optical alignment, fine cable management, and expert tool-use across highly specialized, non-standardized equipment. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, domain-specific perception, and procedural knowledge needed for acceptable setup quality.

task: Edit films and videotapes to insert music, dialogue, and sound effects, to arrange films into sequences, and to correct errors, using editing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One editing session for a short film/video segment (~5–10 min output). Skilled editor arranges sequences, syncs audio, and corrects errors; typically 2–4 hours per finished segment.
robot_success_rationale: Film editing is a cognitive, creative, and software-interface task requiring nuanced aesthetic judgment and complex GUI operation. Current humanoid robots lack the software integration, creative reasoning, and fine motor precision for keyboard/mouse-driven NLE workflows.

task: Remove irregularities from tuning pins, strings, and hammers of pianos, using wood blocks or filing tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing one piano's pins, strings, and hammers. A skilled piano technician spends ~30–60 min on this filing/voicing step per piano.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, precise tactile feedback, and specialized tool use (files, wood blocks) on tiny, delicate piano components in cramped interior spaces. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Attach air hoses to meter inlets, plug outlets, and observe gauges for pressure losses to test internal seams for leaks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = leak test on a single meter. Attaching hoses, plugging outlets, stabilizing pressure, and reading gauges takes ~10 min for a skilled technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise coupling of air hoses to small inlet fittings, threading/plugging outlets, and reading analog/digital gauges — demanding fine dexterity, tool-use, and interpretation. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-thread manipulation and sensor-reading integration for this specialized task.

task: Apply spackling, compounding, or other materials to repair holes in walls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single small-to-medium hole (e.g., nail hole or small drywall damage): apply compound, smooth, allow brief set. ~20 min including prep and cleanup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (putty knife/spackle application), precise surface feathering, visual quality judgment of smoothness and flush finish. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control and surface perception needed for acceptable results.

task: Respond to breech birth presentations by applying methods such as exercises or external version.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one external cephalic version (ECV) procedure or guided exercise session for a single patient. A trained OB typically spends 30–90 minutes including assessment, attempt, and monitoring.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert obstetric judgment, nuanced tactile force control on a pregnant abdomen, real-time ultrasound interpretation, and clinical decision-making. Far beyond any current humanoid robot's medical dexterity or autonomy.

task: Update existing equipment, performing tasks such as installing updated circuit boards or additional memory.
human_time_rationale: One instance = upgrading a single piece of equipment (e.g., swapping a circuit board or adding RAM). A competent technician typically takes 20–40 minutes including disassembly, swap, and reassembly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for small connectors, ESD-sensitive components, and varied form factors. Identifying correct slots, applying precise insertion force, and managing cables in confined spaces far exceeds current humanoid robot capability.

task: Stop machines to remove completed components, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stopping one machine, removing one completed component with hand tools. A skilled operator typically takes 3–7 minutes including stopping, unloading, and setting the part aside.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct machine controls, actuating stops, then grasping varied completed parts with hand tools in a semi-structured industrial environment. Dexterity with hand tools, button/switch operation, and part extraction in tight machine spaces remain challenging for current humanoids.

task: Repair shop equipment, metal furniture, or hospital equipment, including welding broken parts or replacing missing parts, or bring item into local shop for major repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single piece of equipment/furniture. Median repair (diagnosing, welding/replacing a part, or transporting to shop) takes ~1 hour for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosis, fine dexterous welding or part replacement, and potentially driving to a shop — all far beyond current humanoid capabilities. Tool use, open-ended problem-solving, and welding precision are severe barriers.

task: Perform routine maintenance on mechanical, electrical, or electronic equipment or instruments used in the processing of biofuels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one routine maintenance cycle on a single piece of biofuel processing equipment (e.g., pump, sensor, valve). A competent technician typically spends ~45–90 min per unit including inspection, lubrication, adjustment, and logging.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized tool use, fluid handling, electrical diagnostics, fine dexterous adjustments, and contextual judgment in a complex industrial environment. Current humanoids lack the precision, domain-specific tooling, and autonomous diagnostic reasoning needed.

task: Operate equipment to produce programs or broadcast live programs from remote locations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single remote broadcast session (setup, live broadcast, teardown). Typical field production runs 1–3 hours; 2 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized broadcast equipment (cameras, switchers, satellite uplinks, audio boards) in unpredictable outdoor/remote environments, making real-time editorial decisions, and coordinating with production staff — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Operate valves, pumps, engines, or generators to control and adjust production of biofuels or biomass-fueled power.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one operational adjustment cycle (checking, tuning, and adjusting valves/pumps/engines/generators) during a production shift; ~30 min median for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating complex industrial equipment (valves, pumps, engines, generators) in dynamic biofuel plant environments. Humanoids lack the specialized interfaces, tool-use, and process knowledge; safety-critical precision makes autonomous success extremely unlikely in 2026.

task: Examine valves or mechanical control device parts for defects, dents, or loose attachments, and mark malfunctioning areas of defective units.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one valve/control device unit. A competent inspector visually and tactilely checks all surfaces, tests attachments, and marks defects in roughly 5 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can perform basic visual inspection but struggle with tactile defect detection (dents, looseness), reliably marking specific defect locations, handling varied part geometries, and meeting quality standards a supervisor would accept. Perception and dexterous marking remain limiting.

task: Disassemble and assemble motors, and repair and maintain electrical components and machinery parts, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassemble, inspect/repair, and reassemble one motor. A competent mechanic typically takes 60–120 min depending on motor size and fault type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool-use (wrenches, screwdrivers, multimeters), fault diagnosis, and handling varied components in unstructured settings. Current humanoids lack the precision, force control, and contextual reasoning for autonomous motor repair.

task: Operate zoom lenses, changing images according to specifications and rehearsal instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single zoom adjustment to a specified framing during a live shoot or rehearsal. A skilled camera operator performs this in seconds to a few minutes per cue.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor control of zoom rings/servo controls, real-time visual feedback interpretation, cue-following from verbal/script instructions, and creative framing judgment — all far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Align antennae with receiving dishes to obtain the clearest signal for transmission of broadcasts from field locations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning one antenna/dish setup at a field location. A skilled technician typically spends ~15–25 minutes adjusting azimuth, elevation, and polarization while monitoring signal quality metrics.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting RF signal feedback, fine mechanical adjustment of antenna orientation, operating specialized equipment, and working outdoors on varied terrain. Current humanoids lack RF signal perception, cannot operate dish-alignment tools autonomously, and lack domain-specific calibration workflows.

task: Plan, prepare, or carry out individually designed programs of physical treatment to maintain, improve, or restore physical functioning, alleviate pain, or prevent physical dysfunction in patients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single individualized physical therapy session per patient, including assessment, treatment delivery, and documentation. Typical PT sessions run 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical reasoning, individualized program design, nuanced tactile assessment, therapeutic manual techniques, and safe patient interaction. Far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and clinical judgment capabilities.

task: Test electrical components of wind systems with devices, such as voltage testers, multimeters, oscilloscopes, infrared testers, or fiber optic equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing electrical components on one wind turbine. A skilled technician spends ~30–60 min connecting instruments, running tests, and recording results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing/navigating a turbine structure, selecting and precisely connecting multiple specialized test instruments to varied terminals, interpreting readings, and making judgment calls — far beyond current general-purpose robot dexterity, tool-use, and generalization limits.

task: Operate or tend wire-coiling machines to wind wire coils used in electrical components such as resistors and transformers, and in electrical equipment and instruments such as bobbins and generators.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running one production batch/coil on a wire-coiling machine, including loading wire, adjusting parameters, monitoring winding, and unloading the finished coil.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation (threading fine wire, tensioning, securing leads), precise machine parameter adjustment, and fault monitoring. Current humanoids lack the finger dexterity and tactile feedback needed; industrial arms require task-specific fixturing.

task: Calibrate liquid flow devices or meters, including fuel, chemical, and water meters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = calibrating a single flow meter. A competent technician sets up reference equipment, runs test flows, adjusts settings, and documents results — typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of valves and adjustment screws, reading analog/digital gauges, connecting fluid lines without leaks, and interpreting calibration data — well beyond current general-purpose robot capability in unstructured field environments.

task: Repair, restore, and reassemble artifacts, designing and fabricating missing or broken parts, to restore them to their original appearance and prevent deterioration.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restoring a single artifact. Skilled conservators typically spend 2–6 hours per piece including assessment, fabrication, and reassembly; 4 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level fine dexterity, material science judgment, custom part fabrication, and aesthetic matching. Far beyond current robot capability in perception, fine manipulation, and generalization to unique, fragile, irregular artifacts.

task: Determine whether objects need repair and choose the safest and most effective method of repair.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one object, diagnosing damage, and deciding on a repair method. A skilled worker takes ~20 min including evaluation and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal damage diagnosis (visual, tactile, functional), contextual judgment about repair methods, and generalization across object types. Current robots lack reliable fault reasoning and repair-method selection.

task: Inspect biofuels plant or processing equipment regularly, recording or reporting damage and mechanical problems.
human_time_rationale: One inspection round of a biofuels processing area: walking the floor, checking equipment visually/manually, and logging findings. Typical shift inspection takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Biofuels plants have complex, hazardous, unstructured industrial environments. Robots lack reliable gas/odor sensing, contextual fault diagnosis, and safe navigation around piping, valves, and wet/slippery surfaces. Recording and reporting structured findings autonomously is also immature.

task: Clean biofuels processing work area, ensuring compliance with safety regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one biofuels processing work area post-shift; includes spill management, surface wiping, waste disposal, and safety compliance checks. Estimated 45 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Biofuels environments are hazardous, unstructured, and compliance-critical. Robots lack chemical hazard awareness, cannot reliably handle industrial spills, operate specialized equipment, or verify regulatory compliance. Explosion/chemical risks further restrict autonomous operation.

task: Plan placement of caskets at funeral sites or place or adjust lights, fixtures, or floral displays.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a single funeral site: positioning a casket, arranging lights/fixtures, and placing floral displays. A competent worker typically completes this in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires moving a large, heavy casket with precision, fine placement of floral/decorative arrangements, spatial planning in varied layouts, and aesthetic judgment. Current humanoids lack the strength, dexterity, and contextual reasoning for this multi-step, high-stakes setup.

task: Educate, prepare, and reassure patients and their families by answering questions, providing physical assistance, and reinforcing physicians' advice regarding treatment reactions or post-treatment care.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient/family education or reassurance interaction (answering questions, providing guidance, offering physical assist). Typical nursing encounter lasts ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced verbal communication, emotional reassurance, adaptive physical assistance, and medical judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable natural language empathy, clinical reasoning, and safe unstructured caregiving interactions — far below acceptable quality.

task: Preprocess feedstock in preparation for physical, chemical, or biological fuel production processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a batch of feedstock (e.g., shredding, sorting, sizing, or mixing material) for one production run; a competent operator typically takes ~1 hour per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Feedstock preprocessing involves operating heavy industrial equipment (shredders, conveyors, mixers), handling bulk/unstructured materials, and adjusting process parameters — well beyond current humanoid robot generalization, dexterity, and machinery-operation capabilities.

task: Perform routine maintenance or make minor repairs to mechanical, electrical, or electronic equipment in biomass plants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance/repair job (e.g., replacing a belt, lubricating bearings, tightening connections). A competent technician typically spends ~60 min per discrete task in a plant setting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex industrial plant environments, using specialized tools, diagnosing faults, and performing precise mechanical/electrical repairs. Current humanoids lack the tool-use dexterity, fault-diagnosis capability, and safety compliance for unstructured industrial maintenance.

task: Package, store, or move irradiated fuel elements in the underwater storage basins of nuclear reactor plants, using machines or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving/packaging one irradiated fuel assembly in a spent-fuel pool; includes rigging, remote equipment operation, positioning, and verification checks, typically ~60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized underwater nuclear handling machines, working in radiation-controlled wet environments, and meeting strict regulatory tolerances. No general-purpose humanoid robot is rated or capable for this domain; dedicated remote handling systems exist but are task-specific, not general robots.

task: Operate equipment, such as a centrifuge, to extract biofuels products and secondary by-products or reusable fractions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up, running, and collecting output from a single centrifuge batch/cycle. A competent technician typically spends ~30 min including prep, monitoring, and fraction collection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized lab/industrial equipment with precise controls, handling liquid fractions, interpreting instrument readouts, and adjusting parameters. Current humanoids lack the domain-specific tool interfacing, fine fluid handling, and process monitoring needed.

task: Close caskets and lead funeral corteges to churches or burial sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = closing a casket and leading one funeral cortege. Latching/closing takes ~5 min; leading/driving the procession to site takes ~30–40 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Closing a casket lid requires moderate dexterity but is feasible; however, leading a cortege requires driving a hearse or walking/directing traffic in public roads — beyond any current humanoid's capabilities. Social sensitivity and coordination add further barriers.

task: Inspect, clean, test, and maintain respiratory therapy equipment to ensure equipment is functioning safely and efficiently, ordering repairs when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full inspect/clean/test/maintain cycle for one piece of respiratory equipment (e.g., ventilator or nebulizer); a competent RT typically spends 30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly specialized clinical knowledge, fine dexterous disassembly/reassembly of intricate medical components, functional testing with calibrated instruments, judgment to order repairs, and compliance with medical safety standards — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Apply glazes, icings, or other toppings to baked goods, using spatulas or brushes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying glaze/icing/topping to a single baked item (e.g. one cake or a tray of pastries). A skilled baker takes ~2–4 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity with a spatula/brush, controlled force, smooth spreading motion, and aesthetic judgment. Current humanoids lack the precise compliant wrist control and visual quality feedback needed for consistent, acceptable results.

task: Install, maintain, or repair security systems, alarm devices, or related equipment, following blueprints of electrical layouts and building plans.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing or servicing a single security system in a typical commercial/residential site. A competent technician averages ~3 hours per job including wiring, mounting, and testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading blueprints, routing and terminating wires, drilling into walls, mounting devices at varied heights, and diagnosing faults — demanding fine dexterity, tool use, spatial reasoning, and blueprint interpretation far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Operate valves, pumps, engines, or generators to control and adjust biofuels production.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single control adjustment cycle (checking gauges, tuning valves/pumps/engines) during a production shift; a competent operator completes this in ~30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating industrial valves, pumps, and control panels in complex, unstructured process environments. Current humanoids lack the process knowledge integration, fine valve manipulation, and safe interaction with pressurized/hazardous industrial equipment needed for autonomous control.

task: Deliver pianos to purchasers or to locations of their use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one piano delivery trip: loading, driving to destination, unloading/placing the piano. Median trip including transit and placement ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a truck in traffic (humanoids cannot), operating specialized piano dollies/straps, navigating stairs with a heavy bulky load, and precise placement — all far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Inspect biomass power plant or processing equipment, recording or reporting damage and mechanical problems.
human_time_rationale: One inspection round of a biomass plant or major equipment unit; a competent technician walks the facility, checks components, and logs findings — typically 45–90 minutes per round.
robot_success_rationale: Biomass plants involve confined spaces, extreme clutter, heat, vibration, wet/dusty environments, and highly varied industrial equipment. Robots lack reliable fault-recognition, report generation, and safe navigation in such unstructured, hazardous industrial sites.

task: Install solar thermal system controllers and sensors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a controller unit and its associated sensors for a single solar thermal system. A competent technician typically takes 1.5–2.5 hours for mounting, wiring, and commissioning.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise electrical wiring, conduit routing, reading schematics, operating hand tools in confined/outdoor spaces, and commissioning software. Far beyond 2026 humanoid generalization and fine dexterous capability in unstructured environments.

task: Assist in the cleanup of hazardous material spills.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one hazmat spill cleanup event. A competent worker in PPE assesses, contains, neutralizes, and disposes of a typical small-to-medium spill in ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires donning PPE, identifying hazardous substances via chemical perception, applying specific neutralizing agents, safe containment/disposal, and regulatory compliance. Current robots lack chemical sensing, adaptive PPE use, and safe judgment in unstructured hazmat environments.

task: Clean objects, such as paper, textiles, wood, metal, glass, rock, pottery, and furniture, using cleansers, solvents, soap solutions, and polishes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single object (e.g., one piece of furniture or artifact). A competent worker spends ~15 min selecting the right cleanser, applying it, and finishing the surface.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle basic wiping/mopping but struggle with material-specific cleanser selection, gentle pressure calibration on fragile surfaces (pottery, paper, textiles), and tool/solvent switching across diverse object types.

task: Perform pulmonary function and adjust equipment to obtain optimum results in therapy.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient PFT session: setup, test, adjust equipment, review results. A skilled respiratory therapist typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of medical equipment, real-time clinical interpretation of patient physiology, patient coaching/communication, and adaptive decision-making. Far beyond current humanoid robot autonomy in unstructured clinical settings.

task: Combine measured ingredients in bowls of mixing, blending, or cooking machinery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = combining a set of pre-measured ingredients into a mixing bowl or machine; a competent worker pours/scoops and loads in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires pouring liquids, scooping powders, and loading varied containers into machinery — challenging for fluid handling, precise pouring, and operating appliance controls. Dexterity and perception gaps lower success significantly.

task: Instruct trainees in use of television production equipment, filming of events, and copying and editing graphics or sound onto videotape.
human_time_rationale: One training session covering TV production equipment use, event filming, and graphics/sound editing for a group of trainees; ~90 min is a typical instructional block.
robot_success_rationale: Instructing trainees requires natural language explanation, adaptive pedagogy, demonstration, and social interaction. Current robots lack teaching cognition, curriculum design, and effective human communication at an acceptable quality.

task: Operate high-pressure steam boiler or water chiller equipment for electrical cogeneration operations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one operational shift check/adjustment cycle on boiler/chiller systems; a competent operator inspects gauges, adjusts controls, and logs readings roughly once per hour.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting complex analog/digital control panels, adjusting valves under pressure, and making safety-critical judgments. Humanoids lack the specialized tool-use, certified training integration, and reliable fine control for high-stakes industrial plant equipment.

task: Monitor strength, clarity, and reliability of incoming and outgoing signals, and adjust equipment as necessary to maintain quality broadcasts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring/adjustment cycle checking signal meters, assessing quality, and tuning equipment. A competent technician typically spends ~15–25 minutes per pass.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading specialized signal displays, interpreting broadcast quality metrics, and manually adjusting technical equipment controls. Current humanoids lack the domain-specific perception, diagnostic reasoning, and precise tool-use for broadcast hardware.

task: Set time and speed controls for mixing machines, blending machines, or steam kettles so that ingredients will be mixed or cooked according to instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting controls on one machine for one batch. A competent worker reads instructions and adjusts dials/settings in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading instructions, identifying correct machine controls (dials, digital panels, knobs), and making precise adjustments. Perception and fine manipulation of varied control interfaces challenge current humanoids; generalization across machine types is limited.

task: Remove loose wires and other debris after work is completed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing loose wires and debris from a single work area after a job. A competent worker picks up, sorts, and disposes of debris in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Loose wires are flexible, tangled, and irregular — very hard to grasp reliably. Mixed debris requires recognition and sorting. Cluttered, unstructured post-work environments challenge navigation and manipulation significantly.

task: Apply solutions or paints to wired electrical components, using hand tools, and bake components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying solution/paint to a batch of wired electrical components and placing them in a bake oven. A competent worker typically completes this in ~25 minutes including prep, application, and loading the oven.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise brushing/coating on delicate wired components (fine dexterity), navigating hand tools, and operating an industrial oven. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control and tool-use reliability needed; high risk of damaging fragile wiring.

task: Operate slicing or wrapping machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = slicing or wrapping one batch/item (e.g., a loaf of bread or a packaged product). A competent worker completes this in ~5 minutes including setup and placement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise positioning of variable items into industrial machines, managing feed rates, and ensuring safe operation. Robots struggle with varied product shapes, machine interface controls, and safety protocols around moving blades.

task: Store, sterilize, or prepare the special applicators containing the radioactive substance implanted by the physician.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing/sterilizing/storing a set of radioactive applicators for one procedure. A trained radiation therapy tech typically takes ~20–40 min including safety checks and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling radioactive materials under strict regulatory protocols, fine dexterity with specialized medical applicators, sterile technique, radiation safety compliance, and contextual judgment — far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Operate equipment to heat biomass, using knowledge of controls, combustion, and firing mechanisms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one operational firing cycle (startup through stable operation). A skilled biomass equipment operator spends ~45 min on controls, combustion checks, and system stabilization.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial control panels, interpreting analog/digital gauges, adjusting combustion parameters, and responding to dynamic system states. Humanoids cannot reliably interface with varied industrial controls or make combustion judgments autonomously.

task: Climb poles and ladders, use truck-mounted booms, and enter areas such as manholes and cable vaults to install, maintain, or inspect equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one service visit (pole climb, boom use, or confined-space entry). A skilled lineworker spends ~60 min including travel to site, setup, access, and the work itself.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing poles/ladders with safety gear, operating truck-mounted booms (vehicle control), and navigating confined spaces like manholes — all extreme mobility/dexterity challenges far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Lift workpieces to machines manually or with hoists or cranes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lifting a single workpiece to a machine. Includes positioning, attaching hoist/crane if needed, and placing. Typical shop floor cycle: ~3–7 min.
robot_success_rationale: Heavy/varied workpieces exceed humanoid payload limits; operating hoists/cranes requires specialized tool-use and rigging skills; unstructured factory floors add locomotion and perception challenges. Narrow industrial arms excluded.

task: Assist in assembly of individual wind generators or construction of wind farms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one worker's shift contribution to assembling/erecting a single wind turbine unit; full turbine assembly involves a multi-person crew over several days, so one worker-shift (~8 hrs) is the measurable unit.
robot_success_rationale: Wind turbine assembly requires operating heavy cranes, working at extreme heights, handling massive components in outdoor/unstructured terrain, and using specialized tools. Current humanoid robots cannot operate heavy machinery, climb towers, or work safely in high-wind outdoor environments.

task: Observe and reassure patients during treatment and report unusual reactions to physician or turn equipment off if unexpected adverse reactions occur.
human_time_rationale: One instance = monitoring a single patient through one treatment session. Typical sessions (e.g., physical therapy, dialysis, infusion) last 20–45 minutes; 30 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced patient assessment (pain, distress, subtle physiological cues), natural language reassurance, clinical judgment on adverse reactions, and reliable emergency response — all far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Install storm windows or storm doors and verify proper fit.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing and verifying fit of one storm window or door. A competent worker measures, fits, secures, and checks seals in ~30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, lifting/aligning large panels, drilling/fastening into frames, and verifying seal quality. Demands high dexterity, tool use, force control, and judgment in variable outdoor settings — well beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Repair electric meters and components, such as transformers and relays, and replace metering devices, dial glasses, and faulty or incorrect wiring, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/replacing one meter or component (e.g., relay, transformer, dial glass, wiring). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous hand-tool use, precise electrical component handling, fault diagnosis, and wiring work in varied field environments. Far beyond current general-purpose robot dexterity and autonomous diagnostic capability.

task: Examine physiological, morphological, and cultural characteristics, using microscope, to identify and classify microorganisms in human, water, and food specimens.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and examining one specimen slide set, including staining, microscopy, and classification. Typically 30–60 min for a trained microbiologist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine-motor dexterity for slide prep and staining, precise microscope operation, and expert-level visual/cognitive interpretation of microorganisms. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured lab settings.

task: Operate forklifts, pallet jacks, power lifts, or front-end loaders to load bales, bundles, or other heavy items onto trucks for shipping to smelters or other recycled materials processing facilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading one truck. A competent operator spends ~30–60 min maneuvering a forklift/loader to stage and load a full truck with bales/bundles.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating forklifts, pallet jacks, or front-end loaders in dynamic outdoor/industrial yards. Humanoid robots cannot drive or operate these vehicles in traffic/real conditions; task-specific automation exists but not general-purpose humanoids.

task: Set up, adjust, calibrate, clean, maintain, and troubleshoot laboratory and field equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full maintenance/calibration cycle on a single piece of lab or field equipment; a competent technician typically spends 30–60 min per instrument.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert tool-use, fine dexterity for delicate instruments, domain-specific calibration knowledge, fault diagnosis, and adaptability to highly varied equipment types — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Calculate, measure, load, or mix refined feedstock used in biofuels production.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single batch preparation cycle (measuring, mixing, and loading feedstock for one production run), typically 15–25 minutes for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating industrial facilities, operating valves/pumps/scales, precise measurement, and chemical handling. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use with industrial equipment, fine-grained flow control, and safe chemical process generalization.

task: Listen to machines during operation to detect sounds such as those made by dull cutting tools or excessive vibration, and adjust machines to compensate for problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = monitoring a single machine during a short operational cycle (~5 min), detecting an anomalous sound, diagnosing the cause, and making the compensating adjustment.
robot_success_rationale: Acoustic anomaly detection via microphones is feasible, but diagnosing root cause (dull tool vs. vibration vs. other), mapping sound to the correct adjustment, and physically manipulating machine controls in unstructured environments is beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Observe color of products being baked, and adjust oven temperatures, humidity, or conveyor speeds accordingly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = observing one batch and making one round of control adjustments; a skilled operator inspects and adjusts in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Color inspection via vision is feasible, but physically manipulating industrial oven controls (dials, touchscreens, conveyor panels) in an unstructured bakery environment, and correctly mapping color observations to multi-variable adjustments, is well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Examine patients to obtain information about their physical condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient physical examination (history-taking + hands-on assessment). A competent clinician typically spends 15–25 minutes per encounter.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social interaction, clinical judgment, fine tactile perception (palpation, auscultation), adaptive questioning, and interpretation of subtle physical signs — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Deposit recoverable materials into chutes or place materials on conveyor belts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = depositing a single item/batch into a chute or onto a conveyor belt. A competent worker grabs, moves, and releases material in ~1–2 minutes per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Structured pick-and-place onto a conveyor or into a chute is within current humanoid capability, but variable material shapes, weights, and cluttered environments reduce reliability. Perception and grasp generalization are the main bottlenecks.

task: Set up and operate portable field transmission equipment outside the studio.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and operating equipment for a single field broadcast. A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min to unpack, position, cable, configure, and test portable transmission gear on-site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex cable routing, fine connector insertion, antenna alignment, software configuration, and situational judgment outdoors. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, domain-specific tool-use, and autonomous configuration logic needed for broadcast-grade equipment setup.

task: Seal joints with putty, mortar, and asbestos, using putty extruders and knives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sealing one joint (~1–2 m). Worker preps surface, loads putty/mortar, applies bead with extruder, and smooths with knife in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled tool use (extruder gun, putty knife), contour-following along irregular joints, and material consistency judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous tool manipulation and fine surface finishing at acceptable quality.

task: Sort materials, such as metals, glass, wood, paper or plastics, into appropriate containers for recycling.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting a single item or small batch (~5–10 items) into correct recycling bins; a competent worker takes ~1–3 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can grasp and place common recyclables, but material classification across diverse/dirty/irregular items (crumpled paper, mixed plastics, wet glass) requires robust perception and generalization. Bin navigation and grasping varied shapes add challenge.

task: Remove dents and burrs from metal instruments, using mallets and burnishing tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing one metal instrument. A skilled technician typically spends 15–25 minutes assessing, malleting, and burnishing dents/burrs from a single instrument.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly precise force-controlled dexterity, tactile feedback to feel burrs and dents, and expert judgment on mallet/burnishing pressure. Far beyond current humanoid manipulation capabilities; fine metalworking demands sub-millimeter precision and nuanced surface sensing.

task: Make minor repairs using basic hand or power tools and materials, such as glass, lumber, and drywall.
human_time_rationale: One repair instance (e.g., patching drywall, replacing a pane, or fixing a small wood piece) typically takes 30–60 min for a competent handyman, averaging ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step tool use (saws, drills, putty knives, caulk guns) with precise force control, material handling of glass/lumber/drywall, and adaptive decision-making. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity.

task: Compose and frame each shot, applying the technical aspects of light, lenses, film, filters, and camera settings to achieve the effects sought by directors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and capturing a single camera shot on a film/TV set, including framing, lighting adjustments, lens/filter selection, and confirming settings. Competent cinematographer takes ~30–60 min per shot.
robot_success_rationale: Requires deep aesthetic judgment, director communication, nuanced light manipulation, manual lens/filter changes, and creative problem-solving. Current robots lack creative perception, complex tool-use with professional camera rigs, and collaborative artistic decision-making.

task: Perform routine maintenance on wind turbine equipment, underground transmission systems, wind fields substations, or fiber optic sensing and control systems.
human_time_rationale: One routine maintenance visit on a single wind turbine or substation system; a competent technician typically spends 3–5 hours on inspections, lubrication, torque checks, and diagnostics per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing tall turbine towers, navigating confined nacelles, operating specialized tools, interfacing with high-voltage electrical systems, and traversing outdoor/underground terrain — all far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Operate recycling processing equipment, such as sorters, balers, crushers, and granulators to sort and process materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full operating shift (8 hours) running recycling processing equipment, monitoring throughput, adjusting settings, and managing material flow.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large industrial machines with complex controls, responding to jams, adjusting conveyor/sorter settings, and making real-time quality judgments. Humanoids lack the reach, strength, and machine-interface capability for heavy recycling equipment in unstructured facilities.

task: Conduct postmortem studies and analyses to determine the causes of animals' deaths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one animal postmortem examination. A competent veterinary pathologist takes ~60–120 min for a full necropsy, including dissection, tissue sampling, documentation, and preliminary findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert veterinary knowledge, fine dexterous dissection of varied anatomy, interpretive judgment, tissue sampling, and documentation. Far beyond current robot dexterity, generalization, and cognitive reasoning capabilities.

task: Apply weather seal, such as pipe flashings and sealants, to roof penetrations and structural devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sealing all penetrations on a typical residential roof section. A roofer installs pipe flashings, applies sealant to vents/pipes/structural devices in ~30–60 min per job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing/navigating an unstable sloped roof, fine dexterous manipulation of flashing and caulk guns around irregular penetrations, and judgment for sealant coverage. Current humanoids lack safe roof mobility, outdoor unstructured terrain handling, and precise sealant application skill.

task: Operate diagnostic equipment, such as radiographic or ultrasound equipment, and interpret the resulting images.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient imaging session (setup, scan acquisition, and preliminary image interpretation). A competent radiologic technologist/radiologist typically takes ~20–40 minutes end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating complex, calibrated medical imaging hardware, patient positioning, and expert clinical interpretation. No current general-purpose humanoid can safely operate radiographic/ultrasound equipment or provide medically acceptable diagnostic readings.

task: Measure or weigh flour or other ingredients to prepare batters, doughs, fillings, or icings, using scales or graduated containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring/weighing all ingredients for a single batch (e.g., one dough or batter). A competent baker typically completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating varied containers/scales, scooping/pouring granular/liquid ingredients accurately, reading measurement markings, and adjusting pours — demanding fine dexterity, precise force control, and visual feedback. Current humanoids struggle with fluid/powder handling and reading small graduations reliably.

task: Report equipment problems, ensure that repairs are made, and make emergency repairs to equipment when necessary and possible.
human_time_rationale: One instance = identifying a problem, reporting it, and either arranging or performing a basic emergency repair. A competent worker typically spends ~30 min across diagnosis, documentation, and fix.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults via multi-modal perception, communicating reports, and performing unstructured repairs with varied tools. Emergency repairs demand dexterity, generalization, and judgment far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Clean and maintain tools and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and basic maintenance of a standard set of tools/equipment after use (e.g., wiping, lubricating, storing). A competent worker takes ~15–25 minutes per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied grasping of irregular tools, applying precise wiping/lubricating motions, and recognizing different tool types and their maintenance needs. Generalization across tool shapes and conditions is still weak; fine dexterous manipulation and inspection remain limiting factors for 2026 robots.

task: Inoculate animals against various diseases, such as rabies or distemper.
human_time_rationale: One instance = vaccinating one animal. Includes restraint, syringe prep, injection, and recording. A competent vet tech completes this in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires restraining a live, unpredictable animal, precise needle injection into soft tissue, sterile syringe handling, and dose measurement. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, animal-handling skill, and safety compliance needed.

task: Position patients for treatment with accuracy, according to prescription.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning a single patient for one treatment session, including reading the prescription, adjusting patient body/limbs, and confirming alignment. Typically 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, compliant physical handling of a fragile human body, interpretation of medical prescriptions, patient communication, and real-time adjustment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, safety, and medical reasoning capabilities.

task: Inspect sporting equipment or examine participants to ensure compliance with event and safety regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one participant or set of gear. A sports official checks equipment or a single athlete's compliance; typical inspection takes 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual/tactile inspection (weight, dimensions, wear), rule-based judgment, and social interaction with participants. Current robots lack reliable fine-grained compliance reasoning, regulatory knowledge application, and human communication needed for this unstructured task.

task: Operate automated refuse or manual recycling collection vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one collection route shift (~4 hours), the standard unit a supervisor would measure for a refuse/recycling truck operator covering a set of stops.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot drive refuse trucks on public roads. Task requires operating large specialized vehicles in dynamic traffic, navigating varied curb setups, and managing mechanical arms/hoppers — entirely beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Prepare and apply weather-stripping, glazing, caulking, or door sweeps to reduce energy losses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sealing one door or window. A competent worker measures, cuts, and applies weather-stripping, caulk, or a door sweep to a single door/window in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, cutting flexible materials, applying even caulk beads, pressing adhesive strips into irregular gaps, and installing door sweeps with screws — highly dexterous, unstructured fine-motor work well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Set oven temperatures, and place items into hot ovens for baking.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting oven temp and loading one batch of items. A competent worker spends ~1 min adjusting controls and ~2 min placing items on racks and closing the door.
robot_success_rationale: Turning oven knobs/dials or pressing digital controls is marginally feasible, but safely handling hot oven racks, precisely placing varied food items, and operating in a cluttered kitchen environment remains unreliable for current humanoids.

task: Maintain machines and remove and replace broken or worn machine tools, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = identifying a worn/broken tool, removing it, and installing a replacement on a machine. A competent machinist or maintenance tech typically takes 30–60 min depending on tool type and machine access.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing worn tools, navigating cluttered industrial environments, applying precise torque with hand tools on varied fasteners, and verifying proper installation. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous tool-use, diagnostic judgment, and force control for this unstructured maintenance work.

task: Measure and monitor raw biofuels feedstock.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and monitoring a batch/lot of raw feedstock (e.g., corn, switchgrass, soy). Includes sampling, instrument readings, and logging. ~30 min per batch cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires collecting samples from bulk feedstock (unstructured piles/silos), operating specialized lab/field instruments, interpreting readings, and logging data. Current humanoids lack robust tool-use for industrial sampling equipment and domain-specific sensor interfaces.

task: Rebuild, repair, or replace biofuels processing equipment components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing or replacing a single component (e.g., pump, valve, seal) on biofuels processing equipment. A competent technician typically takes 1–3 hours per component.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized tools, high-torque fastener removal, fluid handling, and precise reassembly in cluttered industrial environments. Current humanoids lack the force control, tool-change dexterity, and domain knowledge for autonomous industrial maintenance tasks.

task: Adjust positions and controls of cameras, printers, and related equipment to change focus, exposure, and lighting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting settings on a camera/printer/related device for a single shoot or print job; a competent technician typically spends ~5–10 minutes tweaking focus, exposure, lighting, and controls.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of small knobs/dials, nuanced perceptual judgment of focus/exposure/lighting quality, and contextual decision-making. Current humanoids lack reliable fine motor control and visual feedback loops for such precision adjustments.

task: Attach, alter, and trim materials such as wire, insulation, and coils, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single attach/alter/trim operation on a component (e.g., stripping and connecting a wire, trimming a coil). A competent technician takes ~10–20 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity with hand tools (pliers, wire cutters, crimpers) on small, deformable materials like wire and insulation. Current humanoids lack the precision grip, force control, and tool-use generalization needed for reliable success.

task: Examine animals to detect and determine the nature of diseases or injuries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining one animal patient; a competent veterinarian typically spends 15–25 minutes on a standard physical exam and preliminary diagnosis.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert clinical reasoning, nuanced tactile palpation, auscultation interpretation, and handling uncooperative live animals. Current robots lack veterinary diagnostic cognition, compliant animal-safe manipulation, and integrated multi-modal sensing for disease detection.

task: Check equipment to ensure that it meets health and safety regulations, and perform maintenance or cleaning, as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking one equipment unit for health/safety compliance plus any minor maintenance or cleaning needed. Typical cycle for a competent worker in an industrial or commercial setting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal inspection (visual, tactile, auditory), reading gauges/labels, cross-referencing regulations, and performing varied maintenance. Generalization across equipment types and safety judgment remain well beyond 2026 frontier robots.

task: Examine electronic components for defects or completeness of laser-beam trimming, using microscopes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one electronic component under a microscope for defects or laser-trim completeness; a skilled technician typically takes 2–5 minutes per component.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine microscope manipulation, precise optical alignment, and expert visual interpretation of micron-scale features. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, specialized tool integration, and trained defect-recognition needed for reliable autonomous pass/fail judgments.
