task: String instruments, and adjust trusses and bridges of instruments to obtain specified string tensions and heights.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stringing and adjusting truss rod/bridge on one instrument. A skilled luthier takes ~30–60 min for full setup including tension and action tuning.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (winding strings, adjusting tiny truss rod nuts, filing bridge saddles), precise tactile/auditory feedback, and expert judgment about string height. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Assemble and install communication equipment such as data and telephone communication lines, wiring, switching equipment, wiring frames, power apparatus, computer systems, and networks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single communication equipment installation job (e.g., running data/telephone lines, mounting and wiring a switching unit). A competent technician typically spends 3–5 hours per install.
robot_success_rationale: Requires routing cables through walls/conduits, fine connector crimping, reading schematics, configuring switching equipment, and navigating complex unstructured environments. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and generalization capabilities.

task: Monitor patients' comfort and safety during tests, alerting physicians to abnormalities or changes in patient responses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = monitoring a single patient through one diagnostic test (e.g., stress test, MRI, EEG). Typical duration is 20–45 minutes; 30 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced interpretation of patient distress cues, verbal communication, clinical judgment, and real-time physician alerting. Current robots lack reliable social perception, medical reasoning, and trusted autonomous clinical decision-making in unstructured patient care settings.

task: Collect biofuels samples and perform routine laboratory tests or analyses to assess biofuels quality.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a biofuel sample and running a standard battery of quality tests (e.g., viscosity, flash point, water content). A competent lab tech typically takes 60–120 minutes end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized lab instruments (pipettes, titrators, spectrometers), precise fluid handling, interpreting multi-step chemical tests, and navigating a lab environment — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Provide physicians with assistance during surgery or complicated medical procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting through a single surgical procedure; median surgery (e.g., appendectomy, laparoscopic cholecystectomy) runs ~1.5–2 hours of active OR time.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sterile technique, real-time clinical judgment, and adaptive response to unpredictable anatomy and emergencies — far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capabilities. Specialized surgical robots (da Vinci) exist but are teleoperated, not autonomous humanoids.

task: Determine the number, type, and approximate location of microphones needed for best sound recording or transmission quality, and position them appropriately.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing a single recording environment, selecting mic types/quantities, and physically placing them. A competent audio engineer typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires acoustic expertise (room analysis, frequency response judgment, feedback avoidance), fine cable/clip dexterity, and nuanced perceptual evaluation. Current robots lack audio-domain reasoning and delicate mic-stand manipulation at acceptable quality.

task: Instruct patient in activities and techniques, such as sports, dance, music, art, or relaxation techniques, designed to meet their specific physical or psychological needs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single therapeutic instruction session with one patient. Typical sessions (recreational/occupational therapy) run 30–60 min; 45 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced clinical assessment, adaptive communication, emotional rapport, and individualized instruction across highly varied modalities (art, music, sports, relaxation). Far beyond current robot generalization, social cognition, and therapeutic judgment capabilities.

task: Inspect physical condition of recycling or hazardous waste facility for compliance with safety, quality, and service standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full compliance inspection of one facility. A competent inspector walks the site, checks equipment, documents conditions, and reviews safety/quality standards — typically 1–2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced compliance judgment, reading labels/gauges, navigating cluttered/outdoor hazardous terrain, interpreting regulatory standards, and generating a defensible report. Current humanoids lack the domain reasoning, reliability in unstructured industrial environments, and legal accountability needed.

task: Maintain or repair hydroelectric plant electrical, mechanical, or electronic equipment, such as motors, transformers, voltage regulators, generators, relays, battery systems, air compressors, sump pumps, gates, or valves.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance/repair instance on a single piece of hydroelectric plant equipment (e.g., a motor or relay). Diagnosis, disassembly, repair, and reassembly typically takes 1–3 hours for a skilled technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized domain knowledge, navigating complex industrial environments, operating high-voltage equipment, using varied precision tools, and exercising safety-critical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Refinish and polish piano cabinets or cases to prepare them for sale.
human_time_rationale: One instance = refinishing and polishing one piano cabinet. Includes sanding, applying finish coats, and buffing/polishing to sale-ready quality — typically 3–5 hours for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous sanding, multi-coat finishing, and high-gloss polishing on large curved/irregular surfaces with quality judgment. Far beyond 2026 robot capabilities in generalization, tactile feedback, and tool-use precision.

task: Install or calibrate electrical or mechanical equipment, such as motors, engines, switchboards, relays, switch gears, meters, pumps, hydraulics, or flood channels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing or calibrating one piece of equipment (e.g., a motor or relay). A competent electrician/mechanic typically takes 1–3 hours depending on complexity; 2 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized tools, precise torque application, electrical connections, calibration instruments, and domain expertise. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-use versatility, and diagnostic cognition for unstructured industrial equipment installation/calibration.

task: Inspect and test horses, sheep, poultry, or other animals to detect the presence of communicable diseases.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and testing one animal (e.g., a single horse or sheep). A competent veterinarian/inspector typically spends ~20–40 min per animal including physical exam and sample collection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert clinical perception (auscultation, palpation, mucous membrane assessment), fine dexterous sample collection (blood draws, swabs) on unrestrained live animals, and veterinary judgment — far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Mount, install, align, and secure tools, attachments, fixtures, and workpieces on machines, using hand tools and precision measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a single tool/fixture/workpiece on a machine. A skilled machinist typically spends 15–35 min per setup including measuring and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity with hand tools (torque wrenches, Allen keys), sub-millimeter alignment via precision instruments (dial indicators, micrometers), and contextual judgment on machine geometry — well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Rewire electrical systems, and repair or replace electrical accessories.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rewiring or replacing one electrical component/accessory (e.g., an outlet, switch, or fixture). A competent electrician typically takes 60–120 minutes per unit job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to strip, connect, and secure small wires; reading circuit diagrams; navigating tight spaces like junction boxes; and safety-critical judgment. Far beyond reliable current robot capability.

task: Stack or load finished items, or place items on conveyor systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stacking or loading a single finished item (e.g., a box, part, or product) onto a pallet, conveyor, or stack. A competent worker does this in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Repetitive pick-and-place on flat terrain is a core humanoid strength. However, varied item shapes, weights, and unstable stacks reduce reliability. Conveyor timing and precise placement add perception/coordination challenges.

task: Clean recycling yard by sweeping, raking, picking up broken glass and loose paper debris, or moving barrels and bins.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a typical recycling yard area (~moderate size). Sweeping, raking, debris pickup, and repositioning bins takes a competent worker ~45 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor unstructured terrain, broken glass handling (fragile/hazardous fine manipulation), raking, and moving heavy barrels challenge locomotion, dexterity, and generalization. Current humanoids struggle on rough outdoor ground and safe glass pickup.

task: Preserve or direct preservation of objects, using plaster, resin, sealants, hardeners, and shellac.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preserving a single object (e.g., artifact or specimen). Includes surface prep, mixing/applying preservative material, and allowing initial set. Skilled conservator typical time.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for delicate surface coating, precise mixing of multi-component materials, judgment on coverage and thickness, and adaptation to irregular object shapes — well beyond reliable 2026 robot capability. High failure risk.

task: Climb wind turbine towers to inspect, maintain, or repair equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one turbine tower climb for inspection/maintenance. Includes ascent (~30–60 min), work at height, and descent. Typical field job takes 2–4 hours total.
robot_success_rationale: Climbing a tall ladder inside a wind turbine tower (50–100 m) requires sustained vertical locomotion, fall-arrest harness use, and working in confined spaces. No current humanoid robot has the endurance, climb mechanism, or safety compliance to do this autonomously.

task: Control coolant systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking, adjusting, and logging coolant system parameters (flow rates, temps, valves, levels) for a single machine or zone. Competent technician takes ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Controlling coolant systems requires reading gauges/HMIs, turning valves, and interpreting fluid levels. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-dexterity valve manipulation, industrial HMI interaction, and process-control reasoning in unstructured settings.

task: Monitor stored biofuels products or secondary by-products until reused or transferred to users.
human_time_rationale: One monitoring round of stored biofuels/by-products: checking gauges, levels, signage, and logging status across a storage area. Typical shift check takes ~30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, navigating industrial outdoor/indoor storage yards, interpreting chemical labels, and logging data. Current robots lack reliable gauge-reading, hazardous-environment certification, and workflow integration for this domain.

task: Collect body tissue, feces, blood, urine, or other body fluids for examination and analysis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = single specimen collection (blood draw, swab, or urine sample). Competent clinician/phlebotomist takes ~5–15 min including prep, collection, and labeling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity (venipuncture, tissue biopsy), sterile technique, patient interaction/compliance management, and clinical judgment. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capabilities in real clinical settings.

task: Administer treatment involving application of physical agents, using equipment, moist packs, ultraviolet or infrared lamps, or ultrasound machines.
human_time_rationale: One treatment session (e.g., moist pack, ultrasound, UV/IR lamp) for a single patient; typical physical therapy modality application runs 15–45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, precise patient positioning, safe application of therapeutic agents, and real-time pain/response assessment. Beyond current robot dexterity, perception, and medical safety standards; no frontier humanoid is cleared for clinical treatment delivery.

task: Clean machines and machine parts, using cleaning solvents, cloths, air guns, hoses, vacuums, or other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one machine or set of machine parts. A competent worker typically spends 10–30 min per machine depending on size and grime level.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can wipe flat surfaces and use air guns at moderate success, but navigating around complex machine geometry, selecting correct solvents/tools, and reaching occluded parts in unstructured industrial settings remains challenging for current humanoids.

task: Change oil, hydraulic fluid, or other lubricants to maintain condition of hydroelectric plant equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = changing oil/fluid on a single piece of hydroelectric plant equipment (e.g., one turbine bearing or hydraulic unit). Includes draining, refilling, and basic inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large valves, handling specialized fittings/hoses, navigating industrial plant environments, and using heavy fluid systems. Frontier humanoids lack the tool-use, force control, and domain-specific manipulation for this task. Highly unstructured industrial setting.

task: Inspect water-powered electric generators or auxiliary equipment in hydroelectric plants to verify proper operation or to determine maintenance or repair needs.
human_time_rationale: One inspection round of a hydroelectric plant's generators and auxiliary equipment; a competent operator typically walks the facility, checks readings, and logs findings in ~45–90 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex, wet industrial environments; reading analog/digital gauges; interpreting multi-sensory anomalies (sound, vibration, smell, heat); and making expert maintenance judgments. Current humanoids lack the specialized sensors, domain knowledge integration, and environmental ruggedness needed.

task: Enter commands or load control media, such as tapes, cards, or disks, into machine controllers to retrieve programmed instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a single tape/card/disk or entering a command set into a machine controller; a competent operator typically completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise insertion of media into specific slots and typed command entry on varied legacy interfaces. Poor generalization to non-standard panels, small connectors, and legacy hardware limits current humanoid robots significantly.

task: Change worn cutting tools, using wrenches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing one worn cutting tool (e.g., a lathe insert, mill cutter, or drill bit) using wrenches. A competent machinist typically takes 5–15 minutes including inspection and re-torquing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying worn tool, selecting correct wrench, applying precise torque in confined machine spaces, and ensuring secure re-seating. Dexterity and force-control demands in unstructured industrial settings exceed current humanoid capabilities reliably.

task: Sort metals to separate high-grade metals, such as copper, brass, and aluminum, for recycling.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting a typical batch/bin of mixed scrap metals at a recycling facility; an experienced worker takes ~30 min to visually identify and separate high-grade metals.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-material visual/tactile classification of varied, irregular scrap shapes, then dexterous grasping and sorting. Current robots struggle with unstructured, cluttered scrap piles, reflective surfaces, and reliable metal-type identification without dedicated sensors.

task: Operate biomass fuel-burning boiler or biomass fuel gasification system equipment in accordance with specifications or instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one operational shift startup-to-steady-state cycle, including monitoring, adjustments, and logging. A competent boiler operator typically spends ~1 hour completing this unit of work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating complex industrial control panels, interpreting live sensor readouts, making nuanced fuel-feed and combustion adjustments, and responding to alarms. Humanoids lack the specialized tool integration, safety-critical judgment, and industrial HMI dexterity for this task.

task: Clean packaging containers, line and pad crates, or assemble cartons to prepare for product packing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one container/crate/carton. A competent worker cleans, lines, pads, or folds/assembles one unit in roughly 3–7 minutes depending on type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied manipulation: folding cardboard, placing liners/padding, cleaning surfaces. Current humanoids handle simple pick-and-place well but struggle with flexible materials, precise folding, and multi-step assembly of varied container types without custom fixtures.

task: Cut, strip, and bend wire leads at ends of coils, using pliers and wire scrapers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing both wire leads on one coil: cut, strip, and bend. A skilled worker completes this in ~3–5 min with pliers and scraper.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity: controlled stripping without nicking wire, accurate bending of small leads, and coordinated two-hand tool use (pliers + scraper). Current humanoids lack reliable sub-millimeter finger control for such fiddly wire work.

task: Treat sick or injured animals by prescribing medication, setting bones, dressing wounds, or performing surgery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one clinical treatment episode (exam, diagnosis, and primary intervention). A typical vet visit/procedure ranges 20–90 min; 45 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical reasoning, fine dexterity for surgery/suturing, safe animal handling of uncooperative patients, sterile technique, and drug prescription judgment — all far beyond 2026 frontier humanoid capabilities.

task: Clean calcareous deposits, accretions, and stains from teeth and beneath margins of gums, using dental instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full dental cleaning appointment for one patient, including scaling, root planing, and subgingival cleaning. Typically 45–60 min for a hygienist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity inside a small, wet, sensitive oral cavity; precise subgingival instrument navigation; real-time tactile feedback; patient safety-critical. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Insert control instructions into machine control units to start operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inserting control instructions into one machine control unit to start operation; a competent operator typically takes ~5 minutes including setup, input, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine manipulation of keypads/interfaces, reading machine-specific displays, and interpreting control logic. Humanoid robots lack reliable GUI/button interaction, domain-specific machine knowledge, and generalization across varied industrial control units.

task: Travel to customers' premises to install, maintain, or repair audio and visual electronic reception equipment or accessories.
human_time_rationale: One service visit to a customer's premises: travel + install/maintain/repair A/V equipment. Typical job runs 60–120 min; 90 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving to customer sites, navigating unfamiliar homes, fine cable/connector work, diagnosis of varied faults, and customer interaction. Current humanoids cannot drive, lack generalization for unstructured repair tasks, and have very low fine-dexterity for electronics.

task: Assist in the preparation of sealed radioactive materials, such as cobalt, radium, cesium, or isotopes, for use in radiation treatments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single sealed radioactive source for one treatment session. A competent medical physicist/radiation therapist takes ~30–60 min including verification, handling, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-precise dexterous manipulation of hazardous sealed sources, strict regulatory compliance, specialized shielded tools, real-time dosimetry judgment, and sterile/controlled-environment protocols far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Connect metal parts or components in hydroelectric plants by welding, soldering, riveting, tapping, bolting, bonding, or screwing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting one set of metal parts/components (e.g., a joint, seam, or fastened assembly). A competent welder/fitter in a hydroelectric plant typically spends 45–90 minutes per discrete connection task.
robot_success_rationale: Hydroelectric plants are unstructured, hazardous industrial environments with confined spaces, heavy/irregular parts, and diverse joining methods (welding, riveting, bolting). Frontier humanoids lack the specialized tools, precision force control, and environment adaptability required. Near-zero autonomous success.

task: Demonstrate respiratory care procedures to trainees or other healthcare personnel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single demonstration session covering a respiratory care procedure (e.g., nebulizer setup, suctioning, or ventilator management) for a trainee group; typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert clinical knowledge, real-time verbal instruction, adaptive explanation, fine dexterity with medical equipment, and credible teaching presence. Current humanoids lack clinical expertise, reliable speech-guided instruction, and the fine manipulation needed for medical device demos.

task: Prepare or construct equipment, such as immobilization, treatment, or protection devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating/fitting a single device (e.g., a splint, brace, or protective orthosis). A competent technician typically takes 15–35 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise shaping, trimming, and fitting of thermoplastic or similar materials to patient-specific anatomy, fine tool use (scissors, heat gun, straps), and adaptive judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and task generalization.

task: Inspect instruments to locate defects, and to determine their value or the level of restoration required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single instrument. A competent appraiser/technician spends ~15–25 minutes examining, testing, and documenting defects and valuation for one instrument.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level visual and tactile defect detection, acoustic testing, and nuanced valuation judgment. Current robots lack specialized sensory acuity, domain knowledge, and the generalization needed across diverse instrument types and subtle flaws.

task: Measure glandular activity, blood volume, red cell survival, or radioactivity of patient, using scanners, Geiger counters, scintillometers, or other laboratory equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient measurement session (e.g., thyroid uptake scan or blood-volume study). Setup, acquisition, and initial reading typically take 30–60 minutes for a nuclear medicine technologist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized medical nuclear instruments (scintillometers, gamma cameras), positioning patients safely, interpreting readings, and adapting to patient variability. Humanoid robots lack the certified tool interfaces, clinical perception, and safety protocols needed. Far outside 2026 general-purpose robot capability.

task: Run signal quality and connectivity tests for individual cables, and record results.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one cable end-to-end: connect to tester, run signal/continuity check, read results, and log them. ~8 min per cable for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise connector insertion/removal on varied cable types (RJ45, coax, fiber, etc.), operating handheld test equipment, reading small displays, and logging structured data — well beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use generalization.

task: Verify conformance of machined work to specifications, using measuring instruments, such as calipers, micrometers, or fixed or telescoping gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single machined part. A skilled machinist typically takes 5–12 minutes to measure key dimensions with calipers, micrometers, and gauges and record/verify results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise grasping and seating of delicate instruments (micrometers, telescoping gauges) against tight tolerances, tactile feedback, and consistent repeatable alignment — well beyond current humanoid dexterity. Perception of gauge readings adds further difficulty.

task: Inject contrast medium into patients' blood vessels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one contrast injection per patient. Includes site prep, IV/catheter placement or connection, injection, and initial monitoring. Typically 5–15 minutes for a trained technologist or nurse.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous vascular access or catheter connection, sterile technique, real-time patient feedback, and clinical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and safety standards for invasive medical procedures.

task: Take readings and record data, such as water levels, temperatures, or flow rates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reading and recording a single instrument or sensor (e.g., a gauge, thermometer, flow meter). A competent worker walks to the device, reads the value, and logs it in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot must navigate to gauge location, visually interpret analog/digital displays (challenging for varied formats), and record data. Perception of arbitrary gauges and seamless logging remain unreliable; outdoor/rough terrain lowers mobility scores further.

task: Care for and monitor the condition of animals recovering from surgery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single post-surgical monitoring/care round for one animal, including vital checks, wound inspection, medication administration, and notes. Typically ~20–40 min per animal.
robot_success_rationale: Requires subtle clinical assessment (pain cues, wound color, animal behavior), safe handling of distressed/fragile animals, and adaptive caregiving. Current robots lack veterinary perception, safe animal manipulation, and judgment for unstructured caregiving scenarios.

task: Move machine controls to lower tools to workpieces and to engage automatic feeds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repositioning controls and engaging auto-feed for a single machining pass; a competent machinist completes this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise manipulation of levers, handwheels, and switches on industrial machinery; fine force-feedback and spatial accuracy demands exceed current humanoid dexterity. Machine interfaces are highly varied and unstructured.

task: Adjust equipment and controls according to physicians' orders or established protocol.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single piece of medical equipment (e.g., ventilator, IV pump, oxygen flow) per order. A competent technician/nurse typically takes 3–7 minutes including verification.
robot_success_rationale: Medical equipment varies enormously in interface design; robots lack the fine dexterity, domain-specific tool knowledge, and safety-critical judgment to reliably interpret physician orders and adjust specialized clinical controls. High-stakes environment raises the bar further.

task: Attach electrodes to the patients' chests, arms, and legs, connect electrodes to leads from the electrocardiogram (EKG) machine, and operate the EKG machine to obtain a reading.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full EKG procedure on one patient: positioning, placing 10 electrodes, connecting leads, acquiring a clean tracing. Competent tech typically completes this in 8–12 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise skin prep, firm gel-electrode placement on varied human anatomy, delicate lead-clip attachment, and machine operation — all on a live, potentially uncooperative patient. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, perception, and safe human-contact limits.

task: Roll, knead, cut, or shape dough to form sweet rolls, pie crusts, tarts, cookies, or other products.
human_time_rationale: One batch of dough shaped (e.g., 12 cookies or one pie crust); a skilled baker rolls, cuts, and shapes in roughly 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Dough is deformable and unpredictable, demanding highly sensitive force control, bimanual dexterity, and tactile feedback. Current humanoids lack the fine-touch sensitivity and adaptive manipulation needed for consistent shaping without tearing or over-working dough.

task: Install and maintain geothermal system instrumentation or controls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing or servicing a single instrumentation/control component (sensor, transmitter, or controller) on a geothermal system; a competent technician typically takes 1–3 hours per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous work (wiring, conduit, calibration tools), operating in cramped/outdoor/industrial environments, interpreting technical schematics, and using specialized tools. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Adjust machine feed and speed, change cutting tools, or adjust machine controls when automatic programming is faulty or if machines malfunction.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing a fault and making one corrective adjustment (feed/speed tweak, tool change, or control reset) on a CNC or automated machine; a skilled machinist typically takes 10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing abstract faults from machine feedback, fine dexterous tool changes (tight torque specs, tool holders), adjusting CNC controls, and contextual judgment — well beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity.

task: Operate diagnostic imaging equipment to produce contrast enhanced radiographs of heart and cardiovascular system.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one contrast-enhanced cardiovascular imaging study (e.g., cardiac catheterization/angiography). Includes patient prep, contrast injection setup, equipment calibration, image acquisition, and basic QC review.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized medical imaging consoles (fluoroscopy/cath lab systems), coordinating contrast injection, sterile patient interaction, and real-time clinical judgment. No humanoid robot has relevant capability; patient safety stakes prohibit autonomous operation.

task: Conduct electrocardiogram (EKG), phonocardiogram, echocardiogram, stress testing, or other cardiovascular tests to record patients' cardiac activity, using specialized electronic test equipment, recording devices, or laboratory instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full cardiovascular test session (e.g., a standard 12-lead EKG ~5–10 min; echocardiogram ~30–45 min). Median across test types is ~30 min including patient prep and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical dexterity (precise electrode/probe placement on varied human anatomy), patient communication, real-time adaptive judgment, and operation of specialized medical devices — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities in unstructured care settings.

task: Perform laboratory tests on blood, urine, or feces, such as urinalyses or blood counts, to assist in the diagnosis and treatment of animal health problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one complete lab test panel (e.g., CBC or urinalysis) on a single sample: collecting, preparing, running the analyzer/microscope, and recording results. ~20–30 min for a competent vet tech.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous pipetting, operating specialized analyzers, microscope manipulation, and interpreting biological results. Current humanoid robots lack the precision, tool integration, and cognitive judgment needed for reliable autonomous lab diagnostics.

task: Install plumbing, such as dip tubes, port fittings, drain tank valves, pressure temperature relief valves, or tanks, according to manufacturer specifications and building codes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a complete set of plumbing components on one unit (e.g., water heater). A skilled plumber typically takes 60–120 minutes including prep and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-torque precision wrenching on threaded fittings, pipe tape application, spatial reasoning in confined spaces, code compliance judgment, and leak verification — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and task generalization.

task: Test and repair or replace electrical equipment, such as circuit breakers, station batteries, cable trays, conduits, or control devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing and repairing/replacing a single electrical component (e.g., a circuit breaker or control device). A competent electrician typically spends 60–120 minutes including diagnostics, isolation, swap, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-voltage safety protocols, precise fine dexterous tool use (multimeters, torque wrenches, terminal connections), fault diagnosis reasoning, and navigating industrial electrical panels — well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured settings.

task: Use a variety of testing techniques to assist doctors in cardiac or pulmonary research or to diagnose disorders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient diagnostic/research session (e.g., ECG, spirometry, or stress test). A competent cardiopulmonary tech typically spends ~30–60 min per patient including setup, testing, and preliminary documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert clinical judgment, precise sensor placement on varied human anatomy, patient communication, real-time adaptive decision-making, and interpretation of physiological data. Far beyond current robot autonomy in medical/caregiving contexts.

task: Fill prescriptions, measuring medications and labeling containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one prescription filled: counting/measuring pills, verifying dosage, labeling the container. A competent pharmacist/tech takes ~5–10 minutes per script.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (counting small pills, precise liquid measurement), reading/printing labels, cross-checking dosages, and regulatory accuracy. Current humanoids lack the precision, safety compliance, and pharmaceutical tool integration needed.

task: Dispose of radioactive materials and store radiopharmaceuticals, following radiation safety procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disposing of a batch of radioactive waste and storing radiopharmaceuticals during a single session; a trained technician typically takes 20–40 minutes following safety protocols.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized shielded containers, regulatory compliance, fine dexterous handling of hazardous materials, and nuanced safety judgments. No current humanoid robot is certified or capable of autonomous radiopharmaceutical handling in real environments.

task: Monitor patient's physiological responses to therapy, such as vital signs, arterial blood gases, or blood chemistry changes, and consult with physician if adverse reactions occur.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient monitoring session: observing vitals, reviewing ABG/blood chemistry results, interpreting trends, and consulting physician if needed. Typically 15–25 minutes per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical interpretation of complex physiological data, medical judgment, and physician consultation — all high-level cognitive/communicative tasks far beyond current robot autonomy. Sensor reading alone is insufficient; autonomous clinical decision-making is not feasible.

task: Euthanize animals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = euthanizing one animal. Includes preparation, restraint, sedation/injection, confirmation of death, and documentation. Typical veterinary procedure ~20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise venipuncture or injection, animal restraint on a struggling/distressed subject, clinical judgment, emotional sensitivity, and legal/ethical accountability. Far beyond current robot dexterity and situational judgment.

task: Collect and sort recyclable construction materials, such as concrete, drywall, plastics, or wood, into containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting a typical load of mixed construction debris (e.g., one wheelbarrow or bin's worth) into labeled containers; a competent worker takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Construction debris is highly irregular, heavy, and sharp—challenging dexterity and grasp planning. Material classification (concrete vs. drywall vs. wood) requires robust perception. Outdoor/cluttered terrain and unpredictable object geometries push success low.

task: Install tools in spindles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one tool (e.g., drill bit, end mill, tap) into one spindle. A competent machinist selects, inserts, and secures the tool in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment, fine dexterous force control to seat and torque a tool holder or collet nut correctly, and tool identification. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and torque feedback for this precision task.

task: Operate high voltage switches or related devices in hydropower stations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one switching sequence (isolate/energize a circuit). Includes permit checks, safety verification, and physical operation of switches; typically 15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating restricted high-voltage switchgear rooms, operating specialized industrial controls, verifying safety interlocks, and exercising safety-critical judgment. Current humanoids lack the certified protocols, specialized tool-use, and reliable perception for live HV environments.

task: Provide specialized direct and indirect care to inpatients and outpatients within a designated specialty, such as obstetrics, neurology, oncology, or neonatal care.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient care encounter (assessment, intervention, documentation) in a specialty unit. Competent nurses typically spend ~30–60 min per patient per shift cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, fine dexterity (IVs, catheters, neonatal handling), nuanced patient communication, adaptive response to rapidly changing medical status, and legal/ethical accountability — far beyond current robot capabilities.

task: Preprocess feedstock to prepare for biochemical or thermochemical production processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a batch of feedstock (e.g., sizing, sorting, drying, conveying). Skilled operator spends ~45 min per batch setup and processing cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating industrial grinders, dryers, conveyors, and monitoring moisture/particle size — heavy, specialized machinery a humanoid cannot operate. Highly unstructured, variable feedstock materials further limit autonomy.

task: Apply coatings or operate systems to mitigate corrosion of geothermal plant equipment or structures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying protective coating or operating a corrosion-mitigation system on a single piece of equipment/structure. A competent worker needs ~2 hours for surface prep, application, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial equipment (spray systems, cathodic protection rigs), working in harsh geothermal environments (heat, steam, H₂S), precise surface prep, and tool-use generalization far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Perform therapeutic procedures, such as injections, immunizations, suturing and wound care, and infection management.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one therapeutic procedure (e.g., a single injection, wound suturing, or dressing change). Median across procedure types; simple injections ~5 min, suturing/wound care ~20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sterile technique, real-time tissue feedback, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack the precision, force sensitivity, and regulatory clearance for autonomous patient procedures. Near-zero feasibility.

task: Place dough in pans, molds, or on sheets, and bake in production ovens or on grills.
human_time_rationale: One instance = portioning and placing one batch of dough into pans/molds/sheets and loading into a production oven or grill. A skilled baker completes this in roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Dough is soft, deformable, and sticky — extremely challenging for robot grippers. Portioning, shaping, and placing without tearing or misforming requires fine dexterity and force control far beyond current frontier humanoids. Loading heavy production ovens adds complexity.

task: Collect, prepare, and label samples for laboratory testing, culture, or microscopic examination.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting, preparing, and labeling a single patient/environmental sample (e.g., swab, blood draw, urine). A competent tech typically completes this in ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (swabbing, pipetting, capping vials), sterile technique, legible labeling, and contextual judgment about sample type/patient. Current humanoids lack the precision, sterility compliance, and tool-use generalization needed.

task: Operate fork lifts, skid loaders, or trucks to move or store recyclable materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one load cycle (pick up, transport, deposit recyclable materials). A competent operator typically completes this in ~45 minutes including maneuvering and staging.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate forklifts, skid loaders, or trucks in live recycling environments. These require seated cab operation, heavy vehicle control, spatial judgment under load, and outdoor/unstructured terrain — all beyond 2026 frontier humanoids.

task: Perform preventive or corrective containment or cleanup measures in hydroelectric plants to prevent environmental contamination.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single containment/cleanup event (e.g., spill response or scheduled preventive check) in a hydroelectric plant; a competent technician typically takes 1–3 hours depending on scope.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex industrial/outdoor terrain, operating specialized containment equipment, handling hazardous fluids, and making situational judgments. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-use, and environmental generalization needed for this unstructured, safety-critical task.

task: Detect and map radiopharmaceuticals in patients' bodies, using a camera to produce photographic or computer images.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one nuclear medicine imaging scan session (e.g., bone scan or PET scan). Includes patient setup, camera positioning, acquisition, and basic image review — typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized gamma camera/PET scanner equipment, precise patient positioning, sterile protocols, and clinical judgment. Humanoid robots cannot operate these fixed medical imaging systems or interpret radiopharmaceutical distributions autonomously.

task: Operate processing equipment, such as fiber-sorters and grinders, to sort, crush, or grind recyclable materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one processing run/batch (loading, operating, monitoring, and clearing a fiber-sorter or grinder for a typical shift segment). ~45 min per cycle is a reasonable unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large industrial processing machinery with specialized controls, feeding bulk materials, monitoring output quality, and clearing jams. Humanoids lack the strength, reach, and certified interfacing needed for heavy recycling equipment; generalization to varied machinery is very poor.

task: Inspect or test damaged machine parts, and mark defective areas or advise supervisors of repair needs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single machine part, identifying damage, marking defects or reporting. A competent technician typically takes 10–20 min per part depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced defect perception (cracks, wear, warping), contextual judgment, and communication to supervisors. Current robots lack reliable fine-detail inspection, damage classification, and autonomous reporting in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Add radioactive substances to biological specimens, such as blood, urine, or feces, to determine therapeutic drug or hormone levels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single specimen batch. A lab tech pipettes radiotracer into specimens, mixes, incubates, and sets up for counting — typically 15–25 minutes of active hands-on work.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise micropipetting of hazardous radioisotopes, strict contamination protocols, fine dexterity with small vials, and regulatory compliance. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter precision, sterile technique, and certified safe handling needed.

task: Inspect or test lines or cables, recording and analyzing test results, to assess transmission characteristics and locate faults or malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting/testing a single line or cable run, connecting test equipment, running diagnostics, recording results, and logging findings. Typical telecom/electrical technician job takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting specialized test instruments (TDR, OTDR, multimeter) to cable terminals with precision, interpreting readings, logging structured data, and navigating varied field environments. Current humanoids lack fine connector-mating dexterity, tool fluency, and autonomous fault analysis.

task: Check, test, and maintain cardiology equipment, making minor repairs when necessary, to ensure proper operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking, testing, and performing minor repairs on a single cardiology device (e.g., ECG machine, defibrillator). A competent biomedical tech typically takes 30–60 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert clinical/biomedical knowledge, fine dexterous manipulation of delicate electronics, use of specialized diagnostic tools, and judgment-based minor repairs. Far beyond 2026 frontier robot generalization and dexterity capabilities.

task: Install, maintain, or repair ground or water source-coupled heat pumps to heat and cool residential or commercial building air or water.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full install, maintenance visit, or repair job on a geothermal heat pump system. A typical residential install/major repair takes a full day (6–8 hrs); maintenance ~2–4 hrs. Median ~8 hrs.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor excavation/drilling, heavy equipment operation, pipe fitting, refrigerant handling, electrical wiring, and complex diagnosis. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and domain generalization. No viable autonomous robot system exists for this.

task: Lay out and mark areas of parts to be shot peened and fill hoppers with shot.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single batch of parts: masking/marking areas with tape/paint, then filling the shot hopper. A competent technician takes ~15–25 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise masking of complex part geometries (fine dexterity), reading/interpreting engineering drawings, and filling hoppers with abrasive media (bulk material handling). Current humanoids lack the perception, dexterity, and process knowledge for reliable autonomous execution.

task: Place insulation over conductors, or seal splices with moisture-proof covering.
human_time_rationale: One instance = insulating/sealing one splice or conductor end. A skilled electrician applies heat-shrink tubing, electrical tape, or a moisture-proof cap in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity to handle small components (tape, heat-shrink, wire nuts) on thin conductors, apply even tension, and use a heat gun accurately. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-finger manipulation and tool control for such delicate electrical work.

task: Inspect or evaluate building envelopes, mechanical systems, electrical systems, or process systems to determine the energy consumption of each system.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full energy audit inspection of a single building system (e.g., HVAC, envelope, or electrical). A competent energy auditor typically spends 2–4 hours per system on-site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level multi-domain knowledge (thermal, electrical, mechanical), reading nameplates/meters, using specialized instruments (blower doors, IR cameras, clamp meters), interpreting findings, and generating an evaluation report. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Position and secure workpieces on tables, using bolts, jigs, clamps, shims, or other holding devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning and securing a single workpiece on a work table. A skilled machinist/welder typically takes 5–12 minutes depending on complexity of fixturing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual dexterity to align workpieces, select correct jigs/clamps, apply measured torque with bolts or clamps, and verify secure fitment. Current humanoids struggle with tool selection, fine force control, and spatial precision needed for secure fixturing.

task: Maintain and calibrate radioisotope and laboratory equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one calibration/maintenance session on a single piece of lab or radioisotope equipment; a competent technician typically spends ~1 hour per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity with delicate, high-consequence instruments, radiation safety protocols, specialized tool use, precise calibration adjustments, and expert-level domain knowledge. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Start, adjust, or stop generating units, operating valves, gates, or auxiliary equipment in hydroelectric power generating plants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single start/stop or adjustment cycle for a unit or valve/gate, including pre-checks and logging; ~15 minutes for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Hydroelectric control rooms use specialized consoles, physical switches, large valves, and gates requiring plant-specific training. Humanoids lack the manipulation precision, plant familiarity, and safety-certified interfaces to operate these autonomously.

task: Prepare and administer medications, vaccines, serums, or treatments, as prescribed by veterinarians.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and administering a single medication/vaccine/treatment to one animal patient, including draw-up, restraint, and administration. Typical vet tech task takes ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (drawing up syringes, finding injection sites), animal restraint on unpredictable live subjects, sterile technique, and real-time clinical judgment. Far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Perform dental work, such as cleaning, polishing, or extracting teeth.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient appointment (cleaning, polishing, or extraction). A typical dental hygiene or extraction visit runs 45–75 minutes for a competent professional.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme sub-millimeter dexterity inside a confined oral cavity, real-time tactile/force feedback, clinical judgment, and patient safety guarantees. Far beyond any 2026 general-purpose robot; even specialized dental robots are not autonomous.

task: Splice or terminate cables or electrical wiring in hydroelectric plants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = splicing or terminating a single cable/wire connection. A competent electrician typically takes 30–90 min depending on cable size, type, and access; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (stripping, crimping, heat-shrinking, torquing lugs to spec), operation in confined/wet industrial spaces, use of specialized tools, and safety-critical precision. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Prepare treatment rooms for surgery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one operating/treatment room for a single surgery: sterilizing surfaces, arranging instruments, setting up drapes, checking equipment. Typically 20–45 min for a scrub tech/OR nurse.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile technique, precise instrument layout, complex dexterity, judgment about readiness, and regulatory compliance. Current humanoids lack surgical-grade sterility awareness, fine manipulation of delicate tools, and procedural verification capability.

task: Maintain validation test equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance session on one piece of validation test equipment (e.g., calibrating, cleaning, checking connections, logging results). ~45 min is typical for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of sensitive instruments, reading displays, interpreting calibration results, using specialized software, and making judgment-based adjustments — far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Bathe animals, clip nails or claws, and brush or cut animals' hair.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grooming a single medium-sized animal (e.g. dog): bathing, drying, nail clipping, and brushing/trimming hair typically takes 30–60 minutes for a skilled groomer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme dexterity for nail clipping and hair cutting on an unpredictable, moving animal. Current robots lack fine force control, safe tool use near living tissue, and ability to handle animal resistance/behavior safely.

task: Evaluate manufactured products according to specifications and quality standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single manufactured part/product against a spec sheet; competent QC inspector takes ~5–10 min including visual checks, measurements, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is maturing, but combining mobility, fine tactile/dimensional measurement, cross-referencing spec sheets, and documenting results autonomously in varied factory settings remains inconsistent for generalist humanoids in 2026.

task: Start or restart wind turbine generator systems to ensure proper operations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting or restarting a single wind turbine system; includes safety checks, fault clearing, and confirming stable operation, typically 15–30 minutes for a technician.
robot_success_rationale: Wind turbine start/restart requires climbing tall towers, interfacing with specialized SCADA/control panels, diagnosing faults, and operating high-voltage systems. Current humanoids lack the mobility, tool-use, and safety certification for this environment.

task: Extract chemicals from discarded appliances, such as air conditioners or refrigerators, using specialized machinery, such as refrigerant recovery equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = recovering refrigerant from a single appliance (e.g., one AC unit or refrigerator). Includes connecting recovery equipment, running extraction, verifying completion, and disconnecting. ~20–30 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating and connecting refrigerant service ports with precise fitting engagement, operating specialized recovery machinery controls, and safely handling pressurized chemicals. Current humanoids lack the tool-use precision, domain-specific dexterity, and sensor integration for leak-safe chemical extraction.

task: Check to ensure that workpieces are properly lubricated and cooled during machine operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking lubrication and coolant on a single workpiece/machine setup; a skilled operator visually and tactilely inspects in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal sensing (visual flow detection, thermal imaging, tactile feedback) in a cluttered, dynamic machine environment. Robots lack reliable coolant-flow and lubrication-level perception, and must navigate tight machine spaces safely.

task: Clean and sterilize instruments, equipment, or materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and sterilizing a typical batch of instruments (e.g., surgical or lab tools). Includes pre-cleaning, loading autoclave/sterilizer, cycle run, and unloading — ~30 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous handling of delicate instruments, precise loading of sterilization equipment, operating specialized machines, and verifying sterility. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, domain knowledge, and reliable tool-use for medical/lab sterilization workflows.

task: Take and develop diagnostic radiographs, using x-ray equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning a patient, taking one radiographic series (e.g., chest or limb X-ray), and processing/developing the image. Typically 10–20 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise patient positioning with communication/consent, fine manipulation of X-ray equipment controls, radiation safety compliance, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack medical-grade dexterity, patient interaction, and regulatory authorization for autonomous clinical procedures.

task: Clean kennels, animal holding areas, surgery suites, examination rooms, or animal loading or unloading facilities to control the spread of disease.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one facility area (e.g., a kennel run, exam room, or surgery suite). A competent veterinary technician/kennel attendant takes ~20–30 min per area including scrubbing, disinfecting, rinsing, and drying.
robot_success_rationale: Veterinary spaces are cluttered, wet, and variable (kennels, cages, drains, equipment). Robots struggle with surface-specific disinfection protocols, cage latches, animal waste removal, and tight enclosures. Perception and dexterity in unstructured wet environments remain weak.

task: Observe, interview, and assess patients to identify care needs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient assessment session; competent nurse/clinician typically spends 15–25 minutes observing, interviewing, and documenting care needs.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced verbal/nonverbal communication, clinical judgment, emotional sensitivity, and adaptive questioning. Current robots lack reliable natural-language clinical interviewing, empathetic interaction, and holistic patient assessment capabilities.

task: Administer emergency first aid, such as performing emergency resuscitation or other life saving procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single emergency first aid event (e.g., CPR until patient recovers or EMS arrives), typically lasting ~5–15 minutes for a competent responder.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rapid scene assessment, precise body positioning, sustained forceful/rhythmic chest compressions with correct depth, airway management, AED use, and adaptive real-time decision-making. Current humanoids lack medical-grade dexterity, reliable force control on a human body, and situational medical judgment. Teleoperation excluded.

task: Provide assistance to other healthcare personnel during dosimetry procedures and tumor localization.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting during a single dosimetry/tumor localization session. A competent radiation therapy technologist typically spends 30–60 minutes per patient session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile clinical precision, nuanced interpretation of imaging data, real-time adaptive collaboration with medical staff, and safe patient interaction. Far beyond current general-purpose humanoid robot capabilities in 2026.

task: Strike wood, fiberglass, or metal bars of instruments, and use tuned blocks, stroboscopes, or electronic tuners to evaluate tones made by instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = striking and evaluating tones on a single instrument's bars. A skilled instrument technician takes ~5–10 minutes to systematically test and assess all bars.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, controlled striking force on specific bars, interpreting audio feedback via stroboscopes/electronic tuners, and nuanced quality judgment. Fine dexterous striking control and multi-modal sensory integration are well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Identify and correct malfunctions of geothermal plant equipment, electrical systems, instrumentation, or controls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and correcting a single equipment/system malfunction. Competent technician typically spends 1–3 hours identifying fault, isolating cause, and completing repair/correction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level fault diagnosis, reading specialized instrumentation, operating valves/breakers/controls in a complex industrial environment, and fine electrical work. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capability; environment is unstructured, hazardous, and task demands deep contextual reasoning.

task: Photograph treated area of patient and process film.
human_time_rationale: One instance = photographing a single treated area and processing the resulting film/image. Includes positioning, capturing, and basic processing steps, typical in a clinical/dermatology setting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical context understanding, precise patient positioning, consent, sterile handling, and film processing. Robots lack clinical judgment, patient interaction protocols, and fine-grained medical imaging setup skills. Current humanoids cannot reliably perform this autonomously.

task: Plan, implement, or maintain procedures for mixing, packaging, or labeling pharmaceuticals, according to policy and legal requirements, to ensure quality, security, and proper disposal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one procedure cycle (mix, package, or label a batch). A competent pharmacy tech/pharmacist typically takes 30–60 min including documentation and quality checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile-environment dexterity, precise liquid handling, label reading/verification, regulatory documentation, and judgment about quality/security/disposal — far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capabilities.

task: Grind lens edges, or apply coatings to lenses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single lens (grinding edges or applying one coating cycle). A skilled optician typically takes ~10–20 minutes per lens including setup, grinding/coating, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme precision and fine dexterity on small, fragile, optically sensitive surfaces. Lens grinding and coating demand sub-millimeter tolerances and specialized machinery a humanoid cannot operate or interface with reliably.

task: Give enemas and perform catheterizations, ear flushes, intravenous feedings, or gavages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one procedure (e.g., one enema or one catheterization). Prep, administration, and cleanup for a typical clinical procedure takes ~20–30 min for a competent nurse/aide.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity, sterile technique, patient positioning, real-time pain/discomfort feedback, and intimate anatomical contact. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities; safety risks are extreme.

task: Plan, install, repair, or troubleshoot telehealth technology applications or systems in homes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single home visit to plan, install, repair, or troubleshoot a telehealth system. A competent technician typically spends 60–120 minutes on-site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex tool use (cable routing, device mounting, network configuration), advanced cognitive problem-solving, nuanced client communication, and safe navigation in varied home environments. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Weld piping, such as high density polyethylene (HDPE) piping, using techniques such as butt, socket, side-wall, and electro-fusion welding.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pipe weld joint (e.g., a butt fusion on a typical HDPE pipe). Includes setup, alignment, heating, fusing, cooling, and inspection; ~30–60 min is typical for a competent welder.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipe alignment, sustained force application, temperature control, specialized fusion equipment operation, and quality judgment. Current humanoid robots lack the specialized tool integration, fine force control, and procedural adaptability for pipe welding in field conditions.

task: Assemble eyeglasses by cutting and edging lenses, and fitting the lenses into frames.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pair of glasses. A skilled optician takes ~15–25 min to trace the frame, cut/edge both lenses, and fit/secure them into the frame.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized edging/cutting machinery, sub-millimeter precision fitting of curved ophthalmic lenses into flexible frames, and fine dexterous manipulation — well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Maintain instruments, equipment, or machinery to ensure proper working condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = routine maintenance check on a single piece of equipment (e.g., lubrication, filter change, calibration check). Median across light instruments to heavier machinery.
robot_success_rationale: Maintenance is highly varied: diagnosing faults, replacing parts, calibrating instruments, using specialized tools. Requires expert perception, fine dexterity, tool generalization, and domain reasoning far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Monitor patients' blood pressure and heart rate using electrocardiogram (EKG) equipment during diagnostic or therapeutic procedures to notify the physician if something appears wrong.
human_time_rationale: One instance = monitoring one patient through one procedure; typical EKG-monitored procedures last 20–45 minutes of active observation and readiness to alert the physician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading and interpreting live EKG/BP data, applying clinical judgment to detect abnormalities, and communicating urgently to a physician. Current humanoids lack certified medical-grade sensor integration, clinical interpretation algorithms, and reliable real-time physician communication in unstructured clinical settings.

task: Select, purchase, set up, operate, or troubleshoot state-of-the-art laser cutting equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full cycle of one sub-task (e.g., setting up a job on a laser cutter). Selection/purchase takes hours; a typical setup/operation run is ~30–90 min; troubleshooting ~60 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Laser cutting machines require expert cognitive judgment, software programming, vendor negotiation, and specialized control interfaces. No 2026 humanoid can operate, configure, or troubleshoot industrial laser equipment autonomously at acceptable quality.

task: Provide care to a wide range of animals or specialize in a particular species, such as horses or exotic birds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one daily care session for an animal or small group (feeding, watering, health checks, grooming, cleaning enclosures). ~60 min is a reasonable median across species.
robot_success_rationale: Animal care demands adaptive, species-specific handling; reading animal behavior and stress cues; and fine dexterous interaction with unpredictable living creatures. Current robots lack the perception, safe compliance, and generalization required across even a narrow species set.

task: Conduct testing to determine functionality or optimization or to establish limits of photonics systems or components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single photonics component or subsystem (e.g., laser, fiber, waveguide). Setup, calibration, running test sequences, and logging results typically takes 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for fiber/optical alignment (sub-micron precision), specialized lab instruments (power meters, OSAs, VNAs), interpreting complex data, and adaptive problem-solving. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Perform tunnel or field inspections of hydroelectric plant facilities or resources.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single tunnel or field inspection of a hydroelectric facility. A competent inspector typically takes 2–4 hours to traverse, assess, and document findings across tunnels, penstocks, or field infrastructure.
robot_success_rationale: Hydroelectric tunnels are dark, wet, confined, and structurally complex. Current humanoids lack waterproofing, robust rough-terrain locomotion in flooded/sloped tunnels, specialized inspection tools, and the domain expertise to assess cracks, seepage, or erosion reliably.

task: Identify or address malfunctions of hydroelectric plant operational equipment, such as generators, transformers, or turbines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing a single malfunction event on one piece of equipment. A skilled technician typically spends 60–120 min on inspection, fault isolation, and initial diagnosis.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert electrical/mechanical diagnosis, reading specialized gauges and control panels, interpreting vibration/thermal anomalies, and potentially accessing confined, wet, high-voltage industrial spaces. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and tool-use capabilities.

task: Expose and develop x-ray film.
human_time_rationale: One instance = exposing and developing a single x-ray film sheet, including positioning, exposure, darkroom processing (developer, fixer, wash, dry). Typical radiography workflow takes ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized radiography equipment, precise patient/subject positioning, darkroom chemical processing with fine dexterity, and strict radiation safety protocols. Far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capabilities.

task: Set up or maintain remote sensing data collection systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up or performing a maintenance check on a remote sensing station (e.g., weather station, sensor array). A competent technician typically takes 1–3 hours per site visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized tool use (cabling, calibration instruments, electronics), expert diagnostic judgment, outdoor/unstructured terrain, and configuration of complex software systems — well beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Adjust settings on patients' assistive devices, such as temporary pacemakers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting settings on a single assistive device (e.g., temporary pacemaker) for one patient; a trained clinician typically takes 5–15 minutes including assessment and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterous manipulation of specialized medical device controls, clinical judgment for patient-specific settings, real-time physiological interpretation, and regulatory/safety constraints — all far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Make emergency visits to resolve equipment problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one emergency visit to diagnose and resolve an equipment problem. Includes travel, troubleshooting, repair, and verification; median ~90 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires travel to unknown sites, open-ended diagnosis, unstructured repair with varied tools, and high-stakes decision-making. Current humanoids lack the autonomy, generalization, and dexterous tool-use for unscripted emergency repair scenarios.

task: Wash metal instruments in lacquer-stripping and cyanide solutions to remove lacquer and tarnish.
human_time_rationale: One instance = washing a batch of metal instruments; submerging, agitating, and rinsing in chemical solutions typically takes ~15 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires careful handling of highly hazardous chemicals (cyanide, lacquer stripper), precise immersion/timing, and safe chemical management. Current robots lack reliable chemical-safe manipulation, hazard awareness, and fine process control for such dangerous fluids.

task: Determine the effects of drug therapies, antibiotics, or new surgical techniques by testing them on animals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single experimental session (administering treatment, monitoring, recording observations) on one animal subject. A competent researcher typically spends ~2 hours per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert scientific judgment, precise injection/surgical technique, animal handling, physiological monitoring, and adaptive decision-making. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in fine dexterity, sterile technique, and domain generalization.

task: Assist clients in selecting frames according to style and color, and ensure that frames are coordinated with facial and eye measurements and optical prescriptions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one client consultation. A skilled optician spends ~15–25 min assessing face shape, coloring, prescription needs, and guiding frame selection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social interaction, aesthetic judgment, interpreting client preferences, and correlating facial measurements with prescriptions. Current robots lack conversational consultative ability, style reasoning, and trust-building needed for client-facing retail advising.

task: Dig trenches for system piping to appropriate depths and lay piping in trenches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = digging and laying pipe for a typical residential/utility trench run (~20–30 ft). A crew member with a trencher/shovel and pipe takes ~2 hours including depth checks and pipe placement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a trenching machine or excavator (vehicles/heavy equipment a humanoid cannot drive), outdoor uneven terrain, precise depth measurement, and heavy pipe manipulation. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Administer blood and blood product transfusions or intravenous infusions, monitoring patients for adverse reactions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and monitoring a single transfusion/infusion. Setup ~10–15 min; monitoring throughout a typical 1-hour infusion; total ~60 min of active/supervisory time.
robot_success_rationale: Requires IV catheter insertion (fine dexterity, sterile technique), clinical judgment for adverse reactions, patient communication, and real-time physiological interpretation — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured clinical settings.

task: Prepare stock radiopharmaceuticals, adhering to safety standards that minimize radiation exposure to workers and patients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single stock radiopharmaceutical batch (drawing up, labeling, QC checks). A trained nuclear pharmacist typically takes 30–60 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile technique, precise micro-volume pipetting with radioactive materials, real-time radiation monitoring, regulatory compliance, and operation of specialized hot-cell equipment — all far beyond current humanoid dexterity and safety certification limits.

task: Remove and replace dull cutting tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one dull tool (e.g., drill bit, blade, insert) and installing a sharp replacement, including locating the correct replacement and verifying seating/torque.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to loosen/tighten tool holders, precise alignment of cutting inserts or bits, and identification of correct replacement. Tool-change mechanisms vary widely; current humanoids lack reliable fine-force control for secure, precise seating.

task: Provide patients with help moving in and out of beds, baths, wheelchairs, or automobiles and with dressing and grooming.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one patient through a single transfer/grooming episode (e.g., bed-to-wheelchair + basic dressing). A competent aide typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safe physical contact with fragile humans, fine dexterity for dressing/grooming, adaptive force control, and dynamic balance during lifts. Current humanoids lack safe compliant manipulation and real-time patient-responsive caregiving at acceptable quality.

task: Provide veterinarians with the correct equipment or instruments, as needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retrieving and handing the correct instrument to a vet during a procedure. A competent assistant locates and delivers it in ~1–5 minutes depending on urgency and storage layout.
robot_success_rationale: Requires understanding verbal/contextual cues about which instrument is needed, navigating a clinical environment, identifying specialized veterinary tools, and safely handing them off — well beyond current robot generalization and fine-manipulation reliability.

task: Observe the behavior and condition of animals and monitor their clinical symptoms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = observing a single animal for behavioral and clinical assessment. A competent handler spends ~15–25 min per animal for thorough observation and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced interpretation of subtle behavioral cues, posture, gait, coat/skin condition, and clinical signs. Current robots lack veterinary domain perception models, animal interaction safety, and reliable fine-grained symptom recognition in unstructured animal environments.
