task: Set up, operate, or monitor invasive equipment and devices, such as colostomy or tracheotomy equipment, mechanical ventilators, catheters, gastrointestinal tubes, and central lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up or checking one invasive device (e.g., vent check, catheter change, G-tube flush). A competent nurse typically spends ~15–25 min per device setup/monitoring cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sterile technique, real-time clinical judgment, and patient interaction with fragile anatomy. Far beyond current humanoid capability; patient safety risk makes autonomous operation effectively zero.

task: Perform team support duties, such as running errands, maintaining equipment, or stocking supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single support duty cycle (e.g., one errand run, one restock, or one equipment check). Competent worker completes in ~20 min on average.
robot_success_rationale: Task is highly variable and unstructured—navigating buildings, identifying needed supplies, operating elevators, and stocking shelves require robust generalization, perception, and mobile manipulation that current robots handle inconsistently. Outdoor errands are near-impossible.

task: Provide post-mortem care.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single deceased patient's body (positioning, cleaning, ID tagging, wrapping/shrouding). A competent nurse/aide typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme delicacy with a human body, precise repositioning of limbs, fine manual tasks (tying tags, packing wounds, shrouding), culturally sensitive judgment, and coordination in a clinical room — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Develop or test photonic prototypes or models.
human_time_rationale: One instance = developing or testing a single photonic prototype/model session, including setup, alignment, measurement, and documentation. Typically 2–4 hours for a competent photonics engineer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-micron optical alignment, specialized instruments (spectrum analyzers, oscilloscopes, laser sources), expert scientific judgment, iterative troubleshooting, and deep domain knowledge. Far beyond any 2026 general-purpose robot's capability.

task: Analyze, fabricate, or test fiber-optic links.
human_time_rationale: One instance = analyzing, fabricating, or testing a single fiber-optic link (e.g., splicing two ends, running OTDR tests, and verifying signal integrity). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 minutes per link.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter dexterous manipulation (cleaving, splicing, connector insertion), specialized optical instruments (OTDR, power meter), fine perception of tiny fiber ends, and tool-use generalization — all far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Arrange and maintain displays of optical merchandise.
human_time_rationale: One instance = arranging/refreshing a single optical display case or shelf unit. A skilled optician typically takes ~20–30 min to organize, position, and label frames attractively.
robot_success_rationale: Optical frames are small, fragile, highly varied in shape, and require precise aesthetic placement on specialized pegs/stands. Current robots lack the fine dexterity, visual merchandising judgment, and gentle handling needed for success.

task: Clean, inspect, or lubricate recyclable collection equipment or perform routine maintenance or minor repairs on recycling equipment, such as star gears, finger sorters, destoners, belts, and grinders.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance session on a single piece of recycling equipment (e.g., cleaning/lubricating a star gear or belt unit); a competent technician typically spends 30–60 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating cluttered industrial environments, identifying worn/dirty components, applying lubricant precisely, and performing minor repairs on highly varied machinery. Current robots lack the generalization, tool-use dexterity, and domain-specific perception needed for autonomous maintenance at acceptable quality.

task: Accompany clients to doctors' offices or on other trips outside the home, providing transportation, assistance, and companionship.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one outing (e.g., a doctor's visit). Includes transit to/from destination, wait time, and assisting the client throughout — typically 1.5–2.5 hours total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a vehicle (humanoids cannot), navigating unfamiliar public environments, complex social/communication skills, and responsive caregiving for a vulnerable person — far beyond current robot capabilities.

task: Identify malfunctioning equipment or devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single piece of equipment for faults. A competent technician spends ~20 min on visual checks, functional tests, and diagnosis.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can detect obvious visual anomalies or read sensor outputs, but diagnosing subtle malfunctions requires contextual reasoning, varied tool use, and interpreting complex multi-modal cues — well beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Prepare a major meal, following recipes and determining group food quantities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a full multi-dish meal for a group (~8–12 people), including reading recipes, scaling quantities, and cooking. Typically 2–3 hours total; median ~120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires recipe comprehension, multi-step sequencing, scaling math, fine dexterous manipulation (chopping, stirring, pouring), heat management, and improvisation over 2+ hours. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured kitchens.

task: Monitor patients for changes in status and indications of conditions such as sepsis or shock and institute appropriate interventions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single focused patient assessment/monitoring round, including vital signs check, observation, and initiating interventions if needed. Typically 15–25 minutes per patient.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level clinical reasoning, interpreting multi-modal physiological data, recognizing subtle signs (e.g., mottling, altered mentation), and initiating complex interventions. Far beyond current robot cognition and dexterous caregiving capability.

task: Measure energy usage with devices such as data loggers, universal data recorders, light meters, sling psychrometers, psychrometric charts, flue gas analyzers, amp probes, watt meters, volt meters, thermometers, or utility meters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single energy measurement session at one site/system: setting up instruments, collecting readings, and logging data. ~45 min for a competent energy auditor.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling and correctly connecting diverse specialized instruments (amp probes, flue gas analyzers, sling psychrometers), interpreting analog/digital readings, navigating varied environments, and making measurement judgments. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use generalization and sensor-interpretation capability for this multi-instrument task.

task: Install, calibrate, or maintain sensors, mechanical controls, GPS-based vehicle guidance systems, or computer settings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing/calibrating/maintaining a single sensor or guidance system unit. A competent technician typically spends 45–90 minutes per unit, including setup, calibration checks, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of small connectors/fasteners, reading diagnostic displays, interpreting calibration software, and operating specialized tools — far beyond current humanoid generalization. Also often involves vehicle-mounted or outdoor equipment inaccessible to humanoids.

task: Care for athletic injuries, using physical therapy equipment, techniques, or medication.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single athletic injury care/treatment session (assessment + therapy application). A competent athletic trainer or PT typically spends 30–60 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical assessment, adaptive dexterity for varied PT equipment/techniques, medication handling, and nuanced patient-specific decision-making. Far beyond current humanoid generalization, safety, and fine-motor capability.

task: Repair, adjust, or replace electrical or mechanical components or parts, using hand tools, power tools, or soldering or welding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/adjusting/replacing a single component. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min diagnosing, accessing, and completing one repair.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosis, fine dexterity for soldering/welding, tool switching, and adapting to varied components and configurations. Current humanoids lack the precision, contextual judgment, and specialized tool-use needed for autonomous repair work.

task: Prepare and position patients for testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and positioning a single patient for one test (e.g., imaging, ECG, blood draw). Includes communication, physical repositioning, and draping.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced physical contact with a human body, real-time pain/comfort feedback interpretation, verbal communication, adaptive repositioning, and safety compliance — all far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Line slots with sheet insulation, and insert coils into slots.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lining all slots and inserting all coils for one stator/armature. A competent winder typically takes 30–60 min depending on slot count and coil size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-fingered manipulation of thin, flexible sheet insulation into narrow slots, then threading/seating wound coils without damaging fragile wire — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and compliant force-control capabilities.

task: Operate equipment, such as excavators, backhoes, rock hammers, trench compactors, pavement saws, grout mixers or pumps, geothermal loop reels, and coil tubing units (CTU).
human_time_rationale: One instance = one continuous equipment operation session (e.g., excavating a trench or running a CTU). A typical job segment runs ~3–4 hours before a break or handoff.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate seated cab-based heavy equipment in real field conditions. No 2026 general-purpose robot can drive, control hydraulics, or adapt to unstructured excavation sites autonomously and safely.

task: Determine clients' current lens prescriptions, when necessary, using lensometers or lens analyzers and clients' eyeglasses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring both lenses of one pair of eyeglasses. A skilled optician places each lens on a lensometer, reads sphere/cylinder/axis/add, and records results in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual manipulation of small, delicate eyeglasses, exact alignment on a lensometer eyepiece, and reading optical/digital output. Perception of tiny lens markings and consistent sub-mm positioning exceed current humanoid dexterity and fine manipulation reliability.

task: Aid patients in dressing and grooming themselves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one patient with a full dressing and grooming session (clothing, hair, hygiene basics). A competent aide typically takes 15–25 minutes per patient.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (buttons, zippers, combing), safe physical contact with a fragile/moving person, adaptive responses to patient behavior, and social/emotional sensitivity — all well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Calculate, measure, load, or mix biomass feedstock for power generation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and loading a single batch of biomass feedstock (measuring, mixing, and loading into a feed system). A competent worker typically completes this in ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy industrial equipment (loaders, conveyors, scales), handling bulky/irregular biomass materials outdoors, and making precise measurements. Humanoids lack the tool-use, payload capacity, and generalization for this industrial setting.

task: Perform tests such as blower-door tests to locate air leaks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full blower-door test on a typical home: equipment setup, pressurization, leak location via smoke pencil/IR camera, and logging results. Typically 45–90 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires hauling and precisely installing large specialized equipment (blower-door fan, frame, manometer), sealing it airtight in a door frame, then navigating the full home with IR/smoke tools and interpreting subtle leak signals. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and tool-use capability.

task: Conduct pulmonary assessments to identify abnormal respiratory patterns or breathing sounds that indicate problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full pulmonary assessment on one patient, including auscultation, observation of breathing pattern, and initial interpretation. Typically 10–20 minutes for a competent clinician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise stethoscope placement on a human body, nuanced acoustic interpretation, patient interaction/positioning, and clinical judgment. No 2026 general-purpose humanoid has demonstrated autonomous clinical auscultation or medical diagnosis at acceptable quality.

task: Administer post-anesthesia medications or fluids to support patients' cardiovascular systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and administering a single post-anesthesia cardiovascular medication/fluid to one patient, including assessment, draw-up, IV administration, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile IV/drug preparation, clinical judgment on dosing, patient assessment, interacting with IV lines/pumps, and regulatory/safety compliance. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, medical reasoning, and autonomy limits.

task: Maintain electrical switchgear, process controls, transmitters, gauges, and control equipment in accordance with geothermal plant procedures.
human_time_rationale: One maintenance instance covers a scheduled inspection/calibration/repair cycle on a piece of switchgear or instrument; a competent technician typically spends 60–120 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized electrical/instrumentation knowledge, fine dexterous tool use on high-voltage panels, reading analog gauges, operating proprietary calibration equipment, and strict safety interlocks — far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured industrial environments.

task: Dismantle machines and remove parts for repair, using hand tools, chain falls, jacks, cranes, or hoists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dismantling one machine or major assembly for repair. A competent millwright/mechanic typically spends 60–120 min depending on machine complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating cranes, hoists, chain falls, and jacks — equipment a humanoid cannot control. Also demands high-torque fastener removal, heavy-load rigging, and adaptive problem-solving in cluttered industrial environments far beyond current robot capability.

task: Evaluate patients' post-surgical or post-anesthesia responses, taking appropriate corrective actions or requesting consultation if complications occur.
human_time_rationale: One instance = evaluating a single patient in the post-anesthesia care unit (PACU), from initial assessment through stabilization or handoff; typically 30–60 minutes per patient.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical reasoning, physiological interpretation (vitals, pain, neuro status), nuanced patient communication, and real-time medical decision-making. No current humanoid robot has the medical AI integration, fine-touch assessment, or autonomous clinical judgment to safely perform this.

task: Operate hydroelectric plant equipment, such as turbines, pumps, valves, gates, fans, electric control boards, or battery banks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single operational shift task cycle (e.g., starting/stopping a turbine unit, adjusting valves/gates, monitoring and logging readings). A competent operator typically takes ~30–60 min per major equipment operation cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Hydroelectric plant controls involve specialized industrial panels, analog/digital interfaces, heavy valves, and safety-critical sequencing. Current humanoid robots lack plant-specific tool interfaces, fail on non-standard controls, and cannot safely operate high-voltage or high-pressure systems autonomously.

task: Perform a variety of duties as requested by client, such as obtaining household supplies or running errands.
human_time_rationale: One errand instance (e.g., fetching household supplies or running a single errand) takes a competent worker roughly 20–40 minutes including travel and task completion.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor navigation, vehicle operation or public transit, unstructured environments, and social interaction (e.g., entering stores, paying). Current humanoids lack autonomous driving, outdoor generalization, and financial transaction capability.

task: Remove excess cement from coronal surfaces of teeth.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing excess cement from all coronal surfaces after a single cementation procedure (e.g., crown seating). A skilled dentist/assistant typically takes 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity inside a small oral cavity, precise force control with dental instruments near sensitive tissue, real-time tactile feedback, and patient safety management — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Develop laser-processed designs, such as laser-cut medical devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and iterating a single laser-processed medical device (CAD design, parameter tuning, test cut, review). Skilled engineer typically takes 2–6 hours per design cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Task is primarily cognitive/creative engineering: CAD modeling, design iteration, regulatory awareness, material selection, and laser parameter optimization. Current humanoid robots lack autonomous design cognition, creative problem-solving, and engineering judgment entirely.

task: Make impressions for study casts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = taking upper and lower dental impressions for one patient, including tray selection, material mixing, seating, setting time, and removal. ~15–25 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise intraoral manipulation in a confined, wet, soft-tissue environment; patient cooperation management; fine tactile feedback; and clinical safety — all far beyond current humanoid dexterity and caregiving capabilities.

task: Decorate baked goods, such as cakes or pastries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = decorating a single cake or batch of pastries. A competent baker spends ~15–25 min on piping, spreading frosting, and adding garnishes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterity for piping, precise force control for delicate surfaces, aesthetic judgment, and tool manipulation (piping bag, spatula). Current humanoids lack the fine motor control and creative/visual quality standards needed.

task: Take animals into treatment areas and assist with physical examinations by performing such duties as obtaining temperature, pulse, or respiration data.
human_time_rationale: One instance = escorting one animal to a treatment area and recording temperature, pulse, and respiration. A competent vet tech typically completes this in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safely handling an uncooperative live animal, applying sensors to fur/skin, interpreting subtle physiological cues, and navigating a clinical environment — far beyond current robot dexterity, force-sensitive manipulation, and animal-behavior generalization.

task: Test water sources for factors, such as flow volume and contaminant presence.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single water source site (collecting samples, deploying sensors, recording flow/contaminant readings). A competent field technician typically spends ~45 min per site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/unstructured terrain navigation, fine manipulation to collect water samples and deploy sensors, calibrated instrument use, and safe handling of wet/slippery environments — well beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Operate equipment to start, stop, or regulate biomass-fueled generators, generator units, boilers, engines, or auxiliary systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one start/stop/regulation cycle of a biomass system. A competent operator spends ~20–30 min checking readings, adjusting controls, and confirming stable operation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating complex, specialized industrial control panels, valves, and gauges in a hazardous environment. Current humanoids lack training on such bespoke heavy-energy equipment, fine valve manipulation, and safe fault response.

task: Operate television or motion picture cameras to record scenes for television broadcasts, advertising, or motion pictures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filming one scene or segment. A typical scene setup, rehearsal, and shoot takes ~30–90 min; median ~60 min for a competent camera operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced artistic framing decisions, real-time creative direction response, precise pan/tilt/zoom control, and coordination with crew. Current humanoids lack the dexterous tool-use, aesthetic judgment, and specialized equipment operation needed.

task: Respond to emergency situations by providing airway management, administering emergency fluids or drugs, or using basic or advanced cardiac life support techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single emergency response event (e.g., cardiac arrest or airway crisis), from initial assessment through stabilization or handoff, typically 10–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert clinical judgment, fine dexterity (intubation, IV placement), dynamic patient assessment, and adaptive decision-making under life-or-death pressure. No current humanoid robot can perform any of these reliably or safely.

task: Direct patients in simple prescribed exercises or in the use of braces or artificial limbs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient session directing prescribed exercises or assistive device use; a typical session runs ~15–25 minutes for a competent therapist/aide.
robot_success_rationale: Requires natural language instruction, adaptive feedback, reading patient pain/confusion cues, physical guidance, and safe interaction with vulnerable individuals — well beyond current humanoid social/caregiving capabilities.

task: Examine or conduct laboratory or diagnostic tests on patients to provide information on general physical condition or mental disorder.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient examination or diagnostic test session (e.g., physical exam + basic labs). A competent clinician typically completes this in ~20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires medical-grade dexterity (blood draws, reflex tests), nuanced patient interaction, clinical judgment, and regulatory-safe operation. Current humanoids lack the precision, certified tools, and cognitive reasoning for safe autonomous clinical diagnostics.

task: Stop machines to remove finished workpieces or to change tooling, setup, or workpiece placement, according to required machining sequences.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stopping a machine, removing one finished workpiece, and changing tooling/placement/setup. A skilled machinist typically completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting machining sequences, operating machine controls, safely removing workpieces from fixtures, and changing tooling with precision. Generalization across varied machines, tools, and setups is well beyond current humanoid capability; high failure risk on controls, clamps, and fine tool changes.

task: Show customers how to insert, remove, and care for their contact lenses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one customer education session covering insertion, removal, and care steps; a trained optician typically spends 15–25 minutes per new contact lens patient.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced verbal/visual instruction, fine dexterous demonstration on a human eye, real-time adaptive coaching, and handling tiny lenses near a patient's eye — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, social interaction, and safety limits.

task: Demonstrate therapy techniques, such as manual or creative arts or games.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one therapy demonstration session (e.g., showing a technique to a patient or trainee). A typical focused demo runs ~30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social-emotional engagement, adaptive communication, reading patient affect, and purposeful creative/manual guidance. Current robots lack the social intelligence, therapeutic judgment, and expressive interaction needed. Fine dexterity for manual therapy is also extremely limited.

task: Obtain specimens or samples for laboratory work.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a single specimen/sample (e.g., blood draw, swab, soil core, tissue biopsy). A competent technician typically takes 10–20 minutes including prep, collection, and labeling.
robot_success_rationale: Specimen collection demands extreme fine dexterity (needles, swabs, biopsies), sterile technique, adaptive perception of variable anatomy, and context-specific judgment. Current humanoid robots lack the precision, sterility control, and domain-specific tooling to perform this autonomously at acceptable quality.

task: Examine gums, using probes, to locate periodontal recessed gums and signs of gum disease.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full-mouth periodontal probing for one patient. A competent dental hygienist probes all six sites per tooth across ~28 teeth in ~6–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity inside a small oral cavity, safe force control at sub-millimeter precision, and clinical interpretation of soft-tissue findings. Far beyond 2026 humanoid robot capability; risk of patient injury is prohibitive.

task: Check the quality of raw materials to ensure that standards and specifications are met.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single batch/lot of raw material. A competent worker checks documentation, visually inspects, samples, and compares against spec sheets — typically 15–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading spec sheets, interpreting multi-modal sensory data (color, texture, odor, chemical composition), using measurement instruments, and exercising judgment. Current robots lack reliable tool-use for sampling, chemical testing, and unstructured quality reasoning.

task: Monitor patients' responses, including skin color, pupil dilation, pulse, heart rate, blood pressure, respiration, ventilation, or urine output, using invasive and noninvasive techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full multi-parameter patient assessment round; a competent nurse/anesthesiologist checks all listed vitals and documents findings in ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires attaching/reading invasive lines, interpreting subtle clinical signs (pupil dilation, skin color), manipulating diverse medical devices, and exercising clinical judgment — far beyond 2026 humanoid capability in real patient care settings.

task: Lift workpieces onto work tables either manually or with hoists or direct crane operators to lift and position workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lifting/positioning a single workpiece onto a work table. A competent worker takes ~2–8 min depending on weight and method (manual vs. hoist/crane).
robot_success_rationale: Light workpieces on flat terrain are feasible, but heavy or irregular pieces require hoist/crane operation, which humanoids cannot do. Precise positioning on a work table and directing crane operators adds coordination complexity beyond current capability.

task: Assemble, clean, or maintain equipment or materials for patient use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing/cleaning/assembling one set of patient-use equipment (e.g., IV setup, suction device, or tray). A competent clinical technician typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for sterile assembly, perception of medical-grade components, adherence to hygiene protocols, and context-sensitive judgment. Current humanoids lack sterile technique, fine manipulation precision, and medical tool familiarity needed for safe patient-use equipment prep.

task: Conduct dental health clinics for community groups to augment services of dentist.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one community dental health clinic session, typically 2–4 hours including setup, patient education, screenings, and wrap-up.
robot_success_rationale: Requires oral examination, patient communication, community coordination, clinical judgment, and intraoral dexterity. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, clinical licensing capability, and social/communicative competence needed for autonomous dental health clinics.

task: Heat, shape, or bend plastic or metal frames to adjust eyeglasses to fit clients, using pliers and hands.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting one pair of eyeglasses for one client. A skilled optician typically completes heating, bending, and fitting adjustments in 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity with pliers on small, fragile frames, precise force control to avoid breakage, heat application judgment, and real-time client feedback integration — all well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Massage patients or apply preparations or treatments, such as liniment, alcohol rubs, or heat-lamp stimulation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single therapeutic massage or treatment session on one patient, typically 20–45 minutes for a standard session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced force control, adaptive dexterity across varied body shapes, real-time pain/comfort feedback interpretation, and safe skin contact. Far beyond current humanoid caregiving capability; unstructured body contact with patients is a severe limitation.

task: Calibrate and test anesthesia equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full pre-use calibration and safety check of a single anesthesia machine; a trained anesthesia tech or CRNA typically takes 15–25 minutes per machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise manipulation of specialized valves, breathing circuits, and digital controls; interpreting pressure/flow gauges; running leak tests; safety-critical tolerances. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, domain-specific tool knowledge, and reliable perception for this high-stakes task.

task: Dress and suture wounds and apply splints or other protective devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating a single wound or injury site; includes cleaning, dressing/suturing, and applying a splint/protective device. A competent clinician typically takes 15–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Suturing demands extreme fine dexterity and tactile feedback beyond current humanoids. Splint fitting requires adaptive force sensing on a living person. Safety criticality and unstructured anatomy make autonomous success near-zero.

task: Lay out reference lines and machining locations on work, using layout tools, and applying knowledge of shop math and layout techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out reference lines and machining locations on a single workpiece. A skilled machinist typically takes 15–35 min depending on part complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor tool use (scribers, height gauges, dividers), reading blueprints, applying shop math, and repositioning delicate tools with sub-mm accuracy — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and cognitive integration.

task: Feel lymph nodes under patient's chin to detect swelling or tenderness that could indicate presence of oral cancer.
human_time_rationale: One instance = palpating submental/submandibular lymph nodes on one patient. A skilled clinician completes this in ~1–3 minutes as part of a head/neck exam.
robot_success_rationale: Requires calibrated tactile sensitivity to detect subtle swelling/tenderness, safe patient contact with precise force control, and clinical interpretation of haptic feedback — all far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Diagnose or treat acute health care problems, such as illnesses, infections, or injuries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient encounter (history, exam, diagnosis, treatment plan). A competent clinician typically spends 20–40 min per acute visit.
robot_success_rationale: Diagnosis and treatment require clinical reasoning, nuanced physical examination, interpretation of symptoms/labs, legal authority, and patient communication — far beyond any 2026 general-purpose robot's autonomous capabilities. No frontier humanoid can perform this safely or legally.

task: Manage patients' airway or pulmonary status, using techniques such as endotracheal intubation, mechanical ventilation, pharmacological support, respiratory therapy, and extubation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single airway/pulmonary management episode (e.g., intubation setup through stabilization or a ventilator-management assessment). A competent clinician spends ~30–60 min per episode.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (laryngoscopy, ET tube placement), real-time clinical judgment, pharmacological decision-making, sterile technique, and patient-specific adaptation — all far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Install and maintain geothermal plant electrical protection equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing or performing a maintenance cycle on a protection relay/switchgear unit at a geothermal plant. A competent electrician typically takes 2–4 hours per unit including testing and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-voltage electrical expertise, precise wiring of protection relays, use of specialized test equipment, safety interlocking, and regulatory compliance. Far beyond 2026 humanoid robot capability in unstructured industrial HV environments.

task: Operate single- or multiple-spindle drill presses to bore holes so that machining operations can be performed on metal or plastic workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling a set of holes in one workpiece. A competent machinist takes ~5–10 min to set up, position, clamp, and drill a typical batch workpiece on a drill press.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a specialized industrial drill press with precise workpiece clamping, spindle speed/feed selection, and positional accuracy. Current humanoid robots lack the precision tool control, machine interfacing, and safety judgment needed for unsupervised machining operations.

task: Erect scaffolds, platforms, or hoisting frames to access hydroelectric plant machinery or infrastructure for repair or replacement.
human_time_rationale: One instance = erecting a scaffold/platform at one hydroelectric plant work site. A crew typically takes 2–4 hours depending on height and complexity; 3 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy, irregular components; precise load-bearing assembly; working at height on wet/industrial terrain; team coordination; and safety-critical judgment. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capability in unstructured industrial environments.

task: Maintain equipment or furniture to keep it in good working condition, including performing the assembly or disassembly of equipment or accessories.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance, assembly, or disassembly job on one piece of equipment/furniture (e.g., tightening, lubricating, swapping an accessory). ~45 min is a reasonable median across varied tasks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly varied dexterity (fasteners, tools, connectors), diagnosis of wear/faults, and generalization across countless equipment types. Current humanoids lack reliable tool use, torque sensing, and fault diagnosis for unstructured maintenance tasks.

task: Lift and move loads, using cranes, hoists, and rigging, to install or repair hydroelectric system equipment or infrastructure.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rigging, lifting, and positioning one major load (e.g., a turbine component) at a hydro facility. A competent rigger/crane operator typically takes 1–3 hours per lift.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating cranes/hoists and rigging heavy industrial loads — specialized heavy equipment a humanoid robot cannot drive or operate. Outdoor/wet environments, safety-critical precision, and non-standard rigging further preclude current robots.

task: Train patients to use orthopedic braces, prostheses, or supportive devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient training session covering fitting, donning/doffing, and use of a device; typically 30–60 min for a competent clinician/orthotist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced verbal/physical instruction, patient-specific adaptation, tactile assessment, empathy, and clinical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured caregiving and communication tasks.

task: Collect specimens for laboratory tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a single specimen (e.g., blood draw or swab) from one patient, including prep, collection, labeling, and packaging. Typically 10–20 minutes for a competent phlebotomist/tech.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (venipuncture, swab insertion), safe patient interaction, sterile technique, and situational judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability; even specialized medical robots struggle with unassisted specimen collection on varied patients.

task: Perform routine or annual physical examinations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one routine annual physical exam for a single patient, including history review, vitals, head-to-toe assessment, and documentation. Typically 30–60 minutes in clinical practice.
robot_success_rationale: Requires licensed clinical judgment, fine dexterous palpation, auscultation with nuanced interpretation, patient communication, and trust. No 2026 general-purpose humanoid has the sensory suite, medical certification, or clinical reasoning to perform this autonomously at acceptable quality.

task: Perform emergency medical procedures, such as basic cardiac life support (BLS), advanced cardiac life support (ACLS), and other condition-stabilizing interventions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single emergency intervention (e.g., one BLS/CPR episode or ACLS protocol). Median active intervention time from initiation to stabilization or handoff is ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled chest compressions, airway management, IV/drug administration, real-time clinical judgment, and adaptive response to patient physiology — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, medical tool-use, and autonomous decision-making capabilities.

task: Lead stretching exercises for team members prior to games or practices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single pre-game/practice stretching session for a team, typically lasting 8–12 minutes of guided dynamic and static stretches.
robot_success_rationale: Requires leading a group, demonstrating fluid full-body stretches, cueing verbal instructions, monitoring participants, and adapting in real time. Current humanoids lack social coordination, balance for complex poses, and group-interaction generalization.

task: Assist physicians with procedures such as bronchoscopy, endoscopy, endotracheal intubation, or elective cardioversion.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting with a single procedure (e.g., one bronchoscopy). A competent respiratory therapist or nurse spends ~20–30 min on setup, assistance, and post-procedure care.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile technique, fine dexterity, real-time coordination with a physician, patient interaction, and adaptive responses to complications. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in clinical settings.

task: Participate in fieldwork.
human_time_rationale: One fieldwork session (e.g., site survey, data collection outdoors); a typical half-day excursion including travel to site, sampling, recording, and return.
robot_success_rationale: Fieldwork spans unstructured outdoor terrain, variable weather, complex judgement calls (site selection, sample collection, instrument use, documentation), and multi-hour autonomy — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Backfill piping trenches to protect pipes from damage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = backfilling one standard residential/commercial piping trench (~20–50 ft). A competent worker using a combination of machinery and hand tools takes ~1–1.5 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating excavators/compactors or heavy shoveling in unstructured outdoor terrain. Humanoid robots cannot drive or operate heavy construction equipment; outdoor rough terrain and large-force compaction tasks are well beyond current capability.

task: Prepare sterile solutions or infusions for use in surgical procedures, emergency rooms, or patients' homes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single sterile solution/infusion bag. A trained pharmacy tech or nurse typically takes 15–25 minutes including labeling, mixing, and sterility checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-fine dexterity (syringe handling, needle insertion into vials/bags), strict aseptic technique, compounding calculations, and regulatory compliance. Current humanoids lack the precision, sterile-field awareness, and tool mastery needed.

task: Insert peripheral or central intravenous catheters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inserting a single IV catheter (peripheral or central). Peripheral IVs take ~5–10 min; central lines ~20–30 min; blended median ~15 min for a competent clinician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter needle precision into a moving, compliant vein on a live patient, sterile technique, real-time haptic feedback, and patient safety oversight — far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot dexterity and force-sensing capabilities.

task: Install and seal air ducts, combustion air openings, or ventilation openings to improve heating and cooling efficiency.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing and sealing ducts/openings in a typical residential space. A competent HVAC technician takes ~2 hours per session including measuring, cutting, fitting, and sealing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating tight, confined attic/crawlspace environments, precise cutting and fitting of sheet metal, applying mastic sealant, and tool use in awkward positions — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured spaces.

task: Ensure that equipment or devices are properly stored after use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = returning a single piece of equipment/device to its designated storage location after use; a competent worker typically takes 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and carry common objects reasonably well, but identifying the correct storage location, navigating to it, and placing items precisely (e.g., on shelves, in cases, or on hooks) in varied, unstructured environments remains unreliable.

task: Treat wounds or superficial lacerations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating a single wound/superficial laceration: cleaning, applying antiseptic, bandaging. A competent worker (EMT, nurse, first responder) typically takes 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation on a compliant, unpredictable human body, pain-sensitive positioning, wound assessment, and sterile technique. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and safe human-contact capabilities.

task: Conduct physical examinations and physiological function tests for diagnostic purposes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient physical exam with basic physiological tests (vitals, auscultation, reflex checks, etc.); a competent clinician typically takes 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine tactile dexterity (palpation, auscultation placement), clinical judgment, patient interaction, and use of specialized diagnostic instruments. Far beyond current humanoid capability; high safety/liability bar.

task: Sharpen cutting tools, using bench grinders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sharpening one cutting tool (e.g., a chisel or drill bit) on a bench grinder; a skilled worker takes ~5–10 minutes including setup, grinding, and checking the edge.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise angle control, fine force feedback, and real-time edge inspection against a spinning grinder wheel. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tactile sensing, and tool-specific grinding expertise needed; safety risks are high.

task: Administer blood and blood products, monitoring patients for signs and symptoms related to transfusion reactions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one transfusion episode. Includes setup, IV administration (~30–60 min for a unit of blood), and active monitoring for reactions throughout.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile IV access, clinical judgment to detect subtle reaction signs (rash, dyspnea, vital changes), real-time decision-making, and emergency intervention. Far beyond current robot dexterity, medical perception, and safety certification.

task: Perform pre-anesthetic screenings, including physical evaluations and patient interviews, and document results.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient screening. A competent anesthesiologist/CRNA conducts a physical exam, reviews history, interviews the patient, and documents findings — typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced clinical judgment, nuanced patient communication, auscultation, airway assessment, and medical documentation. Far beyond current robot perception, dexterity, and cognitive generalization. No deployed system approaches this.

task: Conduct periodic public health maintenance activities such as immunizations and screenings for diseases and disease risk factors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient encounter (immunization or screening). A competent nurse/health worker spends ~15–25 min including intake, procedure, documentation, and post-care observation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile needle injection with precise anatomical targeting, patient communication, clinical judgment on contraindications, regulatory/liability constraints, and adaptive caregiving — all far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Participate in patient care tasks, such as assisting with passing food trays, feeding residents, or bathing residents on bed rest.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient interaction (e.g., feeding one resident or one bed bath). A competent aide typically spends 15–25 minutes per resident per care task.
robot_success_rationale: Caregiving requires gentle, adaptive dexterity, safe human contact, social/emotional responsiveness, and fluid decision-making around fragile patients. Current humanoids lack the fine-touch sensitivity, safety guarantees, and generalization needed for bathing or feeding in unstructured clinical settings.

task: Administer prescribed doses of radiation to specific body parts, using radiation therapy equipment according to established practices and standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one radiation therapy treatment session for a single patient, including setup, positioning, verification imaging, and dose delivery. Typical sessions run 15–45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a highly specialized linear accelerator or similar medical device, precise patient positioning, imaging verification, and clinical judgment. Current humanoid robots cannot operate this equipment, interpret treatment plans, or safely interact with vulnerable patients autonomously.

task: Check patients' pulse, temperature, and respiration.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking all three vitals (pulse, temp, respiration) on one patient. A competent nurse/aide typically completes this in ~5 minutes including documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires gentle, precise physical contact with a human patient (thermometer placement, pulse palpation/oximetry positioning, chest observation), patient cooperation, safe force control, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous caregiving and patient-safe interaction.

task: Clean work areas to ensure compliance with safety regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single work area (e.g., a workstation or bay) to safety-compliance standards; includes tidying, wiping surfaces, clearing hazards, and disposing of waste.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep/mop/wipe flat surfaces reasonably well, but identifying safety hazards (spills, blocked exits, improper chemical storage) and acting on compliance standards requires contextual judgment and generalization beyond current capability.

task: Design, install, or troubleshoot manufacturing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single equipment design, installation, or troubleshooting job. Even simple troubleshooting takes 1–4 hours; complex installs take days. 3 hrs is a reasonable median unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level diagnosis, schematic interpretation, precision mechanical/electrical work, tool use in unstructured industrial settings, and adaptive problem-solving far beyond current robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Make wood replacement parts, using woodworking machines and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single replacement wood part (e.g., a panel, rail, or bracket). A competent woodworker spends ~60 min measuring, cutting, shaping, and finishing one part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting blueprints/damaged originals, operating stationary woodworking machines (table saw, router, lathe), precise measuring, and fine hand-tool work. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, machine-operation capability, and adaptive judgment needed.

task: Perform approved therapeutic or diagnostic procedures, based upon patients' clinical status.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one therapeutic or diagnostic procedure (e.g., drawing blood, administering IV, wound dressing). Competent clinician typically takes 15–45 min including prep, execution, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (needle insertion, catheter placement), real-time clinical judgment, patient communication, sterile technique, and adaptive response to patient status — all far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capabilities.

task: Insert arterial catheters or perform arterial punctures to obtain arterial blood samples.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one arterial puncture or catheter insertion. A skilled clinician typically takes 5–15 minutes including site prep, puncture/cannulation, and dressing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter precision needle insertion into a pulsing artery, real-time tactile/haptic feedback, sterile technique, and clinical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and safety limits; even surgical robots require teleoperation.

task: Perform diagnostic tests or measurements, such as motor testing, visual acuity testing, lensometry, retinoscopy, and color vision testing.
human_time_rationale: One full diagnostic session with a patient covering multiple ophthalmic/optometric tests (motor, acuity, lensometry, retinoscopy, color vision). Competent technician typically takes 20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous instrument handling (retinoscope, lensometer), precise patient interaction, verbal instruction comprehension, and clinical judgment. No 2026 humanoid has demonstrated ophthalmic diagnostic capability. Highly unstructured caregiving + specialized tools.

task: Operate balers to compress recyclable materials into bundles or bales.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading material, operating baler controls, and ejecting one finished bale; typical cycle is 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Balers are large, specialized industrial machines with dedicated controls. A humanoid robot cannot safely operate the machinery, feed bulk recyclables, or manage bale ejection/wire-tying without task-specific integration. No demonstrated general-purpose capability.

task: Inspect sample workpieces to verify conformance with specifications, using instruments such as gauges, micrometers, and dial indicators.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single sample workpiece. A competent inspector typically takes 5–15 minutes to measure multiple dimensions with gauges, micrometers, and dial indicators and record results.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with precise tool handling (micrometers, dial indicators require fine dexterity and exact seating), interpreting analog readouts, and repositioning workpieces. Perception and manipulation accuracy at micron-level tolerances remain major limitations in 2026.

task: Apply protective or injury preventive devices, such as tape, bandages, or braces, to body parts, such as ankles, fingers, or wrists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = taping/bracing one body part (e.g., an ankle). A competent athletic trainer typically takes 5–12 minutes depending on technique and body part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous manipulation on a compliant, curved human body part, correct tension control, and adaptive wrapping patterns. Far beyond current humanoid fine-motor and force-sensing capabilities; high injury risk if wrong tension applied.

task: Administer active or passive manual therapeutic exercises, therapeutic massage, or heat, light, sound, water, or electrical modality treatments, such as ultrasound.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single therapeutic treatment session (e.g., massage, ultrasound, or guided exercise) with one patient, typically 20–45 minutes in a clinical PT/OT setting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine-force-controlled contact on human body, dynamic patient assessment, real-time pain/comfort feedback interpretation, adaptive manual technique, and clinical judgment — all far beyond current humanoid capability. Safety constraints also prohibit autonomous unsupervised patient contact.

task: Assist patients to dress, undress, or put on and remove supportive devices, such as braces, splints, or slings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = helping one patient dress/undress or fit one device. A competent aide typically takes 5–15 minutes depending on patient mobility and garment complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires gentle, adaptive dexterity around a moving, pain-sensitive human body; fitting braces/splints demands fine force control, social cues, and compliance with irregular anatomy — well beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Design, fabricate, or repair assistive devices or make adaptive changes to equipment or environments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing/fabricating or repairing one assistive device or adaptive modification. A competent occupational/rehab technician typically spends 60–120 minutes per item.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative design reasoning, client-specific assessment, fine dexterous fabrication (cutting, molding, sewing, soldering), and adaptive problem-solving across highly varied contexts — far beyond current robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Operate and maintain steam autoclaves, keeping records of loads completed, items in loads, and maintenance procedures performed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one autoclave cycle: loading items (~10 min), running the cycle (~20 min supervised), unloading, and logging the load/maintenance record (~5 min).
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial controls, precise loading of varied items, interpreting cycle readouts, and completing written/digital records. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-specific UI interaction, contextual judgment, and record-keeping integration for this workflow.

task: Perform venipuncture or skin pricking to collect blood samples.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one blood draw (venipuncture or finger/heel prick) including site prep, puncture, sample collection, and bandaging. A trained phlebotomist completes this in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter needle precision on a compliant, moving human target, real-time vein localization, sterile technique, patient safety awareness, and pain management — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and haptic feedback capabilities.

task: Perform or manage regional anesthetic techniques, such as local, spinal, epidural, caudal, nerve blocks and intravenous blocks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = performing a single regional anesthetic procedure (e.g., epidural or nerve block). Includes patient assessment, site prep, needle placement, drug administration, and confirmation of effect.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter needle precision near critical anatomy, real-time clinical judgment, patient communication, sterile technique, and adaptive response to patient movement/feedback. Far beyond 2026 humanoid robot capabilities; even specialized surgical robots don't perform these autonomously.

task: Deliver equipment to specified hospital locations or to patients' residences.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one delivery trip (hospital floor or residence). Includes loading equipment, navigating to destination, and handoff. Typical trip ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Hospital indoor navigation is partially feasible, but varied equipment sizes, elevators, tight corridors, patient interaction, and especially off-site residential delivery (outdoor terrain, stairs, door access) are well beyond reliable autonomous capability in 2026.

task: Turn valve handles to direct the flow of coolant onto work areas or to coat disks with spinning compounds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a valve handle to direct coolant or apply compound to a work area; a skilled worker completes this in about 1 minute.
robot_success_rationale: Turning valve handles requires moderate dexterity and grip precision. Robots can manipulate knobs/valves in structured settings at ~40–70%, but varied valve types, tight spaces, and confirming correct flow direction reduce reliability.

task: Conduct specialized procedures, such as animal branding or tattooing or hoof trimming.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one animal procedure (e.g., branding, tattooing, or hoof trimming one animal). A skilled livestock handler typically takes 10–20 min per animal.
robot_success_rationale: Requires restraining a live, uncooperative animal, precise force-sensitive tool use on biological tissue, outdoor/unstructured terrain, and specialized tools. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Administer local anesthetic agents.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one administration (e.g., single injection/topical application to a patient). Includes site prep, dosing, and injection; a competent clinician takes ~5–15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise needle placement on a moving/sensitive patient, real-time pain/response feedback, sterile technique, clinical judgment for dosing, and regulatory/safety constraints. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Repair damaged frames.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing one damaged frame (e.g., picture/door/window frame). A competent worker assesses damage, prepares materials, and applies repair techniques in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Frame repair requires fine dexterity (sanding, filling, clamping, finishing), damage assessment judgment, and precise tool use across varied damage types. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured settings.

task: Perform minor surgical procedures, such as removing warts, moles, or cysts, sampling tissues for skin cancer or lipomas, and applying or removing sutures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one minor procedure (e.g., excising a mole/cyst or removing sutures). Includes prep, excision/sampling, and wound closure; typically 15–45 min for a competent clinician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sterile technique, real-time haptic feedback, anatomical judgment, and patient interaction on non-rigid tissue. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capabilities; even surgical robots are task-specific and teleoperated.

task: Measure dimensions of finished workpieces to ensure conformance to specifications, using precision measuring instruments, templates, and fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single finished workpiece with calipers, micrometers, or gauges and checking against spec. A competent machinist/inspector takes ~5–10 minutes per part.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with precise tool handling (calipers, micrometers require <1 mm placement accuracy), repositioning workpieces, and interpreting varied fixture setups. Vision-based measurement is emerging but lacks reliability for tight tolerances in unstructured environments.

task: Assist hospital staff with patient care duties, such as providing transportation or setting up traction.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient care assist event (e.g., transporting a patient to a department or setting up traction). Typical transport or traction setup takes ~15–25 minutes for a competent aide.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safe interaction with fragile, non-cooperative patients; navigating crowded hospital corridors; operating beds/wheelchairs; and precise medical setups like traction. Current humanoids lack the safety certification, dexterity, and clinical judgment for this environment.

task: Disinfect and sterilize equipment, such as respirators, hospital beds, or oxygen or dialysis equipment, using sterilizers, aerators, or washers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one piece of medical equipment. Includes pre-cleaning, loading sterilizer, running cycle, unloading, and verifying results; ~45 min total typical for complex items.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine manipulation of varied medical devices, operating specialized sterilization machinery, interpreting indicators, and ensuring sterility compliance. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous tool-use, machine interfacing, and QA judgment for medical-grade outcomes.

task: Move toolholders manually or by turning handwheels, or engage automatic feeding mechanisms to feed tools to and along workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning/feeding a toolholder for a single machining pass. A skilled machinist takes ~1–5 min to set, adjust, and engage feed per pass.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled handwheel turning, reading analog dials, and engaging mechanical levers on a manual lathe/mill — highly dexterous, tool-specific interaction with poor generalization for current humanoids.

task: Implement, or assist occupational therapists with implementing, treatment plans designed to help clients function independently.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single therapy session with one client implementing a treatment plan; typical OT sessions run 30–60 minutes, median ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced clinical reasoning, real-time adaptive communication, emotional attunement, and dynamic hands-on guidance. Far beyond current robot dexterity, social cognition, and generalization capabilities.

task: Treat minor cuts, abrasions, or contusions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating a single minor wound (clean, apply antiseptic, bandage). A competent first-aider typically completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous manipulation on a non-stationary human body part, tactile sensitivity to avoid causing pain/injury, antiseptic application, and bandage placement — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and safe human-contact norms.

task: Lift metal stock or workpieces manually or using hoists, and position and secure them in machines, using fasteners and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a single workpiece into a machine, securing it with fasteners. A skilled machinist typically takes 5–15 min depending on part size and fixture complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Heavy/awkward metal stock challenges robot strength and stability; hoist operation, precise fixture alignment, and torquing fasteners with hand tools demand dexterity and force control beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured shop environments.

task: Manage newborn care during the first weeks of life.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full caregiving cycle (feeding, burping, diapering, soothing, monitoring) for a newborn; a competent caregiver completes this roughly every 2–3 hours, taking ~60 min per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Newborn care demands extreme dexterity, tactile sensitivity, real-time physiological monitoring, and nuanced soothing. Current humanoids lack the gentle force control, safe handling of fragile infants, and adaptive caregiving judgment required. Safety risk is critical.

task: Provide nonsurgical interventions, including corrective lenses, patches, drops, fusion exercises, or stereograms, to treat conditions such as strabismus, heterophoria, and convergence insufficiency.
human_time_rationale: One clinical session: assessing a patient, selecting/fitting corrective lenses or patches, administering drops, and instructing on fusion exercises or stereograms typically takes ~30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires licensed clinical judgment, fine-tuned patient assessment (visual acuity, binocularity), precise lens fitting, and therapeutic coaching. Far beyond current robot dexterity, perception, and medical-decision generalization.

task: Perform minor assembly, such as fastening parts with nuts, bolts, or screws, using power tools or hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fastening a small set of parts (e.g., 2–4 fasteners) at a workstation. A competent worker takes ~3–7 min including part positioning and tool use.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place parts and operate powered tools in structured settings, but aligning threads, applying correct torque, and handling varied fastener types/orientations demands fine dexterity and tactile feedback that current humanoids handle unreliably.

task: Examine equipment to detect leaks, worn or loose parts, or other indications of disrepair.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single piece of equipment (e.g., a pump, motor, or valve). A competent technician walks around, checks fittings, listens, and visually scans for ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can perform basic visual inspection and navigate around equipment, but detecting subtle leaks (smell, sound, micro-seepage), worn threads, or looseness via touch/torque requires advanced multi-modal sensing and dexterity that current humanoids handle inconsistently.

task: Administer anesthetics during surgery and monitor the effects on animals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one surgical procedure. A typical veterinary surgery requiring anesthesia lasts ~60–120 minutes including induction, monitoring, and recovery phases.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert clinical judgment, continuous physiological interpretation, drug titration, and emergency response. No current humanoid robot has the sensory integration, medical knowledge, or certified capability for autonomous anesthesia administration on live animals.

task: Transport patients to and from the occupational therapy work area.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient transport trip (fetch patient, travel to/from OT work area, safely transfer/settle). Typical round trip in a care facility takes ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating busy hospital corridors, safely operating wheelchairs/gurneys, physically assisting or transferring patients (fragile humans), and handling unpredictable social/medical situations. Far beyond current humanoid capability safely.

task: Add metal to molds to compensate for shrinkage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adding molten/solid metal to a single mold to compensate for shrinkage; a skilled foundry worker does this in a few minutes per mold.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling molten metal at extreme temperatures, precise pouring into molds, heat-resistant tooling, and judgment about fill levels. Current humanoids lack heat tolerance, safe molten-metal handling, and the fine control needed for foundry work.

task: Start equipment and observe gauges and equipment operation to detect malfunctions and to ensure equipment is operating to prescribed standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting a piece of industrial equipment and completing an observation/monitoring cycle until stable operation is confirmed; typically 10–20 minutes for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized controls/panels, reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting multi-sensory cues (sounds, vibrations, smells), and judging conformance to standards — beyond current humanoid generalization and perception capabilities.

task: Position equipment such as ladles, grinding wheels, pouring nozzles, or crucibles, or signal other workers to position equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning a single piece of equipment (ladle, crucible, etc.) or signaling a co-worker to do so; a skilled foundry/metalworking operator does this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Heavy, hot, irregular equipment demands high payload capacity, heat tolerance, precise spatial positioning, and coordinated signaling — well beyond current humanoid dexterity and payload limits. Unstructured foundry environments add further generalization challenges.

task: Maintain laboratory instruments to ensure proper working order and troubleshoot malfunctions when needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one maintenance/troubleshooting session on a single instrument (e.g., calibration check, cleaning, minor repair). Competent lab tech typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert diagnosis, fine dexterous manipulation of delicate components, reading instrument-specific displays/error codes, and applying domain knowledge. Current humanoids lack the specialized perception, tool-use, and troubleshooting cognition needed.

task: Prepare patients for and assist with examinations or treatments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and assisting one patient for a single exam/treatment, including positioning, draping, equipment setup, and monitoring comfort. Typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced human interaction, consent communication, safe physical repositioning of a person, fine dexterity for draping/equipment, adaptive response to patient distress, and clinical judgment — all far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Secure patients into or onto therapy equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = securing one patient onto one piece of therapy equipment (e.g., tilt table, therapy chair, traction device). A trained therapist typically takes 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced force modulation, patient-specific body assessment, communication/consent, and fine dexterous fastening of straps/buckles on a non-cooperative, variable human body. Far beyond current humanoid capability safely.

task: Collect supporting data, such as climatic or field survey data, to corroborate remote sensing data analyses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single field data collection session (one site visit) to gather ground-truth/climatic data. A competent scientist typically spends ~4 hours traveling to site, deploying sensors, and recording measurements.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor navigation over varied/unpredictable terrain, deploying scientific instruments, making contextual judgments, and adapting to unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor mobility, tool-use generalization, and scientific protocol autonomy for field survey work.

task: Clean instruments to prepare them for sterilization.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one tray/batch of instruments; scrubbing, rinsing, and inspecting a standard set takes ~10–20 min for a sterile processing technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to scrub complex geometries (hinges, lumens, serrations), precise force control to avoid damage, and contamination-aware handling. Current humanoids lack the precision, specialized tool use, and sterility-protocol compliance needed.

task: Dig trenches for underground wires or cables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = digging a typical residential/utility trench (~20–50m). A competent worker with a trenching machine or hand tools takes ~1.5–2.5 hours including setup and cleanup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a walk-behind trencher, mini-excavator, or heavy digging tools in outdoor unstructured terrain. Humanoid robots cannot operate such machinery autonomously; outdoor ground variability, tool control, and safety demands far exceed current capabilities.

task: Pour and regulate the flow of molten metal into molds and forms to produce ingots or other castings, using ladles or hand-controlled mechanisms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling one mold/form with molten metal. A competent caster positions the ladle, pours, regulates flow, and clears in ~10–20 min depending on mold size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, high-force ladle control in extreme heat (~1500°C), fine real-time flow regulation, and thermal/safety tolerance far beyond current humanoid hardware. No known commercial deployment exists; dexterity, heat resistance, and force feedback all fall short.

task: Provide emergency first aid to sick or injured animals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single first-aid intervention on one animal (assess, stabilize, bandage/splint/CPR). Typical emergency triage and initial stabilization takes ~15–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rapid clinical assessment, fine dexterous manipulation on irregular living bodies, real-time adaptive judgment, and safe restraint of a distressed animal — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities. No validated deployment exists.

task: Examine patients with problems related to ocular motility, binocular vision, amblyopia, or strabismus.
human_time_rationale: One patient examination covering ocular motility, binocular vision, amblyopia, and strabismus testing; a typical clinical encounter runs 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced clinical judgment, fine ophthalmic instrumentation (cover tests, prism bars, slit-lamp), patient communication, and medical decision-making — far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Implement load or switching orders in hydroelectric plants, in accordance with specifications or instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = executing a single load/switching order at a hydro plant. Operator reviews specs, coordinates with grid dispatch, and sequences switching steps; typically 15–35 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating high-voltage switchgear, reading SCADA/control panels, interpreting grid dispatch instructions, and coordinating with remote operators — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in safety-critical, specialized industrial control environments.

task: Fit patients for orthopedic braces, prostheses, or supportive devices, adjusting fit as needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting a single patient with one device, including assessment, adjustment, and verification. A competent orthotist/prosthetist typically takes 30–60 minutes per fitting session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical assessment, nuanced patient communication, fine dexterous adjustment of straps/components on a moving human body, and judgment-based fit verification. Far beyond current humanoid manipulation and caregiving capability.

task: Arrange treatment supplies to keep them in order.
human_time_rationale: One instance = organizing/arranging a typical set of treatment supplies on a tray or shelf; a competent healthcare worker takes ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires recognizing diverse medical supply types, precise placement per protocol, and handling small/irregular objects. Current robots lack reliable fine dexterity and domain-specific semantic understanding for correct ordering.

task: Administer traction to relieve neck or back pain, using intermittent or static traction equipment.
human_time_rationale: One traction session: patient setup, harness fitting, parameter programming, monitoring, and removal. Typical clinical sessions run 15–30 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise harness fitting on a patient's body, clinical judgment on force/duration parameters, real-time pain monitoring, and safe patient handling. Far beyond current robot dexterity, perception, and clinical reasoning capabilities.

task: Perform enemas, catheterizations, ear flushes, intravenous feedings, or gavages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one procedure (e.g., one catheterization or enema). Competent nurse/tech takes ~15–30 min including prep, execution, and patient positioning.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sterile technique, real-time patient feedback interpretation, and safe force control near delicate anatomy. Far beyond current humanoid capabilities; safety and regulatory barriers are prohibitive.

task: Use chemicals or other substances to examine latent fingerprint evidence and compare developed prints to those of known persons in databases.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single item of evidence: applying chemical treatment to develop latent prints, then conducting a database comparison. A competent examiner typically takes ~45–90 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise chemical application (ninhydrin, cyanoacrylate, etc.), fine dexterous handling of fragile evidence, high-resolution forensic imaging, and expert cognitive comparison against AFIS databases — all far beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Remove solidified steel or slag from pouring nozzles, using long bars or oxygen burners.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing one blocked pouring nozzle. A skilled steelworker typically spends 10–20 minutes using bars or oxygen burners to break up and remove solidified steel or slag.
