robot_success_rationale: Extreme heat, heavy tooling (long bars, oxygen burners), unstructured molten-metal environment, and high force requirements far exceed current humanoid robot dexterity, thermal tolerance, and safe autonomous operation capabilities.

task: Change dressings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = changing the dressing on a single wound site; includes removing old dressing, cleaning wound, and applying new dressing. Competent nurse/aide takes ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity near sensitive tissue, sterile technique, wound assessment, and gentle force control. Far beyond current humanoid capability; risk of harm makes autonomous deployment essentially infeasible.

task: Examine and analyze blood stain patterns at crime scenes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = analyzing bloodstain patterns at a single crime scene. A competent forensic analyst typically spends 1–3 hours documenting, photographing, measuring, and interpreting patterns.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level forensic reasoning, precise pattern classification, contextual judgment, and legally defensible documentation. Current robots lack the cognitive inference, specialized forensic perception, and domain expertise needed. Mobility and imaging alone are insufficient.

task: Check radiation therapy equipment to ensure proper operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full daily QA check of a radiation therapy unit (e.g., linear accelerator). A qualified medical physicist or therapist typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized clinical equipment (linac, dosimetry phantoms, software consoles), interpreting dosimetric readings, and making safety-critical judgments. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities; no commercial deployment exists.

task: Prepare surgical equipment and pass instruments or materials to veterinarians during surgical procedures.
human_time_rationale: One surgical procedure: ~15 min prep/setup plus ~30 min actively scrubbing in and passing instruments during a typical veterinary surgery.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile technique, precise fine dexterous instrument handling, real-time coordination with a surgeon, contextual anticipation, and immediate error correction. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities in a sterile surgical environment.

task: Clean and maintain kennels, animal holding areas, examination or operating rooms, or animal loading or unloading facilities to control the spread of disease.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one kennel/holding area unit (scrubbing, disinfecting, rinsing, drying). A competent kennel worker handles one space in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating confined kennel spaces, handling latches/gates, applying disinfectants precisely, scrubbing varied surfaces, and avoiding animals. Current robots lack reliable dexterity, situational judgment, and bio-hazard protocol adherence for this unstructured, variable environment.

task: Install holding fixtures, cams, gears, and stops to control stock and tool movement, using hand tools, power tools, and measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and installing a complete set of fixtures, cams, gears, and stops on one machine. A skilled machinist/setup tech typically takes 30–60 min per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity with multiple tool types, spatial reasoning about mechanical relationships, torque-controlled fastening, and real-time measurement verification—far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured industrial setups.

task: Accompany injured athletes to hospitals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = accompanying one injured athlete from injury site to hospital admission. Includes on-site assessment, transport, check-in, and handoff to medical staff (~90 min typical).
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating in/driving a vehicle, dynamic social interaction, navigating hospital environments, and making real-time medical judgments. Humanoids cannot drive; unstructured caregiving and situational communication far exceed current robot capabilities.

task: Purge wastes from equipment by connecting equipment to water sources and flushing water through systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = flushing one piece of equipment. Connecting hoses, running flush cycle, and disconnecting typically takes 15–25 minutes for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct water source, manipulating threaded/quick-connect fittings under variable configurations, managing hoses, and monitoring flow — complex dexterous tool-use with high variability; well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Disassemble and clean anesthesia equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling and cleaning a full anesthesia machine/circuit. A trained technician typically takes 30–60 minutes including component removal, washing, drying, and reassembly-readiness check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to disconnect small medical fittings, valves, and tubing; deep domain knowledge of component identity; sterile handling protocols; and verification steps. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and precision.

task: Initiate emergency interventions to stabilize patients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = initiating emergency interventions (e.g., CPR, airway management, IV access, defibrillation) for one patient until stabilized or handed off; median ~15 min for a trained responder.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (IV insertion, intubation), dynamic patient-specific clinical judgment, force-sensitive contact with fragile human bodies, and real-time adaptive decision-making far beyond current robot capabilities.

task: Measure salinity, acidity, light, oxygen content, and other physical conditions of water to determine their relationship to aquatic life.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one site visit with multi-parameter water quality sampling (deploying sensors/probes, collecting readings for salinity, pH, DO, light, etc.) at a single location. A competent field technician typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to aquatic sites (boats, uneven shorelines, docks), deploying specialized probes into water, handling wet/slippery equipment, and interpreting multi-sensor data. Current humanoids lack waterproofing, aquatic mobility, and the specialized tool-use needed for reliable autonomous field deployment.

task: Operate tracing attachments to duplicate contours from templates or models.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tracing one template/model contour onto a workpiece. A skilled operator sets up and completes a single tracing pass in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise stylus-to-template contact, coordinated tool guidance, and fine force control along complex contours. Current humanoids lack the dexterity and contact-force precision for reliable tracing attachment operation.

task: Request anesthesia equipment repairs, adjustments, or safety tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = submitting a single repair/adjustment/safety-test request for one piece of anesthesia equipment; filling out a form, describing the issue, and routing to biomedical engineering takes ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires navigating hospital IT/paper systems, clinical judgment to identify equipment faults, and communicating with biomedical staff. Current robots lack the autonomy, software integration, and contextual reasoning for this workflow.

task: Fill medication prescriptions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling a single prescription (counting/verifying pills, labeling, and packaging). A trained pharmacy technician typically takes 5–10 minutes per prescription.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pill counting, reading fine labels, verifying drug identity, and safe packaging — all with near-zero error tolerance. Current robots lack reliable fine dexterity, pharmaceutical perception, and regulatory-grade accuracy for autonomous dispensing.

task: Monitor animals recovering from surgery and notify veterinarians of any unusual changes or symptoms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring check/round on a post-surgical animal patient, including observation, vitals assessment, and notification if needed. A competent vet tech completes this in ~30 minutes per patient.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced clinical perception (respiration quality, pain behavior, wound assessment), judgment to distinguish normal vs. abnormal recovery, and reliable escalation decisions. Current robots lack veterinary-grade sensing, domain knowledge, and situational judgment for autonomous clinical monitoring.

task: Read temperature gauges and observe color changes, adjusting furnace flames, torches, or electrical heating units as necessary to melt metal to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one melt cycle check: reading gauges, observing color, and adjusting heat source to hit spec. Typically takes 10–20 minutes per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires pyrometer/gauge reading, interpreting subtle color cues in intense light, manipulating furnace/torch controls precisely, and closing the feedback loop — beyond current humanoid generalization and fine control in harsh thermal environments.

task: Move controls to set cutting speeds and depths and feed rates, and to position tools in relation to workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting controls for a single setup/cut operation on a machine tool. A skilled machinist takes ~3–7 minutes to set speeds, depths, feed rates, and tool position.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise manipulation of varied machine-tool controls (dials, levers, digital panels), reading gauges, and interpreting workpiece geometry. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, domain-specific tool-use, and precision perception needed for reliable autonomous operation.

task: Check for damaged library materials, such as books or audio-visual equipment, and provide replacements or make repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one item and either repairing it (e.g., re-taping a book spine) or flagging/pulling it for replacement. A competent library technician averages ~15 min per item end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual/tactile damage detection across diverse media formats, fine dexterous repair (taping, rebinding), navigating cluttered shelves, and judgment on replacement decisions — well beyond current robot capability.

task: Conduct quality control tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single batch/unit on a production line. A competent QC technician typically spends ~15 min per unit covering visual checks, measurements, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is strong, but QC often requires precise instrument handling, interpreting ambiguous results, and multi-step tool use. Generalization across varied products and test protocols limits autonomous success.

task: Turn valves to circulate water through cores, or spray water on filled molds to cool and solidify metal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = turning valves/adjusting spray for a single cooling cycle on one mold or core set; a competent foundry worker completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Turning industrial valves requires moderate dexterity and force; robots can grasp round handles but struggle with varied valve types, tight torque requirements, precise positioning in cluttered foundry environments, and confirming water flow feedback.

task: Transport patients to and from treatment areas, using wheelchairs or providing standing support.
human_time_rationale: One instance = transporting one patient one way (e.g., room to imaging). Includes positioning patient, transit through corridors, and handoff; typically 5–15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safely maneuvering a wheelchair or physically supporting a standing human through dynamic hospital corridors, elevators, and doorways. High safety stakes, unpredictable patient behavior, complex navigation, and physical steadying demand exceed current humanoid capabilities.

task: Set up and operate machines, such as lathes, drill presses, punch presses, or bandsaws, to fabricate prototypes or models.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and operating a machine to fabricate one prototype part. A competent machinist typically spends 30–150 min depending on complexity; 90 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large, specialized industrial machines (lathes, drill presses, bandsaws) with precise tooling, parameter setting, and adaptive judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, machine interfacing, and safety awareness needed for autonomous machining.

task: Explain test procedures and safety precautions to patients and provide them with assistance during test procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = explaining a procedure and assisting one patient through one test. Typical clinical interaction (e.g., ECG, blood draw, spirometry) takes ~5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires natural language communication, adaptive empathy, clinical judgment, and dexterous patient-contact assistance. Current robots lack reliable verbal explanation quality, medical situational awareness, and safe unstructured caregiving ability.

task: Maintain dental equipment and sharpen and sterilize dental instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full batch cycle: cleaning, sharpening hand instruments, and running a sterilization cycle (autoclave). Competent dental assistant completes this in ~30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous sharpening of small curved instruments, precise tactile feedback, operating specialized sterilization equipment, and quality inspection — all well beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capability.

task: Conduct tests, such as electrocardiograms (EKGs), stress testing, or lung capacity tests, to evaluate patients' cardiopulmonary functions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one cardiopulmonary test session (e.g., EKG ~10 min, stress test ~30–60 min). Median across test types estimated at ~45 min including prep, monitoring, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to place electrodes precisely, clinical judgment to monitor patient safety, real-time adaptive responses to patient distress, and regulatory/ethical constraints. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Maintain and troubleshoot problems with library equipment, including computers, photocopiers, and audio-visual equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and resolving a single equipment issue (e.g., a jammed copier, frozen PC, or AV glitch). A competent library tech typically takes ~20–30 min per incident.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fault diagnosis, UI interaction, cable manipulation, and contextual judgment across diverse device types. Current humanoids lack the software integration, fine dexterity for small connectors, and troubleshooting reasoning to handle unstructured, mixed-device repair autonomously.

task: Examine animals to detect behavioral changes or clinical symptoms that could indicate illness or injury.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining a single animal for health/behavioral signs. A trained handler spends ~10–20 min on a thorough individual assessment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced behavioral interpretation, subtle symptom detection (gait, eye condition, coat, vocalization), safe animal handling, and adaptive interaction with unpredictable living subjects — far beyond current robot perception and caregiving capabilities.

task: Clean and organize work area and disinfect equipment after treatment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tidying and disinfecting one treatment area after a single session; a competent worker typically spends 10–20 minutes wiping surfaces, restocking, and organizing tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a cluttered clinical space, identifying and wiping irregular equipment surfaces with disinfectant, and restoring item placement — all demanding perception, dexterity, and contextual judgment that 2026 humanoids handle poorly in unstructured clinical environments.

task: Exercise animals or provide them with companionship.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one exercise/companionship session (e.g., walking a dog or socializing with a shelter animal). Typical session lasts ~30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic interaction with unpredictable animals, leash handling, reading animal behavior/stress signals, and providing genuine social engagement. Current robots lack animal-behavior perception, adaptive handling, and safe reactive locomotion around animals.

task: Apply fluorides or other cavity preventing agents to arrest dental decay.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying fluoride varnish or gel to one patient's teeth. Includes preparation, application to all tooth surfaces, and post-care instructions; typically 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine intraoral dexterity with soft tissue avoidance, patient compliance management, and clinical judgment. No general-purpose humanoid can safely operate inside a human mouth with dental tools at acceptable quality.

task: Prepare animals for surgery, performing such tasks as shaving surgical areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one animal for surgery (restraint, shaving/clipping the surgical site, skin cleaning). A competent vet tech typically completes this in 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires restraining a live, moving, unpredictable animal, precise fine-motor clipping near skin on irregular body contours, and adapting to varied species/sizes. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, compliance, and animal-handling capability for safe autonomous execution.

task: Provide direct care by performing comprehensive health assessments, developing differential diagnoses, conducting specialized tests, or prescribing medications or treatments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient encounter including history, physical exam, differential diagnosis, and treatment plan. A competent clinician typically spends 30–60 minutes per comprehensive visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced clinical reasoning, nuanced patient communication, physical examination (auscultation, palpation), and legal authority to prescribe — far beyond current robot perception, dexterity, cognition, and regulatory clearance.

task: Drill, countersink, and ream holes in parts and assemblies for bolts, screws, and other fasteners, using power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling, countersinking, and reaming a single hole or set of holes in one part/assembly. A skilled worker takes ~5–10 min including setup, tool changes, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool alignment, high-force pressing, sequential tool changes (drill → countersink → reamer), and fine positional accuracy on varied parts. Current humanoids lack the rigidity, force control, and tool-change dexterity needed reliably.

task: Transport metal ingots to storage areas, using forklifts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one forklift trip moving a pallet of metal ingots from pickup point to storage location, including loading, transporting, and placing. Typical cycle ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate sit-down forklifts in real industrial environments. Specialized autonomous forklifts exist but are not general-purpose humanoid robots. Heavy, dynamic load handling and forklift driving are well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Cut, bend, or shape metal for applications in hydroelectric plants, using equipment such as hydraulic benders or pipe threaders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting/bending/shaping one metal piece or section. A competent pipefitter/metalworker takes ~30–60 min per piece including measuring, setup, and operation of hydraulic benders or threaders.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy specialized industrial equipment (hydraulic benders, pipe threaders), precise measurement, and strong force application in a semi-structured industrial setting. Current humanoids lack the tool-use, strength, and precision for this workflow.

task: Provide patients with direct family planning services, such as inserting intrauterine devices, dispensing oral contraceptives, and fitting cervical barriers, including cervical caps or diaphragms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient visit for a family planning service (e.g., IUD insertion). Includes prep, counseling, procedure, and post-check. Median ~30 min for a trained clinician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for intracavitary procedures, sterile technique, real-time anatomical judgment, patient communication, and clinical decision-making. Far beyond any current general-purpose robot's capabilities.

task: Perform hygiene-related duties, such as clipping animals' claws or cleaning and polishing teeth.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one animal grooming session (e.g., clipping claws or cleaning teeth for a single pet). A competent groomer typically takes 15–25 minutes per animal for these hygiene tasks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity on a live, moving animal — restraint, force sensitivity, and precise tool manipulation near sensitive tissue. Current robots lack safe animal-handling capability, compliant force control, and generalization across animal sizes/species.

task: Prepare rooms, sterile instruments, equipment, or supplies and ensure that stock of supplies is maintained.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one room/procedure setup, including arranging sterile instruments, checking equipment, and restocking supplies. A competent surgical tech or nurse typically takes 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile technique, precise instrument identification and arrangement, equipment checks, and inventory management. Current humanoids lack sterile handling protocols, fine dexterity for delicate instruments, and domain-specific procedural knowledge needed for clinical environments.

task: Compound and dispense medications as prescribed by doctors and dentists, by calculating, weighing, measuring, and mixing ingredients, or oversee these activities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = compounding and dispensing a single prescription; includes calculating, weighing, mixing, and labeling one preparation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-dexterous manipulation of syringes, balances, and vials; exact volumetric measurement; complex math/clinical judgment; sterile technique; and regulatory compliance — all far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capabilities.

task: Take residents on community outings.
human_time_rationale: One community outing (e.g., trip to a park or store) for a group of residents, including preparation, travel, activity, and return — typically 2–4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving or operating a transport vehicle, navigating public outdoor environments, supervising and assisting multiple residents with diverse needs, and handling emergencies — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Order, perform, or interpret the results of diagnostic tests, such as complete blood counts (CBCs), electrocardiograms (EKGs), and radiographs (x-rays).
human_time_rationale: One instance = ordering, performing/overseeing, and interpreting a single diagnostic test. A competent clinician typically spends ~20 min total across ordering, review, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires licensed clinical judgment, patient interaction, operating specialized medical equipment (ECG leads, X-ray machines), and interpreting nuanced medical data. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capability; regulatory and safety barriers are prohibitive.

task: Feel and visually examine gums for sores and signs of disease.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining one patient's gums. A dental professional typically spends 2–4 minutes on a focused gum inspection per patient.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine intraoral tactile sensing, precise dexterous manipulation inside a small mouth, close-range tissue inspection, and clinical judgment. No current general-purpose humanoid has the sensors, dexterity, or validated medical perception for this task.

task: Change linens, such as bed sheets and pillow cases.
human_time_rationale: One instance = changing sheets and pillowcases on one bed. A competent hotel housekeeper or worker takes ~5–10 minutes per bed.
robot_success_rationale: Requires bimanual dexterous manipulation of large, floppy, deformable fabric; precise tucking, stretching fitted sheets over corners, and aligning pillowcases. Deformable textile handling remains a major unsolved challenge for current humanoids.

task: Perform primary care procedures such as suturing, splinting, administering immunizations, taking cultures, and debriding wounds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one procedure (e.g., one suture, one splint, one immunization). Averaged across procedure types, a competent clinician takes ~15–25 min per procedure including prep and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (suturing, debriding), sterile technique, real-time patient response, clinical judgment, and safe force control on human tissue — all far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capabilities.

task: Turn valves and direct flow of coolants or cutting oil over cutting areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting valve(s) and directing coolant/cutting oil flow for a single cutting operation setup; a skilled machinist does this in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Turning industrial valves and precisely aiming coolant nozzles requires moderate dexterity and situational perception. Current humanoids struggle with varied valve types, force feedback, and precise nozzle aiming in cluttered machine shop environments.

task: Enter coordinates of hole locations into program memories by depressing pedals or buttons of programmers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = entering coordinates for a single hole location into a programmer. A competent operator depresses pedals/buttons in a quick, practiced sequence taking ~2–4 minutes per hole.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise button/pedal actuation on specific industrial programmer interfaces, fine dexterity for small controls, and understanding of machine state feedback. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-finger control and interface generalization for arbitrary industrial programmers.

task: Crank machines through cycles, stopping to adjust tool positions and machine controls to ensure specified timing, clearances, and tolerances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one setup/adjustment cycle on a machine: cranking through, measuring clearances/timing, and tweaking controls until spec is met. Typically 15–30 min for a skilled technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating machine cranks/controls, using precision measurement tools (feeler gauges, timing lights), interpreting readings, and making fine adjustments to tolerances — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and closed-loop precision manipulation capabilities.

task: Prepare examination or treatment rooms by stocking them with appropriate supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stocking one exam/treatment room. A competent medical aide checks inventory, retrieves supplies, and restocks all dispensers and drawers in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a clinical environment, identifying and retrieving many small heterogeneous medical items, opening cabinets/drawers, and placing items precisely in specific locations — well beyond reliable autonomous capability in 2026. High failure risk on fine dexterity, item recognition, and task sequencing.

task: Coordinate work with physicians or other healthcare team members, including providing assistance during invasive procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = coordinating and assisting during a single invasive procedure (e.g., central line placement, intubation). Includes prep, active assistance, and post-procedure handoff — typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced verbal/nonverbal coordination with clinicians, anticipating physician needs, sterile field awareness, fine dexterous instrument handling, and adaptive social judgment — all far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Verify conformance of patterns or template dimensions to specifications, using measuring instruments such as calipers, scales, and micrometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = verifying a single pattern/template against spec. A competent worker selects instruments, takes multiple measurements, and records results — typically 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with selecting and manipulating precision instruments (calipers, micrometers) on irregular patterns, reading analog gauges, and adapting measurement strategy to varied geometries. Perception and fine dexterity are key bottlenecks.

task: Maintain, calibrate, or repair plant instrumentation, control, and electronic devices in geothermal plants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = maintaining, calibrating, or repairing a single instrument/device. A competent technician typically spends 60–120 min per device including diagnostics, calibration, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of electronic components, specialized calibration tools, technical diagnostics, and judgment in a complex industrial environment. Current humanoids lack the precision, tool-use capability, and domain-specific reasoning needed for autonomous instrument calibration/repair.

task: Measure clients' bridge and eye size, temple length, vertex distance, pupillary distance, and optical centers of eyes, using measuring devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring all optical parameters for one client. A skilled optician takes ~5–10 minutes to measure bridge, eye size, temple length, vertex distance, PD, and optical centers using a pupillometer and ruler.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter precision measurements on a moving human face, use of specialized optical instruments (pupillometer, PD ruler), fine dexterity for instrument positioning, and client interaction/compliance. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Administer local, inhalation, intravenous, or other anesthetics.
human_time_rationale: One instance = administering anesthesia for a single procedure, including patient assessment, induction, maintenance, and handoff. Typical case takes 30–60 minutes total involvement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced medical judgment, real-time physiological monitoring interpretation, sterile technique, fine dexterity (IV/airway placement), adaptive decision-making under life-critical conditions, and licensed clinical expertise — all far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Select and load materials such as workpieces, objects, and machine parts onto equipment used in coiling processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting and loading one workpiece or batch onto coiling equipment; a skilled operator identifies, positions, and secures parts in roughly 5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct part from varied stock, lifting/aligning it precisely onto industrial coiling machinery, and securing it. Industrial environment, heavy/awkward parts, precise alignment, and machine-specific interfaces challenge current humanoid dexterity and generalization.

task: Rework or alter component model or parts as required to ensure that products meet standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reworking a single component/part to meet standards; includes inspection, identifying defects, and performing corrective modifications. ~45 min median for a skilled technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing non-conformances, selecting appropriate rework methods (filing, trimming, reshaping), and executing precise corrective modifications. This demands adaptive reasoning, fine dexterity, and tool-use generalization well beyond current robot capabilities.

task: Examine parts for defects, such as breakage or excessive wear.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single part visually and tactilely for defects. A competent worker takes ~2–4 minutes per part including handling and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is maturing but generalization to arbitrary parts, lighting conditions, and defect types (cracks, wear, burrs) remains inconsistent. Tactile inspection and handling of varied parts adds further dexterity challenges.

task: Observe and care for patients throughout examinations to ensure their safety and comfort.
human_time_rationale: One instance = observing and caring for a single patient during one examination (e.g., MRI, X-ray, physical exam). Typical exam duration is 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social perception, interpreting subtle distress cues, verbal reassurance, adaptive caregiving, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable affective perception, safe patient-contact dexterity, and medical decision-making needed for autonomous patient care.

task: Establish zero reference points on workpieces, such as at the intersections of two edges or over hole locations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting a single zero reference point on one workpiece. A skilled machinist uses an edge finder or probe, aligns axes, and zeros the DRO/controller in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool manipulation (edge finder/probe), fine tactile/visual alignment to micron-level tolerances, and CNC controller interfacing. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, precision sensing, and machine-tool integration needed for reliable autonomous execution.

task: Clean materials, such as metals, according to recycling requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single batch/load of scrap metal (e.g., removing grease, coatings, or contaminants). A competent worker typically takes ~5–10 min per batch using brushes, solvents, or water.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying contaminants, selecting appropriate cleaning method, and manipulating irregular metal pieces. Current robots lack reliable multi-step process reasoning, tool-switching dexterity, and compliance with varied recycling specs across unstructured material types.

task: Lay out and mark reference points and dimensions on materials, using measuring instruments and drawing or scribing tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking out a single workpiece (e.g., a metal plate or board). A competent tradesperson measures, scribes lines, and marks reference points in ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor control of scribers/markers, accurate reading of measuring tapes/calipers, and consistent force on varied materials. Current humanoids lack the dexterous precision and tool-handling reliability needed for acceptable layout quality.

task: Exchange paper currency for playing chips or coin money.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one currency exchange transaction. A cashier/dealer counts bills, determines chip/coin equivalent, and hands over the exchange in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous handling of thin paper bills and small coins/chips, accurate counting, social interaction, and transaction verification. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-finger currency manipulation and cash-register-level cognition at acceptable error rates.

task: Check sterile supplies to ensure that they are not outdated.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking all sterile supplies in a standard storage area/cart. A competent worker visually scans expiration dates on packages, taking ~15 minutes per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading small expiration dates on varied packaging orientations, navigating storage areas, and making pass/fail judgments. Current robots struggle with fine text recognition on reflective/small labels, cluttered shelves, and varied package orientations.

task: Perform pre- and post-installation pressure, flow, and related tests of vertical and horizontal geothermal loop piping.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full pre- and post-installation test cycle on a single geothermal loop (connecting gauges, pressurizing, recording flow/pressure readings, documenting results). Typically 60–120 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting pressure/flow gauges to buried piping fittings (fine dexterous tool-use), interpreting analog/digital gauges, operating pumps, navigating outdoor/jobsite terrain, and making safety-critical judgments. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured field conditions.

task: Verify that finished lenses are ground to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = verifying one finished lens. A skilled optical technician uses lensometers/gauges to check curvature, power, and thickness in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous handling of delicate precision optics, operating specialized metrology instruments (lensometers, gauges), interpreting subtle optical measurements, and making pass/fail judgments — well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Assemble pattern sections, using hand tools, bolts, screws, rivets, glue, or welding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one complete pattern section set. A competent worker joining parts with mixed fasteners/adhesives/welding typically takes 30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires switching between diverse tools (wrenches, welding equipment, glue), fine fastener alignment, and variable part geometries. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-switching, welding, and precision multi-fastener coordination in unstructured settings.

task: Groom, trim, or clip animals' coats.
human_time_rationale: One grooming session for a typical dog or cat — brushing, trimming, and clipping coat — takes a competent groomer roughly 30–60 minutes depending on breed and size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity with sharp tools on a moving, unpredictable live animal. Current robots lack compliant force control, animal behavior prediction, and safe tool-use near skin needed for acceptable grooming quality.

task: Perform mobilizations and high-velocity adjustments to joints or soft tissues, using principles of massage, stretching, or resistance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient mobilization/adjustment session (e.g., chiropractic or PT visit). A competent clinician typically spends 20–40 minutes per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force calibration, nuanced tactile feedback, and clinical judgment to safely apply high-velocity thrusts or stretches to human anatomy. Current robots lack the fine dexterity, compliant force control, and real-time sensorimotor adaptation needed; risk of patient injury is severe.

task: Select, prepare, or use equipment, monitors, supplies, or drugs for the administration of anesthetics.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and checking all anesthesia equipment, monitors, supplies, and drugs for a single patient case; a competent anesthesiologist or CRNA typically takes 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to draw up and label drugs, configure complex monitors, and make safety-critical clinical judgments. No current humanoid robot has the dexterity, medical domain knowledge, regulatory certification, or reliability required for autonomous anesthesia preparation.

task: Clean and maintain lobby and common areas, such as restocking supplies and watering plants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance round of a typical lobby and common area: sweeping/mopping, wiping surfaces, restocking supplies, and watering plants.
robot_success_rationale: Basic sweeping/mopping is feasible (~50%), but restocking supplies requires recognizing varied items and precise placement, and watering plants demands fine manipulation and judgment. Multi-step, multi-tool generalization across an unstructured lobby remains very challenging.

task: Visit and observe patients on hospital rounds or house calls, updating charts, ordering therapy, and reporting back to physician.
human_time_rationale: One instance = visiting and assessing one patient: reviewing chart, brief exam, ordering therapy, and reporting back. Typically 15–25 minutes per patient on rounds.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, patient interaction, interpreting symptoms, updating EHR, and communicating findings to physicians. Far beyond current robot cognitive, communicative, and medical reasoning capabilities. Mobility alone is insufficient.

task: Administer medication, immunizations, or blood plasma to animals as prescribed by veterinarians.
human_time_rationale: One instance = administering a single prescribed treatment (injection, oral med, or plasma) to one animal, including restraint, preparation, and documentation. Typically 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safe animal restraint, precise needle/syringe dexterity, dose verification, and adaptive response to animal movement/resistance. Current humanoids lack veterinary fine-motor skill, safe animal handling protocols, and reliable bio-task generalization.

task: Measure patient's range-of-joint motion, body parts, or vital signs to determine effects of treatments or for patient evaluations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient evaluation session measuring ROM, body parts, and/or vital signs; a competent clinician typically completes this in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise instrument handling (goniometers, BP cuffs, stethoscopes), safe patient contact, clinical judgment, and adaptive positioning on a human body — well beyond 2026 general-purpose robot dexterity and caregiving capability.

task: Mark identification numbers or symbols onto patterns or templates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking an ID number/symbol on a single pattern or template using a marker, stamp, or scribe. A competent worker completes this in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor writing or stamping on varied surfaces/orientations. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity for legible marking; tool grasping and controlled pressure application remain weak points.

task: Fabricate and fit orthodontic appliances and materials for patients, such as retainers, wires, or bands.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating and fitting one orthodontic appliance (e.g., retainer or band) for one patient. Includes lab work, adjustment, and chairside fitting — typically 30–60 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (sub-millimeter wire bending, intraoral placement), patient interaction and safety, sterile clinical judgment, and specialized dental tools. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured clinical settings.

task: Hold or restrain animals during veterinary procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restraining one animal for a single procedure (e.g., injection, exam). Typically lasts 5–15 minutes depending on animal size and cooperation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly adaptive force control, real-time response to unpredictable animal movement, and safe yet firm grip on varied body shapes/sizes. Current robots lack the dexterity, sensitivity, and animal-behavior perception needed; risk of injury is extreme.

task: Catalogue and sort books and other print and non-print materials according to procedure and return them to shelves, files, or other designated storage areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing and shelving a batch of ~20 returned library items: cataloguing, sorting by classification, and returning to correct shelf locations. ~45 min for a competent library worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading spine labels/barcodes, understanding classification systems (Dewey/LC), navigating complex stacks, precise shelf placement, and handling varied media formats (DVDs, books, binders). Perception, sequencing, and generalization far exceed current robot capabilities.

task: Receive and store incoming supplies, verify quantities against invoices, check for outdated medications in current inventory, and inform supervisors of stock needs and shortages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single incoming shipment: unboxing, counting items, verifying against invoice, scanning inventory, flagging expiries, and reporting shortages to supervisors.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading labels/expiry dates on diverse packaging, fine dexterous unpacking, cross-referencing documents, navigating storage areas, and communicating shortages — too many unstructured, multi-step cognitive-physical subtasks for current robots.

task: Care for children with disabilities or who have sick parents or parents with disabilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single caregiving shift (~4 hours) covering physical, emotional, and supervisory needs of a child with disabilities or in a vulnerable family situation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced emotional attunement, adaptive communication, safe physical assistance with fragile individuals, and crisis judgment. Far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and social cognition capabilities.

task: Stencil identifying information on ingots and pigs, using special hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stenciling one ingot/pig. A competent worker positions the stencil, applies marking compound or paint, and stamps ID info in roughly 2–4 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of stencil on irregular, heavy metal surfaces and controlled force application with hand tools. Perception of irregular casting surfaces, fine dexterous grip on stencil/brush, and consistent ink pressure are challenging for current humanoids.

task: Check for side effects, such as skin irritation, nausea, or hair loss to assess patients' reaction to treatment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing one patient for treatment side effects, including interview, visual inspection, and documentation. A competent nurse/clinician typically takes 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced patient communication, subjective symptom elicitation, clinical judgment, and subtle visual/tactile inspection (e.g., skin texture, hair loss pattern). Current robots lack reliable clinical perception, empathetic interaction, and medical reasoning for autonomous assessment.

task: Collect recyclable materials from curbside for delivery to designated facilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full curbside recycling route shift (~8 hours), collecting bins from dozens of homes along a defined route and driving to a facility.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a large collection truck on public roads, outdoor terrain navigation, lifting/tipping varied bins, and facility drop-off. Humanoid robots cannot operate heavy vehicles in traffic; outdoor unstructured conditions compound failure risk severely.

task: Clean and finish patterns or templates, using emery cloths, files, scrapers, and power grinders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing a single pattern/template. A skilled patternmaker typically spends 30–60 min per piece using multiple abrasive tools to achieve required surface quality and dimensional accuracy.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (files, scrapers, emery cloth), variable force feedback, and micron-level surface judgment across complex geometry. Current humanoids lack the precision, compliance control, and tactile sensing for acceptable surface finishing quality.

task: Paint or lacquer patterns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying a patterned paint/lacquer finish to one surface or object (e.g., a cabinet panel or decorative piece); includes prep, masking, application, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Patterned painting requires precise tool handling, consistent stroke control, masking, and aesthetic judgment. Current humanoids lack the fine motor dexterity, spray/brush control, and visual feedback loops needed for pattern-quality results.

task: Load specified amounts of metal and flux into furnaces or clay crucibles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading one furnace/crucible charge. Weighing, scooping/lifting specified amounts of metal and flux, and loading into furnace or crucible takes ~15 min for a skilled foundry worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling heavy, irregularly shaped metal stock and bulk flux; operating near extreme heat; precise weight measurement; using specialized scoops/tongs. Generalist humanoids lack the strength, thermal tolerance, and task-specific tool-use for reliable autonomous execution in a foundry environment.

task: Deliver artwork on courier trips.
human_time_rationale: One courier trip: pickup artwork, transport to destination, obtain confirmation. Typical urban/metro delivery estimated at ~60 minutes round-trip including loading/unloading.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a vehicle in traffic — humanoid robots cannot operate passenger vehicles on public roads. Even if loading/unloading is feasible, autonomous road transport disqualifies general-purpose humanoids entirely.

task: Set up and monitor medical equipment and devices such as cardiac monitors, mechanical ventilators and alarms, oxygen delivery devices, transducers, or pressure lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and initiating monitoring on one patient with a standard suite of devices (e.g., cardiac monitor, ventilator, O₂, pressure lines). A competent ICU/ER nurse typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (arterial lines, transducers, ventilator circuits), clinical judgment, sterile technique, patient interaction, and integration of many specialized tools. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Design and create templates, patterns, or coreboxes according to work orders, sample parts, or mockups.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and fabricating a single template, pattern, or corebox to spec. A skilled patternmaker typically takes 2–6 hours depending on complexity; 4 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting engineering work orders/drawings, planning 3D geometry, precision woodworking/machining, and iterative quality checking. Far beyond current robot generalization, tool-use, and cognitive planning capabilities.

task: Repair, rewire, and troubleshoot electrical systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single repair/rewire/troubleshoot job (e.g., replacing a circuit, tracing a fault). A competent electrician typically takes 60–120 min per job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (stripping wires, terminal connections), diagnostic reasoning with multimeters/test equipment, reading schematics, navigating confined spaces (panels, conduit), and safe high-voltage handling — all well beyond current humanoid robots.

task: Examine molds to ensure they are clean, smooth, and properly coated.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one mold for cleanliness, smoothness, and proper coating. A skilled worker does a thorough visual and tactile check in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine tactile sensing and high-resolution visual inspection to detect surface defects and coating uniformity. Current robots lack reliable surface-quality perception and tactile feedback needed for nuanced mold inspection; lighting and geometry variability add difficulty.

task: Restock intravenous (IV) supplies and add measured drugs or nutrients to IV solutions under sterile conditions to prepare IV packs for various uses, such as chemotherapy medication.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one IV pack (e.g., a chemotherapy bag): measuring, mixing, and labeling under sterile conditions typically takes 15–25 minutes for a trained pharmacy technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-precise sterile technique, syringe/needle handling, aseptic compounding in a laminar-flow hood, and drug measurement accuracy. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, sterility assurance, and tool-use capabilities.

task: Repair and rework templates and patterns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/reworking one template or pattern. A competent worker inspects damage, adjusts/patches/recuts, and validates fit in ~25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle thin, flexible materials; precise cutting, patching, and alignment; judgment to diagnose and correct defects. Far beyond current humanoid manipulation reliability for unstructured repair tasks.

task: Assist veterinarians in examining animals to determine the nature of illnesses or injuries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one animal examination alongside a vet, covering history intake, physical palpation, vitals, and preliminary findings. Typical vet tech assistance runs 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires unpredictable live animal handling, fine tactile palpation, nuanced behavioral reading, and clinical judgment. Current robots lack the dexterity, gentle force control, and perception needed for safe, effective veterinary assistance.

task: Remove metal ingots or cores from molds, using hand tools, cranes, and chain hoists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing ingots/cores from one mold. Includes rigging, hoisting, and hand-tool knockout; typically 10–20 min per mold.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating overhead cranes, chain hoists, and heavy hand tools in a hot, cluttered foundry environment. Humanoids cannot operate crane controls or rigging safely; heavy payload and unstructured setup far exceed current capabilities.

task: Provide assistance with euthanasia of animals or disposal of corpses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = euthanasia of a single animal by a trained veterinary professional, including preparation, administration, and confirmation of death.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise injection technique, clinical judgment, emotional sensitivity, legal authorization, and species-specific expertise. Far beyond current robot dexterity, perception, and ethical/legal autonomy limits.

task: Perform vision screening of children in schools or community health centers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = screening one child. A trained screener takes ~5–15 min per child: greeting, positioning, administering eye chart/cover test, recording results, advising follow-up.
robot_success_rationale: Requires gentle child interaction, precise instrument positioning on an uncooperative/anxious child, nuanced behavioral observation, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, social rapport, and certified medical capability needed.

task: Record and store suitable images, using camera unit connected to the ultrasound equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = capturing and storing images during a single ultrasound exam. A competent sonographer/tech takes ~5 min to frame, capture, label, and save key diagnostic images.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized medical ultrasound equipment, interpreting diagnostic image quality, precise transducer positioning on a patient, and navigating proprietary software interfaces — far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and fine dexterity capabilities.

task: Administer medications intravenously, by injection, orally, through gastric tubes, or by other methods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = administering a single medication dose to one patient via one route. Includes preparation, verification, administration, and documentation; ~10 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile IV/injection technique, precise fine-motor dexterity, clinical judgment, patient interaction, and regulatory compliance. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, sterile handling, and medical safety certification for any of these routes.

task: Provide towels and sheets to clients in public baths, steam rooms, and restrooms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = handing/delivering towels and sheets to one client. A competent attendant retrieves folded linens and presents them to the client in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Navigating wet, slippery, crowded spa environments is hazardous for robots. Picking and handing folded soft linens is partially feasible, but social interaction, safe navigation in humid spaces, and client-specific service reduce success significantly.

task: Clean and smooth molds, cores, and core boxes, and repair surface imperfections.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/smoothing one mold or core box and repairing its surface defects. A competent foundry worker typically takes 20–30 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine tactile dexterity to detect and repair surface imperfections, use of specialized scrapers/grinders in tight mold cavities, and quality judgment. Current humanoids lack the precision, force control, and surface-inspection capability needed.

task: Set up and perform live shots for broadcast.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up equipment (camera, tripod, IFB, lighting, comms) at a live shot location and executing the broadcast segment; ~45 min total for a typical ENG live shot.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized broadcast equipment, coordinating with a control room via verbal cues, framing shots with editorial judgment, cable management, and real-time adaptation. Far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and communication capabilities.

task: Diagnose and repair vehicle heating and cooling systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and repairing a single HVAC fault on one vehicle. A competent mechanic typically spends 60–120 min including diagnosis, part swap, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting OBD/HVAC diagnostic tools, interpreting fault codes, accessing tight engine-bay components, handling refrigerants, and operating lifts — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and domain reasoning capabilities.

task: Deal cards to house hands, and compare these with players' hands to determine winners, as in black jack.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one round of blackjack for a typical table (2–6 players). A dealer deals, checks hole card, compares hands, and settles bets in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise card handling (thin, slippery objects), rapid dealing motions, reading card faces reliably, applying game logic, and interacting with players — all in a dynamic social setting. Fine dexterity and perception for cards remain very difficult for 2026 humanoids.

task: Provide primary health care, including pregnancy and childbirth, to women.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single primary care visit or prenatal appointment. A competent clinician typically spends 30–90 minutes including examination, consultation, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires licensed clinical judgment, fine tactile examination, ultrasound operation, medication prescription, and emotional rapport. Far beyond any 2026 general-purpose robot's physical, cognitive, and legal capabilities.

task: Open and close cash floats and game tables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = opening or closing a single cash float/game table, including counting chips/cash, logging records, and securing the till. Typically 10–20 minutes per table.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous handling of chips and cash, accurate counting, compliance with strict casino procedures, documentation, and judgment calls. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capability in unstructured, regulated environments.

task: Massage body parts to relieve soreness, strains, or bruises.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single massage session targeting one or a few body parts (e.g., a sore shoulder or leg). Typical targeted therapeutic massage lasts 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced force control, soft-tissue perception, real-time pain-feedback adaptation, and precise dexterous touch on a human body. Current humanoids lack the tactile sensitivity, compliant manipulation, and safety validation for therapeutic human contact.

task: Store personal possessions for patrons, issue claim checks for articles stored, and return articles on receipt of checks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = storing one patron's items, issuing a claim check, and returning items. A competent coat-check or luggage attendant completes this in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling highly varied bags/coats, writing/issuing paper checks, social interaction with patrons, and reliably matching checks to items. Dexterity, generalization, and social fluency are key bottlenecks for current humanoids.

task: Collect laboratory specimens, such as blood, urine, or feces, for testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a single specimen (e.g., venipuncture blood draw or urine sample) from one patient, including labeling and packaging. Typically 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Venipuncture demands sub-millimeter precision on a moving, compliant target under skin; urine/feces collection requires patient interaction and sterile technique. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force control, and clinical safety compliance required.

task: Instruct, motivate, safeguard, or assist patients practicing exercises or functional activities, under direction of medical staff.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient exercise/therapy session. Typical PT/OT-aide-led session runs 20–45 min; 30 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time patient safety monitoring, dynamic physical assistance, verbal motivation, adaptive judgment under medical direction, and nuanced human interaction — far beyond current humanoid capabilities in unstructured caregiving contexts.

task: Maintain a lost-and-found collection.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single found item: logging it, tagging it, and placing it in the collection area. ~15 min including record-keeping.
robot_success_rationale: Requires recognizing varied, irregular objects, labeling/tagging with fine dexterity, organizing in bins, and interacting with record systems. Generalization across unpredictable lost items and admin tasks is well beyond current robot capability.

task: Cut spouts, runner holes, and sprue holes into molds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting spouts, runner holes, and sprue holes into a single mold. A skilled mold maker typically spends ~30 min per mold using hand tools or small power tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, forceful cutting into rigid mold material with specialized tools (chisels, drills, routers). High positional accuracy, fine dexterity, tool-change, and material feedback needed — well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Assist ophthalmologists in diagnostic ophthalmic procedures, such as ultrasonography, fundus photography, and tonometry.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting with one patient's diagnostic session (setup, patient positioning, equipment operation, and documentation across one or more procedures like tonometry, fundus photography, or ultrasonography).
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-fine dexterity near a patient's eye, nuanced patient communication/reassurance, sterile contact procedures (tonometry probe), precise instrument alignment, and clinical judgment — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Skim slag or remove excess metal from ingots or equipment, using hand tools, strainers, rakes, or burners, collecting scrap for recycling.
human_time_rationale: One instance = skimming/removing slag or excess metal from a single ingot or equipment batch; a competent foundry worker completes this in ~10–20 minutes including collecting scrap.
robot_success_rationale: Extreme heat, molten metal, and heavy tools pose severe safety/perception challenges. Identifying and skimming viscous slag requires force-sensitive dexterity in a hazardous, unstructured environment far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Clean and maintain tanks, filter beds, and other work areas, using hand tools and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one tank or filter bed per session; a competent worker using hand and power tools typically spends 60–120 minutes on a thorough clean.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating wet/industrial outdoor environments, selecting and operating varied power tools, scrubbing curved/confined tank interiors, and inspecting filter beds — well beyond current humanoid generalization and tool-use capability in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Provide prenatal, intrapartum, postpartum, or newborn care to patients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient care encounter (e.g., a prenatal check-up visit or postpartum assessment), typically lasting ~45–75 minutes for a competent midwife or OB provider.
robot_success_rationale: Requires advanced clinical judgment, nuanced patient communication, fine tactile examination (e.g., Leopold's maneuvers, cervical checks), sterile procedural skill, and legal licensure. Far beyond current robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Replace worn tools, and sharpen dull cutting tools and dies, using bench grinders or cutter-grinding machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing or sharpening one tool/die. A skilled worker inspects, mounts it on a bench grinder or cutter grinder, sharpens to spec, and reinstalls — roughly 15–25 minutes median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous manipulation of grinding machines, fine tactile/visual feedback to achieve correct cutting geometry, tool mounting/alignment to tight tolerances, and tool change decisions — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and perception capabilities.

task: Set up various apparatus or athletic equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a typical set of athletic equipment (e.g., nets, goals, mats, or gymnastics apparatus) for a session; ~15–25 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Equipment is large, heavy, varied in shape, and often requires high-force manipulation, precise alignment, locking mechanisms, and spatial navigation around a gym. Current humanoids lack reliable strength, tool-use, and generalization for this diversity.

task: Move and position workpieces, such as mold sections, patterns, and bottom boards, using cranes, or signal others to move workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving/positioning a single workpiece (e.g., mold section) using a crane. A skilled foundry/pattern worker typically takes ~5–10 minutes per piece including rigging, signaling, and placement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating overhead cranes or signaling crane operators, handling heavy irregular industrial workpieces, and precise positioning. Humanoids cannot operate crane controls or interpret rigging/signal protocols reliably in unstructured foundry environments.

task: Conduct specified laboratory tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one batch of specified tests on a single sample. A competent lab technician typically takes ~30 min including setup, running the test, and recording results.
robot_success_rationale: Lab tests demand fine dexterity (pipetting, handling fragile glassware, operating specialized instruments), precise calibration, chemical handling, and interpretation of results. Current humanoids lack the precision and domain-specific tool-use required.

task: Attend to needs of athletic teams in clubhouses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attending to a team's needs during one game/session (pre-game prep, during-game support, post-game cleanup), typically ~60 minutes of active work.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires social awareness, anticipating athlete needs, fetching varied items, navigating crowded dynamic spaces, and responding to ad-hoc verbal requests. Current robots lack the generalization, social intelligence, and reliability for unstructured multi-person service roles.

task: Assemble and embed cores in casting frames, using hand tools and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and embedding one set of cores into one casting frame. A competent foundry worker typically takes 15–25 minutes depending on complexity and part size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of heavy, irregular foundry cores into metal frames using hand tools in a cluttered, industrial environment. Current humanoids lack the force control, tool dexterity, and domain-specific manipulation needed for reliable success.

task: Observe nurses and visit patients to ensure proper nursing care.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single observation round across a typical ward (e.g., 8–12 patients), including brief bedside checks and nurse monitoring; a charge nurse typically completes this in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced clinical judgment, social/communication skills with patients and nurses, interpreting subtle patient cues, and navigating a busy clinical environment. Far beyond current robot generalization and caregiving capabilities.

task: Devise and construct tools, dies, molds, jigs, and fixtures, or modify existing tools and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = devising and constructing or modifying one tool/die/mold/jig/or fixture. A skilled toolmaker typically spends 2–6 hours per unit depending on complexity; 4 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level planning, precision machining, fine dexterity with lathes/mills/grinders, material judgment, and iterative design iteration. Current humanoids lack the tool-use repertoire, precision, and autonomous planning for machinist-grade fabrication.

task: Tend machines that bond cope and drag together to form completed shell molds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = bonding one cope-and-drag pair into a completed shell mold. A competent operator aligns, applies adhesive/clamps, and closes the mold in ~5 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of fragile ceramic/sand shell halves, controlled force application, and adhesive handling. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity and tactile feedback needed to avoid cracking brittle mold halves reliably in unstructured setups.

task: Set up and operate machines, and adjust controls to regulate operations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a machine and adjusting controls for a single production run. A competent operator typically takes 15–25 minutes for setup, calibration, and initial adjustment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading gauges/displays, interpreting machine-specific controls, fine-tuning knobs/levers, and adapting to diverse machinery types. Current humanoids lack robust generalization across unfamiliar control panels, fine dial manipulation, and contextual judgment to verify correct operation.

task: Complete physical checks and monitor patients to detect unusual or harmful behavior and report observations to professional staff.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient check round (vitals, visual assessment, behavior observation, and logging). A competent care aide typically completes this per patient in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced behavioral interpretation, social/emotional perception, clinical judgment, and verbal reporting to staff. Current robots lack reliable anomaly detection in human behavior and cannot communicate findings at professional clinical standards.

task: Dust, spray, or bathe animals to control insect pests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating one animal with dust, spray, or bath for pest control; includes prep and application, roughly 15–25 minutes for a competent handler.
robot_success_rationale: Requires restraining an uncooperative, unpredictable live animal, applying chemicals precisely across its body, and adapting to movement. Current humanoids lack animal-handling dexterity, safe force control, and reliable animal behavior prediction.

task: Operate ultrasound equipment to produce and record images of the motion, shape, and composition of blood, organs, tissues, or bodily masses, such as fluid accumulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single diagnostic ultrasound exam. A competent sonographer typically spends 20–45 minutes scanning, adjusting settings, and capturing images for a standard abdominal or obstetric study.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert transducer placement, real-time clinical interpretation, precise hand pressure/angle adjustments on a patient's body, and adaptive decision-making. Current robots lack medical tactile perception, clinical reasoning, and certified patient-interaction capability.

task: Conduct an initial assessment of an athlete's injury or illness to provide emergency or continued care and to determine whether they should be referred to physicians for definitive diagnosis and treatment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single athlete assessment episode, from initial contact through triage decision. A competent athletic trainer typically spends 10–30 minutes on history, physical exam, and referral determination.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced clinical judgment, pain/neuro assessment, palpation sensitivity, communication, and medical decision-making. Current robots lack diagnostic reasoning, reliable tactile sensing for injury assessment, and safe autonomous medical triage capability.

task: Incise stomach and abdominal walls and probe internal organs, using trocar, to withdraw blood and waste matter from organs.
human_time_rationale: One embalming/autopsy aspiration instance on a single body; a skilled mortician/embalmer typically takes 20–40 minutes to trocar and aspirate all major cavities.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, precise anatomical knowledge, controlled force with a trocar, and safe handling of biohazardous fluids. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured, tactile-critical surgical/mortuary procedures.

task: Stencil identifying information on equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stenciling one piece of equipment. Includes positioning stencil, applying paint/ink, removing stencil, and letting it set. ~5–10 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise stencil alignment, controlled paint application pressure, and handling thin/flexible stencil material on varied surfaces. Fine dexterity and force control are still weak in 2026 humanoids; misalignment or smearing likely.

task: Operate controls that regulate temperatures or room environments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a thermostat or HVAC control panel to a target setting. A competent worker locates the control, reads current state, and makes the adjustment in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to a panel and press buttons on well-structured interfaces, but varied control panel designs (dials, touchscreens, toggles), reading display values, and confirming correct settings are challenging for generalist humanoids in unstructured environments.

task: Mount workpieces in fixtures, on arbors, or between centers of machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting a single workpiece in a fixture/arbor/between centers. A skilled machinist typically takes 3–8 minutes depending on setup complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment, force-controlled clamping, and tool use (wrenches, chucks). Varied fixtures and tight tolerances challenge current robot dexterity and perception; part misalignment risk is high.

task: Lay out and draw or scribe patterns onto material, using compasses, protractors, rulers, scribes, or other instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out and scribing one pattern onto a workpiece. A competent tradesperson typically spends 15–25 minutes measuring, marking, and scribing a moderately complex pattern.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter precision tool manipulation (scribes, compasses, protractors), fine-motor control, and accurate force feedback on varied materials. 2026 humanoids lack the dexterity and precision sensing needed; high failure rate expected.

task: Set up and operate computer-controlled machines or robots to perform one or more machine functions on metal or plastic workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a CNC machine for a job and running a batch. Setup (tooling, fixtures, program load) ~20–30 min; monitoring/adjusting a run ~15 min. Median ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool changes, fixture setup, program selection via machine interfaces, and adaptive judgment on machine faults. Humanoids lack fine dexterity for tooling, specialized machine UI interaction, and domain-specific setup knowledge.

task: Provide clinical services or health education to improve and maintain the oral health of patients or the general public.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient visit combining clinical oral care (e.g., cleaning, exam) and/or health education counseling; typical dental hygiene appointment is 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity inside a patient's mouth, safe force control near sensitive tissue, real-time clinical judgment, patient communication, and licensure-level decision-making — far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Care for patients by changing bed linens, washing and ironing laundry, cleaning, or assisting with their personal care.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full care round for one patient: changing linens, laundry handling, light cleaning, and basic personal care assistance. A competent home health aide typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Combines multiple hard subtasks: linen changes require dexterous fabric manipulation, personal care demands safe human contact and situational judgment, and navigating a patient's room adds unstructured mobility. Current robots lack the dexterity, safety assurance, and generalization needed.

task: Construct platforms, fixtures, and jigs for holding and placing patterns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing a single platform, fixture, or jig setup. A competent worker measures, cuts, assembles, and secures components — typically 30–60 minutes per unit.
