human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/maintaining a single metal form or piece of equipment; a competent worker typically spends ~45 min inspecting, hammering, straightening, and reconditioning one unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires forceful, precise strikes with sledges and bars, diagnosing damage, and adapting technique to irregular metal forms. Current humanoids lack the high-force dexterous tool-use, torque control, and damage-assessment judgment needed.

task: Install alarm and electronic access systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a complete alarm/access system in a single building (sensors, control panel, wiring, keypads). A competent technician typically takes 3–5 hours; 4 hrs is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires routing and stripping wire, drilling into walls, mounting hardware in varied locations, reading wiring diagrams, and programming control panels — highly unstructured, fine-dexterous, multi-step work far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Measure distance from reference points and mark guidelines to lay out work, using plumb bobs and levels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out a single work area or section (e.g., one wall or floor zone). A skilled tradesperson measures, plumbs, levels, and marks guidelines in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool handling (plumb bob, level, chalk line), spatial reasoning, accurate marking, and interpreting reference points in unstructured construction environments. Current robots lack the fine dexterity, tool-use generalization, and measurement precision needed.

task: Open and close library during specified hours and secure library equipment, such as computers and audio-visual equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one open or close session: unlocking/locking doors, powering on/off computers and AV equipment, securing items. Typically 20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires opening varied doors, operating key locks or access panels, navigating a large multi-room building, powering down/securing diverse electronics, and making judgment calls — well beyond 2026 humanoid generalization and reliability.

task: Stamp heat-treatment identification marks on parts, using hammers and punches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stamping ID marks on a single part. A skilled worker positions a punch and strikes with a hammer in ~1–3 minutes per part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise punch alignment, controlled hammer strike force, and stable part fixturing. Current humanoids lack the fine positional accuracy and calibrated impact force needed for consistent, legible stamping; grasp and swing dynamics are poorly controlled.

task: Turn knobs, screws, and dials to adjust settings of machines, according to garment styles and equipment performance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting knobs/screws/dials for a single machine setup or style change; a skilled operator typically takes 2–5 minutes per adjustment cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Turning knobs/dials requires fine dexterity and precise torque control; current humanoids struggle with small, varied fasteners and reading/interpreting dial feedback. No reliable autonomous closed-loop adjustment capability yet.

task: Perform food preparation tasks, such as making sandwiches, carving meats, making soups or salads, baking breads or desserts, and brewing coffee or tea.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one food prep task (e.g., making a sandwich ~5 min, a soup ~30 min). Median across the listed sub-tasks is ~20 minutes for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Food prep requires diverse dexterity (carving, kneading, pouring), tool use, sequencing, and judgment in unstructured kitchen environments. Current humanoids lack reliable fine motor control, recipe-following, and safe knife/appliance handling at acceptable quality.

task: Maintain fitness equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = maintaining a single piece of fitness equipment (e.g., treadmill or cable machine): inspecting, lubricating, tightening bolts, cleaning. ~45 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse fine manipulation (applying lubricant, tightening bolts/screws, replacing worn parts), fault diagnosis, and use of tools like wrenches. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use, diagnostics, and dexterous maintenance skill generalization.

task: Prepare for basic food service, such as setting up continental breakfast or coffee and tea supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a single continental breakfast or beverage station. A competent worker lays out items, fills dispensers, and arranges supplies in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to storage, retrieving varied items (urns, boxes, trays, perishables), arranging them neatly, and filling dispensers. Multi-step sequencing, diverse object types, and precise placement in semi-structured environments challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Vacuum dining area and sweep and mop kitchen floor.
human_time_rationale: One instance = vacuuming a typical dining area. A competent worker moves chairs, vacuums under table and around perimeter, done in ~10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can push a vacuum but struggle with maneuvering around chair/table legs, lifting chairs, and covering edges. Robotic vacuums (Roombas) help but a general humanoid handling a full upright vacuum is inconsistent.

task: Maintain clean and orderly checkout areas, and complete other general cleaning duties, such as mopping floors and emptying trash cans.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full round of checkout area cleaning: wiping surfaces, mopping floors, and emptying trash cans. A competent worker completes this in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Mopping flat floors and emptying standard bins are partially within 2026 robot capability, but navigating a busy checkout area, manipulating trash liners, and cleaning varied surfaces with judgment is complex and unreliable for autonomous humanoids.

task: Supply currency, coins, chips, or gaming checks to other departments as needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one supply run delivering currency/coins/chips to a requesting department; involves retrieving, counting, packaging, transporting, and handing off items — ~15 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling small, high-value, varied items (coins, chips, bills), secure counting/verification, navigating multi-department facilities, and accountable handoff. Dexterity with currency/chips is poor; accountability/security checks are unsolvable for current robots.

task: Fabricate and dismantle parts, equipment, and machines, using a cutting torch or other cutting equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting/dismantling a single part or component. A competent welder/fabricator typically spends 30–60 min per piece including setup, cutting, and cleanup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating an oxy-acetylene or plasma torch safely with precision on varied metal geometries. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, high-heat tool handling, and situational safety awareness needed. Highly specialized, hazardous task far beyond general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Cut excess material or thread from finished products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming excess thread/material from a single finished product. A skilled worker uses scissors or snips to trim all loose threads in roughly 1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise detection of fine threads against varied fabric backgrounds, dexterous pinching/cutting with scissors or snips, and avoiding cutting the product itself. Current robots lack the fine perception and dexterity for reliable thread trimming.

task: Inspect event facilities to ensure that they conform to customer requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one event facility against a customer brief. A coordinator walks through, checks layout, AV, seating, lighting, exits, and documents findings. Typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex multi-room venues, reading and interpreting customer requirement documents, cross-referencing abstract specs (ambiance, capacity feel, brand fit), and communicating findings — well beyond current robot generalization and semantic reasoning capabilities.

task: Identify articles' fabrics and original dyes by sight and touch, or by testing samples with fire or chemical reagents.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one article. Skilled textile worker uses sight, touch, burn test, and/or chemical reagent; typically 5–10 min per article.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine tactile discrimination, controlled burn/chemical testing, and expert multi-modal interpretation. Current robots lack sufficient haptic sensing, fine dexterity for reagent tests, and trained fabric-identification models to meet professional quality standards.

task: Measure and monitor raw biomass feedstock, including wood, waste, or refuse materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and monitoring a single feedstock batch/load (e.g., a truck delivery or conveyor intake). Competent worker inspects, weighs, samples, and logs data in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal sensing (moisture meters, scales, visual grading), operating industrial weighing equipment, sampling heterogeneous loose materials, and logging to systems. Current humanoids lack reliable tool use, sensor integration, and unstructured material handling at this fidelity.

task: Conduct therapeutic, recreational, or athletic activities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one structured session (therapeutic, recreational, or athletic) with a participant or group. Typical sessions run 30–60 minutes; 45 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic social interaction, adaptive coaching, emotional attunement, physical demonstration, and real-time participant feedback. Current humanoids lack the social intelligence, generalization, and safety assurance needed for autonomous therapeutic/athletic facilitation.

task: Record casino exchange transactions, using cash registers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single casino chip/cash exchange transaction at a register: counting currency/chips, entering amounts, issuing receipt. Typically 2–4 minutes per transaction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous handling of cash/chips, accurate counting, operating a specialized cash register UI, and financial record-keeping judgment. Current humanoids lack the precision, specialized tool integration, and cognitive accuracy needed for reliable monetary transactions.

task: Supervise and participate in kitchen and dining area cleaning activities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full post-meal cleanup of a kitchen and dining area (wiping surfaces, sweeping/mopping, clearing dishes, sanitizing). A competent worker with helpers takes ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Task blends supervision (requires judgment and communication) with varied physical subtasks (mopping, wiping, stacking dishes) across a large, cluttered space. Robots can handle simple surface cleaning but struggle with multi-zone coordination, oversight role, and unstructured debris/layouts.

task: Assist in preparing food and serving meals and refreshments to children.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and serving a single meal/snack session for a group of children (e.g., a classroom or daycare group). Includes prep, portioning, serving, and basic cleanup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step food prep (cutting, portioning), navigating a kitchen and dining area, safely serving children (unpredictable, mobile), and adapting to varied menus. Far beyond current general-purpose robot reliability and safety standards for child interaction.

task: Clean working surface to remove scale, dust, soot, or chips of brick and mortar, using broom, wire brush, or scraper.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one working surface (e.g., a brick wall section or slab). A competent mason takes ~5–15 min using a broom, wire brush, or scraper.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep flat surfaces moderately well, but wire-brushing or scraping rough, uneven masonry surfaces requires adaptive force control, tool switching, and perception of scale/soot on irregular textures — beyond reliable 2026 capability.

task: Assist with placing caskets in hearses and organize cemetery processions.
human_time_rationale: One instance covers loading one casket into a hearse and staging the procession; a trained funeral attendant typically completes this in 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Caskets are extremely heavy (~200–400 lbs), require coordinated multi-person lifting, precise hearse alignment, and social/logistical coordination of a vehicle procession — far beyond current humanoid payload, dexterity, and task-planning limits.

task: Activate emergency action plans and administer first aid, as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = recognizing an emergency, activating the plan (alerting responders), and administering initial first aid until help arrives. Typically 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires situational judgment, communication (calling 911, alerting staff), nuanced casualty assessment, and delicate/adaptive first aid (CPR, wound care). Current humanoids lack reliable emergency reasoning, verbal coordination, and safe human-contact dexterity at clinical quality.

task: Remove items from washers or dry-cleaning machines, or direct other workers to do so.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unloading one washer or dry-cleaning machine. A competent laundry worker empties a standard machine in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can grasp and extract textile items but tangled, wet, or heavy garments inside a drum are challenging. Perception of crumpled fabrics and reaching deep into a drum limit reliability. Dry-cleaning machines add complexity.

task: Start washers, dry cleaners, driers, or extractors, and turn valves or levers to regulate machine processes and the volume of soap, detergent, water, bleach, starch, and other additives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading and starting one machine cycle, including selecting settings and adjusting valves/levers for additives. A skilled laundry worker completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires turning valves/levers with precise force, reading industrial machine controls, and regulating multiple additive volumes. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity for varied valve types and contextual chemical dosing judgment.

task: Inspect soiled articles to determine sources of stains, to locate color imperfections, and to identify items requiring special treatment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single garment or article. A skilled laundry/dry-cleaning worker visually examines one item for stains, color flaws, and special treatment needs in roughly 1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual perception to classify stain types, subtle color imperfections, and fabric conditions. Current robots lack reliable fine-grained textile inspection, stain-source reasoning, and expert-level material identification needed for acceptable quality.

task: Remove excess mortar with trowels and hand tools, and finish mortar joints with jointing tools, for a sealed, uniform appearance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing joints on a single typical brick course/section (~10–15 bricks). A skilled mason takes ~15–25 minutes to scrape, tool, and inspect joints to a uniform finish.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled scraping along variable mortar joints, fine dexterity with trowels and jointing tools, and tactile quality judgment. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation and compliance control needed for consistent, supervisor-acceptable results.

task: Handle passenger emergencies or disruptions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = responding to a single passenger emergency or disruption from onset to resolution, including assessment, de-escalation, and coordination. Median ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires social intelligence, verbal de-escalation, situational judgment, and emergency protocol execution. Frontier humanoids lack natural language fluency, nuanced social perception, authority, and safe physical intervention capability needed here.

task: Test structures, controls, or mechanical, hydraulic, or electrical systems, according to test plans or in coordination with engineers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a structured test session on a single system (e.g., a hydraulic circuit or control panel). Includes setup, execution of test plan steps, and logging results; ~90 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting test plans, operating specialized instruments and valves, coordinating with engineers, and making judgment calls on anomalies. Far beyond current general-purpose robot generalization, dexterity, and tool-use capabilities.

task: Operate washing machines and dryers to clean soiled apparel and towels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a washer or dryer, selecting settings, and starting the cycle. Active human effort is ~10–20 min; machine run time excluded.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with sorting/loading unstructured laundry, manipulating dials/touchscreens, and pouring detergent precisely. Pick-and-place of clothing is improving but unreliable; control panel interaction adds further failure risk.

task: Mix specified amounts of sand, clay, dirt, or mortar powder with water to form refractory mixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one batch of refractory mixture. A competent worker measures, combines, and mixes dry materials with water to desired consistency, typically taking 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires measuring powdery/granular materials, pouring liquids, and mixing to a specific consistency—challenging for humanoids due to imprecise material handling, liquid control, and tactile/visual assessment of mixture quality. No reliable standardized setup.

task: Requisition and store shipping materials and supplies to maintain inventory of stock.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking stock, placing an order, and physically stowing a received shipment of shipping materials in a stockroom.
robot_success_rationale: Requires inventory tracking via software/ERP (cognitive + UI interaction), judgment on reorder quantities, and organized stowing of varied box sizes and irregular supplies. Current humanoids lack reliable system integration and high-level procurement reasoning.

task: Mount attachments, such as needles, cutting blades, or pattern plates, and adjust machine guides according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting one attachment (e.g., needle, blade, or pattern plate) and adjusting guides on a machine. A skilled operator typically completes this in ~5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle small sharp attachments (needles, blades), precise torque-controlled fastening, and reading/interpreting spec-based guide adjustments. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation precision and tool-use reliability for this multi-step setup task.

task: Drive motor vehicles to transport visitors to establishments and tour site locations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one transport trip for visitors (pickup to drop-off). Typical guided tour/shuttle leg is ~20–40 minutes; 30 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate standard motor vehicles in traffic. L4 AVs exist but are narrow/mapped-route systems, not general-purpose humanoids. No current frontier humanoid can perform this task autonomously.

task: Position items under needles, using marks on machines, clamps, templates, or cloth as guides.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning and positioning a single fabric piece or workpiece under a needle using guides/marks. A skilled operator does this in ~1–2 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor alignment of flexible/deformable materials (fabric) under a needle using visual marks or templates. Deformable material handling, sub-millimeter precision, and generalization across varied guides challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Test parts for hardness, using hardness testing equipment, or by examining and feeling samples.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing hardness of a single part. A competent worker sets up the tester, applies the indenter/load, reads the result, and records it in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of indenter on a specific test point, applying calibrated force, and reading analog/digital gauges. Current humanoids lack the fine force control, precision positioning, and tool-use reliability needed. Tactile "feel" assessment is even less feasible.

task: Issue initial and replacement safe-deposit keys to customers, and admit customers to vaults.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one customer interaction: verify ID, retrieve/issue key(s), log the transaction, and escort/admit customer to vault. Typical bank workflow ~5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identity verification (social/policy judgment), secure key management from a controlled inventory, operating vault access controls, and nuanced customer interaction — well beyond current humanoid generalization and trust/security constraints.

task: Start and control games and gaming equipment, and announce winning numbers or colors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = running a single game round (e.g. roulette spin or bingo draw), including setup, operation, and announcing the result. Typical casino/gaming context ~10–20 min per round cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating varied gaming equipment (wheels, cages, buttons), clear verbal announcement, crowd interaction, and real-time judgment. Robots lack reliable speech-based hosting, fine equipment manipulation, and social situational awareness for gaming environments.

task: Teach skills, such as proper climbing methods, and demonstrate and advise on the use of equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single instructional session teaching climbing skills and equipment use to a group or individual; a typical lesson runs ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic physical demonstration of climbing techniques, adaptive verbal instruction, safety assessment of learners, and nuanced equipment coaching — far beyond current robot generalization, communication, and dexterous demonstration capabilities.

task: Select and prepare specimens and media for cell cultures, using aseptic technique and knowledge of medium components and cell requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one batch of cell culture (specimens + media). Includes aseptic setup, media preparation, and specimen handling; typically 30–60 min for a trained lab technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires strict aseptic technique, fine pipette manipulation, sterile field maintenance, dynamic decision-making on media composition, and biosafety compliance — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, perception, and generalization in unstructured lab settings.

task: Fasten or fuse brick or other building material to structure with wire clamps, anchor holes, torch, or cement.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fastening/fusing one section or course of bricks/material to a structure. A competent mason takes ~30–60 min depending on method and material.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy, precise manipulation of masonry materials, use of specialized tools (torch, wire clamps, cement), outdoor/unstructured environments, and force-controlled placement. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Take up or let down hems to shorten or lengthen garment parts, such as sleeves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = hemming one garment part (e.g., one sleeve or trouser hem). A skilled tailor measures, pins, and hand- or machine-stitches a hem in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-fine dexterity: handling limp fabric, precise measuring, pinning, and consistent stitching. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity; even specialized sewing robots struggle with unstructured garments.

task: Receive, store, or deliver luggage or mail.
human_time_rationale: One instance = receiving, storing, or delivering a single piece of luggage or mail; a competent worker handles this in ~5 minutes including transport to storage/recipient.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can grasp and carry common objects, but varied luggage shapes/weights, navigating elevators/hallways, handing off to recipients, and reading labels reliably remain significant challenges for current humanoids.

task: Install new hardware or software systems or components, ensuring integration with existing network systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single hardware component (e.g., a server, NIC, or switch) or a software system onto a host and verifying network integration. A competent IT tech typically takes 1–3 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous hardware handling (racking, cabling), complex cognitive software configuration, network diagnostics, and contextual decision-making. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities in unstructured IT environments.

task: Use cameras in any of several different camera mounts, such as stationary, track-mounted, or crane-mounted.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting, mounting, and configuring a camera on a chosen mount type (stationary, track, or crane). A skilled camera operator takes ~15 min to set up and adjust.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced tool-use (mounting hardware, adjusting heads), operating complex rigs (cranes, tracks), and judgment-driven framing decisions. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, specialized tool familiarity, and situational creativity needed.

task: Install or repair network cables, including fiber optic cables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing or repairing a single cable run (routing, terminating, testing). A competent technician typically takes 45–90 minutes depending on length and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (fiber cleaving, crimping, splicing sub-mm glass strands), navigating confined spaces (walls, ceilings, conduits), using specialized tools, and diagnostic judgment. Far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Install, adjust, and operate electronic equipment to record, edit, and transmit radio and television programs, motion pictures, video conferencing, or multimedia presentations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setup, adjustment, and operation of AV/broadcast equipment for a single session. A competent technician typically takes ~60 min end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous cable routing, operating complex specialized control panels/software, situational judgment, and coordination with talent/crew. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and tool-use capabilities.

task: Operate extractors and driers, or direct their operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one extraction/drying cycle: loading, configuring controls, monitoring, and unloading. Typical operator active time is 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial machinery with varied control interfaces, loading bulk/varied materials, and monitoring process parameters. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use for industrial controls, bulk material handling, and process judgment.

task: Cut new or duplicate keys, using impressions or code key machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting a single key. A competent locksmith selects the blank, aligns it in the machine, and cuts/finishes it in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation to seat a key blank precisely, operate a specialized key-cutting machine, and verify cut accuracy. Current humanoids lack the precision tooling interface, fine grip control, and machine-specific operation needed.

task: Carry flowers to hearses or limousines for transportation to places of interment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = carrying a floral arrangement from a funeral home/chapel to a waiting hearse or limousine. A competent worker walks a short distance and loads the flowers, typically taking ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating outdoor/semi-structured environments, grasping large delicate floral arrangements without damage, and loading into a vehicle. Perception and gentle manipulation of irregular, fragile objects remain challenging; outdoor terrain and vehicle interaction add further difficulty.

task: Cut materials according to specifications, using blades, scissors, or electric knives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting one piece/batch of material to spec. Worker measures, marks, and cuts in roughly 5–10 minutes depending on material and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, marking, tool selection, and controlled cutting force on varied materials. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity with bladed tools, consistent straight-line cutting accuracy, and safe tool-use generalization across scissors, knives, and blades.

task: Apply bleaching powders to spots and spray them with steam to remove stains from fabrics that do not respond to other cleaning solvents.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating one stained spot on a fabric item. A competent worker applies bleaching powder, waits briefly, then steam-sprays; roughly 5–10 minutes per spot.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise localization of stains, controlled powder application to a small spot, and steam tool use on delicate fabric — demanding fine dexterity, force control, and perception. Current humanoids lack reliable precision for this nuanced multi-step textile task.

task: Operate machines to spread, smooth, level, or steel-reinforce stone, concrete, or asphalt on road beds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one typical road paving shift segment (e.g., laying a lane section ~100–200m). A competent operator takes ~3–4 hours including setup, operation, and finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized heavy machinery (pavers, graders, rollers) in dynamic outdoor environments. Humanoid robots cannot drive or control such vehicles. Task demands complex judgment, precision, and safety awareness far beyond current robot capability.

task: Conduct preventative maintenance and repair, and lubricate machines and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a standard preventative maintenance cycle on a single machine (inspection, minor repair, lubrication). A competent technician typically takes ~1 hour per machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing worn parts, applying lubricants to precise fittings, using varied tools (wrenches, grease guns), and making judgment calls on repairs — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, perception, and generalization limits.

task: Teach individual and team sports to participants through instruction and demonstration, using knowledge of sports techniques and of participants' physical capabilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one coaching/instruction session (individual or group). A typical sports lesson or practice session runs ~60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires adaptive social interaction, real-time reading of participants' physical capabilities, nuanced verbal/physical demonstration, motivational coaching, and safety judgment — far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and social-cognitive abilities.

task: Inspect garments, and examine repair tags and markings on garments to locate defects or damage, and mark errors as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one garment inspected, tags read, defects located, and errors marked. A skilled worker typically spends 3–5 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual perception of subtle fabric defects, reading handwritten/small tags, and precise marking — all at a high quality bar. Current robots lack reliable garment inspection perception, OCR of irregular tags, and dexterous marking tools on deformable cloth.

task: Start machine, engage clutch, and push and move levers to guide machine along forms or guidelines and to control the operation of machine attachments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pass/operation along a form or guideline (e.g., a paving or grading run). A competent operator typically completes a single guided pass in ~30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires seated operation of heavy machinery with clutches, levers, and pedals in dynamic outdoor terrain. Humanoid robots cannot drive or operate such equipment autonomously; no current general-purpose robot has this capability.

task: Clean and sanitize work areas, equipment, utensils, dishes, or silverware.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and sanitizing a typical work area or batch of utensils/dishes in a food-service or kitchen setting; ~15–25 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Wiping flat surfaces is feasible (~50%), but handling varied dishware, silverware, and sanitizing chemicals, navigating sinks, scrubbing, rinsing, and verifying cleanliness across unstructured item types drops success substantially. Dexterity and task sequencing are limiting factors.

task: Let out or take in seams in suits and other garments to improve fit.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting one seam on one garment. A skilled tailor takes ~15–35 min to unpick, repin, re-sew, and press a single seam alteration.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterity: unpicking stitches, handling delicate fabric, guiding a sewing machine precisely, and pressing. Far beyond current humanoid manipulation capabilities; deformable textile control is unsolved at this precision.

task: Clean and drive funeral vehicles, such as cars or hearses, in funeral processions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one funeral vehicle (hearse or car). Washing exterior, wiping interior, and polishing for presentation takes a competent worker ~30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: General-purpose robots can wipe flat surfaces moderately well but struggle with vehicle curves, interior detailing, and glass polishing to a funeral-grade standard. Dexterity and generalization remain limiting.

task: Position material or articles in clamps, templates, or hoop frames prior to automatic operation of machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a single workpiece into a clamp, template, or hoop frame. A skilled operator typically takes 1–5 minutes depending on alignment precision required.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment, grasping flexible/varied materials, and securing into fixtures. Current humanoids handle structured pick-and-place well but struggle with accurate positioning into tight clamps or hoop frames, especially with deformable materials like fabric.

task: Install door hardware, such as locks and closers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one complete door hardware set (e.g., lockset + closer) on one door. A skilled carpenter/locksmith takes ~30–60 min depending on hardware type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise drilling, chiseling mortises, aligning strike plates, driving screws in tight spaces, and adjusting closers — multi-step fine dexterity with varied hardware, tools, and door geometries. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Sift and pack sand into mold sections, core boxes, and pattern contours, using hand or pneumatic ramming tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sifting and packing sand into one mold section or core box. A skilled foundry worker completes one cycle in roughly 10–20 minutes depending on mold complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled ramming into varied contours, sifting granular material, repositioning tools, and adapting to irregular mold geometries — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, granular manipulation, and generalization limits.

task: Distribute flyers, brochures, or other informational or educational documents to inform members of a targeted community.
human_time_rationale: One instance = distributing a batch of flyers across a defined route/area (e.g., a neighborhood block or community center). A competent human takes ~45 min walking and handing out materials.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor navigation on varied terrain, approaching strangers, grasping and handing individual flyers, social interaction, and adapting to unstructured public environments — well beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Clean machines, tooling, or parts, using solvents or solutions and rags.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single machine, tool set, or batch of parts with solvent and rags; a competent worker typically takes 15–25 minutes depending on size and grime level.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping rags, applying solvent, and scrubbing irregular industrial surfaces with appropriate force. Perception of grime removal, tool/part geometry variation, and safe chemical handling are challenging for current humanoids. Pick-and-wipe on flat surfaces is improving but unstructured machinery shapes and solvent logistics lower success significantly.

task: Clean, maintain, and sterilize instruments or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/sterilizing one batch of instruments (e.g., surgical or lab tools). Includes wiping, rinsing, loading an autoclave or sterilizer, and logging — ~15–25 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous handling of delicate/sharp instruments, precise loading of sterilization equipment, verification of cleanliness, and safety compliance. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation, sterile-field awareness, and instrument-specific protocols needed for acceptable quality.

task: Make garment style changes, such as tapering pant legs, narrowing lapels, and adding or removing padding.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one garment alteration (e.g., tapering both pant legs or narrowing lapels). A skilled tailor takes ~45–90 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous sewing, fabric manipulation, seam ripping, pressing, and fitting judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity; no frontier robot can perform needle-and-thread garment alteration reliably.

task: Fit, alter, repair, and make made-to-measure clothing, according to customers' and clothing manufacturers' specifications and fit, and applying principles of garment design, construction, and styling.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single garment alteration or made-to-measure construction job; simple alterations ~30 min, full bespoke garment ~many hours; ~120 min is a reasonable median across the mix.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-fine dexterous manipulation (needlework, sewing, cutting fabric, pinning), complex garment design reasoning, and customer fitting interactions. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and generalization; near-zero success probability.

task: Construct corners by fastening in plumb position a corner pole or building a corner pyramid of bricks, and filling in between the corners using a line from corner to corner to guide each course, or layer, of brick.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting two corners and laying one full course between them for a typical wall section (~10–15 ft). An experienced mason takes ~30–60 min including plumbing, leveling, and line setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise plumb/level judgment, mortar application, brick placement to tight tolerances, line stringing, and iterative course correction — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, perception accuracy, and tool-use generalization for masonry.

task: Shovel blacktop.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shoveling one load/area of blacktop (asphalt) to fill or spread a typical pothole or patch (~1–2 sq meters). A competent laborer takes ~20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Hot/heavy asphalt requires sustained high-force shoveling, precise placement, and outdoor uneven terrain navigation. Current humanoids lack the torque, heat tolerance, and robust outdoor locomotion needed for acceptable quality.

task: Position, secure, and align cutting tools in toolholders on machines, using hand tools, and verify their positions with measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a single cutting tool in a toolholder: selecting, inserting, torquing, and verifying alignment with gauges. Skilled machinist typically takes 10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterous manipulation (torquing hand tools to spec), sub-millimeter alignment verification with measuring instruments, and tool-change generalization—all well beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Fit and study garments on customers to determine required alterations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting a single garment on one customer: having them try it on, observing fit, marking/noting alterations needed. Typically 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social interaction, draping fabric on a live person, precise visual assessment of fit on a moving body, and fine-motor marking — all beyond current humanoid dexterity and social/perceptual generalization.

task: Perform bronchopulmonary drainage and assist or instruct patients in performance of breathing exercises.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single bronchopulmonary drainage + breathing exercise session for one patient, typically 20–40 minutes including positioning, percussion, and coaching.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise postural positioning, therapeutic chest percussion with calibrated force, real-time patient feedback interpretation, verbal coaching, and clinical judgment—far beyond current humanoid dexterity, safety, and adaptive caregiving capabilities.

task: Issue gym clothes, uniforms, towels, athletic equipment, and special athletic apparel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = issuing a set of items to one person. A competent attendant retrieves, checks, and hands over clothing/equipment in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a storage room, identifying varied soft/rigid items by size/type, picking garments and equipment reliably, and interacting with a patron. Soft-goods grasping, semantic categorization, and social handoff remain weak for current humanoids.

task: Repair or alter items by adding replacement parts or missing stitches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one repair job (e.g., patching a seam or replacing a button). A skilled tailor/seamstress typically spends 10–30 min per item.
robot_success_rationale: Sewing and stitching require extreme fine dexterity, thread manipulation, and precise needle control far beyond current humanoid capability. Fabric deformability and varied repair types make generalization nearly impossible in 2026.

task: Light burners or start heating units of machines, and regulate screed temperatures and asphalt flow rates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lighting burners and setting screed temp/asphalt flow on a single paving machine at job start; a skilled operator typically takes ~10 minutes to ignite, verify, and dial in settings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized paving equipment controls, igniting burners, and making precise thermal/flow adjustments. Humanoids cannot operate heavy road machinery; controls, heat hazards, and fine regulation are beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Spray or spread refractory material over brickwork to protect against deterioration.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying refractory coating to a typical section of brickwork (e.g., one wall or furnace lining). Skilled worker takes ~45 min to prep, spray/spread, and finish one section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating confined industrial spaces, handling a spray gun or trowel with consistent pressure and coverage, and judging material thickness on irregular brickwork. Current humanoids lack the specialized tool control, force regulation, and quality assessment for this unstructured industrial task.

task: Inspect, clean, maintain, and repair equipment, using mechanics' hand tools, or report malfunctions to supervisors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single equipment inspection/maintenance cycle on one machine. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min inspecting, cleaning, and performing minor repairs on one unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step tool use with hand tools, fault diagnosis, nuanced inspection, and adaptive repair on varied machinery. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity with wrenches/screwdrivers, contextual fault reasoning, and safe operation around complex equipment.

task: Stock crash carts or other medical supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fully restocking one crash cart or equivalent supply cart; a trained nurse/tech takes ~15–25 minutes to verify, restock, and seal.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise identification of many small medical items, strict placement protocols, tamper-seal application, and inventory verification. Current robots lack the reliable fine dexterity, medical item recognition, and safety-critical accuracy needed in cluttered clinical drawers.

task: Remove holding devices and finished items from machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a holding device (e.g., chuck/fixture) and finished part from one machine cycle. A competent operator typically takes 2–4 minutes per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires recognizing varied fixtures and finished parts, applying correct force/tool to release clamps, and safely extracting parts. High variability in fixture types, part weights, and machine configurations limits current robot reliability.

task: Install dies, cutters, and extensions to screeds onto machines, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a full set of dies/cutters/screed extensions on one machine. A skilled operator typically takes 15–25 min with hand tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force torquing with hand tools, precise alignment of heavy industrial components, and navigating tight machine geometries. Current humanoids lack the force control, tool-grip reliability, and task generalization for this industrial setup work.

task: Position and mark patterns on materials to prepare for sewing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out and marking one garment pattern on fabric. A skilled tailor/seamstress takes ~10–20 min to pin, align grain lines, and mark all pieces.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise handling of limp, deformable fabric and thin paper patterns, fine chalk/pen marking, grain-line alignment, and spatial reasoning on a large flat surface — far beyond current robot dexterity and deformable-object manipulation.

task: Control paving machines to push dump trucks and to maintain a constant flow of asphalt or other material into hoppers or screeds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full paving shift (~8 hours). Operator continuously controls the paver, coordinates dump truck pushes, and manages material flow throughout a paving run.
robot_success_rationale: Requires seated operation of specialized heavy machinery with complex multi-axis controls, real-time coordination with dump trucks, and dynamic judgment calls. No humanoid robot can operate paving equipment autonomously in live road conditions as of 2026.

task: Operate, calibrate, or maintain equipment used in quantitative or qualitative analysis, such as spectrophotometers, calorimeters, flame photometers, or computer-controlled analyzers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one calibration or maintenance session on a single analytical instrument (e.g., running standards on a spectrophotometer). A competent lab tech takes ~20–45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of cuvettes, reagents, and instrument controls; interpreting readouts; adjusting sensitive optics/electronics; and following variable lab-specific SOPs. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Operate machines that clean or cut expansion joints in concrete or asphalt and that rout out cracks in pavement.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/cutting one expansion joint or routing one crack section (~10–20 ft). A skilled operator sets up, runs the machine, and moves to the next joint in ~45 min including setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized ride-on or walk-behind pavement machinery on uneven outdoor terrain, precise tool alignment in cracks, and situational judgment. Humanoid robots cannot operate these machines autonomously; no deployed system handles this.

task: Lay and align bricks, blocks, or tiles to build or repair structures or high temperature equipment, such as cupola, kilns, ovens, or furnaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying and aligning a standard section/course of brickwork (e.g., ~50 bricks). A skilled mason takes ~2 hours per section including mortar prep and alignment checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous manipulation of heavy/irregular masonry units, mortar application, real-time leveling and alignment, and work in confined high-heat environments (kilns/furnaces). Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured field settings.

task: Prepare materials such as fundraising envelopes, bid sheets, or gift bags for charitable events.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a full set of event materials (e.g., ~50 envelopes, bid sheets, or gift bags) for one charitable event; ~45 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling highly varied, flimsy, and irregularly shaped items (paper envelopes, tissue paper, gift bags). Poor dexterity with soft/deformable materials, no reliable generalization across diverse item types and layouts.

task: Issue and store funeral equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = issuing and storing a set of funeral equipment (e.g., casket hardware, drapes, urns, trolleys) for one service. Includes retrieval, logging, and restocking.
robot_success_rationale: Funeral equipment is heavy, varied, and irregularly shaped (caskets, trolleys, drapes). Requires navigating tight storage areas, precise handling, and inventory logging. Current humanoids lack the strength, dexterity, and contextual judgment for reliable autonomous completion.

task: Analyze the results of tests or experiments to ensure conformity to specifications, using special mechanical or electrical devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = analyzing results from a single test/experiment batch using measuring devices, including setup, measurement, and conformity check. ~30 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting multi-modal test data, operating specialized electrical/mechanical instruments, and making conformity judgments. Current robots lack reliable tool-use generalization, instrument reading, and nuanced analytical reasoning in unstructured lab settings.

task: Start and operate or tend machines, such as single or double needle serging and flat-bed felling machines, to automatically join, reinforce, or decorate material or articles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = operating a serging/felling machine to complete one seam or article. A skilled operator sets up, feeds material, monitors, and clears the machine in ~5–10 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous fabric handling, precise feeding of limp/deformable textiles into narrow needle guides, real-time tension adjustment, and machine monitoring. Current humanoids lack reliable soft-goods manipulation and sewing machine operation at production quality.

task: Test repaired items to ensure proper operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one repaired item. Technician runs functional checks, cycles through modes, and confirms operation — typically ~10 minutes per item.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting varied item types, operating controls, reading displays, and judging "proper operation" — demanding perception, generalization, and contextual reasoning. Frontier robots lack robust multi-domain test & diagnosis capability.

task: Place spools of thread, cord, or other materials on spindles, insert bobbins, and thread ends through machine guides and components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a single machine (placing spool, inserting bobbin, threading guides). A skilled textile worker completes this in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Threading thin thread through small machine guides and inserting bobbins requires extreme fine dexterity and precise manipulation of flexible filaments — well beyond current humanoid capability. Perception of fine thread paths and tension-sensitive handling are major blockers.

task: Sprinkle chemical solvents over stains, and pat areas with brushes or sponges to remove stains.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating a single stain: apply solvent and pat with brush/sponge until stain is removed, typically 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise solvent dispensing, locating stains via vision, and controlled patting pressure. Robots struggle with variable stain types, soft/deformable surfaces, and fine force modulation needed to avoid fabric damage.

task: Evaluate, diagnose, or treat disorders of the visual system with an emphasis on binocular vision or abnormal eye movements.
human_time_rationale: One clinical encounter: history-taking, binocular vision testing (cover tests, prism, motility), diagnosis, and treatment plan for one patient — typical optometry/ophthalmology visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert clinical reasoning, fine ophthalmic instrument use, patient communication, nuanced sensorimotor assessment, and medical decision-making. Far beyond any 2026 general-purpose robot's capabilities.

task: Position patterns of garment parts on fabric, and cut fabric along outlines, using scissors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out and cutting all pattern pieces for a single garment. A competent sewer takes ~20–30 min to pin, position, and cut a typical garment's pattern pieces.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling limp, deformable fabric (very hard for robots), precise 2D spatial reasoning for pattern placement, and dexterous continuous cutting with scissors along curved outlines — far beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Load and unload film cassettes used to record images from procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading or unloading a single film cassette. A trained technician handles one cassette in ~2–4 minutes including orientation, seating, and labeling.
robot_success_rationale: Cassettes require precise alignment, latching, and light-tight handling in confined equipment bays. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tactile feedback, and domain-specific perception needed for consistent success in variable medical equipment.

task: Provide assistive devices, supportive technology, or assistance accessing facilities, such as restrooms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one person with a single need (e.g., restroom access, device setup). Typical interaction takes 10–20 minutes for a competent aide.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social interaction, understanding individual disability needs, safe physical contact, navigating varied environments, and operating diverse assistive devices. Generalization, safety, and caregiving judgment far exceed current robot capability.

task: Administer immunizations or other basic preventive treatments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = administering a single immunization to one patient, including prep (drawing vaccine, swabbing site), injection, and brief post-observation. ~5–15 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile technique, precise needle insertion into human tissue, patient positioning/compliance management, and real-time safety judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, medical-grade precision, and regulatory clearance. High-stakes failure risk.

task: Make incisions in arms or thighs and drain blood from circulatory system and replace it with embalming fluid, using pump.
human_time_rationale: One embalming arterial injection session per body: locating vessels, making incisions, inserting cannulas, running the pump until fluid exchange is complete typically takes ~45–90 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise anatomical knowledge, fine dexterous incision-making, cannula insertion into specific vessels, and fluid-pump management. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tactile feedback, and medical-procedure generalization.

task: Attach tape, trim, appliques, or elastic to specified garments or garment parts, according to item specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching trim/tape/elastic to a single garment part. A skilled sewing worker takes ~5–12 minutes depending on complexity and garment type.
robot_success_rationale: Textile manipulation is extremely challenging: limp, deformable fabric requires precise fine-motor control, consistent tension, and following curved seams. Current humanoids lack the dexterity and tactile feedback for reliable garment assembly at acceptable quality.

task: Remove burned or damaged brick or mortar, using sledgehammer, crowbar, chipping gun, or chisel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing damaged brick/mortar from a single localized repair section (~1–4 bricks). A competent mason takes ~30–60 min depending on damage extent and tool setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force percussive tool use (sledgehammer, chipping gun, chisel) with precise targeting on hard, irregular masonry. Current humanoids lack the force output, tool-wielding dexterity, vibration tolerance, and structural judgment needed for safe, effective removal.

task: Prepare classrooms with a variety of materials or resources for children to explore, manipulate, or use in learning activities or imaginative play.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up one classroom before a session. A competent teacher/aide arranges materials, organizes stations, and distributes supplies across the room in ~30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a cluttered classroom, identifying and sorting diverse mixed materials, placing items intentionally across multiple learning stations, and exercising pedagogical judgment. Current robots lack generalization for this unstructured, multi-step, context-dependent setup task.

task: Record required alterations and instructions on tags, and attach them to garments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = writing alteration notes on a tag and attaching it to one garment. A skilled tailor/alterations worker does this in ~2–4 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/writing legible text on small tags, fine dexterous attachment (pinning or tying a tag to fabric), and context-aware decision-making about alteration instructions — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Mount attachments, such as relieving or tracing attachments, to perform operations, such as duplicating contours of templates or trimming workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting a single attachment (e.g. tracing/relieving) to a machine. A skilled machinist aligns, fits, and secures the attachment in ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise mechanical alignment of specialized tooling attachments, torquing fasteners to spec, and interpreting machine-specific interfaces. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, domain-specific tool knowledge, and force-feedback precision this demands.

task: Harvest cell cultures at optimum time, based on knowledge of cell cycle differences and culture conditions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = harvesting one cell culture flask/batch. Includes monitoring confluency/viability, judging timing, and executing harvest steps (trypsinization, centrifugation, resuspension); ~45 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert biological knowledge to interpret microscopy/viability data, fine dexterous lab manipulation (pipetting, centrifuge loading), sterile technique, and contextual decision-making about cell cycle — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Perform a variety of tasks during funerals to assist funeral directors and to ensure that services run smoothly and as planned.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full funeral service. A competent funeral attendant typically spends ~2–3 hours on-site managing logistics, seating, flowers, equipment, and coordination.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social interaction, grief sensitivity, real-time adaptive coordination, tool/vehicle use (hearse, casket carriers), and complex unstructured multi-step tasks across an entire event. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Fill tanks, hoppers, or machines with paving materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling one tank/hopper/machine with paving material. Includes positioning, loading material (e.g., asphalt, aggregate), and checking fill level; typically 10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy equipment (loaders, dump trucks, conveyors) or handling bulk hot/abrasive materials outdoors on uneven terrain. Humanoid robots cannot safely operate this machinery or manage bulk industrial material flows autonomously.

task: Unlock cars and other vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unlocking a single vehicle (e.g. with a key fob, physical key, or keypad). A competent worker (e.g. valet, locksmith) takes ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Simple key-fob press is feasible, but inserting a physical key into a lock requires fine dexterity and precise alignment. Varied vehicle types, lock positions, and key formats hurt generalization. Lockout scenarios (slim jim, etc.) are beyond current robots.

task: Trace out short circuits in wiring, using test meter.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing a short circuit in a single wiring circuit/harness. A competent electrician spends ~15–25 min isolating the fault with a multimeter.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise probe placement on specific terminals, reading a meter display, interpreting results, and navigating complex wiring layouts. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, electrical domain reasoning, and adaptive fault-tracing logic needed for autonomous success.

task: Strip insulation from wire ends, using wire stripping pliers, and attach wires to terminals for subsequent soldering.
human_time_rationale: One instance = strip both ends of one wire and attach to terminals. A competent technician completes this in ~2–4 minutes including positioning and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-grip tool use (wire strippers), controlled force on thin wire, and accurate terminal insertion. Current humanoid dexterity and force sensitivity are far below the precision needed; high failure/damage risk.

task: Transport or accompany clients to scheduled health appointments or referral sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one round-trip escort to a health appointment, including travel, wait time, and return. Typical outing runs 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving or navigating public transit, managing unpredictable outdoor environments, and socially supporting a client — all far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities. Vehicle operation and community navigation are critical blockers.

task: Measure customers, using tape measures, and record measurements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single customer (e.g., chest, waist, hips, inseam). A competent tailor completes a standard measurement set in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires draping a flexible tape around a live human body with social awareness, precise positioning, and reading small markings. Current robots lack the dexterity, compliant human-contact safety, and social interaction needed.

task: Spread soiled articles on work tables, and position stained portions over vacuum heads or on marble slabs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = spreading and positioning one soiled garment on a work table over a vacuum head or marble slab; a skilled worker takes ~1–3 minutes per article.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping limp, varied fabric articles, precisely aligning stained areas over specific targets (vacuum heads/slab). Cloth manipulation and precise stain-to-target alignment exceed current robot dexterity and perception reliability; unstructured garment handling remains a key frontier weakness.

task: Examine cells stained with dye to locate abnormalities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining one slide/sample under a microscope. A trained cytologist/pathologist typically spends 10–20 minutes scanning and assessing a stained slide for abnormalities.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a microscope, fine focus adjustment, and expert visual interpretation of subtle cellular morphology. Current humanoid robots lack the fine dexterity for microscope manipulation and the validated AI perception for cytological diagnosis in a fully autonomous pipeline.

task: Assemble garment parts and join parts with basting stitches, using needles and thread or sewing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and basting one garment unit (e.g., a shirt or trouser piece). A skilled sewer typically takes 20–30 minutes to align, pin, and baste major seams.
robot_success_rationale: Fabric is highly deformable and hard to perceive/manipulate precisely. Threading needles and controlling stitch tension require extreme fine dexterity far beyond current humanoid capability. No commercial robot handles limp textiles reliably.

task: Trace outlines of paper onto cardboard patterns, and cut patterns into parts to make templates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tracing one paper pattern onto cardboard and cutting it into parts. A competent worker completes this in ~15 min including tracing, cutting, and labeling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise 2D tracing with a pen/stylus along irregular paper edges, then controlled cutting with scissors or a craft knife — fine dexterous bimanual tasks with flexible materials. Current humanoids lack the finger precision and force control needed.

task: Hang curtains, drapes, blankets, pants, and other garments on stretch frames to dry.
human_time_rationale: One instance = hanging one batch of mixed fabric items onto stretch frames; a competent worker takes roughly 5–10 minutes to spread and pin/secure each piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping large, floppy textiles, precisely aligning and stretching them onto frame pins/clips, and managing varied shapes. Deformable-object manipulation at this scale and precision is well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Clean work area and wash parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing/wiping a workbench and washing a typical batch of parts (e.g., machined or assembled components) at a sink or parts washer.
robot_success_rationale: Surface wiping is feasible (~40–70%), but washing parts requires grasping varied items, manipulating a sink/parts washer, scrubbing, and rinsing — demanding dexterity, tool-use, and fluid handling well beyond current robot capability.

task: Place strips of material, such as cork, asphalt, or steel into joints, or place rolls of expansion-joint material on machines that automatically insert material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling/inserting material into one joint or loading one roll onto a machine. A skilled worker handles placement, alignment, and securing in ~15 minutes per joint.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment and insertion of strips into tight joints on potentially uneven/outdoor terrain, plus loading rolls onto machinery. Poor generalization across joint types, materials, and environments limits current humanoids significantly.

task: Verify dimensions or check the quality or fit of pieces to ensure adherence to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single part/piece for dimensional conformance using calipers, gauges, or visual checks. A skilled worker typically takes 3–7 minutes per part.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can use vision for basic checks, but precise measurement with calipers/gauges requires fine dexterity and tool handling. Interpreting fit/feel and adapting to varied part geometries and tolerances remains a significant challenge for current humanoids.

task: Arrange indoor or outdoor space to facilitate creative play, motor-skill activities, or safety.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up one activity space (e.g., clearing a room, arranging mats/toys/equipment). A competent childcare worker takes ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level spatial planning, moving diverse furniture/equipment, contextual judgment about safety and play intent. Current robots lack the semantic scene understanding and generalization needed for unstructured environment arrangement.

task: Attach buttons, hooks, zippers, fasteners, or other accessories to fabric, using feeding hoppers or clamp holders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching a single fastener (button, zipper, hook, etc.) to a garment piece using an industrial feeding hopper or clamp holder. Skilled operator completes this in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise manipulation of soft, deformable fabric, alignment of small fasteners, and operation of specialized industrial machinery (hoppers/clamps). Current robots struggle with fabric handling, fine positioning, and tool-use generalization needed here.

task: Measure, cut, and bend wire and conduit, using measuring instruments and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring, cutting, and bending a single run of wire/conduit. A skilled electrician typically completes one such run in ~15–25 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, tool-switching (tape measure, cutters, conduit bender), and applying calibrated force for accurate bends. Conduit bending demands fine force control and spatial planning far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Tape or twist together thread or cord to repair breaks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing one break in thread/cord. A competent worker locates the break, aligns the ends, and tapes or twists them securely in roughly 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterity to handle thin thread/cord, align tiny broken ends, and apply tape or execute precise twisting. Far below current humanoid dexterous capability; finger force/tactile control insufficient for such delicate work.

task: Set up and maintain master key systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a master key system for a typical facility (e.g., 20–50 locks): planning the key hierarchy, pinning cylinders, cutting keys, installing/testing locks, and documenting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (pinning tiny lock cylinders with spring-loaded pins ~1mm), specialized locksmith tools, planning a keying hierarchy, and precise key cutting — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Drill holes and pull or push wiring through openings, using hand and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling holes and routing wiring through one set of openings (e.g., one wall/panel run). A competent electrician typically takes 20–40 minutes per run.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a power drill with precision in tight, varied spaces, threading flexible wiring through narrow openings, and adapting to unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and situational reasoning for this reliably.

task: Measure parts, such as sleeves or pant legs, and mark or pin-fold alteration lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and marking/pinning alteration lines on a single garment part (e.g., one sleeve or one pant leg). A skilled tailor takes ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle limp fabric, place pins precisely, and mark accurate alteration lines on deformable material. Current humanoids lack the tactile feedback and sub-millimeter precision needed for garment tailoring.

task: Install and configure wireless networking equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting/placing one wireless access point or router, running cables, and completing initial configuration. A competent technician typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires mounting hardware, routing cables, fine connector insertion, AND software configuration via GUI/CLI. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity for cable management and cannot navigate IT configuration interfaces autonomously.

task: Mark samples and finished patterns with information, such as garment size, section, style, identification, and sewing instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking a single sample or pattern piece with required info (size, style, etc.) using a pen/chalk/stamp. A competent worker completes this in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous writing/stamping on flexible fabric or paper, precise placement of marks/labels, and interpreting variable garment data. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control and context-aware writing ability needed.

task: Remove stitches from garments to be altered, using rippers or razor blades.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing stitches from one seam/section of a garment. A skilled tailor/seamstress typically spends 5–10 minutes per seam using a seam ripper or razor blade.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity to insert a seam ripper into individual stitches on flexible, deformable fabric without cutting or tearing material. Tactile feedback, tool manipulation, and soft-object handling far exceed current humanoid robot capability.

task: Drive machines onto truck trailers, and drive trucks to transport machines and material to and from job sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a machine onto a trailer, driving to a job site, and unloading. Typical haul with loading/unloading takes ~60–90 min for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a semi-truck in traffic and operating heavy loading equipment — tasks far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities. No general-purpose robot can safely operate these vehicles autonomously on public/job-site roads in 2026.

task: Perform basic diagnostic procedures, such as blood pressure screening, breast cancer screening, or communicable disease screening.
human_time_rationale: One screening instance per patient: setup, measurement/test, recording results. Blood pressure ~5 min; breast/communicable disease screenings ~15–20 min. Median ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise patient interaction, safe physical contact (cuff placement, palpation, swab collection), sterile technique, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack medical-grade dexterity, compliance sensing, and regulatory approval for autonomous patient contact.

task: Examine and measure finished articles to verify conformance to standards, using rulers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single finished article with a ruler; a competent worker measures key dimensions and checks conformance in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot must grasp a ruler, align it precisely, read measurements visually, and judge conformance. Dexterous tool-use and fine visual measurement reading remain unreliable; perception and manipulation accuracy needed are at limits of 2026 capability.

task: Construct controllers and panels, using power drills, drill presses, taps, saws, and punches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing a single controller or panel enclosure. Involves layout, drilling, tapping, sawing, and punching holes; a skilled electrician/technician takes ~2 hours per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated use of multiple power tools (drills, drill presses, taps, saws, punches) with precise force and positioning, tool-swapping, and fine dexterity. Current humanoids lack reliable multi-tool operation, workholding, and the precision needed for safe panel fabrication.

task: Baste edges of material to align and temporarily secure parts for final assembly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = basting the edges of one garment piece or seam. A skilled sewer typically takes 5–10 minutes to align, pin, and hand- or machine-baste a single seam/panel.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise handling of limp, deformable fabric, needle-and-thread or basting foot control, and fine alignment judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterous finger control and tactile feedback needed for textile sewing tasks.

task: Assemble mechanical, electrical, and electronic components into models or prototypes, using hand tools, power tools, and fabricating machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one prototype/model. A competent technician typically spends 1–3 hours integrating mechanical, electrical, and electronic components using varied tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires switching between hand tools, power tools, and fabricating machines; routing wires; reading schematics; fine connector insertion; and adapting to novel prototype layouts — far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Attach trocar to pump-tube, start pump, and repeat probing to force embalming fluid into organs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching trocar to pump tube, starting the pump, and completing cavity/organ probing for one body. Skilled embalmer typically spends 20–30 min on this phase.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise anatomical knowledge, fine dexterous tool coupling, navigating a fluid-filled body cavity with force-sensitive probing, and operating specialized pump controls — far beyond current humanoid capabilities in unstructured, tissue-contact tasks.

task: Determine the best layout of pattern pieces to minimize waste of material, and mark fabric accordingly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out and marking all pattern pieces for a single garment or project. A skilled sewist/cutter typically takes 30–60 min for a moderately complex pattern.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex 2D spatial optimization on deformable fabric, precise chalk/marker use, pattern alignment to grain lines, and interpretation of irregular shapes. Current robots lack fabric-handling dexterity and planning for nesting optimization.

task: Position and cut out master or sample patterns, using scissors and knives, or print out copies of patterns, using computers.
