human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning and cutting one master/sample pattern piece. A skilled pattern maker takes ~20–30 min per piece including layout, alignment, and cutting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor cutting with scissors/knives along complex curves, accurate pattern positioning on fabric, and interpreting 2D layout instructions. Dexterity and generalization are far below required precision; computer printing sub-task is slightly easier but still challenging end-to-end.

task: Administer prescribed oral medications, under the written direction of physician or as directed by home care nurse or aide, and ensure patients take their medicine.
human_time_rationale: One instance = administering one medication session (all prescribed oral meds) to one patient: retrieve meds, verify prescription, prepare doses, hand to patient, confirm swallowed. ~5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/verifying prescriptions, fine dexterous pill handling, patient communication and compliance monitoring, and safety-critical judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation, medication ID, and patient-interaction capabilities for this sensitive task.

task: Drive and operate curbing machines to extrude concrete or asphalt curbing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a typical curbing run (setup, driving the machine, extruding curbing along one stretch of road/lot). A competent operator typically takes ~2 hours per run.
robot_success_rationale: Requires riding/driving a specialized curbing machine outdoors on uneven terrain, setting extrusion dies, and adjusting material flow — far beyond any humanoid robot's current driving, heavy machinery operation, or outdoor generalization capabilities.

task: Heat billets, bars, plates, rods, and other stock to specified temperatures preparatory to forging, rolling, or processing, using oil, gas, or electrical furnaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a batch of stock, setting furnace parameters, monitoring to target temp, and extracting heated material. Typical cycle ~15–35 min depending on stock mass and furnace type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating industrial furnace controls, handling heavy/hot billets (extreme heat, weight), precise temperature monitoring, and safe extraction. Humanoids lack heat-resistant manipulation, load capacity, and furnace-specific tool integration.

task: Fold or stretch edges or lengths of items while sewing to facilitate forming specified sections.
human_time_rationale: One instance = folding/stretching edges of a single fabric section before or during sewing; a skilled seamstress typically takes 3–7 minutes per section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine bimanual dexterity on highly deformable, compliant fabric — precise tensioning, folding, and alignment under a needle. Current humanoids lack the tactile feedback and finger dexterity for reliable fabric manipulation at this fidelity.

task: Examine and sort into lots articles to be cleaned, according to color, fabric, dirt content, and cleaning technique required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting one typical household laundry load (~15–25 mixed items) into appropriate lots. A competent worker takes ~5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-attribute visual classification (color, fabric type, stain level) plus tactile fabric sensing and learned cleaning-method rules. Current robots lack reliable fabric identification, stain assessment, and nuanced sorting logic across all four criteria simultaneously.

task: Trim trees and clear undergrowth along right-of-way.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming trees and clearing undergrowth along a typical 100-meter right-of-way segment. A crew with chainsaws/brush cutters takes ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating chainsaws, brush cutters, or heavy trimming equipment outdoors on uneven terrain. Humanoids lack the specialized tool operation, power, stability, and safety controls needed for arboricultural work at scale.

task: Pull levers to lift ladle stoppers and to allow molten steel to flow into ingot molds to specified heights.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling one set of ingot molds from a ladle. A skilled steelworker monitors flow and adjusts levers over ~5–10 minutes per pour cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Extreme heat, molten metal hazards, and specialized heavy lever controls in an unstructured industrial environment are far beyond current humanoid dexterity, thermal tolerance, and safety certification. Perception of mold fill level in bright/smoky conditions also unsolved.

task: Attach parts or subassemblies together to form completed units, using glue, dowels, nails, screws, or clamps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one completed unit (e.g., a furniture piece or subassembly). A competent worker typically spends 15–25 minutes joining parts with fasteners, adhesives, or clamps.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place parts but struggle with varied fasteners (screws, clamps, dowels), precise alignment, applying correct glue amounts, and generalizing across many unit types. Fine dexterity and tool-switching remain major bottlenecks.

task: Inspect or monitor energy systems, including heating, ventilating, and air conditioning (HVAC) or daylighting systems to determine energy use or potential energy savings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single building/facility energy systems inspection walkthrough, covering HVAC, lighting, and related systems. A competent energy auditor typically takes ~1 hour per inspection visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex mechanical rooms, reading gauges/displays, interpreting system states, and making energy-saving judgments. Current robots lack the domain reasoning, tool use (thermal cameras, meters), and unstructured navigation needed for full autonomous execution.

task: Provide basic health services, such as first aid.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single basic first aid intervention (e.g., cleaning and bandaging a wound, applying ice, checking vitals). A competent human completes this in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: First aid demands precise dexterous manipulation (bandaging, wound care), real-time clinical assessment, patient communication, and adaptive decision-making on an uncooperative or distressed human body — far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Examine tags on garments to determine alterations that are needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining the tag(s) on a single garment. A skilled tailor/alteration specialist reads and interprets the tag in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating a small, often hidden tag, reading fine text/symbols, and interpreting alteration needs — challenging for perception and semantic reasoning. Current robots lack reliable fine-print OCR on crumpled fabric tags and contextual garment understanding.

task: Take and record temperature of food and food storage areas, such as refrigerators and freezers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = taking and logging the temperature of a single food item or storage unit (e.g., one refrigerator). Involves inserting a probe or reading a thermometer and recording the value.
robot_success_rationale: Robot must navigate to unit, open door, insert or point thermometer correctly, read display/probe, and log result. Door opening (~50%), probe handling and precise insertion into food are dexterous challenges; reliable logging adds further complexity.

task: Apply and smooth mortar or other mixture over work surface.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying and smoothing mortar over a single work surface (e.g., one wall section or floor area ~1–2 m²). A skilled mason/plasterer takes ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained forceful contact, precise trowel angle, and smooth consistent strokes on irregular surfaces. Current humanoids lack the tactile feedback, wrist dexterity, and force control needed for acceptable finish quality.

task: Dip, brush, or spray assembled articles with protective or decorative finishes, such as stain, varnish, paint, or lacquer.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing one assembled article (e.g., a furniture piece). Includes prep, application of finish, and brief inspection; ~15–25 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Spray/brush finishing requires precise, consistent tool control, path planning on varied 3D surfaces, and finish-quality judgment. Current humanoids lack fine fluid-control dexterity and surface-coverage perception needed for acceptable results.

task: Receive and mark articles for laundry or dry cleaning with identifying code numbers or names, using hand or machine markers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking one garment/article with an ID code. A competent laundry worker inspects, selects a code, and applies a hand or machine marker in ~1–2 minutes per item.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting garment labels, selecting correct codes, and precisely applying markers to varied soft textile surfaces. Fine dexterous marking on deformable fabric, handling floppy articles, and managing a hand/machine marker are beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Test raw materials, processes, or finished products to determine quality or quantity of materials or characteristics of a substance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single batch/sample. A competent technician spends ~30 min collecting, preparing, running tests, and logging results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous handling of lab equipment (pipettes, instruments), precise fluid manipulation, contextual judgment on results, and adapting to varied unstructured lab setups — well beyond reliable current robot capability.

task: Operate oil distributors, loaders, chip spreaders, dump trucks, and snow plows.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one operational shift/job using these road construction/maintenance vehicles. A typical work session (e.g., paving a road segment or snow plowing a route) takes ~4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate heavy vehicles (oil distributors, dump trucks, chip spreaders, snow plows) on public/work roads. These require seated cab operation, specialized controls, and real-world traffic navigation far beyond current robot capability.

task: Start lathe or turning machines and observe operations to ensure that specifications are met.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting machine, loading workpiece, and monitoring one machining cycle; ~15–25 min per part including setup checks and observation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial machinery with precision controls, loading/securing raw stock, and interpreting gauges/sounds. Humanoids lack interfaces, tooling knowledge, and fine positional accuracy for lathe operation; cannot safely operate cutting machinery autonomously.

task: Measure and mark dimensions of parts on paper or lumber stock prior to cutting, following blueprints, to ensure a tight fit and quality product.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and marking one part from a blueprint. A skilled carpenter/tradesperson typically takes 5–15 minutes per part layout.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading blueprints, precise measuring with tape/square, and marking fine lines on irregular lumber surfaces. Fine motor precision, tool handling, and blueprint interpretation are well beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Photograph or videotape client artwork for inclusion in client records or for promotional purposes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = photographing/videotaping a single piece of client artwork. Includes setup, framing, capturing multiple shots, and basic review. ~15 min for a competent human.
robot_success_rationale: Requires aesthetic framing judgment, camera/tool manipulation, managing lighting, and navigating varied artwork types. Current humanoids lack reliable camera-tool use, compositional vision, and the nuanced quality standards a supervisor would accept.

task: Conduct home visits for pregnant women, newborn infants, or other high-risk individuals to monitor their progress or assess their needs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one home visit. Typical public health nurse/home visitor spends ~60–90 min per visit including travel, assessment, documentation, and counseling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating unfamiliar homes, clinical assessment (auscultation, palpation, vital signs), empathetic communication, nuanced judgment on medical/social risk, and autonomous decision-making — all far beyond 2026 robot capability.

task: Improvise instrumentally, vocally, or physically to meet client's therapeutic needs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single therapeutic improvisation session segment; a music/movement therapist typically improvises with a client for 15–25 minutes within a session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time emotional attunement, clinical judgment, musical/vocal/physical creativity, and adaptive response to subtle client cues — far beyond any current robot's social, expressive, or therapeutic capabilities.

task: Inspect repaired vehicles for proper functioning, completion of work, dimensional accuracy, and overall appearance of paint job, and test-drive vehicles to ensure proper alignment and handling.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full inspection + test drive of one repaired vehicle; ~15–30 min covering visual checks, dimensional gauging, paint review, and an on-road test drive.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving on public roads (humanoids cannot), nuanced paint/finish perception, dimensional gauging with tools, and holistic quality judgment. No current general-purpose robot can do all components reliably.

task: Transport tools, materials, equipment, and supplies to work site by hand, handtruck, or heavy, motorized truck.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single delivery run (loading, transporting, unloading). Typical trip to a work site by hand/handtruck/truck averages ~30 minutes depending on distance and load.
robot_success_rationale: Short-range hand or handtruck transport is partially feasible (~30–50%), but the task explicitly includes heavy motorized truck driving, which humanoid robots cannot do. Mixed-mode requirement and outdoor/variable terrain drag overall probability very low.

task: Set up forms and lay out guidelines for curbs, according to written specifications, using string, spray paint, and concrete or water mixes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up forms and laying out guidelines for one curb run (~50 ft). Reading specs, placing forms, stringing lines, and marking with spray paint takes a skilled worker ~60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading written specs, interpreting survey stakes, precise string-line setup, spray painting, and operating in unstructured outdoor/construction terrain. Far beyond current humanoid generalization, dexterity, and tool-use capabilities outdoors.

task: Conduct chemical analyses of body fluids, such as blood or urine, using microscope or automatic analyzer to detect abnormalities or diseases and enter findings into computer.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one specimen analyzed (e.g., a single blood or urine sample): prep, run on analyzer or microscope, interpret results, and enter findings into computer — ~15–25 min for a lab tech.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly precise pipetting, operating specialized lab analyzers, interpreting clinical results, and entering structured medical data into a computer — far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity, domain cognition, and software integration capabilities.

task: Mount brackets and hangers on walls and ceilings to hold pipes, and set sleeves or inserts to provide support for pipes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting one bracket/hanger and setting one sleeve/insert. A skilled pipefitter marks, drills, and fastens one unit in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise drilling into walls/ceilings at varied heights and angles, torquing fasteners, interpreting layout marks, and handling varied hardware. Current humanoids lack the positional accuracy, force control, and tool-use reliability for this multi-step construction task.

task: Perform specialized or automatic sewing machine functions, such as buttonhole making or tacking.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one buttonhole or tacking operation. A skilled operator positions fabric, selects the function, and runs the stitch cycle in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fabric handling (limp, deformable), exact alignment under a needle, and fine dexterous machine control. Current humanoids lack the finger precision and textile perception needed; high failure risk.

task: Drive heavy equipment and vehicles with adjustable attachments to sweep debris from paved surfaces, mow grass and weeds, remove snow and ice, and spread salt and sand.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one shift/route pass covering a typical road or lot segment. Competent operators complete a standard route in ~2 hours including attachment adjustments.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate heavy vehicles in traffic or outdoors. Task requires driving, dynamic attachment swapping, variable terrain judgment, and safety compliance — far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Instruct and monitor students in the use and care of equipment or materials to prevent injuries and damage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single instructional session with a student or group covering equipment/material safety. Typical safety briefing or demonstration runs 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic verbal instruction, monitoring student behavior, adapting to questions, and intervening unpredictably. Current robots lack reliable speech pedagogy, real-time student behavior interpretation, and autonomous supervisory judgment in complex social settings.

task: Conduct blood tests for transfusion purposes and perform blood counts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a blood sample, running transfusion compatibility tests (type & screen/crossmatch) and a CBC; a trained phlebotomist/lab tech completes this in ~20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile venipuncture on a live patient (extreme dexterity, safety), operating clinical analyzers, and interpreting lab results — all far beyond current general-purpose humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Restock kitchen supplies, rotate food, and stamp the time and date on food in coolers.
human_time_rationale: One restocking instance covers a standard kitchen supply area; a competent worker retrieves, organizes, and fills shelves/stations in ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a kitchen, identifying varied supply types, grasping diverse containers, and placing them correctly. Generalization across SKUs and shelf layouts is still poor; clutter and fragile items add failure risk.

task: Maintain garment drape and proportions as alterations are performed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = maintaining drape/proportions throughout a single garment alteration session (e.g., hemming or taking in a seam on one garment). A skilled tailor continuously adjusts and checks fit over ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert tactile sensitivity, nuanced visual judgment of fabric drape and 3D garment geometry, and fine dexterous manipulation of soft, deformable materials — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in 2026.

task: Set up, operate, and adjust a variety of woodworking machines such as bandsaws and lathes to cut and shape sections, parts, and patterns, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setup + operation of a woodworking machine (e.g., bandsaw or lathe) for a single part/pattern run. Includes machine adjustment, feed, and inspection; ~30–60 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large, specialized stationary machinery (lathes, bandsaws), precise feed control, reading specs, and fine adjustment. No current humanoid robot can safely operate industrial woodworking equipment autonomously at acceptable quality.

task: Obtain, cut, stain, and mount biological material on slides for microscopic study and diagnosis, following standard laboratory procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single tissue specimen from gross sample through sectioning, staining, and mounting. A skilled histotechnician typically takes 30–60 min per specimen batch unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (microtome sectioning at micron scale, coverslipping without air bubbles), precise chemical sequencing, and sterile handling. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability; even specialized lab robots struggle with the full pipeline.

task: Press garments, using hand irons or pressing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pressing one garment (e.g., a shirt). A competent laundry worker typically irons/presses a standard garment in 3–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous manipulation of limp, deformable fabric, controlled heat/pressure application, and dynamic repositioning of the garment — well beyond current humanoid dexterity and compliant-object handling. No commercially deployed robot handles this reliably.

task: Maintain a safe play environment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single play area (indoor or outdoor). A competent worker walks the space, checks equipment, removes hazards, and documents findings in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced hazard recognition (broken equipment, sharp edges, foreign objects, unsupervised children) in unstructured outdoor/indoor spaces. Current robots lack reliable contextual safety judgment, child-interaction awareness, and rough-terrain mobility for playgrounds.

task: Set up or operate machines, including power saws, jointers, mortisers, tenoners, molders, or shapers, to cut, mold, or shape woodstock or wood substitutes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and operating a woodworking machine for a single production run/batch. Setup (blade/fence/depth adjustments) plus cutting takes ~30–60 min for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise machine setup (blade height, fence alignment, depth stops), feeding stock safely through high-speed spinning blades, and adaptive judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, safety awareness, and machine-interfacing capability for autonomous operation of these dangerous, specialized tools.

task: String transmission lines or cables through ducts or conduits, under the ground, through equipment, or to towers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pulling/threading a single cable run through a duct, conduit, or to a tower. A typical run takes 1–2 hours depending on length and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained high-force pulling, threading through tight conduits in cramped/outdoor spaces, climbing towers, and using specialized tools (fish tape, pulling grips). Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, reach, and force output in unstructured environments.

task: Trim, smooth, and shape surfaces, and plane, shave, file, scrape, and sand models to attain specified shapes, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one model/workpiece to specification. A competent craftsperson spends ~30–60 min trimming, filing, sanding, and shaping a typical model piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly precise force-feedback, multi-tool dexterity, and nuanced surface inspection to hit exact shapes. Current humanoids lack the fine tactile control, adaptive grip pressure, and tool-switching finesse for consistent quality results.

task: Attach physiological monitoring leads to patient's finger, chest, waist, or other body parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching a full set of monitoring leads (e.g., pulse ox, ECG electrodes, resp belt) to one patient; a competent nurse/tech takes ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor dexterity on a compliant, irregularly shaped human body, correct anatomical placement, gentle force control to avoid discomfort/injury, and real-time skin/hair adaptation — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Instruct individuals or groups in the use of art media, such as paint, clay, or yarn.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one instructional session (individual or group) covering a single art medium or technique; typical class/workshop session runs ~60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic verbal/social instruction, adaptive teaching, nuanced demonstration of fine motor art techniques, and real-time student feedback interpretation — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in generalization and social interaction.

task: Calibrate magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) console or peripheral hardware.
human_time_rationale: One calibration session on an MRI console/peripherals; a trained MRI physicist or technologist typically spends 45–90 minutes running calibration protocols, adjusting settings, and documenting results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires deep domain expertise, software-driven console interaction, precise knob/switch manipulation, and interpretation of RF/gradient/shimming outputs. No current humanoid robot has MRI-specific perception, safety compliance, or the fine-grained tool-use needed for autonomous calibration.

task: Prepare standard volumetric solutions or reagents to be combined with samples, following standardized formulas or experimental procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single volumetric solution/reagent batch. Involves weighing, dissolving, transferring to volumetric flask, and labeling — typically 15–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting, fine dexterity with glassware, reading meniscus levels, handling powders accurately, and following multi-step chemical procedures. Far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Test patterns by making and fitting sample garments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing and fitting one sample garment from a pattern. Includes cutting fabric, sewing, and fitting adjustments — typically 2–4 hours for a competent sample maker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly dexterous fabric manipulation (limp, deformable material), precise sewing machine operation, iterative fitting judgment, and complex multi-step reasoning. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured garment construction.

task: Perform routine maintenance or standard repairs to networking components or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one maintenance/repair session on a networking device (e.g., router, switch, patch panel). Covers inspection, cable reseating, firmware checks, dust cleaning, and part swap if needed.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for small connectors/cables, reading indicator LEDs, navigating rack-mounted equipment, and conditional decision-making based on diagnostics. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and IT tool integration for autonomous network servicing.

task: Intravenously inject contrast dyes, such as gadolinium contrast, in accordance with scope of practice.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and administering a single IV contrast injection to a patient: vein access, syringe prep, injection, and post-check. Competent clinician typically takes 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise venipuncture on a compliant human patient, sterile technique, real-time patient monitoring, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tactile feedback, sterility protocols, and safety compliance needed for autonomous IV injection.

task: Break up concrete, using airhammer, to facilitate installation, construction, or repair of equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = breaking a typical concrete section (e.g., a sidewalk panel or floor patch ~2–4 sq ft). A competent worker with an airhammer takes ~45–75 min including setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a heavy pneumatic airhammer tool with sustained force, precise positioning, and outdoor/unstructured terrain navigation. Current humanoids lack the strength, tool-coupling ability, and vibration tolerance for this high-force task.

task: Develop or otherwise produce film records of magnetic resonance images.
human_time_rationale: One instance = producing film records for one patient's MRI study. A competent technologist loads images, reviews, prints/exports films via a workstation in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Task is almost entirely computer-based (PACS workstation, image review, laser printer operation). Humanoid robots cannot operate clinical imaging software interfaces or handle sensitive medical IT systems autonomously in 2026.

task: Trim, sand, or scrape surfaces or joints to prepare articles for finishing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one article (e.g., a wood panel or furniture piece). A competent worker trims, sands, or scrapes a single surface/joint in ~15 min depending on size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled contact, varied tool use (scrapers, sanders), and tactile/visual feedback to judge surface quality. Current humanoids lack reliable fine force modulation and finishing judgment for unstructured workpieces.

task: Sew garments, using needles and thread or sewing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sewing one garment (e.g., a basic shirt or pair of trousers). A competent seamstress/tailor typically takes 30–60 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Sewing requires extreme fine dexterity — threading needles, guiding delicate fabric with consistent tension, aligning seams precisely. Deformable fabric manipulation and coordinated bimanual micro-control are far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Provide headphones or earplugs to patients to improve comfort and reduce unpleasant noise.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retrieving and fitting/handing headphones or earplugs to one patient. A competent caregiver typically takes 2–4 minutes including selection, sanitation check, and placement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to patient, handling small/soft items (earplugs), and fitting them near a person's head safely. Dexterous manipulation of deformable earplugs and safe interaction with a human patient are major limitations for 2026 humanoids.

task: Lay out patterns on wood stock and draw outlines of units, sectional patterns, or full-scale mock-ups of products, based on blueprint specifications and sketches, and using marking and measuring devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out and marking one part/pattern on wood stock from a blueprint. A competent woodworker/loftsman typically takes 30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting blueprints/sketches, precise measurement and marking with tools (squares, scribes, chalk lines) on raw stock, and fine dexterous positioning. Current humanoids lack blueprint-reading integration, precise fine-motor marking control, and task generalization for this craft workflow.

task: Apply Masonite, formica, or vinyl surfacing materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying surfacing material to one panel/surface (e.g., a countertop or cabinet face). Includes measuring, cutting, adhesive application, and pressing/trimming.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measuring/cutting of rigid sheet material, even adhesive spreading, accurate alignment, and pressure application with trimming. Current humanoids lack fine bimanual coordination, consistent force control, and tool-use (routers, rollers) for acceptable quality.

task: Construct wooden models, templates, full scale mock-ups, jigs, or molds for shaping parts of products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing a single wooden model, template, jig, or mold. A competent woodworker typically spends 1–3 hours per piece depending on complexity; 120 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting technical drawings, planning multi-step cuts, operating power saws/routers/drills with precision, and validating dimensional accuracy. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use generalization, and cognitive planning for unstructured woodworking.

task: Measure, cut, thread and assemble new pipe, placing the assembled pipe in hangers or other supports.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single pipe run (measure, cut, thread, assemble, and hang one section). A competent plumber/pipefitter typically completes this in 30–60 minutes depending on pipe length and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement in 3D space, operating a pipe cutter and threading die, fine-torque assembly of fittings, and mounting into hangers overhead or on walls — multi-tool, high-force, high-dexterity work far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Trim excess material, using scissors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming excess material from a single workpiece (e.g., a seam, edge, or component). A skilled worker locates, grips, and cuts in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual coordination: holding material taut while guiding scissors along an irregular edge. Fine dexterity, compliant force control, and variable material deformation make this very difficult for current humanoids.

task: Cultivate, isolate, or assist in identifying microbial organisms or perform various tests on these microorganisms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full workflow cycle: preparing media/samples, inoculating cultures, incubating, and performing one identification test. Typically 45–90 min of active hands-on time per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (pipetting, streaking plates, handling fragile specimens), sterile technique, precise tool use, and scientific judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured lab settings.

task: Set out signs and cones around work areas to divert traffic.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a typical road work zone with signs and cones. A competent worker takes ~20–40 min to place, space, and orient all equipment per traffic control standards.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/uneven terrain locomotion, carrying and precisely placing heavy cones and signs per regulatory spacing, reading site context, and operating near live traffic — well beyond current humanoid generalization and safety limits.

task: Assist pipe fitters in the layout, assembly, and installation of piping for air, ammonia, gas, and water systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting with a single piping run/section installation. Competent helper spends ~2 hrs on layout, fitting, and securing one section of pipe.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting, precise alignment of pipes in varied industrial environments, use of specialized tools (pipe wrenches, threaders, torque tools), working in confined/overhead spaces, and coordinating with a human lead — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Solder electrical connections, using soldering iron.
human_time_rationale: One instance = soldering a single electrical connection joint. A competent technician heats, applies solder, and inspects in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter precision, bimanual coordination (hold iron + feed solder wire), thermal control, and visual inspection of melt quality. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and fine tool-use capability.

task: Clean funeral parlors or chapels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single funeral parlor or chapel room (vacuuming, dusting, mopping, sanitizing surfaces). A competent cleaner typically takes 60–90 minutes per room.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle flat-surface sweeping/mopping reasonably well, but funeral settings demand careful navigation around delicate furniture, floral arrangements, caskets, and pews, plus nuanced sanitization standards and varied layouts that challenge generalization and dexterity.

task: Fit, fasten, and assemble wood parts together to form patterns, models, or sections, using glue, nails, dowels, bolts, and screws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one wood pattern/section (e.g. a cabinet frame or model). A competent carpenter typically spends 30–60 min fitting, fastening, and gluing a moderately complex wood assembly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step fine dexterity: aligning wood parts, applying glue, driving screws/nails, inserting dowels. Generalization across irregular wood pieces, varied fasteners, and force-controlled fitting is well beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Assist patients to insert or remove contact lenses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one patient with inserting or removing one pair of contact lenses. A skilled optometric technician typically completes this in 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity near a sensitive eye, millimeter-precision fingertip control, safe force regulation, and patient cooperation. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and safety thresholds; risk of eye injury makes autonomous operation implausible.

task: Conduct inventories to maintain stock of clinical supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single inventory cycle of a clinical supply area (e.g., a medication room or supply cart). A competent worker counts, records, and reconciles stock in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating cluttered clinical spaces, reading small labels/barcodes on varied packaging, opening drawers/cabinets, counting irregularly arranged items, and logging data — beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability in unstructured medical environments.

task: Reinforce joints with nails or other fasteners to prepare articles for finishing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reinforcing all joints on a single article (e.g., a furniture piece). A competent worker nails/fastens multiple joints in roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fastener placement on varied joint geometries, tool handling (nail gun/hammer), and force control. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity and tool-use accuracy for consistent, supervisor-acceptable joint reinforcement.

task: Perform semi-skilled and unskilled laboring duties related to the installation, maintenance and repair of a wide variety of electrical systems and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single discrete laboring task (e.g., pulling wire, moving conduit, digging a trench section) as part of an electrical job. ~45 min is a reasonable median unit of work.
robot_success_rationale: Electrical labor spans highly varied, unstructured environments — trenching, wire pulling through conduit, lifting panels, connecting terminals. Requires tool use, mobility on job sites, and contextual judgment. Current humanoids lack robust outdoor mobility, power-tool operation, and generalization for such mixed physical tasks.

task: Cut pipe and lift up to fitters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measure, cut one pipe section with a pipe cutter/saw, and hand/lift it up to a fitter. A competent pipefitter helper completes this in ~10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, operating a pipe saw/cutter (specialized tool use), handling heavy/long awkward pipe, and coordinating an overhead lift to a human fitter. Beyond current robot dexterity, tool-use, and load-bearing capabilities.

task: Teach group exercise for low-, medium-, or high-risk clients to improve participant strength, flexibility, endurance, or circulatory functioning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one group exercise class session. Typical structured group fitness class runs 45–60 minutes; 60 min is a reasonable median for instructed sessions.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time verbal instruction, dynamic coaching, safety monitoring of diverse participants, adaptive cueing, and interpersonal rapport. No current humanoid robot can lead a live group fitness class autonomously at acceptable quality.

task: Operate optical systems to capture dynamic magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) images, such as functional brain imaging, real-time organ motion tracking, or musculoskeletal anatomy and trajectory visualization.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single dynamic MRI acquisition session (e.g., fMRI scan or real-time organ tracking). Competent MRI technologist sets up, positions patient, configures sequences, monitors, and completes one scan run (~30–60 min typical).
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly specialized clinical MRI console operation, precise patient interaction, protocol selection, real-time monitoring, and safety judgment in a magnetic-field-restricted environment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities; no viable deployment exists.

task: Calibrate exercise or testing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = calibrating a single piece of exercise or testing equipment (e.g., treadmill, dynamometer, ergometer). Includes setup, adjustment, and verification checks.
robot_success_rationale: Calibration requires fine dexterous adjustments (knobs, sensors, fasteners), reading displays, interpreting feedback, and using specialized tools. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and the domain-specific procedural knowledge for pass/fail calibration decisions.

task: Demonstrate correct use of exercise equipment or performance of exercise routines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = demonstrating a single exercise routine or equipment use session; a competent trainer typically takes 15–25 minutes to explain, show, and correct form for one routine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic balance, coordinated full-body movement, real-time adaptation to varied equipment, and persuasive/instructional interaction. Current humanoids lack reliable athletic motion quality and social teaching capability; fall/error risk is high.

task: Set up, operate, or tend saws or machines that cut or trim wood to specified dimensions, such as circular saws, band saws, multiple-blade sawing machines, scroll saws, ripsaws, or crozer machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and completing a batch/run on a sawing machine. A competent sawyer spends ~30 min setting up, calibrating, and running a typical production batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise machine setup (blade changes, fence/stop adjustment, calibration), feeding irregular lumber, operating foot pedals/controls, and quality judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, specialized tool interfacing, and safety-critical precision needed.

task: Measure oxygen consumption or lung functioning, using spirometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient spirometry session: setup, coaching patient through breathing maneuvers, and recording results. Typically takes 10–20 minutes for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise instrument handling, patient coaching/communication, interpreting effort-dependent breathing maneuvers, and clinical judgment. Humanoids lack medical training integration, patient interaction capability, and safe clinical deployment clearance.

task: Assess refractive conditions of eyes, using retinoscopes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full retinoscopy exam on one patient (both eyes). A skilled optometrist typically completes this in ~5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise handheld instrument manipulation millimeters from a patient's eye, real-time interpretation of light reflex, patient cooperation management, and clinical judgment. Far beyond current robot dexterity, perception, and safe caregiving capability.

task: Sew buttonholes and attach buttons to finish garments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sewing buttonholes and attaching all buttons on one garment (e.g., a shirt with ~7 buttons). A skilled tailor takes ~2 min per buttonhole/button pair.
robot_success_rationale: Extremely fine dexterity required: threading needles, guiding fabric through a sewing machine, precise cutting of buttonholes, and hand-stitching buttons. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability; sub-millimeter precision and force control needed.

task: Position patients on cradle, attaching immobilization devices, if needed, to ensure appropriate placement for imaging.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning one patient on a cradle for imaging, including attaching immobilization devices. Typically takes 5–15 minutes depending on patient mobility and device complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate patient handling, safe body positioning, attaching fitted immobilization devices, and adapting to each patient's anatomy/mobility. Current robots lack the dexterity, safe force control, and clinical judgment for this sensitive, variable caregiving task.

task: Remove litter and debris from roadways, including debris from rock and mud slides.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing a typical roadway segment of litter/debris or a moderate slide deposit. A crew with tools completes this in ~1 hour on average.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor unstructured terrain, heavy/irregular debris, and often large-scale removal (rock/mud slides). Humanoids lack the mobility, strength, and heavy equipment operation needed. Extremely low success probability.

task: Provide emergency or other appropriate medical care to participants with symptoms or signs of physical distress.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing and providing initial emergency care to one person in distress, from first contact to stabilization or handoff to EMS. Typical first-responder initial intervention.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced clinical assessment (pulse, breathing, pain localization), adaptive dexterous intervention (CPR, wound care, airway management), dynamic decision-making, and safe physical interaction with a distressed human — far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Act as pallbearers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = carrying a casket from chapel/hearse to graveside, including positioning, lifting, walking ~100m, and lowering. Typically 15–25 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated multi-robot lifting of a heavy, awkward load (~200–400 lbs total), precise synchronization, outdoor terrain navigation, and socially appropriate behavior in a solemn ceremony. Current robots lack multi-agent coordination, load-sharing, and the ceremonial precision required.

task: Glue fillets along interior angles of patterns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying a glue fillet along all interior angles of a single pattern piece. A competent worker takes ~5–10 min depending on pattern complexity and glue cure prep.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bead application along irregular interior angles with consistent pressure and angle. Current robots lack the fine dexterity, real-time path adaptation, and tactile feedback needed for uniform fillet quality in unstructured pattern geometries.

task: Mix bleaching agents with hot water in vats, and soak material until it is bleached.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one vat (mixing bleach + hot water) and soaking one material batch until bleached; ~30–60 min including monitoring.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling large vats of hot liquid, precise chemical mixing, and judging bleach completion visually. Hot liquid safety risks, large/heavy vessels, and unstructured chemical-handling push well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Install hardware, such as hinges, handles, catches, or drawer pulls, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single piece of hardware (e.g., one hinge or handle). A competent human marks, aligns, and screws in hardware in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment, hole-finding, and controlled torque with hand tools (screwdrivers). Current humanoids struggle with tool-use dexterity, variable surface/part positioning, and fine force feedback needed for consistent, non-damaging installation.

task: Maintain ophthalmic instruments or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = maintaining a single ophthalmic instrument (cleaning, calibrating, inspecting). A competent technician takes ~30 min per unit including checks and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Ophthalmic instruments are delicate, optically precise, and require fine dexterity, calibration knowledge, and specialized tool use. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter precision, domain-specific perception, and procedural expertise needed.

task: Produce or assemble components of articles, such as store fixtures, office equipment, cabinets, or high-grade furniture.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a single mid-complexity article (e.g., a cabinet or office fixture). A skilled woodworker/assembler typically takes 60–120 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: High-grade furniture/fixture assembly demands precise force-controlled joinery, varied part geometries, tool use (drills, clamps, fasteners), and quality judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool fluency, and generalization for unstructured, multi-step cabinetry/fixture work.

task: Requisition tools and equipment, select type and size of pipe, and collect and transport materials and equipment to work site.
human_time_rationale: One instance = gathering and transporting tools, selecting correct pipe specs, and moving materials to a single work site. A competent plumber/pipefitter typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step cognitive planning (requisitioning, spec selection), navigating supply areas, transporting heavy/bulky pipe, and operating carts or vehicles across potentially long distances — far beyond current humanoid generalization and payload limits.

task: Erect, install, or repair guardrails, road shoulders, berms, highway markers, warning signals, and highway lighting, using hand tools and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing/repairing one section of roadway infrastructure (e.g., a guardrail span or signal). Skilled highway crew typically takes 1–3 hours per unit section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy outdoor equipment, driving vehicles, post-driving tools, and working on unstructured road environments. Current humanoids lack the mobility, force capacity, and tool-use needed. Outdoor terrain, heavy loads, and specialized machinery are far beyond 2026 frontier capabilities.

task: Fill pipes with sand or resin to prevent distortion, and hold pipes during bending and installation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling and holding one pipe for bending/installation. Packing sand/resin, sealing ends, and holding steady takes ~10–20 min for a typical pipe.
robot_success_rationale: Requires pouring granular/viscous material into pipe openings, sealing ends, and holding pipes at precise angles under load — demanding bimanual dexterity, force sensing, and coordination that current humanoids handle very poorly in unstructured pipe-work settings.

task: Place and secure small, portable magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scanners on body part to be imaged, such as arm, leg, or head.
human_time_rationale: Positioning and securing one portable MRI scanner on a single body part (e.g., knee, wrist, head). A trained technologist spends ~5–10 min aligning and fastening the device.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, gentle contact with a human body part, fine dexterity to align imaging coils, adaptive sensing of patient anatomy, and safe fastening — all on a compliant, variable human subject. Current robots lack reliable human-body-contact safety and fine alignment skills.

task: Attend to children's basic needs by feeding them, dressing them, or changing their diapers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single caregiving episode (e.g., one diaper change ~5 min, one feeding ~15 min, one dressing ~10 min); 15 min is a reasonable median across the mix.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (snaps, buttons, spoon-feeding), safe handling of a moving, fragile child, real-time behavioral adaptation, and high-stakes safety requirements. Far beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Test magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) equipment to ensure proper functioning and performance in accordance with specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full MRI QA/QC test session on one machine, including phantom scans, image analysis, and logging results. A trained MRI technologist or physicist typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a highly specialized MRI console, placing phantoms precisely, interpreting complex imaging data, and navigating a strong magnetic field environment. Current humanoids lack MRI-specific tool-use, software integration, and ferromagnetic safety compliance.

task: Assist physicians in performing ophthalmic procedures, including surgery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting through one ophthalmic procedure/surgery (e.g., cataract surgery). Typical case runs 20–60 min including prep, instrument hand-off, and post-op setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme sub-millimeter dexterity, sterile field compliance, real-time surgeon communication, instrument anticipation, and rapid adaptive response. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capabilities. Even specialized surgical robots (e.g., da Vinci) are teleoperated, not autonomous assistants.

task: Conduct visual field tests to measure field of vision.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient's bilateral visual field test. A skilled optometrist/technician typically spends 15–25 minutes setting up the perimeter, instructing the patient, running the test, and recording results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a specialized clinical perimeter device, precisely positioning a patient's head, providing verbal coaching, and interpreting results. Current humanoids lack clinical tool integration, patient interaction nuance, and medical-grade precision.

task: Bore holes for insertion of screws or dowels, by hand or using boring machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = boring a single hole (marking, positioning drill/bit, drilling, clearing debris). A competent worker completes this in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise positioning, tool gripping, and controlled force application. Robots struggle with accurate drill alignment, workpiece fixturing in varied environments, and switching between hand/machine boring methods. Perception of exact hole placement is challenging.

task: Repair or alter wooden furniture, cabinetry, fixtures, paneling, or other pieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing or altering one piece of furniture/fixture. A competent carpenter typically spends 30–90 min per piece depending on complexity; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly varied dexterous tool use (saws, chisels, sanders, clamps), force-controlled finishing, and adaptive problem-solving on non-standard damaged pieces. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and fine manipulation capability.

task: Adjust saw blades, using wrenches and rulers, or by turning handwheels or pressing pedals, levers, or panel buttons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a saw blade to a target setting (height/angle). A skilled operator uses wrenches, rulers, or controls to dial in the correct position in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (wrenches, rulers), reading scales/gauges, and operating varied controls (handwheels, pedals, levers, buttons). Perception of small measurements and precise force control exceed current humanoid capability. Very low success probability.

task: Clear machine jams, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing a single machine jam. A skilled worker diagnoses the jam, selects appropriate hand tools, and clears it, typically in 5–15 minutes depending on severity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing the jam cause, selecting the right hand tool, applying precise force in tight/occluded spaces, and verifying clearance. Current robots lack reliable tool selection, constrained-space dexterity, and fault-diagnosis needed here.

task: Conduct stress tests, using electrocardiograph (EKG) machines.
human_time_rationale: One stress test instance: prep patient, attach EKG leads, run treadmill/exercise protocol (~10–15 min), monitor, then recovery phase. Typical total ~45 min per patient.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise lead placement on human skin, real-time clinical judgment, patient communication, managing a treadmill, and emergency readiness. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, medical protocol compliance, and safety standards.

task: Observe distribution of paving material to adjust machine settings or material flow, and indicate low spots for workers to add material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = observing a fresh paving pass (~10–20 m stretch), assessing spread quality, signaling adjustments. Happens continuously but each judgment cycle takes ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time visual/tactile assessment of hot asphalt spread quality on active job site, machine setting adjustment via controls not built for humanoids, and reliable communication/signaling to workers — all in a dynamic, high-risk outdoor environment. Perception of low spots in dark, hot material is very difficult; machine interfacing and worker signaling further limit feasibility.

task: Repair or replace defective garment parts, such as pockets, zippers, snaps, buttons, and linings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/replacing a single garment part (e.g. one zipper or button). A skilled tailor/seamstress typically completes one such repair in 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity: threading needles, handling flexible fabric, sewing precise seams, aligning zippers/snaps. Current humanoids lack the finger precision, tactile feedback, and tool-use capability for garment-level needlework.

task: Install gas burners to convert furnaces from wood, coal, or oil.
human_time_rationale: One instance = converting one furnace to gas. A competent HVAC technician typically takes 2–5 hours including removal of old components, fitting the gas burner assembly, connecting gas lines, and testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires licensed gas-line work, complex tool use in tight spaces, torque-critical fittings, leak testing, and safety judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool generalization, and regulatory/safety autonomy limits.

task: Perform routine laboratory tests of blood samples for cholesterol level or glucose tolerance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one blood sample (label, pipette, load analyzer, run test, record result). A competent lab tech handles this in ~15–25 minutes including instrument interaction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting of micro-volumes, operating lab analyzers, reading digital interfaces, and maintaining sterile technique. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, lab-specific tool fluency, and contamination-control reliability needed for autonomous clinical lab work.

task: Fit or assist in fitting valves, couplings, or assemblies to tanks, pumps, or systems, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting one valve, coupling, or assembly to a tank/pump/system. A competent pipefitter or mechanic typically spends ~20–30 min per fitting, including alignment, threading, torquing, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torque application with hand tools (wrenches, pipe wrenches), thread engagement, spatial reasoning around pipes/tanks, and force feedback. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous tool-use, thread-finding, and the high-torque grip needed for pipe fittings in unstructured industrial environments.

task: Break or cut bricks, tiles, or blocks to size, using trowel edge, hammer, or power saw.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting/breaking a single brick, tile, or block to a required size. A skilled mason measures, scores, and cuts/breaks one unit in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, controlled force application with a hammer or power saw, and fine dexterity on hard irregular materials. Current humanoids lack the tool-handling precision, force control, and safety compliance needed for reliable autonomous cutting/breaking.

task: Disassemble and remove damaged or worn pipe.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling and removing one section of damaged/worn pipe (e.g., a typical residential or light-commercial pipe run). Includes shutoff, loosening fittings, cutting, and removal.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-torque tool use (pipe wrenches, cutters), awkward confined spaces, varied pipe materials/fittings, and situational judgment (e.g., residual pressure, leaks). Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use generalization.

task: Drive trucks to transport crews and equipment to work sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one trip driving a crew/equipment truck to a work site. Typical one-way drive including loading/unloading context is ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate trucks in real traffic. L4 AVs exist only on mapped routes with specific vehicle integrations. No 2026 general-purpose robot can drive a truck autonomously on public/work-site roads.

task: Administer topical ophthalmic or oral medications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = administering a single dose of eye drops or oral medication to one patient, including preparation, positioning, and administration — typically 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (eye drop placement), safe patient interaction, compliance handling, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack sub-millimeter ocular targeting, safe force control near eyes, and adaptive caregiving needed for uncooperative or vulnerable patients.

task: Select lumber to be used for patterns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting lumber for a single pattern batch. A skilled worker inspects, evaluates grain/defects, and picks suitable boards in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual/tactile inspection of grain, knots, warping, and moisture — highly unstructured judgment. Current robots lack the fine perceptual discrimination and domain expertise for acceptable quality selection.

task: Examine brickwork or structure to determine need for repair.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single wall section or structure (e.g., one facade or ~20 ft run). A competent mason visually scans, probes, and assesses in ~30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual and tactile assessment of cracks, spalling, mortar deterioration, and structural deformation across varied, unstructured surfaces. Current robots lack reliable fine-grained defect perception, expert reasoning, and safe navigation around outdoor/elevated structures.

task: Draw markings or pin appliques on fabric to obtain variations in design.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking or pinning a single garment/fabric piece. A skilled sewer typically spends 10–20 min measuring, marking, and pinning appliqués on one piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-fine dexterity to handle limp fabric, wield a marking tool precisely, and insert/close pins without tearing fabric. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control, compliant manipulation, and fabric-state perception needed.

task: Photograph objects for documentation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = photographing a single object for documentation: positioning, framing, adjusting lighting, and taking several shots. A competent human takes ~10 minutes per object.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can hold cameras and move, but precise framing, lighting adjustment, focus quality checks, and consistent composition for documentation-grade photos require fine perception and aesthetic judgment current humanoids lack reliably.

task: Erect electrical system components and barricades, and rig scaffolds, hoists, and shoring.
human_time_rationale: One instance = erecting a standard set of electrical components, barricades, and rigged scaffold/hoist/shoring at a single worksite setup; a competent crew member takes ~1.5 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy construction equipment, rigging hoists and shoring under load, erecting scaffolding at height, and handling large/varied electrical components — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, strength, and safety certification limits.

task: Guide workpieces against saws, saw over workpieces by hand, or operate automatic feeding devices to guide cuts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = guiding/feeding one workpiece through a saw cut; a competent operator typically completes this in ~5 min including positioning and safety checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled guiding against a dangerous spinning blade, workpiece alignment, and adaptive grip — far beyond current humanoid dexterity/safety thresholds. High injury risk limits autonomous deployment.

task: Clean or sterilize ophthalmic or surgical instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/sterilizing a full set of instruments for one surgical case. Includes manual scrubbing, loading autoclave or ultrasonic cleaner, cycle run, and unloading — typically 20–45 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to handle delicate micro-instruments, precise inspection for residue, and operation of specialized sterilization equipment. Current humanoids lack the tactile sensitivity, sterile-field compliance, and tool-use generalization needed.

task: Clean and renew steam traps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and renewing one steam trap. A competent pipefitter isolates, disassembles, cleans, inspects, replaces worn parts, and reinstalls one trap — typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating industrial environments, operating isolation valves, disassembling threaded pipe fittings with high torque, cleaning internal components, and reassembling — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and generalization capabilities.

task: Collect blood or tissue samples from patients, observing principles of asepsis to obtain blood sample.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one blood/tissue collection from a patient: prep, tourniquet, venipuncture, tube filling, labeling, disposal. A skilled phlebotomist completes this in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter needle placement into a moving vein, sterile field maintenance, patient interaction, and tactile feedback. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and safety thresholds; no commercial deployment exists.

task: Lubricate or clean machines, using wrenches, grease guns, or solvents.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lubricating or cleaning a single machine. A competent maintenance worker typically spends ~15–25 min locating fittings, applying grease/solvent, and wiping down one machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool switching (wrench, grease gun, solvent rag), precise fitting localization, and variable force application in cluttered industrial settings. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use generalization and dexterous grip changes needed here.

task: Bolt component parts together to form tower assemblies, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one tower section. Bolting multiple structural components together with hand tools typically takes a competent worker ~30–60 min per section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained high-torque fastening of large structural parts, precise bolt alignment, and sequential hand-tool use across varied positions/orientations. Current humanoids lack the force control, tool grip reliability, and generalization for this heavy structural assembly.

task: Remove drill bits from chucks after drilling holes, and insert bolts into chucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one drill bit from a chuck and inserting one bolt. A competent worker does this in ~1–3 minutes including loosening, swapping, and re-tightening.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of small chuck keys or keyless chucks, precise grip on small bits and bolts, and torque-controlled tightening. Current humanoids lack reliable fine finger dexterity and tool-use for such fiddly, force-sensitive operations.

task: Prepare prescribed solutions and administer local, intravenous, spinal, or other anesthetics, following specified methods and procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and administering a single anesthetic procedure (e.g., IV or spinal block). Includes solution prep, patient assessment, administration, and initial monitoring — ~45 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (IV cannulation, spinal needle placement), sterile technique, real-time patient physiological assessment, clinical judgment, and drug handling. Far beyond 2026 robot capability; patient safety stakes preclude deployment.

task: Measure and record lens power, using lensometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and recording power of a single lens. A skilled optician positions the lens, reads sphere/cylinder/axis values, and records them in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of a small optical lens on a lensometer's lens rest, reading a fine reticle/display, and recording values. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity and optical tool-use precision needed; high failure risk at lens positioning and instrument reading.

task: Measure corneal curvature with keratometers or ophthalmometers to aid in the diagnosis of conditions, such as astigmatism.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring both eyes of one patient. A skilled optometrist/ophthalmic tech typically takes 3–7 minutes including setup, alignment, and recording readings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise sub-millimeter alignment of a specialized optical instrument to a cooperative patient's eye, real-time gaze stabilization, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, instrument-specific integration, and patient-interaction reliability needed.

task: Clean and clear debris from culverts, catch basins, drop inlets, ditches, and other drain structures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one drain structure (culvert, catch basin, ditch section, etc.). A crew member typically spends 30–60 min removing debris, sediment, and blockages from a single structure.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/unstructured terrain navigation, confined-space entry, heavy debris removal (shoveling, vacuuming, jetting), and specialized equipment (vac trucks, jet rodders) a humanoid cannot operate. Highly variable environments, mud, and water make autonomous success near-impossible with 2026 robots.

task: Collect and store patterns and lumber.
human_time_rationale: One instance = gathering and storing a typical batch of patterns and lumber pieces in a workshop. A competent worker takes ~30 min to sort, carry, and stow items.
robot_success_rationale: Lumber is heavy, awkward, and varied in size; patterns are flat and floppy. Robots struggle with large/irregular objects, safe carrying, and navigating cluttered workshops. Perception of diverse item types and correct stowage adds difficulty.

task: Set up, clean, and maintain laboratory equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up, cleaning, and maintaining a standard piece of lab equipment (e.g., centrifuge, microscope, or glassware). A competent lab tech typically spends ~45 min per unit per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (handling fragile glassware, small fittings), domain-specific tool use, precise positioning, and quality inspection. Current humanoids lack the precision, lab-specific perception, and safe handling needed for delicate equipment.

task: Start machines, adjust controls, and make trial cuts to ensure that machinery is operating properly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting a machine, adjusting controls, and completing a trial cut. A competent machinist typically takes ~10–20 minutes for this setup verification cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial machinery (lathes, mills, CNC), interpreting analog/digital controls, assessing cut quality via tactile/visual feedback, and making nuanced adjustments — far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and dexterity.

task: Dig trenches or holes for installation of conduit or supports.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one trench or hole for conduit/support. A skilled worker with a shovel or small trencher typically takes 45–90 min depending on soil, depth, and length.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a trencher or repeatedly driving a shovel into varied soil — heavy-force tool use, outdoor unstructured terrain, and sustained high-torque manipulation far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Create a master pattern for each size within a range of garment sizes, using charts, drafting instruments, computers, or grading devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grading one garment style across a standard size range (e.g. XS–XXL). A skilled patternmaker typically spends 1.5–3 hours drafting/grading all sizes manually or via CAD.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires expert cognitive pattern-grading knowledge, precise instrument or CAD use, and fine dexterous drafting. No 2026 general-purpose humanoid can operate patternmaking software or physical drafting tools autonomously at professional quality.

task: Verify dimensions of completed patterns, using templates, straightedges, calipers, or protractors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = verifying dimensions of one completed pattern; worker selects tools, takes multiple measurements, and confirms conformance. Typically 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool selection, fine dexterous handling of calipers/protractors, and accurate reading of analog instruments. Current robots lack reliable fine manipulation and instrument-reading accuracy for quality-accepted inspection.

task: Mark identifying information such as colors or codes on patterns, parts, and templates to indicate assembly methods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking one pattern/part/template. A skilled worker reads assembly instructions and applies color codes or labels with a marker/pen in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine-motor pen/marker control on irregular surfaces, interpreting assembly diagrams, selecting correct codes, and placing precise legible marks. Current robots lack reliable dexterous writing and contextual assembly comprehension.

task: Cut grooves, bevels, or miters, saw curved or irregular designs, and sever or shape metals, according to specifications or work orders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single cut operation (groove, bevel, miter, or curve) on one workpiece per work order. A competent machinist/fabricator takes ~15–35 min including setup, measurement, and cutting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool-use (saws, grinders, torches), fine dexterity for setup and measurement, interpreting blueprints/work orders, and safe handling of heavy/sharp metal stock. Far beyond current humanoid general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Adjust machine tables or cutting devices and set controls on machines to produce specified cuts or operations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a single machine for a specified cut/operation, including adjusting the table, positioning cutting devices, and dialing in controls. A skilled machinist typically takes 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor adjustment of mechanical stops, dials, and levers on varied machines, reading specifications, and validating settings. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force feedback, and domain generalization for reliable autonomous machine setup.

task: Draw outlines of pattern parts by adapting or copying existing patterns, or by drafting new patterns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drafting or adapting a single garment pattern piece. A skilled patternmaker takes ~30–60 min per piece, including measuring, marking, and cutting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor drawing/tracing on paper or fabric, interpreting complex 2D/3D spatial relationships, and creative adaptation judgment. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, pen-control accuracy, and domain reasoning needed.

task: Assess abnormalities of color vision, such as amblyopia.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient screening session using Ishihara plates or similar tests; a competent clinician administers, observes, and records results in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced patient interaction, verbal instruction, interpreting subjective responses, and clinical judgment. Humanoids lack trained clinical perception, patient-rapport capability, and cannot reliably administer or interpret vision assessments autonomously.

task: Conduct tests, such as the Amsler Grid test, to measure central visual field used in the early diagnosis of macular degeneration, glaucoma, or diseases of the eye.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one visual field test session per patient including setup, instructions, test execution, and recording. Typically 10–20 minutes for a trained ophthalmic technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires patient communication, precise clinical instruction, real-time adaptive interaction, and medical-grade interpretation. General-purpose humanoids lack clinical dialogue capability, fine perceptual calibration, and regulatory clearance for autonomous medical diagnostics.

task: Position bolting machines, and insert drill bits into chucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning one bolting machine and inserting one drill bit into its chuck. A skilled worker does this quickly in a few minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous manipulation to tighten a chuck, accurate positioning of heavy industrial equipment, and tool-use generalization. Current humanoids lack reliable fine motor control and force feedback for chuck insertion at acceptable quality.

task: Examine finished workpieces for smoothness, shape, angle, depth-of-cut, or conformity to specifications and verify dimensions, visually and using hands, rules, calipers, templates, or gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one finished workpiece. Skilled operator visually checks, hand-feels, and measures with calipers/templates/gauges; typically 5–12 min per part.
