robot_success_rationale: Robots can do basic vision inspection but multi-modal measurement (calipers, gauges, templates, tactile smoothness) requires fine dexterity, precise tool manipulation, and adaptive reasoning that 2026 humanoids handle poorly on varied workpieces.

task: Troubleshoot technical issues related to magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scanner or peripheral equipment, such as monitors or coils.
human_time_rationale: One troubleshooting instance = diagnosing and resolving a single fault (e.g., coil connectivity error, monitor failure). A competent MRI tech/engineer typically spends 30–60 min per incident.
robot_success_rationale: Requires deep domain expertise, reading error logs on specialized interfaces, interpreting abstract diagnostics, swapping sensitive RF coils, and clinical judgment. Far beyond current general-purpose robot cognitive and dexterous capabilities.

task: Organize and assemble routine or specialty surgical instrument trays or other sterilized supplies, filling special requests as needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one surgical instrument tray. A competent sterile processing tech typically takes 15–25 minutes per tray, including identifying, counting, arranging, and wrapping instruments.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying dozens of specialized surgical instruments by type/condition, fine dexterous placement in precise tray layouts, handling delicate/sharp instruments safely, reading special requests, and working in a sterile environment — far beyond 2026 robot generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Inspect markers to verify accurate installation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single marker (e.g., road sign, survey stake, or boundary marker) for correct placement, alignment, and condition. A competent worker spends ~3 minutes per marker.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can use vision to check marker presence and rough alignment, but precise verification (depth, angle, regulatory spec compliance) requires fine perception and contextual judgment. Outdoor/uneven terrain and varied marker types reduce reliability.

task: Install and adjust blades, cutterheads, boring-bits, or sanding-belts, using hand tools and rules.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing and adjusting a single blade/cutterhead/bit/belt on one machine. A skilled woodworker or machinist typically takes 10–20 min including measurement and fine adjustment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool-use (wrenches, rules), precise torque and alignment to tight tolerances, and swapping varied fastener types. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation, tactile feedback, and calibration accuracy needed for safe, accepted results.

task: Count, sort, or stack finished workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = counting/sorting/stacking a typical batch of ~20–50 finished workpieces at a workstation; a competent worker completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of uniform parts is well within 2026 capability (~70–80%), but counting accuracy, varied part geometries, precise stacking alignment, and generalization across workpiece types reduce reliability meaningfully.

task: Mix or blend ingredients, according to recipes, using a paddle or an agitator, or by controlling vats that heat and mix ingredients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one batch mixed per recipe. Includes gathering ingredients, measuring, loading, operating mixer/vat, and monitoring until blend is complete (~15–25 min typical).
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measuring, pouring viscous/granular ingredients, reading recipe parameters, and adjusting vat controls — complex multi-step dexterity and perception. Unstructured kitchen/plant environments and varied container types push success very low for current humanoids.

task: Cut timber to the right size, and shape and trim parts of joints to ensure a snug fit, using hand tools, such as planes, chisels, or wood files.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting and fitting a single timber joint. A skilled carpenter typically spends 15–35 min measuring, sawing, planing, and test-fitting one joint.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained high-force tool use (planes, chisels), fine tactile feedback for snug-fit judgement, precise bimanual coordination, and iterative test-fitting — all far beyond current humanoid dexterity and force-control capability.

task: Flag motorists to warn them of obstacles or repair work ahead.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one work shift (e.g., 8 hours) of continuous flagging at a construction/repair site, the standard unit a traffic-control manager would measure.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor unstructured terrain, real-time traffic judgment, clear communicative gestures visible to drivers, legal/safety accountability, and weather resilience. Current humanoids lack the situational awareness, reliable outdoor mobility, and traffic-law compliance needed.

task: Push or hold workpieces against, under, or through cutting, boring, or shaping mechanisms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = feeding/holding one workpiece through a cutting, boring, or shaping operation. A competent operator typically completes this in ~5 minutes including positioning, feeding, and clearing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled feeding against active machinery, real-time tactile feedback, and safe proximity to hazardous equipment. Current humanoids lack reliable force modulation, tool-specific gripping fixtures, and safety-rated machine-proximity operation.

task: Set up and tear down equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up AND tearing down a typical set of equipment (e.g., AV gear, folding tables, event staging). A competent worker completes both phases in ~45 min combined.
robot_success_rationale: Highly variable equipment types, cable management, fasteners, and spatial configuration require strong generalization and dexterity. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use, connector insertion, and unstructured multi-step planning at this scale.

task: Remove and replace worn parts, bits, belts, sandpaper, or shaping tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing a single worn part/bit/belt/sandpaper on a tool or machine. A competent worker typically completes this in ~5 minutes including removal and refitting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to unfasten, align, and secure small or irregular parts (bits, belts, sandpaper). Varied tool types and fastener styles demand strong generalization. Current humanoids lack reliable precision for such multi-step tool-change tasks.

task: Drive vehicles over specified routes or to specified destinations according to time schedules, complying with traffic regulations to ensure that passengers have a smooth and safe ride.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one scheduled route trip (e.g. bus run or transit leg), typically 30–60 min including stops for passenger boarding/alighting.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate passenger vehicles in live traffic. L4 AVs exist but are narrow-route, mapped-only systems — not general-purpose humanoid robots. No current humanoid can drive, manage schedules, and ensure passenger safety simultaneously.

task: Repair broken or damaged patterns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing one broken/damaged pattern (e.g., fabric, stencil, template). A competent worker assesses damage, patches or re-stitches, and verifies alignment in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (needle, thread, or adhesive), nuanced visual inspection of damage, pattern-matching alignment, and material handling of flexible/irregular pieces — all far beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Observe gauges and thermometers to determine if the mixing chamber temperature is within specified limits, and turn valves to control the temperature.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one check-and-adjust cycle: glance at gauges/thermometers, assess temperature, and turn valves as needed. A competent worker completes this in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Reading analog gauges/thermometers requires reliable visual perception; turning valves demands moderate dexterity and force control. Current humanoids struggle with precise analog readout interpretation and applying correct torque to varied valve types in an unstructured industrial setting.

task: Assist physicians in performing ophthalmic procedures, including surgery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting through one ophthalmic procedure/surgery (e.g., cataract surgery). Typical case runs 20–60 min including setup, instrument passing, and patient support.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme sub-millimeter dexterity, anticipating surgeon needs, sterile field awareness, and real-time adaptation in an unstructured OR. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities; even surgical robots require full human control.

task: Serve meals or snacks in accordance with nutritional guidelines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and serving a single meal or snack to one person/table, including portioning, plating, and delivery. ~15 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires menu knowledge, nutritional compliance judgment, food portioning, plating, and safe delivery to a recipient. Current humanoids lack reliable food handling dexterity, nutritional reasoning, and safe human-interaction in unstructured serving contexts.

task: Place and remove snow fences used to prevent the accumulation of drifting snow on highways.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing or removing a 100–150 ft snow fence section roadside; worker drives stakes, unrolls/secures mesh, or reverses steps for removal. ~60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor unstructured terrain, driving metal stakes into frozen ground (high-force tool use), unrolling/securing long flexible mesh, and working near traffic — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in dexterity, force, and outdoor generalization.

task: Fill processing or cooking containers, such as kettles, rotating cookers, pressure cookers, or vats, with ingredients, by opening valves, by starting pumps or injectors, or by hand.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling one container. Includes measuring/sourcing ingredients, opening valves or starting pumps, and verifying fill level. Typical in industrial food processing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating and operating varied valve types, pump controls, and switches in unstructured industrial environments. Perception of fill levels, force-appropriate valve turning, and generalizing across equipment types remain challenging for current humanoids.

task: Blend compounds to form adhesive mixtures used for marker installation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one batch of adhesive mixture. A competent worker measures, combines, and blends compounds in ~20 minutes including setup and quality check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement of chemical compounds, careful pouring, mixing to exact ratios, and quality assessment. Fine dexterous handling of containers/tools, chemical perception, and process control are well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Perform fluorescein angiography of the eye.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full fluorescein angiography session: IV dye injection, patient positioning, image acquisition, and initial review. Typically 20–40 minutes for a competent ophthalmic technician/physician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise IV cannulation, sterile technique, operating a slit-lamp angiography camera aligned to a cooperative patient's eye, and real-time clinical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, medical tool-use, and safety standards.

task: Conduct ocular motility tests to measure function of eye muscles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full ocular motility exam on one patient, covering cardinal gaze positions, smooth pursuit, saccades, and cover test. A competent clinician completes this in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise clinical perception (detecting subtle nystagmus, diplopia, muscle weakness), nuanced patient interaction, fine instrument control, and medical judgment. Current humanoid robots lack clinical-grade ophthalmic perception and patient communication capability.

task: Homogenize or pasteurize material to prevent separation or to obtain prescribed butterfat content, using a homogenizing device.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one batch processed through a homogenizer/pasteurizer. Includes setup, loading material, running the device, and monitoring — ~15–25 min for a competent dairy/food worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial valves, gauges, and control panels; connecting hoses under pressure; and monitoring output quality. Current humanoid robots lack reliable tool-use and process-control judgment for dairy equipment. Perception and fine manipulation of plumbing/controls are key bottlenecks.

task: Disassemble defective electrical equipment, replace defective or worn parts, and reassemble equipment, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassemble, replace parts, and reassemble one piece of electrical equipment (e.g., a motor, appliance, or control unit). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity with varied hand tools, fault diagnosis/perception to identify defective parts, and adaptive manipulation of diverse hardware. Current humanoids lack reliable tool use, fault identification, and disassembly/reassembly generalization at acceptable quality.

task: Immerse pipe in chemical solution to remove dirt, oil, and scale.
human_time_rationale: One instance = immersing a single pipe (or batch) in a chemical bath. Includes positioning, submerging, and monitoring soak time (~10–15 min total).
robot_success_rationale: Grasping and submerging a rigid pipe is feasible, but handling chemical baths (hazardous liquids, splash risk), maneuvering long/heavy pipes, and confirming cleanliness quality pose significant perception and safety challenges for current humanoids.

task: Thread conduit ends, connect couplings, and fabricate and secure conduit support brackets, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one conduit segment: thread ends, attach couplings, fabricate and mount one support bracket. A skilled electrician takes ~30–60 min depending on conduit size and location.
robot_success_rationale: Requires forceful threading with a die stock, precise torque on couplings, metal fabrication of custom brackets, and overhead/confined-space mounting — far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity, force control, and tool-use generalization.

task: Set up, program, operate, or tend computerized or manual woodworking machines, such as drill presses, lathes, shapers, routers, sanders, planers, or wood-nailing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and operating a woodworking machine for a typical production run (e.g., routing or sanding a batch of parts). Includes setup, programming/adjustment, operation, and basic teardown.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise machine setup, bit/blade changes, workpiece feeding, and quality judgments around dangerous spinning tools. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, safety awareness, and tool-operation generalization needed for autonomous woodworking machinery operation.

task: Collect ophthalmic measurements or other diagnostic information, using ultrasound equipment, such as A-scan ultrasound biometry or B-scan ultrasonography equipment.
human_time_rationale: One patient eye exam session; skilled ophthalmic tech takes ~10–20 min including probe setup, patient positioning, and data capture.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise placement of ultrasound probe on or near the cornea, patient communication/cooperation, real-time adaptive dexterity, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack medical-grade fine dexterity, sterile technique, and patient-interaction capability for ophthalmic contact procedures.

task: Measure and mark stock for cuts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and marking one piece of stock for a cut. A skilled worker uses a tape measure, square, and pencil/marker — typically 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool use (tape measure, square, marker), fine motor control for accurate marking, and spatial reasoning on varied stock shapes/sizes. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-dexterous tool manipulation and measurement accuracy at trade standards.

task: Operate tamping machines or manually roll surfaces to compact earth fills, foundation forms, and finished road materials, according to grade specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = compacting a standard road section or fill area. A competent operator typically spends 60–120 minutes per section, including setup, passes, and grade checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy tamping/roller machinery on outdoor terrain, reading grade specs, and making real-time adjustments. Humanoid robots cannot operate such vehicles or equipment autonomously at any acceptable quality level in 2026.

task: Operate panelboards of saw or conveyor systems to move stock through processes or to cut stock to specified dimensions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one cut/batch order: reading specs, configuring panelboard controls, running stock through saw/conveyor, and verifying output dimensions. ~15 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large industrial panelboards/control consoles, reading dimensional specs, and monitoring stock flow. Humanoids lack the trained perception, system integration, and safe operation capability for industrial saw/conveyor control panels in live environments.

task: Take anatomical or functional ocular measurements, such as axial length measurements, of the eye or surrounding tissue.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring both eyes of one patient. A skilled ophthalmic technician typically completes axial length/biometry measurements per patient in ~8–12 minutes including setup, alignment, and data recording.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter probe alignment to a cooperative patient's eye, fine dexterous instrument manipulation, patient communication/compliance management, and sterile technique. Current humanoids lack the precision, safety assurance, and medical instrument integration needed.

task: Conduct binocular disparity tests to assess depth perception.
human_time_rationale: One instance = conducting a full binocular disparity test session on one patient/subject, including setup, test administration, and recording results. Typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise instrument handling (stereo optotype cards, Randot/TNO tests), patient communication, behavioral interpretation, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, clinical protocol knowledge, and patient interaction capability needed.

task: Select and measure or weigh ingredients, using English or metric measures and balance scales.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring/weighing a single recipe's set of ingredients. A competent human typically takes ~5 minutes to gather, measure, and set out all components.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (scooping, leveling, pouring accurately), reading scale displays, handling varied containers and utensils, and adapting to different ingredient textures. Current humanoids lack reliable precision for consistent accurate measurement and tool-use generalization.

task: Conduct tonometry or tonography tests to measure intraocular pressure.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient measurement session. A trained clinician takes ~5–15 min including setup, patient positioning, instrument calibration, and result recording.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter precision contact or air-puff alignment on a live human eye, patient communication/cooperation management, sterile technique, and clinical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and safety tolerances for ocular contact procedures.

task: Set up, operate, and tend equipment that cooks, mixes, blends, or processes ingredients in the manufacturing of food products, according to formulas or recipes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one production batch run (setup, operation, and monitoring). A typical batch cycle in food manufacturing takes ~45–90 min; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial machinery, reading control panels, adjusting parameters, and responding to process deviations. Current humanoids lack the tool-use, generalization, and process-knowledge integration needed for unsupervised industrial food equipment operation.

task: Finish completed products or models with shellac, lacquer, wax, or paint.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing one product/model. Includes surface prep, applying finish coat(s), and allowing cure/dry time between coats. ~45 min median for a typical small-to-medium item.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, consistent brush/spray strokes, fine force control, surface-quality judgment, and tool switching (brush, cloth, sprayer). Current humanoids lack the dexterous painting skill and quality perception needed for acceptable finish results.

task: Install copper-clad ground rods, using a manual post driver.
human_time_rationale: One instance = driving a single 8-ft copper-clad ground rod to full depth. A skilled electrician/laborer using a manual post driver typically takes 15–25 minutes depending on soil.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained high-force repetitive overhead striking with a heavy manual post driver, precise rod alignment, and adapting to soil resistance. Current humanoids lack the force output, stability, and coordination for this strenuous outdoor task.

task: Press switches and turn knobs to start, adjust, and regulate equipment, such as beaters, extruders, discharge pipes, and salt pumps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single startup/adjustment cycle on one machine, pressing switches and turning knobs to reach target settings; typically 2–5 minutes for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Industrial panel controls (toggle switches, rotary knobs, push buttons) are spatially dense and require precise finger force and positioning. Current humanoids struggle with small knob turning, reading analog gauges to confirm set points, and navigating cluttered control panels reliably.

task: Sharpen blades, or replace defective or worn blades or bands, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sharpening or replacing a single blade/band. A competent worker uses hand tools to complete this in ~10–20 minutes depending on blade type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity with hand tools (files, wrenches, grinding stones), proper force feedback, and blade alignment judgment. Current humanoid robots lack the fine manipulation and tactile feedback needed for safe, quality blade work.

task: Place products on carts or conveyors to transfer them to the next stage of processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing a single product onto a cart or conveyor. A competent worker does this in roughly 30–90 seconds depending on product size/weight.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of industrial products onto flat conveyors/carts is within current humanoid capability, especially for regular-shaped items. Success drops for heavy, awkward, or delicate products and cluttered environments.

task: Maintain ophthalmic instruments or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting, cleaning, and maintaining a set of ophthalmic instruments (e.g., slit lamp, tonometer, phoropter) in a single session; ~30 min for a trained technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterity to handle delicate optical components, specialized domain knowledge, precise calibration routines, and use of specialized tools. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability without task-specific programming and hardware.

task: Perform roadside landscaping work, such as clearing weeds and brush, and planting and trimming trees.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing/trimming a standard roadside segment (~50–100m). A crew worker takes ~90 min combining weeding, brush clearing, and tree trimming along that stretch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor unstructured terrain, heavy tool use (trimmers, shovels, chainsaws), robust vegetation manipulation, and long-duration autonomy. Current humanoids lack outdoor durability, tool-switching, and force-control for this environment.

task: Photograph patients' eye areas, using clinical photography techniques, to document retinal or corneal defects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = photographing one patient's eye area. Includes setup, positioning, patient cooperation, capturing multiple angles/images, and basic review. ~10–20 min is typical in a clinical setting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme precision aiming specialized ophthalmic equipment at a cooperative but unrestrained patient, fine dexterous camera control, clinical judgment on image quality, and patient communication. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Monitor sawing machines, adjusting speed and tension and clearing jams to ensure proper operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one monitoring shift cycle (~30 min of active oversight), including speed/tension adjustments and any jam clearance on a sawing machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading machine gauges, making precise mechanical adjustments (knobs, levers), and forcefully clearing jams in tight, hazardous spaces. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and domain-specific tool-use for reliable autonomous operation here.

task: Perform advanced ophthalmic procedures, including electrophysiological, electrophysical, or microbial procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full ophthalmic procedure session (e.g., ERG or corneal scraping). A competent ophthalmologist/technician typically takes 30–60 min per patient procedure.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity on delicate ocular tissue, sterile technique, real-time patient communication, clinical judgment, and specialized micro-instruments. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability; <5% anchor applies.

task: Feed stock through feed mechanisms or conveyors into planing, shaping, boring, mortising, or sanding machines to produce desired components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = feeding one piece of stock through a machine (e.g., a board through a planer). A competent operator positions, feeds, and retrieves a single workpiece in ~5 minutes including setup adjustments.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment and controlled feeding force on variable-sized raw stock near fast-moving blades. Perception of grain/orientation, safe machine interaction, and consistent grip force on rough lumber challenge current humanoid dexterity and safety compliance significantly.

task: Inspect stock for imperfections or to estimate grades or qualities of stock or workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single piece of stock/workpiece for defects or grading. A competent worker visually examines and possibly measures one item in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can image surfaces and run vision models, but nuanced grading (texture, subtle cracks, material feel, industry-standard quality tiers) requires fine perception and domain judgment that current general-purpose humanoids handle inconsistently.

task: Cool food product batches on slabs or in water-cooled kettles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cooling one batch on a slab or in a kettle; includes spreading/monitoring product and managing cooling process until target temp is reached, ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating industrial kitchen, manipulating large/heavy slabs or kettle controls, monitoring temperature, and handling semi-liquid food mass. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-use, and food-process generalization needed reliably.

task: Control hoists to remove parts or products from work stations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching rigging, operating hoist controls to lift and move one part/product from a workstation to a destination. Typical cycle is 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating overhead hoist controls, rigging attachment to irregular heavy parts, and spatial coordination. Humanoids lack reliable hoist-control manipulation, load-rigging dexterity, and safe heavy-lift autonomy in unstructured workstation environments.

task: Conduct tonometry or tonography tests to measure intraocular pressure.
human_time_rationale: One instance = both eyes of one patient. Skilled tech takes ~5–15 min including setup, anesthetic drops, instrument contact/positioning, and result logging.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter contact precision on a moving human eye, patient cooperation management, sterile technique, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, safety guarantees, and ophthalmic tool integration required.

task: Unclamp and remove finished workpieces from tables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unclamping and removing a single finished workpiece from a table. A competent worker locates clamps, releases them, and lifts/moves the part in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can reach and grasp objects on flat surfaces, but varied clamp types (toggle, screw, pneumatic) demand fine dexterity and tool-use generalization. Workpiece weight/shape variability further reduces reliability. Feasible in structured settings but inconsistent.

task: Listen for malfunction alarms, and shut down equipment and notify supervisors when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one alarm event: detect, assess, shut down equipment, and notify supervisor. A competent worker completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can detect audible/visual alarms via sensors and may press simple stop buttons, but assessing severity, navigating to correct equipment, operating shutdown controls, and communicating to supervisors reliably is beyond current autonomous capability.

task: Conduct visual field tests to measure field of vision.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient's visual field test (both eyes). A competent technician/optometrist typically takes 15–25 minutes including setup, per-eye testing, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized ophthalmic equipment (perimeter), precise patient alignment, verbal/non-verbal instruction, real-time monitoring of patient fixation and responses, and clinical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid capabilities in a medical context.

task: Observe and listen to equipment to detect possible malfunctions, such as leaks or plugging, and report malfunctions or undesirable tastes to supervisors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single inspection round of equipment in a processing area; a competent worker walks, listens, sniffs, and visually checks key units, taking ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can detect visual leaks and some audio anomalies, but lack olfactory sensing (taste/smell), struggle with unstructured plant environments, and cannot reliably generalize across varied equipment types or report nuanced sensory findings.

task: Match sample colors, applying knowledge of bleaching agent and dye properties, and types, construction, conditions, and colors of articles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = matching a single color sample to an article using bleach/dye knowledge. A skilled textile specialist typically spends 30–60 min testing, comparing, and adjusting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires deep domain knowledge of dye chemistry, bleaching agents, and fabric construction; nuanced color perception under varied lighting; and fine manual dye application. Current robots lack all three at acceptable quality.

task: Examine electrical units for loose connections and broken insulation and tighten connections, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and tightening connections on a single electrical unit (e.g., panel, junction box). A competent electrician takes ~15–25 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (screwdrivers, pliers) in tight, cluttered enclosures, visual inspection of insulation defects, and safe operation near live circuits. Current humanoids lack the precision, compliance, and electrical-safety awareness needed.

task: Put in padding and shaping materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inserting padding/shaping materials into a single garment, product, or package. A skilled worker completes this in roughly 10–20 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise insertion of soft, deformable materials into confined spaces — challenging for robot dexterity and perception. Foam, batting, and fabric padding deform unpredictably; current robots struggle with compliant material manipulation and alignment.

task: Raise, lower, or position equipment, tools, and materials, using hoist, hand line, or block and tackle.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rigging and operating a hoist/block-and-tackle to move one load to its target position; ~15 min covers setup, operation, and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires attaching rigging hardware, operating hoists or hand lines, and coordinating load positioning — tasks demanding high dexterity, force sensing, specialized tool use, and situational judgment that current humanoids cannot reliably perform.

task: Drill bolt holes into roofs at specified distances from ribs or adjacent bolts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling a single bolt hole in a roof at a specified location. A competent worker measures, positions, and drills one hole in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working overhead in confined/unstructured spaces (mine or building roofs), precise measurement from reference points, sustained force drilling upward, and tool handling. Current humanoids lack the stability, overhead dexterity, and confined-space mobility needed.

task: Coordinate or participate in the positioning of patients, using body stabilizing equipment or protective padding to provide appropriate exposure for the procedure or to protect against nerve damage or circulation impairment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning one patient for a single procedure. Competent OR/clinical staff typically spend 5–15 minutes assessing, coordinating, padding, and confirming final position.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced human body handling, clinical judgment on nerve/circulation risk, coordinated multi-person teamwork, and safe force application on a fragile patient — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, perception, and safety guarantees.

task: Incise tissue layers in lower extremities to harvest veins.
human_time_rationale: One instance = harvesting one vein from a lower extremity; a skilled surgeon/tech typically takes 30–60 min for incision, dissection, and extraction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sterile technique, real-time tissue differentiation, and surgical judgment far beyond any 2026 general-purpose humanoid. Risk of catastrophic tissue damage makes autonomous operation implausible.

task: Mount and bolt sawing blades or attachments to machine shafts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting and bolting a single blade/attachment to a machine shaft. A skilled worker loosens, seats, and torques fasteners in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of blade onto shaft, controlled torque on bolts, and working near sharp/heavy tooling. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, force feedback, and tool-use reliability needed for safe, accurate fastening in unstructured shop environments.

task: Attach and adjust guides, stops, clamps, chucks, or feed mechanisms, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = attaching and adjusting one setup of guides/stops/clamps/chucks/feed mechanisms on a machine. A skilled machinist or operator typically takes 5–15 minutes per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (wrenches, screwdrivers), precise spatial positioning of components, and tactile feedback to confirm proper tension/alignment. Current humanoids lack the precision and tool-use generalization needed for reliable setup work.

task: Observe gauges, dials, and product characteristics, and adjust controls to maintain appropriate temperature, pressure, and flow of ingredients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring-and-adjustment cycle; a competent operator checks gauges, interprets readings, and makes control corrections roughly every 10–20 minutes during a production run.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading varied analog/digital gauges, interpreting product characteristics, and making precise multi-axis control adjustments. Current humanoids lack reliable gauge-reading perception, process-domain knowledge integration, and fine manipulation of industrial control panels in unstructured environments.

task: Examine, feel, and taste product samples during production to evaluate quality, color, texture, flavor, and bouquet, and document the results.
human_time_rationale: One instance = evaluating a single product sample: visual check, tactile assessment, tasting, and documenting results. A trained QC worker typically completes this in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Tasting and flavor/bouquet evaluation require chemical taste/smell sensors far beyond current humanoid robots. Visual and tactile inspection are partially feasible, but olfactory and gustatory sensing, plus nuanced quality judgment, remain unsolved in general-purpose robots.

task: Paint traffic control lines and place pavement traffic messages, by hand or using machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = painting/placing one standard road marking segment or pavement message (e.g., a crosswalk, stop bar, or "STOP" legend). A competent crew completes this in ~60 minutes including setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized road-marking machinery or precision hand tools on live/outdoor roadways, precise geo-positioning, and durable paint application. No current humanoid robot can operate road-striping vehicles or safely work in traffic environments autonomously.

task: Connect physiological leads to physiological acquisition control (PAC) units.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting a full set of physiological leads (e.g., ECG/EEG leads) to a PAC unit. A trained technician takes ~3–7 minutes to route and snap/clip connectors correctly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of small, keyed connectors with precise alignment, cable routing, and tactile feedback to confirm secure seating. Current humanoids lack the fingertip precision and connector-recognition for such fine medical-device assembly reliably.

task: Inspect, clean, and repair drainage systems, bridges, tunnels, and other structures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting, cleaning, and repairing a single structure (e.g., a drainage section or bridge span). A typical combined visit for a crew takes ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/subterranean mobility over uneven/wet terrain, operating heavy tools and specialized equipment, complex repair judgment, and multi-step sequencing. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Prepare and apply sterile wound dressings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and applying a single sterile dressing to one wound; a competent nurse/medic takes ~5–15 min including inspection, cleaning, and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile field maintenance, fine dexterity to open sterile packaging without contamination, wound assessment, precise placement on irregular/soft tissue, and secure fastening — all far beyond current humanoid capability at acceptable clinical quality.

task: Assist in the insertion, positioning, or suturing of closed-wound drainage systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting with placement and securing of a single closed-wound drain (e.g., Jackson-Pratt or Blake drain) during or after a surgical procedure, from setup to secured suture.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity for suturing, precise tactile feedback to avoid tissue damage, sterile technique, real-time anatomical judgment, and coordination with a surgical team — all far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Gather, arrange, or assemble instruments or supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = gathering/arranging a set of instruments or supplies for a single procedure/task. A competent worker typically takes ~10 min to collect, sort, and lay out items.
robot_success_rationale: Requires recognizing diverse instruments by type/label, navigating to storage locations, and precisely arranging items. Object recognition and pick-and-place are improving but varied instrument types, drawer navigation, and correct layout order challenge current robots.

task: Measure visual acuity, including near, distance, pinhole, or dynamic visual acuity, using appropriate tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full visual acuity exam (near, distance, pinhole, dynamic) on one patient; a competent optometrist/technician typically completes this battery in ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires administering standardized eye charts, occluding each eye precisely, interpreting verbal patient responses, handling a pinhole occluder, and adapting dynamically to patient feedback — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, clinical tool-use, and patient-interaction generalization.

task: Measure and record lens power, using lensometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and recording power for one lens (or pair). A trained optician takes ~2–4 minutes to position, focus, read, and log the lensometer result.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of a small lens on a lensometer eyepiece, reading an analog/digital optical display, and logging values. Fine dexterity, sub-mm positioning, and instrument-reading in a specialized optical tool are well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Match materials for color, grain, or texture, giving attention to knots or other features of the wood.
human_time_rationale: One instance = matching wood pieces for a single panel or component; a skilled worker spends ~5–10 min comparing color, grain, texture, and knot features across candidate pieces.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual aesthetics, expert pattern recognition of grain/knot subtleties, and subjective judgment. Current robots lack the trained perceptual models and aesthetic reasoning for craftsman-quality wood matching.

task: Rotate chucks to turn bolts and open expansion heads against rock formations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rotating chucks on one bolt/expansion head cycle in a rock formation. A competent driller takes ~5–10 min including positioning, torqueing, and confirming expansion.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized drilling/bolting machinery underground on uneven rock terrain. Humanoid robots cannot operate heavy drill rigs, apply controlled torque via industrial chucks, or safely navigate active mining/tunneling environments autonomously.

task: Apply oil to road surfaces, using sprayers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying oil to a typical road segment (~1 lane-mile). A competent operator drives/operates the sprayer truck and applies oil, taking roughly 60 minutes including setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a specialized sprayer truck on public roads. Humanoid robots cannot drive heavy road-maintenance vehicles in traffic, control onboard sprayer systems, or navigate outdoor road environments autonomously at required precision.

task: Conduct ocular motility tests to measure function of eye muscles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full ocular motility exam on one patient, covering all cardinal gaze directions and assessing extraocular muscle function; typically takes ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine clinical observation of subtle eye movement abnormalities, patient interaction/instruction, and expert medical interpretation. Current robots lack medical perception, fine neurological assessment, and safe patient-facing clinical judgment.

task: Dispose of waste material after completing work assignments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = gathering and disposing of waste from a single work assignment; a competent worker typically takes ~5 minutes to collect scraps and deposit them in appropriate bins/receptacles.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can grasp and carry common waste items and navigate to bins, but identifying/sorting varied waste types, opening bin lids, and handling irregular/flexible debris in unstructured post-work environments remains unreliable for current humanoids.

task: Assess skin integrity or other body conditions upon completion of the procedure to determine if damage has occurred from body positioning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing one patient post-procedure; a competent nurse systematically checks skin/pressure points in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced tactile and visual skin assessment (redness, blistering, blanching), clinical judgment, and patient communication. Current robots lack reliable medical-grade skin perception and contextual reasoning to meet clinical standards.

task: Maintain tools, vehicles, and equipment and keep parts and supplies in order.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a routine maintenance/organization session covering tools, a vehicle, and associated supplies; a competent worker typically spends ~45 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse sub-tasks: visual inspection, fluid checks, part sorting, tool organization, and vehicle interaction. Current humanoids lack reliable fault diagnosis, fine fluid-level reading, and safe vehicle-system interaction at acceptable quality.

task: Teach and demonstrate use of gymnastic and training equipment, such as trampolines and weights.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single instructional session teaching equipment use (e.g., trampoline or weights) to a student or small group; ~45 min is typical for a structured gym session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic full-body demonstration (e.g., jumping on trampoline, controlled weightlifting), real-time coaching, verbal instruction, and social responsiveness — far beyond current humanoid locomotion, balance, and pedagogy capabilities.

task: Select or prepare artistic media or related equipment or devices to accomplish therapy session objectives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing materials/equipment for a single therapy session. A competent art therapist spends ~10–20 min selecting, arranging, and readying media (paints, clay, brushes, canvas, etc.).
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical reasoning to match media to therapeutic objectives, nuanced social/professional judgment, fine dexterous setup of varied art supplies, and contextual interpretation of patient needs — well beyond 2026 robot generalization and cognitive capabilities.

task: Measure amount of body fat, using such equipment as hydrostatic scale, skinfold calipers, or tape measures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full body fat assessment on a single client, including setup, measurements (e.g., multiple skinfold sites or girth measurements), and recording results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise anatomical landmark identification, consistent caliper pressure on soft tissue, safe client interaction, and nuanced tactile feedback. Current robots lack the dexterity, social safety, and reliable soft-tissue perception needed for acceptable quality.

task: Tend auxiliary equipment used in the power distribution process.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single equipment tending round (checking, adjusting, monitoring auxiliary gear like transformers, switchgear, cooling systems). A competent worker typically takes ~45 min per round.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, operating specialized high-voltage controls, adjusting valves, and responding to fault conditions. Current humanoids lack the specialized tool-use, electrical safety protocols, and generalization for high-voltage auxiliary equipment.

task: Transport patients to operating room.
human_time_rationale: One instance = transferring one patient from their room/prep area to the operating room, including bed/gurney navigation through hospital corridors, handoffs, and elevator use.
robot_success_rationale: Requires pushing/steering a gurney through dynamic hospital corridors, communicating with staff, managing elevators, and safely handling a vulnerable patient. Humanoids lack reliable manipulation of large wheeled equipment, situational social awareness, and hospital-certified safety protocols.

task: Measure corneal thickness, using pachymeter or contact ultrasound methods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring both eyes of one patient. A skilled technician completes positioning, probe contact, and reading acquisition in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter precision probe placement on a live human cornea, real-time patient cooperation management, sterile technique, and delicate force control. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, ophthalmic tool integration, and patient-safety guarantees needed.

task: Patrol work areas to detect leaks or equipment malfunctions or to monitor operating conditions.
human_time_rationale: One patrol instance = a single walkthrough of a defined work area (e.g., a plant floor or pipeline section), typically 20–45 minutes for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate and use sensors (gas, thermal, audio) for leak detection on flat indoor terrain, but unstructured industrial environments, varied equipment layouts, and nuanced anomaly judgment (e.g., unusual sounds, smells, visual cues) challenge generalization and reliability significantly.

task: Operate refining machines to reduce the particle size of cooked batches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a cooked batch into a refining machine, setting parameters, running the cycle, and unloading. Typical industrial refining cycle runs 20–40 minutes of active operator involvement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial machinery with controls, gauges, and valves; loading viscous/bulk cooked material; monitoring consistency. Humanoids lack reliable heavy-machine interfacing, fine sensory feedback for process quality, and safe bulk material handling.

task: Make minor repairs, lubricate, and maintain equipment, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance/lubrication/repair job on one piece of equipment. A competent worker typically inspects, lubricates, and makes minor fixes in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires selecting correct tools, applying precise torque, navigating varied equipment geometry, and judging repair quality. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use dexterity, contextual diagnosis, and generalization across diverse machinery types.

task: Administer topical ophthalmic or oral medications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = administering a single dose to one patient (e.g., eye drops or handing oral medication). Includes preparation, administration, and brief confirmation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (instilling eye drops precisely into a moving eye), patient compliance management, sterile technique, and safe oral dosing. Far beyond current humanoid capability; risk of patient harm raises acceptance bar extremely high.

task: Add treating or neutralizing agents to products, and pump products through filters or centrifuges to remove impurities or to precipitate products.
human_time_rationale: One batch: adding treating agents, initiating pumping through filters/centrifuges, and monitoring until complete — typical in chemical plant operations.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating industrial pumps, valves, chemical dosing systems, and monitoring process parameters. Humanoid robots lack the specialized tool interfacing, chemical process knowledge, and industrial control integration needed for autonomous operation.

task: Prepare patient for exam by explaining procedure, transferring patient to ultrasound table, scrubbing skin and applying gel, and positioning patient properly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one patient for one ultrasound exam: explaining procedure (~2 min), transferring to table (~3 min), scrubbing & applying gel (~2 min), and positioning (~3 min).
robot_success_rationale: Requires safe human transfer (high dexterity, force control, liability), verbal communication, social trust-building, precise skin prep, and nuanced patient repositioning — all far beyond current humanoid capabilities in unstructured caregiving contexts.

task: Examine raw woodstock for defects and to ensure conformity to size and other specification standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single piece of raw woodstock. A competent worker visually scans, measures, and checks for defects in ~3 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can use vision and sensors for dimensional checks and surface defect detection, but identifying subtle grain defects, warping, or irregular knots in unstructured raw wood requires nuanced perception and generalization that current systems handle unreliably.

task: Manipulate controls to adjust or activate power distribution equipment or machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting or activating a single piece of power distribution equipment (e.g., throwing a breaker, turning a switch, adjusting a dial). A competent operator completes this in ~5 minutes including safety checks.
robot_success_rationale: Power distribution controls (rotary switches, breaker handles, key switches, panel buttons) are highly varied, often require precise force and positioning, and operate in industrial/utility environments with poor lighting and cluttered layouts. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-specific manipulation and safety-critical precision needed here.

task: Monitor operation of machines and make adjustments to correct problems and ensure conformance to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one monitoring cycle: observing machine outputs, diagnosing a deviation, and making a corrective adjustment. Competent operators typically spend ~15–25 min per intervention cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires integrating multi-modal perception (visual, auditory, tactile, gauges), diagnosing abstract faults, and executing precise control adjustments on varied machine interfaces. Current humanoids lack reliable fault diagnosis and fine-grained machine-control generalization.

task: Measure, weigh, and mix chemical ingredients, according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one batch per spec sheet. Competent technician measures, weighs, and mixes a set of chemical ingredients; ~20 min accounts for setup, precision weighing, and mixing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor dexterity (pipettes, balances, pourings), reading labels/displays, handling liquids and powders, and following spec-driven sequences. Current humanoids lack the precision and liquid-handling reliability needed.

task: Remove patient hair or disinfect incision sites to prepare patient for surgery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = prepping a single incision site or shaving a localized area. A surgical tech typically completes clipping/shaving and antiseptic scrub in ~5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity near sensitive skin, sterile technique, precise force control, anatomical awareness, and patient interaction. Far beyond current humanoid capability; risk of patient harm is a hard blocker.

task: Assess refractive condition of eyes, using retinoscope.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retinoscopy exam on one patient (both eyes). A skilled optometrist/ophthalmologist typically completes this in 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise handheld instrument control millimeters from a patient's eye, fine motor dexterity, real-time interpretation of light reflexes, patient cooperation management, and clinical judgment — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Measure visual acuity, including near, distance, pinhole, or dynamic visual acuity, using appropriate tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full visual acuity assessment (near, distance, pinhole, dynamic) for one patient; a competent optometrist/technician typically takes 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise instrument handling, patient communication, adaptive test sequencing, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterity for ophthalmic tools, reliable verbal interaction, and validated medical protocol compliance.

task: Clean, lubricate, or maintain equipment, such as generators, turbines, pumps, or compressors, to prevent failure or deterioration.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single preventive maintenance session on one piece of equipment (e.g., pump or compressor). Includes inspection, cleaning, and lubrication; ~30–60 min is typical for a skilled technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating industrial environments, identifying correct fittings/ports, applying lubricants with precise force, using specialized tools (grease guns, wrenches), and adapting to varied equipment layouts — well beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use generalization.

task: Collect and examine product samples during production to test them for quality, color, content, consistency, viscosity, acidity, or specific gravity.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a sample from a production line and running a standard battery of quality tests (color, viscosity, pH, etc.). A competent QC technician typically takes 10–20 minutes per sample batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a live production environment, manipulating sampling equipment, operating multiple analytical instruments (viscometer, pH meter, etc.), and interpreting multi-modal sensor readings. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, instrument-operation skills, and chemical-sensing integration needed end-to-end.

task: Trim wood parts according to specifications, using planes, chisels, or wood files or sanders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming a single wood part to spec. A skilled woodworker typically spends 10–20 min fitting, checking, and finishing one piece with hand tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled tool use (planes, chisels, files), iterative measurement, and fine tactile feedback. Current humanoids lack the dexterity and closed-loop precision needed for spec-accurate material removal.

task: Clean or sterilize ophthalmic or surgical instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/sterilizing a full set of instruments for one procedure. Includes manual scrubbing, loading autoclave or ultrasonic cleaner, and cycle time (~20–30 min total active+passive).
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle delicate micro-instruments, precise sequencing (manual scrub, ultrasonic, autoclave loading), sterility verification, and domain-specific protocols. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation and sterile-field compliance needed.

task: Trim lumber to straighten rough edges or remove defects, using circular saws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming one piece of lumber. A skilled sawyer inspects, positions, and runs one board through a circular saw in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise defect identification via vision, accurate board alignment, safe operation of a powered circular saw, and managing unwieldy long lumber — well beyond reliable autonomous capability for current humanoid robots.

task: Adjust controls to regulate temperature, pressure, feed, or flow of liquids or gases and times of prescribed reactions, according to knowledge of equipment and processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single adjustment cycle: reading gauges/displays, interpreting process state, and actuating the relevant controls. A competent operator typically completes this in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting process chemistry knowledge, and precisely actuating valves/dials in varied industrial layouts. Current humanoids lack the process-domain reasoning and reliable fine manipulation of industrial controls at acceptable safety standards.

task: Clean shop, work area, and machines, using solvent and rags.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a typical shop/work area and associated machines with solvent and rags; a competent worker takes ~30–60 min depending on shop size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating cluttered industrial shop floor, identifying grimy surfaces on varied machinery, applying solvent safely, and wiping with rags — complex unstructured environment, solvent handling, and fine wiping motions challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Change bed linens or make beds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stripping and remaking one bed with fresh linens. A competent worker (e.g., hotel housekeeper) typically takes 5–10 minutes per bed.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly coordinated bimanual dexterity to spread, tuck, and align flexible fabric over a mattress. Deformable linen manipulation and fine tucking remain well beyond current humanoid robots' reliable capability.

task: Place standby emergency electrical generators on line in emergencies and monitor the temperature, output, and lubrication of the system.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing a generator online during an emergency and monitoring it until stable. Includes startup checks, switching, and initial monitoring — typically 20–40 minutes for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial electrical switchgear, physical control panels, reading analog/digital gauges, and making dynamic decisions in high-stakes emergencies. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use for complex industrial controls, situational judgment, and safe electrical operation in unstructured emergency conditions.

task: Examine and test electrical power distribution machinery and equipment, using testing devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and testing a single piece of electrical distribution equipment (e.g., a transformer or switchgear panel). A competent electrician typically spends ~30–60 min including setup, testing, and logging results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise probe placement on live HV terminals, interpreting multimeters/oscilloscopes, navigating electrical panels, and making safety-critical judgments. Current humanoids lack fine dexterity, HV safety protocols, and the diagnostic cognition needed for autonomous success.

task: Test samples to determine viscosity, acidity, specific gravity, or degree of concentration, using test equipment such as viscometers, pH meters, or hydrometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single sample for one or more properties. Includes collecting sample, setting up instrument, running test, reading/recording result. ~10–20 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid handling (pipetting, pouring), fine dexterous instrument operation (pH probes, hydrometers), calibration steps, and reading analog/digital displays. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation and lab tool generalization needed.

task: Dump, pour, or load specified amounts of refined or unrefined materials into equipment or containers for further processing or storage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading/pouring a specified amount of material into one container or piece of equipment; a competent worker typically completes this in ~5 minutes including measuring and positioning.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle structured pick-and-pour tasks but struggle with variable material types (powders, liquids, bulk solids), precise metered amounts, unstructured environments, and switching between containers/equipment types. Perception of fill levels and spillage control remain limiting.

task: Assist with patient resuscitation during cardiac arrest or other life-threatening events.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one resuscitation event, from arrest recognition through ROSC or termination. Typical CPR/ACLS events last ~10–30 minutes; 20 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise chest compressions, airway management, defibrillator use, medication administration, real-time clinical judgment, and team coordination. Far beyond current humanoid generalization, dexterity, and medical decision-making capabilities.

task: Assist in volume replacement or autotransfusion techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and executing a single volume replacement or autotransfusion procedure for one patient, including equipment prep, monitoring, and completion. Typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile fluid/blood handling, IV/catheter manipulation, patient-specific clinical judgment, real-time vital sign interpretation, and fine dexterity in a dynamic medical environment — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Weld, repair, and fabricate equipment or machinery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one welding/repair/fabrication job on a piece of equipment. A typical job (setup, weld, inspect, finish) takes ~1–2 hours for a competent welder.
robot_success_rationale: Welding requires precise torch control, filler rod manipulation, heat management, and adaptive decision-making on irregular metal surfaces. General-purpose humanoids lack welding tool integration, sufficient dexterity, and the high-precision perception needed for quality welds. Specialized fixed welding robots exist but are not general-purpose humanoids.

task: Measure or weigh materials to be refined, mixed, transferred, stored, or otherwise processed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring/weighing a single batch or material lot. A competent worker locates the material, selects the correct tool, measures/weighs, and records the result in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick containers and operate simple scales, but reading analog gauges, handling diverse vessels, scooping/pouring accurately, and interpreting measurement tools in unstructured industrial settings remain unreliable for current humanoids.

task: Manipulate products, by hand or using machines, to separate, spread, knead, spin, cast, cut, pull, or roll products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one product batch or unit manipulated (e.g., cut, rolled, kneaded). A competent worker completes a typical unit in ~5–10 minutes depending on product type.
robot_success_rationale: Task spans highly varied operations (kneading dough, casting metal, spinning fiber, cutting). Robots handle simple pick-and-place well but struggle with deformable materials, force feedback, and generalization across such diverse manipulations. No single 2026 humanoid handles all reliably.

task: Assist members of surgical team with gowning or gloving.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one surgical team member into a sterile gown and gloves; a skilled scrub tech completes this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme precision in sterile field handling, fine dexterity to open gloves without contamination, dynamic cooperation with a moving human, and strict aseptic technique — all far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Drain equipment, and pump water or other solutions through to flush and clean tanks or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = draining one tank/piece of equipment and flushing it through. Includes setup, draining, pumping solution, and confirming clean. Typical cycle ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying and operating valves, hoses, pumps, and drain ports on varied industrial equipment. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use for fluid system connections, valve manipulation, and process verification in unstructured environments.

task: Pass instruments or supplies to surgeon during procedure.
human_time_rationale: One instance = passing a single instrument/supply to the surgeon. A scrub tech anticipates needs and hands off an item in ~1–2 minutes on average.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile field compliance, precise dexterous grip of delicate surgical instruments, real-time surgeon intent prediction, and fault-free reliability in a safety-critical OR environment — all far beyond 2026 frontier robots.

task: Cut or drill holes in walls or floors to accommodate the passage of pipes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting/drilling a single hole for pipe passage. A skilled tradesperson takes ~15–35 min including measuring, marking, selecting the right bit/tool, and completing the cut.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measuring, marking, selecting and operating heavy power tools (hammer drill, hole saw, reciprocating saw) with high force, in varied cramped construction environments. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and tool-use generalization.

task: Operate sterilizing devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading items, setting parameters, and starting one sterilization cycle. Prep and setup typically takes ~15 min; the cycle itself runs unattended.
robot_success_rationale: Requires loading varied instruments into autoclave/UV devices (moderate dexterity), reading displays, pressing controls, and verifying cycle completion. Current robots struggle with precise loading of irregular medical instruments, door sealing confirmation, and parameter verification in unstructured clinical settings.

task: Grade food products according to government regulations or according to type, color, bouquet, and moisture content.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grading a single food item/lot. A trained grader inspects type, color, bouquet, and moisture in ~2–4 minutes using sensory evaluation and instruments.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can use cameras/spectrometers for color and visual grading, but olfactory sensing (bouquet) is largely unsolved, moisture measurement requires specialized probes, and regulatory multi-factor judgment is complex for autonomous systems.

task: Open and close valves and switches in sequence to start or shut down auxiliary units.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full start/shutdown sequence for one auxiliary unit, involving ~5–15 valve/switch steps following a checklist; typically 10–15 min for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating industrial environments, identifying diverse valve types (gate, ball, butterfly), applying precise torque, reading gauges, and following a strict sequence. Current robots lack reliable industrial valve manipulation, contextual sequencing, and fault detection.

task: Assist in applying casts, splints, braces, or similar devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying a single cast, splint, brace, or similar device to one patient. A competent medical assistant or technician typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous manipulation on a compliant, pain-sensitive human body, fine tactile feedback for pressure control, patient-specific anatomy adaptation, and sterile/clinical tool use — all far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Measure and record food and liquid intake or urinary and fecal output, reporting changes to medical or nursing staff.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and recording intake/output for one patient at one observation point: reading volumes, logging values, and noting/reporting changes. Typically 5–10 minutes per episode.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fluid volume estimation from irregular containers, reading patient-specific charts, contextual judgment on "reportable" changes, and reliable verbal/written communication with clinical staff. Current robots lack clinical-grade perception, EMR integration, and nuanced reporting judgment.

task: Force bolts into holes, using hydraulic mechanisms of self-propelled bolting machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = forcing one bolt into one hole. Operator positions self-propelled machine, aligns hydraulic mechanism, and actuates press — roughly 5–10 min per bolt including repositioning.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a self-propelled heavy hydraulic bolting machine — a specialized vehicle a humanoid cannot drive or control. Precise hole alignment under high force loads also demands task-specific perception and actuation far beyond current general-purpose robots.

task: Monitor and maintain aseptic technique throughout procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = monitoring and maintaining aseptic technique during one procedure (e.g., a surgical prep or sterile lab process), typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires continuous sterile field awareness, fine dexterous handling of sterile instruments, real-time contamination detection, and adaptive response. Far beyond current humanoid robots' perception, dexterity, and sterility compliance capabilities.

task: Provide physical support to assist patients to perform daily living activities, such as getting out of bed, bathing, dressing, using the toilet, standing, walking, or exercising.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one patient through a single ADL session (e.g., bed-to-chair transfer + basic hygiene). Competent caregiver typically takes 20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Unstructured caregiving requires safe physical contact, force modulation, adaptive handling of fragile/unpredictable human bodies, and high social awareness. Current humanoids lack the safety certification, tactile sensitivity, and generalization for real patient ADL support.

task: Operate gluing machines to glue pieces of wood together, or to press and affix wood veneer to wood surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one gluing/pressing cycle (applying glue, positioning pieces, loading into press, and waiting for initial set). A competent woodworker completes this in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise glue application, alignment of wood pieces/veneer, and operating industrial press controls. Unstructured material handling, irregular surfaces, and machine interfacing exceed current humanoid dexterity and generalization reliably.

task: Start or stop generators, auxiliary pumping equipment, turbines, or other power plant equipment as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting/stopping one piece of power plant equipment. Operator checks gauges, follows checklist, confirms system status — typically 10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Power plant controls involve specialized panels, valves, and safety interlocks in high-risk industrial environments. Humanoids cannot reliably interpret custom HMI displays, operate specialized controls, or make safety-critical judgments autonomously in 2026.

task: Position and clamp stock on tables, conveyors, or carriages, using hoists, guides, stops, dogs, wedges, or wrenches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning and clamping a single piece of stock on a machine table/conveyor/carriage. A skilled machinist/operator typically takes 5–12 minutes depending on stock size and clamping method.
robot_success_rationale: Requires bimanual dexterity to handle varied stock shapes, precise alignment, and torquing wrenches/wedges/dogs with correct force. Generalization across irregular stock, multiple tool types, and tight positional tolerances exceeds current humanoid capability reliably.

task: Insert or remove urinary bladder catheters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inserting or removing a single urinary catheter. A competent nurse/clinician typically takes 10–20 minutes including sterile field setup, insertion/removal, and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity in a sensitive anatomical area, strict sterile technique, real-time tactile feedback, and patient safety awareness. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability; even research systems cannot perform clinical procedures safely.

task: Draw samples of products at specified stages so that analyses can be performed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drawing one product sample at one stage. Includes locating sample point, using correct tool/container, labeling, and handing off. Typically 5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to correct sample point, handling specialized sampling tools (valves, spigots, probes), using proper containers, and labeling accurately. Varied equipment and hygiene/safety requirements limit generalization severely.

task: Record height or weight of patients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and recording height/weight for one patient, including positioning, reading the scale/stadiometer, and logging the result. Typically takes 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires guiding/positioning a patient (unstructured caregiving), operating a scale or stadiometer, reading analog/digital displays, and logging data in an EHR. Patient variability, safety, and tool interfacing are major barriers for current humanoids.

task: Transport specimens, laboratory items, or pharmacy items, ensuring proper documentation and delivery to authorized personnel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one delivery trip (pick up item, transport, hand off to authorized recipient, document). Typical hospital/lab courier run takes ~10–20 minutes end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Navigating hospital corridors is plausible, but chain-of-custody documentation, identity verification of authorized recipients, elevator/door use, and secure handoff in cluttered, dynamic environments challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Assist nurses or physicians in the operation of medical equipment or provision of patient care.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single care-assistance episode (e.g., repositioning a patient, fetching/handing equipment, or supporting a procedure). Median across common nursing-assist acts is ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for medical devices, safe physical contact with fragile patients, adaptive response to clinical context, and tight human collaboration — all far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability. Safety constraints alone effectively prohibit autonomous operation.

task: Undress, wash, and dress patients who are unable to do so for themselves.
