human_time_rationale: One instance = undressing, washing, and dressing one patient. A competent caregiver typically takes 20–30 minutes for the full sequence.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme dexterity (buttons, zippers, limb manipulation), safe force control around a fragile human body, dynamic body positioning, and nuanced social/safety judgment — all far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Test food product samples for moisture content, acidity level, specific gravity, or butter-fat content, and continue processing until desired levels are reached.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single food sample for one or more parameters and adjusting process. Includes sampling, instrument setup, measurement, and recording; ~15–25 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting, operating specialized lab instruments (titrators, Babcock centrifuges, hydrometers), interpreting analogue/digital readouts, and adjusting a food process — far beyond current general-purpose robot dexterity and tool-use generalization.

task: Set mill gauges to specified fineness of grind.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a mill's gauge to a target grind setting. A competent operator reads specs, locates the adjustment mechanism, and sets/verifies the gauge in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, fine rotary or linear adjustment of mill controls, and verifying output fineness. Precise force-controlled manipulation of industrial controls and feedback interpretation remain very challenging for current humanoids.

task: Inspect images for quality, using magnetic resonance scanner equipment and laser camera.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one item/sample using MRI scanner and laser camera, including setup, scan acquisition, and image quality review. Typically 15–25 minutes per item.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized MRI and laser camera equipment with precise controls, interpreting complex imaging outputs for quality, and making expert judgments. Current humanoids lack the specialized tool interfaces, software integration, and perceptual expertise needed.

task: Clean or sterilize tanks, screens, inflow pipes, production areas, or equipment, using hoses, brushes, scrapers, or chemical solutions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/sterilizing one piece of equipment (e.g., a tank or screen section). A competent worker spends ~30–60 min including setup, scrubbing, chemical application, rinsing, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating confined/irregular industrial spaces, manipulating hoses, brushes, and scrapers with force, and handling chemical solutions. Current humanoids lack robust tool-use in unstructured industrial wet environments and struggle with confined tank interiors.

task: Remove full containers from discharge outlets and replace them with empty containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = swapping one full container for an empty one at a single discharge outlet; involves lifting, repositioning, and securing. Typical time ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires detecting a full container, grasping and lifting a potentially heavy/irregular load, precise repositioning under an outlet, and placing an empty container correctly. Weight variability, outlet geometry, and safety constraints challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Carry messages or documents between departments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one delivery trip between two departments in a typical office building; includes picking up, walking, and handing off the item. ~5–10 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids can navigate indoor corridors and carry objects, but unstructured office environments, elevator operation, door-opening, and socially acceptable hand-off to a human remain unreliable at ~2026 capability levels.

task: Test samples of materials or products to ensure compliance with specifications, using test equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single sample with appropriate equipment; includes setup, running the test, and recording results. Typical lab/QC test takes ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise equipment handling, fine dexterous manipulation (calibration, connections, sample loading), reading varied instrument displays, and interpreting results. Current robots lack reliable fine dexterity and generalization across diverse test setups.

task: Flush or clean equipment, using steam hoses or mechanical reamers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one piece of equipment. A competent worker spends ~15–25 min including setup, hosing/reaming, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting/operating industrial steam hoses or mechanical reamers, managing high-pressure/high-temperature equipment, navigating industrial environments, and judgment about cleanliness. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-use, and safety autonomy for this specialized industrial task.

task: Control or maintain auxiliary equipment, such as pumps, fans, compressors, condensers, feedwater heaters, filters, or chlorinators, to supply water, fuel, lubricants, air, or auxiliary power.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a routine inspection/adjustment cycle of auxiliary equipment (e.g., checking pump/fan/compressor settings, adjusting controls, logging readings). A competent operator typically completes one round in ~30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial controls, reading gauges, adjusting valves/switches across varied heavy equipment. Humanoids lack the specialized tool-use, domain-specific interfaces, and reliable fine manipulation needed for safe autonomous control of such systems.

task: Test samples of materials or products to ensure compliance with specifications, using test equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single sample with relevant equipment, logging results. Covers setup, test execution, and recording — typically 15–30 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct test equipment, precise sample handling, operating varied instruments (buttons, dials, digital interfaces), interpreting readings, and logging results. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and instrument-operation generalization for unstructured lab settings.

task: Set up treating or testing equipment, such as oxygen tents, portable radiograph (x-ray) equipment, or overhead irrigation bottles, as directed by a physician or nurse.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up one piece of medical equipment (e.g., oxygen tent, IV irrigation bottle, portable x-ray unit). A trained aide typically takes 8–15 minutes per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Medical equipment setup requires precise fine-motor manipulation of tubes, connectors, and fragile devices; navigating clinical clutter; and safely interfacing with patient-adjacent hardware. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, medical domain knowledge, and safety certification for this high-stakes, unstructured task.

task: Obtain or inspect sterile or non-sterile surgical equipment, instruments, or supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting/obtaining supplies for a single surgical procedure setup. A scrub tech typically spends ~10 min checking and gathering instruments, confirming sterility, and organizing a back table.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading fine sterility indicators, identifying hundreds of specialized instruments by name/type, handling delicate sterile packaging without contamination, and navigating a sterile field — well beyond current robot dexterity, perception, and contamination-awareness limits.

task: Apply clean dressings, slings, stockings, or support bandages, under direction of nurse or physician.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying a single dressing, sling, stocking, or bandage to one patient. A competent aide typically completes this in ~10 minutes including setup and positioning.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate handling of soft, deformable materials on a living, moving human body; precise tension control; safe patient interaction; and contextual adaptation to wound/limb geometry — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and safety standards.

task: Start machines and move levers to engage hydraulic lifts that press woodstocks into desired forms and disengage lifts after appropriate drying times.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one press cycle: start machine, engage hydraulic lift, wait for drying/set time, then disengage. Typical cycle is a few minutes of active work plus short dwell time.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized hydraulic press machinery with levers and controls not designed for humanoids, monitoring drying times, and sequencing steps safely. No current general-purpose robot is qualified for unsupervised industrial press operation.

task: Maintain an unobstructed operative field, using surgical retractors, sponges, or suctioning and irrigating equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = maintaining the operative field throughout a typical surgical procedure (~45 min active involvement), requiring continuous adjustment of retractors, sponges, and suction/irrigation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, real-time surgical situational awareness, sterile technique, dynamic force modulation on delicate tissue, and coordinated multi-tool use in a constrained field — far beyond 2026 general-purpose robots.

task: Adjust or make minor repairs to spectacles or eyeglasses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting/repairing one pair of glasses. A skilled optician typically spends 5–10 minutes bending temples, tightening screws, replacing nose pads, or aligning frames.
robot_success_rationale: Extremely fine dexterity required: tiny screws, delicate wire/plastic frames, precise bending forces, and replacing micro nose pads. Far beyond current humanoid manipulation precision; high risk of shattering lenses or snapping frames.

task: Install, maintain, or repair hoses, pumps, filters, or screens to maintain processing equipment, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single repair/replacement job (e.g., swapping a filter or reseating a hose) on processing equipment; a competent technician typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force torquing, fine-threaded fittings, diagnosing worn parts, and working in tight/cluttered industrial spaces. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and tool-use reliability needed for multi-step mechanical maintenance.

task: Weigh or measure materials, ingredients, or products to ensure conformance to requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = weighing/measuring a single batch or item. A worker picks up the item, places it on a scale/measuring tool, reads the value, and records or acts on the result — typically 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can grasp and place objects on scales and read digital displays via vision, but vary widely in success: irregular/bulky items, fine pouring/measuring, and analog gauges reduce reliability. Generalization across material types and tool formats is inconsistent.

task: Position or hold patients in position for surgical preparation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning a single patient for one surgical procedure, including adjusting table, padding, securing limbs, and confirming alignment. Typically 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires gentle handling of an unconscious/sedated human body, precise anatomical positioning, pressure-injury prevention, and coordination with surgical team. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, force-sensing, and safety standards for patient contact.

task: Maintain cleanliness of bus or motor coach.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one bus/motor coach interior and exterior (sweeping, wiping seats, mopping floor, cleaning windows). Competent cleaner takes ~45 min per vehicle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating confined bus interior with narrow aisles, climbing steps, cleaning overhead racks, wiping curved surfaces, and potentially exterior washing. Current humanoids lack the mobility, reach adaptability, and tool-switching needed for this complex multi-environment task.

task: Prepare or serve food trays.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and delivering one food tray (e.g., cafeteria or hospital tray). A competent worker takes ~2–4 minutes to portion, arrange, and place items on a tray.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step food handling, precise portioning, varied container types, and safe tray transport. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity for food items, spill avoidance, and full kitchen/cafeteria generalization.

task: Modify cooking and forming operations based on the results of sampling processes, adjusting time cycles and ingredients to achieve desired qualities, such as firmness or texture.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reviewing sampling results, deciding on adjustments, and modifying one production run's time cycles/ingredients. A skilled operator typically takes 10–20 min per adjustment cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting quality sampling data, making cognitive judgment calls on recipe/process adjustments, and manipulating industrial food-processing controls. Current robots lack the sensory interpretation, adaptive decision-making, and specialized equipment interfacing needed.

task: Apply sutures, staples, clips, or other materials to close skin, facia, or subcutaneous wound layers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = closing a single wound (e.g., a typical surgical incision or laceration). A competent surgeon/PA takes ~10–20 min depending on wound length and technique.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme sub-millimeter dexterity, force control on delicate tissue, sterile technique, and real-time tactile feedback far beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability. Surgical robots (e.g., da Vinci) require teleoperation, not autonomy.

task: Tend accessory equipment, such as pumps and conveyors, to move materials or ingredients through production processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring/adjustment cycle of accessory equipment (pump, conveyor) during a production shift; a competent operator checks, adjusts, and logs status roughly every 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading gauges, toggling controls, adjusting valves/belts, and diagnosing flow issues across varied industrial layouts. Perception of analog gauges, non-standard controls, and unstructured fault conditions is beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability. Safety constraints also limit autonomous intervention.

task: Excavate and grade ditches, and lay and join pipe for water and sewer service.
human_time_rationale: One instance = excavating, grading, and laying/joining pipe for a single ditch run (~20–50 ft). A skilled pipefitter/laborer crew takes ~3–4 hours per standard residential service connection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating excavators and graders (vehicles a humanoid cannot drive), precise trench grading, and heavy pipe joining in unstructured outdoor terrain. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities across every dimension: mobility, tool-use, strength, and generalization.

task: Clean equipment, such as wheelchairs, hospital beds, or portable medical equipment, documenting needed repairs or maintenance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one piece of medical equipment (e.g., wheelchair or hospital bed) and documenting repair needs; ~15–25 min per item for a competent healthcare aide.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex, irregular equipment geometry, applying disinfectants precisely, detecting damage/wear (inspection), and documenting findings — combining manipulation, perception, and cognitive reporting beyond current robot capability.

task: Collect oil, water, or electrolyte samples for laboratory analysis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting one fluid sample from a designated port/tap, including attaching sampler, drawing fluid, sealing and labeling container. Typical field task takes ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of valves, syringes, and small sample ports; leak-free fluid handling; proper labeling. Current humanoids lack the precision, tool-use, and fluid-handling reliability needed for autonomous lab-quality sampling.

task: Change alignment and adjustment of sanding, cutting, or boring machine guides to prevent defects in finished products, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting guides on a single machine. A skilled machinist typically takes 10–20 min to measure, adjust, and verify alignment using hand tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, fine torque-controlled hand-tool use, and quality judgment on guide alignment. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, calibrated force feedback, and situational reasoning needed for reliable success on arbitrary industrial machines.

task: Turn or reposition bedridden patients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repositioning a single bedridden patient once. A competent caregiver typically takes 5–10 minutes including preparation, repositioning, and securing pillows/supports.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safe handling of a fragile, unpredictably moving human body; high dexterity, force sensitivity, and spatial awareness needed. Current humanoids lack safe compliant contact control, body-awareness perception, and caregiver-grade reliability for human manipulation.

task: Correct patterns to compensate for defects in castings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = correcting the pattern for a single casting defect. A skilled pattern maker inspects, diagnoses, and physically modifies the pattern (adding/removing material, adjusting draft, etc.), typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert tactile and visual defect diagnosis, precise material removal/addition on complex 3D geometry, and iterative judgment. Far beyond current robot dexterity, perception, and domain reasoning capabilities.

task: Serve or collect food trays.
human_time_rationale: One instance = delivering or collecting a single food tray to/from a patient, cafeteria patron, or airline seat; ~1–3 min including walking, placing, and returning.
robot_success_rationale: Flat-tray pick-and-carry is feasible but requires stable bipedal locomotion in crowded spaces, safe tray balancing, and social navigation. Current humanoids struggle with dynamic environments, obstacle avoidance, and gentle placement without spilling.

task: Clamp, ligate, or cauterize blood vessels to control bleeding during surgical entry, using hemostatic clamps, suture ligatures, or electrocautery equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = controlling bleeding at a single vessel/bleed site during surgical entry. A competent surgeon typically clamps, ligates, or cauterizes one site in ~5–10 minutes depending on method and depth.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sub-millimeter precision, force sensing, and real-time surgical judgement on soft deformable tissue with live bleeding. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capability; even dedicated surgical robots need surgeon teleoperation.

task: Reject defective products and readjust equipment to eliminate problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = identifying and rejecting one defective item, then making the associated equipment readjustment; a competent worker on a production line typically completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can detect some defects via vision, but diagnosing root cause, deciding the correct equipment adjustment, and physically manipulating controls/dials/panels on varied machinery is well beyond reliable autonomous capability in 2026.

task: Unclamp workpieces and remove them from machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unclamping and removing a single workpiece from a machine. A skilled machinist typically takes 1–3 minutes per part including loosening clamps and safely extracting the piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires moderate dexterity to operate clamp mechanisms (levers, screws, vises) and grasp varied workpiece shapes. Perception of clamp state and part geometry is challenging; force control needed to avoid dropping heavy/sharp parts. Improving but unreliable in unstructured machine shop settings.

task: Clean and sanitize patient rooms, bathrooms, examination rooms, or other patient areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and sanitizing one patient room or exam room. A competent hospital housekeeper typically completes a full room turnover in 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Healthcare cleaning requires strict sanitization protocols, precise chemical handling, navigating cluttered/variable rooms, moving beds/equipment, and infection-control compliance. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, domain awareness, and reliable tool-use for this high-stakes, unstructured environment.

task: Haul and spread sand, gravel, and clay to fill washouts and repair road shoulders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing one washout or road shoulder section; loading, hauling, and spreading material with equipment takes a competent operator ~1–2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating dump trucks, graders, or loaders on outdoor terrain — vehicles a humanoid robot cannot drive or control. Outdoor unstructured terrain, heavy equipment operation, and precision spreading are far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Exercise patients who are comatose, paralyzed, or have restricted mobility.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one passive exercise session for one patient (range-of-motion movements across major limbs). A competent caregiver typically completes this in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safe, adaptive contact force on fragile human limbs, real-time pain/spasm detection, repositioning a limp body, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack safe compliant manipulation and caregiver-level perception for this sensitive task.

task: Verify the identity of patient or operative site.
human_time_rationale: One instance = verifying one patient's identity and/or operative site before a procedure. Involves checking wristband, verbal confirmation, chart review, and site marking — typically 2–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading wristbands/charts, verbal patient interaction, cross-referencing records, and judgment about site marking accuracy. Current robots lack reliable NLP dialogue, medical record integration, and the clinical judgment needed for safe autonomous verification.

task: Conduct low vision blindness tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full low vision screening session for one patient, including visual acuity, contrast sensitivity, and visual field tests; ~15–25 min for a trained technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires administering standardized charts, adjusting lighting/equipment, interpreting patient verbal/behavioral responses, and adapting tests to patient needs. Current humanoids lack clinical perception, patient interaction nuance, and certified medical-device operation.

task: Clean or maintain products, machines, or work areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/maintaining a single work area, machine surface, or product. A competent worker typically spends ~20 min per unit task (wiping, sweeping, clearing debris, basic upkeep).
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle flat surface wiping and sweeping reasonably well (~40–70%), but unstructured work areas, varied machine geometries, and contextual judgment (what to clean vs. avoid) reduce reliability. Generalization across environments is still limited.

task: Lift or assist others to lift patients to move them on or off beds, examination tables, surgical tables, or stretchers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repositioning/transferring one patient between surfaces. Includes positioning, communication, coordinated lift, and settling — typically 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safe whole-body human handling, adaptive grip on irregular/fragile bodies, coordinated force with other humans, and dynamic balance under heavy load. Current humanoids lack the strength, compliance, and situational judgment for safe patient transfers.

task: Restock patient rooms with personal hygiene items, such as towels, washcloths, soap, or toilet paper.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restocking hygiene supplies in one patient room. A competent aide checks stock, retrieves items from a cart, and places them in the bathroom/bedside — typically 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a hospital room (cluttered, dynamic), identifying stock levels, picking soft/flexible items (towels, washcloths), opening cabinets, and placing items correctly. Perception and soft-goods manipulation remain weak; hospital safety standards are strict.

task: Transport bodies to the morgue.
human_time_rationale: One instance = transferring one body from a hospital room/ward to the morgue, including preparation, use of a gurney/transport cart, and handoff documentation. Typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safely lifting/transferring a heavy, limp human body onto a gurney (extreme dexterity/strength challenge), navigating elevators and corridors, maintaining dignity/safety, and coordinating handoff — far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Transport portable medical equipment or medical supplies between rooms or departments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one delivery trip (pick up supplies/equipment, navigate hallways/elevators to destination department, hand off). Typical hospital internal transport takes 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids can carry items and walk flat indoor corridors, but hospital environments require elevator operation, badge-access doors, avoiding patients/staff, and reliable hand-off to recipients — all challenging for current systems without task-specific integration.

task: Collect soiled linen or trash.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting soiled linen or trash from a single room or zone; a competent worker gathers, bags, and removes items in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate rooms and pick up bagged trash reasonably well, but grasping loose, irregular soiled linen from varied surfaces (floors, hampers, beds) requires robust perception and dexterity that current humanoids handle inconsistently.

task: Assess individuals' degree of drug dependency by collecting and analyzing urine samples.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting one urine sample from a patient and analyzing it (e.g., via test strip or lab submission). Includes intake, collection, and preliminary result reading.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interacting sensitively with a patient, handling biological specimens safely, operating collection equipment, and interpreting clinical results. Caregiving context, biohazard handling, and clinical judgment far exceed current humanoid capabilities.

task: Dislodge and clear jammed materials or other items from machinery or equipment, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing a single jam event. A competent worker diagnoses the jam, selects a hand tool, and clears the obstruction, typically taking 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing jam cause, selecting the right hand tool, applying variable force in tight/unstructured machinery spaces, and verifying clearance. Current robots lack reliable tool selection, confined-space dexterity, and force-feedback judgment for unpredictable jams.

task: Measure and mark locations for installation of markers, using tape, string, or chalk.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and marking locations for one standard installation job (e.g., a row of markers). A competent worker uses tape/string/chalk and completes this in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise spatial measurement, interpreting layout plans, manipulating flexible tools (tape, string, chalk) on varied terrain, and making accurate marks. Current humanoids lack reliable fine measurement tool use and plan interpretation in unstructured outdoor/indoor environments.

task: Dislodge and clear jammed materials or other items from machinery and equipment, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing a single jam. A competent worker diagnoses the jam, uses appropriate hand tools (pry bar, pliers, etc.), and clears it, typically in ~5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing a novel jam, selecting the right hand tool, applying precise force in a confined/awkward space, and adapting to unpredictable machinery geometry. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterous tool-use capabilities.

task: Divide patterns into sections according to shapes of castings to facilitate removal of patterns from molds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dividing a single pattern into sections. A skilled patternmaker studies the casting geometry, plans parting lines, and physically cuts/marks the pattern — typically 30–60 min per pattern.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert spatial reasoning about 3D draft angles and parting geometry, precise fine cutting/marking of complex shapes, and deep domain knowledge. Far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and cognitive planning capabilities.

task: Wash, groom, shave, or drape patients to prepare them for surgery, treatment, or examination.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one patient (washing, grooming, shaving, and/or draping relevant area). A competent surgical prep tech typically takes 10–20 minutes per patient.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme delicacy on a compliant human body, sterile technique, precise shaving near sensitive anatomy, and adaptive draping. Current humanoids lack fine dexterity, force sensitivity, and sterile protocol compliance for clinical caregiving.

task: Transport specimens, laboratory items, or pharmacy items, ensuring proper documentation and delivery to authorized personnel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one delivery trip (pick up item, transport to destination, hand off to authorized personnel). Typical hospital/lab courier run takes ~10–20 minutes including documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with navigating complex multi-floor hospital environments, verifying authorized recipients, completing documentation/chain-of-custody, and safely handling fragile/biohazardous specimens. Autonomous mobile robots (AMRs) do this in structured hospitals, but general-purpose humanoids lack the full workflow integration and compliance capabilities.

task: Dispose of blood or other biohazard fluids or tissue, in accordance with applicable laws, standards, or policies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting and disposing of a single biohazard waste item/container (e.g., sharps, fluid bag, tissue specimen). Includes donning PPE, sealing, labeling, and placing in correct receptacle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying biohazard materials, applying correct PPE, handling fluid-filled or fragile containers without spillage, reading labels, and routing to specific receptacles per regulatory protocol — far beyond current robot generalization and dexterity.

task: Collect samples of materials or products for laboratory analysis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a single sample batch (e.g., one soil core, fluid draw, or product swab). Includes locating the sample point, using appropriate tool/container, and labeling. Typical industrial/lab context.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous tool use (syringes, swabs, corers), correct sample volume judgment, sterile handling, and proper labeling — all highly varied. Current robots lack reliable fine manipulation and contextual judgment for diverse sampling scenarios.

task: Test product samples for specific gravity, chemical characteristics, pH levels, concentrations, or viscosities, or send them to laboratories for testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single product sample for one or more properties (pH, viscosity, etc.) or preparing/dispatching it to a lab. Covers setup, measurement, and recording results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of lab instruments (pipettes, viscometers, pH probes), precise liquid handling, equipment calibration, and judgment-based decisions like labeling and dispatching samples — well beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capability.

task: Control or operate equipment in which chemical changes or reactions take place during the processing of industrial or consumer products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one production run or shift segment monitoring a chemical process unit. A competent operator typically checks, adjusts, and logs readings over ~1 hour per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating complex industrial control panels, valves, gauges, and HMIs while interpreting dynamic chemical process states. Current humanoids lack process-industry tool familiarity, safety certification, and the nuanced judgment needed for unstructured chemical plant environments.

task: Connect pipes between vats and processing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting a single pipe run between one vat and one piece of processing equipment, including alignment, fitting, and securing connections. Competent pipefitter takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy pipe manipulation, precise thread/flange alignment, torquing fittings to spec, and navigating cluttered industrial environments. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and tool-use in unstructured settings.

task: Add or mix chemicals or ingredients for processing, using hand tools or other devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one batch mixing/adding of chemicals or ingredients. A competent worker measures, adds, and mixes a single batch using hand tools, typically 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measuring, pouring liquids/powders, and controlled mixing — challenging for robots due to fluid/powder handling, fine dexterity for measuring tools, and risk of spills or incorrect quantities. Unstructured environments worsen outcomes.

task: Position or hold patients in position for surgical preparation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning a single patient on the OR table for a specific procedure, including securing limbs, padding pressure points, and confirming final position. Typically 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise handling of a fragile, non-cooperative human body; real-time clinical judgment on nerve/pressure safety; sterile awareness; and coordinated team communication. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured, safety-critical OR environments.

task: Clean and sterilize vats and factory processing areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and sterilizing one vat/processing area. A competent worker typically spends 60–120 minutes scrubbing, rinsing, applying sterilant, and verifying cleanliness.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating high-pressure hoses, applying chemicals, reaching into large vats, and verifying sterility — demanding tool use, confined-space mobility, and quality inspection beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Open valves to drain slurry from mixers into storage tanks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = opening valves on one mixer to drain slurry; worker locates valve, turns/opens it, confirms flow. Typically 3–7 minutes per mixer.
robot_success_rationale: Industrial valves vary widely (handwheels, levers, quarter-turns). Robots struggle with high-torque handwheels, non-standard grips, confirming flow visually, and navigating cluttered industrial environments reliably.

task: Postoperatively inject a subcutaneous local anesthetic agent to reduce pain.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single subcutaneous injection of local anesthetic at a postoperative wound site; a competent nurse/clinician draws up, identifies site, injects, and disposes in ~5 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile technique, precise needle insertion at exact subcutaneous depth on a variable human body, real-time force/tissue feedback, and clinical judgment — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and safety certification. Medical procedures on humans are strictly off-limits for current robots.

task: Weigh or measure materials, ingredients, or products at specified intervals to ensure conformance to requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single weighing/measuring event: retrieve sample, place on scale or measuring instrument, read result, record. Typically 2–5 minutes for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick objects and read digital scales in structured settings, but grasping varied containers, placing them precisely on a scale, reading analog/varied displays, and recording results reliably in unstructured environments remains challenging.

task: Collect samples of materials or products for laboratory testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a single sample batch (e.g., one product batch or material lot). Includes locating source, using appropriate tool/container, labeling, and securing sample for transport.
robot_success_rationale: Requires context-dependent tool use (pipettes, scoops, swabs), precise dexterous handling of sample containers, labeling, and navigating varied environments. Generalization across material types and collection methods remains very limited for current humanoids.

task: Turn valves to regulate the moisture contents of materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a valve (turn, check gauge/feedback, fine-tune). A competent worker locates the valve, turns it to target setting, and confirms moisture response in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Turning a valve requires grasping a handle of variable shape/size, applying controlled torque, and reading feedback gauges. Humanoids can grasp and rotate objects but valve diversity, required torque precision, and sensor-feedback interpretation reduce reliability significantly.

task: Remove clogs, defects, or impurities from machines, tanks, conveyors, screens, or other processing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing a single clog or defect from one piece of equipment. A competent worker typically diagnoses, accesses, and clears it in ~20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing fault location, navigating industrial environments, applying variable force to dislodge clogs in confined/awkward spaces, and using specialized tools. Current humanoids lack the situational awareness, tool-use flexibility, and force control for unstructured industrial equipment maintenance.

task: Stock or issue medical supplies, such as dressing packs or treatment trays.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restocking a single supply area (e.g., a cabinet or cart) with dressing packs or treatment trays; a competent worker checks inventory, retrieves, and organizes items in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating sterile/clinical environments, reading labels, handling varied soft/rigid packaging, verifying expiry dates, and placing items precisely. Perception of similar-looking medical items and sterility compliance are major barriers for current humanoids.

task: Organize or clean blood-drawing trays, ensuring that all instruments are sterile and all needles, syringes, or related items are of first-time use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing/restocking one blood-drawing tray. A competent phlebotomist typically takes ~8–12 minutes to clean, inspect, and restock a tray with sterile/single-use items.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle needles/syringes safely, sterility verification via labeling/inspection, precise organization in a clinical tray, and strict safety compliance. Well beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Inspect machines or equipment for hazards, operating efficiency, malfunctions, wear, or leaks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single machine/equipment unit. A competent worker does a walkround check covering visual, auditory, and tactile cues — typically 10–30 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to equipment and capture visual/thermal data, but detecting subtle leaks, abnormal sounds, vibration feel, and contextual hazard judgment requires multi-modal sensing, domain knowledge, and generalization that 2026 humanoids handle poorly.

task: Cut or break up pavement and drive guardrail posts, using machines equipped with interchangeable hammers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = breaking a pavement section or driving one guardrail post; includes machine setup and hammer swap. Competent operator averages ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a large specialty machine (hydraulic breaker/post driver) in an outdoor road environment. Humanoid robots cannot drive or operate heavy machinery in traffic; task demands vehicle control, tool swaps, and terrain navigation far beyond 2026 capability.

task: Secure woodstock against a guide or in a holding device, place woodstock on a conveyor, or dump woodstock in a hopper to feed woodstock into machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning/securing a single piece of woodstock into a guide, conveyor, or hopper. A skilled worker typically takes 1–5 minutes depending on part size and fixture type.
robot_success_rationale: Grasping and placing rigid stock pieces is feasible, but securing against guides, aligning to conveyors, or dumping into hoppers requires precise positioning, varied workpiece sizes, and fixture interaction — challenging for generalist humanoids in unstructured shop environments.

task: Trace outlines of specified patterns onto material, and cut fabric, using scissors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tracing and cutting a single pattern piece onto fabric. A skilled sewer takes ~5 min to trace and ~10 min to cut a typical garment pattern piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise 2D tracing with a marking tool on flexible/deformable fabric, then accurate scissor-cutting along curves — demanding fine dexterity, force control, and fabric manipulation far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Remove defective or substandard materials from machines, and readjust machine components so that products meet standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = identifying and removing a defective/substandard material from a machine and readjusting components to restore standard output; ~5–10 min for a skilled operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing defect root cause, manipulating machine-specific controls/knobs under tight tolerances, and verifying output quality — demanding perception, fine dexterity, and domain generalization well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Move controls to start, stop, or adjust machinery and equipment that crushes, grinds, polishes, or blends materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single start/stop/adjust cycle on crushing/grinding/blending equipment; a competent operator takes ~1–5 min to check status, move levers/dials, and confirm settings.
robot_success_rationale: Industrial control panels have highly varied levers, buttons, and dials requiring precise manipulation and contextual judgment. Humanoids lack reliable tool/panel generalization, force feedback calibration, and safe operation protocols for heavy industrial machinery.

task: Remove completed materials or products from cutting or slicing machines, and stack or store them for additional processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one batch of cut/sliced output and stacking/storing it; a competent worker does this in ~3 minutes per machine cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place uniform items reasonably well, but variable shapes, sizes, and weights of cut materials, plus precise stacking/storing requirements and proximity to active machinery, reduce reliability significantly.

task: Conduct hemoglobin tests to ensure donor iron levels are normal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one donor. A trained phlebotomist performs a fingerstick, applies blood to a hemoglobinometer or copper sulfate solution, and reads the result in ~3–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fingerstick on a live human, sterile technique, fine dexterity with lancet and test strip, and safe caregiving contact. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control, sterile compliance, and human-interaction safety needed for clinical procedures.

task: Feed stock into cutting machines, onto conveyors, or under cutting blades, by threading, guiding, pushing, or turning handwheels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = feeding one batch/sheet/length of stock into a cutting machine. A competent operator typically spends ~5 minutes threading, aligning, and guiding material per run.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment, threading, and handwheel operation with varied stock materials and machine types. Humanoids can turn knobs and guide objects but lack reliable fine alignment, force feedback calibration, and generalization across diverse industrial machine interfaces.

task: Change or replace saw blades, cables, cutter heads, and grinding wheels, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing one blade/cable/cutter head/grinding wheel. A skilled worker uses hand tools to remove the old component and install the new one, averaging ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torque with wrenches/spanners on tight fasteners, fine force control to seat blades safely, and tool-switching. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous tool-use and force feedback for such safety-critical mechanical work.

task: Turn valves to pump sterilizing solutions or rinse water through pipes or equipment or to spray vats with atomizers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a full sterilization/rinse cycle on one piece of equipment or vat, including valve turns and monitoring flow. Typically 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Turning valves (especially non-standard or wheel-type) is moderately difficult for humanoids; navigating industrial pipework, sequencing multiple valves correctly, and operating atomizers in varied wet/cluttered environments pushes success down significantly.

task: Tighten ends of anchored truss bolts, using turnbuckles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tightening all turnbuckles on a single anchored truss. A competent worker inspects, grips, and rotates each turnbuckle to spec; ~15 min for a typical truss with several bolts.
robot_success_rationale: Turnbuckles require precise grip on small, often awkward cylindrical hardware in overhead/elevated, structurally cluttered environments. Torque sensing, tool-use generalization, and reaching into tight truss geometry are well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Place products on conveyors or carts, and monitor product flow.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing a single product on a conveyor/cart and briefly monitoring flow; competent worker does this in ~3 minutes including positioning and visual check.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of common objects is 70–90%, but variability in product shapes, precise conveyor placement, and monitoring product flow (perception + judgment) reduce reliability. Flat indoor terrain is manageable.

task: Tend or operate and control equipment, such as kettles, cookers, vats and tanks, and boilers, to cook ingredients or prepare products for further processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one production batch cycle: setting up, monitoring, and adjusting a kettle/cooker/vat through a complete cook or preparation cycle in an industrial food/beverage setting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large industrial controls, valves, and dials; reading gauges; making judgment-based adjustments; and managing safety-critical processes. Current humanoids lack reliable industrial equipment interfacing, process knowledge, and fine control manipulation at this scale.

task: Transport patients to treatment units, testing units, operating rooms, or other areas, using wheelchairs, stretchers, or moveable beds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = transporting a single patient from origin to destination. Includes transfer, navigation, and handoff; typical hospital trip takes ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safely transferring and physically supporting a human patient, navigating crowded hospital corridors, operating elevators, and managing wheelchairs/stretchers — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, patient-handling, and real-world navigation reliability.

task: Process blood or other fluid samples for further analysis by other medical professionals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single patient sample (e.g., centrifuging, labeling, aliquoting, logging). A competent lab tech typically completes this in ~15 minutes end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous manipulation of small vials, pipettes, centrifuges, and barcoding equipment; sterile technique; fluid handling; and contextual judgment. Far beyond reliable autonomous robot capability in 2026.

task: Monitor blood or plasma donors during and after procedures to ensure health, safety, and comfort.
human_time_rationale: One instance = monitoring a single donor through a full donation procedure (whole blood ~10 min draw + pre/post observation; plasma ~45–60 min). Median across both types ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires continuous empathetic communication, real-time clinical judgment (vasovagal reactions, infiltration, adverse events), IV/needle management, and adaptive caregiving. Far beyond current robot autonomy and safety standards for clinical settings.

task: Lift or assist others to lift patients to move them on or off beds, examination tables, surgical tables, or stretchers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lifting/transferring one patient between two surfaces (e.g., bed to stretcher). A competent caregiver typically completes this in ~5 minutes including repositioning and ensuring patient safety.
robot_success_rationale: Requires whole-body strength, compliant human contact, real-time safety judgment, coordination with co-workers, and adaptation to unpredictable patient posture/weight. Current humanoids lack the load capacity, safe force control, and generalization for unstructured patient handling.

task: Take anatomical or functional ocular measurements of the eye or surrounding tissue, such as axial length measurements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring both eyes of one patient (e.g., axial length biometry). A skilled optometrist/ophthalmologist technician typically completes alignment, measurement, and documentation in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter precise alignment of a specialized ophthalmic instrument to a cooperative patient's eye, patient communication, and clinical judgment. Current humanoid robots lack the fine dexterity, sterile tool operation, and patient-interaction capability needed. Near-zero feasibility.

task: Monitor operation of cutting or slicing machines to detect malfunctions or to determine whether supplies need replenishment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring round/check cycle on a cutting/slicing machine; a competent operator inspects machine state, listens for anomalies, and checks supply levels in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can use cameras and audio for basic anomaly detection, but interpreting subtle mechanical malfunctions, assessing supply levels across varied containers, and acting on findings in unstructured industrial settings remains unreliable for current humanoids.

task: Measure or weigh ingredients, using scales or measuring containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring/weighing a single ingredient. A competent cook locates the container/scale, measures, and sets aside in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping varied containers, reading analog/digital scales, pouring precise amounts, and interpreting recipes. Perception of fill levels, fine motor pouring control, and generalization across vessel types remain significant limitations for current humanoids.

task: Observe or examine patients to detect symptoms that may require medical attention, such as bruises, open wounds, or blood in urine.
human_time_rationale: One instance = observing/examining a single patient during a routine assessment. A competent nurse or aide typically spends 5–15 minutes on a focused physical observation check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, full-body visual inspection, patient communication, and interpreting subtle biological signs (e.g., urine color, skin bruising). Current robots lack medical perception software, safe patient interaction, and the clinical reasoning to meet acceptable care standards.

task: Set up, operate, or tend machines that cut or slice materials, such as glass, stone, cork, rubber, tobacco, food, paper, or insulating material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running one production batch/job on a cutting/slicing machine; a competent operator typically spends ~15 min on setup, loading, and tending per job.
robot_success_rationale: Requires machine setup (adjusting guards, blades, settings), loading varied materials, monitoring output quality, and intervening on jams—demanding dexterity, tool-use, and generalization across diverse machine types that current humanoids struggle with.

task: Compound or process ingredients or dyes, according to formulas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one batch of a compounded formula (ingredients or dyes). A competent worker measures, mixes, and verifies one batch, typically taking 20–45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement of varied materials (powders, liquids), reading labels, operating scales/mixers, following multi-step formulas, and quality verification. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation, liquid handling, and chemical process generalization.

task: Adjust bolts, clamps, stops, guides, or table angles or heights, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single fastener or component (bolt, clamp, stop, guide, or table setting). A skilled worker typically completes one adjustment in ~5 minutes including inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torque, identifying correct fastener, wielding hand tools (wrenches, screwdrivers) in constrained spaces, and verifying result. Current humanoids struggle with tool handling precision, varied fastener types, and confirming adjustment accuracy.

task: Transfer materials, supplies, or products between work areas, using moving equipment or hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = transferring a batch/load of materials between two work areas; a competent worker completes a typical transfer (gather, move, place) in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick-and-place and navigate flat indoor terrain reasonably well, but varied item types, unstructured layouts, operating hand trucks/forklifts, and generalization across real worksites reduce reliability significantly.

task: Assess client needs or disorders, using drawing, painting, sculpting, or other artistic processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one art therapy assessment session with a client, typically lasting 45–90 minutes including observation, prompting, and clinical interpretation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires deep clinical judgment, emotional attunement, reading subtle behavioral/verbal cues, and psychological interpretation. Far beyond current robot perception, social reasoning, and generalization capabilities.

task: Select and install machine components, such as cutting blades, rollers, and templates, according to specifications, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting and installing one component set on one machine; skilled operator takes ~15–25 min including verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading specs, selecting correct parts from inventory, precise torquing with hand tools, and alignment verification. High dexterity, tool-use, and contextual judgment demands exceed current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Inspect chains, belts, or scrolls for signs of wear.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one chain, belt, or scroll; a technician visually and tactilely checks for wear, cracking, or stretching in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires close-up visual perception of subtle wear indicators (cracks, fraying, stretch, discoloration) in tight or awkward spaces, plus tactile judgment. Current robots lack reliable fine-detail inspection and contextual wear assessment without specialized sensors.

task: Clean and sanitize patient rooms, bathrooms, examination rooms, or other patient areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and sanitizing one patient room or exam room, including surfaces, bathroom, and floor. A trained hospital housekeeper typically takes 25–45 minutes per room.
robot_success_rationale: Healthcare cleaning demands precise chemical handling, strict sanitation protocols, obstacle-rich cluttered environments, delicate equipment avoidance, and infection-control compliance. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, domain-specific tool use, and protocol adherence required at acceptable quality.

task: Serve refreshments to donors to ensure absorption of sugar into their systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = serving a single donor a refreshment (juice, cookie, snack). A competent human takes ~5 min to approach, present, and confirm the donor has received and consumed it.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a crowded donor area, grasping varied refreshment items, handing off to a seated/reclining person, and confirming consumption — a complex mix of social interaction, dexterous handling, and unstructured caregiving that 2026 robots handle poorly.

task: Cut stock manually to prepare for machine cutting, using tools such as knives, cleavers, handsaws, or hammers and chisels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single piece/batch of stock for machine cutting; a skilled worker uses hand tools to rough-cut or size material in roughly 5–10 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force tool use (cleavers, handsaws, chisels, hammers) with precision and material-adaptive grip. Current humanoids lack the force control, tool-handling dexterity, and generalization across varied stock materials and tool types needed for acceptable quality.

task: Examine materials, ingredients, or products visually or with hands to ensure conformance to established standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single batch, item, or sample at a workstation. A competent worker typically spends ~5 minutes per unit/lot check, combining visual scan and tactile assessment.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is improving, but tactile ("hands") inspection is still limited on frontier humanoids. Generalization across diverse materials, textures, colors, and standards without task-specific training remains a key gap. Unstructured environments reduce reliability further.

task: Climb communication towers to install, replace, or repair antennas or auxiliary equipment used to transmit and receive radio waves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = climbing a tower and completing a single antenna/equipment installation or repair job. Includes ascent, work, and descent; typically 1–3 hours for a skilled tower technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing lattice/guyed towers at heights up to 500m, outdoor unstructured terrain, precise high-altitude dexterous work with tools, and safety compliance. No current humanoid can climb communication towers or safely operate at such heights.

task: Dump or scoop prescribed solid, granular, or powdered materials into equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scooping/dumping one prescribed batch into equipment; worker locates, measures, and deposits material in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can scoop/pour granular materials at moderate success rates, but challenges include accurately measuring prescribed amounts, handling varied containers, accessing equipment openings, and avoiding spills — especially with powders or fine granules.

task: Measure corneal curvature with keratometers or ophthalmometers to aid in the diagnosis of conditions, such as astigmatism.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient eye exam with a keratometer/ophthalmometer. A competent optometrist/technician typically takes 5–15 minutes including setup, measurement, and recording results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a specialized ophthalmic instrument with sub-millimeter precision, aligning it to a patient's eye, interpreting live optical feedback, and managing patient cooperation — far beyond current general-purpose robot dexterity and medical tool-use capabilities.

task: Position stock along cutting lines, or against stops on beds of scoring or cutting machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning a single piece of stock against a stop or cutting line on a machine bed. A skilled worker does this in ~1–3 minutes including alignment checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise planar alignment of flat stock against stops or marked lines. Perception of cutting lines is challenging; fine positional accuracy needed. Flat/heavy stock handling and confined machine-bed environments limit current humanoid dexterity and generalization.

task: Conduct standards tests, such as blood alcohol, blood culture, oral glucose tolerance, glucose screening, blood smears, or peak and trough drug levels tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = conducting one standard clinical test end-to-end (patient prep, sample collection, labeling, initial processing). Median ~20 min across test types.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile venipuncture on a live patient, precise dexterity, patient interaction/consent, sterile field management, and clinical judgment — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in uncontrolled clinical settings.

task: Add or mix chemicals and ingredients for processing, using hand tools or other devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one batch mixing/addition cycle (measuring, adding, and mixing chemicals/ingredients). Competent worker typically completes this in ~15 min including setup and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, pouring, and mixing of varied containers/substances. Current robots struggle with liquid handling, fine pour control, label reading, and chemical safety judgment in unstructured processing environments.

task: Follow recipes to produce food products of specified flavor, texture, clarity, bouquet, or color.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single recipe batch. A competent cook typically spends 30–90 minutes on a moderately complex recipe from setup to finished product.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step sequencing, precise measurements, fine dexterity (pouring, stirring, tasting/sensing), heat management, and quality judgment (flavor, texture, clarity, bouquet, color). Current humanoids lack sufficient sensory feedback and generalization for full recipe execution.

task: Turn valve controls to start equipment and to adjust operation to maintain product quality.
human_time_rationale: One instance = turning valve controls to start equipment and make quality adjustments. A competent worker locates, grasps, and turns relevant valves in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Valve turning requires locating the correct valve, applying precise torque, and reading gauges/feedback to adjust quality — challenging perception, force control, and contextual judgment. Current humanoids struggle with varied valve geometries and coupled feedback loops.

task: Operate or tend machines to mix or blend any of a wide variety of materials, such as spices, dough batter, tobacco, fruit juices, chemicals, livestock feed, food products, color pigments, or explosive ingredients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one mixing/blending batch cycle. Includes loading materials, setting machine parameters, monitoring the run, and unloading. Typical batch takes ~15–25 minutes for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating diverse industrial machines with varied controls, loading bulk/liquid/powder materials, monitoring process quality, and adjusting settings. Poor generalization across machine types, unstructured material handling, and process judgment severely limits current robots.

task: Collect specimens at specific time intervals for tests, such as those assessing therapeutic drug levels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a single timed specimen (e.g., blood draw at a scheduled interval). Includes patient ID verification, labeling, venipuncture/sample collection, and handoff. ~10 min for a competent phlebotomist.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise venipuncture on a human patient — extreme fine dexterity, real-time vein identification, sterile technique, patient interaction, and safety compliance. Current humanoids cannot perform clinical procedures on humans; unacceptable risk profile.

task: Load materials into machinery and equipment, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a batch of materials into one machine (e.g., feeding stock into a press or hopper). A competent worker typically completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place common objects, but loading varied materials with hand tools into diverse machinery requires tool use, precise alignment, force feedback, and generalization across machine types — still challenging for 2026 humanoids.

task: Press buttons, pull levers, or depress pedals to start and operate cutting and slicing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting/operating a cutting/slicing machine for one job run. A competent operator presses buttons, pulls levers, or depresses pedals to initiate operation in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can press buttons and pull levers at moderate success rates, but industrial machine controls vary widely in layout, force, and pedal depression. Generalization across machine types, precise pedal actuation, and safe operation limit success significantly.

task: Stop mixing or blending machines when specified product qualities are obtained and open valves and start pumps to transfer mixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stopping a mixer/blender when quality specs are met, opening valves, and starting transfer pumps. A routine sequential control action taking ~5 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting product quality sensors or lab results, operating industrial control panels or valves, and sequencing pump starts — highly context-specific industrial interfaces a humanoid robot cannot reliably operate autonomously in 2026.

task: Separate collected materials for disposal, recycling, or reuse, in accordance with environmental policies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting a batch of collected materials (e.g., a cart or bin load). A competent worker inspects, categorises, and distributes items into correct streams in ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires recognising diverse, irregular, often contaminated items and routing them correctly. Vision-based object classification is improving but generalises poorly to novel/mixed waste; grasping irregular shapes and compliance with policy rules remain challenging for 2026 humanoids.

task: Respond to emergency situations, such as emergency medical calls, security calls, or fire alarms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = responding to and managing a single emergency call (arrival to handoff/resolution). Median across medical, security, and fire scenarios is ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating unpredictable environments, real-time situational assessment, complex decision-making, physical intervention (CPR, firefighting, restraint), communication, and legal authority. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Examine or test samples of processed substances, or collect samples for laboratory testing, to ensure conformance to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting or examining a single sample batch. A competent worker spends ~10–20 min gathering, labeling, and conducting initial checks or preparing it for lab submission.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid/material sampling (fine dexterity), container labeling, navigating industrial environments, and contextual judgment on conformance. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and domain-specific testing tool use needed here.

task: Stop equipment and clear blockages or jams, using fingers, wire, or hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stopping a piece of equipment and clearing a single blockage/jam. A competent worker typically diagnoses, stops, clears, and restarts in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires unstructured fault diagnosis, precise insertion of fingers/wire into confined spaces, variable force modulation, and equipment-specific knowledge. Current robots lack reliable dexterous probing and situational fault recognition in unstructured machinery contexts.

task: Train other medical personnel in phlebotomy or laboratory techniques.
human_time_rationale: One training session for a small group covering a phlebotomy or lab technique (demo, supervised practice, feedback). Typical session runs 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Training requires verbal instruction, real-time adaptive feedback, demonstration of fine venipuncture technique, and social/pedagogical judgment. Current robots lack reliable speech-based teaching, nuanced human interaction, and the dexterity to demonstrate phlebotomy safely.

task: Feed patients or assist patients to eat or drink.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one patient through a single meal/snack, including setup, feeding, and cleanup. Typically 15–25 minutes depending on patient needs.
robot_success_rationale: Requires adaptive dexterity to handle varied utensils/food textures, real-time reading of patient cues (swallowing, distress, preference), safe interaction with a vulnerable person, and unstructured caregiving — all well beyond 2026 frontier robots.

task: Run appropriate power, ground, or coaxial cables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = running a single cable (power, ground, or coaxial) from source to destination in a typical residential/commercial setting, including routing, securing, and terminating ends.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating tight spaces (walls, conduits, crawlspaces), fine dexterous manipulation of cables and connectors, tool use (fish tape, crimpers, staples), and contextual routing judgment — well beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Conduct binocular disparity tests to assess depth perception.
human_time_rationale: One instance = administering a full binocular disparity test battery to one subject, including setup, testing, and recording results. Typically takes ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise stereo visual perception, calibrated instrumentation (e.g., stereoscopes, Randot tests), fine manipulation of test materials, subject interaction, and clinical judgment — well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Sing or play musical instruments, such as keyboard, guitar, or percussion instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one song or short performance (~3–5 min play time plus setup/tuning). A competent musician completes this in ~30 minutes including preparation.
robot_success_rationale: Playing instruments requires extreme fine motor dexterity, nuanced force control, real-time auditory feedback, and expressive musicality. Current humanoids lack the precision finger control and auditory-motor loop needed for acceptable musical performance. Singing requires a biological voice.

task: Clean, lubricate, and adjust equipment, using scrapers, solvents, air hoses, oil, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning, lubricating, and adjusting a single piece of equipment (e.g., a machine or tool). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-tool use (scrapers, air hoses, oil cans, hand tools), fine force-controlled adjustments, solvent handling, and contextual judgment about lubrication points. Far beyond reliable autonomous robot capability in 2026.

task: Tighten pulleys or add abrasives to maintain cutting speeds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting one pulley or replenishing abrasive on one machine. A skilled operator inspects, tightens/adjusts, and verifies cutting speed — roughly 10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise torque application on pulleys, fine dexterous tool use (wrenches, tensioners), and judgment on abrasive quantities. Industrial machinery access, tight spaces, and force-feedback demands exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Perform maintenance or repair work on existing tower equipment, using hand or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance/repair job on tower equipment (e.g., tightening hardware, replacing a component). Typical job takes 1–3 hours; 120 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing tall towers (extreme mobility demand), operating hand/power tools at height in unstructured outdoor environments, and fine dexterous repair work. Current humanoids cannot climb towers or safely work at elevation autonomously.

task: Lift equipment into position, using cranes and rigging tools or equipment, such as gin poles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rigging and lifting one load into position. Includes attaching rigging, signaling crane operator, and confirming placement. Typical industrial lift takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating cranes/gin poles, complex rigging, load judgment, and coordinated signaling. Humanoid robots cannot operate heavy lifting machinery, perform safe load-bearing rigging, or manage dynamic crane loads autonomously in 2026.

task: Transport patients to treatment units, testing units, operating rooms, or other areas, using wheelchairs, stretchers, or moveable beds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient transport trip (origin to destination). Typical hospital transport takes 5–20 min including fetch, transfer, transit, and handoff; median ~12 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safely transferring/positioning a human patient, navigating crowded hospital corridors with elevators and doors, operating specialized transport equipment, and coordinating handoff — far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities in unstructured caregiving and patient handling.

task: Mark cutting lines or identifying information on stock, using marking pencils, rulers, or scribes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking one piece of stock with cut lines/labels. A skilled worker measures, positions a ruler, and scribes/pencils marks in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor control to hold and guide a scribe/pencil along a ruler while reading dimensions accurately. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force sensitivity, and visual precision for consistent, acceptable-quality marking on varied stock materials.

task: Press and adjust controls to activate, set, and regulate equipment according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = activating and adjusting controls on a single piece of equipment to reach target specs (e.g., setting a machine's speed, temperature, or pressure). Typical cycle takes ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Pressing buttons or toggling switches is feasible, but reading analog dials, interpreting varied control panel layouts, fine-tuning knobs to spec, and confirming correct output requires strong perception, generalization, and feedback interpretation — challenging for current humanoids.

task: Examine materials, ingredients, or products, visually or with hands, to ensure conformance to established standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single item/batch at a workstation. A competent inspector visually and tactilely checks one unit in ~5 minutes depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle visual inspection via cameras but struggle with tactile nuance, irregular surfaces, and generalizing across diverse standards. Perception and fine haptic feedback remain limited in frontier humanoids.

task: Clean and lubricate cutting machines, conveyors, blades, saws, or knives, using steam hoses, scrapers, brushes, or oil cans.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one machine/conveyor unit. Worker steams, scrapes, brushes debris, and oils moving parts; ~20–30 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool-switching (steam hose, scraper, brush, oil can), force-controlled scraping on varied geometries, precise lubrication of tight joints, and navigating around industrial machinery — well beyond 2026 frontier robot generalization and dexterity.

task: Adjust machine components to regulate speeds, pressures, and temperatures, and amounts, dimensions, and flow of materials or ingredients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single machine component (e.g., a valve, dial, or lever) to hit a target parameter. A competent operator reads gauges, makes the adjustment, and verifies the result in ~5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting process feedback, and manipulating diverse controls (knobs, valves, levers). Perception of small displays and fine motor control of varied industrial controls are challenging for current humanoids; process understanding is limited.

task: Transport specimens or fluid samples from collection sites to laboratories.
human_time_rationale: One instance = carrying a specimen batch from collection site to lab, including handoff. Typical hospital/clinic transport takes ~15–25 minutes total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex, dynamic hospital environments, operating elevators, handling biohazard containers, and completing handoffs. Indoor mobility is feasible but multi-floor navigation, door/elevator use, and reliable specimen handoff remain inconsistent for 2026 humanoids.

task: Adjust ink fountain flow rates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting flow rates across all ink fountain zones for a single press setup. A skilled press operator takes ~5–10 minutes to dial in all zones.
