robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of narrow ink fountain keys/screws, precise tactile feedback, and color-quality perception to verify results. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control and domain-specific sensing needed.

task: Provide physical support to patients to assist them to perform daily living activities, such as getting out of bed, bathing, dressing, using the toilet, standing, walking, or exercising.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one patient through a typical daily living activity (e.g., bathing or dressing). A competent caregiver spends ~15–35 min depending on the activity; 25 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safe physical contact with a fragile human, fine dexterity (buttons, zippers), dynamic balance support, and adaptive social perception. Current humanoids lack safe compliant force control and generalization for unstructured caregiving bodies/environments.

task: Take site survey photos or photos of work performed, using digital cameras.
human_time_rationale: One instance = photographing a single site or work area. A competent worker walks the area, frames and captures relevant shots, ~15–25 minutes typical.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can locomote and hold cameras, but autonomous scene understanding to know *what* to photograph, appropriate framing, navigating unstructured outdoor/construction sites, and meeting a supervisor's quality standard are major unsolved challenges.

task: Start machines to verify setups, and make any necessary adjustments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting one machine, observing its operation, and making setup adjustments. A competent operator typically spends ~15 min per machine cycle check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating machine controls, interpreting sensory feedback (sound, vibration, visual output), diagnosing deviations, and making fine mechanical adjustments — far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterous tool-use capabilities.

task: Clean and maintain equipment, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/maintaining a single piece of equipment (e.g., a machine or tool) with hand tools; a competent worker typically spends ~30 minutes per item.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool selection, varied dexterous hand-tool use (wrenches, brushes, scrapers), and inspection judgment on unstructured equipment surfaces. Current robots lack reliable tool-switching, fine dexterity, and maintenance-specific generalization.

task: Measure the thickness of the retinal nerve, using scanning laser polarimetry techniques to aid in diagnosis of glaucoma.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient eye exam session. A trained technician/clinician sets up the SLP device, aligns the patient, acquires scans, and reviews output — typically 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise patient positioning, operating a specialized ophthalmic instrument, interpreting clinical imagery, and ensuring patient cooperation. No current humanoid robot has ophthalmic tool-use capability, fine clinical dexterity, or certified diagnostic autonomy.

task: Maintain proper storage and security conditions for drugs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single storage area check/maintenance round: verifying temperature/humidity, checking locks, rotating stock, and logging conditions. A competent pharmacy tech does this in ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading labels, operating secured cabinets/locks, logging data, and exercising regulatory judgment. Current robots lack reliable fine dexterous handling of drug containers, secure-access systems integration, and compliance decision-making.

task: Collect fluid or tissue samples, using appropriate collection procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a single fluid or tissue sample (e.g., blood draw, swab, biopsy). A competent clinician/technician typically completes prep, collection, and labeling in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (needle insertion, swab placement, biopsy), precise force control on compliant human tissue, sterile technique, and patient compliance management — all well beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capabilities.

task: Bolt equipment into place, using hand or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = bolting one piece of equipment into place. Includes alignment, positioning, and driving multiple fasteners with hand or power tools; ~15–25 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of bolt holes, sustained torque with a power tool, and adapting to varied equipment geometries and mounting surfaces. Current humanoids lack reliable torque control, tool-switching, and hole-finding precision for arbitrary equipment.

task: Assist with cremations and the processing and packaging of cremated remains.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full cremation cycle for one individual: loading retort, monitoring combustion (~90 min), then processing and packaging cremated remains (~30 min). Total ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized cremation retort controls, high-heat environment tolerance, fine-motor processing of fragile bone fragments (cremulator), precise packaging, and strict regulatory/chain-of-custody compliance — all far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Melt or refine metal before casting, calculating required temperatures, and observe metal color, adjusting controls as necessary to maintain required temperatures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = melting/refining a single batch of metal to casting temperature. Includes setup, heating, color observation, and temperature adjustment; typically 30–60 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized high-temperature furnace controls, interpreting subtle molten metal color gradients under intense heat/glare, and making precise adjustments. Current humanoids lack heat tolerance, furnace tool-use, and reliable pyrometric visual judgment in industrial foundry environments.

task: Stock utility rooms, nonmedical storage rooms, or cleaning carts with supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restocking a single utility room, storage room, or cleaning cart. A competent worker gathers supplies and organizes them in roughly 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to supply source, identifying/grasping varied items (bottles, rolls, boxes), transporting them, and placing in correct locations. Generalization across unstructured, cluttered environments and diverse object types remains challenging for 2026 humanoids.

task: Record vital signs, such as temperature, blood pressure, pulse, or respiration rate, as directed by medical or nursing staff.
human_time_rationale: One instance = recording a full set of vitals (temp, BP, pulse, respiration) for one patient: ~2–3 min to measure, ~1–2 min to document, plus brief patient interaction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safe patient contact, precise sensor placement (BP cuff, thermometer, pulse ox), patient cooperation management, and accurate EHR documentation. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous tool-on-patient skill and clinical safety certification.

task: Install all necessary transmission equipment components, including antennas or antenna mounts, surge arrestors, transmission lines, connectors, or tower-mounted amplifiers (TMAs).
human_time_rationale: One instance = full installation of transmission equipment on a single tower/site, including antennas, mounts, surge arrestors, lines, connectors, and TMAs. A crew of 2–3 skilled technicians typically takes 3–5 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing or working at height on towers, heavy lifting, precise RF connector torquing, and outdoor unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack the mobility, reach, safety systems, and specialized tool-use for tower work.

task: Clean ink fountains, plates, or printing unit cylinders when press runs are completed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning ink fountains, plates, and cylinders on one press unit after a print run. A skilled press operator typically takes 20–30 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle delicate printing plates, precise wiping of complex curved cylinder surfaces, use of solvents/tools, and navigation around bulky press machinery. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capability reliably and safely.

task: Collect samples of materials or products for laboratory testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a single sample (e.g., from a production line, field site, or storage area), including labeling and packaging. Typically 10–20 minutes for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Sampling requires context-dependent navigation, precise tool use (syringes, swabs, scoops, vials), secure sealing, and labeling — all in unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-use generalization, and domain judgment needed for reliable autonomous sampling.

task: Set temperature, pressure, and time controls, and start conveyers, machines, or pumps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting controls (temperature, pressure, time) and starting equipment for a single production run; a trained operator typically takes ~5 minutes to configure and initiate.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting varied industrial control panels, turning knobs, pressing buttons, and entering values accurately. Panel layouts vary widely; fine dexterity for small dials/keypads and reliable HMI interaction remain challenging for current humanoids.

task: Swab molds with solutions to prevent products from sticking.
human_time_rationale: One instance = swabbing a single mold with release solution. A competent worker applies solution evenly to one mold's interior surfaces in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires controlled wiping of irregular, confined interior surfaces with a swab and liquid solution. Perception of complex mold geometry, compliant tool contact, and even coverage verification challenge current humanoid dexterity and manipulation.

task: Position width gauge blocks between blades, and level blades and insert wedges into frames to secure blades to frames.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning gauge blocks, leveling blades, and inserting/securing wedges into a single frame assembly. A skilled worker takes ~10–20 min per frame.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise gauge block placement between blades, fine leveling judgment, and controlled wedge insertion under specific tolerances — demanding dexterity, tactile feedback, and spatial accuracy well beyond 2026 humanoid capability in unstructured settings.

task: Administer subcutaneous or intramuscular injects, in accordance with licensing restrictions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one injection. A competent nurse/medical assistant preps the site, draws/checks the medication, administers the injection, and disposes safely in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter needle placement on a compliant, moving human body, sterile technique, medication verification, and real-time pain/safety response. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and medical safety standards.

task: Dispose of contaminated sharps, in accordance with applicable laws, standards, and policies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disposing of one batch of sharps (e.g., needles, scalpels) into a certified sharps container, including donning gloves and verifying container seal. Typically 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to safely handle sharps without injury, recognition of contaminated vs. safe items, compliant-motion lid manipulation, regulatory compliance awareness, and high-stakes safety demands. Current humanoids lack the precision and reliability needed.

task: Signal and assist other workers loading or unloading materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one loading/unloading operation (e.g., one truck or pallet). A typical load/unload session with signaling takes ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires real-time spatial awareness, dynamic hand/body signaling, verbal/non-verbal communication, and coordinating with other workers. Current humanoids lack reliable human-intent communication, gesture signaling standards, and safe coordination in busy, unstructured loading environments.

task: Clean dies, arbors, compression chambers, and molds, using swabs, sponges, or air hoses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single set of dies, arbors, compression chambers, and molds using swabs, sponges, or air hoses; a competent worker typically completes this in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of swabs/sponges into tight cavities, precise tool switching (air hose, swab, sponge), and inspection of complex geometries. Current humanoids lack the fine-motor control and contextual judgment for thorough, verified cleaning of intricate tooling.

task: Inspect and pack the final product.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and packing a single finished product unit. A competent worker typically spends ~5 min checking quality and placing the item in packaging.
robot_success_rationale: Visual inspection requires defect-detection perception that frontier humanoids lack reliability in. Packing needs dexterous manipulation of varied packaging materials. Combined task success is low due to perception and fine-motor limits.

task: Inspect and mark completed workpieces and stack them on pallets, in boxes, or on conveyors so that they can be moved to the next workstation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one workpiece: visually inspect, apply a mark/stamp, and stack onto a pallet, box, or conveyor. A competent line worker takes ~2–4 min per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Stacking and conveyor placement are within reach (~40–60%), but applying inspection judgment and physically marking/stamping varied workpieces requires fine dexterity, reliable defect perception, and tool-use generalization that frontier humanoids still struggle with consistently.

task: Move levers, pedals, and throttles to stop, start, and regulate speeds of hoist or winch drums in response to hand, bell, buzzer, telephone, loud-speaker, or whistle signals, or by observing dial indicators or cable marks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one lift/haul cycle from signal receipt to controlled stop. A competent hoist operator typically completes a cycle in ~5–10 minutes depending on load and distance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting multi-modal signals (bells, whistles, loudspeakers, visual cues), operating specialized levers/pedals/throttles in a cab, and real-time load judgment. Humanoid robots cannot reliably operate heavy industrial machinery controls autonomously in unstructured environments.

task: Couple air and gas lines to machines to maintain plasticity of material and to regulate solidification of final products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting air/gas lines to one machine. A competent worker locates fittings, aligns connectors, and secures couplings in ~10–20 minutes depending on line count.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment and torquing of threaded or push-fit couplings, leak-free sealing, and identifying correct ports in cluttered industrial environments. Current robots lack reliable fine dexterity and tool-use for pressurized line coupling.

task: Make adjustments or minor repairs, such as tightening leaking gland or pipe joints.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tightening one leaking joint/gland. A competent plumber or maintenance worker locates, accesses, and tightens the joint in ~15–25 minutes including inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating a leak, identifying the correct fitting, selecting and applying the right wrench with calibrated torque in tight spaces. Poor generalization to varied pipe configurations, confined access, and force-sensitive tightening limits current robots significantly.

task: Activate machines to shape or form products, such as candy bars, light bulbs, balloons, or insulation panels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = activating/setting up a machine for one production run or batch. A competent operator loads materials, sets parameters, and starts the machine in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating industrial floor, identifying correct machine controls, pressing buttons/switches, and possibly loading materials. Generalization across varied machine interfaces and physical interactions is weak; current humanoids lack robust manipulation of industrial control panels and material handling in unstructured factory settings.

task: Cover patients with surgical drapes to create and maintain a sterile operative field.
human_time_rationale: One instance = draping one patient for a single surgery. A skilled scrub tech/OR nurse places and secures all required sterile drapes in roughly 5–12 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Demands strict sterile technique, delicate large-fabric manipulation around a human body, real-time contamination detection, and team coordination. Current humanoids lack sterile-protocol compliance, fine fabric dexterity, and OR-environment generalization.

task: Match laboratory requisition forms to specimen tubes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = matching one requisition form to its corresponding specimen tube(s). A trained lab tech cross-checks labels and form fields in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading printed/handwritten labels on small curved tubes, parsing requisition forms, and cross-referencing data. OCR and vision are improving but small-tube label reading, handwriting, and reliable matching logic in cluttered lab settings remain error-prone for current humanoids.

task: Pour, scoop, or dump specified ingredients, metal assemblies, or mixtures into sections of machine prior to starting machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a single machine section with a specified ingredient/assembly/mixture. A competent worker typically pours, scoops, or dumps material in 1–5 minutes depending on quantity and container type.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and tip containers, but precise pouring into specific machine sections, handling varied bulk materials (powders, liquids, metal parts), and targeting confined openings require dexterity and perception that current humanoids handle inconsistently. Spillage and mis-targeting risk is high.

task: Set up or adjust machine controls to regulate conditions such as material flow, temperature, or pressure.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting controls on a single machine to hit target parameters (flow, temp, or pressure). A competent operator reads gauges and turns knobs/dials/switches in ~5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, identifying correct knobs/valves, making fine adjustments, and verifying feedback. Perception of varied industrial controls and closed-loop fine-tuning remain unreliable for frontier humanoids in unstructured settings.

task: Inspect equipment to ensure that specifications are met or to detect any defects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single piece of equipment. A competent technician typically spends ~20 min checking components, measurements, and documentation against spec.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can visually scan surfaces and detect gross defects, but verifying precise specs requires calibrated tool use, multi-modal sensing, and contextual judgment. Fine-detail defect recognition and instrument reading remain unreliable for frontier humanoids.

task: Dump or pour specified amounts of materials into machinery or equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retrieving a specified material and pouring/dumping the correct amount into a machine/hopper. A competent worker typically completes this in ~3 minutes including measurement and positioning.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping varied containers, measuring/controlling pour quantity, and accurately targeting machinery openings. Perception of fill levels and liquid/granular flow control are challenging; spill risk is high. Improving but unreliable in unstructured settings.

task: Collect and inspect random samples during print runs to identify any necessary adjustments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a sample batch and visually/instrumentally inspecting it mid-run; a competent press operator typically spends ~10 min per check cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a live press environment, grasping freshly printed sheets without damage, performing color/registration inspection, and making judgment calls about adjustments — far beyond current general-purpose robot reliability in dynamic, precision-critical industrial settings.

task: Load equipment receptacles or conveyors with material to be processed, by hand or using hoists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a single batch/cycle of material into a receptacle or conveyor. Typical industrial loading cycle takes ~5–10 minutes depending on material volume and hoist use.
robot_success_rationale: Variable material types, weights, and unstructured receptacle/conveyor layouts challenge perception and manipulation. Hoist operation requires specialized tool-use; heavy/bulk loads exceed humanoid payload limits. Partially feasible for lighter, structured items.

task: Draw blood from veins by vacuum tube, syringe, or butterfly venipuncture methods.
human_time_rationale: One venipuncture instance: prep site, apply tourniquet, locate vein, insert needle, draw blood into tubes, remove needle, apply pressure. Typical clinical time 5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter needle insertion into a moving, compliant vein under skin — extreme fine dexterity and force sensitivity far beyond current humanoid capability. Also requires sterile technique, patient safety compliance, and real-time vascular perception.

task: Measure dimensions of products to verify conformance to specifications, using measuring instruments, such as rulers, calipers, gauges, or micrometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring all key dimensions of one product/part. A competent inspector takes ~5–10 min to select correct instruments, position the part, take multiple readings, and record results.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with precise tool selection, delicate grip of calipers/micrometers, exact positioning of instruments on arbitrary parts, and sub-millimeter measurement accuracy. Perception and fine dexterity are key limiters.

task: Turn controls to adjust machine functions, such as regulating air pressure, creating vacuums, and adjusting coolant flow.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a set of machine controls (knobs/valves) for a single function change; a competent worker reads gauges and turns controls in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Turning knobs and valves requires moderate dexterity and precise torque control. Robots struggle with varied handle shapes, reading analog gauges, and confirming correct functional outcomes (pressure, flow). Generalization across machine types is limited.

task: Examine, measure, and weigh materials or products to verify conformance to standards, using measuring devices such as templates, micrometers, or scales.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single part/product batch using calipers, micrometer, or scale. A competent QC inspector typically takes 5–12 minutes per part depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with precise tool handling (micrometers, calipers require fine dexterity), repositioning parts, and reading analog gauges. Perception of fine measurements and tolerances is unreliable on general-purpose humanoids in 2026.

task: Turn or reposition bedridden patients, alone or with assistance, to prevent bedsores.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repositioning a single bedridden patient once. A competent nurse/aide typically takes 5–10 minutes including preparation, repositioning, and securing pillows/supports.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safe handling of a fragile, uncooperative/passive human body on a compliant surface. Current robots lack the force control, body-awareness, and soft-tissue sensitivity needed; high injury risk makes autonomous execution infeasible.

task: Inspect and measure workpieces to mark for cuts and to verify the accuracy of cuts, using rulers, squares, or caliper rules.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and marking a single workpiece. A skilled worker uses rulers/squares/calipers to inspect, measure, and mark in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle calipers/squares precisely, accurate perception of measurement markings, and controlled marking on varied surfaces. Current humanoids lack reliable sub-millimeter manipulation and tool-reading accuracy needed for acceptable quality.

task: Remove materials or products from molds or from extruding, forming, pressing, or compacting machines, and stack or store them for additional processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a single part/batch from a mold or machine and stacking/storing it. A trained operator typically takes 1–3 minutes per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place from known fixtures is feasible, but variable part geometries, heat, flash/burrs, tight mold extraction forces, and precise stacking in industrial settings challenge current humanoids' dexterity and generalization.

task: Pour, dump, or load prescribed quantities of ingredients or products into cooking equipment, manually or using a hoist.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and loading a single prescribed ingredient batch into cooking equipment; includes positioning and pouring/dumping, ~3–7 min per load.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pouring of varied ingredients (liquids, powders, solids), handling heavy containers or a hoist, and navigating industrial kitchen layouts. Generalization across vessel shapes/weights and hoist operation challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Inspect completed work to ensure all hardware is tight, antennas are level, hangers are properly fastened, proper support is in place, or adequate weather proofing has been installed.
human_time_rationale: One inspection instance covers a completed installation site (e.g., a single antenna/telecom mount). A competent technician visually and manually checks all elements in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing/navigating telecom structures, torque-testing hardware, assessing antenna levelness, and judging weatherproofing quality — all in unstructured outdoor elevated environments. Current robots lack reliable climbing, force-sensing inspection, and contextual pass/fail judgment at height.

task: Operate auxiliary machines and equipment, such as grinders, canners, and molding presses, to prepare or further process products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = operating a machine for one production run/batch cycle (setup, monitoring, adjustments, shutdown). Typical batch cycle for grinders, canners, or molding presses is ~15–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating varied specialized industrial machines (controls, dials, levers, loading hoppers), monitoring product quality, and making adjustments. Current humanoids lack reliable generalization across diverse auxiliary machinery controls, process sensing, and safe intervention skills.

task: Discard or reject products, materials, or equipment not meeting specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and physically discarding/rejecting one item. A competent worker quickly checks against spec and removes/tags the item, typically 1–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires perception to detect defects vs. specs (variable lighting, surface finish, geometry), decision-making on pass/fail thresholds, and physical rejection action. Vision-based defect detection is improving but generalizing across product types and subtle flaws remains unreliable; manipulation to discard is feasible but combined autonomy is limited.

task: Set up or operate auxiliary equipment, such as cutting, folding, plate-making, drilling, or laminating machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up OR operating one auxiliary machine for a single job run (e.g., loading a cutting machine, adjusting settings, and running a batch). Typical print/bindery job setup takes 15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial machines (levers, dials, panels, feeders), interpreting job specs, loading media precisely, and adjusting parameters. Humanoids lack the machine-specific tool-use, interface reading, and fine adjustment dexterity for autonomous success.

task: Dump, spread, and tamp asphalt, using pneumatic tampers, to repair joints and patch broken pavement.
human_time_rationale: One instance = patching one pavement section/joint. A crew member dumps, spreads, and tamps asphalt on a typical pothole or joint repair, taking ~30–60 min depending on size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy outdoor equipment (dump vehicle, pneumatic tamper), navigating unstructured road environments, precise spreading and tamping force — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and outdoor mobility capabilities.

task: Open valves or start pumps, agitators, reactors, blowers, or automatic feed of materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = opening a valve or starting a single piece of equipment. Competent operator locates the control, checks status, and actuates it in ~5 minutes including safety checks.
robot_success_rationale: Industrial valves and controls vary widely in type, torque, and layout. Humanoids can turn simple knobs/wheels but struggle with high-torque valves, non-standard handles, panel buttons, and confirming correct operation via gauges. Generalization and safety compliance are major gaps.

task: Stock or issue medical supplies, such as dressing packs or treatment trays.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restocking a single supply area (e.g., a drawer, shelf, or cart) with a batch of medical supplies; a competent worker takes ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Medical supply items vary greatly in shape, packaging, and fragility. Robots struggle with sterile handling protocols, reading labels reliably, navigating cluttered clinical storage areas, and correctly identifying/placing varied items without errors.

task: Install or repair tower lighting components, including strobes, beacons, or lighting controllers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing or repairing one lighting component (strobe, beacon, or controller) on a tower. A skilled tower technician typically takes 1–2 hours including climb, swap/repair, and descent.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing tall towers (extreme locomotion challenge), fine electrical work (connector mating, wiring), tool use at height, and fault diagnosis. No current humanoid robot can safely climb communication towers or operate in such unstructured elevated environments.

task: Mix cleaning solutions, abrasive compositions, or other compounds, according to formulas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing a single batch per formula. A competent worker measures, combines, and verifies one batch of compound in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid/powder measurement, reading labels, operating dispensers, and following multi-step formulas. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation, accurate volumetric dispensing, and chemical-context understanding needed for consistent, safe batching.

task: Clean, adjust, and maintain equipment, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning, adjusting, and maintaining a single piece of equipment. A competent technician typically takes ~30 min per unit for routine maintenance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct tools, applying precise torque, diagnosing adjustments, and navigating varied equipment geometry. Current robots lack reliable fine dexterity, tool-switching, and diagnostic judgment for unstructured maintenance tasks.

task: Observe equipment gauges and indicators and hand signals of other workers to verify load positions or depths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single check of gauges, indicators, and hand signals to verify one load position/depth; a skilled worker scans and interprets all cues in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires simultaneous reading of physical gauges, indicator lights, AND interpreting human hand signals in dynamic, noisy worksites. Current robots lack reliable multi-modal perception integration, hand-gesture recognition in uncontrolled environments, and contextual safety judgment needed here.

task: Adjust, clean, or repair products or processing equipment to correct defects found during inspections.
human_time_rationale: One instance = identifying and correcting a single defect on a product or piece of processing equipment; a competent technician typically spends ~25 min diagnosing and fixing one defect.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing varied defect types, selecting correct tools, applying precise repairs, and validating fixes across unstructured settings. Current robots lack the generalization, fine dexterity, and tool-use reasoning for autonomous repair tasks.

task: Inspect equipment or units to detect leaks or malfunctions, shutting equipment down, if necessary.
human_time_rationale: One inspection round of a typical equipment unit — visually checking, listening, and using sensors for leaks/malfunctions, then deciding on shutdown. ~15–25 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can walk to equipment and use cameras/sensors, but detecting subtle leaks (gas, fluid, sound anomalies), interpreting malfunction signs in unstructured industrial settings, and safely executing emergency shutdown procedures remain very challenging for 2026 general-purpose humanoids.

task: Drive vehicles to or from workshops or customers' workplaces or homes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one trip (pickup or delivery drive). Typical local automotive service drive is ~15–35 min round-trip; median ~25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate passenger vehicles in traffic. L4 AVs exist but are narrow-route systems, not general-purpose humanoids. Task requires full road driving capability — far beyond 2026 humanoid robot scope.

task: Climb towers to access components, using safety equipment, such as full-body harnesses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = climbing a tower to reach a target component and returning. Includes donning harness, ascending, accessing the component, and descending — typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Current humanoids cannot safely climb tall lattice/communication towers, cannot don/use fall-arrest harnesses, lack the grip strength and dynamic balance for ladder climbing under load, and have no certified safety compliance for aerial work at height.

task: Climb ladders to position and set up vehicle-mounted derricks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = climbing to and fully setting up one vehicle-mounted derrick; a competent rigger typically takes 30–60 minutes including stabilization and rigging checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing industrial ladders on an outdoor vehicle, operating heavy specialized derrick controls, and performing safety-critical rigging — all far beyond current humanoid locomotion, dexterity, and tool-use capabilities in unstructured outdoor environments.

task: Clean and polish vehicle windows.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and polishing all windows of a single standard passenger vehicle (interior + exterior). A competent detailer takes ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires mobility around an outdoor vehicle, applying consistent pressure across curved glass, streak-free wiping, and reaching high/low angles. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and generalization for streak-free glass polishing on varied vehicle shapes.

task: Collect and transport infectious or hazardous waste in closed containers for sterilization or disposal, in accordance with applicable law, standards, or policies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting and transporting a round of infectious/hazardous waste containers within a facility (e.g., a hospital floor or lab). Includes donning PPE, sealing containers, loading a cart, and delivering to a sterilization/disposal point.
robot_success_rationale: Requires donning PPE, recognizing biohazard containers in varied locations, sealing/latching lids correctly, navigating facility safely, and strict compliance with protocols. Current humanoids lack reliable PPE handling, regulatory-grade compliance assurance, and safe biohazard awareness.

task: Tend auxiliary equipment, such as jacks, slings, cables, or stop blocks, to facilitate moving items or materials for further processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up/adjusting one piece of auxiliary equipment (jack, sling, cable, or stop block) to facilitate a single move or processing step. A competent worker takes ~5–10 minutes per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling heavy, irregular rigging equipment (slings, cables, jacks) in varied industrial environments. High force demands, complex attachment points, and safety-critical precision exceed current humanoid dexterity and generalization. Mobility on industrial floors is manageable but manipulation is the bottleneck.

task: Measure arbors and dies to verify sizes specified on work tickets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring one arbor or die with a micrometer/caliper and verifying it matches the work ticket spec. A competent machinist completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (micrometer/caliper), reading a work ticket, and precise part handling. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter manipulation precision and reliable instrument-reading needed for quality-acceptable measurement verification.

task: Install, align, and adjust neck rings, press plungers, and feeder tubes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full setup of neck rings, press plungers, and feeder tubes on one machine/mold station; a skilled glassware technician typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-tolerance alignment of hot, heavy, oddly shaped industrial components inside a glass-forming machine. Demands specialized tool use, high-force torquing, thermal awareness, and expert tactile feedback far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Examine, measure, and weigh materials or products to verify conformance to specifications, using measuring devices, such as rulers, micrometers, or scales.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single part/product: visually examine, take 2–3 measurements with calipers/micrometer, and record a weight reading. ~5 min for a competent QC worker.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick up objects and use scales, but precisely wielding micrometers/calipers on varied parts requires fine dexterity and tool manipulation that 2026 humanoids handle poorly. Visual inspection generalization is also limited without specialized vision pipelines.

task: Determine paint flow, viscosity, and coating quality by performing visual inspections, or by using viscometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and measuring a single paint batch/sample. Visual check plus viscometer reading takes a competent technician ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dexterous tool use (viscometer insertion, reading analog/digital gauges), nuanced visual perception of coating quality (sheen, drips, coverage), and fluid handling — all challenging for current humanoids. Perception of subtle coating defects is especially difficult.

task: Move or reposition hoists, winches, loads and materials, manually or using equipment and machines such as trucks, cars, and hand trucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repositioning a single hoist, winch, or load to a new location. Includes rigging, operating equipment, and securing. Typical move takes ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating trucks, hand trucks, and hoisting equipment — vehicles and heavy machinery humanoids cannot drive or operate. Rigging and load control demand high strength, specialized tool-use, and safety judgment far beyond current robot capability.

task: Clean, wash, and sterilize equipment and cooking area, using water hoses, cleaning or sterilizing solutions, or rinses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full cleaning cycle of a commercial cooking station and its equipment (surfaces, tools, appliances). A competent worker typically takes 25–35 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling hoses, applying varied solutions, scrubbing irregular surfaces, and verifying sterilization standards. Current robots lack reliable wet-environment dexterity, hose management, and quality-verification judgment needed for food-safety compliance.

task: Move stock or scrap to and from machines manually, or by using carts, handtrucks, or lift trucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving a load (stock or scrap) between a machine and a staging area. A competent worker loads, transports, and unloads in ~10 minutes including equipment use.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating variable factory floors, operating carts/handtrucks/lift trucks, and handling diverse stock shapes/weights. Humanoids lack reliable vehicle operation and robust generalization in cluttered industrial environments.

task: Design original or special bindings for limited editions or other custom binding projects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and executing one custom bookbinding project. A skilled bookbinder spends roughly 4–8 hours on a single limited-edition or special binding, including design, material prep, and hand-finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative design judgment, fine dexterous craft (hand-sewing, tooling leather, gold-stamping, inlaying), aesthetic evaluation, and open-ended problem solving. Far beyond current robot dexterity, tool-use, and generalization limits.

task: Turn dials, handwheels, valves, or switches to regulate conveyor speeds, machine temperature, air pressure and circulation, and the flow or spray of coatings or paints.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single control (dial, valve, switch) to reach a target setting; a competent operator locates and turns it in ~1–5 minutes including reading gauges.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can turn knobs/valves in structured settings but struggle with varied control types (handwheels, small dials, toggle switches), reading analog gauges for feedback, and fine torque control needed to hit precise setpoints reliably.

task: Clear jams, and remove defective or substandard materials or products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing a single jam or removing one batch of defective material from a machine or line. A competent worker typically resolves this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Jam clearing requires diagnosing irregular, unpredictable configurations, applying variable force, and navigating tight machine spaces. Current humanoids lack reliable fault diagnosis, fine dexterity in confined areas, and safe force control for such unstructured scenarios.

task: Remove cooked material or products from equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a batch of cooked items from one piece of equipment (e.g., oven, fryer, grill). A competent worker takes ~3–7 minutes including handling tools and placing items.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heat-safe tool use (tongs, trays, ladles), recognizing doneness/readiness, navigating kitchen clutter, and handling varied soft/fragile cooked items without damage. Current robots lack reliable dexterity and thermal awareness for this.

task: Sharpen cutting blades, knives, or saws, using files, bench grinders, or honing stones.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sharpening a single blade/knife/saw. A competent worker spends ~10–20 min inspecting, selecting the right tool, and working the edge to acceptable sharpness.
robot_success_rationale: Sharpening requires precise, consistent angle control, tactile feedback, and fine dexterity across varied blade geometries. Current humanoids lack the force/angle precision and adaptive feedback loops needed for acceptable edge quality.

task: Clean or oil presses or make minor repairs, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/oiling or minor repair of a single press. A competent worker typically takes 20–40 min depending on press size and condition.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating industrial equipment, identifying lubrication points, applying oil precisely, and using hand tools for minor repairs — all in unstructured settings. Current humanoids lack fine tool-use, contextual diagnosis, and safe manipulation around heavy machinery.

task: Obtain or mix inks and fill ink fountains.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and filling ink fountain(s) for one press run. Skilled operator spends ~15 min mixing/matching ink color and loading the fountain.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise color-matching (vision + chemistry knowledge), handling viscous fluids, operating specialized ink mixing equipment, and filling narrow press fountains — well beyond current humanoid dexterity and domain generalization.

task: Draw blood from capillaries by dermal puncture, such as heel or finger stick methods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = single dermal puncture blood draw (fingerstick or heel stick), from site prep through collection and bandaging. A skilled phlebotomist completes this in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter precision to puncture skin at correct depth/angle, sterile technique, patient interaction/restraint, and lancet/capillary tube manipulation. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and safety standards for medical procedures.

task: Feed paper through press cylinders and adjust feed and tension controls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = threading paper through press cylinders and tuning feed/tension controls for one print job setup; a skilled press operator takes ~5–15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual threading of thin, flexible paper through tight roller gaps, fine dial/lever adjustments, and real-time tension sensing. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, compliance control, and domain-specific perception for this complex task.

task: Start engines of hoists or winches and use levers and pedals to wind or unwind cable on drums.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one hoist/winch operation cycle (start engine, engage controls, wind/unwind cable to complete a lift or pull). Typical cycle takes ~5–15 minutes depending on load and distance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized heavy machinery with vehicle-like controls (levers, pedals, engine start). Humanoid robots cannot safely or reliably operate such equipment autonomously; controls are non-standard, loads are hazardous, and generalization is extremely limited.

task: Repair, restore, or rebind old, rare, or damaged books, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/restoring one book. A competent bookbinder/conservator typically spends 2–4 hours on a single damaged or rare volume, depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity with hand tools (bone folders, needles, knives, brushes), delicate force control on fragile materials, nuanced visual/tactile assessment of damage, and expert sequencing — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Disassemble and reassemble machines or equipment or remove and reattach vehicle parts or trim, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassemble and reassemble one machine/equipment unit or one vehicle part/trim panel. A competent mechanic typically takes 30–60 min for a moderate-complexity component.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying varied fasteners, applying torque, repositioning in tight spaces, and generalizing across machine types. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tool-change ability, and unstructured-environment generalization needed for full autonomous success.

task: Pre-soak or rinse machine parts, equipment, or vehicles by immersing objects in cleaning solutions or water, manually or using hoists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = soaking/rinsing one batch of parts or one equipment unit. Includes positioning, immersing, and monitoring soak time; ~10–20 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires lifting/submerging varied, often heavy/greasy parts into liquid-filled tanks, using hoists, and handling wet/slippery objects. Hoist operation, fluid handling, and unstructured part geometry challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Press buttons to activate cleaning equipment or machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pressing the required button(s) to activate a single piece of cleaning equipment (e.g., a floor scrubber, washer, or dishwasher). Takes only seconds to under a minute.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to machines and press large, clearly marked buttons reasonably well, but variability in button size, type (toggle, membrane, recessed), labeling, and panel layout reduces reliability. Fine finger control and panel perception remain limiting factors.

task: Compare colors, shapes, textures, or grades of products or materials with color charts, templates, or samples to verify conformance to standards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single product/material sample against a reference standard. A competent QC worker typically takes 2–4 minutes per item including retrieval, comparison, and recording.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can perform basic visual color/shape inspection in structured settings, but nuanced texture grading, gloss variation, and subtle hue matching against physical charts require fine perceptual judgment and context generalization that current systems handle poorly. Physical manipulation of samples and charts adds further complexity.

task: Stitch or glue endpapers, bindings, backings, or signatures, using sewing machines, glue machines, or glue and brushes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing one book's endpapers/binding/signatures. A skilled bookbinder takes ~10–20 min per book for gluing/stitching steps.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous manipulation of thin, flexible paper/fabric materials, tool-switching (brush, glue machine, sewing), and consistent pressure/alignment. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control and tactile feedback needed for acceptable quality.

task: Transport materials and products to and from work areas, manually or using carts, handtrucks, or hoists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single transport trip (load, move, unload) between two work areas; typical in warehouse/factory settings, ~5–10 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Flat indoor navigation is feasible (~85%), but loading varied materials, operating handtrucks/hoists, navigating crowded dynamic work areas, and unloading reliably reduces success significantly. Tool-use and generalization remain limiting factors.

task: Start presses and pull proofs to check for ink coverage and density, alignment, and registration.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting a press, pulling one proof sheet, and visually/instrumentally checking ink coverage, density, alignment, and registration. A skilled press operator typically takes 15–25 minutes per setup check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating industrial printing press controls, pulling a physical proof, then performing nuanced quality inspection (densitometer use, fine registration judgment). Current humanoids lack press operation capability, fine tool use, and reliable print quality perception.

task: Fit boot spoilers, side skirts, or mud flaps to cars.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting one set of spoilers, side skirts, or mud flaps to one car. Includes positioning, drilling/clipping, and fastening; ~30–60 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating around a full vehicle, precise alignment of large awkward panels, drilling, torquing fasteners, and quality judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, reach envelope, force control, and tool-use reliability for this unstructured automotive task.

task: Prepare finished books for shipping by wrapping or packing books and stacking boxes on pallets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wrapping/packing one book and contributing to stacking one box on a pallet. A competent warehouse worker cycles through this in ~3 minutes per book.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of flat/uniform books is manageable, but wrapping with film/paper requires dexterous manipulation. Pallet stacking needs precise placement and lifting of heavy boxes. Generalization across book sizes and packing materials reduces reliability.

task: Collect specimens, such as urine, feces, or sputum.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a single specimen (e.g., urine cup, stool sample, or sputum jar) from one patient, including labeling and storage. ~5–15 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced patient interaction, hygiene protocols, fine dexterous manipulation of containers near a person's body, and handling of biohazardous materials. Current humanoids lack safe patient-contact dexterity and compliance with clinical handling standards.

task: Scrub, scrape, or spray machine parts, equipment, or vehicles, using scrapers, brushes, clothes, cleaners, disinfectants, insecticides, acid, abrasives, vacuums, or hoses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one machine, piece of equipment, or vehicle. A competent worker typically spends 15–35 minutes scrubbing, scraping, or spraying a single item depending on size and soiling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool-switching (scrapers, brushes, hoses, sprayers), high-force scrubbing, and navigating around irregular machinery/vehicle surfaces. Current humanoids lack the force control, tool-use generalization, and mobility for unstructured industrial cleaning environments.

task: Change soiled linens, such as bed linens, drapes, or cubicle curtains.
human_time_rationale: One instance = changing linens on one bed or one set of drapes/curtains. A competent worker strips, replaces, and tidies one unit in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires manipulating large, flexible fabric (sheets, drapes) around obstacles, fitting corners, navigating cluttered spaces, and handling soiled material safely. Current humanoids lack reliable bimanual soft-fabric dexterity and contextual judgment for this at acceptable quality.

task: Weigh or measure specified amounts of ingredients or materials for processing, using devices such as scales and calipers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single specified ingredient/material. A competent worker reads the spec, selects the container, places it on a scale or uses calipers, and records or transfers the result in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick objects and read digital scales via vision, but reliably scooping/pouring precise amounts, zeroing a tare, and handling varied containers and materials (powders, liquids, small parts) with caliper-level dexterity remains inconsistent on frontier humanoids.

task: Set up or operate bindery machines, such as coil binders, thermal or tape binders, plastic comb binders, or specialty binders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running one binding job (e.g., a batch of ~25 documents). A skilled bindery operator takes ~5–10 min to configure the machine and bind a typical small batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise machine setup (dial/knob adjustments, feeding sheets into narrow slots, threading coils, applying heat), fine dexterity with thin paper stacks, and tool-mode switching. Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation and task-specific tool fluency needed.

task: Punch holes in and fasten paper sheets, signatures, or other material, using hand or machine punches and staplers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = punching and fastening a single set/stack of sheets. A competent worker aligns, punches, and staples a small document set in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise paper alignment, gripping thin flexible sheets, operating a hole punch or stapler with exact force/positioning. Dexterity and fine manipulation of flimsy materials remain major challenges for 2026 humanoids.

task: Replace worn or defective equipment parts, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing a single worn/defective part (e.g. a belt, bolt, valve, or filter) using hand tools. Includes diagnosis, removal, and reinstallation; ~30 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying the worn part, selecting correct hand tools, applying precise torque/force in constrained spaces, and validating the fix. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use dexterity, contextual fault recognition, and generalization across varied equipment types.

task: Transport equipment to work sites, using utility trucks and equipment trailers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one transport trip (loading, driving to work site, unloading). Typical round trip including hookup and transit is ~60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a truck with trailer on public/site roads — humanoid robots cannot operate vehicles in traffic. Hitching a trailer, securing loads, and navigating varied terrain add further unsolved challenges. L4 AVs are narrow-domain systems, not general-purpose robots.

task: Monitor machine operations and observe lights and gauges to detect malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring round of a machine station, scanning gauges and indicator lights. A competent operator typically completes one round in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read clearly labeled digital displays and detect obvious indicator lights, but interpreting analog gauges, subtle anomalies, sounds/smells, and contextual malfunctions in unstructured industrial environments remains unreliable. Generalization and noise tolerance are limiting factors.

task: Press control buttons to activate machinery and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pressing the required control button(s) to activate a single piece of machinery; a competent worker locates and presses the button in under a minute.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can visually locate and press large, clearly labeled buttons on flat panels, but varied button types (toggles, recessed, membrane, illuminated), cluttered panels, and required force precision reduce reliability significantly in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Move materials, supplies, components, and finished products between storage and work areas, using work aids such as racks, hoists, and handtrucks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving a batch of materials/supplies from storage to a work area (or vice versa). A competent worker locates, loads, transports, and delivers one batch in ~8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with unstructured warehouse layouts, varied item types/weights, and operating wheeled aids (handtrucks, hoists). Pick-and-place of known objects is improving, but generalization across mixed loads and dynamic environments remains limited.

task: Paint a variety of objects related to electrical functions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = painting one electrical component (e.g., junction box, conduit, panel cover). A competent painter preps and applies paint to a single mid-sized object in ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Holding and maneuvering a paint brush or spray gun with consistent coverage on varied 3D electrical objects (curved conduits, box edges) is challenging for current humanoids; precision, tool-switching, and avoiding overspray limit success.

task: Set up or operate glue machines by filling glue reservoirs, turning switches to activate heating elements, or adjusting glue flow or conveyor speed.
human_time_rationale: One setup instance: filling reservoir, activating heating elements, and dialing in flow/speed settings before a production run — typically 5–15 minutes for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires pouring viscous glue into a reservoir (messy, precision pour), manipulating specific industrial knobs/switches, and reading/adjusting analog or digital dials. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation for fluid handling and machine-specific control interfaces.

task: Lubricate machinery, vehicles, or equipment or perform minor repairs or adjustments, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lubricating or adjusting a single machine/vehicle component (e.g., greasing a bearing, tightening a fitting). A competent worker completes this in ~20–30 min including inspection and cleanup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to equipment, identifying lubrication points, applying grease guns or oil cans precisely, and using hand tools in tight/unstructured spaces. Fine dexterity, tool-use generalization, and fault detection remain well beyond 2026 frontier robots.

task: Monitor environmental factors, such as humidity and temperature, that may impact equipment performance and make necessary adjustments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring round: reading sensors/gauges across a facility zone, assessing readings, and making adjustments (e.g., HVAC, vents). Typical round takes ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read digital displays and navigate facilities, but interpreting multi-factor environmental context, diagnosing causes, and physically adjusting HVAC controls or mixed analog/manual equipment requires generalization and tool-use well beyond current reliable capability.

task: Read dials or meters to verify that equipment is functioning at specified levels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reading a single dial/meter on a piece of equipment and verifying it against a specified range. A competent worker locates, reads, and logs the value in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to equipment and use vision to read digital displays, but analog dial recognition in varied lighting, angles, and occlusion remains unreliable. Logging and pass/fail judgment adds further complexity.

task: Sweep, shovel, or vacuum loose debris or salvageable scrap into containers and remove containers from work areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing debris from a typical work area. A competent worker sweeps/vacuums loose debris, fills containers, and removes them in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Sweeping/shoveling unstructured debris requires tool use, varied surfaces, and navigating cluttered worksites. Container transport demands gripping heavy, irregular loads and navigating exits. Perception of scattered scrap is challenging; success rate is low but non-trivial.

task: Install, connect, or test underground or aboveground grounding systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing or testing one grounding point/rod. A skilled electrician typically takes 60–120 min including digging, driving rod, clamping, and testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/underground digging, driving ground rods with a hammer, making precise electrical connections with specialized tools, and using test equipment — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, strength, and outdoor unstructured terrain capability.

task: Operate engines and pumps to shut off wells according to production schedules, and to switch flow of oil into storage tanks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a scheduled well shut-off and tank switchover cycle; a competent pump operator typically walks the site, checks gauges, operates valves/controls, and confirms flow diversion in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating outdoor oilfield terrain, reading analog/digital gauges, operating heavy industrial valves and pump controls, and interpreting production schedules. Current humanoids lack the specialized tool-use, outdoor mobility, and process knowledge for autonomous oilfield operations.

task: Form book bodies by folding and sewing printed sheets to form signatures and assembling signatures in numerical order.
human_time_rationale: One instance = forming one book body (folding, sewing, and collating signatures for a typical ~200-page book). A skilled bookbinder takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise sheet folding, fine needle-and-thread sewing through paper, and ordered collation of limp signatures — demanding dexterity, tactile feedback, and sequencing far beyond current humanoid capability. Near-zero success probability.

task: Clean work areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single workstation/work area (wiping surfaces, tidying, sweeping/vacuuming). A competent worker typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep, mop, and wipe flat surfaces reasonably well, but cluttered work areas require object recognition, repositioning items, and judgment about what to clean vs. preserve — challenging for current humanoids.

task: Select loads or materials according to weight and size specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting a single load/material item matching weight/size specs. A worker reads specs, identifies, and retrieves the correct item in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can perceive size via vision but weight estimation is unreliable without weighing tools. Varied item shapes, cluttered environments, and spec interpretation reduce success. Improving but not reliable in unstructured settings.

task: Place coated workpieces in ovens or dryers for specified times to dry or harden finishes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a batch of coated workpieces into an oven/dryer and setting the timer. A competent worker does this in ~5 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of workpieces is feasible, but variability in workpiece shapes, wet/coated surfaces (fragile finish), oven door operation, precise placement, and setting controls reduce reliability significantly for a general-purpose humanoid.

task: Oil winch drums so that cables will wind smoothly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = oiling one winch drum. A competent worker locates the drum, applies oil/lubricant to the drum surface and flanges, and checks for smooth operation — roughly 10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to industrial winch equipment (often outdoors or in confined spaces), identifying lubrication points, handling an oil can with precision, and applying controlled amounts. Poor generalization to varied machinery, tight spaces, and unstructured industrial environments limits current robots severely.

task: Clean surfaces of workpieces in preparation for coating, using cleaning fluids, solvents, brushes, scrapers, steam, sandpaper, or cloth.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one workpiece surface before coating. A competent worker spends ~15 min selecting appropriate cleaning method, applying fluids/abrasives, and inspecting for contamination.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool selection (solvent, brush, scraper, sandpaper) based on workpiece material and contamination type, variable force control, and quality inspection. Dexterity and generalization limits make consistent, acceptable-quality surface prep unlikely in unstructured settings.

task: Bind new books, using hand tools such as bone folders, knives, hammers, or brass binding tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = binding one book. A competent hand bookbinder takes ~1.5–3 hours for a standard case or sewn binding, depending on complexity; 120 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Bookbinding demands extreme fine dexterity (folding signatures, sewing, precise knife cuts, bone-folder creasing, gluing thin paper). Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter precision, tactile feedback, and multi-tool sequencing required. Essentially beyond 2026 frontier capability.

task: Turn valves or start pumps to add ingredients or drain products from equipment and to transfer products for storage, cooling, or further processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = turning a valve or starting a pump for a single transfer operation. A competent operator typically locates, identifies, and actuates the correct valve/pump in ~5 minutes including verification.
robot_success_rationale: Industrial valves vary greatly in type, torque, and location; robots struggle with high-torque rotary valves, awkward orientations, and locating the correct valve in a complex piping environment. Pump controls may be panel-mounted. Perception and generalization are limiting factors.

task: Remove molds, mold components, and feeder tubes from machinery after production is complete.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing molds, components, and feeder tubes from one machine after a production run. A skilled worker using hand tools typically takes 20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force torque application, fine dexterity with tools (wrenches, clamps), handling heavy/hot mold components in cluttered machinery, and precise spatial reasoning in confined industrial spaces — well beyond 2026 frontier humanoid capabilities.

task: Set up or operate machines that perform binding operations, such as pressing, folding, or trimming.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up or completing a binding run (e.g., one batch of booklets). Setup takes ~10–20 min; ongoing operation is measured per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial binding machinery with precise controls, adjustments, and material feeding. Current humanoids lack the tool-use proficiency, fine motor calibration, and domain-specific machine-operation skills needed for reliable autonomous use.

task: Hold or position spray guns to direct spray onto articles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = spraying one article/part (e.g. a panel or component). A competent worker typically takes ~5–10 min including positioning, spraying, and repositioning for coverage.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool-holding, controlled wrist/arm motion, consistent standoff distance, and uniform coverage path. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control, spray-path planning, and surface-tracking perception needed for acceptable finish quality.

task: Install or adjust bindery machine devices, such as knives, guides, rollers, rounding forms, creasing rams, or clamps, to accommodate sheets, signatures, or books of specified sizes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up/adjusting all relevant devices on a bindery machine for one job specification. A skilled operator typically takes 20–50 minutes per changeover/setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine-force-controlled installation of precision industrial components (knives, clamps, rollers), reading machine specs, and calibrated adjustment to tight tolerances. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, torque sensing, and domain-specific tool-use for reliable unsupervised bindery setup.

task: Clean and help maintain equipment or work areas and sterilize glassware, according to prescribed methods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a lab work area and sterilizing a typical batch of glassware; a competent lab tech takes ~30 min including wiping surfaces, organizing, and running an autoclave/dishwasher cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Lab environments demand precise handling of fragile glassware, strict sterilization protocols, and fine manipulation (loading autoclaves, pipettes, flasks). Current humanoids lack the dexterity, protocol awareness, and contamination-control reliability needed for autonomous lab sterilization.

task: Start and stop operation of machines, using levers or buttons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pressing a lever or button to start or stop a single machine; a competent worker completes this in roughly 1 minute including walking to the control panel.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can walk to panels and press clearly marked buttons, but variability in button types, force thresholds, panel heights, and confirming correct machine response reduces reliability. Perception and gross manipulation are adequate; fine identification of correct control is limiting.

task: Remove defects, such as chips, burrs, or lap corroded or pitted surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing surface defects from a single workpiece (e.g., metal part). A skilled worker inspects and finishes one part using files, grinders, or abrasives in ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine tactile feedback, precise force control, and defect identification via perception. Current humanoids lack the dexterity and adaptive tool-use needed for nuanced material removal without damaging the part.

task: Make minor adjustments to equipment, such as turning setscrews to calibrate instruments to required tolerances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single setscrew or calibration point on one instrument until it meets tolerance spec. A skilled technician typically takes 5–15 minutes per adjustment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor dexterity to locate, engage, and micro-turn a tiny setscrew to exact tolerance. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter fingertip force control, torque sensing, and closed-loop feedback needed for calibrated instrument adjustment.

task: Clean semiconductor wafers using cleaning equipment, such as chemical baths, automatic wafer cleaners, or blow-off wands.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a batch of wafers through a full cleaning cycle (chemical bath, rinse, dry/blow-off). A competent technician typically takes 15–25 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Semiconductor cleaning demands extreme precision, contamination control, and operation of specialized equipment (chemical baths, automated cleaners, blow-off wands) in cleanroom environments. Current humanoid robots lack the fine dexterity, chemical-safe handling, and integration with fab-specific equipment required.

task: Perform routine maintenance on vehicles and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one routine maintenance session (e.g., oil change, fluid check, filter swap) on a single vehicle; a competent mechanic typically takes 45–75 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Routine vehicle maintenance requires fine dexterity (draining oil, swapping filters, tightening bolts to spec), working in confined/awkward spaces, tool selection, and hazardous fluid handling — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured real-world environments.

task: Apply paints, dyes, polishes, reconditioners, waxes, or masking materials to vehicles to preserve, protect, or restore color or condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating one vehicle (e.g., full wax/polish/reconditioner application). A competent detailer typically takes 1.5–2.5 hours for a full treatment; 2 hrs is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, force-sensitive surface contact over complex curved body panels, masking fine edges, and uniform coating — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and compliance control. No deployed system handles this reliably.

task: Remove products from equipment, manually or using hoists, and prepare them for storage, shipment, or additional processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a batch of products from one piece of equipment and staging them. A competent worker typically takes 10–20 min depending on product size and downstream step.
robot_success_rationale: Task combines varied grasping, hoist operation, and context-switching (storage/shipment/processing prep). Humanoids lack reliable hoist/tool interfacing, struggle with heavy/irregular items, and have limited generalization across equipment types.

task: Select and mix ingredients to prepare coating substances according to specifications, using paddles or mechanical mixers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one batch of coating substance. A competent worker measures/selects ingredients and mixes to spec in ~15–25 minutes depending on batch size and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise ingredient measurement, reading specs/labels, pouring, and operating mechanical mixers or paddles. Complex sequencing, liquid/powder handling, and quality verification (viscosity, consistency) far exceed current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Adjust and maintain operating room temperature, humidity, or lighting, according to surgeon's specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting OR environmental controls (temp, humidity, or lighting) per surgeon request. A competent OR technician locates the control panel and makes the adjustment in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a sterile OR, locating specific control panels or interfaces, and making precise adjustments. Current humanoids struggle with novel panel interfaces, sterility constraints, and confirming settings meet surgical specs reliably.

task: Observe machine gauges and equipment operation to detect defects or deviations from standards, and make adjustments as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring round/check of a machine or workstation, including reading gauges, assessing operation, and making minor adjustments. Typical industrial operator cadence is ~10–20 min per round.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read digital displays and detect gross anomalies, but interpreting analog gauges, diagnosing subtle deviations, and making correct adjustments across diverse, unstructured industrial equipment remains well beyond reliable autonomous capability in 2026.

task: Transfer materials, supplies, and products between work areas, using moving equipment and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single transfer of a load between two work areas; includes gathering items, loading equipment, moving, and unloading. Typical cycle ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and carry common objects, but navigating dynamic industrial floors, operating hand trucks/pallet jacks, handling varied item shapes, and reliably traversing between arbitrary work areas remains unreliable for frontier humanoids.

task: Imprint or emboss lettering, designs, or numbers on book covers, using gold, silver, or colored foil, and stamping machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stamping one book cover. A skilled operator positions the book, aligns the die, loads foil, and runs the stamping machine in ~2–4 minutes per cover.
