robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-positioning of flexible book covers under a die, foil threading/loading, and machine operation. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tactile feedback, and specialized tool-use needed for reliable, acceptable-quality foil stamping.

task: Participate in network technology upgrade or expansion projects, including installation of hardware and software and integration testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one project engagement (e.g., rack-mounting switches/servers, cabling, software config, and integration testing for a typical network upgrade). Competent network tech takes ~3–5 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex multi-step reasoning: physical hardware racking/cabling, software configuration via CLI/GUI, integration testing, and diagnosing failures. Far beyond current humanoid capability in perception, tool-use, and cognitive generalization.

task: Adjust machine controls to alter position, alignment, speed, or pressure.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single adjustment cycle (read setting, identify correct control, adjust to target spec). A competent operator takes ~5 min including verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct control (knob, lever, dial, touchscreen), applying precise force/rotation, and verifying the outcome. Control diversity, feedback reading, and fine dexterity challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Compress sewed or glued signatures, using hand presses or smashing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = compressing one batch of signatures. A skilled bookbinder loads, presses, and removes one set in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of delicate paper signatures, operating a specialized press mechanism, and judging compression quality. Manipulation of thin, flexible paper stacks and tool operation in a niche bookbinding context is challenging for current humanoids.

task: Complete work tickets, and place them with products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing one work ticket (filling out fields) and physically placing it with the associated product. ~5 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/writing or printing a ticket (cognitive + tool use), fine paper handling, and correctly associating it with the right product. Current humanoids lack reliable document manipulation, writing, and semantic product-matching in unstructured environments.

task: Start compressor engines and divert oil from storage tanks into compressor units and auxiliary equipment to recover natural gas from oil.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting compressor engines and diverting oil for one cycle; operator checks, valve manipulation, and flow verification take ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating large industrial compressor controls, turning high-torque valves, reading analog gauges, and safely managing pressurized oil/gas systems. Current humanoids lack the strength, specialized tool-use, domain-safe sensing, and generalization for this hazardous environment.

task: Immerse film, negatives, paper, or prints in developing solutions, fixing solutions, and water to complete photographic development processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = developing a single batch (roll of film or set of prints) through developer, stop bath, fixer, and wash cycles; ~20–30 min total hands-on time.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise chemical handling, exact timing, temperature control, gentle manipulation of delicate film/paper in total darkness, and fluid pouring into small tanks — far beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Clean the plastic work inside cars, using paintbrushes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning all plastic interior surfaces of one car with a paintbrush; a competent detailer typically takes ~15 minutes for this step.
robot_success_rationale: Requires entering/maneuvering inside a confined vehicle cabin, fine dexterous brushwork on irregular plastic trim, reaching vents and crevices, and adapting to varied interior layouts — well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Participate in recreational demonstrations to entertain resort guests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one recreational demonstration session for resort guests, typically 30–60 minutes including setup, performance, and guest interaction.
robot_success_rationale: Demonstrations require charisma, improvisation, crowd-reading, dynamic physical performance, and social interaction. Current humanoids lack expressive entertainment capability, unscripted social fluency, and the athleticism/artistry needed to genuinely entertain guests.

task: Unload and assemble pipes and pumping equipment, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unloading a standard delivery of pipes/pump components and assembling them into a working unit; a competent pipefitter/pump technician typically takes 60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Task combines heavy/awkward pipe handling, varied outdoor terrain, high-torque tool use, and fine threaded-fitting assembly. Current humanoids lack the payload, grip force, generalization, and tool-use precision needed across all sub-steps.

task: Transport materials, equipment, or supplies to or from work areas, using carts or hoists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one trip transporting materials via cart or hoist from source to destination. A competent worker typically loads, moves, and unloads in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate flat indoor spaces and do basic pick-and-place, but operating hoists, maneuvering loaded carts around obstacles, and handling varied/heavy materials in unstructured work areas remains unreliable for current humanoids.

task: Feed fuel, such as coal and coke, into fireboxes or onto conveyors, and remove ashes from furnaces, using shovels and buckets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one fueling cycle: shoveling fuel into a firebox/conveyor and removing ashes. A competent stoker completes this in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy shoveling of loose granular material (coal/ash), operating in hot/dusty environments, and navigating industrial furnace areas. Current humanoids lack robust force control for heavy shoveling, heat tolerance, and reliable granular material handling at scale.

task: Examine injured persons and administer first aid or cardiopulmonary resuscitation, if necessary, using training and medical supplies and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing one injured person and administering first aid/CPR as needed. A trained responder typically spends 5–30 min, median ~15 min, until handoff or stabilization.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced injury assessment (visual, tactile, verbal), precise dexterous medical intervention (wound care, CPR depth/rate), dynamic adaptation to patient response, and safe force control on a human body — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Stamp, etch, or scribe identifying information on finished component according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking one finished component. A competent worker locates the part, selects/sets up the tool, and applies the stamp/etch/scribe per spec in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool alignment, controlled force, and legible mark placement on varied surfaces. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity and force control for consistent, spec-compliant marking, especially with hand tools like scribes or stamps.

task: Transfer medication from vials to the appropriate number of sterile, disposable syringes, using aseptic techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drawing up one medication dose from a vial into a syringe. A trained pharmacy tech or nurse takes ~2–5 min including aseptic prep, draw, labeling, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (needle-into-vial septum, precise volume control), sterile field maintenance, and aseptic technique. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter precision, force control, and contamination-awareness needed.

task: Measure and mix chemicals to prepare solutions for processing, according to formulas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single chemical solution per formula. Involves measuring, weighing, and mixing reagents. A competent lab/processing technician typically takes ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid/powder measurement, fine dexterous manipulation of lab equipment (pipettes, scales, vessels), reading formulas, and safe chemical handling. Current humanoids lack the precision, lab tool-use, and chemical safety awareness needed.

task: Produce color or black-and-white photographs, negatives, or slides, applying standard photographic reproduction techniques and procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single shoot session (e.g., portrait or product shoot), including setup, shooting, and basic review. Competent photographer typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a camera with precise framing, lighting judgment, exposure adjustments, and creative composition. Current humanoids lack fine camera-control dexterity, nuanced perceptual aesthetic judgment, and reliable tool-use for photography equipment.

task: Maintain sanitation, health, and safety standards in work areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a standard sanitation/safety check-and-clean shift cycle for a defined work area (e.g., kitchen, lab bench, production floor section). ~30 min covers wiping, sanitizing, hazard checks.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle basic wiping/sweeping, but sanitation standards require judgment: identifying hazards, checking chemical concentrations, reading labels, ensuring compliance. Multi-step, context-dependent tasks exceed current autonomous generalization.

task: Examine developed prints for defects, such as broken lines, spots, or blurs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single developed print. A competent worker visually scans for defects (broken lines, spots, blurs) in roughly 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is feasible with cameras and ML, but a general-purpose humanoid lacks specialized optical inspection hardware, fine-grained print defect models, and reliable handling of delicate prints without damage.

task: Turn valves or handles on equipment to regulate pressure or flow of water, air, steam, or abrasives from sprayer nozzles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single valve or handle to set desired pressure/flow. A competent worker locates, turns, and confirms the setting in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Turning arbitrary valves/handles requires precise grasp, torque control, and feedback-based adjustment. Current humanoids struggle with varied handle geometries, applying calibrated torque, and reading analog gauges to confirm correct flow/pressure.

task: Examine, measure, weigh, or test sample products to ensure conformance to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single sample product: visually examining, measuring dimensions, weighing, and running a basic conformance test. ~10–20 min for a typical QC check.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can weigh and do structured visual inspection, but picking up varied products, operating calipers/gauges, interpreting multi-modal spec sheets, and adapting to diverse product types strains current dexterity and generalization significantly.

task: Load presses with paper and make necessary adjustments, according to paper size.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading one press with paper and adjusting guides/settings for the paper size. A skilled press operator typically takes 5–15 minutes per setup run.
robot_success_rationale: Requires lifting/fanning heavy paper reams, precisely aligning stacks in the feeder, and manipulating multiple press adjustment knobs/levers. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force feedback, and domain-specific tool-use for reliable press setup.

task: Verify that prepared food meets requirements for quality and quantity.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single prepared dish or batch. A competent worker visually checks portion size, presentation, temperature, and completeness in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal assessment: visual portion/presentation checks are partially feasible via vision, but temperature sensing, smell, taste, and nuanced quality judgments (texture, doneness) are beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities. Generalization across varied dishes is very limited.

task: Clean and maintain equipment, including replacing etching and rinsing solutions and cleaning bath containers and work area.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full cycle: drain/replace solutions, scrub bath containers, clean work area. A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling hazardous chemicals, operating valves/pumps, scrubbing irregular containers, and judging solution quality—demanding fine dexterity, chemical awareness, and multi-step sequencing well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Clean and maintain tools and equipment, using solvents, brushes, and rags.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a typical set of tools/equipment after a work session; ~20 min for a competent worker using solvents, brushes, and rags.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping varied tools, applying solvents precisely, scrubbing with brushes/rags, and judging cleanliness. Unstructured tool geometry, liquid handling, and quality judgment are hard for 2026 humanoids. Dexterity and generalization limits dominant.

task: Create prints according to customer specifications and laboratory protocols.
human_time_rationale: One instance = producing a single print order per customer spec. Includes setup, calibration, printing, and quality check. Typical lab print job takes ~20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting varied customer specs, operating specialized lab printing equipment (controls, software interfaces), fine color calibration judgment, and quality inspection — far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and tool-use capability.

task: Stack and sort cut material for packaging, further processing, or shipping, according to types and sizes of material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting/stacking one batch or pallet of cut material. A competent worker typically takes 5–10 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place uniform flat pieces reasonably well, but classifying varied material types/sizes, handling flexible or irregular cuts, and stacking neatly in sorted order strains current perception, dexterity, and generalization capabilities.

task: Inspect vats after cleaning to ensure that fermentable residue has been removed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one vat post-cleaning. A competent worker visually checks interior surfaces and may use a light or swab test; ~5 minutes per vat.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to vat, entering or peering into confined curved interior, and detecting subtle residue via vision. Current robots lack reliable close-range inspection of vessel interiors, confined-space mobility, and residue-detection perception at acceptable quality.

task: Conduct chemical analysis of body fluids, including blood, urine, or spinal fluid, to determine presence of normal or abnormal components.
human_time_rationale: One instance = analyzing a single patient specimen (e.g., a blood or urine panel). Includes sample prep, running the analyzer, QC checks, and documenting results; ~30–60 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized lab analyzers, precise pipetting, reagent handling, contamination control, and interpreting results. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, lab-specific tool integration, and cognitive judgment for clinical chemistry workflows.

task: Measure, mix, cut, shape, soften, and join materials and ingredients, such as powder, cornmeal, or rubber to prepare them for machine processing.
human_time_rationale: One batch prep cycle (measure, mix, cut, shape, soften, join materials). Competent worker takes ~20–30 min per batch across typical industrial settings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sequential multi-step manipulation across varied materials (powder, cornmeal, rubber), precise measuring, cutting, shaping, and joining. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force control, and multi-tool generalization for full autonomous execution.

task: Prepare trucks and equipment necessary for the type of pumping service required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single truck and associated pumping equipment for a job; includes inspection, loading hoses/fittings, and checking pump systems. Typical pre-job prep for an oilfield/water/concrete pump crew.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating/configuring heavy trucks, loading large industrial hoses and fittings, and adjusting specialized pump systems. Humanoids cannot drive or configure heavy vehicles; extreme dexterity and domain-specific tool-use far exceed 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Deliver medications or pharmaceutical supplies to patients, nursing stations, or surgery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retrieving a medication/supply from pharmacy and delivering it to a destination (patient room, nursing station, or OR). Includes pick-up, transit, and handoff in a typical hospital.
robot_success_rationale: Indoor flat hospital terrain is manageable, but tasks require elevator use, door navigation, social awareness, and safe patient/staff handoff. High safety stakes limit autonomy; unpredictable hospital corridors and handoff protocols reduce success.

task: Attach, fasten, and disconnect cables or lines to loads, materials, and equipment, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one attach/fasten/disconnect cycle on a single load or equipment item; a skilled rigger completes this in roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires strong dexterous tool use (hand tools), routing cables around varied loads, applying correct tension/knots, and safe disconnection. High variability in cable types, anchor points, and spatial constraints far exceeds current humanoid dexterity and generalization.

task: Fill tanks of processing machines with solutions such as developer, dyes, stop-baths, fixers, bleaches, or washes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling one machine's solution tanks (multiple chemicals). A competent darkroom/lab technician takes ~10–20 min to measure, mix, and fill all tanks for one processing machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires pouring precise liquid volumes, reading labels, handling varied containers and funnels, avoiding cross-contamination, and operating in cluttered lab environments. Liquid handling, fine pouring control, and chemical accuracy are well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Collect or select samples for testing or for use as models.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting/selecting a single batch of samples (e.g., soil core, water sample, tissue biopsy, or material specimen). A competent technician typically spends ~15 min locating, selecting, labeling, and storing one sample batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires context-dependent judgment to identify the correct sample, precise dexterous manipulation (vials, swabs, syringes, specimen containers), navigation to varied environments, and proper labeling — all beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Load circuit boards, racks or rolls of film, negatives, or printing paper into processing or printing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a single batch (board, rack, or roll) into a machine. A competent worker typically takes 3–7 minutes including alignment and confirmation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of delicate, thin materials (film, negatives, PCBs) into specific machine slots. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity and tactile feedback needed; perception of transparent/thin media is also challenging. Structured environments help slightly.

task: Place semiconductor wafers in processing containers or equipment holders, using vacuum wand or tweezers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing a single wafer into a processing container/holder. A trained technician takes ~2–4 minutes including tool handling, alignment, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Wafer handling demands extreme precision (<0.1mm), contamination-free contact, and specialized vacuum wand/tweezer use. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter dexterity, force control, and cleanroom-grade tool integration required.

task: Weigh or measure chemicals, coatings, or paints before adding them to machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring/weighing a single batch of chemical/coating/paint before machine addition. A competent worker reads specs, weighs/measures, and prepares the batch in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading labels, precise pouring/measuring to spec, handling varied containers and scales, and interpreting measurement readouts. Fine dexterity for pouring liquids or powders accurately and generalizing across container types is still very limited in 2026 frontier robots.

task: Instruct participants in skiing, swimming, or other recreational activities and provide safety precaution information.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one instructional session (e.g., a beginner ski or swim lesson). Typical sessions run 45–90 minutes; 60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic locomotion on snow/water, real-time adaptive coaching, natural language instruction, safety monitoring of others, and unstructured outdoor environments — far beyond current humanoid capabilities. No viable platform exists.

task: Clean food preparation areas, cooking surfaces, and utensils.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a typical prep area, cooking surface, and set of utensils after a meal/service. A competent worker wipes surfaces, scrubs cooking surfaces, and washes/sanitizes utensils in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can wipe flat surfaces moderately well, but scrubbing varied cooking surfaces (grates, burners), handling diverse utensils, using sinks/spray tools, and meeting food-safety standards requires dexterity, tool-use, and generalization beyond current frontier capability.

task: Perform highly skilled hand finishing binding operations, such as grooving or lettering.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing a single book's binding (grooving + lettering). A skilled bookbinder typically takes 30–60 minutes per book for hand-finishing operations.
robot_success_rationale: Extremely fine dexterous work requiring precise tool pressure, heat control, and artistic judgment on irregular surfaces. Far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity; akin to surgery-level manipulation with specialized tools and aesthetic quality standards.

task: Mix ingredients, such as pancake or waffle batters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing a single batch of batter (e.g., pancake or waffle). A competent cook measures, combines, and stirs ingredients to proper consistency in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating and measuring varied ingredients, pouring liquids without spilling, and judging batter consistency — challenging for perception, fine motor control, and generalization in an unstructured kitchen environment.

task: Inspect recreational facilities for cleanliness.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single recreational facility (e.g., gym, pool area, park restroom). A competent inspector walks through, checks surfaces, equipment, and amenities in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can navigate indoor spaces and use vision to detect obvious dirt, but reliably assessing cleanliness across varied surfaces, odors, and nuanced standards in unstructured recreational spaces exceeds current generalization and perception capabilities.

task: Operate compressed air, diesel, electric, gasoline, or steam-driven hoists or winches to control movement of cableways, cages, derricks, draglines, loaders, railcars, or skips.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one lift/haul operation cycle (rigging, operating hoist/winch, controlled movement, landing/release). A competent operator typically completes this in ~45 min including setup and safety checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial hoist/winch control stations, interpreting load signals, and coordinating with ground crew. Humanoid robots cannot interface with or safely operate these heavy-duty powered machines autonomously in 2026.

task: Feed products into machines by hand or conveyor.
human_time_rationale: One instance = feeding a single product/batch into a machine. A competent worker completes this in ~1–3 minutes depending on product size and machine type.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of products into defined machine inputs is within current robot capability in structured environments, but variability in product shape, orientation, conveyor dynamics, and machine interface reduces reliability significantly.

task: Read food order slips or receive verbal instructions as to food required by patron, and prepare and cook food according to instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and cooking one patron's food order (e.g., a short-order or line-cook dish). Median cook time from order receipt to plating is ~10–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading slips or parsing verbal orders, then executing multi-step cooking (heat control, timing, fine manipulation of varied ingredients/tools). Current humanoids lack reliable kitchen generalization, safe heat handling, and recipe-adaptive dexterity at acceptable quality.

task: Adjust machine components according to specifications such as widths, lengths, and thickness of materials and amounts of glue, cement, or adhesive required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting a single machine's components to a new spec (e.g., width guides, rollers, glue dispensers). A competent operator typically takes 8–15 minutes per changeover.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading specs, locating varied adjustment points (knobs, levers, valves), applying precise torque, and verifying measurements. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation, tool-use precision, and the contextual interpretation of machine-specific controls needed for autonomous success.

task: Operate underwater recovery units.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one underwater recovery operation (e.g., locating, attaching, and surfacing a submerged object). Skilled divers/ROV operators typically spend 2–4 hours per operation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized underwater vehicles (ROVs/AUVs), not humanoid robots. Involves extreme environment navigation, sonar/vision perception, high-force manipulation under water pressure, and complex rigging — far beyond general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Cook the exact number of items ordered by each customer, working on several different orders simultaneously.
human_time_rationale: One "instance" = one service period managing multiple simultaneous orders (e.g., a short restaurant rush). A competent cook juggles ~5–10 orders in roughly 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-task planning, precise counting per order, real-time coordination across multiple cooking stations, fine dexterity, and dynamic re-prioritization. Far beyond current humanoid robots' generalization and multi-step cooking capability.

task: Count, sort, and weigh processed items.
human_time_rationale: One instance = counting, sorting, and weighing a batch of processed items (e.g., a tray or bin). A competent worker completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick, place, and sort common objects and interface with scales, but reliable counting of varied items, fine dexterous sorting by category, and consistent scale interaction in unstructured settings remain challenging for general-purpose humanoids.

task: Transport materials, supplies, and finished products between storage and work areas, using forklifts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one forklift transport trip (pick up load, travel to destination, deposit load). Typical warehouse trip takes ~5–10 minutes including maneuvering.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate a forklift in traffic/warehouses autonomously. Autonomous forklifts exist but are narrow-purpose systems, not general-purpose humanoids. Humanoid sitting in forklift seat and driving is essentially unsolved.

task: Connect reactor to computer, using hand tools and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fully connecting one reactor to one computer system via cables, connectors, and fasteners using hand and power tools. A competent technician typically takes 60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation (threading connectors, routing cables, using power tools precisely), reading technical diagrams, and working in constrained industrial spaces — far beyond reliable autonomous robot capability in 2026.

task: Load and unload equipment chambers and transport finished product to storage or to area for further processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one load/unload cycle of an equipment chamber plus transport to storage. Typical industrial cycle: ~10–20 min for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires opening chamber doors, precise part placement/removal, and navigating to storage. Frontier humanoids handle pick-and-place well but struggle with varied chamber latches, product fragility, and unstructured transport paths.

task: Measure and mix ingredients to prepare glue.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and mixing a single batch of glue. A competent worker gathers ingredients, measures by weight/volume, and mixes thoroughly in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid/powder measurement, pouring, and stirring with correct consistency judgment. Robots struggle with viscous fluid handling, reading graduated containers accurately, and assessing mix quality by sight/texture.

task: Select and install machine components, such as dies, molds, and cutters, according to specifications, using hand tools and measuring devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting and installing a single component (die, mold, or cutter) onto a machine. A skilled machinist typically takes 15–35 min including locating, measuring, aligning, and securing the component.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool-use (wrenches, gauges), fine alignment to tight tolerances, and cross-referencing specs — beyond current humanoid dexterity and perception reliability. Unstructured shop environments and heavy/oddly-shaped components add further difficulty.

task: Inspect materials, components, or products for surface defects and measure circuitry, using electronic test equipment, precision measuring instruments, microscope, and standard procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single batch/component set. A skilled technician spends ~15–25 min setting up instruments, visually scanning surfaces, and recording electronic measurements.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of precision instruments (microscope, probes), sub-millimeter circuitry measurement, and nuanced defect judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, calibration precision, and microscopy handling needed for acceptable quality.

task: Prepare and serve beverages, such as coffee or fountain drinks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and serving a single beverage order (e.g., a cup of coffee or fountain drink); a competent worker typically completes this in 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating varied dispensers, handling cups, lids, and straws with precision, and navigating to serve. Frontier humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation for beverage equipment and generalization across machine types.

task: Type instructions on computer keyboards, push buttons to activate computer programs, or manually set cutting guides, clamps, and knives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single setup sequence (typing a command, pushing a button, or adjusting a cutting guide/clamp/knife). A competent operator completes this in ~1–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Typing on a keyboard requires precise finger dexterity that current humanoids struggle with. Button-pushing is more feasible (~50–70%), but fine adjustments of cutting guides, clamps, and knives demand high-precision manipulation and contextual judgment, dragging overall probability low.

task: Align photo mask pattern on photoresist layer, expose pattern to ultraviolet light, and develop pattern, using specialized equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning, exposing, and developing a single wafer/substrate. A skilled photolithography technician typically takes 30–60 min per wafer including setup, alignment verification, exposure, and development.
robot_success_rationale: Photolithography requires sub-micron precision alignment, operation of highly specialized fab equipment (mask aligners, steppers, developers), and cleanroom protocol compliance. Current humanoid robots lack the nanometer-scale dexterity, tool interfacing, and contamination-control capability needed.

task: Inspect equipment for leaks, diagnose malfunctions, and request repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single piece of equipment, diagnosing any faults, and filing/requesting a repair. Covers visual check, leak detection, and documentation — ~30 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can visually scan surfaces but reliably detecting subtle leaks (smell, sound, pressure drops) and diagnosing root-cause malfunctions requires multi-modal perception, domain reasoning, and tool use far beyond 2026 frontier capability. Filing/requesting repairs adds further complexity.

task: Thread filmstrips through densitometers or sensitometers and expose film to light to determine density of film, necessary color corrections, or light sensitivity.
human_time_rationale: One instance = threading one filmstrip through a densitometer/sensitometer and completing one exposure/reading cycle. A skilled technician takes ~5–10 minutes per strip.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to handle thin, delicate filmstrips and thread them precisely through narrow instrument guides. Current humanoids lack the finger precision and compliance control for such fragile, micron-sensitive material handling.

task: Prepare specialty foods, such as pizzas, fish and chips, sandwiches, or tacos, following specific methods that usually require short preparation time.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single specialty food order (e.g., one pizza, one sandwich, one taco plate). A competent food service worker completes this in ~10–15 minutes following a defined method.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step dexterous manipulation (spreading, layering, wrapping, portioning), tool use (knives, ovens, fryers), and adaptive responses to varied ingredients in unstructured kitchen environments — well beyond reliable 2026 frontier robot capability.

task: Shape, trim, or touch up roughed-out designs with appropriate tools to finish carvings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing a single carving piece. A competent carver typically spends 1–2 hours on detail shaping/trimming/touching up after roughing out, depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity with varied tools (gouges, chisels, knives), tactile force feedback, and artistic judgment on organic 3D forms. Far beyond current humanoid manipulation capabilities; akin to surgery-level precision plus creative interpretation.

task: Manipulate valves, switches, and buttons, or key commands into control panels to start semiconductor processing cycles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and initiating one semiconductor processing cycle. A competent operator typically spends ~5–10 min checking parameters, manipulating controls, and confirming the cycle has started.
robot_success_rationale: Semiconductor fab panels involve precise fine-motor key commands, touchscreens, small switches, and safety-critical sequencing in highly controlled environments. Frontier humanoids lack the fine dexterity, fab-specific tool interfaces, and reliable HMI interaction needed for autonomous success.

task: Carve designs or figures in full or bas relief on stone, employing knowledge of stone carving techniques and sense of artistry to produce carvings consistent with designers' plans.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one carving project (e.g., a single bas-relief panel or figure). A competent stone carver typically spends many hours; ~8 hrs is a reasonable median for a moderate piece.
robot_success_rationale: Stone carving demands extreme precision, sustained high-force tool control, fine 3D artistic judgment, and adaptation to unpredictable stone fracture. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force regulation, and artistic generalization required. No credible 2026 deployment exists.

task: Examine and observe animals to detect signs of illness, disease, or injury.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining one animal (e.g., a dog or cow). A competent vet tech or handler spends ~5–15 min on a routine health observation check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced multimodal perception (visual, tactile, auditory cues), handling of unpredictable live animals, and veterinary domain knowledge. Current robots lack reliable animal-safe interaction, soft-tissue palpation, and clinical judgment.

task: Scribe or separate wafers into dice.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scribing/dicing one wafer batch (typically 1 wafer to a cassette load). A competent technician takes ~20–40 min including setup, scribing/sawing, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-precise micron-level alignment, specialized dicing saws or diamond scribes, cleanroom protocols, and wafer handling without contamination or breakage — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use capabilities.

task: Lubricate, clean, or make minor repairs to machine parts to keep machines in working condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lubricating, cleaning, or performing a minor repair on a single machine or component. A competent technician typically takes ~20 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied industrial environments, identifying specific lubrication/repair points, using tools precisely, and applying correct force. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use dexterity and task generalization for unstructured maintenance work.

task: Thread or feed items or products through or around machine rollers and dryers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = threading/feeding one item or material through a machine's rollers/dryers. A skilled operator typically completes this in ~5 minutes including alignment and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual dexterity to guide flexible/limp materials through tight roller gaps, accurate spatial perception in cluttered machinery, and generalization across machine types. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-tension control for threading tasks.

task: Retouch photographic negatives or original prints to correct defects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retouching one negative or print. A competent retoucher spends ~30–60 min per image depending on defect complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (sub-millimeter brushwork on delicate film/paper), precise visual perception of subtle tonal defects, and specialized artistic judgment — far beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Observe gauges, meters, and control panels to obtain information about equipment temperatures and pressures, or the speed of feeders or conveyors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single inspection round reading all relevant gauges/meters/panels in a work area; a competent operator typically completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can visually read digital displays reasonably well but struggle with analog gauges, varied lighting, cluttered panels, and navigating industrial environments to reach all instruments reliably. Generalization across panel types is still weak.

task: Administer prescribed medications or start intravenous fluids, noting times and amounts on patients' charts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = administering a single medication or IV fluid to one patient: verifying order, preparing dose, administering, and charting. Typically 10–15 minutes for a nurse.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (venipuncture, syringe handling), clinical judgment (patient ID, dose verification, adverse reactions), sterile technique, EHR interaction, and patient communication — all far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Drill holes and cut or carve moldings and grooves in stone, according to diagrams and patterns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single molding/groove cut or drilling session on one stone piece per diagram. A skilled stonemason typically takes 60–120 minutes per piece depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, high-force tool use (drills, chisels, grinders) on hard stone guided by complex diagrams. Current humanoids lack the force control, vibration tolerance, specialized tool-use, and fine spatial accuracy needed for acceptable quality stonework.

task: Place sensitized paper in frames of projection printers, photostats, or other reproduction machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading one sheet of sensitized paper into a frame/machine. A skilled operator aligns and secures a sheet in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, delicate sheet handling (thin, light-sensitive paper), accurate alignment in a frame, and operating specialized reproduction equipment. Poor generalization to varied machine types; high risk of misalignment or paper damage.

task: Mount or load material such as paper, plastic, wood, or rubber in feeding mechanisms of cementing or gluing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading/mounting a single roll or sheet of material into a machine feed mechanism; a competent operator typically completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment, threading, and tensioning of flexible/varied materials into a specific machine mechanism. Perception of correct feed path and dexterous handling of limp materials (paper, plastic, rubber) are significant challenges for current humanoids.

task: Create landscapes that minimize water consumption such as by incorporating drought-resistant grasses or indigenous plants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and planting a typical residential xeriscape bed (~200 sq ft). A competent landscaper needs ~4 hours for plant selection, layout, soil prep, and planting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert horticultural knowledge for plant selection, unstructured outdoor terrain, digging, precise planting depth, and creative spatial design. Current humanoids lack agronomic reasoning, outdoor dexterity with tools, and generalization over varied soil/terrain conditions.

task: Operate special equipment to perform tasks such as transferring film to videotape or producing photographic enlargements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one transfer/enlargement job (e.g., a single film-to-tape transfer or one photographic enlargement). A competent operator typically takes ~20–40 minutes including setup, calibration, and quality check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized analog/legacy equipment (telecine machines, enlargers, chemical darkroom tools) with precise fine motor control, color calibration judgment, and domain-specific tool-use far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Clean, stock, and restock workstations and display cases.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning, stocking, and restocking a single workstation or display case. A competent worker wipes surfaces, arranges/replenishes items in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires combined wiping, precise product placement, inventory awareness, and navigating display cases. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity for neat stocking and contextual restocking judgment in varied retail/workspace environments.

task: Examine stitched, collated, bound, or unbound product samples for defects, such as imperfect bindings, ink spots, torn pages, loose pages, or loose or uncut threads.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single product sample (e.g., a book or booklet). A competent inspector visually and manually checks all defect types in ~2–4 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal inspection: visual perception for ink spots/torn pages, tactile sensing for loose pages/threads, and page-flipping dexterity. Current robots lack reliable fine tactile feedback and dexterous page manipulation needed for thorough defect detection.

task: Set, adjust, and readjust computerized or mechanical equipment controls to regulate power level, temperature, vacuum, and rotation speed of furnace, according to crystal growing specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single adjustment cycle (set, check readings, and readjust controls) during a crystal-growing run; a competent operator typically spends ~10–20 min per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading specialized analog/digital instrument panels, fine manipulation of knobs/controls, and interpreting crystal-growth specs in real time. Current humanoids lack the precise perception, domain-specific HMI generalization, and process understanding needed for reliable autonomous operation.

task: Train animals to perform certain tasks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single training session with one animal (e.g., teaching/reinforcing one specific behavior). A competent trainer typically spends 20–60 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Animal training requires reading subtle behavioral cues, timing reinforcement precisely, adapting dynamically to an unpredictable living subject, and building trust. Current robots lack animal-behavior perception, emotional/social understanding, and safe adaptive interaction with animals.

task: Examine and measure completed materials or products to verify conformance to specifications, using measuring devices such as tape measures, gauges, or calipers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single part/product with a measuring device. A competent inspector typically takes 5–10 minutes to measure key dimensions, check tolerances, and record results.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle with precise tool-use (calipers, gauges) requiring fine dexterity, correct positioning against irregular surfaces, and reading analog/digital instruments reliably. Perception and manipulation combined make this very difficult autonomously.

task: Serve orders to customers at windows, counters, or tables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = delivering a single order to a customer at a window, counter, or table. A competent worker takes ~1–5 min including pickup, transport, and handoff.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied service environments, identifying the correct customer, carrying trays/items stably, and social handoff. Locomotion is feasible indoors, but tray balancing, crowded spaces, customer interaction, and generalization across venues remain significant barriers in 2026.

task: Inspect newly installed energy-efficient equipment to ensure that it was installed properly and is performing according to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one newly installed unit (e.g., HVAC, heat pump). Includes visual check, meter readings, and spec comparison; ~45 min for a competent energy auditor.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading analog/digital gauges, interpreting complex spec sheets, navigating varied mechanical rooms, and making expert judgments on performance adequacy — well beyond current autonomous robot generalization and reasoning.

task: Observe activities in assigned areas, using binoculars, to detect hazards, disturbances, or safety infractions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one observation shift/patrol cycle in an assigned area. A typical patrol or watch period is ~1 hour before logging findings or rotating.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained mobile surveillance over wide areas using binoculars, high-level semantic scene understanding (distinguishing disturbances, hazards, infractions), and contextual judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable long-range optics integration, outdoor terrain mobility, and nuanced situational awareness for autonomous security decisions.

task: Activate agitators and paddles to mix or stir ingredients, stopping machines when ingredients are thoroughly mixed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading ingredients, starting the machine, monitoring the mix cycle, and stopping when done. Typical industrial mixing cycle runs 5–15 minutes total hands-on time.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial controls, judging mix completeness visually/by texture, and responding adaptively. Current humanoids lack reliable manipulation of non-standard machine interfaces and sensory judgment of mix quality.

task: Clean and maintain gluing and cementing machines, using solutions, lubricants, brushes, and scrapers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/maintaining one gluing/cementing machine. A competent worker spends ~20–30 min applying solutions, lubricating, scraping dried adhesive, and brushing components.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity with multiple tools (brushes, scrapers, lubricant applicators) on irregular, adhesive-coated surfaces and tight machine cavities. Tool switching, force control on delicate parts, and residue inspection exceed current humanoid capability reliably.

task: Assist patrons in finding seats, lighting the way with flashlights, if necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = escorting a single patron (or small group) from the entrance to their seat; typically takes 1–5 minutes depending on venue size and crowd density.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating dark, crowded, sloped aisles; social interaction; reading tickets; and illuminating the path. Dim/dynamic lighting, dense crowds, and nuanced human communication severely challenge current humanoids.

task: Patrol or monitor recreational areas, such as trails, slopes, or swimming areas, on foot, in vehicles, or from towers.
human_time_rationale: One patrol shift/circuit of a recreational area (trail loop, beach stretch, or ski slope). A typical patrol round takes ~1–2 hours for a competent ranger/lifeguard.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating unpredictable outdoor terrain (trails, slopes, water edges), exercising judgment about hazards and people, and adapting to weather. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor mobility, situational awareness, and emergency response capability at this scale.

task: Mix pharmaceutical preparations, according to written prescriptions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = compounding a single prescription. A trained pharmacist/tech typically spends 10–30 min measuring, mixing, and verifying one compounded preparation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme precision in weighing/measuring micro-quantities, sterile technique, reading complex Rx documents, operating specialized pharmacy equipment, and regulatory-quality verification — far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Remove and stack completed materials or products, and restock materials to be joined.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing finished joined materials from a workstation, stacking them, and restocking raw materials for the next cycle. Typical in light manufacturing; ~2–4 min per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place and stacking of uniform parts is feasible, but varied shapes, tight restocking tolerances, and reliable stack alignment challenge current humanoids. Locomotion between stock and workstation adds further failure risk.

task: Disassemble equipment to repair it or to replace parts, such as nozzles, punches, and filters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling one piece of equipment to access a replaceable part (nozzle, punch, or filter). A skilled technician typically takes 15–35 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct fasteners, applying precise torque, handling varied tool types, and navigating tight spaces around industrial equipment. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use, torque control, and generalization across machine types.

task: Measure ingredients required for specific food items.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring all ingredients for a single food item/recipe. A competent cook typically spends 3–7 minutes gathering, scooping, weighing, and leveling ingredients.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to scoop, level, and pour precise amounts; perception to read measurements; tool-use with measuring cups/spoons/scales. Current robots struggle with granular/liquid handling precision and varied container shapes.

task: Blow tubing into specified shapes to prevent glass from collapsing, using compressed air or own breath, or blow and rotate gathers in molds or on boards to obtain final shapes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shaping a single glass piece (e.g. a vessel or tube). A skilled glassblower typically spends 5–15 minutes per piece depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise breath/airflow control, continuous rotation, real-time thermal feedback on molten glass, and extremely fine dexterous coordination. No current humanoid robot has these capabilities for hot, deformable material shaping.

task: Cut binder boards to specified dimensions, using board shears, hand cutters, or cutting machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting one binder board to spec. A skilled worker measures, positions, and cuts a single board in ~3–5 minutes including setup and quality check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, firm gripping and aligning of stiff board stock, and operating specialized cutting machinery (shears/cutters). Humanoids lack fine calibration for material feed, blade alignment, and safe machine operation at required tolerances.

task: Give door checks to patrons who are temporarily leaving establishments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = handing a single door check (stamp, wristband, or ticket) to one patron. A competent human completes this in ~30 seconds.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a crowded, dynamic social environment, locating a patron's hand, and precisely placing/stamping a small token. Dexterity with tiny items and social interaction cues remain challenging for 2026 humanoids.

task: Start machines, and turn valves or move controls to feed, admit, apply, or transfer materials and adhesives, and to adjust temperature, pressure, and time settings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting a machine and adjusting its controls/valves for a single production run setup. A competent operator typically takes 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires turning varied valves, reading gauges, operating machine-specific controls, and fine-tuning multiple parameters. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterity for arbitrary industrial controls, tool-specific generalization, and safe process feedback loops.

task: Supply, collect, or empty bedpans.
human_time_rationale: One instance = supplying, collecting, or emptying a single bedpan. A competent aide fetches, positions, retrieves, empties, rinses, and disposes in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate patient interaction, hygienic handling of biohazardous fluids, navigating tight clinical spaces, and precise dexterous manipulation near a person — far beyond current humanoid capability and safety acceptance.

task: Place rubber hoses on ends of tubing and charge tubing with gas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting rubber hoses onto both ends of a single tube and charging it with gas (connecting supply, opening valve, verifying pressure). A competent technician takes ~10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Stretching/seating rubber hoses onto tubing ends demands high grip force and dexterity; connecting a gas supply and managing valve/pressure adds tool-use and safety complexity well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Feed and water animals according to schedules and feeding instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one feeding/watering round for a group of animals. A competent worker measures portions, fills feeders/waterers, and distributes feed in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied, often outdoor/uneven environments, reading schedules, measuring/pouring feed, operating diverse feeders and waterers, and adapting to animal behavior — well beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Maintain and merchandise home healthcare products or services.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stocking, arranging, and merchandising a section of home healthcare products in a retail or care setting; a competent worker typically takes 30–60 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied product handling, planogram compliance, label reading, facing/fronting, and some customer interaction. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterous shelf merchandising and contextual product knowledge to meet retail quality standards.

task: Pre-cook items, such as bacon, to prepare them for later use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pre-cooking a batch of a single item (e.g., a pack of bacon). Includes setup, cooking, monitoring, and storing. ~20 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires stove/oven operation, grease handling, doneness judgment, flipping, and safe storage — complex multi-step cooking with heat, splatter hazards, and nuanced sensory feedback far exceeding current robot capability.

task: Carve rough designs freehand or by chipping along marks on stone, using mallets and chisels or pneumatic tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = carving a single rough design on a stone piece. A competent stone carver typically spends 1–3 hours on a rough carving pass depending on design complexity and stone size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-modulated strikes on irregular stone surfaces, freehand artisanal judgment, and skilled tool-use (mallet/chisel or pneumatic tools). Current robots lack the fine force control, dexterity, and creative spatial reasoning needed for this unstructured, high-consequence task.

task: Fill machines with glue, cement, or adhesives.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling a single machine's glue/adhesive reservoir. A competent worker locates the correct material, opens/connects supply, fills to level, and closes — roughly 10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying fill ports, handling viscous/sticky materials, connecting nozzles or opening containers precisely, and avoiding spills. Fine dexterity, variable container types, and sticky substances pose major challenges for current humanoids.

task: Provide basic patient care or treatments, such as taking temperatures or blood pressures, dressing wounds, treating bedsores, giving enemas or douches, rubbing with alcohol, massaging, or performing catheterizations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient care procedure (e.g., dressing a wound or taking vitals). Competent nursing aide completes a typical procedure in ~15–25 min including setup/cleanup.
robot_success_rationale: Highly varied, intimate, dexterous tasks on a non-rigid human body requiring fine touch, sterile technique, patient positioning, and safety-critical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Exercise animals to maintain their physical and mental health.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one exercise session for one animal (e.g., walking a dog or leading a horse). A typical session lasts ~30 minutes for a competent handler.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic interaction with unpredictable living animals, leash handling, outdoor/unstructured terrain navigation, behavioral reading, and responsive guidance. Current humanoids lack animal-handling dexterity, safe force control, and real-time behavioral adaptation needed.

task: Select chisels, pneumatic or surfacing tools, or sandblasting nozzles, and determine sequence of use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting appropriate tools and planning their sequence for a single job/workpiece. A skilled tradesperson typically takes ~10 min to assess the job, pick tools, and decide order.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level job reasoning, material assessment, and planning across specialist tools (chisels, pneumatic tools, sandblasting nozzles). Current robots lack the cognitive generalization and domain expertise for autonomous tool selection and sequencing in varied trade contexts.

task: Heat glass to pliable stage, using gas flames or ovens and rotating glass to heat it uniformly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = heating one glass piece to pliable stage. A skilled glassworker spends ~5–10 min rotating over flame/oven until uniformly soft.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise continuous rotation of fragile glass over open flame or in high-heat oven, fine tactile/visual feedback to judge pliability, heat tolerance, and dexterous grip — all far beyond current humanoid robots.

task: Work with others to change advertising displays.
human_time_rationale: One instance = swapping out one advertising display (e.g., poster, banner, or digital panel insert) with a coworker. Includes removing old material, installing new, and confirming alignment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated multi-agent collaboration, varied display hardware (frames, latches, mounting rails), navigating retail/public spaces, and precise two-handed manipulation of large, flexible materials — well beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Cut, shape, and finish rough blocks of building or monumental stone, according to diagrams or patterns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting, shaping, and finishing a single rough stone block to spec. A skilled stone mason typically takes 2–4 hours per block depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy-force tool use (chisels, grinders, saws) on large irregular stone, precise pattern interpretation, fine surface finishing, and sustained physical exertion. Current humanoids lack strength, specialized tool integration, and stone-working precision.

task: Remove materials, parts, or workpieces from painting or coating machines, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one batch of parts from a coating machine using hand tools; a skilled worker takes ~2–4 minutes to unclamp, unhook, and clear the workpiece safely.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool use (hand tools), varied part geometries, wet/coated surfaces (slippery, delicate), and navigating machine interiors. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous tool manipulation and generalization across fixture types.

task: Read job orders and inspect workpieces to determine work procedures and materials required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reading a single job order and inspecting the associated workpiece to determine procedures/materials. A skilled worker typically completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting job orders (document understanding), cross-referencing workpiece state via vision, and making procedural/material judgments — complex cognitive + perception integration far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Conceal blemishes in workpieces, such as nicks and dents, using fillers such as putty.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying filler to blemishes on a single workpiece. A competent worker inspects, mixes/applies putty, and smooths it in roughly 5–15 minutes depending on defect count.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (putty knife/applicator), precise tactile feedback to detect and fill subtle surface defects, and nuanced visual inspection. Current humanoids lack the dexterity and haptic sensitivity for acceptable surface finishing quality.

task: Verify depths and dimensions of cuts or carvings to ensure adherence to specifications, blueprints, or models, using measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = verifying all cuts/carvings on a single workpiece. A competent worker measures key dimensions with calipers/depth gauges and cross-checks against blueprints in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool handling (calipers, depth gauges), fine dexterity to seat instruments accurately in carved features, and cross-referencing blueprints. Current humanoids lack reliable sub-mm measurement accuracy and tool-use generalization for this workflow.

task: Fasten safety devices for patrons, or provide them with directions for fastening devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fastening or explaining safety devices for one patron (e.g., seatbelt, harness, lap bar). A competent ride/venue attendant completes this in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to buckle/clip varied harness types, close social interaction, patron compliance handling, and safety-critical judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous manipulation on body-worn restraints and nuanced human interaction needed here.

task: Spray or swab molds with oil solutions to prevent adhesion of glass.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying oil solution to one mold. A skilled worker sprays or swabs a single mold in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a hot industrial environment, holding a spray/swab tool, and achieving full even coverage of complex mold geometry. Uneven application is a quality failure; dexterity, tool-use generalization, and thermal-environment safety all limit current robots.

task: Dress stone surfaces, using bushhammers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dressing one stone surface to acceptable texture; a skilled mason takes ~30–60 min depending on stone hardness and area.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained high-force rhythmic striking with a specialized heavy tool, precise tactile feedback to gauge surface uniformity, and adaptive technique on irregular stone — far beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and tool-use capabilities.

task: Cut cover material to specified dimensions, fitting and gluing material to binder boards by hand or machine.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting and gluing cover material to one binder board set. A skilled bookbinding/case-making worker completes this in roughly 10–15 minutes by hand.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fabric/material cutting, controlled glue application, and dexterous alignment of flexible cover material over boards — all demand fine manipulation and tactile feedback well beyond current humanoid capability. High failure risk on all sub-steps.

task: Measure and weigh amounts of crystal growing materials, mix and grind materials, load materials into container, and monitor processing procedures to help identify crystal growing problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one batch preparation cycle: measuring/weighing, mixing/grinding, loading, and initial monitoring. A competent lab technician typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-dexterous manipulation (weighing small quantities, grinding, pouring powders), operating lab instruments (balances, grinders), interpreting process feedback, and diagnosing anomalies — well beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capability.

task: Start machines and move controls to regulate tension on pressure rolls, to synchronize speed of machine components, and to adjust temperatures of glue or paraffin.
human_time_rationale: One instance = startup and full adjustment cycle for a single machine run. A competent operator takes ~10–20 min to start up, tune tension/speed/temperature to acceptable output quality.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating industrial machine controls (levers, dials, switches), reading analog/digital gauges, and making coordinated multi-parameter adjustments. Current humanoids lack reliable industrial machine interfacing, fine dial manipulation, and closed-loop process judgment.

task: Change press plates, blankets, or cylinders, as required.
human_time_rationale: One instance = changing one press component (plate, blanket, or cylinder) on a printing press. A skilled press operator typically takes 15–25 minutes per changeover.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a large industrial press, applying precise torque to locking mechanisms, aligning flexible blankets/plates to tight tolerances, and operating press-specific controls — far beyond 2026 humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Measure, space, and set saw blades, cutters, and perforators, according to product specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a single machine's blades/cutters/perforators to spec. A skilled operator measures, adjusts spacing, and verifies alignment in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterous manipulation of sharp tooling, reading specifications, using measurement instruments (calipers, gauges), and making sub-millimeter adjustments. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capability in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Inspect parts, equipment, or vehicles for cleanliness, damage, and compliance with standards or regulations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single part, piece of equipment, or vehicle. A competent inspector typically spends 10–20 minutes checking cleanliness, damage, and compliance on one unit.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to an object and capture images, but reliably detecting subtle damage, contamination, or compliance details across varied parts/vehicles/equipment requires nuanced perception and domain knowledge that current humanoids lack consistently.

task: Provide assistance to patrons entering or exiting amusement rides, boats, or ski lifts, or mounting or dismounting animals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting a single patron board/exit a ride, boat, ski lift, or mount/dismount an animal. A competent attendant typically takes 1–3 minutes per patron.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic physical steadying of unpredictable humans, coordinating with moving equipment (lifts, boats), adapting to varied body types, and real-time safety judgment — well beyond current humanoid robot generalization and safe human-contact capabilities.

task: Apply coatings, such as paint, ink, or lacquer, to protect or decorate workpiece surfaces, using spray guns, pens, or brushes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = coating one typical workpiece (e.g. a panel or part). A competent worker preps, applies, and inspects one piece in ~20–30 min depending on size and coating type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, consistent tool-path control, pressure regulation, and surface coverage judgment. Spray guns and brushes demand fine dexterity and adaptive force control; uneven surfaces and quality inspection add difficulty. Current humanoids lack reliable coating-quality generalization.

task: Examine car roofs for wear and damage, and repair defective sections, using roofing material, cement, nails, and waterproof paint.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and repairing the roof of one car. A competent worker spends ~15 min inspecting and ~30 min patching/sealing defective sections.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual/tactile inspection of curved surfaces, precise application of cement, nails, and waterproof paint in varied outdoor conditions. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, tool-use generalization, and damage-assessment judgment needed.

task: Etch, lap, polish, or grind wafers or ingots to form circuitry and change conductive properties, using etching, lapping, polishing, or grinding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one batch of wafers/ingots through a single etching, lapping, polishing, or grinding cycle; a skilled technician typically spends ~30–60 min on setup, processing, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Semiconductor fab tools are highly specialized, precision machines requiring exact chemical handling, ultra-fine parameter control, and cleanroom protocols. Current humanoid robots lack the dexterity, sub-micron precision, and domain-specific tool interfacing needed; existing automation is narrow/fixed, not general-purpose humanoid.

task: Turn valves or disconnect hoses to eliminate water, cleaning solutions, or vapors from machinery or tanks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = draining one machine or tank; locating the valve/hose, actuating it, and confirming drainage complete takes a competent worker roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Turning industrial valves and disconnecting hoses requires varied grip forces, tool-free coupling types, spatial reasoning in cluttered machinery environments, and leak verification — beyond reliable frontier humanoid capability in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Move fingers over surfaces of carvings to ensure smoothness of finish.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one carved piece by touch. A skilled craftsperson runs fingers over all surfaces in ~2–4 minutes to detect rough spots or tool marks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine tactile/haptic perception across irregular 3D surfaces — far beyond current robot fingertip sensor fidelity. Generalization to varied carving geometries is unsolved. Robots lack the nuanced touch discrimination needed for quality acceptance.

task: Sell and serve refreshments to customers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = serving one customer: taking order, preparing/dispensing refreshment, handling payment, and handing off. Typical fast-service interaction ~3–5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires integrating natural-language order-taking, cash/card payment handling, beverage/food prep dexterity, and social interaction. Current humanoids lack reliable POS integration, fluid pouring, change-making, and customer-facing generalization for full autonomous service.

task: Load semiconductor material into furnace.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a batch of semiconductor material (wafers/crucible/ingot) into a furnace, including positioning, securing, and confirming placement. Typically 10–20 min for a skilled technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity to handle fragile wafers/crucibles without contamination or breakage, cleanroom compliance, tool-specific interfaces, and exact spatial tolerances. Current humanoids lack the required precision and contamination-control protocols.

task: Load sandblasting equipment with abrasives, attach nozzles to hoses, and turn valves to admit compressed air and activate jets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fully setting up sandblasting equipment for a session: filling hopper with abrasive, attaching nozzles to hoses, and opening valves. A competent operator takes ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires pouring granular abrasive into a hopper (challenging with free-flowing media), threading/coupling nozzle fittings under torque, and precisely sequencing valve operations. High dexterity, tool-use, and force-control demands exceed current humanoid capabilities. Industrial environment adds clutter and safety risks.

task: Operate refreshment stands during intermission or obtain refreshments for press box patrons during performances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = serving a wave of patrons during a single intermission or press-box run (~15–25 min of active service, taking orders, handling payment, and delivering items).
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating crowded, dynamic venues, handling cash/cards, taking verbal orders, carrying trays through crowds, and social interaction. Current humanoids lack reliable crowd navigation, payment processing, and unstructured customer-service generalization.

task: Place rolls of paper or cardboard on machine feed tracks, and thread paper through gluing, coating, and slitting rollers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading one roll onto feed tracks and threading it through rollers. A competent machine operator typically takes 5–10 minutes per roll change/setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires lifting heavy industrial rolls, precise alignment on feed tracks, and delicate threading through tight roller gaps. Current humanoids lack the payload capacity, fine tension control, and specialized tool-use needed for reliable autonomous execution in an industrial setting.

task: Lay out designs or dimensions from sketches or blueprints on stone surfaces, freehand or by transferring them from tracing paper, using scribes or chalk and measuring instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out a single design/pattern on one stone surface. A skilled mason/stonecutter typically takes 30–60 min to interpret a blueprint, transfer measurements, and scribe accurate layout lines.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool-use (scribe, chalk, measuring instruments) on irregular stone, blueprint interpretation, freehand drawing precision, and spatial reasoning. Current humanoids lack the fine-motor control and domain perception for this craft task.

task: Set or adjust machine controls, according to specifications, type of operation, or material requirements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting controls for a single job/material setup. A competent operator reads specs and sets dials, switches, or digital inputs in ~3–8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading specs, interpreting machine-specific interfaces (dials, touchscreens, keypads), and precise manipulation. Current humanoids lack robust UI interaction, spec-reading comprehension, and generalization across diverse machine types.

task: Examine quality of film fades or dissolves for potential color corrections, using color analyzers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining one fade/dissolve transition with a color analyzer; a skilled colorist typically spends ~5–10 min per transition reviewing waveforms, vectorscopes, and flagging corrections.
robot_success_rationale: Task is primarily perceptual/cognitive (interpreting color analyzer readouts, aesthetic judgment on film quality) with minimal physical manipulation. Current humanoid robots lack the visual cognition, domain expertise, and fine UI interaction needed to operate specialized color-grading equipment autonomously.

task: Align and position materials being joined to ensure accurate application of adhesive or heat sealing.
