human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning and positioning a single set of materials (e.g., two panels or components) before bonding. A skilled worker typically takes 2–5 minutes per join.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place objects but precise alignment for bonding requires fine dexterity, tactile feedback, and perception of material edges/seams. Varied material shapes and compliance reduce reliability significantly in unstructured settings.

task: Repair gas and oil meters and gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single meter or gauge: diagnosis, disassembly, part replacement/adjustment, reassembly, and verification. Competent technician typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for small precision components, diagnostic reasoning, use of specialized tools, and safety-critical judgment with gas/oil systems. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured field settings.

task: Operate and maintain finishing machines to grind, drill, sand, bevel, decorate, wash, or polish glass or glass products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one glass piece through a finishing machine (setup, operation, inspection). Competent operator averages ~15 min per piece including minor adjustments.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial finishing machines, fine dexterous handling of fragile glass, machine parameter adjustment, and quality inspection. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use on complex machinery and safe glass manipulation at production quality.

task: Order, unload, and store feed and supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full cycle of ordering, receiving/unloading a delivery, and storing feed/supplies for a typical farm operation; ~1 hour total across all steps.
robot_success_rationale: Ordering requires internet/phone interaction and decision-making beyond current robots. Unloading heavy/bulky bags of feed and navigating storage areas is feasible but generalization is poor. End-to-end autonomy across all three subtasks is far beyond 2026 capability.

task: Inspect recreational equipment, such as rope tows, T-bars, J-bars, or chair lifts, for safety hazards and damage or wear.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full inspection of one lift (e.g., one chairlift). Inspector walks the line, checks towers, cables, grips, and mechanical components; typically 45–75 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires traversing rugged outdoor/snowy mountain terrain, climbing towers, and performing nuanced wear/damage assessment on specialized machinery. Current humanoids lack outdoor terrain capability, high-altitude mobility, and domain-specific inspection judgment.

task: Design and create glass objects, using blowpipes and artisans' hand tools and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = crafting a single glass object (e.g., a vase or bowl). A skilled glassblower typically takes 30–60 minutes per piece including gathering, shaping, and annealing setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme heat tolerance, continuous breath control via blowpipe, ultra-fine dexterous manipulation of molten glass, and fluid real-time adaptation. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in dexterity, thermal resilience, and sensorimotor coordination.

task: Remove or add stencils during blasting to create differing cut depths, intricate designs, or rough, pitted finishes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = managing stencils on one workpiece during a blasting session, including precise placement, removal, and re-masking steps for layered depth effects.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle thin, adhesive stencil materials precisely on varied surfaces, judgment about timing mid-blast, tool use (blasting equipment), and working in a particulate-filled, harsh environment — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Clean sporting equipment, vehicles, rides, booths, facilities, or grounds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one facility/equipment unit (e.g., a ride, booth, or grounds section). Typical shift-task unit for a venue worker; 30–60 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Cleaning varies enormously across equipment types, vehicles, rides, and outdoor grounds. Unstructured outdoor/semi-outdoor environments, irregular surfaces, varied tools (hoses, brushes, vacuums), and obstacle-rich layouts challenge current robot locomotion, perception, and generalization significantly.

task: Measure and record patients' vital signs, such as height, weight, temperature, blood pressure, pulse, or respiration.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full set of vitals (height, weight, temp, BP, pulse, respiration) for one patient. A competent medical assistant typically completes this in 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires patient cooperation, safe physical contact, precise device placement (BP cuff, thermometer), mobility assistance, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack medical-grade sensor integration, safe patient handling, and reliable fine dexterity on a non-compliant human subject.

task: Order, label, and count stock of medications, chemicals, or supplies and enter inventory data into computer.
human_time_rationale: One inventory session covering a typical medication/supply storage area: physically counting items, labeling, and entering data into a computer system — roughly 30–60 min for a competent pharmacy/lab tech.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading small labels, manipulating diverse containers, navigating shelving, AND operating a computer UI to enter data. Current robots lack reliable OCR-to-keyboarding integration, fine dexterity with varied packaging, and end-to-end autonomy across all sub-tasks.

task: Operate machinery used in the production process, or assist machine operators.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one hour of operating/assisting at a production machine during a shift cycle. A typical shift segment between handoffs or product runs is ~1 hour.
robot_success_rationale: Operating varied production machinery requires specialized controls, situational awareness, and adaptive decision-making. Humanoids lack the machine interfaces, domain-specific training, and safety certifications needed. Simple assist tasks (part feeding) score higher, but overall autonomous operation is very limited in 2026.

task: Smooth surfaces of carvings, using rubbing stones.
human_time_rationale: One instance = smoothing one carving. A competent craftsperson spends ~15–25 min rubbing and inspecting a typical carved piece to an acceptable finish.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced force control, complex 3-D surface tracking, and tactile feedback to judge smoothness on irregular carved geometry. Current robots lack the fine haptic sensing and adaptive compliance needed for consistent, acceptable results.

task: Rinse objects and place them on drying racks or use cloth, squeegees, or air compressors to dry surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rinsing and drying a typical batch of objects (e.g., a few dishes/tools). A competent worker completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can grasp and place objects but struggle with reliable rinsing under a faucet, precisely placing items on drying racks, and wielding tools like squeegees or air compressors with correct force and technique across varied object shapes.

task: Monitor activities to ensure adherence to rules and safety procedures, or arrange for the removal of unruly patrons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one monitoring shift segment (~1 hour). A competent security/venue staff member continuously observes patrons, interprets behavior, and intervenes as needed.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social judgment, crowd behavior interpretation, verbal de-escalation, physical intervention with unruly humans, and legal/ethical authority — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities. Safety and liability constraints also prohibit autonomous removal of patrons.

task: Finish parts, using hacksaws or hand, rotary, or squaring shears.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing one part with a hacksaw or shears. A competent worker typically takes 5–10 minutes depending on material and cut complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force control, secure workholding, and correct tool selection/use (hacksaw, rotary/squaring shears). Current humanoids lack reliable fine tool-use, consistent cutting accuracy, and safe handling of sharp implements on varied parts.

task: Fill glue and paraffin reservoirs, and position rollers to dispense glue onto paperboard.
human_time_rationale: One setup instance: filling both glue and paraffin reservoirs and adjusting roller positions on a paperboard gluing machine — a skilled operator takes ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires pouring viscous/hot fluids into reservoirs without spilling, fine positional adjustment of rollers with precise force feedback, and machine-specific knowledge. Current humanoids lack reliable fluid handling and fine mechanical adjustment capability.

task: Adjust controls to regulate specified temperature and humidity of animal quarters, nurseries, or exhibit areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking and adjusting HVAC/climate controls for a single zone. A competent worker reads gauges/sensors, identifies deviations, and adjusts controls in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading digital/analog displays, interpreting sensor data, and manipulating HVAC control panels or thermostats — challenging for generalist humanoids due to varied UI formats, fine dial/button manipulation, and contextual judgment about target parameters.

task: Provide treatment to sick or injured animals, or contact veterinarians to secure treatment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing a sick/injured animal and either administering basic treatment or contacting a vet. Covers initial triage through first action taken, typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced animal behavioral reading, safe physical restraint of an uncooperative living animal, clinical judgment, and communication. Far beyond current robot perception, dexterity, and autonomous decision-making in unstructured caregiving scenarios.

task: Fill hoppers, reservoirs, troughs, or pans with material used to coat, paint, or spray, using conveyors or pails.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling a single hopper/reservoir/trough/pan. A competent worker gathers material, positions pail or conveyor, and fills to the required level — typically 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires lifting and tilting heavy pails, operating conveyors, judging fill levels, and navigating varied industrial containers. Robots struggle with pouring liquids/powders accurately, variable container geometries, and safe handling of heavy/awkward loads in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Operate, drive, or explain the use of mechanical riding devices or other automatic equipment in amusement parks, carnivals, or recreation areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one ride cycle: greeting guests, loading/restraining riders, operating controls, unloading. Typical cycle including queue management is ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced crowd interaction, safety restraint checks on diverse body types, emergency judgment, and operating specialized ride controls. Humanoids lack reliable dexterity, situational social awareness, and safety certification for this high-stakes public role.

task: Trim edges of books to size, using cutting machines, book trimming machines, or hand cutters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming one book/booklet. A skilled operator feeds, aligns, and cuts a book on a three-knife trimmer or guillotine in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of flexible, variably-sized books, operating industrial cutting machinery with exact positioning, and safety-critical tool use. Humanoid robots lack reliable fine-alignment perception and safe control of guillotine/trimmer machinery in 2026.

task: Calibrate or maintain machines, such as those used for plasma collection.
human_time_rationale: One calibration/maintenance session on a plasma collection machine; includes setup, running calibration checks, adjustments, and documentation — typically 30–60 min per machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of specialized medical hardware, precise tool use, interpreting machine-specific readouts, and following complex protocols. Current humanoids lack the precision, domain tooling, and reliable autonomy for medical device calibration.

task: Fasten seams or joints together with welds, bolts, cement, rivets, solder, caulks, metal drive clips, or bonds to assemble components into products or to repair sheet metal items.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fastening seams/joints on a single sheet metal assembly or repair. A competent worker spends ~20–30 min including setup, positioning, tool selection, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires switching among specialized tools (welder, rivet gun, caulk gun, soldering iron), precise force control on varied materials/geometries, and quality judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use generalization.

task: Fabricate or alter parts at construction sites, using shears, hammers, punches, or drills.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating or altering one part (e.g., cutting, punching, or drilling a structural piece). A competent ironworker/sheet metal worker typically takes ~30–60 min per part.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy, loud power tools (shears, drills, hammers, punches) on a dynamic, unstructured outdoor construction site. Current humanoids lack the force control, tool-use generalization, terrain adaptability, and safety compliance needed.

task: Clean and lubricate equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and lubricating a single piece of equipment (e.g., a machine or tool). A competent worker typically spends ~30 min on inspection, wiping, and applying lubricant.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can wipe surfaces moderately well, but precisely identifying lubrication points, applying correct lubricant amounts, and handling varied equipment configurations requires fine dexterity, tool-use, and domain-specific perception that 2026 robots struggle with.

task: Assemble and use equipment, such as catheters, tracheotomy tubes, or oxygen suppliers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and applying a single piece of medical equipment (e.g., catheter or trach tube setup) on one patient; a competent nurse typically takes 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sterile technique, tactile sensitivity, and real-time patient response awareness. Current humanoids lack the precision, compliance control, and medical-grade reliability needed for invasive procedures.

task: Examine finished surfaces of workpieces to verify conformance to specifications and retouch any defective areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and retouching one workpiece. A competent worker spends ~3–5 min examining surfaces and ~3–5 min retouching defects, totaling ~8 min median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-resolution visual inspection for subtle surface defects AND precise retouching (fine dexterity with tools). Current humanoids lack reliable defect-detection perception and the fine-motor control needed for quality retouching work.

task: Produce timed prints with separate densities or color settings for each scene of a production.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one scene's timed print. A skilled lab technician spends ~45 min evaluating, dialing in density/color settings, running test strips, and producing the final print for a single scene.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized film printing/color timing equipment, making nuanced aesthetic color/density judgments, and interpreting cinematic intent. No current humanoid robot has the perception, tool-use, or domain expertise for this workflow.

task: Repair broken scrolls by replacing them with new sections of tubing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing one broken scroll; a skilled HVAC/refrigeration technician cuts out the damaged section, fits and secures replacement tubing in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise cutting, fitting, and joining (brazing/soldering) of tubing in tight, variable spaces. Beyond current robot dexterity, tool-use, and thermal process control. Highly unstructured and force-sensitive.

task: Transport prefabricated parts to construction sites for assembly and installation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one delivery trip (loading, driving to site, unloading). Typical round trip with loading/unloading averages ~2 hours for a competent driver/logistics worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a truck on public/site roads (humanoids cannot drive), loading heavy/bulky prefab parts onto vehicles, and navigating dynamic construction sites — all beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Guide nozzles over stone, following stencil outlines, or chip along marks to create designs or to work surfaces down to specified finishes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single stenciled or marked design on one stone surface. A competent stoneworker typically takes 30–60 min depending on complexity and size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled tool guidance over irregular stone surfaces, stencil/mark perception, and sustained fine dexterity under vibration and debris. Current humanoids lack the precision, compliance control, and dust-environment robustness needed.

task: Install, maintain, and repair animal care facility equipment, such as infrared lights, feeding devices, and cages.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one equipment job (install, maintain, or repair one item, e.g. a cage or feeder). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min per unit including diagnosis and testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse tool use (screwdrivers, wiring, pliers), fault diagnosis, and working in confined/irregular spaces around live animals. Current humanoids lack the dexterous fault-finding, electrical work, and generalization needed for autonomous repair tasks of this complexity.

task: Place products in equipment or on work surfaces for further processing, inspecting, or wrapping.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing a single product on a surface or into equipment. A competent worker does this in roughly 1–2 minutes including positioning and alignment.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of common objects on tidy surfaces is a core robot strength (~70–90%), but varied product shapes, precise placement targets, and integration with moving equipment or conveyors reduce reliability somewhat.

task: Collect and record animal information, such as weight, size, physical condition, treatments received, medications given, and food intake.
human_time_rationale: One instance = recording a full data profile for one animal (weighing, measuring, observing condition, logging treatments/meds/food). A competent vet tech or zookeeper takes ~15 min per animal.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling live, unpredictable animals (huge dexterity/safety challenge), using scales and measurement tools, interpreting physical condition, and accurately logging multi-field records. Current robots lack animal-handling skill, fine perception of body condition, and reliable structured data entry.

task: Align wheels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning all four wheels on a single vehicle. A skilled technician attaches sensors, adjusts toe/camber/caster, and verifies readings — typically 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a vehicle lift, attaching precision alignment sensors to wheel hubs, interpreting specialized equipment readouts, and adjusting steering/suspension components — far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity and tool-use capabilities.

task: Prepare dough, following recipe.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one batch of dough per recipe: gathering ingredients, measuring, mixing, and kneading. Typical bread/pastry dough takes ~20–30 min hands-on.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measuring of varied ingredients, pouring liquids, kneading with proper force/texture feedback, and interpreting recipe steps. Tactile feedback for dough consistency is beyond current general-purpose robot capability; unstructured kitchen manipulation is also very challenging.

task: Locate and repair frayed wiring, broken connections, or incorrect wiring, using ohmmeters, soldering irons, tape, or hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating and repairing a single wiring fault. Includes diagnosis with ohmmeter, accessing the wiring, and performing the repair (soldering/taping/reconnecting). ~30–60 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation (soldering, stripping wires, using hand tools), fault diagnosis via instruments, and navigation of cluttered/confined spaces. Current humanoids lack the fine motor precision, tool-use fluency, and diagnostic reasoning needed.

task: Draw blood from arteries, using arterial collection techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one arterial blood draw (ABG). A skilled phlebotomist/respiratory therapist takes ~5–15 min including site prep, puncture, compression, and labeling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter needle targeting of a pulsing artery under skin, sterile technique, real-time pressure feedback, and patient safety management. Far beyond current robot dexterity and medical compliance limits.

task: Issue and cash money orders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = issuing and cashing a single money order. Competent teller verifies ID, processes payment, prints/stamps, and hands over the order in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a POS terminal or dedicated machine, verifying identity documents, handling cash, printing, and customer interaction. Humanoid robots lack financial system integration, fraud detection, and reliable fine dexterous cash handling.

task: Inspect, test, or measure materials, products, installations, or work for conformance to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One inspection instance = a single product, installation, or work area reviewed against spec. Competent inspector typically takes 15–30 min including measurement, documentation, and judgment.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle structured visual/dimensional checks well, but conformance inspection spans diverse tools (calipers, gauges, meters), unstructured environments, contextual spec interpretation, and pass/fail judgment — well beyond 2026 frontier capability reliably.

task: Start machines or equipment to begin production processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting a production machine/line. A competent operator checks readiness, flips switches/presses controls, and confirms startup — typically 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to machine, identifying and manipulating varied controls (buttons, switches, dials), reading displays, and verifying startup status. Current humanoids lack reliable generalization across diverse industrial control panels and confirmation feedback loops.

task: Sterilize equipment and supplies, using germicides, sterilizer, or autoclave.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one sterilization batch: gathering items, loading autoclave/germicide, running cycle (~15–20 min), unloading. Total ~25–35 min for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise loading of varied instruments into trays/pouches, operating autoclave controls, handling sterile items without contamination, and judgment on sterilization method. Dexterity and contamination-avoidance protocols are well beyond current general-purpose robots.

task: Apply compresses, ice bags, or hot water bottles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and applying a compress/ice bag/hot water bottle to a patient. A competent caregiver takes ~3–7 minutes to prepare and position it correctly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires filling containers with water/ice, handling flexible/deformable objects, safely positioning on a human body without causing harm, and judging placement — all challenging for current humanoids. Safety sensitivity and unstructured body contact further reduce reliability.

task: Observe patients, charting and reporting changes in patients' conditions, such as adverse reactions to medication or treatment, and taking any necessary action.
human_time_rationale: One instance = observing a single patient, documenting findings, and acting on any changes. A competent nurse typically spends ~15–25 minutes per patient per assessment round.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced clinical judgment, subtle symptom recognition, dynamic communication, and autonomous decision-making in high-stakes unstructured environments — far beyond 2026 robot capabilities in perception, reasoning, and caregiving generalization.

task: Fabricate, install, position, or connect components, parts, finished products, or instruments for testing or operational purposes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling/installing a single component or sub-assembly unit. A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min depending on complexity; 45 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied dexterity, precise positioning, tool use, and contextual judgment across diverse parts. Robots handle structured pick-and-place well but struggle with fastening, alignment, wiring connections, and generalization to novel components.

task: Repair window sash frames, attach weather stripping and channels to frames, and replace window glass, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing one window sash frame, including stripping, channels, and glass replacement. A skilled glazier/carpenter typically takes 30–60 min per window.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool use (putty knife, glazier's points, caulk gun), handling fragile glass, fine force-controlled sealing, and multi-step sequencing in varied window geometries. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Measure amounts of products, lengths of extruded articles, or weights of filled containers to ensure conformance to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single product/article/container using a scale, caliper, or ruler and recording the result. A competent worker typically completes this in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick up objects and place them on scales or use vision for length measurement, but reliable handling of varied containers/extruded articles, precise tool use (calipers), and logging results autonomously remain inconsistent. Perception and dexterity gaps reduce success.

task: Prepare raw materials for processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a batch of raw materials (e.g., sorting, measuring, staging). A competent worker typically takes ~30 min per batch cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Highly variable task: requires identifying diverse raw materials, sorting, measuring, and staging. Perception and generalization across unstructured material types remain weak; dexterous handling of irregular/bulk materials is unreliable for frontier humanoids.

task: Load and unload items from machines, conveyors, and conveyances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one load/unload cycle (e.g., emptying a conveyor bin or loading a machine). A competent worker completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle pick-and-place of uniform items reasonably well (~70–80%), but varied item shapes, sizes, orientations, and dynamic conveyor environments reduce reliability. Generalization across machine types and tight spaces is still limited.

task: Superimpose bent tubing on asbestos patterns to ensure accuracy.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning and checking one bent tube against its asbestos pattern template. A skilled tradesperson does this quickly per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual manipulation to align irregular curved tubing on a flat pattern, fine visual inspection for conformance, and judgment of fit. Current robots lack the dexterous finesse and inspection reasoning for consistent success.

task: Restrain patients to prevent violence or injury or to assist physicians or nurses to administer treatments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restraining a single patient episode, from initial assessment through applying restraints. Typically 5–15 minutes depending on patient cooperation and method.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced force modulation to avoid injury, real-time reading of patient distress/resistance, clinical judgment, and legal/ethical accountability. Current humanoids lack safe, adaptive physical compliance for human contact in dynamic, unpredictable caregiving scenarios.

task: Shape replacement parts, using bench grinders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grinding/shaping one replacement part to spec. A skilled machinist or toolmaker typically takes 10–20 min per part depending on material and tolerance.
robot_success_rationale: Bench grinding demands precise force feedback, fine dexterous control, real-time visual/tactile gauging of material removal, and safe tool handling near high-speed abrasive wheels — well beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Shape metal material over anvils, blocks, or other forms, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shaping one metal workpiece over a form/anvil. A competent metalworker typically spends 30–60 min per piece depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force, precise hammering with nuanced feedback; interpreting metal deformation in real time; repositioning heavy workpieces. Current humanoids lack the strength, fine force control, and tactile feedback needed for this skilled trade task.

task: Refinish wood surfaces on cabinets, doors, moldings, or floors, using power sanders, putty, spray equipment, brushes, paints, or varnishes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = refinishing one standard cabinet door or comparable surface area (sand, fill, apply finish). A competent finisher typically takes ~1.5–2 hours per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step tool switching (sander, brush, sprayer), consistent pressure/motion on varied surfaces, fine tactile feedback for finish quality, navigation around fixed structures, and chemical handling — far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Repair holes in tire tubes, using scrapers and patches.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing one puncture on one tube: locate hole, scrape, apply patch/adhesive, cure, and verify. A skilled technician takes ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate multi-step dexterous manipulation: precise scraping, applying thin adhesive, positioning a small patch under pressure, and tactile quality verification. Far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity and tool-use generalization.

task: Unclamp and hoist full reels from braiding, winding, or other fabricating machines, using power hoists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unclamping and hoisting one full reel from a machine. Attaching hoist, releasing clamps, and lifting takes ~5–10 minutes for a skilled worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating an overhead power hoist (specialized tool), unclamping heavy industrial fixtures, and handling very large/heavy reels. Humanoids lack hoist integration, have limited force output, and poor generalization to varied industrial clamp mechanisms.

task: Wash, cut, and prepare foods designated for cooking.
human_time_rationale: One instance = prepping a standard batch of ingredients for a single recipe (washing, peeling, chopping). A competent cook typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for gripping/cutting varied produce shapes, safe knife handling, force control, and perception of ripeness/readiness. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and safe blade use in unstructured kitchen environments.

task: Grade, classify, or sort products according to sizes, weights, colors, or other specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grading/sorting one item (e.g., a piece of fruit, a manufactured part). A competent worker handles ~30–60 items per minute in light sorting; ~1–2 min per item for detailed inspection/grading.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based classification is strong; robots can pick and place items into bins. Challenges arise with irregular objects, subtle color/texture grading, varied item presentation, and unstructured conveyor environments. Narrow industrial systems score higher; general-purpose humanoids score lower.

task: Perform test production runs and make adjustments as necessary to ensure that completed products meet standards and specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single test production run cycle: setup, running samples, inspecting output, and making adjustments. A competent operator typically completes this in ~1 hour.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level judgment, multi-step decision-making, reading gauges/specs, adjusting machinery controls, and diagnosing quality deviations. Current humanoids lack the cognitive integration and tool-operation generalization needed for autonomous process adjustment.

task: Rinse, drain, or wipe coated workpieces to remove excess coating material or to facilitate setting of finish coats on workpieces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rinsing, draining, or wiping a single coated workpiece. A competent worker completes this in ~1–3 minutes depending on workpiece size.
robot_success_rationale: Simple wiping/draining motions are feasible, but variable workpiece shapes, wet/slippery surfaces, and precise force control to avoid streaking or damaging coating challenge current humanoid dexterity and perception.

task: Tend accessory equipment, such as pumps or conveyors, to move materials or ingredients through production processes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single tending cycle (checking, adjusting, starting/stopping equipment). A competent worker handles this every 15–30 minutes during a shift.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading gauges, adjusting valves/controls, and responding to abnormal states across varied industrial environments. Perception of analog dials, fine valve manipulation, and generalization to diverse equipment layouts are beyond reliable 2026 robot capability.

task: Position spouts or chutes of storage bins so that containers can be filled.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning one spout/chute on a storage bin so a container can be filled. A worker locates the bin, adjusts the spout/chute, and confirms alignment — typically 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise grasping and alignment of a spout/chute, which varies in shape, weight, and attachment mechanism. Perception of correct positioning and confirming seal/alignment is challenging; industrial bin hardware is often non-standardized, limiting generalization.

task: Examine or test operation of parts or systems to ensure completeness of repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting/testing a repaired part or system (e.g., a mechanical assembly or appliance). A competent technician typically spends 10–20 minutes checking function, cycling parts, and confirming quality.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting repair context, operating varied controls/switches, judging functional quality, and generalizing across many system types. Current robots lack reliable multi-modal diagnostic reasoning and tool-use generalization for open-ended inspection tasks.

task: Anesthetize and inoculate animals, according to instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = anesthetizing and inoculating a single animal (e.g., a lab rodent). Prep, administration, and monitoring typically takes ~10–20 minutes per animal.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise needle injection into a live, potentially moving animal, fine dexterity for dosing, and real-time physiological monitoring. Far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity and bio-sensing capabilities. High safety stakes leave near-zero acceptable autonomous margin.

task: Confer with customers, read work orders, or examine vehicles needing repair to determine the nature and extent of damage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing a single vehicle; includes brief customer/work-order review and physical walk-around inspection to identify damage scope.
robot_success_rationale: Requires natural-language customer dialogue, reading unstructured work orders, and expert-level damage diagnosis. Current robots lack conversational social interaction, automotive domain expertise, and nuanced visual damage interpretation at acceptable quality.

task: Insert book bodies in devices that form back edges of books into convex shapes and produce grooves that facilitate cover attachment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inserting one book body into a rounding/backing machine. A skilled bindery worker positions and feeds a book block in ~2–4 minutes including alignment checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of a flexible, deformable book block into a specialized industrial machine, fine positional perception, and careful handling. Current humanoids struggle with deformable objects, precise part registration, and operating non-standard industrial machinery controls.

task: Admit required amounts of water, steam, cooking oils, or compressed air into equipment, such as by opening water valves to cool mixtures to the desired consistency.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single admission event (opening a valve, adjusting flow, and confirming target consistency/temperature). Typically takes a few minutes per adjustment cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating and operating industrial valves (moderate dexterity), reading gauges or sensors for feedback, and judging mixture consistency — perception and generalization across varied equipment layouts limits reliability significantly.

task: Conduct field work at outdoor sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one outdoor field visit (travel to site, data collection, observations, samples). A typical field session runs ~4 hours for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Unstructured outdoor terrain, varied weather, complex multi-step data collection, tool use, and judgment calls are far beyond current general-purpose humanoid robots. Rough terrain locomotion, sample collection, and autonomous mission planning remain severely limited.

task: Select or install combined heat units, power units, cogeneration equipment, or trigeneration equipment that reduces energy use or pollution.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting/installing a single cogeneration or trigeneration unit. A competent technician/engineer spends ~4–8 hours on site assessment, equipment selection, and physical installation hookup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy machinery operation, complex plumbing/electrical connections, engineering judgment for system selection, and working in unstructured industrial environments. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in dexterity, tool-use, and domain reasoning.

task: Lift raw materials, finished products, and packed items, manually or using hoists.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lifting and repositioning a single item (raw material, finished product, or packed item). A competent worker typically completes this in ~5 minutes including setup and placement.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids can pick-and-place common objects but struggle with heavy, bulky, or irregularly shaped loads, hoist operation, and unstructured industrial environments. Payload limits, grasp reliability, and hoist tool-use reduce success significantly.

task: Connect electrical systems to outside power sources, and activate switches to test the operation of appliances or light fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting one electrical system/circuit to an external power source and testing its appliances/fixtures; a competent electrician typically takes ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires identifying correct terminals, making safe electrical connections, operating breakers/switches, and interpreting results. Involves fine dexterous tool-use, safety-critical decisions, and unstructured wiring environments — well beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Repair leaks with caulking compound or replace pipes, using pipe wrenches.
human_time_rationale: One repair instance: locating the leak, applying caulk or swapping a pipe section with wrenches. A competent plumber typically takes 30–60 min per repair.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-torque wrench use in tight/awkward spaces, precise caulk application, leak diagnosis, and working under sinks or in confined areas — well beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and generalization limits.

task: Observe operation of various machines to detect and correct machine malfunctions such as improper forming, glue flow, or pasteboard tension.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one observe-detect-correct cycle per machine; a skilled operator typically identifies and fixes a malfunction (forming, glue, tension) in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained multi-sense monitoring, nuanced fault diagnosis (e.g. glue flow, pasteboard tension), and precise mechanical correction. Current robots lack reliable industrial machine perception, contextual fault reasoning, and tool-use for adjustments in unstructured factory settings.

task: Examine completed work to detect defects and verify conformance to work orders, and adjust machinery as necessary to correct production problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a batch/unit of completed work, checking against a work order, and making one machinery adjustment. Typical cycle in light manufacturing takes ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step reasoning: visual defect detection (moderate), cross-referencing work orders (cognition), AND adjusting machinery controls (dexterous manipulation of varied knobs/panels). Integrated autonomous execution of all three remains well beyond 2026 frontier robots.

task: Transfer animals between enclosures to facilitate breeding, birthing, shipping, or rearrangement of exhibits.
human_time_rationale: One instance = transferring one animal between two enclosures. Includes preparation, coaxing/herding, safe containment, transport, and release. Varies by species; 45 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires unpredictable animal handling, species-specific behavioral knowledge, safe restraint, and navigation through complex zoo environments. Current robots lack animal behavior perception, safe physical interaction with live animals, and the judgment to avoid injury or escape incidents.

task: Adjust guide assemblies, forming bars, and folding mechanisms according to specifications, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting one guide/forming/folding component to spec; a skilled technician spends ~20–30 min positioning, fastening, and verifying alignment with hand tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool use (wrenches, screwdrivers) on varied mechanical assemblies, reading specs, fine positional adjustments, and tactile feedback to verify torque/alignment — far beyond current humanoid dexterity and generalization.

task: Inspect recreational vehicles to diagnose problems and perform necessary adjustment, repair, or overhaul.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full inspection + diagnosis + repair of one RV. A competent technician typically spends 1–3 hours depending on complexity; 2 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: RV repair demands highly varied dexterity, tool use, diagnosis under vehicle, crawling into confined spaces, operating lifts, and expert judgment. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities in unstructured, multi-system vehicle service environments.

task: Develop or perform operational, maintenance, or testing procedures for electronic products, components, equipment, or systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = developing/performing a procedure for one electronic component or system. Competent technician spends ~45 min covering setup, testing steps, diagnostics, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting schematics, connecting test equipment (probes, multimeters, oscilloscopes), fine-pin manipulation, adaptive decision-making from readings, and documentation — far beyond 2026 humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Remove finished cores, and stack or place them on conveyors for transfer to other work areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one finished core and stacking/placing it on a conveyor. Worker lifts, orients, and places; ~3 min per core in a production setting.
robot_success_rationale: Cores vary in size, weight, and shape; grasping and stacking requires reliable perception and moderate dexterity. Conveyor placement adds locomotion/navigation demands. Unstructured variation and payload uncertainty limit current humanoid success.

task: Connect water hoses to inlet pipes of plumbing systems, and test operation of toilets or sinks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting hoses to one fixture's inlet and running a functional test. A competent plumber takes ~15–25 min per fixture.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tight-space dexterity (under sinks/behind toilets), precise thread alignment of hose fittings, torque-controlled tightening to prevent leaks, and interpreting a leak-free flow test. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose robot capability.

task: Perform crop production duties, such as tilling, hoeing, pruning, weeding, or harvesting crops.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one hour of active crop production work (tilling, hoeing, pruning, weeding, or harvesting a typical plot section); a manager measures productivity in hourly increments.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor unstructured terrain, variable crops, and diverse tools (hoes, pruners, harvesters) are highly challenging. Current humanoids lack robust outdoor locomotion, precise plant manipulation, and generalization across multiple distinct crop-care subtasks autonomously.

task: Inspect equipment to detect wear and damage and perform minor repairs, adjustments, or maintenance tasks, such as oiling parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single piece of equipment, noting wear/damage, and performing a minor repair or adjustment (e.g., oiling moving parts). Typical for a maintenance technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires combining visual inspection for subtle wear, tool use, fine manipulation (oiling, adjusting), and judgment across varied equipment types. Current robots lack reliable defect perception and multi-step dexterous repair generalization.

task: Remove damaged exterior panels, and repair and replace structural frame members.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing damaged panels and repairing/replacing one structural frame section (e.g., on a vehicle or building). A skilled tradesperson typically takes 1.5–2.5 hours per such repair cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force tool use (impact wrenches, pry bars, welding/cutting equipment), precise structural alignment, damage assessment judgment, and multi-step unstructured work. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Maintain or repair agricultural facilities, equipment, or tools to ensure operational readiness, safety, and cleanliness.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a typical maintenance/repair session on a piece of equipment or facility area (e.g., lubricating a tractor, sharpening blades, cleaning a barn stall). ~1 hour is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse tool use, fault diagnosis, heavy/awkward parts, outdoor/unstructured environments, and safety judgment. Current humanoids lack the generalization, dexterity, and domain-specific tool skills needed for autonomous agricultural maintenance.

task: Observe tracks to detect obstructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a defined segment of track (e.g., ~0.5–1 mile patrol). A competent track walker covers this on foot in ~25–35 minutes, scanning for debris, misalignments, or obstructions.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor uneven terrain (rail ballast, ties) is challenging for humanoid locomotion (~30–50%). Visual obstruction detection is feasible but generalization to varied, unexpected obstructions in an unstructured outdoor rail environment is unreliable. No commercial deployment exists yet.

task: Prepare and analyze samples to study effects of drugs, gases, pesticides, or microorganisms on cell structure and tissue.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and analyzing a single batch of biological samples (e.g., cell cultures or tissue sections) exposed to one agent. Includes fixation, staining, mounting, and microscopic analysis — typically 60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly precise pipetting, delicate tissue handling, microscopy operation, and expert scientific judgment to interpret cellular/tissue changes. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and cognitive generalization. Lab automation exists but is task-specific, not general-purpose.

task: Provide medical treatment or personal care to patients in private home settings, such as cooking, keeping rooms orderly, seeing that patients are comfortable and in good spirits, or instructing family members in simple nursing tasks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single home care shift (half-day ~4 hrs), covering cooking, tidying, comfort checks, and family instruction across multiple sub-tasks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires simultaneous cooking, social/emotional engagement, adaptive caregiving, family communication, and unstructured home navigation. Current humanoids lack reliable generalization, social reasoning, and safe patient interaction for this multi-domain task.

task: Rescue distressed persons, using rescue techniques and equipment.
human_time_rationale: One rescue instance (e.g., locating, stabilizing, and extracting one distressed person) typically takes a trained responder 30–60 minutes depending on scenario complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic unstructured environments (rubble, water, fire), complex physical extraction, victim assessment, adaptive decision-making, and safe handling of a human — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in autonomy, robustness, and dexterity.

task: Maintain equipment, making repairs or modifications when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single repair or maintenance event on a piece of equipment. Competent technician typically spends 30–60 min diagnosing and fixing a typical issue.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fault diagnosis, tool selection, fine dexterous manipulation, and adaptive problem-solving across highly varied equipment types. Far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and tool-use capabilities.

task: Develop or implement engineering solutions to clean up industrial accidents or other contaminated sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = developing/implementing a remediation plan for one contaminated site. Involves site assessment, engineering design, permit coordination, and overseeing cleanup operations — typically spanning days to weeks of professional effort; ~40 hours is a conservative median unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert environmental engineering judgment, regulatory navigation, specialized heavy equipment operation, chemical analysis interpretation, multi-stakeholder coordination, and adaptive problem-solving over weeks. Far beyond any current general-purpose humanoid robot's autonomous capability.

task: Repair plumbing or propane gas lines, using caulking compounds and plastic or copper pipe.
human_time_rationale: One repair instance (e.g., a single leaking joint or section): cutting, fitting, soldering/threading, and sealing a pipe segment typically takes a skilled plumber 45–90 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous tool use (pipe cutters, torches, wrenches), working in tight confined spaces, soldering/brazing, leak testing, and safety-critical judgment with gas lines — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Operate, test, or maintain robotic equipment used for green production applications, such as waste-to-energy conversion systems, minimization of material waste, or replacement of human operators in dangerous work environments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one operate/test/maintain session on a robotic green-production unit (e.g. waste-to-energy robot). A typical shift task cycle takes ~45 min including checks, adjustments, and logging.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized heavy/industrial machinery, running diagnostics, interpreting sensor data, and performing maintenance repairs. Current humanoids lack tool-use generalization, domain-specific interfaces, and safe operation of complex industrial systems.

task: Prepare dressings or bandages and apply or assist with their application following surgery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and applying a post-surgical dressing to one wound site; includes gathering supplies, cleaning, and securing bandage. Competent nurse/tech takes ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile technique, fine dexterity to handle delicate wound tissue, precise tension control on bandaging, and patient-specific judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, sterile field awareness, and safe force control for clinical wound care.

task: Prepare patients for examinations, tests, or treatments and explain procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single patient for one exam/test/treatment, including positioning, draping, explaining steps, and answering questions. Typically 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires natural language explanation, empathetic communication, adaptive physical assistance (draping, positioning), reading patient distress, and clinical judgment — all far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Disassemble machines to maintain, repair, or replace broken or worn parts, using hand or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One disassembly instance of a typical machine (e.g., pump, motor, gearbox). A competent mechanic takes ~30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied tool use (wrenches, screwdrivers, power tools), high torque application, recognizing worn/damaged parts, and adapting to unstructured machine configurations — well beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity limits.

task: Count finished products to determine if product orders are complete.
human_time_rationale: One instance = counting finished products for a single order batch. A competent worker scans, counts, and verifies a typical batch in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based counting of uniform products on shelves or conveyors is feasible, but varied product shapes, stacking, occlusion, and cross-checking against order records reduce reliability. No heavy machinery needed, but perception generalization is still limited.

task: Assemble electrical or electronic systems or support structures and install components, units, subassemblies, wiring, or assembly casings, using rivets, bolts, soldering or micro-welding equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling a single electrical/electronic unit or subassembly (e.g., a control panel or wiring harness). A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for soldering, micro-welding, riveting, and routing wiring in varied configurations. Current humanoids lack the precision, force control, and multi-tool generalization needed for acceptable quality across these mixed fastening methods.

task: Inspect or test wiring installations, assemblies, or circuits for resistance factors or for operation, and record results.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting/testing one wiring installation or circuit (e.g., a panel or junction box), probing connections, measuring resistance/continuity, and recording results. ~30 min for a competent electrician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise probe placement on small terminals, reading multimeter displays, navigating varied electrical configurations, and logging data. Fine dexterity, tool use, and contextual interpretation are well beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Turn valves to regulate flow of liquids or air, to reverse machines, to start pumps, or to regulate equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating and turning a valve to achieve a desired flow/state change. A competent worker typically completes this in ~3 minutes including walking to and adjusting the valve.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to valves and attempt turning, but varied valve types (handwheel, ball, gate, lever), torque requirements, tight spaces, and confirming flow outcome remain challenging for current humanoids. Success is inconsistent without task-specific tuning.

task: Break up defective products for reprocessing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = breaking down one defective product unit (e.g., disassembling/crushing it). A competent worker handles one unit in roughly 5 minutes depending on size and method.
robot_success_rationale: Requires variable force application, gripping irregular/defective parts, and safe handling. Robots can do structured pick-and-place but breaking/disassembling varied defective items is unstructured, demanding perception and adaptive force control.

task: Examine tickets or passes to verify authenticity, using criteria such as color or date issued.
human_time_rationale: One instance = verifying a single ticket/pass. A trained human glances at color, date, hologram, etc. in ~20–30 seconds.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read printed text/dates via vision, but detecting subtle authenticity cues (holograms, microprinting, color nuance, wear) in varied lighting with arbitrary ticket formats is unreliable. Generalization across ticket types is poor.

task: Reset hardware, using chisels, mallets, and screwdrivers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = resetting one piece of hardware (e.g., a hinge, lock, or fitting) using hand tools. A competent worker takes ~10–20 min depending on hardware complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled tool use (chisel, mallet, screwdriver) with varied grip/orientation, tactile feedback, and spatial reasoning in unstructured settings. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous tool-switching and fine force control for this.

task: Assist dentist in management of medical or dental emergencies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one emergency event (e.g., syncope, allergic reaction, airway obstruction) from onset to stabilization/handoff. Typical duration is 10–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rapid clinical judgment, fine dexterous intervention (airway management, injections, CPR coordination), communication with the dentist and patient, and adaptive response to unpredictable, high-stakes medical situations — far beyond current robot capabilities.

task: Install and adjust speed and gear mechanisms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing and adjusting a single speed/gear mechanism (e.g., a bicycle derailleur or gearbox unit). A competent mechanic typically takes 30–60 minutes including fitting, cable tensioning, and test adjustment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for cable routing, precise torque on fasteners, and iterative tactile/auditory feedback for adjustment. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control, force sensing, and domain-specific judgment needed for reliable gear tuning.

task: Wash work areas, machines, equipment, vehicles, or products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = washing a single work area, machine, equipment unit, vehicle, or product. A competent worker typically spends ~20–30 min per unit depending on size and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Simple surface wiping is feasible (~40–70%), but vehicles, machinery, and varied industrial equipment demand mobility over uneven terrain, tool-switching (hoses, brushes, chemicals), and strong generalization — well beyond current frontier capability.

task: Paint car exteriors, interiors, and fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fully painting one car (exterior, interior, fixtures). A competent auto body painter takes ~6–8 hours including prep, masking, spraying, and finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires masking, surface prep, operating spray equipment, managing paint viscosity/distance/angle, and navigating around complex 3D vehicle geometry. Far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capability at acceptable quality.

task: Operate, assemble, adjust, or monitor sterilizers, lights, suction machines, or diagnostic equipment to ensure proper operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up, checking, and monitoring a single piece of medical equipment (e.g., sterilizer cycle or suction machine check). A competent surgical/clinical tech takes ~10–20 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Medical equipment interfaces are highly varied and require fine dexterity, reading displays, interpreting alarms, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack the specialized tool knowledge, safety-critical decision-making, and reliable fine manipulation needed in clinical settings.

task: Install, repair, and replace equipment or accessories, such as handlebars, stands, lights, and seats.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing/repairing/replacing one accessory (e.g., handlebars or seat) on a bicycle. A skilled mechanic takes ~20–30 min including removal, fitting, adjustment, and torque check.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool-use (wrenches, hex keys), precise torque application, and adjustment on irregular, curved bicycle parts. Cable routing, alignment checks, and varied fastener types push beyond current humanoid dexterity and generalization limits.

task: Help patients with bathing, dressing, maintaining personal hygiene, moving in bed, or standing and walking.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one patient through a full personal care session (bathing, dressing, hygiene, repositioning/ambulation). Competent caregiver typically takes 20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires gentle, adaptive dexterity on a fragile human body, safe physical support during ambulation, nuanced perception of patient discomfort, and dynamic balance during transfers — all far beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Clean rooms and make beds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one room (tidying, vacuuming/sweeping, dusting) plus making the bed; a competent housekeeper completes this in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Basic sweeping/mopping is feasible (~40–70%), but making a bed requires complex fabric manipulation, tucking, and spatial reasoning over a large flexible object — still very challenging for current humanoids. Combined success is low.

task: Lubricate, adjust, or repair machinery and replace parts, such as gears, bearings, or bucket teeth.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance job (lubricate, adjust, or replace one component like a bearing or gear). Competent mechanic typically takes 30–60 min per task.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity with wrenches, grease guns, and varied heavy parts under real-world conditions. Diagnosing wear, applying correct torque, and replacing bucket teeth or bearings demands tool-use and judgment far beyond 2026 robot capabilities.

task: Prepare patient, sterilize or disinfect instruments, set up instrument trays, prepare materials, or assist dentist during dental procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing for and assisting through a single dental procedure. Typical setup, sterilization, patient prep, and chairside assistance spans ~15–25 minutes combined.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for instrument handling, sterile field management, dynamic patient interaction, real-time response to dentist cues, and navigating a constrained clinical space. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and safety standards for medical settings.

task: Improve fuel efficiency by testing vehicles or components that use lighter materials, such as aluminum, magnesium alloy, or plastic.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full test session (setup, instrumentation, test runs, data logging) for one vehicle/component; typically 1–3 hours for a competent test engineer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized test equipment, driving vehicles on test tracks, interpreting multi-sensor instrumentation, and making engineering judgments. Humanoid robots cannot operate vehicles or run complex dynamometer/test rigs autonomously.

task: Maintain inventories of equipment, storing and retrieving items and assembling and disassembling equipment as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single inventory cycle (retrieve, log, assemble/disassemble, store one piece of equipment). A competent worker typically spends ~30 min per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigation to storage, identifying varied equipment, fine dexterous assembly/disassembly, and accurate inventory tracking. Current humanoids struggle with varied tool recognition, multi-step assembly of arbitrary equipment, and record-keeping integration.

task: Measure distances, clean sightlines, and record data to help survey crews.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one survey setup point: clearing vegetation/obstacles, measuring distances, and logging readings. A competent rodman/chainman takes ~15–25 min per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor locomotion over uneven terrain, tool use (measuring tape, rod), vegetation clearing, and precise data logging. Current robots struggle with rough terrain, unstructured clearing tasks, and coordinated survey workflows without teleoperation.

task: Pack and store materials and products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = packing and storing a typical batch of materials/products (e.g., a cart or pallet's worth). A competent worker takes ~15 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle structured pick-and-place well, but packing varied items into boxes (sizing, orientation, fragility) and navigating to storage locations across unstructured environments strains current dexterity, generalization, and mobile manipulation reliability.

task: Collect data on physical characteristics of specified areas, such as geological formations, climates, and vegetation, using surveying or meteorological equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a field survey session at a designated site. A competent geographer/surveyor typically spends ~2 hours collecting multi-parameter physical data (geological, climatic, vegetation) at one location.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor terrain navigation, deploying varied specialized instruments (GPS, anemometers, soil probes, vegetation sampling), adaptive judgment, and data logging across unstructured environments. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor mobility, tool-switching, and domain-specific sensor operation for autonomous field science.

task: Cut lengths of tubing to specified sizes, using files or cutting wheels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting one tube to a specified length. A competent worker measures, marks, clamps, and cuts a single piece of tubing in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires measuring/marking to precise spec, securely clamping a round tube, and applying controlled force with a rotary cutter or file — demanding dexterity, force control, and tool manipulation beyond typical 2026 robot capability.

task: Adjust, repair, or replace electrical or electronic components to correct defects and to ensure conformance to specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing and fixing a single defective electrical/electronic component (e.g., replacing a faulty relay, resoldering a PCB joint). Median across simple swaps and moderate repairs.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation (soldering, connector seating), diagnostic reasoning, tool use with precision, and adaptation to varied components. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured real-world settings.

task: Lift tote boxes of finished cartons, and dump cartons into feed hoppers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lifting one tote box and dumping its cartons into a feed hopper. A competent worker picks up the tote, carries it to the hopper, tilts/dumps, and sets the tote aside — roughly 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can lift totes and navigate to hoppers, but reliably gripping varied tote sizes, managing payload weight, precisely orienting to dump into a hopper opening, and avoiding spillage remains challenging for current humanoids without task-specific fixtures.

task: Secure printing plates to printing units and adjust tolerances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting and aligning plates on one printing unit. A skilled press operator typically takes 15–25 minutes per unit, including registration checks and tolerance adjustments.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise sub-millimeter registration, high torque fastening, tactile feedback for plate tension, and interpretation of press-specific mechanical tolerances — far beyond current general-purpose humanoid dexterity and perception capabilities.

task: Measure and verify levels of rock or gravel, bases, or other excavated material.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and verifying material levels at a single site/section. A competent worker uses a grade rod, laser level, or tape measure; ~15 min including checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor rough-terrain navigation, precise depth/grade measurement with specialized tools (grade rod, laser level), and interpretation of uneven, granular surfaces. Current humanoids lack terrain capability, tool-use proficiency, and measurement accuracy for this task.

task: Adjust dig face angles for varying overburden depths and set lengths.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting dig face angle for one overburden depth/set-length combo; experienced operator calculates and applies adjustment in ~8 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized mining/excavation machinery with domain-specific controls, reading real-time geological conditions, and making engineering judgments. Humanoid robots cannot operate such equipment autonomously in field conditions.

task: Wash and dress bodies of deceased persons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = washing and dressing one deceased person. A skilled mortuary technician typically takes 30–60 minutes depending on body condition and garment complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme whole-body dexterity on a limp, irregular, fragile human form — lifting, repositioning, bathing, and dressing with varied clothing. Far beyond 2026 robot dexterity/manipulation limits. Dignity/quality standards are unforgiving.

task: Provide students with disabilities with assistive devices, supportive technology, and assistance accessing facilities, such as restrooms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one student with one need (e.g., setting up a device, escorting to restroom). Typical interaction takes ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social communication, individualized assessment of disability needs, fine dexterous setup of varied assistive devices, and safe physical caregiving in unpredictable environments. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and caregiving capabilities.

task: Hand instruments and supplies to surgeons and surgeons' assistants, hold retractors and cut sutures, and perform other tasks as directed by surgeon during operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one surgical procedure. Typical general surgery case runs ~60–120 min of active OR time; 90 min is a reasonable median for a scrub tech's involvement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sterile-field awareness, dynamic anticipation of surgeon needs, handling of delicate sharp instruments, and real-time verbal/nonverbal communication—far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities. Safety constraints alone preclude deployment.

task: Conduct laboratory or field experiments with plants, animals, insects, diseases, and soils.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single experiment session (setup, execution, observation, and data recording). A typical lab/field experiment run takes 1–3 hours for a competent researcher; 120 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert scientific judgment, adaptive experimental design, live organism handling (insects, animals), fine manipulation of lab equipment, and outdoor/unstructured field environments. Far beyond current general-purpose robot autonomy.

task: Perform manual labor to prepare or finish sites, such as shoveling materials by hand.
human_time_rationale: One instance = shoveling/moving a typical load of materials (e.g., gravel, dirt, or debris) at a single site prep or finish task; a competent laborer completes this in ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor unstructured terrain, varied loose materials (dirt, gravel, debris), and sustained forceful shoveling demand strong locomotion stability, high-force tool use, and generalization — all weak spots for current humanoids. Limited commercial deployment outdoors.

task: Prepare or present agricultural demonstrations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one demonstration event (e.g., showing a planting, irrigation, or pest-control technique to an audience). Includes setup, live demo, and brief Q&A; typical extension-agent session ~60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires communication, teaching, audience engagement, adapting explanations, and operating varied agricultural equipment outdoors. Current humanoids lack speech fluency for instruction, contextual judgment, and equipment-operation capability needed here.

task: Tie products in bundles for further processing or shipment, following prescribed procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tying one bundle of products (e.g. pipes, lumber, boxes). A competent worker ties, secures, and verifies one bundle in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping and tensioning straps/twine around irregular loads, tying knots or fastening clips — fine dexterity and force control under variability. Current humanoids struggle with rope/strap manipulation and consistent knot-tying at acceptable quality.

task: Explain and demonstrate artistic techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = demonstrating a single artistic technique (e.g., watercolor wash, charcoal blending) to a student or audience, including setup and execution.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (brushes, pencils), nuanced pressure control, and expressive communication. Current robots lack the fine motor finesse and real-time adaptive teaching ability needed for acceptable quality.

task: Perform site activities required of green certified construction practices, such as implementing waste management procedures, identifying materials for reuse, or installing erosion or sedimentation control mechanisms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a full green site activity session (e.g., setting up sedimentation controls, sorting waste, or tagging reusable materials on a typical workday shift segment). ~90 min is a reasonable unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor unstructured terrain navigation, complex multi-step judgment (identifying reusable materials, reading site conditions), installing staked erosion barriers, and regulatory compliance reasoning — far beyond current humanoid generalization and dexterity outdoors.

task: Collect samples, such as blood, urine, or sputum from patients, and perform routine laboratory tests on samples.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a sample (e.g., blood draw) from one patient and running a routine lab test. Includes prep, collection, labeling, and basic processing (~20 min total).
robot_success_rationale: Venipuncture requires sub-millimeter precision on a moving, compliant, variable target. Robots also cannot safely operate centrifuges/analyzers, manage patient interaction, or ensure sterile technique autonomously at acceptable clinical quality in 2026.

task: Check antenna positioning to ensure specified azimuths or mechanical tilts and adjust as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking and adjusting a single antenna. Technician reads azimuth/tilt, compares to specs, loosens mounts, adjusts, re-torques — typically 20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing towers or rooftops, using inclinometers/compass tools, reading analog indicators, and precise mechanical adjustment of bolted mounts outdoors. Mobility, height access, tool use, and outdoor unstructured terrain far exceed 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Survey, measure, and map access roads and forest areas such as burns, cut-over areas, experimental plots, and timber sales sections.
human_time_rationale: One instance = surveying, measuring, and mapping a single forest area or section (e.g., a burn or timber sale block). A competent forestry technician typically spends 3–5 hours in the field plus basic data recording per site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires traversing rough, unstructured outdoor terrain, operating specialized survey instruments (total stations, GPS units, clinometers), making skilled measurements, and producing mapped outputs. Current humanoid robots lack outdoor terrain mobility, tool-use generalization, and domain-specific survey cognition for this task.

task: Test or balance newly installed HVAC systems to determine whether indoor air quality standards are met.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing/balancing one newly installed HVAC system in a typical commercial or residential building; includes airflow measurements, sensor readings, and adjustments, typically 1–2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized test instruments (manometers, anemometers, air quality meters), accessing ductwork/diffusers in varied locations, interpreting results, and making mechanical adjustments. Current humanoids lack tool integration, domain knowledge execution, and reliable fine-tuned equipment operation for HVAC diagnostics.

task: Assemble components of energy-efficient optical communications systems involving photonic switches, optical backplanes, or optoelectronic interfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one optical communications subsystem (e.g., a photonic switch module or optoelectronic interface card). Skilled technician takes ~2–4 hours including alignment, fiber routing, and verification.
