human_time_rationale: One instance = a single cycle of cleaning, sterilizing, and disposing of contaminated supplies for a typical procedural tray; includes rinsing, autoclave loading, and waste disposal (~20–30 min).
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise handling of sharp/fragile instruments, operating sterilization equipment (autoclave controls), biohazard waste segregation, and strict protocol compliance. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, domain knowledge integration, and safety-critical reliability needed.

task: Cut metal division strips, and press them into terrazzo base so that top edges form desired design or pattern.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting and pressing strips for one design section/panel (~10–20 sq ft). A skilled terrazzo worker takes ~30–60 min per section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise metal cutting, interpreting a design pattern, and pressing thin strips into semi-set terrazzo base with exact alignment and consistent depth — far beyond current robot dexterity, force control, and spatial planning capabilities.

task: Install anchor bolts, steel plates, door sills and other fixtures in freshly poured concrete or pattern or stamp the surface to provide a decorative finish.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing fixtures (anchor bolts, plates, sills) or stamping/patterning one typical concrete pour section (~100–200 sq ft). A skilled worker takes ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise placement into wet, unstructured concrete with tight tolerances, force-sensitive dexterous tool use, and decorative stamping — far beyond current humanoid manipulation in dynamic, outdoor, unstructured environments.

task: Collect animal or crop samples.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a sample set from one animal or one crop plot. Includes locating the subject, preparing collection tools, and securing/labeling the sample.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/farm locomotion on uneven terrain, precise fine-motor sampling (swabs, blood draws, plant clippings), and adapting to highly variable biological subjects. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor mobility and bio-sampling dexterity.

task: Perform fire protection or suppression duties, such as constructing fire breaks or disposing of brush.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a half-day shift of fire suppression/break construction work (clearing brush, digging lines). A crew typically measures output per shift/day; 4 hours is a reasonable mid-estimate.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor rough-terrain locomotion, wielding heavy tools (Pulaskis, chainsaws, shovels), operating in smoke/heat/uneven ground, and dynamic hazard judgment. Current humanoids lack the power, durability, and situational awareness for wildland firefighting.

task: Drive truck-mounted units to well sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one trip from base/yard to a well site. Typical oilfield drive times range 30–180 min; 90 min is a reasonable median including pre-trip inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate a truck in traffic. L4 AVs are limited to mapped urban routes, not remote oilfield roads. No current general-purpose robot can drive a truck-mounted unit to a well site autonomously.

task: Operate machinery and heavy equipment, such as forklifts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one typical forklift operation shift task (e.g., moving a pallet load from storage to staging area), estimated ~45 min including setup, travel, and placement.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate forklifts or heavy equipment — they lack the physical interface, seated controls, and vehicle-operation capability. No current general-purpose robot can drive or operate such machinery autonomously in real environments.

task: Provide assistance to forest survey crews by clearing site-lines, holding measuring tools, or setting stakes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one survey setup task (clearing a sight-line, holding a measuring rod, or setting a stake) in the field; a skilled field hand takes ~30 min per setup.
robot_success_rationale: Uneven forested terrain severely limits bipedal mobility (~20–40% baseline). Swinging tools to clear brush, driving stakes with a mallet, and navigating dense undergrowth require robust outdoor locomotion and forceful dexterous tool use far beyond current frontier robot capabilities.

task: Load vehicles with listed goods, ensuring goods are loaded correctly and taking precautions with hazardous goods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading one vehicle with a listed manifest of goods, including hazmat precautions. A competent worker typically takes 30–60 minutes depending on load size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading manifests, identifying hazardous goods, navigating a loading dock, handling varied package sizes/weights, and applying compliance rules. Current humanoids lack reliable hazmat identification, load planning, and heavy/bulky item handling at acceptable quality.

task: Adjust window shades or seat cushions at the request of passengers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = responding to a single passenger request to adjust one shade or cushion. A competent flight/train attendant completes this in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires social perception (understanding verbal/gestural requests), navigating tight cabin aisles, and dexterous manipulation of varied shade mechanisms or cushion placements. Current humanoids struggle with confined spaces, soft object handling, and passenger interaction reliability.

task: Make regular rounds to ensure that residents and areas are safe and secure.
human_time_rationale: One round through a typical residential facility (e.g., care home, apartment complex, or dormitory), checking rooms and common areas; typically 15–30 minutes per circuit.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate corridors and detect obvious hazards, but assessing resident well-being, interpreting ambiguous safety cues, opening varied doors, and responding to incidents requires human judgment and social interaction far beyond current capability.

task: Load debris and refuse onto trucks and haul it away for disposal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one truckload cycle (loading + haul to disposal site). A competent worker with equipment takes ~60 min including transit.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids cannot drive trucks on public roads. Loading varied, irregular debris requires robust dexterity and strength. Both the vehicle operation and unstructured heavy manipulation components are beyond current frontier robots.

task: Provide assistance with patrons' special needs, such as helping those with wheelchairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one patron with a special need (e.g., helping a wheelchair user navigate a space, reach an item, or get seated). Typical interaction lasts ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires social perception, adaptive physical interaction with a person and wheelchair, nuanced communication, and safety-critical caregiving. Current humanoids lack reliable human-contact manipulation, empathy, and real-time adaptive caregiving in unstructured public spaces.

task: Monitor forest-cleared lands to ensure that they are reclaimed to their most suitable end use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single cleared land parcel for reclamation progress. A competent forester/land monitor walks the site, assesses vegetation, soil, drainage, and documents findings — roughly 2 hours per parcel visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor rough-terrain navigation, multi-factor ecological assessment (soil, plant species ID, erosion, hydrology), regulatory interpretation, and long-range surveying. Current humanoids have poor outdoor/uneven terrain mobility and lack ecological judgment.

task: Cut, trim and shape hair or hairpieces, based on customers' instructions, hair type, and facial features, using clippers, scissors, trimmers and razors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full haircut for a single customer. A competent barber/stylist typically spends 20–45 minutes cutting, trimming, and shaping, depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity near a human face/head, dynamic interpretation of customer instructions, safe blade/scissor use on moving person, and real-time aesthetic judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Optimize photonic process parameters by making prototype or production devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating and testing one prototype/production photonic device run (mask alignment, deposition, etching, measurement). Typical lab session is ~2–4 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-micron precision lithography, cleanroom tool operation (spinners, etchers, CVD systems), optical metrology, and iterative parameter judgment — far beyond any current general-purpose humanoid's dexterity, perception, or domain knowledge.

task: Apply tar or other protective substances to cut surfaces or seal surfaces and to protect them from fungi and insects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying protective substance to a single cut surface (e.g., a pruned tree limb or lumber cut). A competent worker brushes or sprays tar/sealant onto one surface in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires mobile navigation to the cut surface, grasping a brush/applicator, and applying even coating with moderate force control. Perception of surface boundaries is feasible but uneven terrain, varied surface geometry, and precise coating thickness challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Polish surface, using polishing or surfacing machine.
human_time_rationale: One instance = polishing one workpiece/surface area. A competent worker sets up, operates the machine, and inspects the result, typically 15–25 minutes per piece.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling/positioning a powered machine or workpiece with consistent pressure and path control. Current humanoids lack the fine force feedback, tool-use generalization, and quality-inspection perception needed for acceptable surface finish outcomes.

task: Verify that items are addressed correctly, marked with the proper postage, and in suitable condition for processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single mail item for correct address, postage, and physical condition. A trained postal worker checks each piece in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based OCR and postage detection are feasible, but verifying address validity, assessing physical condition (tears, moisture, sealing), and handling varied shapes/sizes challenge current robot dexterity and generalization.

task: Perform laboratory or field testing, using spectrometers, nitrogen determination apparatus, air samplers, centrifuges, or potential hydrogen (pH) meters to perform tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one complete test run using a given instrument (setup, sample prep, operation, recording). A competent lab tech typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of fragile glassware, precise pipetting, calibrating sensitive instruments, and interpreting readings. Current humanoids lack the precision, lab-specific tool-use, and procedural generalization needed for autonomous lab testing.

task: Examine animals or crop specimens to determine the presence of diseases or other problems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single animal or crop specimen. A competent agronomist/vet technician takes ~10–20 min to visually inspect, assess symptoms, and note findings.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert-level biological perception, disease pattern recognition across highly variable specimens, tactile assessment, and nuanced judgment. Current robots lack robust biological diagnostic vision, fine tactile sensing, and domain generalization for live or varied specimens.

task: Seal or open envelopes, by hand or by using machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sealing or opening one envelope. A competent worker can do this in ~15 seconds by hand or faster with a machine.
robot_success_rationale: Handling thin, flexible paper envelopes requires fine dexterity and precise force control. Robots struggle with limp, deformable objects; licking/moistening a seal and tucking flaps cleanly is error-prone. Machine-assisted sealing is more feasible but still requires setup.

task: Fabricate concrete beams, columns, and panels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating one precast concrete element (beam, column, or panel): setting forms, placing rebar, pouring/vibrating concrete, and stripping. Roughly 2–4 hours per element for a skilled crew.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy formwork, pouring and vibrating wet concrete, rebar placement, and coordinating with cranes/mixers. Extreme weight, specialized tools, unstructured environments, and multi-step precision far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Water, root-feed, and fertilize trees.
human_time_rationale: One instance = watering, root-feeding, and fertilizing a single tree. A competent worker sets up hose/equipment, deep-waters, injects root feed, and applies fertilizer in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor navigation over uneven terrain, connecting hoses, operating pressurized root-injection equipment, and precisely distributing fertilizer around a tree — all highly unstructured tasks well beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Demonstrate use or operation of merchandise.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single product demonstration to a customer (e.g., showing how a blender, tool, or gadget works). Typical retail demo runs 5–15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fluent verbal explanation, adaptive social interaction, handling diverse/unfamiliar merchandise, and persuasive showmanship. Current robots lack reliable generalized object manipulation, natural language customer engagement, and contextual improvisation needed for acceptable demos.

task: Clean shelves, counters, and tables.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wiping down a typical set of shelves, counters, and tables in one room; a competent worker with cloth/spray takes ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can wipe flat surfaces reasonably well but struggle with cluttered shelves, varied heights, object avoidance, and applying consistent pressure on uneven or cluttered surfaces. Mobility and reach across multiple surface types reduce reliability.

task: Collect and analyze pollution samples, such as air or ground water.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting and analyzing one set of pollution samples (e.g., one site visit with air or groundwater sampling). Includes travel to site, setup, sample collection, and basic field analysis — typically 1–2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires specialized equipment (probes, pumps, sample vials), outdoor/unstructured terrain navigation, precise fluid handling, and chemical analysis interpretation. Current humanoids lack the tools, domain-specific sensors, and generalization for autonomous fieldwork.

task: Assist in responding to aircraft and medical emergencies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = responding to a single in-flight emergency (medical or aircraft), from onset to stabilization/handoff. Typical duration is 15–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic clinical judgment, communication with crew/ATC, adaptive physical caregiving, and situational awareness in a cramped, turbulent aircraft. Far beyond current robot generalization, dexterity, and decision-making capabilities.

task: Deliver merchandise and collect payment.
human_time_rationale: One delivery instance: travel to customer location, hand over merchandise, collect payment (cash/card), and return. Assumes a typical local/last-mile delivery stop.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating public roads or complex outdoor terrain, interacting with strangers, handling varied payment methods (cash/card/POS), and adapting to unpredictable social/environmental contexts — all far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities.

task: Process incoming or outgoing mail, packages, or deliveries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a typical batch of incoming/outgoing mail or packages at a workstation (sorting, labeling, logging, routing). ~15 min per batch for an average office or mailroom.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and sort standard packages but struggle with irregular envelopes, labels at odd angles, reading/scanning varied barcodes, interacting with mailroom equipment, and navigating to correct drop points. Generalization across package types is limited.

task: Wet concrete surface, and rub with stone to smooth surface and obtain specified finish.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing one standard concrete slab section (~10–20 sq ft). A skilled cement mason wets and rubs the surface with a rubbing stone, typically taking 30–60 minutes per section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained, high-force, precisely controlled rubbing strokes with a heavy stone over an uneven surface, while managing water application and judging surface finish quality by feel and sight — far beyond current robot dexterity, force control, and tactile sensing capabilities.

task: Provide boarding assistance to elderly, sick, or injured people.
human_time_rationale: One instance = helping one person board a vehicle (bus, plane, train). Includes positioning, steadying, guiding, or lifting. Typical interaction lasts 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic physical support of a fragile, unpredictable human body, social communication, adapting grip and force in real time, and navigating crowded boarding environments. Far beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Analyze samples, such as air or water samples, for contaminants or other elements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting and analyzing a single sample (e.g., one water or air sample) including collection, preparation, and running it through lab instruments. ~30–60 min is typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting, operating complex lab instruments (spectrometers, chromatographs), interpreting results, and handling hazardous materials. Current humanoids lack fine dexterity, lab tool interfacing, and chemical analysis cognition needed end-to-end.

task: Assemble and maintain portfolios, print composite cards, and travel to go-sees to obtain jobs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling/updating one portfolio plus printing composite cards. A competent model/agent spends ~1–1.5 hrs selecting images, arranging pages, and printing cards.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative aesthetic judgment, digital content curation, precise inkjet/laser printer operation, and delicate page arrangement. Far beyond current humanoid generalization; printing and fine paper handling alone are very low probability.

task: Use masks, gloves, and safety glasses to protect patients and self from infectious diseases.
human_time_rationale: One instance = donning full PPE set (mask, gloves, safety glasses) before a patient interaction. A trained worker completes this in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Donning gloves, fitting a mask snugly, and placing safety glasses requires fine dexterity, self-referential body awareness, and compliance checks (seal, fit). Current humanoids lack the fine manipulation and self-dressing capability needed reliably.

task: Prepare materials and classrooms for class activities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one classroom for one session: arranging desks, distributing handouts/supplies, setting up AV or activity materials. Typical prep takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diverse sub-tasks: furniture rearrangement, handling varied paper/supplies, AV setup, and interpreting lesson-specific instructions. Poor generalization, limited manipulation of thin/flat items, and unstructured environment hamper success.

task: Examine unserviceable wells to determine actions to be taken to improve well conditions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one unserviceable well. A competent technician spends ~30–60 min examining the well, assessing components, and documenting findings/recommendations.
robot_success_rationale: Well inspection requires outdoor/unstructured terrain navigation, lowering sensors into confined shafts, interpreting water/mechanical conditions, and generating actionable remediation plans — far beyond current humanoid robot generalization and tool-use capabilities.

task: Select fishing methods or tools for removing obstacles such as liners, broken casing, screens, or drill pipe.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting appropriate fishing tools/methods for a specific downhole obstacle. A competent driller/engineer reviews wellbore data and specifies tools in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Highly specialized oilfield engineering decision requiring deep domain knowledge, wellbore diagnostics, and tool catalogue expertise. No current humanoid robot has the cognitive or operational capability for this unstructured technical judgment task.

task: Repair or adjust seats, doors, or windows.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/adjusting one component (seat, door, or window). A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min diagnosing and fixing a single item.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing faults, using varied hand tools with high precision, working in tight vehicle spaces, and adapting to many component types. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-use generalization, and fault-diagnosis needed.

task: Bag or package purchases and wrap gifts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = bagging/packaging one purchase or wrapping one gift. A competent worker takes ~2–5 min depending on item size and wrap complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for folding paper, taping precisely, tying ribbons, and handling varied item shapes/sizes. Current humanoids struggle with compliant materials, tight folds, and aesthetic quality standards supervisors expect.

task: Install assemblies, such as flashing, pipes, tubes, heating and air conditioning ducts, furnace casings, rain gutters, or downspouts in supportive frameworks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single assembly unit (e.g., a gutter run, duct section, or pipe segment). A competent sheet metal worker/HVAC tech typically takes 45–75 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/unstructured environments, climbing ladders, overhead work, heavy/awkward components, precise fastening, and site-specific adaptation. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, mobility, and tool-use limits.

task: Keep areas neat while working and return items to correct locations following demonstrations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tidying a small work area and returning displaced items to correct locations after a task; a competent worker takes ~3 minutes per tidy-up cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Robots struggle to generalize "correct location" from demonstrations, handle diverse cluttered objects, and maintain context across tasks. Pick-and-place is feasible but semantic placement memory and generalization remain weak in unstructured environments.

task: Suggest and apply the use of supports such as straps, tapes, bandages, or braces if necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assessing a patient and applying one support device (strap, tape, bandage, or brace) to one body area; a competent athletic trainer or clinician typically takes ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical assessment/judgment, nuanced communication, and highly dexterous fine-motor work (wrapping, tensioning, fastening) on a moving human body. Far beyond 2026 frontier robot capability in unstructured caregiving.

task: Push roller over surface to embed chips in surface.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rolling a hand roller over one surface section (~10–20 sq ft) to embed aggregate chips; a skilled worker completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained two-handed force application with a heavy roller over a defined surface. Humanoids lack the grip strength, force control, and stable pushing locomotion needed to maintain even pressure and coverage across a real work surface.

task: Set up camps, and prepare meals for tour group members.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a full camp for a tour group (~5–15 people): pitching tents, laying out bedding, organizing gear. A competent guide takes ~90 min.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor uneven terrain, tent assembly (flexible fabric, stakes, poles), and coordinated multi-step setup far exceed current robot capabilities in generalization, dexterity, and unstructured outdoor locomotion.

task: Determine manner in which mail is to be sent, and prepare it for delivery to mailing facilities.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a batch of outgoing mail (sorting, selecting postage method, sealing, labeling). A typical office batch takes ~10 minutes for a competent mail clerk.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/interpreting mail content to decide send method, applying labels/stamps with precision, handling varied envelope sizes and weights. Cognitive decision-making plus fine dexterity on flimsy paper materials are significant barriers for current humanoids.

task: Select, order, store, issue, and inventory classroom equipment, materials, and supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full cycle of selecting, ordering, storing, issuing, and inventorying supplies for a classroom period or restocking event; ~45 min median for a school aide/clerk.
robot_success_rationale: Task requires cognitive planning (selecting/ordering supplies), internet/system interaction, judgment about quantities, and multi-step logistics. Physical handling is only a small part; autonomous procurement and inventory decisions far exceed current robot capabilities.

task: Apply makeup to face and style hair to enhance appearance, considering such factors as color, camera techniques, and facial features.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full makeup application and hair styling for one person. A professional makeup artist/stylist takes ~30–60 min for a complete look.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity near sensitive facial features, nuanced aesthetic judgment, color/camera adaptation, and safe force control on a human. Far beyond current humanoid robots' manipulation and perception capabilities.

task: Maintain and perform safety inspections on equipment and tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a safety inspection of a single piece of equipment or tool set. A competent worker checks condition, wear, calibration, and compliance in ~30 min on average.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can visually scan surfaces and detect obvious damage, but nuanced wear assessment, functional testing, calibration checks, and interpreting safety standards require domain judgment, fine dexterity, and tool-use generalization well beyond 2026 frontier capability.

task: Perform a series of manual adjustments to the spine or other articulations of the body to correct the musculoskeletal system.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single chiropractic/osteopathic adjustment session per patient, including assessment and manual manipulation; typically 20–40 minutes for a competent practitioner.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise, calibrated force on specific anatomical landmarks of a compliant human body, nuanced tactile feedback, real-time clinical judgment, and safety-critical dexterity. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Process e-waste, such as computer components containing lead or mercury.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single batch/unit of e-waste (e.g., one computer). A competent technician spends ~30 min sorting, disassembling, and segregating hazardous components like lead solder boards or mercury-containing displays.
robot_success_rationale: E-waste disassembly requires identifying highly varied, irregular components with hazardous materials, using tools (screwdrivers, cutters), fine dexterity, and safety-compliant handling. Current humanoids lack the perception, dexterous tool use, and generalization needed for unstructured e-waste streams.

task: Dig drainage ditches around wells and storage tanks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = digging drainage ditches around one well or storage tank. A competent worker using a shovel/trencher typically takes ~2 hours for a standard perimeter trench.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a trencher or heavy digging equipment outdoors on uneven ground. Humanoid robots cannot operate such machinery autonomously; manual digging with a shovel is physically demanding, unstructured, and far beyond current robot capability at acceptable quality.

task: Administer first aid to injured group participants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating one injured participant. A competent first-aider typically assesses and treats a single injury (wound, sprain, shock, etc.) in ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: First aid requires rapid adaptive clinical judgment, fine dexterous manipulation (bandaging, wound pressure, CPR), safe contact with humans, and situational triage — all well beyond current humanoid capability. Safety constraints alone make autonomous deployment infeasible.

task: Send film to photofinishing laboratories for processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = packaging and sending one batch of film rolls to a lab, including labeling, filling out order forms, and dropping off/mailing. Typical turnaround prep takes ~20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a computer or phone to place orders, handling delicate film canisters, packaging, labeling, and navigating to a drop-off or post office — complex multi-step tool use and mobility beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Operate paging systems or other systems of bells or buzzers to notify recipients of incoming calls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = receiving a call notification and activating the paging/bell/buzzer system to alert the recipient. A competent operator does this in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interfacing with specialized console panels, buttons, or software UIs; identifying the correct recipient and zone; and possibly speaking into a microphone. Current humanoids lack reliable UI interaction, voice output, and contextual judgment for switchboard-style tasks.

task: Handle slides, mud, or pit cleanings or maintenance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/maintaining one slide, mud area, or pit. Includes inspection, debris removal, hosing/scrubbing, and minor repairs; ~45 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Unstructured outdoor/semi-outdoor environments with mud, standing water, slippery surfaces, and varied debris severely challenge locomotion, perception, and tool-use. Requires hose/brush manipulation, rough terrain navigation, and judgment calls — well beyond current general-purpose robot capability.

task: Transport, assemble, and disassemble materials used in presentations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and/or breaking down a typical presentation (e.g., podium, screen, projector, chairs, tables, signage) for a single event. ~15 min setup + ~15 min teardown.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied venues, handling diverse furniture/AV equipment, connecting cables, and making context-dependent layout decisions. Current humanoids lack reliable generalization for multi-step assembly of heterogeneous, large/heavy objects in unstructured environments.

task: Keep information facilities clean during operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a single information facility (server room, data center, or office area) during an operational shift; includes wiping surfaces, sweeping/mopping floors, and tidying.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep/mop flat floors and wipe surfaces at moderate success rates, but active information facilities add obstacles (cables, live equipment, personnel), restricted access zones, and fragile hardware requiring careful avoidance — reducing generalization and safety margins significantly.

task: Move pipes to and from trucks, using truck winches and motorized lifts, or by hand.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving a load/batch of pipes to or from a truck. A competent worker with winch/lift assistance typically takes ~30 min per load, depending on pipe count and site conditions.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating truck winches and motorized lifts (heavy equipment humanoids cannot drive/operate), handling heavy/long irregular pipes outdoors on rough terrain, and coordinating rigging — far beyond current robot capability.

task: Help customers try on or fit merchandise.
human_time_rationale: One instance = helping a single customer try on one item or set of items; includes fetching sizes, adjusting fit, and providing feedback. Typically 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social interaction, understanding customer preferences, fetching varied garments, assisting dressing on a human body, and giving subjective fit feedback — far beyond current robot generalization and dexterity.

task: Unscrew or tighten pipes, casing, tubing, and pump rods, using hand and power wrenches and tongs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = making up or breaking out a single pipe/tubing joint on an oil/gas well. A competent roughneck typically completes one connection in 10–20 minutes including positioning tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme torque with heavy specialized tongs/power wrenches, precise alignment of large pipe joints, hazardous outdoor/rig environments, and tool-switching. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, force output, and generalization.

task: Clean chipped area, using wire brush, and feel and observe surface to determine if it is rough or uneven.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and inspecting one chipped area. A competent worker brushes debris away and assesses surface texture in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Wire-brush scrubbing requires moderate force control and tool manipulation; tactile sensing for "rough/uneven" surface feel is severely limited in current humanoids. Visual inspection partially compensates but haptic assessment is near-absent.

task: Position products, components, or parts for testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning a single product/component/part in a test fixture or station. A competent worker typically takes 1–5 minutes depending on alignment precision required.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place in structured environments is ~70–90%, but precise fixture alignment, varied part geometries, and connector/jig mating reduce success. Good perception and gripper dexterity help, but fine positional tolerances remain a challenge for current humanoids.

task: Bolt together pump and engine parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = bolting together a pump-engine assembly. A competent mechanic aligns parts, selects fasteners, and torques bolts in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of heavy, irregularly shaped parts, correct fastener selection, torque-spec compliance, and tool use in varied orientations. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterity and force control for multi-bolt mechanical assembly with precision torque requirements.

task: Prepare laboratory samples for analysis, following proper protocols to ensure that they will be stored, prepared, and disposed of efficiently and effectively.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a batch of lab samples (e.g., 6–12 samples): labeling, aliquoting, centrifuging, storing, and disposing of waste. A competent lab tech typically takes ~45 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (pipetting, capping vials, centrifuge loading), protocol adherence, hazardous material handling, and contextual judgment. Current humanoids lack the precision, sterile technique, and lab tool integration needed.

task: Inspect mail machine output for defects and determine how to eliminate causes of any defects.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a batch of mail machine output, identifying defects, and diagnosing root causes. A competent operator typically spends ~20 min per inspection cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is feasible, but diagnosing root causes requires contextual reasoning, machine adjustment knowledge, and multi-step decision-making well beyond current general-purpose humanoid capabilities.

task: Prepare merchandise for purchase or rental.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single item (tagging, bagging, labeling, or staging it). A competent retail worker takes ~5 minutes per item on average.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied dexterity: attaching tags, folding, bagging, scanning barcodes across diverse merchandise shapes. Perception and manipulation of irregular, deformable items remain challenging for 2026 humanoids. Low generalization across merchandise types.

task: Serve warrants and make arrests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating a subject, serving a warrant, and completing an arrest including paperwork. A typical arrest takes 30–60 minutes for an experienced officer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires legal authority, dynamic social judgment, de-escalation, restraint of resisting subjects, situational improvisation, and firearm handling in unpredictable environments. Far beyond any current general-purpose robot's capability.

task: Pose as directed, or strike suitable interpretive poses for promoting and selling merchandise or fashions during appearances, filming, or photo sessions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single photo/filming session involving posing for merchandise or fashion promotion; competent models typically spend ~30–60 min per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expressive, aesthetically nuanced full-body poses, facial expression, and stylistic interpretation. Current humanoids lack grace, emotional expression, and fashion-context generalization needed to satisfy a human creative director.

task: Weigh packages or letters to determine postage needed, using weighing scales and rate charts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = weighing a single package/letter, reading the scale, and looking up the rate chart to determine postage. A competent worker does this in ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can pick up and place items on a scale and read digital displays via vision, but interpreting rate charts, handling varied package shapes/sizes, and confirming correct postage requires reliable perception, manipulation, and document reading — still error-prone for frontier humanoids.

task: Participate in search-and-rescue operations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one search-and-rescue operation (locating and extracting one victim). Competent SAR teams typically spend 1–4 hours per successful find/extraction in typical scenarios.
robot_success_rationale: SAR requires navigation over highly unstructured/rubble terrain, victim detection under occlusion, physical extraction, and real-time decision-making. Current humanoids lack robust rough-terrain mobility, victim-handling dexterity, and autonomous mission planning needed for end-to-end SAR.

task: Conduct training programs to develop or maintain desired animal behaviors for competition, entertainment, obedience, security, riding, or related purposes.
human_time_rationale: One training session with an animal (e.g., dog, horse) typically lasts 30–90 minutes; 60 min is a reasonable median for a structured session a trainer/manager would track.
robot_success_rationale: Animal training requires reading subtle behavioral cues, applying nuanced reinforcement timing, and dynamically adapting to an unpredictable living animal. Current robots lack animal-behavior perception, social/emotional intelligence, and the fine-grained real-time judgment this demands.

task: Drive vans, industrial trucks, or other vehicles required to travel to, or to perform, cleaning work.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one round-trip drive to/from a cleaning site in a van or industrial truck; typical urban/suburban transit averages ~45 minutes total drive time.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate vans or industrial trucks in real traffic. L4 AVs are narrow, mapped-route systems. No general-purpose robot can legally or practically drive these vehicles autonomously in 2026.

task: Start machines that automatically feed plates, stencils, or tapes through mechanisms, and observe machine operations to detect any malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting a machine feed run and observing it through a cycle. Typical setup + observation per run is ~10 minutes for a competent operator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires pressing machine controls (moderate dexterity), loading media (plates/stencils/tapes), and visually monitoring for subtle malfunctions. Current robots lack reliable fine-grained anomaly detection and specialized machine interfacing needed here.

task: Unpack, sort, count, and label incoming merchandise, including items requiring special handling or refrigeration.
human_time_rationale: One shipment/delivery batch: unpacking boxes, sorting items by type, counting units, and affixing labels. Typical retail/warehouse receiving task taking 30–60 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step reasoning across varied item types, reading/applying labels, identifying refrigeration needs, opening packaging, and counting accurately. Current humanoids lack reliable generalization for this full unstructured workflow end-to-end.

task: Mold expansion joints and edges, using edging tools, jointers, and straightedge.
human_time_rationale: One instance = edging and jointing a single concrete slab section (e.g., a sidewalk panel). A skilled finisher takes ~5–10 min per panel using hand tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tactile pressure control on wet concrete, tool-edge alignment along a straightedge, and reading surface consistency. Current robots lack the fine force feedback and fluid-surface adaptation needed for acceptable quality.

task: Set up laboratory or field equipment as required for site testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a suite of lab/field equipment at one test site; a competent technician takes ~30–60 min to unpack, position, connect, and verify instruments.
robot_success_rationale: Requires highly varied, context-specific dexterity (cable connections, calibration, fragile instruments), outdoor/unstructured terrain, domain knowledge of correct configuration, and tool generalization—all well beyond current frontier robots' reliable capability.

task: Repair car upholstery.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single damaged area (e.g., a tear or stain) on a seat or panel; a skilled upholstery technician typically takes 30–60 min per repair.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for stitching, patching, and adhesive application on flexible, deformable fabric/leather in a confined vehicle interior. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured settings.

task: Apply prepared specimen and control to appropriate grid, run instrumentation, and produce analyzable results.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying specimen/control to a grid, loading into instrument, running the analysis cycle, and confirming output. Typically 20–30 min for a trained lab tech.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (micro-scale grid handling), sterile lab technique, instrument-specific UI interaction, and result validation. Current humanoids lack sub-millimeter precision and lab equipment interfacing needed here.

task: Set up and display sample merchandise at parties or stands.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a single merchandise display at one party/stand, including unpacking, arranging, and positioning items attractively. Typically 30–60 minutes for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires unstructured environments, aesthetic judgment, varied objects, navigation in crowded/outdoor spaces, and social context awareness. Current humanoids lack generalization for creative display arrangement and novel venue layouts.

task: Inspect work areas or operating equipment to ensure conformance to established standards in areas such as cleanliness or maintenance.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single work area or piece of equipment for cleanliness/maintenance compliance; a competent worker typically takes 15–25 minutes to systematically check, note, and document findings.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate and use cameras to detect visible dirt or damage, but nuanced standard-conformance judgments, accessing all surfaces, interpreting context-specific criteria, and documenting findings reliably remain well beyond current general-purpose humanoid capability.

task: Operate specialized equipment to remove obstructions by backing off or severing pipes by chemical or explosive action.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pipe obstruction removal operation (setup, deployment of chemical/explosive charge or back-off tool, execution, and verification). Typically 60–120 min for a skilled oilfield/pipeline technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized downhole/pipeline equipment, handling explosives or corrosive chemicals, precise tool deployment in confined/hazardous environments, and expert judgment — far beyond 2026 humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Listen to engines, rotary chains, or other equipment to detect faulty operations or unusual well conditions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single listening/monitoring pass of equipment at one well site; a competent worker typically takes ~10–20 minutes to walk around, listen, and assess all relevant equipment.
robot_success_rationale: This task relies on trained auditory perception and experiential pattern recognition of subtle acoustic anomalies in complex industrial equipment. Current humanoid robots lack reliable real-world acoustic fault-detection and contextual interpretation in unstructured oilfield environments.

task: Bathe patients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = bathing one patient (bed bath or assisted shower). A competent aide typically takes 15–25 minutes including prep, washing, drying, and repositioning.
robot_success_rationale: Requires gentle, adaptive touch on human skin, safe handling of a fragile/mobile patient, complex dexterity (washing all body areas), and real-time safety judgments. Far beyond 2026 humanoid capabilities in unstructured caregiving.

task: Sprinkle colored marble or stone chips, powdered steel, or coloring powder over surface to produce prescribed finish.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sprinkling material over one surface section (e.g., a countertop or floor panel). A skilled worker spreads, adjusts density, and checks coverage in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires controlled, even dispersal of loose granular/powder materials over a surface with aesthetic precision. Current robots lack fine motor control for calibrated sprinkling, real-time visual feedback for even coverage, and generalization to varied surface sizes and material types.

task: Operate x-ray, electrocardiogram (EKG), or other equipment to administer routine diagnostic tests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one diagnostic test session (e.g., a 12-lead EKG or single X-ray series) including patient prep, equipment setup, image/signal acquisition, and labeling. Typical clinical time: 15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced patient interaction, precise electrode/probe placement on varied human bodies, operating specialized medical equipment, and clinical judgment. Current humanoids lack medical device interfaces, safe patient-contact protocols, and fine dexterity for clinical prep.

task: Place radioactive waste, such as sweepings or broken sample bottles, into containers for shipping or disposal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one batch of radioactive waste containerized from a single work area, including PPE donning, placing waste, sealing, and labeling; typically 15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling sharp/irregular broken glass, fine manipulation of loose sweepings, operating in a hazardous/regulated environment, reading and affixing labels, sealing specialized containers, and full situational awareness — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Monitor machine operation, and make adjustments as necessary to ensure proper operation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring cycle/check on a machine, including observing readings, diagnosing state, and making minor adjustments. Typical cycle ~15–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading gauges/displays, interpreting abnormal conditions, and making contextual mechanical adjustments. Current robots lack reliable generalized machine-state interpretation and adaptive adjustment across diverse industrial machines.

task: Load computer tapes and disks, and install software and printer paper or forms.
human_time_rationale: One instance covers loading media, installing software, and loading printer paper/forms; a competent worker completes this routine setup cycle in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for inserting tapes/disks into drives, navigating software GUIs, and precisely loading paper into trays. Poor generalization across device types, GUI interaction, and form alignment challenges current robots significantly.

task: Monitor and control temperature of products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking and adjusting temperature controls for a batch/zone of products; a competent worker reads gauges/sensors and makes adjustments in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can read digital displays and operate simple controls, but interpreting analog gauges, navigating varied industrial environments, and making nuanced control adjustments remains unreliable for frontier humanoids. Perception and generalization are limiting factors.

task: Lift and unload containers of mail or parcels onto equipment for transportation to sortation stations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lifting and unloading one container (tub, tray, or sack) onto transport equipment. A competent worker does this in ~2–4 minutes including positioning and placement.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids can pick and place tote-like containers at moderate success rates, but variable container shapes/weights, tight conveyor interfaces, and cluttered dock environments reduce reliability. Locomotion on dock floors is manageable but grasping irregular mail sacks is challenging.

task: Remove from machines printed materials, such as labeled articles, postmarked envelopes or tape, and folded sheets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing printed output from a single machine cycle (e.g., a stack of labeled articles or folded sheets). A competent worker completes this in ~1 minute.
robot_success_rationale: Pick-and-place of flat/thin items (envelopes, folded sheets, tape) from machines is feasible but challenging — requires reliable perception of thin, flexible objects and precise gripper control near machinery. Generalization across item types limits success.

task: Sort and route incoming mail, and collect outgoing mail, using carts as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single mail run (sorting + routing incoming + collecting outgoing) for a typical office floor. A competent mail clerk takes ~30–60 min per run.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading addresses/labels (OCR in varied orientations), navigating office hallways, opening mail slots/boxes, pushing a cart, and social awareness. Cart use and unstructured office navigation remain unreliable; label reading and sorting logic add further failure points.

task: Operate power vibrator to compact concrete.
human_time_rationale: One instance = vibrating a freshly poured concrete pour/section (e.g., a wall panel or slab segment). A competent worker inserts and moves the vibrator head systematically across the pour, typically 15–25 minutes per section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires carrying and maneuvering a heavy vibrator tool on active construction terrain, judging insertion depth/spacing/duration by feel and sound, and operating safely near fresh concrete forms. Current humanoids lack construction-site mobility, tool-force feedback, and domain generalization for this task.

task: Transport the deceased to the funeral home.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving one deceased person from pickup location (home, hospital, or morgue) to a funeral home, including transfer to a cot/stretcher, loading into a vehicle, driving, and unloading.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving a specialized vehicle on public roads (humanoids cannot do this), plus careful transfer of a full human body — heavy, awkward, legally sensitive. Current robots lack all of: driving, safe body handling, and navigation to arbitrary locations.

task: Perform administrative duties, such as assisting in school libraries, hall and cafeteria monitoring, and bus loading and unloading.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one duty shift (e.g., one cafeteria/hall/bus monitoring period). A typical school duty rotation lasts ~30–60 minutes per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced social judgment, crowd management, child safety interventions, and dynamic unstructured environments. Current robots lack reliable child interaction, behavioral assessment, and authority communication needed for autonomous school supervision.

task: Extract, measure, dilute as appropriate, label, and prepare DNA for array analysis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single sample batch through extraction, QC measurement, dilution, labeling, and array prep. A competent lab tech typically takes ~2 hours end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pipetting at sub-microliter scales, operating centrifuges/spectrophotometers, reading liquid levels, labeling tubes, and following conditional protocols. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and lab-tool integration.

task: Distribute product samples or literature that details products or services.
human_time_rationale: One instance = distributing samples/literature to a single location or batch of recipients (e.g., a trade show booth visit or a door-to-door stop). Typically 20–40 min including travel and handoff.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigation in unstructured public/outdoor spaces, social interaction, handing items to varied people, and adapting to dynamic environments. Locomotion and basic pick-and-place are feasible, but social engagement, crowd navigation, and reliable item handoff to humans are weak.

task: Assemble new bicycles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one new bicycle from a boxed kit (attaching handlebars, front wheel, pedals, seat, adjusting brakes/gears). A competent bike mechanic typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high dexterity for torquing bolts to spec, threading cables, aligning brakes/derailleurs, and handling large asymmetric parts. Current humanoids lack the fine tool-use, force feedback, and multi-step sequencing needed for this complex assembly.

task: Create or maintain inclines or ramps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing or significantly maintaining one ramp/incline (e.g., a loading dock ramp or earthen grade). Involves grading, compacting, and finishing; typically 1–3 hours for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy grading/compacting machinery, precise earthwork or concrete forming, and outdoor unstructured environments. Current humanoid robots cannot operate such equipment or perform the physical grading/compaction work autonomously.

task: Stamp dates and times of receipt of incoming mail.
human_time_rationale: One instance = stamping one piece of mail. A competent worker picks up mail, aligns stamp, and presses — takes ~30 seconds.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grasping varied mail shapes, precisely aligning a hand stamp on a specific area, and applying controlled force. Dexterity and consistent placement are challenging; ink stamp tools are not robot-native. Perception of stamp zone adds difficulty.

task: Set up and arrange displays or demonstration areas to attract the attention of prospective customers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a single display/demo area (e.g., a retail or trade-show booth). A competent worker arranges props, signage, and products in ~30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires creative spatial judgment, aesthetic decision-making, handling diverse irregular objects (signage, banners, products), and contextual marketing intent — far beyond current robot generalization and task planning capabilities.

task: Remove interior and exterior finishes on safes and vaults, and spray on new finishes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = refinishing one safe/vault unit. Stripping old finish, prepping surfaces, and spray-applying new finish takes ~90 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling spray equipment, navigating confined interior surfaces, masking hardware, and achieving uniform finish quality. Current humanoids lack the fine tool control, spray calibration, and spatial reasoning for interior/exterior finishing work on complex metal structures.

task: Prepare and process papers for use in scanning, microfilming, and microfiche.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a batch of documents (e.g., a folder/file of ~50–100 pages): removing staples, unfolding, ordering, and staging for scanning/microfilming.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to remove staples, unfold fragile/varied paper, detect damage, and precisely order/align documents. Paper manipulation is highly challenging for current robots; inconsistent paper types and sizes worsen reliability.

task: Inspect proposed building sites to determine suitability for construction.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one proposed building site. A competent surveyor/inspector typically spends 1–3 hours walking the site, assessing terrain, soil, drainage, access, utilities, and hazards.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor rough-terrain mobility, multi-modal environmental assessment (soil, drainage, slope, utilities), expert judgment synthesis, and report generation. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor traversal, sensing tools, and professional-grade geotechnical reasoning.

task: Install pressure-control devices onto wellheads.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a pressure-control device (e.g., choke, valve, BOP component) onto a single wellhead, including alignment, bolting, and pressure testing. Competent oilfield hands typically take 1–2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating in harsh outdoor/industrial terrain, handling very heavy and awkward flanged equipment, precise torque sequencing on large bolts, and pressure testing. No current humanoid has these capabilities or the specialized tools needed.

task: Show patients to examination rooms and prepare them for the physician.
human_time_rationale: One instance = escorting one patient from waiting area to exam room, taking vitals, and preparing them (gown, seating, brief intake). Typically 8–12 minutes for a medical assistant.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a clinical environment with patients, guiding/communicating with diverse individuals, taking vitals with medical devices, and ensuring patient safety/comfort — far beyond current humanoid generalization, dexterity, and social interaction capabilities.

task: Dig holes, set forms, and mix and pour concrete into forms to make foundations for wood or steel derricks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and pouring a single derrick foundation. Includes digging, form-setting, mixing, and pouring concrete — typically a full crew half-day (~8 hours).
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating excavation equipment, mixing/placing large concrete volumes, setting structural forms outdoors on uneven terrain, and using heavy tools — far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured construction sites.

task: Inspect individual parts, assemblies, or installations, using measuring instruments, such as calipers, scales, or micrometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single part/assembly with measuring instruments. A competent inspector typically takes 5–15 minutes per part depending on complexity and number of measurements.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise tool grasp, repeatable contact placement of calipers/micrometers, and accurate reading of analog/digital instruments. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity and tactile feedback for consistent metrology-grade measurements; perception of instrument readings is also unreliable.

task: Wear costumes or sign boards and walk in public to promote merchandise, services, or events.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a typical promotional shift (e.g., 4-hour street marketing appearance), the standard unit measured by a marketing manager.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sustained outdoor locomotion on varied terrain, wearing bulky costumes, interacting socially with the public, and adapting to crowds — far beyond 2026 humanoid generalization, safety, and social engagement capabilities.

task: Practice demonstrations to ensure that they will run smoothly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full practice run-through of a demonstration. A competent presenter typically takes 30–60 minutes to rehearse and verify all elements run smoothly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level planning, judgment about what "runs smoothly" means, troubleshooting failures, coordinating equipment/software, and adapting content — far beyond current robot cognitive and generalization capabilities.

task: Sort tree seedlings, discarding substandard seedlings, according to standard charts or verbal instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting one seedling (inspect, classify, accept/discard). An experienced worker handles dozens per minute; ~1.5 min accounts for pauses, trays, and reference checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual grading of delicate, irregular organic objects against nuanced quality standards, precise gentle grasping of fragile stems, and chart/instruction interpretation. Current robots lack reliable fine perception and gentle manipulation for variable seedling morphology.

task: Add ink, fill paste reservoirs, and change machine ribbons when necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = servicing one machine (refilling ink/paste or replacing a ribbon). A competent worker takes ~10 min including retrieving supplies and verifying output.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to open small reservoirs, handle ink cartridges/ribbon spools, align ribbons, and avoid spills. Varied machine form factors and tight tolerances challenge current humanoid perception and manipulation significantly.

task: Wrap packages or bundles by hand, or by using tying machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = wrapping/tying one package or bundle. A competent worker typically takes 2–4 minutes depending on size and method (hand vs. machine).
robot_success_rationale: Wrapping and tying require bimanual dexterity, managing flexible materials (twine, tape, film), and adapting to varied bundle shapes. Current humanoids struggle with deformable material handling and consistent knot-tying or tape application at acceptable quality.

task: Weigh products or estimate their weight, visually or by feel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = estimating the weight of a single product by visual inspection or handling it briefly. A skilled worker does this in under a minute.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick up objects and use force/torque sensors to estimate weight by feel, but visual-only weight estimation requires learned priors and generalizes poorly. Accuracy on arbitrary novel products is unreliable without scale integration.

task: Demonstrate the operation or use of technical or scientific products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single product demonstration (e.g., showing how a device works to a customer or audience). A competent demonstrator typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fluid verbal explanation, adaptive social interaction, handling diverse unfamiliar products, and improvising to audience questions. Current humanoids lack robust language-grounded physical demonstration and real-time social generalization.

task: Perform tests on seeds to evaluate seed viability.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a batch of seeds (e.g., germination test or tetrazolium staining for ~100 seeds). Setup, incubation monitoring, and recording results typically takes ~1 hour of active work, excluding multi-day germination wait times.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise handling of tiny seeds, pipetting chemicals (tetrazolium, moisture), reading subtle color/germination changes, and interpreting biological results. Current robots lack fine dexterity for seeds and reliable bio-visual interpretation in unstructured lab settings.

task: Close and seal wells no longer in use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = plugging and abandoning one well. A typical well abandonment involves fluid removal, setting cement plugs, and surface casing cutting/capping — commonly a full workday (6–8 hours) for a crew.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy specialized oilfield equipment (workover rigs, cement pumps, pipe cutters), working at a wellhead in unpredictable outdoor terrain, and making safety-critical decisions. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Evaluate premises for cleanliness, such as proper garbage disposal or lack of vermin infestation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one premises (e.g., a restaurant or facility). A competent inspector walks all areas, checks garbage, drains, storage, and signs of pests — typically 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced multi-room locomotion, detecting subtle infestation signs (droppings, odors, gnaw marks), and contextual judgment. Current robots lack reliable olfactory sensing, pest-sign recognition, and unstructured-environment generalization needed for a full inspection.

task: Take and record measures of patients' physical condition, using devices such as thermometers or blood pressure gauges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single patient's vitals (temp, BP, pulse, SpO2). A competent nurse/aide completes a standard vitals set in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterous placement of sensors on a compliant, variable human body, safe patient interaction, and accurate data recording. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-touch caregiving dexterity and safe human-contact protocols needed for autonomous clinical use.

task: Open safe locks by drilling.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling open one safe lock. A skilled locksmith/safe technician typically takes 30–90 min depending on safe type, averaging ~60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise drill positioning on a hardened lock mechanism, sustained controlled pressure, tool changes, and diagnostic judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and tool-use generalization. No known deployment.

task: Melt metals or mix plaster, porcelain, or acrylic pastes and pour materials into molds or over frameworks to form dental prostheses or apparatuses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and pouring material for a single dental prosthesis/mold. Includes mixing, heating, and casting; typically 30–60 min for a trained dental technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, temperature control, fine dexterous manipulation of viscous materials, and specialized lab equipment. Current humanoids lack the fine motor control, tactile feedback, and domain-specific tool-use needed for acceptable dental-lab quality.

task: Stock carts or stands.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading/arranging items onto a single cart or stand. A competent worker takes ~15 min to retrieve, organize, and stock one cart fully.
robot_success_rationale: Requires mobile navigation, varied item grasping, and organized placement on shelves/hooks. Current humanoids handle pick-and-place moderately well but struggle with diverse item shapes, tight spacing, and maintaining order on a moving cart.

task: Move picklocks in cylinders to open door locks without keys.
human_time_rationale: One instance = picking one pin-tumbler door lock. A competent locksmith typically opens a standard lock in 2–10 minutes; median ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Lock-picking requires extreme fine dexterity, precise tactile feedback, and delicate tension control inside a 1–2 mm keyway. Current humanoid robots lack the fingertip sensitivity and micro-force control needed for this highly unstructured, tactile-dominant task.

task: Irrigate soil, using portable pipes or ditch systems, and maintain ditches or pipes and pumps.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up/running irrigation for a field section per day shift. Moving pipes, opening/closing valves, checking ditches, and adjusting pumps takes ~2 hours for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy pipe manipulation outdoors on uneven terrain, operating pumps/valves, digging/clearing ditches, and navigating flooded fields. Far beyond current humanoid locomotion, payload, and outdoor generalization capabilities.

task: Perforate well casings or sidewalls of boreholes with explosive charges.
human_time_rationale: One instance = perforating a single borehole zone. Includes rigging up the perforating gun, running it to depth, firing charges, and verifying results. Typically 1.5–2.5 hours for a competent crew.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating wireline trucks, specialized downhole perforating guns, explosive handling procedures, and real-time logging interpretation at a wellsite. Humanoid robots cannot drive/operate this equipment or handle licensed explosives autonomously at acceptable safety standards.

task: Monitor building security and safety by performing tasks such as locking doors after operating hours or checking electrical appliance use to ensure that hazards are not created.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single security/safety walkthrough of a typical building, checking doors, appliances, and hazards. A competent guard completes this in ~30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can navigate corridors and detect some hazards via vision, but locking varied door types, operating light switches/appliances, and making contextual safety judgments in unstructured environments remain unreliable for current humanoids.

task: Adjust, repair, and service farm machinery and notify supervisors when machinery malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single repair/adjustment/service session on one piece of farm equipment. Competent mechanic typically spends 60–120 min diagnosing, fixing, and verifying one fault.
robot_success_rationale: Farm machinery repair requires specialized tool use, high-force manipulation, diesel/hydraulic systems expertise, unstructured outdoor environments, and contextual fault diagnosis — all far beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capabilities. Notification alone is trivial but the full task is not.

task: Examine lifting or conveying devices, such as elevators, escalators, moving sidewalks, hoists, inclined railways, ski lifts, or amusement rides to ensure safety and proper functioning.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full inspection of one device (e.g., one elevator). Certified inspectors typically spend 45–90 min per unit covering mechanical, electrical, and safety checks.
robot_success_rationale: Inspection requires navigating confined machine rooms, pits, and shafts; reading gauges/labels; operating test controls; interpreting complex mechanical/electrical systems; and producing a compliant report. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and safety capability.

task: Fill chipped or low spots in surfaces of devices, using acrylic resins.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling chips/low spots on one device surface. Includes prep, mixing resin, applying, smoothing, and initial cure check — typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise defect detection, fine dexterity to mix and apply viscous resin into irregular micro-voids, and surface-quality judgment. Current humanoids lack the tactile sensitivity, fine motor control, and inspection capability needed.

task: Clean up spilled oil by bailing it into barrels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = scooping/bailing a typical industrial oil spill into barrels; a competent worker with scoops and pumps takes ~60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires scooping viscous, unstructured liquid from irregular surfaces, maneuvering heavy barrels, and working in a slippery, hazardous environment. Current humanoids lack the fluid-handling tools, stability on oily surfaces, and force/flow control needed.

task: Participate in firefighting efforts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one active firefighting engagement (arriving on scene, deploying equipment, suppressing fire, securing area). Typical structure fire takes 2–4 hours total.
robot_success_rationale: Firefighting demands extreme heat tolerance, operating heavy hoses under high pressure, navigating collapsed/smoke-filled structures, coordinating with a crew, and making split-second life-safety decisions. Current humanoids lack thermal protection, hose-handling strength, situational awareness in chaotic environments, and regulatory clearance for such operations.

task: Fold letters or circulars and insert them in envelopes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = folding one letter and inserting it into one envelope. A competent worker can do this in ~60–90 seconds, including folding and stuffing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise paper handling, consistent folding along straight lines, and inserting a flexible sheet into a narrow envelope opening — all demand fine dexterity and bimanual coordination that current humanoids handle poorly. Paper deformation and slipping add further unreliability.

task: Chip, scrape, and grind high spots, ridges, and rough projections to finish concrete, using pneumatic chisels, power grinders, or hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing one section (~10–20 sq ft) of concrete surface. A skilled worker uses pneumatic/hand tools to chip and grind defects in roughly 20–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled tool use on irregular surfaces, perception of subtle high spots/ridges, and wielding heavy pneumatic tools. Current humanoids lack the fine force feedback, sustained tool-holding strength, and surface-defect perception needed for acceptable quality.

task: Signal truck driver to position truck to facilitate pouring concrete, and move chute to direct concrete on forms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one truck positioning and chute setup for a pour. Skilled worker signals driver into place, adjusts chute direction/extension — typically 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor unstructured terrain navigation, real-time hand/flag signaling communication with a human truck driver, and physically manipulating a heavy concrete chute. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor locomotion, inter-human signaling protocols, and force capacity for heavy chute control.

task: Use photographic or video equipment to document evidence or crime scenes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = documenting a single crime scene or evidence set. A competent forensic photographer spends ~30–60 min capturing photos/video from multiple angles, distances, and with scale markers.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced scene interpretation, strategic camera positioning, scale placement, lighting judgment, and legal/procedural compliance. Current robots lack forensic domain knowledge, fine compositional control, and reliable unstructured-environment navigation needed for acceptable evidentiary quality.

task: Seize equipment used in fish and game law violations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = seizing equipment during a single enforcement stop. Includes identifying violations, documenting, and physically confiscating gear (nets, traps, weapons, vehicles).
robot_success_rationale: Requires legal authority/judgment, suspect interaction, varied outdoor terrain, identifying illegal equipment, and potentially confrontational human engagement. Far beyond current robot autonomy in unstructured field law enforcement.

task: Select tree seedlings, prepare the ground, or plant the trees in reforestation areas, using manual planting tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = planting one seedling: select it, make a hole with a dibble/hoedad, place seedling, firm soil. Experienced planters do ~20/hour on good terrain.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor rough/sloped terrain locomotion, bimanual tool use (hoedad/dibble), seedling selection, and precise planting depth — all in unstructured forest environments. Far beyond 2026 frontier robot capability.

task: Mow or trim lawns or shrubbery, using mowers or hand or power trimmers, and clear debris from grounds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mowing/trimming a typical residential property (~0.25 acre) including debris clearing; a competent groundskeeper completes this in ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate ride-on mowers or walk-behind mowers reliably outdoors. Rough terrain, varied vegetation, tool changeovers (mower→trimmer), and unstructured debris clearance far exceed 2026 general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Read prescriptions or specifications and examine models or impressions to determine the design of dental products to be constructed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining a single prescription/model set for one patient case. A dental lab technician typically spends ~5–15 minutes reviewing specs and impressions before design work begins.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert interpretation of handwritten/printed dental prescriptions, 3D dental impressions, and clinical nuance. Current robots lack dental domain knowledge, fine perception of impression details, and cognitive reasoning needed for design determination.

task: Perform work activities of subordinates, such as cleaning and organizing shelves and displays and selling merchandise.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single session of cleaning/organizing a shelf section or display and assisting with sales; ~45 min for a typical retail floor section.
