robot_success_rationale: Basic shelf wiping and object repositioning are feasible at ~40–60%, but selling merchandise requires social interaction, language, and judgment. The composite task is difficult; unstructured retail environments and customer interaction drag success low.

task: Prime all scratches on pinchwelds with primer and allow to dry.
human_time_rationale: One instance = priming all pinchweld scratches on one vehicle. A competent auto body worker spends ~25 min identifying scratches, masking, applying primer, and waiting for initial dry.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (spray/brush application), precise scratch identification on irregular surfaces, masking, and quality judgment. Current humanoids lack the perception precision and dexterous control for acceptable automotive prep work.

task: Cut away dead and excess branches from trees, or clear branches around power lines, using climbing equipment or buckets of extended truck booms, or chainsaws, hooks, handsaws, shears, and clippers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = trimming one tree or one power-line span. A competent arborist typically spends 60–120 minutes setting up, climbing/booming, cutting, and clearing debris for a single tree.
robot_success_rationale: Requires climbing or operating a boom truck (impossible for humanoids), outdoor unstructured terrain, precise chainsaw/shear use in a canopy, real-time hazard judgment near live power lines. Far beyond any 2026 general-purpose robot's capabilities.

task: Clean and file master copies or plates.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and filing one master copy/plate; worker cleans surface and places it in correct labeled storage in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate handling of fragile flat media, precise cleaning without damage, and filing into a specific labeled slot — demanding fine dexterity, semantic understanding of filing systems, and careful force control beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Hammer out dents and bends in frames and weld tears and breaks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single damaged frame section. A skilled metalworker spends ~30–90 min hammering, checking, and welding depending on damage severity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine force-feedback hammering to correct metal deformation, skilled weld quality judgment, and precise torch/MIG control. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tactile sensing, and specialized tool-use needed for acceptable results.

task: Operate auxiliary machines such as collators, pad and tablet making machines, staplers, and paper punching, folding, cutting, and perforating machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one job run (loading, operating, and unloading a batch of documents through one auxiliary machine). A competent operator completes a typical batch in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires loading precise paper stacks, adjusting machine settings, monitoring output quality, and clearing jams on varied specialized machines. Current robots lack reliable fine-paper manipulation, machine-specific control interfaces, and fault-recovery generalization needed for autonomous operation.

task: Sort, assemble, and proof completed work.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reviewing, sorting, and proofing a single batch of completed work (e.g., a document set or output stack). ~20 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Sorting physical items is feasible (~50–70%), but proofreading requires language comprehension, quality judgment, and contextual reasoning well beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities. Combined success is very low.

task: Remove containers of sorted mail or parcels and transfer them to designated areas according to established procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing a container of sorted mail/parcels and transferring it to a designated area; a competent worker does this in ~5 minutes including handling and transport.
robot_success_rationale: Grasping and transporting varied mail containers is feasible, but navigating a mail facility, identifying correct designated areas per procedure, and handling diverse container types/weights reduces reliability significantly for current humanoids.

task: Insert detection instruments into wells with obstructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inserting a detection instrument into a single obstructed well. A competent worker assesses the obstruction, selects the right tool/instrument, maneuvers it past the obstruction, and confirms seating — roughly 5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to a field well site (outdoor/uneven terrain), diagnosing the specific obstruction type, applying adaptive force and fine dexterity to maneuver instruments past blockages — well beyond current humanoid perception, tool-use, and generalization capabilities.

task: Perform endotracheal intubation to maintain open airways for patients who are unable to breathe on their own.
human_time_rationale: One instance = intubating one patient. A skilled clinician (anesthesiologist/paramedic) typically takes 2–8 minutes from prep to tube confirmation, median ~5 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, precise force control in a fragile airway, real-time anatomical adaptation, sterile technique, and patient-safety assurance far beyond any 2026 general-purpose humanoid's demonstrated capability.

task: Split logs or wooden blocks into bolts, pickets, posts, or stakes, using hand tools such as ax wedges, sledgehammers, and mallets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = splitting a single log/block into its pieces. A competent worker positions, strikes, and splits one log in roughly 5–10 minutes depending on wood type and size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-force swinging of heavy tools (sledgehammer, maul, wedge) with precise aim on irregular natural material outdoors. Current humanoids lack the power output, dynamic swing control, grip strength, and balance stability needed for forceful overhead strikes on unpredictable log shapes.

task: Clean machines, perform minor repairs, and report major repair needs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one machine, checking for minor issues, and logging any major repair needs. A competent worker typically takes ~30 min per machine.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle basic surface cleaning (~40–70%), but minor repairs (tightening, replacing parts) and fault diagnosis require tool use, generalization, and judgment that current humanoids handle poorly. Combined task success is low.

task: Insert new or repaired tumblers into locks to change combinations.
human_time_rationale: One instance = rekeying one lock cylinder. A competent locksmith inserts new tumblers/pins, springs, and reassembles in ~10–20 minutes depending on lock complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity: inserting tiny spring-loaded pins/tumblers into small keyway channels under spring tension. Tolerances are sub-millimeter; current humanoid robots lack the fingertip precision and force control for reliable execution.

task: Perform quality checks on products and parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single product/part. A competent worker typically spends ~5 minutes examining, measuring, and logging results for one item.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is mature, but autonomously handling varied parts, repositioning them, using gauges/calipers, and making accept/reject decisions across diverse products in unstructured settings remains unreliable for general-purpose humanoids.

task: Perform regular maintenance of laboratory equipment by inspecting, calibrating, cleaning, or sterilizing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full maintenance cycle (inspect, calibrate, clean/sterilize) on a single lab instrument. A competent technician typically takes 30–60 min per device.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for calibration tools, instrument-specific tool-use, reading displays, handling chemicals/sterilants, and generalizing across diverse lab equipment. Far beyond current humanoid capability at acceptable quality.

task: Measure or weigh ingredients used in laboratory testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring/weighing a single ingredient for a lab test. A competent lab tech spends ~3–7 min accounting for taring, dispensing, and recording.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity (spatulas, pipettes, analytical balances), reading small digital displays, handling powders/liquids without spilling, and recording values — well beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Reassemble frames and reinstall engines after repairs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reassembling a vehicle frame and reinstalling one engine after repair. A competent mechanic typically takes 6–10 hours for full engine reinstallation and frame reassembly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires heavy lifting (engine hoists/cranes), precise torque specs on dozens of fasteners, routing hoses/wiring, and working in cramped bays. Current humanoids lack the force, tooling, and generalization for this complex multi-step heavy mechanical task.

task: Prepare merchandise for display or for purchase or rental.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a batch of merchandise (e.g., a shelf unit or a small lot of items): unboxing, tagging, arranging, and placing items for display or checkout. ~15 min per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires varied manipulation: unboxing, tagging, folding, hanging, and arranging diverse items with aesthetic judgment. Current robots handle simple pick-and-place but struggle with fine tagging, varied packaging, and display arrangement decisions.

task: Fabricate, alter, or repair dental devices, such as dentures, crowns, bridges, inlays, or appliances for straightening teeth.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating or repairing a single dental device (e.g., a crown or partial denture). A skilled dental lab technician typically spends 1–3 hours per unit across casting, shaping, and finishing steps.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sub-millimeter precision, specialized lab tools (wax carving, porcelain layering, polishing), and expert tactile/visual judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Remove excess metal or porcelain and polish surfaces of prostheses or frameworks, using polishing machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = polishing one dental prosthesis/framework. A skilled dental lab tech typically spends 20–30 min removing excess material and achieving an acceptable polish on one unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterity, precise force control on delicate curved surfaces, and expert tactile/visual feedback to avoid over-removal. Current humanoids lack the micro-precision, specialized tooling integration, and quality-judgement needed for dental lab finishing.

task: Set the forms that hold concrete to the desired pitch and depth, and align them.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting and aligning forms for a single concrete pour section (e.g., a slab or footing run). A competent worker typically spends 30–60 min measuring, staking, leveling, and aligning forms to spec.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, leveling to grade, driving stakes, and aligning heavy form panels outdoors on uneven terrain. Current humanoids lack the tool-use precision, load-bearing capability, and spatial surveying skills needed for acceptable quality.

task: Supply equipment to rig floors as requested and provide assistance to roughnecks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one supply/delivery run to a rig floor, fetching, carrying, and handing off tools or materials to roughnecks. Typically 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Rig floors are highly hazardous, unstructured, and dynamic environments with heavy rotating machinery, slick surfaces, irregular terrain, and complex large equipment. Current humanoids lack the robustness, safety certification, and specialized tool-handling needed.

task: Unbolt and remove wheels from vehicles, using lug wrenches or other hand or power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing all wheels from one vehicle (4 wheels). A competent mechanic takes ~2–3 min per wheel with an impact wrench, ~10 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise socket alignment on lug nuts, high-torque tool use, supporting a heavy wheel during removal, and working in tight low/awkward positions under a vehicle. Current humanoids lack reliable torque tool handling and wheel-weight management.

task: Set up and adjust machines, regulating factors such as speed, ink flow, focus, and number of copies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and adjusting a printing/copying machine for a job run. A competent operator typically takes 10–20 min to calibrate speed, ink flow, focus, and copy count.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading machine displays, manipulating fine control dials/buttons, interpreting print quality feedback, and generalizing across machine types. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-dexterity and machine-specific tool-use for quality judgment.

task: Fasten roof panel edges or machine-made moldings to structures by nailing or welding.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fastening one roof panel edge or molding run (nailing or welding). A competent worker takes ~15–25 min per panel/section including positioning, fastening, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires working at height on uneven roof surfaces (poor locomotion stability), precise alignment of panels, and skilled welding or pneumatic nailing. Generalization to varied roof geometries, materials, and conditions is far beyond 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Place original copies in feed trays, feed originals into feed rolls, or position originals on tables beneath camera lenses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading one set of originals into a scanner/copier feed tray or positioning on a flatbed/camera table. A competent worker does this in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Handling flat paper (aligning, fanning, loading into narrow feed trays) requires precise dexterity and thin-object manipulation. Perception of paper orientation and tray alignment is challenging; current humanoids struggle with limp, thin materials reliably.

task: Test appliances for conformance to specifications and accuracy of occlusion, using articulators and micrometers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single dental appliance for occlusal conformance. A skilled dental lab technician typically spends 10–20 min mounting, articulating, and measuring one appliance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter dexterous manipulation of delicate dental appliances, precise articulator setup, and micrometer use with expert tactile feedback. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, dental domain perception, and tool-use precision needed.

task: Apply porcelain paste or wax over prosthesis frameworks or setups, using brushes and spatulas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying porcelain paste/wax to a single prosthesis framework. A skilled dental lab technician typically spends 30–60 min per unit for layering, contouring, and finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity with small brushes/spatulas on a tiny, irregular dental framework; sub-millimeter precision; tactile feedback critical. Far beyond current humanoid robot dexterity and perception limits.

task: Use dental air turbines, hand instruments, dental appliances, or surgical implements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single dental procedure (e.g., cavity prep + filling). A competent dentist/hygienist typically spends 30–60 minutes per patient procedure.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme sub-millimeter dexterity inside a small oral cavity, real-time tactile/visual feedback, sterile technique, and patient-adaptive movement. No general-purpose humanoid robot can safely perform this in 2026.

task: Maneuver completed roofing units into position for installation.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning a single roofing unit (e.g., shingle bundle or panel) into its correct installation spot on a roof. A skilled roofer takes ~10–20 min per unit including alignment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor uneven/sloped rooftop locomotion, heavy lifting, precise placement, and significant balance demands. Current humanoids lack the stability, load capacity, and outdoor terrain capability for safe rooftop work.

task: Drill holes in timbers or lumber.
human_time_rationale: One instance = drilling one hole in a timber/lumber piece. A competent worker positions, aligns, and drills a single hole in ~2–4 minutes including setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires gripping a heavy power drill, precisely aligning it perpendicular to an irregular wood surface, applying sustained force, and managing torque reaction. Perception of exact hole placement and force control on uneven lumber are challenging for current humanoids.

task: Build and shape wax teeth, using small hand instruments and information from observations or dentists' specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sculpting a single wax tooth. A skilled dental lab technician typically takes 30–60 min per tooth, depending on complexity and type.
robot_success_rationale: Requires ultra-fine dexterous sculpting with small instruments on soft wax, precise 3D form matching specifications, and artistic interpretation. Far beyond current robot dexterity and fine-motor control; analogous to surgery or fine craft work.

task: Prepare culture media, following standard procedures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one batch of culture media (weighing reagents, dissolving, pH adjusting, autoclaving/filtering). Typically 30–60 min active work for a lab tech.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid handling, fine manipulation of lab glassware, operating analytical balances, pH meters, and autoclaves, plus aseptic technique. Far beyond current humanoid generalization in unstructured lab settings.

task: Perform routine laboratory tests and sample analyses.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a standard battery of routine lab tests on a single sample (e.g., centrifuge, pipette, run assay, record results). Typical bench time is 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Lab work demands extreme dexterity (pipetting µL volumes), precise instrument operation, hazard handling, and contextual judgment. Current humanoids lack the fine-motor precision and lab-specific tool integration required for autonomous, error-free sample analysis.

task: Operate office machines such as high speed business photocopiers, readers, scanners, addressing machines, stencil-cutting machines, microfilm readers or printers, folding and inserting machines, bursters, and binder machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing a single office machine job (e.g., a copy/scan/fold run). A competent operator loads materials, configures settings, runs the job, and retrieves output in ~8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating to varied machines, loading paper/originals, operating diverse control panels/touchscreens, and troubleshooting jams. Each machine has unique interfaces and fine-paper-handling demands that challenge current robot dexterity and generalization.

task: Affix postage to packages or letters by hand, or stamp materials, using postage meters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = affixing postage to a single package or letter. A competent worker peels/stamps and applies postage in ~1–2 minutes per item.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to handle stamps/stickers, align precisely on varied envelope sizes, and operate a postage meter's controls. Perception of correct placement and peel-and-stick manipulation are still error-prone for 2026 humanoids.

task: Discard inferior or defective products or foreign matter, and place acceptable products in containers for further processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and sorting a single item. A trained worker on a production line takes ~20–30 seconds per item to visually inspect, discard defects, and place accepted items in a container.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based defect detection is mature, and pick-and-place of common objects scores ~70–90%. However, generalizing across varied product types, subtle defects, and foreign matter identification reduces reliability. Grasping irregularly shaped or fragile items adds further uncertainty.

task: Move heat units and clamping frames over screen beds to form Braille impressions on pages, raising frames to release individual copies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one Braille page. Operator moves heat unit, clamps frame over screen bed, applies impression, raises frame to release — roughly 1–3 min per page.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise positioning of heat unit and clamping frames over a screen bed, controlled force application, and sequenced release without damaging thin paper. Specialized tooling, fine force control, and thermal safety make autonomous success unlikely with 2026 humanoids.

task: Operate pumps that circulate water, oil, or other fluids through wells to remove sand or other materials obstructing the free flow of oil.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pump operation cycle to clear an obstructed well, including setup, monitoring, and shutdown; typically 60–120 minutes for a competent oilfield worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized oilfield pump equipment, reading gauges, adjusting valves, and reacting to dynamic downhole conditions in a hazardous outdoor environment. Current humanoids lack the tool-use, domain-specific controls interface, and situational judgment for this task.

task: Identify tire size and ply and inflate tires accordingly.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reading the tire sidewall markings on all four tires and inflating each to spec. A competent worker takes ~2–3 min per tire including connecting/disconnecting the inflator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading curved sidewall text (perception challenge), identifying ply/load rating, locating valve stems, attaching an inflator hose precisely, and controlling pressure — multi-step fine manipulation around a vehicle that current humanoids lack the dexterity and tool-use reliability for.

task: Signal transportation operators to stop or to proceed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one signal event (stop or proceed gesture/flag to a vehicle/operator). A competent flagger or ground crew member completes this in ~1–3 minutes including positioning and acknowledgment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor unstructured environments, precise situational awareness of moving vehicles, real-time decision-making on traffic/operational context, and clear human-readable gestures or flag signals. Current humanoids lack reliable outdoor mobility, vehicle-context perception, and standardized signaling capability at acceptable safety margins.

task: Mount, balance, change, or check condition or pressure of tires.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one tire on a passenger vehicle. A competent technician mounts, balances, and checks one tire in ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a hydraulic jack, torque wrench, tire mounting machine, and balancer — specialized heavy tools a humanoid cannot reliably operate. High-force dexterous steps (breaking bead, seating tire) exceed current capability.

task: Develop and print exposed film, using chemicals, touch-up tools, and developing and printing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = developing one roll of film and making a set of prints. Includes chemical mixing, agitation, fixing, washing, enlarger printing, and drying — typically 60–120 min for a competent darkroom technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise chemical handling, timed agitation in darkness, fine motor control of enlarger and touch-up tools, temperature regulation, and sequential multi-step judgment. Far beyond current general-purpose robot capability in an unstructured darkroom environment.

task: Compound, package, and label pharmaceutical products, under direction of pharmacist.
human_time_rationale: One instance = compounding, packaging, and labeling a single prescription order. A trained pharmacy tech typically takes 15–35 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement of pharmaceutical compounds, sterile handling, fine dexterity for small containers/labels, regulatory-grade accuracy, and pharmacist-directed judgment. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured pharmacy settings.

task: Grade and sort products according to factors such as color, species, length, width, appearance, feel, smell, and quality to ensure correct processing and usage.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grading and sorting one product/item. A competent worker inspects color, size, texture, smell, and quality, then routes it correctly — typically 2–4 minutes per item.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle vision-based color/size sorting well, but multi-modal grading (smell, feel, species ID, nuanced appearance) remains very difficult for general-purpose humanoids. Unstructured conveyor/bin environments add further challenges.

task: Maintain and clean equipment, work areas, or shelves.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning/maintaining one equipment unit, work area, or shelf section. A competent worker wipes, inspects, and tidies a typical station in ~20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can sweep, wipe flat surfaces, and move items at moderate reliability indoors. However, varied clutter, equipment-specific maintenance steps, reaching into tight spaces, and quality judgment reduce success. Cleaning alone scores ~40–70%; maintenance/inspection complexity pulls it lower.

task: Perform general maintenance tasks for funeral homes, such as maintaining equipment or caring for funeral grounds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full daily/weekly maintenance round at a funeral home: checking equipment, minor repairs, and grounds care (mowing, tidying). Typical competent groundskeeper/maintenance worker takes ~90 min.
robot_success_rationale: Task spans outdoor unstructured terrain, varied equipment inspection, grounds care (mowing, pruning), and sensitive indoor spaces. Current humanoids lack outdoor navigation reliability, tool generalization, and heavy equipment operation needed for an acceptable full round.

task: Disassemble mechanical or electrical locking devices, and repair or replace worn tumblers, springs, and other parts, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassemble, repair, and reassemble one lock. A skilled locksmith takes 30–60 min depending on lock type and part condition.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to handle tiny tumblers, springs, and pins; diagnostic judgment on worn parts; precise tool use in constrained spaces. Far beyond current general-purpose robot manipulation capabilities.

task: Load machines with materials such as blank paper or film.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a single machine (e.g., printer/copier) with a ream of paper or roll of film; a competent worker does this in ~5 minutes including alignment checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of flexible/thin materials (paper, film) into trays or feeders, fine dexterity to fan pages and seat them correctly, and generalization across varied machine types — challenging for current humanoids.

task: Perform minor cosmetic procedures, such as chin and cheekbone enhancements.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single cosmetic procedure (e.g., dermal filler injection for chin or cheekbone). A competent aesthetic practitioner typically completes consultation, prep, and injection in ~45–75 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme sub-millimeter precision, real-time anatomical judgment, sterile technique, and patient interaction. Far beyond current robot dexterity and medical-context generalization; legally and ethically restricted. Near-zero feasibility.

task: Install replacement glass in vehicles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = replacing one vehicle glass pane. Skilled technician takes 45–75 min including removal, surface prep, adhesive application, and fitting.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating in/around a vehicle in variable outdoor settings, handling large fragile glass panels with suction tools, applying precise urethane beads, and working in tight spaces — far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Place incoming or outgoing letters or packages into sacks or bins based on destination or type, and place identifying tags on sacks or bins.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting and tagging one sack/bin of mail. A worker reads labels, places items, and attaches a tag in ~1–2 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Reading address labels (OCR/vision) is feasible, pick-and-place of varied parcels/letters is moderately capable, but attaching physical ID tags and handling irregular, floppy mail items reliably remains challenging for current humanoids.

task: Deliver medication to treatment areas, living units, residences, or clinics, using various means of transportation.
human_time_rationale: One delivery run to a treatment area/unit/residence; includes retrieving medication, navigating to destination, and handing off — ~15–25 min median per trip.
robot_success_rationale: Indoor flat-floor navigation is feasible (~60–70%), but multi-site delivery spanning outdoor/vehicle transport, varied environments, secure hand-off to correct patient, and medication verification chain drops success sharply. No humanoid can drive between sites.

task: Release packages or letters to customers upon presentation of written notices or other identification.
human_time_rationale: One instance = verifying a customer's ID/notice and handing over a single package or letter. A competent clerk typically completes this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading/verifying ID documents (OCR + fraud detection), social interaction, locating the correct item, and handing it over. Current robots lack reliable document verification, nuanced social judgment, and generalized retrieval in varied storage environments.

task: Observe and listen to machinery operation to detect equipment malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one machine; a competent operator spends ~15 min observing and listening for abnormal sounds, vibrations, or behavior patterns.
robot_success_rationale: Current robots lack reliable multimodal fault-detection (acoustic, vibration, thermal, visual combined). Perception and generalization to novel machine types is poor; no deployed general-purpose robot does this autonomously at production quality.

task: Explain products or services and prices and demonstrate use of products.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single product explanation and live demonstration to a customer, covering features, pricing, and hands-on use. Typical retail/sales interaction ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fluid natural language explanation, adaptive social interaction, real-time Q&A, and nuanced product handling. Current humanoids lack conversational persuasion, contextual judgment, and reliable improvised demo dexterity for arbitrary products.

task: Create pen-and-paper images to be scanned, edited, colored, textured, or animated by computer.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one hand-drawn image created for scanning/editing. A competent illustrator typically spends 30–60 minutes per piece depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine artistic dexterity, creative intent, and controlled pen strokes far beyond current humanoid hand precision. No frontier robot can produce acceptable illustrative drawings autonomously; output quality would be rejected by a supervisor.

task: Load newly constructed teeth into porcelain furnaces to bake the porcelain onto the metal framework.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading a batch of porcelain-fused-to-metal teeth into a furnace tray and initiating the firing cycle. A skilled dental lab tech takes ~5–10 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely fine dexterity to handle tiny, fragile porcelain-coated dental frameworks without chipping or misaligning them on firing pegs. Precision placement on specialized trays, tool familiarity, and delicate force control far exceed current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Cut down and remove trees and brush to clear drill sites, to reduce fire hazards, and to make way for roads to sites.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing a single drill site or road segment of trees/brush. A competent crew with chainsaws and heavy equipment typically takes 3–5 hours per site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating chainsaws, feller-bunchers, bulldozers, or skidders in rugged outdoor terrain. Humanoid robots cannot operate heavy forestry/land-clearing machinery, handle large falling trees safely, or navigate dense unstructured wilderness autonomously at this scale.

task: Repair and adjust safes, vault doors, and vault components, using hand tools, lathes, drill presses, and welding and acetylene cutting apparatus.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing/adjusting a single safe or vault door. A competent locksmith/safe technician typically spends 1–3 hours diagnosing, disassembling, machining, welding, and reassembling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity with hand tools, operating lathes/drill presses/welding apparatus, diagnosing mechanical faults, and precise tolerances. Current humanoids lack the tool-use generalization, machining skill, and welding capability needed.

task: Lay out, measure, and mark dimensions and reference lines on material, such as roofing panels, using calculators, scribes, dividers, squares, or rulers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out and marking one roofing panel or similar material piece. A skilled tradesperson typically takes 8–15 minutes to measure, calculate, and scribe reference lines on a single panel.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor tool use (scribes, dividers, squares), accurate force control, reading measurements, and adapting to varied material surfaces. Current humanoids lack reliable dexterous tool-use precision and math-to-marking integration for tradework quality.

task: Place tooth models on an apparatus that mimics bite and movement of patient's jaw to evaluate functionality of model.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting and evaluating a single patient's dental models on an articulator. A skilled dental lab technician typically takes 15–25 minutes to mount, articulate, and assess occlusal function.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extremely precise fine dexterity to handle fragile plaster/stone models, align occlusal registrations, mount on an articulator, and interpret subtle bite dynamics. Far beyond current humanoid robot capability in unstructured dental lab settings.

task: Restock storage areas, replenishing items on shelves.
human_time_rationale: One restocking instance = filling one shelf section or zone from a cart/box. A competent worker takes ~15–25 min to retrieve stock, orient items, and place them neatly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied aisles, grasping diverse item shapes/sizes, reading labels for correct placement, and rotating stock — challenging for generalization. Pick-and-place improving but shelf-level accuracy and item variety still limit reliability.

task: Evaluate animals for trainability and ability to perform.
human_time_rationale: One instance = evaluating a single animal. A trained handler spends ~45 min observing temperament, responsiveness, physical ability, and scoring trainability.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced behavioral observation, interpretation of animal body language, stress signals, and temperament — tasks demanding deep domain expertise and social/empathic judgment far beyond current robot perception and cognition.

task: Cut new or duplicate keys, using key cutting machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting one key. A competent locksmith selects the blank, secures it in the machine, traces/cuts, and deburrs in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise key blank selection, secure fixture alignment in a specialized key-cutting machine, fine dexterous manipulation, and quality verification. Current humanoids lack the precision tool-use and machine-interfacing needed for reliable autonomous operation.

task: Deliver floral arrangements or other items to family members of the deceased.
human_time_rationale: One instance = delivering a single floral arrangement or item to one family member's location (e.g., home or funeral home). Includes travel, doorstep delivery, and brief interaction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires driving/navigating public roads to destination (humanoids cannot drive), outdoor terrain traversal, doorbell interaction, and sensitive social engagement with grieving individuals — all well beyond 2026 frontier robot capability.

task: Dismantle and repair oil field machinery, boilers, and steam engine parts, using hand tools and power tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = disassembling, repairing, and reassembling a single piece of oil field machinery or boiler component; a competent millwright/mechanic typically takes 2–5 hours per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-torque tool use, navigating cluttered industrial environments, diagnosing faults, and precise reassembly of heavy, varied components. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity, force control, and autonomous reasoning capabilities.

task: Visit trade shows, stores, community organizations, or other venues to demonstrate products or services or to answer questions from potential customers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single venue visit/demo event. A typical trade show booth shift or store demonstration runs ~3–4 hours including setup, demos, and Q&A.
robot_success_rationale: Requires traveling to external venues (driving, navigation), complex social interaction, persuasive communication, answering novel questions, and adapting to unstructured public environments — all well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Mail merchandise samples or promotional literature in response to requests.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fulfilling a single mailing request: retrieving items, packaging, labeling, and depositing for shipment. ~10 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step tool use: retrieving varied items, folding/stuffing envelopes/boxes, printing/applying labels, and navigating to a mail drop. Current humanoids lack reliable label printing integration, fine paper-handling dexterity, and end-to-end workflow autonomy.

task: Install safes, vault doors, and deposit boxes according to blueprints, using equipment such as power drills, taps, dies, truck cranes, and dollies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a single safe or vault door per blueprint. Includes positioning, anchoring, drilling, and hardware fitting; typically 1–3 hours for a skilled installer.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating truck cranes and dollies to move heavy loads, precision blueprint-following, high-torque power tool use, and fine threading with taps/dies — all far beyond 2026 humanoid capability. Heavy equipment operation is a hard blocker.

task: Prepare metal surfaces for bonding with porcelain to create artificial teeth, using small hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing the metal substructure (coping/framework) for a single artificial tooth unit; a skilled dental lab technician takes ~15–25 minutes for sandblasting, cleaning, opaquing prep, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity on tiny, irregular dental castings (mm-scale), precise tactile/force control with specialized hand tools, and expert-level inspection. Far beyond current humanoid robot fine-manipulation capability.

task: Dress in sample or completed garments, and select accessories.
human_time_rationale: One instance = dressing in one garment and selecting/attaching matching accessories. A fashion worker or fit model takes ~10 min including fastening, adjusting, and accessory selection.
robot_success_rationale: Dressing oneself requires extreme dexterity (buttons, zippers, fabric manipulation on a body), aesthetic judgment for accessory selection, and body-awareness. These are well beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in unstructured settings.

task: Monitor sound wave-generating or detecting mechanisms to determine well fluid levels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = monitoring a single well's acoustic fluid-level device, reading output, and logging results. A competent technician typically takes ~15–25 minutes per well visit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized oil-field acoustic equipment (e.g., echometer gun) at a wellhead in outdoor/rough terrain, interpreting waveform data, and adjusting settings. Current humanoids lack the tools, domain software integration, and rugged field mobility for this.

task: Prepare wax bite blocks and impression trays for use.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing bite blocks and trays for one patient; dental assistant softens wax, shapes blocks, selects and sizes impression trays in ~10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of soft/pliable wax, precise shaping to dental standards, correct tray sizing by visual/tactile judgment, and use of specialized dental tools — far beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Assist mechanics and perform various mechanical duties, such as changing oil or checking and replacing batteries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one service job (e.g., oil change or battery swap). A competent mechanic's assistant completes a typical task in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Automotive service requires high-force torque, under-vehicle crawling, tool selection, fluid handling, and tight-space dexterity. Current humanoids lack the specialized tool use, force control, and confined-space mobility needed for reliable autonomous success.

task: Wet surface to prepare for bonding, fill holes and cracks with grout or slurry, and smooth, using trowel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing one typical surface section (~10 sq ft): wetting, filling cracks with grout/slurry, and trowel-smoothing to acceptable finish.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step sequencing, fluid handling, precise trowel pressure/angle on varied surface geometry, and tactile feedback to achieve smooth finish — well beyond current humanoid dexterity and force-control capabilities.

task: Apply hardening and sealing compounds to cure surface of concrete, and waterproof or restore surface.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating a typical concrete surface area (e.g., ~100 sq ft). A competent worker preps, applies, and spreads compound in ~45 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires handling liquid/viscous compounds, operating sprayers or rollers on uneven outdoor surfaces, judging coverage quality, and navigating construction environments — well beyond current humanoid generalization and tool-use capability.

task: Waterproof or restore concrete surfaces, using appropriate compounds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating a typical concrete surface section (e.g., a floor or wall area ~20–30 sq ft): surface prep, mixing/applying compound, and finishing. Competent worker takes ~90 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires surface assessment, mixing compounds to correct consistency, applying sealants/patching material evenly with appropriate tools, and adapting to varied surface conditions. Current robots lack the nuanced force control, material handling, and situational judgment for quality results.

task: Calculate dimensions or tolerances, using instruments, such as micrometers or vernier calipers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring a single part/feature with a micrometer or caliper, recording the reading. A competent machinist typically takes 3–7 minutes including setup and notation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor grip on small instruments, accurate jaw placement on a part feature, and reading an analog/digital scale. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter dexterity and tactile feedback needed for reliable, repeatable metrology-grade measurements.

task: Clear jams in sortation equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing a single jam event. A worker walks to the jammed sorter, diagnoses the blockage, removes the item(s), and restores operation — typically 5–12 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating a dynamic warehouse, diagnosing an unpredictable jam, applying variable force to extract oddly wedged items, and safely restoring equipment — challenging perception, dexterity, and generalization; near-moving machinery adds safety risk.

task: Inspect fire suppression systems or portable fire systems to ensure proper working order.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one fire suppression system or portable extinguisher; technician checks pressure gauges, seals, mounting, tags, and logs findings in ~20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied environments, reading small gauges/labels, checking tamper seals, manipulating inspection tags, and making safety-critical judgments. Current robots lack reliable fine perception, judgment, and documentation capability for this compliance task.

task: Inventory, pack, and remove items left behind by former residents.
human_time_rationale: One instance = clearing one typical residential unit. Inventorying, boxing, and removing miscellaneous left-behind items takes a competent worker roughly 60–120 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires cataloguing arbitrary unknown objects, dexterous packing of varied items, navigating cluttered multi-room spaces, and loading boxes — all highly unstructured. Current robots lack reliable generalization, perception of novel objects, and the dexterous/heavy manipulation needed end-to-end.

task: Operate controls that raise derricks or level rigs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = raising a derrick or leveling a rig from start to secured position. A competent driller/operator typically takes 20–45 minutes depending on rig type and site conditions.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized heavy-equipment controls in a hazardous outdoor environment, interpreting rig-specific instrumentation, and making safety-critical judgments. No humanoid robot has relevant capability for this domain; generalization and tool-use fall far short.

task: Embalm, dress, cosmeticize, and casket the deceased.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single deceased individual. Embalming alone takes ~60–90 min; dressing, cosmetizing, and casketing add ~30–60 min more.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (arterial embalming, suturing, cosmetic application), precise manipulation of a limp, irregular human body, specialized chemical tools, and high-stakes quality standards. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Create a model of patient's mouth by pouring plaster into a dental impression and allowing plaster to set.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pouring plaster into one impression tray and waiting for it to set (~10 min prep/pour + ~30 min set, but active human time is ~10–15 min; setting is passive).
robot_success_rationale: Requires mixing plaster to correct consistency, precise pouring into a small irregular impression tray without spillage or bubbles, and careful handling of a fragile mold — demanding fine dexterity, fluid control, and tactile feedback well beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Escort patients to medical appointments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = escorting one patient from their room/waiting area to an appointment location and returning. Includes transit, wait, and handoff; typical hospital escort ~20–40 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex hospital environments, safely guiding or transporting a patient (mobility aids, wheelchairs), social communication, and responding to unpredictable patient needs. Far beyond current humanoid generalization and caregiving capability.

task: Place products in containers according to grade and mark grades on containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = placing one product into the correct graded container and marking/labeling that container. Repetitive but simple per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires visual grade assessment, fine dexterous marking/labeling, and correct container selection. Perception-based grading and marker/label writing are still weak points for 2026 humanoids; pick-and-place alone is feasible but grading + marking adds complexity.

task: Cut copies apart and write identifying information, such as page numbers or titles, on copies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting apart one set of copies and labeling them (e.g., a single print job batch). A competent worker completes this in ~3 minutes with scissors and a pen.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise cutting along lines (fine dexterity with scissors), then legible handwriting on paper — both near-frontier limits for humanoids. Perception of cut lines is feasible, but controlled scissor use and writing remain very low-success tasks in 2026.

task: Select or cut trees according to markings or sizes, types, or grades.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting/cutting one tree. A skilled logger identifies markings/grade, positions equipment, and fells the tree in ~10–20 min depending on size.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor uneven forest terrain navigation, visual identification of tree markings/grades, and operating a chainsaw or feller-buncher. Current humanoids lack the mobility, tool-use, and perception for safe autonomous forestry work.

task: Adjust guides, rollers, loose card inserters, weighing machines, and tying arms, using rules and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full adjustment pass on one machine (guides, rollers, inserters, etc.) using rules and hand tools; a skilled operator typically takes 15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement with rules, context-specific tool selection, fine torque control on varied fasteners, and diagnostic judgment on multiple interacting machine components — far beyond current general-purpose robot dexterity and generalization.

task: Operate, control, or monitor gasifiers or related equipment, such as coolers, water quenches, water gas shifts reactors, or sulfur recovery units, to produce syngas or electricity from coal.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one 8-hour operating shift monitoring and controlling a gasifier system; competent operators work continuous shift cycles managing process parameters.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating complex industrial control consoles, interpreting dynamic process data, adjusting valves/controls on large specialized equipment, and responding to alarms — far beyond current humanoid robot generalization, dexterity, and process-control capabilities.

task: Accept and check containers of mail or parcels from large volume mailers, couriers, and contractors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = accepting and checking one container/lot from a single mailer or courier; verifying count, condition, documentation, and logging typically takes ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading labels/manifests, cross-referencing documentation, social interaction with couriers, and nuanced judgment on acceptance/rejection. Current robots lack reliable OCR-in-context, document handling, and workflow decision-making at this complexity.

task: Do facility laundry and clean, organize, maintain, and disinfect animal quarters, such as pens and stables, and equipment, such as saddles and bridles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full cycle for one pen/stable plus associated equipment (saddle, bridle). A competent stable hand typically takes ~1 hour including laundry, mucking, scrubbing, and tack cleaning.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor/semi-structured barn environments, heavy mucking tools, flexible tack with straps and buckles, wet disinfection spraying, and laundry handling all compound difficulty. Current humanoids lack reliable manipulation for this unstructured multi-step task.

task: Examine products purchased for resale or received for storage to assess the condition of each product or item.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one product/item upon receipt. A competent worker spends ~1–5 minutes checking condition, packaging integrity, labeling, and any damage.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle basic visual inspection and pick-and-place, but assessing nuanced damage, deformation, odor, or labeling across highly varied product types requires broad generalization and judgment that current systems lack reliably.

task: Position safety jacks to support underground mine roofs until bolts can be installed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning one safety jack under a mine roof section: carrying, placing, and extending it to contact and support the roof. ~15 min for a competent miner.
robot_success_rationale: Underground mines are dark, confined, uneven, and hazardous. Robots lack mobility on rough mine floors, cannot reliably assess roof contact pressure, and must operate heavy jacking equipment safely. No deployed humanoid is rated for this environment.

task: Cut and file pieces of jewelry such as rings, brooches, bracelets, and lockets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting and filing a single jewelry piece (e.g., a ring). A skilled jeweler typically spends 15–35 minutes per piece depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity with jeweler's saws and files on tiny, irregular metal pieces. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter precision, tool control, and tactile feedback needed for acceptable jewelry work.

task: Perform slit lamp biomicroscopy procedures to diagnose disorders of the eye, such as retinitis, presbyopia, cataracts, or retinal detachment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient examination. A competent optometrist/ophthalmologist typically spends 10–20 minutes performing a slit lamp exam including setup, adjustment, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating a precision optical instrument, aligning a cooperative patient, performing fine dexterous adjustments, interpreting subtle ocular pathology, and making clinical diagnoses. Far beyond current humanoid robot perception and dexterity capabilities.

task: Fit and assemble parts to make or repair machine tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting and assembling parts for one machine tool repair/build. A competent machinist typically spends 1–3 hours on fitting, aligning, and assembling components.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-precision mechanical fitting, scraping, shimming, and torquing of heavy/complex parts. Demands expert tactile feedback, fine dexterity, and adaptive problem-solving far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Unload mixtures into containers or onto conveyors for further processing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unloading a single batch/vessel of mixture into a container or onto a conveyor; a competent worker typically completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Pouring/scooping semi-fluid mixtures requires precise force control and spill avoidance. Robots can handle structured pick-and-place but unstructured mixtures, variable container shapes, and conveyor alignment reduce reliability significantly.

task: Monitor patient intra-operative status, including patient position, vital signs, or volume and color of blood.
human_time_rationale: One instance = monitoring throughout a typical surgical procedure (~2 hours). A scrub nurse/surgical tech continuously tracks position, vitals, and blood loss for the duration.
robot_success_rationale: Requires integrating multi-modal clinical data (vitals, blood color/volume, patient positioning), real-time medical judgment, sterile field awareness, and communication with surgical team — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities. No deployed system approaches this.

task: Move controls, buttons, or levers to start blowers and regulate flow of materials through nozzles.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single setup/adjustment cycle of operating controls, buttons, or levers to start blowers and tune material flow. A competent operator completes this in ~3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can press large buttons but struggle with lever-style controls, precise knob/dial adjustment, and interpreting unlabeled industrial panel layouts. Varied control types, force feedback, and real-time flow regulation reduce success significantly.

task: Obtain, set up, and load videotapes for scheduled productions or broadcasts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = locating, retrieving, and physically loading a videotape into a deck/player for a single scheduled production or broadcast. Includes labeling/verification steps.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of small tape cassettes, precise insertion into narrow deck slots, operating specialized broadcast equipment controls, and reading labels — all in varied, unstructured broadcast environments beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Turn cranks or press buttons to activate winches that move cars under sawing cables or saw frames.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning one car under a saw via winch controls. Turning cranks/pressing buttons to move a car into place typically takes a few minutes per cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Pressing buttons is feasible (~60-70%), but turning large industrial cranks requires significant torque and precision. Coordinating winch speed with saw alignment in an unstructured, hazardous quarry/mill environment greatly reduces success probability.

task: Mount hairsprings and balance wheel assemblies between jaws of truing calipers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mounting one hairspring & balance wheel assembly into truing calipers. A skilled watchmaker takes ~5–10 min for careful alignment and seating.
robot_success_rationale: Extreme sub-millimeter dexterity required; hairsprings are fragile, tiny (~5–15mm diameter), and require precise tactile feedback. Far beyond current humanoid fine-motor capability. Risk of instant part destruction is near-certain.

task: Close incisions, using needles and sutures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = closing a single surgical incision. A competent surgeon or resident typically spends 10–30 minutes suturing, depending on length and technique; ~20 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, precise force feedback, sterile tool handling, and adaptive judgment on tissue tension. Current humanoid robots lack the sub-millimeter precision, haptic sensitivity, and certified medical-grade control needed for suturing.

task: Attach wires between controllers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = routing and connecting a single wire harness between two controllers; a skilled technician identifies ports, routes the wire, and secures connectors in ~5 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise connector insertion into specific ports (fine dexterity), cable routing in constrained spaces, and port identification. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-finger manipulation and connector-alignment accuracy for arbitrary wiring tasks.

task: Perform final touch-ups with sandpaper or steel wool.
human_time_rationale: One instance = finishing a single surface/piece. A skilled worker spends ~10 min on light sanding/steel-wool buffing to achieve acceptable smoothness and finish quality.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced force control, tactile feedback, and visual inspection to judge finish quality. Current robots lack reliable fine-pressure regulation and surface-quality perception for consistent, supervisor-acceptable results.

task: Evaluate new supplies and equipment to ensure operability in specific laboratory settings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = evaluating a single new supply/equipment item in a lab setting: unboxing, physical inspection, functional test, and documentation. ~45 min for a competent lab technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert domain knowledge to judge operability, read specs/labels, run functional tests on specialized lab equipment, and make pass/fail decisions. Current robots lack lab-domain reasoning, fine instrument manipulation, and contextual judgment needed.

task: Test operation of engines and other systems, using test equipment, such as ignition analyzers, compression checkers, distributor timers, or ammeters.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full engine/systems diagnostic on one vehicle. A competent mechanic typically spends 30–60 min connecting tools, running tests, and interpreting results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires connecting specialized diagnostic tools (ignition analyzers, ammeters, compression checkers) to specific ports, interpreting analog/digital readings, and diagnosing faults. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tool-use generalization, and diagnostic reasoning for autonomous success.

task: Splice broken or separated film and mount film on reels.
human_time_rationale: One instance = splicing one break and mounting one reel. A skilled film technician locates the break, trims, aligns, and tapes/cements the splice, then mounts the reel in ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine-motor dexterity to align thin film frames precisely, apply splice tape or cement accurately, and thread onto a reel. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity; high failure risk on delicate, thin media.

task: Remove extra compound after surfaces have been covered sufficiently.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing excess compound from one surface/section (e.g., a drywall panel or body panel). A competent worker scrapes and smooths a section in ~8 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced force-controlled scraping, visual judgment of "sufficient" coverage, and tool handling on varied surface geometries. Current robots lack the fine tactile feedback and surface-quality perception needed for acceptable results.

task: Unload and position prefabricated steel units for hoisting, as needed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unloading and positioning one prefab steel unit ready for hoisting; a skilled ironworker crew typically takes 30–60 min per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating forklifts/cranes, rigging heavy steel (hundreds to thousands of kg), navigating an active construction site, and precise load positioning — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Administer medications or treatments, such as catheterizations, suppositories, irrigations, enemas, massages, or douches, as directed by a physician or nurse.
human_time_rationale: One instance = administering a single treatment (e.g., enema, catheterization, or massage) to one patient; median across procedure types is ~15–25 minutes including prep, administration, and cleanup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for sterile instrument insertion, nuanced force control on soft human tissue, real-time patient response assessment, and clinical judgment — all far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities. Safety constraints also prohibit autonomous medical procedures.

task: Replace existing antennas with new antennas as directed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing one old antenna and installing one new antenna. Includes disconnecting cables, unmounting, mounting new unit, and reconnecting. Typical for a telecom or electronics technician.
robot_success_rationale: Antenna replacement involves outdoor/elevated environments, precise cable reconnection, torque-specific fastening, and varied mounting hardware. Humanoids lack safe high-reach mobility and fine connector-mating reliability needed here.

task: Assist passengers, such as elderly or individuals with disabilities, on and off bus, ensure they are seated properly, help carry baggage, and answer questions about bus schedules or routes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting one passenger boarding/alighting, seating, baggage, and a brief Q&A. Typical interaction takes ~5–10 minutes end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated physical support of unpredictable human bodies, social interaction, real-time speech Q&A, and navigating a dynamic bus environment. Current humanoids lack safe human-contact caregiving, reliable NLP-to-schedule lookup, and mobile dexterity in crowded transit settings.

task: Operate ophthalmic equipment, such as autorefractors, phoropters, tomographs, or retinoscopes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = operating one piece of ophthalmic equipment on one patient (e.g., autorefractor or phoropter exam). A competent optometric technician completes this in ~10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor alignment of delicate instruments with a patient's eye, real-time adaptive interaction with a responsive human, verbal communication, and clinical judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and patient-interaction capabilities.

task: Lay and set mosaic tiles to create decorative wall, mural, and floor designs.
human_time_rationale: One instance = completing one mosaic panel/section (~1–2 sq ft of decorative design). A competent tile setter spends ~3–4 hours planning layout, cutting, adhering, and grouting a detailed mosaic section.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-dexterous placement of dozens of small irregular tiles, artistic pattern interpretation, tile cutting, adhesive application, and grouting—all demanding perception, fine manipulation, and creative judgment far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Align and secure pattern film on reference tables of optical programmers, and observe enlarger scope views of printed circuit boards.
human_time_rationale: One instance = aligning and securing one pattern film on a reference table and inspecting one PCB via enlarger scope. A skilled technician takes ~5–10 min per board.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sub-millimeter precision film alignment on a specialized optical reference table, fine dexterous securing, and interpreting enlarger scope imagery of PCBs — all highly specialized, precision tasks far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Direct or escort mourners to parlors or chapels in which wakes or funerals are being held.
human_time_rationale: One instance = guiding one mourner or group from entrance to the correct parlor/chapel. A competent funeral home attendant completes this in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can navigate indoors and gesture, but lacks social sensitivity, reliable natural-language interaction, and the nuanced human judgment needed to handle grieving individuals with appropriate decorum and adaptability in an unstructured social setting.

task: Operate cutting torches and welding equipment, while working with conduit and metal components to construct devices associated with electrical functions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating a single conduit/metal electrical component assembly. A skilled tradesperson typically takes 45–90 minutes per unit, median ~60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating open-flame/plasma cutting torches and welding equipment — extreme heat, precise torch control, and safety demands far exceed current humanoid dexterity and tool-use capabilities. No commercial deployment exists for autonomous robotic torch/welding operations in unstructured settings.

task: Apply sizing to seal surfaces and maximize adhesion of coverings to surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying sizing to one surface area (e.g., one wall section or workpiece). A competent worker takes ~20 min to prep, apply, and spread sizing evenly.
robot_success_rationale: Requires controlled liquid application with a brush/roller, surface pressure calibration, and even coverage judgment. Current robots struggle with fluid tool-use, force modulation, and quality inspection of coating uniformity.

task: Assemble studio sets and select and arrange cameras, film stock, audio, or lighting equipment to be used during filming.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up a studio set for a single shoot. A competent crew member takes ~1–3 hours to assemble set pieces and arrange A/V/lighting equipment for a typical production setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires high-level creative/technical judgment (selecting equipment, composing lighting), fine manipulation of fragile/complex gear, interpreting director intent, and navigating cluttered sets. Well beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities.

task: Clean and sanitize athletic training rooms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning & sanitizing one athletic training room. Includes wiping tables, mopping floors, sanitizing equipment. A competent worker typically takes 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Basic sweeping/mopping feasible (~50%), but athletic training rooms require sanitizing complex equipment (taping tables, hydrocollators, TENS units), precise chemical application, and navigating cluttered layouts — well beyond current reliable autonomy.

task: Apply heat to plastic panels, using hot-air welding guns or immersion in hot water, and press the softened panels back into shape by hand.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reshaping one plastic panel. Heating with a hot-air gun or water bath, monitoring softness, then pressing back to shape takes ~10–20 min for a skilled technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced thermal sensing to judge plastic softness, safe manipulation of a hot-air welding gun, and precise bimanual hand-pressing guided by tactile/visual feedback — all well beyond current humanoid robot capability.

task: Photograph layouts, using camera, to make layout prints for supervisors or clients.
human_time_rationale: One instance = photographing a single layout setup. Includes positioning, framing, adjusting lighting/camera settings, and taking several shots. ~10–20 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise camera framing, aesthetic judgment, lighting adjustment, and intentional composition. Current humanoids lack fine camera-control skills, nuanced visual quality assessment, and the ability to operate DSLR/studio camera controls reliably.

task: Start machine by engaging controls.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting one machine via its controls (switches, buttons, levers). A competent worker locates, identifies, and engages the correct controls in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can press buttons and flip switches on known, well-structured panels, but arbitrary machine controls vary widely in layout, force, and sequence. Generalization to unfamiliar control interfaces is poor; dexterity and perception limit reliability on levers/knobs.

task: Load and unload baggage in baggage compartments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading/unloading all bags for a single aircraft. A ground crew team takes ~20–40 min per flight; ~30 min median for a competent worker's share.
robot_success_rationale: Highly unstructured: varied bag sizes/weights, cramped hold, awkward postures, ramp/conveyor belt navigation, time pressure. Current humanoids lack the robust dexterity, confined-space mobility, and load-handling reliability needed.

task: Connect hoses or lines to pumps or other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting a single hose/line to a pump or fitting. A competent worker locates the port, aligns the fitting, and secures it (hand-tighten + wrench) in ~5–10 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of threaded or push-fit couplings, torque control, leak-free sealing, and tool use (wrench). High variability in hose types and orientations; current robots lack reliable fine dexterity and torque sensing for plumbing connections.

task: Send product samples to laboratories for analysis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and dispatching a batch of product samples to a lab: labeling, packaging, completing paperwork/forms, and handing off to courier or mail. Typical time for a competent worker is ~30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step coordination: identifying correct samples, labeling, secure packaging, form-filling, and interfacing with a courier system. Generalizing across varied sample types, containers, and paperwork is far beyond current humanoid robots' autonomous capability.

task: Comply with traffic regulations to operate vehicles in a safe and courteous manner.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one typical local driving trip (~5–15 miles). A competent licensed driver completes this in ~15–25 minutes including navigation and traffic stops.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot physically operate standard passenger vehicles in live traffic. L4 AVs exist but are narrow-domain, mapped-route systems — not general-purpose humanoid robots. No 2026 general-purpose robot meets this requirement.

task: Examine footwear, tire tracks, or other types of impressions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = examining one impression (footwear, tire, etc.), covering visual inspection, documentation, and initial analysis; ~15–25 min for a trained technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced forensic perception (fine pattern detail, depth, substrate variation), contextual judgment, and careful documentation. Current robots lack forensic-grade visual analysis, delicate non-disturbance handling, and expert interpretation capabilities.

task: Inspect pulleys, drive belts, guards, or fences on machines to ensure that machines will operate safely.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full inspection of one machine's pulleys, belts, guards, and fences. A competent worker visually and manually checks all components in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual inspection of wear, tension, alignment, and damage on varied machine configurations. Current robots lack reliable fine-detail defect detection, understanding of acceptable tolerances, and dexterous probing of guards in unstructured industrial settings.

task: Determine sampling locations and methods, and collect water or wastewater samples for analysis, preserving samples with appropriate containers and preservation methods.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting a sampling location, collecting a water/wastewater sample, and preserving it in the correct container. A competent technician typically takes ~30–60 minutes per site visit/sample set.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating variable outdoor/industrial environments, operating valves and sampling taps, selecting correct chemical preservatives and containers, and exercising regulatory judgment — far beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities. Outdoor terrain, wet conditions, and complex decision-making crush success probability.

task: Cut or bore openings in building or surrounding concrete, access infested areas, insert nozzle, and inject pesticide to impregnate ground.
human_time_rationale: One treatment session: locating access points, cutting/boring openings in concrete, inserting nozzle, and injecting pesticide into an infested zone typically takes ~45 min for a competent pest control technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy cutting/boring tools on concrete, navigating cluttered/confined spaces, locating hidden infestation zones, and precisely inserting a nozzle — far beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity, tool-use, and unstructured-environment generalization.

task: Inspect premises to assess the need for repairs and to ensure that climate and pest control issues are addressed.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting one premises (e.g., apartment or small commercial unit) for repairs, climate, and pest issues. A competent inspector typically takes 30–60 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating complex unstructured spaces, detecting subtle damage, pest signs, odors, moisture, and HVAC issues. Current robots lack multi-modal sensory integration, contextual judgment, and reliable locomotion across varied premises layouts.

task: Collect samples of dust, gases, vapors, or other potentially toxic materials for analysis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting one set of samples at a site. A competent industrial hygienist sets up sampling equipment, labels containers, and documents collection in ~30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of small vials, filters, pumps, and calibrated instruments; precise placement in varied environments; complex multi-step protocol adherence. Current humanoids lack the dexterity and domain-specific tool-use reliability needed.

task: Mix pesticides, herbicides, or fungicides for application to trees, shrubs, lawns, or botanical crops.
human_time_rationale: One instance = mixing a single batch of chemical solution. A trained worker reads the label, measures concentrates, dilutes in a tank/sprayer, and confirms the mix — typically 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid measurement of hazardous chemicals, reading labels, operating pour spouts/valves, and confirming ratios. Current robots lack reliable fine liquid handling, chemical perception, and safety compliance in unstructured outdoor/shed settings.

task: Investigate hazardous conditions or spills or outbreaks of disease or food poisoning, collecting samples for analysis.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single site investigation (hazmat spill, disease outbreak, or food poisoning scene); includes assessment, sample collection, labeling, and documentation. Typically 60–120 min on-site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires expert adaptive reasoning, unstructured hazardous environments, fine dexterous sampling (swabs, vials, air monitors), situational judgment, and complex decision-making far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Lift, push, and swing nozzles, hoses, and tubes to direct spray over designated areas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = spraying a designated area (e.g., a section of field, surface, or room). A competent worker typically takes ~15 min per area/zone.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can lift and move hoses but sustained directional control of flexible, heavy hoses with precise spray coverage is challenging. Perception of "designated area" coverage, hose management, and force feedback for nozzle direction limit success.

task: Spray or dust chemical solutions, powders, or gases into rooms, onto clothing, furnishings, or wood, or over marshlands, ditches, or catch basins.
human_time_rationale: One instance = treating one room or 1000 sq ft area with spraying/dusting equipment, including setup, application, and basic cleanup.
